modules.moduleContent: The purpose of this course is to equip students with an explanation of management accounting as a basis for developing the tools and knowledge required to meet management information needs. Students will study the organisational content of management accounting; cost accumulation for inventory valuation and profit measurement; decision making; information for planning control and performance measurement, and ethics.
learningOutcomes: Describe and evaluate the management accountant’s role, organizational environment, accounting information systems and possible ethical issues they face.
Define and apply cost and revenue classification, terms and behavioural patterns for planning, control, decision making and evaluation.
Apply and evaluate appropriate cost accounting techniques to identified circumstances.
To describe and evaluate nature, source and purpose of management information, information systems and how they contribute to the decision-making process.
Select, generate, explain and interpret decision making techniques to evaluate business choices.
Select and generate appropriate investment appraisal techniques and justify course of action considering relevant non-financial factors and financial options.

Describe and evaluate the role of budgets and budgetary control as a tool to enable organizations to develop and assess performance and use budgetary techniques to prepare budgets and financial plans.

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to provide students with an understanding of the theoretical and legal framework of taxation. It aims to give students a thorough understanding of the manner in which individuals and businesses are taxed in Ireland. It addresses the application of legislation, practices and procedures governing  routine aspects of Income Tax, Local Property Tax, Corporation Tax, Capital Gains Tax and VAT in Ireland.
learningOutcomes: Evaluate the theoretical framework of taxation and the role of taxation in the Irish economy;
Apply the general principles for administration and computation of Income Tax, Local Property Tax, Corporation Tax and Capital Gains Tax;
Identify income that is subject to Income Tax, Corporation Tax and Capital Gains Tax legislation;
Apply the appropriate allowances, reliefs and credits to Income, Income tax and Corporation Tax liabilities;
Apply the appropriate reliefs and exemptions to Capital Gains Tax and Local Property Tax;
Apply the general principles for administration and computation of VAT;
Demonstrate the ability to calculate a VAT liability for a business; and

modules.moduleContent: This module aims to enable students to work with large amounts of data using Microsoft Excel. Management and analysis of large data sets is a fact of life for the modern business and is applicable across the main domains of business, such as marketing, financial reporting, and operations management. This module will improve the level of Excel capability of students from the beginner to mid-Intermediate level. The students will learn how to manage and analyse large datasets to get key management insights into business processes of an organisation.
learningOutcomes: Store, clean and organise large datasets using Excel;
Analyse the data and choose appropriate analytical techniques for different types of data; and
Use insights from data analysis to make business decisions.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The aim of the module is to ensure students understand the context of financial management and the role of the financial manager. It will equip students with the knowledge and skill expected of a finance manager in investment, financing and dividend decisions of a company.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of the finance function, the organisation and the financial environment in which it operates. Assessing alternative sources of finance to meet  organizational finance requirements and to be able to determine the cost of borrowing;
Demonstrate an ability to understand and advise on working capital techniques;
Demonstrate an ability to use appropriate investment appraisal techniques;
Understand the elements contributing to capital structure decisions and the consequences of theories governing the debt/equity mix;
Appraise merger and acquisitions decisions including the calculation of the value of a business;
Understand the nature of dividend decision, alternative policies that companies can adopt and their significance to investors and alternatives to cash dividends;
Explain the foundations to portfolio theory and CAPM, understanding risk and risk diversification using portfolio investment; and

modules.moduleContent: This module will look at the legal, ethical and business environment in which audit and assurance is conducted, internal control and accounting systems, detailed audit testing, audit and assurance reporting and current issues in auditing
learningOutcomes: Recognise the legal and regulatory framework that governs audit practice in Ireland;
Apply knowledge of auditors’ ethical standards to situations which may arise in professional practice;
Demonstrate an awareness of current issues in auditing both in Ireland and in a global market;
Understand the stages in the audit process and the auditor’s role and responsibilities within each stage;
Demonstrate the ability to select and apply audit procedures to key Profit or Loss and Statement of Financial Position account balances; and
Understand the process of forming an audit opinion.

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to provide students with insights into how to engage in accounting research and ultimately develop skills as accounting researchers. Students will be assigned reading lists appropriate to the undertaking of research in accounting. Students will attend the School of Business research seminar series and present their research proposal at the PhD colloquium of the Irish Accounting and Finance Association.

Students must be registered on a structured doctoral programme with the School of Business.
learningOutcomes: Position their accounting research question in existing accounting research literature;
Clarify their proposed contribution to the field of accounting research; and
Prepare constructive feedback to peers and interpret and apply the feedback received from peers.

modules.moduleContent: Aim: This module is designed to enhance learning from semester one developing deeper critical reflection about the equality implications of education through a process of critical reflection on their educational journeys and biographical experiences of the education system. In semester two, the module allows students to learn more about community engagements and the effects of these engagements on education.
learningOutcomes: This module will be experiential and the teaching methods will be underpinned by democratic, facilitative, reflective and dialogical approaches.
Develop a deeper understanding of the social, egalitarian and political context of Irish education.
Critique how theories of social justice and equality add to our understanding of education.
Have a critical perspective on the theories and practices of equality, diversity and inclusion policies.
Enhance capacity for critical reflection about the social justice implications of education.
Give learners the opportunity to develop and appraise differing interpretations of equality in education.
Become more engaged with diverse communities as a student and a potential teacher.

modules.moduleName: COMMUNITY DRAMA ADULT, COMMUNITY AND FURTHER EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: This is a hands-on participatory module that uses a mixture of creative activities in its delivery.  These include theatre-based games and exercises, reflection and discussion.  In addition to introducing the field of participatory theatre to participants the module will also act as a forum for exploring issues of power, gender, colonisation and domestication in relation to education and the arts.
learningOutcomes: recognise the association of theories underpinning radical community drama with adult and community education practice
associate the principles of community drama with best practice in adult and community education
display competence in basic skills of community drama
employ community drama in adult and community education practice

modules.moduleName: SOCIAL ANALYSIS FOR ADDICTION STUDIES: BEYOND DEVIANCE IN MODERN SOCIETY
modules.moduleContent: In this course students will be introduced to some of the major theoretical perspectives in Sociology. Focusing on behaviours that are considered to fall outside of the norms in modern society, the course will cover the following topics: Functionalism - Addiction and Suicide; Crime and Drugs in a Capitalist Society; Symbolic Interactionism and Deviant Behaviour; Gender Stereotyping, Prostitution and Sex Work; Alcohol, Treatment and Recovery Movements.
learningOutcomes: Explain what is meant by the Sociological Imagination.
Apply the Sociological Imagination to some of the most pressing social issues of modern times.
Engage critically with a variety of theoretical concepts in sociology. Appreciate that personal troubles are also social issues
Explore the history of the alcohol, treatment and recovery movements from a sociological perspective.

modules.moduleContent:
On completion of this module, students will have completed their research thesis. 
To this end, they will have completed:
(a) Literature review 
(b)Statement of the issue(s) to be addressed in the MEd thesis
(c) identified the sources of data used in the research 
(d) identified their research approach 
(e) completed an analysis and findings from their research 
(f) identified future development, if appropriate

Research methodology; Presentation skills; writing skills
learningOutcomes:
Have completed their research thesis in Adult and Community Education. 
To this end, they will have completed:
(a) Literature review
(b) Statement of the issue(s) to be addressed in the MEd thesis
(c) identified the sources of data used in the research
(d) identified their research approach
(e) completed an analysis and findings from their research
(f) identified future development, if appropriate

modules.moduleContent: This module will provide the students the opportunity to develop their research methodologies and practice and to explore reflexivity in the context of research in adult and community education, education, lifelong learning, feminism, sociology and other related fields.
learningOutcomes: Explore their research methodologies and practice in an open and discursive environment.
Explore various aspects of research and writing in an open and discursive environment.

modules.moduleContent:
Research methodology; Presentation skills; writing skills

On completion of this module, students will have completed their research thesis. 
To this end, they will have completed:
(a) Literature review 
(b)Statement of the issue(s) to be addressed in the MEd thesis 
(c) identified the sources of data used in the research 
(d) identified their research approach 
(e) completed an analysis and findings from their research 
(f) identified future development, if appropriate
learningOutcomes: have completed their research thesis in Adult Guidance Counselling
have completed a literature review
have completed a statement of the issue(s) to be addressed in the MEd thesis
have identified the sources of data used in the research
have identified their research approach
have completed an analysis and findings from their research
have identified future development, if appropriate.

modules.moduleName: CRITICAL REFLECTIVE PRACTICE IN GLOBAL CITIZENSHIP EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent:
This module will engage adult, community and further education practitioners and others, with key texts on critical pedagogy, adult and community education, critical thinking, and reflexivity as it relates to global citizenship education. Arising, students will be able to better place topical issues of international development, development education and global citizenship education within their mainstream adult, community, and further education work and facilitate their students to accommodate these topics in their learning. The outcome will be to provide opportunities to facilitate social transformation in community and in educational outcomes. 
This module contributes to the UN Sustainable Development Goal four ((SDG 4) “Ensure inclusive and equitable quality education and promote lifelong learning opportunities for all” and particularly target 4.3 “By 2030, ensure equal access for all women and men to affordable and quality technical, vocational and tertiary education, including university.”
The module contributes to SDG 5 target 5.6. Gender equality, SDG 8 target 8.6. Decent work and sustainable growth, and SDG 13 target 13.3. Climate change mitigation.
learningOutcomes: Articulate their educational position on global citizenship education.
Apply global citizenship education to their critical practice.

Demonstrate a breadth of engagement with scholarly literature on critical pedagogy, adult and community education, critical thinking, and reflexivity as it relates to global citizenship education in the education of adults.
Demonstrate their conceptual capacity to develop their own approaches to reflective global citizenship education practice.
Applicants should note that personal attendance is required for this module.

modules.moduleContent: This module will provide an overview of research philosophies, methods and processes which will support students to develop their research. This module will present the theoretical underpinnings of mainstream quantitative and qualitative research methodologies within their historical and epistemological contexts.
learningOutcomes: be familiar with a range of appropriate social science research philosophies, methods and processes.
have enhanced capacity of applying these when undertaking new research and appraising existing research.

modules.moduleContent: This module will include a deepening of research capacities to engage in a systemic analysis of education and educational organisations and the wider political and policy contexts in which they exist.
learningOutcomes: have an appreciation and an understanding of the diverse traditions and approaches of different research methods in education.

have an appreciation and an understanding of theoretical and practical implications of different research philosophies and methodologies in relation to education as a site of social, political and cultural control and resistance.
have an appreciation and an understanding of the implications of using different research approaches for your research project.

modules.moduleContent: This module asks students to consider the practical implications of their epistemological and ontological research positions. It draws together learning from other modules on the programme and applies this learning to the students’ specific research projects.
learningOutcomes: Adopt a critical approach to research and to the self as researcher within both theoretical and practical contexts.
Undertake research in higher and adult education.
Critically analyse your research positioning and thinking in the education world.

modules.moduleContent: This module will support students to develop their capacity to use appropriate means and modes of reflecting on existing practitioner knowledge and how to design this in a way that can be embedded in their professional field of practice and within their community of practitioners.
learningOutcomes: Enhance their capacity to use appropriate means and modes of reflecting on existing practitioner knowledge
Demonstrate how they can design this capacity in a way that can be embedded in their professional field of practice and within their community of practitioners
Present a critically reflective analysis of their learning during this process designing research engagements in their field of practice

modules.moduleContent:
This module will:
• Review Irish and international literature on drugs and alcohol with specific reference to analysing the rationale for popular P and E initiatives.  
• Introduce students to critical thinking perspectives on various P and E strategies and initiatives ranging from Total abstinence to Harm Reduction including Primary, Secondary and Tertiary philosophies.
• Explore the role of various agencies and organisations, locally, nationally and internationally that are engaged in P and E work.
• Explore key issues in working with a diverse range of individuals, groups and ideals.
• Highlight key issues in course planning, design and implementation.
learningOutcomes: Describe the history of drug and alcohol education over the last four decades
Critically analyse  perspectives on  prevention and education strategies and initiatives ranging from total abstinence to harm-reduction philosophies
Critically appraise and apply theories of adult education
Describe and critically analyse the role and contribution of various agencies and organisations, at national to local level, in prevention and education work
Apply facilitation skills and adult education theories to the preparation and delivery of prevention and education initiatives.

modules.moduleName: STUDY SKILLS & STUDENT SUPPORT FOR ADDICTION STUDIES
modules.moduleContent:
Students will become familiar with the processes of Critical Reflective Thinking in the context addiction. Students will become familiar with academic study skills regarding Reading, Researching and Writing and Exam Preparation. Time Management; Stress Management. Communication skills, presentations, debating, group facilitation, leadership skills, team-working, co-production of workshop content and self directed learning tasks will all form part of personal academic development.
This module will afford a space and time to developing critical thinking faculties and will actively encourage reflection and discussion on the following indicative list of topics: Power, Privilege and Position; Education; Introduction to Discourse Analysis-Foucault; Writing Reflexively-Moon.

Learning and teaching styles; time management and techniques for effective learning; discourse analysis, interpreting texts, constructing arguments using primary and secondary source materials, using library reference systems; reading and recording skills; essay writing skills, completing projects, reviews, articles; technical writing skills i.e. bibliographies, citation and referencing.
learningOutcomes: Identify their personal learning style.
Organise and plan their time in a way that will enable them to participate fully in academic learning and university life over the academic year.
Record information, ideas and analysis from lectures, small group leaning activities, field trips and presentations.
Select and appraise data, ideas and information relevant to their course of study.
Read critically and evaluate arguments, ideas and information from text books and academic resources.
Practice techniques for sit-down examinations.
Write scholarly assignments that show: clarity of ideas, evidence based knowledge, critique of subject content and competence in academic referencing.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed to facilitate learners to explore their educational and biographical journeys through a process of critical reflection that will help them identify their emerging educational philosophies and values about the teachers they would like to become.

This process of critical reflection will be facilitated through learner-centred engagement with the following:
• A contextualised introduction to the philosophy and sociology of education, which forms the theoretical basis for this module. 
• An introduction to the education system, schooling in Ireland, what a teaching career is ‘like’, and what ‘type’ of teacher they want to be through fluid and reflexive engagement with educational 
        theories, issues, policies and their own biographies.  
• An introduction to pedagogy and school placement with a strong focus on learner-centred, equity-sensitive and active approaches to teaching and learning and school leadership and cultures.
learningOutcomes: Critically and collaboratively reflect on their educational biographies in developing an emerging sense of their values and aspiration for teaching.
Recognise the relationship between the foundation subjects/key theorists and the practice of teaching and their emerging ideas about education.
Examine and critique the Irish education system against a framework of national and international research on equality, inclusion and diversity.

Engage positively and actively with school settings and in the process reflect on experiences of planning, observation, co-teaching, evaluation and professional dialogues with teachers, mentors, student teachers, etc.

modules.moduleContent: The aim of the English Literature module is to allow students to explore a wide range of Irish and international literature and to provide them with some of the theories and critical tools necessary for literary analysis.  It equips students to read a variety of sorts of writing. It is concerned with narrative forms and techniques and their function in identity formation from the early twentieth century to today, including Irish drama, fiction, picture books and young-adult fiction. The module will provide a good overall grounding in English Literature, and it affords transferable analytic skills and the pleasures of reading literary texts they might not otherwise encounter.
learningOutcomes: Outline some of the key features of literature across multiple genres which may include but are not limited to non-fiction, poetry, novels, plays, short story, picture books, youth literature;
Analyse texts through close reading of language, syntax, grammar;
Examine themes of texts through the lenses of relevant social/historical/political contexts;
Describe the role of narrative in fictional or dramatic texts;
Discuss the role of identity formation in several course texts;
Examine the relationship between form and content in several texts;
Articulate in clear written prose, personal views on the value of literature today and their engagement with it.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO UNIVERSITY STUDY AND LIFE
modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to equip learners with the knowledge, skills and confidence for successful engagement with study and student life in higher education.

Learners will be guided to develop the critically reflective skills required for successful engagement with university study and life.

Learners will develop research, writing, referencing, critical thinking, and academic skills appropriate for preparation for undergraduate level study.

Learners will develop confidence in using the University's student communication and learning platforms: Microsoft Office 365, Teams and Moodle.

Learners will also be encouraged to develop their knowledge and confidence in engaging autonomously with wider students' services, MSU, societies and supports.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate capacity for critically reflexive thinking and writing.
Research, write, reference and employ academic conventions to the University's required standards.
Engage confidently and competently with the University's student communication platforms, namely Microsoft Office 365, Teams and Moodle, etc.
Engage autonomously with college services, supports and societies.

modules.moduleContent: This module surveys the main theories surrounding how second language acquisition, connecting it to best practices in second language teaching. The origins of second language acquisition theories can be traced back to cognate fields such as linguistics (formal approaches), sociolinguistics (emergentist and sociocultural approaches), psychology (input processing), and education (teaching methods). Thus, this course will investigate the connections between these cognate fields and the different approaches to second language acquisition and teaching to exemplify the diversity of points of view and, as a result, multiplicity of answers to the inquiry of second language acquisition.
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms the salient issues in the areas of second language acquisition and teaching.
Evaluate and apply the various theories discussed in the module to broaden their understanding of the diversity in the outcomes of second language development.
Evaluate second language teaching methods and strategies based on research produced in the field of second language acquisition and teaching.
Research and construct a critical analysis of academic discourses on a chosen topic in the area of second language acquisition.
Research, develop and articulate in writing balanced and well-structured arguments.

modules.moduleContent: Students will undertake supervised research and write their Minor Thesis to completion.
learningOutcomes: Illustrate and explain in detail an innovative approach to one particular aspect of Applied Linguistics and/or Intercultural Studies.
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and well-structured argument.
Evaluate critically, apply and reference appropriately a wide range of library, electronic and online resources.
Employ appropriate vocabulary, terminology, register and style to produce a high-quality piece of writing.
Process and integrate constructive feedback from the supervisor of the research project.
Work autonomously and take responsibility for all aspects of the planning, research, composition and completion of a research project.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: UNDERSTANDING DATA IN LANGUAGE DEVELOPMENT RESEARCH
modules.moduleContent: This module introduces the students to research methods used in research on L2, L3/Ln, and bilingual language development. We will discuss different methodologies by examining previous research and applying different methods to data, collected by students or available through online corpora. In this hands-on module, the students will carry out empirical linguistic research.
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms the application value of linguistic data to some approaches to linguistics.
Evaluate and apply the various research methodologies to data analysis discussed in the module.
Apply the basic quantitative methods necessary in carrying out a linguistic investigation.
Research and construct a critical analysis of academic discourses on a chosen topic in the area of applied linguistics.
Research, develop and articulate in writing balanced and well-structured arguments.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The prospective disappearance of up to half of the world’s languages by 2021 has become a major concern for linguists and speakers alike. This module introduces students to the intertwined phenomena of language endangerment and revitalisation. In the first half of the module, students examine research into endangerment and related topics including Language Contact and Change, Language Shift, and Language Documentation. In the second half of the module, students investigate responses to language endangerment, including Language Revitalisation, Language Planning and Policy, and Standardisation.
learningOutcomes: Describe the processes that lead to language endangerment.
Apply scholarship on language endangerment to contemporary case studies.
Investigate metrics of endangerment and vitality commonly applied to marginalised languages.
Compare and contrast varied community-based approaches to revitalisation in Indigenous and European contexts.
Devise and complete a brief research project.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will introduce you to anthropology as the subject that studies human behaviour and cultural diversity. You will learn about the massive cultural differences that divide the world. But you will also learn about our evolution as a species, and the behaviours that all humans display in common. We will discuss, interactively, group identity, language and power, and the societal structures that bind some and free others. The first half of the module includes close reading of the book When I Wear My Alligator Boots, a fascinating study of drugs, violence and family on the US-Mexico border, which we will use to explore themes raised during the first few weeks. In the latter part of this class we will continue exploring the concept of culture through which anthropologists study human societies. In the process of reading and watching captivating accounts of other peoples’ ways of life, we will develop our anthropological imagination – the capacity to see and describe the world around us in cross-cultural terms. In addition to readings and audio-visual materials, we will engage in hands-on practical exercises to learn ethnographic skills, nurturing our powers of observing, listening and writing.

This introductory course in Anthropology is compatible with every subject (excluding those in Timetable group 4) in the University and it will change the way you see human life forever.
learningOutcomes: Differentiate between similarities that we share as a human species and cultural variations that distinguish various groups of people across the world;
Question stereotypical images and treatment of people different from ourselves and apply intercultural skills in their daily life;
Understand how anthropologists work, i.e., the methods they use in their research, along with the challenges they face in doing their research;
Gain first-hand experience in ethnographic observation and writing;
Gain an understanding of your own experiences as a social and cultural being.

modules.moduleName: BEING HUMAN: UNDERSTANDING OUR MATERIAL AND DIGITAL WORLDS
modules.moduleContent: This module looks at anthropological approaches to material, digital and visual culture. We will first consider object worlds in some detail, and focus on cloth and clothing, fashion and faith, recycling and fashion revolution. As an object of material and visual culture, cloth is frequently co-opted as a vehicle for multiple social and cultural agendas. We will look at specific items of clothing and explore how they may act as a lighting rod for emotive stereotypes regarding race, religion, gender and geopolitical forces. We will then take these insights and consider how people create their own worlds through online interactions and visual media. Student participation in class and peer discussion will be expected in all sessions.
learningOutcomes: Examine and unpack the complexity of any cultural phenomenon through skills gained in the close examination of two cases, such as ‘the veil’.
Have an understanding of what a material, digital and visual culture approach to anthropological questions entails.
Should have some insight into what anthropological research consists of and should be able to discuss one example of anthropological analysis in some depth.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This second year anthropology module introduces anthropological approaches to studying religion. We will explore diverse religious beliefs, meanings, experiences, expressions, and practices across various sociocultural contexts. Lectures will cover classical theorists and early anthropologists of religion, and in the second half of the semester, we will focus on more recent and contemporary scholarship and emergent religious practices. 

By engaging with anthropological works on topics like ritual, sacrifice, prayer, fasting, meditation, mysticism, sabbaticals, healing, pilgrimage, religious nationalism, secularism, and identity, students will gain insights into how religion is understood, experienced, and expressed across different sociocultural environments, both historically and in contemporary times. By the end of the module, students will be able to use anthropological theory to analyse and explain religious phenomena in diverse contexts.
learningOutcomes: Describe how anthropologists have studied religion.
Identify and describe the principal practices of major world religions.
Apply anthropological theory to analyse and explain religious phenomena in diverse contexts.

modules.moduleName: EXCAVATION AND COMMUNITY ARCHAEOLOGY: CONSTRUCTING PASTS, PLACES AND PERSONHOOD
modules.moduleContent: This two week credited summer school module gives students a hands-on orientation of archaeological field techniques and field anthropology on a holistic research project entitled the Monastic Midlands. The module forms part of a program that investigates both the early medieval monasteries and their associated later medieval religions houses and castles. The program is predominantly focused on non-invasive archaeological investigations such as geophysical surveys, graveyard surveys, building surveys, historical research and mapping, post-excavation lab-work, folklore recording etc. However, skills need on an archaeological excavation may also be taught on the program, based on the demands of community partners in individual seasons. See https://iafs.ie/field-anthropology-summer/ for more details.
learningOutcomes: Understand the broad range of techniques used by archaeologists to infer past human behaviour from cultural sites and material culture;
Appreciate the origins/role of community archaeology;
Have a good base understanding of the medieval history of Ireland, with a focus on the midlands;
Understand the significance of the coming of evangelist monks to the midlands of Ireland in the seventh to ninth centuries, and how they helped to shape a new Irish society;
Learn new skills that are applicable in a Cultural Resource Management setting.

modules.moduleName: TROUBLING IDENTITIES: RACE, GENDER, AND SEXUALITY
modules.moduleContent:
This module explores the concept of identity politics from different perspectives. It delves into how identities have evolved over time and how they have become a fundamental problem in society. The module takes a neutral stance and aims to observe the culture war with equanimity and good humor.
 
We will cover various dominant frameworks that shape modern identity politics such as multiculturalism, politics of recognition, representation, cultural appropriation, inequality, intersectionality, violence, victimhood, and the shifting line between what is viewed as innate in human nature and what can be transformed.
 
Through examining the norms and forms of thought that make identity intelligible as a political problem, we aim to generate fresh thinking for overcoming social injustice.
learningOutcomes: Describe and analyse diverse forms of inequality and injustice – as well as their intersections - in society today.
Place the emergence of identity politics into social context, understanding how the ‘self’ has become a central problem of modern life.
Students will be able to show how forms of disenfranchisement and domination create the conditions through which personhood becomes the subject of politics.
Understand key contemporary concepts in social analysis and cultural politics, from scholarly concepts such as ‘intersectionality,’ to popular ones, such as ‘cancellation.’
Articulate critiques of different aspects of identity politics discourse.
Gain greater sensitivity to matters of abiding importance in society today, such as misogyny, racism, ableism, homophobia, transphobia, and more.

modules.moduleName: KEY CONCEPTS AND ETHNOGRAPHIC PRACTICE I
modules.moduleContent: This module is divided into two sections (6 weeks each) – one focusing on Key Concepts and the other one on Ethnographic Practice. The Key Concepts section introduces some of anthropology's most important ideas, exploring several of the analytical approaches characteristic of ethnographic inquiry today. In this part, our ambition is to provide a short and intensive introduction to the discipline by focusing on culture as a central concept in anthropology. Tracing the place of culture and its critique and development within the discipline will enable us to engage with a number of other key categories of anthropological knowledge and knowing. The Ethnographic Practice section discusses ethnography as a core methodological tool in anthropology. In this section of the module, we will discuss key examples of different approaches to ethnography to foreshadow preparations for student research proposals. We will examine some classic and contemporary ethnographic works while considering a range of methodological problems: entry to the field, data collection, engagement and conceptualization of one’s research through scholarly literature, ethical dimensions of research, and others. Student participation and peer discussion will be expected in all sessions.
learningOutcomes: Indicate how the key concepts discussed in the module developed in the history of anthropology as a discipline.
Apply anthropological concepts to the analysis of empirical observations.
Plan and prepare an anthropological research proposal, including consideration of methodological and conceptual issues.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED STUDIES IN ADDICTION, RECOVERY AND CULTURE
modules.moduleContent: This course is designed to interrogate the concepts of “addiction” and “recovery” in ethnographic and historical contexts. We examine different models of addiction, from the biological to the social. We then look at several case studies examining the use of ostensible  addictive substances or the engaging in supposedly addictive behaviour. We then go on to examine varying understandings of “Recovery”, that is leaving the compulsion to use a substance or engage in a behaviour, that is considered problematic. We conclude with the examination of broader questions of what addiction and recovery can yell us about being human.
learningOutcomes: To understand Addiction and Recovery in ethnographic and historical contexts.
To be able to critically assess models of addiction and recovery.
To be able to show such understanding in both oral presentations and written assignments.

modules.moduleContent: To be determined by visiting lecturer
learningOutcomes: be able to evaluate the validity of existing anthropological and other social science research on a particular special topic in Anthropology and
will be able to orally present relevant theoretical and ethographic data pertaining to the topic in question and
will be able to discuss critically, in essay form, recent theoretical and ethnographic works in Anthropology on the topic in question.

modules.moduleContent: This module offers an advance introduction to the broad field of Medical Anthropology, focusing on the classical anthropological contributions to this important subfield, from the work of Farmer and Kleinman to current debates around Global health, health care systems; care more generally, and suffering (social, medical and psychological. Students will refer to key works in this area, such as studies of structural violence and health outcomes, critiques of bio-medicine, healing and health care. Students will also explore ethnographic work on patient-physician relationships, the social and community contexts of care provision, and the impact of bio-medicine on Western and non-Western populations.
learningOutcomes: Show detailed knowledge of the history of Medical Anthropology
Compare and contrast different methodological approaches in the sub-discipline
Show detailed knowledge of the key concepts in Medical Anthropology
Show evidence of having acquired scholarly skills of interpretation and analysis and have reflected on the fieldwork experience with particular reference to medical matters.

modules.moduleContent: This module offers an advance introduction to the broad field of Global Health in Medical Anthropology, focusing on the classical anthropological contributions to this important subfield, from the work of Farmer and Kleinman to current debates around Global health, health care systems; care more generally, and suffering (social, medical and psychological. Students will refer to key works in this area, such as studies of structural violence and health outcomes, critiques of bio-medicine, healing and health care. Students will also explore ethnographic work on patient-physician relationships, the social and community contexts of care provision, and the impact of bio-medicine on Western and non-Western populations.
learningOutcomes: Show detailed knowledge of the history of Global Health in Anthropology
Compare and contrast different methodological approaches in the sub-discipline
Show detailed knowledge of the key concepts in Global Health in Anthropology
Show evidence of having acquired scholarly skills of interpretation and analysis and have reflected on the fieldwork experience with particular reference to medical matters

modules.moduleContent: This module’s objective is to provide advanced postgraduate students with a mentored experience of participating in a professional conference. In consultation with a mentor, the student will: (i) Select a conference; (ii) Prepare a paper abstract or panel abstract; (iii) Submit the abstract to the conference committee and register for the conference; (iv) Prepare for the oral presentation of the paper as appropriate to the conference context, or prepare to moderate the panel; (v) Attend the conference and deliver the paper, or moderate the panel; (vi) Submit the completed paper, or a written report on the moderated panel, to the staff mentor, along with evidence of conference participation in the form of copied pages from the conference programme showing the student’s paper/panel.
learningOutcomes: Advance their professional development by providing a structured context to facilitate the stages of the process of participating in a conference. By the end, the student will have prepared for, written, and presented a conference paper, or prepared for and moderated a conference panel.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed for students undertaking anthropological fieldwork abroad. Long-term fieldwork is central to the discipline of anthropology; it is expected that anthropology students at the PhD level will spend one year or more doing independent, original research at fieldsites remote from the student’s home institution. Fieldwork abroad requires extensive planning and preparation that can include learning a foreign language, cultivating fieldsite contacts, obtaining local research permissions, etc. Anthropological fieldwork is like an artisan craft in that it is learned through the process of engaging in it over the long term, and the pedagogical issues will be different for each student and for each fieldsite. Therefore, the content of this module is built around the student’s particular field project. In consultation with a designated departmental mentor, the student will: (i) Draw up a logistical plan for field research; (ii) Complete pre-field preparations, including making contacts at the fieldsite, securing permissions, etc.; (iii) Send written updates on progress to the mentor at least quarterly during the fieldwork; (iv) Present a preliminary summary of the data obtained upon return from the field.

Note: This module is open only to Anthropology students who are beyond their first year of PhD study.
learningOutcomes: Provide a structured context in which an advanced anthropology student can prepare for and carry out anthropological field research, mentored by an experienced field anthropologist. By the end of this module, the student will have gained sufficient supervised experience to be qualified to plan and carry out future fieldwork independently.

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to introduce students to the principles of accounting enabling them to support decision making and meet business challenges.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of the accountant and the financial environment in which they operate;
Develop an understanding of the double entry book-keeping system and its role in the preparation of financial statements of a business;
Develop the ability to interpret the various elements of a set of financial statements to inform decision-making;
Evaluate the performance of a business using financial statement analysis;
Demonstrate an ability to advise on working capital management techniques including the preparation of cash budgets.; and
Assess alternative sources of finance to meet organisational finance requirements.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to the regulatory framework of accounting including the International Accounting Standards framework. The module will examine International Accounting Standards governing the preparation and presentation of company financial statements including accounting policies; the preparation of cash-flow statements; accounting for events after the reporting period; accounting for property, plant and equipment; investment properties; leases; income tax; financial instruments; government grants; contingencies; intangible assets and the correction of errors.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an awareness of the role and function of the Regulatory Framework of Accounting, the history and context of its development and the professional bodies involved in its development and implementation;
Appreciate the development of International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) and the significance of their implementation on the preparation of company financial statements;

Apply the principles of IFRS to the treatment of specific business transactions including the preparation of relevant journal entries, relevant extracts from the financial statements and appropriate narrative descriptions; and
Prepare and interpret a cash flow statement for a company prepared in accordance with IFRS.

modules.moduleContent: The AC202 module covers three distinct areas; the application of International Accounting standards to the preparation of financial statements, the preparation of financial statements for businesses operating as partnerships and an introduction to the principles of auditing.
learningOutcomes: Apply international accounting standards to the preparation of financial statements;
Discuss the implications of international accounting standards application;
Prepare partnership financial statements encompassing as appropriate allocation of profits and preparation of partners current and capital accounts; and
Evaluate at a high level, the role and processes of audit and assurance in the business environment.

modules.moduleContent: This module aims to develop students's understanding of financial management issues and to equip students with the necessary financial management skills to apply to the operation of medium to large corporate entities. Covering the financial management environment, working capital, cost of capital and capital structure,  portfolio analysis, capital investment appraisals (incorporating capital rationing, taxation, risk and inflation), valuation of companies and risk management.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the role of the finance function in an organisation and how it supports the organisations' financial policy and management of working capital;

Formulate and critically appraise alternative investment opportunities available to businesses using qualitative and quantitative information in situations which also include capital rationing, tax and inflation;
Formulate and advise the organisation on alternative sources, and raising, of finance to enable an appropriate mix of debt and equity can be applied;
Discuss and apply alternative methods of valuing a business and their implications for the business; and
Advise on the types of risk businesses are exposed to, apply approaches to managing such risk and critically appraise the impact on the finance of the business.

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to Group Accounts and Group Structures: accounting for subsidiary undertakings; accounting for goodwill and intangible assets; accounting for pre-acquisition profits, post acquisition profits, non-controlling interests, inter-company transactions, non-current asset revaluations, acquisition of a subsidiary during an accounting period. This course is primarily designed for students who wish to pursue a professional qualification in accountancy on attainment of their primary degree.
learningOutcomes: Explain and apply the regulatory and legal framework surrounding the preparation of consolidated financial statements;
Identify and correctly account for the main accounting issues involved in consolidated financial statements;
Demonstrate a comprehensive knowledge of IFRS and its application in the preparation and presentation of consolidated financial statements; and

Be competent in the preparation of the consolidated statement of profit or loss and consolidated statement of financial position to include subsidiaries and associates; and other practical accounting issues.

modules.moduleContent: This module will look at a number of modern management accounting techniques, exploring the decision-making techniques that can be used to evaluate business choices allowing for risk, uncertainty, scarce resources and sustainability considerations. We will learn how to use advanced budgeting techniques and variance analysis to assess business performance from both a financial, as well as from a non-financial, perspective.
learningOutcomes: Evaluate different modern management accounting techniques.
Select decision making techniques to evaluate business choices.
Evaluate alternative methods of budgeting, planning and control.
Use standard costing systems to measure and control business performance and to identify remedial action.
Assess the performance of a business from both a financial, non-financial and sustainability viewpoint.

modules.moduleName: THE CONTEXT OF EQUALITY IN IRISH EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to facilitate learners to explore education's role as a force for social justice and equality, reviewing the context of Irish education through a process of critical reflection on their educational journeys and biographical experiences of the education system. This review and reflection will help learners comprehend the Irish education system and its role in wider society in an in-depth manner and identify their education philosophies and values for assessing their potentials to become teachers.
learningOutcomes: Develop an understanding of the social, egalitarian and political context of Irish education.
Learn theories of education, social justice and equality.
Build capacity for critical reflection about the social justice implications of key contexts in education.
Practice and appraise their critical reflective skills in a supportive environment.
Encourage critical reflection on the social justice implications of education.
Critically and collaboratively reflect on their educational biographies in developing an emerging sense of their values and potential aspiration for teaching.

modules.moduleName: TEACHING & LEARNING IN ADULT, COMMUNITY AND FURTHER EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: This module will introduce students to the theories of adult, community and further education with an emphasis on the philosophies that inform teaching and learning. The module will expose students to a wide range of philosophical orientations and provide an opportunity for reflection on their own styles.
learningOutcomes: describe the basic practice theoretical tenets of adult teaching and teaching/learning
demonstrate competence in interpreting learning needs of various adult learning groups
comprehend the different philosophies that inform adult teaching and learning
demonstrate competence in teaching and learning skills in adult and community education

modules.moduleContent: At postgraduate level students are required to move beyond best practice models to adopt a proactive, analytic stance vis-Ã¿-vis the broader contexts in which their educational initiative is located. The module will cover: Situating the self in the system: Establishing a reflective theoretical framework: Modelling reflection on systems: Models of reflective practice: Becoming a reflective practitioner and researcher
learningOutcomes: describe and understand the concept of reflectiveness in an adult learning situation
demonstrate competence as a reflective adult and community education practitioner
employ a range of methods to compliment the process of reflection as a learner
produce a reflective presentation associated with reflective practice in adult and community education.

modules.moduleName: HISTORY AND POLICY OF ADULT AND FURTHER EDUCATION IN IRELAND
modules.moduleContent:
This module will assist students to reflect critically on the development of the Further Education And Training (FET) system in Ireland.  It explores the evolution of FET within the social, cultural and policy context of the Irish education system. It critically analyses how the nature of adult learning and pedagogy within the sector has influenced and been shaped by these contexts.  The module will support students to assess how national and international policy legislation and polices intersect with practice in the field and to consider the future implications for the practice of Further Education and Training.

A minimum of 80% attendance is required and active participation is expected in this module.
learningOutcomes: Have adequate knowledge to debate the history and evolution of adult, community and Further Education in Ireland.
Critique the relationship between Further Education and other tertiary provision.
Review the historical progression/development that underpins current educational policy and provision.
Demonstrate an insight into how public policy relating to Further Education and Training (FET) is made both in Ireland and internationally.
Critique the multifaceted nature of FET policy development and implementation in Ireland

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO COUNSELLING IN ADULT, COMMUNITY AND FURTHER EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent:
On completion of the module, the students will have explored a rationale for learning about counselling in adult learning settings, located their practice in the context of counselling and related activities, explored the role of personal awareness in facilitating adult development and change, and explored the kinds of relationship that facilitate adult development.

A minimum of eighty per cent attendance is required and active participation is expected in this module.
learningOutcomes: Examine the role of counselling in adult learning settings and locate the  practice of a  facilitator of adult learning in the context of counselling and related activities.
Interpret the role of personal development in facilitating adult development and change brought about through adult learning.
Sketch the practice of counselling and related activities in the process of adult, further and community education.
Locate the process of counselling in adult, further and community education in the educative experience of the adult learner.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will explore a range of theories from philosophy that helps us to think carefully about the purpose of education in society. It introduces students to influential philosophical ideas in the field of adult education which are sometimes described as radical, humanistic, progressive, liberal and behaviourist. Students will encounter key thinkers such as Paulo Freire, Jack Mezirow, Carl Rogers and bell hooks but will also be encouraged to look beyond the traditional philosophical canon. Overall, the main purpose of the module is to offer concepts and theories which will develop students’ capacity to critically reflect on their own practice, to develop their own philosophy of education, and challenge some of the ways we traditionally think about the purpose of education. 

 

80% attendance is required for this module.
learningOutcomes: Identify and critique the main philosophical theories pertinent to adult education.
Explore the relevance of philosophical theories to adult, community and further education learning contexts.
Articulate their own philosophy of education and consider ways of applying this in an adult learning environment.
Challenge dominant discourse on the purpose of education in society.

modules.moduleName: SOCIOLOGY OF ADULT, COMMUNITY AND FURTHER EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent:
In this module a range of theories and research are introduced to provide insights to the web of social, cultural, economic and political factors that underpin learning in contemporary society. This module acknowledges the embedded and interdependent nature of learning in modern society and how this is being challenged and debated socially, politically, economically and culturally. The module also critically assesses the role of education in society at the individual level (learner, teacher, family member, etc), community (local, regional, global), institutional level (the schooling system) and the broader social, cultural, economic & political level. The module will pay particular reference to diversity, exclusion, differentiation, gender, community, and hegemonic issues. 

A minimum of 80% attendance is required and active participation is expected in this module.
learningOutcomes: Identify and critically reflect on the key sociological features of education affecting the direction of change in Irish society.

Draw on a range of sociological and educational theories, concepts and research to investigate the interdependent nature of learning and the role of schooling as a social institution contributing to or hindering social change.
Explore the role of education as a social institution.
Critically locate their professional practices as a teacher in a wider social, cultural, political and economic context and the role of the teacher in this context.

Demonstrate understanding of the global context within which education occurs and how education systems are influenced by international agendas and policies, such as Agenda 2030 and the Sustainable Development Goals.
Critically examine the role of education in responding to global issues of inequality, racism, resource distribution, and environmental degradation.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module will introduce students to key concepts in adult learning, facilitating critical reflection on the students own positions, experiences and practice. 
Specifically it will focus on exploring

1. The  learning process across the lifespan through different models of learning with a particular focus on current debates and practices surrounding lifelong learning
2. Key concepts in social perception or how we come to understand other people: focusing on impression formation and attribution theory.
3. Group processes: Stages of group development and the impact of the group on behaviour; including group roles, psychological dynamics
4. Facilitating learning with reference to models of motivation and leadership.
learningOutcomes: Describe the key features of the major schools of learning theory and engage with current debates in the field. Support and develop adult learning
Understand key models of social perception and causal attribution when relating to others, be better equipped to identify patterns of non verbal communication
Understand key concepts in self awareness and identity, take an active part in their own learning, critically reflect on their own practice and experiences.
Identify key group processes, be able to reflect on their preferred roles in groups, facilitate group learning more effectively and be familiar with the psychological dynamics of the group.
Identify learning blocks, and consider relevant theories of motivation in terms of facilitating learning
Be able to critically recognise and apply theories of transformational and authentic leadership.

modules.moduleName: RESEARCH METHODS IN ADULT & COMM. EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: This module will focus on Research Methodologies and will explore the technical and practical as well as the philosophical and political underpinnings of different approaches to research.
learningOutcomes: Have an understanding of the background to and influences on research in Adult and Community Education/ Adult Guidance and Counselling
explore the roles of different types of research in this field
explore the relationship between theory and practice
develop the knowledge and identify, research and write a research project/thesis.

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED THEORIES IN ADULT AND COMMUNITY EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent:
Critical engagement with the theories and major debates around the field of Adult and Community Education.
The development of own epistemological positioning with regards to the field. 
Extending the knowledge base of the field through dialogue, writing and scholarly discussion.
learningOutcomes: Continue to explore theories underpinning the work with adults
Develop the students own epistemological stance

modules.moduleName: EDUCATION: INFRASTRUCTURES, POLITICAL AND POLICY CONTEXTS
modules.moduleContent: The module will include a familiarisation with a systemic approach to education and educational organizations and the wide political and policy contexts in which they exist.
learningOutcomes: develop an understanding and knowledge of the systemic, political and policy contexts in which educational organisations exist
demonstrate an enhanced analysis of the location and manifestations of power within educational organisations
develop an understanding and knowledge of the conduits through which both explicit and implicit power operates
critically assess the cultural assumptions and imperatives which inform both educational organisations and the environments in which they exist
assess how knowledge is shaped by the cultural assumptions and systemic influences within educational organisations
evaluate conceptual and theoretical frameworks which help to critically analyse power in and through institutions and environments.

modules.moduleContent: This module will support students develop appropriate means and modes of reflectively researching within their professional field of practice and community of practice in a contextually appropriate and useful way.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate enhanced capacity to reflectively researching within their professional field of practice and community of practice in a contextually appropriate and useful way.
Present a critically reflective analysis of their learning during this research engagement in their field of practice.

modules.moduleContent: This module will support students to develop appropriate means and modes of reflectively engaging with their constituent communities to develop a research dialogue within their professional field of practice
learningOutcomes: develop appropriate means and modes of reflectively engaging with their constituent communities.
demonstrate how they can develop a research dialogue within their professional field of practice.
present a critically reflective analysis of their learning during this process about communications and engagements in their field of practice.

modules.moduleContent: The module provides an important overview of the discipline of Applied Linguistics and contemporary issues affecting the field today. It adopts a twofold approach. It begins by introducing students to the foundations of the field of Linguistics (historical overview of core theories, syntax and morphology, phonetics, phonology, semiotics, semantics and pragmatics) as important precursors to the entering the field of Applied Linguistics. Building on such foundations, the module continues by examining the most salient issues and debates in the field based on three pillars: the monolingual, the bilingual and the multilingual language user. In the case of each, a range of issues are discussed including language acquisition; language contact, variation and change; language and migration; language policy and planning; globalisation and language; and language and identity.
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms the structural, phonetic, phonological, semantic and pragmatic similarities and differences between English and other languages.
Describe in some detail more specific aspects of these differences, using examples where appropriate.
Evaluate and apply the various theories discussed in the module to broaden their understanding of language.
Develop a clear, coherent and well-structured presentation, demonstrating appropriate use of pertinent theory, illustrative examples and guide student discussions.
Research, develop and articulate in writing balanced and well-structured arguments.

modules.moduleName: METHODOLOGIES OF TEACHING AND LEARNING LANGUAGES
modules.moduleContent: This module provides an overview of current foreign language teaching methods, with an emphasis on communicative task-based language teaching approaches. Students will review foundational concepts in second/foreign language acquisition theory and will engage with research to inform best practices in lesson planning, implementation, classroom management and assessment. The module also focuses on the development of critical reflective skills for foreign language teaching professionals.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of core phenomena in second language acquisition.
Critically evaluate foreign language teaching practices and instructional materials.
Design lesson plans and learning tasks that are informed by current foreign language acquisition theory and research.
Implement instructional strategies that effectively promote the acquisition of foreign languages.
Critically reflect on their continuous professional development as foreign language educators.

modules.moduleContent: Witchcraft, magic, and other forms of occult or mystical powers have always been fascinating. Sometimes, these practices seem to represent exotic beliefs and challenge preconceptions about rationality and human nature. However, anthropological studies of witchcraft have also shown that modern ideas about risk and responsibility follow similar social scripts to those of seemingly irrational beliefs in witchcraft. The anthropological lesson is that the dangers and mysteries societies imagine for themselves, along with their conventional habits of blaming others for misfortunes, function to reproduce core cultural values. If this is true, how can we understand the increasing interest in occultism today, from Wicca to #WitchTok? This module explores anthropological analyses of black magic, juju, sorcery, flying witches, spiritual warfare, shamanism, magical healers, and more, demonstrating how these fantastical figures do not belong to a past superseded by a scientific and secular modernity, but have even found a home in the internet, a world that we find hard to understand.
learningOutcomes: Understand the diverse sociocultural functions social institutions such as witchcraft belief or magical practices;
Understand how states and institutions of global governance are confronted by the problems of violence associated with accusations of witchcraft;
Critique the notion that such beliefs belong to the past;
Learn innovative internet research skills by conducting a project focused on in the intersection of the occult and the online world;
Interpret the resurgence of interest in topics like magic and witchcraft in Europe and North America.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module will be dedicated to critical examination of race, racism and anti-racist responses in historical and contemporary contexts. We will learn how race as an inaccurate representation of human biological variation, has been employed as a basis of racist classification systems that have structured people’s experience in the world. We will learn about the role of racism in justifying the atrocities of colonialism and slavery, the European Holocaust and anti-Black, Anti-Irish, Anti-Roma, and Anti-Traveller prejudice and discrimination. We will study the legacy of slavery, police violence and incarceration in the United States and consider the role of collective memory in inspiring the ongoing critique of institutional and everyday racisms.  Throughout the module, we will focus on the resistance against racism in different sites and historical moments, including anti-colonial and civil-rights movements. A special attention will be paid to the complexity of the Irish colonial and post-colonial experience. 100% CA assessment will be based on individual reflections and group projects, focused on developing students’ competencies to think through and engage in discussions on issues tied to racism and other forms of systemic discrimination. The goal is to provide students with knowledge-based capacities to confront racism in their everyday lives - personal, professional and civic - and across transnational fields of belonging in the interconnected world.
learningOutcomes: Understand race as a socially and historically constructed concept
Interpret contemporary racisms through historically and socio-culturally informed perspectives
Distinguish between different forms of racism in varied historical and geopolitical contexts
Critically reflect on sociocultural and political constructions of whiteness
Critically assess and provide constructive response to instances of structural and everyday racisms

modules.moduleContent:
In Anti-Racism II, we will examine the continued salience of racism as a tool of structural discrimination today. The semester will feature lectures and conversations with academics from MU and other universities, as well as community experts who will take us to the heart of their research on and lived experiences of racism and anti-racist strategies and practices.  Examples of topics include: racism and schools; racism and nationalism; colonialism, racism and indigenous people’s experience; racism and Apartheid; racism and international law in Palestine; racism in intersectional perspectives – including gender, class and sexuality; racism, work and politics, racism and the welfare state; racism and statistics; and anti-Asian racism; racism and international development. 
Our discussions will take us around the world – from Ireland and Europe to Central and South America, North America, the Middle East, Asia, South Africa, and Australia. Throughout the module, we will focus on different forms of anti-racist activism and debate how democratic societies can respond to racial prejudice and nurture more inclusive and just forms of citizenship and belonging. 
100% CA assessment will be based on individual reflections and group projects, focused on developing students’ competencies to think through and engage in discussions on issues tied to racism and other forms of systemic discrimination. The goal is to provide students with knowledge-based capacities to confront racism in their everyday lives - personal, professional and civic - and across transnational fields of belonging in the interconnected world.
learningOutcomes: Describe how racism in different parts of the world impacts people’s lives
Interpret contemporary racisms building on historically and geopolitically situated knowledge
Illustrate how people in varied geographic contexts respond to racism and organize resistance
Critically reflect on contemporary expressions of racism
Articulate an informed response to racist expressions and ideologies
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module tries to familiarise students with critical anthropological perspectives on sustainability. This will involve understanding the environmental dimensions of the simultaneous historical emergence of mass poverty and mass prosperity in distinct world regions, and critically evaluating the sustainable development efforts used to address the former, using a core ethnography and shorter key texts. The module begins by considering the environmental antecedents and implications of the long historical process of the making of the contemporary Global South and goes on to probe the exacerbation of both global inequality and ecological crisis in the era of globalization. Excerpts from landmark texts by Mike Davis, Peter Worsley and Amita Baviskar are among the readings assigned for the first segment of this module. The second segment is based on a close textual reading of an important recent ethnography on the subject of struggles over natural resources, Nikhil Anand’s ‘The Hydraulic City: Water and the Infrastructures of Citizenship in Mumbai’. 

This module is a standalone module offered in the Anthropology Department (under Code AN242); it is also the second half of the elective stream 'Prioritising People and Planet', which is a teaching collaboration between the International Development and the Anthropology departments.
learningOutcomes: Gain an introductory knowledge of the intertwined roots of global inequalities and ecological crises.
Encounter a variety of perspectives regarding sustainable development interventions.
Understand how power and privilege can be seen through the prism of sustainability.
Gain further familiarity with ethnographic and theoretical modes of argument.

modules.moduleContent: There is a wide academic literature that focuses on consumer culture in explaining radical changes in Euro-American societies, and such attention is usually accompanied by statements regarding the consequences of modern life. Where best to understand this modern moment than in ethnographic examples across time and space. Taking objects that we regard as trivial and holding them up to scrutiny, this course will cover a series of themes including: (1) historical trajectories and the consumer revolution, (2) branding and brandscapes (3) the gendered, political and spatial logics of shopping (4) different material forms such as the fetish, the animated artefact and the biographical object. All students are required to attend and participate in all sessions.
learningOutcomes: outline and evaluate key arguments on aspects of consumption studies current in the anthropological literature.
orally present relevant theoretical and ethnographic data pertaining to topics covered in class.
discuss critically, in essay form, recent theoretical and ethnographic works on topics covered in the course and selected by the student.

modules.moduleName: SHIFTING WORLDS: THEORIES AND ETHNOGRAPHIES OF GLOBAL CHANGE
modules.moduleContent: In this compulsory module, we will explore how anthropology and anthropologists explore contemporary processes of accelerated change and uneven global interconnectedness. Unlike most disciplines that focus on the macrolevel of globalization, anthropology centres on globalization’s situated nature and the lived experiences of diverse peoples who find themselves at the intersections of global developments. Focusing on these articulations of the global and the local, the module opens with three class sessions dedicated to sketching out conceptual and theoretical approaches and debates concerning contemporary global change . These will help us explore concrete thematic areas of anthropological research, such as: identity and citizenship in transnational fields; mobilities and migration; borders, security and militarism; culture and media; global economics and trade; environment and global health; cultural and political transformations, or ethical challenges of globalisation. The module places emphasis on students' active participation in classes.
learningOutcomes: Apply anthropological concepts to the analysis of global events and processes.
Discuss critically, in an essay form, issues of global developments drawing on recent theoretical and ethnographic works in Anthropology.
Define and debate one’s own position on issues arising from global changes.
Present summary and reflection on assigned readings.
Implement knowledge from the critical consideration of globalization in individual reflection and civic action.

modules.moduleName: INTERNATIONAL EXCAVATION AND COMMUNITY ARCHAEOLOGY: CONSTRUCTING PASTS, PLACES AND PERSONHOOD
modules.moduleContent: This two week credited summer school module gives students a hands-on orientation of archaeological field techniques and field anthropology on a holistic research project entitled the Monastic Midlands. The module forms part of a program that investigates both the early medieval monasteries and their associated later medieval religions houses and castles. The program is predominantly focused on non-invasive archaeological investigations such as geophysical surveys, graveyard surveys, building surveys, historical research and mapping, post-excavation lab-work, folklore recording etc. However, skills need on an archaeological excavation may also be taught on the program, based on the demands of community partners in individual seasons. See https://iafs.ie/field-anthropology-summer/ for more details.
learningOutcomes: Understand the broad range of techniques used by archaeologists to infer past human behaviour from cultural sites and material culture;
Appreciate the origins/role of community archaeology;
Have a good base understanding of the medieval history of Ireland;
Understand the significance of the coming of the Anglo-Normans to Ireland, and how this continues to shape contemporary Ireland and Irish personhoods 850 years later.

modules.moduleContent: The summer school programme will be delivered at the campus of the Irish Archaeology Field School/Irish Heritage School in Birr, County Offaly. This course will cover human and comparative osteology, determining sex, approximate age at death, living stature, identifying palaeopathological conditions and understanding how these techniques are used in archaeological and forensic contexts
learningOutcomes: Learn skills for the excavation of human remains (in a controlled environment).
The consideration when handling human remains, focusing on ethical protocol.
Understand skeletal analysis techniques, including human and comparative osteology, determining sex, approximate age at death, living stature, identifying palaeopathological conditions etc.
Understand how each of the other learning outcomes relate to forensic analysis and forensic cases.

modules.moduleContent: Anthropologists have been writing for many years about water as a resource, as a commodity, and as a source of social connection. This module will draw on selected ethnographies to, first and foremost, gain a nuanced understanding of what Alex Loftus calls “the injustice of water poverty”, of the fact that billions of people globally live without access to safe supplies of a resource that is indispensable for human survival. Secondly, recent water ethnographies have made noteworthy theoretical contributions, to the anthropology of infrastructures for instance. Thus, an ethnography like Nikhil Anand’s ‘The Hydraulic City’ offers startling new insight by focusing on the infrastructures of water, both in the sense of built networks of pipes and valves, and of the social relationships that flow through them.
