Finally, some water ethnographies have transformed the craft of ethnography itself, Andrea Ballestero’s ‘A Future History of Water’ being a case in point. A close textual – and contextual – reading of these “water works” will familiarise students with anthropology’s unique way of addressing one of the most urgent issues of our times.
learningOutcomes: Be conversant with issues of water poverty and inequality of access to water, key concerns in our times, and therefore in the field of ecological anthropology.

Be familiar with the new theoretical vistas that have been opened up by water ethnographies, such as the anthropology of infrastructures, as well as the classic theoretical framework that undergirds political ecology.
Develop the scholarly skill of close textual reading.
Think intuitively about the ethnographer’s craft as they read and compare water ethnographies for this module.

modules.moduleContent:
How are new technologies transforming society, and what are the ethical and social implications of these transformations? This third-year anthropology module explores the relationship between society and technology. The module takes a critical perspective on how technological advances have shaped, and are continuing to shape, social structures, practices, and relationships. You will engage with a range of texts that examine the social implications of new technologies, including artificial intelligence, virtual reality, the sharing economy, and more. Through these readings, you will develop a nuanced understanding of the complex ways that technology interacts with society, including how it shapes social identities, power structures, and inequalities.

Over the course of the semester, you will be encouraged to think critically about the social implications of technological innovations, and to consider the ways in which social structures, practices, and relationships can either support or undermine these innovations. By the end of the module, you will have developed a deep understanding of the impact of new technologies on society, and will be equipped to critically evaluate and engage with technological change.
learningOutcomes: Analyze and critique the social and cultural impacts of emerging technologies.
Apply sociological theories and concepts to the study of new technologies.
Evaluate the potential of new technologies to address social challenges and create social change.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: In this module, we will look at the production of heritage in Africa within tourism consumption routes and zones. We will examine and discuss several examples that show how both natural and cultural heritage is packaged in presenting “authentic tourist experiences” in countries like South Africa, Zimbabwe, Lesotho, Botswana, Zambia, Mozambique and Kenya. In turn, the search for authenticity will be critiqued in the class as a concept that is fluid, constantly staged and subsequently contributing towards cultural commodification. As far as the theoretical part is concerned, we are going to reflect on the role of museums and world heritage sites as resources for cultural tourism. At the same time, the authorised heritage discourse which is underpinned by a nomination of monuments and sites in Africa to the prestigious World Heritage List will be closely examined. We will look at selected examples of World Heritage Sites which are popular with international tourists such as Robben Island Museum in Cape Town, SA, Great Zimbabwe Monument and Victoria Falls in Zimbabwe and Mt Kenya National Park. Students will have a chance to critically engage with silenced intangible uses of cultural heritage sites in Africa by local communities. In fact, we will show in this class following an argument made by Laurajane Smith (2006) that all heritage is intangible. Other examples of cultural heritage tourism routes in Africa will be discussed relative to a recent proliferation of townships tours, cultural villages, festivals, and community/living museums. This class will be interactive with several visual illustrations of museums, sites, places and parks under discussion and students are encouraged to actively participate.
learningOutcomes: Understand authorised discourses surrounding the production of cultural heritage in Africa for tourism purposes.
Engage with the concept of Outstanding Universal Value (OUV) that determines nomination of cultural heritages on the World Heritage List.
Critically analyse staged authenticity in African cultural heritage tourism set-ups.
Present contestations that have emerged between heritage communities in Africa and authorised institutions as a result of exclusions.
Critically engage with the binary division between nature and culture in tourism routes of Africa.

modules.moduleName: WHO ARE WE? BIOLOGY AND IDENTITY IN THE 21ST CENTURY
modules.moduleContent:
We live in a technoscientific age where science and technology permeate every aspect of our daily lives. Yet, we are relatively poorly equipped with the conceptual tools and knowledge to responsibly analyze and criticize the relationships between science and technology and the society with which they are co-produced. This graduate anthropology seminar will foster a critical anthropological approach to the social, cultural, historical, and political implications of science and technology. 

This seminar engages source material from contemporary developments in global science and technology, particularly biology and biotechnology. The seminar involves reading, analyzing, and critically integrating works by scholars in STS, the Anthropology of Science, the Sociology of Knowledge, and related literatures. The texts jointly address topics such as: the basis of scientific and political authority; the relation of science to the state; theories of the relationship between science and democracy; the politics of technology; and theories of state building. Overall, the readings will explore how states produce and use scientific knowledge and technologies to service the political order and how science and technology relate to social identities. 

Topical content includes genomics, artificial intelligence, transhumanism, and artificial reproductive technologies, and how these technologies are in a transformative relationship with understandings of race, gender, kinship, nationality, class, profession, and other contemporary identities.
learningOutcomes: Describe the socio-political, cultural and social issues of science and technology.
Mobilize social theory to critically assess the role of science and technology in shaping social identities.
Describe how science and technology are a world-building enterprise that shape social identities and establish social orders by drawing on real world examples.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module’s objective is to provide a structured context in which an advanced postgraduate student can critically engage with areas of literature needed for the PhD thesis. It will enhance the student’s preparedness for carrying out thesis research and/or writing the thesis. In consultation with a mentor, the student will: (i) develop a reading list; (ii) undertake the reading; (iii) discuss the readings in periodic meetings with the mentor; (iv) write a review essay synthesizing the ideas in a selection of the readings.
learningOutcomes: Assimilate a body of literature and become prepared to carry out thesis research and/or write the thesis.

modules.moduleContent: The objective of this module is to provide a rigorous but supportive environment for students to write up their thesis in a timely fashion and obtain peer review of their writing. For each seminar, students share a chapter they are working on, which is read and critiqued by. A staff mentor overseas the process and may be present to contribute challenging questions, but the seminar is expected to be student-driven. Issues to be considered in discussions include the craft of ethnographic writing, descriptively integrating primary data into an academic argument; learning writing through reading; ethnographic genres; audiences; ethnographic authority and issues of representation.
learningOutcomes: Complete thesis chapters and gain experience in peer review of their own writing and that of others.

modules.moduleName: WRITING FOR PEER-REVIEWED PUBLICATION IN ANTHROPOLOGY
modules.moduleContent: The module’s objective is to provide advanced postgraduate students a mentored experience to learn first-hand about academic publishing and to prepare and submit an article to an academic journal for peer review. It will advance their professional development by providing a structured context to facilitate the stages of the process, from selecting a journal to drafting and submitting an article, through to receiving the reviewer feedback. In consultation with a mentor, the student will: (i) Select a journal; (ii) Draft an article; (iii) Submit the article to the journal for review; (iv) Respond to the reviewer comments received; (v) Revise and resubmit the paper as appropriate; (vi) Submit evidence in the form of the reviewer comments received and correspondence with journal editor.
learningOutcomes: Advance their professional development by providing a structured context to facilitate the stages of the process of submitting an article to a peer-reviewed journal. By the end, the student will have prepared for, written, and submitted an article.

modules.moduleContent: This module is a comprehensive introduction to ethnography. 'Ethnography is more than a method': it comprises a whole style of thought encompassing forms of observation, analysis, and writing. The module therefore emphasizes analysis and theory in addition to the research practices (interviewing, participant observation, note-taking) conventionally associated with qualitative research methodology. Themes covered include: culture and difference, contexts and cases (working in NGOs, clinics, corporations), styles of representation and the politics of knowledge, research ethics and ethnographic engagement. The module is also structured as a workshop, so that ethnographers at various stages of their careers -- from students planning proposals, to dissertation writers analyzing previously collected material, to research professionals who may not be based in academia -- will be able to produce work within the module that relates to their respective career stages, locations, and goals. This work, such as a proposal draft or a stretch of ethnographic writing, forms the basis for module assessment.
learningOutcomes: Become familiar with the practical aspects of conducting anthropological field research;
Understand contemporary debates relating to ethnographic practice;
learn analytical techniques for working with anthropological field materials;
feel confident to begin their own independent field research project.

modules.moduleContent: This module is a comprehensive introduction to ethnography. Ethnography is more than a method: it comprises a whole style of thought encompassing forms of observation, analysis, and writing. The module therefore emphasizes analysis and theory in addition to the research practices (interviewing, participant observation, note-taking) conventionally associated with qualitative research methodology. Themes covered include: culture and difference, contexts and cases (working in NGOs, clinics, corporations), styles of representation and the politics of knowledge, research ethics and ethnographic engagement. The module is also structured as a workshop, so that ethnographers at various stages of their careers -- from students planning proposals, to dissertation writers analyzing previously collected material, to research professionals who may not be based in academia -- will be able to produce work within the module that relates to their respective career stages, locations, and goals.
learningOutcomes: Become familiar with the practical aspects of conducting anthropological field research.
Understand contemporary debates relating to ethnographic practice.
Learn analytical techniques for working with anthropological field materials.
Feel confident to begin their own independent field research project.

modules.moduleContent:
To provide an introduction to the core concepts and principles in cell biology, biochemistry, genetics, microbiology and evolutionary biology. To introduce practical biology techniques such as microscopy, spectroscopy, DNA and Protein extraction/analysis and bacterial and fungal culture.

Topics covered include: structure and function of DNA, RNA and proteins; basic animal and plant cell structure; cell division; diseases of the cell; cellular organelles; cytoskeleton; viral structure and function; bacterial cell structure, growth and control of bacteria; fungal cell structure, fungal diseases; principles of genetics; mitosis and meiosis; patterns of inheritance; transcription and translation; mechanisms of evolution; microscopy.
learningOutcomes: Outline the differences between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
Describe animal and plant cell structures, organelle function and the components and function of the Endomembrane and Cytoskeletal systems.
Demonstrate an understanding of the connection between chemical composition and function in biological molecules and macromolecules.
Describe the defining characteristics, structure and replication abilities of viruses, bacteria and fungi.
Describe the dominant and recessive patterns of Mendelian inheritance.
Demonstrate an understanding of the Central Dogma and the relationship between genes and proteins.
Describe the processes driving evolution at the population level, including the process of speciation.

modules.moduleContent:
Topics covered include: basic concepts of immunology including innate and adaptive immune responses, regulation of the cell cycle, cancer including the anti-tumour immune response, basic molecular and cellular concepts of the nervous system, select neurological disorders, basic molecular and cellular concepts of chronic metabolic disorders, cardiovascular, inflammatory and respiratory disease. Students carry out library-based research and give a short oral presentation on a biological topic. In addition, students work in groups to research and present a poster on a biological topic. The posters are presented for public display and are assessed on content and presentation. 

Lecture list:
Immunology-5 lectures 
Cancer-6 lectures 
Chronic Inflammatory Disorders-6 lectures 
Neurological Diseases-6 lectures
Class Based MCQ-1 lecture class
learningOutcomes: Discuss the functions of both the cellular and non-cellular components of the innate and adaptive immune responses.
Describe the cellular components of the immune system and discuss the anti-tumour immune response, and describe how the normal cell cycle becomes deregulated in cancer.
Compare and contrast the cellular characteristics of the nervous system in health and disease.
Describe general aspects of diseases of the brain, spine and nerves.
Discuss the key cellular and molecular components of chronic inflammatory conditions.
Discuss the link between inflammation and metabolic disorders, cardiovascular disease and connective tissue diseases.
Describe general aspects of chronic respiratory disease.

modules.moduleContent: This module will introduce students to the fundamentals of accounting. Topics covered include: Principles of Accounting, Double Entry book-keeping, Extraction of a Trial Balance, Calculation of depreciation, bad debts, bad debt provisions, accruals and prepayments, journal entries and bank reconciliations, Preparation of the Financial Statements for a sole trader .
learningOutcomes: Record business transactions using the double entry system of bookkeeping.
Construct, complete and balance where appropriate the accounting records of a business.
Prepare a trial balance and initial set of financial statements.
Identify and execute adjustments necessary to finalize financial statements.
Prepare control reconciliations and use other checking procedures to ensure accuracy in completed financial statements.

modules.moduleContent: This module covers the fundamentals of finance and accounting. Topics include: The Finance Function, Introduction to Capital Markets, Sources of Finance, Working Capital Management, Analysis of Financial Statements, Introduction to Investment appraisal, Dividend Policy, Personal Finance
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of the finance function of an organisation and the financial environment in which it operates;
Assess financial markets and alternative sources of finance to meet organisational finance requirements;
Demonstrate an ability to understand and advise on working capital techniques;
Demonstrate the ability to evaluate alternative investment decisions;
Evaluate the performance of a company using financial statement analysis;
Understand the nature of the dividend decision, alternative policies that companies can adopt, their significance to investors and alternatives to cash dividends; and
Demonstrate an ability to understand and advise on personal financial management including: risk management, debt, savings, investments and retirement planning.

Describe and evaluate the management accountant’s role in decision-making, planning, performance measurement, development of pricing strategies and management of risks in a business and the associated ethical issues they may face;
Apply and evaluate appropriate cost accounting techniques to identified circumstances;
Select, generate, explain and interpret decision-making techniques to evaluate business choices;
Describe, evaluate and prepare budgets and financial plans as a tool to assess performance in the business; and
Apply standard costing systems to measure and control business performance and to identify remedial action.

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to provide students with an understanding of the ethical and governance issues that arise in the accounting environment and examines how ethical theories, value systems and principles can guide decision making in organisations. We will examine corporate social responsibility from both a business and stakeholder perspective and explore what universal principles should be applied globally in business. In this module, students develop a deep understanding of corporate governance within organisations and, using real business case studies, examines some high profile corporate governance failures. The module will also focus on the respective roles and responsibilities of boards of directors and board committees and the role of organisational culture in driving ethical behaviour. Students will also develop reflection and group work skills.
learningOutcomes: Explain the role of corporate governance in achieving long-term sustainability in businesses;
Explain and critique the common ethical issues that arise in business and the global ethical issues facing multi-national corporations, with reference to international case studies;
Formulate and apply ethical approaches to business issues drawing on philosophical and psychological theories of ethics;
Research corporate governance mechanisms at play in listed organisations; and

Demonstrate and employ important workplace skills (e.g. research, reflection, co-operation, teamwork, meeting deadlines, problem solving, project management, and presenting professional written reports) in coursework.

modules.moduleContent: This module builds on students existing knowledge of accounting principles to develop an ability to apply generally accepted accounting practices, international accounting standards and relevant legislation to the preparation of financial statements.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a knowledge of The Regulatory Framework of Accounting including the professional bodies that are responsibilities for its development and implementation;
Develop an appreciation of International Accounting Standards and demonstrate the significance of their implementation on the preparation of financial statements;

Apply the principles of various International Accounting Standards and generally accepted accounting principles to the treatment of specific business transactions by preparing extracts from financial statements and journal entries;
Demonstrate the knowledge of International Accounting Standards studied by providing narrative descriptions and disclosure requirements on the treatment of specific accounting related issues;
Outline the key differences in accounting treatment for transactions under international accounting standards and local GAAP (UK. and Ireland); and
Build on the existing knowledge of the preparation of financial statements.
Have the ability to apply and critically evaluate generally accepted accounting practice (IFRS) in both group and non-group situations;

Have a sound knowledge base in all aspects of financial reporting and financial information presentation including the regulatory environment, standard setting structure and ethical issues in financial reporting;
Have the ability to apply IFRS to business transactions including disclosure issues; and
Be proficient in the preparation of single and group financial statements including cash-flows in accordance with IFRS.

modules.moduleName: CURRICULUM THEORY AND APPLICATION IN PROGRAMME DEVELOPMENT
modules.moduleContent: This module examines theoretical frameworks and key concepts in curriculum design. Its emphasis is principally technical as it equips learners to develop effective curricula in totality including researching a rationale, determining an overall aim for a programme, designing learning outcomes, and understanding the role of assessment and evaluation. Each of these practices are understood in the context of literature that critiques their implementation.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of national policy guidelines and applications processes involved in the validation and/or re-validation of major and minor awards.
Prepare an award for validation in line with relevant national policy guidelines.
Understand assessment of, for and as learning.
Develop creative, innovative approaches to curriculum design, implementation, and evaluation.

modules.moduleName: CRITICAL EVALUATION AND QUALITY ASSURANCE IN PROGRAMME VALIDATION
modules.moduleContent: The central focus of this module is to engage participants in professional, self-analytical, reflective and evaluative approaches to curriculum design in programme development. Students will consider the different preparations required for programme review at two levels - internal peer evaluation and external validation panels. The module will offer a variety of practice experiences in evaluative activities that will enable participants to reflect on the implications of programme design for providers, practitioners and learners. Through these processes, students will increase their knowledge of methods of evaluation and their confidence in defending programme design and assessment.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an ability to critically reflect on practice, processes and structures of programme design and validation.
Critically assess theoretical approaches informing evaluation processes.
Demonstrate understanding of appropriate evaluation concepts and approaches.

modules.moduleName: GROUP WORK IN ADULT, COMMUNITY AND FURTHER EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: The aim of this module is to introduce  theory and practice of group work and to explore the skills necessary for the effective facilitation of adult learning groups. The module will particularly focus on facilitating adult learning groups, styles and skills in group facilitation; Experiential learning; Managing challenging situations and conflict resolution.
learningOutcomes: differentiate group concept and group work as an approach in the context of adult and community education
recount the skills of effective group facilitation applicable to adult and community education
have a conceptual understanding of the theories underpinning group work in adult and community education
practice competent facilitation skills for work in adult and community education.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will expose students to a wide range of teaching and learning methods and provide an opportunity for reflection on their own teaching practice. It seeks to develop student capacity in creative, interactive teaching practice and draws on a range of methods used in adult and community education practice. This is a hands-on, practice-based modules where student teachers will gain a grounding in group work, facilitation skills and presentation skills. This module helps learners put into practice the theoretical perspectives they are introduced to across other course modules. Students will also engage in peer learning. 



80% attendance is required for this module.
learningOutcomes: Identify the range of methodologies that can be applied in learning settings
Facilitate learning in small/large group settings
Identify and analyse group work theories and methods
Draw connections between teaching practice and philosophies of adult education
Identify and analyse the diversity of student needs and create inclusive learning environments that reflect diversity as the norm
Reflect on and evaluate their own pedagogical practice.

modules.moduleContent:
The central focus of this module is on enabling students to develop a professional, self-analytical, ethical and reflective approach to teaching and learning practice and to be critically aware of the contexts, and structures in which these activities take place. The module is closely linked to teaching practice modules and draws from students’ experiences on placement.  

Through the module students will, through a range of creative and reflective, processes learn about:  

situating the self in the system:  

establishing a reflective theoretical practice framework:  

engaging in reflective practice:  

becoming a reflective and ethical practitioner. 

 


A minimum of 80% attendance is required and active participation is expected in this module.
learningOutcomes: Comprehend the concept of reflective practice.
Reflexively and critically analyse their own positioning, within the life-long and life-wide adult education context.
Reflect critically on how they themselves learn.
Incorporate reflective learning in a learning journal and a portfolio of practice.

modules.moduleContent:
This is the second of two modules that provides an opportunity for students to develop their practical teaching/facilitation skills and to become familiar with the culture and operation of further education settings. This can include further education colleges, accredited community education, prison education, Youthreach settings, private providers, or any setting that uses FE accredited awards. Across both Teaching Practice modules (AD549 and AD 550) students will complete 100 hours teaching practice in a designated educational setting that delivers accredited further education awards. Students engage in teaching practice in collaboration with a designated co-operating teacher/mentor within the placement setting and under the supervision of Maynooth University. During the course of the teaching practice placement students will engage in professional practice, apply ethical processes, engage actively with the supervision and mentoring process and model reflective practice. Students will incorporate and integrate theory and practice, plan for and undertake group teaching, undertake learning assessment using a wide range of strategies, develop organisational skills in the teaching setting and be involved in a wide range of activities. Students will reflect critically on their practice, receive and respond to feedback on their practice, seek and receive advice and guidance from their supervision team in a supportive environment. 

A minimum of eighty per cent attendance is required and active participation is expected in this module.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate teaching that adheres with the Teaching Council Professional Code of Conduct.
Have completed 100 hours of observed supervision.
Model original and innovative teaching methods that are participatory, dialogic and democratic in nature.
Demonstrate a capacity for reflection both during and following each learning session.
Demonstrate an awareness of and critique the key equality, diversity and inclusion issues facing contemporary Ireland.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides an overview of instructional and assistive technologies that can be integrated into classroom practice.
learningOutcomes: Explore the range of teaching strategies that use technology to maximize student learning.
Apply emerging technologies in teaching and learning in a range of educational environments.
Develop technology-enabled assessment and evaluation strategies.
Critically evaluate social, ethical, and legal issues surrounding the use of technology in educational settings.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED THEORIES IN ADULT AND COMMUNITY EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: This module will provide the opportunity to students to explore the field of adult education and learning through key and emerging theories.
learningOutcomes: Critical engagement with the theories and major debates around the field of adult learning and education.
A critical understanding of the main features and specificities of the scholarship on adult learning and education.
Extending the knowledge base of the field through dialogue, writing and scholarly discussion as it relates to the specific research interests of participants.

modules.moduleName: POWER, POLITICS AND SOCIETY: ADULT EDUCATION IN CONTEXT
modules.moduleContent: This module will provide students with the tools and concepts to explore and critically reflect on issues of power as they relate to adult and community education and its position in wider society.
learningOutcomes: Engage with the key issues and concepts around power and politics, in the context of work with adults, both contemporary and historical.
Critically analyze the sociological debates within academic literature.
Interrogate their own perspectives on power and politics in the practice of work with adults.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will provide the underpinning theories of ethics in work with adults. 
It will contextualize these theories with the wider obligations and responsibilities in work with adults. 
It will provide students with the opportunity to engage with these theories in order to build their own perspectives in order to conduct their research.
learningOutcomes: Develop their own ethical perspectives in their work with adults.
consider the issues of ethics in related fields, underpinned by theories of radical humanistic psychology, critical theory and public sphere issues.
have a breadth of reading to inform their understanding of ethical considerations, and contextualize these understandings with the literature on morality, justice and fairness.
review the issue of justice and equality in the wider society.

modules.moduleName: THEORY AND PRACTICE OF ASSESSMENT IN FURTHER EDUCATION AND TRAINING (F.E.T.)
modules.moduleContent:
This module provides the foundation content for this certificate course. this module will address the theory and practice of assessment as it applies to further education and training (FET) in Ireland. Drawing on the experience of the course participants lectures will present theory that will contextualize those experiences and lead discussions on the application of theory to the practice of assessment in FET. lectures will invite participants to share challenges in applying assessment in FET and will encourage participants to link the theory and practice of assessment to FET policy and program planning.


This module contributes to the UN Sustainable Development Goal four ((SDG 4) “Ensure inclusive and equitable quality education and promote lifelong learning opportunities for all” and particularly target 4.3 “By 2030, ensure equal access for all women and men to affordable and quality technical, vocational and tertiary education, including university.”
The module contributes to SDG 5 target 5.6. Gender equality, SDG 8 target 8.6. Decent work and sustainable growth, and SDG 13 target 13.3. Climate change mitigation.
learningOutcomes: Critique assessment theory and practice in AFET
Review how assessment practice could better meet diverse learner needs.
Critically review AFET programme design, learner progression and learning objectives.
Reflect the assessment challenges of AFET settings.

modules.moduleName: INTEGRATING THEORY AND PRACTICE IN ADULT GUIDANCE COUNSELLING
modules.moduleContent:
Group work from a Psychodynamic Perspective:
Organisation Systems and Dynamics:
Human Resource Management:
Occupational Psychology:
Theories of Psychometric Testing:
Statistics:
Key Psychometric tests and their application in Adult Guidance:
New and Emerging Issues in Adult Guidance:
Models of Best Practice in working with Disability, Multicultural Issues and Mental Health:
Humanistic, Psychodynamic and Cognitive Behavioural Approaches to Counselling:
The self of the Counsellor:
Boundaries between Guidance and Counselling:
Referral  issues in the context of Adult Guidance:
Leading and Managing a Guidance Service.
learningOutcomes: Capacity to reflect in the light of theory  on the emotional and psychological dynamics and demands of interacting in a group setting
Mastery of the key theories and approaches to Counselling
Capacity to apply and critique models of Counselling in the context of Adult Guidance
A critical appreciation of  organisational theory in terms of structure, culture and development
A critical understanding of the key components of Human Resource Management
A critical engagement with principles and best practice in Occupational Psychology and its relevance in Adult Guidance
Knowledge of  key psychometric tests and their application in Adult Guidance
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The module introduces students to the study of language as a social phenomenon, in particular the reciprocal relationship between language and society, and deepen their knowledge of such. This overview is contextualised particularly within the realities of modern globalised societies with competing local, regional, national, supranational, and international agendas. The topics examined in this module include language and communities, identity, gender, education, politics, power and rights. Additionally, given Sociolinguistics’ anchoring in empirical data generation and analysis, students will also engage with the various avenues and approaches to sociolinguistic enquiry.
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms the salient issues in the areas of language and communities, identity, gender, education, politics, power and rights, with regard to English and other languages.
Evaluate and apply the various theories discussed in the module to broaden their understanding of the relationship between language and society.
Research and construct a critical analysis of academic discourses on a chosen topic in the area of Sociolinguistics.
Research, develop and articulate in writing balanced and well-structured arguments.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides an overview of Intercultural Studies, an interdisciplinary field that aims to understand how language, culture and communication intersect in the context of (inter)cultural encounters. The module begins by exploring definitions of culture, language, identity, competence, multiculturalism, interculturality, and cultural awareness, and by providing an overview of some of the main approaches to Intercultural Competence. Building on such foundations, the module continues by focusing on the connection between Intercultural (Communicative) Competence, and language teaching and learning. The final section of the module provides empirical case studies that illustrate the development of Intercultural Competence in the foreign language learning and teaching environment. Given the phenomenological nature of interculturalism, this module values both theory and experiential knowledge.
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms different concepts of language and culture and assess their relevance for foreign language learning.
Describe different concepts of (inter)culture, language and competence.
Explain in detail the relevance of intercultural competence for foreign language learning/teaching.
Evaluate different models of intercultural competence.
Develop a clear, coherent and well-structure presentation, demonstrating appropriate use of pertinent theory and illustrative examples.
Research, develop and articulate in writing balanced and well-structured arguments.

modules.moduleContent: The module investigates the phenomenon of Global English, examining its historical evolution and exploring its current sociolinguistic reality across an array of national and supranational contexts before engaging with two of the most tangible manifestations of Global English in the educational context, TESOL (Teaching English to Speakers of Other Languages) and EMI (English Medium of Instruction). Students will engage with the language and pedagogical dimensions underlying each phenomena and analyse the sociolinguistic debates surrounding the development of such phenomena into language political industries which impact significantly on the status and learning of native and non-native varieties of English, and other international, national, regional and minority languages.
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms the evolution of Global English and the current sociolinguistic arrangements surrounding it in a selection of national and supranational contexts.
Research and construct a critical analysis of academic discourses in the area of Global English.
Demonstrate an understanding of the debates surrounding the status and teaching of native and non-native varieties of English.
Evaluate the impact of Global English on selected local, regional, national and supranational contexts and their respective languages.
Research, develop and articulate in writing balanced and well-structured arguments.

modules.moduleContent: Crime saturates the media, pop culture, and everyday conversations. Recently, the police have come under greater scrutiny in political and social debates that question its practices and demand either reform, or an overall rethinking of crime control. But anthropologists have long argued that neither crime nor policing are stable categories – for example, crime control relies on many different and evolving social mechanisms. In this course, we look at some of the most interesting insights available in ethnographies of crime and policing, from early writing about crime control in stateless societies to contemporary studies of transnational gangs, and from violent customary justice to the role of elite special forces and secretive intelligence units. Ethnography will illuminate the institution of policing and highlight some alternative possibilities. Along the way, students will learn about ethnographic research and writing on crime and policing, its limitations, and the rich insights that it may provide.
learningOutcomes: Understand the ethnographic approach to crime and policing, with its ethical, methodological, legal, and political dilemmas and its advantages and limitations
Understand the complexities and variability of “crime” and “policing” as social categories in various temporal, social, and cultural contexts

Understand and analyse critically different forms of crime (petty crime, drug trade, terrorism, trafficking, ganghood & other forms of organised crime) and the policing of those phenomena in different times and places
Analyse critically social discourses and academic approaches to crime and policing.

modules.moduleContent: Dizzying advances in biomedical knowledge and technology have the potential to save lives and improve health. However, they can also create new risks, social control, inequality, and ethical uncertainty. Biomedicine has the ability to transform individuals and social relationships along societal divisions, leading to the emergence of new types of people, populations, and publics. As health and well-being have become a primary objective of governments and the market, the meanings of citizenship and styles of subjectivity have changed. This module explores these dynamics through two main themes: (a) the logic of optimization and contemporary aspirations for human enhancement through technoscience, and (b) the understanding of risk as a contemporary social and political problem. Case studies include elective surgeries and treatments categorized as "cosmetic," disputed diagnoses such as fibromyalgia, long-COVID, chronic Lyme, and the use of nootropics.
learningOutcomes: Describe and analyse features of different medical systems;
Understand important critiques of clinical biomedicine;
Reflect with greater sophistication upon students’ own encounters with disease and ill health’;
Gain greater understanding of the cultural dynamics that have created more social visibility for heterodox beliefs related to health and medicine (such as ideas about anti-vaccination);
Understand how social inequality produces differential health outcomes;
Understand how to put disease and ill health – as well as biomedicine or traditional forms of medicine – into social and cultural context.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The module will introduce students to ethnographic museums as problematic spaces of cultural knowledge production. From the outset, the establishment of ethnographic museums in colonial construction and European imagination was heavily influenced by the need to represent cultures of the colonised ‘other’ as timeless, frozen, and static. Henceforth, together with students we will critique the anthropological category of ‘objects’ that projected the makers, users, and owners as anonymous thus silencing and obscuring biographies and meanings embedded in these ‘objects’. At the same time, museum curatorship will be critically discussed in view of extending the principle of care beyond ethos of ‘object’ conservation for posterity towards addressing what it also means to care for originating communities whose living cultures are contained in ethnographic museums. In the discussions we will present an outlook for the ethnographic museum of future that is informed by a commitment to inclusivity and collaborations with originating communities in curatorial practices. Collaborations, open engagements, co-curatorship, co-production, multi-vocal conversations and shared authority with originating communities in Africa, Asia, Oceania and the Americas from where the objects were collected from during the colonial period will be explored as methodologies that can be embraced by ethnographic museums of the future. Together with students we will have a chance to visit the National Museum of Ireland to analyse its representation of ethnographic objects in exhibitions.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of anthropology in the classification, categorisation and presentation of cultures of the ‘other’ in ethnographic museums.
Rethink the role of museum curators as facilitators of dialogue and custodians of people’s heritage and not as unchallenged authorised experts.
Critically present different histories of collecting which include missionary led, anthropological researches, donations, looting and appropriations.
Analyse the ways in which exhibitions are being co-curated/ co-produced with diaspora and originating communities within ethnographic museums.
Apply their conceptual understanding of objects in museums as living cultures with active biographies.
Present a nuanced case for the ethnographic museum of the future which should be inclusive, caring and collaborative.

modules.moduleContent: This course is designed to introduce the student to how the relationships between personal minds and socio-cultural phenomena have been approached by anthropologists over the past one hundred years or so. The lectures focus on how specific thinkers have understood the problem of the individual mind within various social-cultural contexts in pursuit of models of social analysis and understandings of individuals that might have some actual relationship to how humans variably fashion their lives in different times and places.
learningOutcomes: Feel confident in her/his knowledge and understanding of how the relationships between personal minds and social-cultural phenomena have been approached by anthropologists over the past one hundred years or so.
critically assess these different traditions, including common sense notions of ‘human nature’ at large in European and North American societies.
successfully approach novel problems with the critical tools developed in this course.

modules.moduleContent: This module tries to familiarise students with critical anthropological perspectives on sustainability. This will involve understanding the environmental dimensions of the simultaneous historical emergence of mass poverty and mass prosperity in distinct world regions, and critically evaluating the sustainable development efforts used to address the former, using a core ethnography and shorter key texts. The module begins by considering the environmental antecedents and implications of the long historical process of the making of the contemporary Global South and goes on to probe the exacerbation of both global inequality and ecological crisis in the era of globalization. Excerpts from landmark texts by Mike Davis, Peter Worsley and Amita Baviskar are among the readings assigned for the first segment of this module. The second segment is based on a close textual reading of Misreading the African Landscape, a classic environmental ethnography by James Fairhead and Melissa Leach. This module is a standalone module offered in the Anthropology Department; it is also the second half of the elective stream 'Prioritising People and Planet', which is a teaching collaboration between the International Development and the Anthropology departments.
learningOutcomes: Gain an introductory knowledge of the intertwined roots of global inequalities and ecological crises,
Encounter a variety of perspectives regarding sustainable development interventions,
Understand how power and privilege can be seen through the prism of sustainability,
Gain further familiarity with ethnographic and theoretical modes of argument.

modules.moduleContent: This module explores the built environment from a material culture perspective. We will focus on examples that range from south-east Asian long-houses and theme parks to toilets and kitchens in student accommodation. Through diverse examples that focus on both the practices and the objects found in homes, we will explore how public buildings and private housing play an active role in mediating, constituting and intervening in human relationships. Student participation, group-work and peer discussion will be expected in all sessions.
learningOutcomes: Apply anthropological theory to concrete examples in their own lives.
Have an understanding of anthropological theory and where material culture fits within a broader disciplinary canon.
Be familiar with the key figures and theoretical movements in Anthropology.

modules.moduleContent: This module is an individualized ethnographic workshop that develops student thesis projects through structured exercises that are overseen by the thesis supervisor. Students will understand how the concrete details and data gathered in ethnographic research and fieldwork are synthesized in anthropological analysis and writing by doing it themselves. This is where the 'magic' of anthropology happens. Individually guided by supervisors, students will learn how to bring together their research and their own cultural knowledge, as resources for constructing analytic or descriptive exercises that develop the ideas and arguments of the thesis. Thus, the module is ‘autopoetic’: it generates its own content through its practice. Writing and research exercises comprise the principal work of the course. Each writing and research milestone is assembled into a portfolio that the thesis supervisor critiques and assesses. Criteria for assessment will include the richness, creativity, seriousness, and care exhibited in the work assembled in the portfolio.
learningOutcomes: Imagine, develop, and execute a complex research project.
Generate forms of evidence or findings needed to make an argument persuasive.
Conduct ethical social research under complex cultural, legal, and technological conditions.
Create ethnographic representations – using writing, imagery, bibliographic knowledge, and so on – that are powerful and persuasive.
Develop their own unique interests in anthropology, and how to kindle their own ethnographic voice.
Make their own anthropological voice impactful in society.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This course involves the writing up and completion of a B.A. thesis.
learningOutcomes: As a result of this module, the student will have completed all requirements towards their B.A. thesis.
The student will be able to orally present relevant theoretical and ethnographic data pertaining to their thesis.
The student will be able to analyse and develop data collected for their thesis for use in constructing a theoretically-informed argument of relevance to current anthropological issues.

modules.moduleContent: Visual Ethnography is a practice focused module that teaches students to critically evaluate the utility of visual methods in the production of anthropological knowledge. Students will become conversant in the history, major ‘turns’ and challenges of visual anthropology and receive a practical introduction to ethnographic filmmaking and photography for fieldwork, lighting, sound and editing.
learningOutcomes: Be conversant in filmmaking and photography techniques and discuss the utility of visual methods in the production of anthropological knowledge.

Demonstrate an informed understanding of the elements of ethnographic film production such as how to plan, research, storyboard, project manage and produce an ethnographic film shoot, including negotiating access and consent with gatekeepers.

Demonstrate the ability to use filmmaking and photographic equipment, control sound and light and use Adobe Creative Cloud editing software to efficiently create short films whilst showing awareness of the decisions and biases implicit to editorial decision making.
Critically discuss the history and major ‘turns’ within visual anthropology, including approaches and challenges around representation.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This course is designed to interrogate the concepts of “addiction” and “recovery” in ethnographic and historical contexts. We examine different models of addiction, from the biological to the social. We then look at several case studies examining the use of ostensible  addictive substances or the engaging in supposedly addictive behaviour. We then go on to examine varying understandings of “Recovery”, that is leaving the compulsion to use a substance or engage in a behaviour, that is considered problematic. We conclude with the examination of broader questions of what addiction and recovery can tell us about being human.
learningOutcomes: To understand Addiction and Recovery in ethnographic and historical contexts.
To be able to critically assess models of addiction and recovery.
To be able to show such understanding in both oral presentations and written assignments.

modules.moduleContent: Thesis on a topic of contemporary and theoretical interest
learningOutcomes: Select research methods appropriate to a research project
Deploy research methods appropriate to a research project
Analyse ethnographic and other data and derive empirical conclusions.

modules.moduleContent: Thesis on a topic of contemporary and theoretical interest
learningOutcomes: Select research methods appropriate to a research project.
Deploy research methods appropriate to a research project.
Analyse ethnographic and other data and derive empirical conclusions.

modules.moduleContent: In this module students gain credits by working iteratively with their supervisor(s) on research methodology as their thesis evolves.
learningOutcomes: Select research methods appropriate to a research project
Deploy research methods appropriate to a research project
Analyse ethnographic and other data and derive empirical conclusions.

modules.moduleContent: Advanced Studies in Ethnographic Filmmaking is a practice-led module that teaches students to critically evaluate visual media and provides instruction on how to create short ethnographic films. The course is composed of a mix of filmmaking workshops in Maynooth and local fieldwork. Practical instruction will be given in filmmaking, photography, storyboarding, lighting, sound and editing. Small group film projects comprise a substantial element of this module.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate and discuss ethnographic and documentary films.
Create a short ethnographic film and demonstrate an awareness of all key steps from pre to post production.
Understand camera, sound and lighting equipment and their challenges under different environmental, political, and social conditions.
Edit and export a simple ethnographic documentary including subtitles, credits, graphics and sound.

modules.moduleContent: This module is a comprehensive introduction to ethnography. Ethnography is more than a method: it comprises a whole style of thought encompassing forms of observation, analysis, and writing. The module therefore emphasizes analysis and theory in addition to the research practices (interviewing, participant observation, note-taking) conventionally associated with qualitative research methodology. Themes covered include: culture and difference, contexts and cases (working in NGOs, clinics, corporations), styles of representation and the politics of knowledge, research ethics and ethnographic engagement. The module is also structured as a workshop, so that ethnographers at various stages of their careers -- from students planning proposals, to dissertation writers analyzing previously collected material, to research professionals who may not be based in academia -- will be able to produce work within the module that relates to their respective career stages, locations, and goals.
learningOutcomes: Become familiar with the practical aspects of conducting anthropological field research.
Understand contemporary debates relating to ethnographic practice.
Learn analytical techniques for working with anthropological field materials.
Feel confident to begin their own independent field research project.

modules.moduleContent: This module confronts a contradiction in the experience of social life familiar the world over:  whilst evermore concerned about individual privacy in the era of the internet, people go on divulging the most private details of their lives into the vast unknown auditoria comprising the world wide web.  Make it make sense.  Alongside the multiplication of publics and the tacit equation of (economic, moral) value with publicness (even 'publicity'), there have arrived new types of nonpublic, perhaps anti-public, social formation, but also the contrapuntal feeling that the domain of 'the public good' has been diminished by the intrusions of 'private interest.' Appearing in public, as (a) public, may exhibit one’s commitment to the constitutive role communicative reason plays in politics. However, at the same time, it may involve exposure to surveillance by either state or corporate actors. Even as ‘context collapse’ and ‘the selfie’ seem to have made private life a relentless source of 'social' content, a rich vein of monetizable value, the right to privacy is conflated with the ever-expanding expectation thereof. A quizzical mood of increasing distrust, even paranoia, accompanies a higher incidence of that obsessional disorder we have all grown familiar with: 'posting.' Thus, we observe evolving tactics of disguise, camouflage, hiding, discretion, anonymity, and invisibility in a social ecology that seems to turn every device into an agent of the grid. That distrust indexes a constitutive irony to the liberating, novel, and unique forms of subjectivity and sociality that (counter)publics create. If display/watching defines both the communicative context of politics and the transactional context of the market, state, and corporate power (or instead the neoliberal obviation of that distinction), it pervades or tries to pervade every public. Unwanted attention is a primary 'digital risk,' and privacy is considered a source of protection. Paradoxically, insufficient attention is also a risk: what gets seen is controlled by invisible forces. Thus, when reduced to 'content,' cultural discourse — indeed social life itself — is structured by the (proprietary) algorithms that determine what surfaces in our timelines and search results. And suppose algorithms are computer codes invisibly influencing what becomes public online. In that case, online circulation further presupposes vast unseen infrastructures linking the concrete and the esoteric: content is created and broadcast through enormously complex sociotechnical systems of information and circulation, state surveillance and citizen sousveillance, product design and prosumer impulse purchase, programmer skill, and machine code. All of which makes it necessary to ask: What is happening in private? What even is 'the private' or 'privacy' today? Might we see the private something other than what is left in the shadows of our incessantly flashing ring lights?  Concretely, this module explores accounts of ‘surveillance capitalism,’ attempts to regulate surveillance capitalism (GDPR), the fantasy of privacy, and resurgent rightwing populism worldwide.
learningOutcomes: Advocate both for and against the value of ‘privacy’ as a political issue.
Reverse engineer social technologies which dissolve the public/private distinction, as well as those that enable its reinscription.
Brainstorm marketing strategies and tactics that reflect the emerging social ecology.
Apply novel social theory to their own postgraduate projects.
