modules.moduleContent: To be determined by visiting lecturer.
learningOutcomes: be able to evaluate the validity of existing anthropological and other social science research on a particular special topic in Anthropology and
will be able to orally present relevant theoretical and ethographic data pertaining to the topic in question and
will be able to discuss critically, in essay form, recent theoretical and ethnographic works in Anthropology on the topic in question.

modules.moduleContent:
As part of the CREOLE curriculum, an Intensive Programme (IP) is organised each year by a partner institution. The IP usually takes places in summer. All Creole students have to attend the IP after their first year in the programme. The general topic of each IP is decided each year by the local organiser in agreement with the steering committee of the Creole programme.
The overall structure includes presentations of teachers to accord with the general topic of the IP; student presentations are about their MA research project; the presentation should deal with the following aspects: a short description of the thematic; the main research question; the construction of the fieldwork and research methods to be applied; the main theory(ies) used; 5-10 main literature titles. Furthermore, there may be specific structures at an IP, like interactive workshops, depending on the decision of the steering committee and the local organisers.
learningOutcomes: Show detailed knowledge of their research thematic, main research question; together with
detailed knowledge theories and fieldwork methods.
Be able to provide and discuss a short literature review.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides the foundations for the study of Anthropology and Development by situating the long process of the making of the contemporary Global South at the intersection of world historical and political economic flows. We will begin with a close reading of key texts in the field of historical anthropology in order to trace the emergence of mass poverty, inequality and conflict in our world today. The latter part of the module introduces current anthropological perspectives on, and engagements with, issues of sustainable international development.
learningOutcomes: Develop a detailed understanding of relevant anthropological writings that focus on the emergence of mass poverty in its present form.
Learn to think anthropologically and critically about the structures and practices that sustain poverty, inequality and conflict in the contemporary world.
Become attuned to the contested and contingent nature of culture, and to the ways in which it intersects with contemporary global challenges.
Develop the scholarly skills of analytical writing and presentation.

modules.moduleName: THEORY AND PRACTICE FOR ANTHROPOLOGISTS AND ETHNOGRAPHERS
modules.moduleContent: This course is built on the close reading ethnographic monographs, stressing theoretical, ethical and methodological issues. In each seminar, students meet to intensively discuss their ideas about the ethnographies. A member of staff is present for each seminar and will contribute challenging questions, but discussion is expected to be student-driven. The objective of this module is to form a cohort out of incoming PhD students and provide a context for them to explore how contemporary anthropologists frame problems of evidence, representation, and theory in the building of arguments. Students will critically question how an ethnography weds evidence to theory in order to persuade.
learningOutcomes: Be familiar with the Anthropological genre of Ethnography at a sophisticated level of critique;
Develop skills to argue their ideas among peers;
Sharpen skills to write critically about Anthropological theory.

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to International Accounting Standards and company accounts. Published company reports and accounts. Cash budgets, Incomplete records, Suspense Accounts, Control Accounts.
learningOutcomes: Gain an introductory knowledge of the accounting regulatory regime and the role of accounting standards in the preparation of financial statements.
Prepare the financial statements of a limited company in accordance with Company Law requirements and International Accounting Standards.
Prepare financial statements for a business where incomplete accounting information is available.
Prepare cash budgets for a business.
Prepare control reconciliations and use other checking procedures to ensure accuracy in completed financial statements.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is about applying accounting to understand the world around us. We assume students have not previously taken accounting as a subject. 

The module will address the nature and role of accounting information, classify the users of financial information and how each might use this information. Students will prepare trading, statement of profit and or loss accounts and balance sheet statements of financial position for a sole trader. They will also demonstrate knowledge and understanding of financial statements by being able to analyse and interpret a set of accounts.
learningOutcomes: Record business transactions using the double entry system of bookkeeping
Construct, complete and balance where appropriate the accounting records of a business
Complete and balance bank reconciliations
Analyse and interpret financial information
Understand Cash Budgeting

modules.moduleContent: This module will look at a number of modern management accounting techniques, exploring the decision making techniques that can be used to evaluate business choices allowing for risk, uncertainty, scarce resources and sustainability considerations. We will learn how to use advanced budgeting techniques and variance analysis to assess business performance from both a financial as well as from a non-financial perspective. Finally, we will examine other advanced management and cost accounting techniques such as inventory and working capital management, transfer pricing and performance measurement of different types of companies.
learningOutcomes: Evaluate different modern management accounting techniques.
Select decision-making techniques to evaluate business choices.
Evaluate alternative methods of budgeting, planning and control.
Use standard costing systems to measure and control business performance and to identify remedial action.
Assess the performance of a business from both a financial, non-financial and sustainability viewpoint.
Appreciate how best to exercise control of divisionalised, profit and non-profit seeking companies.
Identify the impact of inventory and working capital working on a business.

modules.moduleContent:
Legal and regulatory framework governing financial management.
The role of the chartered accountant in the framing of financial strategy. 
Financial strategy development including stakeholders interests, long and short term financial objectives. 
Raising capital. 
Dividend policy.
Investment, financing and distribution decisions including sources of short, medium and long-term finance, equity market issues and dividend policy.
Strategic investment decisions including advanced capital investment appraisal. 
Company valuations for merger and acquisition purposes.
Options for reconstructions of troubled companies. 
Managing risk including foreign exchange risk. 
The role of a treasury function within an organisation.
Advanced aspects of budgeting, variance analysis, decision making and divisional performance. 
Performance management including measurement techniques, advanced variance analysis in performance measurement and transfer pricing.
learningOutcomes: Identify the role of the accountant in setting financial objectives and strategies within the context of overall corporate strategy
Assess all financing aspects of organizations including external sources of finance
Demonstrate the ability to critically evaluate the performance of an organization for both a financial and non-financial perspective
Critically assess merger and acquisitions decisions including the calculation of the value of a business
Advise on alternatives for managing currency and interest rate exposure in given business situations
Comprehensively evaluate alternative divisional performance and transfer pricing measurement techniques
Demonstrate an awareness of current developments in management accounting/financial strategy

modules.moduleContent: The objective of this module is to provide an insight into and appreciation of the impact of taxation on individuals and business. The module addresses the application of legislation, the practices and procedures governing Corporation Tax, Capital Acquisitions Tax, Capital Gains Tax, Income Tax, Stamp Duty and VAT in Ireland, building on the knowledge obtained at undergraduate level.
learningOutcomes: Determine and advise on advance aspects of Corporation Tax including the treatment of research and development expenditure, close company, and groups;

Understand and apply the general principles for the administration and computation of Capital Acquisitions Tax, understand and apply appropriate exemptions and reliefs, and identify the timing and manner of payments;
Determine and advise on Capital Gains Tax in a number of scenarios, including understanding and applying appropriate exemptions and reliefs;
Determine and advise on advance aspects of Income Tax including understanding the concepts of residence and domicile, the tax treatment of company directors, foreign and other income;
Understand and apply the general principles for the administration and computation of Stamp Duty;
Understand and apply the principles to advanced aspects of VAT; and
Understand and advise on issues incorporating more than one tax head

modules.moduleContent: Financial information analysis is concerned with the recording of financial transactions, the reporting of the effects of these transactions and the analysis and interpretation of annual reports. The aim of the course is to enable students to understand and interpret the financial statements, and other elements of the annual report, produced by organisations for external users.
learningOutcomes: comprehend the financial reporting environment;
describe and explain the specialised terminology used in financial reporting;
prepare and present a report analysing the information contained in the annual report;
interpret a cash flow statement;
demonstrate an understanding of the principles of group accounts;
demonstrate an understanding of the application of the core accounting standards to financial transactions and events.

modules.moduleName: VALIDATION OF PROGRAMMES IN F.E.T., HISTORY, POLICY AND GOVERNANCE
modules.moduleContent: This module explores the historical foundations of course validation and the legislative frameworks for course validation in FET. It examines and critically analyses national and international policy and examines the roles and responsibilities of the different governance and awarding bodies including, but not limited to, Quality and Qualifications Ireland (QQI), SOLAS, Department of Education and Skill, Education and Training Boards (ETBs). The terminology and concepts employed will be problematized and critiqued using appropriate theoretical frameworks.
learningOutcomes: Analyse the historical, legislative and policy frameworks that guide programme design and validation with an emphasis on the Irish experience.
Understand the contested nature of many concepts at the heart of programme validation and design for example, learning outcomes approaches, national qualifications frameworks, and quality assurance.
Contemplate the impacts that policy and practice have on their own pedagogy and/or oversight in the delivery of tertiary awards.
Demonstrate competence in curriculum design and assessment.
Critically reflect on experiences of a range of FET curricula (PLC, vocational, community training & education, skill/ workplace/in service learning, CPD, etc.).
Identify innovative dimensions of curricular programmes and assessment applicable in practice.
Be confident engaging in debate on curriculum development and assessment in adult and further education settings.

modules.moduleContent:
This is the first of two modules that provides an opportunity for students to develop their practical teaching/facilitation skills and to become familiar with the culture and operation of further education settings. This can include within further education colleges, accredited community education, prison education, Youthreach settings, private providers, or any setting that uses FE accredited awards. 

Over two modules (Teaching Practice 1 and Teaching Practice 2) students will accumulate 30 hours observation and 100 hours teaching practice. This is in collaboration with a designated co-operating teacher/mentor within the placement setting and under the supervision of Maynooth University. During the course of the teaching practice placement students will engage in professional practice, apply ethical processes, engage actively with the supervision and mentoring process and model reflective practice. Students will incorporate and integrate theory and practice, plan for and undertake group teaching, undertake learning assessment using a wide range of strategies, develop organisational skills in the teaching setting and be involved in a wide range of activities. Students will reflect critically on their practice, receive and respond to feedback on their practice, seek and receive advice and guidance from their supervision team in a supportive environment. 



A minimum of 80% attendance is required and active participation is expected in this module.
learningOutcomes: Be familiar with the Teaching Council Professional Code of Conduct.
Have completed 30 hours of observed supervision.
Have commenced independent teaching/facilitation in an accredited FE educational setting.
Demonstrate a capacity for reflection both during and following each learning session.

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED GENDER STUDIES IN ADULT AND COMMUNITY EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent:
To provide the opportunity to explore key aspects of the production and performance of gender in cultural and social contexts and in the context of working with adults in order to develop the students own epistemological stance.

The course will explore
• Key concepts in gender studies  
• Key feminist theories and theories of masculinity
• Explore discourses around the social construction and social organisation of gender in relation to work and education 
• Be familiar with feminist research methodologies.
learningOutcomes: Critically engage with key concepts in gender studies
Consider the intersection of feminism and race
Analyse feminist theories and theories of masculinities
Explore the emergence of new gender movements
Be familiar with feminist research methodologies
Trace the history of the women’s movement and appraise the impact on Irish society

modules.moduleName: HISTORY OF ADULT AND COMMUNITY EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: This module will provide an account of the origins and development of adult and community education.  It will look at the connections and links that this field has with other fields, for example, in applied social studies, in community studies, in equality studies, in labour history and within the women’s movement, and within other social movements.
learningOutcomes: Contextualize their work with adults with the background of the story of adult education in Ireland.
contextualize their work with adults with the background of the story of adult education in the wider world.
review the development of policy in adult education in Ireland, from the response of the Catholic Church to workers education, to the emergence of feminist education and women’s community education.
explore their own areas of interest in policy development or in a vision for the future direction of the field.

modules.moduleContent:
Students will have completed their research thesis. To this end, they will have completed:
(a) Literature review (b)Statement of the issue(s) to be addressed in the IMAESC thesis (c) identified the sources of data used in the research (d) identified their research approach (e) completed an analysis and findings from their research (f) identified future development, if appropriate.

The aims of the dissertation are:
• to provide students with experience of planning and conducting an applied research project
• to familiarise students with the methodological options associated with applied research
• to provide experience of conducting a review of current research as a component of an applied research project
learningOutcomes: Explore critical theoretical and practical issues of research design and implementation.
To develop an understanding of the background to and influences on applied research.
Engage in critical reflexivity.
Engage in critical reading and writing.
With supervisor support, be able to identify, research and write a research project/thesis.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will give an introduction to the  role of Recognition of Prior Learning (RPL) in lifelong education. The principles and guidelines for recognition of prior learning will be studied, and located within the theory and practice of RPL in educational contexts.
Students will critically appraise experiences and learn how to prepare a descriptive and critically reflective portfolio of evidence of prior learning.
learningOutcomes: Critically appraise the relevance of their prior learning with reference to the modules on the doctorate programme.
Prepare a descriptive and critically reflective portfolio of evidence of prior learning

modules.moduleContent:
This module supports students to design and prepare a personal progress strategy for their research.  It contextualises this within the politics and theory of research. 
It focuses on appraising life experience in the context of proposed, enveloping your research design and critiquing the research proposal. It supports students to write for the creation and interpretation of new knowledge. It also builds students transferable skills for Self-management skills, time management, stress management, library and study skills for doctoral research, writing supports, problem-solving.
learningOutcomes: Create a personal progress programme that will guide their progress for the duration of the programme.

modules.moduleName: RESEARCH METHODS IN ADULT AND COMMUNITY EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: This module develops student’s capacities in advanced research methods in Adult Education. It explores the specificities of researching in adult education and learning context, focusing on research approaches congruent with learning contexts, such as arts-based research, narrative inquiry, and participatory approaches analysis of data.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the background to and influences on research in Adult and Community Education/ Adult Guidance and Counselling
Explore the roles and applicability of different types of research in this field
Explore the relationship between theory and practice in relation to research methods in adult education
Develop their critical capacity in research methods to complete a research thesis.

modules.moduleContent: This module will create a variety of learning contexts whereby students will draw on th knowledge they have encountered in all sections of the programme and in their professional experinece in order to consolidate and devel;op their learning within a cohesive framework.
learningOutcomes: locate their research within a coherent perspective
critically engage with educational theories and literature
apply this learning in their research and everyday professional endeavours
use their assignments to build their knowledge around their thesis topic
build and sustain a research community of practice.

modules.moduleName: CRITIQUE OF KNOWLEDGE IN THE CONTEXT OF EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: In this module students will encounter key ideas and new developments in educational thinking, practice, and research and hone their critical awareness of how knowledge, learning and critical pedagogy is understood and approached higher and adult education.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate familiarity with a number of key and emerging theoretical approaches to education.
explored conceptual and theoretical frameworks which help to critically reflect on your ontological and epistemological positioning as an educator and researcher
critically reflect about knowledge,  learning and pedagogy in contemporary education
enhanced capacity to identify and critique sites and practices which exemplify and uphold the world of education as it currently operates

modules.moduleContent: This module will explore the nature of the learner / educator relationship, learning theories and the relationship between research and critical practice. There will be an emphasis on critical reflecting on education, pedagogy and society.
learningOutcomes: have an appreciation of the complexity of factors and beliefs that determine how educators engage with learners and how researchers engage with their world
have a capacity to discuss adult education and learning theory relevant to your research

modules.moduleContent: This module supports students to complete their research thesis, enhancing their capacities in research analysis, completion strategies; presentation writing skills.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate their capacities in research analysis
Develop completion strategies for their research
Enhance their research writing capacities in thesis form

modules.moduleName: RESEARCH METHODOLOGIES IN ADULT GUIDANCE COUNSELLING
modules.moduleContent:
Qualitative Research Methods 
Applied Practitioner research Reflexivity in Qualitative research design and implementation.
Narrative inquiry and autoethnography as practitioner research.
learningOutcomes: A critical appreciation of different models of qualitative research design and implementation in the field of Adult Guidance and Counselling
Production of a Research Dissertation that contributes to the development of adult guidance practice
An ability to design and implement an inquiry in the context of scholar practitioner research approaches
Production of a Qualitative Research Dissertation focussed on Practitioner knowledge

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to allow students to critically reflect on current models of pest practice in relation to advising, supporting and intervening with substance misuse and other addictive behaviours.

On completion of the Module, students will be familiar with frameworks for assessment, care pathways, planning and referral in relation to substance misuse and other addictive behaviours. Students will have explored current evidence based research and initiatives in substance misuse treatment and interventions. The module will provide participants with a theoretical framework and basic skills in the areas of motivational interviewing and brief intervention.
learningOutcomes: Describe frameworks for assessment, care pathways, planning and referral in relation to substance misuse and other addictive behaviours
Critically reflect on current models of best practice in relation to advising supporting and intervening with substance misuse and other addictive behaviours
Critically appraise the theoretical framework for brief interventions
Demonstrate a theoretical knowledge and practical skills in motivational interviewing and brief intervention
Demonstrate a theoretical knowledge and skills in assessment, care pathway planning and making referrals

modules.moduleName: PARTICIPATORY TEACHING PRACTICE - FE TO HE PATHWAY
modules.moduleContent:
The course provides current students who are studying in further education colleges and centres outside of universities, with an insight into what a career in teaching would be like and what it would be like to study on a university campus.

Two specific pieces of facilitation work need to be completed by the participants:

1. Deliver a workshop with other students from a different educator provider that engages them in discussions around the benefits and limitations of going to college.

2. Facilitate a 20 minutes workshop to their fellow-students that explores a topic of their choosing. The students must design a workshop that actively engages a group of learners around a specific topic. Examples from previous groups include – exploring mental health perceptions; the pros and cons of social media; what is homelessness?

Through facilitating both of these workshops the students get to experience themselves as teachers/educators and get a real sense of what it is like to be the teacher in a room of students.

The students also learn about the many different, and complicated ways, they can pursue a career in teaching through studying in university. They are also provided with a variety of real-life opportunities that put them in the position of being an educator with the responsibility of teaching a group of school and college students. The students are provided with practical, capacity building training, focused on enhancing their ability to facilitate a group of learners and design and deliver an educational workshop.

The objective of the course is to provide students with a real experience of being in the role of a teacher/educator. Then following on from this the students are provided with in-depth but clear guidance on the various routes to finding a career in the field of education be that in teaching in primary or secondary schools, further education colleges, community education or early years.
learningOutcomes: Develop an understanding of group dynamics and approaches to group facilitation
Enhance understanding of teacher identities and diversity in the teaching profession
Design and facilitate participatory workshops
Enhance confidence in learning on, and navigating, a university campus

modules.moduleContent: In the spring, we will build upon what we will have learnt about culture and anthropological imagination and show how anthropology can help us explore some of the key developments in the contemporary world. The themes will include urbanization, migration, aging and life course transitions, climate change, human rights, and transformations in family organization, etc. We will pay attention to how people in diverse cultures live their lives, learn and adapt in the context of social, political, technological, economic and environmental changes that are sweeping the world, which we collectively inhabit. Anthropological focus on culture and cultures as both a universal and a specific phenomenon will allow us to examine the richness of human experience in diverse settings across the globe.
learningOutcomes: Apply anthropological concepts to the description and analysis of real-world phenomena;
Compare and contrast effects of ongoing global change for different cultures and peoples in the world, including our own society;

Interpret key developments that characterize the contemporary world, such as urbanization, technological and scientific progress, migration, aging, climate change, transformations in family organization, or human-animal relations;
Discover how people in diverse cultures live their lives and adapt in the context of social, technological, economic and environmental changes; and
Understand challenges to our own lifeworlds through the prism of experiences and strategies of diverse people’s around the globe.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This course of lectures and tutorials explores how anthropological field research is designed and carried out in settings ranging from remote villages to urban or digital environments, from organic communities of people to complex formal organizations or processes, distributed across many sites and cultures. The course addresses how such research gets written up as ethnographies and the class will have the opportunity to interact with professional ethnographic practitioners working in government, NGO and business environments. Students will learn practical ethnographic field techniques by carrying out a field exercise in participant-observation, and will explore how to design an anthropological research project, including planning, literature and ethics review, fieldwork, analysis and write-up phases. Moreover, students will learn the epistemological foundations of anthropological research, how to prepare an annotated bibliography, and develop an ethnographic research proposal.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of key theoretical concepts, philosophies and critiques of ethnographic research methods.
Develop cultural sensitivity and show awareness of the ethical frameworks and issues involved in conducting qualitative research in different social and cultural contexts.
Apply ethnographic research methods to conduct a participant observation exercise and demonstrate the ability to prepare fieldnotes including a critical reflection.
Develop necessary skills to formulate a research proposal and a correctly referenced annotated bibliography.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: As the hallmark feature of social anthropology, ethnography is at the core of methodological, ethical, and theoretical debates in the discipline. This module invites students to deepen their understanding and knowledge of ethnography as a method, and of ethnographies as the written end result of anthropological research. Firstly, we will study (excerpts from) various classic ethnographies examine the methodological issues, ethical discussions, and theoretical commitments of each kind. Later in the module, we will develop ethnographic writing skills by critically examining issues of style.
learningOutcomes: Understand the place and evolution of various kinds of ethnography in the discipline of anthropology, and the methodological, ethical, epistemological and theoretical issues at stake in each kind

Read ethnographic texts with a critical eye to key issues such as positionality, voice, representation, etc., and analyse critically how ethnographic texts reflect methodological and epistemological questions in anthropology
Identify the features of different writing styles in ethnography
Develop ethnographic writing skills and techniques by practicing and experimenting with various kinds of ethnographic writing.

modules.moduleContent: Security is a frequent concern in our time. This seminar involves critical overviews of surveillance and security studies, historically and in the contemporary moment. Seminar participants will understand how various phenomena, groups of people, or things are constructed and managed as a security threat in a variety of contexts. From predictive policing to crime prevention, from complex security apparatuses such as US national security to the control of migration, and from prepper culture to counterterrorism, we will understand the historical, political, and social dynamics that contribute to the production of (in)security. We will examine processes such as gatedness, risk, preparedness and, of course, the cultural production of fear. From biometric technologies to refugee displacement and from migration control to bioterror, this seminar involves close attention to contemporary examples with the aim of staking out an anthropological position in relation to security.
learningOutcomes: Understand how securitization and surveillance are situated in the social sciences, as well as the specificities of the anthropological approach to security
Understand processes of securitization, gatedness, risk, preparedness and the cultural production of fear in a variety of historical and geopolitical contexts

Distinguish between specific conditions and features of security and surveillance and analyse critically security apparatuses such as US national security, crime prevention, migration governance, or counterterrorism
Explore contemporary examples of anthropological studies of biometric technologies, refugee displacement, migration control or bioterror in relation to security.

modules.moduleContent: This module explores service innovation through the context of human experience. It will equip undergraduate students with the transferable skillset required to bridge academic learning with the applied contexts of UX research practitioners. Students will explore user experience in terms of both qualitative and quantitative research methods. Using methods such as design ethnography, experience-centred design and co-design, students will develop a toolkit for capturing experience across a range of societal and organisational issues. This module will illustrate how students can create innovative ways of responding to these issues, bringing about positive change through evidence-based practices. Major international organisations are seeking individuals who can bring creative but also critical responses to problem-solving. This module will expose students to the potential of innovation to initiate or manage change in the design of private and public sector products and services, while also reflecting on public good and societal responsibility.
learningOutcomes: Evaluate the validity of existing anthropological and other social science research on a particular special topic in Anthropology, and
Orally present relevant theoretical and ethnographic data pertaining to the topic in question, and
Discuss critically, in essay form, recent theoretical and ethnographic works in Anthropology on the topic in question.

modules.moduleName: FORENSIC ANTHROPOLOGY: COLD CASE ANALYSIS, MISSING PERSONS AND UNIDENTIFIED HUMAN REMAINS
modules.moduleContent: The module is an overall introduction to the use of forensic human identification disciplines (aimed toward students of Anthropology and Criminology). In the first six weeks, students will learn about the practical application of skeletal excavation and analysis in both archaeological and forensic contexts. It will detail the development of this specialism and will focus in particular on the methods used in analyses, and the information which may be ascertained, illustrated through case studies. Factors which impact the successful recovery and identification of the deceased, such as taphonomy, will be explored during the course. In the second half of the semester, students will learn about forensic death investigation in Ireland and internationally, particularly related to missing persons and unidentified human remains cases. We will explore how crime scene staff will process a scene and how photography is used throughout the investigation. During this course, students will have the opportunity to hear directly from forensic anthropologists, osteoarchaeologists and guest speakers (including international experts) drawn from key organisations such as the Irish Coroners Service, AGS Technical Bureau, the National Missing Persons Helpline and Forensic Science Ireland.
learningOutcomes: Students will understand the history of the forensic discipline(s) as well as scientific disciplines and analytical skills used and the legal frameworks within which these disciplines work.
Students will understand the basics of Forensic Human Identification (in an Irish context), including the importance of the ‘three primary identifiers’.
Students will understand the practical application of skeletal excavation and analysis in both archaeological and forensic contexts.
Students will gain a sense of the multitude of forensic disciplines, and how forensic training can be applied across an array of professions both nationally and internationally.

modules.moduleContent: This course will cover human and comparative osteology, determining sex, approximate age at death, living stature, identifying palaeopathological conditions and understanding how these techniques are used in archaeological and forensic contexts. Available to Visiting International students only.
learningOutcomes: Learn skills for the excavation of human remains (in a controlled environment).
The consideration when handling human remains, focusing on ethical protocol.
Understand skeletal analysis techniques, including human and comparative osteology, determining sex, approximate age at death, living stature, identifying palaeopathological conditions etc.
Understand how each of the other learning outcomes relate to forensic analysis and forensic cases.

modules.moduleName: KEY CONCEPTS AND ETHNOGRAPHIC PRACTICE II
modules.moduleContent: This module is a continuation of the first-semester AN618 and is similarly divided into two sections (6 weeks each) – one focusing on Key Concepts and the other one on Ethnographic Practice. The Key Concepts section covers some of anthropology's most important ideas, exploring examples of analytical approaches characteristic of ethnographic inquiry today. The Ethnographic Practice section will focus on ethnography as a research method and interpretative framework in anthropology. We will continue to engage with high-quality examples of published ethnographies and discuss students’ own research projects. Student participation and peer discussion will be expected in all sessions.
learningOutcomes: Discuss and critically evaluate a range of key classic and contemporary ethnographic works.
Explain how cultural categories discussed are articulated across different cultures.
Show evidence of having acquired scholarly skills of interpretation and analysis, and demonstrate the ability to concisely outline and discuss emergent ethnographic research.

modules.moduleContent: Thesis on a topic of contemporary and theoretical interest
learningOutcomes: Select research methods appropriate to a research project.
Deploy research methods appropriate to a research project.
Analyse ethnographic and other data and derive empirical conclusions.

modules.moduleContent: Thesis on a topic of contemporary and theoretical interest
learningOutcomes: Select research methods appropriate to a research project
Deploy research methods appropriate to a research project
Analyse ethnographic and other data and derive empirical conclusions.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides an advanced foundation to key social theories, especially from the European Enlightenment tradition but also including the work of recent post-structural and postcolonial figures. Social and cultural anthropology draws influences from social theory, and advanced students of anthropology are expected to become familiar with key contributions to understanding what constitutes the human, the range of human diversity, the nature of “nature”, the power of social categories, that the capacity in societies to change or develop alternatives. This module introduces key figures such as Hobbes and Rousseau, together with Marx, Foucault, and contemporary postcolonial writers. Students will be challenged to grapple with different theories, comparing and contrasting theories before developing their own analysis.
learningOutcomes: Show detailed knowledge of the history and current movements within social theory
Compare and contrast different social theories
Describe the influence of different social theories on social and cultural anthropology
Develop a clear and consistent theoretically informed analysis of cultural issues
Show evidence of having acquired traditional scholarly skills of interpretation and analysis

modules.moduleContent: : More and more of us are leading digital lives, but because the internet is a global phenomenon it can carry a series of assumptions regarding how it is used or who it is for. In this seminar we will consider digital media in diverse socio-political and cultural contexts to explore how individual, group and institutional interactions are increasingly mediated by these technologies. Are ideas and norms regarding human interaction changing? In Part One of this module we will focus on themes such as the presentation of self in online fora, ideas surrounding the internet and privacy, the encroachment of commercial interests in branding and advertising on digital media. In Part Two we will consider these topics through a close reading of ethnographic examples including Filipina migrants in the UK, hashtag activists in the United States, digital migration in urban China, display and disguise in mobile phone use amongst young Mozambicans and smartphone use among older adults in Ireland. Student and attendance and participation is required in all sessions.
learningOutcomes: Show a detailed knowledge of the role of digital media in anthropological scholarship and theory.
Understand digital media in cross-cultural perspective and be able to identify some of the specific social and cultural practices that different media contribute to.
Show evidence of having acquired scholarly skills of interpretation and analysis and have reflected on a concrete case-study of their own choosing.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN THE CLASSROOM AND BEYOND
modules.moduleContent:
This course is composed of 6 3-hour reflexive practice seminars over the year, or equivalent thereof, with an eye to developing the presentation and teaching skills of the student as an anthropologist.  It may be taken concurrently with the Teaching and Learning Course and, if so, the student will gain a further 5 ECTS credit for their Generic/Transferable Requirement.  The Deliverable is an undergraduate syllabus for a course in the students speciality.

Permission from Department must be sought before taking this module.
learningOutcomes: Successfully develop an undergraduate syllabus with correct presentation and teaching skills as an anthropologist.

modules.moduleContent: Cardiff student exchange

Major topics covered include: (i) Introduction to animal evolution, anatomy and physiology; (ii) Introduction to plant diversity, anatomy and physiology and (iii) Introduction to ecology. The animal section focuses on the evolutionary origins, adaptive characteristics and diversity of animals, and gives an introduction to the physiological functions necessary for life. The second part of the course focuses on plants and introduces plant diversity with an emphasis on the diversity and anatomy of flowering plants. Also discussed are plant responses to internal and external stimuli, including plant hormones, and the transport of water and nutrients. The Ecology section reviews succession and climaxes, mineral cycles and energy in ecosystems.
learningOutcomes: Provide an account of factors influencing the adaptation and radiation of vertebrate species.
Describe major animal systems in terms of anatomy and physiology and the principles underlying the maintenance of homeostasis.
Describe current views on land plant evolution and the characteristics that define the four main lineages of extant land plants.
Describe the main types of plant hormones and their role in plant development and physiology and understand how plants respond to different environmental stimuli.
Describe the functioning of the biogeosphere, including the movement of energy and nutrients within ecosystems and the impact of atmospheric pollution.
Perform enquiry-based practical work and submit short reports on this work.

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered include: Human biochemistry, chemistry of the main bio-molecules. Humans as Chordates: Amphioxus as a model chordate, range of chordate animals, Humans as examples of advanced chordates. Evolutionary origins of humans, bipedalism, intelligence, hominid family tree, cultural evolution. The molecular basis of human genetic diseases covering single gene disorders, X-linked disorders, complex diseases, chromosomal imbalances and rearrangements and mitochondrial mutations. A brief outline on the cloning and identification of disease genes, genetic testing, gene therapy and ethics surrounding medical genetics. Human infectious disease, transmission of disease, diagnosis and treatment. Human development: fertilization, embryogenesis, developmental model organisms, organogenesis, myogenesis, maturation, ageing.
learningOutcomes: Describe the processes involved in Chordate evolution and how modern Chordates still retain features first seen in the earliest Chordates.
Describe the anatomical and behavioural changes that have occurred during human evolution, from primate ancestors to the present.
Describe approaches used in the identification and study of human genetic disorders.
Give an account of Mendelian and non-Mendelian inheritance of human genetic disorders.
Discuss the factors affecting the transmission and control of disease.
Explain how advances in microbiology have impacted on improvements in human health.
Describe the main processes involved in fertilization, development, tissue maturation and ageing.

modules.moduleContent:
This module has a strong emphasis on proteins, the regulation of their activity and their participation in cellular processes. There is a particular focus on enzymes and essential metabolic pathways, which will be described in detail, but also presented within a physiological context. An effort is made to present simple experimental evidence to support essential concepts in order to introduce common techniques in the field of biochemistry. Practicals focus on developing data analysis skills, problem-solving, and writing skills. 

More specifically, the following topics are covered: (1) Introduction to biochemistry and a brief reminder of important notions related to proteins. (2) Enzyme kinetics, including enzyme inhibition and applications for drug design. (3) Basic concepts of metabolism and essential metabolic pathways for energy production, including glycolysis, citric acid cycle, oxidative phosphorylation and photosynthesis. (4) Methods in biochemistry, including protein purification, monitoring enzyme activity, monitoring redox reactions.
learningOutcomes: Understand the importance of proteins and their regulation for the adequate functioning of a cell and an organism.
Discuss how the physical and chemical properties of amino acids influence the properties and function of proteins in the cell.
Describe how the physical and chemical characteristics of proteins can be used for their purification and to determine their structure.
Understand that enzymes are dynamic molecules whose activity can be regulated.
Explain how enzymes are regulated, using fundamental notions of enzyme kinetics.
Describe in detail important metabolic pathways, including glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, oxidative phosphorylation and photosynthesis.
Understand the regulation of these metabolic pathways and the connections that exist between them.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to biotechnology, concepts and products; bioreactor design and operation; bioreactor and media sterilisation; process biochemistry (downstream processing) including centrifugation, filtration and process-scale protein purification; lyophilisation.
learningOutcomes: Describe the role of biotechnology in either the production of food (e.g. high fructose corn syrup additives or amino acids) or wastewater treatment.
Compare and contrast different types of fermentation systems.
Explain the processes used for culture medium sterilisation and various bioreactor designs.
Describe the factors influencing cell growth and product formation in bioreactors.
Demonstrate knowledge of the sequential steps involved in downstream processing of protein products.
Explain the differences between downstream processing steps required for industrial proteins and proteins for therapeutic/ diagnostic applications.
Compare and contrast different chromatographic methods for protein purification.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The module provides an overview of microbial cell biotechnology and mammalian cell biotechnology applications spanning the production of beer and the role of fermentation to the development of novel human cell therapy applications. Topics covered include basic anabolic and catabolic pathways in microbial cells including glycolysis, Krebs cycle, oxidative phosphorylation, synthesis of amino acids, nucleic acids, fats and carbohydrates. Two of the lab practicals will focus on the products of fermentation: ethanol and lactic acid. The mammalian cell biotechnology aspect will focus heavily on human cell culture applications including production of recombinant proteins, stem cells and organoids and the use of cell-based assays for drug discovery and toxicity testing. The remaining two labs will focus on aseptic cell culture technique and the development of cytotoxicity assays. Finally, the module will introduce students to cutting edge cell therapies and to the novel chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)-T cell therapies used to treat cancer. Social justice aspects associated with the human cell culture applications and human cell therapies will also be discussed.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the role of metabolism in microbial and industrial fermentation.
Develop an understanding of the importance of microbial growth and culture conditions and use of microbial fermentation to produce food and organic compounds.
Apply that fermentation knowledge to make wine, beer and yoghurt in lab practicals.
Discuss the importance of cell culture conditions and compare cell viability and cytotoxicity applications of mammalian cell culture in vitro.
Outline the steps involved in the generation of chimeric antigen receptor T cells and debate the opportunities and problems associated with CAR-T cells for treatment of cancer.
Evaluate the role of organoids and stem cells in drug discovery/drug testing.
Appraise stem cell therapies approved for use in treating disease or results of current clinical trials.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Students select a broad topic for review. Students may convert this broad topic into a question that is answered in the literature review. Students are instructed on: accessing the scientific literature, reviewing the scientific literature on a specific topic, scientific writing, originality & academic integrity, referencing and citation technique, time management, and documentation of research activity.
learningOutcomes: Use scientific databases to access the scientific literature.
Review the information available on a specific biological topic in the scientific literature.
Evaluate the scientific literature available on a specific biological topic.
Write a properly researched and structured scientific review article on a biological topic.
Understand the importance of Academic Integrity in the process of composing their work.
Prudently engage with digital technologies to assist their learning without compromising the principles of Academic Integrity
Apply proper scientific referencing and citation technique.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered include: Protein characterisation and analysis including one-dimensional/two-dimensional (1D/2D) gel electrophoresis, selective enzymatic/chemical protein fragmentation, peptide purification and amino acid sequencing; Matrix Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionisation- Time of Flight (MALDI-ToF) mass spectrometry; genome database mining; gene/protein identification by alignment protocols; biologicals and biopharmaceuticals; implications of contamination/impurities in therapeutic products. Trends in the production of therapeutic proteins.
learningOutcomes: Describe the structure and mode of action of antibodies, antigens and epitopes.
Compare and contrast monoclonal and polyclonal antibodies.
Discuss the differences between normal, hybrid and humanised monoclonal antibodies.
Describe the potential uses of antibodies as therapeutic and diagnostic tools.
Describe techniques to sequence, separate and identify proteins.
Explain how recombinant proteins can be generated and describe specific therapeutic recombinant proteins.
Discuss the significance of contamination in therapeutic products.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
General laboratory techniques include: laboratory safety; molarity calculations, making solutions, aseptic technique, correct handling and use of equipment; report writing. 

More advanced techniques include: bacterial transformation and plasmid DNA preparation, polymerase chain reaction; Reverse Transcription-Polymerase Chain Reaction; restriction enzyme digestion; agarose gel electrophoresis; immunoblotting; cellular staining.
learningOutcomes: Implement appropriate safety precautions for general laboratory work and work in an aseptic manner when appropriate.
Operate standard laboratory equipment, prepare solutions and write short laboratory reports.
Carry out a bacterial transformation and isolate plasmid DNA from bacterial cells.
Understand the concepts of Reverse Transcription-Polymerase Chain Reaction and reporter gene expression.
Explain the principle of the polymerase chain reaction, design primers and apply this technique at the practical level.
Carry out a restriction enzyme digest of plasmid DNA and analyse the results by agarose gel electrophoresis.
Explain the principle of immunological detection of immobilised antigens by immunoblot.

modules.moduleContent:
Topics covered include: databases, database searching, multiple sequence alignment, next-generation genome sequencing, gene finding, comparative genomics and phylogeny reconstruction. 

This module is capped at 110 students and CANNOT be taken with BI306 as there is a timetable clash.

International students / Erasmus exchange students MUST be available to attend three compulsory labs on Wednesdays/Thursdays from 2-5pm as well as lectures.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the interactions between bioinformatics, computer science, engineering and biological science.
Explain how database searching is best carried out.
Describe modern genome sequencing methods.
Describe how genes are annotated in genomes.
Explain the process of generating multiple sequence alignments.
Discuss the strengths and weaknesses of various protein structure prediction methods.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Topics covered include: study of structure and function of research papers, critical reading of research publications: papers, reviews and other forms of scientific literature, assessment of quality of research publications, use of databases, use of spatial data and information, writing a paper summary, writing a paper abstract, writing up a biological technique, oral presentations, presentation of scientific information to the non-scientific community.

Students taking this module cannot also take, or have taken BI312.
learningOutcomes: Describe the format and content of a research paper, a review paper and a chapter.
Critically analyse the content of a research paper.
Write a concise and accurate account of an environmental biology technique.
Access, analyse and interpret spatial data from remote sources, including supplementary files from a published paper.
Write detailed summaries of a number of scientific publications.
Compose a succinct abstract for a piece of biological research.
Interpret key considerations for presenting scientific research to a non-science audience.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered include: procedures for micropropagation of plants using tissue culture plants, the production of valuable chemicals by cell cultures, in vitro mutagenesis, somatic hybridization, procedures for genetic transformation of plants, and applications in the following areas: shelf-life of fruit and vegetables, herbicide resistance, pest and disease resistance, tolerance of environmental stresses, improved nutritional quality of crops, improved production or altered composition of natural products such as wood and oils, and the production of pharmaceuticals including antibodies and vaccines. Socioeconomic consequences of the technology are also considered.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the range of plant tissue culture procedures and their contribution to a range of biotechnological areas such as micropropagation, production of plant-derived chemicals, and extension of classical approaches (mutation and recombination) to plant breeding.
Describe the procedures used in the genetic engineering of plants.
Critically evaluate the progress in utilizing the procedures used in the genetic engineering of plants for the production of commercially useful genetically modified crops and novel plant products.
Demonstrate a capacity for accessing, reading, and comprehending the primary literature relating to plant biotechnology.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objectives: This course (i) provides the student with a solid understanding of the principles of Immunology, and (ii) discusses specific topics of Innate and Adaptive Immunity in detail.

Topics covered include: Pattern recognition receptors and their signalling pathways, anti-viral immune responses and viral Immune Evasion, Transplantation Immunology, Self-tolerance and Autoimmunity, cross-presentation, T cell activation and differentiation, and T cell exhaustion.
learningOutcomes: Compare and contrast the different pattern recognition receptors, their ligands and signalling pathways.
Discuss viral immune evasion strategies targeting different aspects of anti-viral immunity.
Describe how allografts are rejected by the immune system of transplant recipients.
Describe how self-tolerance is normally maintained and discuss how its failure can lead to autoimmunity.
Describe how dendritic cells can lead to activation of naïve CD8+ T cells through the cross-presentation pathway.
Explain the intracellular signalling events involved in T cell activation and differentiation into various effector T cells during an immune response.
Describe the effector functions of activated and exhausted T cells and discuss therapeutic approaches to restore function in disease.

modules.moduleContent: Bioethics component includes: Identification of ethical questions; major bioethical principles; stakeholders and values; elements of a strong justification; processes for tackling ethical issues; basic Western ethical ideas including classical utilitarianism and Kantian theory, rights approaches; application to issues in biology, biotechnology, medicine and environment. Current cases histories with stakeholder analyses: these may include clinical trials, genetic engineering, cloning, patenting of biological material. Detailed knowledge of relevant biotechnological science will form a central part of the bioethics component of this module. Biobusiness component: To provide the student with an appreciation of how scientific research is commercialized. How funding can be obtained, identifying technology investors, sources of state funding for research output commercialisation. Students are also introduced to the concept of, and implementation systems for, quality management systems that are employed to control the bio-manufacturing processes.
learningOutcomes: Identify where ethical issues are likely to arise in biotechnology and biobusiness.
Discuss pervasive Western ethical positions and interpret their likely implications.
Carry out case-history analyses.
Use the language of ethicists.
Critically analyse the process of idea to product.
Evaluate the various options open to an Inventor with respect to technology protection and commercialisaton.
Describe the various types of quality management system suitable for application in the manufacture of biotechnological products.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objectives:  To expose the students to cutting-edge published or unpublished research results and allow them to extract/filter information and write succinct, abstract style summaries of advanced oral research presentations 

Module Description:  The module is composed of twelve 1-hour advanced research seminars presented by active researchers working in diverse areas of biology, including fungal biology, evolution/bioinformatics, immunology, plant biology and ecology. The majority of speakers will be from external institutions based in Ireland or abroad. Specific module content will change each year depending on the research areas of invited seminar speakers. 

Two mandatory tutorials will also be organized to provide guidance and feedback on note taking, writing of a summary and common mistakes.

NOTE FOR VISITING STUDENTS: Module suitable for students visiting for the full academic year only.
learningOutcomes: Listen to an advanced biological research seminar and filter important information from presented data.
Describe advanced aspects of diverse biological research topics- relating to topics presented as part of the seminar series during the academic year.
Write succinct, abstract-style, summaries of advanced oral research presentations.

modules.moduleContent:
The departmental academic staff set the research topics. Collectively the topics cover a wide range of biological disciplines, and where possible the student will have an element of choice on the subject area. Individual topics will have a narrow enough focus to ensure a survey of the primary literature is appropriate. The student must identify relevant sources of information, assimilate their contents, and use the information to synthesise an informative discussion of the research topic. The student must show evidence of project development (online drafts) and present their work as a written thesis and as an oral presentation.

Students should note that there are a limited number of literature projects available. Literature projects cross semester 1 and 2. The Biology Department oversees the allocation of projects in the first week of the new academic year and allocation is based on third-year grades. Therefore, students may not be allocated a literature project and may be required to amend their module registration after project allocation to ensure that they are registered for sufficient credits in each semester.
learningOutcomes: Gather information on a biological subject from a variety of appropriate and relevant sources.
Integrate, analyse and evaluate information from different sources in a structured and coherent manner.
Attribute information to sources appropriately.
Document sources using standard formats.
Prepare a thesis according to guidelines.
Deliver an oral presentation on the subject demonstrating knowledge of the state of the art in a field of Biology.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Students select three advanced practicals (3x3 hours each) and three professional modules (8 hours total). While the choice of advanced practicals offered may vary from year to year, typical topics include: Protein Bioinformatics, Applied Medical Mycology, Biopharma, Clinical Applications, Bacterial Sequencing, Comparative Genomics of Pathogenic Bacteria, Immunology, Mammalian Cell Culture, Therapeutic Targets in Cancer, Cleanroom Technology, Fungal Ecology and Biomarker Discovery.

The choice of personal/professional development options offered may vary from year to year, typically topics include: Public Health & Emergency Planning, Scientific Communication and Public Engagement, Peer Review and Scientific Communication, Business Risk Management, Patenting & Licensing of Biological Products and Data Visualisation and Interpretation. 

Details will be provided to students in week 1 of Semester 1 by the Biology department.

Restricted Entry: Third year grades may be used to allocate a limited number of spaces. Biology department will oversee allocation. Students may be required to amend module registration after project allocation to ensure sufficient credit in the year. 

Only suitable for international/visiting students attending for a full academic year.
learningOutcomes: Operate a range of standard and specialized laboratory equipment, recognize common laboratory hazards and implement standard safety procedures for laboratory work.
Visualise and analyse data produced during laboratory experiments.
Formulate scientific hypotheses based on observations and experimental results.
Prepare detailed laboratory reports describing experimental protocols and results.
Apply scientific knowledge to areas outside of basic research, such as Health Planning, Risk Management, Science Communication and Commercialisation and Intellectual Property Protection.
Develop strategies for better time management, presentation, dissemination and interpersonal skills.

modules.moduleContent:
Students select one advanced practical (3x3 hours each) and two professional modules (8 hours total). While the choice of advanced practicals offered may vary from year to year, typical topics include: Protein Bioinformatics, Applied Medical Mycology, Biopharma, Clinical Applications, Bacterial Sequencing, Comparative Genomics of Pathogenic Bacteria, Immunology, Mammalian Cell Culture, Therapeutic Targets in Cancer, Cleanroom Technology, Fungal Ecology and Biomarker Discovery.

The choice of personal/professional development options offered may vary from year to year, typically topics include: Public Health & Emergency Planning, Scientific Communication and Public Engagement, Peer Review and Scientific Communication, Business Risk Management, Patenting & Licensing of Biological Products and Data Visualisation and Interpretation. 

Details will be provided to students in week 1 of Semester 1 by the Biology department. 

Restricted Entry: Third year grades may be used to allocate a limited number of spaces. Biology department will oversee allocation. Students may be required to amend module registration after project allocation to ensure sufficient credit in the year.

Only suitable for international/visiting students attending for a full academic year.

Visualise and analyse data produced during laboratory experiments.
Formulate scientific hypotheses based on observations and experimental results.
Prepare detailed laboratory reports describing experimental protocols and results.
Apply scientific knowledge to areas outside of basic research, such as Health Planning, Risk Management, Science communication and Commercialisation and Intellectual Property Protection.
