modules.moduleContent: This module focuses on examples of how fundamental laboratory-based research has led to improvements in the diagnosis of, or therapy for, diseases. Students will also gain understanding of technologies used for bioproduct generation and the in vitro diagnostics and blood product industry. The module examines the development of novel diagnostics and therapeutics and shows how these have been brought through clinical trials, obtained regulatory clearance, and introduced into routine clinical practice. Case studies focus on the development of an innovative Parvovirus diagnostic assay or high sensitivity detection systems. How murine animal models are used in medical research to identify new drug targets will be discussed. Candidate biologic therapies in clinical use for the treatment of inflammatory diseases and the development of checkpoint inhibitors for cancer immunotherapy will be explored. The course also focuses on the different types of molecular targets that can be exploited in drug development with specific emphasis on how basic research around receptor signaling pathways has contributed to the discovery of new anti-inflammatory drugs. This module examines the link between fundamental research and clinical practice and demonstrates how the two activities are mutually beneficial in terms of patient care and therapy.
learningOutcomes: Describe how the various components of human blood can be used as therapeutic products and evaluate the relative importance of the various components.
Assess the relative importance of serological and molecular tests for improvement of blood product safety, using parvovirus B19 as an exemplar.
Discuss biologic therapies in clinical use for the treatment of inflammatory diseases and the development of ‘checkpoint inhibitors’ for cancer immunotherapy.
Explore the role of antagonists for pro-inflammatory cytokines in inflammatory disorders, and IFN treatment of cancer/multiple sclerosis.
Provide a detailed description and understanding of the process of carcinogenesis.
Describe the key steps involved in pharmaceutical or biopharmaceutical clinical trials.

modules.moduleContent:
Course Aim: This course covers the parallel stories of Earth history and biological evolution, with a focus on understanding of the geographic nature of genetic diversity. Beginning in Part 1 with an in-depth study of the mechanisms and analysis of population genetics, in Part 2 the narrative explores how geological and climatic forces have impacted on the survival and distribution of organisms. 

Topics covered include: [Part 1] DNA polymorphism and molecular markers; Statistical methods and bioinformatics tools for analyzing gene flow and inferring historical biogeographic relationships; Gene flow and genetic structure in wild populations; Conservation genetics of endangered species; [Part 2] Plate-tectonics, climate cycles and glaciation; Historical biogeography and tracing migration using nuclear, mitochondrial and chloroplast DNA markers; The postglacial colonization of Europe by animals and plants; The biogeographic impact of continent collisions – recent migratory histories in Tropical Central America and Southeast Asia.
learningOutcomes: Apply statistics such as Wright's Fst and associated formulae to determine the genetic structure of populations.
Explain the association between gene-flow, isolation, selection and drift in the evolution of species populations.
Discuss the variety and utility of molecular markers, and the context in which they are informative for understanding gene-flow within and between populations.
Interpret and compare phylogeographic datasets for different species.
Compare and contrast different lines of fossil and genetic evidence as a means for exploring historical and contemporary species distributions.
Explain the impact of recent and ongoing climate change on the distribution and genetic diversity of species in Alpine, Arctic and Tropical habitats.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: ANTIBIOTICS:  DISCOVERY, MODES OF ACTION AND RESISTANCE
modules.moduleContent:
To introduce the students to antibiotic action and antibiotic resistance mechanisms and the tools used for both. 

The series of lectures would start with a short introduction into how the antibiotics that we use today were discovered and developed over the past century. The next section introduces the students to the different classes of antibiotics, how they differ and how they interact with the bacteria to inhibit their growth or kill them. This section would also give an overview of the process within the bacteria e.g. cell wall synthesis. The following lectures would be a discussion of the different mechanisms of resistance and emerging resistance problems and epidemics of resistance currently of concern. The techniques used to measure antibiotic susceptibility or resistance in hospital laboratories and the molecular methods that we can now use would be described to highlight how a combination of phenotypic and molecular tools can aid the understanding of resistance. There will also be a section on the origins of antibiotic resistance and how resistance mechanisms may have entered the human food chain or other possible routes of transmission to human pathogens and the importance of human waste in the propagation of resistance in the water supply and environment. The module encompasses human, agricultural and environmental antibiotic use and resistance to discuss the problem from a One Health perspective. 

Lecture content (two lectures for each topic):
1. Antibiotic history and discovery from 20th to 21st century (1 lecture). 
2. Antibiotic classes, mode of action and bacterial inhibition or killing and mechanisms of antibiotic resistance (10 lectures). 
3. Emerging antibiotic resistance problems and epidemics worldwide (3 lectures). 
4. 4. Measuring antibiotic resistance (2 lectures).
One tutorial at the midway point to discuss any aspects of the course that the students wish to date.
learningOutcomes: Identify the different antibiotic classes and their modes of action.
Understand the mechanisms of resistance associated with each class.
Describe the techniques used and available for use in the identification and epidemiology of antibiotic resistance.
Examine the roles of global health in the emergence and spread of local antibiotic resistance.

modules.moduleContent:
Students who have taken a research-intensive project (min 6 weeks duration) in the previous 12-month period may gain accreditation for that research in lieu of a conventional research project. Typical examples of such research include (but are not limited to) an HRB summer student scholarship, a Maynooth University SPUR scholarship, Irish Cancer Society Summer scholarship etc. For accreditation, students are required to: Either a) provide a 10 minute oral presentation of their research findings to a department academic, and a short written thesis outlining their research goals, methods, findings and outcomes with a brief literature review OR b) a 10 minute oral presentation of their research findings to a department academic plus a draft scholarship application in the format of the IRC studentships (or other student identified source) with an identified investigator/mentor.

Restricted entry: Biology department will oversee allocation. Third year grades, and appropriateness of prior research may be used to allocate students to modules.
A brief written statement of the prior research project will be submitted by student at start of year and be assessed by the year coordinator to assure appropriateness.
learningOutcomes: Review the scientific literature pertaining to a specific research question/topic.
Prepare a scholarship application or thesis according to guidelines.
Show evidence of designing and implementing a coherent experimental strategy to critically evaluate a research question.
Analyse and interpret the research findings in the context of existing literature in the field.
Communicate scientific findings orally.
Compile a research thesis or grant application format detailing the background literature, methodology, results, and implications of the research findings.

modules.moduleContent:
This module comprises an applied data analysis project to be completed in partnership with external environmental data organisation as a remote internship. A data portfolio will be shared with students, comprising a data rescue exercise, where the purpose is to compile, analyse, evaluate and interpret the supplied dataset. Supervision will be provided by a MU supervisor and a Data Partner supervisor. 

The module comprises independent research and data analysis, completed by the student, with academic supervision. The range of analytical objectives will be agreed between supervisors and students on a case by case basis, depending on the dataset and the identified priorities of the host partner. Direct contact hours will comprise 2-way meetings between the student & MU supervisor, with additional 3-way meetings including the External Data Partner supervisor.

Students should note that there are a limited number of projects available in each academic year. The Biology Department oversees the allocation of projects in the first week of the new academic year and allocation is based on ranking of student applicants according to their personal statement and third-year grades.  Therefore, students may not be allocated a project and may be required to amend their module registration after project allocation to ensure that they are registered for sufficient credits in each semester.

Please note students can choose only one 10 credit module from modules BI450, BI452 or BI453 in any academic year.
learningOutcomes: Compile and organise datasets, and generate descriptive statistics of data arrays in spreadsheet form.
Generate complex data analysis from raw data, for example multivariate ordinations; spatial and map-based data analysis; R-based implementation of statistical tests; R-based data visualisation.
Discuss and interpret data patterns in light of the available relevant literature.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The course has a strong patient focus and examines how the immune system contributes to pathology and human disease.  The first major focus is on autoimmunity.  The theoretical basis of autoimmunity as a failure of tolerance is examined and the major concepts/experimental support studied. The concepts and mechanisms of central tolerance, peripheral tolerance and oral tolerance are described, and routes to initiate and sustain autoimmunity laid out.  Alternative theories such as the idiotypic network theory are introduced, and the impact of hygiene, obesity and gender on autoimmunity discussed.  Autoimmune and autoinflammatory diseases are then surveyed in organ specific and systemic manifestations. The second focus of the course is on transplantation/graft rejection.  The third focus is on Types 1-IV Hypersensitivities, whereas the fourth focus is on primary and secondary Immune deficiency. Cross cutting themes include diagnostics and therapeutics.
learningOutcomes: To identify how the immune system contributes to pathology.
Diagnose common autoimmune or other diseases in humans.
Recommend appropriate diagnostics or therapeutics.
Communicate accurate and appropriate scientific information to peers and the public.
Use information at the state of the art to recommend new therapeutic or diagnostic modalities.

modules.moduleContent: The course will provide a practical approach to familiarise students with a number of bioinformatics approaches through lectures and workshops.  In addition students will be given an introduction to the principles behind database queries, structure, tree construction etc.  Lectures will be supported by tutorials with specific assignments designed to reinforce lecture material.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the interactions between bioinformatics, computer science, engineering and biological science.
Explain how database searching is best carried out.
Describe modern genome sequencing methods.
Describe how genes are annotated in genomes.
Explain the process of generating multiple sequence alignments.
Discuss the strengths and weaknesses of various protein structure prediction methods.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This course will build on previous Immunology to provide students with the skills for the rational design of vaccines. Concepts such as the mechanism and correlates of protection will be discussed with a particular emphasis on adjuvants to shape immunity. A survey of current and future vaccines will follow, always emphasising how vaccines were designed.  This will begin with the history of polio vaccines comparing the Salk v Sabin vaccines, the lesser known failed vaccines of the 1930s and the Cutter incident. Finally, the module will examine the prospect for novel vaccines against the three major infectious diseases of poverty (TB, HIV, malaria). Students will be expected to perform considerable reading of current literature in vaccinology and prepare a policy report with recommendations on a vaccination challenge specific to a resource poor region.

(existing module - BI608 with change in assessment.  Check with Prof Bernard Mahon (bernard.mahon@mu.ie) for the current BI608 timetable.)
learningOutcomes: To give student a full understanding of successful vaccine design and the challenges facing protection of the neonate in the developing world with particular reference to TB, malaria and HIV.

modules.moduleContent:
Topics covered include: databases, database searching, BLAST search algorithm, multiple sequence alignment, next-generation genome sequencing including short and long reads, gene finding, comparative genomics, synteny, gene ontology and phylogeny reconstruction.  This module is capped at 5 postgraduate students.

Please note students who have previously completed BI315 or BI607 cannot register for this module.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the interactions between bioinformatics, computer science, engineering and biological science.
Explain how database searching is best carried out.
Describe modern genome sequencing methods.
Understand genome assembly and short read assembly (in particular) and have a working knowledge of how genes are called in genomes
Describe how genes are annotated in genomes.
Explain the process of generating multiple sequence alignments.
Generate phylogenetic trees and assign confidence to evolutionary inferences.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: CONTROL OF PROTEIN ACTIVITY AND ABUNDANCE
modules.moduleContent:
Proteins are fundamental cellular components that regulate practically all processes in the cell. The control of their activity and abundance is essential for the survival of an organism. This module focuses on essential cellular mechanisms that participate to the control of protein activity and abundance (see topics below). For each topic, experimental evidence is provided to illustrate the mechanisms and concepts discussed in the lectures. These examples typically connect the different types of protein regulation to (i) essential biological processes (e.g. cell cycle, regulation of gene expression by transcription factors and epigenetic mechanisms,…) and (ii) diseases (e.g. cancer, neurodegenerative diseases). This module provides an integrative framework that allows the students to grasp how regulation of protein activity and abundance affects cellular and organismal functions.

Two tutorials are scheduled and focus on the analysis of original research articles, as well as the critical presentation of experimental data.

Topics covered include: 
- In-depth discussion of protein degradation by the ubiquitin/proteasome system.
- Non-proteolytic roles of the ubiquitin system; the ‘ubiquitin code’.
- Regulation of protein activity and biological processes by ubiquitin-like proteins.
- Phosphorylation, cell cycle regulation and signal transduction pathways.
- Other covalent protein modifications, with a link to the epigenetic regulation of gene expression.
- Crosstalk between the different types of covalent modifications; complexity of biological systems and fine-tuning of cellular processes.
- protein/protein interactions and changes in protein activity; prions and associated diseases.
- protein/ligand interactions.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish between different types of protein modifications and their individual roles in the regulation of protein activity and stability.
Describe in detail the mechanisms underlying protein modification and regulation.
Understand how different protein modifications regulate protein activity and abundance in a combinatorial manner.
Use examples to argue how different types of protein modifications, and their combinations, alter cellular functions.
Acquire a more integrated view of how essential cellular processes, such as cell cycle, transcription, signal transduction pathways, are regulated.
Discuss how the mis-regulation of protein activity can lead to disease.
Understand how different modes of protein regulation combine and crosstalk, resulting in biological complexity and fine-tuning of cellular processes.

modules.moduleContent: Attendance and research poster presentation at an international conference.  A suitable national conference is acceptable with prior agreement.
learningOutcomes: Develop a well-designed poster for presentation at a scientific conference.
Engage in scientific discourse during the poster session of the chosen scientific conference.
Reflect on the learning from conference participation and poster presentation.

modules.moduleContent:
This module covers three important areas of Physical Chemistry: 

(i) Thermodynamics. Material covered includes: the first law of thermodynamics; the temperature dependence of enthalpy; entropy; the second law of thermodynamics; the Gibbs free energy function; and the relationship between the Gibbs energy and equilibrium. 

(ii) Kinetics. This topic is introduced with a review of model rate laws, and with basic calculus, establishes integrated rate laws. From the rate laws, expressions for the lifetime and half-life are obtained.  Kinetic parameters that govern chemical catalysis and enzyme activity are investigated.  Determination of enzymatic kinetic parameters using Lineweaver Burk plots.

(iii) Molecular Orbital Theory. The Molecular orbital approximation and chemical bonding. Application of qualitative molecular orbital theory approximations to construction of energy level diagrams for homonuclear and heteronuclear diatomic molecules. Introduction to molecular photoelectron spectroscopy (PES).
learningOutcomes: Show how thermodynamics is used in the study of chemical systems and processes.
Demonstrate an understanding of the factors underlying the measurement and interpretation of chemical reaction rates.
Determine basic kinetic parameters of enzyme catalyzed reactions.
Construct molecular orbital diagrams for diatomic molecules.
Apply the rules of qualitative Molecular Orbital Theory to predict bonding.
Demonstrate an understanding of Photoelectron Spectroscopy of simple molecules.

modules.moduleName: INORGANIC CHEMISTRY: SOLID STATE AND P-BLOCK CHEMISTRY
modules.moduleContent:
(i) Type of crystalline solids: Covalent, Molecular, Ionic and Metallic solids. Unit cells and crystal structures. Packing of spheres, close packing, hexagonal and cubic packing. Holes in close packed structures. Structure of the most important ionic compounds (NaCl, CsCl, ZnS, rutile ABX3 solids …). Defects of solids (Frenkel and Schottky defects). Electronic structures of solids: conductivity, semiconductors, band theory (n- and p- type). Superconductivity. X-Ray diffraction, Chemical Analyses Techniques (CHN Analyses and Thermal Analyses).

(ii) Chemistry of the p-block elements and compounds. Material covered includes: covalent bonding- Revision of determining the structure of molecules using Lewis Structures and VSEPR Theory, valence bond theory, hybridization and its relationship to structure, hypervalent compounds, the principle and examples of hard/soft and strong/weak Lewis acids and bases. General trends among the p-block elements and compounds. Frost diagrams and phase diagrams. The elemental structures of carbon, silicon, nitrogen, phosphorus, oxygen and sulphur. A brief overview of the chemistry and compounds of Group 13- 18.
learningOutcomes: Discuss and explain aspects of the physical and chemical properties of p-Block elements/compounds;
Apply bonding theories to predict the shapes and structures of p-Block elements/compounds;
Explain aspects of Group Trends in the p-Block;
Discuss and describe the different types of crystalline solids and their structures;
Understand the electronic properties of solids;
Determine the composition of a inorganic solid using analyses techniques.

modules.moduleContent:
(i) Physical and chemical trends of selected d-block metals (electron configuration, size, electronegativity, oxidation states…). Occurrence, extraction, uses, Ellingham Diagrams. Binary compounds, oxides and halides. Electrochemistry of “d-block” elements. Latimer and Frost-Ebsworth diagrams.

(ii) Inorganic Reaction Mechanisms. Material covered includes: inner sphere/outer sphere electron transfer mechanisms of metal complexes; ligand substitutions of square planar complexes. Trans effect.

(iii) Introduction to crystal field theory (octahedral, tetrahedral and square planar). Jahn Teller distortion. Common types of electronic transitions in d-block metal complexes d-d (use of Orgel diagrams), Metal-Ligand Charge Transfer, Ligand-Metal Charge Transfer.  Magnetism of d-block metal ions in coordination complexes.
learningOutcomes: Recognise reaction mechanisms common to inorganic compounds.
Predict product formation by using inorganic reaction mechanisms.
Discuss the optical and magnetic properties of co-ordination complexes of the transition metals in terms of crystal field theory.
Discuss and explain the source and aspects of the physical and chemical properties of d-block elements/compounds.
Calculate the redox potential and the Gibbs free energy variation using Latimer/Frost-Ebsworth diagrams.

modules.moduleContent:
Biopharmaceutical chemistry and structure determination methods for biomolecules.

This module examines two important areas of pharmaceutical and biomedical chemistry:
(i) Biopharmaceutical chemistry. This part of the module focuses on the concepts behind the design and development of biopharmaceuticals and biologics and their roles in modern pharmaceutical chemistry and biotechnology. The course material reviews the different sources of biopharmaceuticals, and mostly focuses on the biomanufacturing process. The biochemical and molecular mechanisms of action of specific classes of biopharmaceutical drugs, such as antibodies, and the role of glycosylation of biologics is also reviewed.

(ii) Structural determination methods for biomolecules: Material covered includes: Structure of biomolecules for example, biological macromolecules- proteins, nucleic acids, polysaccharides and fats and small biomolecules for example small peptides, amino acids, monosaccharides and nucleotides; chemical analysis of proteins and peptides, ESI, MALDI and Tandem Mass spectrometry, NMR spectroscopy and hardware development, FT-NMR, 2-D NMR, X-ray crystallography. Emphasis will be placed on relating each technique to determining protein structure.
learningOutcomes: Discuss a number of factors that are important to the techniques commonly used to obtain structural information on biomolecules.
Be able to interpret the mass spectrum of a biomolecule.
Be able to interpret or predict the NMR spectrum of small biomolecules.
Demonstrate an understanding of what biopharmaceuticals and biologics are and their role in modern pharmaceutical chemistry.
Discuss the different sources of biopharmaceuticals and the role of genomics and proteomics in biopharmaceutical chemistry.
Discuss the mode of action and the uses of specific biopharmaceutical drugs.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED PHARMACEUTICAL AND BIOMEDICAL CHEMISTRY LABORATORY
modules.moduleContent:
- The students will carry out learning activities based on instruments and techniques which are relevant to work carried out in the Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Industry, such as UV, GC, HPLC/UPLC and IR.
- The students review literature papers regarding other analytical techniques of relevance to the chemical and Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Industry.
- The students also carry out simulations and problem-based learning activities with focus on different aspects of the above techniques.
learningOutcomes: Gain an understanding in advanced methods of chemical analysis.
Acquire knowledge of the theoretical aspects behind instruments and techniques used in the Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Industry.
Describe the use of analytical instruments and techniques relevant to the chemical and Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Industry.
Carry out independent study and problem-based exercises.
Review literature papers and present their findings in a short presentation.
Assess, recognize and understand the issues of safety regulation in relation to the use of chemicals in laboratory experiments.

modules.moduleContent:
Material covered in this module includes the following:

(i) Organometallic Chemistry: Definition of Organometallic complexes, The Eighteen Electron Rule, electron counting, oxidation state of d-Block organometallic compounds. MO (Molecular Orbital) theory applied to organometallic d-block complexes s-donor, pi-acceptor and pi-donor ligands. Examples of organic Ligands: alkyls, aryls, carbon monoxide, carbenes, alkenes, arenes and cyclopentadienyl rings. Back bonding and hapticity. Examples of d-block organometallic complexes e.g. metal carbonyls, Fischer and Schrock carbenes, metallocenes such as ferrocene. Examples of auxiliary ligands in d-block organometallics i.e. phosphines (Tolman cone angle and electronic parameter). Homogeneous catalysis; the steps involved in catalytic cycles; examples of catalytic cycles; alkene metathesis; Ziegler Natta polymerization. Examples of main group organometallic reagents and reactions that are important in synthesis e.g. Grignard reagents, alkyllithiums, and alkylaluminium.

(ii) Mechanistic aspects of the steps involved in the catalytic cycles of organometallic complexes. Oxidative addition and reductive elimination. Migratory insertion and de-insertion (e.g. ß-hydride elimination) reactions.
learningOutcomes: Describe the bonding/properties of d-Block organometallic compounds.
Be able to determine the valence electron count of d-Block organometallic complexes.
Be able to determine the oxidation state on the metal centre of d-Block organometallic complexes.
Predict or explain steps in catalytic cycles catalysed by metal complexes.
Show knowledge of aspects of the organometallic chemistry of a number of main group elements.
Identify and formulate the fundamental reaction types and mechanisms occurring during organometallic (catalytic) reactions.
Explain and rationalise important organometallic catalytic reactions in terms of their elementary mechanistic steps.

modules.moduleContent:
This module examines two important areas of Physical Chemistry:

(i) Statistical Thermodynamics. This course starts with the partition function and the contributions that translation, rotation and vibration make to the partition function. The application of statistical thermodynamics to the calculation of entropy, enthalpy, Gibbs energy, heat capacity and equilibrium constants is then described.

(ii) Reaction Dynamics. The basic theories of chemical kinetics (Arrhenius Equation and simple collision theory) are outlined and then developed with a detailed description of Transition State theory. Use is made of the molecular partition functions treated in (i) to predict rates of selected reactions. State to state reaction dynamics are discussed in the framework of the model LEPS potential energy surface.
learningOutcomes: Compute partition functions for simple molecules.
Calculate thermodynamic functions for simple chemical systems using statistical thermodynamics.
Apply simple collision theory to predict the rate of a chemical reaction.
Use Transition State Theory to interpret the energy disposal in product molecules.

modules.moduleName: QUALITY ASPECTS FOR BREWING AND DISTILLING
modules.moduleContent:
The course aims to equip learners with the necessary scientific knowledge and instrumentation competencies to develop skills relevant to the alcoholic drinks industry. The course was created in response to the strong demand for an analytical course to support the growing Irish craft brewing and traditional whiskey industries. 
This course in Brewing & Distilling Science is both co-designed and co-delivered by industry experts. The Department of Chemistry has been analysing Irish whiskies over the past number of years, and this coupled with their engagement with beverage industries provides the perfect platform for the co-creation of a course in Brewing & Distilling (B&D).

This course is ideal for:

• A new entrant to Brewing & Distilling (B&D) looking to upskill and develop applicable knowledge in QC quality control and analysis.
• A professional already in the B&D industry who is looking to upskill to enhance their career development. 
• A craft brewer or traditional whiskey distiller seeking a quality training opportunity to enhance employee skills. 
• Irish Whiskey, Spirits and brewing Aficionados as private individuals
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a knowledge of the science underpinning the analytical techniques in brewing and distilling and to apply these fundamental techniques to your individual brewery or distillery.
Apply principles and calculations relating from beer and spirit analysis in aiding process efficiency in a laboratory setting.
Discuss when and where in the brewing and distilling process measurements should be taken and how these measurements are integrated into the necessary quality systems.

Develop an understanding of the main organoleptic components of beer and whiskey flavour, how and where in the process they are formed and the key controls required to ensure flavour quality and consistency.

modules.moduleContent: Postgraduate students teach undergraduate students various experimental techniques and/or concepts in chemistry.
learningOutcomes: To equip research students with practical teaching skills in chemistry.

modules.moduleContent:
Students perform an in-depth analysis of research papers and then present the analysis in a group discussion setting.  

Typical Examples:

(i)  Review of research papers in Organic Chemistry - for Synthetic Organic Chemists. Weekly workshops are held over eight semesters.

(ii) Review of papers in Electrochemistry and Materials Chemistry - for Electrochemistry and Materials Chemists. Weekly workshops are held over eight semesters.
learningOutcomes: To train students in the process of peer review, i.e. research paper analysis and critique.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to equip students with the requisite knowledge and skills to study effectively and present academic assignments in an appropriate format.

Learning and teaching styles; time management and techniques for effective learning; using texts, directories, library reference systems; reading and recording skills; writing skills; essays, reports, projects, theses, reviews, articles; technical writing skills.
learningOutcomes: Engage critical academic skills for the purpose of reflection and self development in higher education.
organise and plan their time in a way that will enable them to participate fully in academic learning and university life over the academic year.
record information, ideas and analysis from lectures, small group leaning activities, fieldtrips and presentations.
select and appraise data, ideas and information relevant to their course of study.
read critically and evaluate arguments, ideas and information from text books and academic resources.
write scholarly assignments that show: clarity of ideas, evidence based knowledge, critique of subject content and competence in academic referencing.

modules.moduleName: THEORY & PRACTICE OF WORKING WITH GROUPS
modules.moduleContent: An introduction to: the theory and experience of group dynamics; systematic thinking and experience of a leaderless group.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate group work theory
Understand the impact of group dynamics in group work;
Critically assess the boundaries between self and group, particularly in relation to issues of power, choice, role, culture

modules.moduleContent: This module looks at the literary historical representation of disability from a range of different historical periods and sources.  These texts and other media will be placed within their historical context and real-world social and economic policies that impacted disabled people throughout history from the ancient world to the present will be considered.  |It will explore how stories such as Grimm's Fairy and Folktales depict the birth and evolution of tropes relating to disabled characters over a significant period.
learningOutcomes: Identify recurring patterns of discrimination and violence against disabled people throughout various time periods.
Understand social attitudes towards disabled people in their historical context through reading literary texts.
Have knowledge of systemic oppression against disabled people, intersectional oppression and mainstream media attitudes towards disabled people.
Critically evaluate, mainstream historical, literary and folk narratives and what they say (and don’t say) about disability.

modules.moduleName: EQUALITY AND DIVERSITY: CULTURAL MODELS AND SOCIAL MOVEMENTS
modules.moduleContent: Applying various models to a diverse range of phenomena including addiction/recovery, disability/learning, dyslexia, ADHD, behavioural disorders, we see that these models impact on how these conditions are viewed and subsequently treated. In this module too, it is recognised that while relations of dominance and subordination are typical of many social divisions, including those of gender, ethnicity, sexuality, disability and social class” (Baker et al 2009) as part of a broader equality movement, each subordinate group can challenge these inequalities in one or more dimensions.
learningOutcomes: Consider-addiction/recovery, disability/learning, dyslexia, ADHD, behavioural disorders, - as socio-political constructs within a right- based discourse (Model)
Understand how structural inequalities including systems of gender relationships impede women in many areas of their lives.
Challenge Gender Role Stereotyping.
Comprehend multiple and interconnected forms of inequality (intersectionality).

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims at developing students' competence in Mandarin Chinese and is suitable for students who have never studied the language before. This is an intensive accelerated course in Chinese language and culture that is designed to provide a solid foundation for a degree programme in Chinese Studies.  The course aims to develop students' confidence and accuracy when using Mandarin in a range of situations. Students will also be introduced to Chinese culture and society through various media. In-class activities will include repetitions, dictations, re-creations of dialogues, group conversations and role plays. By the end of the module, students will have acquired a vocabulary of around 100 Chinese characters and be able to express themselves in spoken and written Mandarin.

Core lectures are supported by language laboratory work and intensive language tutorials. Additional work with self-access material is also required.
This module is aligned to the Chinese Proficiency Test (HSK) at level 1, where the emphasis is on achieving a fundamental level of communication in all four skills, (listening, speaking, reading and writing). A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with HSK level 1.
learningOutcomes: Understand Chinese phonetics (Pinyin), and be able to use it to read and write;
Understand the way Chinese characters are formed, and be able to recognize and write some simple characters or words that are closely related to everyday life;
Understand basic greetings and phrases and commonly used dialogues based on everyday life;
Introduce themselves briefly, in simple vocabulary and grammar (describe basic information about themselves, their family, and their friends);
Provide brief written and oral answers to relevant questions, using simple vocabulary and expressions.

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered include: origin of life, origin of eukaryotes, origin of photosynthesis, origin of animals and plants, the colonization of land, origin of amniotes (including mammals, birds, and reptiles). Species concepts and modes of speciation. Macroevolutionary patterns and key evolutionary transitions: evolutionary trends and emerging phylogenetic relationships in animals and plants. Population genetics: the Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium; evolutionary forces in natural populations - mutation, migration, natural selection and genetic drift.
learningOutcomes: Explain what the tree of life means.
Describe the origin of mitochondria and chloroplasts and relate this to the evolution of eukaryotes.
Compare and contrast the various species concepts.
Describe mechanisms by which reproductive isolation can be achieved.
Use the Hardy-Weinberg equation and the Chi-square test to evaluate if a population is in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium.
Distinguish between the major groups of animals (both living and extinct) and summarize their phylogenetic relationships.
Discuss the evolutionary origins of land plants.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objectives: This course builds upon the molecular concepts covered in BI101. Material covered is focused on the molecular biology of DNA, RNA, proteins, carbohydrates and lipids and the involvement of these molecules in central cellular metabolic processes. 

Topics covered include: Prokaryotic and eukaryotic cell biology; structure of biological membranes; DNA, RNA and protein structures; chromosomes and chromatin; DNA as the genetic material; DNA damage and DNA repair mechanisms; mutations and functional consequences; DNA replication in prokaryotes; transcription and translation in prokaryotes and eukaryotes; cell structures and their functions including the nucleus, protein sorting, protein turnover and post-translational modification; cell processes including cell signalling; cytoskeleton, regulation of the eukaryotic cell cycle, cell death and cancer.
learningOutcomes: Describe the structures and functions of the major macromolecules, cytoskeleton, organelles and intracellular structures of a cell, be able to discuss these in the context of evolution and between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.

Explain in detail the molecular processes involved in the Central Dogma, distinguishing the molecular details that evolved from prokaryotes through eukaryotes, and explain how DNA mutations and DNA repair processes effect this.
Discuss the concepts of gene and protein expression, regulation, sorting, trafficking and degradation.
Define the molecular details of eukaryotic cell signalling, cell cycle, and cell death processes and apply this knowledge to explain the development of cancer.

modules.moduleContent: Recombinant DNA technology; Methods for analysing genomic DNA samples, Regulation of eukaryotic gene expression at the transcriptional and post-transcriptional level, chromatin structure and introduction to epigenetics.
learningOutcomes: Describe the various tools and methods used for recombinant DNA technology and explain how these are applied for research and diagnostic purposes.
Describe the processes of transcription, mRNA processing, and translation.
Explain how eukaryotic gene expression can be regulated transcriptionally and post-transcriptionally (with examples).
Describe chromatin structure and explain how chromatin status can influence gene expression.
Define the terms epigenetics and imprinting, and explain how epigenetic processes affect gene expression.
Comment on the frontiers in the field and open research questions.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The objective of the module is to enable students to appreciate how the behaviour and physiology of animals (including humans) are adapted to their environments. 

Includes topics such as: instinct, learning and the development of behaviour; how behaviour evolves; communication; motivation; living in groups; migration, homing and navigation; anti-predator behaviour; biological rhythms; dormancy; temperature relations; adaptation to extreme environments.
learningOutcomes: Explain how genes and the environment interact during development to influence the expressed behavioural phenotype.
Discuss the role of natural selection in the evolution of animal behaviour.
Describe the various ways in which animals learn.
Explain the physiological basis of motivation.
Explain how animals communicate, avoid being eaten by predators, and find their way while migrating or homing.
Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of living in groups.
Describe how animals respond to environmental variables such as temperature and water availability.

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered include: study of structure and function of research papers, critical reading of research publications: papers, reviews and other forms of scientific literature, assessment of quality of research publications, use of databases, writing a paper summary, writing a paper abstract, writing up a biological technique, oral presentations.
learningOutcomes: Describe the format and content of a research paper, a review and a chapter.
Critically analyse the content of a research paper.
Write a concise and accurate account of a biochemical technique.
Write detailed summaries of a number of scientific publications.
Compose a succinct abstract for a piece of biological research.
Undertake self-directed library research and study.
Take personal responsibility for their learning.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This course outlines basic molecular and cellular concepts of modern pharmacology and toxicology. Molecular pharmacology is concerned with the development, design, structure and action of drugs and their effects on biological systems. Toxicology is the scientific study of poisons/toxins and their action, as well as the investigation of adverse drug-induced side effects. Pharmacological agents and toxins are also routinely used as analytical and investigative tools for the assessment of biomolecular or pathophysiological processes. In this module, various classes of drugs and toxins will be presented in the context of their biological effects in therapy and cytotoxicity.
learningOutcomes: Describe the main classes of drugs used in medical applications and biomedical research.
Compare and contrast the structural and functional characteristics of different classes of pharmacological agonists and antagonists.
Describe the main types of molecular interactions between drugs and target receptors.
Explain the basic concepts of pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics.
Describe the main types of toxins involved in organ-selective toxicity and cytotoxic side effects.

modules.moduleContent:
Advanced bioreactor concepts including mass transfer; the relevance of mass transfer in homogenous and heterogenous bioreactions; fluid dynamics; detailed discussion of large-scale prokaryotic and eukaryotic cell culture systems for recombinant protein production; use of nanoparticles in bioproduction of reagents for high sensitivity assay systems; lignocellulose as a source of carbohydrate for bioethanol production by fermentation; enzymatic degradation of lignocellulose; fungi as a source of enzymes required for bioprocessing; directed evolution of enzymes and application in the improvement of industrial enzymes.

NOTE TO VISITING STUDENTS: Only suitable for international/visiting students attending for a full academic year.
learningOutcomes: Understand the use of, and requirement for, large-scale production of recombinant proteins.
Explain the concept of mass transfer in bioprocessing, and compare and contrast mass transfer limitations of reactions in homogenous and heterogenous bioreactions.
Analyse the importance of fluid dynamics in bioprocessing.
Evaluation of nanobiotechnology and its applications in assay development.
Compare and contrast the various strategies used in modern biotechnology for high-value reagent production.
Describe and assess the importance of bioprocessing in the generation of biological products (e.g., bioethanol, diagnostics, and other biological products).
Describe the properties of lignocellulose, and enzymes and treatments that can be used to facilitate its use for bioethanol production by fermentation.

modules.moduleContent:
The components of, and chemical interactions in, food and food products; microbial aspects of fermentation; use of starter cultures; food processing and preservation; food safety; microbiological aspects of food safety; bacterial and fungal toxins; Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point (HACCP); food legislation.

NOTE TO VISITING STUDENTS: Only suitable for international/visiting students attending for a full academic year.
learningOutcomes: Describe the main components of food.
Discuss food components with respect to composition, properties and impact on food processing.
Compare and contrast the various methods used to improve food shelf-life, quality and stability.
Evaluate the effect of microorganisms on food.
Describe the main innovations in the modern food industry and their role in manufacturing.
Discuss, in detail, the difference between food spoilage and food poisoning.
Explain the role played by legislation in controlling the development and availability of novel foods.

modules.moduleContent: Assessing information: the library and on-line databases. Introduction to lab safety and use of equipment. Introduction to general laboratory techniques: aseptic techniques, basic biochemical techniques, microscopy and cell biology techniques. Introduction to basic and advanced Excel, statistical analyses (e.g. t-tests, ANOVA, linear regression) and their selection, dataset preparation and data visualisation.
learningOutcomes: Implement appropriate safety precautions for general laboratory work and work in an aseptic manner when appropriate.
Use on-line databases to access scientific literature.
Explain the principle of the polymerase chain reaction and apply this technique at the practical level.
Explain the basic principles of electrophoresis, high performance liquid chromatography, mass spectrometry and flow cytometry.
Measure protein concentration and determine enzyme activities using spectrophotometry.
Be proficient in the use of a light microscope, an inverted microscope, a haemocytometer.
Subculture mammalian cells, isolate white blood cells from spleen, assess cell viability.

modules.moduleContent: The module has a lecture and practical component.  Lectures deal with: laboratory safety, research instrumentation, handling genetically modified organisms, assessing information and career planning.  Practicals deal with: introduction to general laboratory techniques, aseptic techniques, molecular biology techniques, Introduction to Excel.
learningOutcomes: Implement appropriate safety precautions for general laboratory work and work in an aseptic manner when appropriate.
Use on-line databases to access scientific literature.
Explain the principle of the polymerase chain reaction and apply this technique at the practical level.
Explain the basic principles of electrophoresis, high performance liquid chromatography, mass spectrometry and flow cytometry.

Analyse experimental data using appropriate computer software, such as Microsoft Excel.

modules.moduleName: CONTROL OF PROTEIN ACTIVITY AND ABUNDANCE
modules.moduleContent:
Proteins are fundamental cellular components that regulate practically all processes in the cell. The control of their activity and abundance is essential for the survival of an organism. This module focuses on essential cellular mechanisms that participate to the control of protein activity and abundance (see topics below). For each topic, experimental evidence is provided to illustrate the mechanisms and concepts discussed in the lectures. These examples typically connect the different types of protein regulation to (i) essential biological processes (e.g. cell cycle, regulation of gene expression by transcription factors and epigenetic mechanisms¦) and (ii) diseases (e.g. cancer, neurodegenerative diseases). This module provides an integrative framework that allows the students to grasp how regulation of protein activity and abundance affects cellular and organismal functions. 

Two tutorials are scheduled and focus on the analysis of original research articles, as well as the critical presentation of experimental data. 

Topics covered include: 
- In-depth discussion of protein degradation by the ubiquitin/proteasome system.
- Non-proteolytic roles of the ubiquitin system; the ‘ubiquitin code’.
- Regulation of protein activity and biological processes by ubiquitin-like proteins.
- Phosphorylation, cell cycle regulation and signal transduction pathways.
- Other covalent protein modifications, with a link to the epigenetic regulation of gene expression.
- Crosstalk between the different types of covalent modifications; complexity of biological systems and fine-tuning of cellular processes.
- protein/protein interactions and changes in protein activity; prions and associated diseases.
- protein/ligand interactions.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish between different types of protein modifications and their individual roles in the regulation of protein activity and stability.
Describe in detail the mechanisms underlying protein modification and regulation.
Understand how different protein modifications regulate protein activity and abundance in a combinatorial manner.
Use examples to argue how different types of protein modifications, and their combinations, alter cellular functions.
Acquire a more integrated view of how essential cellular processes, such as cell cycle, transcription, signal transduction pathways, are regulated.
Discuss how the mis-regulation of protein activity can lead to disease.
Understand how different modes of protein regulation combine and crosstalk, resulting in biological complexity and fine-tuning of cellular processes.

modules.moduleContent:
This advanced module focuses on the molecular and cellular mechanisms of human metabolism, as well as the pathogenesis of selected metabolic disorders. 

Specific sections will be concerned with:
Nutrient requirements, and the body's physiological response to their intake. 
The biochemical and cellular regulators of food intake and bodyweight. 
The diagnosis of metabolic disease and pathobiochemical aspects of major metabolic disease will be examined, including Obesity and Cancer-Associated Cachexia (CAC).
The current therapeutic strategies for treating metabolic diseases including lifestyle modification, hormone analogues, immunotherapies and surgery. 
Discussing the impact each has on the biochemistry and physiology of food intake and the cellular regulators of metabolism.
learningOutcomes: Outline the major macronutrients and micronutrients.
Discuss the factors which regulate food intake and bodyweight.
Discuss the cellular players involved in the regulation of metabolism and bodyweight.
Identify the main diagnostic methods of detecting metabolic diseases in humans.
Define major aspects of metabolic diseases including obesity and Cancer-Associated Cachexia.
Define the major strategies for treating metabolic diseases in humans.
Examine the mode of action of defined interventions including molecular and cellular aspects.

modules.moduleContent:
Students undertake an 8-week research project under the supervision of a member of staff. Laboratory work is scheduled in either semester 1 or 2 depending on constraints on laboratory space and limits on laboratory occupancy. Students may be provided with a reading list but are expected to perform a literature search to familiarize themselves with the assigned research topic. Students are required to prepare a 2500-word literature review on their research topic to be submitted at the end of appropriate semester. Over the duration of the project students should become proficient in the techniques and equipment relevant to the project. All students provide a 10-minute oral presentation of their research findings, and on completion of the project must submit a thesis outlining their research findings.

Restricted Entry: Students should note that there are a limited number of laboratory projects available. Laboratory work is scheduled in either semester 1 or 2 depending on laboratory space available. The Biology Department oversees the allocation of projects in the first week of the new academic year and allocation is based on third-year grades. Therefore, students may not be allocated a laboratory project and may be required to amend their module registration after project allocation to ensure that they are registered for sufficient credits in each semester.
learningOutcomes: Review the scientific literature pertaining to a specific research question/topic.
Prepare a literature review according to guidelines.
Design a coherent experimental strategy to critically evaluate a research question.
Perform experiments using the technique(s) learned during the project.
Analyse and interpret the research findings in the context of existing literature in the field.
Compile a research thesis in publication format detailing the background literature, methodology, results and implications of the research findings.
Communicate effectively with researchers and scientific audiences.

modules.moduleContent:
This module comprises a field research placement at a host partner affiliated with the MH203 programme, completed during Summer vacation prior to the beginning of semester 1 in fourth-year. Field work carried out during the placement including but not limited to field surveys, environmental data gathering, landscape and eco-system analysis, biodiversity evaluation and stakeholder surveys, will be used as a basis for preparation of a research dissertation on the completed work.  Students will be paired with host partners based on placement type, geographic/access considerations and overall suitability of the student for the role.

Students should note that there are a limited number of projects available in each academic year. The Biology Department oversees the allocation of projects in the first week of the new academic year and allocation is based on ranking of student applicants according to their personal statement and third-year grades.  Therefore, students may not be allocated a project and may be required to amend their module registration after project allocation to ensure that they are registered for sufficient credits in each semester.

Please note students can choose only one 10 credit module from modules BI450; BI452 or BI453 in any academic year.
learningOutcomes: Review the scientific literature pertaining to a specific research question/topic.
Prepare a literature review according to guidelines.
Show evidence of designing a coherent sampling strategy to critically evaluate a field research question.
Perform observations and sampling using the technique(s) learned.
Analyse and interpret the research findings in the context of existing literature in the field.
Communicate scientific findings orally.
Compile a research thesis detailing the background literature, methodology, results, and future directions arising from the research findings.

modules.moduleContent:
The departmental academic staff set the research topics. Collectively the topics cover a wide range of biological and environmental disciplines, and where possible, the student will have an element of choice on the subject area. Individual topics will have a narrow enough focus to ensure a survey of the primary literature is appropriate. The student must identify relevant sources of information, assimilate their contents, and use the information to synthesise an informative discussion of the research topic. The student must present their work as a written thesis and as an oral presentation. 

Third year grades and/or a written statement of interest may be used to allocate a limited number of projects. The Biology department will oversee allocation in week 1. Students may be required to amend module registration after project allocation to ensure that they are registered for sufficient credits in the academic year.

Please note students can choose only one 10 credit module from modules BI450; BI452 or BI453 in any academic year.
learningOutcomes: Gather information on a biological or environmental subject from a variety of appropriate and relevant sources.
Integrate, analyse and evaluate information from different sources in a structured and coherent manner.
Attribute information to sources appropriately.
Document sources using standard formats.
Prepare a thesis according to guidelines.
Deliver an oral presentation on the subject demonstrating knowledge of the state of the art in a field of Biology.

modules.moduleContent: Lecture topics will include: bioethics in relation to scientific research and drug development; the regulatory issues facing the introduction of pharmaceuticals; the design and management of clinical trials. Assessment for this module involves a group-based assignment and written assignments.
learningOutcomes: Contrast differing ethical perspectives on medical research.
Connect the regulatory frameworks associated with human medicines.
Evaluate the suitability of clinical trial designs.

modules.moduleContent: The course will examine all aspects of vaccine design and delivery.  Special attention will be paid to adjuvants and excipients building on concepts delivered in modules covering innate immunology. The bulk of the course will survey the rational design of vaccines and correlates of immunity. This will begin with the history of polio vaccines comparing the Salk v Sabin vaccines, the lesser known failed vaccines of the 1930s and the Cutter incident. The course will then survey all vaccines in common use and examine the particular challenges of protecting the neonate in the developing world. Finally the course will examine the prospect for novel vaccines against the three major poverty related infectious diseases  (TB, HIV, malaria). Students will be expected to perform considerable reading of current literature in vaccinology and participate in a small group project.
learningOutcomes: Design a novel vaccine/ immunisation strategy for an emerging pathogen using rational methods.
Organise working with others to communicate an immunisation or information campaign for peers and public.
Analyse the potential for an immunisation campaign against an emerging or re-emerging pathogen and describe this in the form of a professional technical report.
Analyse a public health infectious emergency and recommend prioritised responses to government and civic society based on available data.

modules.moduleContent:
Topics covered include: cellular and non-cellular components of the innate and adaptive immune system, inflammation and the complement pathway, the structure and downstream signalling pathways of the B cell receptor (including VDJ rearrangements), T cell receptor, cytokine receptors and pathogen recognition receptors, the role of the professional antigen presenting cells in initiating the adaptive immune response, the roles of the effector T cells and B cells in the immune response to pathogen, central tolerance, the structure and role of Major Histocompatibility complex (MHC) Class I and Class II molecules. 

(Existing BI301 module with change in assessment. Use the BI301 timetable and identify yourself to the lecturer.)
learningOutcomes: Describe the cellular and non-cellular components of the innate and adaptive immune system.
Inflammation and the complement pathway.
Describe the structure and downstream signalling pathways of the B cell receptor (including VDJ rearrangements), T cell receptor, cytokine receptors and pathogen recognition receptors.
Discuss the role of the professional antigen presenting cells in initiating the adaptive immune response.
Discuss the roles of the effector T cells and B cells in the immune response to pathogen.
Describe the process of central tolerance for both B and T cells.
Compare and contrast the structure and role of Major Histocompatibility complex (MHC) Class I and Class II molecules.

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered include: DNA polymorphism and molecular markers, particularly microsatellites and Amplified Fragment Length Polymorphisms (AFLPs); Sexual recombination, allele inheritance and natural selection; Statistical methods and bioinformatics tools for analyzing gene flow and inferring historical biogeographic relationships: Wright’s indices, pairwise dis/similarity and hierarchical clustering; Gene flow between crops and wild plants; The risks and hazards of gene-flow from GM crops; Plate-tectonics, climate cycles and glaciation; Historical biogeography and tracing migration using nuclear, mitochondrial and chloroplast DNA markers; The postglacial colonization of Europe by animals and plants: dispersal waves, hybridization zones and extinction events; Population structure of tropical rainforest plants; Conservation genetics of endangered mammals; The biogeographic impact of continent collisions – recent invasions in Tropical Central America and Southeast Asia.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate expert comprehension of contemporary biogeography.
Demonstrate expert comprehension of the processes of landscape change.
Demonstrate expert comprehension of the occurrence and measurement of molecular DNA polymorphism and mutation, and the statistical inference of biogeographic history.
Demonstrate self-learning by accessing and writing critically about peer-reviewed scientific literature in this area.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will focus on the principles and practices of qualitative methodologies for research. Various approaches to research design will be explored, including: how to come up with a research question; how to carry out a literature review; how to select an appropriate research methodology; and how to cite and reference sources appropriately. 

The course will explore a variety of qualitative approaches, such as case study, historiography, grounded theory, ethnography, phenomenology and action research. Students will investigate the application of these approaches to their research through discussion and critical examination of key studies in relevant areas. They will learn how to apply various techniques of data collection, such as observation, participation, textual analysis, 
interviews and focus groups. The course will also cover the importance of ethics in undertaking research and in handling personal data, including sensitive data.
learningOutcomes: Critically appraise the validity, strengths and weaknesses of a variety of methodological and critical frameworks.
Select appropriate methods and/or critical approaches to design a research project.
Identify ethical issues that they are likely to encounter in their research project and propose strategies for dealing with them.

modules.moduleContent: This module equips students with key components of a professional chemist's toolkit. It introduces students to appropriate chemical software for information retrieval, and guides them in critically evaluating and compiling information. It provides opportunities to develop skills for professional scientific communication: in report writing, in structure drawing, in data analysis and data presentation focusing on topics related to the pharmaceutical industry. It fosters independent working, problem solving skills and objective analysis. It provides opportunity for reflection, development of a skills portfolio and creation of a CV.
learningOutcomes: Search a range of scientific databases, retrieve, analyse and evaluate information for a variety of applications including atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS).

Communicate by oral and written modes through the use of a range of subject specific and generic software packages to include Excel, chemical drawing packages, bibliographic management systems, MS-Word and anti-plagiariam software.
Deliver an oral presentation to their peer group.
Write scientifically and evaluate their work.
Recognise the structures of the major classes of biomolecules and pharmaceuticals and appreciate the relationship between chirality, 3-D structure and the biological activity of some pharmaceuticals.
Analyse large data sets by statistical methods.
Work in teams to solve problems associated with the pursuit of quality and excellence in the Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Industry.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module examines two important areas of physical chemistry:
(i) Group Theory. The symmetry characteristics of molecules are described and related to mathematical matrix operations. This allows the use of character tables to predict the physical properties of molecules. In particular reducible and irreducible representations are handled with the reduction formula, derived from the Great Orthogonality Theorem, to analyse translational, rotational and vibrational motion.

(ii) Thermodynamics. Material covered includes the Gibbs energy and the direction of spontaneous change, the Van't Hoff equation and the temperature dependence of equilibrium constant, the application of thermodynamics to phase diagrams, the Claussius-Clapeyron equation and the application of phase diagrams.
learningOutcomes: Identify the symmetry elements and operations of molecules and thereby make point group assignments.
Predict the vibrational spectroscopy of polyatomic molecules.
Use the Gibbs energy function to predict the direction of spontaneous change.
Describe the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant and the application of the Van't Hoff equation.
Describe the application of the Claussius-Calpeyron equation to phase equilibria.

modules.moduleContent: Students make a detailed plan of their final thesis. General instruction and guidance is given in an initial introductory session, followed by regular meetings with supervisors.
learningOutcomes: To equip research students with the necessary skills to plan and formally present, in written scientific format, the results of their research.

modules.moduleName: DEVELOPING AN ORIGINAL RESEARCH PROPOSAL IN CHEMISTRY
modules.moduleContent: Students carry out an independent study in order to develop a topic (related to their on-going PhD research topic) into an original research proposal with details on state-of-the-art in the research field, aims and objectives and methodology.
learningOutcomes: To equip students with the skills necessary to develop and advance a research topic, e.g. to a level where it would form the initial basis of a research grant proposal.

modules.moduleContent:
Typical Examples:

(i)  Mechanistic Organic Chemistry - Students work on the modelling of reaction mechanisms and pathways in Organic Chemistry. Weekly workshops are held over eight semesters.

(ii) Electrochemical and Spectroscopic Data - Students work on the analysis of electrochemical/spectroscopic data to extract fundamental parameters and also learn skills in a range of electrochemical/spectroscopy methodologies. Weekly workshops are held over eight semesters.
learningOutcomes: To train students in advance topics in the different sub-disciplines of chemistry.

modules.moduleContent: Students supervise the work of 4th year project students, and/or summer research students. This involves providing training in experimental methodology and writing of the project work.
learningOutcomes: To equip students with the skill required for research project coordination and supervision.

modules.moduleName: THE MANAGEMENT & USE OF IRISH HERITAGE
modules.moduleContent: "Defining Heritage: History / Evolution of Concept "Value" of Heritage Classification / types Heritage Mgmt: Government Departments

Basic Concepts of IT - Computer Operation and Use; MS Word (Intermediate level); Operate MS Excel (Elementary level); Word Processing; Spreadsheets; Internet and E-Mail; Introduction to Virtual Learning Environment.
