modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO LOCAL STUDIES & COMMUNITY STUDIES
modules.moduleContent:
This module combines the two strands of the BA Local Studies/Community Studies for New Students as an introduction to programmes.  Students will complete both elements of the module before progressing on to the degree stream of their choice in Semester 2.  The experience gained on participating in both modules allows students to gain valuable insights into the other stream that will be of benefit when they opt for Common Modules later in the degree.  

Unit 1:
Module Content: Local Studies
• What is local history? Why local studies? 
• Concepts of community and locality, definitions and approaches.
• Selected source materials: documentary; oral; visual: maps, photographs,
built fabric; historical literature. Principal repositories; catalogues and
finding aids. Primary vs. secondary sources.
• Questioning and critically appraising sources.
• Presenting local studies: ethics and academic rigour; referencing,
bibliographies, writing conventions.
• Case study: Maynooth or another suitable location.
(Dept. of Modern History: 16 hours)
Introductory sessions in:
• Maps, landscapes and the history of place.
• Archaeology: the earliest evidence for place and community.
• Manifestations of place and community in writing: the Irish literary tradition.
(Depts. of Geography & Modern History; Roinn na Nua-Ghaeilge: 8 hours)

Unit 2:
Module Content: Community Studies
• Introduction to Community Studies
• Definitions of community
• Models of community development
• Diversity & difference within Irish communities
• The impact of social change on Irish community life.
(Dept. of Adult & Community Education: 12 hours)
• Introductory Sessions in:
• Youth & community work
• Anthropology of local communities
• Sociological perspectives on local communities
(Centre for Applied Social Studies, Dept. of Anthropology
& Dept. of Sociology: 12 hours)
learningOutcomes: define community studies as a study subject area and differentiate it from sociology and/or anthropology.
comprehend the module range in the community studies programme.
have a theoretical and applied understanding of community.
make an informed decision on the direction of further study on the degree programme.

modules.moduleContent: This module explores the origins and development of sport in Ireland from the early eighteenth century to the end of the twentieth century. Focusing primarily on ‘modern’ or codified sport, it investigates many of the important ways in which sport helped to shape Irish life and was in turn affected by contemporary social, economic and political developments in Irish society.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a knowledge and understanding of the origins and development of sport in Ireland from the eighteenth to the twentieth centuries
Demonstrate a knowledge and understanding of scholarly debates regarding the origins and development of sport in Ireland in the period covered by the module
Critically engage with relevant primary sources
Write an in-depth, scholarly analysis of one of the main topics covered by the module

modules.moduleContent: The prolonged conflict witnessed in Northern Ireland between 1969 and the Good Friday (Belfast) Agreement [GFA] (1998) has often been euphemistically referred to as the ‘Troubles’. This module is an introduction to the history of the Troubles from a conflict resolution perspective, examining the underlying political, economic, sociological and military contexts.
learningOutcomes: have a knowledge of the history of the Troubles from a conflict resolution perspective
have a critical approach to relevant historical sources
write an analytical essay related to the topic

modules.moduleName: DELIVERING EQUALITY, DIVERSITY AND INCLUSION WITHIN ORGANISATIONS
modules.moduleContent: Management of equality and diversity at an organisational level. The influence of equality, diversity, and inclusion across the employment cycle, and diversity management. An overview of coaching and mentoring concludes the module.
learningOutcomes: Describe the primary drivers that underpin equality and diversity policies.
Provide an overview of how equality and diversity issues are managed at work as well as what constitutes inclusive recruitment and selection.
Identify the relevant components for EDI training plus discuss the purpose of mentoring and facilitation as methods of EDI development within a company.
Be familiar with the main concepts of facilitation and mentoring within an equality context.

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered include: cellular and non-cellular components of the innate and adaptive immune system, inflammation, the complement system, the structure and downstream signalling pathways of the B cell receptor (including VDJ rearrangements), T cell receptor, cytokine receptors and pattern recognition receptors, the role of the professional antigen presenting cells in initiating the adaptive immune response, the roles of the effector T cells and B cells in the immune response to pathogen, the structure and role of Major Histocompatibility complex (MHC) Class I and Class II molecules, the antigen processing pathways.
learningOutcomes: Describe the cellular and non-cellular components of the innate and adaptive immune system.
Describe the inflammatory process and the complement system.
Describe the structure and downstream signalling pathways of the B cell receptor (including VDJ rearrangements), T cell receptor, cytokine receptors and pathogen recognition receptors.
Discuss the role of the professional antigen presenting cells in initiating the adaptive immune response.
Discuss the roles of the effector T cells and B cells in the immune response to pathogen.
Compare and contrast the structure and role of Major Histocompatibility complex (MHC) Class I and Class II molecules.
Describe the antigen processing pathways.

modules.moduleContent: This module will enable the student to become familiar with relevant aspects of the biotechnology industry (relevant technologies, Research and Development operations, manufacturing operations, quality management systems, patenting etc.). The student will be required to obtain information from the scientific literature, company information, biological, social and internet resources on specific areas of biotechnology in order to collate a detailed literature report. Expertise will also be gained in how to perform biochemical calculations to aid understanding of the scientific literature. The student will also acquire presentational skills, both verbal and written, by class participation, oral presentation, and literature review. Individuals working in the biotechnology industry or research area will be invited to present to the class.
learningOutcomes: Understand the different types of peer-reviewed literature including primary literature, review articles and patent literature.
Operate university- and journal-based websites to obtain peer-reviewed data.
Analyse the scientific literature to obtain relevant information for incorporation into literature reviews and oral presentations.
Analyse biochemical data and understand the difference between amount, concentration and volume.
Summarise key data present in scientific literature.
Compose a well-structured literature review on a topic of biotechnological relevance.
Compose and present complex scientific information by oral presentation using electronic media.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The course is taught over a 5-day period during the Easter Vacation of Semester 2. This is a residential course, based at a host centre in Ireland and involving excursions to a range of different field sites. Each day involves training, field work and case investigations across a range of ecological topics and habitat settings. Activities may include, inter alia, vegetation studies, habitat descriptions, studies of plant, animal, freshwater, forest and agricultural ecology, biodiversity assessment, habitat restoration, environmental impact assessment, human-ecosystem interactions and climate-ecosystem interactions. Field work and workshops will be led by MU academic staff and guest experts and leaders from across the environmental sector in Ireland. A preliminary workshop and learning guide will be provided to students prior to the course, and during the field week technical training and guidance will be provided for all activities. A significant element in the field course is group-based learning and problem-solving activities. A post-field course workshop will be provided to guide students in preparation of their final project report.
learningOutcomes: Complete a range of on-site and off-site ecological analyses.
Work with team members to deliver a co-operative shared-responsibility task report.
Incorporate additional reading and external technical information into a written scientific report of a field site or ecological topic.
Complete an independent project report on an ecological topic that incorporates first-hand data and observations from one or more field sites in Ireland.
Execute on-site ecological analyses using a range of data acquisition techniques, and complete post-hoc evaluations of this data alongside other data of the same format.

modules.moduleContent: The course is lecture based with prescribed additional reading and self directed private study. The course examines the question "What is Cancer?" To answer this, the following topics are explored: Control of the Cell division cycle; Cyclins and cyclin dependent kinases; Oncogenes, Tumour suppressor genes; Familial cancers; a detailed study of the role of the Rb gene; P53 as the guardian of the genome; Cell death Mechanisms; Intrinsic and Extrinsic pathways of apoptosis; detailed understanding of the anti-tumour immune response; understanding failures in the anti-tumour immune response; immunotherapies with a focus on immune checkpoint inhibitors; Breast cancer; Ovarian cancer; tumour progression; Angiogenesis, how diagnosis is made; the major therapeutic interventions (existing therapies and new therapies).
learningOutcomes: Identify the fundamental biological processes that contribute to cancer.
Describe the multifactorial relationship between molecular, cellular and environmental processes, with regard to carcinogenesis.
Evaluate various types of scientific information including recent primary research articles with regard to cancer biology, diagnosis and treatment.
Communicate effectively in written and oral forms.
Construct simple hypotheses consistent with current scientific knowledge as a basis for future research in cancer.

modules.moduleContent: The course is lecture based with prescribed additional reading and recommendations for self-directed study. Topics may vary from year to year but typically include how plant polarity is established during embryogenesis, how shoot and root apical meristems are established and maintained, how floral meristem identity is established during reproductive growth, how flower pattern and floral organ identity is established. An evolutionary approach to aspects of plant development is emphasized as much as possible.
learningOutcomes: Discuss current views on the molecular and genetic factors that regulate aspects of plant development e.g. embryogenesis and flower development.
Explain how studying development in model plant species can contribute to our understanding of how plant diversity has been generated.
Evaluate recent primary literature relevant to enhancing their understanding of this subject.
Take personal responsibility for their learning.

modules.moduleContent:
Bioethics component includes: Identification of ethical questions; major bioethical principles; stakeholders and values; elements of a strong justification; processes for tackling ethical issues; basic Western ethical ideas including classical utilitarianism and Kantian theory, rights approaches; application to issues in biology, biotechnology, medicine and environment. Current cases histories with stakeholder analyses: these may include clinical trials, genetic engineering, cloning, patenting of biological material. Detailed knowledge of relevant biotechnological science will form a central part of the bioethics component of this module. Biotechnological component comprises: Fungi are amazing reservoirs of bioactive molecules, such as penicillin and statins, which are used to treat human diseases. Collectively, these molecules are known as natural products (NP) or secondary metabolites (SM) and are made by fungi, and bacteria, using processes known as non-ribosomal peptide synthesis or polyketide synthesis. 

This course will provide the student with a thorough understanding of these biosynthetic processes at the molecular and proteomic level. This topic is of special relevance as many microbial genome mining programmes are identifying ever more genes involved in NP biosynthesis. Consequently, research in this area is beginning to reveal a range of new molecules with biomedical potential.
learningOutcomes: Identify where ethical issues are likely to arise in biotechnology and biobusiness.
Discuss pervasive Western ethical positions and interpret their likely implications.
Carry out case-history analyses.
Use the language of ethicists on ethics committees and such bodies.
Describe the concepts of non-ribosomal peptide synthesis and polyketide synthesis, and understand the differences between both pathways.
Describe the nature of non-ribosomal peptide synthetases, and associated enzymes, and assess how the interplay between both leads to NP formation.
Evaluate the importance of modern biological and chemical techniques (e.g., fungal proteomics) for revealing the intricacies of non-ribosomal peptide biosynthesis.

modules.moduleContent:
The student will be introduced to genomic and proteomic analysis including structural, functional and comparative genomics, protein-protein interactions and post-translational modification of proteins. The module is divided equally between formal lectures on genomics and proteomics and discussion of current literature examples on the application of genomics and proteomics.

NOTE TO VISITING STUDENTS: Only suitable for international/visiting students attending for a full academic year.
learningOutcomes: Describe DNA sequencing technologies and recent advances for high throughput genomic sequencing.
Compare and contrast different methods for functional genomic analysis.
Provide examples of how genomics technologies have been applied to improve our understanding of biological systems.
Compare and contrast classical approaches to understanding protein structure and function with recent proteomic approaches.
Describe proteomic approaches and techniques used to investigate protein structure and function.
Explain how specific proteomics techniques can contribute to understanding of biological systems.

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered include: generating genetically modified animals; studying gene functions; isolating leukocyte subsets; flow cytometry; confocal microscopy; DNA transcription; techniques for assessing mRNA expression levels, transcription factor activation and promoter regulation; assessing protein-protein interactions; assessing novel protein functionality.
learningOutcomes: Describe the approaches used to generate genetically modified animals.
Compare and contrast transgenic approaches with other techniques used to study gene function.
Describe how antibodies can be used as molecular tools for the identification and isolation of leukocyte subsets.
Outline experimental approaches to investigate leukocyte subsets using methods discussed in the module.
Describe the process of DNA transcription and the factors governing gene expression regulation.
Describe approaches used to assess for changes in gene expression at the mRNA level and to measure transcription factor activation and promoter regulation.
Discuss the various techniques used to assess novel protein-protein interactions.

modules.moduleContent:
To introduce students great potential of fungal and bacterial secondary metabolites and control of their production by genetic as well as epigenetic factors.

Fungal and bacterial secondary metabolites have great potential due to their potent physiological influences on cellular functions such as antibiotics, antivirals, antifungals, antiapoptotics, cytotoxics, immunosuppressives, and deadly mycotoxins. Therefore, they are extremely important for medical, biotechnological and chemical applications. The focus of this advanced module is the fungal and bacterial secondary metabolites and the control of their production by genetic and epigenetic factors. Specific sections found in this module will be connected with chemical biology, genetics, epigenetics and fungal molecular biology. The major classes of microbial natural products and their biosynthetic pathways will be introduced. Potential impact of the bioactive metabolites in biotechnology, medicine and chemical biology will be discussed in depth. The term “gene clusters” will be introduced by analogy to prokaryotic operons. Control of gene clusters in fungi at the chromatin and epigenetic level will be examined by examples of histone modifications. Cellular signalling elements (MAPK, PKA, PKC) regulating the biosynthesis of fungal secondary metabolites will be analyzed.
learningOutcomes: Identify the major classes of fungal and bacterial secondary metabolites.
Discuss the biomedical and biotechnological potentials of fungal/bacterial secondary metabolites.
Define the secondary metabolite gene clusters.
Describe the importance of histone modifications for the control of gene clusters.
Examine the cellular signaling elements controlling secondary metabolism.

modules.moduleContent:
Students work in small groups to undertake a 16-week research project under the supervision of a University tutor and an industry sponsor. The goal of the project is to solve or advance solutions for real-world scientific and business problems. Typically, the problem will be set by a sponsor from industry, social enterprise or other who may also provide input at update meetings and with regard to the final assessment. An academic from the department will act as an advisor and will be responsible for assessment and overseeing the project. Project will be based off-campus depending on the nature of project and sponsor. 

Restricted Entry: Third year grades, a written statement of interest and an interview may be used to allocate a limited number of Sandbox projects. Biology department will oversee allocation. Places available for non-Biology students from Business or other departments are allocated subject to the approval of the Head of the appropriate Department (eg Business).
learningOutcomes: Work effectively in small groups.
Work autonomously to solve a real-world scientific or business problem.
Adapt and develop their scientific knowledge and skills to a practical problem.
Maintain excellent records.
Project manage their workload to meet project deadlines, milestones and deliverables.
Communicate effectively with tutors, academics and external partners.
Compile an enduring document to the specifications of the industry sponsor.

modules.moduleContent: The taught component of the module will include innate immunology, B cell biology, the structure and function of antibody and how diversity is generated. Cell mediated immunology will be examined, contrasting T cell recognition with B cell recognition of antigen. T cell effector and regulatory functions and cytokine production will be discussed in detail, following an in depth examination of the MHC structure, organisation and function to give students a good grounding in the scientific theory underlying modern Immunology. In addition, students will undertake 6 hours of practical work to introduce basic techniques in Immunology.
learningOutcomes: have a thorough grounding in the fundamental aspects of Immunology,
have a core understanding of the principles of Immunology,
understand fundamental concepts in how living organisms counter infection.

modules.moduleName: SEMINARS IN ADVANCED IMMUNOLOGY AND GLOBAL HEALTH
modules.moduleContent: The objective of the module is to develop critical thinking and interpretation skills at an advanced level. Specifically, students are expected to be able to assess recent research findings in the area of Immunology and Global Health and convey such developments to both specialist and non-specialist audiences.
learningOutcomes: Critically assess and discuss current research findings in Immunology.
Appraise recent activity in Global health research.
Interpret scholarly activity in Immunology and Global health for a non-specialist audience.
Formulate a summary suitable for a specialist academic group.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will provide the students with a detailed understanding of the immune system, including the signalling pathways and effector molecules that mediate immune effector functions. Topics covered include: Pattern recognition receptors and their signalling pathways, anti-viral immune responses and viral Immune Evasion, Transplantation Immunology, Self-tolerance and Autoimmunity, cross-presentation, T cell activation and differentiation, and T cell exhaustion

The schedule is the same as BI405.  Contact Dr Martina Schroeder martina.schroeder@mu.ie for timetable.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an advanced level of understanding of the immunological topics covered in the module.
Comprehend current published research in the discipline and be able to critically review it
Write well-researched and referenced scientific essays on selected topics of Immunology covered in the module and discuss how these relate to their postgraduate research project.

modules.moduleContent: Lecture topics will include: bioethics in relation to scientific research and drug development; the regulatory issues facing the introduction of pharmaceuticals; the design and management of clinical trials. Assessment for this module involves a group-based assignment and written assignments.
learningOutcomes: Contrast differing ethical perspectives on medical research.
Connect the regulatory frameworks associated with human medicines.
Evaluate the suitability of clinical trial designs.

modules.moduleContent:
The course is lecture-based with prescribed additional reading and self-directed private study. The course examines the question “What is Cancer?” To answer this, the following topics are explored: Control of the Cell division cycle; Cyclins and cyclin dependent kinases; Oncogenes, Tumour suppressor genes; DNA and RNA tumour viruses; Familial cancers; a detailed study of the role of the Rb gene; P53 as the guardian of the genome; Cell death; Positive and negative induction of apoptosis; the execution phase of apoptosis; beyond the molecular biology of cancer; how the body resists neoplasia; tumour progression; Angiogenesis, how diagnosis is made; the major therapeutic interventions (existing therapies and new therapies).



Contact Dr. Marion Butler (marion.butler@mu.ie) if you register for this module.
learningOutcomes: Identify the fundamental biological processes that contribute to cancer.
Describe the multifactorial relationship between molecular, cellular and environmental processes, with regard to carcinogenesis.
Evaluate various types of scientific information including recent primary research articles with regard to cancer biology, diagnosis and treatment.
Communicate effectively in written and oral forms.
Construct simple hypotheses consistent with current scientific knowledge as a basis for future research in cancer.

modules.moduleContent:
Twelve one-hour research seminars presented by leading national and international researchers working in a variety of different research areas. Cannot be taken together with BI830 (Immunology Seminar Series).  Credit can be given for student attending a research seminar series at another national university, with prior agreement of the module coordinator.

Contact Dr Ozgur Bayram (ozgur.bayram@mu.ie) for the schedule.
learningOutcomes: Listen to an advanced biological research seminar and filter important information from presented data,
Describe advanced aspects of diverse biological research topics- relating to topics presented as part of the seminar series during the academic year,
Write succinct, abstract-style, summaries of advanced oral research presentations.

modules.moduleContent: Students perform a research visit of not less than two weeks, organised in collaboration with the Research Supervisor.  The visit will be to a commercial laboratory to perform a defined research activity.
learningOutcomes: To provide student with an appreciation of research in an industrial/commercial/ or regulatory environment.

modules.moduleContent: The objective of the module is to develop research communication skills at an advanced level. It aims to foster formal presentation skills, the ability to participate in scientific discourse, the ability to chair a scientific session (e.g. at a conference) and the ability to critically evaluate a scientific paper. The module also aims to encourage a reflective process on feedback received during scientific discussions.
learningOutcomes: Confidently present research findings to an audience of peers and experts.
Actively engage in discussions of their own research and that of peers.
Chair a scientific session and formulate questions for the speaker.
Critically evaluate and appraise a scientific publication relevant to their area of research.
Engage in a reflective process based on received questions and feedback.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:
The objective of the module is to develop critical thinking and interpretation skills at an advanced level. Specifically, students are expected to be able to assess recent research findings in the area of Immunology and Global Health and convey such developments to both specialist and non-specialist audiences.

Module Content:
Narrative

Contact Dr. Karen English (karen.english@mu.ie) to confirm registration and for a schedule.
learningOutcomes: Critically assess and discuss current research findings in Immunology,
Appraise recent activity in Immunology research,
Interpret scholarly activity in Immunology for a non-specialist audience,
Formulate a summary suitable for a specialist academic group.

modules.moduleContent: Students will carry out an international research visit of not less than two weeks, organised in collaboration with the Research Supervisor..  The scope of the research will be defined by the supervisor, student and host laboratory. The visit is arranged by (and subject to the agreement of) the Research Supervisor..
learningOutcomes: Navigate research environments in an international setting.

modules.moduleName: ANTIBIOTICS: DISCOVERY, MODES OF ACTION AND RESISTANCE
modules.moduleContent:
Module objectives: To introduce the students to antibiotic discovery and antibiotic resistance mechanisms and the tools used for both. 

Module content: The series of lectures would start with a short introduction into how the antibiotics that we use today were discovered and developed over the past century. This would incorporate the discovery of the first antibiotics all the way to the use of screening genomes for the 'next big thing', to explaining that most pharmaceutical companies have abandoned their R & D in this area. The next section would introduce the students to the different classes of antibiotics, how they differ and how they interact with the bacteria to inhibit their growth or kill them. This section would also give a brief introduction to the pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics that are necessary for the antibiotic to function in the body. The following lectures would be a discussion of the different mechanisms of resistance and emerging resistance problems and epidemics of resistance currently of concern. The techniques used to measure antibiotic susceptibility or resistance in hospital laboratories and the molecular methods that we can now use would be described to highlight how a combination of phenotypic and molecular tools can aid the understanding of resistance. There will also be a section on the origins of antibiotic resistance and how resistance mechanisms may have entered into the human food chain or other possible routes of transmission to human pathogens and the importance of human waste in the propagation of resistance in the water supply and environment. The module would encompass human, agricultural and environmental antibiotic use and resistance to discuss the problem from a One Health perspective. 

Lecture content (two lectures for each topic): 
1. Antibiotic history and discovery from 20th to 21st century (1 lecture). 
2. Antibiotic classes, mode of action and bacterial inhibition or killing and mechanisms of antibiotic resistance (10 lectures). 
3. Emerging antibiotic resistance problems and epidemics worldwide (3 lectures). 
4. Measuring antibiotic resistance (1 - 2 lectures). 



Contact Prof Fiona Walsh (fiona.walsh@mu.ie) if you have registered for this module.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the processes required to discover novel antibiotic compounds;
Identify the different antibiotic classes and their modes of action;
Understand the mechanisms of resistance associated with each class;
Describe the techniques used and available for use in the identification and epidemiology of antibiotic resistance;
Examine the roles of global health in the emergence and spread of local antibiotic resistance.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective
The purpose of this module is to allow postgraduate research students to develop and apply the skills required for publishing their research.   This module covers the submission of a manuscript to a peer-reviewed journal.

Module Content
• Attend lectures/seminars/conferences in the area of research  that is intended for publication
• Undertake an informed literature survey around the research that is intended for publication
• Draft hypothesis and framework for manuscript
• Write up the manuscript to include abstract, introduction and results and discussion content
• Submission of manuscript to a peer-reviewed journal.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the current state-of-the-art in their research field;
Evaluate results for potential publication;
Concisely present and critically discuss their research findings;
Articulate the significance of the research in the wider context;
Submit a manuscript that meets formatting standards as set out by the publisher.

modules.moduleContent: Module content will vary depending on the course being taken. Usually Advanced Workshops/Courses consist of a mixture of advanced lectures and practical classes. A student who wishes to take this module must provide a detailed outline of the Workshop or Course to the module coordinator and get prior approval that the Workshop or Course is of a standard acceptable to the programme.
learningOutcomes: Acquire specialist knowledge in an area of Biology or in a methodology that enhances the student’s individual research programme.
Correctly summarise and describe the key content from the workshop/summer school/course.
Consider how material presented during the workshop/summer school/course can be applied to the student’s own research project.

modules.moduleContent:
Dependent on international module.

Agreement with Research Supervisor and Postgraduate Co-ordinator that international module is of an acceptable standard and equivalent to 5 credits.
learningOutcomes: Acquire specialist knowledge in an area of Biology or in a methodology that enhances the student’s individual research programme and that is not available at MU.
Consider how the module content can be applied to the student’s own research project.
Successfully complete assignments linked to the international module.

modules.moduleContent:
This module explores four important areas in Chemistry:

(i) Thermodynamics and Equilibrium.
The course starts with the concept of internal energy and changes in the internal energy of reactants and products as a reaction proceeds. The enthalpy function, exothermic and endothermic reactions, Hess’ law and bond energies are introduced. The concept of chemical equilibrium and the equilibrium constant is then introduced.

(ii) Chemical Trends in the Periodic Table.
General properties of metals, non-metals and metalloids. Metal and non-metal oxides, and oxide trends across/down the Periodic Table. Chemical Group trends in the s-Block elements. p-Block chemistry: sequential Group discussion (Groups 18-13) to include outer electronic configurations for the Groups and typical chemical reactions of selected main members of the Groups and properties of representative compounds.

(iii) Chemical Kinetics.
This course starts with the introduction of the rate of a reaction and how this can be measured. Then, material on the rate law, reaction order, the Arrhenius equation, the activation energy and the influence of temperature on the rate of a reaction is covered.

(iv) Organic Chemistry.
Material covered includes: Recognising functional groups (alkanes, cycloalkanes, alkenes, alkynes, aromatic hydrocarbons, alkyl halides, alcohols, ethers, amines, carboxylic acids, ketones, aldehydes and amides); The IUPAC system of nomenclature for selected members of these families; Point chirality; R- and S-configurations and the Cahn-Ingold-Prelog rules; Introduction to mechanisms and curly arrows; Nucleophilic substitution reactions, Elimination reactions.
learningOutcomes: Identify the chemical trends within Groups in the s- and p-blocks of the Periodic Table.
Predict the general chemistry of an element in accordance with its location in the Periodic Table.
Recognise state functions and chemical equilibria and calculate associated parameters.
Outline the factors that determine the rates of chemical reactions and interpret the order of a reaction from experimental data.
Recognise and name selected organic molecules.
Explain bonding in relation to aliphatic organic molecules.
Describe the mechanisms or organic reactions using curly arrows.

modules.moduleContent:
Stereochemistry.  Chemistry of the alkene functional group.  Carbonyl group chemistry. 

This module, incorporating theory and laboratory learning, explores three important areas of organic chemistry: 

(i) Stereochemistry - exploring chemistry in 3-D. Material covered includes chirality, asymmetric centers, stereoisomers, and (R)- and (S)-. The stereochemistry and mechanism of SN1 reactions, SN2 reactions, and additions to the carbonyl group are also covered. 

(ii) Examining one of the most important functional groups in chemistry, the carbonyl group. Material covered includes the structure of carbonyl compounds, oxidation/reduction of carbonyl compounds, substitution reactions of carboxylic acid derivatives, reversible and irreversible addition reactions of carbonyl compounds, ester synthesis and hydrolysis. (iii) The chemistry of the carbon-carbon double bond. Material will include (i) Bonding and Nomenclature, (ii) Addition chemistry, including regiochemical and stereochemical aspects, 

(iii) Alkene formation reactions.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the principles of stereochemistry.
Explain the structure and reactivity of the carbonyl group.
Explain the structure and reactivity of the alkene functional group.
Provide a mechanistic explanation for the regiochemical and stereochemical outcome of selected alkene addition reactions.
Describe selected reactions for alkene formation.
Conduct syntheses of selected organic compounds and prepare associated laboratory reports.  Use molecular models to understand structure and utilise Software for drawing Chemical Structures.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module examines three important areas of spectroscopy and analytical chemistry.

(i) Spectroscopic methods for structure determination. Material covered includes an introduction to nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy; infra-red (IR) spectroscopy - basic theory, the identification of functional groups, mass spectrometry (MS) - basic principles and instrumentation, and an introduction to UV-vis spectroscopy.

(ii) Analytical Chemistry. Material covered includes: an introduction to analytical chemistry and the process of analysis; common measuring techniques and the means by which data is statistically analysed; chemical separations - chromatography.

(iii) Introduction to the Russell-Saunders coupling scheme for electrons on elements with low atomic mass. Use of selection rules in the electric-dipole approximation to predict optical transitions.
learningOutcomes: Outline the basics of the analytical process.
Discuss the theory of HPLC and GC.
Discuss the basic principles behind 1H NMR spectroscopy.
Examine and interpret the 1H NMR spectrum of simple compounds.
Use IR and MS data in the determination of compound structure.
Determine the states of an atom from an electronic configuration.
Predict the optical transitions of atoms.

modules.moduleName: INORGANIC CHEMISTRY:  CO-ORDINATION CHEMISTRY AND S-BLOCK GROUPS
modules.moduleContent:
Introduction to coordination chemistry and the chemistry of the s-block metals.

(i) Coordination Chemistry: Introduction to transition metals. Historical development of co-ordination chemistry and definitions. Co-ordination numbers and structures of complexes. Nomenclature and isomerism. Bonding in transition metal complexes. Complex formation. Thermodynamic and kinetic stability. Irving-William series.

(ii) Summary of some important concepts such as electronegativity, electronic affinity, ionisation energy and atom/ion sizes. S-block chemistry: Hydrogen (isotopes, occurrence, synthesis, physical properties. H+ and H-, Hydrides, bond energies in the hydrides, hydrogen bonding, hydrogen compounds across the periodic table). Chemistry of the Group 1 and Group 2. Occurrence, uses, physical properties. Halides, oxides, oxoacids and salts. Carbonate and hydrogen carbonates. Fajan's rules, solubility in water and conductivity. Coordination chemistry of group 1 and 2. Structure of the most important ionic compounds (NaCl, CsCl, ZnS, rutile ABX3 solids …). Solid state energetic of group 1-2 salts: Born-Haber cycles, lattice energies and lattice density.
learningOutcomes: Apply the rules of naming complexes and draw structures of named complexes;
Interpret selected physical properties of complexes in terms of bonding models;
Determine the stepwise and overall stability constants of complexes;
Predict ligand substitution based on ligand-field stabilisation energies;
Understand the chemistry and reactivity of hydrogen and of group 1 and group 2 elements;
Relate thermodynamic aspects of group 1 and 2 salts to their solubility and conductivity;
Use Born-Haber cycles to calculate the lattice energy and lattice density of group 1 and 2 salts.

modules.moduleContent:
Introduction to Molecular Pharmacology and Drug Action

This module examines fundamental aspects of the biomedical chemistry of drug action:
(i) Molecular structure and function.  Material covered includes protein structure and function, drug-target interactions, intermolecular forces and energy of binding, introduction to drug design and case studies.

(ii) Molecular pharmacology.  Material covered includes an introduction to the pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic phases of drug action, classification of drug targets, molecular recognition and receptor models, modes of drug action, saturation binding curves and dose-response relationships, introduction to structure activity relationships and case studies.
learningOutcomes: Discuss ligand-target interactions involved in the pharmacodynamic phase of drug action.
Explain the information provided by saturation binding curves and dose-response curves.
Recognise and have a basic understanding of the mode of action of some of the most important types of pharmacological agents.
Demonstrate an understanding of the relationship between chemical structure and function/activity.

modules.moduleName: QUALITY ASSURANCE, FORMULATION AND PROCESS DEVELOPMENT
modules.moduleContent:
This module examines three important areas of pharmaceutical chemistry:
(i) Formulation of drug products. The course introduces the principles and purpose of formulating active pharmaceutical ingredients. The factors that must be considered in developing an effective formulation are considered and the role of drug delivery as part of the drug development process is discussed. Formulation of both small molecule and biopharmaceutical drugs is examined.

(ii) Pharmaceutical Industry Overview : This course covers topics relevant to the structure of the Pharmaceutical Industry including drug research & development, fundamentals of Good Manufacturing Practice (including Case Studies), Quality Assurance, Quality Control and Validation. It includes calculations of chemical reaction hazards and the importance of plant & process safety will be considered. 

(iii) Thermodynamics of Scale-up. Material covered includes a description of the important considerations in the scale up of any chemical reaction; differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and the application of DSC in the analysis of pharmaceutical compounds; reaction calorimetry and the application of reaction calorimetry in the scale-up of chemical reactions.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the basic principles that are considered in designing an appropriate dosage form for a drug.
Describe the most common types of dosage forms for different routes of delivery.
Describe the different challenges encountered when formulating small molecule drugs compared to biopharmaceutical products.
Demonstrate a detailed knowledge of the structure of a Pharmaceutical Plant, and the importance of GMP within this area.
Describe the steps used in a typical drug development process from discovery to market.
Discuss the responsibilities of the FDA and describe how to identify and evaluate hazards in the Pharmaceutical Industry.
Identify the important steps in the scale-up of a chemical reaction.

modules.moduleContent:
Heterocyclic chemistry, carbohydrate chemistry and amino acid/peptide chemistry.

This module covers three areas of organic chemistry:
(i) The chemistry of aromatic and saturated heterocyclic chemistry. 
The material covered in this section includes an introduction to the structure and reactivity of pyridine and aromatic heterocycles, as well as stereoelectronics in saturated heterocycles; thermodynamics of ring formation and Baldwin's rules.

(ii) Carbohydrate chemistry. Material covered includes terminology, general reactions of aldoses and ketoses, chain extension and chain reduction, glycosides, disaccharides and polysaccharides

(iii) Amino acid and peptide chemistry. Material covered includes: amino acids - structure, functions, synthesis; peptide synthesis - protecting group strategies, coupling reagents; protein structure and function.
learningOutcomes: Describe the structure of pyridine and saturated heterocycles.
Describe reactions of pyridine using curly arrows.
Discuss saturated heterocycle reactivity and ring formation.
Recognise and employ carbohydrate terminology.
Explain the reactivity of carbohydrates.
Discuss amino acid and peptide structure, function and synthesis.
Conduct syntheses of selected organic compounds and prepare associated laboratory reports.

modules.moduleContent:
Nanochemistry, polymer chemistry and colloid chemistry.

This module examines three important areas of materials science:

(i) Nanochemistry: This course introduces the new topic of Nanochemistry and the application of Chemistry to Nanotechnology. The course starts with AFM and STM (nanoprobe techniques). The synthesis of nanoparticles is then introduced and some of the more recent applications of nanoparticles are described. Finally, carbon nanotubes are described together with some of their recent applications.

(ii) Organic Polymer Chemistry: This course starts with the history and classes of synthetic organic polymers. Polymerization mechanisms (radical, cationic, anionic) and initiator types, chain transfer, head-to-tail addition, polymer chain branching and polymer stereochemistry are discussed. Thermoplastic and thermosetting polymers. Biodegradable polymers: synthesis and structural properties. The physical properties of polymer: molecular weight and glass transition temperature.

(iii) Colloid Chemistry: This course introduces the basic principles of colloid science, including the nature of colloid solutions, the forces involved in colloidal stability, and the link between colloids and surfaces. Topics covered are: surface tension and wetting, self-assembly, emulsions, charged colloids and granular materials. Industrial and biological applications of colloid science are also discussed.
learningOutcomes: Construct synthetic protocols for common organic polymers.
Calculate the polymer molecular weight and the poly-dispersity index of common polymers.
Interpret and predict the physical properties of polymers.
Describe the fundamental principles on which the AFM and STM techniques are based.
Describe simple methodologies that are used in the synthesis of nanomaterials.
Describe what a colloidal solution is and discuss the factors affecting colloidal stability.
Discuss the applications of colloid science in industry and in biology.

modules.moduleName: ICT FOR RESEARCH AND  LEARNING (PART B)
learningOutcomes: identify the appropriate search technique for the task and implement it competently
use various online resources appropriate to their field of study, and be able to navigate these competently
use library catalogues, subscription based databases competently
critically evaluate information found online in terms of relevance, currency, reliability, objectivity, etc
cite online sources accurately and consistently
use the universities Virtual Learning Environment competently.

modules.moduleName: ECHOES OF THE PAST: THE RESONATING EFFECTS OF GREEK CIVILIZATION IN THE TWENTY-FIRST CENTURY
modules.moduleContent:
Is the past ever really the past, or can its echoes continue to shape our present environment in various and unexpected ways? The Greeks were one of the most advanced civilizations of the ancient world and their innovations resonate all around us: from government and legal systems, artistic and cultural developments, to the use of language and critical thinking. This module introduces students to these various facets of Ancient Greek society in order to promote a clearer understanding of modern politics and culture in the Western world. Students will evaluate this iconic period of ancient history, and investigate its ongoing influence into the twenty-first century.

Module Content:
The following topics will be covered during the semester:
* Who were the Greeks? The Ancient Greek World
* Citizens and ‘Others’
* Stones that Speak: Greek Art & Architecture in Civic Spaces
* Greek Politics from Aristocracy to Democracy 
* Power to the People: the Athenian Assembly & the Lawcourts
* It’s all Greek to Me: the Power of Language & Rhetoric
* Greek Philosophy from the Pre-Socratics to Aristotle  
* Greek Science & Technology
* Entertaining the Masses: Competition & Sport
* Festival Culture: Greek Drama
learningOutcomes: Understand Ancient Greek civilization in its broader historical context, and appreciate its importance for Western political, cultural and social life in the modern world.
Be familiar with key historical episodes, and with written and visual evidence that reflects interaction between the political environment and culture.
Understand the power of language and oratory, and their role in facilitating populism.
Demonstrate an understanding of the possible relationships and issues in exploring political power, wealth, and the development of culture.
Critically assess the influence of Ancient Greek civilization on modern life.
modules.notes: Inactive module, not currently for selection on dept. degrees.

modules.moduleName: REFLECTING ON EQUALITY, DIVERSITY, AND INCLUSION
modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed to introduce students to the nature of equality, diversity, and inclusion issues in Irish society.  We will explore topics such as:

• Reflective Practice
• Sources of Prejudice and Discrimination.
• Introduction to Sociological Concepts

Students will be encouraged to actively reflect on and apply the concepts discussed to their own personal, community, and working lives, to focus on the meaning of equality, diversity and inclusion, and to consider the origins of prejudice and discrimination.
learningOutcomes: Reflect on their own personal biases and assumptions in relation to inclusive practices and behaviours.
Consider how bias and assumptions can influence decision making in the context of  personal, community, and working environments.
Define and explain the concepts of equality, diversity, and inclusion (EDI) and related terms such as bias and intersectionality.
Discuss the processes that underlie prejudice and discrimination in society.

modules.moduleName: THE IMPACT OF STRUCTURAL INEQUALITIES IN AN INCREASINGLY DIVERSE IRISH SOCIETY
modules.moduleContent: Using a sociological lens to explore attitudes, prejudice and active discrimination informed by inequalities that are embedded in the structure of society itself. Present the case for an inclusive society that celebrates diversity.
learningOutcomes: Apply the sociological imagination that informs Ireland’s demographic profile, and which gives Ireland its unique shape.
Explore the structural inequalities identified in the equality framework (Baker et al, 2009), that is, Capitalism, Patriarchy and Racism and how they impact on modern Irish society.
Appreciate the ways in which professional service workers as well as policy makers might challenge societal inequality to build a more inclusive, integrated and more equitable society.

modules.moduleContent: The module will be taught by an experienced lecturer with an emphasis on structure and principles to provide a solid foundation of core grammar. Practical reading, writing, speaking and listening skills will be taught through tutorials and language laboratory classes with experienced tutors (native speaker). In-class activities will include repetitions, dictations, re-creations of dialogues, group conversations and role plays. By the end of the module students will have acquired a vocabulary of around 100 Chinese characters and be able to express themselves in spoken and written Mandarin.
learningOutcomes: Understand Chinese phonetics (Pinyin), and be able to use it to read and write;
Understand the way Chinese characters are formed, and be able to recognize and write some simple characters or words that are closely related to everyday life;
Understand basic greetings and phrases and commonly used dialogues based on everyday life;
Introduce themselves briefly, in simple vocabulary and grammar (describe basic information about themselves, their family, and their friends);
Provide brief written and oral answers to relevant questions, using simple vocabulary and expressions.

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered include: principles of molecular, cellular and integrative physiology.  An introduction to the organization of nervous systems and endocrine systems in vertebrates.  Principles of membrane potential, action potential generation and propagation in neurons.  Principles of synaptic transmission.  Physiology of contraction in striated and smooth muscle.  Physiology of circulatory systems, gas-exchange mechanisms, energy metabolism, osmoregulation and excretion.  Physiology of sensory systems including the biology of eye design, colour vision, sound and hearing, mechanoreception, olfaction and taste.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the principle physiological systems in animals.
Describe how physiological function relates to organ function in humans.
Explain interrelationships between biochemistry, cell physiology and whole animal physiology
Discuss the role and maintenance of cellular homeostasis in the functioning of animals
Perform quantitative and qualitative experiments to explore physiological responses.
Demonstrate an ability to collate information, evaluate evidence and interpret quantitative data.

modules.moduleContent: Application of ecological concepts, theories and methods, to the management of biological resources. Includes topics such as: the ecology and control of insect pests; the roles of microorganisms in the environment and how they impact on One Health (human, animal and plant health); the ecology of invasive species; marine and sea-floor (benthic) ecology.
learningOutcomes: Describe basic insect structure and function and discuss the ways in which insects are beneficial and injurious to humans, their livestock, crops and products.
Compare and contrast various approaches to controlling insect pests.
Describe the vital processes that require bacteria, and understand the interrelationships of microorganisms in the environment, agriculture, food, human, animal and waste.

Discuss non-human sources or reservoirs of microbial disease & their roles in modern infectious diseases, and describe how the environment provides antibiotics and the risk that it poses as a source of antibiotic resistance.
Discuss the relationship between colonisation and extinction in the MacArthur and Wilson Island Biogeography model.
Describe the impact of invasive plant species on ecosystems, for example Rhododendron ponticum in Ireland and Eichhornia crassipes in the tropics.
Describe ecological processes such as the zonation of seashore organisms and primary productivity in marine ecosystems.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO FIELD ECOLOGY METHODS AND ANALYSIS
modules.moduleContent: The course is taught over 6 individual training workshops, each focusing on a specific skill, technique or information resource, supplemented by independent and group learning. These workshops cover a number of techniques, including -inter alia - animal identification, plant identification, biodiversity assessment, field sampling, habitat surveys, field GIS tools, data analysis, report writing, understanding and working with legal conservation designations, and data presentation. Exercises will involve a mixture of laboratory, on-campus and off-campus settings. Tuition will be provided by MU staff, supplemented by Experts from external organisations involved biodiversity analysis and assessment. A significant element in the course is group-based learning activities. Module grading is entirely by continuous assessment. Students are required to submit individual reports and exercises based directly on workshop activities, as well as individual and group-based learning assignments.
learningOutcomes: Identify and classify the principal animal and plant groups present in Irish ecosystems.
Complete a standard ecological site survey, including compiling environmental descriptors and geodata, and gathering quadrat and transect data.
Describe in general the range of ecological habitats present in Ireland.
Understand the range of conservation designations for protected sites in Ireland and the EU.
Complete standard statistical analysis of ecological data, including parametric and non-parametric methods, and the use of descriptive diversity indices.
Deliver a succinct account of the above in written, audio-visual and oral presentation formats.
Collaborate with peers in the creation of an ecological report.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objectives:  To provide a grounding in the general biology of bacteria, fungi and viruses, and to emphasize the diverse nature of the microbial world.

Topics covered include: Classification methods for bacteria, fungi and viruses; structural features of bacteria, fungi and viruses; microbial growth requirements; controlling microbial growth, aspects of medical microbiology and disease; antibiotics; fungal life cycles; mycelial growth; fungal diseases of animals and plants; viral replication mechanisms; viral strategies to avoid host detection.
learningOutcomes: Describe and explain the various systems used to classify bacteria, fungi and viruses.
Discuss the structural features of bacteria, fungi and viruses, and relate these features to habitat and pathogenicity.
Describe bacterial growth requirements and the physical and chemical agents that can be used to control bacterial growth.
Describe the systematic relationships and taxonomic diversity of the five Phyla of true fungi.
Describe saprotrophic and pathogenic life strategies evident among the fungi.
Explain how fungal mycelia grow and how fungi cause disease in animals and plants.
Compare and contrast the various features of viruses using the Baltimore and Classical classification systems.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Aim: To understand how biotic and abiotic factors together govern the ecology of the Earth’s habitats, impacting on the composition, productivity, population biology and biodiversity of ecosystems. 

Topics covered: The module is divided into two sections. Section 1 (Communities and Change) includes: Soil ecology; The interaction of climate and soil in the formation of the 10 principal global biomes; Succession in Vegetation: patterns, mechanisms and examples from Forests, Peatland and Sand Dunes. Section 2 (Biotic Interactions) includes: Lotka-Volterra modelling of population interactions; Intra-specific competition within species; Inter-specific competition between species; Predatory-Prey relationships; Density-dependent and Density-Independent selection mechanisms; Gradients and patterns of global species diversity. Soil ecology and competition is covered in a lab practical, while succession and habitat analysis is covered in a weekend field course.
learningOutcomes: Describe how terrestrial biomass is organized into biomes that have specific climatic, geographic and latitudinal characteristics.
Discuss and give examples of how habitats and ecosystems are in a constant state of flux that is dependent on both biotic and abiotic (including human) factors.
Explain the ecology and dynamics of the soil, and representative above-ground habitats including sand-dunes, peatlands, forests and seashore habitats.
Demonstrate, with examples, how climate change and human disturbance is likely to impact on global biomes, local habitats, and individual species.
Describe how organisms interact with one another in natural habitats by means of competition, cooperation and predation, using appropriate field and laboratory studies as examples.
Describe how biotic and abiotic interactions can be harnessed to conserve and restore ecosystems.
Evaluate how all the above factors combine to influence the manner in which biodiversity is distributed over the Earth.

modules.moduleContent:
Plants perceive and respond to a multitude of environmental signals, which are essential for regulating how plants grow and develop. The environment is also a source of biotic (e.g. pathogens) and abiotic (e.g. drought) stresses, which plants need to be able to sense and respond to for their survival. This module provides foundations, as well as more detailed insights, into how plants perceive and respond to environmental signals and stresses. The knowledge presented is relevant to key societal, ecological and agricultural issues caused by global climate change. The topics covered are aligned with several United Nations Sustainable Development Goals: Zero Hunger, Climate Action and Life on Land.

Topics covered include: perception of environmental signals; signal transduction pathways; regulation of gene expression; plant responses to light and temperature; environmental signals that regulate flowering; plant interactions with other organisms; perception of pathogens by plants; plant immunity; systemic acquired resistance; plant responses to abiotic stresses.

This module CANNOT be taken with BI315 Bioinformatics as there is a timetable clash.
learningOutcomes: Explain core concepts of how plants perceive environmental signals.
Describe signal transduction pathways that can be activated downstream of signal perception.
Explain how gene expression is regulated in response to environmental signals and link these changes to growth and stress responses.
Explain how plants perceive light as a developmental signal.
Describe how light and temperature impact on seasonal aspects of plant development, including transition to flowering.
Explain how plants perceive and respond to pathogens, including signal transduction pathways, key hormone signaling pathways and changes in gene expression.
Understand how plants sense and respond to abiotic stresses, in particular water-related stresses such as drought and flooding.

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered include: Fungal fermentation systems, brewing and single cell protein production by fungi, fungal gene alteration, heterologous gene expression in fungi. Bioremediation, engineering microbes to degrade toxic products. Generation of recombinant DNA libraries, library screening, clone characterization and DNA sequencing, expression and purification of recombinant proteins in bacteria.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the importance of fungi in biotechnology.
Consider the role of molecular biology in increasing the yield of fungal fermentation products.
Recount significant historical advancements in the field of genetic engineering.
Describe features of bacterial biology that are useful for various areas of biotechnology.
Summarise the steps involved in the construction and screening of recombinant DNA libraries for the production of biopharmaceuticals by bacteria.
Provide examples of biotechnology applications in health and food production.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: INQUIRY BASED BIOLOGY TEACHING AND LEARNING
modules.moduleContent:
This module provides pre-service biology teachers the opportunity to explore and apply an inquiry-based approach to teaching senior-cycle practical biology.

Module content focuses on a range of prescribed Leaving Certificate (LC) practical activities.  The module focuses on student subject knowledge and how to approach teaching biology practical work through inquiry using a framework specifically developed for this module.
learningOutcomes: Understand how practical Biology can be taught using an inquiry-led approach.
Understand the framework for inquiry-led practical biology teaching developed at MU.
Design and teach a LC biology practical incorporating an inquiry-led approach.

modules.moduleContent: Includes topics such as: optimal foraging; decision-making by parasitoids; sexual selection, sperm competition, and sexual conflict; parental care and mating systems; the role of behaviour in parasite transmission; conflict between parasites and hosts over the host’s behaviour
learningOutcomes: Describe behavioural strategies of animals such as those associated with parasitism, foraging or reproduction.
Discuss how behavioural strategies of animals contribute to the animals’ fitness.
Discuss concepts of behavioural ecology in relation to the scientific literature.

modules.moduleContent:
The departmental academic staff set the research topics. Collectively the topics cover a wide range of biological disciplines, and where possible the student will have an element of choice on the subject area. Individual topics will have a narrow enough focus to ensure a survey of the primary literature is appropriate. The student must identify relevant sources of information, assimilate their contents, and use the information to synthesise an informative discussion of the research topic. The student must show evidence of project development (online drafts) and present their work as a written thesis and as an oral presentation.

Students should note that there are a limited number of literature projects available. Literature projects cross semester 1 and 2. The Biology Department oversees the allocation of projects in the first week of the new academic year and allocation is based on third-year grades. Therefore, students may not be allocated a literature project and may be required to amend their module registration after project allocation to ensure that they are registered for sufficient credits in each semester.
learningOutcomes: Gather information on a biological subject from a variety of appropriate and relevant sources.
Integrate, analyse and evaluate information from different sources in a structured and coherent manner.
Attribute information to sources appropriately.
Document sources using standard formats.
Prepare a thesis according to guidelines.
Deliver an oral presentation on the subject demonstrating knowledge of the state of the art in a field of Biology.

modules.moduleContent:
Students undertake a 6-week research project under the supervision of a member of staff. Laboratory work is scheduled in either semester 1 or 2 depending on constraints on laboratory space and limits on laboratory occupancy. Students may be provided with a reading list but are expected to perform a literature search to familiarize themselves with the assigned research topic. Over the duration of the project, students should become proficient in the techniques and equipment relevant to the project. All students provide a 10 minute oral presentation of their research findings, and on completion of the project must submit a short thesis outlining their research findings.

Restricted entry: Students should note that there are a limited number of laboratory projects available. The Biology Department oversees the allocation of projects in the first week of the new academic year and allocation is based on third-year grades. Therefore, students may not be allocated a laboratory project and may be required to amend their module registration after project allocation to ensure that they are registered for sufficient credits in each semester.

Notes
1. Visiting/International students: Module suitable for students visiting for less than the full academic year.
2. This module is for double major students only.
learningOutcomes: Review the scientific literature pertaining to a specific research question.
Design a coherent experimental strategy to critically evaluate a research question.
Perform experiments using the technique(s) learned during the project.
Analyse and interpret the research findings in the context of exisiting literature in the field.
Compile a research thesis in publication format detailing the background literature, methodology, results and implications of the research findings.
Present the research work in the format of a scientific seminar.

modules.moduleContent: Students work in a biotechnology company or academic research laboratory in a research, manufacturing, quality or clinical trials function. The student will be required to compile a detailed report on the outplacement, upon completion of same, and also give an oral presentation based on their outplacement experience. As a consequence of dependence on third parties, if a placement cannot be secured alternative arrangements for completion of module will be found.
learningOutcomes: Identify and list the business area(s) in which the placement company operates.
Develop an understanding of the company structure and report same in the final written report.
Describe the work programme carried out while on placement.
Explain techniques and skills that were acquired while on work placement.
Understand the main personality attributes for working as a successful team member.
Prepare a detailed report that describes the responsibilities held, and duties performed, while on placement.
Create and present an oral presentation that enables assessment of the work performed while on work placement.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Fungal pathogens are a major cause of superficial and systemic disease in immunocompromised (e.g. HIV+ patients) and immunodeficient (e.g. transplant recipients) patients and may contribute to over 4% of hospital-based deaths. The diagnosis and treatment of fungal infections can be difficult and there is a limited range of effective anti-fungal agents currently in use. This module will examine the molecular and cellular mechanisms employed by fungal pathogens to colonise and disseminate within the host, and to evade the immune response. Specific sections will examine the biology of the yeast Candida albicans and its ability to colonise mucosal surfaces. The role of toxins in the pathogenesis of Aspergillus fumigatus, a pulmonary pathogen, will be discussed. The emergence of ‘new’ fungal pathogens will be studied and the factors that have lead to their emergence will be characterized. Other areas to be studied include means of diagnosing fungal infections, treatment options, mode of action of antifungal agents, and the immune response to fungal infection.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the factors affecting the ability of fungi to colonise tissue and cause disease in humans.
Identify the main diagnostic means of detecting and treating fungal infections in humans.
Describe the mode of action of antifungal agents.
Discuss the main mechanisms employed by fungi to resist antifungal agents
Characterise the factors leading to the emergence of ‘new’ fungal pathogens.
Identify the response(s) of fungal pathogens to antifungal agents.

modules.moduleContent: Module objectives: To expose students to genetic, biochemical and cell biological aspects of neuromuscular biology and muscle pathophysiology. This advanced module focuses on the molecular and cellular mechanisms of normal skeletal muscle functions, as well as the molecular pathogenesis of selected neuromuscular disorders. Specific sections will be concerned with the biochemistry, physiology, cell biology and ultrastructure of skeletal muscle fibres, focusing on the molecular mechanisms underlying development, differentiation, fibre transitions and metabolic adaptations to changed functional demands. The diagnosis of muscle diseases and pathobiochemical aspects of major neuromuscular pathologies will be examined, including a discussion of disorders related to myasthenia gravis, myotonia, motor neuron disease, malignant hyperthermia, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, disuse muscular atrophy and sarcopenia of old age. Cell biological and biochemical research tools for studying the molecular pathogenesis of genetic, autoimmune and pharmacogenetic muscle disorders are described. The potential sites for genetic, cell biological and/or pharmacological interventions at different stages of the neuromuscular disease process will be discussed.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the factors affecting muscle development, differentiation, transitions and aging.
Identify the main diagnostic methods of detecting neuromuscular diseases in humans.
Define major aspects of neuromuscular pathology.
Describe the molecular pathogenesis of muscle wasting diseases.
Examine the molecular and cellular mechanisms of fibre degeneration.

modules.moduleName: CLINICAL PROTEOMICS:  DISCOVERY, VALIDATION AND MEDICAL UTILITY
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objectives: Describe to students the role of clinical proteomics in the area of individualised healthcare and technological advances in this area.

Module Content: This advanced module focuses on the field of clinical proteomics, which can be divided into the analysis of body fluids and tissues. Soluble biomarkers will be discussed, which are found in biofluids including blood, urine and saliva, are considered indicator biomolecules that assist in detecting diseased conditions at an early stage, make discrimination between different diseases, and are useful for monitoring progression and response to specific therapeutic strategies. Established clinical biomarkers such as carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) will be discussed, and problems associated with their diagnostic utilities will be addressed. Expression of tissue-based proteins (up-regulation or down-regulation) in various pathological conditions will be explored with emphasis on metabolic and signalling pathways as potential therapeutic targets for treatment of disease. The relationship between biomarkers and therapeutic targets will be examined and the role of companion diagnostics in this area assessed. Underpinning clinical proteomics are the recent developments in quantitative mass-spectrometry, array-based high-throughput protein microarrays and novel fractionation technologies, which will be examined in detail. The role of other "omic" methodologies that are complementary and synergistic to clinical proteomics will be reviewed, specifically looking at metabolomics as an example.
learningOutcomes: Describe how the identification of novel diagnostic marker proteins for cancer and other human diseases is approached.
Define the role of ultra-sensitive proteomics-based methods in biofluid and tissue analysis, from discovery to validation.
Explore how novel therapeutic targets are discovered and their utility as candidate biomarkers.
Discuss individualised healthcare and personalised proteomics for medicine/therapy.
Examine the challenges in the translation of “bench-side" research to “bed-side" applications.
Investigate how other "omic" methodologies are both complementary and synergistic to clinical proteomics, with a particular focus on metabolomics.

Students undertake an 8-week research project under the supervision of a member of staff. Laboratory work is scheduled in either semester 1 or 2 depending on constraints on laboratory space and limits on laboratory occupancy. Students may be provided with a reading list but are expected to perform a literature search to familiarize themselves with the assigned research topic. Over the duration of the project students should become proficient in the techniques and equipment relevant to the project. Therefore, students may not be allocated a laboratory project and may be required to amend their module registration after project allocation to ensure that they are registered for sufficient credits in each semester.
learningOutcomes: Review the scientific literature pertaining to a specific research question.
Design a coherent experimental strategy to critically evaluate a research question.
Perform experiments using the technique(s) learned during the project.
Analyse and interpret the research findings in the context of existing literature in the field.
Compile a research thesis in publication format detailing the background literature, methodology, results and implications of the research findings.

Communicate research in a manner accessible to the informed public.

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to provide fourth-year undergraduate students with a comprehensive understanding of global food security and sustainability issues. Students will examine the challenges and opportunities for achieving sustainable food systems that meet the needs of a growing global population while protecting the environment. The module will explore topics such as the impact of climate change on food production, food waste and loss, food distribution and access, sustainable agriculture, and the role of technology in addressing food security.
Module Content: 

This module will be assessed with a formal class debate (10%) and associated short written assignment (10%) and written assessment (80%).
learningOutcomes: Understand the complex global food system and its key actors and stakeholders.
Analyse the impact of climate change, food waste and loss, and food distribution and access issues on food security.
Evaluate sustainable agriculture practices, technology solutions, and nutrition strategies for promoting food security and sustainability.
Evaluate policy and governance frameworks for food security and sustainability at the national and international level.
Analyse future trends and challenges in global food security and sustainability, and evaluate the role of innovation and technology in addressing these challenges.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objectives: This course (i) provides the student with a solid understanding of the principles of Immunology, and (ii) discusses specific topics of Innate and Adaptive Immunity in detail.

Topics covered include: Pattern recognition receptors and their signalling pathways, anti-viral immune responses and viral Immune Evasion, Transplantation Immunology, Self-tolerance and Autoimmunity, cross-presentation, T cell activation and differentiation, and T cell exhaustion.
learningOutcomes: Compare and contrast the different pattern recognition receptors, their ligands and signalling pathways.
Discuss viral immune evasion strategies targeting different aspects of anti-viral immunity.
Describe how self-tolerance is normally maintained and discuss its relevance for autoimmune conditions and transplantation immunology.
Describe how dendritic cells can lead to activation of naïve CD8+ T cells through the cross-presentation pathway.
Explain the intracellular signalling events involved in T cell activation and differentiation into various effector T cells during an immune response.
Describe the effector functions of activated and exhausted T cells and discuss therapeutic approaches to restore function in disease.
Discuss the interplay between components of the innate and adaptive immune response.

modules.moduleContent: The course will focus on the high-end immunology technologies, beginning with an introduction to modern molecular techniques. Through a series of lectures students will learn about the creation of transgenic animals, the Cre-lox system, knock-out mice, knock-in mice and tissue specific expression. Students will also learn about modern molecular immunological techniques, as well as the power of Flow cytometry. Finally a series of lectures will introduce practical aspects of probing cell signal Transduction pathways. In addition students will undertake a 3-hour problem-based workshop on flow cytometry covering topics such as compensation, controls and data interpretation.
learningOutcomes: have a detailed understanding of the technical applications and methodologies used in modern Immunology.

experience laboratory-based research,
gain organisational and communication skills.
