This student will attend the one-day demonstrator’s course at the beginning of the semester. They will then be assigned to demonstrate at the laboratory sessions of two undergraduate modules. These modules cannot be used to also gain credits in CS894.
learningOutcomes: become an effective demonstrator to undergraduate students,
ensure that the undergraduate students are comfortable with the lab exercises that they must carry out.

modules.moduleContent: The student must participate in 6 significant promotional activities that are run by the department. These activities can run over a longer period than one semester but the student will only register for the final semester in which they are carried out.
learningOutcomes: understand and plan activities which promote the working of the Computer Science department.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL CERTIFICATE  IN POSTGRADUATE TEACHING & LEARNING : TUTORS & DEMONSTRATORS
modules.moduleContent:
The Professional Certificate in Teaching and Learning for Tutors and Demonstrators (CTL1) is a 5 credit module offered to Maynooth University postgraduate research students with tutoring/demonstrating or teaching assistant roles within their departments. The reflective and practical nature of the teaching, learning and assessment approaches of the module requires that participants have adequate tutor/demonstrator/teaching hours to allow them to engage in the learning and fulfil the module learning outcomes. It is therefore essential that those applying for this module can demonstrate that they have a minimum of 9 tutor/demonstrator/teaching assistant hours in the semester that they take the module.

Course Aims:

· To introduce participants to basic pedagogical and teaching and learning theory and practice in higher education in Ireland.

· To introduce them to practical approaches to classroom management, relevant to their own roles and context and to engage students in the learning process.

· Instil confidence and a developmental and reflective approach to their support of student learning in their roles as tutors, demonstrators and teaching assistants.

CTL1 runs twice each academic year, once in each semester (subject to confirmation).

PLEASE NOTE THAT THE CENTRE FOR TEACHING AND LEARNING HAS A SEPARATE APPLICATION PROCESS FOR THIS COURSE. FULL DETAILS ON HOW TO APPLY FOR CTL1, TOGETHER WITH A COPY OF THE APPLICATION FORM, CAN BE FOUND ON THE CENTRE FOR TEACHING AND LEARNING WEBSITE:

https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/centre-teaching-and-learning/accredited-programmes/professional-certificate-teaching-and-learning

Participants should be currently registered Maynooth University postgraduate students currently tutoring/demonstrating/teaching at Maynooth University. It is essential that participants are engaged in teaching, preferably for the duration of the course. A minimum of nine hours teaching spread over the semester is required. Please contact the Centre for Teaching and Learning if you are unsure about whether your current teaching responsibilities meet the requirements for this course, or if you have any other queries (email: ctlprogrammes@mu.ie).
learningOutcomes: Identify issues and solutions in respect of teaching and supporting students in their individual roles as tutors, demonstrators or teaching assistants.
Describe their personal philosophy of teaching and how it influences their own practice and continuous professional development (CPD).
Reflect on their own teaching, using feedback from a range of sources including students, peers and course materials.
Design a teaching session using recognised conventions for writing learning outcomes and planning at a micro level.
Identify and apply relevant methods, strategies and techniques to engage students in the learning process.
Assess students’ learning both formally and informally.
Refer to recognised higher education teaching and learning research and practice and how it informs their own philosophy and approach to teaching.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module will teach students about data structures and programming techniques which will allow them to gather, manipulate, store and graph data sets. 

This module will be assessed on a Pass/Fail basis.

Indicative content:

1. Learning Python
1.1) Installing, Whitespace, Basic constructs, Functions, Modules, Packages, Third-party libraries.

2. Working with in-memory data
2.1) Ordered/unordered data, lists, tuples, dictionaries, sets.

3. Working with persistent data
3.1) TXT, CSV, Pickles, Binaries, JSON, XLSX, Local Databases.

4. Manipulating data
4.1) Curation, Sorting, Searching, Transforming, Mapping, Filtering, Comprehensions.

5. Working with web data
5.1) Scraping, HTML, XML, NLTK.

6. Working with large numerical datasets
6.1) Numpy and Scipy.

7. Working with data frames, time series, financial and economic data
7.1) Pandas.

8. Producing graphs and plots from your data
8.1) Matplotlib, Jupyter notebooks, Bokeh.

9. Working in the cloud
9.1) Accessing datasets via a REST based API and publishing data programmatically on the web.

10. Other programming technologies
10.1) R
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate and compare the effectiveness of programming technologies for data analysis.
Research, identify and critically evaluate the most appropriate data structures for storing and analysing data sets.
Research, identify and critically evaluate libraries for the processing of datasets.

Devise, implement and critique appropriate programming technologies for the management of datasets - involving the scraping of data from the web, preparation for consumption over computer networks and design and development of RESTful APIs.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aims of this module are to provide students with knowledge of the detailed principles of crop biotechnology, with particular emphasis on genetically engineering crops for biotic stress tolerance, crop productivity and quality improvement. In addition, an interactive learning environment will be created to encourage students to develop an appreciation of using innovative biotechnological approaches relating to bioenergy crops, forestry and applications of crop biotechnology to human and animal health.

Indicative content

1. Introduction to soil and crop science
1.1) Land utilisation; Soil nutrient management; Soil biology; Yield formation; Current and future biostimulants in agriculture; Production and application of biofertilisers; Molecular and systems biology of plants; Integrated pest management (Legislation, health concerns, biotech alternative to traditional chemistry); Entomology; Biopesticides; Ecological plant physiology; Molecular phytopathology.

2. Genetic engineering
2.1) Plant protection and bioengineering; Gene editing; Molecular breeding; Tissue culture methods for crop improvement; Plant metabolic engineering; Plants as bioreactors. Bioinformatics for plant biotechnology.
2.2) Methods of genetic engineering for; Manipulation of the rhizosphere (enhancing plant roots, symbiotic relationships); biotic stress tolerance (insects, viruses, weeds); crop productivity (manipulation of nutrient uptake); quality improvement (food nutritive value, protein, lipids, carbohydrates).

3. Forestry biotechnology
3.1) Breeding technologies and genetic engineering technologies; Lignin-modified transgenic Trees; Fungal strategies for lignin degradation; Fibre modification, lignin reduction and extraction, and sterility; Forestry productivity; enhancement of bio-based products; gene flow and introgression.

4. Non traditional crops
4.1) Bioenergy crop production; Fibre, hemp, flax, algae and fibre modification. Barriers to bioenergy development; Energy crop models; Production of valuable proteins for food supplements and pharmaceuticals; Alternative protein sources for fuel and food sources.

5. Ethics of crop biotechnology industry
5.1) Risk analysis; International regulation of crop biotechnology; Case studies: GM event integration into established agricultural systems.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an advanced level of knowledge in the area of advanced and innovative technologies utilised in crop biotechnology.
Comprehend current published research in the area of genetically engineering crops.

Interpret outcomes of previous crop biotechnological investigations and legislative actions, and demonstrate an ability to formulate hypotheses, determine associated risks and inform change based on the information obtained.
Write well-researched and referenced scientific literature review on selected topics of crop biotechnology and discuss how these relate to their postgraduate research project.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to the core issues that affect the behaviour of economic agents. Using a mixture of theory and real-life applications, topics to be analysed include: demand; elasticity; monopoly; revenue maximisation; costs; profit maximization; perfect competition; efficiency; price controls; taxes and subsidies.
learningOutcomes: identify the determinants of supply and demand;
distinguish between movements along supply/demand and shifts of supply/demand curves;
determine and explain market equilibrium;
discuss the effect of government economic policies and their welfare implications;
distinguish between the accounting and economic costs of economic agents;
discuss how firm incentives are affected by the market structure in which they operate;
compare market outcomes across different market structures;

modules.moduleContent: This module analyses the core issues affecting the behaviour of individual economic agents and how their choices affect market outcomes. Building on the material covered in EC101, topics may include: consumer behaviour, individual and market demand, production, costs, profit maximisation, perfect competition, monopoly, oligopoly, price discrimination, externalities, public goods.
learningOutcomes: Explain how consumers with limited incomes choose between competing alternatives.
Show how the theory of rational individual choice can be used to derive individual and market demand curves.
Discuss the basic economic theory of the firm.
Analyze the production decisions of profit maximising and cost minimising firms.
Illustrate how market outcomes are affected by market structure.
Discuss the various pricing strategies available to a monopolist.
Discuss how market failure can occur and how government intervention in the market can improve outcomes.

modules.moduleContent: In this module we (i) examine the banking and financial markets and institutions through a European lens, (ii) examine the role of credit and credit granting institutions, such as banks and other financial institutions, taking note of how banking and other financial institutions provide benefits and impose costs on the economy; (iii) inquire into the regulation of banking and other financial institutions taking note of regulatory successes and failures; (iv) consider the role of central banks, especially the ECB, in creating macroeconomic stability and promoting economic growth.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of banks and other financial institutions, financial markets, and systems in Europe and the world.

Understand (i) the process of banking and financial regulation in the European Union, (ii) the evolution of banking and financial regulation both pre- and post-financial crisis, and (iii) the challenges of designing regulations for the euro area and the EU as a whole.
Develop a theoretical and practical knowledge of monetary policy from the perspective of the ECB.
Analyze the causes and consequences of financial and other crises on the European banking and financial systems.

Critically evaluate financial micro- and macroprudential regulatory policy and competition policy and the effects of these policies on the economy as a whole, the financial sector, individual financial institutions, especially banks, and purchasers of financial services.
Understand the costs and benefits of financial innovation and be able to analyze whether financial innovations benefit society.
Describe the different players in the financial sector and explain how the services they offer fulfill the six functions of the financial system, of which banks are only one element.

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered may include: market failure and the foundations of public sector theory; externalities; public goods; the trade-off between efficiency and equity; principles and incidence of taxation; evaluation of public expenditure
learningOutcomes: Identify the economic conditions in which government intervention is necessary.
Discuss various types of market failure and how to remedy them.
Understand the trade-off a government faces between equity and efficiency.
Assess a government’s tax policy.
Explain the difference between private and social cost-benefit analysis.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at students who are interested in learning Chinese alongside the core subjects of their degree programmes. It will appeal to all types of students – those who have a genuine interest in learning about the Chinese language and culture for their own sake as well as those who are motivated to develop Chinese language skills and (inter)cultural competence to enhance their employment prospects in Ireland and further afield. Practical reading, writing, speaking and listening skills will be taught through in-person classes with experienced tutors who are native speakers. Classroom-based learning will be complemented by asynchronous online material and exercises to be completed weekly.

Please note: Prospective students should note that this language elective is intended for beginners. Students who took Mandarin Chinese for the Leaving Certificate examination (or equivalent) cannot be accommodated in this class but should consider signing up for a Beginners’ language elective in French, German, or Spanish instead.
learningOutcomes: Understand Chinese phonetics (Pinyin), and be able to use it to read and write;
Be able to recognize and write some simple characters or words that are closely related to everyday life;
Understand basic phrases and common used dialogues based on everyday life;
Conduct simple conversations and exchange ideas with others;
Provide brief written and oral answers to relevant questions using simple vocabulary and expressions.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The module will be taught by an experienced lecturer with an emphasis on structure and principles to provide a solid foundation of core grammar. Practical reading, writing, speaking and listening skills will be taught through tutorials and language laboratory classes with experienced tutors (native speakers). In-class activities will include repetitions, dictations, re-creations of dialogues, group conversations and role plays. By the end of the module students will have acquired a vocabulary of around 500 Chinese characters (accumulatively) and be able to express themselves in both spoken and written Mandarin.
learningOutcomes: Comprehend simple and familiar language material that is closely related to everyday life;
Exchange ideas with others on common topics;
Recognise the main information in brief texts that are commonly seen in everyday life;
Describe one's personal life or provide brief written answers to relevant questions in Chinese characters.

modules.moduleName: PROJECT-BASED LEARNING: CHINESE LANGUAGE AND CULTURE
modules.moduleContent:
This module will allow students to develop their interests in contemporary Chinese language, linguistics, literary and or cultural studies through developing an independent research project in collaboration with a group. Students will be introduced to certain key research themes and topics and will be able to choose an area that is of particular interest to them.
Topics include the following:
• Media/Film Studies or Communications
• Intercultural Studies
• National Identities
• Chinese business
• Chinese philosophy
• Chinese history
• Chinese language teaching and learning
Participants should note that this module will have a substantial language-based element, whereby they will work on further developing their skills in reading, spoken production, spoken interaction, listening, and writing in Mandarin Chinese.

*****Before registering, participants should be aware that thy must be able to provide evidence of linguistic competence in Mandarin Chinese commensurate with CEFR level B2.2 or HSK Level 4 in order to take this module. Contact the department with any queries.

This module will help the students to understand the processes involved in planning and writing an academic essay; conduct independent research; apply social theories and concepts and relate them to particular problems, issues and phenomena in Chinese society; achieve a better understanding of modern and contemporary China.
learningOutcomes: Illustrate and explain in detail an innovative approach to one particular aspect of Chinese culture, history and society.
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and well-structured argument.
Evaluate critically, apply and reference appropriately a wide range of library, electronic and online resources.
Employ appropriate vocabulary, terminology, register and style to produce a high-quality piece of writing.
Process and integrate constructive feedback from the supervisor of the research project.
Work autonomously and take responsibility for all aspects of the planning, research, composition and completion of a research project.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: FUNDAMENTALS OF LOGIC FOR COMPUTATIONAL THINKING
modules.moduleContent: The module will cover the following topics: Inferences and validity, Propositional logic including Conjunctive Normal form and Disjunctive normal form, Set theory, Syllogistic reasoning, Relations, and Predicate calculus. Examples of applications will be discussed, and some advanced topics will be described. Programming implementation will be included as Laboratory work.
learningOutcomes: To understand the fundamentals of using inference to interrogate statements;
To understand the mathematical representations of truth tables, propositional logic, sets theory, and first-order logic;
To understand how to apply these techniques to represent and solve logical problems;
To learn how to write simple programs in a declarative language such as or similar to Prolog.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Programming fundamentals: variables, types, expressions and assignment; simple I/O; Conditional and iterative control structures (if statements and while loops); Strings and string processing; Use of class APIs for creating objects and calling methods; Understanding data abstraction and encapsulation; Problem-solving: understanding and developing algorithms; Implementing algorithms as simple programs. 
Data organisation; file systems; storage and access methods; conceptual modelling; relational database model; data structure; data integrity; data manipulation; Structured Query Language (SQL); relational algebra; basic transaction processing.
Introduction to networking: network types, layered models, OSI and TCP/IP models. Internet services.
learningOutcomes: Understand, evaluate and create algorithms;  design and write structured programs; compile programs; debug runtime errors; select and use appropriate programming structures.

Select and implement appropriate data types; identify major DBMS functions and describe their role in a database system; prepare a relational schema from a conceptual model developed using the entity- relationship model.

Demonstrate use of the relational algebra operations from mathematical set theory (union, intersection, difference, and Cartesian product) and the relational algebra operations developed specifically for relational databases (select (restrict), project, join, and division); demonstrate queries in the relational algebra; use a declarative query language to elicit information from a database; write programs that execute queries and use results within an application.

For each of the layers in the TCP/IP reference model explain their implementation, functions, common protocols and data structures;  discuss the techniques that can be used to share network resources.

Explain how IP addresses can be assigned to hosts using sub netting and super netting, and will be able to identify situations in which these are appropriate solutions; set up a web server and provide simple web services.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module will study several different aspects of music systems programming. These will include audio programming, signal processing and component development (plugins, opcodes, classes, etc.).
learningOutcomes: Describe the principles underlying sound generating and processing sections of software synthesizers.
Identify the causes and solutions for aliasing distortion.
Implement synthesizer elements in software
Implement sound effect processing in software.
Construct a software that can interpret and respond to midi protocol messages.
Discuss the issues of real-time implementations.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Mathematical preliminaries; regular languages, finite automata, and regular expressions; nondeterminism and determinism in finite automata; finite automata minimisation; properties of regular languages; nonregular languages; context-free languages, context-free grammars, and pushdown automata; nondeterminism and determinism in pushdown automata; properties of context-free languages; non-context-free languages; multi-stack machines; Turing machines and Church Turing thesis; decidable and recognisable languages; undecidability of languages.
learningOutcomes: Design the corresponding machine model to accept a specified language
Explain how some problems have no algorithmic solution
Convert among equivalently powerful notations for a language, including among DFAs, NFAs, and regular expressions, and between PDAs and CFGs, explain the Church-Turing thesis and its significance
Assess and prove the computational power required to decide a language
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Machine learning principles; The probabilistic perspective on machine learning; Supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning; Biological neurons and their relation to linear classifiers; Supervised learning techniques: linear regression, the perceptron learning rule, backpropagation, recurrent networks, deep learning, support vector machines; Unsupervised techniques: k-means clustering, EM of gaussian mixtures, hidden markov models; Reinforcement learning: Policy-value iteration, Q-learning, TD-learning, deep reinforcement learning systems.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish the different categories of Machine Learning techniques and identify situations in which they might be used;
Describe the implementation of simple algorithms for machine learning;
Discuss the adaptation of a standard technique to a given specific problem;
Describe the implementation and evaluation some standard machine learning algorithms in a general purpose programming language such as Java;
Obtain a basic understanding of machine learning approaches and neural networks;
Discuss the relationships between training set, test set, generalisation, cross validation;
Describe how various machine learning techniques work and what their strengths and limitations are;
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to both artificial intelligence (AI) and natural language processing (NLP). Defining intelligence and multiple Intelligences, Turing's imitation game; Ethics. Problem-solving, state-spaces, generate and test, game playing; two-player games, graph-searching; Optimisation strategies; biologically inspired computation. Introduction to neural networks and deep learning, Text processing, parsing natural languages (especially English), semantics and knowledge representation; embeddings and Word2Vec, knowledge graphs, information retrieval, brief overview of reinforcement learning. Large language models (GPT-4), prompt, temperature, hallucinations, and bias.
learningOutcomes: Explain the underlying principles, advantages and limitations of the AI approaches to solving problems
Demonstrate the application of artificial intelligence techniques to problem solving
Describe AI algorithms with their application to a range of problem types
Describe the concepts of modern AI different systems, explaining differences to classical techniques
Assess the strengths and weaknesses of AI approaches to different problems
Compare classical and learning (embedding) approaches
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: In this course students will be given a broad introduction to the field of computer vision, including the following topics: Introduction to computer vision and its applications; cameras and sensing; visual attributes, histograms and kernel density estimation; contour extraction; interest point detection and matching; machine learning and deep neural networks for computer vision.
learningOutcomes: Explain the underlying principles, advantages and limitations of the AI approaches to solving problems.
Demonstrate the application of artificial intelligence techniques to problem solving.
Describe AI algorithms with their application to a range of problem types.
Describe the concepts of modern AI different systems, explaining differences to classical techniques.
Assess the strengths and weaknesses of AI approaches to different problems.
Compare classical and learning (embedding) approaches to processing text.
Assess the impacts of representational alternatives for problems and linguistic information.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module is about the design of programming languages. In particular it is concerned with the syntax, the semantics and usability of a programming language. The basics of lambda calculus, both untypes and simply typed, will be studied, with an eye towards its use in formalizing the semantics of programming languages. Working knowledge of three languages Scheme, Haskell, and Prolog) will be acquired. Semantics will be studied using both formal machinery and an interpreter-based approach.
learningOutcomes: Read and write simple programs in Scheme, Haskell, and Prolog;
Understand lambda calculus reductions, renaming, variable capture, and confluence;
Understand the erasure theorem, the termination properties of both typed and untyped lambda calculus, and typing rules;
Describe the syntax and semantics of Scheme, Haskell, Prolog, and lambda calculus.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Time domain speech processing; frequency domain speech analysis; time frequency representations; implementations of digital audio effects. Algorithms for the analysis, synthesis, and modification of instrumental sounds.
learningOutcomes: Illustrate the mathematical theory underlying each signal processing approach for speech and audio
Design, implement and critically evaluate signals processing algorithms for speech and audio
Integrate individual algorithmic elements to create an audio processing system to extract particular features from speech or audio
Describe key digital filter structures used for the processing of audio signals
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Topics may include: Theory: Primitives, 2D&3D, Image Formats, Colour, Lighting and Shading, Homogeneous co-ordinates, Quaternions, Camera models, Transformations, Rendering, Animation; Hardware: Graphics Cards, Data formats, display technologies, MoCap; Software: OpenGL, Blender and Unity.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the history of computer graphics; explain application areas of graphics and current areas of research
Distinguish between ray tracing and rasterisation methods of image formation
Implement code to describe an object and scene using ray-tracing methods
Implement code to render 2D graphics
Implement code to render and manipulate 3D models within a skybox
Explain and implement colour and lighting models
Explain how to model motion
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: A variety of advanced and current topics in Computer Science will be examined in weekly seminars. These seminars will help students to develop a general and integrative appreciation of Computer Science as a research and professional discipline. Many of the key issues raised during the degree programming will be revisited, compared and contrasted in order to identify common themes and critical differences within the discipline. Students'' knowledge of a wide range of issues relevant to Computer Science will be assessed in the end of year paper that will function as the assessment for this module.
learningOutcomes: Describe the background to several advanced research topics  in the concepts, technologies or major theorems in the foundations of Computer Science
Discuss and explain the major issues addressed by each topic and/or the individual sections of the text of each theorem
Critically evaluate the relevance and use of each research topic or theorem
Implement algorithms derived from the theory and/or practice of each topic
Where appropriate, apply algorithms from each topic to practical situations and problems
Compare and contrast each topic with alternative approaches to the same problems, where they exist
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: FINAL YEAR PROJECT- SINGLE MAJOR SCIENCE
modules.moduleContent: Depends entirely on the individual project, which may be proposed by either the student or a member of staff.
learningOutcomes: Conduct a reasonably thorough investigation into a topic of interest, importance or relevance to computer science or its applications
Where appropriate, accomplish an acceptable implementation (or other useful model) to fulfil the main goals of the project
Work largely independently, within agreed project requirements, with minimal supervision
Deliver technical presentations during the project development cycle
Complete a detailed project report covering all central aspects of the project
Work within all project deadlines, interim report deadlines and consultation requirements giving regular and timely feedback to your project supervisor(s)
Plan and manage all your own project milestones
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: THE MATHEMATICS & THEORY OF COMPUTER SCIENCE
modules.moduleContent: Students will learn how to identify problems that cannot be solved with a computer, how to identify problems that should not be solved (but only approximated) with a computer, and how to proceed in both cases.
learningOutcomes: Students will gain an understanding of the fundamental and practical limits of computers.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to requirements engineering and software design. Behavioural and non-behavioural requirements. Gathering, validating and tracing requirements. Development methodologies, test driven development. Analysis and design with UML (Unified Modeling Language), UML tools. Use Case, sequence and collaboration models, Class diagram, State machines, Activity diagrams, Component and Deployment diagrams. Elaborating and decorating diagrams, tailoring UML. Verification, specification and the OCL (Object Constraint Language). Describing and applying software patterns. Case studies, sample problems and projects. Leveraging assistive technologies (LLM like ChatGPT, Copilot) for requirements & design.
learningOutcomes: Select and apply various requirements elicitation and documentation techniques.
Create verifiable user requirements for various software projects.
Model specific aspects of software systems using various UML diagrams.
Choose and apply a selection of software design patterns.
Describe implementation strategies for alternative software designs and patterns.
Design for a comprehensive, high-quality software system situated in a problem context.
Assess a software project.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module provides students with an understanding of the principles of testing, and experience in applying these in an automated test environment. Topics include fundamental principles of testing, automated test tools, testing in the software process, black-box test techniques, white-box test techniques, testing object-oriented software, system testing, test coverage, performance testing, and test selection criteria.
learningOutcomes: describe the principles of software testing;
describe and compare a number of standard testing techniques for unit and system testing;
select test techniques and design test cases;
design and implement automated tests;
evaluate test coverage;
describe and evaluate the relevance of testing in the software development process.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The content of the module examines the course objectives using a variety of Internet programming technologies, making comparisons with alternative technologies and emphasising the application of software engineering principles. Internet solutions engineering using best-practice design patterns are examined and used to deploy a large-scale multitiered assignment.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The module is a deep dive into the details of deep learning architectures with a focus on learning end-to-end models for signal processing (e.g. time series, images, video, audio, IMU, lidar etc…).
The module will present some of the basics of deep neural networks as well as more recent ones, with their applications to various AI tasks. 

Indicative Syllabus
• Neural Networks Architectures (e.g. CNNs, Transformers, GNNs)
• Neural Networks Optimization (e.g. Backpropagation)
• Transfer Learning, contrastive learning, and fine-tuning performance
• Limits and issues with deep learning
• Use of standard frameworks for deep learning (e.g. Pytorch, TensorFlow, TensorFlow Lite, pytorch, deep graph library, OpenVINO toolkit)

By the end of the module, it is expected that students will have significant familiarity with the subject and be able to apply Deep Learning to a variety of tasks (e.g. robotics, computer vision, etc.).
learningOutcomes: Explain the basic principles behind neural networks and deep learning & compare modelling aspects of various neural network architectures
Implement and evaluate the deep learning architecture
Apply and evaluate deep learning on real data sets
Provide successful examples how deep learning can be used in different contexts in the society
Read and critically assess papers on artificial neural networks and their applications
Select the appropriate deep learning architecture for a processing task
Demonstrate an understanding of the use of engineering tools to build and fine tune deep learning systems
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Content and structure of a thesis; Formulating a research question; Preparation of a thesis outline; Appropriate application of research methods; Appointment of thesis supervisor.

**The thesis must be successfully completed before award of the Degree.

NOTE: Students must have successfully completed a minimum of 120 ECTS on the BA Community Studies MH803.
learningOutcomes: Identify a research topic.
Write an initial research proposal.
With supervision prepare a proposal to the point where research can begin.

Identify informants, groups or individuals who may provide primary research data. Subject to approval of the supervising department, such data can include published primary documents, publicly available survey datasets, and similar materials.
Understand the methodological process involved in carrying out an extended piece of social science research.
Under supervision bring the research topic to a successful conclusion.

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to requirements and design engineering. Software Requirements, behavioural and non-behavioural requirements, gathering and validating requirements. Analysis and design with UML (Unified Modelling Language), UML case tools. Software process, the external perspective and Use Case modelling. Sequence and collaboration models, structural models and the class diagram, State charts, activity diagrams, Component and Deployment diagrams. Decorating diagrams, stereotypes, the OCL (Object Constraint Language). Introduction to software Patterns, describing and finding patterns. Case studies and sample problems.
learningOutcomes: Select and apply various requirements elicitation and documentation techniques
Create verifiable user requirements for various software projects
Model specific aspects of software systems using various UML diagrams
Choose and apply a selection of software design patterns
Describe implementation strategies for alternative software designs and patterns
Design for a comprehensive, high-quality software system situated in a problem context
Assess a software project.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: A combination of advanced object-oriented programming techniques and concepts in object-oriented languages are discussed. Topics covered include Classes, Objects, Encapsulation, Information Hiding, Inheritance, Composition, Object Lifetimes, Files, Input/Output Streams, Operator Overriding, Operator Overloading, Polymorphism, Dynamic Binding, Templates, Exceptions and the Standard Template Library.
learningOutcomes: construct object oriented programming solutions for reuse, using abstract data types that incorporate encapsulation, data abstraction, information hiding, and the separation of behavior and implementation;

construct multiple-file or multiple-module programming solutions that use classes, subclasses, class hierarchies, inheritance, parameterised classes, generics and polymorphism to reuse existing design and code;
compare and contrast a range of object-oriented programming languages. Create programming solutions that use data structures and existing libraries;
perform object-oriented design and programming with a high level of proficiency in more than one object-oriented programming language.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Programming fundamentals: variables, types, expressions and assignment; simple I/O; Conditional and iterative control structures (if statements and while loops); Strings and string processing; Use of class APIs for creating objects and calling methods; Understanding data abstraction and encapsulation; Problem solving: understanding and developing algorithms; Implementing algorithms as simple programs. Introduction to algorithms and data structures. Review of elementary programming concepts suitable for the implementation of abstract data types (operators, types and expressions; control of flow; methods; recursion; input & output); Algorithms for searching: linear, bounded linear and binary searches; Algorithms for sorting: selection, insertion, bubble and quick sorts; Fundamental linear data structures: stacks, queues, linked lists; Object-oriented programming: encapsulation and information hiding, classes, interfaces, class hierarchies, inheritance, polymorphism, basic exception handling; Analysis of basic algorithms.

This module normally commences up to four weeks before the start of the academic term and runs for three weeks (five days per week and from 9:00 am to 5:30 pm each day).
Next offering provisional start date is 2nd September 2024.  
Contact the Computer Science department to confirmation of the start date.
learningOutcomes: understand, evaluate and create algorithms, write structured programs, debug runtime errors, select and implement Data Structures.

modules.moduleContent: The course is designed to improve the academic writing skills of students so that they can organise and structure a range of academic assignments including essays and theses. Lectures/Workshops will focus on skills development key areas; Structure, Content, Argument Construction, Citation, Presentation and Style.
learningOutcomes: Be familiar with how to organise and structure an academic essay into key component parts.
Apply analysis and argument in terms of academic writing.
Consider how to develop an extended piece of writing such as a minor thesis from its component parts.
Understand the importance of compiling a bibliography and organising a literature review.
Be capable of referencing content from a range of media besides print and conforming to stylistic conventions.

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims at training and assessing Computer Science research students in technical, scientific, communication and general research skills in the second year of the CS PhD programme. This will include the submission and examination of the substantive review thesis by the student, as detailed in the CS Research Postgraduate Handbook. The module will also involve an important focus on learning and applying the Open Science (FAIR) principles of CS research.

Assessment :
- Research: Substantive review thesis (April deadline) and presentation / interview (May)
- Student to complete Modules https://openscience101.org : “open code” and “open results”
- Other: submission of forms (e.g. GSF1, GSF2) as defined by course coordinator, submission of other certificates as defined by supervisor and funding agency (e.g. Epigeum, EPE). Participation to departmental events as defined by course coordinator (e.g. October Poster presentation, June workshop presentation)

Please note that you should have successfully completed module CS865 (Computer Science PhD Year 1) before registering for this module.
learningOutcomes: Develop and communicate research questions and ideas beyond  SoTA in their field of research.
Understand and apply Open Science principles in all aspects of their research.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 

On successful completion of this module students will have examined different theoretical approaches to military decision making, they will be able to apply such processes to practical examples relating to the land component and they will understand the application of decision making processes in the planning of military operations and crisis management operations. 

Module Content: 

This module runs in parallel to DM1 and requires an analysis and understanding of methodologies associated with military decision making processes. Students will be required to evaluate the strengths and limitations inherent in such processes and to apply decision making theories to practical case studies. They will test their knowledge and understanding in a series of case study exercises, tutorials and classroom discussions.

Module Objective: On completion of this module students will be able to critically analyse historical battles and campaigns. They should be able to examine them within an appropriate historical context and be able to relate their findings to relevant debates about strategic theory and the utility of military force. Students should also be able to articulate the key debates within strategic studies and be able to use these to examine and explain features of the contemporary security environment.


Module Content: The module will include lectures, tuition and seminars relating to the subjects of military history, campaign analysis, strategic studies and contemporary security. Students will participate in two formal group presentations in which they must demonstrate knowledge and understanding of these topics. They will also complete a 2,500 word essay. During the course of the module students will also take part in a military-history field trip in which they engage in a number of campaign analysis discussions and debates.

This module will enable the student to develop the advanced technical, critical thinking and communication skills required to explore and present data to deliver valued insights to a targeted audience within the context of a data analytics project lifecycle. This module will be assessed on a Pass/Fail basis.

1. Data Analytics Processes
1.1) Review of lifecycle and processes (CRISP-DM, SEMMA). Types of problems. Role of stakeholders. Statement of problem and related business value. Case studies with guest presentations in areas such as sports analytics.

2. Visualisation Theory
2.1) Overview of visualisation including Information/data/scientific visualisations and infographics. Static, dyanamic and interactive visualisations. Exploratory vs explanatory visualisation. Data types. High dimensional data. Hierarchical data. Principles of visualisation design (perception, colour, font). Data density. Data ink maximisation and graphical design. Colour theory.

3. Visualisation Techniques for Data Exploration and Explanation
3.1) Exploration of high dimensional data. Multidimensional scaling. Data preprocessing and cleaning. Exploration of outliers and missing values. Visualisation methods and technologies for structured and unstructured data types such as temporal, geospatial, network, hierarchical and text data. Charts(number and graphical), plots, maps, diagrams, cluster maps, matrices, trees and network diagrams. Simulations. Immersive data analytics.
3.2) Dealing with big data. Application through appropriate programming, spreadsheet and BI technologies.

4. Communication
4.1) Reporting and presenting. Effective visualisation. Visualisation and presentation tools. Spreadsheets. Dashboards. Business Intelligence tools. Interactive visualisations. Case studies.
learningOutcomes: Research, identify and critically evaluate the key insights required to deliver value for a targeted set of stakeholders related to a data analytics project.
Devise, implement and critique appropriate visualisation techniques for data exploration within a data analytics process.
Devise, implement and critique appropriate data visualisation theory and techniques to communicate results and insights to stakeholders.
Effectively present and defend the validity of key insights at the final stage of a data analytics project.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The last twenty years has seen the gradual emergence of new technology, government policy and practice that is responding to the challenges of climate change and scarcity of natural resources. As public understanding grows of the need to guard the Earth’s natural resources, a global and local demand is emerging for renewable energy technology. Businesses are also trying to find ways to reduce costs through greater resource efficiency and energy saving measures. This has spawned the ‘Green Economy’ which is one of the fastest-growing markets in the world.

Indicative Content

1. Impact of climate change.
1.1) Stern review; IPCC reports;Kyoto protocol; Copenhagen accord; Doha agreement; Paris Energy Summit.

2. Modelling Energy Scenarios
2.1) Using scenario development as a tool for energy solutions; Steps in scenario planning; Shell's Energy scenario 2050. STEEPLE analysis.

3. Global, European and National Energy Policy
3.1) E.g. The Green Deal, Europe 2050, etc.

4. Green Finance
4.1) Energy funding streams; Electricity trading and carbon trading; FITS and ROCS; Methods of Financial appraisal; Economic externalities; Costing externalities;

5. Clean tech innovation and the green economy
5.1) Appraisal of economic policy instruments designed to enhance innovation in the green economy; development of the supply chain; collaborative networks; energy clusters.
learningOutcomes: Apprise the impact of climate change on the environment and the global economy.
Research and assess global, European and National Energy policy and the role of renewable technology and energy efficiency measures.
Model future energy solutions, developing energy scenarios for communities, cities and countries globally.
Develop financial and economic appraisals of proposed energy projects.
Assess the energy supply chain and the opportunity for 'clean tech' innovation and the development of the Green Economy.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The module aims to strengthen the relationship between research and wider society outreach and public engagement activities. Participants must undertake a minimum of 15 hours voluntary work within the semester with a recognised and approved organisation across a range of areas and relevant to their study discipline e.g. H2O Heroes, Cell Explorers, United Youth Initiative programme. Through a mentoring process students will be provided with appropriate training, guidance and support. Students will be required to reflect in a structured way on their volunteering and community experience(s) and apply it to their research project.

Indicative content

1. Organisation
1.1) Student must register with a volunteer organisation relevant to their study discipline such an involvement in outreach education programmes, clubs and/or societies or with a community-based organisation, locally, nationally or internationally. Garda vetting and/or child protection training must be completed if required.

2. Volunteer Training
2.1) Volunteer training will include topics such as the nature of community, social capital, concept of volunteers, benefits of volunteering. Ethical principals and procedures. Engaging with Ethical Guidelines on volunteering. Training specific to volunteer organisation/activity will also be provided.

3. Volunteering Activities
3.1) Students must undertake a minimum of 15 hours voluntary work. Demonstrating activities; Working as part of a dynamic and diverse team; Answering questions from participants; Communicating STEM/research concepts to the general public.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the concept of volunteering and personal social capital.
Analyse the application of Ethical Volunteering Practice.
Appraise and reflect on personal motivations and benefits of volunteering and engaging with local communities for researchers.
Evaluate the range of transversal and employability skills acquired through volunteering for researchers.

modules.moduleContent: This course covers probability and statistical techniques for data analytics and data science. Topics include: Exploratory data analysis and visualisation. Probability basics, independence, Bayes theorem. Probability models for data, including Binomial, Poisson, Exponential and Normal. Confidence intervals and hypothesis testing: one and two samples, paired samples, proportions. Case studies in R.
learningOutcomes: Analyse data using R.
Write statistical reports.
Model data using probability distributions.
Choose and construct appropriate data visualisations.

modules.moduleContent: This course is a continuation of DS663. Topics are:  Parameter estimation; method of moments maximum likelihood and least squares. Simple linear and multiple regression. Analysis of Variance, R2, sequential sums of squares, general F-tests.  Case studies in R.  Model checking via residual plots.  Regression diagnostics: residuals, leverage, outliers, influence and Cook's Distance.
learningOutcomes: Analyse data using linear models and analysis of variance.
Assess assumptions.
Use model diagnostics.
Choose and construct appropriate data visualisations.

modules.moduleName: ECONOMETRICS (STATISTICAL METHODS FOR ECONOMICS AND BUSINESS)
modules.moduleContent: This is an introductory statistics course. Topics will include descriptive statistics, probability theory, discrete and continuous probability distributions, sampling distributions, interval estimation and hypothesis testing.
learningOutcomes: Calculate and interpret measures of location and variability using data sets.
Use the laws of probability to compute the probabilities of events.
Compute probability values for continuous and discrete probability distributions.
Describe the characteristics of the sampling distribution of the sample mean and sample proportion.
Construct confidence intervals for population means and population proportions.
Formulate and test appropriate research hypotheses.

modules.moduleContent: Students will work in groups on a project, using statistical analysis to address a topical economic issue. Using published data, students will compile descriptive statistics and test one or more economic hypotheses. They will make oral and written presentations of their findings.

modules.moduleContent:
The course begins with a short exposition of why the history of economic thought matters to current students of economics and to students of social sciences in general.  Starting with mercantilism, the course then examines the classical economic thinkers (such as Smith, Ricardo and Malthus), after which we turn to Marxist economics and non-mainstream economic thinkers such as Veblen.  Subsequently, we study neoclassical economists (e.g., Marshall, Walras), followed by Keynesianism and the study of Schumpeter's ideas.  The course ends by looking at some newer developments in economic thought.

Note: Students cannot take this module if they are taking EC240.
learningOutcomes: identify how and why different economic concepts evolved
critique ideas of key economic thinkers
assess the relevance of economic concepts pioneered in the past for today's economic problems

modules.moduleContent: Topics include: the rise of Chinese film in the early 20th century; the development of commercial cinema since the 1980; famous Chinese directors; TV drama, talent shows and reality shows in China; government censorship and cultural policies; the rise of the internet and new media in China.
learningOutcomes: Understand new trends in the television and film industry in China
Analyse influences of new media on cultural production and consumption in China

Develop enhanced skills of oral and written analysis and communication

modules.moduleContent: This module will examine key elements of social theories that developed in modern and contemporary China and their influence on Chinese society. It is an introduction to major aspects of social theories in contemporary China. Students should reflect upon social, philosophical and ideological thoughts of socialism, nationalism, neo-liberalism, communism, Confucianism, all of which have influenced social development in China. Ethnographic accounts of traditional and contemporary China will be used in the discussion of each theory.
learningOutcomes: Understand social, philosophical and ideological thoughts of Chinese social theories;
Apply Chinese social theories and concepts and relate them to particular problems, issues and phenomena in Chinese society;
Use social theories to explain social phenomena in contemporary China;
Achieve a better understanding of modern and contemporary Chinese society.

modules.moduleContent: The module will be taught by experienced lecturer with an emphasis on structure and principles to provide a solid foundation of core grammar. Exchange ideas with others on common topics;
Comprehend simple and familiar language material that is closely related to everyday life;
Converse about daily life in Chinese and read simple Chinese texts.

modules.moduleContent: The module will be taught by an experienced lecturer with an emphasis on structure and principles to provide a solid foundation of core grammar. modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO USER EXPERIENCE (UX), USER INTERFACE (UI) AND INTERACTION DESIGN
modules.moduleContent: This course presents an introductory overview of the theory and practice of User Experience (UX), User Interface (UI) and Interaction Design. The module considers design, development and evaluation of interactive sites and apps as highly iterative and connected processes. It takes a user-centred perspective, rather than a system-centred one. Central themes include: (i) understanding human interaction, (ii) the relationships between UX and UI design, (iii) the importance of user involvement and testing of designs, and (iv) iterative development of low and high fidelity interactive prototypes suitable for testing. The course examines bridging the gap between the needs and requirements of human users and the capabilities and limitations of technology.
learningOutcomes: Describe key theories, principles and psychology underpinning User Experience (UX) and User Interaction (UI)
Describe the differences between User Experience (UX) Design, User Interface (UI) Design and Interaction Design
Describe how user research methods provide a deep understanding of users, and assist in the creation of user-centred products and services
Develop UX for an app and website that create positive user experiences
Develop UI components, implement clickable prototypes and test designs with users
Develop and implement a User Testing plan for developed prototypes

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this course is to introduce the principles of computer networking using a top down approach, that is, by beginning at the application layer and working down toward the physical layer. This top-down approach has many advantages pedagogically. Teaching networking applications near the beginning of the course is a powerful motivational tool. In the first few lectures students learn about how networking applications work—applications such as e-mail and the Web, which most students use on a daily basis. Once a student understands the applications, the student can then understand the network services needed to support these applications. The student can then, in turn, examine the various ways in which such services might be provided and implemented in the lower layers. The transport, network, link and physical layers are covered in similar detail; in each case the service models that are required from the layer above is used for motivation to understand the principles and protocols of the latter below. The Internet’s architecture and protocols as used as primary vehicles for studying the fundamental computer networking concepts throughout the course from the top down, while concepts and protocols from other network architectures are also included. Much of the course is based on lab practicals using Wireshark, which help to reinforce the various aspects of the course as well as provide a form of continuous assessment.
1. Computer Networks & the Internet, packet switching, delay, loss, throughput (3 lectures) 
2. Application Layer: Web, eMail, SMTP, DNS, P2P, CDNs, Socket Programming (5 lectures) 
3. Transport Layer: Multiplexing, UDP, TCP, Congestion Control (4 lectures) 
4. Network Layer: Virtual circuits, IP and Routing Algorithms, Routers, ICMP (5 lectures) 
5. Link Layer: Error detection/correction, ALOHA, CSMA, Sliding window protocols, Multiple Access, Addressing, Switching (5 lectures) 
6. Internet security/Wireless Networks and Security, Wifi 802.11, mobility management, mobile IP (2 lectures)

Erasmus/Study abroad students
In order to take this module, you should have previously successfully completed an Introduction to Computer Science course in your home university. Please contact the department with any queries.
learningOutcomes: Understand the functional layering of computer network architectures.
Explain the concept of packet-switching, and identify and analyze the different types of packet delay in packet-switched networks.
Understand major internet applications such as the Web and email and the service model that the application layer requires.
Describe the essential principles of a transport layer protocol (reliable data transfer, flow control, congestion control)
Write simple socket based applications using Python
Use IP addressing and apply routing algorithms to find shortest paths for network-layer packet delivery
Describe and compare data link layer services and multiple access techniques and describe network security issues and some of the methods that address them

Motivation for software verification and formal methods; overview of syntax and semantics and formal proofs in first-order logic; Design by Contract; Hoare logic and program verification; SMT solvers; temporal logic and model checking; brief case studies using selected formal methods.

Erasmus/Study abroad students

Create mathematically precise specifications and designs using logic-based specification languages

Analyse the properties of formal specifications and designs

modules.moduleContent: This module teaches students skills to design virtual (XR) worlds. It covers the meaning and history of XR, XR technology (e.g., head-mounted displays and haptics) and teaches concepts in content creation and interaction design (for XR and 3D interfaces). Students will learn how to author content (e.g. with Blender) and design larger interactive spaces with gaming engines (e.g., with Godot or Unreal) by developing an immersive XR application, game, or experience. Learning will be guided by the practical project work in the labs and the theory and examples in the lectures.
learningOutcomes: Compare and discuss XR technology in its historical context;
Relate user experience (UX) and usability guidelines to practical XR requirements;
Discuss and apply methods and theories underpinning XR;
Apply current tools/APIs/gaming engines for XR;
Design and implement an interactive and immersive XR application, game, or experience.

modules.moduleContent: This course explores the ideas and practices associated with the concept of risk, from the point of view of the Social Sciences. Special relevance will be given to the social processes of risk-identification, categorisation and asessment as well as to the impact such processes may or may not have on behaviour. Through examination of case studies and narratives the course will examine the lived experience of individuals as they locate and navigate risk in their lives.
learningOutcomes: have an insight into the various theories of risk which currently exist and are drawn on by individuals, communities and society;
have an understanding of how risk operates in society;
and be able to identify and engage with this in a real and concrete way.

modules.moduleContent: The aim of this module is to examine the concepts of ‘Youth’ & ‘Community’ from a critical sociological perspective. ‘Youth’ and ‘Community’ are important concepts not only as terms which denote the social categories or groups with whom practitioners are engaged and whose interests they are concerned to promote, but also as clusters of ideas and assumptions - often unspoken - which shape and inform youth and community work practices, and understandings of the social world (Devlin, 2008). The aim of this module is to attempt to make these (often conflicting) ideas and assumptions explicit; to provide an introduction to the key concepts, theories and key debates in the study of youth and of community; to explore the concepts of youth and community (and other, related concepts) from a variety of theoretical perspectives; to examine empirical studies of communities and of young people in Ireland and elsewhere; and to explore the political and ideological dimensions of these key concepts and their relationship with class, gender, ethnicity, culture and identity, and other areas of contemporary debate.
learningOutcomes: have a knowledge and critical understanding of the historical emergence and evolution of ideas of 'community' and 'youth';
understand the main theoretical perspectives on 'community' and 'youth', and their implications for practice;
understand the 'politics of community' and 'youth' and of conflicting definitions ad interpretations.
have a knowledge and critical understanding of empirical findings of sociological studies of local communities and young people in Ireland and elsewhere.

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered may include but are not limited to: Symmetric cryptography implementation and cryptanalysis. Hash Functions and authentication. Public key cryptography and cryptanalysis. Emerging concepts. Difference between secure systems and textbook implementations. Elliptic curve cryptography. NTRU systems. Mathematical and non-mathematical attacks.
learningOutcomes: Introduction to the concepts, implementation, and deployment of practical cryptography.

modules.moduleContent:

CV creation; use of social media and professional networks; job applications; interview preparation.

modules.moduleContent:
Reinforcement learning (RL) is a powerful paradigm for creating autonomous systems that can learn to make good decisions.  RL is relevant to an enormous range of tasks (e.g. robotics, gaming).
This module will provide a solid introduction to the field of reinforcement learning. In this module, students will become knowledgeable in key ideas and techniques for RL as well as deep reinforcement learning that combines deep learning techniques with reinforcement learning.

Indicative Syllabus
• Introduction to reinforcement learning (RL) 
• Markov decision processes (MDPs), Monte Carlo Methods, Dynamic  Programming 
• RL policy, Q-learning, 
• Reinforcement learning in continuous state-action spaces. Function approximation problem. 
• Introduction to deep reinforcement learning.
learningOutcomes: Define the key features of reinforcement learning
Given an application problem (e.g. from computer vision, robotics, etc), decide if it should be formulated as a RL problem
Given an application problem, be able to define it formally with RL (e.g. in terms of the state space, action space, reward model, etc), and state what algorithm is best suited for addressing it
Describe criteria for analyzing RL algorithms and evaluate algorithms on these metrics: e.g. computational complexity

modules.moduleContent: Covering the development of software – with emphasis on rigour and formality. Concepts covered include the foundations for reasoning about software correctness with emphasis on the underlying logics used, state of the art tools, research developments and usage in industry. Topics include what to specify (Design by Contract); Logics such as Hoare Logic, Temporal Logic or Separation Logic; Specification Languages such as JML, Eiffel, Dafny or Event-B; as well as verification techniques and tools used for reasoning about program correctness including deductive verification, model checking, refinement and use of SMT solvers. The impact of AI on verification tools and the role of verification in AI will also be discussed.
learningOutcomes: Understand the reason about why software projects fail
Identify the role of rigor in the software process in improving the chances of success
Create mathematically precise specifications and designs using formal specification languages
Analyse the properties of formal specifications and designs, performing proofs of correctness
Analyse the correctness of programs (eg. static analysis, simulation, model checking)
Describe how tools that assist in this analysis are designed and implemented

describe and compare a number of standard testing techniques for unit and system testing;
select test techniques and design test cases;
design and implement automated tests; 5. evaluate test coverage;
describe and evaluate the relevance of testing in the software development process.

Internet solutions engineering using best-practice design patterns are examined and used to deploy a large-scale multitiered assignment.
