modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective
The purpose of this module is to allow postgraduate research students to develop and apply the skills required for publishing their research. This module covers the submission of a manuscript to a peer-reviewed journal.

Module Content
• Attend lectures/seminars/conferences in the area of research  that is intended for publication
• Undertake an informed literature survey around the research that is intended for publication
• Draft hypothesis and framework for manuscript
• Write up the manuscript to include abstract, introduction and results and discussion content
• Submission of manuscript to a peer-reviewed journal.
learningOutcomes: Conduct a short but thorough investigation into a relevant research topic.
Work largely independently, within agreed project requirements, with minimal supervision.
Complete a short project report covering all central aspects of the project.
Work within all project deadlines, consultation requirements and give regular feedback to your supervisor(s).
Work effectively on a challenging topic.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective
This module will consist of seminars, presentations, workshops and/or master class sessions tailored for PhD students. The Summer/Winter School may be comprised of thematically specialized and/or inter-disciplinary sessions.

Module Content
To be defined by summer/winter school organisers
learningOutcomes: Describe the field of inquiry represented in the summer/winter school
Identify major theories and paradigms employed and define key concepts and ideas
Apply theories and concepts and relate them to particular problems, issues and phenomena addressed in the student's own research
Analyse aspects of the student's own field of inquiry and illustrate them in terms of theories and concepts presented in the summer/winter school
Explain phenomena and formulate particular research problems in terms of general theories presented in summer/winter school
Criticise topics and evaluate issues and debates in terms of paradigms and theories presented in the summer/winter school.

modules.moduleContent:
Sociology and the social sciences; Key concepts, theories and perspectives in sociology; Sociology in everyday life; in youth and community work.
Public administration in Ireland; the respective roles of the legislature, the executive, both central and devolved, and the judiciary in policy making, and the inter-relationships between them; theories of public policy making; case studies in policy development.
Definitions of equality and inequality; theoretical perspectives on inequality; defining and identifying inequality; equality and inequality in our own lives and work; practical and policy responses to inequality

Total of 520 hours of professional skills development and practice; supervision and reflective practice. Full-time students complete a full-time block fieldwork placement and part-time in-service students undertake a work-based practice development project or an external full-time block fieldwork placement. Students must complete a placement in either community work OR youth work so that by the end of Year 3 they will have completed 2 placements in both youth work and community work. All coursework and assignments must be completed before commencement of fieldwork placement.
The module builds on the foundations of CY102/CY202 in Years 1 and and provides for the enhancement and completion of professional formation processes
learningOutcomes: demonstrate professional competence in relation to the core areas of practitioner ability that underpin the programme and fieldwork practice overall
demonstrate reflective practice skills in writing and through oral presentations
identify responses (in consultation with staff) to their own ongoing and emerging needs for further professional development as learner-practitioners
engage with the critical issues in theory and practice relation to two of the following three optional areas of study: Youth Justice, Mediation and Conflict studies and Development Studies
articulate personal and professional values into practice in professional relationships including a consideration of the boundaries of ethical practice in relation to their role as practitioner

modules.moduleName: THE SOCIAL SCIENCE CONTEXT OF PRACTICE
modules.moduleContent: The module builds on the foundations of social science modules in Years 1 and 2. It aims to consolidate students’ understanding of key concepts and insights developed in the social sciences and of the interaction between community work, youth work and the social context within which they take place.
learningOutcomes: critically discuss the relevance of social scientific theories and concepts to community work and youth work
Identify, compare and contrast the major theoretical perspectives on community and youth in society
demonstrate an appreciation of the value and the relevance for community and youth work of social scientific insights and perspectives
critically outline the development of policy and services in selected social   policy areas relevant to community work and youth work contexts
describe and assess the key empirical findings from selected community and youth research in Ireland and elsewhere
articulate key sociological theories and concepts relevant and up-to-date empirical data, particularly in the Irish context
analyse key points of convergence and divergence and key debates in the social professions

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: At the end of this module students will be able to analyse and evaluate contemporary leadership theories and debate their relevance to military operations. The will be able to demonstrate an understanding of the role of law and of the media in military operations. In addition, students will be able to evaluate the relevance of learning, knowledge and change management theories to the Irish Defence Forces. 


Module Content: This module examines various theories relating to command, leadership and organizational studies and engages with concepts of learning, knowledge and change management. The module also includes an analysis of the challenges facing contemporary commanders, including legal, social, technological and ethical factors that may inhibit or support military operations.
This module aims to enhance scientific communication, comprehension and presentation skills through attendance at and participation in research seminars.
learningOutcomes: Comprehend and appraise the latest scientific research developments in the field of environmental science and it's application to various industries and organisations.
Communicate and present various research topics and relevant research findings in a clear and articulate manner.
Apply the specific knowledge, experimental designs and data analytics acquired from the applied seminar series to their own research study.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module will enable learners to enhance their critical appraisal skills and to develop an in-depth knowledge of research methodologies that underpin evidence-based practice. This will develop competencies in relation to the enactment of evidence based practice and its critical application its application in the context of their own research.

1. Research methodologies
1.1) Research methodologies. Traditions of inquiry. Exploring the processes of qualitative and quantitative research.
1.2) Philosophical underpinnings of research methodologies.
1.3) Developing research designs and shaping a study inquiry. Conceptual analysis and research.
1.4) Clarifying conceptual frameworks within the context of research.

2. Evidence-based practice
2.1) Underpinning philosophy, principles and practice, place in critical professional practice, influences of organisation's cultures and contexts, social and political issues associated with warranting knowledge, enacting evidence based practice, ethical imperatives.

3. Ethics and research
3.1) Ethical principles of research. Informed consent and the vulnerable person.
3.2) Advocacy and consent within the context of research in clinical practice. 
3.3) The role of the researcher in practice. Insider research and its debates.

4. Literature search, retrieval and Critique
4.1) Systematic Database Searching. Primary and secondary sources of literature retrieval. Constructing Search Tables and Systematic Searching Prisma Flow Charts and equivalent.
4.2) Analysing and synthesising data collection methods. Choosing appropriate research methods for research purposes.
4.3) Developing and analysing research questions and research hypotheses. Critically analysing and evaluating research, critically reading. Evaluating literature

5. Sampling and data collection
5.1) Analysing and synthesising sampling methods in research. Choosing sampling methods. Strategies for identifying sampling frames/populations. Statistics and quantitative research.
5.2) Ethics and analysis. Graphs using summary statistics. Critical interpretation of statistics. Data protection in health and research.
5.3) Analysing and synthesising research findings. Strategies for questioning and reporting. Presenting findings. 
5.4) Verifying results; implementing findings in practice; dissemination of findings: preparing a poster, conference paper and journal article.

6. Quality Improvement
6.1) Theoretical perspectives and processes, Audit purposes, approaches and uses, Practice governance, Clinical effectiveness, Application of EBP (the best knowledge, clinical experience and service users preferences) to achieve optimum outcomes.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a comprehensive and critical understanding of the philosophy, principles and practice underpinning evidence based practice and discuss how these relate to their postgraduate research project.
Construct and apply effective literature search strategies.
Critically discuss research ethics within vulnerable populations and discern the ethical issues which pervade methodology.
Analyse ethical issues within the context of their postgraduate research project.
Critically apply a deep understanding of contemporary debates surrounding the research literature and evidenced based practices and their application to their postgraduate research project.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: LEARNING AND TEACHING: LEARNER CENTRED APPROACHES
modules.moduleContent:
This module provides a thorough introduction to theories, principles and research in the field of learning and teaching and encourages critical reflection through analysis of evidence from a range of relevant sources, including peers and students. Linking theory and practice, it supports the development of research-informed approaches to learning and teaching. It will empower learners to apply these principles in the context of their own research and support the development of reflective practice.

Indicative content

1. Action Research and Reflective Practice
1.1) Reflection-in and reflection-on practice: the work of Donal Schon. Action Research and the work of John Elliott. Collecting and analysing evidence within ethical boundaries: methods used to evaluate teaching.

2. Theories of Learning
2.1) Bloom, Gardner, Biggs, Mezirow, Wenger and Kolb. Constructivist principles and student-centred learning. Teaching for Understanding and Understanding Teaching.

3. Models and styles of teaching
3.1) Student-centred versus teacher-centred styles: the “PAR” model.; didactic versus interactive models and respective/ compared effects on student learning. Collaborative/peer learning and group work.

4. Communication in the Classroom
4.1) Effective questioning and explaining; set induction and closure; stimulus variation, pacing and effective speech usage; classroom environments and effective teaching.

5. Student-centred Learning Strategies
5.1) Interactive, flexible and active learning; collaborative/co-operative learning.

6. Large and small group teaching
6.1) Planning and management; the roles of students vis à vis participation, collaboration and engagement. From lecturing to presenting and strategies to promote interactive teaching/collaborative learning. Group work for active learning.

7. Inclusion and diversity
7.1) Inclusive teaching and inclusive assessment; Universal Design for Learning.

8. Assessment and Learning
8.1) Inclusive assessment; transparency, manageability and choice; forms and methods of assessment; classroom assessment techniques/strategies (CATs: Angelo and Cross). Giving feedback.

9. Evaluating teaching
9.1) Self-evaluation, peer evaluation, handling and responding to feedback.

10. Designing curricula
10.1) Constructive alignment; Bloom’s Taxonomy and outcomes writing; the “SMART” principles of planning; relevance and manageability.

11. Scholarship of Learning and Teaching
11.1) The Scholarship of Teaching and Learning (SoTL) promotes teaching as scholarly endeavour and as a focus of ongoing research. It aims to improve teaching through the building of a community of scholarly teachers who share practice thereby adding to the body of knowledge.
learningOutcomes: Critique contemporary theories of learning, teaching and assessment and discuss their application within the context of their own research.
Apply evidence to critique own practice in the context of their postgraduate research programme.
Within the context of their own research, design learning that is inclusive and exemplifies best practice.
Critically reflect on and in practice.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO MACROECONOMICS AND TO QUANTITATIVE METHODS
modules.moduleContent: The macroeconomics component of the module addresses economic issues at an aggregate level. Emphasis is placed on the measurement of living standards, economic growth, inflation, unemployment, economic inequality, savings and investment, the monetary system and monetary and fiscal policy. The quantitative component aims to develop mastery of basic quantitative techniques and how to apply these techniques to problems of an economic nature. Topic covered include linear and non-linear functions, simultaneous equations, calculus and optimisation.
learningOutcomes: Understand basic concepts relating to the economy at the aggregate level, such as GDP, unemployment, and inflation.
Know how the basic concepts are measured.
Understand basic theories to analyse central topics of Macroeconomics such as economic growth, economic fluctuations, unemployment, and inflation.
Interpret simple graphs and tables depicting the evolution and relationship between basic Macroeconomic variables.
Apply relevant mathematical techniques to economic issues.
Demonstrate how to solve for the optimal/equilibrium values of relevant economic variables.
Use mathematical techniques to explain the intuition behind various economic concepts.

modules.moduleContent:

modules.moduleContent: This is a project module where students will develop analytical and empirical skills as well as written and oral communication skills. This module allows students to apply their understanding of economic principles, economic theory, quantitative methods and data analysis to real-life economic issues and policies. Areas covered might look at the effects of rent control on housing,  the effects of minimum wages on employment, income inequality, economic perspectives on regulation of illicit drugs and crime. Students will make use of economic arguments and increase their familiarity with relevant software.
learningOutcomes: utilise their understanding of economic principles and economic theory to explain real-life economic issues;
clearly rationalise and assess appropriate economic policies for specific issues;
identify the optimal economic policy from a menu of policy options;
exhibit an understanding of economic reports from various media platforms;
critically analyse the economic arguments of others;
utilise appropriate quantitative methods to explain real world economic issues and develop and support any economic argument;
identify reputable sources of economic data;

modules.moduleName: INTERMEDIATE QUANTITATIVE ANALYSIS FOR ECONOMICS AND BUSINESS
modules.moduleContent: The aim of this course is to provide students with the mathematical tools used in economics and business. Economists often rely on mathematics as the chosen language to describe and analyse economic behaviour. This course will discuss key mathematical concepts and explain how each tool is used in analysing economic behaviour. Topics covered include functions, financial mathematics, differentiation and linear algebra.
learningOutcomes: understand the mathematics needed in their study of economics.
translate between English, mathematical and graphical discussion of economic problems

modules.moduleContent: The module will be taught by an experienced lecturer with an emphasis on structure and principles to provide a solid foundation of core grammar. Practical reading, writing, speaking and listening skills will be taught through tutorials and language laboratory classes with experienced tutors (native speaker). In-class activities will include repetitions, dictations, re-creations of dialogues, group conversations and role plays. By the end of the module students will have acquired 750 Chinese characters (accumulatively).
learningOutcomes: Comprehend general language materials encountered on certain occasions;
Understand formal or informal conversation or speech;
Translate familiar written materials into English and Chinese;
Give a brief description or explanation on familiar topics;
Write short essays to express one's point of view.

modules.moduleName: CONFLICT AND COMMUNICATION, A CHANGING CHINA IN A GLOBALIZING WORLD II
modules.moduleContent: Focusing on the themes of conflict, communication and globalisation, this module provides interdisciplinary studies of modern and contemporary China. It offers theoretical and methodological considerations on dealing with conflict and communication between China and foreign countries, e.g. The Opium Wars, the Sino-Japanese Wars, the Cold War and Sino-American relations. This module also explores the conflict and communication between social classes and ethnic groups within China, such as the Anti-Manchu movement and the Nationalist Revolution, the Chinese Civil War, the Cultural Revolution, the Free Tibet Movement, ethnic conflicts in Xinjiang and Hong Kong's Umbrella Movement. Students will be required to reflect upon social, philosophical and ideological thoughts of socialism, capitalism, communism, nationalism, neo-liberalism, Confucianism all of which have influenced the development of China.
learningOutcomes: Translate Chinese into English, with a high degree of accuracy, authentic Chinese texts such as newspapers
Translate English into Chinese, with a high degree of accuracy, authentic Chinese texts such as newspapers
Further understand Chinese language and culture through documentaries, movies, TV dramas and other digital sources
Discuss current affairs in Chinese in the context of historical, cultural, societal and economic developments
Achieve a better understanding of contemporary China thought newspapers and magazines

Theoretical Computer Science; Languages: regular expressions, context-free grammars, recursive definitions; Automata: finite automata (deterministic and non-deterministic), conversion from grammars to automata, transition graphs, push-down automata; problem-solving: understanding and developing algorithms; Implementing algorithms to apply language definitions and to simulate automaton: iteration, nested iteration, arrays, multidimensional arrays, scope of variables, static methods, defining classes, creating objects, defining and calling instance methods and constructors, parament passing recursion, simple searching and sorting algorithms..

Erasmus/Study abroad students:
Please note that CS161 and CS162 are designed to be taken together.

understand and describe the concept of finite state machines and regular languages;

modules.moduleContent:
Data Representation: Number systems, integer and floating point formats. 
Functional Logic Design: Boolean algebra, transistor operation fundamentals and implementing boolean logic operations from transistor combinations. Building functional circuits for arithmetic, logic and interfacing operations. Design optimisation and performance issues, boolean theorems, duality, gate sets and alternative implementations, circuit simplification process. 
Sequential Logic Design: Flip-flop design and operation, synthesis procedure for sequential circuits from word specifications and state diagrams. Circuits for sequenced arithmetic operations and pattern recognition.
Memory Technology: Evolution of memory technology, operation of SRAM, DRAM and non-volatile technologies based on floating gate transistors. Implementing reliable memory systems, error detection and correction systems theory and implementation.
The lab work involves construction of simulated circuits for computing.
learningOutcomes: Students can expect to obtain a basic understanding of how numeric data is represented in different formats and manipulated in digital circuits and how these circuits interact to perform processing.

Boolean Algebra is used to express, manipulate and optimise circuit design and students gain an understanding of this process through the construction and simulation of elementary circuits capable of aithmetic function operations, data storage, algorithmic sequencing, counting, selecting and interfinterfacing, culminating in a “mini programmable CPU”.
Students will also understand the operation of basic memory technologies and how to deal with error detection and correction in memory systems.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This blended-learning course provides an introduction to client-server information processing followed by an in-depth overview of the components and architecture of HTTP-based web applications. The course also provides a comparative analysis of alternative approaches to web application development using different architectures (LAMP, MEAN), frameworks, programming languages and databases. REST Web services, together with associated development technologies are explained using practical and real-world examples. All the concepts described focus on progressive enhancements (separation of structure, presentation and behaviour) and best practices in interactive, database-driven, web application development. The course also provides an overview of MVC, MVP and MVVM design practices in relation to web development.
learningOutcomes: Describe web technologies, protocols and architectures.
Describe the difference between LAMP-like and MEAN-like architectures.
Design and build a dynamic, database-driven, interactive browser-based web-based applications.
Describe and use various approaches for state data management for web applications.
Understanding the difference between MVC, MVP and MVVM design practices in relation to web development.

modules.moduleContent: This module will focus on multimedia technologies for the generation, analysis, and production of multimedia content. The module will first introduce multimedia and its convergence within the media of television, the internet and mobile communications. The module will provide a thorough study of software processes for the generation of multimedia content. Topics will include: introduction to multimedia and multimedia systems and standards; multimedia representation and processing; multimedia storage and compression; software, user interfaces and authoring tools.
learningOutcomes: Understand the basic theory of multimedia technology and multimedia applications.

modules.moduleContent: In this module students will be introduced to principles and practices of object oriented software analysis, design, and programming using modern object oriented programming languages, e.g. C#, Java or C++. The course will be delivered in two parts. The first part will focus on software development; taking students from the basics of the chosen object-oriented language, through to objected oriented and generic programming. The course assumes that students already have an introductory level of programming competency and be familiar with the basics of object oriented programming. The second part will cover the general principles object oriented analysis and design (OOA/D) and in particular the application of design patterns in developing well-structured, extensible, and reusable software systems. This module may be taught using a traditional or blended-learning approach.
learningOutcomes: Analyse, design, and implement software based solutions to problems using the specified object oriented programming language.
Develop generic implementations of algorithms using the specified object oriented programming language.
Compare and contrast different software designs based on principled quality criteria.
Apply OOA/D techniques to the development of software solutions to real-world problems.

Understand a selection of design patterns and apply them to their software solutions.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides an introduction to hybrid mobile development with Javascript libraries or frameworks which are used for building user interfaces for mobile applications. The course will focus on handling the view layer for platform independent web and mobile applications. Students will be introduced to the capabilities and limitations of modern mobile platforms and the challenges which are posed by this development environment. In particular, the module will deal with providing an effective user interface for mobile devices. Solutions to managing application data within the hybrid mobile application will be discussed using Object Oriented Programming (OOP) approaches. Through appropriate coursework and a team project students will gain experience in the design and development of mobile application using the React Javascript library. Students will learn how the mobile applications they frequently use work and how these applications are developed. A discussion of the enterprise scale requirements (performance, scalability, availability, and security) of mobile applications will also be discussed during the course. The course requires no previous experience of Javascript or application development. However, some programming knowledge in another high-level programming language is assumed.
learningOutcomes: Describe the capabilities and limitations of mobile platforms that affect hybrid application development and deployment.

modules.moduleContent: Covering the development of software – with emphasis on rigour and formality. Concepts covered include the foundations for reasoning about software correctness with emphasis on the underlying logics used, state of the art tools, research developments and usage in industry. Topics include what to specify (Design by Contract); Logics such as Hoare Logic, Temporal Logic or Separation Logic; Specification Languages such as JML, Eiffel, Dafny or Event-B; as well as verification techniques and tools used for reasoning about program correctness including deductive verification, model checking, refinement and use of SMT solvers. The impact of AI on verification tools and the role of verification in AI will also be discussed.

Design an interface that adheres to good interaction design principles.

modules.moduleContent: A combination of advanced object-oriented programming techniques and concepts in object-oriented languages are discussed. Topics covered include Classes, Objects, Encapsulation, Information Hiding, Inheritance, Composition, Object Lifetimes, Files, Input/Output Streams, Operator Overriding, Operator Overloading, Polymorphism, Dynamic Binding, Templates, Exceptions and the Standard Template Library.
learningOutcomes: Construct object oriented programming solutions for reuse, using abstract data types that incorporate encapsulation, data abstraction, information hiding, and the separation of behavior and implementation.

Construct multiple-file or multiple-module programming solutions that use classes, subclasses, class hierarchies, inheritance, parameterised classes, generics and polymorphism to reuse existing design and code.
Compare and contrast a range of object-oriented programming languages.
Create programming solutions that use data structures and existing libraries.
Perform object-oriented design and programming with a high level of proficiency in more than one object-oriented programming language.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Depends entirely on the individual project, which may be proposed by either the student or a member of staff and be approved by the course co-ordinator.  The dissertation submitted may be either research or industrially based. It must be directly pertaining to the MSc specialisation that the student is undertaking.

The student must have successfully completed their taught modules before submitting the Dissertation for CS640.
learningOutcomes: Conduct a reasonably thorough investigation into a topic of interest, importance or relevance to software engineering or the application of software engineering principles to some aspect of your work placement.
Work largely independently, within agreed project requirements, with minimal supervision.
Complete a detailed project report covering all central aspects of the project and make a presentation based on the project.
Work within all project deadlines, consultation requirements and give regular feedback to your supervisor(s).
Work effectively on a challenging topic.
Research alternative solutions.
Develop and evaluate a solution, and to evaluate the process that lead to the solution.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Depends entirely on the individual project, which may be proposed by either the student or a member of staff.
learningOutcomes: Conduct a reasonably thorough investigation into a topic of interest, importance or relevance to computer science or its applications
Where appropriate, accomplish an acceptable implementation (or other useful model) to fulfil the main goals of the project
Work largely independently, within agreed project requirements, with minimal supervision
Deliver technical presentations during the project development cycle

modules.moduleContent: This course presents basic components of human-computer interaction concepts, theory, and practice. It takes a user-centred perspective, rather than a system-centred one. A central theme is on design and evaluation as highly iterative and connected processes. The course looks at bridging the gap between the needs and requirements of human users and capabilities and limitations of technology.
learningOutcomes: describe the main features of interaction design as applied to computer systems,
analyse trends in computer hardware and software that have implications for future modes of interaction,
construct low-fidelity interface prototypes,
implement a simple user interface on a mobile computing device,
recognise strengths and weaknesses in particular interaction designs.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered may include but are not limited to: Symmetric cryptography implementation and cryptanalysis. Hash Functions and authentication. Public key cryptography and cryptanalysis. Emerging concepts. Difference between secure systems and textbook implementations. Elliptic curve cryptography. NTRU systems. Mathematical and non-mathematical attacks.
learningOutcomes: identify the concepts, implementation, and deployment of practical cryptography.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective
The purpose of this module is to allow postgraduate research students to develop and apply the skills required for presenting their research.   This module covers the presentations of results in poster or oral format at a conference as agreed with the departmental supervisory committee.

On completion of the module the student will be able to:
• Discuss the current state-of-the-art in their research
• Interpret results in the context of the impact on the particular field
• Evaluate results for potential presentation
• Formulate a hypothesis for the presentation 
• Concisely present and critically discuss research highlights that can prove/disprove the hypotheses
• Draw conclusions on the work and articulate the significance of the research in the wider context
• Identify a conference.

Module Content
• Attend lectures/seminars/conferences in the area of research 
• Undertake an informed literature survey around the research that is intended for presentation 
• Draft hypothesis and framework for oral presentation 
• Write up the presentation to include abstract, introduction and results and discussion content
• Delivery of an oral presentation at an international conference.
learningOutcomes: describe their work through a formal presentation and a poster presentation;
be able to ask valuable questions;
have experienced the culture of another country.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
Firstly, to ensure that the student broadens their knowledge about different research areas of Computer Science. Secondly, to give the student the opportunity to prepare and present their own seminar. 

Module Content: 
The student must attend the annual Computer Science Postgraduate Workshop. Additionally, they must prepare and deliver a presentation on the student’s own PhD research as part of the workshop. This must be significantly different to the talk given for the assessment of CS860. In particular the student should present their work in a form that is suitable for a broader computer science audience (i.e. not experts in their specific field).
learningOutcomes: prepare and deliver a presentation on the student’s own PhD research and present their work in a form that is suitable for a broader computer science audience (i.e. not experts in their specific field).
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Total of 520 hours of professional skills development and practice; supervision and reflective practice. Full-time students complete a full-time block fieldwork placement and part-time in-service students undertake a work-based practice development project or an external full-time block fieldwork placement. Students must complete a placement in either community work OR youth work so that by the end of Year 2 they will have completed 2 placements in either youth work or community work.  All coursework and assignments must be completed before commencement of fieldwork placement.

Management theories, concepts and principles; Organisational culture, development; Management roles, responsibilities, skills; Planning and evaluation; Legal and financial issues in management.
Perspectives on gender, class, racism & ethnicity, age, disability, sexuality and related issues; policy implications and developments relating to inequality.
Theory and method in social research; Research designs; qualitative and quantitative research methods; Participative research; action research; Research as reflexive practice; values and ethics in research

Sociological perspectives on ''youth'' and ''community''; Key debates in community/youth studies; Young people in contemporary society; Changing and emerging communities.
Sociology of education; Social stratification and social class; Crime and deviance; Media and popular cultgure; Contemporary social change.
Introduction to basic concepts and principles: - welfare, equality, redistribution, citizenship, and welfare state, selectivity & universality, citizenship and social policy; Origins of social policy and the welfare state - historical and sociological approaches; Key policy issues today: social exclusion, ageing, migration; disability; Application to Ireland.
Psychology: its nature and concerns, major theoretical approaches; Perspectives on development; Social Psychology, interpersonal communication; prosocial behaviour; prejudice and oppression.

The module builds on the foundations of CY111 and CY211 in Years 1 and 2.  The emphasis throughout is on the central importance of the skills of enquiry, research and analysis to the work of the professional youth and community work practitioner, whatever the context.
learningOutcomes: identify and critically discuss the key philosophical and methodological   concepts in social scientific research.
reflect on and speak/write confidently (and on the basis of appropriate evidence) about the relationship between theory and method in social research;
examine the role of practitioner research and associated methodologies  in generating knowledge of practice

make informed judgements as to the appropriateness and adequacy of given research questions or ‘research problems’ and as to the suitability of particular research methods for addressing such questions/problems;
articulate the ethical dimensions  of social research practice with particular reference to community work and youth work settings
apply all of  the above insights in the context of the social professions and specifically with regard to the practice of youth work/community work
demonstrate key skills in practice research

modules.moduleContent: The module builds on the foundations of community work and youth work in Years 1 and 2.It prepares students to engage with challenging  emerging issues in professional practice
learningOutcomes: evaluate the main features of contemporary community work and youth work policy and practice  in Ireland
critically engage with debates on community work and youth work professional identify and future direction in the context of emerging policy
critically evaluate current debates in relation to frameworks for quality, accountability, outcomes, impact and other emerging mechanisms
articulate key aspects of a range of theoretical models of community work and youth work including a consideration of their implications for practice

demonstrate a critical understanding of the key theories, principles, processes and methods in organisation and management, and of how these apply in practice settings emphasing leadership and strategic capacities
examine the equality, human rights and social justice focus of community work and youth work practice in a changing political and policy context
articulate community work and youth work values into practice in professional relationships including a consideration of the boundaries of ethical practice in relation to their role as practitioner

modules.moduleName: COMMUNITY WORK & YOUTH WORK PRACTICE: LOCAL ISSUES, GLOBAL LENS
modules.moduleContent:
Community work and youth work practitioners seek to work with communities and young people to identify, analyse and address the issues and inequalities that are facing them. This module will provide an opportunity for students of the Certificate in Global Citizenship Education develop an understanding of how community workers and youth workers do this. It will support students to develop and apply a global lens on the issues that emerge for communities and young people. 

The module will provide students with an overview of key areas of debate at local and global levels and their implications for the future, seeking to link community and youth work practice in the global north with issues and outcomes in the global south. 

It will also explore aspects of development and development education appropriate to youth work and community work contexts and consider how concepts and practices relating to Global Citizenship Education can contribute to identifying, analysing and addressing the issues and inequalities that emerge at local level.

On successful completion of the module, students should be able to: 

Demonstrate familiarity with some major theoretical, policy and practice issues in contemporary development discourses 

Critically analyse a variety of dimensions of development including gender, health, ethnicity, geographic, economic etc 

Explain some connections between poverty, migration, globalisation, human rights, development, and community work and youth work

Situate development work and community work as related and complementary practices, including consideration of key organisations, statutory and voluntary, operating nationally and internationally 

Critically evaluate the contribution of global youth work to an appreciation of global dimensions of development 

Demonstrate an appreciation of the principles, values and ethical basis of Global Citizenship Education practice 

Demonstrate an understanding of how community and youth workers seek to integrate the SDG’s and other global commitments into their analysis, practice, and policy development processes
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate familiarity with some major theoretical, policy and practice issues in contemporary development discourses
Critically analyse a variety of dimensions of development including gender, health, ethnicity, geographic, economic etc
Explain some connections between poverty, migration, globalisation, human rights, development, and community work and youth work
Situate development work and community work as related and complementary practices, including consideration of key organisations, statutory and voluntary, operating nationally and internationally
Critically evaluate the contribution of global youth work to an appreciation of global dimensions of development
Demonstrate an appreciation of the principles, values and ethical basis of Global Citizenship Education practice
Demonstrate an understanding of how community and youth workers seek to integrate the SDG’s and other global commitments into their analysis, practice, and policy development processes

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 

On successful completion of this module students will have examined different theoretical approaches to military decision making, they will be able to apply such processes to practical examples relating to the land component and they will understand the application of decision making processes in the planning of military operations. 

Module Content: 

This module introduces the different methodologies associated with military decision making. It will place a particular emphasis on the theories and methods that underpin the Military Decision Making Process as currently applied by the Irish Defence Forces and students will be required to demonstrate analysis and critical thinking in the application of this process. Students will receive lectures on decision making theory and practice and will test their knowledge and understanding in a series of case study exercises, tutorials and classroom discussions.

Module Objective: 

On completion of this module students should have an understanding of the different elements that comprise operations by the land component and be able to evaluate the contribution that can be made by different arms. They should be able to discuss the conceptual framework for military operations, and be able to evaluate the concept of principles of war. They should also be able to articulate the contribution that can be made by joint forces.

Module Content: 

The module will examine the theory and practice of military operations, focusing particularly on the land component. It will address in detail ideas about the role of different arms, within the context of joint operations, and students will test such ideas against historical case studies and examples of contemporary practice.

Module Objective: 

On completion of this module students will be able to demonstrate an understanding of the impact of military force on peace support operations. They will be able to appraise the role of international/regional organizations on peace support and crisis management operations. They will also be able to evaluate and demonstrate the importance of these to the contemporary Irish Defence Forces.

Module Content: 

The module will examine the theory and practice of crisis management operations today and in the recent past. It will examine theories relating to the use of military force in such operations and also address the role of non-governmental organisations and the challenges posed by civil-military cooperation. Legal and ethical dimensions will also be addressed. The module will include an examination of the theory and practice of insurgency, counter-insurgency and counter-terrorist operations.

This module shall give the candidates the required knowledge and practice concerning embedding the principles of research ethics and integrity within their research PhD programme.

Indicative content:

1. Defining research integrity and misconduct are
1.1) This shall involve defining what research integrity is and the various forms that research misconduct can take. This shall cover examples of research misconduct cases and help students identify possible signs of misconduct.

2. Understanding the European and National landscape
2.1) This shall involve students acquiring a knowledge of current national and European norms concerning research integrity and ethics.

3. Ethical processes and procedures
3.1) The student will become familiar with the current institutional policies and procedures concerning ethics involving both animal and human subjects.

4. Embedding research integrity
4.1) This shall entail students understanding the key principles required to ensure research integrity and ethical research is central to their research programme.

5. Research Data Management
5.1) This shall define what date is, the various forms it can take and how research data should be recorded, accessible, stored appropriately and shared where appropriate
learningOutcomes: To understand the principles of research integrity and ethical research whilst embedding it into a research programme of study.
Become aware and understand DkIT's processes and procedures concerning research ethics and integrity.
Understand and implement best practice concerning research data management.
To assess a case study of research integrity failure.
To plan an application for ethical approval in a research context.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to enhance scientific communication and comprehension skills through attendance at and participation in research seminars provided by leading national and international researchers in the field of physiology and pharmacology.

Indicative content:

1. Research Seminar Series
1.1) Twelve one-hour research seminars presented by leading research and international researchers in the field of physiology and pharmacology
learningOutcomes: Comprehend the latest scientific research developments in the field of pharmacology and physiology and refine relevant information from the research presented.
Communicate diverse research topics in pharmacology and physiology in a succinct and accessible manner.
Apply the knowledge which they have obtained from the research seminar series to their own research study.

modules.moduleContent:
This module focuses on issues of current importance to the agriculture sector in the areas of bioremediation and bioenergy. This module examines recent developments in the areas of bioremediation, bioenergy and biofuel development, including consideration of regulatory and ethical aspects where appropriate.

Indicative content:

1. Bioremediation
1.1) Fundamental principles; Site characterization; In situ and ex situ techniques for treating soils and aquatic systems contaminated by pollutants including hydrocarbons, solvents, pesticides and dioxins; Bioreactor design and application; Bioremediation using adapted cultures and genetically-engineered microbes; Phytoremediation; Phycoremediation.

2. Sustainable Bioenergy Production
2.1) Technical and economic feasibility and environmental sustainability of different biomass feedstocks; Sustainable energy options in agriculture; Bioenergy processing technologies (Fermentation, transesterification, anaerobic digestion, pyrolysis, gasification);
2.2) Design and analyses of bioenergy production systems; Different biorefinery systems and concepts including lignocellulosic feedstock, microalgae, whole-crop and green biorefinery; Emerging technologies.

3. Energy Smart Agriculture
3.1) Global energy trends; Energy security; Sustainable energy options in Agriculture; Direct and indirect energy consumption in Agriculture; Energy efficiency and energy conservation; Energy inputs and GHG emissions. Methods of energy analysis; Carbon balances; Use of biomass and biomass waste for carbon-neutral production of biofuel, electricity and biofertilizers.

4. Energy Policy and Regulation
4.1) EU legislation and energy policy (RES-Directive, Biofuels Directive); Overview of National Regulations (National Renewable Energy Action Plans); Emissions trading.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an advanced level of comprehension including existing and emerging technologies in the area of bioremediation.
Comprehend current published research in the area of bioremediation and bioenergy and critically review this compiled information.
Appraise the environmental impact, efficiency and sustainability of current and future energy sources and uses.
Write well-researched and referenced scientific essays on selected topics of environmental biotechnologies covered in the module and discuss how these relate to their postgraduate research project.

modules.moduleContent:
To provide students with a deeper and more practical understanding of data exploration and analysis

Topics include:
Messy data and data cleaning
Observational Studies
Controlled experiments
Sample Surveys
Data privacy and GDPR
Introduction to data anonymization
Image and text data
Supervised and unsupervised learning
Predictive Analytics
Model validation and evaluation
learningOutcomes: Know how to uncover useful information from messy data;
Be aware of various ways in which data are collected and compiled;
Be aware of the differences between observational and experimental studies;

Have a knowledge of the biggest challenges currently facing data security and
privacy;
Be familiar with image and text data;

Be familiar with common methods used in predictive analytics for making
predictions about future unknown events;

Be informed about model evaluation and validation approaches with the goal of
understanding how we can make our models as generalizable as possible;
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Overview of the data science process and data analytics life cycle in industry. Data science methods using software such as R or SAS. Topics such as: data manipulation and cleaning, data visualisation, time series, text mining tools, missing data mechanisms and imputation techniques. Guest Lecturers to present examples of data science projects in industry. Data science group project.
learningOutcomes: Understand the life cycle of a data science project and the role of the data scientist within that.
Collate and manipulate data in software such as SAS or R.

Identify tools to handle the breadth of challenges that can arise within a data science project, such as missing data or time series data or complex 
data needing visualisation.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This course covers probability and statistical techniques for data analytics and data science. Topics include: Exploratory data analysis and visualisation. Probability basics, independence, Bayes theorem. Probability models for data, including Binomial, Poisson, Exponential and Normal. Confidence intervals and hypothesis testing: one and two samples, paired samples, proportions. Case studies in R.

Lectures and labs as for DS663.
learningOutcomes: Analyse data using R.
Write statistical reports.
Model data using probability distributions.
Choose and construct appropriate data visualisations.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This course is a continuation of DS663. Topics are: Parameter estimation; method of moments maximum likelihood and least squares. Simple linear and multiple regression. Analysis of Variance, R2, sequential sums of squares, general F-tests. Case studies in R. Model checking via residual plots. Regression diagnostics: residuals, leverage, outliers, influence and Cook's Distance.

Lecturers and labs as for DS665.
learningOutcomes: Analyse data using linear models and analysis of variance.
Assess assumptions.
Use model diagnostics.
Choose and construct appropriate data visualisations.

modules.moduleName: BUSINESS WRITING AND PROFESSIONAL COMMUNICATION IN ENGLISH 1
modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at international students of Business who may require a higher level of written Business English, to level C1 CEFR, for the completion of essays, reports and other written work related to their studies and assessments.

Please note: Entry onto this module is restricted to students whose first language is not English.
learningOutcomes: Communicate correctly, clearly and efficiently in English in a variety of different types of written texts and oral discourses based on their communicative purpose, using the necessary strategies to adapt the communication to the situation and the addressee.

Participate in oral, written and multimodal interactions in the English language and produces texts of different types, demonstrating appropriate use of structures and vocabulary for the situation and context.
Recognize and use specialized registers and languages within the professional field (company, business, or organisation) in English.
Produce a variety of textual genres needed for different business situations and contexts, so that communication circulates within and between companies.
Manage the internal and external communication of the company: reports, electronic mail, memoranda, catalogues and communications with clients, investors, and other relevant stakeholders.

Identify linguistic mediation needs in professional contexts, as well as situations and issues that can generate cultural misunderstanding, while recognising and counteracting one's own cultural expectations regarding the behaviour of the participants in the intercultural encounter.

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered may include: demand and supply for health and health care; health care insurance markets; the role of the government in health care provision; comparative analysis of health care systems (Ireland and abroad); measuring and valuing health; population ageing and the future of health policy; obesity
learningOutcomes: Understand the demand curve for health care
Investigate the of role of health as a consumption good and as an input into production in the Grossman model
Understand socioeconomic disparities in health
Analyse the role of adverse selection and moral hazard in health insurance markets
Understand the health technology assessment
Compare and evaluate national health policies across countries
Apply specific topics such as obesity and population ageing to the field of health economics
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module will predominantly focus on game theory. In particular, the module will cover static and dynamic games under complete and incomplete information. Each topic will contain relevant economic applications. As well as game theory, the module may cover other advanced topics in consumer behaviour and in information economics.
learningOutcomes: Present a game in normal and/or extensive form.
Determine any and all pure-strategy and mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium solutions to a game.
Discuss the relative strengths of various solution concepts.
Determine dynamic equilibrium solutions that rule out irrational strategies or incredible threats.
Determine the strategies and conditions under which players can maintain a collusive agreement as a dynamic equilibrium solution.
Determine Nash equilibrium strategies when agents have incomplete and/or imperfect information.
Apply game theory concepts to problems in imperfect competition and information economics.

modules.moduleName: ECONOMICS OF THE ENVIRONMENT & NATURAL RESOURCES
modules.moduleContent: The first part of this module looks at the role of government in attaining socially optimal environmental pollution levels through the use of taxes, standards and tradable permits, and compares the merits of these policies. The issues raised in forming effective international environmental agreements will also be discussed. The module then analyses various techniques used to quantify the value environmental resources. Finally, the module outlines how to optimally extract natural resources and discusses how government policy towards natural resource extraction can affect environmental quality.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the economic issues relevant to environmental and natural resource matters.
Determine how different environmental policies affect the incentive to undertake pollution reduction measures.
Discuss and illustrate the relative merits of various environmental policies.
Outline why attempts to form an international environmental agreement may fail and how they might be successful
Discuss the objectives, strengths and weaknesses of environmental valuation methodologies.
Determine the conditions required to ensure the optimal extraction of natural resources.
Outline how the optimal extraction and price path of a natural resource is affected by market structure, the availability of substitute resources and changes in other relevant factors.

modules.moduleContent:
The course focuses on four questions: (i) Why do countries trade?, (ii) What do countries trade?, (iii) Who gains from trade? and (iv) How and why do governments intervene in trade?  

These questions are addressed by examining a few simple trade models.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the arguments put forward by policymakers to justify their trade policy.
Identify the reasons for international trade.
Explain the pattern of trade observed between different trading blocks in the real world.
Identify the losers and the winners from international trade.
Explain the effects of the different trade policy instruments on various groups in the economy.
Demonstrate when and why trade policy is likely to be influenced by specific vested interests within an economy.

modules.moduleName: ECONOMICS OF WORK AND LIFE DECISIONS
modules.moduleContent: Employees, firms and the government all make decisions that have an impact on the labour market. The aim of this course is to provide students with an understanding of how the interaction of these agents determines labour market and other outcomes that affect an individual’s utility. Topics covered may include: Labour supply, Labour demand and minimum wages, Unemployment, Immigration, Economics of Education and Discrimination.  Where possible topics will reflect current developments in the Irish labour market.
learningOutcomes: Describe the key components of the economic approach to analyzing behaviour
Explain how the economic approach to analyzing behaviour can be applied to the labour market.
Relate differences in the labour market conditions to differences in labour market outcomes.
Discuss how to distinguish between competing models of the labour market.
Analyse the consequences of labour market interventions.
Identify aspects of the Irish Labour market where interventions may lead to welfare improvements.
Recommend labour market policy measures to improve welfare.
modules.notes: Module covers how to analyse labour market data to uncover causal effects.

modules.moduleContent: Students will complete a single substantial independent thesis by integrating the economics, econometrics and statistics they have learned throughout the programme. This will provide students with greater understanding of what they have learned and enable them to apply their economic knowledge and econometric and statistical techniques to a significant, supervised, self-directed project.
learningOutcomes: Review, evaluate, critique and summarize existing research
Formulate and develop a research question and proposal
Use statistical software and/or develop algorithms in R effectively
Identify, prepare and analyse data for applied economic research
Interpret empirical results in the context of the existing literature
Write effectively in a style appropriate to economic research
Present research to different audiences.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module will go beyond standard models of economics, introducing psychological, sociological, institutional, and informational constraints. We will discuss how humans behave in different social situations, when faced with risk, and when there is imperfect information. We will also briefly discuss some other heterodox approaches including, feminist economics, institutional economics and complexity economics which attempt to address some perceived failing of the standard models.
learningOutcomes: Knowledge of preference orders and their connection to utilities
Understanding of the differences between economic and psychological approach to human decisions
Getting familiar with main heuristics and biases in human decisions, be able to interpret them.
Understanding of the main normative and descriptive approaches to understanding of the principles of decision making under risk.
Knowledge of applications of behavioural theory to interpretation of real decisions and public policy.
Knowledge of approaches to human decision in time, and the problems related to inconsistency in intertemporal preferences
Knowledge of the principles of game theory and ability to interpret incentives of people in interpersonal interactions.

modules.moduleContent: This module will cover the main topics in development economics by studying both from a theoretical and empirical perspective the market failures which prevent economic development in the poorest countries in the world. In doing so, the module will also explain the causal inference tools used by development economists to identify these market failures and quantify their impact on the lives of the poor. The module will be based on the most recent findings of the related literature.
Modern research techniques and strategies in economics.
TBA

The Sociology of Education situates schools in the wider context of society and other institutions. It raises questions of class, gender and race and asks how these questions interface with schools, teacher identity and student experiences and opportunities. It involves critically examining approaches to diversity and it examines the relationship between education and economic, cultural and political systems, as well as understanding how educational experiences affect people’s lives. This module enables students to understand the practice of teaching and education in a context that extends beyond their subject and their school. It asks them to critically examine their own positions and assumptions and to understand how their beliefs, values and practices have come into being through reflection and to build critically reflective practitioners who explore educational questions from multiple perspectives. It also enables a more expansive understanding of issues and questions central to educational theory and practice.

This module examines the social context of learning, locating the school as a key institution and learning site in contemporary society. A range of theories and research are introduced to provide insights to the web of social, cultural, economic and political factors that underpin learning in contemporary society. It highlights the embedded and interdependent nature of learning; critically assesses its role at a personal level (as a learner, teacher, family member, etc), institutional level (the schooling system) and the broader social, cultural, economic and political level.
learningOutcomes: Understand and evaluate the key discourses and theories that underpin sociology of education.
Articulate informed, personal theories of sociology of education for their current and future professional practice.
Critically debate key sociological influences and constructs relating to students’ learning in education contexts.
Demonstrate academic, professional and personal understandings, values and knowledge within and across sociology.
Integrate sociological theory and professional practice through a process of individual and group critical reflection.
Develop a critical perspective on the challenges and opportunities of contemporary education and teachers’ roles.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The general aim of this module is to develop students’ pedagogical content knowledge in STEM teaching. Students will engage in various elements of this module depending on their 2 teaching subjects, which are drawn from Science (Biology, Physics, Chemistry), Mathematics and Applied Mathematics, and Computer Science. Students will consider the influences of the wider community and classroom interactions on their teaching and students’ learning. Issues related to assessment for, of and as learning will be examined. The use and integration of ICT in Science and Mathematics teaching and learning will be explored. The secondary aim of the module is to enable students to integrate their two teaching subjects and gain a wider perspective of STEM education.
learningOutcomes: Identify key concepts in STEM pedagogy
Deploy a range of pedagogical tools, including ICT resources, to support students’ learning of STEM
Combine content knowledge with appropriate pedagogical strategies in STEM teaching for understanding
Demonstrate understanding of relevant assessment practices in STEM education
Develop strategies to embed literacy and numeracy into STEM teaching
Support students in reflecting on, selecting, developing and critically appraising relevant technologies and teaching techniques for possible use in their professional lives.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The focus of this module will be on active pedagogies. Students will consider the influences of the wider community, school ethos and classroom interactions on mathematics instruction. Issues related to assessment for, of and as learning will be examined. The use of ICT in mathematics teaching and learning will be explored. Teaching mathematics in contexts that allow learners to see connections within mathematics, between mathematics and other subjects, and between mathematics and its applications to real life will form a central theme in this module. Students will examine the nature of mathematics and of mathematics learning, through engagement with research literature and lesson study in professional learning groups.
learningOutcomes: identify key concepts in mathematics pedagogy
combine content knowledge of mathematics with appropriate pedagogical strategies in teaching for understanding
deploy a range of pedagogical tools, including ICT, resources to support students’ learning of mathematics
explore the needs of various groups of students and consider the range of factors that can influence learning outcomes
apply mathematical knowledge and skills to solve problems in familiar and unfamiliar contexts
critically interrogate application of theory to practice in real contexts
demonstrate understanding of relevant assessment practices.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO RESEARCH METHODS IN EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: This module will develop students’ research capacity, with a view to developing their own thesis research. An overview of different research methodologies and methods will be provided by staff working in these areas, to inspire and guide students’ own research work. We will explore the rationale and practicalities of research including theoretical and methodological perspectives, research design, sampling, ethics, conducting and presenting research. Methods covered will include survey research, individual and focus group interviewing, observations, visual and arts-based approaches, narrative and documentary research, and action research.
learningOutcomes: Critically assess the different theoretical underpinnings and philosophical considerations of research paradigms.
Explore a range of research methods to a level that enables them to undertake their own dissertation research.
Describe the characteristics of different methodologies and how they can be used on an educational setting.
Recognise and respond to key methodological and ethical issues underpinning educational research.

modules.moduleContent: In this module students undertake an individual research project related to an aspect of education to be presented as a 20– 25,000 word thesis. In doing this, they may complete (a) a statement of the research objectives (b )a literature review (c) appropriate methodologies (d) data collection and analysis and (e) present the findings of their research and recommendations. ED604, ED617 and ED618 must be taken to support research project. ED618 must be taken in the same year as the thesis/capstone.
learningOutcomes: Design, manage and complete an educational research project.
Read and interpret educational research.
Select and defend appropriate research methodologies.
Draw appropriate insights from research findings.
Communicate research findings in a rigorous and defensible manner.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to current theories on group leadership, membership, and group dynamics, fostering understanding of how guidance and counselling groups operate. 

Students engage in group developmental work as a group member for six group counselling sessions and are invited to bring this knowledge and awareness to their work in school placement. 

Participants are trained in:
Group Theory
Group Practice
learningOutcomes: Articulate a familiarity with current theory on fundamental issues of Group Leadership;
Demonstrate good understanding of group membership and how groups function;
Recognise the professional and ethical issues in Group Counselling, Guidance and group work, and have developed their own individual professional policy for groups in the school context;
Reflect on oneself in a group context and have developed awareness and skills from this experience;
Initiate, lead and bring closure to groups with reasonable competence;
Evaluate group outcomes and one's own role as leader in the group process.

modules.moduleContent:
This module emphasizes major counselling theories and basic counselling skills for guidance counselling practices. The module explores counselling theories and their relationship to psychological development. Counselling skills and process are practiced with an emphasis on integrating theories, practice and life experience. Students will demonstrate that they have discussed and reflected on the various theories of counselling and have developed an understanding of their own psychological development and its relationship to the practice of counselling. The following major schools of counselling are studied: Psychoanalysis, Adlerian, Person-Centred, Behavioural, Cognitive Behavioural, Rational Emotive Behavioural, Gestalt, Narrative and Reality Approaches. 

Students will learn about the counselling process, infusion of culture into the counselling process, techniques and interventions in counselling to the students. Students will also explore case conceptualization skills, assessment of clients’ problems, termination counselling process via examples, videos, exercises, and role-play.
learningOutcomes: Articulate a knowledge and appreciation of the major counselling theories;

Demonstrate that they have discussed and reflected on the various theories of counselling and have developed an understanding of their own psychological development and its relationship to the practice of counselling;
Manifest a working knowledge of how counselling theories and skills assist counsellors in addressing the psychological needs of young people;
Appreciate how their own life narrative can affect the counselling relationship;
Evaluate their own style of counselling practice in a self-reflective way;
Conduct one-to-one counselling sessions competently with young people aged 12-18 years under supervision;
Understand the ethical principles involved in counselling, and demonstrate an ability to apply those principles in ethical decision-making;

modules.moduleContent: This module offers students an alternative to writing a thesis. The Capstone Project is a summative piece of work, allowing students to explore an area of interest to them.  It can take various forms in consultation with a supervisor. For example, students might choose to write a review of research and scholarship in a particular area of study, use video or photographic forms to present their ideas along with a short written text, use creative expression such as visual art, dance or drama accompanied by written exploration of themes, build a narrative study reflecting on one’s own learning. ED604, ED617 and ED618 must be taken to support research project. ED618 must be taken in the same year as the thesis/capstone.
learningOutcomes: Identify key themes in the chosen area of study.
Critically evaluate these themes through the carrying out of a major project

modules.moduleContent:
In this module, participants will explore the key issues as they relate to knowledge, ability and the skills required for leading the school as an educational organisation. It will fully equip the aspiring leader to appreciate the complex nature of learning processes and the multiplicity of their roles as ‘lead learner.’

Module Content will focus on key themes including:
• The purposes of education.
• Leadership for quality teaching, learning and assessment.
• Culture and contexts.
• Curriculum innovation.
• Change management: reform and renewal.
learningOutcomes: Interrogate their assumptions of values and vision for the school and how these impact on leading the school as a learning organisation.
Appreciate key aspects in distributing leadership for learning.
Appreciate the critical importance of building and supporting relationships within the wider school community.
Engage with the responsibilities of leading a professional learning organisation.
Employ national and international indicators to deepen appreciation of what constitutes quality teaching and learning.
Explore current Teaching and Learning initiatives within and across the continuum of learning.
Explore the concepts as outlined, so as to nurture and develop the highest quality of educational provision across diverse and unique contexts.

modules.moduleName: THE PERSON AND THE PROFESSIONAL: WHO AM I AS A LEADER?
modules.moduleContent: This module is one of two anchor modules of the course. The objective of it is to create participants’ awareness of thoughts, attitudes and behaviours that influence their leadership outcomes as well as decide what changes, if any, they wish to introduce in their lives. It introduces aspiring leaders to some thought-provoking, evidence-based concepts that will help them shape their own leadership styles in the future. Furthermore, it acts as an accelerator to enhance future leaders’ self-directed, reflective learning and continuous professional growth.
learningOutcomes: Critically review the literature relating to leadership knowledge and skill development.
Become aware of how their behaviours and attitude influence leadership outcomes.
Understand the role of values and ethics in their personal/professional development.
Demonstrate an enhanced capacity for both self-reflection and continuous professional growth.
Produce evidence-informed decisions about the future of their personal and career development.

modules.moduleContent: This module is one of two anchor modules (along with Module – The Person & Professional: Who am I as Leader?) which will run across the 2 semesters. The understanding of educational research in a collaborative setting for the development of a collaborative research culture in the participant’ school is the key focus of this module. The skills, competencies and capabilities required for this endeavour are essential skills for the school leader at all levels to support staff in development work within the school.
learningOutcomes: Understand the process and theoretical underpinnings of educational research and use it as a significant leadership tool in the enhancement of their professional practice.
Utilise the skills required in carrying out investigative research to enhance educational understanding and practice.
Understand fully the range of ethical considerations central to the conduct of educational research.
Engage with colleagues in a collaborative process of educational research.
Understand group process and theory.
Engage with cultural concepts of ‘conversation’ to enhance learning and collegiality for professional practice.
Work as a team member in the completion of a joint project.

modules.moduleContent:
This module gives students the foundational knowledge and skills to engage in successful doctoral research in the field of education by introducing them to the practice of educational research, specifically in respect of educational research methods. The framework of the seminar supports the development of a community of research amongst the students, both online and offline. 

Course content is designed to support the development of students' research and refinement of their research question and methods adopted, as well as to support their emerging reading and writing practices.

Students should register for ED802 and ED879 in their first year of study. Note these modules must be completed  before the programme is completed and the thesis submitted.
learningOutcomes: locate a research question at doctoral level;
critically evaluate theoretical literature and methodological literature, and relate course content to their own projects;
understand and critically evaluate a range of different research methodologies and methods in educational research
understand the relationship between the different elements of the research process
identify the key issues required for engaging in ethical research and secure ethical approval.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: FIELDS IN THE MAKING: NARRATIVE AND ARTS-BASED RESEARCH METHODOLOGY IN EDUCATION.
modules.moduleContent: Contemporary theories of self view subjectivity as fluid and contextual; constructed and reconstructed through the stories people tell themselves and others about who they are (Riessman, 2008). These stories/narratives are stitched into the ways of seeing, knowing and being that are made available to us in our culture.
