Construing the self as narratively structured has led to the bourgeoning of ethnographies and alternative approaches in the social sciences and humanities. Poststructuralist and postcolonialist writers have produced, in combination with literary, feminist and queer studies, a diverse range of transgressive, experimental and emancipatory research genres that attempt to make visible alternative knowledge often silenced and/or contested. Unrecognized, unacknowledged or unsayable stories can be made more accessible by the use of visual image-making and arts based approaches to research. These highlight particular ways of learning and knowing the world. This module invites participants to consider notions of subjectivity and research methodology and provides a creative pedagogical space to explore contemporary, transformative research in education.

The process of analysing qualitative data involves moving from the data to understanding, explaining and interpreting the phenomena in question (Taylor and Gibbs, 2010, p.1.). There is no single or correct way to do this as fitness for purpose is a guiding principle in qualitative data analysis. In this module, students will learn to analyse qualitative data by engaging in analysis of their own data.  
Note: In order to participate in this module, students must have gathered qualitative data and be ready to engage in data analysis. The module is not appropriate for doctoral students who are developing their research question or designing their data gathering instruments.
learningOutcomes: Prepare and organise the data for analysis
Identify units for analysis
Employ coding/content analysis/discourse analysis etc. to their data as appropriate
Use appropriate data analysis software
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Curriculum Studies is enjoying a period of resurgence which now addresses the theory-practice divide. This renaissance is timely, as the new millennium has also witnessed the formation of new variants of curriculum policy, variously categorised as learner-centred and technical-instrumental by advocates and opponents. These new policy forms have been controversial on a number of fronts, notably concerning the place of knowledge in the curriculum, and the demands of preparing citizens to live in modern pluralist democracies. 
This module will look at curriculum theory, educational policy, and pedagogy and its enactment. Against a backdrop of globalisation in for example economical, communicative, social justice and everyday domains, it will examine transnational policy discourses and comparative studies. Various theoretical lenses will be used to analyse 'curriculum' bringing together curriculum theory and critical educational studies with practical examples of teaching for social justice to argue for a transformative curriculum that challenges existing inequity in social, educational, and economic relations.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an in-depth capacity to analyse concepts, theories and pertinent research concerned with curriculum theory and practice.
Demonstrate an advanced capability in interrogating and making discerning judgements on issues of policy, practice and research influences that impact on curriculum trajectories
Demonstrate a critical awareness of curriculum as policy and the process chosen for enactment
Critique the broader implications of international, national and local factors that inform the field of curriculum practices.

modules.moduleContent:
Professional and practitioner knowledge modules (with a total weighting of 60 credits) run throughout the four years of the programme, developing the participants’ capabilities as critically reflective practitioners. These professional discourse modules allow a student to:
• reflect on the contribution of new knowledge on their practice e.g. 
· Improved professional standards in their professional practice 
· Developed new/improved guidelines in their professional practice 
· Increase inter-agency collaboration 
• Build network links within practice and academic communities 
· disseminate research to peers in practice 
Note that professional practice learning and reflection occurs concurrently in the student’s professional workplace/practice, captured/informed by the proposed modules, while undertaking the Ed D.
learningOutcomes: Make explicit connections between their everyday practices as professionals and their doctoral studies.
Articulate the specific contribution(s) of their studies within their practice.
Critically engage with the interplay between disciplinary concepts and professional practice.

modules.moduleContent: The module aims to explore curriculum and pedagogy in terms of context, principles and  processes emphasising Aistear, the Early Childhood Curriculum Framework. Adopting a  rights-based approach, students will consider a range of curriculum approaches against the  backdrop of the Aistear Framework. Politics and power in curriculum will be examined, who develops curriculum and whose voice is heard. Pedagogical practices will be explored as key tools in supporting children’s learning, and the view of the child as a competent and active agent in his/her own learning will underpin the module content
learningOutcomes: Identify curriculum as practice and process, as content and outcomes;
Examine Aistear as a curriculum framework;
Outline the underlying principles, and practices of a range of curricula and align with Aistear aims and learning outcomes;
Explore the pedagogical roles and practices of adults in Early Childhood;
Discuss the politics and policies informing curriculum.

modules.moduleName: HEALTH AND WELL-BEING FOR INFANTS AND TODDLERS
modules.moduleContent: This module aims to help students develop the knowledge, practice and values that are core to ensuring the health, care and wellbeing of infant and toddlers. Students will be supported to develop and understanding of appropriate practices in relation to infants’ and toddlers’ safety, hygiene and nutrition. This will include legislative requirements, policy guidelines, practices and procedures relating to child protection in Early Childhood settings.
learningOutcomes: State legislative requirements, policy guidelines, practices and procedures relating to child protection in Early Childhood settings;
Outline the requirements necessary to provide for the health and well-being of infants and toddlers as informed by national guidelines to include child protection;
Describe the factors that contribute to the overall well-being of infants and toddlers;
Identify requirements for the promotion of healthy and safe indoor and outdoor environments for infants and toddlers;
Perform appropriate personal care routines for infants and toddlers including the care of the child with additional needs or the child with common childhood illnesses;
Implement appropriate policies and procedures to include record keeping as required by legislation and good practice guidelines;
Identify the pedagogical importance of interactions between an educator and an infant or toddler during care routines such as feeding, changing, etc.

modules.moduleContent: The module will explore key psychological processes, theories and concepts in relation to early childhood (birth – 6years). Theories of attachment, gender identity, self-regulation, language development will be considered. The module will explore current research on developmental neuroscience and its relevance for learning, development and motivation in early childhood. Throughout the module, students will build on their own experiences, reflect on the interplay between theory and practice and apply theories to practice.
learningOutcomes: Understand and analyse key psychological processes, theories and concepts in relation to early childhood learning and development.

Describe the role and centrality of relationships for well-being, development and learning and supporting healthy attachment and self regulation (birth to six years) and explore the formation of relationships and friendships and their role in supporting well-being, identity and belonging with emphasis on play.
Interpret the implications of brain development for children’s learning and development and develop strategies to support learning and development.
Outline the development of gender and identity in babies, toddlers and young children and examine the implications for pedagogical practice.
Examine the emergence, development and use of language(s) and communication in early childhood.

modules.moduleName: BUILDING RELATIONSHIPS WITH CHILDREN, FAMILIES AND COMMUNITIES
modules.moduleContent: This module will support students to work effectively with children, (birth to six years) parents and families, understanding the integrated nature of relationship within an ecological framework. It will consider working with parents and families as a democratic practice and examine how these relationships are built and developed. The module will also explore national and international models of effective partnership and integrated delivery to improve outcome for children, families and communities.
learningOutcomes: Explore conceptualisations of social class and cultural difference and examine how this affects their understanding of parenting and families

Identify the role of, and theoretical basis for, working with families and community in supporting children’s holistic development, in particular exploring and analysing the new sociological paradigm on childhood and the impact of social class, cultural contexts, community relations and parenting practices
Examine policies and practices of the ECEC settings that effect relationships with families and communities

Identify strategies for working with families and community in supporting children’s holistic development and to strengthen links with early childhood services and related professionals when appropriate
Compare and contrast concepts and models of integrated services that support effective partnerships and outcomes for children and families

modules.moduleContent: The module aims to explore curriculum in terms of context, principles and processes emphasising Aistear, the National Early Childhood Curriculum Framework. It will consider a range of curricula against the backdrop of Aistear. Politics and power in curriculum will be examined, who develops curriculum and whose voice is heard. Pedagogical practices will be explored as key tools in supporting children’s learning.
learningOutcomes: Explore curriculum as enquiry led, integrated and emergent.
Identify curriculum as practice and process, as content and outcomes.
Outline the underlying principles, and practices of a range of curricula.
Examine Aistear as a curriculum framework.
Explore the pedagogical roles and practices of educators in a range of curricula (birth to six years).
Discuss the politics and policies informing curriculum.
Reflect on the role of play as a pedagogy in high quality early year’s curricula.

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL PRACTICE: FOCUS ON INTERACTIONS & RELATIONSHIPS
modules.moduleContent:
This module will explore occupation profiles, the role of the professional and the core competencies required to work in early years’ settings. With an emphasis on developing their interaction skills and their relationships with children, students will use a range of 
methodologies to support the implementation of the curriculum and manage routines and transitions in everyday practice. Incorporating previous learning on the course and their experience in the sector, students will work under the guidance of a placement supervisor to develop their ability to provide for the care and education of babies, toddlers and young children (birth- 6 years).
learningOutcomes: Identify occupation profiles in the ECCE sector;
Explore the role of the professional in ECCE;
Engage with colleagues as part of a professional team;
Describe the core competencies required to work in ECCE;
Further develop observation skills;
Explore and document opportunities for play and children's learning through play across the curriculum;
Interact effectively with individual and groups of children (birth to six years)

modules.moduleName: SOCIAL AND LEGAL CONTEXT OF EARLY CHILDHOOD
modules.moduleContent: This module provides a forum for discussion on current national and international policies that impact on the lives of children from birth to six years, families and the provision of early childhood education and care in Ireland. Within this context, Irish legislation relevant to ECEC settings will be examined. Finally, students will appraise the range of voluntary and statutory bodies and services that support children, families and ECEC settings.
learningOutcomes: Examine aspects of Irish social policies relating to all children, their families and the early childhood education and care sector and discuss the implications of contemporary policy development to practice and to services.
Outline the structure of the Irish Legal System, with relevance to early years and the implications of legislation for early childhood education and care practices, including issues of inclusion.
Examine legislation in relation to the impact on ECEC settings of family formations and re-formations, guardianship, etc. and policies in relation to same.

Assess the impact of international legislation, directives and frameworks on Irish social policy development relating to ECEC settings with particular emphasis on the United Nations Convention on Rights of the Child.
Compare and contrast the role and purpose of a range of voluntary and statutory agencies that support children, families and ECEC services.

modules.moduleContent: This module will explore occupation profiles, the role of the professional and the core competencies required to work in early years’ settings. With an emphasis on developing their interaction skills and their relationships with children, students will use a range of methodologies to support the implementation of the curriculum and manage routines in everyday practice. Incorporating previous learning on the course and their experience in the sector, students will work under the guidance of a placement supervisor to develop their ability to provide for the care and education of babies, toddlers and young children (birth- 6 years).
learningOutcomes: Identify and explore occupation profiles, the role of the professional and the core competencies required to work in ECEC settings.
Outline concepts of team working and team roles, reflect on personal strengths and challenges as a team member, and engage with skills development for team working.
Examine opportunities for play and children's learning through play across the curriculum, linking with the National Frameworks.
Interact effectively with individual and groups of children (birth to six years).
Utilise a range of observation approaches in ECEC settings.
Demonstrate an understanding of democracy, children’s rights and ethical practice in an ECEC setting.

modules.moduleContent: This module draws on sound leadership theory and relates it to the field of Early Childhood Care and Education. It aims to create opportunities for students to strengthen their professional skills and practices, and to develop their ability to guide others to improve pedagogical practice.  The module reflects a deep understanding of the contextual issues and challenges facing early childhood leaders in changing social and practice contexts.
learningOutcomes: Conceptualise theories, practices and approaches to leadership in the context of ECCE (birth to six years).
Reflect on personal leadership styles and practices.
Appraise the relationship between leadership and power; management; gender; culture and expectation.
Engage in coaching and  mentoring relationships within the ECCE team to include a focus on self-care and well-being.
Create structures, environments and conditions in the ECCE service to support professional inquiry and critical reflection.
Recognise and support leadership in children

modules.moduleContent: Building on their professional practice in previous years and the learning from modules covered, students will work independently under the guidance of a supervisor to develop their ability to plan and provide for the care and education of a diverse group of young children.
learningOutcomes: Provide for the care and education of a diverse group of children in an early childhood setting.
Plan, implement and assess curriculum to meet the learning needs and interests of the children in an early childhood setting and use a range of tools to assess children’s learning and development.
Support children’s well-being and sense of identity and belonging.
Engage in practice that supports children’s critical thinking and learning, and promotes dispositions of mastery and agency.
Critically reflect on their practice and use their reflections to plan future practice.
Organise, equip and manage an inclusive environment for young children and adults cognisant of affordance, constraints, needs and interests.
Contribute to and work effectively as part of a professional team and engage effectively with parents, families and the wider community.

modules.moduleName: REFLECTIVE PRACTICE: EDUCATOR AS CHANGE AGENT
modules.moduleContent: This module introduces the students to educational research. It will focus on basic research methodologies suitable for the practitioner as researcher, with particular emphasis on action research (praxiology). Students will be introduced to literature reviews, data collection methods and ethics in research. The potential for action research to provide a mechanism for transformative change will be a central concern of the module.
learningOutcomes: Explain and give relevant examples of the research methodologies suitable for early childhood education (birth to six years);
Conduct a literary review of a chosen topic in early childhood care and education;

Examine and evaluate data collection methods – e. g. questionnaires, images, reflective journals in research in early childhood settings with particular emphasis on creative methodologies suitable for accessing the voices and perspectives of babies and young children;
Demonstrate understanding of the ethical implications of conducting action research with and about young children;

Interrogate action research as a mechanism for supporting transformative practice in early childhood education, and reflect upon their own role as a potential change agent through their educational practice.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: REFLECTIVE PRACTICE: EDUCATOR AS CHANGE AGENT
modules.moduleContent: This module will explore concepts of advocacy at individual child and family, professional and societal/political levels. Students will be supported to interrogate their own role as a change agent for children and families through both theoretical and practical lenses. It will support personal reflection on the potential impact of self-advocacy, peer advocacy and group advocacy within a personal philosophy of ECEC. Students will begin to develop an understanding of action research in early childhood education and care practice
learningOutcomes: Ethically and effectively communicate their own needs and rights as an early childhood educator on their own behalf and on behalf of the sector as a whole, promoting democracy, solidarity, active citizenship, creativity and personal fulfilment
Identify the importance and power of a research evidence base for policy, practice and service development to realise the rights of babies, toddlers, young children, families and educators

Synthesise and apply theoretical perspectives on how social change happens and how advocacy impacts on this, with a view to adopting a democratic and inclusive approach to the education and care of babies, toddlers, young children and families in order to sustain social cohesion

Acknowledge and describe how this knowledge is applied in daily practice by advocating for individual children and families, with commitment to welfare and wellbeing for all adults, babies, toddlers and young children in ECEC settings
Reflect upon and articulate their own philosophical stance on the child and ensuring the realisation of the rights of babies, toddlers, children and adults in ECEC settings and broader society
Explain what ‘action research’ is, and evaluate the potential role of action research as a vehicle for positive change in their own practice

modules.moduleName: LANGUAGE AND LITERACY IN EARLY CHILDHOOD
modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to generate an understanding of the centrality of language in the development of young children, particularly in terms of the relevant theoretical perspectives. It explores dimensions of early language development and literacy and its significance within Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework. Students will discover how playful experiences and literacy-rich environments contribute to language and literacy development. The features of bilingualism and multilingualism in young children will be reviewed with a particular emphasis on how educators support language learning in early year’s environments with young children from multilingual families. Students will identify the challenges of speech language and communication needs (SLCN) in the young child and will explore the importance of collaboration with family and engagement with other professionals and professional organisations in support of all children’s learning, well-being and development.
learningOutcomes: Identify functions of language, key stages in and influences on emergent literacy and ‘multiliteracies’ and critically reflect on relevant theoretical perspectives.
Explore the key role of the adult in supporting communication, language learning and all aspects of emergent literacy within an inclusive educational approach.

Display a range of interactive pedagogical strategies, and review and develop resources to nurture all forms of language learning in the early years guided by Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework.
Consider children’s language development from a multilingual perspective including recognising children’s home language and supporting second language acquisition.
Appreciate the language learning challenges of children with speech, language and communication needs (SLCN).
Articulate and apply a philosophy for using play to support language acquisition with children (birth to six years).

Utilise pedagogical knowledge in supporting and providing for a literacy rich indoor and outdoor enquiry-based learning environment for young children in according with Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework.

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL PRACTICE: FOCUS ON CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT
modules.moduleContent: Building on their professional practice in previous years and the learning from previous modules, students will work under the guidance of a supervisor to assess their ability to assist in planning and providing for the care and education of babies, toddlers and young children (birth – 6 years). Using a range of strategies and methodologies, students will plan and implement curriculum and evaluate children’s learning and development. Students will engage in reflection on the hidden curriculum and their practice, working with colleagues, families, community and other professionals.
learningOutcomes: Engage in practice that supports children’s thinking and learning and promotes dispositions and agency.
Work effectively as part of a team and engage effectively with families and the wider community.
Plan, implement and evaluate an aspect of curriculum to meet the learning needs and interests of the children in an early year’s setting, with reference to the hidden curriculum.
Collaborate with work colleagues in planning and providing for the care and education of a group of children (birth to six years) in an ECEC setting.
Develop critical awareness of the role of an ECEC professional educator.
Critically reflect on practice to capture individual experience of children’s care, learning and development.

Deconstruct the role of the educator in creating nurturing environments for children’s relationships, and critique the impact of systems’ functioning on this with reference to the concept of a ‘competent system’.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is based on a view of children that emphasizes children´s participation and children´s competences and a view on culture that embraces both cultural heritage and cultural criticism. The course gives the students an insight into children´s culture and the formative influences that are part of the processes in a child´s development.

The students are expected to take joint responsibility and to have a joint influence during the course module. The students are required to work independenty and actively to be well prepared for co-operation with children, colleagues, parents and the community, both during the study and in a lifelong learning process. 

At the end of the course module the students has developed knowledge, skills and competence as a teacher of Pri-primary Education.
learningOutcomes: Learning & Development: Identify implications for policy and practice, with particular reference to the professional role of the educator in creating nurturing, playful environments for children’s relationships, and critiquing the impact of systems’ functioning on this with reference to the concept of a ‘competent system’.
Creativity & Imagination: Facilitate, extend and support children’s imaginative engagement through playful exploration and artistic co-creation
Supporting Enquiry/ Enacting the Curriculum: Critically discuss curriculum as enquiry led and emergent and the image of the child underpinning curriculum.

modules.moduleName: STUDY ABROAD, QUEEN MAUD UNIVERSITY COLLEGE OF EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: The courses give the students a theoretical and practical platform to get to know everyday life in Norwegian early childhood education institutions. The students will have 25 days of practical training in a local ECEC institution.
learningOutcomes: Learning & Development: Identify implications for policy and practice, with particular reference to the professional role of the educator in creating nurturing, playful environments for children’s relationships, and critiquing the impact of systems’ functioning on this with reference to the concept of a ‘competent system’.
Creativity & Imagination: Facilitate, extend and support children’s imaginative engagement through playful exploration and artistic co-creation
Supporting Enquiry/ Enacting the Curriculum: Critically discuss curriculum as enquiry led and emergent and the image of the child underpinning curriculum.

modules.moduleContent: This module aims to explore a broad range of challenges that impact on children’s holistic learning and development. It will adopt an inquiry-based approach and will include consideration of challenges such as additional needs, behaviour, giftedness, etc. The module will examine implications for educators as they work with parents, colleagues and other professionals in the field to support children’s learning and development in a social context. The module adopts a broader view in examining mechanisms and processes to work with other agencies to support children’s learning.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explore the broad range of challenges that may impact on children’s (birth to six years) holistic learning and development drawing on current thinking and research in the early childhood field;
Reflect on personal values underpinning work with families and children with additional needs;
Examine the implications for practice for educators as they work with parents, colleagues and other professionals in the field to support children’s learning and development;
Appraise an inter-agency, collaborative approach to supporting young children and their families;
Reflect on personal values underpinning work with families and children with additional needs.

modules.moduleContent: This module draws on sound leadership theory and relates it to the field of Early Childhood Care and Education. It aims to create opportunities for students to strengthen their professional skills and practices, and to develop their ability to guide others to improve pedagogical practice. The module reflects a deep understanding of the contextual issues and challenges facing early childhood leaders in changing social and practice contexts.
learningOutcomes: Conceptualise theories, practices and approaches to leadership in the context of ECCE (birth to six years);
Reflect on personal leadership styles and practices;
Appraise the relationship between leadership and power; management; gender; culture and expectation;
Engage in coaching and mentoring relationships within the ECCE team;
Create structures, environments and conditions in the ECCE service to support professional inquiry and critical reflection;
Recognise and support leadership in children;
Explore leadership to support the effective operation of a sustainable, ethical and legislatively compliant early childhood setting;

modules.moduleContent: The module will explore and highlight the interconnected nature of curriculum, pedagogy and assessment. It will focus on assessment as a social practice and as a tool for feedback and dialogue, emphasising the child’s identity as a competent learner, while making links with Aistear and Síolta. The module will consider modes, models, purposes and outcomes of assessment appropriate to the early childhood setting. Finally, this module will draw together learning from previous years to complete the cycle of planning, implementing and evaluating curriculum.
learningOutcomes: Examine the integrated and interdependent nature of curriculum, pedagogy and assessment within the curriculum (birth to six years);
Appraise the impact of pedagogy on curriculum and assessment;
Locate assessment within social, historical, theoretical and cultural contexts;
Document and share children’s learning using a range of media and engaging a range of stakeholders;
Utilise assessment as part of a team to build on children’s interest and extend their learning;
Critically evaluate the curriculum using Síolta, the National Quality Framework and Aistear, the Early Childhood Curriculum Framework;
Make linkages with current national policy.

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL PRACTICE: FOCUS ON PROFESSIONALISM & LEADERSHIP
modules.moduleContent: Building on their professional practice in previous years and the learning from modules covered, students will work independently under the guidance of a supervisor to develop their ability to plan and provide for the care and education of a diverse group of young children.
learningOutcomes: Provide for the care and education of a diverse group of children in an early childhood setting;
Plan, implement and assess curriculum to meet the learning needs and interests of the children in an early childhood setting and use a range of tools to assess children’s learning and development;
Support children’s well-being and sense of identity and belonging and Engage in practice that supports children’s critical thinking and learning, and promotes dispositions of mastery and agency;

Critically reflect on their practice and use their reflections to plan future practice and Organise, equip and manage an inclusive environment for young children and adults cognisant of affordance, constraints, needs and interests;
Contribute to and work effectively as part of a professional team and engage effectively with parents, families and the wider community;
Engage in praxiology (action research) and identify areas of continuing professional development;
Consider management strategies that support the sustainability of the setting;

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the economic issues involved in the organisation and undertaking of sporting and sports-related activities.
Topics that may be analysed include: demand for sport; pricing of sports events; Pareto efficiency; organisation of clubs and leagues; competitive balance; labour markets in sport; broadcasting of sport; public finance and sport; cheating in sport; gambling and sport.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the issues that are relevant to how sporting agents maximise their expected payoff.
Outline the rationale behind ticket-pricing schemes for sporting contests.
Discuss ways in which bargaining by sporting agents can increase economic efficiency.
Outline and critique ways in which competitive balance can be measured and how it can be compared within and across sports.
Demonstrate how attempts to improve competitive balance can depend on the structure of a league and objectives of club owners.
Evaluate whether government bodies should financially support the hosting of major sporting events and/or construction of facilities.
Determine what conditions are required to prevent cheating in sporting contests.
modules.notes: Analysis of sports data is a key component of the module.

modules.moduleContent: Modern research techniques and strategies in economics.
learningOutcomes: succinctly specify a research question.
place the proposed research in the literature.
identify the expected contribution and it importance
specify the methodological tools, theoretical, empirical or both, required to undertake the proposed research
explain why the proposed methodologies are the most appropriate to answer the research question
identify existing data, should it be required, to conduct the proposed research.
evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of the available data, should data be required for the proposed research

modules.moduleContent: Modern research techniques and strategies in economics

This module introduces students to the Philosophy of Education as one of the core foundational disciplines of the field. Students will be encouraged to understand key issues from multiple perspectives and to interrogate the fundamental assumptions and presuppositions shaping educational theory and practice, exploring areas such as ethics and existence in education, values and aims in education, knowledge and curriculum, identity and relationality. The module will also open up imaginative ways of re-engaging with these questions and support students in connecting theory to practice.

Students will be encouraged to understand key issues from multiple perspectives and to interrogate the fundamental assumptions and presuppositions shaping educational theory and practice, exploring areas such as ethics and existence in education, values and aims in education, knowledge and curriculum, identity and relationality. The module will also open up imaginative ways of re-engaging with these questions and support students in connecting theory to practice.
learningOutcomes: Describe, identify and analyse key educational concepts and themes.
Evaluate diverse perspectives and arguments in the field of education.
Effectively use a philosophical lens to enquire into a range of educational topics and issues.
Identify and evaluate the presuppositions underpinning different approaches to education.
Think critically about central educational problems, issues, and questions.
Analyse specific ethical issues, challenges and dilemmas arising from contemporary education.
Develop and critically assess arguments and positions informed by the wider field.

modules.moduleContent: School based experience on the BSc Science Education is of critical importance and provides students with opportunities to develop skills in planning, implementing and evaluating teaching and learning. Learning to be a teacher in the second-level system includes experience both inside the classroom, as teachers of specific subjects, and outside the classroom, as part of a team of educators who work with second-level students. The intention of the school-based dimensions of the BSc Science and Mathematics with Education is to give student teachers opportunities to experience the daily life of the school, both inside and outside the classroom. The expectation is that over the course of the five-year degree, school-based experience will provide important perspectives through which educational theory and practice will be viewed. The goal is to ensure that the science education students’ learning is practically relevant as well as built on a solid foundation of a coherent philosophy of learning and teaching.
learningOutcomes: observe, in a structured manner, school and classroom practice with cooperating teachers and university tutors
hone and develop skills in curriculum planning, subject knowledge, pedagogical content knowledge and related methodologies
reflect critically on practice on an ongoing basis so as to inform that practice
develop models of planning coherent, differentiated and integrated teaching programmes which are informed by ongoing reflection on professional practice
develop necessary technological skills to improve teaching

show an understanding of school culture and how it impacts on teaching and learning and implementation of curriculum policy;

create and maintain a safe, interactive and challenging environment using strategies that promote and maintain positive behaviour, in accordance with school policy.

develop strategies for the promotion of positive relationships and communicating effectively with pupils, parents, colleagues, the school principal, school management, co-professionals and the wider community and apply knowledge of the individual potential of pupils, dispositions towards learning, varying backgrounds, identities, experiences and learning styles to planning for teaching, learning and assessment
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Curriculum design, implementation and evaluation will be studied in this module. Students will engage with key readings in the area of STEM curriculum development. Students will investigate the relationship between curriculum theory and educational practice and the relationship between school programmes, society and the culture in which schools are located. The module will also include emphasis on the development of critical literacy and numeracy within each of the teaching subjects.
learningOutcomes: understand the dynamic processes by which curriculum is designed and implemented
apply knowledge of the individual potential of pupils, dispositions towards learning, varying backgrounds, identities and experiences to planning for teaching, learning and assessment
implement a range of methodologies to achieve planned outcomes
evaluate learner progress towards those outcomes
conduct and apply relevant research, as appropriate, to his/her teaching context: identifying, critically analysing and critically integrating new pedagogical insights into his/her practice
critically interrogate the process of curriculum development, design, implementation and evaluation
critically evaluate curriculum provision in the Irish context.

modules.moduleContent: The module chronologically studies the key historical landmarks and emergent themes in the evolution of the Irish education system. It explores contemporary issues and developments in Irish education in the light of their historical background and critically engages with a broad range of commission reports, policy statements and legislative documents pertinent to contemporary Irish educational history.
learningOutcomes: Articulate a cogent, balanced and knowledgeable appreciation of sectoral developments within education from earliest traditions to contemporary schooling systems.
Critically interrogate the array of controversies and complexities inherent in the historical education record.
Display an ability to draw on a diversity of considered viewpoints and skillfully marshal convincing interpretations from across a range of conflicting source evidence.

Display evidence of a critically reflective and analytical engagement with the broad range of commission reports, policy statements and legislative documents pertinent to the study of Irish educational history.
Articulate a decisive insight of the relationship between the nature and purpose of education and the wider imperatives and inputs of vested agencies.

modules.moduleName: LIFESPAN DEVELOPMENT AND THE PSYCHOLOGY OF THE ADOLESCENT
modules.moduleContent:
This module emphasizes areas of learning and career-related information, career counselling theories and include a 32-hour season school in psychometric testing that accredited BPS Ability Level. 

The focus is on developing students' awareness of the theories and historical development of careers guidance and counselling, and enabling them to apply and critique conceptual knowledge in practice. Emphasis is placed on developing skills in the application of counselling interventions to career decision-making. 

Psychometric testing aims to develop an understanding of the ethical and other critical issues involved in test selection, administration, interpretation of tests and feedback of results. Content includes a perspective on career guidance and counselling: origins, traditions and historical development; a study of key career theorists, career decision-making and practice; appropriate and ethical use of tests; recent and future trends in career development. Skills for career counselling interventions with students at any stage of their school career; a conceptual understanding of ICT and its basic applications; resources or guidance including Qualifax, Careers Portal, etc. Definitions and purposes of testing; the history of psychological testing, concepts such as intelligence, aptitude, interest; evaluative and ethical issues relating to selection, administration, interpretation of tests, feedback of test results and report writing. The Discreet Psychometric Testing module comprises a 32-hour, week-long module over the October mid-term break. Throughout Semester 1, students are also required to undertake a further 20 hours (minimum) in administering, scoring and giving feedback to students in an integrated vocational context.
learningOutcomes: Describe the main influences shaping human development;
Appreciate the interactive nature of genetic inheritance and environment;
Demonstrate an understanding of the developmental tasks of the period of adolescence;
Identify the key variables which influence young people in contemporary society;
Value the importance of mental health and recognize risks to mental health of young people;
Distinguish the factors which influence student behaviour especially in school contexts;
Recognize the role of the School Guidance Counsellor and key stakeholders in the lives of adolescents;

modules.moduleName: LEADING AND MANAGING THE GUIDANCE COUNSELLING SERVICE: PROFESSIONAL, ETHICAL AND REFLECTIVE PRACTICE
modules.moduleContent: Guidance counselling involves a special relationship of trust and requires the highest standards of professional, ethical and reflective practice. This module explores areas of competence required of the guidance counsellor. Content includes, ethical and legal perspectives on child protection and safeguarding, critical incident management, information processing and data management, assessments and evaluation; consultation and referral, collaboration, teamwork and accountability, and the process of planning and managing a whole school guidance service. Throughout the module, students will engage in critical reflection on their personal and professional values, self-knowledge and relational styles. Through an ongoing process of reflective journaling and discussion, students will be supported to develop accurate and compassionate self-awareness, as a necessary condition for effective guidance counselling.
learningOutcomes: Articulate a clear understanding of the role of the guidance counsellor as leader and manager of a school guidance counselling service.
Explain how a school operates as a system, and how the guidance counsellor's role is effective in that system.
Show a confident understanding of the management and work procedures for the school guidance service.
Operate strategies and skills which will enable effective collaboration with staff, parents, and external agencies.
Understanding their role within the whole-school guidance counselling team. Appreciate the legal and ethical implications of their role in the schools.
Demonstrate knowledge of pastoral care and social, personal and health programmes in schools. Appreciate how school guidance counsellors can exercise appropriate leadership in school contexts.

Reflect critically on their practice through use of logs, reflective journal and professional dialogue. Show that an integration of new understandings and awareness of self and others has been facilitated.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: LEADERSHIP FOR ENHANCING CULTURES OF COMMUNICATION
modules.moduleContent: In this module participants will be expected to engage with key concepts, theories, literature and practical understanding of school development and its many facets. Central to this is the cultivation of fruitful and positive relationships which will enhance the community of the school. The role of the leader in these endeavours is critical and central, and this module will give opportunities to participants to consider and engage with a ‘real-life’ learning situation where they are asked to ‘audit’ communication systems in their own school. The management of this process and resultant learning has application for the leadership role in participants’ schools as they begin to engage with colleagues in the completion of leadership tasks and in their future work.
learningOutcomes: Understand the critical nature of school culture and its influence on shaping school life.
Interrogate the core elements of leading a school which has successful communication systems in place.
Understand how to build such systems and how to ensure that a collaborative and inclusive process is used in doing so.
Engage with the developmental learning required in the building of healthy school cultures which facilitate and nurture positive relationships between all members of the community.
Interrogate and understand the nature of conflicts within the school community as they arise and consider the nature of ‘difficult conversations’ and resolutions to these issues for all.
Understand the theories relating to change processes and have an awareness of how to navigate these while still ensuring that the school community is in support.
Engage with and understand evaluative processes for the school and in the Irish context, while also considering the wider international context as applicable.

modules.moduleContent: This programme is aimed at practitioners who are working in contexts where trauma is impacting on the individuals and communities they serve. The module explores the nature, prevalence and impact of trauma, drawing on the latest theory and research in the field. Content includes adverse childhood experiences (ACEs), attachment disruption, community adversity, intergenerational trauma, and the conditions necessary for healing and resilience. Through discussion, reflective journalling and critical praxis, practitioners will develop an understanding of people’s lived experience through a trauma lens, and explore the implications for trauma-informed education, care, or service design within their organisations. With a strong grounding in mindful compassion, the module also supports practitioners to develop their own self-care strategies to help protect against vicarious trauma and compassion fatigue.
learningOutcomes: Explain how trauma impacts mind, body and behaviour.
Discuss trauma in relation to social injustices and the negative operation of power.
Discuss the knowledge, skills and attitudes required to practice trauma-informed care in various contexts.
Reflect upon one’s own practice and work environment to identify barriers and facilitators to adopting trauma-informed approaches.
Identify ways to embed trauma-informed principles into one’s own practice.
Consider and utilise self-care and self-compassion strategies and reflect on systemic structures required to enhance practitioner wellbeing.

modules.moduleName: TEACHING ABOUT TEACHING. FROM TEACHER TO TEACHER EDUCATOR
modules.moduleContent: Those who become teacher educators in the Republic of Ireland do so without any requirement to register with a professional body, without any formal period of study of the foundation disciplines of teacher education, without engagement with appropriate pedagogical strategies for teaching adults and with no formally supervised practice of teaching. This is not unique to Ireland. In almost all European countries one becomes a teacher educator without any formal qualification, preparation or induction into the role (Kosnik, 2008). Two key European Commission policy documents, The Green Paper on Teacher Education in Europe (Buchberger, Campos, Kallos & Stevenson, 2000) and Supporting Teacher Educators for Better Learning Outcomes (European Commission, 2012) have highlighted concern about the lack of training for and induction into the teacher education profession, including appropriate methodologies for working with adult learners. This module focuses on the cognitive and practical processes of transitioning from first-order to second-order practitioner, with specific emphasis on the knowledge and capabilities required.
learningOutcomes: Work with adult learners in facilitating their professional development in a range of roles on the continuum of teacher education: initial, induction and in-career education
Demonstrate an advanced capability in interrogating and developing practitioner knowledge by critical reflection on first order experience
Conduct one-to-one tutoring sessions competently with beginning and experienced teachers
Conduct small group seminars competently with beginning and experienced teachers
Design and develop curriculum and assessment for teacher education

modules.moduleContent: This module seeks to engage participants in a collaborative, critical and creative exploration of the central concepts, assumptions and considerations involved in 'Pedagogical Thinking'. For the purpose of this module, we will conceive of Pedagogy (or a pedagogy) as a system of educational purposes, principles and practices. As a way of scaffolding our thinking, participants will be asked to develop their own pedagogical framework. We will draw upon our own experience as students and practitioners and make connections to ideas from fields including, philosophy, sociology and psychology. Our inquiry will call on us to confront questions about the status of children in contemporary culture, the privileged status of particular ways of knowing, the role of school in society and the kind of life we deem worth living. A committed and continuous connection to practice (both practical and potential) will ground our inquiry and bring our thinking to bear on our daily lives.
learningOutcomes: • Identify and expose the assumptions associated with actual and proposed, contemporary practices
• Articulate their personal views on the purpose of education in society
• Identify practical methods that align with their views
• Account for and justify the ethical and methodological principles that inform their pedagogical commitments
• Understand and apply a general pedagogical framework to critically evaluate models of teaching, schooling and education.

modules.moduleName: CONTEMPORARY ISSUES IN WORKING WITH FAMILIES AND COMMUNITIES
modules.moduleContent: This module will support professionals in early years in building effective partnerships with families and communities. It will consider a range of theoretical perspectives on working with families and communities and the contemporary issues that impact on these relationships.
learningOutcomes: Examine a range of theoretical perspectives on working with families and communities
Critically reflect on contemporary issues - social, economic and cultural contexts of families and their impact on children’s lives
Forge respectful and ethical partnerships with a range of stakeholders -children, families, colleagues, professionals, community
Critically appraise national policies that impact on early years professional practice in working with diverse families and children

modules.moduleContent: This module discusses the nature, prevalence and impact of childhood adversity and it considers what the appropriate educational responses might be. Childhood adversity - whether resulting from social injustices, poverty, discrimination or trauma, like abuse and violence - is common and the effects can be devastating. Recognising that all teachers have contact with students who have been affected by overwhelming adverse experiences, this module draws on latest research in trauma-informed approaches in education. Students will be supported to identify ways that they can integrate strengths-based, trauma-informed principles into their everyday educational practice. With a strong grounding in mindful compassion, the module also supports students to develop their own personal self-care strategies.
learningOutcomes: Explain how childhood adversity and trauma impact mind, body and behaviour.
Discuss the knowledge, skills and attitudes required to practice trauma-informed care in schools or other educational environments.
Reflect upon own practice and work environment to identify barriers and facilitators to adopting a trauma-informed educational approach.
Identify ways to embed trauma-informed principles into own educational practice.

Consider and utilise self-care and self-compassion strategies, and reflect on how support structures for teachers/education professionals could be integrated more systemically into education practice and policy.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides participants with the opportunity to engage in writing and to be part of a writing group. It will focus on supporting participants in developing their academic writing, including the use of referencing software. It will also assist participants in effectively communicating their ideas in both written and oral forms.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate the understanding of writing as process, including researching, drafting, editing, and proofreading.
Communicate their ideas in a clear manner, with appropriate academic conventions.
Edit their own writing, paying attention to issues of clarity, coherence and logic.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Professional and practitioner knowledge modules (with a total weighting of 60 credits) run throughout the four years of the programme, developing the participants’ capabilities as critically reflective practitioners. These professional discourse modules allow a student to: 

Reflect on the contribution of new knowledge on their practice e.g. improved professional standards in their professional practice, developed new/improved guidelines in their professional practice, increased inter-agency collaboration  

Build network links within practice and academic communities  

Disseminate research to peers in practice  
Note that professional practice learning and reflection occurs concurrently in the student’s professional workplace/practice, captured/informed by the proposed modules, while undertaking the Ed D.
learningOutcomes: Make explicit connections between their everyday practices as professionals and their doctoral studies.
Articulate the specific contribution(s) of their studies within their practice.
Critically engage with the interplay between disciplinary concepts and professional practice.

modules.moduleName: EARLY CHILDHOOD LEARNING AND DEVELOPMENT: PRACTICES (BIRTH-6 YEARS)
modules.moduleContent: The module will explore key psychological processes, theories and concepts in relation to early childhood (birth – 6years). Theories of attachment, gender identity, self-regulation, language development will be considered. The module will explore current research on developmental neuroscience and its relevance for learning, development and motivation in early childhood. Throughout the module, students will build on their own experiences, reflect on the interplay between theory and practice and apply theories to practice.
learningOutcomes: Understand and analyse key psychological processes, theories and concepts in relation to early childhood learning and development.
Interpret the implications of brain development for babies’, toddlers’ and young children’s learning and development.
Outline the development of gender and identity in babies, toddlers and young children and examine the implications for pedagogical practice.
Explore the formation of relationships and friendships and their role in supporting well-being, identity and belonging.
Examine the emergence, development and use of language(s)and communication in early childhood.

Describe how to support and respond appropriately to babies’, toddlers’ and young children’s behaviour using psychological theories, adopting a democratic and inclusive approach that values diversity.

modules.moduleContent: This module will allow students to further develop knowledge of working with parents and local communities (knowledge about families, poverty and diversity). In particular, this module will be underpinned by bioecological theory (Bronfenbrenner and Morris, 2006), which highlights children developing in the places, spaces and contexts of local and distal communities.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse the establishment of partnership, relationships with communities based on mutual understanding, trust and cooperation;
Organise initiatives involving parents, as well as members of local communities;
Show evidence of involving parents in decision-making processes and taking their perspectives into account in their practice;
Relate bioecological theory to practice in early childhood settings, with particular reference to parents and communities.

modules.moduleContent: This module will support educators to broaden their own knowledge-base in the areas of the arts to more effectively support children’s creativity, imagination and learning in these dimensions of the curriculum. Within this module, students will also examine the wide range of resources required and available within the setting and the broader community to realise the curriculum. Finally, the module will focus on the identification and implementation of pedagogical practices that will inspire imagination and foster creativity while enhancing children’s holistic development.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of the arts and its role in early childhood care and education (birth to six years)
Support and appraise children’s modes of representation and expression;
Identify the range of resources and stimuli required to effectively implement arts within the curriculum drawing on theory and linking to National frameworks;
Draw on community resources to enhance children’s experiences of art and to collaborate with local agencies, arts organisations and artists;
Implement practices within the curriculum that support children’s engagement with dance, music, movement, drama, literature and visual arts;
Facilitate, extend and support children’s imaginative engagement through playful exploration and artistic co-creation.

modules.moduleName: INCLUSIVE PRACTICE FOR CHILDREN AND FAMILIES
modules.moduleContent: This module aims to provide a platform for students to continue to explore and cultivate their awareness of including children with additional needs in early childhood care and education settings. It will progress students’ understanding of the historical development of provision for children with additional needs from both a national and international perspective. The theory and practice that underpins the care and education of children with extra needs will be considered. Local policies (LINC, Diversity and Inclusion Charter) will be interrogated. The three key areas associated with effectively including children with additional needs, developing an inclusive culture; engaging in inclusive practice and adopting an inclusive pedagogy, identified by Access and Inclusion Model (AIM), will be reviewed.
learningOutcomes: Describe the historical situation of inclusive practices in the broader local, national and international context.
Identify the theory and pedagogical practices impacting on the inclusion of children with extra needs in an early childhood education and care setting.
Develop a basic understanding of the key policies and legislation pertaining to the inclusion of children with additional needs in an early childhood care and education setting.
Explore and develop an understanding of how the Access and Inclusion Model (AIM) works to support inclusion in early childhood education and care settings.
Describe how their own inclusive pedagogy and practices supports an inclusive culture in an early childhood education and care setting.
Discuss how collaboration with colleagues, parents and other professionals promotes children’s and families’ rights and participation.

modules.moduleContent: This module will support educators to broaden their own knowledge-base in the areas of the arts (visual arts, music, movement, drama, literature and dance) to more effectively support children’s creativity, imagination and learning in these dimensions of the curriculum. Within this module, students will also examine the wide range of resources required and available within the setting and the broader community to realise the curriculum. Finally, the module will focus on the identification and implementation of pedagogical practices that will inspire imagination and foster creativity while enhancing children’s holistic development.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of the arts and its role in early childhood care and education for babies, toddlers and young children (birth to six years).
Support and appraise children’s modes of representation and expression.
Identify the range of resources and stimuli necessary to effectively implement arts within the curriculum drawing on theory and linking to the national curriculum framework.
Utilise community resources to enhance children’s experiences of art and to collaborate with local agencies, arts organisations and artists.
Plan for the implementation of a curriculum that supports children’s engagement with visual arts, music, movement, drama, literature and dance.
Facilitate, extend and support children’s imaginative engagement through playful exploration and artistic co-creation.

modules.moduleContent: Study abroad in Etvos Lorand Budapest.
learningOutcomes: Learning & Development: Identify implications for policy and practice, with particular reference to the professional role of the educator in creating nurturing, playful environments for children’s relationships, and critiquing the impact of systems’ functioning on this with reference to the concept of a ‘competent system’.
Creativity & Imagination: Facilitate, extend and support children’s imaginative engagement through playful exploration and artistic co-creation
Supporting Enquiry/ Enacting the Curriculum: Critically discuss curriculum as enquiry led and emergent and the image of the child underpinning curriculum.

modules.moduleName: REFLECTIVE PRACTICE: EDUCATOR AS ACTION RESEARCHER
modules.moduleContent: This module will provide a structured reflective space for students to share and disseminate learning. The module will also further develop students’ knowledge and skills of educational research methodologies and key methodological issues. Students will use these skills to design and implement an Action Research Project.
learningOutcomes: Integrate the theory and practice of reflection in all aspects of early childhood education (birth to six years);
Engage in group reflections as a community of learners to construct and share understandings of theory and practice;
Identify and apply ethical considerations in conducting research with young children;
Write an Action Research Proposal and design and implement a small-scale action research project discussing the implications for policy and practice;
Acknowledge, examine and synthesise links between relevant theories and practice.

modules.moduleName: LEADERSHIP & MANAGEMENT IN EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION & CARE
modules.moduleContent: This module draws on leadership and management theories and relates it to the field of Early Childhood Education and Care. It aims to create opportunities for students to strengthen their knowledge of policies and sectoral supports as well as their professional skills and practices while developing their ability to guide others to improve pedagogical practice. The module reflects a deep understanding of the contextual issues and challenges facing early childhood leaders in changing social and practice contexts.
learningOutcomes: Explain theories, practices and approaches to leadership and mentoring in the context of ECEC (birth to six years).
Analyse the relationship between leadership and power; management; gender; culture and children’s rights.
Develop effective and democratic organisational structures, environments and conditions in the ECEC settings to develop philosophical approaches through professional inquiry and critical reflection.

Appraise effective and democratic leadership to support the effective operation of a sustainable, ethical and legislatively compliant ECEC setting, taking cognisance of current policy and legislative requirements.

Identify relevant supports for educators with particular focus on a commitment to their welfare and wellbeing both in ECEC settings and within the sector, with reference to national voluntary supports, statutory supports and unions.
Consider business models that support the effective operation of an ECEC setting, including relevant national funding programmes.

modules.moduleContent:
This course is an introduction to advanced macroeconomic theory. This an introduction to the rational forward-looking approach to macroeconomics and its application to macroeconomic topics, such as the determination of household consumption.

If selecting this modules, it is recommended that students will have already successfully completed EC202A or EC202B and EC205 in addition to the pre.requisites of EC103 or FN106
learningOutcomes: Discuss the IS – MP model and how it can be used for policy analysis
Discuss how the preferences of governments and interest groups affect macroeconomic outcomes
Discuss the links between financial crises and the real economy.
Discuss optimal monetary policy and how this relates to the policies of the major central banks.
Discuss fiscal policy and the causes of fiscal crises

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to Mathematical Economics.  Static and dynamic optimisation. Theory and examples.
learningOutcomes: Write an economic problem in a logically consistent, concise and mathematical way
Use the tools of mathematics to solve economic problems
Assess the usefulness and limitations of the quantitative approach to economics

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to key econometric techniques used for applied causal analysis. Students will first be introduced to the key data management tasks required prior to undertaking any empirical research. Students will then be introduced to the latest techniques for causal inference, which may include multivariate regression, maximum likelihood, difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity and instrumental variables. For each estimator, students will learn about the history of its development; the theory and assumptions underlying the approach; and practical implementation. Students will have the opportunity to apply estimators to real-world data using statistical software.
learningOutcomes: Source and retrieve economic data from archives such as ISSDA and the CSO
Undertake the necessary data management required for causal analysis
Understand, perform and interpret linear regressions, including conducting hypothesis tests
Understand when linear regression is not suitable for causal analysis

Understand and perform estimation of causal effects using alternative estimators; these may include regression discontinuity estimators, difference-in-differences estimators and instrumental variable (IV) estimators
Interpret and explain the output of these alternative estimators.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This course examines the economics of the European Union. Topics covered may include the theory of the positive and negative effects of the customs unions on welfare; the benefits of the single market; the theory of optimal currency areas and monetary union; the Euro crisis of 2009-13; European labour markets; the Common Agricultural Policy; competition policy; EU trade policy; and Brexit.
modules.notes: GenAI used in CA assignment

modules.moduleContent:
This course will introduce students to the core topics in international macroeconomics. During the classes, I will present the most relevant economic models to frame the insights in a technical, yet accessible, way. Real world examples will be introduced to clarify and justify the interest in the issues covered, and I will pay a particular attention to the unique Irish environment. Findings from the existing economic literature will also be presented.

The main topics covered are: the balance of payment, international capital flows, exchange rate determination and parity conditions. Some topics in international trade will also be covered.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of the history and possible futures of international financial regimes
Explain the different exchange rate quotes and instruments and the differences between them
Access, graph, manipulate and analysis exchange rates quotes and other international financial information
Demonstrate knowledge of how exchange rates are determined and how exchange rate fluctuations affect the real economy
modules.notes: Students must access online data sources and use graphing tools to analyse them.

modules.moduleContent: This is a course on economic growth. We will review some stylized facts in economic growth and then study the most relevant and up-to-date theoretical models that can explain the performance of economies in the long run. This includes models with exogenous and endogenous growth and models that explain economic growth thousands of years ago. We will also study traditional and new empirical strategies to test growth models.

The objective of this module is to explore the key contextual factors and milestones that have shaped the current education system in Ireland. Using an inquiry-based and thematic approach, students will explore the historical policy and legislative documents that underpinned the education system and reflect on the impact of salient historic milestones on current educational provisions. The module cross-references relevant international developments to facilitate comparative analyses.

The module is designed to encourage students to develop a holistic view of education as a practice and to enable them to understand the current education landscape in its historical context. Challenging students’ own assumptions and beliefs and supporting them to articulate their own stances and perspectives across a range of issues (such as the purposes of education, patronage and management, equality, curricular and system reform, partnership, etc.) are core to the module. Extensive discussion on and critique of key readings, theories and historical documentation are central features of the inquiry-based approach of the module.
learningOutcomes: Critically understand the key milestones and contextual factors in the historical evolution of the education system in Ireland.
Construct and sustain critical and coherently reasoned arguments about key issues that have shaped Irish education.
Reflect and communicate the impact of this historical evolution on the current system of education and their professional practice as student teachers.
Display an ability to draw on a diversity of considered viewpoints and marshal convincing interpretations from across a range of source evidence.
Critically engage with the broad range of commission reports, policy statements and legislative documents pertinent to the study of the education system in Ireland.

modules.moduleContent: School-based experience on the BSc Science and Maths Education is of critical importance and provides students with opportunities to develop skills in planning, implementing and evaluating teaching and learning. Learning to be a teacher in the second-level system includes experience both inside the classroom, as teachers of specific subjects, and outside the classroom, as part of a team of educators who work with second-level students. The intention of the school-based dimensions of the BSc Science Education is to give student teachers opportunities to experience the daily life of the school, both inside and outside the classroom. The expectation is that over the course of the five-year degree, school-based experience will provide important perspectives through which educational theory and practice will be viewed. The goal is to ensure that the science education students’ learning is practically relevant as well as built on a solid foundation of a coherent philosophy of learning and teaching.
learningOutcomes: observe, in a structured manner, school and classroom practice with cooperating teachers and university tutors
hone and develop skills in curriculum planning, subject knowledge, pedagogical content knowledge and related methodologies
reflect critically on practice on an ongoing basis so as to inform that practice
develop models of planning coherent, differentiated and integrated teaching programmes which are informed by ongoing reflection on professional practice
show an understanding of school culture and how it impacts on teaching and learning and implementation of curriculum policy

develop strategies for the promotion of positive relationships and communicating effectively with pupils, parents, colleagues, the school principal, school management, co-professionals and the wider community

apply knowledge of the individual potential of pupils, dispositions towards learning, varying backgrounds, identities, experiences and learning styles to planning for teaching, learning and assessment

modules.moduleName: TEACHER AS A CRITICALLY REFLECTIVE PRACTITIONER
modules.moduleContent: The process of becoming a teacher involves changing one’s perspective. This module facilitates that transition using the lenses of Philosophy of Education, Sociology of Education and Psychology of Education to provide the necessary conceptual tools. The philosophical writings in the area of reflective teaching and diversity in educational settings will be explored.
learningOutcomes: critically evaluate their own and others teaching
use digital technology to support self evaluation and reflection
critically integrate theory with practice in real contexts
participate in a professional learning community and contribute in a meaningful way to the group learning processes
develop skills in communication, working with others, and being personally effective
research, develop and present findings using a variety of media.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: CHEMISTRY, PHYSICS AND BIOLOGY  TEACHING AND LEARNING
modules.moduleContent: The focus of this module will be on enactive pedagogies. Students will consider the influences of the wider community, school ethos and classroom interactions on science instruction. Issues related to assessment for, of and as learning will be examined. The use of technology in STEM teaching and learning will be explored. Students will examine the nature of science and of science learning, through engagement with research literature and lesson study in professional learning groups.
learningOutcomes: show a mastery of key concepts in STEM pedagogy

develop skills in scientific enquiry, including the ability to interpret and analyse qualitative and quantitative data from different sources and to consider the validity and reliability of data in presenting and justifying conclusion

develop skills in laboratory procedures and techniques, including the use of technology, carried out with due regard for safety, together with the ability to assess the uses and limitations of these procedures through engagement in a wide variety of practical activities
combine content knowledge of STEM education with appropriate pedagogical strategies for inquiry-based science teaching and learning
deploy a range of pedagogical methods to support students’ learning of STEM
explore the needs of various groups of students and consider the range of factors that can influence learning outcomes
demonstrate understanding of relevant  assessment practices
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: MATHEMATICS, COMPUTER SCIENCE AND APPLIED MATHEMATICS: TEACHING, LEARNING AND ASSESSMENT
modules.moduleContent: The general aim of this module is to develop deeper pedagogical content knowledge in Computer Science teaching and incorporate various teaching strategies to teach at senior cycle level. A particular focus will be placed on supporting students to use active methodologies in their teaching. In this module, there will be a special emphasis on differentiation strategies to consider needs of all students in a STEM classroom environment. Prerequisites: ED312
learningOutcomes: Deploy a range of pedagogical tools, including ICT resources, to support students’ learning of STEM
Combine content knowledge with appropriate pedagogical strategies in STEM teaching for understanding
Demonstrate understanding of relevant assessment practices in STEM education
Develop strategies to embed literacy and numeracy into STEM teaching
Explore the needs of various groups of students and consider the range of factors that can influence learning outcomes
Support students in reflecting on, selecting, developing and critically appraising relevant technologies and teaching techniques for possible use in their professional lives
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: CAREER THEORY AND PROFESSIONAL PRACTICE/PSYCHOMETRIC TESTING
modules.moduleContent:
The focus is on developing the students’ awareness of the theories and historical development of vocational guidance and counselling and enabling them to apply and critique conceptual knowledge in practical settings. There is an emphasis on developing skills to apply counselling interventions to career decision-making.  With psychometric testing, the rationale is to cultivate an understanding of the ethical and other critical issues involved in test selection, administration, interpretation of tests and feedback of results.
