Content includes perspective on career guidance and counselling: origins, traditions and historical evolution; a study of the main Vocational Theorists; career-decision-making and practice; appropriate and ethical use of tests; Recent and future trends in career developments. Skills for career counselling interventions with students at any stage of their school career; a conceptual understanding of ICT and its basic applications; resources or guidance including Qualifax, Careers Portal etc. Definitions and purposes of tests; The history of psychological testing; Concepts such as intelligence, aptitude, interest; Evaluative and ethical issues surrounding selection, administration, interpretation of tests, feedback of test results and report writing. Discreet Psychometric Testing module includes a 32 hours, week long module over the October midterm break. Throughout semester 1 students are also required to do a further 20 hours (at least) in administering, scoring tests and giving feedback to students in an integrated vocational context.
learningOutcomes: Develop a perspective on the origin of career guidance and its evolutionary and revisionary nature.
Articulate with familiarity a range of vocational guidance theories and their application.
Appreciate the multicultural dimensions of vocational choice.
Demonstrate competence in the skills required to conduct interviews with a range of clients.
Use a range of information and communications technology to support vocational guidance.
Demonstrate an awareness and understanding of the ethical issues involved in test selection, administration, interpretation of tests, feedback of results.
Appreciate the underlying concepts of certain tests, e.g. aptitude, intelligence, ability, interest, with a view to determining if and/or how these can best be measured.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module provides students with the skills and competencies required to critically engage in the practice of guidance counselling in a school setting. It facilitates the integration and application of theory with professional and ethical practice. The school placement practice includes conducting vocational guidance interviews, personal counselling sessions, guidance-related group work, delivering guidance classes, participating in school care-team meetings, engaging in one-to-one meetings with mentoring guidance counsellor, and participating in all aspects of the management of the school guidance counselling service. As an extra support for trainee guidance counsellors related to school placement practice, small group-based supervision sessions are offered in Semester 2. These aim to mirror and prepare for the practice of professional supervision in which qualified guidance counsellors engage.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate professional and ethical competence in conducting vocational guidance interviews, personal counselling sessions, guidance-related group work, and care-team meetings.
Engage in professional conversations with mentoring guidance counsellor, and participate in all aspects of the management of the school guidance counselling service.
Facilitate guidance-related group work in personal, educational and career development.
Plan and deliver guidance classes with different year groups.
Demonstrate advanced administrative skills pertaining to the role as a school guidance counsellor.
Reflect critically on one’s own practice with the use of school placement journal. Demonstrate an openness to feedback and a willingness to adapt practice in response to feedback.
Integrate theory, research and insights gained from other modules on the programme with school-based guidance counselling practice.

modules.moduleName: RESEARCHING THE EDUCATION GUIDANCE COUNSELLING CONTEXT
modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to provide the students with the skills to critically engage in research about issues of relevance to guidance counselling in an educational setting. Students are required to design a group-based research project that will demonstrate their knowledge of research methodology in an area of education guidance policy and practice.

The module will:

· provide students with the conceptual knowledge and capacities to understand, design and conduct research in their professional field.

· Enable students to critically assess, communicate and engage in research activities in their professional field
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate familiarity with key concepts and theories used in social science research.
Demonstrate a capacity to critically assess existing research reports, policy and theoretical literature in the field.
Appreciate basic principles and processes involved in conducting research in an educational context.
Appreciate the ethical considerations which must be adhered to when conducting research.
Apply some of these techniques and principles in the design of their own research projects.

modules.moduleName: LEADERSHIP FOR QUALITY TEACHING AND LEARNING: LINKING POLICY, RESEARCH AND PRACTICE
modules.moduleContent: A key aim of this module will be to evaluate national and international criteria in terms of understanding what constitutes quality teaching and learning and explore those practices of curriculum-focused leadership which nurture such environments. It will explore different perspectives on learning with particular attention paid to theories and principles of metacognition and self-regulated learning. The module will also trace and analyse key national and international policy developments relevant to quality teaching and learning. These policy developments include, but are not restricted to: Special Education Policy and Practice, Information and Communication Technologies, School Governance and Assessment.
learningOutcomes: On completion of this module students will be familiar with principles and practices of leadership of and for learning and what constitutes quality teaching and learning within the school as learning environment. Quality as understood here is different to that of effectiveness of teaching and learning which is grounded in neo-liberal, technical functional modes of measurement.

Students will have explored a range of pertinent research insights relevant to practices of leadership of and for learning and analysed key national and international policy developments relevant to quality teaching and learning.

modules.moduleName: CONTEMPORARY ISSUES IN WORKING WITH FAMILIES AND COMMUNITIES
modules.moduleContent: This module will support professionals in early years in building effective partnerships with families and communities. It will consider a range of theoretical perspectives on working with families and communities and the contemporary issues that impact on these relationships.
learningOutcomes: examine a range of theoretical perspectives on working with families and communities
critically reflect on contemporary issues - social, economic and cultural contexts of families and their impact on children’s lives
forge respectful and ethical partnerships with a range of stakeholders -children, families, colleagues, professionals, community
critically appraise national policies that impact on early years professional practice in working with diverse families and children

modules.moduleContent:
This module engages with and builds on the learning of Research Methods (ED684) module. The design assists students in carrying out an independent capstone project from its inception through to writing up for submission. The Capstone Project is a summative piece of work that enables students to explore an area of interest to them. The project is intended to develop the students’ critical skills in applying theories and methods to work within a defined academic area. The assignment is a coherent presentation of a project demonstrating an ability to clearly articulate the focus of the research, formulate a research question and conduct a unique practice-based research project. The research design must provide a clear rationale for choice of methodology, literature, and evaluation.

The focus must be drawn from one of leadership-focused thematic areas:

1. Leading & Managing Educational Innovation  
2. Leadership for Enhancing Cultures of Communication 
3. The Person and Professional: Who Am I as a Leader 
4. Educational Leadership: Legal Contexts, Policy & Practice 
5. Coaching & Mentoring in & as Leadership Practice 

All students are offered the support of a supervisor and the supervision model is designed in order to provide structured supports for peer-to-peer learning in a group setting alongside individual supervision.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate ability to undertake research that is responsive to professional practice.
Display an ability to engage critically with issues relating to methodologies and ethics.
Evidence of critical engagement with literature.
Demonstrate evidence of process of critical reflection and learning on your journey as leader and researcher.
Provide rationale for choice and importance of project given leadership within school/education context, culture, and needs.
Demonstrate how the project foregrounds a ‘leadership’ lens.

modules.moduleName: SHARING THE WORLD: EDUCATION, PLURALISM, AND CONFLICT
modules.moduleContent: This module will address contemporary issues relating to difference, pluralism, inclusion, and identity. It will examine the relationship between education, concepts of pluralism and what it means to share the world. In so doing, it will address positions that may be understood as ‘extremist’ in that they seek purity of identity, are hostile to compromise, and are intolerant of difference. For this reason, particular attention is paid to racism. Key thinkers introduced in the course will include Glissant, Wynter, Andreotti, and Gilroy amongst others. The embodiment of ideas in practice can be challenging. This module will explore ‘how ideas feel’ and the investment in different positions on a personal and professional level, in terms of educational cultures, and institutionally. It offers a pedagogical and experiential framework to analyse and unpack injustices and exclusions, alongside skills to engage in difficult conversations. The course will introduce a range of creative pedagogical approaches to support educators in exploring and addressing these issues.
learningOutcomes: Students will have a critical understanding of key theoretical perspectives relating to difference, pluralism, and identity and will be able to analyse and practically respond to those positions that are divisive or even hateful.
Students will have a pedagogical repertoire to support listening, dialogue, creativity, and imagination in unpacking these issues in education.

modules.moduleName: UDL:LEARNING AND TEACHING WITH VARIABILITY IN MIND
modules.moduleContent: This module is aimed at but not exclusive to early childhood, primary and post-primary teachers and teacher educators. The module explores the move away from thinking about learning, teaching and assessment in terms of ability and disability to thinking in terms of designing learning, teaching and assessment experiences from the mindset of variability. The module begins with developing an understanding of the concept of variability before exploring the key concepts of UDL. Through discussions, professional reflections on practice and the critical examination of a chosen learning, teaching and assessment experience, participants will develop a knowledge and understanding of the three principles of the UDL Guidelines and begin applying these principles to their practice.
learningOutcomes: Explain the concept of variability.
Discuss the key concepts of UDL.
Critically reflect on the goals of their learning, teaching and assessment design and delivery through a UDL lens.
Discuss the three principles of UDL in the context of their educational setting and practice.
Apply appropriate aspects of the UDL guidelines to their learning, teaching and assessment planning, design and delivery.
Redesign a learning, teaching and assessment experience to make one change based on their understanding of variability and the UDL guidelines.

modules.moduleContent: Academic Writing 2 – Developing your writing process builds on the work of the previous module, with an emphasis on the process of writing. Small writing groups will be created to support the students as they progress their capacity to structure their writing for different genres and audiences. The seminars will offer students a group-based approach to undertaking their writing and will provide opportunities to present and discuss writing concerns within a supportive, collaborative atmosphere.
learningOutcomes: Design an academic poster and a presentation that demonstrate clarity of expression, readability and fluency.

modules.moduleContent: This module aims to help students explore and develop an understanding of inclusive practice and its relevance to working with children whose family backgrounds, ethnicity and levels of physical and intellectual ability varies. An Anti-Bias Curriculum approach will be appraised and the role of the educator with regards to ensuring access, participation, equality, diversity and inclusion will be evaluated. Strategies to support the inclusion of all in early year’s settings will be reviewed.
learningOutcomes: Identify a range of needs that might require additional support in early year’s settings;
Examine the national support frameworks and practice guidelines and review their impact on the children, their families and practice in early year’s settings;
Explore the Anti-Bias Curriculum and its implementation in early year’s settings;
Review relevant policies and procedures within early year’s settings;
Consider the role of the educator in relation to an inclusive early year’s environment;
Identify the various strategies and techniques that support the inclusion of all children in early year’s settings.

modules.moduleName: REFLECTIVE PRACTICE: INTEGRATING THEORY AND PRACTICE
modules.moduleContent: This module will build on previous learning to further develop educators’ knowledge and skills to study effectively and present academic assignments in an appropriate format. The module will explore understandings of ‘reflection’ as it relates to student’s personal and Professional development.
learningOutcomes: Analyse the implications of own attitudes, values, beliefs and assumptions in relation to issues of relevant policies and best practice.
Describe models of reflection drawing on relevant theory (including National frameworks) and critically reflect and document reflections using a reflective model.

Compare and contrast a variety of models of reflection, and evaluate their usefulness for practice in the context of ongoing personal and professional growth, goals and philosophy of and for early childhood education and care.
Identify and document the personal qualities and skills needed to be an effective early childhood educator, and identify learning and personal development goals linking theory and practice.
Develop communication approaches that will assist in their personal and professional lives, and consider the skills necessary to enable them to give and receive feedback in an effective manner.

Develop academic skills to critique and synthesise information from a range of material and write appropriately, following the conventions of language usage, applying the conventions of Harvard referencing.

modules.moduleContent: This module seeks to enhance students' understanding of children's play and to support them to engage with diverse perspectives and approaches. It provides a theoretical and practice foundation for implementing a play-based curriculum in the early years. A critical awareness of the complex relationship between play and well-being, identity and belonging, communicating and exploring and thinking (Aistear 2009) will facilitate a reflective approach to play pedagogy. Beginning with reflections on play experiences, the module moves to provide for the critical examination of theoretical, historical and cultural perspectives on play, followed by a detailed analysis of what children do in play in a cross section of settings and contexts. Finally, it builds the capacity of students to provide for, support, and assess children’s learning through play.
learningOutcomes: Locate play within its historical, cultural, social and philosophical foundations and examine the role of play in the lives of young children (birth to six years);
Evaluate children’s experiences in play using a variety of theoretical perspectives and national frameworks;
Support children through play, cognisant of diversity;
Integrate play into their practice;
Engage family and community in children’s learning and development through play.

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to introduce, explore, and analyse the vital role of children’s environments in relation to their well-being, health, development, and learning.  The module is underpinned by the recognition that early year’s environments must be seen in the context of children’s rights and needs. It is vital that these spaces offer quality experiences and home from home atmospheres. Throughout this module, indoor, outdoor and local area environments are given due attention as students are supported to consider these environments and their affordances to the developing child. Addressing the concept of Universal Design and guided by the Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework and Síolta, the National Quality Framework, environments will be explored in relation to Policy and Legislation, Sensory/Motor Needs, The Affective Environment, Curriculum Approaches, Pedagogical Values, Emerging Interests and Play Needs, Layout, Materials and Activities.
learningOutcomes: Analyse environments from the perspective of the rights of children to quality indoor, outdoor, and local area environments and identify the unique affordances of indoor, outdoor, and local area environments and how they intertwine to provide holistic experiences
Appraise the legislative requirements relating to the physical environment of an ECEC setting and explore the main elements of Universal Design and its impact on early year’s provision
Critically examine theories of physical and affective environment as sites for encounters and nurturing relationships and consider the concept of the environment as the 3rd teacher
Compare and contrast the varied environments of a range of curriculum approaches and articulate how the environments provided supports the chosen curriculum /pedagogical approach being implemented

Design a range of environments that support implementation of Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework, including the selection of quality layouts, resources, loose parts, materials and equipment that meet the changing needs of the developing child
Summarise and discuss the opportunities and challenges of a risk benefit approach to early year’s environment design

modules.moduleName: EXPLORING CURRICULA AND PEDAGOGY: SUPPORTING ENQUIRY
modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to positively promote children’s dispositions and capabilities in the all areas of learning and development. Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework, will be used to support effective pedagogy and curriculum development. 
Adopting a child-centred curriculum approach that supports children to develop in their own way and at their own pace, this module considers the essential concepts and content that the educator needs to positively influence children’s learning and development. The pedagogical approaches of play and enquiry together with other effective approaches to curriculum development will be examined. Consideration will be given to the resources within the setting and the broader community to effectively support children’s learning and development.
learningOutcomes: Describe the image of the child underpinning your curriculum.
Explore curriculum as enquiry led, integrated and emergent.
Describe how Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework, the Early Childhood Curriculum Framework can be used to support curriculum development and practice.
Develop and implement an enquiry led curriculum for children (birth to six).
Demonstrate the skills of observation, documentation and assessment of children’s learning.
Reflect on the role of play as a pedagogy in high quality early year’s curriculum.

modules.moduleContent: This module seeks to build on students' concept of children's play/playful learning and will support them to engage with diverse perspectives and approaches. It provides a theoretical and practice foundation for implementing a play-based curriculum in the early years. A critical awareness of the complex relationship between play, children’s learning & development and the themes of Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework will facilitate a reflective approach to play pedagogy. Beginning with reflections on play experiences, the module will provide opportunities for the critical examination of theoretical, historical and cultural perspectives on play, followed by a detailed analysis of different kinds of play and what children do in play in a cross section of settings and contexts. Finally, it builds the capacity of students to provide for, support, and assess children’s learning through play.
learningOutcomes: Locate play within its historical, cultural, social and philosophical foundations and examine the role of play in the lives of young children (birth to six years).
Support and appraise children’s experiences in play using a variety of theoretical perspectives and the national frameworks.
Analyse the nature of play as pedagogy in the early year’s curriculum.
Appraise the role of adult intervention in play in specific contexts.
Identify the factors which influence children’s play and learning and reflect on how these issues affect practice, including the impact of play spaces.
Utilise assessment strategies of, through and for children’s play and learning and development informed by Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework.
Collaborate with families and community to enhance children’s learning and development through play.

modules.moduleContent: This module will use a ‘spiral curriculum’ approach to build upon the learning from module EDC212. It will continue to locate, expand and apply theories of learning and development under the four strands of Aistear (Well-Being; Identity and Belonging; Communicating; Exploring and Thinking). It will interrogate the implications for practice of theoretical perspectives. Students will be encouraged to further develop their criticality in engaging with psychological theory, particularly extending their learning to deconstruct implications for policy and practice in the ECEC setting
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse how early relationships may impact on later development  and defend a focus on relationships as a core element of pedagogical quality

Identify implications for policy and practice, with particular reference to the  professional role of the educator in creating nurturing, playful environments  for children’s relationships, and critiquing the impact of systems’ functioning  on this with reference to the concept of a ‘competent system’
Further interpret the implications of brain development for children’s  learning and development and develop strategies to support learning and  development
Develop interactional strategies, rooted in a solid foundation of theory and  research, that support children’s learning and development, and articulate  the evidence for their efficacy
Appraise the role of cultures and societies in children’s learning and development
Critically explore the concept of ‘home learning environment’, adopting a holistic vision of education that encompasses learning, care and nurture

modules.moduleName: EARLY CHILDHOOD LEARNING AND DEVELOPMENT IN CONTEXT
modules.moduleContent: This module will use a ‘spiral curriculum’ approach to build upon the learning from previous modules. It will continue to locate, expand and apply theories of learning and development under the 4 strands of Aistear. It will interrogate theoretical perspectives and the implications for practice. Students will be encouraged to further develop their criticality in engaging with psychological theory, particularly extending their learning to deconstruct implications for policy and practice in the setting.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse how early relationships may impact on later development and defend a focus on relationships as a core element of pedagogical quality
Identify implications for policy and practice, with particular reference to the professional role of the educator
Further interpret the implications of brain development for children’s learning and development and develop strategies to support learning and development
Develop interactional strategies, rooted in a solid foundation of theory and research, that support children’s learning and development, and articulate the evidence for their efficacy
Appraise the role of cultures and societies in children’s learning and development
Critically explore the concept of ‘home learning environment’, adopting a holistic vision of education that encompasses learning, care and nurture

modules.moduleContent: This module explores Aistear - the Early Childhood Curriculum Framework and its application to the development of Literacy, Numeracy and Scientific Enquiry in an emergent curriculum in the early years. Theories of emergent numeracy, literacy and scientific enquiry appropriate to the early years will be examined. Students will investigate integrated emergent curriculum development in an enquiry rich environment both indoor and outdoor.
learningOutcomes: Identify functions of language, key stages in and influences on early language and numeracy development and critically reflect on relevant theoretical perspectives;
Articulate and apply a philosophy for using play to support language acquisition, mathematical and scientific learning with children (birth to six years);
Apply Aistear - the Early Childhood Curriculum Framework to early year’s literacy, numeracy and scientific enquiry through integrated emergent curriculum development
Utilise pedagogical knowledge in supporting and providing for a literacy, numeracy and scientific rich indoor and outdoor enquiry-based learning environment for young children;
Review, select and use appropriate resources and materials for developing literacy, numeracy and scientific enquiry with young children in an early childhood setting.

modules.moduleName: REFLECTIVE PRACTICE: EDUCATOR AS ACTION RESEARCHER
modules.moduleContent: This module will provide a structured reflective space for students to share and disseminate learning. The module will also develop students’ knowledge and skills of educational research methodologies and key methodological and ethical issues for research. Students will use these skills to design and implement an Action Research Project.
learningOutcomes: Explain and give relevant examples of the research methodologies suitable for research in ECEC settings (birth to six years).
Conduct a literature review of a chosen topic in early childhood education and care.

Examine and evaluate data collection methods in research in early childhood settings with particular emphasis on creative methodologies suitable for accessing the voices and perspectives of babies and young children.
Engage in group reflections as a community of learners to construct and share understandings of theory and practice.
Identify and apply ethical considerations in conducting research with adults, babies, toddlers and young children in early years' settings.

Write an Action Research Proposal and design and implement a small-scale action research project discussing the implications for practice and policy through a democratic and critically reflective approach to the education and care of babies, toddlers and young children.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: DOMINANT DISCOURSES / GLOBAL PERSPECTIVES IN EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION AND CARE
modules.moduleContent: This module introduces participants to a range of sociological terms and concepts necessary for the exploration and critical appraisal of pertinent national and international contemporary issues in early childhood education and care (ECEC). The module provides an opportunity to examine and critique new and emerging models of practice and provision from an international perspective. It also provides a space for critical reflection and debate on patterns, trends and learning from other contexts and jurisdictions in order to consider and appraise the functions of early childhood education and care in contemporary Ireland. The purpose of this module is to enhance student’s critical thinking skills and academic engagement by taking a sociological and global perspective on early childhood education and care and early childhood discourse.
learningOutcomes: Appraise a range of contemporary issues in early childhood education and care that have relevance both nationally and internationally.
Examine the impact of contemporary issues on the lives of children, families and society.
Analyse national and international ECEC models of policy, provision and approaches to practice.

Examine emerging international trends as they relate to and influence ECEC in Ireland, and map historical and global variations in the discourses of childhood, with reference to the child-adult relationship.
Examine the contemporary sociological paradigm on childhood and discuss the relationship between social structures, discourse and agency.
Analyse the impact of gender, social class, community relations and parenting practices on early childhood in Ireland from a sociological perspective.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will cover uncertainty (insurance, forward markets, hedging, etc. ), search cost, observational learning  and asymmetric Information  and other additional selected topics as time permits.

Note: students cannot take this module if taking EC241.
learningOutcomes: analyze observational learning and herding
understand the impact of search cost on equilibrium outcomes in competitive markets
analyze economic behavior under uncertainty and understand the fundamentals of insurance, forward markets and hedging
master analysis with asymmetric information:
adverse selection and its applications (credit rationing, discrimination in the job market . . .)
signalling and its applications in the labor and in the goods market.
hidden action and its applications (efficiency wages, incentive schemes . . .)

modules.moduleContent:
The course begins with a short exposition of why the history of economic thought matters. Starting with mercantilism, the course then examines the classical economic thinkers (such as Smith, Ricardo and Malthus), after which we turn to Marxist economics and non-mainstream economic thinkers such as Veblen. Subsequently, we study neoclassical economists (e.g., Marshall, Walras), followed by Keynesianism. The course ends by looking at some newer developments in economic thought.

Note: students cannot take this module if taking module EC216.
learningOutcomes: identify why and how key economic concepts have evolved over time.
critique ideas from key economic thinkers.
assess today's relevance of economic concepts pioneered in the past.

modules.moduleContent:
Note that Leaving Certificate Higher Level Mathematics strongly recommended for this module.

In this module we familiarize students with modern influential ideas in microeconomic theory. We study the behaviour of agents, firms and society when faced with information problems and examine the value of information, inquire into the effects of lack of common knowledge of rationality. We use rational social learning models to understand herding and information cascades. We also discuss how   markets serve the purpose of improving efficiency when agents are faced with uncertainty.

Note: students cannot take this module if taking EC230.

The aim of this course is to provide students with the tools needed to undertake empirical research in economics. In order to distinguish between competing models and provide useful policy advice we often need to quantify the relationships involved in economic models. In this course we will learn how to use the data we observe to improve our understanding of economic behaviour. Students will be taught how to estimate economic models and how to test a variety of hypothesis relating to those models.
learningOutcomes: Describe the key components of a statistical model.
Explain the criteria used to evaluate the performance of an estimator.
Discuss the motivation behind the least squares estimator.
Estimate a linear regression model using ordinary least squares.
Interpret the results of a linear regression model.
Outline the conditions required for the Gauss-Markov theorem to hold.
Conduct hypothesis tests on the linear regression model

modules.moduleContent: Each student is invited to choose a thesis topic from a set determined by an assigned departmental supervisor. The Supervisor will be available to give advice on a regular basis at an agreed time. The central aim of the thesis is to assess the student''s ability to formulate and test an economic theory or model. The projects should be no more than 20 pages in length and must be word-processed and suitably bound. Detailed information will be found in the 3rd-year student handbook.
modules.notes: Data analysis is used in the thesis.

modules.moduleContent:
Research methodology, thesis development, thesis preparation, thesis presentation.

Students must take EC599 or FN599, but cannot take both.
learningOutcomes: Prepare and present economic analyses
Search the academic literature in the relevant topic
Isolate and analyse a question in Economics or Finance
Write in a concise, correct and cogent fashion
Reference correctly

modules.moduleContent: The thesis should entail original research on a topic in Economics. It should be of a length and style similar to articles published in professional journals, typically around 5,000 words.

Module Objective
The module aims to give students the opportunity to:
• Further their knowledge in diverse research areas
• Develop critical analysis skills
• Develop abstract writing skills and/or develop presentation skills and/or problem solving skills.

Learning Outcomes
On successful completion of this module, students should:
• Have learnt about current topics having attended a series of seminars covering diverse range of subject matter
• Gained experience in critical analysis of speakers at seminar 
• Become experienced in writing abstracts relating to seminars attended (optional)
• Develop problem solving skills (optional)
• Improved presentation skills (optional).

Module Content
In this module the students will attend a series of seminars.  
Students will attend a minimum of 8 seminars (e.g. conference presentations, seminar series).

The rationale that underpins the BSc Science, Mathematics, and Computer Science Education courses is that good teachers, as well as being committed to and capable of promoting fruitful learning among their pupils, also need to be proficient, constructive critics of their own work. This means developing among student teachers, the attitudes and practices of proactive, co-operative, reflective practitioners.

In this module, with the aid of video recording and editing technology, student teachers will practise and develop the skills of lesson planning and lesson delivery and will engage in practical tasks that are designed to encourage critical reflection in a safe environment. These include video recording, editing and presenting in conjunction with group and classroom discussion. Through engagement with Junior Cycle curriculum and methodology classes, students will begin the process of developing skills for curriculum planning and development.

Another component of the module is the introduction of restorative practices. Working as reflective practitioner’s student teachers will need to develop skills in building positive, sustainable relationships by evaluating how they communicate. This module will seek to scaffold communication by demonstrating a value based restorative approach to building and maintaining relationships. Student teachers will be given the opportunity to work in restorative circles and will be supported to practice restorative questions and conversations. The programme will enable participants to reflect on their emotional intelligence, use of empathy and understanding of fairness. By studying anger, blame and shame student teachers will evaluate their ability to problem solve and transform how they communicate.

This module continues to explore the complexity and breadth of the teacher’s role in teaching and learning of science, maths, and computer science in semester two. The theoretical underpinnings of STEM education are explored in tandem with school-based experience in a post-primary school. The concept of curriculum alignment will be explored in general and in subject-specific context. Students will continue to develop skills in becoming critically reflective practitioners, including participation in cooperative learning groups and peer evaluation. The module is designed to prepare student teachers for their first placement experience. They will develop teaching and presentations skills and receive feedback from peers and lecturers. They will also learn to deliver professional feedback to colleagues. Meanwhile, they will explore restorative practices through discussion and practice-based sessions.

The placement experience affords student teachers the opportunity to observe teaching (including team teaching), to lead group discussions and to assist in the development of a variety of instructional materials.
learningOutcomes: Show their mastery of the subject matter, pedagogical issues  and related methodology for Junior Cycle science,  mathematics, and computer science teaching and identify key concepts in their subject areas at Junior Cycle level
Access, develop and utilise a variety of curriculum resources and critically appraise their own practice and that of others, and  provide appropriate feedback and guidance
Show evidence of the skills needed to teach Science, Maths, and Computer Science through peer evaluation
Identify the main structures within the Irish education system,  including the different state and non-state bodies and the different  types of schools
Observe models of planning coherent, differentiated and integrated teaching programmes which are informed by ongoing reflection on professional practice
Link observations to curriculum through planning, implementing and evaluating teaching and learning
Identify and demonstrate restorative communication and relationship building skills

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the discipline of Psychology of Education. Throughout the course, students will be encouraged to develop their critical reflective skills, and to bring a psychological perspective to the experiences they encounter in their school-based practice.

Students will be encouraged to understand key educational issues from different psychological perspectives. The module aims to cover key learning theories including constructivist theories (e.g., the work of Piaget, Vygotsky and Bruner), behavioural approaches to learning, information processing theories (including memory and attention), metacognition and motivation. The module also covers topics related to the physical, cognitive and socio-emotional development of adolescents. Topics will include selfhood and identity, puberty, cognitive development, peer relationships, student-teacher relationships, childhood adversity, resilience and flourishing. Topics will be contextualised within contemporary developments nationally and internationally
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate various theories of learning and motivation.
Explain how theories can be applied in classrooms to facilitate learning and promote positive behaviour.
Discuss the key developmental transitions of adolescence and apply knowledge to promote the holistic development of young people.
Describe identity development in adolescence and the role of teachers in facilitating a positive sense of self.
Describe research in the area of peer relations and bullying and explore their implications for school and classroom practices.
Describe major determinants of psychological distress in young people and discuss the school-related factors that promote a sense of safety, belonging and trust.
Begin to critically interrogate applications of theory to practice in real contexts.

modules.moduleName: EDUCATION FOR A CONNECTED WORLD: GLOBAL CITIZENSHIP, SUSTAINABILITY AND TECHNOLOGY
modules.moduleContent: This module offers students opportunities to study and to put into practice strategies for teaching and learning for a connected world. It identifies key educational issues emerging across three domains of educational research, policy and practice: education for global citizenship; education for sustainable development; and education, technology and society. Its overall aim is to enable students to identify where their responsibilities lie as educational professionals, and to integrate concepts across these domains into teaching, learning and assessment practices.

modules.moduleContent: This module will enhance students' awareness of and critical reflection on a series of key issues in education. Initial sessions will build a collaborative working identity for the group, and develop students’ skills in critical reflection, reading and writing. Students will reflect upon their practice in the school, cultivating a culture of reflective inquiry about their professional work and research interests.  These reflections will be framed by discussions about key issues in contemporary education to contextualise students’ reflections.
learningOutcomes: Engage in a process of critical reflectivity about their professional practices and experiences.
Critical reflection about key issues in contemporary educational policy, practice and theory.
Engage in group work to build a collaborative group identity amongst this MEd student group.
Develop their academic skills in reading, writing and communications.

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to support the writing and design of students’ theses and projects. It will enhance students’ communicative and collaborative capacities by focusing on academic writing and the research process, broadly conceived. The seminars will offer students a group-based approach to undertaking their research and will provide opportunities to present and discuss research concerns within a supportive, collaborative atmosphere. Students will identify their research topics and problems, contextualise them within relevant educational research traditions and develop a coherent framework in which to explore them.

**When selecting ED618 students are required to take either ED607 or ED674
learningOutcomes: Present, discuss and critically explore research, conceptual and professional issues emerging from thesis work in the MEd group context.
Critically analyse the educational field and theories underpinning students’ thesis research.
Demonstrate an understanding and critical analysis of the educational theories and empirical research presented by guest speakers
Explore the communication processes and relationships with key stakeholders in education (students, parents, teachers, management, community and policy-makers) that is evident in educational research

modules.moduleName: EXPLORING DIFFERENCE: BRINGING A CRITICAL LENS TO THE ROLE OF THE GUIDANCE COUNSELLOR
modules.moduleContent:
The teaching profession has tended to attract a broadly similar cohort of students who often share broadly similar educational histories and biographies. Many teachers enter postgraduate educational programmes directly after undergraduate studies or undertake concurrent programmes. As such, many may have little experience of other educational sites or work settings. The purpose of this module is to broaden horizons with the theme of ‘exploring difference’ underpinning it. It will support a deeper understanding of the lives that students will lead after school. It will also provide frameworks to interrogate the assumptions that can shape students’ experiences in school: how is sameness understood? Normality? Typical? Difference? 
 
Students will explore questions of difference theoretically and in terms of practice. This will involve three parts: 1. Mapping and analysing the relevant literature; 2. Mobilising this literature critically as a lens to explore experiences of difference 3. Critically evaluating their emergent learning and how it impacts their role as guidance counsellor in a school context. 
 
Students will create a reflective portfolio throughout the module that will be informed by a critical lens of difference as the assessment for this module.
learningOutcomes: Appreciate the importance of difference and diversity in schools and educational life more broadly;
Demonstrate awareness of the role of the Counsellor in response to student diversity and progression;
Consider the position of schools within the broader network of their local communities;
Demonstrate cultural sensitivity and an understanding of difference;
Develop a critical stance towards work and community cultures;
ranslate learning into the school context and the role of the guidance counsellor.

modules.moduleName: TEACHING ABOUT TEACHING. FROM TEACHER TO TEACHER EDUCATOR
modules.moduleContent: Those who become teacher educators in the Republic of Ireland do so without any requirement to register with a professional body, without any formal period of study of the foundation disciplines of teacher education, without engagement with appropriate pedagogical strategies for teaching adults and with no formally supervised practice of teaching. This is not unique to Ireland.  In almost all European countries one becomes a teacher educator without any formal qualification, preparation or induction into the role  (Kosnik, 2008). Two key European Commission policy documents, The Green Paper on Teacher Education in Europe (Buchberger, Campos, Kallos & Stevenson, 2000) and Supporting Teacher Educators for Better Learning Outcomes (European Commission, 2012) have highlighted concern about the lack of training for and induction into the teacher education profession, including appropriate methodologies for working with adult learners. This module focuses on the cognitive and practical processes of transitioning from first-order to second-order practitioner, with specific emphasis on the knowledge and capabilities required.
learningOutcomes: Work with adult learners in facilitating their professional development in a range of roles on the continuum of teacher education: initial, induction and in-career education
Demonstrate an advanced capability in interrogating and developing practitioner knowledge by critical reflection on first order experience
Conduct one-to-one tutoring sessions competently with beginning and experienced teachers
Conduct small group seminars competently with beginning and experienced teachers
Design and develop curriculum and assessment for teacher education

modules.moduleName: EDUCATIONAL LEADERSHIP:LEGAL CONTEXTS, POLICY AND PRACTICE
modules.moduleContent: Legal, policy and other obligations with which schools must be compliant are investigated and explored. The vision and values, school culture, power and authority that contribute to school life are investigated and interrogated. The role of the different stakeholders in the education system and their varying responsibilities are also a key focus.
learningOutcomes: Explore and understand models of educational leadership – Irish, European and International.
Explore and understand the key philosophical and historical underpinnings of the Irish Education context.
Identify, trace and analyse key policy developments.
Interrogate and critique key educational legislation and its impact on the role of school leader.
Demonstrate knowledge of structures in Irish Education (such as governance structures, supports for inclusion, links with outside agencies etc.)
Interrogate the concepts of Natural Justice/Fairness of Procedures.
Understand and ensure the safe functioning of the school.

modules.moduleName: COACHING AND MENTORING IN & AS LEADERSHIP PRACTICE
modules.moduleContent: This module aims to explore the role of a leader as both a mentor and coach in an educational setting. It provides an array of evidence-based approaches to coaching and associated processes that help students apply them effectively in their work. The module gives participants an opportunity to develop skills and practice leadership coaching in a one-to-one and group context. Furthermore, it provides them with tools they need to introduce coaching as a daily situational leadership practice.
learningOutcomes: Differentiate between coaching and mentoring.
Assess and apply an appropriate leadership style, such as coaching or mentoring, based on the employees’ levels of competence and motivation.
Critically review the main approaches to coaching in the workplace and associated processes.
Practice coaching and mentoring skills.
List evidence-based coaching and mentoring tools that can be applied when coaching in an educational context.

modules.moduleContent: The module builds on the learning of your studies in postgraduate educational leadership and/or management and aims to provide students with the necessary knowledge, skills and understanding to enable them to plan, design and conduct research in education. They will learn to critically engage with educational research and relevant literature, differentiate between methodologies, critically evaluate the theoretical underpinnings and philosophical considerations of research paradigms, develop a sound research question, understand and make decisions about different methods of data collection and analysis and consider ethical implications. The module will consider research in a range of contexts relevant to education.
learningOutcomes: Understand a range of research methods and methodologies relevant to educational research.
Critically evaluate the theoretical underpinnings and philosophical considerations of research paradigms.
Critically integrate theory and research.
Engage critically and reflectively with the rationale for, and practice of, research design.
