modules.moduleContent: This programme is aimed at but not exclusive to early childhood, primary and post-primary teachers and teacher educators. The micro-credential explore the move away from thinking about learning, teaching and assessment in terms of ability and disability to thinking in terms of designing learning, teaching and assessment experiences from the mindset of variability. The course begins with developing an understanding of the concept of variability before exploring the key concepts of UDL. Through discussions, professional reflections on practice and the critical examination of a chose learning, teaching and assessment experience participant will develop a knowledge and understanding of the three principles of the UDL Guidelines and begin applying these principles to their practice.
learningOutcomes: Explain the concept of variability.
Discuss the key concepts of UDL.
Critically reflect on the goals of their learning, teaching and assessment design and delivery through a UDL lens.
Discuss the three principles of UDL in the context of their educational setting and practice.
Apply appropriate aspects of the UDL guidelines to their learning, teaching and assessment planning, design and deliver.
Redesign a learning, teaching and assessment experience to make one change based on their understanding of variability and the UDL guidelines.

modules.moduleContent:
Educational research is often seen as research that investigates aspects or dimensions of education. It does so in order to gain better/new/different understandings of what is going on, often with the ambition to contribute to the improvement of educational practice. This all sounds straightforward, but actually raises a number of practical, intellectual and political questions which this module seeks to foreground and explore. One question is what would actually ‘count’ as education and how one might be able to identify it – which is a prerequisite before one can even begin to conduct research ‘on’ it. Another question is what it would mean to improve education and how research might be of help in this task rather than get in the way. And then there is the question of research itself, particularly what kinds of ‘access’ research actually provides, what the possibilities and limitations of such access are, and how different approaches can be understood and justified. This module is intended to help beginning research students in education and other fields to explore these questions in order to better understand what it might mean to engage in the conduct of educational research and why, when and under what conditions it might be worthwhile to do so.

Students should register for ED802 and ED879 in their first year of study. Note these modules must be completed before the programme is completed and the thesis submitted.

This module will look at curriculum as networked, relational and transversal. This holistic approach to curriculum draws and connects to/with/in all aspects of life:  cultural, natural, historical, political, ecological, theological, aesthetic and the autobiographical.  It will explore how knowledge is negotiated and go beyond the taken for granted and already defined, the predetermined positions and habitual ways of thinking, talking and doing. 

The postmodern themes of diverse and pluralistic understanding of gender, race, sexual identity, ethnicity, culture, and religion will be explored in relation to curriculum using case studies from Ireland and other jurisdictions.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate the capacity to differentiate and critique the different forms and purposes of knowledge as proposed by curriculum policy.
Demonstrate an advanced capability in interrogating and making discerning judgments on issues of social justice in curriculum development.
Demonstrate a critical awareness of how curriculum reflects a prejudice in favour of particular pedagogies, methodologies, politics or worldviews.

modules.moduleName: KNOWLEDGE AND POLICY IN EDUCATIONAL PRACTICE
modules.moduleContent: The introductory module to the Doctorate in Education focuses on knowledge and policy in educational practice. The first section of the module will critically explore the nature and forms of knowledge and the ways in which different kinds of knowledge are developed and communicated. There will be a particular focus on knowledge-of and -for practice. The module then considers the development and implementation of educational policy. Participants will examine the different kinds of policy, the ways in which policy is developed and will use critical policy sociology to analyse how policies may advantage and disadvantage different groups within education.
learningOutcomes: • Expand and redefine existing procedural and practical knowledge
• Critique the broader implications of using different kinds of knowledge in particular contexts
• Analyse policy development and implementation effects within an education system and the society within which it is embedded.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to the personal and professional skills required to function and flourish as a reflective early years professional in their current studies and future career. It will consider communication skills, knowledge of team working, academic reading and writing, and reflection on personal learning and development goals.
learningOutcomes: Analyse the implications of own attitudes, values, beliefs and assumptions in relation to issues of relevant policies and best practice;
Identify the personal qualities and skills needed to be an effective early childhood educator and Identify learning and personal development goals;
Develop communication approaches that will assist in their personal and professional lives;
Establish an e-portfolio to develop skills of reflective practice and personal and professional learning;

Develop academic skills to critique and synthesise information from a range of material and write appropriately, following the conventions of language usage, applying the conventions of Harvard referencing;
Engage in discussion with other adults and identify learning from consensus and / or disagreement;
Outline concepts of team working and team roles, reflect on personal strengths and challenges as a team member, and engage with skills development for team working;

modules.moduleContent: This module aims to help students explore and develop an understanding of various developmental aspects of children from a holistic perspective. Drawing on a range of theoretical perspectives from developmental psychology, students will be introduced to the knowledge, practices and values relevant to the development of the child.
learningOutcomes: Describe different theoretical models of child development change and continuity in infancy and childhood from 0-6 years;
Reflect on child development theories and their application on early childhood education practice;
Discuss how the role of social, biological, cultural and historical perspectives affect the development of young children;
Explain the holistic development of the child to include physical, cognitive, language, social, emotional, moral, spiritual, creative and cultural development;
Describe the contribution of the practitioner/parent/guardian to the development and learning of children in the home and in the early childhood setting;
Identify the importance of transitions in influencing a sense of self;
Evaluate personal and team practice in meeting child development needs in an early childhood setting.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO SOCIAL AND LEGAL STUDIES
modules.moduleContent: This module aims to explore a wide range of social and legal issues that impact on the health and welfare of children in early year’s settings. National legislation and frameworks, as well as the various funding initiatives aimed at supporting children and their families will be examined. Developing an understanding of the Child Protection Guidelines and how they relate to everyday practice will be an integral part of this module.
learningOutcomes: Identify a range of agencies that impact on the health and welfare of children;
Engage with statutory regulations, national frameworks and practice guidelines and review their impact on early year’s settings;
Examine national funding programmes and their implementation;
Explore Child Protection guidelines and procedures in relation to the protection of children and staff;
Engage with Child Protection Training opportunities;
Review relevant policies and procedures within early year’s settings.

modules.moduleName: PHILOSOPHY, CULTURE & CONSTRUCTS OF EARLY CHILDHOOD CARE & EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent:
The module is an introduction to the history, theories and philosophies underpinning early childhood care and education from Rousseau to the present day. It will interrogate current practices and provision against a rich backdrop of philosophers and theorists including:
Rousseau, Pestalozzi, Froebel, Steiner, Isaacs, McMillan, Montessori, Dewey, Freire, Malaguzzi etc.

Integral to this module will be the examination and theorising of childhood through a social, cultural and political lens to understand the changing constructions of childhood through time.
learningOutcomes: Locate the historical, social, cultural and political constructions of childhood;
Identify the history, theories and philosophies that have influenced practices in early childhood care and education (birth to six years);
Explore the development of the early childhood education sector in Ireland;
Establish philosophies of early childhood care and education within current practice in early childhood settings in Ireland;
Explore international perspectives of early childhood education.

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL PRACTICE 1: FOCUS ON OBSERVING YOUNG CHILDREN
modules.moduleContent: This module aims to help students understand what it means to be an early years’ professional. Students will be supported to connect their study of the theories of learning and development to the learning of babies and young children (birth – 6 years) observed in early year’s settings. With a focus on the development of observation skills, students will engage with different models of observation, both of learning and for learning.
learningOutcomes: Review the history of childcare in Ireland and develop an understanding of the main stakeholders in the sector and their roles;
Develop an awareness of the professional skills required to be a good early years’ educator;
Demonstrate an ability to interact effectively with young children and support their play ideas;
Work effectively as part of a team and Identify why and how observations should be carried out in an early year’s setting;
Recognise how observations of learning and for learning differ;

Demonstrate, through the development of a portfolio, their ability to:
o carry out a range of observations in different contexts
o evaluate observations using theories of early child development and
o support programme planning for individual and groups of children and make
recommendations for learning
Demonstrate an understanding of the ethical implications of being a professional early years’ educator;

modules.moduleName: HEALTH AND WELL-BEING FOR YOUNG CHILDREN
modules.moduleContent: This module will explore concepts, policies and practices that support young children’s (3-6 years) well-being, health and safety in ECEC settings. It aims to develop students’understanding of how to promote the health and well-being of all children from an ecological, inclusive perspective and develop skills, knowledge and understanding of the holistic nature of health and well-being. This module will explore the broader determinants of health and social and emotional well-being of children and families and discuss key issues that impact on supporting a healthy childhood. The role of the early years’ educator in promoting safe, supportive and healthy environments for young children will be considered, from an evidence-based perspective, in keeping with current legislation and policy (including all national frameworks).
learningOutcomes: Summarise the broader determinants of children’s health and well-being from an ecological perspective and explore the concepts of both health and well-being
Identify strategies to promote children’s health and well-being in keeping with current legislation and policy
Consider the implementation of Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework in practice
Implement appropriate policies and procedures to include record keeping as required by legislation and good practice guidelines
Explain the pedagogical importance of interactions between an educator and young child during care routines

modules.moduleName: REFLECTIVE PRACTICE: INTEGRATING THEORY AND PRACTICE
modules.moduleContent: This module will build on previous learning to further develop educators’ use of reflective practice processes and communication skills to enhance and develop as a professional. Understandings of ‘reflection’ as it relates to student’s personal and professional development will be explored.
learningOutcomes: Analyse the implications of own attitudes, values, beliefs and assumptions in relation to issues of relevant policies and best practice.
Describe models of reflection drawing on relevant theory (including National frameworks) and critically reflect and document reflections using a reflective model.

Compare and contrast a variety of models of reflection, and evaluate their usefulness for practice in the context of ongoing personal and professional growth, goals and philosophy of and for early childhood education and care.
Identify and document the personal qualities and skills needed to be an effective early childhood educator, and identify learning and personal development goals linking theory and practice.
Develop communication approaches that will assist in their personal and professional lives, and consider the skills necessary to enable them to give and receive feedback in an effective manner.

Develop academic skills to critique and synthesise information from a range of material and write appropriately, following the conventions of language usage, applying the conventions of Harvard referencing.

modules.moduleName: BUILDING RELATIONSHIPS WITH CHILDREN, FAMILIES AND COMMUNITIES
modules.moduleContent: This module will support students to work effectively with children (birth to six years), families and community, understanding the integrated nature of relationship within an ecological framework. It will consider working with families as a democratic practice and examine how these relationships are built and developed. The module will also explore national and international models of effective collaboration and integrated delivery to improve outcome for children, families and communities.
learningOutcomes: Explore how students conceptualise social class and cultural difference and examine how this affects their understanding of parenting and families.
Explore and analyse the new sociological paradigm on childhood and the impact of social class, cultural contexts, community relations and parenting practices.

Identify strategies for working with families and community in supporting children’s holistic development and to strengthen links with early childhood services and related professionals when appropriate.
Examine policies and practices of the ECEC settings that effect relationships with families and communities.
Compare and contrast concepts and models of integrated early childhood services that support effective outcomes for children and families.

modules.moduleContent: This module will explore concepts, policies and practices that support young children’s (birth - 6 years) well-being, health and safety in ECEC settings. It aims to develop student’s understanding of how to promote the health and well-being of all children from an ecological, inclusive perspective and develop skills, knowledge and understanding of the holistic nature of health and well-being. This module will explore the broader determinants of health and social and emotional well-being of children and families and discuss key issues that impact on supporting a healthy childhood. The role of the early years’ educator in promoting safe, supportive and healthy environments for young children will be considered, from an evidence-based perspective, in keeping with current legislation and policy (including all national frameworks).
learningOutcomes: Summarise the broader determinants of children’s health and well-being from an ecological perspective and explore the concepts of both health and well-being.
Identify strategies to promote children’s health and well-being in keeping with current legislation and policy.
Consider the implementation of Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework in practice.
Implement appropriate policies and procedures to include record keeping as required by legislation and good practice guidelines.
Explain the pedagogical importance of interactions between an educator and young child during care routines.

modules.moduleContent: This module will provide a structured reflective space for students to share and disseminate learning. The module will also further develop students’ knowledge and skills of educational research methodologies and key methodological issues.  Students will use these skills to design and implement an Action Research Project.
learningOutcomes: Integrate the theory and practice of reflection in all aspects of ECCE (birth to six years).
Engage in group reflections as a community of learners to construct and share understandings of theory and practice.
Identify and apply ethical considerations in conducting research with young children.
Write an Action Research Proposal and design and implement a small-scale action research project discussing the implications for policy and practice.
Acknowledge,  examine  and  synthesise  links  between relevant theories and practice.

modules.moduleContent: This module recognises the importance of transitions in the lives of young children. It aims to  investigate the impact  of making transitions on children, family and staff.  The module will also explore a range of practices that empower and support children to manage change into, within, and moving on from, early years settings and equip students with skills to support and facilitate transitions.
learningOutcomes: Identify the major and minor transitions in early childhood (birth to six years).
Describe transitions as a learning space.
Examine the importance and impact of transitions for children, families and communities.
Assess the use of an interactionist approach to scaffolding and supporting transitions for children
Implement processes and practices to support transitions and ensure that they empower children’s holistic development with reference to National Frameworks.

modules.moduleContent: The module involves critical appraisal of pertinent national and international contemporary issues in early childhood education and explores implications for policy, provision, and practice across settings. The module provides an opportunity to examine and critique new and emerging models of practice and provision from an international perspective.  It also provides a space for critical reflection and debate on patterns, trends and learning from other contexts and jurisdictions.
learningOutcomes: Appraise a range of  contemporary issues in early childhood care and education that have relevance both nationally and internationally.
Examine the impact of contemporary issues on the lives of children, families and society.
Analyse  national and international ECCE models of policy, provision and approaches to practice.
Examine emerging international trends as they relate to and influence ECCE in Ireland.

modules.moduleContent: The module will explore and highlight the interconnected nature of curriculum, pedagogy and assessment.  It will focus on assessment as a social practice and as a tool for feedback and dialogue, emphasising  the  child’s  identity as  a  competent  learner,  while making links with Aistear and Síolta. The module will consider modes, models, purposes and outcomes of assessment appropriate to the early childhood setting.  Finally, this module will draw together learning from previous years to complete the cycle of planning, implementing and evaluating curriculum.
learningOutcomes: Examine the integrated and interdependent nature of curriculum, pedagogy and assessment within the curriculum (birth to six years).
Appraise the impact of pedagogy on curriculum and assessment.
Locate assessment within social, historical, theoretical and cultural contexts.
Document and share children’s learning using a range of media and engaging a range of stakeholders.
Utilise assessment as part of a team to build on children’s interest and extend their learning.
Critically evaluate the curriculum using Síolta, the National Quality Framework.
Make linkages with current national policy (Síolta/Aistear).

modules.moduleContent: This module explores Aistear - the Early Childhood Curriculum Framework and its application to the development of Literacy, Numeracy and Scientific Inquiry in the Early Years. Theories of emergent numeracy, literacy and scientific inquiry appropriate to the early years will be examined. Students will investigate integrated curriculum development in an inquiry rich environment both indoor and outdoor.
learningOutcomes: Identify functions of language, key stages in and influences on early language and numeracy development and critically reflect on relevant theoretical perspectives
Articulate and apply a philosophy for using play to support language acquisition, mathematical and scientific learning with children (birth to six years)
Apply Aistear  - the Early Childhood Curriculum Framework to early years literacy, numeracy and scientific enquiry through integrated curriculum development
Utilise pedagogical knowledge in supporting and providing for a literacy, numeracy and scientific rich indoor and outdoor enquiry-based learning environment for young children
Review, select and use appropriate resources and materials for developing literacy, numeracy and scientific enquiry with young children in an early childhood setting.

modules.moduleContent: This module will interrogate ethics and social justice in terms of relationships within the preschool and wider community. As a foundation, students will explore the concept of ethics and the principles and purpose of a ‘Code of Ethics’ for the sector. Students will examine situations that arise in the ECCE sector and reflect on their own responses within an ethical framework. The module uses concepts of social justice, ethics and diversity to explore values, attitudes and practices in ECCE.
learningOutcomes: Discuss concepts and practices of social justice;
Locate social justice as it relates to ECCE within a political, historical and economic context;
Proof policies, practices and provision in the context of ECCE as it relates to equality and the rights of young children (birth to six years);
Critically evaluate understanding of ‘equality’ as it relates to children, families, staff and the wider community;
Examine the concepts, principles and practices of advocacy for young children;
Reflect on personal principles and practices relating to social justice, ethics and diversity in early childhood provision;
Critique the conceptual and policy aspects of children's rights;

modules.moduleContent: This module considers the content of the curriculum under the headings of: Knowledge – The essential concepts, inquiry tools and structure of content areas that the practitioner needs to know to positively influence children’s learning and development Practices – The wide array of effective approaches, strategies and tools that the practitioner uses to engage children in learning within the curriculum Resources – The materials and other resources within the setting and the broader community that positively support children’s learning and development. This module aims to positively promote children’s dispositions and capabilities in the areas of science, philosophy, maths, literacy and numeracy, ecology and anthropology.
learningOutcomes: Discuss curriculum as enquiry led and emergent;
Describe the image of the child underpinning your curriculum;
Develop an enquiry led curriculum for children (birth to six);
Demonstrate the skills of observation, documentation and assessment;
Design ways of sharing children’s learning.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides a broad-based introduction to national and European policies that impact on the lives of Children from birth to six, families, and the provision of early childhood care and education in Ireland. Within this context, Irish legislation relevant to early childhood education and care settings will be examined. Finally, students will appraise the range of voluntary and statutory bodies and services that support children, families and early childhood services.
learningOutcomes: Critically examine aspects of Irish social policies relating to all children,  their families and the early childhood education and care sector and  discuss the implications of contemporary policy development to practice  and to services
Outline the structure of the Irish Legal System with relevance to early years and the implications of legislation for early childhood education and care practices, issues of inclusion
Critically examine legislation in relation to the impact on ECEC settings of  family formations and re-formations, guardianship, etc. and policies in relation to same

Critically assess the impact of international legislation, directives and  frameworks on Irish social policy development relating to ECEC settings with particular emphasis on the United Nations Convention on Rights of the Child
Compare and contrast the role and purpose of a range of voluntary and statutory agencies that support children, families, and ECEC services

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL PRACTICE: FOCUS ON CURRICULUM & ASSESSMENT
modules.moduleContent: Building on their professional practice in previous years and the learning from modules covered, students will work independently under the guidance of a supervisor on their ability to assist in planning and providing for the care and education of a group of young children. They will use a range of teaching strategies and methodologies in planning and implementing curriculum, managing routines and transitions in everyday practice and assessing children’s learning and development. Students will engage in reflection on teaching and learning, and working with parents, families and other professionals.
learningOutcomes: Assist in planning and providing for the care and education of a group of children (birth to six years) in an ECCE setting;
Support children’s well-being and sense of identity and belonging;
Plan, implement and assess an aspect of curriculum to meet the learning needs and interests of the children in an early childhood setting;
Develop a reflective portfolio to capture their experience of children’s care, learning and development and critically reflect on their practice;
Engage in practice that supports children’s thinking and learning and promotes dispositions of mastery and agency;
Work effectively as part of a team;
Engage effectively with parents, families and the wider community;

modules.moduleName: CURRICULUM AND PEDAGOGICAL DOCUMENTATION AND ASSESSMENT
modules.moduleContent: This module aims to interrogate the impact of various influences, particularly political and cultural influences, on curriculum development. Appraising the development of early year’s curricula and their philosophies, in particular the emergent curriculum, it will explore the various factors that influence curriculum implementation in Early Childhood Education and Care settings. It aims to critically develop the students’ understanding of documenting children’s learning and the curriculum to support short, medium and long-term planning. The use of documenting the curriculum to support quality provision will be critically reviewed. Using a strengths-based approach, documentation to support sharing of learning will be demonstrated. Emphasis on the child as a confident and competent learner at the centre of provision will be upheld through links with both Aistear, the Curriculum Framework and Síolta, the Quality Framework.
learningOutcomes: Critically examine a range of curricula, evident in both national and international settings and explore the various underpinning theories and their impact on provision.
Appraise the political and cultural influences on curriculum from both a national and international perspective.
Consider the principles of curriculum design and their impact on planning and implementation within an Early Childhood Education and Care setting.
Review various modes, models, purposes and outcomes of assessment used in Early Childhood Education and Care settings.
Document and share children’s learning using a range of media and utilise assessment as part of a team to build on children’s interest and extend their learning.
Critically describe how documenting the curriculum supports assessment of and for learning in addition to supporting short, medium and long-term planning.
Critically evaluate the impact of Aistear, the Curriculum Framework and Síolta, the Quality Framework on curriculum and pedagogy in an Early Childhood Education and Care setting.

modules.moduleName: ESTABLISHING QUALITY INDOOR AND OUTDOOR ENVIRONMENTS IN EARLY CHILDHOOD
modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to introduce, explore, and analyse the vital role of children’s environments in relation to their well-being, health, development, and learning. The module is underpinned by the recognition that early year’s environments must be seen in the context of children’s rights and needs. It is vital that these spaces offer quality experiences and home from home atmospheres. Throughout this module, indoor, outdoor and local area environments are given due attention as students are supported to consider these environments and their affordances to the developing child. Addressing the concept of Universal Design and guided by the Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework and Síolta, the National Quality Framework, environments will be explored in relation to Policy and Legislation, Sensory/Motor Needs, The Affective Environment, Curriculum Approaches, Pedagogical Values, Emerging Interests and Play Needs, Layout, Materials and Activities.
learningOutcomes: Analyse environments from the perspective of the rights of children to quality indoor, outdoor, and local area environments and identify the unique affordances of indoor, outdoor, and local area environments and how they intertwine to provide holistic experiences
Appraise the legislative requirements relating to the physical environment of an early year’s setting and explore the main elements of Universal Design and its impact on early year’s provision
Critically examine theories of physical and affective environment as sites for encounters and nurturing relationships and consider the concept of the environment as the 3rd teacher
Compare and contrast the varied environments of a range of curriculum approaches and articulate how the environments provided supports the chosen curriculum /pedagogical approach being implemented

Design a range of environments that support implementation of Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework, including the selection of quality layouts, resources, loose parts, materials and equipment that meet the changing needs of the developing child
Summarise and discuss the opportunities and challenges of a risk benefit approach to early year’s environment design

modules.moduleContent: This module recognises the importance of transitions in the lives of young children. It aims to investigate the impact of making transitions on children, family and staff. The module will also explore a range of practices that empower and support children to manage change into, within, and moving on from, preschool and equip students with skills to support and facilitate transitions.
learningOutcomes: Identify the major and minor transitions in early childhood (birth to six years);
Describe transitions as a learning space;
Examine the importance and impact of transitions for children, families and communities;
Assess the use of an interactionist approach to scaffolding and supporting transitions for children;
Implement processes and practices to support transitions and ensure that they empower children’s holistic development with reference to National Frameworks.

modules.moduleName: DOMINANT DISCOURSES IN EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION & CARE
modules.moduleContent: The module involves critical appraisal of pertinent national and international contemporary issues in early childhood education and explores implications for policy, provision, and practice across settings. The module provides an opportunity to examine and critique new and emerging models of practice and provision from an international perspective. It also provides a space for critical reflection and debate on patterns, trends and learning from other contexts and jurisdictions.
learningOutcomes: Appraise a range of contemporary issues in early childhood care and education that have relevance both nationally and internationally;
Examine the impact of contemporary issues on the lives of children, families and society;
Analyse national and international ECCE models of policy, provision and approaches to practice;
Examine emerging international trends as they relate to and influence ECCE in Ireland.

modules.moduleName: EARLY CHILDHOOD LEARNING AND DEVELOPMENT CHALLENGES (BIRTH-6 YEARS)
modules.moduleContent: This module aims to explore a broad range of challenges that may impact on children’s holistic learning and development. It will adopt an inquiry-based approach and will include consideration of challenges such as additional needs, behaviour, giftedness, etc. The module will examine implications for educators as they work with parents, colleagues and other professionals in the field to support children’s learning and development in a social context. The module adopts a broader view in exploring mechanisms, processes, policies and legislation to work with other agencies to support children’s learning. It will also support students to develop an understanding of the historical development of provision for children with additional needs from both national and international perspectives.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explore the broad range of challenges that may impact on babies’, toddlers’ and young children’s (birth to six years) holistic learning and development.
Drawing on relevant research, identify potentially appropriate responses to supporting children with additional needs, and critically explore their appropriateness for ECEC practice.
Examine current thinking and research, interrogating historical models of disabilities and deconstructing societal attitudes to different abilities through a contemporary inclusive lens.
Reflect on personal values and attitudes underpinning work with families and children with additional needs with the child at the centre of practice.
Examine the implications for practice for educators as they work with families, colleagues and other professionals in the field to support children’s learning and development.
Defend and apply an inter-agency, cross-disciplinary, collaborative approach to supporting babies, toddlers and young children and their families.

modules.moduleName: TRANSITIONS IN EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION & CARE
modules.moduleContent: This module recognises the importance of transitions in the lives of babies, toddlers and young children. It aims to investigate the impact of transition experiences on children, families and staff. The module will explore a range of practices that empower and support children to manage change into, within, and beyond ECEC settings and equips students with the skills to support and facilitate all transitions.
learningOutcomes: Identify the major and minor transitions in early childhood (birth to six years) and articulate ways to ensure continuity of experience for babies and young children through policy and practice.

Describe transitions as processes offering opportunities for learning and development, understanding learning as a co-constructed and open-ended process so that positive transitional experiences may ensure children’s social engagement and encourage further learning.
Examine the importance and impact of transitions for babies, children, families and communities and identify the importance of involving and consulting with all.

Critique a range of theoretical approaches to scaffolding and supporting transitions for babies, toddlers and young children, with a particular focus on attachment and a democratic and inclusive approach that values diversity.
Implement processes and practices to support transitions, ensuring that they empower babies’, toddlers’ and young children’s holistic development with reference to National Frameworks

Develop a policy for transitions in early years settings considering babies’, toddlers’ and young children’s needs in order to promote their full potential and their participation in the life of ECEC settings.

modules.moduleContent: This module will interrogate ethics and social justice in terms of relationships within the preschool and wider community. As a foundation, students will explore the concept of ethics and the principles and purpose of a ‘Code of Ethics’ for the sector. Students will examine situations that arise in the ECEC sector and reflect on their own responses within an ethical framework. The module uses concepts of social justice, ethics and diversity to explore values, attitudes and practices in ECEC.
learningOutcomes: Discuss concepts and practices of social justice.
Locate social justice as it relates to ECEC within a political, historical and economic context.
Proof policies, practices and provision in the context of ECEC as it relates to equality and the rights of young children (birth to six years).
Critically evaluate understanding of ‘equality’ as it relates to children, families, staff and the wider community.
Examine the concepts, principles and practices of advocacy for young children.
Reflect on personal principles and practices relating to social justice, ethics and diversity in early childhood provision.
Critique the conceptual and policy aspects of children's rights.

modules.moduleContent: Building on their professional practice in previous years and prior learning from modules, students will work under the guidance of a supervisor to develop their ability to plan and provide for the care and education of a diverse group of babies, toddlers and young children (birth – 6 years), while demonstrating leadership skills and professionalism.  Through their ability to link theory to practice and their reflection on their own practice, students will plan and organise all elements of care, pedagogy and management to ensure the sustainable delivery of a quality ECEC setting.
learningOutcomes: Identify areas of continuing professional development through sharing and exchanging expertise with colleagues.
Describe the process of engaging with colleagues as a leader within a professional team.
Organise, resource and manage an inclusive environment for of babies, toddlers, young children and adults cognisant of affordance, constraints, needs and interests.

Contribute to and work effectively as part of a professional team and communicate effectively with families, community and all stakeholder partners in the learning, well-being and development of children.

Collaborate in leading the planning and provision for the care and education of a diverse group of of babies, toddlers and young children (birth – 6 years) in an ECEC setting including recognising and supporting leadership skills in children.
Critically reflect on all elements of practice (care, curriculum, pedagogy, leadership and management) to plan future practice.

modules.moduleName: PLACEMENT LEARNING 1 (EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION  4 TO 7 YEARS)
modules.moduleContent:
School placement is a central feature of the BEd initial-teacher-education programme. The term ‘school placement’ refers to that part of the programme which takes place in school settings, and which is designed to give the student teacher an opportunity to integrate educational theory and practice in a variety of teaching situations and school contexts. The school placement experience encompasses a range of teaching and non-teaching activities and affords the student teacher opportunities to participate in school life in a way that is structured and supported.
With a clear focus on the early years of the primary school, this module is designed to prepare and equip student teachers for school placement in Semester 1 and Semester 2 of Y1 of the BEd. Child Developmental Stages, Child Protection Guidelines, the learning environment, play, and teaching methodologies are critically examined in this module, along with the philosophical influences on teaching and learning in Junior and Senior Infant classes. The module will explore Aistear: The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework and students will organise Aistear play centres on placement.
Professional relationships will be explored, including how best to work with parents and Special Needs Assistants (SNAs) for the benefit of the children.
Students will experience a high support/high challenge model of placement where their strengths are nurtured and fostered, while areas for further learning and development are identified in an overarching context of collaborative professionalism as they engage with their Treoraithe and HEI tutors. 
Students will learn about management strategies that support suitable and effective learning for all pupils and that promote and maintain positive behaviour. 
Students will observe experienced teachers teaching and will be involved in a wide range of school activities. They will have opportunities to actively learn from experienced or practising teachers about relevant and innovate methodologies, including digital technologies, appropriate to their practice. They will also engage meaningfully with Treoraithe on school placement.
Students will reflect critically on their practice and programme of study and how both inform and shape each other. During placement block weeks, students will receive and respond to feedback on their practice from HEI/ School Placement Tutors and Treoraithe in order to identify areas for further professional learning. While on school placement, the student teacher should demonstrate the link between the sites of practice (HEI and school). The student teacher shall, through the use of their Taisce, demonstrate an understanding of inclusive education as applicable to their teaching context and demonstrate an understanding of working with parents.
School Placement consists of two elements: Observation in Semester 1, and School Placement Teaching Block in Semester 2. Students will observe in their placement setting on one day per week in Semester 1. This allows students to become familiar with the culture and practices of schools through observation in an infant classroom. In Semester 2, students will undertake a three-week block placement in the same infant classroom which will enable them to develop their repertoire of teaching and classroom management skills. 
On this placement, students are expected to teach two to three lessons per day along with organising and facilitating play activity centres. On all placements, students are expected to work closely with their Treoraithe.
Students will engage in small group Learning Circles with their peers to promote professional dialogue in a community of learners. The Learning Circles programme helps to prepare student teachers for life in the classroom and for active engagement in teaching within a professional learning community.
learningOutcomes: Plan, teach and evaluate a number of lessons on school placement, using a Froebelian approach.
Demonstrate a working knowledge of the structure, purpose and appropriate application of Aistear – the Early Childhood Curriculum Framework.
Observe and record children in a Junior or Senior Infant class and use their findings to plan future teaching and learning.
Identify and utilise a range of methodologies suitable for use in a Junior or Senior Infant class.
Review, source, select and use appropriate resources and materials for teaching and learning in a Junior or Senior Infant classroom.
Demonstrate ability to establish classroom management strategies that support suitable and effective learning for all pupils and that promote and maintain positive behaviour.
Reflect on time spent observing in classes from Junior Infants to Second in order to enhance their practice and professional development.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Foundation Module 1 encourages student teachers to engage with key theorists and thinkers that provide a critical framework for thinking and learning in education. Examples include: Bourdieu in Sociology of Education; Piaget, Vygotsky and Froebel and Dewey, in Philosophy of Education. 
All courses will draw heavily from relevant national and international research in the area, enabling newly-qualified teachers to facilitate quality teaching and learning for all pupils. Recognising the need to prepare students to benefit fully from their Student Placement, particular emphasis is placed upon Psychology of Education in Year 1. This course is designed specifically to provide students with the empirical knowledge to understand the process of learning, both for themselves and for the children in the classes in which they are placed.
An important objective of this module is strengthening student teachers’ reflexivity on their own professional learning and that of their pupils, and to prepare student teachers to support their pupils in achieving their full potential. This is achieved through engagement with the following topics covered in this module: 
• Sociology of Education: Types of Schooling; Society, Culture and Identity; Norms, Values and Roles; and the Hidden Curriculum.
• Psychology of Education: Stages of Cognitive Development and Critical Reflection.
• Philosophy of Education as a global phenomenon and as reflection on experience.
• History of Education: Irish Education pre-1831; History of Irish Primary Education in the modern era 1831; present, historical perspectives on great educationalists including Froebel, Dewey, Montessori, Gandhi, Tagore, Nyerere, and Freire.
learningOutcomes: Reflect on central educational issues in teaching and learning which will help foster professional identity and agency.
Recognise the relationship between the foundation subjects and the practice of teaching and relate the work of key theorists to their school placement.
Explain the relevance of national and international research in deepening our understanding of teaching and learning.
Examine the Irish education system against a framework of national and international research.
Examine the Irish education system against a framework of national and international research.
Establish the role of learning theories using examples from practice.
Reflect, individually and in collaboration with their peers and HEI lecturers, on their attitudes and beliefs about teaching and learning which inform and guide their professional practice.

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to complement and integrate with the module EDF131 Language 2/Teanga 2 through student teachers’ engagement in discussion seminars and reflection activities. Through engagement with peers, in-group activities, and task-based work, student teachers will develop their own literacy and literary pedagogical knowledge and skills as well as digital literacy skills. The teaching of early reading, early writing, and speaking and listening in the junior classes as well as the importance of home-school relationships will be examined. Student teachers will be challenged to creatively develop their approaches to teaching and learning of language, literacy, and literature in ways that inclusively build on the unique identity and experiences of all pupils. Informed by Froebelian philosophy, student teachers will reflect on their own attitudes, dispositions, skills and experiences as literacy learners and their own relationships with literature in order to better understand the factors that have shaped their values, approaches, and practice in the teaching and learning of language, literacy, and literature.
learningOutcomes: Describe the process of typical child-language development and underlying factors that impact on patterns of child-language development.
Identify key stages in language acquisition and relate these to the expected age range of development.

Identify, plan, apply and reflect on appropriate and creative pedagogical approaches, including play and the use of the emergent curriculum, in the teaching and learning of oral language, writing and reading to early-years pupils.

Demonstrate an understanding of a range of pedagogical approaches, curricular content, knowledge, and strategies to develop an effective classroom programme for promoting inclusive literacy and experiences with literature.
Reflect on their own experiences as language learners and as agentic professionals.

Demonstrate a knowledge of a range of children’s literature as well as these texts' potential for supporting and stimulating learning environments, aesthetic engagement, and creative opportunities for children.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
With a clear focus on the early years in the primary school, this module is designed to prepare and equip student teachers for the provision of quality teaching and learning of Mathematics and Science for all pupils. 
As part of this module, student teachers will have opportunities to actively learn from experienced HEI lecturers about relevant and innovate methodologies, appropriate to the teaching of Mathematics and Science. Theories of emergent numeracy, number acquisition, and mathematical and scientific teaching and learning appropriate to Junior Infants to 2nd class will be examined. 
Student teachers will engage meaningfully with Treoraithe on school placement with regard to the teaching of Mathematics and Science. The student teacher shall, through the use of their Taisce, demonstrate an understanding of Mathematics and Science education as applicable to school placement. 
The creation of an enquiry-based and independent learning environment involving the utilisation of indoor and outdoor environments in keeping with Froebelian philosophy to promote STEM is emphasised in this module. Linkage and integration across curriculum subjects, particularly through project work will be explored to promote an enquiry based, emergent curriculum. Methodologies such as station teaching, guided discovery, fieldwork, hands-on learning, development of subject-based vocabulary and questioning, linkage and integration, problem solving, hands-on activities and games, investigation table and displays, reflective practice, outdoor learning, digital learning and children’s literature will be utilised. 
Methods of pupil recording, documentation and assessment of pupils’ work including self and peer evaluation will be explored to support quality teaching and learning for all pupils.  
Significant emphasis is placed on education for sustainable development and greater sustainable practices. There is demonstrable integration of environmentally sustainable practice in teaching and learning rooted in the principle of care for others and the planet, throughout the module. 
Links to Mathematics Competency modules will be explicitly made.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a working knowledge of teaching and learning of the Primary School Curriculum of Mathematics and Science for Junior Infants to 2nd class along with the themes, principles and aims of Aistear: The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework informed by a Froebelian approach.

Demonstrate the content, pedagogical knowledge, and vocabulary of mathematics and science necessary for teaching in primary school along with Emergent Numeracy and Numeracy Acquisition Theories in practice.
Utilise a range of methodologies specific to mathematical and scientific enquiry in Junior Infants to 2nd classes (including use of ICT and the Outdoor Environment).

Review, select and use appropriate resources and materials for teaching and learning mathematics and science appropriate for Junior Infants to 2nd class with reference to their Taisce and in discussion with HEI lecturers.
Create and utilise an enquiry-based environment and consistently encourage an enquiry-based culture indoors and outdoors, appropriate for Junior Infants to 2nd class.
Explore and facilitate the use of playful, innovative, and place-based pedagogies in mathematical and scientific learning.
Facilitate and develop the use of Froebelian approaches in Junior or Senior Infant classrooms.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: PERSONAL AND PROFESSIONAL SKILLS FOR TEACHING AND LEARNING 1
modules.moduleContent:
The course focuses on developing the communicative skills of the student teacher, introducing the theory and practice of active listening and the skills of presentation. Academic writing purposes, styles and skills are compared, and the practice of academic referencing is introduced. 
Strong links are identified between theory and practice by means of engagement with pertinent national and international literature which emphasise the school as a learning community. 

Student teachers will engage critically with literature pertaining to teaching itself through the investigation of relevant concepts and theoretical and conceptual frameworks. This can lay the foundation for the development of sound self-critical learning with ample opportunities for individual and collaborative reflection, in fostering a creative mindset among student teachers as reflective practitioners. 

From a personal competency-building perspective, student teachers are afforded opportunities to enhance their own literacy and numeracy in the following areas: Maths, Written and Oral Irish, and Literacy, in addition to Music Education, ICT, and Visual Arts Education. 
They will then choose and study one of those areas. 

Student teachers will be introduced to new and emerging technologies for teaching and learning. Their personal confidence and competence with digital skills is a focus of practical workshops, through the creation of their Taisce to support the process of portfolio-based learning.
learningOutcomes: Create the Taisce to support the process of portfolio-based learning in the organisation, management, and evaluation of learning.
Consult and engage critically with literature pertaining to teaching itself through engagement with theoretical and conceptual frameworks.
Experiment with and explore new and emerging technologies for teaching and learning.
Employ a range of digital technologies including multi-media resources, effectively to facilitate teaching and assessment practices and to aid pupil learning.
Advance the communicative and written production skills of student teachers, including their use of technology through Taisce to support effective communication.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: CUMAS NA GAEILGE, ENGLISH & MATHEMATICAL COMPETENCY LEVEL 1
modules.moduleContent: Oral and written Irish-Language assessment will be informed by The Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (2020). Students’ entry level competency in each subject area will be assessed at the start of the first semester and this will be used in supporting student teachers to identify and meet their own learning targets. In English and Mathematics, student teachers will develop competency and confidence around the use of relevant concepts, skills and terminology. In Irish and English, students will develop their communication and presentation skills across different language registers and genres. Student teachers will engage in reflective practice in competency utilising their developing digital capacity.  This development in professional growth in competency will enable student teachers to facilitate quality teaching and learning for all pupils. This module will complement EDF130, EDF131, and EDF132, and methodologies of teaching in these subject areas.
learningOutcomes: Reflect on their own language use and numeracy and define appropriate personal learning targets and actions suitable for a first-year student to provide for reflexivity and professional growth.
Demonstrate and develop competency, accuracy, confidence and fluency in spoken and written Gaeilge and English, appropriate to a first-year student.
Demonstrate and develop competency, accuracy and confidence in Mathematics.
Use and develop a range of language registers, grammar and vocabulary in Irish and English.
Develop in their understanding and capacity to confidently lead the learning in these subjects in the classroom in their professional role.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: SOCIAL CONSTRUCT OF IDENTITY IN THE PRIMARY SCHOOL
modules.moduleContent:
Following an introduction to the ideas of Narrative Practice, students will explore the multi-storied and fluid nature of identity through experiential exercises. Links to developmental psychology and its application to schools will allow for reflection on the 
quality of their relationship with children and the idea of school ‘as secure base’.  
The student teacher will consider the teacher-pupil relationship and the power of language in fostering good relationships with and among pupils based on mutual respect, trust, and meaningful interactions.    
Drawing on the ‘Maps’ of Narrative Practice: Externalising, Re-authoring and Re-membering Framework (White, 2007), student teachers will learn skills for conducting conversations with children about problems they face and about positive directions in their lives. Heteronormativity will be explored as an example of how school culture can silence identity stories.
Guided by Froebelian teaching about looking for what the child can do instead of what they can’t do, student teachers will become aware of how language can shape a child’s experience in school.  Student teachers will learn how to excavate storylines of talent, effort, and commitment in the lives of their pupils and contribute to positive identities and nurturing school experiences.
learningOutcomes: Discern when the language and the discourse of school are impacting on the identity of pupils in negative ways.
Understand the importance of teacher–pupil relationships in the teaching/learning process and the power of the teacher’s language to create children’s identities.
Foster good relationships with and among pupils based on mutual respect, trust and meaningful interactions.
Reflect critically on their practice on an ongoing basis and adjust their practice to meet the needs of the child.
Conduct conversations with children who are struggling in school with negative reputations and change those reputations over time.
Use school documents, events and performances to highlight positive identities which are emerging in the lives of children.

modules.moduleContent:
This module integrates aspects of Irish methodologies and children’s literature in Irish and is designed to complement and integrate with Modules EDF130 Language 1, EDF131 Teanga 2 and EDF231 Language 3 through student teachers’ engagement in discussion seminars and reflection activities. 
 
Particular emphasis in this module will be placed on Froebelian approaches to language pedagogy appropriate for the teaching of Irish in the primary school. The teaching of oral language, reading and writing will be explored, along with classroom practices for literacy development. Particular emphasis will be placed on the adoption of creative, play-based methodologies Immersion education in Irish-medium schools will also be studied.  

Student teachers will engage in dialogue with HEI lecturers about the importance of folklore in children’s literature. Story-telling, poetry, folklore and translation of picturebooks in Irish language literature will be studied in the context of their value for all age groups within the primary school. The contribution of these genres to cultural self-identity will be analysed both inside and outside the classroom environment.  Digital resources for the teaching of Irish will be critically evaluated and student teachers will use this knowledge to inform their development of a digital picturebook for the classroom based on the folklore of their chosen Tréimhse Foghlama sa Ghaeltacht, demonstrating their digital literacy skills. This assessment will be housed in the student teachers’ TAISCE portfolios. To further underline the integrative nature of this course, student teachers are required to spend one week on placement in a Gaelscoil and will use the EDF232 learning outcomes in their teaching to meaningfully connect with their teaching and learning and their Tréimhse Foghlama sa Ghaeltacht. Reflective practice is embedded in the module, where student teachers reflect on themselves as language learners and language teachers and also on the implication of the Gaelscoil placement on their future practice.  

Building on EDF130, EDF131 and EDF231, in the language seminars, student teachers continue to explore the integrated nature of language and literacy teaching and learning, as advocated in the Primary Language Curriculum (DES, 2019).  Through offering this space for professional dialogue and reflection on and in practice, student teachers will explore their professional identity as a teacher of languages and literacy in schools.
learningOutcomes: Identify linkages between language theories, literature, methodologies and classroom practice in order to promote literacy and language in English and Irish.
Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of methodologies and an integrated approach to the teaching of Irish in the middle and senior classes informed by a Froebelian, child-centred approach.
Develop knowledge and understanding of the genres of story-telling, poetry, folklore, myth and legend in Irish language literature.
Evaluate and plan for the appropriate use of Irish literature texts for the classroom setting.

Critically integrate research and theoretical perspectives into teaching, learning and the Gaeltacht placement by reflecting on central educational issues, including immersive education and supporting professional judgements and actions with convincing argument.
Utilise their digital skillset in order to create a digital Gaeltacht-based picturebook connected to the folklore of their chosen Gaeltacht region and housed in their TAISCE portfolio.
Think critically, analyse, and solve problems as an individual and as part of a team and through a pedagogy of community engaged learning.
