modules.moduleName: PLACEMENT LEARNING 3: THE TEACHER AS A CHANGE AGENT
modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed to provide student teachers with a solid foundation of high-level beginning knowledge, skills, and professional values critical to the success of school placement and the development of their professional identities.

School placement is a central feature of the B.Ed initial teacher education programme. 

This module is designed to prepare and equip student teachers for school placement in Semester 1 and Semester 2. Students will extend their repertoire of teaching skills to include group teaching, cross-curricular skills, integration, monitoring pupil progress, medium to long-term planning and designing age-appropriate activities. Students will also engage in thematic planning, implement a variety of strategies for teaching and learning; assess pupils learning and employ effective classroom management skills. 

A particular focus of year 3 will be formative assessment practices and differentiation and how to effectively motivate pupils to learn. They will explore how digital technology can be used effectively for teaching and learning. At all times, students will be encouraged to reflect on their own experiential learning and evaluate and review planning. Professional relationships will be explored, including how best to work with parents and Special Needs Assistants (SNAs) for the benefit of the children.

In Semester 1 students will be placed a 5th or 6th class. In Semester 2, students will be placed for three weeks in an infant setting. 

Students will also be placed for one week in a special class or special school setting. Students will further develop and extend their repertoire of teaching skills and be expected to partake in the daily life of the school.

In Semester 2, students will critically examine the concept and potential of Teacher as Change Agent. Agency refers to teachers’ understanding of their status as autonomous professionals teaching in community contexts (community of practice; school community). It also refers to their capacity to act on this understanding in intentional, responsible and innovative ways that reflect and enhance their relationships with peers, students, parents and the wider community. School culture and how it impacts on teaching and learning and the implementation of curriculum policy will also be examined.
learningOutcomes: Effectively manage and organise teaching and learning in the classroom
Apply subject and pedagogical knowledge to plan, teach and assess and reflect on their teaching and learning
Demonstrate competence in teaching performance and planning at infant and 5th/ 6th class levels and use Froebelian approaches to achieve planned learning outcomes
Provide for the holistic development of the pupil, particularly through differentiated approaches and appropriate Assessment Of and For learning and differentiation strategies
Engage with a variety of learning resource professionals on placement and articulate their understanding of the complex role of these professionals
Recognise dilemmas and complexities in the practice of teaching, including Teacher Agency and Teacher as Change Agent in educational settings.
Be able to understand school culture and how it impacts on teaching and learning and the implementation of curriculum policy.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module equips students with the knowledge, creativity, competencies, attitudes, and values needed to develop student teachers’ commitment to education as ethical, rational, compassionate, defensible, sustaining, and valuable.

This module fosters student teachers’ reflexivity on their own professional learning and that of their pupils by highlighting the relationship between schools and the wider society. Acknowledging that this association is critical and complex, the module explores structural forces at play and contextualises them against a theoretical framework of social justice and equality.

The overarching aim of this module is to foster student teachers’ agency and to enhance the development of criticality. The module is designed to encourage student teachers to develop an integrated view of education, preparing student teachers for entry to their professional role. 

Curriculum studies highlights the role of policy formation pertaining to curriculum choice, which forms a significant link to learning outcomes in the programme and is problematised as a central aspect to looking at curriculum and assessment for intended/unintended purposes. The field of curriculum instruction, sociology of curriculum and curriculum development within the teacher education sector is explored in the context of national priorities. Leadership for curriculum in schools aims to encourage fostering of teacher agency for curriculum development in schools and within professional learning communities.

Philosophy of education acknowledges education as a human right and awareness of how pervasively this right is not vindicated, or is vindicated only partially and inadequately, both nationally and globally encourages participants to articulate their own appropriation of these issues and enables them to adopt concepts such as restorative justice to meet these contemporary challenges.


In Psychology of Education students are introduced to psychological science and its relevance to teaching and learning. Particular emphasis is placed on the effect of memory, attention and motivation in the classroom. As well as fostering of teacher’s agency to implement practical strategies to help improve pupil’s learning.

Sociology of Education provides an opportunity to engage with topics such as race, ethnicity, identity, types of schooling, which will support newly qualified teachers to facilitate quality teaching and learning for all pupils. The sociology of education is the study of how social institutions and individual experiences affects education and its outcome. The topics covered in this module seek to explain how people relate with one another, how hidden structures play an important role in everyday life, how individuals behave and how all these combined impacts on education and its outcome. In total, the topics addressed in sociology of education foster student teachers’ abilities to support their pupils in achieving their full potential.
learningOutcomes: Foster reflexivity on their own professional learning and that of their pupils.

Nurture student teachers’ agency and mind-set to be open to professional growth and learning over the course of their careers, supporting their pupils in achieving their full potential by integrating this knowledge into their practice as teachers.

Assume their professional identity through critical reflection upon issues relating to Curriculum, Social Justice, Equality as a Philosophical Concept and Ethics and apply them to everyday schools’ experiences.
Critically reflect on educational philosophies that shape and inform curriculum and evaluate the social, political and economic influences on curriculum policy in Ireland since the 1970s.
Examine the integrative nature of curriculum and assessment practices from a theoretical perspective and consider teacher agency in developing curriculum leadership in schools.

modules.moduleContent:
This module develops more complex understanding of the principles, aims and methodologies of national curricula/curricular frameworks explored in Year 1 and Year 2. Student teachers implement a range of methodologies and continue to experiment with and explore new and emerging technologies for teaching and learning to achieve planned outcomes in the curricular areas of Wellbeing, Arts Education, and Social, Environmental and Scientific Education. 

The module will build on student teacher understanding of the significance of knowledge of the individual potential of pupils in their planning for teaching, learning and assessment of Visual Arts, Drama, Music, PE, SESE and SPHE, through Froebelian and child-led approaches.   
Student teachers will connect relevant principles and theories of education, in the context of the relevant national curricula curricular frameworks, using well-developed skills of enquiry, to inform his/ her professional practice. 

 Student teachers will also examine and apply a broad range of assessment principles and practices, preparing them for teaching, learning, creative and reflective practice and assessment in their schools. To this end, research associated with Froebelian practice and principles will be elaborated and extended upon. Collaborative and problem-based learning and differentiated teaching strategies will be examined in depth.
learningOutcomes: Access, develop, adapt and use a variety of curriculum resources and materials (including new and emerging technologies) for learning and teaching to challenge all pupils.
Set clear, challenging and achievable expectations for all pupils in line with the curriculum.
Reflect critically on the effectiveness of their practice on an ongoing basis so as to inform and adjust their practice.
Utilise assessment and differentiation strategies, appropriate to the developmental stages of the individual primary school child.
Consider the value of problem-posing approaches to promote a Froebelian inquiry-based learning environment.
Engage in Taisce e-portfolio-based learning and identify area(s) for further learning in the Droichead induction phase of the continuum.

modules.moduleName: TEACH FOR TOMORROW: EDUCATION FOR SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed to provide students with an opportunity to engage with emerging issues in education for sustainable development (ESD) from three distinct perspectives: the political, the socio-economic and the environmental. It will examine the politics, ethics, and science behind our relationship with the environment and wider global community. The module will support student teachers in reflecting on their rights and responsibilities as global citizens and introduce them to creative pedagogical approaches to explore new global realities with children in the primary classroom. This ESD module will build upon prior learning in Global Citizenship (1) and Global Citizenship (2) modules in B.EdY2&3 and PMEdy1&2.

This module will utilise a range of creative, active, and experiential methodologies to explore global justice issues related to sustainability. Child-centred pedagogical approaches will be modelled to embed inclusive approaches to quality teaching and learning in school for all pupils. The use of the outdoor environment will be promoted both on campus, and during an off-site immersive study visit. 

The following topics will be explored:
• Human rights
• Ethical consumerism (food and fashion) 
• ‘Bigger than self’ concepts: Interdependence and solidarity 
• Biodiversity 
• Climate justice and climate action 
• Peace education
• Zero Waste 
• Sustainability in the context of the Global South 

This ESD module will also develop education partnerships on campus, for example by involving representatives from Trócaire, the Green Campus Initiative and the College Biodiversity committee in relevant sessions. It will also further develop our existing partnership with the Cloughjordan Eco-Village education team as well as a local primary school, who will be invited to campus for a station teaching exercise.
learningOutcomes: Appreciate the value of ESD as an agent of change in the primary school
Enhance their awareness of the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDG’s)
Become familiar with relevant pedagogical content knowledge in ESD and opportunities for integration within the new primary curriculum framework
Appreciate and experience the benefits of outdoor teaching and learning and recognise biodiversity in their local environment
Understand the concept of interdependence and the significance of linking the local to the global in teaching and learning
Develop key skills for ESD resource and lesson development

modules.moduleContent: This course will equip teachers with theoretical and practical ideas for supporting children in schools when they are encountering difficult times. Students will consider the impact of trauma, bereavement, parental separation, parental drug misuse, and abuse/neglect on the lives of children. Students will explore how such challenges impact on children’s family life and education, explore school-based initiatives, which offer protective structures, and clarify their responsibilities in relation to child protection. Students will learn about the Meitheal process and build confidence in participating or leading a Meitheal.
learningOutcomes: Students will understand the impact of challenging life experience on children and their parents and families, and how this can impact on learning in school
Students will have a theoretical framework for understanding their role as carers in school
Students will have a range of school-based strategies to support children.
Students will be able to discern when a child needs a support services outside the school and will be able to make appropriate referrals and cooperate with protective agencies including Tusla.

modules.moduleContent:
There are two distinct but related components to this module. The first is to develop student teachers’ subject-matter knowledge of mathematics to aid in their numeracy development. This will be done by engaging student teachers in problem-solving activities involving mathematics that spans the mathematics curriculum. As well as students socially constructing mathematical knowledge, there are specific instances of direct instruction to model mathematical ideas to further develop student-teacher understanding and to avoid possible misconceptions.  

Secondly, this module problematises the reasons that primary-school pupils require intervention in mathematics and supports student teachers in choosing and using a range of models of intervention across all mathematical strands to respond to the needs of all pupils. Students will be challenged to examine and reflect upon the various contextual and systemic factors at play in relation to pupil attainment and to consider their professional agency and responsibility in this regard. There will also be a focus on the use of assessment as the basis of determining, planning for, and monitoring pupil learning.

As part of a suite of numeracy-related modules across the BEd programme, student teachers will be encouraged to create meaningful links to see mathematics as a coherent whole and address gaps between theory and practice. To facilitate this, students will engage in reflective practice utilising their developing digital capacity. This development in professional growth in competency will promote professional agency to enact quality teaching and learning for all pupils.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate the ability to apply mathematics subject-matter knowledge to solve practical problems.
Reflect on new knowledge from engaging in problems and evaluate how it can be integrated with relevant theories of education to inform their professional practice.
Demonstrate an appreciation of mathematics as a creative process as opposed to a product of rules and procedures.
Describe, utilise, and reflect upon a range of models of intervention in mathematics education in primary school and demonstrate an in-depth knowledge of one of these models.
Demonstrate an understanding of why some primary pupils require intervention in mathematics and critically reflect upon their own role and professional agency toward addressing these factors.
Demonstrate an understanding that high expectations underpin effective and inclusive teaching and learning for all pupils.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with knowledge and understanding in Restorative Practices in schools and the local communities by combining theoretical and practical applications so that the students will progress with a set of values and practical skills to enable them to implement Restorative Practices in the classroom, school, and beyond.

Restorative Practices are increasingly used in schools, both primary and secondary, as a core part of teacher-pupil engagement, to support the development of positive relationships with staff, children and parents, behaviour management and conflict resolution. The work of Restorative Practices in education encompasses the school community in its entirety: staff, students, and their parents. The values and principles of Restorative Practice in action in our schools support the cultivation of a caring, inclusive culture. Using the Restorative Practice lens can have a tremendous impact on how we teach social justice and resolve conflict. Studies have shown that using Restorative Practices reduces disciplinary problems, enhances peer and student-teacher relations, supports the development of emotional literacy, and empowers individuals to have a voice and agency in the healthy resolution of conflict
learningOutcomes: Understand and use restorative language (feelings, needs, doable requests), as a means of building and strengthening their personal and professional relationships.
Apply Fair Process and the Relationship Window in their teaching practice and the world beyond the classroom.
Demonstrate competency in the use of restorative circles in schools and the wider community.
Plan, participate in, and facilitate relationship-building circles.
Demonstrate an in-depth knowledge of the theory, methodology, and applications for restorative conversations and restorative meetings.
Experience facilitating and/or participating in a restorative meeting.
Apply Restorative Practice values and skills in key school processes such as reflective practice, managing conflict, consultation processes, collaborative learning, and educational policy priorities.

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to provide students with a wide-ranging and comprehensive overview of the range of reading disabilities as well as information about related difficulties of writing and spelling. There is a comprehensive and balanced examination of the reading process and of the varying theories and practices relating to reading difficulties.  Students are introduced to the most effective assessment strategies and teaching practices; the issues and trends raised in relation to intervention are evidence-based.  Information is provided in relation to definitions, characteristics, diagnosis, intervention learning styles and eligibility / placement issues. The role of speech / language in any specific or general reading difficulty is explored as is the possible co-occurrence of motor-related difficulties. Students are provided with a range of possible diagnostic approaches to intervention.
learningOutcomes: • have experience of using at least one assessment suitable for measuring reading fluency and reading comprehension
• describe instructional strategies for improving comprehension and fluency
• have experience of at least one specific intervention for improving reading comprehension
• understand the relevance of content area materials to the teaching of reading comprehension
• be familiar with the characteristics of pupils with reading difficulties and specific reading disability

• discuss the relationship to pupils’ reading ability of: attention / concentration, speech/language including phonological awareness,  memory, information processing patterns,   
        cognitive styles and learning strategies
• understand the consequences of speech / language delay, visual / auditory processing difficulties and delays in gross / fine motor development for literacy acquisition

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to provide students with knowledge and skills that will enable them to teach students with a range of low-incident disabilities at both primary and second-level. Students are provided with specific input in relation to pupils with: specific speech and language disorder; students with social, emotional and behavioural difficulties including pupils with ADD/ADHD and pupils with conduct disorders and serious levels of aggression and pupils on the Autistic Spectrum. The range of difficulties addressed on the Course changes from year-to-year in order to address the need of those taking the course. Practical strategies for adapting instruction to meet the learning needs of pupils with GLD are provided. Course input on each of the low-incidence SEN reflects current research in the field. Where applicable suitable technology, software and assistive devices are highlighted. There is a close relationship between this Module and the planning and teaching requirements of the Teaching Practice Module.
learningOutcomes: • have an understanding about the cognitive, behavioural and psychological effects of the categories of low incidence SEN
• understand how different groups of pupils with different low incidence SEN learn and develop
• describe strategies for enhancing students’ social emotional development, including social relationships, communication skills and self-esteem
• possess information about the repertoire of possible instructional strategies suitable for use with the different categories of low incidence SEN
• possess an understanding of the language / communication difficulties of pupils within the different low-incidence disability groupings
• be aware of a range of language and communication strategies suitable for use with pupil within the different low-incidence populations
• describe ways in which particular disabilities can be prevented
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: MATHEMATICS AND CURRENT ISSUES IN SEN
modules.moduleContent: Students are introduced to the nature and range of difficulties which pupils across the SEN spectrum experience with mathematics. These in turn are related to the strategies and resources appropriate to the particular difficulties. They have further input on particular low-incidence disabilities. Aspects of ICT of particular relevance to pupils with physical impairment are covered, together with hardware and software of wider application in the general SEN area. Students working on either side of the primary / post-primary divide are presented with findings from relevant literature and examples of best-practice pertaining to transition. Students address ethical / responsibility issues arising from the context where considerable discretion is devolved to schools / teachers in identifying pupils’ special educational needs and ensuring that the education the pupils receive is appropriate.
learningOutcomes: understand how the characteristics of mathematical disabilities are related to information processing, language, reading and cognitive learning strategies
possess a range of strategies for teaching mathematics concepts, skills and problem solving
synthesise information gleaned from maths assessments to develop an appropriate programme of work for a pupil with SEN
have a awareness of the range of ICT resources suitable for use with pupils with SEN
describe the personal characteristics of pupils identified as having sensory and physical impairments
describe the impact sensory and physical impairment have on interactions in the home, school, and larger social system
have the capacity to apply strategies to mediate the environment for learners identified with sensory or physical impairment
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
In all, students will be visited at three different times during the course of this programme. The first two visits are supportive in nature, the third is evaluated. The first visit(s) occur(s) between Module One and Two; the second visit(s) between Module Three and Four with the final, assessed visits usually occurring just before Module 5. Students have the same supervisor for Visits one and two and receive written and oral feedback with pointers on how they might improve their practise. The course coordinator also sees all of the students at some point over the course of the First and Second Supportive Visits. For the final visits students are expected to demonstrate that they have developed their skills in relation to: planning, timetabling, organisation and management, ability to differentiate, ongoing monitoring of progress, ability to write IEPs & IPLPs as well as the ability to use data from assessment to inform planning and teaching. 

For the final visit, students are visited by a ‘new’ supervisor. In addition, each of the Course participants is visited by the Course Coordinator. This is to ensure consistency of marking across supervisors.  In addition, the Extern examiner visits a random sample of Course participants. The Extern examiner determines who he/she wishes to visit. Again, the external examiner ensures quality control in terms of the marking system across the Special Ed. Teaching Practice.  Where there is a difference of opinion concerning the grade a course participant is to receive or in the event of a course participant not meeting the expected standards laid down for this Teaching Practice or where a course participant is in danger of failing the teaching or planning elements of the teaching practice, the External examiner will visit the student in question.
learningOutcomes: • understand how and why content, presentation and classroom placement are vital considerations in planning for pupils with special educational needs
• demonstrate the ability to write long-and short-term plans
• demonstrate the ability to update plans on an ongoing basis
• show how planning is subject to review in response to pupil response to learning or need
• show how classroom instruction is differentiated in terms of (a) planning and (b) teaching

implement strategies to facilitate transitions
demonstrate the system in place for record keeping
• write IEPs and IPLPs in accordance with the EPSEN Act (2004) and / or the Learning Support Guidelines

modules.moduleContent: The module is divided into two parts. The first will provide, in a postgraduate context and from a Froebelian perspective, preparation for short-term planning (the construction of lesson plans) and long-term planning (the production of schemes) for the teaching of religious education on School Placement (Junior Infants to Second Class). It will introduce students to the range of programmes of religious education currently in use in Primary Schools in Ireland. The second part will consist of an overview of religious affiliation in twenty-first century Ireland, along with an introduction to the main world religions, including the non-religious worldview, and to the wide variety of branches of Christianity.
learningOutcomes: Engage in short-term and long term planning for the teaching of religious education from a Frobelian perspective on School Placement (Junior Infants to Second Class);
increase their familiarity with the four principal programmes of religious education currently in use in Primary Schools in Ireland;
identify, describe and appreciate some of the main world religions;
increase their awareness of the many forms Christianity takes in twenty-first century Ireland and beyond.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will develop the student’s competency in Mathematics necessary to confidently and competently teach across all ages in the primary classroom.
Student teachers will develop competency and confidence around the use of relevant mathematical concepts, skills, and terminology. The module will support student teachers’ engagement with mathematics methodology through their development relational understanding of mathematics which can be applied across the curriculum. To facilitate this, students will engage in reflective practice utilising their developing digital capacity. This development in professional growth in competency will promote professional agency to enact quality teaching and learning for all pupils.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate and develop competency, accuracy and confidence in Mathematics relevant for primary-school teaching.
Demonstrate the ability to reflect on their mathematical knowledge and how it can be integrated with relevant theories of education to inform their professional practice.
Develop their understanding and capacity to confidently lead the mathematics learning in their professional role.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
There are two aims for this module. The first is to develop the students’ mathematical understanding and problem solving abilities. Mathematical investigations will be central to this component. The investigative nature of the module will encourage creativity and learning through exploration. Through this exploration, students will take a step away from rules and procedures often associated with mathematics. Concepts, skills and terminology relevant to the primary school curriculum will be used throughout the module. The module will help to develop students’ overall ability, knowledge and understanding of mathematics. 

The second aim is to explore models of intervention in mathematics education in the Primary School from a range of intervention models such as Maths Recovery, First Steps in Mathematics, etc. The implementation of these models will be explored within the context of the Primary School Curriculum and Irish classrooms.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a creative approach to solving mathematical problems.
Apply mathematical knowledge and logical reasoning to systematically investigate and solve mathematical problems.
Reflect on, and appreciate, mathematics as a process as opposed to a product of rules and procedures.
Critically analyse and evaluate multiple solutions to a given problem.
Evaluate the usefulness and creativity that is at the heart of mathematics and demonstrate the ability to apply this within the context of the primary school mathematics curriculum.
Demonstrate an in depth knowledge of one model of intervention in mathematics education in Primary School.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to reflective practice by inviting them to systematically review their learning experiences to date, their current experience in school and educational settings. They will be required to think critically about their educational ideas, values and concepts and locate the emergence of these ideas in their own educational philosophy. Students will be invited to review what they value in education. They will be introduced to key contributors and to the notion of reflective practice such as Dewey, Schön, Brookfield, Mezirow, Zwozdiak-Myers and Moon. 

The module will focus on the development of professional practice using reflection to challenge self and support best practice and examine one’s ‘Self’ as a professional practitioner. Student skills in reflective writing will be developed through the use of creative arts-based methods of reflection, journal writing and the creation of a portfolio. The use of appropriate reflective frameworks for reflection-on-practice and the consideration of new ways of thinking, learning and improving practice will be examined. Students will be encouraged to apply reflective frameworks to their practice and examine how reflection can be integrated as a lifelong process within their professional practice.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a critical self-awareness of their learning potential and how the use of reflection can increase personal and professional effectiveness.
Review and reflect on their core values and beliefs about teaching and learning.
Evaluate the usefulness of reflection as a tool for your personal and professional development.
See the connections between reflective practice and professional learning.
Utilise the core skills and perspectives of a reflective practitioner.
Demonstrate how reflective processes might enrich critical thinking, analysis and evaluation in their practice.
Critically appraise how, through reflective practice, students might contribute to making a significant difference in meeting the needs of pupils in educational settings.

modules.moduleContent: To provide research-informed insights to support pre-service teachers’ understanding of the practices of teaching, learning and assessment for all students Additionally, the module will explore the relationship between principles of curricular development, lesson activities, and the effective use of various forms of assessment in support of curricular designs. Lectures and module activities will focus on the exploration of curriculum within the Irish education system and relevant contexts. Student teachers will be asked to use this exploration to inform their curriculum making and lesson planning efforts. Time will be spent critically and collaboratively evaluating the effective integration of key skills, literacy and numeracy and well-being into subject area instructional designs. Students will collaborate, co-plan and share insights of their emerging pedagogical thinking.
learningOutcomes: apply pedagogical insight, including subject specific pedagogical content knowledge in their curriculum development, planning and enactment
use a range of strategies to support, monitor and assess pupils’ approach to learning and their progress – including effective feedback strategies
experiment with and explore new and emerging technologies for teaching and learning in their disciplines

advance their communicative skills and ensure that their teaching is understood and practised as a form of self-critical learning, underpinned by opportunities for individual and collaborative reflection, and engagement in and with research
practice adaptive expertise through reflection in, on and with action
demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the importance of sharing professional learning in a collegial manner to support and enhance teaching and learning
apply knowledge of the individual potential of pupils, dispositions towards learning, varying backgrounds, identities, experiences to planning for teaching, learning and assessment

modules.moduleName: CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT 3 (LESSON STUDY RESEARCH PROJECT)
modules.moduleContent:
This module is focused on student teachers as competent, reflective pedagogic researchers. A key modular objective is a focus on action-research and the building of teachers’ capacities as practitioner researchers. This module will explore the lesson study process and invite students to undertake substantive pedagogical research in subject-specific or cross-curricular groups.  Together they will research, plan, teach and critique (in evidenced ways) a number of study lessons (iteratively linked) based on the agreed pedagogic dilemma. 
The module aims to build a strong sense of professional identity in student teachers, recognising the unique role they play in the leadership of their own professional development as reflective practitioners. It aims to support student-teachers in building a research-informed repertoire of teaching and assessment capacities, along with the judgement to discern the appropriate use of these capacities. Working through a lesson study, action research framework the module will strengthen student-teachers capacity to collaboratively reflect on, research and modify their teaching. In doing so, the module aims to raise student-teachers awareness of the life-long journey of teacher development. 
Research from within our own department has convincingly shown that where teachers engage in collaborative reviews of their own work, they readily enhance their capabilities for perceptive analysis and constructive practical action. In doing so, they build their capacity as resourceful, articulate practitioners, with the confidence to publicly share illuminating and convincing accounts of their work.

Module objectives align with the Teaching Council Cosán document which highlights the necessity for:
        Professional ownership of teaching by teachers themselves
 High-quality professional engagement linked to teachers’ daily work
 Accessibility that is matched to the timescales of teaching
Students will engage with key readings in the area of pedagogy, curriculum development, and examine the relationship between curriculum theory and educational practice and the relationship between school programmes, society and the culture in which schools are located. 
The module will also explore Research Methods relevant to the conduct of practitioner-based research. It will assist students in carrying out a group lesson study from its inception through to writing up for submission.
learningOutcomes: conduct a systematic and research rigorous lesson study project
implement a range of methodologies to achieve planned outcomes
evaluate learner progress towards those outcomes
conduct and apply relevant research, as appropriate, to their teaching context: identifying, critically analysing and critically integrating new pedagogical insights into their collective practice
engage in action research on his/her teaching.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is an exploration of the professional life of the teacher outside of their classroom. It aims to help emerging teachers expand their understanding of the role and professional responsibilities of teachers and develop their own agency in contributing to the broader school/educational community. It further offers student teachers the opportunity to critically reflect upon and deliberately begin to craft their own teacher identity.

Through engaging in a ‘professional project’, student teachers are invited to collaborate with school colleagues, external educational stakeholders, school leadership and Maynooth Department of Education Faculty to address a pressing need in their school or contribute to their professional community. The project calls upon student teachers to develop and enhance professional relationships and to understand the potential individual teachers have for supporting the stewardship of their school and their profession.
Lectures and tutorials in this module will explore the nature of the teacher, the teaching profession, and the leadership capacity inherent in the role of teacher as a professional. It will also invite student teachers to critically consider and curate their emerging teacher identity via a guided journal. Students will also be tasked with developing a portfolio that captures their work and thinking as it relates to an Ethical-Relational Concept of Teaching (as reflected in the department’s conceptual framework for ITE). Aspects of teacher identity and relationality explored in the lectures include: teacher agency and autonomy; plurality and diversity in teaching and education; responsibility and care for the personal, social/political, cultural and professional demands of teachers work.
learningOutcomes: Think critically and creatively in response to issues that emerge in the daily lives of schools
Plan, initiate and facilitate curricular and extracurricular events and engagements that contribute to the development of the student, pupils and colleagues
Develop skills in communication, working with others, and being personally effective

Conduct and apply relevant research, as appropriate, to his/her professional context: identifying, critically analysing and critically integrating new professional and educational insights into his/her practice
Critically reflect upon and articulate how everyday actions of teachers reflect and reinforce their teacher identity and the identity of teaching as a profession
Assess and evaluate the experiences and learning of participants engaging in professional and extra-curricular engagements.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will help future teachers understand and critically reflect on the principles and practices of education that embrace the diversity (cognitive, emotional, physical, and linguistic) of learners in the classroom. Student teachers will develop their ability to identify and apply developmentally appropriate practice and lesson design to respond to the learning needs of pupils who exhibit varying competencies and challenges. The module will also increase awareness that additional needs occur across a continuum. Students will be aware of the various supports, services and interventions available in keeping with the continuum of support, as well as the educational legislation that supports them.
Over the course of the module, student teachers will explore the meaning and importance of inclusion for contemporary educational contexts. The lecturer and students will examine the roles and responsibilities of the inclusive practitioner and identify strategies for responding to established and emerging differences in how pupils learn. The various lectures in this module will cover central topics and issues including: Universal Design for Learning; the continuum of support; differentiation in planning, instruction and assessment; understanding student differences (cognitive, emotional, physical and linguistic); practical and concrete strategies for classroom practice; and the appropriate use of ICT to support diverse learners. Attention will also be given to effective collaboration with in-class support professionals.
learningOutcomes: Identify appropriate interventions for varying educational needs
Engage in practical methodologies to support inclusive practice for all
Realise that language and communication is fundamental to all learning
Evidence an enhanced knowledge around the many indicators of an educational need
Identify meaningful and enduring changes to their classroom practice because of their learning of Inclusive and Special Education

modules.moduleContent:
This module seeks to help students examine the social contexts of teaching, learning and schooling utilising foundational principles and theories from the disciplines of Sociology and Sociology of Education. Research and theory from the field will help enhance student’s critical awareness and practical understanding related to the web of social, cultural and political factors that underpin learning in contemporary society.
This module examines the social context of learning, locating the school as a key institution and learning site in contemporary society. A range of theories and research are introduced to provide insights to the web of social, cultural, economic and political factors that underpin learning in contemporary society. 
Module lectures, activities and materials will highlight the embedded and interdependent nature of learning; critically assesses its role at a personal level (as a learner, teacher, family member, etc), institutional level (the schooling system) and the broader social, cultural, economic & political level.
learningOutcomes: Understand and evaluate the key discourses and theories that underpin sociology of education
Articulate informed, personal theories of sociology of education for their current and future professional practice
Critically debate key sociological influences and constructs relating to students learning in education contexts
Demonstrate academic, professional and personal understandings, values and knowledge within and across sociology
Integrate sociological theory and professional practice through a process of individual and group critical reflection
Develop a critical perspective on the challenges and opportunities of contemporary education and teachers’ roles.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module offers students opportunities to study and to put into practice strategies for teaching and learning across cultures and with respect to developing global citizenship. It identifies key educational issues concerning migration and race, Travellers, LGBTQ and gender. Its overall aim is to prepare students for developing culturally appropriate modes of relating to students and to integrate concepts concerning cultural, racial, and social diversity into teaching, learning and assessment practices.
The module focuses on developing a sense of teacher professional responsibility with respect to teaching across cultures. The module focuses on:
1. Key issues in education for global citizenship, with attention paid to issues of cultural diversity in both global and national contexts;
2. Concepts and ideas informing responsive pedagogies that have emerged out of these issues, including culturally responsive pedagogy, anti-racist pedagogy, feminist pedagogy, multicultural and 
        intercultural pedagogy. 
3. Pedagogical strategies that can inform students’ teaching within their educational settings.
learningOutcomes: Identify key issues surrounding the research and practice of global citizenship education
Foster a culturally sensitive approach to addressing issues facing LGTBQ, Traveller, migrant, and racialized communities in their classrooms
Critically engage with concepts and ideas emanating from a range of responsive pedagogies (eg, anti-racist, feminist, multicultural, and culturally responsive pedagogies)
Plan and where possible implement lessons rooted in cultural awareness
Motivate the importance of global citizenship and cultural awareness practices in their teaching.

modules.moduleName: TEACHING AND LEARNING BIOLOGY WITH JUNIOR SCIENCE
modules.moduleContent:
This module offers students an opportunity to expand their knowledge of the curricula and their subject area specifications, syllabi and assessments at both junior cycle and senior cycle levels. It will enable them to develop an understanding of the theoretical underpinnings of active approaches to teaching and learning and their application to their practice in real contexts. Students will explore ways to apply a cross-curricular approach to their lessons through, for example, Development Education, Content and Language Integrated Learning (CLIL), Science Technology and Engineering, Arts and Maths. (STEAM). Through individual and group based work it will advance their pedagogical content knowledge and their knowledge of the range of methodologies applicable to their subject area(s), developing their understandings of what constitutes and contributes to high quality teaching, learning and assessment. 

The module focuses on:  
• How to plan lessons and units of work that are appropriate to the needs and interests of learners and curricular demands, 
• How to integrate innovative methodologies and a range of resources for differentiated learning, progression, formative and summative assessment
• How to integrate theories of active learning and teaching into practice
• How to integrate theories and tenets of cross-curricular approaches into teaching, learning and assessment practice
• How to access, adapt, develop, create and use resources and supports for their subject area to scaffold and challenge learners
• How to participate in communities of practice to develop, critically analyse and integrate new pedagogical insights into practice
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a deep understanding of current national curricula and their subject area specifications, syllabi and assessments contextualised in an awareness of curriculum requirements in preceding and subsequent stages of learning
Exemplify a critical knowledge of the theories and tenets underlying cross curricular approaches, including knowledge of the associated current curricular and assessment policy context
Apply their knowledge of the subject matter, pedagogical content and related methodologies of the relevant curricula/specifications and guidelines to planning for teaching, learning and assessment
Integrate relevant principles and theories of education, in the context of the relevant curriculum/syllabus, using well-developed skills of enquiry, to inform their professional practice
Access, develop, adapt and use a variety of curriculum resources and materials for teaching, learning and assessment in their subject area(s) to support and challenge all pupils

Set clear, challenging and achievable expectations for all pupils in line with the curriculum and use a range of strategies to support, monitor and assess pupils’ needs, their approach to learning and their progress

Conduct and apply relevant research as appropriate to their teaching context, identifying, critically analysing and integrating new knowledge regarding curriculum, pedagogy and assessment into their practice

modules.moduleName: RESEARCHING THE EDUCATION GUIDANCE COUNSELLING CONTEXT
modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to provide the students with the skills to critically engage in research about issues of relevance to guidance counselling in an educational setting. Students are required to design a group-based research project that will demonstrate their knowledge of research methodology in an area of education guidance policy and practice.

The module will:

· provide students with the conceptual knowledge and capacities to understand, design and conduct research in their professional field.

· Enable students to critically assess, communicate and engage in research activities in their professional field
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate familiarity with key concepts and theories used in social science research.
Demonstrate a capacity to critically assess existing research reports, policy and theoretical literature in the field.
Appreciate basic principles and processes involved in conducting research in an educational context.
Appreciate the ethical considerations which must be adhered to when conducting research.
Apply some of these techniques and principles in the design of their own research projects.

modules.moduleContent:
The Aims of this module are as follows:
· To introduce students to the study of dynamics of systems.
· To introduce students to the concept of feedback control. 
· To analyse a number of simple systems, including circuits, mechanical and flow systems. 
· To introduce students to relevant simulation software in the area of modelling and control. 
· To illustrate the concept of system dynamics and control using a practical laboratory-based system.

Indicative Syllabus:
· Introduction to open-loop, closed-loop and feedback control systems.
· Mathematical modelling of simple dynamic and static systems (circuit, mechanical and flow systems)
· Basic Laplace Transforms
· Transfer function models
· Block diagram analysis
· System analysis and simulation of first and second order systems
· Stability
· Transient and steady-state characteristics of first and second order systems
· Introduction to controller design – On/off, proportional and PID controllers

For further information on the module please use the following address:https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: Develop mathematical models for a range of simple dynamical systems.
Represent a mathematical model in transfer function form (using Laplace Transforms).
Calculate responses of simple dynamical systems.
Determine the stability of dynamical systems.
Design proportional and PID controllers for simple systems.
Use suitable simulation software to simulate and analyse dynamical systems.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

learningOutcomes: Explain the role of electronics in modern products and society.
Detail the relationship of current, voltage and power.
Undertake simple circuit analysis, up to and including Kirchoff’s current law, multi-loop nodal and mesh analysis.
Design and construct simple electronic circuits.
Describe household electricity systems.
Demonstrate knowledge of electrical safety.

modules.moduleContent: In this module students are required to undertake an embedded electronic design project, to program an embedded microprocessor to control simple electronic hardware.

The aims of this module are as follows:
* To provide an understanding of basic transistor electronics.
* To be able to analyse and design basic FET transistor circuits.
* To be able to construct a range of Op-amp circuits

A more detailed module descriptor can be found at the following address: https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: Explain the basic operating principles of a FET transistor (JFET and MOSFET)
Construct small signal equivalent circuits for transistor circuits
Design a small signal low frequency amplifier using either FETs or BJTs.
Analyze and synthesize DC bias circuits for FET transistors
Construct and measure basic transistor circuits in a laboratory.
Design and build a selection of basic operational amplifier circuits (integrator, adder, amplifier, etc.)
Design a multi-stage filter using op-amps.

modules.moduleContent:
· To introduce students to the world of digital design. 
· To equip students with the necessary skills to tackle real-world problems in the design of complex digital systems.

· Binary numbers & Binary representation
· Boolean algebra
· Minimisation using Karnaugh maps
· Sequential Logic – SR, D, JK, T and Master-slave flipflops
· Finite State Machines – Mealy and Moore machines
· Implementation using NAND and NOR functions
· Memory (ROM and RAM) and storage devices.
· Analogue to Digital and Digital to Analogue converters.
· Multiplexers – Combinational logic design using Multiplexers/Decoders as Universal Logic Module
learningOutcomes: Perform basic Boolean algebra.
Minimise logic using Karnaugh Maps.
Implement a logic circuit using only NAND / NOR gates.
Describe the operation of basic flip-flops.
Design combinational logic circuits using multiplexers as universal logic modules.
Analyse and design Finite State Machines.
Explain the basic operation of Analogue to Digital and Digital to Analogue converters.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
· To introduce students to the world of digital design with the skills to tackle real-world analysis and design problems
 ·To equip students with the basic analogue interfacing skills needed to support analogue sensor integration in digital systems.

Digital
· Binary numbers & Binary representation
· Boolean algebra
· Minimisation using Karnaugh maps
· Sequential Logic – SR, D, JK, T flipflops
· Finite State Machines – Mealy and Moore machines

Analogue
· Basic analogue interfacing considerations (e.g. impedance matching, electrical limits, etc.)
· RC filter circuits
· Brief introduction to op-amps, DC and small signal behavior
· Basic op-amp circuits, including amplifiers, adders, integrators and differentiators.
· More advanced op-amp circuits – including multi-stage filters
· Bode plots as used for op-amp circuits.
learningOutcomes: Perform basic Boolean algebra.
Minimise logic using Karnaugh Maps.
Describe the operation of basic flip-flops.
Analyse and design Finite State Machines.
Design and build a selection of basic operational amplifier circuits (integrator, adder, amplifier, etc.).
Design a multi-stage filter using op-amps.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
In this module students build upon the skills and experiences accumateled in EL217. Students will apply their new learning in a makerspace-based group design project which has been inspired by problems sourced from a community context.  Students will be exposed to a design-thinking philosophy and a problem-based learning environment where they can iteratively conceive, design, implement and test solutions.  Students will be encouraged to work in multidisciplinary teams and practise cross-disciplinary communication, collaboration and project interaction. 
Learning Outcomes.  Students after completing this module should be able to:
• Develop an interactive prototype/model
• Appreciate issues from a community/civic/public perspective 
• Develop a sense of commitment to community and of their role as civic professionals 
• Reflect on their experience of the engagement experience 
• Share their learning from the process with others in a context-appropriate manner. 
• Work effectively as part of a team to complete a creative project 
• Contribute their discipline-specific expertise to a project 
• Communicate effectively with team members and partners 
• Develop the skills of collaborative and independent learning
learningOutcomes: Develop an interactive prototype/model
Respond to feedback from the partners and/or users
Appreciate issues from a community/civic/public perspective
Develop a sense of commitment to community and of their role as civic professionals
Reflect on their experience of the engagement experience
Share their learning from the process with others in a context-appropriate manner.

modules.moduleContent:
· To facilitate experiential project based learning in the field of embedded and intelligent devices.
· To instill the creative spirit in students. 
· To develop oral and written communication skills.
· To develop students experience of working in a group.
· To engender an awareness of ethical issues in engineering.

A more detailed module descriptor can be obtained at the following web address: https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: To facilitate experiential project based learning in the field of embedded and intelligent devices.
To instill the creative spirit in students.
To develop oral and written communication skills.
To develop students experience of working in a group.
To engender an awareness of ethical issues in engineering.

modules.moduleContent:
Aims:
To cover the principles of modulation and transmission, covering the range of basic analog and digital modulation techniques.
Learning Outcomes:
On completing this module students will be able to:
1. Derive the basic equations for a range of modulation schemes.
2. Describe the time-domain and frequency domain characteristics of a range of modulation schemes.
3. Sketch and explain the operation of one or more basic circuits for performing modulation/demodulation.
4. Quantify and explain the effect of noise on communication systems.
5. Calculate the channel capacity of a dispersive channel using the water-filling theorem
6. Describe different channel access methods used in wireless communications (TDMA, FDMA).
7. Investigate and explain a communication system not described in class.

Full Module Descriptor: https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: 1. Analyse magnetic circuits.
2. Describe and explain the operation of simple motors and generators
3. Analyze simple problems relevant to DC machines.
4. Analyze simple problems relevant to transformers.
5. Analyze simple propagation problems related to general EM fields.
6. Apply the EM principles to practically solve various problems in a lab setting.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
To introduce students to the theory and practice of software engineering and software design. Software engineering is the discipline concerned with effectively and efficiently building software systems that satisfy the requirements of users and customers. Software design is the activity which translates a set of requirements into a coherent software model to be implemented.

· Software processes and agile software development
· Software requirements and specifications
· Software design
· Software implementation and test
· Software tools and development environments


See full module descriptor https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: Apply a software engineering process to address project management issues.
Consider the ethical implications of software engineering practice and software quality.
Analyse problem descriptions to extract requirements.
Design software solutions to constrained problems.
Communicate and interpret design elements using the Unified Modelling Language.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
• Introduction to Control and Review of Key Results from System Dynamics
• Properties of Feedback Systems – Sensitivity and Disturbance Rejection
• Stability of Feedback Systems
o    Routh criterion
o    Nyquist, Bode criteria
• Transient Response Specifications and Root Locus
• PID Control
o    Design concepts, tuning rules, advantages / disadvantages
• State-space control design
o    Pole placement and Ackermann’s formula
o    Use of nonzero reference input
o    Optimal control
o    State estimation
learningOutcomes: Recognise the utility of  feedback control
Relate the position of a system’s closed-loop poles to its step response
Design output feedback controllers using PID and lead-lag design
Design state-feedback controllers using pole placement and optimal control

modules.moduleContent:
• Robot perception using non-visual sensors including sensor modelling and signal processing
• General introduction to robot modelling, sensing, planning, and control
• Practical implications and effects of various actuators, electro-mechanical drives, and sensors
• Motion control algorithms (e.g. positional control, torque control, force control, impedance control, and gravity compensation)
• Motion and trajectory planning
• Case studies (e.g. robot manipulator arm, humanoid robot locomotion)
• Machine learning techniques for motion control
• High level software control of robot motion
learningOutcomes: Recognise, differentiate, and describe the general properties of different sensors
Apply appropriate signal processing and probabilistic modelling techniques to sensor inputs
Select the appropriate control approach based on robot hardware and desired operational features
Model and develop solutions to certain robot control problems
Implement the solution to motion control problems using appropriate software frameworks.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The Aims of this module are as follows:
To give students the experience of a large scale design problem, with all the associated administrative, communication and managerial aspects.

Project Description:
Each student will be allocated a unique individual project.  The project will be composed of a combination of some of the following technical areas:
*Analogue Electronics and signal processing.
*Digital Signal Processing.
*Control Systems.
*Embedded Software Design.

Students will be asked to identify preferred projects from a list of project proposals.  Where there is competition for the same project, allocation will be decided based on the 3rd year Engineering subject grade ranking,
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Contact Department for details.

See full module descriptor at https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors

Students complete 5 credits worth of work in semester 1 and a further 15 credits worth of work in semester 2.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate appropriate management techniques in the execution of their project (including time management and project planning)
Combine technical expertise from multiple technical areas to design and specify solutions for their projects.
Apply theoretical knowledge in solving problems they encounter.
Develop and improve personal skills to undertake new challenges posed by their project.
Demonstrate competent technical communication skills (including meeting, presentation and reporting)
Discuss any ethical issues, environmental impacts, health and safety issues associated with their project.

modules.moduleContent:
AIMS:

To introduce the cognitive architectures and techniques that can be used to implement autonomous robot behaviour and decision making that is responsive to dynamic sensory input. The implementation of robot behaviours using practical architectures (e.g. as part of the ROS ecosystem) is also addressed.
learningOutcomes: Describe the key elements of a robot cognitive architecture
Analyse a task and design the implementation of that task using finite state machines, behaviour trees, or similar. Reason about such a design.
Analyse a problem and design the task plan and goal using the Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL) or similar. Reason about a task plan.
Conduct research into state-of-the-art developments in robot behaviour and cognitive robotics and summarise findings.

modules.moduleContent:
Professional production of research paper and conference presentation (oral
and poster); Revision of research paper in light of referees’ comments; Attendance at a research
conference; Presentation of work at conference
learningOutcomes:
develop written and oral communication skills specific to thesis research
area and to give students an experience of the peer review process and public presentation of
their research at a conference.

modules.moduleContent: Advanced topics related to the specific research area of the PhD
learningOutcomes:
develop knowledge in advanced topics related to the specific research
area of the PhD.

modules.moduleContent:
The module is focused on the experiential learning of contributing to a fully integrated robot system, using modern team-based synchronous and asynchronous working methods, targeted at competing in international robotics competitions. Unlike many research projects which focus on just one aspect of a robot system in isolation, in this module students experience working with a system that must work as an integrated whole, and the challenges that entails. The module also provides the opportunity to develop leadership and organisational skills both within the team and in the robot competition organisation itself. Participants will attend regular 2hr lab sessions throughout the two semesters comprising a course of structured training sessions followed by development (design, implementation, and test/evaluation) sessions. Team members may be invited to travel to international competition based on their contributions, availability, and available funding.

This module can only be undertaken with the supervisor’s consent. Available places may be limited.
learningOutcomes: Describe their experience of the interdependent nature of integrated robot systems, the simulation-to-real gap, the possibilities for unexpected emergent behaviour in real-world and competition settings, and the possibility for small faults to manifest as large failures.
Collaborate in a large team project with diverse members having a range of skills and develop leadership skills as appropriate within the team.

Contribute to and integrate with the work of other team-members, using appropriate tools for synchronous and asynchronous communications, code development (e.g. git/GitHub) and sharing, testing, issue reporting, and collaborative task assignment.
Demonstrate the capacity to learn relevant systems, paradigms, and idioms both through structured training and independent learning, including code-reading where needed.
Analyze problems and develop (design, implement, evaluate) solutions within the scope of the system.

modules.moduleContent: This module will introduce students to a variety of prose and fictional texts, revealing the power of writing to surprise, engage, move, anger and persuade the reader. The course will pay particular attention to texts that engage with important historical, social, moral, and political questions, giving specific attention to the manner in which good writing utilises different literary and rhetorical strategies to further its ideas and achieve particular effects. The module requires students to engage actively and critically with a wide range of texts through reading, discussion and writing. The module is designed to both enhance students’ experience of reading, and to provide them with the knowledge, experience and writing skills they require to develop and express well-informed opinions about prose and fictional texts.  These subject specific skills will include how to read and critically engage with a text; how to make use of secondary criticism; how to develop strong theses and arguments and how to plan, organise and write an essay. Small group seminars, supporting the core lectures, will facilitate discussions and incorporate formal writing exercises to ensure that students acquire the necessary skills for studying English at University.

This is an optional module only open to students taking the Higher Diploma in English. 

This module offers students an introduction to key concepts in English Literature and culture. This course will focus on key concepts and ideas in the history of poetry and drama. The module will introduce students to methods of reading, analysing, and contextualising poetry and drama and will enable students to become familiar with the textual and formal elements that need to be considered when approaching poetic and dramatic texts. The module will enhance the student’s understanding of poetry and drama and provide students with the knowledge, experience and writing skills to develop and express well-informed opinions about what they are reading.

This module examines how literary forms acquire their meaning, how they are structured and used in literature and how they change over time. It introduces students to the analysis and understanding of a selection of literary forms within the broad genres of poetry, fiction and drama by concentrating on the techniques deployed in specific literary forms, their development, their ideological accumulations of meaning, and the historical moments at which genres have changed and altered.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of literary form;
Demonstrate an ability to understand the relationship between form and content in a number of exemplary texts;
Show an ability to read texts critically with close attention to detail;
Develop and write a research essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings;
Write responses to exam questions, demonstrating the ability to synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions;
Show capacity to write clear, grammatically correct prose;
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to the range and variety of Irish literature in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. The module will facilitate students to become careful readers of different forms (poetry, drama, fiction) and aesthetic modes (realism; modernism; Revivalism; the Gothic). Through studying on this course students will explore how generations of Irish writers sought in their work to represent and critically evaluate the condition of Irish life, while also imagining and debating what ‘Ireland’ could be. Furthermore, the module will facilitate students to situate the specificity of Irish literature within global literary and cultural history, as well as understanding the contribution of Irish writers to world literature.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a detailed understanding of the literature, politics and culture in nineteenth and twentieth century Irish writing;
Demonstrate an ability to understand Irish literature in an historical context;
Show an ability to identify and analyse recurring themes and tensions in Irish writing and within the relevant critical debates;
Participate in tutorial discussions; debate issues related to specific literary texts;
Develop and write a research essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings;
Write responses to exam questions, demonstrating the ability to synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions;
Show capacity to write clear, grammatically correct prose;

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to the major characteristics and themes of Romantic literature and culture.  The module looks at these elements as they manifest in poetic, fictional and intellectual writings of the era.  The module also discusses the relationship between various literary forms and the wider context of political and social development and intellectual change of the period.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a thorough knowledge of major literary and cultural innovations associated with Romanticism;
Develop an understanding of the historical and cultural contexts from which Romanticism emerges;

Demonstrate an ability to read and understand a select number of exemplary texts in close detail; an ability to situate these texts in relation to each other, the ideas of literary periods and a range of contexts, historical and theoretical;
Participate in tutorial discussions; debate issues related to specific literary texts;

Develop and write a research essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions; to construct a coherent, substantiated argument;
Show capacity to write clear, grammatically correct prose;
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.

modules.moduleContent: “Colonialism” describes how Europeans annexed, planted and secured territories in other parts of the world, initially, for capital and commercial gain but, later, for the purpose of consolidating and expanding their power and spheres of influence. For the people of colonised territories, it involved not only the physical traumas of military violence and subjugation, dispossession and forced migration but also the loss of language and culture, subjection to falsified images and narratives, alienation from history and a loss of familiarity with “self”. In this module, we will consider the work of selected writers and film-makers who have recognised that challenging colonialism, and its contemporary neo-colonial and global reiterations, requires not only instigating and achieving politico-economic transitions – entailing, for instance, the signing of declarations, the raising and lowering of flags, and the establishment of new governments, states and systems of socioeconomic regulation – but also a rigorous and ongoing contestation of its language, narratives and aesthetic orders. These are artists, poised between worlds, who recollect, reaffirm and celebrate (sometimes ambivalently) the agency and cultures of their peoples but also ones who have used their hybrid, and often contradictory, positions to creatively engage pressing issues of migrancy, movement, (dis)location, transcultural communication and change in the contexts of globalization and global social justice movements. During the module, we will analyse the works closely, attending primarily to the thematics and material histories of “self”; issues of form and formal innovation; and questions about the social and political functions of literature and film, more generally. The writers and film-makers we will consider may include, for example, Tayeb Salih [Sudan], Chimamanda Adichie [Nigeria], Binyavanga Wainaina [Kenya] or Jonas Carpignano [Italy].

In this module students will be guided through a research topic by the seminar leader. All students will research the same author, text or defined topic and will develop a research essay in consultation with their tutor, reporting back to the group on their work. The module will conclude with a mini-symposium and may include the development of a website highlighting the group’s work.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a thorough knowledge of variety of literary texts
Demonstrate an strong ability to assimilate and scrutinise critical readings
Show an ability to appraise, identify and use varied contemporary critical and theoretical approaches to literature and culture
Participate in seminar discussions; debate issues related to cultural context and socio-political circumstances;
Demonstrate high quality verbal and written expression; the ability to make clear points in a lucid grammatical and confident manner.
Write an essay on a chosen topic, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify complex critical approaches and opinions.
