modules.moduleName: STEAM (SCIENCE, TECHNOLOGY, ENGINEERING, ARTS AND MATHEMATICS) IN EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION AND CARE
modules.moduleContent: Using a holistic approach to learning and building on the innate curiosity and wonder of babies, toddlers and young children, this module explores Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework and its application to the development of STEAM concepts in an emergent curriculum in the early years. Theories of emergent curriculum and STEAM enquiry appropriate to the early years will be examined. Students will investigate integrated emergent curriculum development in an enquiry rich environment both indoor and outdoor.
learningOutcomes: Identify key stages in and influences on early STEAM development and critically reflect on relevant theoretical perspectives.
Develop and apply a philosophy for using play to support STEAM learning with children (birth to six years).
Apply Aistear The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework to early year’s STEAM enquiry through integrated emergent curriculum development.
Utilise pedagogical knowledge in supporting and providing for a rich indoor and outdoor enquiry-based learning environment for young children to support STEAM enquiry.

Review, select and use appropriate resources and materials for developing integrated science, technology, engineering, arts and mathematics enquiry with babies, toddlers and young children in an ECEC setting.
Evaluate the role that language plays in the development of STEAM in the early years.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Student teachers will explore the dynamic processes by which curriculum is designed and facilitated for all pupils, with a particular focus on the junior primary classes. The recognition of the central role of play, as outlined in Aistear (NCCA, 2009), and pupils as active agents in their own learning will guide experiential learning activities across Visual Arts Education, Drama Education, Music Education, PE, SESE, and SPHE. The organisation and attributes of a Froebelian learning environment with concrete materials will be introduced so the student teachers become aware of the interdependence of teachers’ learning and pupil learning and the importance of teacher-pupil relationships in the teaching/learning process through guided discovery and inquiry-based learning. A range of methodologies to achieve planned outcomes will be experienced including opportunities to experiment with and explore new and emerging technologies for teaching and learning.
learningOutcomes: Connect curriculum to children’s lives in meaningful ways to promote integration in the primary classroom and enact the principles of curriculum
Plan, organise and manage a variety of learning environments to support learning through applying Froebelian, play, inquiry-based and child-centred principles
Implement a range of methodologies to achieve planned outcomes in Visual Arts, Drama, Music, PE, SESE and SPHE
Reflect upon and assess the achievement of curriculum objectives and adapt their teaching accordingly

Employ pedagogical expertise, including subject-specific pedagogical content knowledge and new and emerging technologies for teaching and learning in Visual Arts, Drama, Music, PE, SESE and SPHE at junior primary class level

modules.moduleContent: This module encourages student teachers to engage with the concept of inclusion at a systemic, organisational level, to explore what it means to be an inclusive teacher, within an inclusive classroom, within an inclusive school. Inclusion in this context is taken to refer to children who are potentially vulnerable to reduced participation in education such as pupils from linguistic, cultural and ethnic minorities, pupils from disadvantaged communities, but with a particular focus on pupils with disabilities or neurodiverse pupils. This module addresses responsive teaching in the form of Universal Design for Learning Approaches for all learners in a diverse classroom. Student teachers critically analyse effective ways of communicating and collaborating with all parents for the benefit of the each child.
learningOutcomes: Understand the impact of equality and diversity issues among students within the inclusive classroom.
Support the assessment, teaching and learning of all children, regardless of background, identity, experiences and needs.
Cater for all learning abilities through multiple means of engagement, representation, action and expression.
Analyse theoretical and pedagogical principles and strategies of assessment in providing effective and inclusive teaching and learning for children with diverse needs and abilities.
Support the holistic development of all children by developing effective collaborative and communicative relationships with parents and other professionals.

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL AND PERSONAL SKILLS FOR TEACHING AND LEARNING 2
modules.moduleContent:
This module seeks to support the development of the teacher as a self-reflective,
autonomous professional who demonstrates the four values outlined in the Code
of Professional Conduct for Teachers, and the teacher as a lifelong learner in relation to the continuum of teacher education. 

Connections are forged between theory, practice and reflection through the introduction of the four ethical values of the Teaching Council, along with the theory of group dynamics, as part of the various professional relationships that are built during the course of School Placement.
The Code of Professional Conduct (Teaching Council, 2016) embeds the four ethical 
values of integrity, care, respect, and truth within teaching itself. This module considers their relationship to the educational philosophies and practices of Friedrich Froebel. Student teachers will engage critically with literature pertaining to relevant concepts and theories drawing on national and international literature.

The course focuses on advanced communicative skills through a range of lenses and 
the use of seminar-style discussions. A strong focus is placed on professional relationships with particular reference to partnerships. Partnerships within EDF234 are located within the school as a learning community and encompass all working relationships. For example, student teachers collaborate with a peer on paired placement and are supported to communicate effectively with their host teacher/ Treoraí and HEI/School Placement Tutor. 

From a personal competency building perspective, student teachers are afforded opportunities to enhance their own literacy and numeracy in the following areas: Maths, written and oral Irish, Music Education, Literacy, ICT, and Visual Arts Education. They will then choose and study one of those areas, which they have not previously studied. Students will contribute to their e-portfolio Taisce as part of an on-going professional development process.
learningOutcomes: Consult and analyse a wide range of literature/resources, which will ensure that teaching itself is understood and practised as a form of self-critical learning by student teachers, with ample opportunities for individual and collaborative reflection.
Engage critically with the ethical values for teaching as presented by the Teaching Council (2016).
Draw explicit links between theory and practice in reflecting upon identities of student and student-teacher.

Advance the communicative skills of student teachers in collaborating and communicating with school partners and through the provision of opportunities to develop self-reflection, creative and reflective practice.
Experiment with and explore new and emerging technologies for teaching and learning through ongoing Taisce e-portfolio contributions.
Student-teachers will enhance their own literacy skills through research and referencing for the purposes of academic writing.

modules.moduleName: CUMAS NA GAEILGE / ENGLISH AND MATHEMATICAL COMPETENCY 2
modules.moduleContent:
This module will further develop the student teacher’s competency in Irish, English and Mathematics necessary to confidently lead the teaching across all ages in the primary classroom.

Oral and written Irish-Language assessment will be informed by the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (2020). Students’ exit level competency in each subject area in Year 1 will be used in supporting students to identify and meet their own learning targets, appropriate to Year 2. In English and Mathematics, students will further develop competency and confidence around the use of relevant concepts, skills and terminology. In Irish and English, students will further develop their communication and presentation skills across different language registers and genres. Students will further engage in reflective practice in competency utilising their developing digital capacity.  This development in professional growth in competency will better enable student teachers to facilitate quality teaching and learning for all pupils. This module will complement EDF 231, 232 and 233, and methodologies of teaching in these subject areas.
learningOutcomes: Further reflect on their own language use and numeracy and define appropriate personal learning targets and actions suitable for a second-year student.
Demonstrate and develop further competency, accuracy, confidence and fluency in spoken and written Irish and English, appropriate to a second-year student.
Demonstrate and develop further competency in the Mathematical concepts, skills and terminology necessary for teaching across all ages in the primary school.
Use and develop a more advanced range of language registers, grammar and vocabulary in Irish and English.
Continue to develop in their understanding and capacity to confidently lead the learning in these subjects in the classroom in their professional role.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
To cultivate a democratic responsibility to the inclusion of children who are neurodiverse. 

This module encourages student teachers to critically analyse theoretical and pedagogical principles and strategies of teaching, learning and assessment in providing effective, enriching and inclusive assessment, teaching and learning for children with diverse needs and abilities. This module supports student teachers in assessing, profiling and planning, using strengths based approaches, to meet the needs of neurodiverse pupils. To further underline the integrative nature of this module, student teachers spend one week on placement in a Special Education School in Year 3 and an additional 4 weeks on placement in a Special Education Team/Department of a mainstream school developing their professional identity as a teacher of all learners.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the unique role of the Special Education teacher as professional in providing for the holistic development of pupils.
Show an understanding of the Continuum of Support as well as the theory, concepts and methods pertaining to effective teaching, learning and assessment.
Use a range of strategies and methodologies including new and emerging technologies for teaching and learning to support, monitor and assess pupils’ approach to learning and their progress.
Conduct a systematic, holistic assessment of pupil strengths, abilities, talents and needs.
Collaborate with Special Needs Assistants in the provision of support to pupils.

Apply knowledge of the individual potential of pupils, dispositions towards learning, varying backgrounds, identities, experiences and learning styles to planning for teaching, learning and assessment.
Develop effective communicative and collaborative relationships with parents of children who have additional needs and other relevant professionals.

modules.moduleName: LANGUAGE, MATHEMATICS AND SCIENTIFIC ENQUIRY ACROSS THE CURRICULUM
modules.moduleContent:
Using a spiral approach and continuing to develop skills, values and knowledge in the cognate areas, this module prepares student teachers to adopt an integrated approach to quality teaching and learning for all pupils across the curriculum. 

This bilingual module further develops students’ understanding and implementation of relevant theories for the effective linkage, integration and differentiation of teaching and learning introduced in Year 1 and 2. Students’ competence in adopting an integrated approach to teaching by using literacy, numeracy and scientific education across all curriculum areas will be enhanced. The use of Content and Language Integrated learning (CLIL) in the teaching of Gaeilge will be explored and the implications for practice reflected upon. Inclusive practices for supporting children learning English as an Additional Language will be reviewed with emphasis on home-language maintenance and the transferability of methodologies and approaches within the Irish-language curriculum for quality teaching and learning for all pupils. 

Current curricular documents including Aistear: The Early Childhood Curriculum Framework (NCCA, 2009) will be explicated, discussed and explored in active-learning experiences. Through this, student teachers gain an insight into the concepts, skills and practical methodologies (station teaching, guided discovery, fieldwork, hands-on learning, problem solving, reflective practice, outdoor learning, digital learning, and talk and discussion) required for inclusive high-quality teaching and learning for all pupils underpinned by Froebelian philosophy. Through offering space for professional dialogue, student teachers will continue to explore their professional identity and agency as a teacher in primary schools.

Student teachers will be enabled to adopt a child-centred, Froebelian approach to curriculum development across languages, mathematics and science education. Differentiation, assessment for and assessment of learning are key components of this module. Methods of children recording their work, documentation and assessment of children’s work including self and peer evaluation will be evaluated to create and inclusive learning environment for all pupils.

Student teachers further develop their understanding of professional relationships and importance of collaboration with parents and families in the development of language, literacy, mathematics, and science education.

Through collaboration with external agencies and other departments, student teachers deepen their understanding of the quality teaching and learning of STEM and MSE. 

Emphasis is placed on education for sustainable development and greater sustainable practices. There is demonstratable integration of environmentally sustainable practice in teaching and learning rooted in the principle of care for others and the planet throughout the module.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate proficient knowledge of theories underpinning curriculum integration and of the relevant curriculum and policy documents to implement and review cross-curricular planning for quality inclusive teaching and learning.
Review, select and use appropriate methodologies, resources and materials for the integration of the cognate areas across the curriculum (including use of outdoor learning and digital skills).
Document and assess children’s cross-curricular work including self and peer evaluation and utilise the information gathered for inclusive, differentiation purposes.
Develop strategies to communicate, collaborate and co-operate with parents/families to facilitate teaching, learning and assessment

Demonstrate a knowledge of relevant theories, terminology and concepts around language learning and teaching and evaluate the applicability of these systems for informing their own practice (including CLIL).
Develop a creative emergent curriculum to Integrate languages, literature, mathematics and science concepts and skills across the curriculum informed by the Froebelian philosophy.

Reflect on how the skills, concepts and attitudes involved in language, literature, mathematics and science can support children’s learning and their active and creative experiences across all curriculum areas.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO COACHING GAELIC GAMES IN THE PRIMARY SCHOOL
modules.moduleContent:
The Introduction To Coaching Gaelic Games is the new entry level course that can be run for coaches from across the Gaelic Games Family;- GAA, Ladies Gaelic Football and Camogie. Once you complete this course you will be eligible to coach in all the above codes. 

The course is the first Coaching Course on the GAA Coach Education Pathway. No prior coaching experience is required.
There will be workshops on Child Safeguarding, Cumann na mBunscol, GAA and the Primary Curriculum, Rounders, Special Education Needs and an inclusive school.
To enable students to qualify as beginner coaches for Gaelic Games.

The module will also explore the specific context at primary school level, connections to physical education, extra-curricular activities and developing partnerships with local GAA clubs.

Our objective will be to encourage students to transfer their love and passion for the GAA and connect it to the Primary curriculum, the classroom and school life.
learningOutcomes: Assist a school in organising a programme of Games and skill development activities appropriate to each participants needs and abilities
Identify the Skills of Hurling, Camogie and Ladies & Gaelic Football
Organise a course of progressive activities (drills and fun games) to develop 5 key skills
Enhance the children’s learning experience through a love of GAA and enabling children to access the Primary curriculum.
Contribute to organising and managing players for an effective school practice session
Prepare progressive activities to develop movement skills appropriate to Gaelic games and other physical activity opportunities.
Demonstrate the How to Coach Skills – Build Rapport, Observe, Analyse, Explain, Provide Demonstration and Provide Positive Feedback to Players

modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed to provide student teachers with a solid foundation of knowledge, skills, and professional values critical to the success of school placement and the development of their professional identities as they progress to their first year as newly qualified teachers.

This module aims to develop a commitment towards a rights-based and affirmative model of disability and to understand the convergence between inclusive school cultures and nurturing relationships, as teachers develop their professional identities.

This module explores the topic Teacher as Professional through a wide ranging series of presentations from partners in education.

This module has three main elements:
Element 1: Placement Learning to prepare students for an extended 10-week placement
Element 2: Inclusive Education with a specific focus on SEN placement
Element 3: Teacher as Professional – Aspects of the Role

Element 1: Placement Learning
School placement is a central feature of the B Ed initial teacher education programme. 
This module is designed to prepare and equip student teachers for an extended 10-week placement spanning Semester 1 and Semester 2 with a focus on a fully integrated, Froebelian approach to teaching and learning. School Placement in Year 4 consists of the following elements: 
1: Teaching: Special Educational Needs (4 weeks) 
2: Teaching: Mainstream (4 weeks) 
3: School Experience (2 weeks). 

Element 2: Inclusive Education with a specific focus on SEN placement
  
A key focus of the module is the creation of an inclusive Froebelian environment. The module examines the role of nurturing relationship including the role of parents as partners in the education system.    Issues which practicing teachers often find personally challenging during their careers will be interrogated, along with the role of external agencies in school communities. With regard to Inclusive Education, key concepts underpinning educational provision for neurodiverse pupils are examined.  


Element 3: Teacher as Professional – Aspects of the Role

The concept of Teacher as Professional will be explored in depth through a series of guest lectures in Semester 2 from a variety of education partners such as INTO, DES, Management Bodies, IPPN, NPC, Teaching Council and NIPT. Professional relationships will be explored, including how best to work with parents for the benefit of children. The roles of stakeholders and partners, and the importance of engagement and cooperation with them, will be explored. Some aspects of a teacher’s work from a legal perspective will be explored. A principal of a local school will present on the legal aspects of teaching.
learningOutcomes: Develop their own identity as a professional teacher in the classroom.
Develop a critical understanding of the concept of professionalism and explore relationships between teachers, school partners and stakeholders.
Identify and meet the needs of pupils with additional educational needs.
Provide for the holistic development of the pupil, particularly through differentiated approaches and appropriate assessment for learning and differentiation strategies.
Plan for coherent, Froebelian teaching informed by ongoing reflection on professional practice.
Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the legal and professional aspects of a teacher’s position and responsibilities in relation to his/her pupils.
Organise and maintain their Taisce portfolio of learning to identify areas for further learning in the Droichead induction phase of the continuum.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides opportunities to integrate various digital tools and resources across the curriculum. Students will explore how digital technologies can be meaningfully embedded into teaching, learning and assessment in line with contemporary policy, learning theories, and best practice. Topics such as digital creativity, digital citizenship, online safety, assessment, and UDL are featured.
learningOutcomes: Employ relevant technical knowledge and skills of a range of digital technologies (including multi-media resources), effectively to facilitate teaching and assessment practices and to aid pupil learning.
Demonstrate personal digital-creativity skills in a practical cross-curricular Froebelian enquiry-based environment.
Demonstrate best practice on cyber-ethics including issues of privacy, information security, copyright and on-line safety and understand the complex interplay between technology and society.
Identify and utilise a range of assistive technologies to support the learning needs of all pupils.
Engage in Taisce e-portfolio-based learning and identify area(s) for further learning in the Droichead induction phase of the continuum.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Cumas na Gaeilge will develop the student’s necessary written and oral competence in the Irish language to teach the Irish language effectively  in T1 and T2 primary school settings. 

Oral and written Irish-language assessments will be informed by the Common European Framework of Reference for languages. The student’s current-level competence in these areas will be referenced from the previous academic years and this will be used in supporting students to identify and meet their own learning targets. Students will develop their communication, digital skills, presentation skills, and  grammatical accuracy across different language registers and genres. Students are supported to develop the language needed to critically design, deliver and reflect upon the content, communication, cognition and cultural components in CLIL lessons, utilising the TFG Fillteán Foghlama housed in TAISCE as a guide. Outdoor learning and use of the local environment, underpinned by Froebelian philosophy, guide students’ grammatical detective work to aid independent  lifelong-learning amongst students.  Self and peer assessment skills are developed and digital tools such as discussion fora and digital journals,  are used to aid students in their reflective journey through the Irish language. 

Space is provided to encourage professional dialogue between students and HEI lecturers in order to strengthen and effectively understand students’ identity as Irish language learners and educators.
learningOutcomes: Reflect on their own language use and define appropriate personal learning targets and actions by utilising the appropriate digital skillset
Demonstrate and develop competency, accuracy, confidence and fluency in spoken and written  Irish, appropriate to the level of the course
Use and develop a range of language registers, genres and vocabulary in the Irish language
Demonstrate skills in self-directed and autonomous learning

Utilise the  TFG Fillteán Foghlama housed in TAISCE to further create and plan accurate language content for CLIL lessons engaging with the 4Cs (communication, collaboration, critical thinking and creative thinking)
Critically analyse and reflect upon their identity as Irish language educators in a T1/T2 setting

modules.moduleName: MODERN FOREIGN LANGUAGE LEARNING IN THE PRIMARY SCHOOL
modules.moduleContent:
The objective of this module is to inform student teachers of key research underpinning modern foreign language learning, while also engaging students through the presentation of and participation with, a broad variety of effective pedagogical methodologies, and finally, developing educational leadership skills in students, empowering them to potentially lead modern foreign language teaching and learning in their school.

In this elective, Froebel student teachers will be presented with progression-based learning in the area of Primary Modern Foreign Languages (MFL). Student teachers will reflect on their own language learning and the implications of this for practice.  Student teachers will develop pedagogical expertise through critically evaluating and analysing various theories and research behind how children learn languages. Through experiential learning, practical approaches and methodologies in MFL lessons with an emphasis on communicative competence, linguistic awareness and translanguaging, and intercultural competence will be highlighted. Student teachers will also engage with research on effective, inclusive and innovative approaches to teach the MFL, making the most of opportunities for cross-curricular integration in the classroom. The role of digital technologies to support teaching, learning and assessment in MFL will be critically evaluated. 
Additionally, through workshops and collaborative group work, student teachers will be enabled to potentially lead the teaching and learning of MFL in their future practice, contributing to creating and sustaining learning communities in schools and professional networks.

This module will be facilitated plurilingually.
learningOutcomes: Examine theoretical underpinnings behind teaching and learning in bilingual and multilingual school environments.
Critically evaluate current policy and curricular approaches to language teaching and learning including MFL through a Froebelian lens.

Develop research-informed pedagogical expertise in effective teaching of MFL in the primary classroom, including specific pedagogical content knowledge, digital-learning opportunities, methodologies and cross-curricular integration.

Articulate key considerations if leading the introduction of MFL in a primary school, including language choice, resources, planning, whole-school approach primary/secondary transition and their role as a leader of learning and teaching.

Critically reflect upon themselves as a language learner and teacher and their philosophy of language learning examining how this will support them in facilitating quality language teaching and learning for all pupils.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: IN THIS TOGETHER: HOW TO BUILD EFFECTIVE PARTNERSHIP WITH PARENTS AND GUARDIANS
modules.moduleContent:
The purpose of the module is to equip student teachers with theory, practical information and strategies in order to build their confidence to work effectively and empathetically with parents and guardians.

Student teachers gain experience in how to develop effective working relationships with children and school staff on school placement. They will also gain effective communication skills including the appropriate use of communication methods using digital technology. However, it is during their first year as a qualified teacher that they will have the first meaningful opportunity to work with parents and guardians. This module is designed to help student teachers with several aspects of parent/teacher relationships with an emphasis on Froebelian philosophy and pedagogies.

Student teachers will be given practical information and advice on how to prepare for events and tasks, including parent/teacher meetings, end-of-year reports, and regular unofficial communications. Student teachers will learn the appropriate skills to develop positive and meaningful relationships with parents and guardians. Student teachers will also learn the benefits of developing effective relationships to support parents/guardians in cases of bereavement, family separation and other such reasons. Students will gain a basic knowledge of current family law and how it pertains to them as teachers as well as matters relating to their role as mandated persons. 

The importance of emotional intelligence will also be explored, with reference to managing expectations of both the parent and the teacher. Student teachers will learn about barriers to parental involvement due to potential social inclusion issues, from educationally disadvantaged backgrounds and from new-comer families.  
Student teachers will learn how to work with parents to assist them in understanding the process involved in educational assessments. Student teachers will learn strategies for handling conflict with a parent/guardian, both within the class or from the wider school community and how to maintain their resilience during and after such encounters, based on case-study scenarios based on content covered. 

Students will be required to design a start-of-year presentation for parents highlighting importance of play within the context of the school curriculum and how parents can be a partner in this at home. The presentation will focus on making curriculum content accessible to parents.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of the parent or guardian as primary educator of the child with reference to Bunreacht na hÉireann and other relevant legislation.
Have increased confidence to initiate and develop respectful and effective relationships with parents and guardians.
Utilise appropriate styles and systems of communication to suit particular situations and context, including the appropriate use of digital technology for communication.
Approach and complete tasks such as parent-teacher meetings, end-of-year reports and the hosting of information seminars for parents, utilising available digital technology as required.

Understand and critically analyse factors that can enhance or impede effective parent/guardian relationships while identifying the key challenges facing some parents/guardians and how to support the child.
Have broad knowledge of the legal elements of the role of the school and parent/teacher relationships.
Be aware of the supports that exist for newly qualified teachers.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: NEW EYES TO SEE: THE USE OF FIELDWORK AS PART OF PRIMARY GEOGRAPHY TEACHING
modules.moduleContent:
The module highlights the importance of geographic fieldwork as part of primary geography teaching. Students will be encouraged to explore the literature, policy as well as best practice in the use of fieldwork as part of teacher practice. 
Topics covered will include the use of enquiry to frame fieldwork, the use of the local outdoor school environment, the cognitive, affective and social benefits of fieldwork for children, integration, as well as connecting teacher self-efficacy with fieldwork use. The use of Froebelian principles will also be applied as a lens through which the methodology will be viewed. Froebel viewed children as active, curious, creative learners who learn best through activity, play, talk, and self-reflection. The role of the teacher as a constructivist scaffolder and facilitator of child learning using fieldwork will be discussed to this end. 

The elective will also include exemplification and modelling of the fieldwork methodology in the local MU outdoor environment. 
Assessment will involve the students designing and presenting a fieldwork lesson for pupils at a class level of their choice, framed by a focus of enquiry.
learningOutcomes: Critically reflect on educational literature and policy that underpins and frames fieldwork use
Demonstrate an understanding of geographic pedagogical approaches, concepts and skills relating to the use and implementation of fieldwork.
Design, structure, and present effective fieldwork lessons, as part of a focus of geographic enquiry
Become familiar with geographic frameworks, through which fieldwork lessons can be designed and taught.
Develop and understanding of the embedding of child environment identity through repeated exposure to the outdoors as part of geographic fieldwork

Understand practical considerations associated with fieldwork use, including health and safety, parental consent, weather, appropriate clothing, curriculum planning and pupil behaviour to enhance student confidence in using fieldwork.

modules.moduleName: FROM CRISIS TO CALM: SUPPORTING CHILDREN'S BEHAVIOUR
modules.moduleContent:
This module focuses on communication and relationship-building skills Creating an inclusive learning classroom environment using a restorative approach. It works to establish classroom management and organisational skills, establishing routines and procedures as proactive strategies in managing challenging behaviours.
Critical evaluation of using rewards and consequences in the classroom is facilitated.
 
A whole-school approach from policy to practice is modelled founded on empathy and trauma-informed practice. Creating and maintaining a safe, interactive and challenging environment is prioritized by using strategies that promote and maintain positive behaviour, in accordance with school policy, and by working with parents to support behaviour for learning.

Utilising the Continuum of Support, a Behaviour Support Plan is created. Triggers and phases of escalation are identified and positive physical interventions and restrictive practices introduced.

This module covers the areas of assessment, planning and ongoing recording of progress. The course combines basic statistical knowledge with theory of assessment; provides students with an overview of the range of assessment tools available for use across the range of special education needs along with 'onward referral' ensures, through application, that students have the necessary skills to make assessment for learning and diagnosis an integral part of teaching pupils with special educational needs. Students are guided in the interpretation of data gathered through assessment and in how to integrate this information into the development of the Student Support plans (SSPs). Through class activity, students are taken through the steps involved in writing a SSP that meets the requirements of both the EPSEN Act (2004) and International best practice. Students have practice in writing targets. Students are exposed to a wide range of possible ways of monitoring progress; there is an emphasis on the seamless integration of instruction and intervention that is guided by outcomes. Students share their own practice as part of class activity and there is a close relationship between this Module and the planning and teaching requirements of the Professional Practice Module.  The contribution of classroom organisation and management to effective teaching and learning for these pupils is emphasised. Again, this is directly related to the Professional Practice Module.
learningOutcomes: understand the role / purpose of assessment in relation to instruction
have a working understanding of key statistical concepts and terminology
demonstrate an ability to select and evaluate assessments most suitable to meet the diagnostic needs of individual pupils
effectively build a programme of instruction based on the information gathered

write a SSP that is accurate and appropriate for a pupil incorporating all of the key components
- the pupil's strengths, interests, needs and priority learning needs
- the pupil's present levels of academic achievement and functional performance
- the pupil's special educational need
- targets that are measurable and achievable for this pupil
- specific methodologies and resources for each of the targets set
- the special and related services the pupil is receiving
demonstrate an ability to 'pull together' information from assessment into an individual education plan
incorporate record keeping and progress monitoring into ongoing planning for instruction

modules.moduleContent:
Module Overview: This module is designed to provide student teachers with a solid foundation of high-level beginning knowledge, skills, and professional values critical to the success of school placement.
It will also equip students in how cultivate a democratic responsibility to the inclusion of children who have special needs and to prepare students for inclusive teaching.
The Early Childhood Education element of this module is designed to give the students a deep and meaningful understanding of the frameworks and Curriculum that underpin both their teaching practice and their professional identity.

This module has three main elements:
Element 1: Placement Learning to prepare students for school placements
Element 2: Inclusive Education
Element 3: Early Childhood Education

Element 1 
School placement is a central feature of the B Ed initial teacher education programme.
With a clear focus on the early years of the primary school, this module is designed to prepare and equip student teachers for school placement in Semester 1 and Semester 2. 

In Semester 1 students will be placed in an infant setting to observe one day a week. They will also under a 3-week block placement in the same infant class. In Semester 2, students will be placed for three weeks in a 1st or 2nd class setting, one week in a Gaelscoil setting and undertake an Alternative Service Placement (ASP). 
In Semester 2, students will critically examine the DEIS Plan which sets out the Department of Education and Skill’s vision for the provision of better opportunities for those in communities at risk of disadvantage and social exclusion. A Home School Liaison Teacher from a partner school will present to the students based on their questions and gaps in their knowledge.


Element 2: Inclusive Education

This module critically examines bias with regards to disability and fosters positive attitudes to inclusion. It examines the landscape of Inclusion across the Irish context. It encourages students to critically analyse theoretical and pedagogical principles and strategies of teaching, learning and assessment in providing effective, enriching and inclusive assessment, teaching and learning for children with diverse needs and abilities. This module supports student teachers in assessing, profiling and planning, using strengths-based approaches, to meet the needs of neurodiverse pupils. 

Element 3: Early Childhood

The Early Childhood Education element of this module is designed to give the students a deep and meaningful understanding of the frameworks and Curriculum that underpin both their teaching practice and their professional identity.  Methods of data collection to build a holistic picture of a child’s development are explored to enable the students to analyse and reflect on each child’s learning dispositions, skills and knowledge.

IMPORTANT NOTE
all elements of EDF601 must be passed in order to progress to EDF620
learningOutcomes: Plan, teach, and evaluate lessons on school placement and implement a range of methodologies and Froebelian approaches to achieve planned learning outcomes.
Review, source, select, and use appropriate resources and materials for teaching and learning.
Demonstrate ability to establish classroom-management strategies that support suitable and effective learning for all pupils and that promote and maintain positive behaviour.
Identify and utilise planning practices with a specific emphasis on project methods, integration of subjects, and group teaching practices.

Assess the achievement of curriculum objectives and adapt their teaching accordingly, Individually, and in collaboration with colleagues, reflect on their attitudes, and beliefs about teaching and learning, and Alternative Service Placement, which inform and guide their professional practice.

Organise and maintain a digital School Placement folder for planning purposes.  Demonstrate the ability to use appropriate data-collection tools to identify dispositions, skills, and attitudes of children from Junior Infants to 2nd Class by undertaking a case study and conducting a variety of observations on one child.
Relate theories of early-childhood development to children from Infant to 2nd Class to help develop strategies to encourage each child to learn to their best ability.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
In this module student teachers will explore the dynamic processes by which curriculum is designed and facilitated for all pupils. These are underpinned by a broad range of theoretical lenses, including literature pertaining to Integration, Assessment and Universal Design for Learning and Digital Learning methodologies.

Student teachers are supported to engage with pupils in order to develop effective, creative and imaginative strategies that promote individual and shared learning, further embedding playful pedagogies, in alignment with national curricula/curricular frameworks. Assessment process and procedures will be integrated using a variety of assessment strategies to facilitate quality teaching and learning for all pupils.

Through reflection on, and evaluation of, their own teaching and learning experiences, student teachers will be encouraged to think critically about characteristics, organisation and management of an inclusive, Froebelian, and enquiry-based learning environment.

Student teachers will plan and implement the subject matter, pedagogical content and related methodologies of Visual Arts, Drama, Music, PE, SESE and SPHE, through Froebelian and child-led approaches.

An awareness of the interdependence of teachers’ learning and pupil learning and the importance of teacher-pupil relationships in the teaching/learning process will be explored. A range of methodologies to achieve planned outcomes will be experienced and implemented, including a variety of opportunities to experiment with and explore new and emerging technologies for teaching and learning.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate an understanding of theory, concepts and methods pertaining to effective teaching, learning, and assessment both summative and formative.
build upon cross-curricular links and themes, connecting curriculum to children’s life experiences in meaningful ways, to promote and embed integration in the primary classroom.
design and manage a variety of differentiated, learning environments to support learning through the application of Froebelian, play, enquiry based and child-centred principles.
plan and implement a range of methodologies to achieve planned outcomes in Visual Arts, Drama, Music, PE, SESE and SPHE.
apply principles and methods of observation, recording and assessment to inform future planning of teaching and learning.
reflect upon their development of strategies to support, monitor and holistically assess pupils approaches to learning and their progress, including effective feedback techniques.
make links to theory, practice and lived-experiences to inform and develop professional practice, to support the process of Taisce e-portfolio-based learning.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: FOUNDATION STUDIES: PSYCHOLOGY, PHILOSOPHY AND CURRICULUM STUDIES
modules.moduleContent:
The interdisciplinary nature of Year 1 Foundation Studies is re-introduced in Year 2 allowing students to draw on theories from Philosophy of Education, Curriculum Studies and Educational Psychology in order to  facilitate systematic, critical reflection that enables students to develop and articulate their own personal educational philosophy. 

In keeping with the unique ethos of the Department, EDF621 will particularly address the contribution of Friedrich Froebel as an educational thinker and philosopher, exploring key principles such as play, imagination, creativity and learning through self-activity. The module is designed to enable student teachers to apply research insights to challenges that they face in their own practice. Issues relating to “professionalisation” and “de-professionalisation” are explored, particularly in the context of the Teaching Council Codes of Professional Practice, self-evaluation, professional collaboration in learning communities and the ethics of practice. 
 
The module provides a research-informed framework for reflective practice. Furthermore, it encourages students to understand their school-based experiences in the context of a philosophical framework which highlights issues in relation to ethical behaviours, children’s rights and the nature of education
learningOutcomes: Critically reflect on curriculum philosophies that shape and inform curriculum and evaluate the social, political and economic influences on curriculum policy in Ireland since 1970s.
Examine the integrative nature of curriculum and assessment practices from a theoretical perspective and consider teacher agency in developing curriculum leadership in schools.
Think sympathetically and critically about meaning and value in and of education and pertinent educational issues including identity, inclusiveness and ethics.
Develop and articulate a sustaining and defensible philosophy of education.

modules.moduleContent:
Oral and written Irish-language assessments will be informed by the Common European Framework of Reference for languages. The student’s current-level competence in these areas will be referenced from the previous academic years and this will be used in supporting students to identify and meet their own learning targets. Students will develop their communication, digital skills, presentation skills, and grammatical accuracy across different language registers and genres. Students are supported to develop the language needed to critically design, deliver and reflect upon the content, communication, cognition and cultural components in CLIL lessons, utilising the TFG Fillteán Foghlama housed in TAISCE as a guide. Outdoor learning and use of the local environment, underpinned by Froebelian philosophy, guide students’ grammatical detective work to aid independent  lifelong-learning amongst students.  

Self and peer assessment skills are developed and digital tools such as discussion fora and digital journals,  are used to aid students in their reflective journey through the Irish language. 

Space is provided to encourage professional dialogue between students and HEI lecturers in order to strengthen and effectively understand students’ identity as Irish language learners and educators.
learningOutcomes: Reflect on their own language use and define appropriate personal learning targets and actions by utilising the appropriate digital skillset
Demonstrate and develop competency, accuracy, confidence and fluency in spoken and written Irish, appropriate to the level of the course
Use and develop a range of language registers, genres and vocabulary in the Irish language
Demonstrate skills in self-directed and autonomous learning
Utilise the TFG Fillteán Foghlama housed in TAISCE to further create and plan accurate language content for CLIL lessons engaging with the 4Cs
Critically analyse and reflect upon their identity as Irish language educators in a T1/T2 setting
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This interdisciplinary elective module will support students to reflect on complex global issues such as decolonisation, ethnocentrism, stereotyping and the ethics of representation; as well as concepts such as power, privilege and solidarity. Multiple perspectives will be infused into teaching and learning by drawing on a range of theorists from the Global South, and scholars from other under-represented backgrounds. Co-teaching will be adopted as a signature pedagogy of the module and guest lecturers from minoritized backgrounds will feed into particular sessions. 

• What do we mean by decolonising the curriculum?
• Anti-racism: Ethnocentrism, ‘othering’ and privilege  
• Charity versus justice approaches
• Active unlearning and a pedagogy of discomfort 
• Supporting students to take meaningful action
• Representation of the Global South in the curriculum
learningOutcomes: Challenge dominant educational discourse and thinking in relation to teaching and learning about the Global South.
Critically reflect on their own assumptions, biases and privileges about their pedagogical choices when enacting meaningful global citizenship education.
Become familiar with tailored methodologies for the critical examination of teaching materials and resources used in the classroom.
Enhance their competence and confidence in integrating global citizenship education content and methodologies across all levels of the primary and post-primary curricula.

modules.moduleContent: In this module, students undertake an Action Research project, specifically related to an aspect of their practice. This work will be presented as a 20,000-word dissertation and will include the following: Action Research design, Literature Review, Ethical issues associated with Action Research, research participants, sites and appropriate methodologies pertaining to Action Research. Data collection techniques and forms of analysis, presentation of the findings and implications for policy and practice are also key components of the dissertation.
learningOutcomes: Complete an action research dissertation.
Conduct an analytical literature review appropriate to the research area under investigation.

Document various facets of the research process, articulating appropriate questions/issues, research approaches, sampling strategies, employ the use of primary/secondary data and justify appropriate research methods
Present findings, conduct an analysis of same, draw conclusions and provide recommendations.
Meet ethical standards required when conducting research in educational settings.
Relate research to professional attitudes, beliefs and values with explicit reference to Froebelian principles.
Maintain consistent standards of academic presentation.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The objective of this module is to build on students’ knowledge of and competencies in their teaching subjects that they have gained through individual subject methods’ modules in PME 1 and throughout the BSc.Ed. Using this as a springboard, this module uses a range of modalities to explore advanced and innovative approaches to teaching and learning in a cross-curricular way. It brings together approaches to enhance students’ capacity to teach their subjects but also to conceptualise their role as educators in its broadest and most holistic sense. Fostering collaboration and self-reflective practices with treoirithe and other professionals is a key objective of this module as students engage in communities of practice to advance their competencies as student teachers and future educators. The objectives and outcomes of this module will be achieved by providing a number of electives, one of which is pursued depending on the preference and identified learning needs of the student. These electives will be responsive to research, policy and practice developments and to the expertise available in the Department. The proposed themes of the elective module to be offered in 2022-23 are:
• Team Teaching
• LGBTQI+ Studies
• Active Methodologies
• Film Studies
In each of these electives, students will explore implications and opportunities for advancing their pedagogical repertoire from theoretical, professional, practical and policy dimensions.
learningOutcomes: Understand the dynamic processes by which curriculum and assessment are designed, implemented and evaluated
Use a range of advanced methodologies and teaching approaches to achieve planned outcomes for a wide diversity of learners
Actively participate in professional learning communities which prioritise self -evaluation and self-reflective practices
Critically integrate theory, policy and practice in real contexts
Develop skills in communication, professional reflection, working with others, and being personally effective using a range of media and digital resources
Identify, critically analyse and integrate new pedagogical insights into their practice
Conduct and apply relevant research, as appropriate, to their teaching contexts.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The objective of this module is to support students to actively engage in and critically reflect on building positive relationships. The development of positive practices of proactive, communication, through modelling and role play will be demonstrated. These demonstrations present practical ways to establish and maintain a positive and effective learning environment for all students in their care. The theoretical underpinnings of the science of relationships are explored in tandem with school-based experience /practice in a post-primary school. Students will develop communication and relationship building skills and will be given the opportunity to demonstrate and assess these through participation in cooperative learning groups, peer evaluation and practice in their placement schools. 

Working as reflective practitioner’s student teachers will develop skills in building positive sustainable relationships by evaluating how they communicate. This module will seek to scaffold this communication by demonstrating a value based restorative approach to building and maintaining relationships. Student teachers will be given the opportunity to work in restorative circles and supported to practice restorative questions and conversations. The programme will enable participants to reflect on their emotional intelligence, use of empathy and understanding of fairness. In studying anger, blame and shame student teachers will evaluate their ability to problem solve and transform how they communicate while also examining the challenges of implementing these techniques.
learningOutcomes: identify key learning in relation to the use of a ‘values-based philosophy’ in the classroom including the challenges, for schools, of implementing such an approach
access, develop and use a variety of resources in order to create and maintain a safe, interactive and challenging learning environment
use strategies that promote and maintain positive respectful meaningful communication among students in line with school policy
model restorative language, conversations and relationship building skills in a group setting
demonstrate the use of these skills within the wider school community
critically assess their and others use of these skills within the classroom setting
engage in various forms of data gathering and critically analyse and evaluate relevant knowledge and research.

modules.moduleContent:
The purpose of this module is to help students become familiar with an array of traditions, issues, themes, and perspectives in educational thought that help develop a conceptual base for teaching and learning in multiple educational settings.  

Asking fundamental questions about education supports students in exploring the different aims and conceptions of education and to understand how ideas, beliefs and values shape educational theory, purpose, and practice. It enables students to explore their own positions, to reflect on curriculum, and to build critically reflective practitioners who explore educational questions from multiple perspectives. It also enables a more expansive understanding of issues and questions central to educational theory and practice.
The module will approach education through a philosophical lens. The focus of the module will be on engaging in students in educational thinking. Students will engage with a range of texts addressing key questions in the philosophy of education and will be supporting in connecting these questions with their experience as students and as educators. 

Students will be encouraged to understand key issues from multiple perspectives and to interrogate the fundamental assumptions and presuppositions shaping educational theory and practice, exploring areas such as ethics and existence in education, values and aims in education, knowledge and curriculum, identity and relationality. The module will also open up imaginative way of re-engaging with these questions and support students in connecting theory to practice.

Given the objectives outlined above, the module will explore historical and contemporary responses to a variety of questions that have shaped the field and discourse of Philosophy of Education, including but not limited to: 
 What is an educated person?   
 What is knowledge?   
 What is the relationship between education and society?   
 What is the relationship between educational philosophy and educational practice?  
 How to educate and what is the role of the educator/s? 
 Where to educate (place, context, time, space)? 
 What should be the content of an education (curriculum)? 
 What are the connections between education, power, knowledge, and justice? 
 What kinds of experiences are educational experiences?
 How does philosophy of education approach existential themes in education?
 What perspective does philosophy of education offer when engaging with questions of ethics and education?
 Who participates in the conversations about education, who writes about education, who teaches, whose voices are not in the conversation? Who should?
learningOutcomes: Describe, identify and analyse key educational concepts and themes
Evaluate diverse perspectives and arguments in the field of education
Effectively use a philosophical lens to enquire into a range of educational topics and issues
Identify and evaluate the presuppositions underpinning different approaches to education
Think critically about central educational problems, issues, and questions
Analyse specific ethical issues, challenges and dilemmas arising from contemporary education
To enable students to reflect upon the relationship between philosophy and other subject areas, as well as teaching practice

modules.moduleContent:
This module offers students an opportunity to expand their knowledge of the curricula and their subject area specifications, syllabi and assessments at both junior cycle and senior cycle levels. It will enable them to develop an understanding of the theoretical underpinnings of active approaches to teaching and learning and their application to their practice in real contexts. Students will explore ways to apply a cross-curricular approach to their lessons through, for example, Development Education, Content and Language Integrated Learning (CLIL), Science Technology and Engineering, Arts and Maths. (STEAM). Through individual and group based work it will advance their pedagogical content knowledge and their knowledge of the range of methodologies applicable to their subject area(s), developing their understandings of what constitutes and contributes to high quality teaching, learning and assessment. 

The module focuses on:  
• How to plan lessons and units of work that are appropriate to the needs and interests of learners and curricular demands, 
• How to integrate innovative methodologies and a range of resources for differentiated learning, progression, formative and summative assessment
• How to integrate theories of active learning and teaching into practice
• How to integrate theories and tenets of cross-curricular approaches into teaching, learning and assessment practice
• How to access, adapt, develop, create and use resources and supports for their subject area to scaffold and challenge learners
• How to participate in communities of practice to develop, critically analyse and integrate new pedagogical insights into practice
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a deep understanding of current national curricula and their subject area specifications, syllabi and assessments contextualised in an awareness of curriculum requirements in preceding and subsequent stages of learning
Exemplify a critical knowledge of the theories and tenets underlying cross curricular approaches, including knowledge of the associated current curricular and assessment policy context
Apply their knowledge of the subject matter, pedagogical content and related methodologies of the relevant curricula/specifications and guidelines to planning for teaching, learning and assessment
Integrate relevant principles and theories of education, in the context of the relevant curriculum/syllabus, using well-developed skills of enquiry, to inform their professional practice
Access, develop, adapt and use a variety of curriculum resources and materials for teaching, learning and assessment in their subject area(s) to support and challenge all pupils

Set clear, challenging and achievable expectations for all pupils in line with the curriculum and use a range of strategies to support, monitor and assess pupils’ needs, their approach to learning and their progress

Conduct and apply relevant research as appropriate to their teaching context, identifying, critically analysing and integrating new knowledge regarding curriculum, pedagogy and assessment into their practice

modules.moduleContent:
Introduction: Motivation and scope of module
Renewable energy – national and international context (energy usage, resources, grid, etc)
Renewable energy technologies (wind, hydro, solar PV and heating, heat pumps, ocean energy). Note that each technology component will have the following elements:
• Resource assessment and quantification
• Technology, including grid interfacing, as appropriate
• Example case study

Group project: Each focussing on one technology. Project outcomes will be presented to classmates in order to proliferate knowledge.

modules.moduleContent:
To introduce C language programming in an embedded system environment. In this course you will learn the basics of designing, interfacing, configuring, and programming embedded systems. We will use the Arduino platform, which is a popular embedded system used by hobbyists, researchers, and industry alike and TinkerCAD will be used as a learning environment.

Indicative Syllabus:
· Introduction to C language and embedded system development environment
· Review of programming basics incl. conditional and loop blocks
· Literals versus constants; bitwise operations and bit masks; Scope and extent: global, static, local
· Basic functions in C using primitive non-pointer types
· More advanced data types: typedef, enum, structures, unions, and bit fields
· Pointers and the typical mapping of code and data to memory
· Advanced pointers: functions with composite types; pass by value, out, and in-out parameters; functions returning non-primitive types; arrays and pointer arithmetic
· Arrays, Strings: literals, character arrays, and string arrays
· Other concepts: using multiple .c/.h files; recursion (and limits in embedded environment)

A full module descriptor is available at https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: 1. Design, write and debug small C programs (in an embedded system environment) using good coding and naming conventions.
2. Decompose a problem into sub-problems and implement the solution using the appropriate code features.
3. Use and manipulate C pointers and arrays.
4. Understand and Explain the operation of a C programme.
5. Explain key C programming concepts.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this modules is to learn to analyze mechanical problems and use mathematical tools and physics principals to develop solutions.

• Statics of Particles 
• Rigid Bodies: Equivalent Systems of Forces 
• Equilibrium of Rigid Bodies 
• Distributed Forces: Centroids and Centres of Gravity 
• Forces in Beams and Cables 
• Distributed Forces: Moments of Inertia 
• Method of Virtual Work 
• Kinematics of Particles
• Kinetics of Particles: Newton’s Second Law 
• Kinetics of Particles: Energy and Momentum Methods 
• Systems of Particles 
• Kinematics of Rigid Bodies 
• Plane Motion of Rigid Bodies: Forces and Accelerations 
• Plane Motion of Rigid Bodies: Energy and Momentum Methods

modules.moduleContent:
Makerspaces are at the forefront of digital culture and are an increasingly common social element in digital societies. They are creative, inventive, do-it-yourself spaces where people come together to build things, share ideas,  solve problems, design, invent and learn from others in a collaborative atmosphere. Inherently multidisciplinary and frequently fusing the arts with engineering and technology, makerspaces are hubs of innovation and invention. In this module you will participate in a university maker space where you will learn by doing, in a problem-based learning enviroment.  The courses emphasises basic digital prototyping and fabrication skills and takes places in a friendly, collaborative, eclectic learning atmosphere. 
Learning Outcomes. Students after completing this module should be able to: 
• Create 3D digital artefacts 
• Effectively use a 3D printer  
• Understand open source principles 
• Demonstrate basic physical prototyping skills 
• Use principles of introductory coding to develop solutions 
• Use an Arduino to read from simple sensors 
• Create physical interaction through application of servo motors and actuators
• Demonstrate basic circuit skills including use of soldering, switches, lights and audio
• Effectively use social media and digital content creation tools to communicate and share their ideas
learningOutcomes: Engage with a community partner to determine their needs/issues/interests
Digital and Information Literacy Skills
Effective information gathering and evaluation
Use principles of introductory coding to develop solutions
Work effectively as part of a team to complete a project
Contribute their discipline-specific expertise to the team
Communicate effectively with team members and partners

modules.moduleContent:
Indicative Syllabus
• Probability Theory
• Introduction to Probability Theory and Forms of Data Presentation - Contingency Tables 
• Review of Basic Set Theory 
• Definition of Events in terms of sets
• Complementary and Mutually Exclusive Events.
• Interpretation of Probability in terms of relative frequencies.
• Axioms of Probability Theory
• Addition Formula for probability 
• Conditional Probability
• Bayes Theorem and Law of Total Probability
• Concept of Independent Events
• Network Problems and Determination of Reliability of Networks
• Counting Techniques and Application to Sample Spaces with large numbers of sample points
• Discrete Random Variables and the Probability mass function
• Special Discrete Probability Distributions – Binomial, Poisson Distribution
• Application of Poisson distribution to engineering problems - Queuing Theory
• Expected Value and Variance, Chebychev's Inequality
• Continuous Random Variables (Probability Density Function and Cumulative Distribution Function)
• Properties of a probability density, expected value and variance.
• The Gaussian (Normal) distribution and its properties and importance in application.
• The exponential distribution and its relation to the Poisson process and queuing theory
• The Gamma distribution and the Weibull Distribution and their application to modelling times to failure
• Statistics
• Introduction to Statistics – Inference and Estimation of Parameters.
• The central limit theorem.
• Large/Small Sample confidence interval estimates for the population mean and the T-distribution.
• Large Sample confidence interval estimates for a population proportion.
• Introduction to hypothesis testing and the idea behind the process.
• Hypothesis testing on a population mean (large and small samples).
• Hypothesis testing on a population proportion (large sample).
• Categorical Data: Chi-squared goodness of fit test. Chi-squared independence test.
• Simple Linear Regression. Correlation/Causation. Prediction Intervals. Hypothesis testing.
• Discussion of relation of studied material to simple engineering experiment design.
learningOutcomes: Apply appropriate probability models to solve problems requiring a probabilistic approach
Calculate the reliability of series/parallel networks using independence and probability theory
Derive interval and point estimates of unknown population parameters
Apply statistical inference techniques to test various hypotheses about population parameters and to make appropriate decisions based on these tests
To use chi-squared procedures to ascertain independence and goodness of fit of probability models
to fit least-squares regression lines to observational data and use the regressions line to make predictions on the dependent variable hypothesis testing.
Appreciate the factors involved in simple experiment design

modules.moduleContent:
The aims of this module are to introduce the students to the design and analysis of computer feedback control systems with an emphasis on the design of digital compensators and implementation issues. 

Syllabus:
Discrete-Data Theory:
*Introduction to the key features of digital control and some of the problems to be addressed.
*The theory of sampling, non-ideal and ideal sampling.
*Frequency spectrum of a sampled signal, Nyquist sampling rate, aliasing.
*Data reconstruction - from sampled signal to continuous-time signal, Zero-order hold.
*The Z-transform, relationship between the s-plane and z-plane.
*Properties of the z-transform.  The pulse transfer function.
*The inverse z-transform - 4 methods - (i) power series (ii) partial fractions (iii) residues or inversion integral (iv) difference equations
*Stability considerations. W-plane, Hurwitz-Routh.

Design of Control Algorithms:
*Overview of Design Techniques 
*Direct Design in the Z-domain
*Discrete PID
*Pole placement & Ackerman’s formula

Implementation Considerations:
*Realisation of algorithms using direct, cascade and parallel forms.
*Selection of the sampling time, T.
*Hardware and software considerations, interfacing, hardware options.

See full module descriptor at https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: 1. Develop a variety of discrete time models (e.g. Z transfer function, difference equation) for a range of dynamic systems and be able to simulate them
2. Design a digital controller using a number of popular digital design methodologies
3. Compare the performance of digital control systems and know what effect the parameters of the controller, such as data sample interval, have on the closed loop system response
4. Explain the advantages and disadvantages of digital control systems compared with continuous control systems.
5. Present their work in the form of a technical report.

To ensure students have had a working experience of electronic, computer and telecommunications engineering as practised in a representative, modern industrial or commercial environment.
Available Placements
Placements are made available through:

• The Placement Office on behalf of the Department of Electronic Engineering in consultation with companies and enterprises, both national and international
• The Department of Electronic Engineering in consultation with companies and enterprises, both national and international
• Individual students, where the student has made private contact with an engineering company or enterprise. In order to propose a placement, students should furnish a one-page proposal, containing: 
- Own name
- Company name and address
- A brief job description (1/2 page)
- Name and contact details of contact in company

Selection/Assignment of Placements
