modules.moduleName: ADDITIONAL STUDIES IN ENGLISH 2B: LITERARY CRITICISM AND THEORY
modules.moduleContent: This module takes a conceptual approach to the study of English literature and will allow students to broaden and deepen their knowledge and understanding of literature. The module will focus on exploring particular conceptual and theoretical frameworks for understanding literature and culture. The module aims to develop an awareness and understanding of key themes and concepts underlying literature and culture today. Small group seminars, supporting the core lectures, will facilitate student discussion and engagement with the module ideas and allow students to further develop their writing skills.

This module examines literary texts in their historical contexts. The module discusses the relationship between literature and history and how this can be understood in a variety of ways (through, for example, materialist, gendered, political, philosophical and other critical perspectives). The module concentrates on examples of historical moments when the literary interacts with historical events and phenomenona.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a thorough knowledge of the major literary and cultural innovations of a particular literary period;
Demonstrate knowledge of the material and historical contexts that shape the making of literature and the circulation of literature;
Show an ability to situate literary texts in relation to each other and the ideas of literary periods;
Develop and write a research essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings;
Write responses to exam questions, demonstrating the ability to synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions;
Show capacity to write clear, grammatically correct prose;
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides an introduction to film as an aesthetic, economic, and cultural phenomenon. Mastering the vocabulary associated with narrative, editing, cinematography, mise-en-scene, and sound, which are the fundamental tools required for the close analysis of film texts, lays the groundwork for the interpretive analysis of film texts. We will further consider key historical, economic and ideological contexts that inform the production and consumption of films and film genres. Topics that link to second semester modules in the Film/Screen studies elective stream will be incorporated.
learningOutcomes: Utilize the technical terminology of film studies in writing and speaking
Analyse and interpret narrative, visual form, and sound design in film/screen texts
Situate cinema as an aesthetic and cultural form within historical and social context
Construct written and oral arguments related to film/screen studies subjects.

modules.moduleContent: In this module students will study in detail the work of one or more authors and/or themes. Students will be introduced to the methodologies of close study of one specific research topic and will discuss critical, historical and theoretical contexts for understanding the chosen specialist area of literary study. Students will be encouraged to undertake a deep study of the writer(s) and/or theme(s) under discussion in the module.

modules.moduleName: BOOKS THAT MADE THE WORLD - I
modules.moduleContent: We are living in a world where the key discourses which have dominated our understanding of politics, economics and culture at large feel inadequate to the task of addressing the urgent and multiple crises consuming our world. This module will consider the significant role that books have played, both in the past and in the present, in reproducing and analysing cultural values and ideologies, but also in articulating responses and resistance to those. Through the prism of a range of books students will explore the impact of writing in a range of areas, such as economic and social conditions, power structures, individual desire, sexuality, gender, the unconscious, art, literature, science, and philosophy. The module’s themes will vary from year to year and include issues such as gender and sexuality, race and ethnicity, migration and ecology. Books will be selected on the basis that they influence, directly or indirectly, the assumptions and contexts of the world in which we live. The module emphasises the study of primary texts in their original contexts and the history of their subsequent transmission and reception.

This module examines the literary representation of sociocultural patterns of everyday movement and activity that have escaped, or actively evaded, the disciplinary spatial practices and functionalist management strategies of different historical systems of power to constitute vibrant, translocal and transnational cultures of modernity. Addressing histories of forced removal and transportation, restriction of movement and regulation of travel, and the demarcation of cities and nation-states that embodies both unequal socioeconomic relations and distortions of historical memory and public space, the module will explore the ethics and politics of real and imagined, alternative geographies of justice and liberation in light of various influential interdisciplinary, literary-geographical approaches.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an ability to understand and analyse complex theoretical ideas, and to apply these ideas to literary texts
Show an ability to appraise, identify and use varied contemporary critical and theoretical approaches to literature and culture
Demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.

Develop and write a research essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions; to construct a coherent, substantiated argument;
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.

modules.moduleName: GLOBAL ARAB MIGRANT WRITING AND FILM
modules.moduleContent:
This interdisciplinary module engages the increasingly important area of global migration/(im)mobilities through collaboratively co-created seminars and public engagement events. 
It explores the transcultural, transnational, and translingual spaces imaginatively rendered in the works of contemporary Arab writers and filmmakers who are originally from the Middle East and North Africa and migrated to Europe and North America. The aesthetically and politically influentially works (in English and translation) comprise experimental auto- and hybrid fiction, documentary and feature film. They address, among other topics, socio-political oppression, war and associated human rights violations in the Arab world, while focusing on the broader context of undocumented and forced migration, especially to Europe. 
Theoretical frameworks include bio/necropolitics, trauma theory, the abject, zombie theory and hauntology, intersectional critical race theory and decoloniality, radical democracy, as well as the environmental humanities and displacement studies.

The historical novel is a very popular literary genre, and it continues to capture the imagination of fiction readers. It has also been of great interest to theorists of the novel. A key insight of this criticism is that a historical novel is less interesting for the story it tells us about history – the events of the past – than for those ideological perspectives on history – the weltanschaunng or world-view – encoded in the texture of the novel. These are some of the key questions we will be thinking about in this course: Does the novel convey a static or a dynamic sense of history? Is history merely a setting, a colourful backdrop, against which the action unfolds? Or does the author find a way of writing that conveys the complexity of historical development as it is experienced – intellectually, emotionally and sensuously – by individuals in the flux and flow of their lives as social beings? And how does the novelist achieve that – what artistic choices did they make? This module is seminar-based. The seminars are primarily based on close textual analysis of the novels, interspersed with discussion of examples of literary theory about the genre. Currently the novels on the course have in common that they are set during times of war. These novels are: Pat Barker, Regeneration (1991) Sarah Water, The Night Watch (2006) Sebastian Barry, Days Without End (2016).
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a complex knowledge of key literary texts.
Demonstrate an strong ability to assimilate and scrutinise critical readings
Show an ability to appraise, identify and use varied contemporary critical and theoretical approaches to literature and culture
Participate in seminar discussions; debate issues related to cultural context and socio-political circumstances;
Demonstrate high quality verbal and written expression; the ability to make clear points in a lucid grammatical and confident manner.
Write an essay on a chosen topic, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.

modules.moduleContent: In this module, we will explore both Irish cinema and Irish film studies. During the semester, we will examine Irish cinema in the twentieth century as well as focusing on the contemporary moment. The module is timetabled in parallel to the Dublin International Film Festival and will engage with new Irish work that will be premiered at the festival. Beyond the films themselves, it will consider their industrial and institutional contexts, including not only the film festival but also such organizations as the Irish Film Board and the Irish Film Institute. If you are taking this module, you should be prepared to travel to Dublin to experience the film festival and to attend regular on-campus screenings associated with weekly seminars.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a complex knowledge of key cinematic texts.
Demonstrate a strong ability to assimilate and scrutinise critical readings
Show an ability to appraise, identify and use varied contemporary critical and theoretical approaches to literature and culture
Participate in seminar discussions; debate issues related to cultural context and socio-political circumstances;
Demonstrate high quality verbal and written expression; the ability to make clear points in a lucid grammatical and confident manner.
Write an essay on a chosen topic, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.

modules.moduleContent: This module invites you to study a range of contemporary poetry. What does poetry make happen in this febrile and vulnerable twenty-first century world? This module will examine how lyric and experimental poetries adapt to historical change, and how poetry responds to pressing questions about nationalism, race, migration, gender and sexuality, and climate breakdown.

How have writers used the university as a setting for novels about young people’s experiences and aspirations? How has our understanding of what university education might mean for the individual evolved over the twentieth century and into our own time? Is the university a utopian place where students enjoy unique opportunities to educate themselves and to form relationships with their peers? Does university education enable young people to overcome inequalities of class, gender or race? Or do universities tend merely to reproduce, or even reinforce, social inequality? Beginning with Thomas Hardy’s Jude the Obscure (1895), a bleak account of an English working-class man’s obsession with Oxford University (called “Christminster” in the novel), we will then explore Evelyn Waugh’s Brideshead Revisited (1945) – a classic account of the freedoms and pleasures enjoyed by two young male students at Oxford before the Second World War. Moving forward to the contemporary elite American university, as it is depicted in Zadie Smith’s On Beauty (2006), we will explore how the increased presence of women and people of colour in institutions of higher education has contributed to complicating our ideas about “universal” cultural values. We will conclude with a recent best-selling narrative about an Irish university, Sally Rooney’s Normal People (2018), which demonstrates the impact of economic crisis and Irish politics on the lives of the students depicted in the narrative.
Creative writing workshops with the 2023 Booker Prize Winner and Maynooth University Distinguished Writing Fellow Paul Lynch.

In this module students will address the dynamic and volatile relationships between literature, criticism, society and politics. Students will engage with both literary texts and literary and cultural theory in order to deepen their understanding of how the literary configures and is shaped by the worlds it inhabits. Through a series of case studies students will examine the ways in which literature can be understood in its moment. Students will be expected to acquire critical tools and concepts which enable a deep engagement with literature at postgraduate level. Case studies will draw on the range of expertise in the Department and will ask students to re-imagine the role and construction of literature from a variety of critical and historical viewpoints. The aims of this module include: allowing students to examine their understanding of what "literature" is; to enhance their ability to formulate questions about the relationship between texts and contexts; and to become more adept at critically positioning their own voices in regards to both literary texts and criticism.

Literature is a feature of contemporary cultural and social life with myriad existences and manifestations. Literature is assigned cultural positions which can render it a harmless leisure activity or a force for social change. In recent decades the notion of the “creative economy” has swept up literary production and consumption to the extent that critic Sarah Brouillette argues that even the “authentic self” associated with the creative writer has come to have a “market value”. Through critical reading and case studies this module will examine the ways in which literature is engaged with and consumed in our time. The module begins with an examination of the “sociology of literature” and its current declensions, and then moves on to case studies of cultural manifestations of the literary, its construction, distribution and consumption. Students will hear directly from cultural literary and practitioners (for example, writers, editors, organisers of literary festivals, reading groups, writing groups) and will develop a research essay during the module which will concentrate on a specific example of literary production/consumption. 
Possible research topics include: the role of writing groups for children and adults; literature and tourism; the literary festival industry; reviewing cultures of newspapers, magazines and online; the funding of literary organisations; curriculum development and choices in schools and third-level; literary celebrity; literary journals; new digital literatures.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The module will combine presentations by research students with talks by invited speakers and members of the English Department faculty.  All student participants will be expected to take part - either presenting a paper, or responding to a paper - in the course of a given year.  Full attendance at the seminar is required.

Students on this module will be guided through a series of seminars on topic chosen by the seminar leader. The aims of the seminar are to deepen and broaden students’ ongoing engagement with literary and/or cultural theory, to allow for the high-level interchange of ideas, and to hone presentation skills. Students participating in the seminar will be each asked to lead elements of the discussion.

The objective of this module is to develop and deliver a research presentation before a panel of the Department.

Students will develop a formal research paper in conjunction with their research supervisors.

To take part in an internship relevant to the field.

Students will take part in an internship programme in a relevant area or subfield.  Students who wish to undertake this module will be facilitated in arranging such an internship, subject to availability.

The aim of this module is to develop a solid understanding of the principles of Electricity, Magnetism, and fundamental Electronics.

Module content

Electricity & Magnetism: magnetic effects of electric currents, magnetic fields, magnetic force on a current carrying conductor, motion of a charged particle in a magnetic field, The Biot-Savart Law, force between parallel conductors, Ampere's Law, field in a solenoid, magnetic flux, magnetism in matter, Faraday's Law, Lenz's Law and induction, transient circuits, resonance.

Electronics: pn junctions, diodes and photodiodes, transistors, amplifiers, introduction to digital electronics, gates, truth tables, flip-flops.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- DC and AC circuit analysis to complex impedance, transients and resonance

- Gauss, Faraday, Ampère, Lenz and Lorentz laws to the level of their vector expression
learningOutcomes: Apply the basic laws of electromagnetism to a number of simple physical systems in order to calculate magnetic field strengths, forces on moving charges and electric currents.
Analyse transient response of simple circuits consisting of resistances (R), capacitances (C) and inductances (L).
Describe the operation of simple semiconductor devices such as a p-n diode and a bipolar transistor.
Analyse and design simple combinational digital circuits.
Carry out relevant laboratory experiments to demonstrate and test physical principles and report the results.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The main aim of this module is to allow students to develop further skills in solving basic problems in classical mechanics and thermodynamics.

Topics covered include:
1-D and 2-D kinematics, dynamics, work and energy, conservation laws, collisions, centre of mass calculations, thermal equilibrium, temperature, thermal expansion, First law of thermodynamics, heat transfer, kinetic theory.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- Newton’s laws, conservation laws including rotation

- Newtonian gravitation, Kepler’s laws

- Mechanical properties of matter to include elasticity and thermal expansion

- Free, damped, forced and coupled oscillations to include resonance and normal modes (introductory)

- Temperature scales, work, internal energy and heat capacity

- Kinetic theory of gases and the gas laws to Van der Waals equation

- Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
learningOutcomes: Solve kinematics problems in one and two dimensions assuming constant acceleration.
Use Newton's 2nd law to solve basic dynamics problems.
Use conservation laws (energy and momentum) to solve problems.
Calculate centres of mass for simple systems.
Discuss the behaviour of ideal gases.
Solve problems involving basic kinetic theory.
Use the First law of thermodynamics to solve elementary problems involving ideal gases under various conditions: isothermal, isobaric, isometric, adiabatic.

modules.moduleContent:
The main aim of the module is to let the students develop practical and theoretical skill in basic electronics and computer interfacing techniques.

Topics that are covered include: transducers, op-amps, scaling, filtering, power supplies, analogue computing, microprocessors, serial communications, input/output, bus systems.
learningOutcomes: Describe the principle of operation of transducers that are used to measure specific physical parameters, to include temperature, light level and position.
Discuss the need to use signal conditioning circuits between sensors and display and recording equipment.

Explain the reasons for employing negative/positive feedback in electronic circuits, and calculate the gain, input and output impedance of a number of basic operational amplifier circuits as a function of frequency.
Convert values between different number systems in use in microprocessor systems (binary, decimal and hexadecimal).
Demonstrate how a fundamental arithmetic and logic unit forms the basis for a microprocessor system, and show how it can be formed from basic digital circuits.
Write and test a simple assembly language program for a typical microprocessor.
Discuss the operation and specifications of analog-to-digital and digital-to-analog converter circuits.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to introduce the basic principles of structural computer programming and to introduce simple numerical methods of relevance to modelling of physical systems.

Module content
structural programming, basic input/output, mathematical functions, repetition and conditional operations, arrays, user defined functions, file handling. Numerical Integration, numerical root finding.
learningOutcomes: Develop simple working text-based programs in an established programming language using appropriate structures and libraries.
Use structural programming paradigm to build programs consisting of multiple functions/procedures where appropriate.
Discuss the syntax of the language, available data types and scope of variables (local vs global).
Develop numerical solutions to simple problems using convergence testing where appropriate.
Implement simple numerical integration, differentiation and root-finding algorithms.
Programmatically create simple plots, using existing libraries appropriate for that programming language.
Use text files to input and output data from a program.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to introduce students to stellar structure & evolution.

Stellar structure, hydrostatic equilibrium, nucleosynthesis, radiation pressure, solar interior and atmosphere, stellar evolution, gravitational collapse, star formation, main sequence, H-R diagram, red giants, variable stars, stellar death and stellar clusters.
learningOutcomes: Describe the observational and theoretical basis for deducing the internal structure of a star.
Derive the equation of hydrostatic equilibrium and the equation of state for stellar matter in terms of mean molecular weight and the contribution due to radiation pressure.
Describe stellar energy sources including stellar nucleosynthesis and the various nuclear reaction cycles.
Discuss with relevant thermodynamic theory the physical processes governing energy transport in stellar interiors including radiative transport and convection.
Describe the process of stellar model building based on the stellar structure equations including polytropic models.
Describe the physical principles for understanding the evolution of low mass stars of about 1 solar mass, from leaving the main sequence to star death as a white dwarf.
Describe the evolution of high mass stars (greater than about 8 solar masses) including the later stages of stellar evolution and core collapse supernovas.

modules.moduleContent:
The main aim of this module is to allow the students to develop practical and theoretical skills in instrumentation and computer interfacing through a blend of lectures and practicals.

Topics covered include: sensors and actuators, computer interfacing using a software platform like Python, LabView or SignalExpress, analog-to-digital conversion, digital-to-analog conversion, digital input/output, counters, RS232 standard, scaling, calibration, signal processing, noise, presentation of results, automation of measurement procedures.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the physical principles, techniques, components and equipment used in computer interfacing and instrumentation.
Interpret datasheets/manuals of electronics equipment and components.
Demonstrate practical electronics skills, to include building and testing a breadboard prototype of a circuit from its schematic diagram.
Design a method of monitoring a physical parameter using a computer.
Simulate electronic circuits using commercially available software.
Develop simple software routines in interfacing and instrumentation scenarios.
Report work done during the practical sessions in a written format.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to further develop the student's knowledge of physics and develop the skills required in research.

Module content: Student carries out a short advanced physics project under the supervision of individual departmental staff members. Research skills are developed, a project report is to be produced and an oral presentation made of the work completed.
learningOutcomes: Carry out an advanced level research project to demonstrate physical principles
Keep an accurate and detailed record of experimental work
Discuss research progress and issues arising with academic and technical members of staff and their peers
Analyse and critically assess experimental data and possible sources of error
Work as a member of a team to define and achieve goals in an effective way
Write a high-quality and timely technical report detailing the background and results of their work
Deliver an oral presentation to communicate the most important aspects of their work to staff and fellow students

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to further develop the student's knowledge of electromagnetics as applied to electromagnetic radiation.

Module content
Electromagnetic Radiation: Maxwell's equations, numerical methods, electromagnetic waves, electromagnetic energy and momentum, reflection and refraction, Fresnel equations, polarization of waves and behaviour at plane interfaces, wave propagation in conductors, skin effect, retarded potentials, Hertzian dipole, antennas, Thomson scattering, Rayleigh scattering.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- Vectors to the level of div, grad and curl; divergence theorem and Stokes’ theorem

- Waves on strings, sound waves and EM waves

- Maxwell’s equations and plane EM wave solution; Poynting vector

- EM spectrum

- Polarisation of waves and behaviour at plane interfaces
learningOutcomes: Apply Maxwell's equations to propagation of electromagnetic radiation in vacuum, in a dielectric with permittivity e and permeability µ, and in a metal.
Derive the Fresnel equations for the reflection and transmission of radiation at the interface between two dielectrics and at the surface of a good conductor.

Explain the meaning and role of retarded potentials in the calculation of electric and magnetic fields, and apply them to the case of the Hertzian dipole to obtain an expression for the power radiated in the far-field.
Discuss the cross-section for scattering of electromagnetic radiation by a free electron (Thomson scattering), atoms or molecules (Rayleigh scattering).

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to provide a comprehensive overview of semiconductor physics, semiconductor manufacturing techniques and a variety of semiconductor devices.

Module Content

Semiconductor Manufacturing: Taking raw materials and transforming them into high purity silicon wafers (purification and single crystal formation).

Device Manufacture: photolithography, epitaxial growth, etching techniques, doping, plasma processes and metal deposition.

Revision of semiconductor physics: intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors, continuity equations, electron transport, Haynes-Shockley experiment.

Integrated circuits: processes of IC production, large scale integration, types of ICs, technology families, future challenges.

Junctions: p-n junction, forward and reverse bias, metal-semiconductor junction, insulator-semiconductor junction, the MOS capacitor, bipolar junction transistors and field effect transistors.

Opto-electronics: physics and operation of LEDs, solar cells, photodiodes, diode lasers, OLEDs, fibre optics, semiconductor optics and applications.

Imaging devices: CCDs and CMOS devices and CCD readout.
learningOutcomes: Describe the essential processes used in the manufacturing of semiconductor structures, devices and circuits.
Discuss the physics of intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors, and the processes involved in carrier transport in semiconductors.
Discuss the properties of the different junctions used in semiconductors, including the p-n junction, and the operation of bipolar junction transistors and field effect transistors.
Explain the principle of operation of a laser including the concept of population inversion and stimulated emission.
Describe the physics and operation of a variety of opto-electronic devices based on the p-n junction.
Explain the structure and operation of imaging devices such as CCDs and CMOS sensors.
Discuss key aspects and applications of integrated circuits and nanotechnology, including, manufacturing, technology families and future challenges.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to introduce the main topics and techniques in radio astronomy and outline the physical processes of the ISM (Interstellar Medium), also an important source of radio emission in the Universe.

Specific topics that are covered include:

Radio telescopes & receivers, Radio observing techniques, Radio sources and radiometry, Radio interferometry & principles of aperture synthesis

General properties of the ISM. Physical conditions in HI and HII regions, radiatively excited regions. Interstellar dust and extinction. Masers and stimulated emission processes, Heating and cooling processes, introduction to gas dynamics, molecular clouds and the early processes of star formation, spectral line analysis and observational techniques.
learningOutcomes: Describe and explain the essential features of radio astronomy techniques.
Describe the process of radio interferometry and aperture synthesis and recognise the use of Fourier transform in aperture synthesis.
Solve problems relevant to radiometry and general radio astronomy techniques.
Summarise the physics of different classified regions of the ISM and identify radio techniques to measure characteristics of the ISM dust and gas.
Describe the mechanism of radiative transfer and its role in ISM radio observations of different regions.
Identify the role of spectral line analysis in ISM observations and solve associated numerical problems.
Examine the physics of HI and HII regions and associated ionisation/recombination balance.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to develop teaching skills through training and practice in lecturing, tutoring or demonstrating in Experimental Physics. (The same work cannot be submitted for credit in EP804 and PCTL.)

Module Content:
Teaching practice (1 semester) in Experimental Physics through the delivery of lectures or tutorials or demonstrating in laboratories. Training in laboratory demonstrating and the teaching of Experimental Physics.
(The same small-group teaching cannot be used for this module and the PCTL.)
learningOutcomes: Competently perform the required preparations before a teaching assignment.
Competently perform undergraduate teaching duties under staff supervision.
Deal with students queries in a professional and courteous manner giving appropriate amount of assistance.
Demonstrate a record of timely and consistent appraisal of student work with constructive feedback to students.

modules.moduleName: ORAL PRESENTATION OF RESEARCH WORK AT A CONFERENCE, MEETING OR WORKSHOP (ADVANCED SPECIALIST)
modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to train students in oral presentation skills exposing them to international academic review through presentation of results at international conferences, meetings and workshops

Module Content: Students write and submit a research abstract and conference paper and then present their work at a conference in the form of an oral presentation. (The same work cannot be submitted for this module and module GSE1).
learningOutcomes: Compose a conference abstract which succinctly summarizes the research work to be presented.
Write a conference paper which describes in more detail the work to be presented at the international conference.
Prepare an oral presentation on the student's research to the appropriate professional level for an international conference.
Deliver an oral presentation on the student's research at an international conference.
Develop self-confidence in presentation and networking skills.

modules.moduleName: POSTER PRESENTATION OF RESEARCH WORK AT A CONFERENCE, MEETING OR WORKSHOP (ADVANCED SPECIALIST)
modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to train students in research presentation skills and to expose them to peer review through presentation of results at conferences, meetings and workshops.

Module Content:
Students write and submit an research abstract and then present their work at a conference in the form of an oral or poster presentation. (The same work cannot be submitted for this module and any GS module). The presentation may be a poster presentation at a national or international conference or an oral presentation at a national meeting.
learningOutcomes: Compose a conference abstract which succinctly summarizes the research work to be presented.
Prepare a poster or oral presentation on the student's research to the appropriate level.
Present and defend personal research in an academic forum.
Demonstrate self-confidence in presentation and networking skills.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to further student's knowledge and skills through participation in focused technical course that offers advanced training or subject specific knowledge or skills
Module Content: Content depends on the supervisor approved course.
learningOutcomes: Register for, make arrangements for and travel to the technical course.
Demonstrate increased technical knowledge or skill appropriate to student's research area.

modules.moduleContent:
The purpose of this module is to allow postgraduate research students to develop and apply the skills required for publishing their research. This module covers the publication of a paper in a journal.

Module content:

· Undertake an informed literature survey around the research that is intended for publication.

· Draft research question/hypothesis and framework for manuscript.

· Write up the manuscript to include abstract, introduction and results and discussion content.

· Submission of manuscript to a peer-reviewed journal.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the current state-of-the-art in their research.
Interpret results in the context of the impact on the particular field.
Evaluate results for potential publication.
Formulate a research question/hypothesis for the publication.
Concisely present and critically discuss research highlights that can prove/disprove the hypotheses/question
Draw conclusions on the work and articulate the significance of the research in the wider context
Identify a journal, chapter, edited book or conference proceedings, with appropriate scope for publication

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO FINANCE: MARKETS, MONEY AND INVESTMENT
modules.moduleContent: This module examines the role and function of the financial system in the overall economy. We examine key concepts such as the measurement of living standards, economic growth, inflation and savings at an aggregate level and analyse how these concepts influence the financial system. We explain the role of money in the economy and how Central Banks implement policy through the management of money and interest rates. We investigate the savings versus consumption decision of individuals and provide an overview of portfolio management. Topics include investment decisions; risk and return; how firms are financed; derivative securities and risk management; the nature and evolution of financial markets. Interactive learning, using Bloomberg Market Concepts, allows students to complete Bloomberg modules in Economics Indicators.
learningOutcomes: Present elementary models and theories describing economic processes in a market economy;
Apply these economic concepts and theories to analyse numerical data and real world situations;
Complete the Economic Indicators modules on Bloomberg Market Concepts;
Discuss and assess the role of the government and central bank in the economy;
Describe the operation of the markets that make up the financial system and their interactions;
Explain the concept of the 'Time Value of Money' and compute the present and future values of investments;
Use investment appraisal techniques to distinguish between good and bad investments; and

modules.moduleContent: This course provides students with a foundation in financial risk modelling and risk management. The course considers the statistical estimation and business application of key risk metrics including variance, value-at-risk, expected shortfall, and tail risk; explores the role of factor models of security returns in building risk models; considers credit and liquidity risk; examines the value of historical and Monte Carlo simulation, stress testing, and scenario analysis in risk analysis, and considers risk dynamics and long-horizon risk planning.
learningOutcomes: Describe the key empirical methods used in financial risk modelling;
Explain how different estimation techniques are used and their relative strengths and weaknesses;
Illustrate the relative merits of various estimation methods; and
Critique the various estimation methods typically used in financial risk modelling.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to software that is widely used across the Financial industry. It is run as an intensive two-week course over the Summer term. Students will learn about Relational Database Management Systems to search and sort databases and produce user-friendly output. Students will also learn to code using a modern, state-of-the-art statistical software package (such as R). Students will learn to manipulate and graphically present data, and analyse relationships between financial variables building on the statistical skills acquired on earlier taught modules. Real-world data from the Bloomberg terminals brings real-world relevance to the application of exercises. Familiarity with these essential software packages are designed to give students a head start in their early careers.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of relational database management systems;
Query databases and produce user-friendly output;
Demonstrate a competency in a coding language;
Present and visualise financial data; and
Perform financial analysis using statistical and analytical techniques.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module focuses on the role of the end consumer of financial services and their impact on the market for sustainable finance products and services. Learners will be made aware of psychological theories that at an individual level come together to link to market outcomes. This is particularly important for the sustainable finance industry as there is a strong individual bias towards sustainable products.
learningOutcomes: Apply the fundamental principles of behavioural finance to sustainability issues, such as climate change, environmental degradation, and social responsibility.
Critically analyse the behavioural biases, heuristics, and cognitive processes that influence decision-making in finance and sustainability.
Demonstrate how nudge theory can be applied to sustainable finance initiatives and how they have been implemented in different financial services contexts.
Evaluate, from case studies of companies, how behavioural finance can address climate issues and sustainability.

modules.moduleContent: As investors are increasingly applying Environmental, Social, and Governance factors as part of their analysis process to identify material risks and growth opportunities, this module will focus on teaching the fundamentals of ESG investing and ESG metrics, which might not be commonly part of mandatory financial reporting, but are increasingly included in annual reports or in a standalone sustainability report.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the ESG reporting ecosystem, with an overview of how financial institutions utilize this information to make decisions and the challenges associated with that.
Critically assess the different accounting frameworks for sustainable reporting, including SASB, GRI, IASB, FASB and GAAP.
Identify and evaluate the impact of different ESG investment strategies, such as exclusionary screening, positive screening, and ESG integration.
Demonstrate a high level of understanding of the climate-economy link and the associated investment risks and opportunities across different asset classes in the financial sector.

modules.moduleContent: This module focuses on principles of ethical finance, its application to sustainability and the role of various regulatory bodies, including governments and central banks. The module will cover polices on carbon and fuel subsidies, government investment policy and regulatory mechanisms to hold firms accountable.
learningOutcomes: Critically discuss different policies and regulations and supervisory expectations related to sustainable finance are evolving in different jurisdictions.

Evaluate what policymakers do in practice to steer/mobilise private capital towards more sustainable companies/investments and to force financial sector stakeholders to take ESG factors into account in financial decision-making.
Evaluate how complex regulations give rise to financial markets for carbon and renewable energy certificates.
Analyse the ethical dilemmas faced by financial institutions in the context of sustainable finance, such as conflicts of interest, greenwashing, and short-termism.

modules.moduleContent: Introduction of innovations in a complex regulatory regime such as financial services require strong project management skills. This module is designed to ensure that the learner will be aware of the latest trends in project management and gain hands-on experience of managing projects.
learningOutcomes: develop comprehensive project plans, including defining project goals and objectives, identifying project tasks and resources, establishing project timelines and milestones, and developing project budgets.

demonstrate proficiency in executing and controlling projects, including monitoring project progress, managing project risks, ensuring project quality, and communicating project status to stakeholders.
evaluate project outcomes and identify areas for improvement, including conducting post-project reviews, analysing project performance data, and applying lessons learned to future projects.

Critically analyse the utility of a range of project management tools and techniques, including project management software, project scheduling techniques, cost estimation techniques, risk analysis methods, and agile project management methodologies.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Sustainable finance is an exciting and rapidly evolving area of legal and financial practice. Understanding core drivers, risks, and influences in the sustainable finance world will provide students with skills that will be relevant across a range of careers.

This ‘Climate Risk & Climate Investing’ micro-credential focuses from an investor’s perspective on the great threat that climate changes pose, which could negatively impact economic growth, inflation, and investment returns. The module will examine the different climate risks and the approaches for effective portfolio decarbonization.

The online lectures are integrated with a set of case studies, readings, papers, video materials, and expert-led class discussion to provide an engaged understanding of relevant concepts and challenges. Students will learn from subject specialists from Maynooth University’s School of Business and external experts. Students will also have an opportunity to engage with industry speakers.

This introductory level module will be suitable for individuals with limited to no-prior knowledge and experience to sustainable finance.
Prospective Participants:
1. Individuals from private and public sectors and institutions with an interest in sustainable finance or are dealing with investment products and regulatory requirements.
2. Working professionals in the field of consultancy and research looking to expand their knowledge in the recent developments in the field of sustainability.
3. Public sector and private sector employees involved in policy making.
learningOutcomes: Diagnose the effects of climate change on prices and risks across a variety of asset classes, with a particular focus on equities, fixed income, energy, real estate, and mortgage-based assets.
Evaluate climate leaders and laggards across industries and those positioned to seize transition opportunities.
Judge the economic and financial foundations of potential climate regulations, including emissions trading schemes.
Critically discuss approaches of constructing the Social Cost of Carbon, including an understanding of the appropriate discount rates.
Provide a synthesis of how climate risk interacts with other risks, such as geopolitical risks.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed to complement and integrate with the module EDF130 Language 1/Teanga 1 through students’ engagement in discussion seminars and reflection activities. 

Student teachers will become familiar with the communicative approach to the teaching of Irish and will examine its implications within the context of the Irish educational system.  Particular emphasis will be placed on the adoption of Froebelian approaches and methodologies for the teaching of Irish in the junior classes in the primary school classroom. Digital resources for the teaching of Irish in junior classes, including online narrative texts, will be evaluated. 

Student teachers will explore Irish-language literature as adult learners/readers, and as classroom teachers. Student teachers will be exposed to a variety of literature including appropriate genre for development of the concept of reading for pleasure, and also of language and literacy in the Irish language in primary school.  

Reflective practice is embedded in the module, where student teachers reflect on themselves as language learners, as language teachers and on their prior engagement with Irish-language literature. 

Through the language seminars, student teachers explore the integrated nature of language teaching and learning, as advocated in the Primary Language Curriculum (DES, 2019).  Through offering this space for professional dialogue, student teachers will begin to explore their professional identity as a teacher of languages in schools.
learningOutcomes: Recognise the linkages which exist between language learning theories, literature, methodologies and practice in order to deepen their understanding of literacy and language teaching.
Demonstrate knowledge and application of methodologies for the teaching of Irish in the primary classroom, informed by a Froebelian approach.
Identify and apply the principles of the communicative approach to language teaching.
Evaluate literature written in the Irish language from the perspective of its themes, genre and appropriateness for classroom use.
Examine the narrative genre in Irish literature and demonstrate knowledge of appropriate uses of these texts for the classroom.
Reflect upon themselves as a language learner and teacher and the implications of this for their practice and their identity as a teacher of languages.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: PLACEMENT LEARNING 2:EDUCATION FOR THE MIDDLE YEARS
modules.moduleContent:
School placement is a central feature of the B Ed initial teacher education programme. 

With a clear focus on the middle years of the primary school, this module is designed to prepare and equip student teachers for school placement in Semester 1 and Semester 2. Students will extend their repertoire of teaching skills to include group teaching, cross-curricular skills, integration, monitoring pupil progress, medium to long-term planning and designing age-appropriate activities. Students will also engage in thematic planning, implement a variety of strategies for teaching and learning; assess pupils’ learning and employ effective classroom management skills. They will familiarise themselves with co-operative teaching and learning methodologies and how to effectively motivate pupils to learn. They will explore how digital technologies can be embedded and utilised as pedagogical tools. At all times, students will be encouraged to reflect on their experiential learning and evaluate and review planning. Professional relationships will be explored, including how best to work with parents and Special Needs Assistants (SNAs) for the benefit of the children.

In Semester 1 students will be placed in pairs for three weeks in a 1st or 2nd class setting. In Semester 2, students will be placed for three weeks in a 3rd or 4th class setting, one week in a Gaelscoil setting and complete Alternative Service Placement (ASP)

Prior to each mainstream block placement, students will observe in their settings on Orientation Days. This allows students to become familiar with the culture and practices of schools. On all placements, students are expected to work closely with their Treoraithe and hosts.

In Semester 2, students will critically examine the DEIS Plan which sets out the Department of Education and Skill’s vision for the provision of better opportunities for those in communities at risk of disadvantage and social exclusion. A Home School Liaison Teacher from a partner school will present to the students based on their questions and gaps in their knowledge.
learningOutcomes: Plan, teach and evaluate lessons on school placement and implement a range of methodologies, including digital technologies, and Froebelian approaches to achieve planned learning outcomes.
Review, source, select and use appropriate resources and materials for teaching and learning in Middle Years classrooms.
Demonstrate ability to establish classroom management strategies that support suitable and effective learning for all pupils and that promote and maintain positive behaviour.
Identify and utilise planning practices with a specific emphasis on project methods, integration of subjects and group teaching practices for Middle Years Classrooms.
Assess the achievement of curriculum objectives and adapt their teaching accordingly.

Individually, and in collaboration with colleagues, reflect on his/her attitudes, and beliefs about teaching and learning, and Alternative Service Placement, which informs and guides his/her professional practice.

Gain an understanding of how education can contribute to social justice.
Critique the current DEIS plan for primary schools.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The module is designed to provide student teachers with the knowledge, skills, methodologies and resources critical to the teaching and learning of Mathematics and Science appropriate to 1st to 4th Class in line with The Primary School Curriculum Mathematics (DES, 1999a) and Science (DES, 1999b) and the Primary Mathematics Curriculum (DES, 2023).

With a clear focus on 1st class to 4th class in the primary school, this module is designed to prepare and equip student teachers for the provision of quality teaching and learning of Mathematics and Science for all pupils. 
The creation of an enquiry-based and independent learning environment involving the utilisation of the indoor and outdoor environments in keeping with Froebelian philosophy to promote Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM) is emphasised in this module. Linkage and integration across curriculum subjects, particularly project work will be explored to promote an enquiry based, emergent curriculum. Methodologies required for inclusive high-quality teaching and learning for all pupils underpinned by Froebelian philosophy (station teaching, guided discovery, fieldwork, hands-on learning, problem solving, reflective practice, outdoor learning, digital learning and talk and discussion) will be explored through the student teacher’s own experiential learning experiences. Methods of children recording their work, documentation and assessment of children’s work including self and peer evaluation will also be addressed to support quality teaching and learning for all pupils. Significant emphasis is placed on education for sustainable development and greater sustainable practices. There is demonstrable integration of environmentally sustainable practice in teaching and learning rooted in the principle of care for others and the planet, throughout the module. Links to Mathematics Competency modules will be encouraged.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a working knowledge of the Primary School Curriculum (1999) Mathematics and Science – Knowledge, skills, values and attitudes – for 1st to 4th class.
Demonstrate content and pedagogical knowledge and vocabulary of mathematics and science necessary for teaching in Primary School.
Examine theories of mathematical and scientific learning relating same to practice.
Utilise a range of methodologies specific to mathematical and scientific enquiry in 3rd - 6th classes (including use of ICT).
Review, select and use appropriate resources and materials for teaching and learning Mathematics and Science for 3rd - 6th classes.
Create and utilise an enquiry-based and independent learning environment, indoors and outdoors.
Link and integrate mathematics and science across the curriculum with an emphasis on project work.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The objective of this module is twofold: to prepare student teachers to build on their experience of teaching Religious Education on School Placement and, to enable them to advance their knowledge and appreciation of religion as a global phenomenon and of the search for meaning as a universal facet of the human person.

Two complementary constituents form the basis of this module, as in Year I. The pedagogical element extends participants’ teaching experience to the design and delivery of a longer series of lessons with children from First Class to Fourth Class. Secondly, participants’ familiarity with the range of R.E. programmes currently in use in primary schools in Ireland is enhanced and deepened. Under the rubric of subject knowledge, participants are introduced to the academic fields of faith and belief, the question of God, global humanity’s search for meaning, and the relationship between faith and science.
learningOutcomes: Implement and amplify professional agency and creativity by designing and delivering a longer series of lessons on School Placement from one or more of the various R.E. syllabuses in use in Irish primary schools.
Show an increased awareness and appreciation of the place of inclusive education in the various R.E. syllabuses.

Demonstrate an enhanced understanding of the professional identity of the teacher in providing for the holistic development of children, particularly through the nurturing of their spiritual and religious dimensions.
Deepen the process of reflective practice as a teacher by applying their skills of reflecting on and evaluating in greater depth the lessons they will have taught.

Evince advanced literacy skills by preparing and presenting an academic paper based on a specific topic in one of the following areas of research: the relationship between religion and science; faith and belief; the universal search for meaning, and the question of God.
Refine further their digital skills by including a digital presentation to accompany their paper.

modules.moduleContent:
The module serves to further develop student teachers’ knowledge and understanding of drama and theatre education, and their skill and practice therein, to inform their engagement with pupils to develop effective, creative, and imaginative strategies for individual and shared learning in creative storytelling, theatre, and drama.

This module seeks to critically develop student teachers’ personal knowledge, skill, and practice in dramatic storytelling for use in primary school contexts, enabling them to apply their creative, knowledge and skills within and across disciplines. Links are forged between theory and practice as Storytelling Theatre theory is actively connected to creative workshop practice. By experiencing creative and collaborative processes in the exploration of module content, student teachers’ own abilities to support pupils in achieving their potential is fostered through Froebelian planning of process drama for primary classes. The provision of opportunities for student teachers’ creative and reflective practice to be developed are also offered through their participation and reflections on workshops devising group performance and a related workshop, which serve to illuminate the inclusive and integrated nature of the art form. Student teachers are prepared for entry to their professional role through learning collaboratively to plan and present a performance and integrated lesson plan reflective of national curriculum/ curricular frameworks, and suitable for a primary school audience. Thus, the development of a strong group work ethic is critical to the success of this module.
learningOutcomes: Apply creative knowledge and skills within and across disciplines through devising performance in drama.
Demonstrate an advanced understanding of theory, concepts and methods pertaining to effective Froebelian, teaching and learning in devising performance and planning process drama.
Collaboratively plan and implement a range of drama methodologies to achieve planned outcomes in devising performance and integrated process drama content.
Enhance skills of creative and reflective practice as students learn collaboratively to devise performance and integrate and evaluate lesson content based on it.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with knowledge and understanding in Restorative Practices in schools and the local communities by combining theoretical and practical applications so that the students will progress with a set of values and practical skills to enable them to implement Restorative Practices in the classroom, school, and beyond.
Restorative Practices are increasingly used in schools, both primary and secondary, as a core part of teacher-pupil engagement, to support the development of positive relationships with staff, children and parents, behaviour management, and conflict resolution. The work of Restorative Practices in education encompasses the school community in its entirety: staff; students; and parents. The values and principles of Restorative Practice in action in our schools support the cultivation of a caring, inclusive culture. Using the Restorative Practice lens can have a tremendous impact on how we teach social justice and resolve conflict. Studies have shown that using Restorative Practices reduces disciplinary problems, enhances peer- and student-teacher relations, supports the development of emotional literacy, and empowers individuals to have a voice and agency in the healthy resolution of conflict.
learningOutcomes: Understand and use restorative language (feelings, needs, doable requests), as a means of building and strengthening their personal and professional relationships.
Apply Fair Process and the Relationship Window in their teaching practice and the world beyond the classroom.
Demonstrate competency in the use of restorative circles in schools and the wider community.
Plan, participate in, and facilitate relationship building circles.
Demonstrate an in-depth knowledge of the theory, methodology, and applications for restorative conversations and restorative meetings.
Experience facilitating and/or participating in a restorative meeting.
Apply Restorative Practice values and skills in key school processes such as reflective practice, managing conflict, consultation processes, collaborative learning, and educational policy priorities.

modules.moduleName: CREATIVE RESPONSES FOR LIFELONG PRACTICE FOR TEACHING AND LEARNING
modules.moduleContent:
This module provides opportunities to identify and examine the core values, vulnerabilities and professional commitments of the role of the teacher, along with seeding a rooted authentic critical reflection and exploring it’s impact on lifelong teaching and learning through a creative arts-based approach.

This elective will focus on exploring and developing the fundamental elements of critical reflective practice for the student teacher through active engagement and research-informed creative arts-based responses which can be directly applied to professional and pedagogical practice. ABR responses are designed to stimulate and accommodate a deeper, more sensory exploration of personal and professional reflection, along with cultivating an understanding of the unique nature of the role of the teacher which is vital for fostering a steadfast, sustainable approach to critical reflection and a commitment to lifelong teaching and learning. As part of the exploration of teacher identity and in augmenting a robust critical reflective practice for life, student teachers will engage in examination of one the core conditions of teaching; that of vulnerability. Understanding the well-documented micro-political reactionary strategies to vulnerability in teaching, as well as the positive impact of vulnerability as a practice in creating trusting learning environments and promoting connection, can equip student teachers with the ability to adapt within every day challenges and to embody the phenomenon of the teacher as a perpetual learner and reflective practitioner.

modules.moduleName: WORKING IN AN EDUCATE TOGETHER SCHOOL
modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed to provide students with an understanding of the historical background, philosophical rationale and methodological approaches of the patron’s curriculum in equality-based schools in Ireland. Learners will discuss and analyse the strands of the Learn Together Curriculum used in Educate Together Schools. They will explore the broad range of teaching and learning strategies which aim to affirm diversity and promote respect and inclusion in this growing sector. The module will equip learners with the necessary knowledge and approaches to teach in an Educate Together school.

This module equips student teachers with knowledge about the ethos and associated teaching strategies of the Learn Together ethical-education curriculum of Educate Together schools. Student teachers will develop their understanding and awareness of the four key principles of this curriculum which centralises effective relationships with parents and guardians, affirming diversity, promoting inclusion, fostering student voice and empowering children to make a difference in our unequal world.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an awareness of the social and cultural context in which equality-based education developed.
Demonstrate an awareness of the content of the four strands of the Learn Together Ethical Education curriculum and the potential for integration with the Primary Curriculum Framework.
Demonstrate an awareness of different teaching strategies and classroom practices used in the delivery of the four strands of the Learn Together curriculum.

Demonstrate an awareness of the ethos of Educate Together schools and the role the Learn Together curriculum plays in contributing to the characteristic spirit of Educate Together schools in centralising effective relationships with parents and guardians.

modules.moduleName: PERSONAL AND PROFESSIONAL SKILLS FOR TEACHING 4 : RESEARCHER IN SCHOOL
modules.moduleContent:
Through the enactment of a small-scale, narrow, action-research project during extended School Placement, students will enhance their skills as a critical, reflective, teacher researcher. Students will be supported to engage with educational action-research on a targeted component of their own practice, and plan in collaboration with the Treoraí in their placement setting.
 
The module will cover matters such as the principles of action research, ethical considerations, critical reflection, researcher reflexivity, strategies for data collection and analysis, academic writing, and many other topics designed to develop the necessary rigour required for enhancing practice through classroom-based research. Formative assessment on academic writing and preparation for a Viva Voce will enhance students’ communicative skills. 

Students are required to attend a minimum of five meetings with their Tutor, contribute to structured formative feedback within peer groups, and work independently throughout the year in order to enhance their status as autonomous professionals. Drafts of the literature review, methods chapter, and overview of findings will be submitted at various stages of the year.
learningOutcomes: Develop an individual interest in a particular aspect of teaching and learning and deepen their knowledge of one specific component of their practice through action research in the primary classroom.
Conduct and write up an action-research dissertation project, based on their fourth-year school placement experience and defend this research at a Viva Voce exam.

Become familiar with a wide range of strategies for data collection and data analysis in classroom-based educational research and be supported to provide the rationale for these methodological choices.
To consolidate a creative teacher-researcher mindset, and become confident in the infusion of creative teaching, learning and research methods into their action plan for school placement.

Meet the necessary professional ethical standards required when conducting research with and about children, in keeping with existing policy and university guidelines on GDPR and statutory frameworks in relation to child protection.
Engage in critical reflection on their practice and programme of study and how both inform and shape each other.
Maintain high standards of academic writing, referencing and presentation consistent with Harvard protocols.

modules.moduleName: THE WONDER OF STORIES: PICTUREBOOKS AND PHILOSOPHY
modules.moduleContent:
This module examines a range of philosophical ideas and enquiries in contemporary picturebooks and equips student teachers to identify and explore potential for philosophical enquiry in picturebooks for children. 

Over the course of the elective, a range of international picturebooks will be examined through various philosophical lenses to explore philosophical dilemmas and the potential of children’s literature as a socio-cultural, reflective and pedagogical platform for philosophical thinking and discussion. The module will focus on narrative techniques, the didactic and ideological elements of stories for young people, power relations between children and adults, and the philosophical fields of ethics, metaphysics, philosophy of language, social and political philosophy, and aesthetics. Students will develop their reflective practice and criticality around children’s literature, the philosophical potential of children’s literature, and their digital skills during their design of a digital educational resource about their selected picturebook.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a familiarity with, and an effective application of, relevant literary theories and philosophical theories to the critical study of picturebooks.
Increase their reflexivity and sensitivity to, and critical analysis of, the ideological, ethical, literary, socio-cultural, and creative dimensions of/around children’s literature.
Enhance their practical skills in effectively participating in collaborative critical analysis.
Conceptualise, design, structure, and present effective sessions of peer teaching.
Independently select, use and critically evaluate picturebooks, suitable for different levels of discussion and readership.
Reflect on how the skills, concepts and attitudes in studying picturebooks can support young people’s philosophical thinking and their philosophical enquiry and learning across all curriculum areas.
Develop digital skills by designing and creating a digital resource about their philosophical reading of their selected picturebook.
