modules.moduleName: GET THEM TALKING: DEVELOPING ORAL LANGUAGE SKILLS IN DEIS CLASSROOMS
modules.moduleContent:
In this elective, student teachers will be presented with effective ways in which to develop oral language of students whom they teach. This module will reflect upon oral language acquisition in children and how educators can play an important role in developing language in the classroom. Student teachers will develop pedagogical expertise through critically evaluating and analysing various theories and research behind how children learn language. The module will focus upon the DEIS scheme in schools which aims to improve educational outcomes for those who come to education at a disadvantage (DES 2017).  In relation to Literacy, Talktime is one example that will be presented in this module as an approach for teaching vocabulary.  The First Steps Writing approach is another example of how Oral Language can be explicitly taught as part of the writing process. 

In relation to Numeracy, this module reflects upon how schools, and infant teachers in particular, can support parents in developing mathematical vocabulary and understanding in the early years.  ‘Home Numeracy’ can be developed in the early years and should be supported by schools in doing so.  Strategies such as Ready, Set, Go Maths, Maths for Fun and a focus on informal numeracy development are examples of how DEIS schools can support pupils and parents in developing early numeracy skills. 

This module will also reflect upon EAL pupils in schools as well as those pupils who may have Special Educational Needs and how teachers can plan for these pupils to ensure that their experience enhances their oral language acquisition.
learningOutcomes: Examine research underpinning oral language acquisition in children.
Critically evaluate research-informed teaching and learning through a Froebelian lens.
Critically reflect upon how oral language can be developed in the classroom for both Literacy and Numeracy development.
Critically reflect upon the DEIS scheme and initiatives that aim to Deliver Equality of Opportunity in schools.
Critically reflect upon how oral language can be developed in pupils who may present with English/Irish as a second language or a child who may be pre-verbal.

modules.moduleContent: Students are introduced to the context of Inclusive and Special Education at an International and National level. There is a particular focus on the New Resource Allocation Model and the implementation of the Continuum of Support in schools.
learningOutcomes: identify key influences, persons and movements in the history of special education in Ireland and elsewhere
describe defining features of effective special education
demonstrate knowledge of important elements in legislation and policies pertaining to special education in Ireland at primary and post-primary levels
outline, explain and discuss legislation and policies  that govern the delivery of special education in Ireland today
have a familiarity with the NCCA SEN Curriculum Guidelines
identify pertinent issues relating to eligibility for special education services

modules.moduleContent:
This module has a dual purpose to support skill development in the areas of research and reflective practice. Designed with two linked component parts, it prepares student teachers for entry to their professional role by fostering agency and reflexivity on their own professional learning.  

Firstly, It aims to help student teachers conduct and apply relevant research as appropriate to their teaching context identifying, critically analysing and integrating new knowledge regarding curriculum, pedagogy and assessment into their practice and to engage in critical reflection on their practice and programme of study and how both inform and shape each other. 

Secondly, it fosters a creative mind set, individually, and in collaboration with colleagues, through reflection on the attitudes, beliefs and core values,  which guide their professional practice. Through a broad variety of structured creative reflective and auto-ethnographic exercises student teachers, learn how to create and maintain a safe, inclusive Froebelian-learning environment using strategies that promote congruent, unconditional regard and relational esteem approaches to foster positive behaviour. This component has a particular focus on how to establish good relationships based on mutual respect, trust and meaningful interactions, while also demonstrating how student teachers can communicate effectively with all school stakeholders and the wider community.  

These combined components ensure that teaching itself is understood and practised as a form of self-critical learning by student teachers, with ample opportunities afforded for creativity in artistic individual and collaborative reflection, and engagement in and with research. Student teachers are encouraged to connect School Placement and lived-experiences to social and policy contexts, the rights of children, school culture and dominant discourses in education.
learningOutcomes: critically analyse and evaluate a range of research methodologies applicable to educational contexts and an ability to design and undertake research assignments as part of the programme.
engage in Taisce e-portfolio-based learning.

develop a research question, informed by lived experience and reflective practice essay to deepen their knowledge of one specific component of their practice through action research in the primary classroom.
develop a structure to support reflective practice and reflexivity on their own professional practice.
recognise and reflect on their emerging professional identity as a Froebelian teacher.
forge connections between lived experiences, personal and professional identities and their influence on emerging practice.
reflect critically on the nature and purposes of education and the social and policy contexts in which the aims of education are defined, implemented and impact on the identity of emerging teachers.

modules.moduleContent: Students’ entry-level competency will be assessed at the start of the first semester and this will be used in supporting students to identify and meet their own learning targets. Students will develop their communication skills, pedagogical and professional terminology, and grammatical accuracy skills across different language registers and genres. Students will engage in reflective practice in English-language competency. This development in professional growth in competency will enable student teachers to facilitate quality teaching and learning for all pupils across the primary school. This module will complement EDF606 and methodology of teaching in this subject area.  70% is the pass threshold for this module.
learningOutcomes: Reflect on their own language use and define appropriate personal learning targets and actions suitable for a first-year student to provide for reflexivity and professional growth.
Demonstrate and develop competency, accuracy, confidence, and fluency in spoken and written English.
Use and develop a range of language registers, grammar, punctuation, and vocabulary in English, suitable for use as a professional in the classroom and in the broader environment.
Develop in their understanding and capacity to confidently lead the learning in this subjects in the classroom in their professional role.

modules.moduleName: INTEGRATION OF LANGUAGES, LITERACY, MATHEMATICS AND SCIENTIFIC ENQUIRY ACROSS THE CURRICULUM
modules.moduleContent:
This module will examine the theory and practice underpinning the integration of language, literacy, mathematics, and scientific enquiry across the curriculum and will explore ways of developing conceptual knowledge and understanding through a cross-curricular approach and the integration of other subjects into these subject lessons. Through this student teachers gain an insight into the concepts, skills, and practical methodologies (station teaching, guided discovery, fieldwork, hands-on learning, problem solving, reflective practice, outdoor learning, digital learning, and talk and discussion) required for inclusive high-quality teaching and learning for all pupils underpinned by Froebelian philosophy. Through offering space for professional dialogue, student teachers will continue to explore their professional identity and agency as a teacher in primary schools. 
The development of language and literacy in both English and Gaeilge will be studied in this module. The use of Content and Language Integrated learning (CLIL) in the teaching of Gaeilge will be explored and the implications for practice reflected upon. Inclusive practices for supporting children learning English as an Additional Language will be reviewed with emphasis on home-language maintenance and the transferability of methodologies and approaches within the Irish-language curriculum to quality teaching and learning for all pupils.  
In terms of Language and Literacy, Mathematics, and Science, this module further develops student teachers’ understanding and application of relevant theories for the effective linkage, integration, assessment, and differentiation of language, mathematics, and scientific enquiry in teaching and learning. Student teachers will examine, select and apply a wide range of strategies to support children’s literacy, communicative skills, and engagement with literature across the curriculum and also apply a wide range of strategies to support children’s mathematical and scientific enquiry through an integrated-curriculum approach. 

Student teachers will explore the role and impact of digital skills for educational issues around language, literature, literacy, mathematics, numeracy, and science. Student teachers further develop their understanding of professional relationships and importance of collaboration with parents and families in the development of language, literacy, mathematics, and science education. 

Emphasis is placed on education for sustainable development and greater sustainable practices. There is demonstratable integration of environmentally-sustainable practice in teaching and learning rooted in the principle of care for others and the planet throughout the module.
learningOutcomes: Critically examine theories underpinning the integration of languages, literacy, mathematical and scientific enquiry across the curriculum.
Examine the principles and strategies of integration within the context of the Primary School Curriculum, including the use of Content and Language Integrated Learning (CLIL) in teaching Gaeilge.
Develop, implement and review cross-curricular planning for teaching mathematics and scientific enquiry and for the integration of other subjects into mathematics and science learning.
Develop a creative emergent curriculum to Integrate languages, literature, numeracy, mathematics, and science concepts and skills across the curriculum informed by the Froebelian philosophy.
Review, select and use appropriate methodologies, resources, and materials for the integration of the cognate areas across the curriculum (including use of outdoor learning and digital skills).

Reflect on how the skills, concepts and attitudes involved in language, literature and communication can support children’s learning and their active and creative experiences across all curriculum areas.

Reflect on how the skills, concepts and attitudes involved in language, literature, and communication can support children’s learning and their active and creative experiences across all curriculum areas.

modules.moduleName: THE TEACHING OF READING IN THE PRIMARY SCHOOL
modules.moduleContent:
The module will examine a variety of approaches to teaching reading and how this fits into the science of reading. It will explore how to assess pupil’s development in reading. How to choose effective teaching emphases based on this assessment to cater for pupils with differing needs.

Current national and international research in the teaching of reading will be examined. Changes in national literacy policy will be outlined. Case studies will be used to develop strategies for supporting pupils with reading difficulties. Digital literacy will be explored to support the teaching of reading.

This module will prepare student teachers for teaching, learning, reflective practices, and assessment of reading  in their schools.
learningOutcomes: Conduct an assessment of a child’s reading ability
Collect, record and analyse information about reading development
Critique current important issues relating to the teaching of  reading  in the primary school
Examine national literacy policy and its impact on practice
Plan effective strategies in the teaching of reading for children with differing needs
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: CURRICULUM, METHODOLOGY, ASSESSMENT AND DIGITAL SKILLS
modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to further develop students' understanding of the principles, aims and key methodologies of national curricula /curriculum frameworks. Emphasis is placed on middle and senior primary classes so the student teachers can implement a range of methodologies to achieve planned outcomes creatively in the curricular areas of Wellbeing, Arts Education and Social and Environmental Education in an inclusive Froebelian learning environment. Principles and practices of assessment will be developed and critiqued. 

This module also aims to develop student teachers’ digital confidence and competence through opportunities to experiment with and explore new and emerging technologies for teaching, learning and assessment for all learners. It seeks to foster a creative mindset and develop critical awareness of how digital skills and technologies can be integrated in the classroom. 

It aims to help student teachers conduct and apply relevant research as appropriate to their teaching context identifying, critically analysing and integrating new knowledge regarding curriculum, pedagogy and assessment into their practice and to engage in critical reflection on their practice and programme of study and how both inform and shape each other. 

A practical workshop approach across the curricular areas of Visual Arts, Music, Drama, PE, SESE, SPHE and Digital Learning will include opportunities to creatively employ a range of methodologies using an integrated approach. Multimedia resources and school learning platforms will be explored. Topics such as Assistive Technology (AT), coding and computational thinking are featured. Digital Safety is also a particular focus of the module to raise awareness of issues such as cyberbullying. 

Through the enactment of a small-scale action research project during extended School Placement, student teachers as researchers will enhance their skills as critical, reflective practitioners. Student teachers will be supported to engage with educational action research on a targeted component of their own practice, and plan in collaboration with the Treoraí in their placement setting. The module will cover matters such as the principles of action research, ethical considerations, critical reflection, researcher reflexivity, strategies for data collection and analysis, and other topics designed to develop the necessary rigour required for enhancing practice through classroom-based research.
learningOutcomes: integrate relevant principles and theories of education, in the context of the relevant curriculum/syllabus, using well-developed skills of enquiry, to inform their professional practice

access, develop, adapt and use a variety of curriculum resources and materials and employ relevant technical knowledge and skills of a range of digital technologies (including multi-media resources and assistive technology) effectively to facilitate¿teaching, learning and assessment practices for all pupils
use a range of strategies to support, monitor and assess pupils’ approach to learning and their progress – including effective feedback strategies
demonstrate best practice on cyber-ethics including issues of privacy, information security, copyright and on-line safety and understand the complex interplay between technology and society
engage in Taisce e-portfolio-based learning
develop an individual interest in a particular aspect of teaching and learning and deepen their knowledge of one specific component of their practice through action¿research in the primary classroom

become familiar with a range of strategies for data gathering and analysis in classroom-based educational research and to conduct and write up an action-research dissertation project, based on their school placement experience

modules.moduleContent: Through this module, student teachers are provided with an opportunity to engage with concepts such as inequality, justice and solidarity, in a way that will support newly qualified teachers to facilitate quality teaching and learning for all pupils. Topics such as global poverty, social justice, fair trade, activism, culture, migration, human rights and anti-racism will be explored both theoretically and experientially as a model for classroom practice. Multiple perspectives are provided through guest lecturers sharing their narrative experiences, with a particular emphasis on the educational experiences of the Traveller community and those who have lived through the system of direct provision in Ireland. Student-teacher confidence and competence in global citizenship education is enhanced through the addition of a discrete school placement opportunity, whereby in groups, students collectively plan a station teaching exercise on a ‘controversial issue’. This placement is hosted by a partner school, with high levels of diversity within the pupil population, affording student-teachers the opportunity to enact culturally responsive pedagogy in practice.
learningOutcomes: Understand the interdependent nature of our new global reality and critically reflect on systems which compound global inequality.
Critically reflect on instances of racism, colour-blindness and micro-aggressions using case studies and real-life scenarios as stimuli.
Appreciate the potential of action and activism as mechanisms which can empower learners of all ages to assume active roles, both locally and globally, in the fight for social justice.
Infuse a range of active, participatory and experiential methodologies into their plans for teaching and learning.
Respond to the life stories of asylum seekers, Irish Travellers and other minoritized ethnic voices, and reflect on how these stories challenge their prior knowledge ideas and values.
Design a station teaching exercise on a ‘controversial’ global topic in a primary school with a diverse population.

modules.moduleContent: This module gives participants an understanding of the processes involved in undertaking research and provides a broad understanding of the research methodologies used in action research. This module also aims to prepare participants for planning a research proposal at Masters’ dissertation level based on research in a school setting or in an educational setting. The module will begin with an introduction to the research process, and will develop into an exploration of alternative approaches to research methods. Each stage of the research process will be examined: research design, ethics, literature review, research methodologies and data gathering techniques, data analysis, drawing conclusions and making recommendations. The module will also involve evaluating the benefits of primary and secondary data associated with qualitative and quantitative collection methods. Additionally, validity and rigour in action research will be investigated. By the end of the module, participants will have a thorough grasp of the principles and practicalities of how to conduct action research. Key documents will be submitted during the module timeframe for research approval in order that a research project can commence in Semester 2.
learningOutcomes: Identify and articulate their educational values.

Identify, and justify in terms of educational values, a particular aspect of education that he/she wishes to examine through undertaking action-research in a school or alternative educational setting.
Draft and justify a proposed project plan for action research in an area of practice.
Meet ethical standards required when conducting research in educational settings.
Source and critique a range of literature, including theoretical and practitioner-based research journals, conference papers, professional and policy documents.
Identify, explore and justify appropriate methods for data collection in the research in practice process.
Develop a criticality towards data interpretation and the related methodological approaches chosen.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The thesis content should entail original research on an educational topic planned, conducted, analysed and reported by the individual student with support from her / his supervisor.
learningOutcomes: Design, manage and complete an educational research project
Read and interpret educational research
Select and defend appropriate research methodologies
Draw appropriate insights from research findings
Communicate research findings in a rigorous and defensible manner.

modules.moduleContent:
The objective of this module is to provide student teachers with opportunities to develop skills in lesson planning, teaching and assessment in a structured, incremental, supportive and challenging way. In addition to the direct teaching of classes, student teachers will learn about and contribute to the wider life of the school and experience the rhythm of school life by engaging in wider school activities as detailed in the Teaching Council’s School Placement Guidelines (2013). Through reflective work and professional conversations with treoirithe and others, student teachers will develop professionally relevant skills such as communication, time management, teamwork and the effective use of educational technology. A particular focus will be placed on the ‘Personal’ and ‘Professional’ dimensions of the student teacher’s identity within the Department’s Ethical-Relational Conceptual Framework for Initial Teacher Education. This module will act as a fulcrum for the programme, providing a context for practical, professional and conceptual aspects of the PME to inform one another.
Following a period of observation in the placement school, student teachers will teach classes on their own and increase the number of teaching classes in semester 2. Each student teacher will be expected to collaborate closely with one or more treoirithe. Opportunities for discussion about observations, teaching and school-based activities will be provided at university-based tutorials as well as one-to-one professional conversations with a tutor. Student teachers are expected to behave professionally both inside and outside the school, in line with the Teaching Council’s Code of Professional Conduct for Teachers (Teaching Council, 2016).
learningOutcomes: Critically observe, in a structured manner, school and classroom practice and culture with the support of treoirithe and wider professionals

Plan coherent and integrated lessons that reflect their subject knowledge, pedagogical content knowledge and related methodologies for a wide diversity of learners (both alone and for team teaching contexts)
Teach planned content in an appropriate manner using effective, creative and imaginative strategies that promotes individual and shared learning
Use a variety of assessment strategies to promote student learning

Develop strategies for the promotion of positive relationships and communicating effectively with pupils, parents, colleagues, the school principal, school management, co-professionals and the wider community
Create and maintain a safe, interactive and challenging environment using strategies that promote and maintain positive behaviour, in accordance with school policy
Receive, reflect on and respond to feedback on their practice from treoirithe, tutors and others
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT 2 (LESSON AND UNIT PLANNING)
modules.moduleContent: To provide research-informed insights to support student teachers’ understanding of the practices of teaching, learning and assessment for all students. To build on year one work and move to developing competences in curriculum planning for terms. Develop competences in summative assessment. Lectures and activities will invite students to explore and apply the skills and principles of effective planning of teaching, learning and assessment with a focus on the Unit of Learning Plan. Students will work collaboratively to develop, evaluate and creatively extend their pedagogical thinking as it relates to plans and activities generated with peers in class and independently outside of class.
learningOutcomes: apply pedagogical expertise, including subject specific pedagogical content knowledge in their curriculum development, planning and enactment
use a range of strategies to support, monitor and assess pupils’ approach to learning and their progress – including effective feedback strategies

advance their communicative skills and ensure that their teaching is understood and practised as a form of self-critical learning, underpinned by opportunities for individual and collaborative reflection, and engagement in and with research
practice adaptive expertise through reflection in, on and with action
demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the importance of sharing professional learning in a collegial manner to support and enhance teaching and learning
apply knowledge of the individual potential of pupils, dispositions towards learning, varying backgrounds, identities, experiences to planning for teaching, learning and assessment
integrate relevant principles and theories of education, in the context of the relevant curriculum/syllabus, using well-developed skills of enquiry, to inform his/ her professional practice

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of the module is to support participant’s understanding and competency in digital literacy for teaching and learning. The module will expand student’s literacy through engagement with relevant research and exploration of the European Framework for The Digital Competence for Educators as well as the Irish Digital Learning Framework. The module will increase students’ capacity to identify and evaluate digital technologies as a pedagogical tool in teaching, learning and assessment.

Students will explore the use of digital technologies for teaching, learning and assessment through reflection on the current use of technology in their placement schools and its relation to the literature and research on best practice. They will plot their own journey of technology use and reflect on their learning in a continuous way. Various methods of technology supported teaching, learning and assessment will be analysed and different contexts explored. The benefits and challenges of these will be assessed. Particular focus will be paid to developing an awareness of how their digital competencies as educators can be developed.
learningOutcomes: Reflect on the pedagogical and environmental factors that influence the use of technology in second level education
Evaluate the opportunities that digital technology provides for teaching, learning and assessment within their own contexts
Create/ Design digital learning resources that adhere to good principals of pedagogical practice
Evaluate digital technology resources in line with best practice in inclusivity and accessibility design using the Universal Design for Learning (UDL Framework)
Monitor, develop and reflect on personal competencies in digital literacies in conjunction with the European Framework.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: TEACHING AND LEARNING ACCOUNTING WITH BUSINESS STUDIES
modules.moduleContent:
This module offers students an opportunity to expand their knowledge of the curricula and their subject area specifications, syllabi and assessments at both junior cycle and senior cycle levels. It will enable them to develop an understanding of the theoretical underpinnings of active approaches to teaching and learning and their application to their practice in real contexts. Students will explore ways to apply a cross-curricular approach to their lessons through, for example, Development Education, Content and Language Integrated Learning (CLIL), Science Technology and Engineering, Arts and Maths. (STEAM). Through individual and group based work it will advance their pedagogical content knowledge and their knowledge of the range of methodologies applicable to their subject area(s), developing their understandings of what constitutes and contributes to high quality teaching, learning and assessment. 

The module focuses on:  
• How to plan lessons and units of work that are appropriate to the needs and interests of learners and curricular demands, 
• How to integrate innovative methodologies and a range of resources for differentiated learning, progression, formative and summative assessment
• How to integrate theories of active learning and teaching into practice
• How to integrate theories and tenets of cross-curricular approaches into teaching, learning and assessment practice
• How to access, adapt, develop, create and use resources and supports for their subject area to scaffold and challenge learners
• How to participate in communities of practice to develop, critically analyse and integrate new pedagogical insights into practice
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a deep understanding of current national curricula and their subject area specifications, syllabi and assessments contextualised in an awareness of curriculum requirements in preceding and subsequent stages of learning
Exemplify a critical knowledge of the theories and tenets underlying cross curricular approaches, including knowledge of the associated current curricular and assessment policy context.
Apply their knowledge of the subject matter, pedagogical content and related methodologies of the relevant curricula/specifications and guidelines to planning for teaching, learning and assessment
Integrate relevant principles and theories of education, in the context of the relevant curriculum/syllabus, using well-developed skills of enquiry, to inform their professional practice
Access, develop, adapt and use a variety of curriculum resources and materials for teaching, learning and assessment in their subject area(s) to support and challenge all pupils

Set clear, challenging and achievable expectations for all pupils in line with the curriculum and use a range of strategies to support, monitor and assess pupils’ needs, their approach to learning and their progress

Conduct and apply relevant research as appropriate to their teaching context, identifying, critically analysing and integrating new knowledge regarding curriculum, pedagogy and assessment into their practice
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to current theories on group leadership, membership, and group dynamics, fostering understanding of how guidance and counselling groups operate. 

Students engage in group developmental work as a group member for six group counselling sessions and are invited to bring this knowledge and awareness to their work in school placement. 

Participants are trained in:
Group Theory
Group Practice
Group Dynamics (personal growth and awareness through group experience)
learningOutcomes: Articulate a familiarity with current theory on fundamental issues of Group Leadership;
Demonstrate good understanding of group membership and how groups function;
Recognise the professional and ethical issues in Group Counselling, Guidance and group work, and have developed their own individual professional policy for groups in the school context;
Reflect on oneself in a group context and have developed awareness and skills from this experience;
Initiate, lead and bring closure to groups with reasonable competence;
Evaluate group outcomes and one's own role as leader in the group process.
Explain the role of Electronics in modern products and society
Detail the relationship of current, voltage, power for resistors, capacitors, inductors, diodes and transistors.
Undertake simple circuit analysis, up to and including Kirchoff's current law, multi loop nodal and mesh analysis.
Design and construct simple electronic circuits.
Demonstrate electrical safety in the context of the home and work environments.
Explain the roles and responsibilities of engineers to their customers, their colleagues, their employers and to society.

learningOutcomes: 1. Develop mathematical models for a range of simple dynamical systems.
2. Represent a mathematical model in transfer function form (using Laplace Transforms).
3. Calculate responses of simple dynamical systems.
4. Determine the stability of dynamical systems.
5. Design proportional and PID controllers for simple systems.
6. Use Matlab and Simulink to simulate and analyse dynamical systems.

Write code for simple C++ programs.
Design and implement software using different object oriented programming design methodologies and techniques.
Applying knowledge to design a program addressing an unseen problem.

modules.moduleContent:
To equip students with the knowledge and ability to analyse circuits containing resistors, inductors and capacitors. To provide an in-depth knowledge of filter design.

· Resistors, Inductors and Capacitors – basic concepts
· RLC Circuits - Resonant Frequency, Bandwidth and Q factor
· Passive filter design – Low Pass, High Pass, Band stop and band pass filters
· RLC filter effects stage, Butterworth filters
· Bode plots
· Realisation of various filter specifications
· Stability of filter circuits
· Oscillators
learningOutcomes: Explain conceptually, the difference between DC and AC operation.
Explain conceptually what resonance and bandwidth mean in the context of RLC networks.
List and draw the frequency specifications of the four basic types of filters.
Draw a bode plot of a network function.
Design and build a passive filter.
Use RLC circuits to modulate bandwidth and resonance as needed.
Use software to analyse signals with the appropriate filter hardware.

· To promote project based learning in the field of system dynamics and control.

· To develop students experience of working in a group

Apply structured design to a range of problems.

• To expand on the knowledge of analogue circuits in of single and multiple transistor design (CMOS and BJT) 
• To cover the design of circuits such as power amplifiers, active loads, sample-and-hold circuits.

2. Analyze and predict the performance of PWM based converters

4. Design a high speed circuit such as may be used for an FM radio receiver
5. Design a range of circuits for handling and manipulating analog signals (level shifters, amplifiers, load matching)
6. Design a BJT and a CMOS amplifier circuit.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to examine the analysis, design, and systems issues for real time systems with and without a real time operating system.

Each assignment tests one or more of the learning outcomes and all outcomes are tested at least once. All questions on all assignments are compulsory. All outcomes are also assessed in the semester examination.

**Commencing ay 2024-25

To register for this module, students must have programming experience in C or C++.
learningOutcomes: Recognise and explain key real time system concepts.
Determine the real time requirements of a system.
Analyze a system for potential real time problems.
Design solutions to common real time system problems.

learningOutcomes: Analyze the current state of the art in the field of his/her project, and suggest where further work could be done to add to it.
Carry out the planned research work energetically, with curiosity, enthusiasm and discipline

modules.moduleContent: Practical experience of laboratory demonstration and/or tutorial support over two semesters/modules with mentoring from academic staff.
learningOutcomes: Have developed practical teaching/demonstrating skills in Electronic Engineering

modules.moduleContent: This module can only be undertaken with the supervisors consent. This module aims to provide students with an understanding of digital outreach and how it can be applied to enhance the visibility, outreach, and reputation of academic departments. Students will engage with real-world projects that involve activities such as the development of websites, digital content, online/digital strategies, or social media communication to promote the department's research groups, attract future undergraduate students, and foster an improved culture regarding Equality, Diversity, and Inclusion (EDI). At least one training course must be completed as part of this module which will train the student in some aspect of digital outreach. A maximum of half of the 100hr activity can be spent on self-learning activities relating to training or workshops on web design or content generation or similar. The supervisor must agree on the initial plan for the student activity, including intended audience, training plan and expected content/design output, and how the student can assess the quality or impact of their work.
learningOutcomes: Develop and implement a digital outreach plan to enhance the department's online visibility and reputation.
Appreciate platform audience differences and the professional objective of the  communication that is delivered by the student.
Create or deliver effective digital content that aligns with the strategic goals of the department.
Understand privacy & copyright issues in sharing images, referencing online content, generative AI, etc.
Evaluate the impact of their communication strategies on the department's objectives.
Demonstrate an understanding of EDI issues and incorporate EDI principles into communication strategies.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module will introduce key concepts and ideas in the history of poetry and drama. It is designed to build on skills introduced and developed in ‘Foundational English 1’ with the aim of furthering the student’s understanding of the formal and thematic aspects of poetry and drama. The module will introduce students to methods of reading, analysing, and contextualising poetry at university level and allow students to become familiar with the thematic and formal elements that need to be considered when approaching the poetic text (rhythm, form, poetic terminology, national/canonical traditions). The module will also consider a number of key theatrical texts and explore the shifting historical and performance contexts that govern theatrical expectations and dramaturgical practice. The module will enhance the student’s understanding of poetry and drama and provide students with the knowledge, experience and writing skills to develop and express well-informed opinions about what they are reading. Small group seminars, supporting the core lectures, will facilitate discussions and incorporate formal writing exercises to ensure that students continue to acquire the necessary skills for studying English at University.

modules.moduleName: ADDITIONAL STUDIES IN ENGLISH 2A: WRITING IN HISTORY
modules.moduleContent: Cli-Fi: Climate Crisis and the Literary Imagination. How can writers respond to and represent climate crisis? Can literary texts and cultural productions provide us with tools to think about the challenge of climate catastrophe? These are among the questions this module will explore. It focuses on a selection of texts that respond to, represent and negotiate climate change as a major social and cultural crisis of the 21st century. As Greta Thunberg asserts in No One is Too Small To Make a Difference, we must “treat the climate crisis like the acute crisis it is and give us a future.” The module aims to introduce students to debates about how writers grapple with the scale of climate crisis and to attend closely to the representational challenges as well as possibilities of depicting climate change. It begins with a selection of short stories by established writers and moves on to consider how the novel, a form that some critics have claimed is unresponsive to the climate reality around us, has recently engaged with what is termed Anthropogenic climate change. Consideration will also be given to how mainstream film has represented climate crisis. Through lectures and also tutorials, the module will encourage students to consider how texts imagine alternative worldviews that decentre the human and map out a more sustainable ecosystem.

This is an optional module only open to students taking the Higher Diploma in English. 

This module offers students an introduction to key concepts in English Literature and culture. The course will focus on a variety of prose and fictional texts, including novels, essays and short fiction. Students will focus on both the formal and thematic aspects of prose writing The course requires students to give specific attention to the manner in which good writing utilises different literary and rhetorical strategies to further its ideas and achieve particular effects. The module requires students to engage actively and critically with a wide range of texts through reading, discussion and writing.

This module will treat the literature of the United States in terms of its distinctively American cultural claims, claims which evolve and become contested as its citizenry becomes more diverse.  The texts chosen will illustrate formal as well as thematic innovations in fiction, poetry, and/or drama showing how the need to tell new and unfamiliar stories requires traditional literary forms to adapt to the rhythms of new voices. Students will be required to consider texts that reflect a prolonged historical experience and which engage issues of political controversy.  Most importantly, students will be encouraged to consider what makes a text distinctively “American” and to consider the implications of the fact that America, like American Literature itself, is very much “a work in process”.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the development of specific literary genres, forms, and themes in American literature;
Demonstrate an informed appreciation of the relation between American literature and important related historical and intellectual developments;
Demonstrate an informed appreciation of specific American authors and texts;
Participate in tutorial discussions; debate issues related to specific literary texts;

Develop and write a research essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions; to construct a coherent, substantiated argument;
Show capacity to write clear, grammatically correct prose;
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.

modules.moduleContent: This module examines Modernism in literature and related art forms, introducing students to key themes and writers in the field. We will consider how literature and culture responded to the profound social, cultural and political changes that characterised the beginning of the twentieth century. The module will concentrate on the defining characteristics of Modernism, considering, but not limited to, questions of time and space; representation; interiority and self-consciousness; history and politics. Students will have the opportunity to focus on a variety of literary forms and to develop close readings skills of some of defining texts within Modernism.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a thorough knowledge of major literary and cultural innovations associated with Modernism;
Develop an understanding of the historical and cultural contexts from which Modernist writing emerges;
Formulate critical arguments about, and critically analyse, a number of key modernist texts;
Participate in tutorial discussions; debate issues related to specific literary texts;

Develop and write a research essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions; to construct a coherent, substantiated argument;
Show capacity to write clear, grammatically correct prose;
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.

modules.moduleName: BOOKS THAT MADE THE WORLD - II
modules.moduleContent: We are living in a world where the key discourses which have dominated our understanding of politics, economics and culture at large feel inadequate to the task of addressing the urgent and multiple crises consuming our world. This module will continue our consideration of the significant role that books have played, both in the past and in the present, in reproducing and analysing cultural values and ideologies, but also in articulating responses and resistance to those. Through the prism of a range of books students will explore the impact of writing in a range of areas, such as economic and social conditions, power structures, individual desire, sexuality, gender, the unconscious, art, literature, science, and philosophy. The module’s themes will vary from year to year and include issues such as gender and sexuality, race and ethnicity, migration and ecology. Books will be selected on the basis that they influence, directly or indirectly, the assumptions and contexts of the world in which we live. The module emphasises the study of primary texts in their original contexts and the history of their subsequent transmission and reception.

This module examines how literary forms change rapidly and radically through experimentation and asks why this happens. It introduces students to the ways in which literary forms (in drama, fiction, prose, or poetry) undergo sudden alterations, explores the reasons behind such changes, and asks students to consider the aesthetic, political, historical and literary impact of such moments of formal experimentation. The module will discuss case studies in experimentation, concentrating on the formal and textual nature of that experimentation, while setting texts in their historical moments. Case studies might include texts which exemplify: the rise of the novel; Romantic poetry; magic realism; modernism; postmodernism.
learningOutcomes: Illustrate an complex awareness how the formal elements of language and genre shape meaning.
Recognize how writers can transgress or subvert generic expectations, as well as fulfil them
Demonstrate high quality verbal and written expression; the ability to make clear points in a lucid grammatical and confident manner.
Demonstrate an ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.
Write responses to exam questions, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions

modules.moduleContent: This module explores literature’s role in revolutionary times, and how literature creates a space in which dissident voices can find articulation. The module asks students to examine the relationship between literature and power, literature and momentous historical events, and literature and alternative futures. Students will be asked to place texts in historical context and to develop the ability to explore how literature can work with or against the grain of history. Case studies may include, but are not limited to, the French Revolution, the Russian Revolution, the Harlem Renaissance, the 1960s, the Troubles.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a complex knowledge of the major literary and cultural innovations of a particular literary period;
Demonstrate the ability to apply relevant cultural and aesthetic theories and concepts to literatures and artistic practices
Demonstrate high quality verbal and written expression; the ability to make clear points in a lucid grammatical and confident manner.
Demonstrate an ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources
Write an essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.

modules.moduleContent: In this module students will study an author, a range of texts, or a set of comparative texts. Texts will vary from year-to-year. Students will examine the set texts in detail, learn advanced skills of close reading and textual analysis, have the opportunity to engage with and think about the relationship between primary texts and secondary criticism, and be encouraged to deepen their knowledge of the author and/or texts under consideration while honing their critical, analytical and research skills.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate the detailed knowledge of an area of literary studies
Demonstrate the a strong ability to write and research  in a scholarly manner
Summarise material effectively and synthesise complex arguments and make clear points in a lucid, grammatical and confident manner
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.
Write an essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.

modules.moduleContent: Independent research on topic chosen in consultation with mini-dissertation supervisor. The project will be either an Academic Dissertation or Creative Writing Project.
learningOutcomes: Identify a feasible research topic/writing project  and design a suitable research strategy
To take person initiative, to think independently and to make value judgments
Undertake advanced research and gather materials for writing using a variety of secondary sources ranging from articles and books to online databases.
Demonstrate the ability to critically evaluate material and make effective notes from sources.
Synthesize and integrate sources into their writing through citation and paraphrasing, applying correct referencing styles.
Compile a detailed bibliography.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module is a deep dive into one, significant literary text. The text may vary from year to year, but the module will always take as its subject of study a substantial single work of prose or poetry. Employing a literary and contextual approach, we will explore the life and work of the author; the genesis of the text and the conditions of its production; the text's place in its historical and literary context; its major themes; its genre; and, the critical commentary it has generated.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a knowledge and understanding of the literary text on the module
Demonstrate an ability to understand the historical and authorial contexts of the text
Show an ability to read the text on the module critically, with close attention to textual detail and historical context
Demonstrate an ability to integrate a wide range of source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions; construct a coherent, substantiated argument
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources

modules.moduleName: THE ART OF SHORT FICTION: CRITICAL AND CREATIVE APPROACHES TO THE SHORT STORY
modules.moduleContent:
Henry James argued that it is useful to think of the short story as a creative transaction between brevity and complexity. Indeed, for many contemporary writers the short story is understood as a form that best responds to the concerns of our age. Fast-paced and fragmentary, the brevity of the short story is particularly adept at communicating the complex intersections of race, ethnicity, gender, sexuality, class, and national identity that invest the immediacy of our contemporary lives. Some of the module’s central concerns will include a sustained analysis of the aesthetics of brevity and its narratological dimension in relation to other literary forms; and the concept of intersectionality and its importance in forging new forms of subjectivity and resistance in a world marked not only by tremendous complexity but by growing inequalities and fragility. By encouraging a balance between close reading and detailed critical analysis the module aims to provide a means for revisiting the relation of the literary and the contemporary formation of subjectivity in a global context.

The prescribed stories will vary from year to year, and will draw on a selection of global writers, including Arivand Adiga, Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie, James Baldwin, Samuel Beckett, Colin Barrett, Italo Calvino, Raymond Carver, Anton Chekhov,  Kate Chopin, Teju Cole, Junot Diaz, Anita Desai, Cyprian Ekwensi, Nikolai Gogol, Ernest Hemmingway, James Joyce, Deborah Levy, Katherine Mansfield, Alice Munro, Slawomir Mrozek, Haruki Murakami, R.K. Narayan, Intan Paramaditha, Dorothy Parker, Tao Lin, David Foster Wallace and Jeanette Winterson.

modules.moduleName: LATE IMPERIAL ROMANCE: FICTION AND THE END OF EMPIRE
modules.moduleContent: This module will introduce students to nineteenth and twentieth century fictions which deal with the experience of empire and its ending.. Starting with writers of the moment of ‘high imperialism’ in the 1880s such as H Rider Haggard and Joseph Conrad, we will examine the interweaving processes by which the European empires came to be contested and literary narratives of empire passed into political and aesthetic crisis. With writers such as Graham Greene and George Orwell, we will see the European empires crumble; with contemporary novelists such as Robert Stone and Joan Didion, we’ll see how writers figured America’s newfound role as the dominant global power of the late twentieth century and dramatized critique of and opposition to it. We’ll conclude by considering the future of imperial romance in a post-Western world. Films we may consider will include Lawrence of Arabia, The Year of Living Dangerously and Apocalypse Now.

Why read James Joyce’s epic masterpiece Ulysses?   The critic Sean Latham offers some possible answers to this question: because it is there; because it is a humane and intellectual challenge; because it celebrates everyday life; because it puts hard and urgent questions to us; because it links us to communities of readers; because it continues to be an extraordinary imaginative resource.  Perhaps every new reader can come up with their own reasons. This module will provide an introductory, chapter-by-chapter investigation of Joyce’s novel and of some of the key ways in which it has been interpreted.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a complex knowledge of key literary texts.
Demonstrate a strong ability to assimilate and scrutinise critical readings
Show an ability to appraise, identify and use varied contemporary critical and theoretical approaches to literature and culture
Participate in seminar discussions; debate issues related to cultural context and socio-political circumstances;
Demonstrate high quality verbal and written expression; the ability to make clear points in a lucid grammatical and confident manner.
Write an essay on a chosen topic, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.

modules.moduleContent: In this module students will work, under individual supervision, on a supervised writing project. This module provides tailored advice and meeting time for students as they develop their ideas and their writing, ensuring that students receive feedback on ongoing submission of work, and providing the opportunity to discuss particular issues that the student might face in their work over the course of the year. Students will be assessed on a single piece of work and their ability to develop and redraft that piece of work over the course of the semester.
learningOutcomes: Developed sense of one’s writing voice; themes; literary interests.
Increased ability to edit one’s own work
Developed sense of strategies for addressing structural/ thematic/ formal issues in one’s work.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will be taught by intensive workshops, providing a space in which students will be guided towards honing their writing, reading, critical and self-editing skills. Working as a group, students will follow a curriculum of workshops designed by the seminar leader. Seminars will focus on specific aspects of writing, such as plot, characterisation, place, dialogue, backstory. Students will be set exercises which will extend their ability and critical knowledge in specific areas of writing and will be expected to engage with literary and other texts which influence their writing and which they can learn from. Seminar leaders will devise a programme of tasks and readings which will develop these skills through short exercises, discussion and peer-to-peer feedback. Students will engage with contemporary writing as a way of familiarising themselves with current trends in writing and giving students the ability to situate their own writing in terms of current developments on creative writing.
Students will compile a portfolio of short pieces through this module’s set exercises.
learningOutcomes: Develop a sense of how to contemporary literary practice and how to situate their own work within that practice
Increase their knowledge of the specifics of prose writing
Increase their ability to identify specific strengths and weaknesses in their own work

modules.moduleContent: Students will complete a mini-dissertation based on independent research. The research topic will be developed by the student in consultation with their supervisor. The research topic will be relevant to areas identified in taught components of the MA programme.

The module will combine presentations by research students with talks by invited speakers and members of the English Department faculty.  All student participants will be expected to take part - either presenting a paper, or responding to a paper - in the course of a given year.  Full attendance at the seminars is required.

This module introduces student teachers to reflective practice and journaling and prepares them for entry to their professional role by fostering agency and reflexivity on their own professional learning. It promotes critical reflection on practice and foundation modules and seeks to identify how both inform and shape each other. They will be required to think critically about their ideas of the ‘good teacher’ and locate the emergence of these ideas in their own educational history.
The dialogical approaches and professional conversations between student teachers and HEI lecturers employed foster a creative mind set, individually, and in collaboration with colleagues, through reflection on the attitudes, beliefs and core values that guide their professional practice. 
Using a variety of structured creative and reflective exercises, student teachers learn how to create and maintain a safe, inclusive Froebelian learning environment using strategies that promote congruent, unconditional regard and relational esteem approaches to foster positive behaviour. This component has a particular focus on how to establish good relationships based on mutual respect, trust and meaningful interactions, while also demonstrating how student teachers can communicate effectively with all school stakeholders and the wider community. 
Through a variety of interactive, learner-led Froebelian lessons, this module ensures that teaching itself is understood and practised as a form of self-critical learning by student teachers, with ample opportunities afforded for creativity in artistic individual and collaborative reflection, and engagement in and with literature. Student teachers are encouraged to connect School Placement and lived experiences to social and policy contexts, the rights of children, school culture and dominant discourses in education. Ongoing, structured journaling activities and engagement with outdoor spaces on campus connect to Taisce e-portfolio-based learning and opportunities for further reflection.
learningOutcomes: Develop a structure to support reflective practice approaches and reflexivity on their own professional practice to enable articulation of key learning points with creativity
Recognise and reflect on their emerging professional identity as a Froebelian teacher
Recount significant learning experiences, linked to personal and professional identities and their influence on emerging practice
Use reflective practice to journal on supportive and dismissive feedback received and assess the impact it had on their identity, motivation and achievement
Identify characteristics of a safe Froebelian enquiry-based indoor and outdoor learning environment
Reflect on the nature and purposes of education and the social and policy contexts and their impact on the identity of emerging teachers
Engage in Taisce e-portfolio-based learning

modules.moduleContent: Two complementary constituents form the basis of this module. First, the pedagogical dimension focuses on the design and delivery of a series of lesson plans for infant classes before and during School Placement. Secondly, there is the constituent of subject knowledge. Participants are introduced to the variety of R.E. syllabuses currently in use in Primary Schools in Ireland (Áirítear Gaelscoileanna ina measc.) and the academic discipline of world religions including the humanist world view.
learningOutcomes: Implement early professional agency and creativity by designing a series of lesson plans for School Placement.

Show an awareness of and a positive regard towards inclusive education by extending their knowledge of the variety of R.E. programmes currently in use in primary schools in Ireland and by delivering a series of lessons based on one of these programmes.

Demonstrate positive initial understanding of the professional identity of the teacher in providing for the holistic development of children, particularly through the nurturing of their spiritual and religious dimensions.
Begin the process of reflective practice as a teacher by applying their skills of reviewing and evaluating the lessons they will have taught.
Evince knowledge and appreciation of global citizenship and of religion as a global phenomenon by preparing and presenting a seminar paper based on one specific world religion or Weltanschauung
Refine their digital skills by including a digital presentation to accompany their paper.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
In relation to the middle primary classes, students will continue to explore the dynamic processes by which curriculum is designed and facilitated for all pupils. These are underpinned by a broad range of theoretical lenses, including literature pertaining to Integration and Problem-based Learning, Assessment and Universal Design for Learning and digital learning methodologies. 
 
Student teachers are supported to engage with pupils in order to develop effective, creative and imaginative strategies that promote individual and shared learning, further embedding playful pedagogies, in alignment with national curricula/curricular frameworks. 
 
Through reflection on, and evaluation of, their own teaching and learning experiences, student teachers will be encouraged to think critically about characteristics, organisation and management of an inclusive, Froebelian, and enquiry-based learning environment. 
 
Student teachers will plan and implement the subject matter, pedagogical content and related methodologies of Visual Arts, Drama, Music, PE, SESE and SPHE, through Froebelian and child-led approaches. 
 
An awareness of the interdependence of teachers’ learning and pupil learning and the importance of teacher-pupil relationships in the teaching/learning process will be further built upon. An extended range of methodologies to achieve planned outcomes will be experienced and implemented, including a variety of opportunities to experiment with and explore new and emerging technologies for teaching and learning.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of theory, concepts and methods pertaining to effective teaching, learning, and assessment both summative and formative.
Build upon cross-curricular links and themes, connecting curriculum to children’s life experiences in meaningful ways, to promote and embed integration in the primary classroom.
Design and manage a variety of differentiated, learning environments to support learning through the application of Froebelian, play, enquiry-based and child-centred principles.
Plan and implement a range of methodologies to achieve planned outcomes in Visual Arts, Drama, Music, PE, SESE and SPHE.
Reflect upon the development of strategies to support, monitor and holistically assess pupils' approaches to learning and their progress, including effective feedback techniques.

Further expand on the use of pedagogical expertise and criticality, including subject-specific pedagogical content knowledge and new and emerging technologies for teaching and learning in Visual Arts, Drama, Music, PE, SESE and SPHE at middle primary class level.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed to complement and integrate with the modules EDF130 Language 1/Teanga1, EDF131 Language 2/Teanga2 and EDF232 Language 4/Teanga 4 through students’ engagement in discussion seminars and reflection activities. This module integrates aspects of language development, English-language pedagogy, and engagement with children's literature in order to equip students with the knowledge, skills including digital skills, reflective practice, and strategies to develop an effective and creative classroom programme for teaching and learning relating to literacy, languages, and literature in ways that support the learning of all pupils.

With particular focus on the middle and senior classes and informed by Froebelian philosophy, this module develops students' pedagogical and curricular content knowledge regarding language, literacy and literature, and their awareness of inclusive education and understanding of pupils’ learning English as an additional language and incorporation of their home language(s) in the classroom. The teaching of oral language, reading, writing and spelling will be explored, in tandem with effective classroom practices for literacy development and for developing children’s reading, creative experiences, and aesthetic engagement with literature.
learningOutcomes: Describe and analyse the structure of language in terms of its phonology, morphology, syntax, and semantics.
Demonstrate an informed knowledge of the significance of meaning in language and communication and relate their significance to the classroom.
Identify, plan, apply, and reflect on appropriate pedagogical approaches and methodologies for the teaching of oral language, reading, and writing.
Display an understanding of how to inclusively organise, group, and assess children effectively for language teaching and learning.
Develop knowledge and understanding of how bilingualism and multilingualism work as well as working with parents and pupils in these areas.
Develop knowledge and understanding of various literary genres and their impact on children’s cultural self-identity, language development, and creative literary experiences.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Through engagement with Global Citizenship Education, Sociology of Education, and Psychology of Education, student teachers will be supported to examine how structures and systems in place, both locally and globally, explain how people interact with each other, the impact on educational outcomes and the undeniable interconnectedness of modern life.
learningOutcomes: Deepen their understanding of complex societal and psychological dynamics at play which may impact upon a child’s learning.
Relate psychological and sociological theories to their school-based experience.
Appreciate how societies are structured through key social institutions, focusing on issues of power, justice, and (in)equality.
Apply sociological concepts to understandings of ‘race’, racism and inequality.

Nurture their agency and mind-set to be open to professional growth and learning over the course of their careers, supporting their pupils in achieving their full potential by integrating this knowledge into their practice as teachers.
Critically reflect on their own values, attitudes, and positionality in relation to what it means to be an active global citizen.
Enhance their competence and confidence with a wide range of participative teaching strategies designed to facilitate a meaningful exploration of complex global issues across the curriculum.

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL AND PERSONAL SKILLS FOR TEACHING AND LEARNING 3
modules.moduleContent:
This module further develops students’ professional communicative, literacy and numeracy skills and develops creative and reflective practice in advance of undertaking an action research project in Year 4.  

Students will be introduced to the theory and practice of educational action research in the primary classroom in order to engage in research on their own practice. Student-teachers will be supported in self-reflection to identify a targeted aspect of teaching and learning and to develop a dissertation proposal through Taisce, in advance of their extended school placement in fourth year. Student-teachers receive individual formative feedback on their dissertation proposal, including guidance on referencing, reflective frameworks and academic writing. 

Exploring professional relationships between schools and parents necessitates acknowledging and respecting parents and parental bodies as key stakeholders in primary education. In alignment with the yearly theme, Teacher as Professional, this module offers a theoretical and practical perspective on working with parents. Through workshops, student-teachers explore how to develop positive relationships and communicate effectively with parents. 
Students will engage in reflective practice in competency utilising their developing digital capacity.  From the perspective of professional growth in competency, students will study Gaeilge and Maths using goal setting and self-directed study skills.
learningOutcomes: Become familiar with the foundations of educational research, using practitioner action research as the framework for reflecting on theory and practice.
Develop a creative teacher-researcher mind-set and demonstrate enhanced confidence and competence in the use of creative and arts-based research methods e.g. PhotoVoice.

Create a dissertation proposal which reflects an identified area of interest from their Taisce connected with their practice, suitable for a small-scale, classroom-based, action-research project in Year 4.
Demonstrative effective communicative skills in their professional interactions with parents.
Support student-teachers to reflect on appropriate forms of communication, including digital communication, in their correspondence with parents and the wider school community.
Challenge student teachers to become culturally responsive practitioners by acknowledging, respecting, and affirming the cultural heritage and home life of their pupils.
Critically reflect upon, identify, and review personal learning goals, with a view to enhancing their own proficiency in literacy and numeracy.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: WORKING WITH PARENTS TO SUPPORT THEIR CHILDREN'S LEARNING
modules.moduleContent:
This elective focuses on the benefits of parental involvement for children in their education and learning.

The module highlights the importance of parental engagement in children’s literacy and numeracy learning. It includes an exploration of current research in the area and the practical application of the same, while imparting students with the knowledge and tools to develop ongoing improvement and evaluation on parental involvement within a whole-school approach. Students will learn the importance of positive relationships and will learn how to communicate effectively with parents.
learningOutcomes: Develop positive relationships and communicate effectively with parents.
Communicate effectively with parents, using styles and systems to suit the given situation and setting.
Reflect on their attitudes, and beliefs about teaching and learning which inform and guide their professional practice.

Understand the origins and development of the statutory and policy-making framework pertaining to education, their specific role and responsibilities emanating from that framework, together with the roles and responsibilities of all stakeholders, including parents.
Understand educational research and its contribution to teaching, learning and assessment with regards to parental involvement in education.
Learnt strategies for developing positive relationships and communicating effectively with parents.
Understand the roles of parents as stakeholders and the importance of engagement and cooperation with them.

modules.moduleContent: This module builds upon learning from the Global Citizenship Education (1) module in second year and scaffolds critical reflection using a ‘Pedagogy of Discomfort’ (Boler, 1999) as an approach to teaching and learning that has the capacity to disorient, disrupt and unsettle, to challenge what learners think they already know, and to support them in acquiring new thinking about themselves, the world and others. Content explores notions of culture and ‘othering’, contemporary policy on migration, refugees and asylum, the significance of action and activism with primary pupils, and racism and white privilege. Multiple perspectives are provided through guest lecturers sharing their narrative experiences, with a particular emphasis on the educational experiences of the Traveller community and those who have lived through the system of direct provision in Ireland. Student-teacher confidence and competence in global citizenship education is enhanced through the addition of a discrete school placement opportunity, whereby in groups, students collectively plan a station teaching exercise on a ‘controversial issue’. This placement is hosted by¿a partner school, with high levels of diversity within the pupil population, affording student-teachers the opportunity to enact culturally responsive pedagogy in practice.
learningOutcomes: Use simulation activities to explore the terms culture, culture shock, privilege and ethnocentrism.
Critically reflect on instances of racism, colour-blindness and micro-aggressions using case studies and real life scenarios as stimulus.
Become familiar with philosophy with children as a methodology for exploring global citizenship education themes in the classroom.
Appreciate the potential of action and activism as mechanism which can empower learners of all ages to assume active roles, both locally and globally, in the fight for social justice.
Infuse a range of active, participatory and experiential methodologies into their plans for teaching and learning.
Respond to the life stories of asylum seekers, Irish Travellers and other minoritized ethnic voices, and reflect on how these stories challenge their prior knowledge ideas and values.
Design a station teaching exercise on a ‘controversial’ global topic in a primary school with a diverse population.

modules.moduleName: CREATIVITY AND COMPUTATIONAL THINKING IN THE CLASSROOM
modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed to align Froebelian teaching and learning philosophies of creativity and playful approaches with computational thinking and constructionist principles within the primary classroom.

This elective focuses on the purposeful design of creative and playful computational thinking and learning experiences using BeeBots and SPIKE Essentials Lego Education materials alongside other suitable resources for the primary classroom. Students actively engage in hands-on experimentation in this module and critically examine construction-oriented learning opportunities. There is an emphasis on reflective learning and practice as students engage in digital portfolio design. The module incorporates readings, classroom best practice videos, workshops and ongoing discussions to illustrate how these experiences align with theory, classroom application rooted in Froebelian philosophies, and the primary curriculum framework. Underpinned by a constructionist learning environment, the module aims to deepen students' understanding of creativity and playful approaches to Computational Thinking (CT) by designing authentic, child-centred learning activities.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: A NEW LENS TO VIEW PLAY THROUGH- EXPLORE HOW CHILD-LED PLAY SUPPORTS SOCIAL & EMOTIONAL DEVELOPMENT & ENHANCES WELLBEING ACROSS ALL OF PRIMARY SCHOOL.
modules.moduleContent:
This module provides opportunities to identify and examine the value of child-led play in enhancing social & emotional development as well as fostering wellbeing in an experiential manner.

Explore how child-led play can be used across all class settings to foster social and emotional development.  Determine how child-led play can be resourced, observed, and assessed. Examine the teacher's role in child-led play. Explore how sensory play supports our 8 sensory systems impacting our overall wellbeing. Learn to identify and understand the ‘language of the nervous system’.  Identify how play supports regulation of the nervous system contributing to our overall wellbeing. Look specifically at ‘ordinary everyday’ interactions to foster emotional balance, resilience, insight and empathy through play. Examine how child-led play can be used to enhance relationships, which are at the core of wellbeing. This module highlights and fosters 5 of the Froebelian Principles of ‘Unity and connectedness’, ‘Autonomous learners’, ‘Relationships matter’,’ The central importance of play’ and ‘Knowledgeable and nurturing educators.’

Students undertake a variety of periods of school placements in this year of the programme. This module allows for practical engagement in setting up and resourcing play areas to nurture creativity and innovation across all class levels. It will also cover observation and assessment of play development.
