Play is advocated for across all class levels in the New Primary Curriculum Framework this course brings insight into the practicalities of how this can be facilitated. 

This module will outline Tina Bruce’s ‘Twelve Features Characterising a Froebelian Approach To Childhood Play.’
learningOutcomes: Use child-led play as a methodology to enhance pupil's relationships with one another thus enhancing their connection and wellbeing
Set up multiple play areas to cater for a variety of interests thus enhancing both creativity and innovation of pupils across all classroom settings.
Appreciate the value of sensory play in supporting regulation.
Understand the role of the nervous system in regulation.
Observe and assess how play supports and fosters social and emotional development.
Aware of ‘ordinary, everyday’ interactions to foster emotional balance, resilience, insight and empathy through play.

modules.moduleName: BIOBEO – THE INTERCONNECTEDNESS OF ALL LIFE
modules.moduleContent:
The BioBeo elective focuses on teaching student teachers how to share education for sustainable development in the classroom. In this elective, student teachers will learn about the idea of a bioeconomy and how to live and teach in a more sustainable manner.

The BioBeo elective will include digital learning, outdoor activities, and outdoor environmental detective work in a participatory research approach and connection to indigenous ways of knowing and crafts through a scientific and folkloric lens. This elective will help to unlock this aspect of our national psyche - our love of the landscape, culture and heritage so often culturally reinforced by native language. Particular emphasis will be placed upon the BioBeo bioeconomy themes, Sustainable Development Goals, The Ethics of Care and the SESE strands of Environmental Awareness and Care and Materials and Change. An awareness of how our words can impact human behaviour with regard to environmental issues such as food waste and biodiversity will be examined. The elective will be conducted through the lens of a Froebelian Ecofeminist philosophy.
learningOutcomes: Examine how bioeconomy is one solution to climate change and learn how to share this in a positive light through nature-based pedagogy in the primary classroom
Understand how communities around the world are making ‘waste’ valuable (valorising waste)
Show an awareness of how our words can impact on human behaviour with regard to environmental issues

Demonstrate an understanding of the application of the BioBeo bioeconomy themes, Sustainable Development Goals, The Ethics of Care and the SESE strands of Environmental Awareness and Care and Materials and Change
Acquire a skill of minimal one traditional craft for use in the primary classroom
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
A significant component of this module is the facilitation of regular systematic, critical reflection in order to allow students to develop, and articulate, their own personal educational philosophy and thus empower their professional identity as primary school teachers. In this way students are expected to engage in the theory and philosophy of education ‘in the making’. Foundation Module 4 will enable students to reflect upon the importance and implications of a commitment to teaching and learning as a way of life including lifelong personal and professional learning.  

In Philosophy of Education issues relating to “professionalisation” and “deprofessionalisation” are explored, particularly in the context of the challenges they potentially present. This module encourages students to understand their school-based experiences over previous placements in the context of a philosophical framework which highlights issues in relation to ethical behaviours, children’s rights and the nature of education. 
 
In History of Education, students will examine current issues in Education from an historical perspective. Recent legislation since 1990 which affects primary teacher education will be examined and its impact analysed. Research informed exploration of the impact of Douglas Hyde and the Gaelic League on the revival of the¿Irish language through the education system will be integral to the module.¿This module investigates the significance of Scéim na Scol, wherein students will actively participate in the examination of the folklore collection housed within the National Folklore Collection Ireland. This scholarly pursuit aims to augment their comprehension of the historical evolution of the Irish education system. 

 
This module critically examines the establishment and development of care and education for children with SEN in Ireland from the 1800s up to the turn of the millenia.  The history of education for children with Special Educational Needs is analysed through the lens of wider contextual developments, including political, socio-economic, cultural and religious factors. It encourages students (in light of the historical context) to critically analyse teacher identity and the current education system which emphasises citizenship with a democratic responsibility to each other, children’s rights and children’s voice.
learningOutcomes: Think sympathetically and critically about Inclusive Education and Global Citizenship Education.
To recognise a commitment to education as a democratic process.

Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the unique role of the teacher as situated practitioner in providing for a holistic development of student and the complex and intricate nature of teaching with reference to the Teaching Council’s Code of Professional Conduct for Teachers (2016) and teacher identity.
Recognise a commitment to education as ethical, rational, caring defensible, sustaining and valuable.

Critically integrate research and theoretical perspectives from all the foundation disciplines into placement learning by reflecting on central educational issues and supporting professional judgements and actions with convincing argument.
Utilise students’ digital skillset in order to engage in a folklore collection project with their future primary school community.
Think critically, analyse, and solve problems as an individual and as part of a team and through a pedagogy of community engaged learning.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: In line with the principle of facilitating quality teaching and learning for all children, student teachers are supported in understanding, developing and using a wide range of assessment strategies to support, monitor and assess pupils’ approaches to learning and their progress – including effective feedback techniques.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the unique role of the Froebelian student teacher as professional in providing for the holistic development of pupils, particularly through differentiated approaches
Show an understanding of the theory, concepts and methods pertaining to effective teaching, learning and assessment, both summative and formative

Use a range of strategies and methodologies including new and emerging technologies for teaching and learning to support, monitor and assess pupils’ approach to learning and their progress – including effective feedback strategies
Conduct a systematic, holistic assessment of pupil needs
Collaborate with Special Education Teachers by referring pupils for specialised educational support as required and participating in the provision of that support, as appropriate

Apply knowledge of the individual potential of pupils, dispositions towards learning, varying backgrounds, identities, experiences and learning styles to planning for teaching, learning and assessment
Contribute to effective school self-evaluation and planning

modules.moduleContent: Student teachers will develop competency and confidence regarding relevant mathematical concepts, skills, and terminology. The module supports student-teachers’ engagement with mathematics methodology through their development of relational understanding of mathematics which can be applied across the curriculum. Students will engage in reflective practice utilising their developing digital capacity. This development of competency and professional growth will promote professional agency to enact quality teaching and learning for all pupils.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate and develop competency, accuracy, and confidence in Mathematics relevant for primary school teaching.
Demonstrate the ability to reflect on their mathematical knowledge and how it can be integrated with relevant theories of education to inform their professional practice.
Develop their understanding and capacity to confidently lead mathematics learning in their professional role.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: THE TEACHING OF READING IN THE PRIMARY SCHOOL
modules.moduleContent:
The module will examine a variety of approaches to teaching reading and how these approaches draw on the science of reading. It will explore how to assess pupils’ development in reading and how to choose effective teaching pedagogies based on this assessment to cater for pupils with differing needs.

Current national and international research in the teaching of reading will be examined. Changes in national literacy policy will be outlined.

Case studies will be used to develop strategies for supporting pupils with reading difficulties. Digital literacy will be explored to support the teaching of reading.

This module will prepare student teachers for teaching, learning, reflective practices, and assessment of reading in primary schools.
learningOutcomes: Conduct an assessment of a child’s reading ability
Collect, record and analyse information about reading development
Critique current important issues relating to the teaching of reading in the primary school
Examine national literacy policy and its impact on practice
Plan effective strategies in the teaching of reading for children with differing needs
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module builds on previous lectures on dyslexia in the EDF228 module, ‘Including Every Child’. It provides a comprehensive and detailed level of knowledge and understanding of dyslexia. It encourages a strengths-based approach and equips prospective Froebelian teachers with the confidence and skills to identify and support students at an individual level, within the classroom and at a whole-school level. It promotes the early recognition of possible reading difficulties and examines best practice approaches to inclusive teaching for students with dyslexia across the age range.

This module interrogates theory and best practice for neurodiverse children with dyslexia. Student teachers have the opportunity to learn from the experiences of neurodiverse people with dyslexia. There is an emphasis placed on the emotional learning environment and the importance of relationships for children with dyslexia. Assistive Technology and the Wilson language programmes and other curricula approaches are explored.
learningOutcomes: Build positive relationships with all pupils, particularly neurodiverse pupils and those with dyslexia.
Create inclusive environments where neurodiverse pupils feel accepted and valued for who they are.
Embed inclusive, flexible and responsive teaching strategies for the neurodiverse learner with dyslexia.
Develop agency within the neurodiverse learner.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: THE USE OF JUMPSTARTING LITERACY AND NUMERACY STRATEGIES TO SUPPORT LEARNING IN THE MODERN CLASSROOM
modules.moduleContent:
This module looks at the exciting benefits of using the Jumpstarting Literacy and Numeracy strategies, particularly mental imagery, for all children in the modern classroom. It explores how anxiety affects both teacher’s & children’s performance and looks at simple and effective strategies to overcome it. It looks at how these strategies help create a calm and focused learning environment.

It also looks at how Dyslexia and Dyscalculia affects children’s learning. It explains how to explore the challenges around learning difficulties and how mental imagery can assist in overcoming, or at least minimising their effects, by using strategies that complement what is already being used, rather than replacing teaching methods that are already working.
learningOutcomes: Use Jumpstarting Literacy and Numeracy strategies to boost children’s learning in the areas of spelling, reading comprehension, multiplication, division & problem solving.
Have an understanding of the effects of anxiety on children’s learning and to use strategies to control it.
Use grounding to create a calm learning environment
Assess left or right eye dominance
Use positive language to boost confidence & self-esteem.
Develop strategies to plan S.E.T. for children with Specific Learning Difficulties as both a mainstream and Special Education Teacher.

modules.moduleName: LANGUAGE, LITERACY, MATHEMATICS AND SCIENTIFIC ENQUIRY
modules.moduleContent:
This module addresses two distinct components in the primary classroom i.e. 1) language, literature and literacy and 2) mathematics and scientific enquiry. 

In relation to language and literacy, it addresses the processes and experiences involved in the teaching and learning of languages, literacy practices and the socio-cultural dimensions of literature. It provides students with an overview of oral, reading and writing systems of communication and the pedagogical and literary approaches to children's linguistic engagement and development, with particular focus on English and Irish. 

This bilingual module will encompass the cognate subject areas of English methodologies, Children's Literature, Modhanna Múinte, Litríocht na Gaeilge do Pháistí and Language Development.

In English Methodologies, emergent literacy will be studied in depth with particular emphasis on play as a methodology. Students are introduced to the definitions of reading, the principles underpinning highly effective literacy pedagogy, stages in the teaching of reading and writing in addition to practical activities which promote language enrichment and development. 
In English literature, students will explore a range of literary genres and multimedia narratives as well as these texts' potential for supporting and stimulating learning environments and creative opportunities for young people.

The communicative approach to the teaching of additional languages and associated methodologies in the primary school classroom will be explored. The focus will be on methodologies that can be used to develop pupils’ listening, speaking, reading and writing in the Irish language in primary school. Current theory and best practices will be explored. 

In the mathematics and scientific enquiry component of this module, students are introduced to theories of emergent numeracy, number acquisition, mathematical and scientific learning, content knowledge, methodologies, skills and resources appropriate to all classes across the spectrum in primary schools. Current mathematics and science curriculum documents will be explicated, discussed and experienced in hands-on workshops in order that students gain an insight into the concepts, skills and practical methodologies required for teaching and learning in these subjects. 

The module addresses the creation of an enquiry-based and independent learning environment involving the utilisation of the indoor and outdoor environments. Linkage and integration across the curriculum, particularly through project work is also explored. Emphasis is placed on education for sustainable development and greater sustainable practices. There is demonstratable integration of environmentally sustainable practice in teaching and learning rooted in the principle of care for others and the planet throughout the module.

Additionally, this module addresses methods of children recording their work, documentation and assessment of children’s work including self and peer evaluation. Students will investigate the appropriate uses of ICT regarding the teaching and learning of language, literature and literacy , mathematics, and scientific enquiry.
learningOutcomes: Reflect, identify, plan, and apply appropriate classroom knowledge, values, methodologies, and skills creatively to support effective teaching and learning across languages, literature, mathematics, and science education.
Develop a creative emergent curriculum to Integrate languages, literature, mathematics, and science concepts and skills across the curriculum informed by Froebelian philosophy.
Critically engage with and evaluate a variety of literature genres and texts in English and Gaeilge, and their potential for cross-curricular use in the primary classroom.

Apply an understanding and critical engagement with relevant national primary-school curriculum documents including Aistear: The  Early Childhood Curriculum Framework (NCCA, 2009) and relevant policies.
Demonstrate the content, concepts, pedagogical knowledge, terminology, and resources appropriate to languages, literacy, mathematics, numeracy, and science education.
Demonstrate an ability to create and utilise an enquiry-based environment, indoors and outdoors.
Utilise a range of methodologies and assessment strategies, including the use of ICT and digital skills, relevant to these disciplines and curricular areas.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module has four main elements:
Element 1: Placement Learning to prepare students for an extended 10-week placement
Element 2: Inclusive Education with a specific focus on SEN placement
Element 3: Trauma Informed Practice for Teachers in Schools and Communities.
Element 4: Formative and Summative Assessment Practices

Element 1: Placement Learning
School placement is a central feature of the B Ed initial teacher education programme. 
This module is designed to prepare and equip student teachers for an extended 10-week placement spanning Semester 1 and Semester 2 with a focus on a fully integrated, Froebelian approach to teaching and learning. School Placement in Year 4 consists of the following elements: 
1: Teaching: Special Educational Needs (4 weeks) 
2: Teaching: Mainstream (4 weeks) 
3: School experience (2 weeks). 

The concept of Teacher as Professional will be explored in depth through a series of guest lectures in Semester 2 from a variety of education partners such as INTO, DES, Management Bodies, IPPN, NPC, Teaching Council and NIPT. Professional relationships will be explored, including how best to work with parents for the benefit of children. The roles of stakeholders and partners, and the importance of engagement and cooperation with them, will be explored. Some aspects of a teacher’s work from a legal perspective will be explored. A principal of a local school will present on the legal and professional aspects of a teacher’s position and responsibilities in relation to his/her pupils.


Element 2: Inclusive Education with a specific focus on SEN placement
  
A key focus of the module is the creation of an inclusive Froebelian environment. The module examines the role of nurturing relationship including the role of parents as partners in the education system. With regard to Inclusive Education, key concepts underpinning educational provision for neurodiverse pupils are examined.  This element of the module specifically prepares students for the SEN element of their 10-week Extended School Placement.


Element 3: Trauma Informed Practice for Teachers in Schools and Communities

IMPORTANT NOTE
all elements of EDF601 must be passed in order to progress to EDF620

On this element of the module students will understand the principles of a trauma-informed approach to supporting traumatised children in their care.  Using the principles of Froebel, students will look at where the child is developmentally and what they are able to do (using a strengths-based approach).  Students will use acceptance and understanding of the unique stage of the child and build on this.  Students will explore how contextual factors in the lives of children impact on capacity to learn, school life and participation. 

Element 4 Formative and Summative Assessment Practices
In line with the principle of facilitating quality teaching and learning for all children, student teachers are supported in understanding, developing and using a range of assessment strategies to support, monitor and assess pupils’ approaches to learning and their progress – including effective feedback techniques. Students will have the opportunity to examine assessment strategies, both summative and formative.
learningOutcomes: Effectively manage and organise teaching and learning in the classroom.
Develop effective communication and relationship-building skills.
Apply subject and pedagogical knowledge to plan, teach, and assess and reflect on their teaching and learning.
Develop their own identity as a professional teacher in the classroom.
Provide for the holistic development of the pupils, particularly through differentiated approaches and appropriate assessment for learning and differentiation strategies.
Plan for coherent, Froebelian teaching informed by ongoing reflection on professional practice.
Observe, plan, teach, and evaluate lessons in a safe, caring and Froebelian environment on School Placement while observing The Code of Professional Conduct for Teachers (Teaching Council, 2016).

modules.moduleContent:
The objective of this module is twofold: to prepare participants to build on their experience of teaching R.E. on School Placement in Year 1 and, to enable them to advance their knowledge and appreciation of religion as a global phenomenon and of the search for meaning as a universal facet of the human person.

Two complementary constituents form the basis of this module, as in Year I. The pedagogical element extends participants’ teaching experience to the design and delivery of a longer series of lessons. Secondly, participants’ familiarity with the range of R.E. syllabuses currently in use in primary schools in Ireland is enhanced and deepened. Under the rubric of subject knowledge, participants are introduced to the academic fields of faith and belief, the question of God, global humanity’s search for meaning, and the relationship between faith and science.
learningOutcomes: Implement and amplify professional agency and creativity by designing and delivering a longer series of lessons on School Placement from one or more of the various R.E. syllabuses in use in Irish primary schools.
Show an increased awareness and appreciation of the place of inclusive education in the various R.E. syllabi.

Demonstrate an enhanced understanding of the professional identity of the teacher in providing for the holistic development of children, particularly through the nurturing of their spiritual and religious dimensions.
Deepen the process of reflective practice as a teacher by applying their skills of reflecting on and evaluating in greater depth the lessons they will have taught.

Evince advanced literacy skills by preparing and presenting an academic paper based on a specific topic in one of the following areas of research: the relationship between religion and science; faith and belief; the universal search for meaning; and the question of God.
Refine further their digital skills by including a digital presentation to accompany their paper.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Teacher enquiry is the systematic, intentional study of one's own professional practice, which is a key Froebelian principle. This module will explore the development of understandings of action research in its many forms within educational settings. The module introduces students to the concept of educational enquiry and, in particular, the principles and practices of action research. It is designed to provide students with a critical awareness of the central theories, philosophies, approaches and concepts employed in educational research. This will provide a foundation for their understanding and application of practitioner action research and the dialectical relationships between policy, pedagogy and practice. Students will be introduced to the range of philosophical and theoretical ideas and debates underpinning educational and social science research, including exploring in some depth the key technical, philosophical and theoretical concepts and terms used in current research reports and debates, and the ethical and political dimensions of research. The relationships between theory and practice will be explored along with the nature of knowledge, evidence-informed and evidence-based research. The module will allow students to investigate their own values, norms and beliefs around research. Case studies of Action Research projects within a variety of contexts will be examined. The policy environment will also be examined to provide an awareness of the wider environment in which schools operate. The implications of Froebelian principles in modern contexts will be explored.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the development of the tradition of action research and investigate the relevance of Froebelian principles in the context of action research.
Identify and address the contextual, ethical and quality issues in the planning and carrying out of action research.
Critically reflect on the process of practitioner research.
Demonstrate an ability to apply different paradigms of research inquiry.
Develop an understanding of the political and policy contexts in which schools exist.
Demonstrate an understanding of practical, political and theoretical issues posed by action-orientated research in a variety of settings.
Develop a theoretical stance towards action research and related approaches.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to explore the implications of students’ action research investigations on their own practice and how it can inform local and wider education communities. Professional learning communities of practice will be nurtured in this module.  Opportunities for sharing learning through collaborative learning communities will be explored. Promotion of the concept of professional collaboration through Teaching Council policy and initiatives such as Cosán will be investigated. A range of formats for sharing findings will serve to optimise the level of engagement to ensure a thorough interrogation of the process of research and consider future steps for the improvement of their own practice. The significance of sharing new learning in terms of influencing self, educational settings and policy will be emphasised. Linkages to relevant professional learning networks will be developed. 
This module brings together these ideas and provides students with a platform to consider the implications of their work within educational settings. Researchers’ professional agency in educational contexts will also be explored.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the variety and importance of forms of evidence in action research.
Develop an understanding of professional collaboration in educational settings.
Develop an awareness of the affordances and constraints of professional agency in educational contexts.
Critically reflect on the process of engaging in practitioner-based research and identify the challenges associated with analysis of findings.
Interrogate the implications of data findings with the developed knowledge of the policy environment associated with educational settings.
Develop an understanding of the complexity of contexts in which action research takes place and consider the implications of such complexities in relation to findings.
Critique and analyse the findings of other Action Research projects designed by course participants and create a community of criticality as part of the process.

modules.moduleContent:
The objective of this module is to build on the prior placement experience of student teachers in PME 1 (and BSc.Ed years 2-4) by providing an extended and deeper range of experiences and expectations. It develops student teachers’ capacity to plan for units of study across longer timeframes in their teaching subjects and to engage in more complex teaching and assessment approaches in a structured, incremental, supportive and challenging way. A particular objective will be to develop student teachers’ capacity to focus on and cater for the individual and differentiated needs of students in their classrooms. Student teachers’ capacity as researchers will be developed through professional research projects and through scaffolded opportunities to reflect on their growing professional identity. In addition to the direct teaching of classes, student teachers will learn about and contribute to the wider life of the school and experience the rhythm of school life by engaging in wider school activities as detailed in the Teaching Council’s School Placement Guidelines (2013). This module will act as a fulcrum for the programme, providing a context for conceptual, practical, professional and conceptual aspects of the PME to inform one another. It will support the realisation of the Department’s Ethical-Relational Conceptual Framework for Initial Teacher Education, integrating the Personal; the Social-Political; the Cultural; and the Professional to a greater extent and ensuring student teachers are ready to engage in the next stage of their professional journey with Droichead post-qualification.
Following a short period of structured observation and gaining familiarity with the new school context, student teachers will undertake placement for 3 days each week in their placement school teaching an increased number of placement classes and deepening their engagement in the wider professional work of the school. Each student teacher will be expected to collaborate closely with one or more treoirithe. Opportunities for discussion about observations, teaching and school-based activities will be provided at university-based tutorials. Student teachers are expected to behave professionally both inside and outside the school, in line with the Teaching Council’s Code of Professional Conduct for Teachers (Teaching Council, 2016).
learningOutcomes: Plan coherent and integrated units of study (curriculum plans) that reflect their subject knowledge, pedagogical content knowledge and related methodologies for a wide diversity of learners (both alone and for team teaching contexts)
Teach planned content in an appropriate manner using effective, creative and imaginative strategies that promotes individual and shared learning

Apply knowledge of the individual potential of pupils (e.g., their disposition towards learning, their varying backgrounds, identities, experiences and learning styles) to plan for teaching, learning and assessment (both formative and summative)

Develop strategies for the promotion of positive relationships and communicating effectively with pupils, parents, colleagues, the school principal, school management, co-professionals and the wider community
Receive, reflect on and respond to feedback on their practice from treoirithe, tutors and others
Engage in research on their own practice that informs their professional growth

Demonstrate a commitment to meeting wider professional responsibilities in terms of critical reflection, recording, professional engagement with colleagues and contribution to school and university life

modules.moduleContent: The Teaching Lab is a weekly micro-teaching assignment for students. It is called a ‘Lab’ to conjure up the idea of an experimental space for student teachers to try out ideas. Working in small teams the Teaching lab process takes students through the planning, delivery and critique of lessons in two cycles while other group members participate in the dual roles of ‘pupils’ and observers. Success in the Teaching Lab is based on the quality of students’ critical reflection on the process as demonstrated through a team-based oral presentation. Students build on detailed formative feedback from Cycle 1 to repeat the process in Cycle 2 with a summative assessment.
learningOutcomes: Present clear, comprehensive and original analysis of insights gained through the Teaching Lab process
Demonstrate evidence of having critically reflected on the challenges faced in the Teaching Lab process, how these were addressed and overcome
Identify, evaluate and present key learning moments in relation to teaching, learning and assessment with particular reference to planning, delivery and reflection
Support communications with references to relevant literature in the areas of teaching, learning and assessment
Demonstrate evidence of team-work
Employ advanced communication skills.

modules.moduleName: BECOMING A TEACHER 1 (POLICY, CURRICULUM AND LAW)
modules.moduleContent:
The objective of this module is to support students to be more critically aware of the key policy, curriculum and legislative frameworks informing their work as teachers and the wider education system. It links strongly with the key elements of the Conceptual Framework for ITE by situating the teacher’s personal and professional role and identity in the wider social, political and cultural context of their work.
The module will comprise inputs and discussions on key curriculum, legislative and policy frameworks and discourses (both national and international) that inform the current education system in Ireland. A particular focus will be placed on the close reading and interrogation of key legislative, policy and curriculum documents in the Irish context. This will be framed both theoretically and conceptually within the extant literature. Policy makers from key departments and agencies will be invited as guest lecturers to illuminate students’ understanding of the curriculum and policy development process.
learningOutcomes: Articulate an understanding of education policy and its relevance to their work as teachers
Analyse national and international influences on education and curriculum policy development in Ireland
Document policy and legislative journeys of key educational issues relevant to their professional practice
Describe the dynamic processes by which curriculum is designed and enacted with reference to curriculum theory
Critically interrogate the process of curriculum development, design, implementation, assessment and evaluation
Explore key dimensions of the professional context for assessing students with an emphasis on inclusion, diversity, equity, validity and reliability.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the discipline of Psychology of Education. Throughout the course students will be encouraged to develop their critical reflective skills, and to bring a psychological perspective to the experiences they encounter in their school-based practice.

Students will be encouraged to understand key educational issues from different psychological perspectives. The module aims to cover key learning theories including constructivist theories (e.g., the work of Piaget, Vygotsky and Bruner), behavioural approaches to learning, information processing theories (including memory and attention), metacognition and motivation. The module also covers topics related to the physical, cognitive, and socio-emotional development of adolescents. Topics will include selfhood and identity, puberty, cognitive development, peer relationships, student-teacher relationships, childhood adversity, resilience and flourishing. Topics will be contextualised within contemporary developments nationally and internationally.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate various theories of learning and motivation
Explain how theories can be applied in classrooms to facilitate learning and promote positive behaviour.
Discuss the key developmental transitions of adolescence and apply knowledge to promote the holistic development of young people
Describe identity development in adolescence and the role of teachers in facilitating a positive sense of self.
Describe research in the area of peer relations and bullying and explore their implications for school and classroom practices.
Describe major determinants of psychological distress in young people and discuss the school related factors that promote a sense of safety, belonging and trust.
Begin to critically interrogate applications of theory to practice in real contextS.

modules.moduleContent:
The objective of this module is to explore the key contextual factors and milestones that have shaped the current education system in Ireland. Using an inquiry-based and thematic approach, students will explore the historical policy and legislative documents that underpinned the education system and reflect on the impact of our history on current educational provisions. The module cross references relevant international developments to facilitate comparative analyses.

The module is designed to encourage students to develop a holistic view of education as a practice and to enable them to understand the current education research, policy and practice landscape in its historical context. Challenging students’ own assumptions and beliefs and supporting them to articulate their own stances and perspectives across a range of issues (such as the purposes of education, patronage and management, equality, inclusion, democracy, gender and assessment) are core to the module. Extensive discussion on and critique of key readings, theories and historical documentation are central features of the inquiry-based approach of the module.
learningOutcomes: Critically understand the key milestones and contextual factors in the historical evolution of the education system in Ireland
Construct and sustain critical and coherently reasoned arguments about key issues that have shaped Irish education
Reflect on and communicate the impact of this historical evolution on the current system of education and their professional practice as student teachers
Display an ability to draw on a diversity of considered viewpoints and marshal convincing interpretations from across a range of source evidence
Critically engage with the broad range of commission reports, policy statements and legislative documents pertinent to the study of the education system in Ireland.

modules.moduleContent:
Extending knowledge gleaned in Global Citizenship-Teaching with Cultural Awareness, this module aims to provide an overview of broader issues of social justice education, offering students different perspectives on and conceptualisations of social justice and providing opportunities for connecting these to their role and identities as teaching professionals.
The module focuses on contemporary issues in social justice education, including social equality, inclusion, human rights, student voice, climate justice and sustainability.  In particular, the module focuses on viewing social justice through the relationships we have to others and explores the purpose of social justice education for students’ professional practice. Curriculum and pedagogical strategies that can contribute to informing a social justice framework in teaching practice will also be explored and integrated with the theoretical readings.
learningOutcomes: identify and critically engage with the range of concepts, theories and research in education dealing with human rights, social justice and sustainability
convey the purpose of social justice education and justify it in relation to their own teaching and professional development
apply curricular and pedagogical strategies emanating from these concepts and theories to their educational settings and reflect on how to relate to students through socially just practices
critically locate their practice within a social justice framework.

modules.moduleContent:
This module offers students an opportunity to expand their knowledge of the curricula and their subject area specifications, syllabi and assessments at both junior cycle and senior cycle levels. It will enable them to develop an understanding of the theoretical underpinnings of active approaches to teaching and learning and their application to their practice in real contexts. Students will explore ways to apply a cross-curricular approach to their lessons through, for example, Development Education, Content and Language Integrated Learning (CLIL), Science Technology and Engineering, Arts and Maths. (STEAM). Through individual and group based work it will advance their pedagogical content knowledge and their knowledge of the range of methodologies applicable to their subject area(s), developing their understandings of what constitutes and contributes to high quality teaching, learning and assessment. 
The module focuses on:  
• How to plan lessons and units of work that are appropriate to the needs and interests of learners and curricular demands, 
• How to integrate innovative methodologies and a range of resources for differentiated learning, progression, formative and summative assessment
• How to integrate theories of active learning and teaching into practice
• How to integrate theories and tenets of cross-curricular approaches into teaching, learning and assessment practice
• How to access, adapt, develop, create and use resources and supports for their subject area to scaffold and challenge learners
• How to participate in communities of practice to develop, critically analyse and integrate new pedagogical insights into practice
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a deep understanding of current national curricula and their subject area specifications, syllabi and assessments contextualised in an awareness of curriculum requirements in preceding and subsequent stages of learning
Exemplify a critical knowledge of the theories and tenets underlying cross curricular approaches, including knowledge of the associated current curricular and assessment policy context
Apply their knowledge of the subject matter, pedagogical content and related methodologies of the relevant curricula/specifications and guidelines to planning for teaching, learning and assessment
Integrate relevant principles and theories of education, in the context of the relevant curriculum/syllabus, using well-developed skills of enquiry, to inform their professional practice
Access, develop, adapt and use a variety of curriculum resources and materials for teaching, learning and assessment in their subject area(s) to support and challenge all pupils

Set clear, challenging and achievable expectations for all pupils in line with the curriculum and use a range of strategies to support, monitor and assess pupils’ needs, their approach to learning and their progress

Conduct and apply relevant research as appropriate to their teaching context, identifying, critically analysing and integrating new knowledge regarding curriculum, pedagogy and assessment into their practice
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

• How to plan lessons and units of work that are appropriate to the needs and interests of learners and curricular demands, 
• How to integrate innovative methodologies and a range of resources for differentiated learning, progression, formative and summative assessment
• How to integrate theories of active learning and teaching into practice
• How to integrate theories and tenets of cross-curricular approaches into teaching, learning and assessment practice
• How to access, adapt, develop, create and use resources and supports for their subject area to scaffold and challenge learners
• How to participate in communities of practice to develop, critically analyse and integrate new pedagogical insights into practice
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a deep understanding of current national curricula and their subject area specifications, syllabi and assessments contextualised in an awareness of curriculum requirements in preceding and subsequent stages of learning
Exemplify a critical knowledge of the theories and tenets underlying cross curricular approaches, including knowledge of the associated current curricular and assessment policy context.
Apply their knowledge of the subject matter, pedagogical content and related methodologies of the relevant curricula/specifications and guidelines to planning for teaching, learning and assessment
Integrate relevant principles and theories of education, in the context of the relevant curriculum/syllabus, using well-developed skills of enquiry, to inform their professional practice
Access, develop, adapt and use a variety of curriculum resources and materials for teaching, learning and assessment in their subject area(s) to support and challenge all pupils

Set clear, challenging and achievable expectations for all pupils in line with the curriculum and use a range of strategies to support, monitor and assess pupils’ needs, their approach to learning and their progress

Conduct and apply relevant research as appropriate to their teaching context, identifying, critically analysing and integrating new knowledge regarding curriculum, pedagogy and assessment into their practice

modules.moduleContent:
Each student will pick a technology area (some adjustment of this may be necessary to ensure a roughly even spread across technology areas) from the following: Wind energy (micro or commercial),Small hydro, Solar PV, Solar thermal, Heat pumps.

Each student will complete the following project components:
• Resource quantification
• Design and specification of renewable energy installation
• Calculation of likely annual energy output
• Approximate economic analysis of project
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The primary goal of this introductory Python course is to provide students with a solid foundation in Python programming and enable them to use it for various applications in the future. 

Indicative Syllabus:

• Introduction to computer architecture and the main components of a computer system
• Introduction to algorithm design and pseudocode
• Python basic data types and variables
• Operators and expressions
• Basic input/output
• Booleans and comparisons
• Conditional statements (if/else)
• Loops (for/while)
• Break, continue, and pass statements
• Python data structures (e.g. lists, tuples, dictionaries and sets)
• Calling functions that someone else has defined
• Writing your own functions
• Working with files (reading/writing)
• Error checking and Exception handling

Full module descriptor at https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: Explain the basics of programming. Students should be able to explain and make use of fundamental concepts like variables, data types, conditional statements, loops, and functions.
Evaluate basic python programs and algorithms.
Write python programs that solve simple problems.
Execute and debug basic python programs.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This is designed for students who have taken an introductory python module and aims to further develop programming skills applicable to a range of problems, both reinforcing the use of fundamental python features and introducing more advanced python features and application programming interfaces. The latter part of the module introduces students to the Java language.

Indicative syllabus
• Write programmes that solve problems by harnessing the power of predefined python modules/libraries (e.g. NumPy, SciPy, and Matplotlib)
• Basic principles of objected oriented programming
• Classes and objects
• Methods and attributes, class scope, and object (instance) scope
• Inheritance and overriding methods
• Compare Python and Java (similarities and differences) and learn which language to use in different situations
• Implement basic object-oriented programmes in Python and Java
learningOutcomes: Apply problem solving techniques to programming problems
Explain, develop, and debug python programmes that use python library/module features
Explain, develop, and debug python programmes using object-oriented programming features
Write and execute basic programmes in Java (a compiled object-oriented programming language)
Solve programming problems using appropriate object-oriented programming techniques in Python or Java.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
· To promote project based learning in the field of robotics and control. 
· To re-introduce students to structured engineering design. 
· To instill the creative spirit in students.
 · To develop oral and written communication skills
 · To develop students’ experience of working in a group
 · To engender an awareness of ethical issues in engineering
learningOutcomes: Apply project-based learning to solve unforeseen problems in the field of robotics and control.
Apply structured design to a range of problems.
Apply theoretical knowledge in solving problems encountered.
Discuss any ethical issues, environmental impacts and health and safety issues associated with their project.
Write a technical report.
Prepare and deliver an oral presentation.
Defend their work through interview.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to gain a general overview of the principles of telecommunications engineering.
A more detailed module descriptor can be found on the following address: https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
The indicative syllabus is as follows:
* Overview of communications
* Use of communication systems in the real world (mobile phone, satellite television, etc)
* The nature of data, analogue/digital, bandwidth requirements – real-time versus non-real-time communications (e.g. phone vs email)
* Communication signals – electrical, optical, quantum, electromagnetic (radio)
* Communication concepts
* Basic communication concepts 
* Transmitter, receiver, channel, additive noise
* Bit rate, power, the dB and dBm – amplification/attenuation/SNR/Noise figures
* Point-to-point vs broadcast systems
* Channels properties and utility –  co-axial cable, twisted pair, optical fibre, air interface
* Signals and noise
* Communications by means of signals – definition of a signal
* Signal characterization in the time and frequency domain
* Analogue to digital conversion of signals
* Noise definition – noise margin, thermal noise, white noise, Friis equation
* Analogue and digital modulation (and demodulation)
* The need for modulation, introduction to basic analogue modulation types (AM/FM)
* Introduction to basic baseband and band-pass digital modulation
* Study of different types of AM ¬¬– time domain and frequency domain
* AM transmitter and receiver architectures
* Communication limits and channel multiplexing
* Power, noise and channel capacity
* Spectrum as a limited resource – spectrum allocation issues
* Channel multiplexing – TDM, FDM, spatial multiplexing (GSM use case example)
* Communication networks and ethical issues
* Communication network topologies – pros/cons, packet concept, congestion concept
* Ethical issues related to telecommunications – carbon footprint, security, health
learningOutcomes: Describe the major components of a telecommunication system.
Calculate the basic parameters of signal, channel and system in time and frequency domain (having introduced the FFT).
Analyse the system impairments, quantify their effects and introduce the concept of error correction.
Describe the different analog and digital modulation techniques.
Design a basic communication system either using analog or digital modulation technique.
Calculate basic communication link budgets considering signal power, noise power, channel attenuation and data rate.
Describe techniques for sharing resources within a communication system.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Indicative Syllabus
• Differential equations
• Review of Basic Differentiation techniques and an introduction to modelling.
• Second-order differential equations
• Linear constant coefficient equations. Solution using method of variation of parameters and method of undetermined coefficients.
• Application to RLC series circuit and spring-mass systems.
• Higher order linear differential equations.
• Laplace Transform
• Introduction to the Laplace transform. Idea of a linear operator. Rationale for solution method for differential equations.
• Inverse Laplace Transform and its relation to the solution to a differential equation.
• Laplace transforms of the elementary functions.
• Shifting theorems: s-shifting and t-shifting.
• Applying the Laplace transform to solving first and second-order differential equations with elementary forcing functions.
• Introduction to the unit step function and its use in modelling switching functions.
• Introduction to the Dirac delta function and its use in modelling impulsive functions.
• Applying the Laplace transform to solving first and second-order differential equations with non-trivial forcing functions (switching, impulsive, periodic).
• Laplace transform of periodic functions (square waves, triangular waves) and solving differential equations with forcing functions of this type.
• Laplace transform of integrals.
• Convolution theorem. Solving integral equations using the convolution theorem and their relation to time-delay systems.
• Fourier Series
• Introduction to Fourier series and its importance in applications.
• Derivation of the Fourier coefficients.
• Calculation of Fourier series for several types of periodic function.
• Development and motivation of the Fourier transform.
• Calculation of the Fourier Transform for various and special functions 
• Relation of the Fourier transform to signal analysis.
• Z-Transform
• Introduction to simple difference equations. Idea that solution is a sequence. 
• Motivational examples of how difference equations arise in practice.
• Solution techniques for simple linear first and second order difference equations.
• Introduction to discrete transforms and specifically the Z-transform.
• Calculation of the Z-transform of certain special sequences. (Unit Step sequence, Unit Impulse sequence)
• Using the Z-transform to solve difference equations and the analogy between this method and the use of the Laplace Transform.
learningOutcomes:
Upon completion of this module the student should be able to:
Find solutions to linear second-order ODEs and apply solution techniques to simple physical models
Use the Laplace Transform to solve simple ODEs with elementary forcing function terms
Formulate first and second-order differential equations for physical problems involving switching/impulsive/periodic forcing functions
Solve integral equations using Convolution theorem and the Laplace transform.
Obtain Fourier series representations (trigonometric & exponential) of general periodic functions.
Obtain Fourier transforms of arbitrary and special functions.
Solve simple linear difference equations using elementary techniques and using the Z-transform approach.

modules.moduleContent:
· To give a detailed introduction to signal analysis techniques. 
· To develop from first principles the theory for the analysis and design of discrete-time signals and systems.

· Introduction: Signals, Systems, Linear Systems, Time-Invariant Systems, Continuous-time signals and systems, Discrete-time signals and systems, Fourier Series representation of signals and systems.
· Continuous-time signals and systems: The impulse function, properties of the impulse function, Fourier transform of the impulse function, impulse response and frequency response of continuous-time systems, convolution, the sifting integral, the Laplace transform, pole-zero models.
· Discrete-time signals and systems: Sampling, uniform and non-uniform quantization, Linear difference equations, frequency response of discrete-time systems, discrete-time convolution and unit sample response, sampled bandpass signals. Intro to digital filters.
· The Z-transform: Convergence of the ZT, properties of the ZT, Applications of the ZT.
· The Fourier transform (FT): The discrete-time FT (DTFT), properties of the DTFT, Parseval's Theorem, Sampled signal spectrum, Repeat spectra, Alias, Periodogram.
· Random Signals: Types of noise, Frequency-response estimation using noise. Random variables, Probability Density Function (PDF), Random processes. Spectral estimation. The periodogram. Bartlett's method.

Module Descriptor: https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: Represent and analyse signals in the time and frequency domains.
Represent and analyse linear, time-invariant systems in the time and frequency domains.
Perform continuous-time and discrete-time convolution of signals.

Compute the continuous-time and discrete-time Fourier transform of signals and systems.
Explain the fundamentals of sampling theory, alias and repeat spectra.
Compute the Z-transform of discrete-time signals and systems.
Compute the discrete Fourier transform of discrete-time signals and systems.

Determine the frequency-response of a system using a white noise input.
Compute the periodgram of a signal.
Analyse the frequency content of a signal using Bartlett's method.
Work as part of a team and learn through collaboration.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
To introduce students to the design issues of real time and embedded systems.

· Introduction to embedded systems
· Basic microcontroller features and development environment characteristics
· Interfacing and Input/Output
· Communication
· Real time applications
· Processor technologies and system architecture
· Requirements, specification and design choices
· Reliability, fault tolerance, health, safety and ethical considerations
· Non-microcontroller differences (e.g. memory and addressing)

See full module descriptor at http://www.nuim.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: Analyse embedded systems requirements and select appropriate technologies based on factors including cost.
Identify the ethical considerations of embedded systems, particularly in relation to fault handling and safety.
Design simple embedded systems.
Implement and test embedded applications.
Implement and test superloop-based real-time embedded applications.

modules.moduleContent:
To introduce Verilog HDL language in the field of digital system design. 
To enable the students to undertake a design project in this field using Verilog.
learningOutcomes: 1. Elucidate the critical issues in the design and testing of a digital logic silicon chip.
2. Interpret a functional design specification
3. Devise a design using Verilog.
4. Implement and validate the performance of a digital system using Verilog.
5. Work as a team in terms of project planning, review and implementation to deliver a working digital system using Verilog.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
To introduce students to the skills associated with professional practice relevant to robotics and intelligent devices, through work experience in appropriate companies and organisations in Ireland or abroad.

Placements are made available through:
• The Placement Office on behalf of the Department of Electronic Engineering in consultation with companies and enterprises, both national and international 
• The Department of Electronic Engineering in consultation with companies and enterprises, both national and international
• Individual students, where the student has made private contact with an engineering company or enterprise. In order to propose a placement, students should furnish a one-page proposal for approval.
• The Department of Electronic Engineering, which may, from time to time, have vacancies for undergraduates on a variety of research (or other) projects.

For further information please contact the Department. Contact information can be found on the web address below.
https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: Work productively in a technical role in an organisation.
Appreciate the importance of the multi-disciplinary relationships and interfaces.
Perform as a team member, with typical industrial deadline and reporting relationships, to solve practical engineering problems in real life circumstances.
Apply learnt theory and principles within a practical project.
Appreciate the issues surrounding plan adherence and performance in a practical situation.
Outline the ethical framework within which the organisation operates.
Apply general ethical principles to identified operational circumstances encountered in the IWE placement.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
• To instill the creative spirit in students.
• To develop oral and written communication skills.
• To develop students experience of working in a group.
• To engender an awareness of ethical issues in engineering.
• To have students engage with a non-engineering community.
learningOutcomes: 1. Develop their own community-related engineering-based project specification.
2. Apply problem-based learning to solve unforeseen problems in the general area of electronic engineering.
3. Apply structured design to a range of problems.
4. Apply theoretical knowledge in solving problems encountered.
5. Discuss any ethical issues, environmental impacts and health and safety issues associated with their project.
6. Defend their work through interview.
7. Demonstrate appropriate project management techniques (including time management and project planning).

modules.moduleContent: See full module descriptor at https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: 1. Perform source coding on a stationary data source with known statistics using Huffman coding, Shannon coding, arithmetic coding.
2. Calculate the entropy (self, mutual, conditional) of a random variable and interpret the result in terms of information content and theoretical limits to source coding performance.
3. Perform data compression of a stationary data source with unknown statistics.
4. Calculate the channel capacity of simple noisy channels including the binary symmetric case, and interpret the result in terms of theoretical limits to channel coding performance.
5. Design linear block codes (including Hamming and cyclic) and determine the error correcting capability of such codes.
6. Implement syndrome decoding for block codes.
7. Implement simple convolutional codes using state transition and trellis diagrams.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
To give students an experience of a small-scale design problem, with all the associated administrative, communication and managerial aspects.
A more detailed document can be found on https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: 1. Combine technical expertise from multiple technical areas to design and specify solutions for their project.
2. Apply theoretical knowledge in solving problems they encounter
3. Demonstrate competent technical communication skills (including meeting, reporting and interview).

modules.moduleName: ELECTROMAGNETIC EMISSIONS - COMPATIBILITY AND BIOLOGICAL EFFECTS
modules.moduleContent:
To introduce two important fields involving the electromagnetic (EM) emissions of telecommunications equipment, EM compatibility (EMC) and biological effects. On one hand, EMC establishes the ability of the equipment to function satisfactorily in its electromagnetic environment without introducing intolerable electromagnetic disturbances to anything in that environment. On the other hand, the course focuses on the existing exposure limitations for humans to EM emissions and their biological and environmental impact. The importance of both fields in recent decades has stimulated the research work in these areas, and the corresponding publication of numerous regulations, guidelines and recommendations.

Indicative Syllabus
• Introduction to EM emissions, compatibility and biological effects
• Time-varying EM fields
• Antennas
• Interference coupling mechanisms
• EMC for electronic devices
• EMC design guidelines
• Biological effects of non-ionizing radiation
• Limitations of exposure to non-ionizing EM fields
• Body-centric wireless communications
• RF coverage surveys

For more information on this module please refer to the web link: https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: Explain time-varying EM fields fundamentals
Explain different antenna properties and types of antennas
Describe the main regulations and tests involved in EMC
Analyse the possible internal and external sources of EM interference
Design printed circuit boards compliant with EMC requirements
Explain current research fields in relation to EM emissions and biological effects
Describe the recommendations and regulations in relation to exposure to non ionising EM fields
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The module is an introduction to Human-Robot Interaction. discuss a wide range of topics, including the different communication modalities such as speech, non-verbal communication and the processing of emotions, as well as ethical issues around the application of robots today.
learningOutcomes: Summarise the main domains of Human-Robot Interaction.
Describe the primary research methods in the subject area.
The practical applications of robots and their role in society.

modules.moduleContent: This Challenge based module will immerse you in a life like scenario; a group of engineers developing a robotic or embedded AI system for a problem set by or inspired by industry partners.  As a group you will design the system.  Depending on the practicality of the problem protypes may be developed and evaluated.  You will gain practical experience of project management and team skills.
learningOutcomes: Develop a project concept and value proposition.
Develop a project plan that uses agile methods and teamwork to design and construct a robotic system.
Work within an assigned budget.
Evaluate the working system.
Present a working prototype of the solution.

modules.moduleContent: It is anticipated that the seminar programme will be somewhat fluid, depending on the availability of speakers. However, the following topics should be included: Typical company/agency involvement is likely to include: Wavebob, Airtricity, EirGrid, ESB, ESB International, Energy Regulator, Open Hydro, Marine Current Turbines, Ocean Energy Development Unit (SEI), Ocean Energy, Vestas, General Electric, Genersys, Niland Solar.
learningOutcomes: The student will develop an appreciation of real world renewable energy technology and economics, together with an appreciation for the procedures involved in developing a renewable energy application. Possible spin-offs from this module include the networking of industry/student groups and possible project titles.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The EE690 Dissertation module is a pivotal component of the MSc Robotics and Embedded AI program, allowing students to demonstrate their ability to apply advanced theoretical knowledge and technical expertise to an independent research project. This module is designed to integrate and showcase the skills acquired throughout the program, preparing students for professional roles in robotics, embedded systems, and artificial intelligence.

The dissertation provides an opportunity for students to address a real-world problem, often in collaboration with industry partners or within the university's robotics research group. Students take ownership of their projects, manage their timelines, and demonstrate their capabilities in problem-solving, innovation, and technical communication.

For further details or queries, students are encouraged to contact the module coordinator.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Student will be spend a period of time in international research laboratory
working with specialists in their field of research on a project related to their PhD research.
learningOutcomes:
support students spending a period in an international research
laboratory to gain specialist training and advance their PhD research.

modules.moduleContent: An introduction to research supervision; Participation in the supervision of at least two BE/ME project students and/or summer research students with mentoring from academic staff.
learningOutcomes: Coordinate research projects and to provide supervision of individuals and teams working on research.

modules.moduleContent: Student will be spend a period of time in a relevant industry position with appropriate high level work tasks, working with specialists in their field of research on a project related to their PhD research.  The work will be pre-approved by the PhD supervisor that it is of a sufficient standard to enhance the skills of the PhD student.
learningOutcomes:
* support students spending a period in an industry work placement
      to gain specialist training and advance their PhD research.

* inform their research on the priorities of industry and the
      challenges of transferring research to the commercial domain.

modules.moduleContent: This course is designed to introduce students to the basic elements of communication, to provide practical experience in the preparation and delivery of public presentations, while also delivering key critical listening skills. Businesses expect university graduates to be able to deliver a high level of written and oral communication. In fact, communications skills are amongst some of the highest rated skills expected of Irish graduates in the Irish business community. Businesses and other organizations rely on successful communications to be able to operate successfully in a challenging local and global market. We hope to improve the students’ communications skills and give them the confidence they need to succeed in college presentations as well as in their future professional endeavours.
learningOutcomes: relate the principles of public speaking to a variety of extemporaneous speech situations.
develop skill in researching a topic for a speech.
prepare and organize the content for speech in an outline.
improve the use of language in conveying messages.
develop critical analysis while listening to speeches.
deliver appropriate speeches with increased confidence and skill.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to some of the major schools of contemporary literary theory. It outlines their philosophical, literary and cultural impact and seeks to help students understand their importance as a way in which to enhance their critical understanding of literary texts. Theoretical positions discussed in the module might include, but not be limited to: Marxism; feminism; post-colonial theory; cultural materialism & new historicism; post-structuralism; deconstruction; queer theory; new directions in literary studies.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the key theoretical approaches and concepts in literary and cultural criticism;
Show an ability to analyse and compare a variety of theoretical texts and approaches;
Analyse the potential uses and problems of particular theoretical approaches to language, culture, representation and literature;

Develop and write a research essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions; construct a coherent, substantiated argument;
Show capacity to write clear, grammatically correct prose;
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.

modules.moduleContent: This module examines the geographies of literature and looks at how literary culture reflects on and shapes the places which it inhabits. The module begins by discussing spatial theories of culture in terms of, for example, colonial expansion, ecocriticism or psychogeography, and then studies examples of geographical areas which have produced and are produced by literary texts. Examples might include, but are not limited to: utopias and dystopias; colonization; the representation of cities in literary texts; nature writing.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the relationship between literature and place;
Demonstrate an ability to understand and analyse relevant theoretical ideas, and to apply these ideas to literary texts;
Show an ability to read a select number of exemplary texts critically with close attention to detail;

Develop and write a research essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions; to construct a coherent, substantiated argument;
Show capacity to write clear, grammatically correct prose;
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.

modules.moduleContent: This module explores how language creates imaginary worlds and how imagined worlds, in turn, shape and change the real world. It focuses on the great Renaissance texts of exploration, fantasy, travel and imagination; it looks at fairyland, the ghostly and the supernatural, at the marvellous and the monstrous, at the dreams and nightmares of a civilisation. It examines questions of, for example, colonialism, migration, globalisation, race, exoticism and religion and, in doing so, it raises big questions about the relationship between the imaginary and the real. Students will be invited to consider how literary texts provoke us to see the world in new and complex ways.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of some of the key themes in Renaissance writing;
Demonstrate an ability to understand the relationship between form and content in a number of exemplary texts;
Demonstrate a clear understanding of the historical and cultural contexts of texts;
Participate in tutorial discussions; debate issues related to specific literary texts;

Develop and write a research essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions; to construct a coherent, substantiated argument;
Show capacity to write clear, grammatically correct prose;
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.

modules.moduleContent: This module examines fiction and poetry of the Victorian age, introducing students to key writers and themes during the period. We will discuss how poetry and fiction of the period reflects and interacts with the dynamics of the nineteenth century, concentrating on aspects such as, but not limited to: imperialism; industrialisation; the emergence of an urban working class; and the rise of aestheticism; and a new politics of gender and sexuality towards the end of the nineteenth century. Students will have the opportunity to study texts in detail and will be able to contextualise Victorian literature historically and socially.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a knowledge and understanding of literature of the Victorian period
Demonstrate an ability to understand and analyse nineteenth-century contexts, and to apply these ideas to literary texts
Show an ability to read a select number of Victorian texts critically with close attention to detail and historical context
Demonstrate an ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions; construct a coherent, substantiated argument
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources

modules.moduleContent: Over the course of the semester students will be introduced to the practice of creative writing. Workshops will focus on the practicalities of writing, editing, and giving and taking criticism of creative work. Students will be expected to produce writing for discussion and criticism and to work on developing and revising their writing in the light of that criticism. Students will also look critically at the work of established writers and will be encouraged to reflect on their own writing in the light of their reading.

This module introduces students to some of the major developments in contemporary literary theory. It examines the relationship between such theoretical debates and contemporary politics and philosophy. Students will extend their knowledge of literary and cultural theory from second year, deepening their understanding of contemporary cultural and literary theory and examining the ways in which theory allows for an opening and questioning of the meaning of literary texts. Theoretical positions discussed in the module might include, but will not necessarily be limited to, post-Marxism; intersectionalism; eco-criticism; the ‘ethical turn’; the posthuman.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the key concepts in literary and cultural criticism;
Show an ability to analyse and compare a variety of complex theoretical texts and approaches;
Analyse the potential uses and problems of particular theoretical approaches to language, culture, representation and literature;
Write on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.
Write responses to exam questions, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.

modules.moduleContent:
The objective of this module is to develop a research proposal, chapter outline, methodology summary and selected bibliography for the PhD dissertation.

Students will develop a formal research proposal in conjunction with their research supervisors.  This proposal will include a) a description of the research project; b) an outline methodology statement; c) a working chapter outline, with targeted delivery dates; d) a select bibliography.

The objective of this module is to develop teaching skills in relation to Third Level English.
