Students will take part in a series of seminars directed towards the teaching of English at Third Level.  Seminars will include such areas as writing skills, marking, a curriculum development workshop, and literary analysis.

The objective of the module is to learn a language relevant to the student's area of study.

The English Department will recognise, for the purpose of this module, appropriate courses of instruction in a language relevant to the student's research.  The Department will not provide the instruction for such programmes.  Each module recognised in this manner will be assessed individually, and will be approved by the Department as a whole at its regular meetings.

modules.moduleName: QUANTUM PHYSICS AND MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICS
modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to introduce students to the origins and fundamental concepts of quantum physics, including elementary quantum mechanics.  In parallel, it also aims to develop core mathematical methods for physics, relevant to quantum physics and other physics modules, building on the foundation set by 1st year Mathematics.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:
- Trigonometric and hyperbolic functions; complex numbers
- Series expansions, limits and convergence
- Calculus to the level of multiple integrals; solution of linear ordinary and partial
differential equations
- Three-dimensional trigonometry
- Fourier series and transforms including the convolution theorem
- Black body radiation
- Photoelectric effect
- Wave-particle duality
- Heisenberg’s Uncertainty Principle
- Wave function and its interpretation
- Standard solutions and quantum numbers, to the level of the hydrogen atom
- Tunnelling
- Quantum structure and spectra of simple atoms
- Waves in linear media to the level of group velocity
learningOutcomes: Describe the underlying principles of the mathematical methods introduced in this course.
Apply relevant mathematical methods to physical problems.

Discuss observations that have helped verify astrophysical theories as well as those that challenged the established physical theories at the beginning of the 20th century and prompted the development of quantum mechanics.
Discuss the early quantum theories and models: Planck's model, Einstein's model of photo-electric effect, Compton's model of X-ray scattering, Bohr's model of hydrogen atom.
Describe how the observed particle-wave dualism leads to the notion of particle wavefunction, the Uncertainty Principle and Born's interpretation of quantum mechanics.
Write the Schrödinger equation in simple one-dimensional models.
Solve simple numerical problems relevant to early quantum theories using appropriate units.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to further develop the basic concepts of electromagnetism using vector calculus and study applications in AC circuits and transmission lines.

Module content:
AC theory and complex impedance, resonance, transmission lines, line termination, impedance matching. Electrostatic Fields, Coulomb's Law, Gauss's Law, Divergence, 1st Maxwell equation, electrostatic potential, gradient, energy in electrostatic fields. Polarization, dielectrics, induced charge, displacement field. Magnetic fields, current density, Ampere's Law, Curl, Stokes' Theorem, 3rd Maxwell equation, vector potential, magnetic materials, displacement currents, 4th Maxwell equation, electromagnetic waves.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- Trigonometric and hyperbolic functions; complex numbers

- Vectors to the level of div, grad and curl; divergence theorem and Stokes’ theorem

- Electrostatics and magnetostatics

- DC and AC circuit analysis to complex impedance, transients and resonance

- Gauss, Faraday, Ampère, Lenz and Lorentz laws to the level of their vector expression

- Maxwell’s equations and plane EM wave solution; Poynting vector
learningOutcomes: Analyze series and parallel AC circuits to the level of complex impedances including resonant circuits and AC bridges.
Explain the concept of field lines and flux and the significance of Gauss' Law.
Discuss the electric and magnetic properties of matter including polarization and magnetization.
Discuss the integral versions of the Faraday and Ampere laws to the level of their vector expression, and the significance of the Lenz and Lorentz laws.
Discuss the divergence theorem and Stokes' theorem.
Deduce Maxwell's equations from the fundamental laws of electromagnetism using the formalism of vector calculus including the gradient, divergence and curl operators.
Discuss the concept of electromagnetic induction and displacement current.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to develop a solid understanding of the principles of quantum physics, the structure of atoms, the periodic system of the elements, the structure of nuclei, the fundamental forces and elementary particles.

Module content

Quantum Physics: Wave-particle duality, Heisenberg's uncertainty principle, the wavefunction and its interpretation, eigenvalue equations, the Schrödinger equation, the particle in a box, tunneling, the harmonic oscillator, wavefunctions and the quantum numbers of the Hydrogen atom.

Atomic Physics: Pauli exclusion principle, bosons and fermions, Zeeman effect, Stern-Gerlach experiment, electron spin, multi-electron atoms, optical transitions, X-ray emission, periodic system, rotations, vibrations and electronic excitations in diatomic molecules.

Nuclear Physics: nuclear structure, binding energy, radioactivity, nuclear reactions, nuclear fission and reactors, nuclear fusion, fundamental forces and the standard model.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- Matrices to the level of eigenvalues and eigenvectors

- Heisenberg’s Uncertainty Principle

- Wave function and its interpretation

- Standard solutions and quantum numbers, to the level of the hydrogen atom

- Tunnelling

- Quantum structure and spectra of simple atoms

- Nuclear masses and binding energies

- Radioactive decay, fission and fusion

- Pauli exclusion principle, fermions and bosons and elementary particles

- Fundamental forces and the Standard Model
learningOutcomes: Describe the essential concepts of quantum physics, and apply these to a selection of simple one-dimensional physical systems.
Discuss the quantum numbers, the energy levels and the optical transitions of the hydrogen atom, and in diatomic molecules.
Distinguish between bosons and fermions, and apply the Pauli exclusion principle to the electronic structure of the ground states of multi-electron atoms and the periodic table of the elements.
Discuss the structure of nuclei and nuclear binding energy and the application of nuclear reactions in fission and fusion processes.
Solve relevant conceptual and numerical problems.
Carry out relevant laboratory experiments to demonstrate and test physical principles and report the results.

modules.moduleContent:
This module covers various topics in celestial and relativistic mechanics. General relativity is covered at an introductory level.

Module content: Classical & Celestial Mechanics: harmonic motion (simple, damped, forced), longitudinal and transverse waves, the wave equation. Rigid body motion. Newtonian gravitation, celestial mechanics, Kepler's Laws.Special Relativity: Frames of reference, Galilean transformations, the Michelson-Morley experiment, Lorentz transformations, length contraction, time dilatation, equivalence of mass and energy, conservation laws.
General Relativity: Introduction to general relativity, principle of equivalence, gravitational red shift and time dilation, Schwarzschild metric, black holes. Newtonian cosmology & general relativity, expansion of the Universe.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- Calculus to the level of multiple integrals; solution of linear ordinary and differential equations

- Newton’s laws, conservation laws including rotation

- Newtonian gravitation, Kepler’s laws

- Lorentz transformations and energy momentum relationship

- Mechanical properties of matter to include elasticity and thermal expansion

- Free, damped, forced and coupled oscillations to include resonance and normal modes

- Waves in linear media to the level of group velocity

- Doppler effect
learningOutcomes: Discuss the application of the laws of classical mechanics to wave motion and rigid body motion.
Explain and derive the laws of mechanics relevant to motion of celestial bodies including Kepler's Laws.
Describe the fundamental experiments and key ideas that led to the development of the theory of Special Relativity.
Apply the formalism of Special Relativity to simple problems involving transformations between frames of reference.
Perform simple calculations involving curved spacetime and the Schwarzschild metric.
Describe how black holes are formed and their effect on matter in their vicinity.
Carry out relevant laboratory experiments to demonstrate and test physical principles and report the results.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to develop basic technical skills in electronic and mechanical workshop practice that will be useful to a research student in Experimental Physics.

Module Content:

Health and safety: best practice in Experimental Physics.

Electronic Workshop: Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs); PCB design software; production of PCBs; assembly and testing of PCB; specific safety issues in electronics.

Mechanical workshop: Introduction to mechanical workshop machines and capabilities; CAD design software for mechanical drawings; student mechanical workshop training; specific safety issues in mechanical workshop. 3D
learningOutcomes: Create a schematic diagram in a commercial schematic capture program.
Design a PCB layout from a simple schematics.
Manufacture a one-off PCB.
Populate and test a PCB.
Use CAD software to design mechanical components for additive (3d printing) and subtractive (workshop job workflow) manufacturing.
Create technical drawings for communicating designs to workshop staff.
Confidently use range of tools available in the student mechanical and electronics workshop.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to increase the student’s background knowledge in advanced Experimental Physics topics and train them in the skills of research presentation.

Module Content:

Ten 50-minute research seminars given by leading researchers in a variety of areas of Physics. Credit can be given for attendance at a seminar at another university, for example as part of the Institute of Physics seminar series. The student must submit a summary of one of these seminars. Preparation and delivery of a 45-minute departmental seminar on the student’s own MSc or PhD research.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a record of attendance at research seminars in Experimental Physics or of relevance to the student's research.
Summarize the key points of a research seminar on an advanced topic.
Prepare a full-length seminar presentation on one's own research.
Deliver a full-length seminar presentation to an audience of peers and academics.

modules.moduleName: EUROPEAN SPACE AGENCY ALPACH SUMMER SCHOOL
modules.moduleContent:
The purpose of this Summer School is to foster the practical application of knowledge derived from lectures, to develop organisational and team-work skills and to encourage creativity. Teams will compete to design the best project, judged by an independent jury. The teams themselves are responsible for the selection of the subject of the project and for the team structure and working methods.

The School is delivered by experts in the field and coordinated by the European Space Agency. Department of Experimental Physics has no direct input in the exact topic, which changes from year to year. The cost of registering for and attending this summer school might be covered by Enterprise Ireland or departmental project grants in some cases.
The Summer School Alpbach objectives

*  motivating participants to seeing space as an exciting and challenging endeavour
*  working in international, multi-disciplinary teams by posing challenging topics for designing space missions
*  teach a range of scientific topics relevant to the range of possible science missions to design, and to teach about the different aspects of the complex interplay between scientific objectives and requirements on the one hand, mission- and spacecraft design as well as mission costing on the other hand
*  to develop the ability to work in teams for a common end, to prepare presentations and reports under serious time constraints
*  to develop a professional network
learningOutcomes: Contribute to the development of a unique space mission.
Present own contribution to the project.
Present goals, methods and design of the the proposed space mission.
Demonstrate the ability to work as a part of a focused team under severe time constraints.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to further student's knowledge and skills through participation in a summer school that offers advanced training or subject specific knowledge or skills.

Module Content: Content depends on the supervisor approved course.
learningOutcomes: Register for, make arrangements for and travel to an external summer school.
Demonstrate increased technical knowledge or skill appropriate to student's research area.

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED ENGLISH FOR THE EMI CLASSROOM
modules.moduleContent:
This 2-week intensive programme is a course of learning through the medium of English, for specific academic purposes, for academic staff working and teaching in Higher Education. The emphasis of this course is on Academic English language usage, especially in its written form, within the field of EMI/EME in Higher Education.
The module will highlight the importance of possessing an advanced level of English language proficiency for academic staff, especially when participating in or delivering a programme through EMI/EME. Participants will discuss this importance through their shared experiences of using English in an academic setting and how it has contributed to their professional development to date.
The module uses immersive methods of language instruction, contextual usage, academically-focussed discussion and practical application of key functional academic terms and lexis for both instruction and practical writing tasks. Through instruction, analysis of exemplars and practice, participants will not only recognise what constitutes good academic style in English language use in an EMI/EME context of learning but also analyse their own writing style, identifying any problematic areas and seeking to improve these using knowledge and skills gained from the module. All lexis utilised for instruction on this module is academic in style and aligns closely with the Academic Word List (AWL). In terms of its form and function, it corresponds to authentic language usage across a variety of contexts in today’s Higher Education EMI/EME environment. This will provide successful participants with the linguistic knowledge and skills to progress their competency in using Academic English for professional purposes in Higher Education.

The programme level is set at CEFR C1.1
learningOutcomes: Identify the features of appropriate academic style in English and practice these in written texts and oral presentations.
Analyse and reflect on their own academic style in written and spoken English to identify and improve areas of weakness.
Plan and deliver effective lessons and lectures in their subject areas, using EMI/EME.
Evaluate the appropriateness of their students’ written work in Academic English.
Demonstrate an improved level of verbal academic style of English in presentations and talks, which will also enable more confident and effective interactions with colleagues.

Participate in group discussions on theories, topics and themes related to academic writing style and how it is used in Higher Education, sharing their experiences and insights with other members of the group.

modules.moduleContent:
Students may have an opportunity, during their research programme, to organise a conference as an individual or as part of an organising committee. The conference may address a general or specific theme and may attract participants from Ireland and/or abroad. This module aims to give students the opportunity to learn how to organise a conference and gain the practical experience of so doing. The student must be centrally involved in organising the conference.

For this module a conference may also be taken to mean a workshop, seminar series or Winter/Summer School. Sample activities include drafting and disseminating calls for papers, assessing and selecting papers, inviting speakers, identifying and inviting conference chairs, organising the conference venue, organising travel and subsistence for speakers, making up conference packs, arranging paper streams, administering finances, managing the event on the day and handling publication of any conference proceedings.

Completion of this module requires the student to maintain a reflective journal recording the student’s experience of their learning and/or completion of specific tasks or assessments. The content will be designed by the student’s supervisor so as to ensure that the student can achieve the module’s learning outcomes and that the supervisor can assess that the learning outcomes have been met by the student.
learningOutcomes: Summarise the conference theme.
Describe the entire process of conference organisation.
Manage a conference.
Communicate effectively with conference speakers, chairs, attendees and other conference organisers.

modules.moduleName: ENGAGING WITH THE COMMUNITY:RESEARCH, PRACTICE AND REFLECTION.
modules.moduleContent:
This experiential learning module aims to give students the opportunity to enhance their personal effectiveness, capacity for innovation and professional competence by applying their discipline-specific knowledge/skills to the design, conduct and reporting of a community-engaged, applied research project. Working with their supervisor, each student will devise an individualised learning experience. The module is intended to accommodate a range of interests; from those students whose doctoral research is in social research through to students carrying out theoretical or laboratory-based projects, and those including PPI.

Research topics/themes/problems for a research project may be identified by a community partner, the individual student/s, a multidisciplinary team, and by relevant academic or research staff/units/groups within the university. This professional input will combine expertise and experience in applied, collaborative and participatory research methods.

The availability of this module to doctoral students and community partners will contribute to the realisation of the civic engagement envisioned by the university as part of the student experience.

Indicative content may include: Negotiating with community partner; Group meetings with members of a team; planning the research design; conducting a project using collaborative and/or participatory methods; preparation for presentation of outcomes.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the concept, nature and purpose of community engagement and be cognisant of different modes of engagement.
Engage appropriately with people from other disciplines and/or as part of a research team in a community-engaged research project.
Reflect critically on their own research practice, doctoral research and future professional practice.
Apply their disciplinary knowledge and skills to real world research inquiry.
Critically consider ethical issues that arise in real world research.
Develop research skills in an applied, real-world setting, in response to an identified research need.
Scrutinise and reflect on social norms and their own role as agents of change in society.

modules.moduleContent: The module aims to give students the opportunity to further their knowledge in diverse research areas linked to the student’s discipline and enhance their critical analysis, writing and presentation skills. Students will normally attend a minimum of 5 seminars over the course of the year (e.g. Conference presentations, Seminar series).
learningOutcomes: Describe current topics having attended a series of seminars covering diverse range of subject matter.
Critically analyse seminars attended.
Write abstracts relating to seminars attended.
Apply presentation skills observed in the seminars attended.

modules.moduleContent: This introductory course examines the financial decision making of firms; the different possible ways in which they can finance their activities; how they decide the appropriate capital structure; how they choose between issuing dividends and retaining profits. A brief introduction to the nature and uses of derivative securities is also given and issues in international finance are discussed.
learningOutcomes: Discuss market efficiency.
Contrast the different methods used by corporations to finance their activities.
Examine the relationship between the firm’s capital structure and its value.
Determine the price and value of Futures and Option contracts.
Explain the use of Futures, Option and SWAP contracts for hedging purposes.
Analyze the issues surrounding the dividend controversy.

modules.moduleContent: This course examines forwards, futures and swaps; their economic role in capital markets, the equilibrium pricing of these securities, and uses of these securities in hedging and speculative trading. Topics covered include the economic role of derivative securities, the mechanics of futures and swap markets trading, non-arbitrage pricing of forwards and futures, commodity vs. equity-index vs. foreign exchange futures, the term structure of interest rates and its relationship to swap rates, and hedging and speculative strategies using forwards, futures and swaps.
learningOutcomes: Describe the operation of Forward, Futures and Swap markets, the practical uses of these derivatives and the pricing models employed in these markets.
Explain the role and operation of margins in exchange traded derivative contracts.
Determine the price and value of Futures and Swap contracts.
Evaluate the outcomes of hedging strategies.
Illustrate the uses of interest rate derivatives.
Demonstrate the uses of swap contracts.

modules.moduleName: FIXED INCOME MARKETS (INTERNATIONAL FINANCIAL MARKETS & INSTITUTIONS)
modules.moduleContent:
This course introduces students to fixed income securities (bond instruments). It looks at the valuation and properties of fixed income securities as well as the trading strategies pursued by market participants. Topics covered include bond’s price volatility, bond returns, term structure of interest rates and immunization strategies.

This module will use real world financial data from the Bloomberg database accessed via terminals. The students will be introduced to the Bloomberg Database and will be expected to complete the Bloomberg Markets Concepts (BMC) certificate during the course of the module.
learningOutcomes: Critically assess the different measures used to compute the bond’s price volatility;
Compute the price of a bond and the bond’s price volatility using different measures;
Describe the operation of Bond markets and the practical uses of bonds;
Derive the returns from investing in bonds;
Construct the term structure of interest;
Identify the factors that determine the price of a bond and explain their influence on this price; and
Use the Bloomberg database/interface and have completed the Bloomberg Markets Concepts (BMC) certificate.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module brings the world of financial data analysis to the classroom. Students will learn to source, manipulate, visualise and present financial data using the industry’s leading databases. Students will learn how to use this data to analyse corporate financial performance, compare performance across firms and sectors and address other interesting current topics in finance.
learningOutcomes: Source and download data from financial databases;
Manipulate financial data to compile company reports;
Visualise and present data to address fundamental financial questions;
Use financial data to estimate relationships between financial variables; and
Write and present financial reports in a user-friendly and professional format.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to financial markets, asset pricing and portfolio diversification. It provides an extensive overview of financial markets and the key concepts that underlie this field of study. Topics covered include equity and bonds markets; Time value of money and term structure of interest rates; Risk and Uncertainty; Risk vs Return; Diversification; Portfolio Choice; Asset Pricing: CAPM; Arbitrage & APT; and International Parity Conditions. Examples, using real-world data, are used to illustrate concepts, giving the students the opportunity to turn theoretical concepts into practical applications.
learningOutcomes: Understand the financial system and its importance to a well-functioning economy;
Describe the main financial assets and the markets in which they are traded;
Analyse and calculate the value of stocks, bonds, and other financial assets;
Explain the relationship between return and risk in models of asset returns;
Describe and apply modern portfolio theory;
Understand how security prices are determined in the Capital Asset Pricing Model, and the role played by the assumptions in the model;
Understand the role played by arbitrage in finance markets, its impact on security prices and its application in the Arbitrage Pricing Theory; and

modules.moduleContent: The purpose of this module is for students to use the skills acquired in the 75-ECTS of taught modules to address a real-world financial question or issue. The question or issue may be motivated by the academic literature or identified as a problem facing a particular company or industry. Students will be required to draw on their data management and analytics knowledge and skills to develop solutions to business and finance problems. Students will produce a report encompassing a review of literature, data and methods for solving the problem under investigation, presentation and interpretation of results and draw conclusions that are supported by the analysis.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an ability to undertake independent research in Finance;
Clearly present a written report in a professional manner;
Produce a concise and focussed review of the literature on the topic;
Identify, gather and present appropriate data to analyse the problem;
Use analytic skills and critical thinking evaluate the question;and
Accurately interpret results and draw conclusions supported by the data.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The objective of this module is to explore the role of data science in promoting sustainable and socially responsible practices in the financial sector. This module will provide students a deep understanding of the principles and techniques of data science, as well as an understanding of the ethical and social implications of these tools and techniques in the context of finance. The module will also focus on working with specialized data resources, such as Bloomberg, Eikon and Sustainalytics, to gain insights into developments in sustainable finance.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse financial and non-financial data from various sources, including ESG ratings, climate data, and social impact indicators.
Evaluate and apply key concepts to asset allocation and portfolio analysis using climate and ESG/SDG data.
Develop data visualizations to communicate insights from financial and sustainability data to different stakeholders.
Apply, and develop specific recommendations from, Anti-Greenwashing tools for specific organisational types.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module focuses from an investor’s perspective on the great threat that climate changes pose, which could negatively impact economic growth, inflation, and investment returns. The module will examine the different climate risks and the approaches for effective portfolio decarbonization
learningOutcomes: Diagnose the effects of climate change on prices and risks across a variety of asset classes, with a particular focus on equities, fixed income, energy, real estate, and mortgage-based assets.
Evaluate climate leaders and laggards across industries and those positioned to seize transition opportunities.
Judge the economic and financial foundations of potential climate regulations, including emissions trading schemes.
Critically discuss approaches of constructing the Social Cost of Carbon, including an understanding of the appropriate discount rates.
Provide a synthesis of how climate risk interacts with other risks, such as geopolitical risks.

modules.moduleContent:
Sustainable finance is an exciting and rapidly evolving area of legal and financial practice. Understanding core drivers, risks, and influences in the sustainable finance world will provide students with skills that will be relevant across a range of careers.

As investors are increasingly applying Environmental, Social, and Governance factors as part of their analysis process to identify material risks and growth opportunities, this module will focus on teaching the fundamentals of ESG investing and ESG metrics, which might not be commonly part of mandatory financial reporting, but are increasingly included in annual reports or in a standalone sustainability report.

The online lectures are integrated with a set of case studies, readings, papers, video materials, and expert-led class discussion to provide an engaged understanding of relevant concepts and challenges. Students will learn from subject specialists from Maynooth University’s School of Business and external experts. Students will also have an opportunity to engage with industry speakers.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the ESG reporting ecosystem, with an overview of how financial institutions utilize this information to make decisions and the challenges associated with that.
Critically assess the different accounting frameworks for sustainable reporting, including SASB, GRI, IASB, FASB and GAAP.
Identify and evaluate the impact of different ESG investment strategies, such as exclusionary screening, positive screening, and ESG integration.
Demonstrate a high level of understanding of the climate-economy link and the associated investment risks and opportunities across different asset classes in the financial sector.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: STATES OF EXPOSURE: AMERICAN LITERATURE IN THE 21ST CENTURY
modules.moduleContent: This module examines contemporary American literature through the theme and meanings of exposure—including vulnerability, revelation, experience, transparency, and the effects of lens-based and digital technology—to critically examine current American social and ecological realities. Employing a contemporary theoretical apparatus that includes a range of thinkers, the module considers some of the most pressing concerns for American writers, citizens and residents today, including: how identity narratives of state, gender and race shape structures of nationhood; how these narratives are played out on and through the human body; the affective implications of neoliberal and capitalist systems of control; enduring ideologies of exceptionalism; the precincts of the ‘real’ and of the ‘human’; the exigencies of visuality and the ethics of attention. Students will have the opportunity to work closely with their seminar leader on a range of contemporary American texts and to critically address exposure as a pressing area of inquiry for writers and artists today.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module offers students the opportunity to work closely with select Shakespeare plays and to attend to their adaptation in contemporary popular culture and digital media. Informed by current critical debates within Shakespeare studies as well as theories of adaptation, the module will address a range of texts that variously appropriate, cite and reimagine Shakespeare. From novels and plays to film and digital media productions, the module will consider the aesthetics and politics of adaptation. In particular, it will examine how and why Shakespeare ghosts contemporary literary texts, popular culture and social media, ask what it means to adapt a Shakespearean text, or to describe something as “Shakespearean”, and investigate the ideologies that iterations of Shakespeare might serve. To this end, the module asks to what extent Shakespeare functions as a metalanguage for race, gender, and class in our contemporary world. By the end of the elective, students should have an understanding of different theories of and approaches to Shakespeare and a rich appreciation of the forms Shakespeare takes across media. Students will be encouraged to develop a writing journal or online portfolio that details their responses to the texts and to curate
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a complex knowledge of key literary texts.
Demonstrate a strong ability to assimilate and scrutinise critical readings
Show an ability to appraise, identify and use varied contemporary critical and theoretical approaches to literature and culture
Participate in seminar discussions; debate issues related to cultural context and socio-political circumstances;
Demonstrate high quality verbal and written expression; the ability to make clear points in a lucid grammatical and confident manner.
Write an essay on a chosen topic, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: In this project students will work, under individual supervision, on a supervised writing project which will lead to the development of their dissertation. This module will provide individual, face-to-face meeting time for students with tutors as they move towards the development of a long project. Students will learn how to develop a sustained writing project, and how to plan and map such a project. Students will be assessed on their ability to develop and draft a plan for their dissertation over the course of the semester.
learningOutcomes: Develop a coherent and deliverable plan for their dissertation
Increase their ability to map out a complete piece of creative writing
Address and solve problems with a long piece of creative writing

modules.moduleContent:
Conventional autobiography, involving a writer's retrospection on their public life and career, has always been defined against other co-existent forms of life writing (Whitlock, 2015). Jean-Jacques Rousseau’s Confessions (published posthumously 1782-1789), generally regarded as the first modern autobiography, was historically proximate with Olaudah Equiano’s story of enslavement and manumission in The Interesting Narrative (1789) but Equiano has only recently begun to receive recognition as an innovator of the genre. This module explores the associations between literary and critical practices, life narratives and human rights, focusing, in particular, on the status of life narratives as testimonies, their circulation and re-circulation within specific campaigns and eras, and how we read and evaluate them. 
During the module, you will be introduced to a range of narratives and contexts linked to the histories of slavery, empire and apartheid, the global movement for climate justice, and the #Me Too movement. We will focus on the following topics: the expectation of truthfulness and the risks associated with making a life story public; the volatility of testimonial networks and the public dynamics of discrediting, shaming, stigmatization and denunciation; the comparative elasticity of life narratives over the testimonies permitted in the courts of law and the forms of witness they can provide; their capacity for resurrection within new cycles and for finding new jurisdictions; the impact of their accumulation and their role in human rights activism. 
As we encounter these various texts and contexts, we will consider the relations among literature and politics, and our own implication, as readers and as critics, in the testimonial cycles currently unfolding. We will ask how we can create a holding environment for the testimonies we encounter in the texts we will read and how we can bear witness or could otherwise respond.
learningOutcomes: Recognise and describe key features of autobiography and other genres of life writing with respect to their historical provenance, form, and style.
Analyse life narratives with attention to questions of truthfulness, privacy, norms of personhood, and personal and collective memory.
Demonstrate an understanding of life narratives’s roles within human rights campaigns and the cyclical trends in testimonial cultures.
Participate in seminar discussions; debate issues related to cultural context and socio-political circumstances.
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources in evaluating life narratives.
Demonstrate high quality verbal and written expression; the ability to make clear points in a lucid grammatical and confident manner.
Develop an essay on a chosen topic, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.

modules.moduleContent: This module includes three components: dissertation techniques and processes; presenting research to academic and non-academic audiences; and planning beyond your degree. The workshops are delivered by the English Department academic staff members as well as colleagues from the Careers Office and university centres and institutes.

Students on this module will be guided through a series of seminars on topic chosen by the seminar leader. The aims of the seminar are to deepen and broaden students’ ongoing engagement with literature, to allow for the high-level interchange of ideas, and to hone presentation skills. Students participating in the seminar will be each asked to lead elements of the discussion.

modules.moduleName: PROF DEVELOPMENT FOR ENGLISH RESEARCH STUDENTS
modules.moduleContent: This module will enable students to professionalise themselves as English research students. It will provide students with a forum for discussing the elements which make up a career as a researcher in English Studies. Discussions will be led by colleagues from the Department of English, the University and from outside the University. Topics covered may include finishing the PhD, conference papers, publications, compiling a cv and teaching statement and postdoctoral funding.

Module Objective
The purpose of this module is to allow postgraduate research students to develop and apply the skills required for publishing their research.   This module covers the submission of a manuscript to a peer-reviewed journal.

Learning Outcomes
On completion of the module the student will be able to:
• Discuss the current state-of-the-art in their research field
• Interpret results in the context of the impact on the particular field
• Evaluate results for potential publication
• Formulate a hypothesis/question for the publication 
• Concisely present and critically discuss research highlights that can prove/disprove the hypotheses/question
• Draw conclusions on the work and articulate the significance of the research in the wider context
• Identify a journal, chapter, edited book or  conference proceedings,  with appropriate scope for publication
• Submit a manuscript to meet formatting standard as set out be the publisher.

Module Content
• Attend lectures/seminars/conferences in the area of research  that is intended for publication
• Undertake an informed literature survey around the research that is intended for publication
• Draft hypothesis and framework for manuscript
• Write up the manuscript to include abstract, introduction and results and discussion content
• Submission of manuscript to a peer-reviewed journal.

The objective of the module is to develop understanding of the field through participation in a summer school.

Students will take part in a structured summer/winter school in the field, chosen with the permission of the supervisors.

The objective of the module is to prepare and present  a conference paper arising from the student's research.

Students will present a research paper at an academic conference, with the approval of the supervisors.

The objective of the module is to organise an event in an area relevant to the field.

Students will organise, or assist in the organisation of, an event relevant to the field. Students who wish to make such arrangements will be facilitated, subject to availability.

This module serves as further introduction to topics in Experimental Physics, a co-requisite to EP101. Three important topics of electricity and magnetism, light and sound and modern physics are introduced and related modern technology applications are outlined.

This module covers several topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics, but only at an introductory level.
learningOutcomes: Describe the fundamental principles of electricity and magnetism.
Discuss the techniques required to analyze simple electric circuits.
Analyze the behaviour of sound and light in various physical situations using the theory of vibrations and waves.
Describe the operation of a variety of optical instruments using geometrical optics.
Apply wave-particle duality to the study of a range of physical phenomena at the atomic and sub-atomic level.
Contrast the classical and quantum mechanical pictures of the atom/nucleus particularly in regard to atomic hydrogen and nuclear decay.
Solve simple numerical problems relevant to fundamental electricity & magnetism, optics & waves and modern physics.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to develop practical skills in statistical analysis and data reduction.

Module content
Scientific Python environment is used to develop a suite of routines which have general applicability in a wide range of data reduction applications. Data sets with the desired properties are first simulated using Monte Carlo rejection, and separate functions are then used to analyse the data, comparing the results with the parameters used during the simulation stage.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- Probability distributions
learningOutcomes: Carry out statistical tests on datasets produced by random number generators.
Use Monte Carlo rejection to produce datasets drawn from continuous probability distributions.
Analyse a set of Gaussian deviates (find mean, standard deviation, uncertainty on mean, interpretation of standard deviation in terms of probability) and study propagation of errors.
Apply the principle of maximum likelihood in the context of linear least squares fitting on given data.
Apply distribution testing (chi-squared test) to a given dataset.
Analyse a time series using epoch folding and Fourier analysis to search for periodicities embedded in noise.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to provide the student with a number of practical computational tools which may be used to study optical systems from both wave and ray viewpoints.

Module content: Interference, division of wavefront, division of amplitude, multiple beam interference, dielectric films, Fraunhofer diffraction, Fresnel diffraction, Fourier transforms, Fourier transform spectroscopy, numerical modelling of interference and diffraction, ray optics, computational ray tracing, optical instrumentation, Gaussian beam optics.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- Geometrical optics to the level of simple optical systems

- Interference and diffraction at single and multiple apertures

- Dispersion by prisms and diffraction gratings
learningOutcomes: Discuss the methods that can be used to observe and utilize interference
Use approximations to the Fresnel integral in diffraction theory to calculate far-field and near-field diffraction patterns
Use computational techniques to calculate 1-D and 2-D diffraction patterns
Discuss the relevance of the Fourier transform in optics, and explain its role in Fourier transform spectroscopy
Use the fast Fourier transform (FFT) to calculate far-field diffraction patterns
Use the fast Fourier transform (FFT) to calculate far-field diffraction patterns
Use computational ray tracing to study optical systems
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to explore topics in planetary science, the structure and evolution of the Earth, the solar system and exoplanetary systems.

Module Content:

The solar system: origin of the solar system, planet formation, physics of planetary interiors, formation and structure of planetary atmospheres.

Exoplanets: exoplanet detection, planet classes, exoplanet systems, habitable zone.

Physics of the Earth: the Earth as a planet, plate tectonics, seismology and the interior structure of the Earth, the magnetic field of the Earth and paleomagnetism, the gravitational field of the Earth.
learningOutcomes: Outline the physics of weather analysis and forecasting and solve related numerical tephigram problems.
Identify the key physical evidence of climate change and analyse related feedback mechanisms.
Discuss the key points in relation to planetary system formation theories and exoplanet observation techniques.
Outline the properties of a stable planetary atmosphere and interior.
Describe the structure of the surface of the Earth in terms of plate tectonics, and the interior structure of the Earth based on seismology.
Discuss the properties of the gravitational and magnetic fields of the Earth and their relevance for the physical environment at the surface and in the vicinity of the Earth.
Solve selected numerical problems associated with solar system planets and exoplanets.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this modules is to review the main topics in extragalactic astronomy and observational cosmology and their implications for our understanding of the origin and evolution of the universe.

Module content: The Big Bang, cosmological models, Big Bang nucleosynthesis & the abundance of light elements, the Hubble flow, large-scale structure of the Universe, dark matter, inflation and the cosmological constant, dark energy, the growth of structure, the cosmic microwave background radiation, current experiments. Non-thermal processes, synchrotron radiation, active galactic nuclei, the Unified Model, quasars as probes of the early Universe.
learningOutcomes: Describe and explain the essential features and evidence for the Big Bang model of the early universe.
Solve the Friedmann equation to find how matter- and radiation-dominated universes evolve with time.
Discuss the significance of the cosmological parameters.
Summarise the theory of primordial nucleosynthesis and its implication for the observed abundances of the light elements.
Explain the flatness and horizon problems with the Big Bang theory and describe how the theory of cosmic inflation solves them.
Describe current observations of the cosmic microwave background radiation and how they can be used to test cosmological models.
Explain the origin and observed properties of synchrotron radiation in Radio Astronomy.

modules.moduleName: MEASUREMENT CAMPAIGN AT INTERNATIONAL PARTNER LABORATORY
modules.moduleContent: The aim of this module is to further student's knowledge and skills through participation in a measurement campaign at a laboratory of a partner institution.
learningOutcomes: Make individual arrangements for  a short period of research work at a partner institution.
Complete own research work within the local constraints.
Successfully collaborate with the local research team.
Demonstrate increased technical knowledge or skill appropriate to student's research area.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to further student's knowledge and skills through participation in a summer school that offers advanced training or subject specific knowledge or skills.

Module Content: Content depends on the supervisor approved course.
learningOutcomes: Register for, make arrangements for and travel to an external summer school.
Demonstrate increased technical knowledge or skill appropriate to student's research area.

modules.moduleContent:
This 2-week intensive programme is a course of learning through the medium of English, for specific academic purposes, for staff working and teaching in Higher Education. Language does not exist in isolation; it is used in both social and professional contexts to facilitate actions/needs/wants and to achieve specific goals or tasks. The emphasis of this course is not only on Academic English language use but also the critical skills and pedagogical areas within Higher Education where it is used. The MU programme, taught via English as a medium of instruction (EMI), will expose participants to a wide variety of Academic English lexis and metalanguage (the language of teaching) used in some of the most relevant areas of Higher Education today. This will facilitate a fully authentic teaching and learning context, where participants acquire through usage new linguistic content in Academic English.
Each day’s instruction will begin with a discussion on key pedagogical topics and themes from the programme, which will allow participants to share their own experience, knowledge and ideas in these key areas of practice in Higher Education. Discussion will be followed by the input (instruction) stage, using a variety of modes, e.g. lecture, PowerPoint, MP4s and PDFs. Participants will then actively apply their learning, both pedagogical and linguistic, in a variety of individual and group tasks. Finally, each class session will conclude with a summary of content covered, with an emphasis on English-language lexis and metalanguage. A Q&A session will also be included.
The programme level is set at CEFR C1.1.
learningOutcomes: Analyse the key benefits and challenges of teaching disciplinary content through the medium of English.
Develop a range of classroom activities to foster students’ creativity and interaction in the EMI/EME classroom.
Create appropriate forms of assessment and design feedback mechanisms to properly support students’ learning through the medium of English.
Reflect on the usefulness and potential impact of blended learning and pedagogical technologies on teaching and learning through the medium of English.
Participate in group discussions on key pedagogical topics and themes and share their experiences and insights with other members of the group.
Explain the relevance and applicability of new insights and perspectives on professional practice in Higher Education to their own teaching and assessment at their home universities.

modules.moduleContent:
This 2-week intensive programme is a course of learning through the medium of English for professional services staff working in Higher Education. The emphasis of this course is on Academic English language usage within the context of internationalisation.

The module will highlight the importance of possessing a high level of English language proficiency for professional services staff, especially when supporting their university’s internationalisation strategy. Participants will discuss their shared experiences of using English in a professional services setting and how it has contributed to their professional development to date.

The module uses immersive methods of language instruction, contextual usage and focussed discussion to help raise awareness of specific language areas in need of improvement. Practical application of learning will contribute to improvement in these areas, in both spoken and written modes of communication. All lexis utilised for instruction on this module is academic in style, helping ensure that professional services staff possess the necessary language range and flexibility to communicate effectively and confidently with both academic staff and students, as required.

The design and delivery of presentations will also be covered, allowing module participants to improve their level of engagement and communicative competency in this important area.

The programme level is set at CEFR B2.2.
learningOutcomes: Identify areas and aspects of their specific administrative areas and day-to-day work in which they currently use English or would like to be able to use English with greater competence and confidence.
Analyse and reflect on their own style in written and spoken communications in English to identify and improve areas of weakness.
Read, analyse, and summarise written and aural texts related to internationalisation in Higher Education.
Participate in group discussions on topics and themes related to internationalisation in Higher Education, sharing their experiences and insights with other members of the group.
Plan and deliver effective and engaging presentations related to their specific administrative areas, using appropriate vocabulary and register.
Plan and produce effective written texts related to their specific administrative areas, using appropriate vocabulary and register.

modules.moduleName: SKILLS FOR SUCCESS: PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT AND EMPLOYABILITY
modules.moduleContent:
The key purpose of this module is to facilitate undergraduate students in their academic, personal and professional career development, so that they will be well equipped to secure internships and to successfully enter the graduate labour market. Students from a diverse range of disciplines will be encouraged to develop a strong sense of self-awareness, as well as enabled to identify and develop a range of employability skills which will be pivotal to their future career. The module provides students with the opportunity to engage in effective career planning, to gain an insight into the graduate labour market and an appreciation of how to effectively manage the recruitment process. Therefore, this module will contribute to the development of well-informed and skilled graduates who can compete effectively in the labour market and beyond, as well as excel in their chosen career.
After completing this module, the learner will be able to:
• Gain an insight into organisational culture and preferred working styles.
• Develop self-awareness and understand the importance of reflective practice and its role in effective, continuous professional development and lifelong learning.
• Reflect on and assimilate information to help inform future career planning.
• Structure and articulate ideas effectively, orally and in writing.
• Recognise the skills, qualities and abilities graduate employers seek in potential employees, and how they are evaluated.
• Identify methods used to assess candidates during selection processes.
• Understand how to exploit their skills in sourcing, securing and excelling in their chosen future career.
learningOutcomes: Gain an insight into organisational culture and preferred working styles
Develop self-awareness and understand the importance of reflective practice and its role in effective, continuous professional development and lifelong learning
Reflect on and assimilate information to help inform future career planning
Structure and articulate ideas effectively, orally and in writing.
Recognise the skills, qualities and abilities graduate employers seek in potential employees, and how they are evaluated
Identify methods used to assess candidates during selection processes
Understand how to exploit their skills in sourcing, securing and excelling in their chosen future career.

modules.moduleContent:
Students, through their Department, may have an opportunity to visit an academic or research group outside Maynooth University to gain more extensive research experience. Examples of such research experience include, but are not limited to, training in an advanced technique, extended access to specialised equipment/resources or appropriate field work. Such opportunities are limited and are only possible when arranged by the student’s supervisor and agreed with the host organisation and supervisor. It is recommended that a supervisor in the external host organisation is involved in planning the specific research learning outcomes for the student along with the student’s MU supervisor. These research-specific learning outcomes must be appropriate to the period of the research placement.

Completion of this module may require the student to attend workshops or seminars, complete training (including online training), maintain a reflective journal recording the student’s experience of their learning and/or completion of specific tasks or assessments. The content will be designed by the student’s supervisor so as to ensure that the student can achieve the module’s learning outcomes and that the supervisor can assess that the learning outcomes have been met by the student. The duration of the research placement should be decided by the supervisor in consultation with the host organization.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate new knowledge and/or skills.
Exchange new knowledge and/or skills between the external research environment and MU.
Write a comprehensive report describing their achievements during this placement.
Present orally the research findings/training completed to an audience in the host organisation and/or a local audience.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The module aims to aid research students in developing key communication skills whereby the student will learn how to interact and communicate effectively with a non-academic audience. Students may be asked to attend appropriate training, planned learning activities and independent activities, as indicated by the supervisor relevant to the aims and objective of the module.
learningOutcomes: Develop their communication skills through training and direct experience
Develop their organizational skills.

modules.moduleContent:
The purpose of this module is to allow postgraduate research students to develop and apply the skills required for presenting their research. This module covers the presentation of results in poster or oral format at a conference as agreed with the Supervisor.

Module content:

· Undertake an informed literature survey around the research that is intended for presentation.

· Draft research question/hypothesis and framework for oral presentation.

· Write up the presentation to include abstract, introduction, results and discussion content.

· Delivery of an oral presentation at a national/international conference.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the current state-of-the-art in their research.
Interpret results in the context of the impact on the particular field.
Evaluate results for potential presentation.
Formulate a research question/hypothesis for the presentation.
Concisely present and critically discuss research highlights that can prove/disprove the research question/hypotheses.
Draw conclusions on the work and articulate the significance of the research in the wider context.
Identify a conference.

modules.moduleContent: This module will consist of seminars, presentations, workshops and/or master class sessions tailored for research students. The Winter/Summer School/Short Course may be comprised of thematically specialized and/or inter-disciplinary sessions.
learningOutcomes: Describe the field of inquiry represented in the course.
Identify major theories and paradigms employed and define key concepts and ideas.
Apply theories and concepts and relate them to particular problems, issues and phenomena addressed in the student's own research.
Analyze aspects of the student's own field of inquiry and illustrate them in terms of theories and concepts presented in the course.
Explain phenomena and formulate particular research problems in terms of general theories presented in the course.
Criticize topics and evaluate issues and debates in terms of paradigms & theories presented in the course.

modules.moduleContent: This introductory course deals with investment decisions. The topics include financial statement analysis, investment appraisal, cash management, risk measurement and the returns on risky assets.
learningOutcomes: Solve time value of money problems involving present value, future value, and annuity and perpetuity formulas.
Choose between investment projects using investment criteria such as NPV, IRR, accounting rate of return rule, and the payback method.
Value debt and equity securities.
Estimate expected returns and risk for individual assets and portfolios of individual assets.
Illustrate what happens to risk and return when securities are combined into a portfolio?
Distinguish between the minimum variance frontier and the efficient frontier.
Describe the risk/return relationship in the context of the CAPM and APT Models.

modules.moduleContent: This course presents the fundamental elements of portfolio and asset pricing theory. The course demonstrates how portfolios of assets are constructed, selected and evaluated by individual investors. A detailed analysis of modern portfolio theory is presented. The Markowitz method for calculating a portfolio with an optimal mix of assets will be discussed in detail. The student will also learn a variety of techniques that are required to provide inputs for this portfolio problem. Two of the main equilibrium asset pricing models will be thoroughly explored. The student will learn how these models can be used to infer what equilibrium prices will be for individual assets and how to empirically test these theories. Students will be introduced to the Bloomberg Terminal and will complete the Bloomberg Markets Concepts (BMC) certificate during the course of the module.
learningOutcomes: Estimate expected returns and risk for individual assets and portfolios of individual assets;
Illustrate the difference between the minimum variance frontier and the efficient frontier;
Develop algebraically, expressions for expected return, variance of return, and covariance among returns using the Single and Multi-Index Models;
Solve for the optimal portfolio using the Markowitz Method;
Develop expressions for the expected return on a stock using the CAPM and APT Pricing Models;
Outline the differences and similarities between the Capital Market Line (CML) and the Security Market Line (SML);
Evaluate the core empirical tests related to the CAPM and APT; and

modules.moduleName: DERIVATIVES 2: AN ANALYSIS OF OPTIONS AND CREDIT DERIVATIVES
modules.moduleContent: This course examines a broad range of option contracts, including stock options, stock index options, and currency options,. We develop the properties of option contracts and the trading strategies pursued by market participants. We  study the pricing of options using the Binomial Option Pricing Model (BOPM) and the Black-Scholes model. We develop risk management strategies using options and conclude with an analysis of credit risk and credit derivatives.
learningOutcomes: Describe the operation of Option markets, the practical uses of options and the pricing models employed in these markets.
Explain how relevant factors determine the price of an option.
Design option trading strategies to deliver specified payoff structures at maturity.
Compute the price of an option.
Demonstrate the role of stock index and currency options in providing portfolio insurance.
Discuss credit risk.
Illustrate the uses of credit derivatives in managing credit risk.

modules.moduleContent: Students are introduced to Sustainable and Green finance. We analyse the role of banks, equity and debt markets in promoting sustainable industries. We introduce the concept of Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) investing and evaluate its impact on the financial industry. We examine how Carbon Accounting can help in the reduction of Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions and the role of the financial sector in incentivising its adoption.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the impact of sustainability on financial and non-financial corporations;
Identify the key stakeholders in sustainable finance;
Discuss the role of banks, equity and debt markets in promoting sustainable business practices;
Describe the impact of Environmental, Social and Governance investing in Equity Markets;
Evaluate ESG portfolio performance; and
Discuss the role of Carbon Accounting in reducing Greenhouse Gas emissions.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces the key concepts of Financial Econometrics with an emphasis on the application of statistical techniques to analyse relationships between financial variables. The module covers the theoretical underpinnings of regression analysis, hypothesis testing and specification testing. Students will then apply different estimation methods and learn time series econometric methods that are used in many finance applications. Using econometric and statistical software and real-world data from the Bloomberg terminals, we learn to implement the theory and estimate, interpret and critically evaluate relationships among financial variables.
learningOutcomes: Ability to perform linear, logit and time series regressions.
Conduct and interpret statistical hypotheses.
Appraise different philosophies of how to build and econometrics model.
Conduct time series predictions and test their accuracy.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED DERIVATIVES: FUTURES, OPTIONS AND CREDIT DERIVATIVES
modules.moduleContent:
This module provides an extensive coverage of the main types of Derivative contracts traded on the markets. In part one, we focus on Forwards, Futures and Option contracts. The mechanics of each market is analysed and the uses of each contract is explored. The module develops and implements pricing models for each type of Derivative and demonstrates how they can be used to hedge risks. 

Part two of the module concentrates on Credit risk and the Credit Derivatives that are traded on markets to manage this risk. We cover both single- and multi-name derivatives, demonstrating their practical uses. We explore the pricing models of each instrument.
learningOutcomes: Describe the functions and uses of Derivative contracts in terms of risk management and speculation in financial markets;
Derive models of derivative pricing;
Evaluate hedging and speculative strategies based on portfolios of derivative securities;
Discuss credit risk and in particular its interaction with derivative securities;
Explain the operation credit derivative securities; and
Discuss the interaction of risk management and asset securitization strategies.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at familiarising students with current developments and challenges in financial markets. This is to help them put their Finance theory into practice and prepare them for life in the workplace.  Students will have to study topics of current interest, discuss these topics, make extensive use of Bloomberg data / reports and present their ‘solutions’. Sessions will be led by either academics or experts from the financial sector.

The module is experiential in design. Time in class will include some lectures and discussion, accompanied by exercises that lead to the development of essential problem-solving skills. Students will prepare for and reflect on their experiences both individually and in collaborative learning groups.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate and articulate a knowledge of current financial market conditions, developments and challenges;
Critically evaluate policy responses to market problems;
Devise and solutions to macro-financial problems and firm-specific problems; and
Write financial reports and present results to in a user-friendly and professional format.

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to bring together the learning from the programme and apply it to a specific situation pertinent to the operation of the Sustainable Finance Industry while also facilitating students toward developing research skills that will be of use to them in the future.
learningOutcomes: Manage a consulting project focusing on sustainable finance and innovation and deal with different stakeholders;
Apply theoretical knowledge and use appropriate research methods to a real-world business problem;
Develop alternative strategic recommendations for a specific business problem and assess their advantages and disadvantages;
Develop recommendations for the implementation of the recommended strategies; and
Present their analyses and recommendations in a professional, concise, and accurate manner.

modules.moduleContent:
An academic dissertation provides students with the opportunity to develop and demonstrate highly specialised knowledge which is at the forefront of an area of work or study, as the basis for original thinking and research. An academic dissertation is distinguished from other forms of writing by its focus on analysing and understanding situations via a theoretical lens. An academic dissertation evaluates literature, makes comparisons, seeks explanations and offers conclusions all of which can be used to better understand the case in context, as well as the theory in general. Academic dissertations generally involve some practical primary data gathering by students, but can also centre on secondary sources.

Students completing an academic dissertation will: identify a suitable research topic; set research objectives; locate, organise and evaluate the relevant authoritative literature; devise an appropriate research methodology; gather and analyse primary data; and offer relevant conclusions, recommendations and implications for the theory and future research.
learningOutcomes: Independently manage a sizable research project to meet project milestones, resolve academic and procedural complexities and manage communications with relevant stakeholders;
Write a literature review using a systematic process of collection, synthesis and appraisal;
Design an appropriate research methodology;
Gather and analyse primary and secondary data relevant to a research question;
Discuss findings in the context of the academic literature and the broader business context; and
Apply and evaluate theory in a business context.

modules.moduleContent: The course introduces students to financial theory and traces the development of financial theory from the academic literature. The module is structured as a readings based course where students are expected to engage with the academic literature that underpins our knowledge of finance. The arguments presented in the literature will be analysed from the perspective of the practitioner with a focus on areas relevant to corporate finance. The aim of this module is to help students to understand the interface between a company and the financial markets and the influence of the latter on management’s decisions about investment, financing and operational matters.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of the finance function, the organisation and the financial environment in which it operates. Assessing alternative sources of finance to meet organizational finance requirements and to be able to determine the cost of borrowing;
Demonstrate an ability to understand and advise on working capital techniques;
Demonstrate an ability to use appropriate investment appraisal techniques;
Understand the elements contributing to capital structure decisions and the consequences of theories governing the debt/equity mix;
Appraise merger and acquisitions decisions including the calculation of the value of a business;
Understand the nature of dividend decision, alternative policies that companies can adopt and their significance to investors and alternatives to cash dividends;
Explain the foundations to portfolio theory and CAPM, understanding risk and risk diversification using portfolio investment.

modules.moduleContent: This module examines debates and films classified under the rubric of Global Cinema. Since the 1920s, US cinema has dominated cinema screens around the world, while European cinemas have most often defined themselves in national terms and/or in relation to such art practices as neorealism, impressionism and expressionism. Cinema beyond these US- and Eurocentric models has often been ghettoized as the consumption category “world cinema.”  Moving beyond these categories, this module focuses on film and filmmaking practices that meaningfully address the global, examining how a medium consisting of moving images and recorded sound engages with experiences of international mobility, border crossing, migration and population flows.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a critical and complex awareness of the key debates within Film Studies
Show an understanding of the formal, historical and theoretical methodologies within cinema
Demonstrate high level verbal and written expression; the ability to make clear points in a lucid grammatical and confident manner
Demonstrate knowledge of the specialized vocabulary of Film Studies as it has developed in the academic context and the film industry.
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.

modules.moduleContent: The planet is in crisis: climate change and ‘the great extinction’ are already well underway. Recent attention to the high levels of plastic in the world’s oceans and the loss of natural ecosystems has focused minds on how much the world is losing.  As we become increasingly conscious of the impact of humanity on the natural and biophysical environment, this module asks how it came to this and what we can do about it. Primarily focusing on early modern literature, the module draws on developments in ecocriticism to consider how literary texts provide ecological insights, how they represent the complex interrelations between humans and nature, how they unsettle human exceptionalism, and how they address questions of sustainability. The early modern period, associated with the rise of capitalism, advances in cartography and navigation, colonial incursions into the “New World”, Africa and Asia (the first globalisation), offers a unique case study for eco-critical interpretation and for exploring the literatures and histories of environmentalism. The module encourages students to read from this present moment of climate change and to consider the creative and ethical insights available within past expressions of nature, of animals and non-human life, and of oceans, which Shakespeare calls “Neptune’s empire”.
learningOutcomes: Illustrate an detailed and critical awareness of a number of literary texts
Demonstrate the ability to apply relevant cultural and aesthetic theories and concepts to literatures and artistic practices
Demonstrate high quality verbal and written expression; the ability to make clear points in a lucid grammatical and confident manner.
Demonstrate an ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.
Identify and use relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources.
Write an essay on a chosen topic or topics, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.

modules.moduleContent: Students on this module will deepen their experience of creative writing by developing a mini-portfolio of writings around a particular theme, exploring the possibilities of their creative practice through writing in a variety of genres linked by that theme. Workshops will guide students towards the creation of their final portfolio via readings around the theme and a list of writing tasks which will lead students into writing in a range of genres (such as auto-fiction, documentary, poetry, found text, short story, flash fiction).

modules.moduleName: STRONG FEMALE CHARACTER: GENDER AND NEURODIVERSITY IN WRITING AND CULTURE
modules.moduleContent: This module examines the complex and evolving portrayal of gender, race and neurodiversity in contemporary texts through an intersectional lens which will also consider questions of class, ethnicity, and dis/ability. There has been a recent efflorescence of writing and cultural representations of neurodivergent girls, women/AFABs, which is challenging and changing stereotypes:  much of it is by ‘Aut’ writers/directors/artists, and is aligned with recent paradigm shifting interdisciplinary research on women, girls, and non-binary people. We examine how they explore familiar tropes and stereotypes of emphasised femininity, such as the ‘hot mess’, the ‘drama queen’, the ‘strong female character’, the ‘manic pixie dream girl’,  the ‘Sapphire/ angry back woman’, the ‘geek girl’, the ‘Jezebel’, and the ‘Karens’. We will also explore how these new texts interact with broader societal attitudes towards gender, race and neurodiversity.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a critical understanding of the ways in which formal elements of language and genre shape the representation of strong female characters and neurodiversity in the texts studied. (Links to assessment: Presentation, Essay)
Analyse how writers challenge, subvert, or reinforce generic expectations in their portrayals of gender and neurodiversity. (Links to assessment: Presentation, Essay)
Communicate complex ideas effectively through clear, concise, and grammatically accurate written and spoken English. (Links to assessment: Presentation, Essay)

Critically engage with a range of source materials, including literary texts, critical theory, and relevant media, demonstrating the ability to synthesize arguments, justify critical approaches, and develop independent thought. (Links to assessment: Presentation, Essay)

Apply critical thinking and research skills to produce a well-structured and evidence-based essay that integrates source material and demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of the module's key themes. (Links to assessment: Essay)
