modules.moduleContent: Oil “is at once everywhere and nowhere, indispensable yet largely unapprehended, not so much invisible as unseen” (2017, 11), writes Columbia University scholar and sometime Texan, Jennifer Wenzel, echoing and elaborating a problem first posed by Indian novelist, Amitav Ghosh (29) when he asked in his 1992 essay, “Petrofiction”, why the global oil industry, having produced such profound twentieth-century political, economic and sociocultural shifts, had not been addressed within the century’s hegemonic literary form, the novel. This module begins with the premise that the fossil-fuelled climate crisis is in part a crisis of representation and that the challenge of moving to a post-oil economy involves not only visibilising oil in literature and culture but apprehending the limits to our capacity to see or understand “what ‘it’ is doing at any given place at any given time” (Szeman, 2019, 7). We will explore oil representation in a range of contexts from Kuwait to Canada to the Niger Delta to Ireland and across a range of media, including poetry, life narrative, short stories, petrocultural theory, documentary film and television drama. The module will involve a visit to the Maynooth Library archives where students will view the death-row correspondence of writer and environmentalist, Ken Saro-Wiwa to Irish solidarity worker, Sr. Majella McCarren. Students taking this module will need access to a streaming service, such as Netflix, to screen the award-winning Danish political drama, Borgen: Power and Glory.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a complex knowledge of key literary texts.
Demonstrate a strong ability to assimilate and scrutinise critical readings
Show an ability to appraise, identify and use varied contemporary critical and theoretical approaches to literature and culture
Participate in seminar discussions; debate issues related to cultural context and socio-political circumstances;
Demonstrate high quality verbal and written expression; the ability to make clear points in a lucid grammatical and confident manner.
Write an essay on a chosen topic, demonstrating the ability to integrate source material and critical readings; synthesise arguments; justify critical approaches and opinions.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will be taught by intensive workshops, providing a space in which students will be guided towards honing their writing, reading, critical and self-editing skills. Working as a group, students will follow a curriculum of workshops designed by the seminar leader as an introduction to consideration of the formal, thematic and structural elements of their work. Students will explore a range of generic possibilities, both in their own work and by reading and hearing the work of other students, while working with selected reading material, and this will allow students to learn to ‘read’ the ways in which, for example, dialogue, character, plot or setting function in a given text. The workshops thus provide a space in which students are asked to analyse and create literary work in a focused way.
Such analysis and discussion will lead into the production of students’ own work, which functions as the central aim of the module. Students will draft work on an ongoing basis through the term, presenting material in class in addition to receiving feedback from their lecturer. Developing editing skills is also key to these workshops, as students learn how to draft and revise writing through work on both their own and on other students’ work. Incorporating feedback from both peers and their lecturer, students will submit, at the end of term, a finished piece of writing in addition to draft material.
learningOutcomes: Develop a sense of how to critically evaluate literary texts, including their own
Increase their knowledge of editorial processes
Increase their ability to edit their own work
Develop a sense of strategies for addressing structural/ thematic/ formal issues in one’s work

modules.moduleContent: This module examines whether and how literature can be 'engaged' with the actualities of the world. The module will investigate how literature and the acts of reading and interpreting texts can be understood to interrupt, subvert or alter material, ideological and political orthodoxies, and how literature functions as a broadly political force for change. Via a series of case studies, and by immersing themselves in theories of literature and culture as forms of engaged thought, students will explore texts which enable a re-imagining and reconfiguration of how things are and might be. Case studies will draw on the range of expertise in the Department to examine moments and places in which literature and its readers have sought to intervene in, engage with and transform the course of history, political orthodoxies and social norms. The aims of the module include: encouraging students to think in an informed and critical way about literature as a form of liberation, resistance or conformity; to develop well-constructed and incisive research questions about the socio-historic roles of literature.

modules.moduleName: LITERATURE OF POST-GOOD FRIDAY AGREEMENT NORTHERN IRELAND
modules.moduleContent: The Good Friday Agreement of 1998 (GFA) is regarded as marking the end of the Troubles in the North of Ireland. Literature played a key and fraught role during the Troubles. Once the peace process had resulted in the GFA, literature’s relationship to Northern Irish society and its history necessarily altered. This module examines poetry and fiction produced in the North of Ireland since 1998 and asks how writers and their literary texts have dealt with the ‘new’ Northern Ireland, with the trauma of the past, and with the uncertainty of the future, and whether new forms of identity have emerged inside the post-GFA period.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the historical and cultural contexts of literature in Northern Ireland since 1998
Demonstrate an ability to analyse literary texts informed by complex cultural contexts
Show a capacity to critique literary texts with close attention to detail

Define and explore a research question which arises from contextual and textual reading; understand and respond to a variety of critical perspectives; formulate an independent critical response to a research question

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to methods of conducting postgraduate research including obtaining, analysing, interpreting and synthesising information from a range of primary and secondary sources (print, e-resources, archives etc.) to engage research questions, develop an argument and substantiate conclusions while ensuring intellectual independence and research integrity. The module consists of a series of workshops that provide essential skills both for completing essays and the mini-dissertation as well as for any future career, especially with respect to conducting research efficiently and ethically; distinguishing facts from persuasive rhetoric; writing clearly, concisely, and compellingly. The workshops are delivered by the English Department academic staff members as well as colleagues from the Maynooth University Library and Writing Centre.

The module will combine presentations by research students with talks by invited speakers and members of the English Department faculty.  All student participants will be expected to take part; every student will be required to write a report on his/her attendance, in the course of a given year.  Full attendance at the seminars is required.

The objective of this module is to develop and deliver a research presentation before a panel of the Department.

Students will develop a formal research paper in conjunction with their research supervisors.

The objective of the module is to take part in an international or interinstitutional event in the field.

The student will take an active part in an international or interinstitutional event within the field.

This module is a foundational workshop that provides Creative Writing BA students with a grounding in key elements of structure, technique and form through writing practice, including fiction, creative non-fiction, and poetry. The course assists students in establishing and developing their writing practice through exercises and assignments, as well as through reading and the giving and receiving of feedback.

Following on the first semester’s foundational work in ENCW101, this module continues to develop students’ skills, expanding their experience working with different techniques and forms. Students will be introduced to a variety of texts that exemplify techniques and complement exercises and assignments, and will continue to develop their editing, revision and feedback skills.

This module serves as an introduction to Experimental Physics. Students initially explore concepts of physical measurement before an algebraic approach to analysing motion and forces is outlined. Subsequently both the mechanical and thermometric properties of matter are introduced with simple examples and numerical problems.

Topics in introductory astronomy including physics of the Earth, Solar System and large scale Universe, are also covered.

This module covers several topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics, but only at an introductory level.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the nature of physical knowledge, measurement of physical quantities and the use of units in describing the laws of nature.
Describe the physics of motion and forces (kinematics & dynamics) using an algebraic approach and vectorial description of motion.
Discuss the basic properties of matter and thermal physics.
State fundamental concepts and principles of modern astronomy, including the terminology and nomenclature relating to a range of astronomical classification (eg. stellar and galactic).
Discuss the distance scale of the Universe, the Big Bang theory of origin and the fate of the Universe.
Discuss the Earth as a planet in terms of its composition, atmosphere and origin.
Solve simple numerical problems relevant to fundamental mechanics, thermodynamics and astronomy.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to develop a solid understanding of the principles of Physical Optics and give the students an introduction to modern optics including the principle of lasers, fibre optic technology and other applications such as holography and polarisation.

Optics: Interference, Young's double slit experiment, phasor addition of waves, multiple slits, interference in thin films, Michelson interferometer; Diffraction, single slit, circular aperture, resolution, diffraction grating, spectral resolution; Polarization, Brewster's angle, law of Malus.

Lasers: Principle of operation with outline of various types of laser and related application, introduction to Einstein coefficients, Q-switching, laser modes and analysis of laser light output

Fibre Optics: Basis of operation, single versus multi-mode operation and introduction to telecommunication system,

Holography: Basic principles, experimental arrangements and modern applications

Polarisation: Basic principles, polarising techniques with examples and modern applications

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- Interference and diffraction at single and multiple apertures

- Dispersion by prisms and diffraction gratings

- Optical Cavities and laser action
learningOutcomes: Compare and contrast geometric and physical optics.
Apply the mathematics of interference of light to explain relevant optical phenomena and the operation of instruments based on those phenomena.
Explain the different emission and absorption processes for a photon with a simple atomic model and describe the different processes through the Einstein coefficients.
Describe the operation of a typical laser in terms of its main components and their role. Discuss basic types of lasers (solid state, gas liquid) and identify their areas of application.
Describe the principle of holography, including the optical arrangement to produce a hologram and potential applications.
Outline the methods of polarising light and the different polarisation states that can be generated.
Solve simple numerical problems related to lasers and optical fibres.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to introduce students to a range of topics in modern astronomy, as well as astronomical telescopes, instrumentation & techniques.

Astronomy: Celestial coordinates & time, the ISM, star formation, stellar nucleosynthesis, binary systems, exoplanets, gamma-ray bursts,  the cosmological distance ladder, large-scale structure, introduction to special relativity.

Astronomical instrumentation: Telescopes, resolution, aberrations, atmospheric seeing, Schmidt telescope, active optics, adaptive optics, optical detectors; Radio telescopes, interferometry; X-ray telescopes, other wavebands.
learningOutcomes: Describe the classification and basic properties of astronomical objects.
Discuss time measurements, coordinate frames and distance scales used in astronomical observations.
Describe the factors important in limiting image quality in astronomical telescope observations including angular resolution, aberrations and atmospheric seeing.
Apply the principles governing telescope optics to different telescope configurations including Cassegrain and Schmidt telescopes.
Explain the operation of interferometry in astronomy including the michelson stellar interferometers, radio interferometers and modern optical interferometers.
Solve simple numerical problems relevant to astronomy using appropriate units.
Carry out relevant laboratory experiments to demonstrate and test physical principles and report the results.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to develop both macroscopic and microscopic approaches to thermodynamics, to explain why systems move into particular equilibrium states. The classical macroscopic approach uses cyclical processes and heat engines to explore the Second law of thermodynamics (increase in entropy of an isolated system undergoing an irreversible process). The approach of statistical mechanics links the concept of entropy to the number of possible arrangements of the microscopic particles comprising the system, and uses the system partition function to derive thermodynamic properties.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- Probability distributions

- Black body radiation

- Pauli exclusion principle, fermions and bosons and elementary particles

- Phonons and heat capacity

- Temperature scales, work, internal energy and heat capacity

- Entropy, free energies and the Carnot Cycle

- Changes of state

- Kinetic theory of gases and the gas laws to Van der Waals equation

- Statistical basis of entropy

- Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution

- Bose-Einstein and Fermi-Dirac distributions

- Density of states and partition function
learningOutcomes: Analyse cyclical processes (heat engines, heat pumps, refrigerators).
Discuss the necessity for the Second law of thermodynamics, to include using the Carnot cycle to impose limits on performance of real devices.
Predict equilibrium states of systems using thermodynamic potential functions and Maxwell relations. Analysis of phase changes.
Distinguish between concepts of macrostates and microstates with particular emphasis on statistical interpretation of entropy.
Use the Boltzmann distribution law and the partition function to derive thermodynamic properties of systems.
Discuss concepts of degeneracy, distinguishable particles, indistinguishable particles, and classical versus quantum statistics.
Discuss examples of systems described by Fermi-Dirac and Bose-Einstein statistics.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to develop a solid understanding of the theories, methods and experimental evidence in introductory Solid State Physics, including crystallography, thermal, electric, magnetic and optical properties of crystalline solids.

Specific topics that are covered include:

Crystallography: crystal structures, X-ray diffraction, Bragg and Laue diffraction conditions, reciprocal lattice, Brillouin zones.

Thermal properties: vibrations in crystals, monatomic and diatomic chain, acoustical and optical dispersion curves, specific heat models, Dulong and Petit model, Einstein model, Debye model, phonons, phonon-phonon scattering, thermal conductivity.

Magnetic properties: diamagnetism, paramagnetism, ferromagnetism, anti-ferromagnetism, magnetic domains.

Electric properties: free-electron theory, band theory and classification of solids, methods of calculating band structure, Bloch functions, Fermi surfaces, real metals, electron and hole dynamics, intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors, carrier mobility. 

Optical properties of solids.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- Calculus to the level of multiple integrals; solution of linear ordinary and differential equations

- Mechanical properties of matter to include elasticity and thermal expansion

- Phonons and heat capacity

- Crystal structure and Bragg scattering

- Electron theory of solids to the level of simple band structure

- Semiconductors and doping

- Magnetic properties of matter

- Waves in linear media to the level of group velocity

- Bose-Einstein and Fermi-Dirac distributions



   MP464 cannot be taken together with EP402.
learningOutcomes: Discuss established models and theories of thermal, optical, electric and magnetic properties of crystalline solids.
Outline historical models and theories of thermal, optical, electric and magnetic properties of crystalline solids and their limits of applicability.
Describe experimental methods that are used to investigate properties of solids.
Outline the key experimental evidence relevant to introductory solid state physics.
Solve simple numerical problems related to introductory solid state physics using appropriate units.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to introduce students to a range of techniques for solving numerical problems in the physical sciences and develop skills in computer programming.

Module content: graphing functions, rootfinding, numerical differentiation and integration, interpolation, Euler & Runge-Kutta methods for solving ordinary differential equations, Monte-Carlo methods, physical examples.
learningOutcomes: Use  basic programming structures in recognised programming language (variables, mathematical expressions, control statements, functions).
Write algorithms and implement programs to solve numerical problems involving differentiation, integration, root-finding, differential equations and Monte-Carlo simulations.
Discuss performance issues such as numerical error, stability, speed and memory requirements.
Develop a working program that models a simple physical system.
Present project results through a written report and an oral presentation.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to further develop the student's knowledge of physics and develop the skills required in research.

Module content: Student carries out an advanced physics project under the supervision of individual departmental staff members. Research skills are introduced, a project report is to be produced and an oral presentation made of the work completed.
learningOutcomes: Carry out an advanced level research project to demonstrate physical principles
Keep an accurate and detailed record of experimental work
Discuss research progress and issues arising with academic and technical members of staff and their peers
Analyse and critically assess experimental data and possible sources of error
Work as a member of a team to define and achieve goals in an effective way
Write a high-quality and timely technical report detailing the background and results of their work
Deliver an oral presentation to communicate the most important aspects of their work to staff and fellow students

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to develop advanced concepts and formal methods of quantum mechanics and their application to atomic physics.

Module content

Quantum Physics: Time-dependent Schrödinger equation, operator formalism, eigenvalue equations, probability amplitudes, angular momentum, commutation relations, Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle, matrix mechanics, spin one-half system, perturbation theory, two-particle systems.

Atomic Physics: fine structure of hydrogen atom, Lamb shift, radiative transitions, introduction to laser spectroscopy, alkali atoms and quantum defect, hyperfine structure, helium atom, exclusion principle, LS and jj coupling in multi-electron atoms, atoms in external fields, cooling and trapping of atoms, Bose-Einstein condensation.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- Calculus to the level of multiple integrals; solution of linear ordinary and differential equations

- Three-dimensional trigonometry

- Matrices to the level of eigenvalues and eigenvectors

- Wave function and its interpretation

- Standard solutions and quantum numbers,to the level of the hydrogen atom

- First order time independent perturbation theory

- Quantum structure and spectra of simple atoms
learningOutcomes: Describe the essential concepts of quantum mechanics, such as the wavefunction, the wave equation, eigenvalue equations, operators, angular momentum and spin, commutation and uncertainty relations, matrix mechanics, perturbation theory.
Apply the concepts of quantum mechanics to a selection of model physical systems.
Apply quantum mechanics to the hydrogen atom and discuss electron spin, spin-orbit interaction, fine structure, selection rules, optical transitions, and laser spectroscopy.
Describe the relevant interactions in multi-electron atoms and how these affect the properties of these atoms.
Discuss the properties and interactions of atoms in external fields, in particular magnetic fields (the Zeeman effect) and laser fields (cooling and trapping of atoms).
Solve relevant conceptual and numerical problems.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to develop a solid understanding of nuclear physics, its applications in nuclear medicine, and elementary particle physics.

Module content: 

Nuclear and Particle Physics: liquid drop model, shell model of nucleus, nuclear spin, Gamov, Condon & Gurney model of alpha-decay, Fermi theory of beta-decay, gamma-decay, applications in nuclear medicine: PET, CT, MRI, human exposure to radiation, radiation dosimetry, the Standard Model, quarks, leptons and hadrons, the Eightfold Way, conservation laws, isospin, strangeness, parity violation, CP violation in neutral K-meson decay, CPT theorem.

This module specifically addresses the following topics of the Core of Physics, as defined by Institute of Physics:

- Nuclear masses and binding energies

- Radioactive decay, fission and fusion

- Pauli exclusion principle, fermions and bosons and elementary particles

- Fundamental forces and the Standard Model
learningOutcomes: Describe simple nuclear models including the liquid drop model and the shell model.
Discuss the important features and theoretical models of alpha, beta and gamma decay.
Solve basic numerical problems involving radioactive decay.
Explain the basic principles and the underlying nuclear physics of important medical applications such as PET scanning, MRI and radiaiton therapy.
Describe the Standard Model of particle physics.
Explain the concept of CP violation in weak interactions and its implication for symmetry in the early universe.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this modules is to further develop the student's knowledge of astrophysics and develop the skills required in research.

Module content: Student carries out an advanced astrophysics project under the supervision of individual departmental staff members. Research skills will be developed, a project report is to be produced and an oral presentation made of the work completed. Project may involve a field trip to a remote observatory abroad during the first semester to collect data.
learningOutcomes: Carry out an advanced level research project to demonstrate astrophysical principles.
Keep an accurate and detailed record of experimental work.
Discuss research progress and issues arising with academic and technical members of staff and their peers.
Analyse and critically assess experimental data and possible sources of error.
Work as a member of a team to define and achieve goals in an effective way.
Write a high-quality and timely technical report detailing the background and results of their work.
Deliver an oral presentation to communicate the most important aspects of their work to staff and fellow students.

modules.moduleName: SKILLS FOR SUCCESS: PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT AND EMPLOYABILITY
modules.moduleContent: The key purpose of this module is to facilitate undergraduate students in their academic, personal and professional career development, so that they will be well equipped to secure internships and to successfully enter the graduate labour market. Students from a diverse range of disciplines will be encouraged to develop a strong sense of self-awareness, as well as enabled to identify and develop a range of employability skills which will be pivotal to their future career. The module provides students with the opportunity to engage in effective career planning, to gain an insight into the graduate labour market and an appreciation of how to effectively manage the recruitment process. Therefore, this module will contribute to the development of well-informed and skilled graduates who can compete effectively in the labour market and beyond, as well as excel in their chosen career.
learningOutcomes: Gain an insight into organisational culture and preferred working styles
Develop self-awareness and understand the importance of reflective practice and its role in effective, continuous professional development and lifelong learning
Reflect on and assimilate information to help inform future career planning
Structure and articulate ideas effectively, orally and in writing.
Recognise the skills, qualities and abilities graduate employers seek in potential employees, and how they are evaluated
Identify methods used to assess candidates during selection processes
Understand how to exploit their skills in sourcing, securing and excelling in their chosen future career.

modules.moduleContent:
Students, through their Department, may have an opportunity to visit an appropriate workplace outside Maynooth University to gain work-related experience relevant to their research. Such opportunities are limited and are only possible when arranged by the student’s supervisor and agreed with the host organisation. It is recommended that a supervisor in the host organisation is involved in planning the specific learning outcomes for the student along with the student’s MU supervisor. These learning outcomes must be appropriate to the period of the work-based placement. A reflective journal should be maintained by the student to record their learning experience on this module. It is also expected that the supervisor in the host organisation will report to the MU supervisor on the student’s activities during the placement.

Indicative content: Completion of this module may require the student to attend workshops or seminars, complete training (including online training), maintain a reflective journal recording the student’s experience of their learning and/or completion of specific tasks or assessments. The content will be designed by the student’s supervisor so as to ensure that the student can achieve the module’s learning outcomes and that the supervisor can assess that the learning outcomes have been met by the student. The duration of the work-based placement should be decided by the supervisor in consultation with the host organization.
learningOutcomes: Describe the new knowledge, skills and perspectives gained from their experience in the workplace.
Demonstrate ability to solve problems and carry out research in a work environment.
Write a comprehensive report describing their achievements during this placement.
Present orally their work-based experience to an audience in the host organisation and/or a local audience.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This course introduces students to the use of Excel in Financial Modeling. The course covers standard financial models in the areas of corporate finance, financial statement simulation and portfolio selection. 
We will use real-world data from the Bloomberg Terminals to implement the financial models.
learningOutcomes: Construct detailed financial models and be able to make full use of advanced spread sheet features.
Develop financial models appropriate in areas such as capital investment appraisal and portfolio diversification.
Apply a variety of techniques to reach a plausible valuation of a firm.
Analyse differences in valuations between firms.
Use and develop spreadsheet based solutions to financial problems.

modules.moduleContent: This course studies the core financial decisions that companies make with a particular emphasis on equity and firm valuation. We reformulate financial statements into their operating and financial components, for the purpose of valuation. We perform financial statement analysis based on the reformulated income statement, balance sheet, cash flow statement, and statement of shareholders' equity to provide a better understanding of the underlying sources of firm profitability and firm growth. We apply accounting-based equity valuation techniques and other valuation technologies to value companies.  We derive leverage adjusted price-earnings and price-book ratios. With the aid of real-life examples, we show that free cash flow and dividend valuation models are flawed models of company valuation.
learningOutcomes: Interpret and reformulate financial statements for the purpose of equity and firm valuation;
Perform financial statement analysis using reformulated financial statements which clearly distinguishes between the operations and financing activities of a business;
Understand the underlying sources of firm profitability and firm growth;
Apply accounting-based equity valuation and other valuation methods to value a company;
Calculate leverage adjusted price-earnings and price-book ratios;
Understand the limitations of the free cash flow and dividend valuation models; and
Perform active valuations.

modules.moduleContent: The objective of this module is to introduce the concepts of sustainable finance from the viewpoint of asset owners and managers. This module will help students understand how the current financial system is been transformed due to climate change and environmental concerns and how this shift is driving the flow of capital.
learningOutcomes: Apply the principles of sustainable finance to distinct scenarios in the sector,
Synthesize the effects of global environmental challenges into a framework that demonstrates impact on the financial system,
Diagnose the sustainability challenges facing business and financial systems,
Critically evaluate the role of finance in the transition to a sustainable economy.

modules.moduleContent: The module introduces the students to IFS 2025, the strategy to ensure Ireland continues to stay competitive as one of the world’s leading financial centres. It discusses the key elements of the Irish Fintech ecosystem, the regulatory bodies and the key regulations and regulatory approaches that ensure Fintech businesses are aligned with relevant EU directives.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate current problems and/or innovations at forefront of the field
Consolidate complex knowledge from a variety of sources to assess their impact on sustainable financial technology and innovation
Evaluate the potential long-term impact of regulatory actions.
Develop informed and innovative solutions to complex industry issues.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the ways in which language and culture are interwoven, with a particular focus on French language and culture. It trains students in the skills necessary for exploring language as a system and looks at how linguistic issues are represented in particular examples from film, literature, music, and visual culture. Students will learn how to identify and talk about linguistic and cultural phenomena and explore how they play out in everyday life. 
The module will equip students with the vocabulary and terminology to talk about language, as well as helping them to understand how language is used in different social and cultural contexts. Students will have an opportunity to explore this through close reading of different types of aural, oral, written, and digital texts. This module will consider how language and culture both reflect and shape the way we view the world. It will consist of three units, each of which will focus on a particular linguistic and cultural issue: 
Unit 1: Language Variation and Change 
Unit 2: Language Politics 
Unit 3: Language and Migration 
This module will offer students who want to study a language to degree-level a thorough grounding in the skills necessary for success in their studies. It will also appeal to students with an interest in and passion for languages and how they work in different social contexts.

IMPORTANT NOTE: 
Since this module focuses on French-language texts, including aural, oral, written, and digital texts, and parts of the module may be delivered through the medium of French, some prior knowledge of French is required. (Recommended: Higher-level French at Leaving Certificate or equivalent)
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms the structural similarities and differences between French and English;
Describe in some detail how the French language is used in different regions and countries where French is spoken, using examples where appropriate;
Interpret and analyse how language is used in cultural texts and performances through close readings and contextualisation;
Design and create an infographic that represents relevant data in a visually stimulating way;
Research, prepare and deliver an oral presentation;
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and well-structured argument.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Translating texts from English to French based on a dossier of passages from texts by contemporary writers.  Grammatical, stylistic and syntactic differences between English and French.
Students will be taught to be sensitive to elements of style, language, and tone in their translations.
learningOutcomes: Define key terms describing the changes in syntax that take place when translating.
Demonstrate a high level of accuracy in the translation of texts from English to French.
To recognise grammatical, stylistic and syntactic differences between English and French, and to use this awareness when translating.

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims at developing students' competence in French and is suitable for students who have never studied French before.  Students will also be introduced to the cultures and societies of French-speaking countries through various media.  The course also aims to develop students' confidence and accuracy when using the language in a range of situations.  It is an intensive accelerated course in French language and culture, designed to provide a solid foundation for students' further studies in French.  Lectures are supported by language laboratory work and conversation classes.

This module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR) at level A1, where the emphasis is on achieving a fundamental level of communication in all four skills, (listening, speaking, reading and writing).  A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A1.
learningOutcomes: Recognise and construct sentences based on the rules of French grammar.
Develop basic vocabulary and apply it to the discussion of everyday situations.
Explain and summarise the content of relatively straightforward written and audio texts.
Read and comment on short French literary texts.

modules.moduleContent: This module explores what it means to be French and how French identity is constructed through film, tv series, and graphic novels. We will pay specific attention to bodies to investigate questions of history, nationhood, gender, sexuality, race, and class. This module looks at key moments of French history and culture—Resistance, French feminism, colonization, Islam, HIV/AIDS—and analyses power dynamics that shape or undo Frenchness
learningOutcomes: Explain in broad terms key moments that define French history and culture.
Demonstrate critical and analytical skills by delivering subtle and cogent readings of various visual media.
Interpret visual media through technical reading, application of theories and contextualisation.
Critically evaluate secondary literature.
Plan, research and deliver an oral presentation, using appropriate visual aids where necessary.
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and well-structured argument.

modules.moduleName: FRENCH FOR BUSINESS AND PROFESSIONAL PURPOSES
modules.moduleContent: Optional intermediate module in French focused on developing students’ linguistic and intercultural competencies in the francophone professional world.
learningOutcomes: Read and extract information from business-related texts, charts and graphs, and summarise that information in oral and written form.
Describe working environments, relationships with colleagues, employee and company profiles, business graphics, and business performance using appropriate vocabulary and structures.

Communicate effectively with a variety of interlocutors, e.g. colleagues, customers, and executives, and demonstrate appropriate language skills for negotiation and mediation with these interlocutors in written and oral form.
Produce some business-related texts, such as formal and informal emails and written reports.
Justify a business idea or product in both written and oral form.
Work effectively as part of a team to deliver a presentation in a coherent and meaningful manner.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness and sociolinguistic competencies, as they relate to the business world.

modules.moduleContent:
Grammar, written and aural comprehension, oral discussion and expression on a variety of topics.

Students obtaining a grade of 60% and above on completing this module can be deemed to have attained a CEFRL level of B1.1
learningOutcomes: Recognise and produce accurately (orally and in written form) a wide variety of grammatical and syntactical structures in French;
Engage in discussion and critical analysis in both the written and oral forms with a degree of spontaneity and fluency;
Analyse a selected text/extract in detail and identify its key points;
Understand, analyse and retrieve information from a passage in oral French at the required level.

modules.moduleContent:
Grammar, written and aural comprehension, oral discussion and expression on a variety of topics.

Students obtaining a grade of 60% and above on completing this module can be deemed to have attained a CEFRL level of B.1.2. This module is required for students wishing to fulfill Teaching Council requirements.
learningOutcomes: Recognise and manipulate accurately a wide variety of grammatical and syntactical structures in both spoken and written French;
Demonstrate critical reading skills and analyse complex texts in French;
Produce accurate and concise summaries in French on a variety of topics;
Develop personal ideas and opinions and express them accurately through written and spoken French.

modules.moduleName: BEAUVOIR ET APRES? LES ÉCRITURES FÉMININES AU XXE ET XXIE SIÉCLES
modules.moduleContent: Starting from the key concepts of "Le Deuxième sexe", this module will explore aspects of contemporary women's writing in French, with a close study of two representative texts.
learningOutcomes: Analyse the role and influence of feminist thought in the texts studied, including construction and implications of gender;
Demonstrate an in-depth knowledge of the texts studied and an ability to compare and contrast the texts;
Evaluate the role played in textual communication by elements such as genre, voice and point of view.

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims at developing students' competence in French further, building on the skills and knowledge acquired in FR151.  The course continues to develop students' confidence and accuracy when using the language in a broad range of situations.

This module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR) at level A2, where the emphasis is on achieving a pre-intermediate level of communication in all four skills, (listening, speaking, reading and writing).  A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A2.2.
learningOutcomes: Construct brief essays on a variety of subjects; translate sentences from French to English; show an understanding of grammar rules.
Converse in French with some degree of fluency about areas of immediate relevance - basic and family information, university studies, student life etc.  Apply new vocabulary introduced in class.
Produce a variety of texts based on work done in classes; summarise the meaning of texts; employ vocabulary suited to a particular topic or area of language.
Complete continuous assessment and class assignments correctly; answer exam questions with relevant and precise information based on course work throughout the semester.
Read and analyse short French literary texts; comment orally and in writing.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the ways in which language and culture are interwoven, with a particular focus on French language and culture. It trains students in the skills necessary for exploring language as a system and looks at how linguistic issues are represented in particular examples from film, literature, music, and visual culture.
Students will learn how to identify and talk about linguistic and cultural phenomena and explore how they play out in everyday life. The module will equip students with the vocabulary and terminology to talk about language, as well as helping them to understand how language is used in different social and cultural contexts. Students will have an opportunity to explore this through close reading of different types of aural, oral, written, and digital texts.
This module will consider how language and culture both reflect and shape the way we view the world. It will consist of three units, each of which will focus on a particular linguistic and cultural issue:
Unit 1: Visual Culture, Arts and the Media
Unit 2: Intertextuality - Borrowing and Stealing
Unit 3: Gender & Diversity in Language and Culture
This module will offer students who want to study a language to degree-level a thorough grounding in the skills necessary for success in their studies. It will also appeal to students with an interest in and passion for languages and how they work in different social contexts.

IMPORTANT NOTE: 
Since this module focuses on French-language texts, including aural, oral, written, and digital texts, and parts of the module may be delivered through the medium of French, some prior knowledge of French is required. (Recommended: Higher-level French at Leaving Certificate or equivalent)
learningOutcomes: Interpret and analyse how language is used in cultural texts and performances through close readings and contextualisation;
Interpret and critically analyse literary and visual texts related to the French-speaking world;
Research, prepare and deliver a group presentation using visual aids, where appropriate;
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and well-structured argument.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Intensive skills in French: intensive reading, comprehension, writing and speaking in French. Grammar exercises, writing of short essays, conversation and the phonetics of the language, as applied to students' pronunciation, texts on topics relating to French culture and society, introduction to reading literary texts in French.


Students obtaining a grade of 60% and above on completing this module can be deemed to have attained a CEFRL level of B1.1
learningOutcomes: Recognise and effectively manipulate a wide range of tenses and other grammatical structures in the French language;
Extrapolate information from a variety of written and audio texts and summarise the key points;
Derive and reuse new vocabulary from reading and listening exercises;
Converse in French with a degree of fluency and spontaneity;

Produce written texts that display an awareness of French syntax, a level of accuracy of approximately B1 level according to the European Framework and an ability to construct coherent arguments in a second language;
Analyse a literary text and employ appropriate terminology when analysing a text/passage;
Identify formal characteristics of a literary text and identify key themes in texts which have been studied.

modules.moduleContent: In this module, students will reflect critically on the French language as an object of inquiry. Over the course of the semester, the module will offer an overview of the language’s structure through the study of its semantics, lexis, syntax, morphology and phonology. With its emphasis on usage, the module provides insight into major sites of variation as well as normative structures. It will contribute to the improvement of students’ understanding of how French works and provide a foundation for future study in linguistics.
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms key concepts related to language structure, including phonetics and phonology, morphology, syntax, lexis, and semantics;
Describe the patterns of the normative sound system of French;
Identify major sites of variation in the pronunciation of contemporary French;
Apply knowledge of French phonology to improve their accuracy in normative spoken French;
Apply knowledge of contrasts between written and spoken forms in French to improve their own expression in various situations.

modules.moduleContent: The relationship between the visual arts and literature has been attracting increasing critical attention over the past twenty years, and photography has been written about and used for a variety of purposes by literary authors since its invention in 1839. Exploring several modern writers’ appropriations of this new image-making system gives us insights into the way they think about writing, as practice and product, as well as their understanding of photography as a distinctive image-making system.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate a general understanding of the history of photography.
situate the works studied in relation to the cultural practices connected with photography at the time they were written, and draw comparisons between the texts.
explore the ways photography is used thematically and stylistically by the writers on the course.
trace the changes that have appeared over 180 years of photographic history.
apply critical reading skills to various forms of written French.

select, evaluate, and organize primary and secondary material, demonstrating an ability to synthesize material from disparate sources and to take account of the wider cultural context of the topic under consideration.
articulate your views in speech and writing using the appropriate discourse for the subject.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at students who are interested in learning French alongside the core subjects of their degree programmes.  It will appeal to all types of students - those who have a genuine interest in learning about the French language and culture for their own sakes as well as those who are motivated to develop French language skills and (inter) cultural competence to enhance their employment prospects in Ireland and further afield.  French as an elective subject is open to Second Year undergraduate students from all three faculties as well as postgraduate students of any level and will be delivered on a blended-learning basis with 2 live contact hours per week accompanied by asynchronous content accessible on Moodle at any time.  The module aims at developing confidence and accuracy in French through various media (texts, images, videos, online resources) and to enable students to use the French language in a range of contexts and situations.

This module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFRL) at level A1.1, where the emphasis is on achieving a fundamental level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing).  A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A1.1.

Please note: Prospective students should note that this language elective is intended for beginners. Students who took French for the Leaving Certificate examination (or equivalent) cannot be accommodated in this class but should consider signing up for a Beginners’ language elective in Chinese, German, or Spanish instead.
learningOutcomes: Answer simple questions and offer some personal details, e.g. name, nationality, address, age, hobbies, in written and spoken form, and ask the same questions of another person;
Extract key information from simple written, audio and video texts related to situations from everyday life that contain words and expressions dealt with in class;
Write simple messages and communicate in a very simple way about situations related to everyday life and using vocabulary dealt with in class.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at students who are interested in continuing to develop their French language skills as a follow on from FR260.  It will appeal to all types of students - those who have a genuine interest in deepening their knowledge of the French language and culture as well as those who are motivated to develop French language skills and (inter)cultural competence to enhance their employment prospects on the domestic and international labour markets.  French as an elective subject is open to Second Year undergraduate and all postgraduate students and will be delivered on a blended-learning basis with two live contact hours per week accompanied by asynchronous content accessible on Moodle at any time.  This module aims to continue to develop students' confidence and accuracy and to enable them to use the French language in a range of contexts and situations.

This module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR) at level A1.2, where the emphasis is on achieving a pre-intermediate level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing). A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A1.2.

Please note: Prospective students should note that this language elective is intended for beginners. Students who took French for the Leaving Certificate examination (or equivalent) cannot be accommodated in this class but should consider signing up for a Beginners’ language elective in Chinese, German, or Spanish instead.
learningOutcomes: Explain and summarise key information from relatively straightforward written, audio and video texts related to situations from everyday life that contain words and expressions dealt with in class;

Exchange relatively straightforward information in spoken or written form about family, occupation, plans, holidays, daily and leisure activities, and/or other familiar matters relating to everyday life;

Produce short written texts using familiar structures and expressions and communicate in relatively straightforward terms on topics of relevance to everyday life and using vocabulary dealt with in class.

modules.moduleName: THE POWER OF NETFLIX IN FRANCE
modules.moduleContent: This module focuses exclusively on Netflix TV series in French and explores the ways in which these shows shape and/or expand our understanding of Frenchness. Through an intersectional approach that considers race, ethnicity, sexuality, class, and nationhood, and focusing on different genres (comedy, horror, thriller, YA, etc.), we analyse how global and local networks of power relations give way to 1) new sites of racial difference, 2) more inclusive models of feminism/queerness, 3) a reassessment of the Paris/Province dichotomy, and 4) new explorations of French history.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate critical and analytical skills by delivering subtle and cogent readings of tv series.
Interpret visual media through technical reading, application of theories and contextualisation.
Critically evaluate secondary literature.
Plan, research and deliver an oral presentation, using appropriate visual aids where necessary.
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and well-structured argument.

modules.moduleContent:
Intensive skills in French: intensive reading, comprehension, writing and speaking in French. This course will cover a range of grammatical topics, grammar exercises, writing of short texts on a variety of topics, conversation and aural comprehension, reading of different types of texts in French and the phonetics of the language, as applied to students' pronunciation.

Students obtaining a grade of 60% and above on completing this module can be deemed to have attained a CEFRL level of A2.2
learningOutcomes: Recognise and effectively manipulate a range of tenses and other grammatical structures in the French language;
Extrapolate information from a variety of written and audio texts;
Derive and use new vocabulary from reading and listening exercises;
Converse in French with a degree of fluency and spontaneity;

Produce written texts that display an awareness of French syntax, a level of accuracy of approximately A2.2 level according to the European Framework and an ability to construct coherent arguments in a second language;
Read short texts pertaining to topics in contemporary French society and culture.

modules.moduleContent: Translating from French to English.  Reflecting on translation as a process and explaining translation choices. Introduction to practical and theoretical aspects of translation using a wide variety of text types.  The course will follow a translation text book.
learningOutcomes: Recognise different text types and the translation challenges relating to these.
Perform an analysis of both grammar and lexis prior to translating.
Have an understanding of the main translation challenges presented by unseen texts.
Be able to produce an accurate translation of an unseen text.
Understand the main translation strategies available when translating between English and French, based on the inherent structures of the two languages.
Use and evaluate different kinds of translation tools.

modules.moduleContent:
Academic and technical writing in French on selected topics relating to contemporary France. Revision of grammar and other related written work designed to foster comprehension and accuracy. Oral discussion, interaction and expression on topics relating to contemporary France and the French-speaking world.

Students obtaining a grade of 60% and above on completing this module can be deemed to have attained a CEFRL level of C1.1. This module is required for students wishing to fulfil Teaching Council requirements.
learningOutcomes: Produce grammatically accurate and syntactically coherent sentences in written and spoken French;
Construct a coherent written argument in French using a plan to explain the pros and cons of a given topic;
Produce technical writing for a specific industry, target;
Communicate in spoken French with a high level of accuracy and spontaneity, with an awareness of register and idiom;
Express in spoken French a range of opinions on a topic relating to contemporary French society or to the French speaking world.

modules.moduleName: MAKING ROME: CULTURE, POLITICS AND SOCIETY IN THE ROMAN REPUBLIC
modules.moduleContent: The ancient Roman Republic was a sophisticated society with a culture which still resonates today. Its political fortunes were shaped by such colossal figures as Julius Caesar, Cicero, Cleopatra and Augustus, but it was not just an arena for political debate. This module offers you the chance to explore the lives of the ancient Romans: men and women, children and adults, senators and slaves. It finds them at work and at leisure, presents their myths and heroes, and examines their art and their literature. Finally, the module asks what led such a powerful state to fail. The final century of the Roman Republic saw the rise of popular politics, political infighting, assassination and war, much of it related in the first-hand accounts of the generation who lived through it. How was Caesar greeted on his return from conquering Gaul? What did Cicero really think of Cleopatra? And what was the purpose of Virgil’s epic poem, the Aeneid? As we shall see, all of them had a role in the making and unmaking of Rome.
learningOutcomes: Summarise the major historical developments of the Roman Republican period.
Identify the key cultural elements and values which characterised the Roman Republic.
Explain the principles underlying the political, social and cultural changes in this period.
Recognise the methods by which an ancient society can be approached and understood.
Display a broadened understanding of the differences between ancient and modern societies.
Demonstrate the ability to communicate ideas in written form.

modules.moduleContent: After a brief survey of the Pre-Socratics and Socrates, we move to themes in Platonic philosophy, particularly through Plato’s most famous dialogue, the Republic. This dialogue is ostensibly concerned with justice/morality, but soon, with the literary Socrates as our guide, Plato turns this into an examination of the nature of the ideal state. The ideas considered in the early Platonic dialogues are seen to recur in the Republic, and the module demonstrates the essential interconnectedness of Plato’s principal themes. The second part of the module turns to the philosophy of Aristotle, particularly through his Nicomachean Ethics, arguably the most important work on ethics in antiquity. Aristotle is chiefly concerned with identifying the ‘good’ for human beings. Unlike Plato, who sought human fulfilment on a higher metaphysical level, Aristotle investigates the activities of ordinary living at ground level, so to speak. He outlines the kinds of behaviour we ought to engage in and identifies some of the requirements for a happy life: health, good fortune, and friendship. The module seeks to elucidate the immediate political and social context of Aristotle’s ideas and to examine the way(s) in which he thought we might achieve happiness.
learningOutcomes: Explain the key philosophical ideas of Plato and Aristotle.
Discuss the philosophical background to Plato’s Republic.
Explain the relationship between philosophy and politics in Plato’s Republic.
Discuss the philosophical background to Aristotle’s Nicomachean Ethics.
Understand Aristotle’s concept of virtue.
Demonstrate the ability to communicate complex ideas in both oral and written form.

modules.moduleName: A WORLD FULL OF GODS: RELIGIOUS LIFE IN THE ROMAN EMPIRE
modules.moduleContent:
"The various modes of worship, which prevailed in the Roman world, were all considered by the people as equally true; by the philosopher, as equally false; and by the magistrate, as equally useful.” Edward Gibbon’s famous statement points to the complexity of religious life in the Roman world, where religion was inseparable from all aspects of life: this was indeed “a world full of gods.” 
This module offers a wide-ranging study of religion in the Roman world, from early to late. We begin with religion in the Republic, as it was intimately bound up with the family, the state, and ultimately the conquest of the Mediterranean, bringing about the formation of a pantheon of “all” the gods. We turn then to religion under the Empire, examining how practices such as emperor worship, gladiatorial spectacles, and voluntary associations often served to consolidate imperial authority. Finally, we will look at later periods of the Empire, as religious enthusiasm was often used to bolster political unity while at the same time it posed challenges to the Roman order itself, most notably in the cases of Judaism and Christianity. Throughout, attention will be given to primary sources in historiography, literature, art, philosophy, graffiti and other material culture, as well as modern sociological approaches to religious phenomena.
learningOutcomes: describe the various functions which religious practices and beliefs fulfilled for communities, groups and individuals in the Roman world;
understand the interaction between religion and imperial power, both at Rome and in the wider world;
show familiarity with a wide range of evidence, including literary, material, artistic, epigraphic and numismatic evidence, pertaining to religious life in the Roman world;
evaluate the contributions of important modern scholarship on Roman religions;

appreciate how the application of anachronistic modern ideas about religion can distort our understanding of ancient religious conceptions, including in the cases of ancient Judaism and early Christianity;
demonstrate the ability to communicate complex ideas in both oral and written form.

modules.moduleContent: Kingship was the dominant political institution in the ancient Mediterranean world and exercised a continuing fascination even for Greeks and Romans who lived in proudly non-monarchical regimes. We will begin by surveying kingship in the Archaic Mediterranean world, primarily through the lens of Homer’s poems and Herodotus’ History but with some reference to comparative Persian and Hebrew material. We turn then to central texts of the fourth-century BC: selected passages from Plato’s Republic, Aristotle’s Politics, Xenophon’s Cyropaedia and Isocrates’ kingship orations offer different visions of the ideal ruler as well as competing definitions of philosophy and wisdom. After touching on Hellenistic monarchs and Roman views, we will spend several weeks on Lives of Plutarch and how they appropriate ‘classic’ themes of the education and virtues of the king/leader, the nature of wisdom and its relation to power, the use and abuse of law, rhetoric and myth, and differing relations of rulers and ruled. We will end by glancing at the adaption of classical themes in the late antique, Medieval and Renaissance periods when new ideals of Christian monarchy evolved.
learningOutcomes: Analyze the institution of kingship in different periods of ancient Mediterranean history.
Explain major thinkers’ approaches to the virtues of leadership, notably wisdom.
Identify and analyze representative themes in Plutarch’s Lives.
Identify and assess continuities and changes across a range of genres and periods.
Demonstrate the ability to communicate effectively in both oral and written form.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the ways in which language and culture are interwoven, with a particular focus on German language and culture. It trains students in the skills necessary for exploring language as a system and looks at how linguistic issues are represented in particular examples from film, literature, music, and visual culture. Students will learn how to identify and talk about linguistic and cultural phenomena and explore how they play out in everyday life. The module will equip students with the vocabulary and terminology to talk about language, as well as helping them to understand how language is used in different social and cultural contexts. Students will have an opportunity to explore this through close reading of different types of aural, oral, written, and digital texts. 
This module will consider how language and culture both reflect and shape the way we view the world. It will consist of three units, each of which will focus on a particular linguistic and cultural issue: 
Unit 1: Language Variation and Change 
Unit 2: Language Politics 
Unit 3: Language and Migration 
This module will offer students who want to study a language to degree-level a thorough grounding in the skills necessary for success in their studies. It will also appeal to students with an interest in and passion for languages and how they work in different social contexts.

IMPORTANT NOTE: 
Since this module focuses on German-language texts, including aural, oral, written, and digital texts, and parts of the module may be delivered through the medium of German, some prior knowledge of German is required. (Recommended: Higher-level German at Leaving Certificate or equivalent)
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms the structural similarities and differences between German and English;
Describe in some detail how the German language is used in different regions and countries where German is spoken, using examples where appropriate;
Interpret and analyse how language is used in cultural texts and performances through close readings and contextualisation;
Design and create an infographic that represents relevant data in a visually stimulating way;
Research, prepare and deliver an oral presentation;
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and well-structured argument.

modules.moduleContent:
Grammar, reading and listening comprehension, composition (free writing), conversation.
Although all students taking this module will cover the same course and take the same exam/s, students will be internally streamed in accordance with their linguistic competence.

The module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages at level A2, where the emphasis is on achieving a lower intermediate level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing). A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A2.1.
learningOutcomes: Identify the basic elements of a German sentence and arrange them in a syntactically and grammatically correct manner;
Construct simple sentences based on grammatical rules and using vocabulary dealt with in class;
Explain and summarise the meaning of relatively straightforward written and audio texts;
Answer questions on a variety of subjects of relevance to everyday life.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to Linguistics (the scientific study of language) and engages them to develop comprehensive meta-linguistic awareness (the cognitive and physical skill to analyse and understand all human language fundamentally). In the meta-linguistic awareness portion of the module, students will develop a more nuanced understanding of the semantic (meaning), morpho-syntactic (structure) and pragmatic (social use of language) composition of English and German. This is achieved through continuous hand-on practical work involving the marking, dissecting, and analysing of all linguistic features. In the German Linguistics portion, students will engage with topics such as ‘Prescriptivism’ (who determines the norms), ‘Denglisch’, ‘Linguistic Relativity’ (language influences thought), ‘Grammatical Gendering in Language’, and ‘Phonetics and Phonology’.
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms the structural similarities and differences between German and English.
Explain, in a more in-depth way, specific reasons for such  differences, using examples where appropriate.
Describe and interpret in English key grammatical terms introduced in the module, using examples where appropriate.
Write responses to exam questions, making convincing arguments in a clear and structured manner and giving examples where appropriate.
Explain and apply the various theories discussed in the module to maximise your meta-linguistic awareness.

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims at developing students' competence in German further, building on knowledge and skills acquired in First year. It also continues with sensitising students to the cultures and societies of the German-speaking world and further deepens their knowledge and understanding of such through various media (texts, images, videos, online). The module aims at building confidence and a degree of accuracy when using the language in a broad range of situations. 

The module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages at level B1, where the emphasis is on achieving an intermediate level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing). A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level B1.1.
learningOutcomes: Recognise a wide range of tenses and other grammatical structures, employ them to produce meaningful and correct German sentences;
Interpret and apply new vocabulary introduced in class;
Comprehend, explain and interpret the meaning of written and audio German-language texts on a range of familiar issues of relevance to their lives (study, work, media etc)
Provide oral and written answers in German to information- and opinion-based questions on German-language written and audio texts.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: NO PLACE LIKE HEIMAT? NARRATIVES OF BELONGING IN C20 & C21 GERMANY
modules.moduleContent: This course examines ideas of home and Heimat, looking at the different ways in which belonging and the desire to belong are articulated, problematised and understood in twentieth and twenty-first century German-language literature and film. By critically examining stories of real or imagined belonging and of lost, interrupted or non- belonging, we will explore different modes of attachment and alienation to show how people may belong in a variety of ways (ranging from anchorage in a physical or imagined place, a real or imagined group, a cultural practice or tradition to a more abstract grounding in a remembered or prospective time in space). At issue are literary and filmic accounts of the politics of belonging, the disturbances and obstacles that obstruct inclusion, and the experience of exclusion. Outlining a textured landscape of belonging marked both by a persistent longing to belong and an anxiety of belonging or fear of entrapment, this course investigates the wider significance of the quest for attachment.
learningOutcomes: Critically reflect on Heimat, concepts of belonging, and the politics of belonging in Germany and further afield;
Further develop their ability to read and analyse complex literary texts and relate them to wider contexts and discourses;
Develop their ability to analyse films belonging to the Heimat film genre and filmic depictions of belonging;
Further develop their critical awareness, analytical and written skills;
Enhance their ability to discuss complex topics through in-class discussions and seminar participation;
Engage critically with theoretical literature and use this to support arguments;
Develop key transferable skills including effective and efficient communication, self-motivation, self-reliance, co-operation, and time and information management.

modules.moduleContent:
The Graduate Research Academy, in partnership with academic and support units at MU, offers research students the opportunity to enhance their understanding of their current professional competence profile and to work in a coherent and structured way to develop their skills over the course of their research programme. This training is offered within the graduate skills module GST1: Professional Development & Employability, which is comprised of the following mandatory sessions:

1) Reflective Writing & Appraisal of Skills – Alan Waldron, Academic Writing Support
This initial workshop will provide research students with the opportunity to develop their reflective writing skills and the ability to appraise their own competency profile. Research students will learn what reflective writing is, the value of reflective writing, how to write reflectively, models for structuring reflective writing, and how to address global reflection with an emphasis on self-appraisal of skill and abilities. Students will also have the opportunity to practice reflective writing and engage in an in-class review and peer feedback.

2) Working with your Supervisor – Dr Bernie Grummell, Adult & Community Education
The focus of this workshop is for research students to learn about the role, purpose and possibilities of supervision in doctoral studies and beyond. The workshop explores expectations, experiences and supportive structures for diverse supervisory relationships, as well as supervision policies, practices and feedback processes.

3) Developing Resilience – Deirdre McDonagh, MU Counselling Service Team
This workshop aims to provide research students with guidance on how to build their own resilience and cope in the face of challenges and change in light of the increasingly dynamic nature of the research and the world of work. This workshop will also highlight the services offered by the MU Counselling Service Team.

4) Legal Aspects in Managing your Research – Dr Miriam Ryan, Research Development Office, & Stephen McCarthy, Freedom of Information Office & Data Protection Office
This workshop will focus on the legal aspects of conducting research in a university setting and within industry, including topics such as contracts and obligations, funded research and working with collaborators. There will also be a Q&A session with the objective of providing practical advice on the implications of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the Data Protection Law on research and on any issues specific to research students regarding data collection and processing, transparency, security and accountability.

5) Unconscious Bias, Diversity & Inclusion – Sam Blanckensee, Office of the Vice President for Equality and Diversity
The focus of this workshop is to inform research students about how unconscious bias can affect behaviour and decisions in the workplace and create barriers to diversity and the formation of inclusive relationships. Attendees will also learn about the Athena SWAN charter and the advancement of gender equality and intersectionality in research and more broadly.

6) Communicating your Research – Dr Patrick Boyle, Research Development Office
In this workshop, research students will learn about communicating information effectively and in a way that is accessible to various audiences; vital to getting your research funded, published and to making it influential. There will also be a focus on how these skills transfer to other sectors.

7) Considering your Future Career – Brendan Baker, Career Development Office
The aim of this workshop is to assist research students in developing self-awareness regarding their own skills, abilities, interests and personality, empowering them to identify diverse opportunities and plan for careers in multiple sectors.

Research students must speak to their supervisor before registering for this module. Students can register via their Student Web Portal or by emailing rsdp@mu.ie.
learningOutcomes: Appraise their own competency profile
Identify the knowledge, understanding and skills required to meet particular academic and professional development goals
Work in a coherent and structured way to develop the knowledge, understanding and skills to meet these goals
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to provide researchers with the skills required to identify the impact of their research, commercialise a research outcome, effectively interact within a team and with industry, and to improve their ability to innovate and act with an entrepreneurial mindset. Some of the key themes explored in this module include understanding the current research landscape, intellectual property, legal contracts, spin-out companies, technical marketing, team building and interacting with industry and NGOs. This module adopts a group work approach to introduce students, in a practical way, to team, leadership and relationship building, as well as to enable the development of their communication skills.

Module Content

The module content is explored over four days and includes the following sessions:

1) Introduction to Commercialisation of Research
2) Intellectual Property
3) Legal Contracts
4) Spin-Out Companies
5) Technical Marketing & Product Development: Understanding the Impact of your Research
6) Interacting with Industry and/or NGOs
7) MaynoothWorks
8) Business Exercise: Team Building

Research students must speak with their supervisor(s) before registering for Graduate Skills (GST) modules. Students can register via their Student Web Portal or by emailing rsdp@mu.ie.
learningOutcomes: Identify and discuss the key players involved (State, University, Industry/NGO’s), and the systems/interactions required to commercialise research.
Evaluate the potential impact of research/knowledge and its potential value proposition.
Demonstrate an awareness of IP issues relating to research.
Articulate the legal obligations of the University and the research student, as well as the legal constraints in relation to dissemination of research and confidential information.
Articulate the implications for research when entering into contracts with different funding agencies.
Develop a business plan and present it to potential investors, and structure a funding transaction.
Distinguish market research options and channel partners for commercially viable research.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

learningOutcomes: Identify funding sources and grant opportunities;
Write a proposal for a research award;
Incorporate feedback into a research award application;
Submit a research award application to a funding body.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

learningOutcomes: - Assess your own research practice to meet the highest standards of integrity and ethics in your discipline
- Understand Maynooth University's policy on Research Conduct and Integrity.
