- Apply these principles and policies, understanding the consequences of non-compliance
- Apply the best research practice in data management, image use and publication
- Articulate the broad meaning of ethics in research and specific MU policy and procedures on research ethics
- Plan an application for ethical approval for a research project at Maynooth University
- Summarise international cases of research misconduct and evaluate these within the framework of your field of research
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Creative thinking is one of the most sought-after skills of the new economy. In a recent study made by like LinkedIn (2018), data showed that Creativity is the second most important skill that companies need the most, just behind Cloud Computing. About 60% of Chief Executives recognized that creativity is the most important leadership quality, even more than integrity (52%) and Global thinking (35%). 94% of the managers in Adobe Co. said that creativity is important to consider when hiring a candidate (2014). The world economic forum considered that Creativity is in the top 10 skills essential for in 2020 and beyond. 

This module will help researchers and postgraduate students to find new methods and techniques to spark their creative thinking in their professional and academic career. In a fun way, we will learn and practice creative problem-solving in different scenarios. There are multiple methods to unleash students’ creativity. We will be revised some of them to find the best fit for each student. 

All the weekly activities are planned to foster collaboration and teamwork. Creative ideas usually lead to projects and projects into ventures. We aim to have enough ideas that can feed future modules where you can apply your creative toolkit. There is an incredible opportunity to make evident the purpose, value, and impact of your research.

Research students should get approval from their supervisor before registering for this module. Students can register via their Student Web Portal, or by emailing rsdp@mu.ie.
learningOutcomes: - Understand the different models of creativity
- Appreciate the difference between individual and group creativity
- Experiment with different models of problem solving
- Appreciate the role of divergent and convergent thinking in creativity and problem solving
- Be conversant with the steps in the process of problem solving

- Be fluent in the use of the six step model: The six steps include: problem identification, problem delineation, information gathering, idea generation, idea evaluation and refinement, and idea implementation
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
GST8: Grant Management & Compliance is offered to IRC funded research students to support them in complying with the general terms and conditions and the reporting requirements set out in IRC scholarship documentation.

Compliance includes:

· The preparation and timely submission of reports detailing matters such as project and career development progression, financial updates and project outputs to the funder.

· The appropriate use of research expenses.

· The recording of all training and career development plan activities.

· Applying to the funder/academic supervisor for approvals or permissions and recording and retaining the approvals/permissions.

· Lodging of wholly/partly funded publications and other research outputs in an Open Access repository in accordance with the scholarship terms and conditions.

· Acknowledgement of the funder’s support (and Strategic Partner support where applicable) and inclusion of logo(s) in all publications and publicity materials as outlined in the funder’s terms and conditions and retention of copies of all publication/publicity material with appropriate acknowledgements.

Research students should not register for this module if they are not IRC funded scholars. Students can register via their Student Web Portal or by emailing rsdp@mu.ie.
learningOutcomes: Articulate what’s involved in complying with the terms and conditions of their research scholarship;
Demonstrate the management of their own research project expenses and the completion and timely submission of technical and financial reports to the funder.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Living Landscapes 1 introduces students to the origins and diversity of physical landscapes across the modern world. Landscapes are shaped by both physical and human processes, which are often interdependent. Living Landscapes will allow students to investigate world-forming processes and be introduced to the platform geographical skills of description, interpretation and communication via classes and aligned assessments. Topics include viewing the Earth as a system, the development of scientific knowledge, what we know of Plate Tectonics and the theory’s development, Ireland’s own tectonic history, and an introduction to the Earth's ice cover particularly the rapidly dwindling sea ice cover of the Arctic ocean.
learningOutcomes: Outline the principles underpinning the science of geology, including scientific method, uniformitarianism and stratigraphy.
Outline the history of development of Plate Tectonics Theory, detailing the major ideas, concepts and evidence that combined during the period of its formulation.
Identify and describe the principal sedimentary, igneous and metamorphic rock types making up the Rock Cycle and found in Irish geology and building stone.
Discuss the geological history of Ireland that has led to its large-scale geological structure and unique geological column.
Outline the operation of processes within the Earth’s oceans including the Global Themohaline Circulation system.
Explain the main physical processes operating in the Arctic region within the context of the global climate system.
Access, manipulate and interpret online numerical and geological data to produce evidence in support of a set of reasoned arguments.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Living Landscapes will introduce students to the diversity of human and physical landscapes across the modern world.  Landscapes are both ‘vast and past’, shaped by physical and human geographical processes, which are often interdependent.  Living Landscapes will allow students to investigate world-forming processes by introduction to the platform geographical skills of analysis, description and communication via a blend of traditional lecture and small group learning environments.   The unit content will examine how humans across the world are part of, and also create, unique landscapes that have issues including hazard prevention, poverty and resource depletion.
learningOutcomes: Define and discuss key concepts in Geography
Gather, organise and present information from a range of sources effectively
Exemplify connections between contemporary events and relevant human and physical geography concepts
Critically assess competing viewpoints on geographical themes and concepts
Demonstrate skills in numerical, graphical and spatial analysis
Demonstrate oral and written communication skills
Work effectively as an individual and in co-operation with others in small groups

modules.moduleContent:
The main objective is to enable students to develop an understanding of the principal processes that underpin the restructuring of agricultural production and the formulation of rural development policies and strategies.

This module explores contemporary issues in rural geography in both Ireland and Europe. The module examines the main drivers of change affecting rural areas and how our understanding of the ‘rural’ has changed over time. Particular attention will be given to existing and emerging challenges for rural areas and the role of public policies, both at European and national levels. Specific thematic areas addressed will include the changing role of agriculture, urban-rural relations, rural poverty and strategies for sustainable rural development.
learningOutcomes: Identify contemporary issues in rural geography in Ireland and Europe.
Explain the main drivers of change affecting rural areas.
Assess the role of national and European policies in the changing geographies of agriculture and rural development.
Analyse the ways in which the meaning of ‘rural’ has changed over time.
Evaluate the sustainability of rural areas and systems.
Work actively, effectively and ethically on your own and in groups to achieve the objectives of the course and to co-create knowledge.

modules.moduleContent: This module will study the historical development of geography as an academic discipline and the social, political and historical contexts in which the subject developed. It will study how the development of geography has been shaped by different philosophical and theoretical approaches, and also analyse how these different schools of geography may approach specific topics or themes in geography in different ways. This module also aims to develop students’ critical skills in terms of developing critiques of the different approaches taken to Geography, and aims to offer students theoretical frameworks to draw from, so as to provide them with a sound basis to build geographical research on.
learningOutcomes: Describe the historical development of Geography as an academic discipline
Explain the social and political contexts for the development of academic Geography
Discuss the different philosophical and theoretical approaches that have influenced the practice of Geography
Analyse how specific themes or topics are addressed by different schools of geographic thought

modules.moduleContent: Growing pressures on the natural environment through intensification of human land use may compromise ecosystem functions that are central to both the human and the natural world. According to the UN commissioned Millennium Ecosystem Assessment report published in 2004, 60% of ecosystem services were degraded or used unsustainably. More recent State of the Environment reports show continuing threats to ecosystems. This module examines rising ecosystem pressures focusing particularly on intensification of anthropogenic land use and projected demands in future output. It explores effects of these pressures on ecosystems. Questions asked include: What changes can these 'stressors' cause in wild population structure and biodiversity, and how might they ultimately affect such ecosystem services like water purification, food production, biological pest control, pollination, soil conservation and carbon sequestration? We end by examining case studies and examples of better management of ecosystems for a sustainable future.
learningOutcomes: Describe historic land-use intensification and understand the likely future demands on natural resources and outline the pressures on ecosystems and ecosystem functions arising from anthropogenic land use.
Explain anthropogenic influences on a range of natural ecosystems, habitats and biodiversity, including direct and indirect drivers and impacts.

Connect the changes in specific environmental stressors with modification of wild population structure and biodiversity, and express how these stressors might ultimately affect ecosystem services such as food production, biological pest control, pollination, soil conservation, water purification, and carbon sequestration.
Assess and debate the potential for sustainable ecosystem management.

modules.moduleContent: This module explores issues relating to the environmental and social causes of human disease. It considers the importance of Geography in the context of medical/health data and the uses of, and challenges involved with using such data. It considers the various ways that environment influences health, including the relationship between early life exposures and disease in later life. It considers the social, political and economic contexts for various disease patterns across the globe and their implications. It reflects on the relationship between poverty, inequality and health and examines the role of social capital, in this regard.
learningOutcomes: Critically assess the various approaches to Medical/Health Geography.
Describe the various methodological problems that arise when using spatial health data.
Analyse the impacts of various environmental influences on health.
Investigate spatial patterns of disease distribution and their potential influencing factors.
Critically assess the impact that poverty and inequality have on health.
Gather, analyse and interpret existing geographical health data across various scales.
Work actively, effectively and ethically on your own and in groups to achieve the course objectives and to co-create knowledge.

modules.moduleContent: The Earth is facing multiple, interconnected, global-scale environmental challenges in the 21st century. This module aims to allow the investigation of these challenges, focussing on a several topics of global concern. The learning activities associated will comprise different mixtures of in-class, field-based, data analysis and/or laboratory work. The module will allow students time in classes, discussion, planned learning activities and aligned assignments to explore these issues in depth including how and where to source up to date data and information on the topic.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Surveying involves accurately determining the position, dimensions and detail of natural and artificial features in a locality – features such as elevation, contours, bodies of water, vegetation, and artificial structures. This includes being familiar with both the hardware and software that enable the surveyor to create detailed accurate topographic maps.  The module will include teaching on the practical methods of measuring and mapping terrestrial features at the field scale (relatively small land areas).  This will be complimented by the theory of data capture.  Learning activities will include using field (outdoor) and computer-based exercises that will provide students with an opportunity to apply relevant data processing and analysis methods to real survey data. This module compliments the wider synoptic mapping processes introduced in GY314 Environmental Remote Sensing.
learningOutcomes: Identify and review the hardware and software currently in use in the world of surveying and mapping.
Undertake a critical analysis of the differences between contact and non-contact measurement techniques.
Propose and apply suitable surveying techniques for a range of terrestrial applications.
Experiment with the digital processing and analysis of spatial data.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The module addresses key debates on the nature and practice of geography, drawing on historical and contemporary scholarship from a range of academic contexts. The module will focus on key concepts that underpin geographical thoughts, and key approaches to the practice of geography.
This module introduces students to the nature and practice of geography as an academic discipline.
learningOutcomes: Identify and analyse key theoretical debates in the discipline of Geography
Locate and contextualise their independent research within the key theoretical debates of Geography
Critically analyse the ways in which their independent research contributes to broader theoretical debates in Geography

modules.moduleName: SPATIAL JUSTICE: GEOGRAPHIES OF SOCIAL & ENVIRONMENTAL CHANGE
modules.moduleContent:
From climate change to toxic pollution, the distribution of environmental harms and the resources to deal with them are spatially unequal. At one level, this is obvious. What is not so obvious is how these spatial and environmental injustices have been produced and reproduced over time. Spatial and environmental injustice is inseparable from a deeper understanding of colonialism, racism, patriarchy and class inequality. Taking an historical and theoretical approach is necessary to make sense of these many layered and intersectional geographies of in/justice.

As well as seeking to critically interrogate spatial justice today, this module aims to do two things: first, to create a dialogue between critical concepts in the literature and the Irish context: how can our understanding of spatial and environmental justice in Ireland be broadened and deepened through an engagement with places and experiences that seem at first glance far removed from here? Second, to learn from Irish-based community organisers, activists and practitioners whose work challenges different forms of spatial and environmental injustice today: how can the place-based work of these individuals and communities help us to imagine more just, sustainable futures on the island?

This module will introduce students to key texts, concepts, writers, and artists working on questions of spatial and environmental justice. Class-based discussions and guest talks will examine the political, ethical, and aesthetic challenges of fostering hopeful politics in a time of environmental crisis, reactionary politics, and deepening global inequalities, with a particular focus on Irish-based artistic and activist interventions.
learningOutcomes: Critically discuss the key concepts, texts, writers, and social movements that have informed perspectives on spatial and environmental justice.
Apply this knowledge to diverse contexts and forms of spatial and environmental injustice in Ireland and abroad.
Undertake independent research on a topic of their choice.
Communicate knowledge effectively and fluently through a range of forms (including essay-writing, podcasts and presentations).
Debate and discuss resistances to inclusive social progress and environmental justice

modules.moduleContent:
Climate change is having both positive and negative impacts in many areas of our natural, social, economic and political world. This module is designed to equip students with knowledge and understanding on a diverse range of potential and real climate change impacts. Issues surrounding these impacts and related policy approaches for mitigation will be scrutinised. The implications of modelling based projections of the enhanced greenhouse effect for Ireland and other parts of the world will be analysed, as will options to mitigate global warming. 

This module will be delivered by several academics working on specific impact areas. Possible topics to be incorporated into the module may include agriculture, soils, biodiversity, marine/coasts, pests, energy, transport, health, construction, tourism and planning.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish a diverse range of potential and real climate change impacts.
Identify and discuss issues surrounding these impacts and related policy approaches for their mitigation.
Analyse the implications of modelling based projections of the enhanced greenhouse effect for Ireland and other parts of the world.
Appraise options to mitigate global warming.

modules.moduleName: ANALYSING SPATIAL AND TEMPORAL DATA USING R
modules.moduleContent: This module provides an introduction to the basics of data analysis, exploration and visualisation,  with particular focus on spatial and temporal data.   The module consists of a series of lectures including an introduction and start-up session to a take away practical exercise using the statistical programming language R.  The module begins with basic methods to explore, describe and graphically represent one- and two-dimensional data,  before moving on to consider more advanced methods to manipulate and visualise geographical information,  and explore and identify trends and seasonal patterns in time series data. In addition,  some methodological aspects of data analysis are introduced,  in particular the use of open data and ‘citizen science’ data and the idea of reproducibility in data analysis.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate basic proficiency in coding and data analysis using R,  in particular
Understand the basics of time series modelling
Understand the basics of spatial trend modeling
Perform interactive graphical exploration of spatial and temporal data
Create interactive web maps, time series visualisations and reports
Demonstrate awareness of the need for critical evaluation of data used in the above.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Changes to the physical and biotic environment are both natural and arise because of human activity. Recently these changes have been so significant that we now clearly recognise planetary scale change e.g. biodiversity loss and climate change derived directly from human actions rather than natural processes. The scale of these climatic and environmental changes is becoming ever more evident and urgent, as droughts, floods, species loss, and viral pandemics occur with increasing frequency and intensity. At the same time, the fall-out of these overlapping ongoing crises are not experienced equally across society. Responding to these pressing challenges requires a critical and interdisciplinary understanding of how social and physical processes interact in a complex, unequal world by being informed about earth systems science and how the environment is changing and national and international governance and policy landscapes.

In this 5 ECT micro-credential, students will gain a broad, critical understanding of approaches to studying environmental change, the nature and scale of that change, as well as reflecting on their own place within a changing world. The module will introduce students to key debates and approaches to sustainability and climate action. Students will interrogate different approaches to sustainable development and climate action through an examination of key sectors, such as agri-food production, energy, flood risk and decarbonisation. Students will also gain a critical understanding of key concepts such as climate justice, degrowth, circular economy and the just transition. The module will require students to reflect upon different scales of action and their impact and effectiveness in achieving transformative change – from their own individual behaviour to workplaces, national policies and global political and economic systems. The module will equip students with the foundational knowledge to understand and think critically about how we currently approach sustainability and climate action and how we might into the future.
learningOutcomes: Explain the Earth systems and the interconnections between global ecosystems;
Demonstrate, using various lines of evidence, how humans are contributing to climate change;
Explain key means of measuring and monitoring environmental systems and processes;
Understand the root causes of environmental change;
Design and deliver a climate or sustainability action in a school-based or local setting ranging from school to community, county, or province;
Describe and provide examples of climate mitigation and adaptation in Ireland;
Explain the demands and achievements of climate justice movements, including their role in shaping climate policy;

modules.moduleContent:
The course will examine advanced methods and techniques (e.g. quantitative and/or qualitative statistics; geographic information systems (GIS)) for undertaking research.
Module Objectives: Students will acquire a familiarity with advanced research methodologies for use in their research area
learningOutcomes: Practice the design of effective data analysis techniques
Analyse data using appropriate methods and techniques
Display data using graphical methods
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module will train students in the use and application of specialised equipment, (e.g Multi-Sensor Core Scanner; High Performance Computing; Electromagnetic equipment, Resistivity, Ground Penetrating Radar) or other piece of specialised equipment required for their specific research areas.
learningOutcomes: Practice the design  and implementation of effective techniques for undertaking their research
Demonstrate competency in use of specialised equipment
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This course builds on language skills acquired throughout your studies at Maynooth University and focuses on new creative writing skills; it should be of particular interest to (but not restricted to) those with an aptitude and passion for literary expression. Students will study all of the major genres (e.g., Fiction, Nonfiction, Poetry, Screenwriting) with a goal to discover, experiment with, and strengthen one’s strands of interest. The emphasis is on practice-based learning and experience, allowing students to engage with existing writing forms and produce original texts in the target language.
learningOutcomes: Read as writers, developing new critical faculties and their confidence to produce creative content in the target language.
Identify and use a range of writing techniques.
Structure their work through a variety of voices and forms and write in compelling, engaging, and innovative ways.
Produce traditional and experimental work with greater attention to detail and knowledge of the rich history of writing in French.

modules.moduleContent: This module examines the myth, culture, and literature of the early Greek world, seen primarily through the medium of Homer’s great epics. We begin with the end of the Bronze Age and the sweeping changes across the Near Eastern Mediterranean that sets the legendary backdrop for Homer’s poetic rendering of the Trojan War. We turn then to Homer’s epics, particularly the Iliad, a literary masterpiece that explores tales of war, love, travel, and heroism. But it also serves as a ‘mythistorical’ document reflecting the economic and political conditions of Homer’s own times. The final part of the module explores the enduring importance of Homer and his tales in early Archaic Greece. Here supplementary material from visual art and lyric poets such as Archilochus, Sappho and Tyrtaeus gives insight into the early Greek polis, warfare, athletic competitions, symposia, religious life and more. In all, the module offers an interdisciplinary survey of a vibrant cultural history that still resonates.
learningOutcomes: Situate the Homeric epics in relation to mythological tradition and historical evidence of early Greece.
Analyse the characters, imagery, themes and structure of Homer’s Iliad.
Explain social, political and religious themes in the Homeric epics.
Discuss similarities and differences between Homeric epic and Archaic lyric poetry.
Develop imaginative, lateral thinking.
Demonstrate the ability to communicate complex ideas in both oral and written form.

modules.moduleName: ATHENS AND SPARTA AT WAR: UNDERSTANDING THUCYDIDES
modules.moduleContent: Towards the end of the fifth-century BC the Greek world was embroiled in a conflict which lasted with little interruption for 27 years. This module examines this conflict – the Peloponnesian War – and the parts played by the principal adversaries, Athens and Sparta. Central to it is a close study of the Greek historian Thucydides’ account of this war. In particular, we examine the principles of historiography that he employed. On account of his methodology, Thucydides would acquire a reputation as the first scientific historian of the western world, a claim the module scrutinizes. We also, briefly, make use of other perspectives, such as those of the comic playwright Aristophanes, who enriches the picture, adding a cultural dimension by examining the impact the war had on the general population, male and female, citizen and slave. The module closes with an assessment of the legacy of Thucydides and his place in the Greek historiographical tradition.
learningOutcomes: Identify and recount important military engagements of the Peloponnesian War.
Discuss Thucydides as an objective historian of that war.
Assess the didactic value of Thucydides’ Peloponnesian War.
Analyse Thucydides’ Peloponnesian War as an exercise in historiography.
Argue coherently about chief issues pertaining to the Peloponnesian War and Thucydides.
Demonstrate the ability to communicate complex ideas in both oral and written form.
modules.notes: Inactive module, not currently for selection on dept. degrees.

modules.moduleName: GENDER & SEXUALITY IN THE ANCIENT WORLD
modules.moduleContent: This module looks at gender relationships and the depiction of men and women – constructions of ‘masculinity’ and ‘femininity’ – in the Classical world through the literature of ancient Greece and Rome. It explores the intellectual, philosophical, political, and social reasons for the various depictions and representation of gender relationships through the Classical period. Focussing on, and contrasting, evidence from Greek and Roman texts, the aim is to review a range of texts from different genres as we explore the full variety of life experiences across the ancient world. The module will also make use of new approaches in the areas of feminism and gender studies which have informed recent research in this expanding area of classical scholarship.
learningOutcomes: Evaluate and assess the ancient sources material on gender and sexuality, while also analysing critically key themes within appropriate historical contexts and debates.
Demonstrate an understanding of key themes and concepts in relation to gender and sexuality in the ancient world.
Select key exemplars to illustrate significant themes and ideas.
Compare and contrast the lived experiences of Greek and Roman men and women.
Demonstrate the ability to communicate complex ideas in both oral and written form.

modules.moduleName: LOVE, WAR AND POWER IN ROMAN EPIC
modules.moduleContent: With their tales of gods, heroes, and monsters, epic poems constituted the most important and prestigious literary form in ancient literature. This module explores how Virgil and Ovid transformed this Greek genre into a form that explored contemporary Roman issues through mythological subject matter: Virgil’s Aeneid follows the Trojan refugee Aeneas on his mission to found a new homeland for his people in Italy, while Ovid’s Metamorphoses weaves together tales of transformation from the beginning of the cosmos down to his own time. We will examine how these texts navigated the political climate in which they were written, under the principate of Augustus, but also how they relate to other aspects of their social, cultural, and literary context. Over the course of the semester, the module will trace the thread of the Roman epic tradition to explore how writers challenged, reread, and emulated earlier works.
learningOutcomes: Describe key themes and techniques in the poems.
Demonstrate a sound understanding of how epic developed up until the early Principate.
Analyse and critically evaluate ancient texts in relation to their socio-cultural context.
Show an ability to appraise, identify and use varied contemporary critical and theoretical approaches to literature.
Demonstrate the ability to communicate complex ideas in both oral and written form.

modules.moduleName: HUMANS, NATURE, AND THE ENVIRONMENT IN CLASSICAL ANTIQUITY
modules.moduleContent: It has been said that the roots of our ecological crisis lie in the past (White 1967). Western concepts of nature and of humanity’s place in and proper relationship to the environment are deeply rooted in the Classical past. This module explores the relationship between humanity and the natural environment in the literature and visual and material cultures of ancient Greece and Rome from bronze age Greece to the Roman empire. We will uncover key issues in the environmental history of the Mediterranean while considering ancient concepts of the natural world from an ecocritical perspective.
learningOutcomes: Describe the different perceptions of nature in Greek and Roman literature and visual and material culture.
Understand key problems and themes in relation to the relationship between humanity and the natural world in Graeco-Roman antiquity.
Show familiarity with a wide range of evidence, including literary, material, and artistic evidence, pertaining to nature in Graeco-Roman antiquity.

Evaluate key debates in relevant critical fields on the idea of nature and situate discussions of nature in the ancient within wider context of contemporary debates about the relationship between humanity and the natural world.
Demonstrate the ability to communicate complex ideas in both oral and written form.

modules.moduleContent:
To guide the student in producing a substantial piece of critical writing on a designated topic.

This module allows the student to pursue in-depth study and research on one of a number of designated topics. The student will have a choice from a number of topics related to those studied at Levels 2 or 3.  Candidates will be expected to argue at length from source material and to discuss critically the judgments of secondary literature.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate breadth and depth of knowledge in one specific area of Classical studies.
Develop broadly transferable research skills (e.g. analysis and comparison of primary materials, application and evaluation of different methodologies and approaches).
Demonstrate the ability to write effectively at an advanced level.
Demonstrate a capacity for imaginative, lateral thinking.
modules.notes: Inactive module, not currently for selection on dept. degrees.

modules.moduleContent:
The Dissertation provides students with the opportunity to engage in independent in-depth research on an approved topic relevant to the study of Ancient Classics. Typically, that research will be in an area that links to modules previously or currently taken; however, material examined elsewhere on the degree cannot be resubmitted as part of the dissertation. Working under the guidance of an assigned supervisor across both Semesters 1 and 2, the final goal is the production of a written dissertation of c.8,000 -10,000 words to cap the student’s work in Ancient Classics.

NOTE: GC351 is a compulsory module for 40-credit Major/Minor students. For Double Major students (i.e. those with Classical Studies as one of two subjects), the ten-credit GC351 option is available only if a minimum of 650 has been achieved across Second Arts Classical Studies modules (i.e. a 65% average in the subject for that year).
learningOutcomes: Identify a suitable research topic in the field of Ancient Classics and demonstrate a capacity to work independently.
Interrogate the major ancient sources and modern scholarship on their research topic.
Demonstrate the ability to critically evaluate and analyse ancient and modern material and construct effective arguments based on that review.
Demonstrate the ability to write effectively at an advanced level and an ability to use consistent referencing and formatting skills.
Demonstrate a capacity for independent work to produce an undergraduate dissertation.

modules.moduleContent: Often known as the ‘Father of History’, Herodotus is also a major literary figure with a wide range of styles and registers, from epic grandeur to tragic intensity to comic farce.  This module considers Herodotus from a variety of angles: as an ethnographer of the eastern Mediterranean, a historian of the Persian Wars, a contemporary of Pericles and Sophocles, and a thinker in his own right, with a nuanced vision of the complexity of human phenomena.   In addition to Herodotus’ selective understanding of archaic Greece and the Persian Wars, themes for consideration include Ionic historia, justice and reciprocity, the importance of chance and the divine in human affairs, and historiography in relation to other genres.
learningOutcomes: Summarise the major historical events that inform Herodotus work.
Categorise Herodotus’ work in relation to contemporary literary genres.
Situate Herodotus’ work in relation to contemporary intellectual movements.
Apply and evaluate different modern scholarly methodologies.
Demonstrate excellent communication skills, both written and oral.
Develop research skills and a flair for independent questioning.

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims at developing students' competence in German and is suitable for students who have never studied German before. It also introduces students to the cultures and societies of the German-speaking countries and deepens their knowledge and understanding of them through various media (texts, images, videos, online resources). The module aims at developing confidence and accuracy when using the language in a range of situations, with a view to bringing students to the same standard as the non-beginner students by the end of the first year. It is, therefore, an intensive accelerated course in German language and culture that is designed to provide a solid foundation for students' future studies in German.

This module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR) at level A1, where the emphasis is on achieving a fundamental level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing). A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A1.2.
learningOutcomes: Identify the basic elements of a German sentence and arrange them in a syntactically and grammatically correct manner.
Construct simple sentences based on grammatical rules and using vocabulary learnt in class.
Explain and summarise the meaning of relatively straightforward written and audio texts.
Answer questions on a variety of subjects of relevance to everyday life.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Grammar, reading and listening comprehension, composition (free writing), conversation.
Although all students taking this module will cover the same course and take the same exam/s, students will be internally streamed in accordance with their linguistic competence.

The module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages at level A2, where the emphasis is on achieving a lower intermediate level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing). A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A2.2.
learningOutcomes: Recognise a range of straightforward grammatical structures and employ them to produce meaningful and correct German sentences;
Interpret and apply new vocabulary introduced in class;
Converse in German with some degree of fluency about areas of immediate relevance – basic and family information, university studies, student life etc.;
Produce short written texts on a variety of topics related to everyday life.

modules.moduleName: GERMAN FOR PROFESSIONAL AND BUSINESS PURPOSES
modules.moduleContent:
This course offers students an opportunity to further develop their language skills, with a focus on strengthening their ability to partake in professional level conversations within the business world. Through an introduction to intercultural awareness, discussion based practical exercises and examining real-life examples, students can confidently ready themselves for a career in the business world where German is a language requirement. The course covers a range of diverse topics including correspondence and communication, customer conversations, marketing, and presentations. Students will read and understand business-related texts and will be able to generate summaries describing charts and/or graphs. The course will be useful for all students who wish to live and work in any German-speaking country, or in any industry where German knowledge would be a desirable or required competency.

IMPORTANT NOTe
Since this module involves aspects of the German language, which will also be tested in the Continuous Assessment and/or exam, an intermediate knowledge of German is a prerequisite.
learningOutcomes: Read and extract information from business-related texts, charts and graphs, and summarise that information in oral and written form.
Describe working environments, relationships with colleagues, employee and company profiles, business graphics, and business performance using appropriate vocabulary and structures.

Communicate effectively with a variety of interlocutors, e.g. colleagues, customers, and executives, and demonstrate appropriate language skills for negotiation and mediation with these interlocutors in written and oral form.
Produce some business-related texts, such as formal and informal emails and written reports.
Work effectively as part of a team to deliver a presentation in a coherent and meaningful manner.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness and sociolinguistic competencies, as they relate to the business world.
Apply appropriate vocabulary and grammar rules to complete quizzes and oral and written assignments, using relevant ITC, when necessary.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at students who are interested in learning German alongside the core subjects of their degree programmes. It will appeal to all types of students – those who have a genuine interest in learning about the German language and culture for their own development as well as those who are motivated to acquire German language skills and (inter)cultural competence to enhance their employment prospects in Ireland and further afield. German as an elective subject is open to Second Year undergraduate students from all three faculties as well as postgraduate students of any level and will be delivered on a blended-learning basis with 2 live contact hours accompanied by asynchronous content accessible on Moodle at any time. The module aims at developing confidence and accuracy in German through various media (texts, images, videos, online resources) and to enable students to use the German language in a range of contexts and situations.

This module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR) at level A1.1, where the emphasis is on achieving a fundamental level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing). A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A1.1.

Please note: Prospective students should note that this language elective is intended for beginners. Students who took German for the Leaving Certificate examination (or equivalent) cannot be accommodated in this class but should consider signing up for a Beginners’ language elective in Chinese, French, or Spanish instead.
learningOutcomes: Answer simple questions and offer some personal details, e.g. name, nationality, address, age, hobbies, in written and spoken form, and ask the same questions of another person;
Extract key information from simple written, audio and video texts related to situations from everyday life that contain words and expressions dealt with in class;
Write simple messages and communicate in a very simple way about situations related to everyday life and using vocabulary dealt with in class.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at students who are interested in continuing to develop their German language skills as a follow-on from GN260. It will appeal to all types of students – those who have a genuine interest in deepening their knowledge of the German language and culture as well as those who are motivated to develop German language skills and (inter)cultural competence to enhance their employment prospects on the domestic and international labour markets. German as an elective subject is open to Second Year undergraduate and all postgraduate students and will be delivered on a blended-learning basis with 2 live contact hours accompanied by asynchronous content accessible on Moodle at any time. This module aims to continue developing students' confidence and accuracy and to enable them to use the German language in a range of contexts and situations.

This module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR) at level A1.2, where the emphasis is on achieving a pre-intermediate level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing). A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A1.2.

Please note: Prospective students should note that this language elective is intended for beginners. Students who took German for the Leaving Certificate examination (or equivalent) cannot be accommodated in this class but should consider signing up for a Beginners’ language elective in Chinese, French, or Spanish instead.
learningOutcomes: Explain and summarise key information from relatively straightforward written, audio and video texts related to situations from everyday life that contain words and expressions dealt with in class;

Exchange relatively straightforward information in spoken or written form about family, occupation, plans, holidays, daily and leisure activities, and/or other familiar matters relating to everyday life;

Produce short written texts using familiar structures and expressions and communicate in relatively straightforward terms on topics of relevance to everyday life and using vocabulary dealt with in class.

modules.moduleContent:
This module helps students to further enhance their German, building and extending knowledge and skills acquired thus far. It continues to sensitise students to the cultures and societies of the German-speaking world and further deepens their knowledge and understanding of such through various media. Students will engage with complex text types, constructing complex and compelling arguments (oral and written), and writing longer texts such as opinion and overview pieces. The module aims to further build confidence and hone accuracy when using the language in a broad range of situations.
The module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages at level C1, where the emphasis is on achieving an advanced level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing). A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level C1.1.
learningOutcomes: Further enhance vocabulary range and sensitivity to register and nuance;
increase the degree of fluency and further improve pronunciation and intonation;
encourage grammatical accuracy and the use of more complex syntactical structures;
help negotiate different communicative situations;
further develop the ability to scan materials and extract important information and summarise this in own words in German;
develop, express and defend your opinion in the target language;
present a balanced overview of complex opinions and topics in the target language;

modules.moduleName: ETHICS AND ACADEMIC PRACTICE FOR RESEARCH STUDENTS
modules.moduleContent: The Graduate Skills module GST15: Ethics & Academic Practice for Research Students is designed to provide social science research students with the skill set to contextualise research ethics in a university setting, enhance their understanding of the principles of research practice and integrity and make a successful ethics application to the Maynooth University Ethics Committee. Topics include ethics, confidentiality, copyright, freedom of information in the research and writing process, and data protection and storage requirements.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate best practice in completing an ethics protocol;
Articulate, assess and critically evaluate the ethical issues arising in research;
Identify copyright, data protection and data storage requirements with regard to their research;
Successfully complete a research submission for ethical approval.

modules.moduleContent:
The Graduate Research Academy, in partnership with MU Library, offers the graduate skills module GST2: Finding Information for your Research to support research students in developing and enhancing their research skills and awareness over the course of their research degree. GST2 is primarily facilitated by MU Research Support Librarian Ciarán Quinn and is comprised of the following mandatory sessions:

1) Reflective Writing & Appraisal of Skills – Alan Waldron, Academic Writing Support.
This initial workshop will provide research students with the opportunity to develop their reflective writing skills and the ability to appraise their own competency profile. Research students will learn what reflective writing is, the value of reflective writing, how to write reflectively, models for structuring reflective writing, and how to address global reflection with an emphasis on self-appraisal of skill and abilities. Students will also have the opportunity to practice reflective writing and engage in an in-class review and peer feedback.

2) Finding Information for your Research
The aim of this workshop is to familiarise research students with the complete process of planning, searching, finding, evaluating and storing material for their research.

3) Reference Management Software & Avoiding Plagiarism
In this workshop, research students will learn how to use the different referencing tools available (EndNote, Mendeley, Zotero, etc.), how to create bibliographies and in-text citations, and how to store research results online in order to ensure accurate referencing and avoid plagiarism.

4) Doing Literature Reviews: What You Need To Know
The objective of this workshop is to provide research students with an understanding of what a literature review is, the process involved in conducting a literature review and possible challenges research students are likely to encounter when conducting a literature review.

5) Research Data Management
The focus of this workshop is to facilitate research students in understanding the full cycle of research data management, why it is necessary and the issues involved with making research data available to the broader research community. Research students will also learn how to access the tools necessary to put together a Data Management plan.

6) Publishing in Academic Journals: Finding the Right Journal for your Research
In this workshop, research students will learn how to identify the best place to publish their research. The workshop will focus on how to plan your publishing strategy in advance to maximise the impact of your research article, as well as the use of available databases to identify the best journal title to publish your research.

7) Measuring and Improving your Research Impact using Bibliometrics
This workshop will look at the bibliometric analysis tools available to measure research impact by citation analysis. Research students will learn how to measure the impact of journal articles, journal titles, individual researchers, groups of researchers and institutions, as well as how to improve their own research impact, their online researcher profile and identify potential opportunities for research collaborations.

Research students must speak to their supervisor before registering for this module. Students can register via their Student Web Portal or by emailing rsdp@mu.ie.
learningOutcomes: Appraise their own competency profile
Identify the knowledge, understanding and skills required to meet particular academic and professional development goals
Work in a coherent and structured way to develop the knowledge, understanding and skills to meet these goals
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: GY202 is a weekly methodology class covering aspects of research including planning, data collection, analysis and presentation techniques, building on the work carried out in GY201. Students will carry out a number of projects during the module each of which will involve different aspects of geography and different research approaches and methodologies.
learningOutcomes: Gather, analyse and interpret geographical data across a range of scales building on work in First Year and in GY201.
Further develop computer skills required for work in Second and Third Year Geography.
Further develop analytical and presentation skills to communicate geographical knowledge effectively.
Evaluate and conduct different geographical research techniques, including mapping, GIS and surveys, useful in many areas of employment.
Apply theoretical learning to practical situations and societal challenges.
Relate theoretical learning across local and national scales.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Fieldwork is one of the richest learning experiences open to any discipline, and a specialism of Geography.  In this module, within the island(s) of Ireland, students will engage in the collection and analysis of data, prepare and make presentations on daytime site visits and independent research.
learningOutcomes: Identify the geographical processes at work in the visited Irish locations.
Practice the design of effective field data recording techniques.
Collect and record data on specific topics in field based situations.
Recount and report on research findings about specific topics related to field based projects.
Analyse the geographical processes at work in Ireland using collected field data, observations and literature review.

modules.moduleContent: Experiencing another country is a very valuable, enriching learning experience in Geography.  It is a chance to apply observational skills in an unknown (at least in the fieldwork context) location.  This module will involve travel to an international location to expand the opportunities for observation and reflection, particularly on more familiar and commonly accepted aspects of 'home'.  The directed fieldwork activities will involve the collection and analysis of data at field sites and during coach excursions.  The emphasis will be on group-based activity and learning experiences.
learningOutcomes: Identify the geographical processes at work in the selected overseas locations
Practice the design of effective field data recording techniques
Collect and record data on specific topics in field based situations
Recount and report on research findings about specific topics related to field based projects
Contrast geographical processes at work in Ireland to those operating in a specific overseas locations

modules.moduleContent: How do we know about Physical Geography? How do we do research? The fascinating ideas and information about our physical and biological environment that you read about in textbooks and papers are all based on careful and often painstaking data collection and research. It is contributing to this research that excites us as physical geographers. How do you become a Geographer rather than merely studying geography? For students the transition to research starts in your final year when you undertake your own thesis or research project. After this come opportunities in academia, industry, government and consultancies, to investigate geographical problems as a career. Also, if you want to teach geography it helps to have field skills to teach others. This module offers a behind-the-scenes look at what makes effective research in physical geography. You will learn skills in how to do Geography. The module combines hands-on experience in collecting data in fieldwork sessions and analysing that data in computer labs. There will be fieldwork in this module attendance at which is essential. Most of the fieldwork will be local, or on campus, in 2-hour scheduled sessions.
learningOutcomes: Plan research in a logical flow from idea to reporting.
Develop an understanding of the research process in Physical Geography.
Develop skills in data collection, analysis, and presentation with real data.
Develop skills in independent enquiry, critical thinking, decision-making, teamworking.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module offers an introduction to different aspects of life within a University Geography Department, with especial reference to Maynooth Geography Department. Students will see what Geography lecturers/researchers do, what they research and what they write, in addition to gaining a better appreciation of the diverse nature of Geography as a subject. 
This module will also explore, and begin to develop a critical appreciation of, writings in Geography. It will examine different structures of writing, including book chapters, journal papers and policy reports, and establish how arguments are formulated and constructed. It will draw out the selection of appropriate research methods, which may subsequently inform the students' research dissertations.
learningOutcomes: Know more about Maynooth Geography Department; in particular, the individual geographers in Maynooth Geography Department and what they do.
understand how Geographers do research and how different styles of research are more appropriate to different branches/sub-disciplines of Geography.
understand how Geographers write peer reviewed academic books and journal articles/papers.
know how to access, and read, peer reviewed academic books and journal articles/papers.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed to impart a basic awareness of atmospheric processes and their function in explaining climatic variation on various area and temporal scales.

Major advances have been made in the past few decades in the understanding both of the nature and significance of atmospheric processes and the manner in which regional variations in climate can be understood in terms of the balance between such processes. During the past decade a growing awareness of the climatic dimension to many pressing problems such as food supply, population growth, resource depletion and environmental pollution has also become widespread, as have concerns regarding the impacts of imminent climatic changes associated with anthropogenic effects on the atmosphere. This module examines the main controls on climate at a number of different spatial and temporal scales.
learningOutcomes: Identify the main controls on climate across a number of different spatial and temporal scales.
Outline the nature and significance of the atmospheric processes affecting regional variation in climate.
Express the climatic dimension to pressing modern environmental problems.
Summarise the concerns regarding the impacts of imminent anthropogenically driven climatic change.

modules.moduleContent: Biogeography is a key component of the physical environment through its focus on plant and animal distributions and ecosystems. This module will provide an introduction to what is biodiversity, how it is perceived and how it is measured. We will examine patterns of biodiversity at local, regional and global spatial and temporal scales. Changing patterns in the geography of life are at the heart of this truly interdisciplinary subject. The module explores the distribution of plants and animals from an environmental perspective, where climate and habitats dictate the spread of populations. It goes on to examine ecosystem theory and how interactions between organisms contribute to their distribution patterns. The section on historical biogeography considers the age-old processes of evolution, plate tectonics and quaternary environmental change and their effects on biodiversity. Colonisation and extinction are at the heart of island biogeography and their investigation provides an insight into key processes shaping species distribution patterns. The module concludes with an evaluation of the current extinction crisis, the spread of invasive species, the challenges faced by small populations, and conservation management.
learningOutcomes: Broadly define key ecosystem, evolutionary and biodiversity concepts in biogeography.
Recognise and interpret processes that result in the current distribution patterns of plants and animals.
Explain the main threats facing biodiversity.
Interpret scientific publications, graphs and maps relating to biogeography.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides a grounding into Economic Geography, the longest established and one of the most dynamic sub-discipline of Human Geography. The module starts with the approaches to understanding the spatial organisation of production and consumption. Subsequently the module will explore of a number of current topics of the sub-discipline. The module focuses on the evolution of the global economic map, the spatial expression of contemporary economic change, with particular reference to globalisation, transnational corporations, the growth of service activities and related social polarisation in cities, new trends in consumer behaviour, shopping malls and the geographies of finance. Special sections are devoted to the impact of these changing processes on Ireland. Enrolment does not require any previous grounding in economics.
learningOutcomes: Describe and critically discuss the various approaches to understanding the spatial organisation of production and consumption.
Interpret the evolution and nature of the changing global economic geography at local, national and global scales, and apply knowledge to the challenge of uneven and sustainable development.
Reflect on the theories of the transnational corporation and their changing geographies, focussing on global production networks and global interdependency to appraise Ireland’s place in the world.
Discuss the spatial dimension of the specific geographies of the modern informational/knowledge economy, and the related growth of the services sector.
Describe and discuss the spatial-economic aspects of consumption and finance.
Gather, analyse and interpret geographical data across a range of scales.
Communicate geographical knowledge effectively and fluently by written and graphical means.

modules.moduleContent: Considering that we now live in a predominantly urban world and that levels of global urbanisation are set to increase, the study of cities and urban places is of major significance. This module introduces students to some of the key themes, concepts and debates characterising historical and contemporary urban geography. Among the topics covered initially are conceptualisations of the urban condition, theories of urban evolution and processes of historic and contemporary urbanisation. The course then focuses on urban geographies of the global north through an examination of contemporary shifts in urban structure, urban form and urban economy, the crisis of the inner city and cities as key sites of accumulation in the global economic system. Finally, the module carefully considers themes of conflict and contestation in urban environments through an exploration of inner-city regeneration policies, processes of gentrification and new approaches to city governance. Case studies for this module will be drawn primarily from cities in Europe, North America, Australia and China.
learningOutcomes: Describe and explain key concepts and debates in urban geography
Explain processes and trends underlying historical and contemporary urbanisation
Evaluate causes and outcomes of processes of conflict and contestation in urban environments
Analyze the changing urban geographies of the global north
Illustrate their understanding of urban geography with examples drawn from European, North American, Australian and Chinese cities

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to social geography and cultural geography, two of the most dynamic fields of study within human geography. It focuses on the relationship between space and society, expressed through the emergence of social and cultural differences and inequalities. The module also examines key themes such as power, identity and exclusion, and their theoretical and empirical importance within social and cultural geography.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of the development of social geography AND cultural geography as important approaches within the discipline of geography
Identify key themes in social and cultural geography, such as power, difference, inequality and exclusion, and analyze their explanatory power
Discuss the nature and form of the relationship between space and society from social and cultural geography perspectives
Explain developments in contemporary Ireland using social and cultural geography frameworks

modules.moduleContent:
This module examines the processes involved in making urban space and questions what it means to build healthy and inclusive cities. Looking predominantly at city planning from the periods of the Industrial Revolution to the contemporary focus on sustainable and smart cities, this module considers the main stakeholders involved in urban development and includes an examination of how these stakeholders and their interests have changed over time. Underpinning the module will be a focus on idealised visions of the city and how and why these visions conflict with realities on the ground. This is done through case study examples of that demonstrate how the planning process and policy making can create both positive and negative ruptures in the city development process. 
 
While much of the course relates to Dublin, a number of international examples serve to illustrate particular issues and concerns. Additionally, while much of the course relates to cities, we will also analyse how and why the focus on the city has changed over time and dispel the existence of an urban-rural binary.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a thorough understanding of key stakeholders involved in the development of the city and the politics behind the logics that drive urban development.
Exemplify familiarity with how urban planning can impact the built environment, economy, society and life.
Demonstrate a detailed knowledge of the key conceptual and policy debates relating to spatial planning.
Critically appraise and apply the value of different policies to evaluate different growth and development paradigms.
Apply core concepts to practical case studies through written and oral means.

modules.moduleContent: Migration is one of the key challenges facing us, and it affects the world in a variety of ways. This module provides a engaging and up-to-date overview of migration patterns, processes and consequences. It uses geographical approaches to understand migration, and to explore the relationship between migration and belonging. The module uses a wide range of sources to consider the importance of migration and belonging for contemporary societies. The module will provide you with the knowledge and skills to understand migration and its relationship with belonging, and to form and express your own evidence-based views on this significant global challenge.
learningOutcomes: Describe key trends in migration at global, regional, national and local scales
Demonstrate knowledge of different forms of belonging, and assess the relationship between migration and belonging at a range of different scales
Demonstrate an understanding of geographical approaches to migration and belonging

Critically evaluate how migration and belonging are represented in different sources of information – for example media, policy reports, civil society reports, statistics, and cultural representations
Develop and effectively communicate evidence-based arguments that engage with an important global challenge
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This course will examine the relations between power and space at a variety of scales. It will have three main areas. First, the Political Geography of Ireland and its place in colonial and postcolonial worlds. Second, the geopolitics of race in the United States. And, finally, the global geopolitics of the Cold War and the New World Order that has replaced it. The central concepts of the course will be scale, race, colonialism, postcolonialism, power, exclusion, and imperialism.
learningOutcomes: show an appreciation of the interaction between the scales of power from the personal through the urban to the national and the international.
have an appeciation of the relations between political economy and cultural geographies as expressed in landscapes of race, nationalism, and imperialism.
situate the Irish experience in appropriate historical and political-geographic contexts.

modules.moduleContent: Films reflect the remarkable changes in urban life that have occurred since the turn of the twentieth century and represent both the promises and failures of the city, including the excitement of living with strangers together in concentrated centres of politics, economy, and the arts, as well as the unequal access to green spaces, schools, and housing that residents who live in segregated spaces face. As an aesthetic form, film emerged at the turn of the last century and soon became a new way of representing and imagining the urban built environment. As early as the 1920s, film also reflected urban theories, influenced planning practices in many countries, and was critical of urban inequalities. As a social scene, cinema created new viewing publics through a shared leisure experience (‘going to the movies’) and urban form (the movie house on main street or in the mall). Visions of the modern city also reflect (and sometimes reinforce) societal desires and fears, from idealistic utopian hopes (socially just and beautiful worlds), to fears of ‘the Other’ (aliens, foreigners, nature, women, persons of colour) or of unchecked government power or corrupt corporate control. In this module, students will view and discuss films from 1908 to the present-day, with a focus on early European films, and more recent U.S. and Irish films. Students will gain an understanding of such themes as: race and gender in the city; chronic urban trauma; social and cultural conflict in cities; urban environments, technologies, and economies; and utopian and dystopian views of urban futures.
learningOutcomes: Identify key themes and patterns in the representation of the city in film
Assess the significance of film as a means of understanding urban form, aesthetics, landscape, planning and design
Analyse the political and economic processes tied to urbanization as represented in film
Evaluate the importance of film as a means of illustrating and understanding social and cultural conflict in cities
Demonstrate understanding of critical film analysis tools
Write evidence-based academic essays

modules.moduleContent: The assisted conception, development and delivery of an independent research project are key experiences that synthesise many aspects of undergraduate education and allow ownership of a topic in a significant and lasting way.  This module will provide training in the development of a research project, and allow independent research to be carried out on an agreed topic with the assistance of University staff.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate skills for undertaking independent research and for write up/presentation of results
Identify and develop an appropriate geographical research topic
Conduct self-directed primary and secondary research on an agreed geographical topic
Introduce, discuss and analyze research findings in a relevant theoretical context
Demonstrate effective writing and presentation skills

modules.moduleContent: The module will explain and illustrate, through case studies and examples, the important role of geography in explaining the global burden of disease, health inequalities, and linkages between the physical environment and health.
learningOutcomes: Analyse the impacts of various environmental influences on health.
Investigate spatial patterns of disease distribution and their potential influencing factors.
Critically assess the impact that poverty and inequality have on health.
Gather, analyse and interpret existing geographical health data across various scales.
Work actively, effectively and ethically on your own and in groups to achieve the course objectives and to co-create knowledge.
Communicate geographical health knowledge effectively and fluently by written, oral and graphical means.

modules.moduleContent: To conduct an original piece of geographical research, including the collection, analysis and interpretation of data, and contextualisation within the wider academic literature.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of the theory and concepts of a specific area of geography

Conduct original geographical research under the supervision of a thesis advisor, including the collection, analysis and interpretation of data using a variety of methods and contextualization within the wider academic literature
Produce a substantive piece of original geographical research and present it coherently in the written form of a thesis

modules.moduleContent: A written proposal outlining the research project; writing up the research; seminar presentations during the course of the year.
learningOutcomes: Identify and develop an appropriate research topic in the arena of Climate Change
Conduct self-directed primary and secondary research on an agreed research topic
Introduce, discuss and analyze research findings in a relevant theoretical context
Demonstrate effective writing and referencing skills

modules.moduleContent: This module deals with key issues in doing climate science and in linking climate science with policy and action. Over the course of the module we will examine key issues from the physical and social sciences and communication of climate change with the public. Delivery will be workshop based and be led by leading academics in the field. Learning will be supplemented with field visits, working and engaging with practitioners and policy makers. The focus of workshops will vary from year to year but will focus on key topics such as data collection, quality assurance, detection and attribution of climate change in historical records, attribution of extreme events, working with different sources/types of climate information, the importance of the social sciences in developing sustainable climate policy, evaluating the outcomes of adaptation actions and communicating climate change. The key objective of the module is to better understand how climate research, broadly defined can inform decision making and make real world impact.
learningOutcomes: Have a critical appreciation of approaches and methods for data collection in the field
Understand the vital importance of navigating the science policy interface
Understand how climate change science can inform decision making
Have a critical understanding of the value of social and physical science in informing sustainable climate action
Analytical skills to detect and attribute climate change signals
Critical appreciation of approaches to communicate climate change to different audiences
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Key debates on the nature and practice geography, drawing on historical and contemporary scholarship from a range of academic contexts. The module will focus on key approaches to the practice of geography (for example, the quantitative revolution and the cultural turn), and key concepts that underpin geographical thought (for example, nature, scale and place).
learningOutcomes: Identify and analyse key theoretical debates in the discipline of Geography
Locate and contextualise their independent research within these broader theoretical debates
Critically analyse the ways in which their independent research contributes to broader theoretical debates in Geography

modules.moduleContent: This module will require a student to undertake the collection and analysis of raw data in either a field or laboratory situation. Students will develop key skills in developing and preparing fieldwork, undertaking primary data collection and developing analytical techniques for assessing collected data. Students will be required to prepare health and safety statements and to clear ethical approval with responsible authorities when appropriate, in addition to developing suitable research methods, for each site or experiment prior to undertaking field work or laboratory work.
learningOutcomes: Design an individual research project, and carry out appropriate primary and secondary research.
Collect and record data on specific topics in field or laboratory based situations.
Demonstrate competency in use of specialised equipment.
Recount and report on research findings related to the research.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Academic and technical writing in French on topics relating to contemporary France. Revision of grammar and other related written work designed to foster accuracy. Oral discussion and expression on topics relating to contemporary France and the French-speaking world. 

Students obtaining a grade of 60% and above on completing this module can be deemed to have attained a CEFRL level of B.2.2. This module is required for students wishing to fulfil Teaching Council requirements.
learningOutcomes: Construct a coherent essay in French on a given topic with an introduction, well-argued demonstration and conclusion;
Explain in written French a viewpoint on a topical issue, outlining the most important aspects of the topic;
Produce technical work for a specific industry, target;
Recognise a wide range of complex tenses and some advanced grammatical structures and employ them to produce meaningful and accurate written French;
Explain in oral French a viewpoint on a topical issue, giving the most important aspects of the topic;
Converse in French with a good degree of accuracy and spontaneity about social, political and cultural topics.

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED LITERARY AND CULTURAL STUDIES IN FRENCH (POSTGRADUATE LEVEL)
modules.moduleContent: This module involves an independent piece of research on an aspect of French and Francophone literature or culture. Students develop a project related to an area of their own interest and complete an extended dissertation under the guidance of a supervisor. Students can choose to work on the novel, poetry, short story, drama, film, bande dessinée or television.
learningOutcomes: Interpret texts works through close reading, application of theory and contextualisation.
Carry out independent research and respond to feedback.
Critically evaluate secondary literature.
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and well-structured argument.
Demonstrate evidence of innovation and original research.

modules.moduleContent: Ancient tragedy has long been admired and its influence stretches even into the modern period. The great plays of antiquity remain among the most profound and important expressions of the human condition.
