Evaluate the impacts of the informational economy on socio-spatial polarization and on rural development
Analyze the spatial dimensions of the growth of information-based economic activities
Demonstrate the ability to write a report containing a critical analysis of policy or other sectoral developments
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Why do people living in Donegal enjoy lower levels of wealth than people living in Dublin and what can be done to address this? The module aims to give students a thorough understanding of the processes driving unbalanced regional development and related regional development policies and planning strategies. Core topics of the module include: changing perspectives on what constitutes regional development; the evolution of theories of regional development; regional development policies and instruments; the role of indigenous and multinational companies. The module will focus specifically on regional development problems and policies in Ireland, notably the impact of the Celtic Tiger, the current economic crisis and Ireland’s National Planning Framework.
learningOutcomes: Analyse processes of spatially-uneven development
Describe and compare traditional and recent regional development and theories
Describe and critically discuss different types of regional development policies and interventions
Explain the crucial role of local government and governance for regional development and discuss different governance arrangements
Analyze the evolution of regional development problems, processes and policies in Ireland with specific attention to the current National Spatial Strategy.
Demonstrate the ability to write a report containing a critical analysis of policy or other sectoral developments

modules.moduleContent: We are aware that the ecological environment is changing and that humans are the cause of much of that change; however, our perspective of human driven environmental change is often very recent. A longer-term perspective is vital as change has been ongoing throughout the Holocene. We will explore such questions as: What impact did hunter gatherers have on megaherbivores such as the woolly mammoth? What were the impacts of the first farmers? Did these early impacts change climate as well as ecology? How did medieval and post medieval human populations change landscapes and what did this mean for biodiversity? The first part of this module will focus on long-term ecological change via palaeoecological approaches. We will examine and critique how we use pollen and other microfossils to investigate ecological and environmental change in the last c.11,700 years of the Holocene Epoch. We will then examine the major ecological changes that have taken place, developing an understanding of both natural and human-driven changes in the landscape. We will discuss the impact of understanding our past to managing our future by examining the implications of palaeoecological results for climate change, nature conservation, land management options, and proposals for rewilding.
learningOutcomes: Comprehend and critique a range of palaeoecological approaches to the reconstruction of past environments
Understand palaeoecological methods and work with palaeoecological data
Have a spatial and temporal understanding of environmental change, being able to discuss Holocene environmental change in an Irish and European context and with other international examples
Apply knowledge of palaeo-environmental change to current and future issues including ecological conservation and climate change

modules.moduleContent: With concerns over environmental degradation ever increasing due to rising pressures on natural and semi-natural systems, the role and practices of conservation match this trend in ever increasing importance.  This module introduces conservation practice and efforts with how conservation and landscape management may be developed for a range of scenarios and locations.
learningOutcomes: Recoginse the need for conservation actions to ensure Earth system sustainability across a range of global environments.
Discuss the impact of wide reaching global envioronmental challenges such as climate change on natural systems and landscapes.
Work within a group to devise possible solutions to local-scale environmental issues that promote conservation.

modules.moduleContent: This module will explore both methodological and applied uses of palaeoecology in more detail, building on modules GY217 and GY367. The module will run as a team-based research project. The project will comprise a palaeoecological investigation of a specific site.
learningOutcomes: Display skills in species/pollen identification (use of keys etc.).
Develop effective scientific investigation and synthesise new findings with existing palaeoecological knowledge.
Use knowledge of research processes to understand a range of palaeoecological texts.
Demonstrate an in depth understanding of long-term changes in Irish ecosystems.
Effectively communicate evidence-based research results from palaeoecology, understand their potential impact and integrate these with policy-based ideas for land management.

modules.moduleContent:
This module relates to the content of other substantive themes in the course, while offering students field interpretation and analysis skills that will allow them to think critically about spatial-social issues of relevance in a range of spaces and environments. This module is required and cross-listed with all MA Geography streams to encourage trans-disciplinary spatial thinking.

This field immersion module is an application of a geographical methodology, fieldwork, through a range of case studies.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate familiarity with field-based geographical research skills
Demonstrate a critical awareness of some current and practical spatial-social issues relating to the places, landscapes and environments studied
Identify and critically reflect on the connections between the practice of fieldwork and academic research, generally and in their own work

modules.moduleContent:
In this module, students work on a ‘real world’ project as developed with a partner through such platforms as: community service learning, an internship with an artistic institution or collaborative, advocacy for a civil society group, or other public engagement framework.
(This compulsory module for the MA in Spatial Justice is open to MA in Geography students, as well students from other programmes. But MA students in Anthropology, Media Studies and Sociology are required to gain instructor consent before registering for this module.)
learningOutcomes: Apply key theoretical concepts in geography to real world projects
Demonstrate familiarity with the theoretical and practice-based debates with respect to public engagement, and participatory and action research design
Demonstrably apply one’s own critical lens on the world
Implement applied empirical research and creative practice on a project defined by a partner working on a geographically relevant topic

modules.moduleContent:
This module is taught within three broad areas. The first (i) introduces the key concepts of passive airborne surveys, including image capture methodologies, navigation and sensor technology and photogrammetric principles. A second area (ii) introduces the students to an active airborne survey technique, Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) and the potential complementary capabilities of this technology for different environments. The final component (iii) demonstrates the opportunities provided by drones as a new airborne survey platform, encompassing hardware, datasets, flight planning and operational restrictions. The module is a combination of theoretical and practical based sessions using both commercial and open source software.

Lecture Topics include; Applications of Aerial Surveys; Historical Development of Photogrammetry; Global Navigation Satellite Systems and Inertial Measurement Units; Determining Camera Interior and Exterior Orientation; Orthophotography and Image Distortions; Collinearity equations; Aerial triangulation; Bundle block adjustment; Structure from motion; LiDAR survey platforms; Types of laser scanner; LiDAR accuracy and errors; LiDAR processing and filtering; Sensor calibration and performance; Point cloud classification; Generation of DEMs and DSMs; Drone Operations and Restrictions; Planning Aerial Survey Campaigns; Drone hardware and datasets.
learningOutcomes: Describe the theoretical principles underpinning the use of photogrammetry for data provision.
Identify and suggest methods to remove distortions from aerial images.
Critically compare typical outputs of aerial hardware for different environments.
Apply different processing techniques to create DSMs, DEMs, point clouds and orthophotgraphy.
Contrast satellite and inertial navigational aids for image registration.
Appreciate the importance of proper flight planning in aerial survey campaigns.
Detail the restrictions and regulations for drone operations in Ireland.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Modern climate change, at its most fundamental level, is the consequence of the radiative imbalance caused by increased and increasing anthropogenic greenhouse gases in the atmosphere. This radiative imbalance causes an excess of heat to be trapped in the atmosphere, which is where the term ‘global warming’ arises. Over 90% of this excess heat trapped in the atmosphere has been stored in the ocean---in other words, ‘global warming’ could as accurately be described as ‘ocean warming’. Understanding the ocean’s response to climate change is key to understanding climate change itself. 
This course uses the IPCC AR5 report as the fundamental jumping off point for investigation of the ocean and climate change. The fundamental properties of the ocean (temperature, salinity, chemistry, freezing) are introduced first and threaded through the course to study ocean warming, salinity, sea ice, sea level rise and the ocean’s overturning circulation.
learningOutcomes: Appreciate the subtleties of observation and calculation associated with the most iconic climate change indicators, knowing their strengths, weaknesses, and quirks
Perform back of the envelope calculations to interpret and contextualise climate change figures
Read and perform basic manipulation of climate and ocean datasets
Appreciate the major challenges facing the oceans in a changing climate

modules.moduleContent:
The course will examine methods and techniques (e.g. quantitative and/or qualitative statistics; geographic information systems (GIS)) for undertaking research. 

Module Objectives: Students will acquire a familiarity with research methodologies for use in their research area.

This module will normally be taken in Semester 1.
learningOutcomes: Practice the design of effective data analysis techniques
Analyse data using appropriate methods and techniques
Display data using graphical methods
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The course will examine advanced methods and techniques (e.g. quantitative and/or qualitative statistics; geographic information systems (GIS)) for undertaking research.
Module Objectives: Students will acquire a familiarity with advanced research methodologies for use in their research area.
learningOutcomes: Practice the skills associated with the manipulation of data structures
Analyse data using appropriate methods and techniques
Display data using graphical methods
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: THE GAELIC ATHLETIC ASSOCIATION AND NATIONALISM
modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to provide students with a broad introduction to the development of the Gaelic Athletic Association (GAA)  towards the end of the nineteenth century and the part the organisation played in the development of nationalism and the cultural revival.
learningOutcomes: understand the context in which the GAA was founded and developed.
identify primary sources relevant to the study of the GAA and nationalism.
analyse and critically assess both primary and secondary historical sources relating to the topic.

modules.moduleName: A HISTORY OF SPORT AND LEISURE IN MODERN IRELAND
modules.moduleContent: This module explores the modernisation of sport in Ireland during the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.
learningOutcomes: • identify and analyse the economic, social and political contexts of changes to sport in Ireland.
• understand the significance of the gender, class and education in determining who participates in sporting activities.
• critically engage with primary material and historiographical debates.
• demonstrate awareness of the importance of sport to an understanding of politics and society in nineteenth- and twentieth-century Ireland.

modules.moduleName: SEARCHING FOR PEACE DURING THE COLD WAR
modules.moduleContent: The module seeks to explain, in historical and comparative contexts, how Yugoslavia, under the leadership of Tito and the Communist Party, re-emerged from the Second World War as a multi-ethnic federation which developed its own brand of socialism.
learningOutcomes: • demonstrate and apply understanding and knowledge of the key events and themes in the history of socialist Yugoslavia;
• demonstrate and apply understanding of Yugoslavia’s place in the Non-Aligned Movement;
• demonstrate the capacity for independent thought in the handling of both primary and secondary sources
• critically engage with the relevant main historiographical debates, and develop their own ideas and positions in relation to them

modules.moduleContent: The phenomena of Terrorism has been one of the defining issues in International Relations (IR) and Security Studies in both the 20th and 21st Centuries. This module will look at the evolution and development of Terrorism Studies that will also encompass the often-contentious nature of how to define terrorism.  Encapsulated within this will be several case studies from the historical archives of various terrorist campaigns taken from around the globe.
learningOutcomes: have a broad understanding of how terrorism has impacted societally, governmentally, and socially within countries which it has impacted.
have an understanding of how and why terrorist campaigns evolve and develop
have further developed writing and analytical skills
write an analytical essay related to the topic

modules.moduleName: IRELAND IN THE MODERN WORLD: THE TWENTIETH CENTURY
modules.moduleContent:
This module examines the major events in twentieth century Ireland as it went from being part of the United Kingdom, ruled directly from Westminster to a Dominion, a Republic and a member of international bodies such as the United Nations and the European Union

The module examines key issues such as the rise of the physical force nationalism, cultural nationalism, feminism, the labour movement and the increasing power of the Catholic Church. As well as analysing the major figures, this module also pays attention to the lives of ordinary people, from the experiences of the slums of tenement Dublin at the turn of the century to the events of the civil rights movement in Northern Ireland in the 1960s and 70s.
learningOutcomes: reflect on, and write critically about twentieth century Irish history
have a critical understanding of themes in social history
have a critical understanding of themes in economic history
have a critical understanding of themes in political history
use digital and traditional historical sources
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The importance of multidisciplinary approaches to the early landscape will be stressed, with an emphasis on history and archaeology and the natural sciences. Irish historic settlement will also be discussed in its European context.
learningOutcomes: be familiar with major developments in Ireland in the period c.AD400–1600;
be aware of where and how people lived during this time;
be able to recognise Irish medieval archaeological monuments and landscapes;
understand the significance of the medieval past in the modern world.

modules.moduleContent:
This elective examines European visual culture during the Renaissance period.  It explores the production and consumption of art, the role of politics and patronage in shaping the art market, and the evolution of style from the ‘rebirth’ of interest in the ancient world to the visual sophistication of the High Renaissance.  Though focusing primarily on painting, it also considers other media including sculptures, ceramics and furniture, and investigates issues such as the use of art in both public and private spaces, artist-patron relationships and the techniques used in the production of sacred and secular art.
No previous knowledge of the History of Art is required.
A site visit to the NGI will be a required component of this course.
learningOutcomes: Identify the key artistic developments that occurred in Italy during the Renaissance.
Analyse primary and secondary sources relating to this topic
Demonstrate analytical skills through completing an essay and additional continuous-assessment assignment.
Present written work that conforms to the professional citation conventions specified in ‘Rules for contributors to Irish Historical Studies’.

modules.moduleName: EASTERN AND CENTRAL EUROPE UNDER COMMUNISM
modules.moduleContent: This module will look at the history of Eastern and Central Europe under communism from 1945 until 1989. Along with the political narrative and history of the period, we will think about how societies functioned under state socialism and what were the lived experiences of people in these times. We will thus look at key historical actors and events, but also leisure time, work, holidays, travelling, culture (movies, TV, music) and sport under socialism.
learningOutcomes: • Have a detailed understanding of the political history of Eastern Europe under communism
• Have a grasp of how communist parties and regimes ruled during 1945-1989, how they came to power and why they collapsed.
• Have a sense of the ‘lived experience’ of ordinary people under state socialism.
• Have an understanding of communist ideology and practice in the twentieth century.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will explore the political, social, economic and cultural modernisation of Ireland.
In the academic year 2024-25, the focus will be on the Tudor period (1485 to 1603).
learningOutcomes: have developed a general knowledge and understanding of the major political, religious, social, cultural and economic developments that occurred in Ireland as the country made the transition from lordship to kingdom to colony.

modules.moduleContent: This module will investigate methodological questions in the discipline of history. It will do this by examining the relationships between academic history and popular works that employ historical subject matter or address historical themes such as documentaries and popular biographies.
learningOutcomes: learn to differentiate between historical and fictional narratives
engage in the analysis of primary sources
formulate evidence-based critical historical analyses

modules.moduleContent: The module begins with the medieval origins of the Holy Roman Empire during the time of Charlemagne.  We then consider its evolution, constitutional structure, ethnic diversity and social development during the tumultuous era of Protestant Reformation ending with the Thirty Years War. Students participate actively in a series of in-class discussions, role-plays, panel discussions on historical documents and oral reports, composing an essay for submission at the end of the semester.
learningOutcomes: understand the development of the Holy Roman  Empire from the Middle Ages until the Reformation
learn to work together in groups
hone public presentation skills
formulate evidence-based critical historical analyses

modules.moduleName: THE COUNTRY HOUSE C. 1720 TO PRESENT
modules.moduleContent: This module will consider various aspects of the social, economic, political and cultural history of country houses from growth in the eighteenth century to decline in the twentieth. It is also intended to introduce students to significant issues affecting the country house in the twenty-first century, focusing on topics such as the presentation and promotion of historic properties as competitive attractions in the heritage and cultural tourism markets.
learningOutcomes: • have a knowledge of the reasons for the historical growth and decline of country houses.
• have an awareness of their material culture and landscape design
• have an understanding of the major themes and issues that shape the future of country houses
• be able to identify primary sources available for the study of country houses and landed estates, and how to assess their strengths and weaknesses.
• be able to engage with historiographical debates and to develop an understanding of historical research
• demonstrate in their written work an understanding of the main trends and factors that have shaped the history of the country house and be able to present cogent, coherent arguments.

modules.moduleContent: In tutorial sessions the student is introduced to key elements of historical research, including the nature of a literature review, how to engage with historiography, the theoretical concepts underpinning certain kinds of history, and the methodological and ethical issues involved in certain types of research. The student completes technical exercises in bibliography and presentation, and delivers short presentations on his/her work.
learningOutcomes: locate, identify and evaluate primary sources that will be fundamental to their own research
identify and evaluate the writings of key authors in their research subject area
present a short paper on their research to the class group

modules.moduleContent: Under the guidance of an assigned supervisor, the student undertakes research on an approved topic for a Master’s thesis. The student will begin the research and complete a draft introduction, table of contents and bibliography by the end of the first semester. It is expected that the thesis will be distinguished by the care taken in the design of the research, the range of sources, and the quality of the analysis. It is also expected to be underpinned by extensive reading and serious reflection.

The module will address the changing character of war since the end of the First World War through to the twenty-first century, and will examine the evolution of military thought and practice throughout this period. It will cover a wide range of issues and students will analyse the conduct of war on land, sea and in the air in a variety of wars and conflicts around the globe.
learningOutcomes: Identify key phases in the development of warfare during the period in question.
Discuss key technological developments and their impact.
Show an awareness of the main theorists in strategic thought during the relevant period.
Display knowledge of key debates and the main archival sources and publications.
Discuss these elements in an informed and articulate manner.

modules.moduleName: READINGS IN MODERN EUROPEAN HISTORY I
modules.moduleContent: In this module students are exposed to readings topically related to a theme (e.g. colonialism, genocide, empire, etc.). They are asked to conduct weekly readings which form the basis of weekly presentations and in-class discussion. These readings, presentations and discussions in turn form the basis for written reviews.
learningOutcomes: - recognize existing and emerging fields in European history
- distinguish different categories of historical research and discuss their respective analytical values in application to specific problems in European history
- process a large body of secondary source material, and appreciate, based on able selection, approaches, methods and significance of seminal studies
- monitor the coherence and consistence of historical argumentation critically
- formulate a critical position towards body of existing research
- transfer approaches and methods to other fields of historical research

modules.moduleContent: The module deals with the nature of historical evidence including the ways in which that evidence is created and transmitted. It also provides skills for the interpretation of some of that evidence in the form of palaeographical skills and statistical techniques. It will focus primarily on the sources for Irish history 1500-1939.
learningOutcomes: recognize and locate the main classes of evidence for the construction of Ireland’s past.
distinguish different categories of evidence.
recognise the limitations and strengths of various classes of source material.
critically examine a broad range of relevant primary source material relating to Ireland’s past.
reflect, in an essay, on the range of sources for a particular project and assess their potential.

modules.moduleContent:
To develop students’ knowledge (during the fourth year of research) of current scholarship in the wider discipline. 

Attendance at the History Department's Research Seminar (See History Postgraduate Handbook for details.) Students must attend all specified obligatory meetings of the Seminar and another seven over the course of the academic year.
learningOutcomes: continued to deepen their independent critical engagement with current trends,  methodological challenges, and innovations in scholarship in the discipline;
continued their active participation in research seminars through engaging in increasingly reflective discussion with speakers and peers;
continued to refine their professional presentation skills through ongoing critical observation of, and reflection on, the presentation styles of peers and invited speakers;
demonstrated their initiative in positioning their research within the scholarly context of collective and interdisciplinary exercise;
developed advanced skills in convening a University Research Seminar.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To develop Ph.D. students’ skills in refining and communicating their research to an audience of professional academics in the setting of a national/international conference.

Module Content: Research paper delivered to a national/international academic conference.
learningOutcomes: Working in conjunction with their respective supervisor, students will learn how to prepare a substantial paper based on their research findings for delivery to a group of professional academics at a national/international event.
Students will learn to develop visual and other aids to help them convey their research findings to a professional academic audience.
Students will acquire the experience of delivering a research paper to an audience of professional academics.
Students will acquire the experience of fielding questions from an audience of professional academics.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To allow and encourage Ph.D. students to work on and publish an article/chapter related to their research topic in a peer-review academic publication of national/international standing.

Module Content: Article/chapter published in a peer-review academic publication of national/international standing.
learningOutcomes: Working in conjunction with their respective supervisors, students will learn how to prepare a substantial paper based on their research findings for inclusion in a peer-review journal/book published by and for professional academics.
Students will gain the experience of adapting their writings to varying standards of academic presentation.
Students will learn to navigate the professional peer-review process.
Students will acquire the experience of proofreading their work in preparation for a professional publication.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To allow and encourage Ph.D. students to work on and publish an article/chapter related to their research topic in a peer-review postgraduate-level journal/book.

Module Content: Article/chapter published in a peer-review postgraduate-level publication.
learningOutcomes: Working in conjunction with their respective supervisors, students will learn how to prepare a substantial paper based on their research findings for inclusion in a peer-review journal/book published by and for postgraduates.
Students will gain the experience of adapting their writings to varying standards of academic presentation.
Students will acquire the habit of working to a deadline not set by the department.
Students will acquire the experience of proofreading their work in preparation for publication.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To allow the student in their third year of research to study abroad and acquire specific skills relevant to their research.

Module Content: The module will allow students to study abroad to obtain particular skills not available in MU.  Students may take one or more modules in the host institution amounting to 10 ECTS.

The module will provide an introduction to politics and to social studies. The Politics element of the module will focus on developing the students’ understanding of political theory and institutions. It will focus, in particular, on relevant Irish and European institutions and will include an exploration of the theory and practice of international politics. The Social Studies element will explore issues relating to an understanding of Irish society today, topics include class, religion, gender and ethnicity.

IMPORTANT INFORMATION
Students must be enrolled on DADEF. Not available to any other students.

modules.moduleName: MILITARY DECISION MAKING AT BRIGADE LEVEL
modules.moduleContent:
Responsible Department:
History, through the Centre for Military History and Strategic Studies, in partnership with the teaching staff at the Officer Training Wing of the Infantry School at the Military College (DFTC, Curragh Camp).

Module Content: This module introduces the different methodologies associated with military decision making and builds student knowledge and understanding of how these can be applied in a variety of contexts at brigade level. Students will examine and apply relevant concepts, including Military Decision-Making Process as currently applied by the Irish Defence Forces, and will be required to demonstrate advanced level analysis and critical thinking in the application of this process. Students will receive lectures on decision making theory and practice and will test their knowledge and understanding in a series of case study exercises, tutorials and classroom discussions.
learningOutcomes: Examine and critically analyse different theoretical approaches to military decision making.
Apply such processes to practical examples relating to contemporary operations involving the land component.
Understand the application of decision-making processes in the planning of military operations at brigade level.

modules.moduleContent:
Responsible Department:
History, through the Centre for Military History and Strategic Studies, in partnership with the teaching staff at the Officer Training Wing of the Infantry School at the Military College (DFTC, Curragh Camp).

Module Content:
The module will examine the theory and practice of contemporary military operations at battalion and brigade level, with a particular focus on the land component and relevant supporting arms. It will include an analysis of current military doctrine and of relevant concepts and theories associated with the topic, including the role played by Combat Service and Combat Service Support assets and also by relevant air and naval capabilities.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate their understanding of contemporary military operations within the land environment.
Show how organic battalion and brigade assets, and joint capabilities, can be employed effectively in land component and crisis management operations in accordance with military doctrine.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective2: 

1. To develop the students' knowledge and comprehension of Defence Studies (including military history and strategic studies) while recognising the need for reflection, self-awareness and self-directed learning. 

2. To lay the foundations for future learning and development at higher levels. The module will focus on developing the students' understanding of military theory, including relevant theory from strategic studies and international relations, and explore relevant historical case studies with reference to such theory. Topics examined include: 1. Strategy and the principles of war 2. The international system 3. Sources and instruments of power 4. The development of modern warfare 5. Air warfare 6. Maritime strategy and naval warfare 7. European security architecture 8. Irish foreign and defence policy 9. Military sociology
Contact Department for further details.
Contact department for further details.

The ‘Royal Sites’—so-called because of the identification in the early medieval period as the pre-Christian residences of provincial kings—have been the subject of intense recent archaeological investigation. This research has revealed a deep history of activity at each of these—Tara, Emain Macha (Navan Fort), Dún Ailinne (Knockaulin) and Cruachain—stretching back in some cases to the Neolithic period before 3000 BC. These complexes and their attendant landscapes also play an important anchor role in early medieval texts and stories with a particular association with kingship. The archaeological evidence and the later synthetic histories will be appraised through a series of lectures and site visits.

Note: This module will also be available in Semester 2, under the code, ID013X The Cultural Heritage of 'Royal Sites'
learningOutcomes: Identify and access the archaeological sources pertaining to the ‘royal sites’.
Detail chronologically evidence for the main developments at these sites.
Identify the key narratives and textual sources pertaining to key sites e.g. Tara & Cruachain.
Consider the concept of early Irish kingship as represented in these sources.
Combine the archaeological and documentary evidence to provide a balanced understanding of the key features of these sites.

modules.moduleName: THE CULTURAL HERITAGE OF EARLY IRISH SOCIETY
modules.moduleContent:
This is a foundation course in Irish Cultural Heritage Studies introducing students to a general understanding of early Irish society. Late prehistoric social organisation was controlled by a warrior aristocracy who ruled dispersed agricultural communities but with some evidence of emerging provincial political structures. Early medieval society was based around the family (fine, a complex multigenerational social and legal unit) and the tribe (about 150 examples) cradled in a number of important social systems including kinship, clientship, fosterage and formal concepts of honour. Students will be introduced to this period through multidisciplinary perspective including archaeology, history and literature that will include the role and status of women. Sources will include both historical documents (such as the Annals, Law Tracts, Genealogies, Hagiographies and a wealth of early literature) and archaeological data derived from modern excavation of many residential and funerary sites.

Note: Students taking the Certificate in Irish Cultural Heritage in Semester 1, must take ID001 or ID014 as the core module. This can also be included as an optional module for the Certificate in Irish Cultural Heritage.

modules.moduleName: ISSUES AND STRATEGIES IN CONTEMPORARY MODERN LANGUAGE STUDIES
modules.moduleContent:
Bibliography, methodology, understanding and contextualising current research in specific fields, engaging with current research and utilizing it for students' own research purposes.

IMPORTANT NOTE
MA in German or equivalent
learningOutcomes: discuss issues arising from current research in the students' specific areas to be decided on the basis of attending students' respective research interests.

modules.moduleName: GENDER AND SEXUALITY IN SOCIETY AND CULTURE 1
modules.moduleContent:
This module will be made up of five interconnected units on a range of gender and sexuality related topics such as are listed below. Please Note that the units listed below are representative and may differ from year to year. Although each unit theme may vary from year to year, the course will always offer a wide and interesting coverage of gender and sexuality in culture and society.

Unit 1: Gender Identity and Law: Transgender and Intersex
Unit 2: Contemporary Constructions of Motherhood
Unit 3: Gender and Opera
Unit 4: Masculinity, Same-sex Passion and Narrative
Unit 3: Recent history of reproductive justice activism in Ireland
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain key aspects of the production and performance of gender and sexuality in chosen cultural and social contexts.

Summarise in broad terms how gender and sexuality are produced, performed and/or legislated for in a variety of social and cultural contexts and analyse closely a selection of texts showing how this happens.
Interpret how gender and sexuality are explored in cultural texts and performances through close readings and contextualisation.
Critically evaluate secondary literature.
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and well-structured argument.

modules.moduleName: GENDER AND SEXUALITY IN SOCIETY AND CULTURE 2
modules.moduleContent:
This module will be made up of five interconnected units on a range of gender and sexuality related topics such as are listed below. Please Note that the units listed below are representative and may differ from year to year. Although each unit theme may vary from year to year, the course will always offer a wide and interesting coverage of gender and sexuality in culture and society.

Unit 6: Gender and Music
Unit 7: Gender and Reproduction
Unit 8: Marginalised Mothers
Unit 9: Queer battlegrounds: Contesting space at LGBT Pride events
Unit 10: Desire and Identity:  Some Contemporary Kinks
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain key aspects of the production and performance of gender and sexuality in chosen cultural and social contexts.

Summarise in broad terms how gender and sexuality are produced, performed and/or legislated for in a variety of social and cultural contexts and analyse closely a selection of texts showing how this happens.
Interpret how gender and sexuality are explored in cultural texts and performances through close readings and contextualisation.
Critically evaluate secondary literature.
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and well-structured argument.

modules.moduleName: STATES, MARKETS AND GLOBAL DEVELOPMENT CHALLENGES
modules.moduleContent:
This module will review some of the biggest global development challenges we face as a planet and in our societies. 
It will examine questions such as why some countries are rich, and others are poor? 
How vital are states and state legitimacy in promoting development? 
Given climate and environmental challenges, what are the alternatives to economic growth, and will they be popular? 
How much progress has the world made, and how can we measure it? Are the SDGs being achieved? 
As the world population slows, how will it impact countries, and what policy responses are required? Finally, who rules the world? 
What roles do the UN and other international organisations play in global development?
learningOutcomes: Understand the roles of geography, history and institutions in state development.
Have examined the importance of states and state legitimacy in promoting socio-economic development.
Have looked at the debates around the need to replace current economic growth systems and the proposed alternatives -  Green Growth and Degrowth?
Have reviewed the SDGs and the progress and challenges remaining to 2030.
Have an appreciation for global poverty and inequality trends and steps to reduce their incidence.
Have examined current demographic trends and related policy responses.
Understand global governance and the roles played by international institutions in addressing global development issues.
Be familiar with humanitarian principles and standards and key debates within contemporary humanitarianism.
Be familiar with international humanitarian law and the legal framework applicable to humanitarian contexts.
Understand what an emergency is and how emergency needs are prioritised.
Understand how the international system responds to large scale emergencies.
Understand key concerns and priorities in thematic responses.

modules.moduleName: SUSTAINABLE TOURISM AND VOLUNTEERING: ALL SUNSHINE AND SMILES?
modules.moduleContent:
Often billed as one of the world's largest industries, after the hit of the pandemic, tourism is bouncing back. According to UN Tourism, in 2023, over 1.3 billion international tourism arrivals spent $1.1 trillion in their destination countries, 
and these figures do not include the sizeable domestic tourism sector. 
From an economic perspective, tourism offers tremendous development opportunities through growth, foreign exchange earnings, and employment opportunities. 
The industry has significant structural weaknesses, with examples of unbalanced tourism having significant environmental impacts, precarious and poorly paid labour conditions, and larger cultural implications.  
The module will critically assess tourism's social-economic and environmental footprints and explore examples of sustainable tourism practices and the role and impact of voluntourism.
learningOutcomes: Provide a critical overview of the main effects of global tourism and volunteering regarding the environment, local economies, and labour market conditions.
To outline a typology of volunteering and its potential impacts from development and tourism perspectives.
To identify the environmental, social, and economic challenges around promoting sustainable tourism.
To critically assess various tourism and volunteering models proposed to promote sustainable tourism including eco and community tourism and ethical volunteering.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to present a rationale for the concept and broad approach of Global Citizenship Education (GCE),  its relationship to Development practice, and encourage critical reflection on its place within broader global development strategies. 
Its relationship to other educational approaches such as 'Global Learning', 'Development Education', and 'Education for Sustainable Development' will be examined and reflected upon. 
As the general approach to GCE can be understood as a participatory process of learning, the module will encourage students to explore and critique this, and enable their practical appreciation of this through group-centered exercises and activities, with specific focus on the role of facilitation as an alternative to more didactic teaching methods.
learningOutcomes: Have an understanding of the concept of Development Education as it is interpreted by educators, development practitioners, NGOs and statutory bodies in Ireland and internationally.

Have developed a critical appreciation for Development Education as a global development strategy and where it is situated in relation to other approaches with similar objectives, such as Global Learning, and Global Citizenship Education.
Have a clear awareness of the practicalities and overall role of facilitation as an approach to delivering Development Education.
Have acquired confidence and competence to design a Development Education workshop on a specific theme for a particular group of participants.
Have developed basic group facilitation skills and will have experienced using these as part of the module.

modules.moduleName: CONTEMPORARY GLOBAL ISSUES, CHALLENGES AND RESPONSES
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate understanding of some of the main social and economic theories of global development.
Critically analyse the complex interdependencies in our world today in terms of social, political, economic, cultural and environmental factors and power relations shaping global development.
Demonstrate an ability to identify various personal and institutional responses to different global development challenges at local and global levels.
Analyse contemporary literature and research on specific global development issues, with reference to chosen case studies, in terms of their implications for education practice.
Develop an education/public engagement project on a global development issue of their choice.
Demonstrate an understanding of how educators and community and youth workers seek to integrate the SDG’s and other global commitments into their analysis, practice, and policy development processes.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Díreofar, sa mhodúl seo, ar phointí deacrachta sa Ghaeilge, mar shampla an clásal coibhneasta, rialacha an tséimhithe, an Caighdeán Oifigiúil agus canúintí réigiúnacha.
learningOutcomes: Pointí deacrachta sa Ghaeilge a láimhseáil go cumasach, muiníneach.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will provide students with an opportunity to disseminate their research to the wider research community. Students will develop skills in assembling and presenting their research findings.

This module will require a student to prepare and deliver a poster or oral presentation(s) at recognised national or international research conferences, fora or colloquia. The selection of appropriate conferences, fora or colloquia will be determined by an oversight committee comprised of the Head of Department, Postgraduate Director and the course director of the Structured PhD Geography Programme.

This module will normally be taken in Semester 1
learningOutcomes: Introduce and discuss research findings.
Discuss research methodologies and findings in oral form.

modules.moduleContent:
Advanced specialist modules can be taken from those on offer from the Department of Geography, NIRSA, other Departments at NUIM, or a module on offer in another partner institution. Students select module from a range of options.
Module Objectives: To enhance students’ ability to explore and debate complex issues at an advanced level.
learningOutcomes: Identify and understand key geographical processes
Evaluate critically a variety of methods and approaches in geography
Discuss and analyze research findings in a research report format
Discuss research methodologies and findings in oral and written forms

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO MEDIEVAL HISTORY: VIKINGS AND NORMANS
modules.moduleContent:
This module gives students a flavour of medieval history by focusing on two important and illustrative topics. The first component surveys the expansion and activities of Viking-age Scandinavians in Europe and beyond and their impact as raiders, military and political conquerors, settlers and traders. The second features the Normans—descendants of the Vikings—in Normandy, Italy, Sicily, England, Ireland and the Near East. Among the themes for this part of the module are war and defence, religion and the church, death and burial, town and country, and trade and industry. Both components address the connection between the narratives of history offered by historians (historiography) and the evidence that underpins or may undermine those narratives.

The module will be supported by a continuous assessment programme, with assignments covering both components, spread across the full semester, aimed at honing the intellectual and technical skills (researching, synthesis, interpreting a variety of sources, referencing and citation) that students will require in order to pursue the study of history in second and third years.
learningOutcomes: have a sound knowledge of the Viking and Norman worlds
have an appreciation of the variety, value and limitations of the sources
have a recognition of the ways in which the Vikings and Normans have made an impact on the modern world

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO MILITARY HISTORY AND WAR STUDIES
modules.moduleContent: The module will provide an introduction to the study of war in the past and in the present. It will draw on methodologies of military history and strategic studies to explore alternative ideas as to the causes, conduct and impact of war. It will include an exploration of the economic, political, societal impact of war, including engagement with ideas about race, gender and war. In addition, a range of battles and campaigns will be examined in order to foster an understanding of evolution in the theory and practice of war over the course of the last century.
learningOutcomes: Identify appropriate methodologies for military history.
Critique alternative explanations for the causes of war and conflict.
Demonstrate understanding of changes in the conduct of war over the past century.
Explore the impact of war on society, and of society on war.
Examine key debates on current security issues.
Present their ideas in a convincing manner, with reference to relevant history.

modules.moduleContent: This module will examine the economic, political and social history of Ulster from the first Home Rule Bill in 1886 to the reinstatement in Northern Ireland of Direct Rule from Westminster in 1972
learningOutcomes: • have a critical understanding of the economic, social, and political history of Ulster from 1886 to 1969.
• assess the causes and consequences of partition.
• engage with the defining social, economic and cultural elements of unionist and nationalist identities during this period.
• reflect upon the causes of division and civil unrest during this period.
• analyse and interpret primary source material and historiography relating to this topic

modules.moduleContent: In this module, students are introduced to the science of categorizing, classifying and analysing historical documents as material objects. Structured around a major theme or period in history, the aim is to familiarise participants with the various types and structures of documentary and ancillary evidence used to present cogent interpretations of the past.
learningOutcomes: critically engage with various types of documentary evidence
have an understanding of classifying and analysing historical documents as material objects
be familiar with the variety and location of primary sources which are available on the topics covered

modules.moduleName: EUROPE IN THE EARLY MODERN WORLD
modules.moduleContent: This core module surveys the period c. 1450 to 1789, focusing on themes such as gender and sexuality, medicine, religion, political and social theory and representations of others.
learningOutcomes: have developed knowledge of the major formative developments in social, cultural, economic and political life that made Europe distinctly modern during this period;
have developed knowledge of Europe’s evolving position and relationships in a global context;
have developed skills in locating and interpreting primary and secondary source material relevant to a variety of aspects of European history in this period;
have developed skills in collating and synthesising source material;
have developed capacity for writing a coherent narrative;
have developed capacity for presenting a report and essay that conforms to the professional citation conventions specified in ‘Rules for contributors to Irish Historical Studies’.

modules.moduleContent: This module examines major developments that shaped modern Britain
learningOutcomes: engage with key historiographical debates relating both to slavery and the Atlantic World
have developed a nuanced understanding of the multifaceted nature of British involvement in the slave trade and an appreciation of its on-going significance
use a range of traditional and online archival resources
have developed written and oral presentation skills by completing written assignments and contributing to class discussions

modules.moduleContent:
The dissertation gives the final year History student a high degree of autonomy to develop, design, conduct, present and write-up a significant piece of research. With the support of dissertation tutorials and library sessions (semester 1), and under the guidance of an assigned supervisor (semesters 1 and 2), each student undertakes research on an approved topic. This will be in an area that links with a module the student has already taken or is taking. The topic may also be in the area of a SPUR internship or similar, subject to the approval of the department. The student will present orally on their topic to their peers, while the final dissertation will be assessed under the following headings: introduction; sources utilised; presentation; bibliography; overall achievement. The referencing should be in accordance with ‘Rules for contributors to Irish Historical Studies’. Please note that material examined in another module cannot be re-submitted as part of the dissertation.

INFORMATION INFORMATION
HY301 History Dissertation (10 credits) is available as an option only if a minimum of 650 has been achieved in Second Arts History (65% average for the year).
learningOutcomes: Evaluate historiographical debates within a field of study.
Interrogate historical evidence to construct effective arguments.
Demonstrate a capacity for independent work to produce an undergraduate dissertation.
Present written work that conforms to the professional citation conventions specified in ‘Rules for contributors to Irish Historical Studies’.

modules.moduleName: THATCHER’S BRITAIN: A POLITICAL AND SOCIAL HISTORY OF BRITAIN IN THE 1980S
modules.moduleContent: British politics and society were transformed in the 1980s. At the helm in Downing Street was Margaret Thatcher, elected three times as Prime Minister and the first woman to hold this position. This module examines the contradictions and complexities of Thatcher’s Britain. It explores the economic changes that underpinned the politics of the period and their repercussions across the society.
learningOutcomes: • have a critical understanding of the main political, economic and social developments in Britain in the 1980s.
• have a good understanding of the role Margaret Thatcher played in shaping British politics while she was Prime Minister.
• be conversant in the main economic and ideological arguments of this period.
• have a critical understanding of the main historiographical debates surrounding this period.
• analyse and critically engage with primary texts related to Britain in the 1980s.

modules.moduleContent: This module looks at the transformation of the Balkans from the medieval period to the end of the eighteenth century.
learningOutcomes: • demonstrate a good understanding and knowledge of main developments in the history of the Balkans in the middle ages and the early modern period
• demonstrate and apply understanding of the transformations which the Balkan societies underwent due to migration, war, imperial rule and transfer of ideas
• demonstrate the capacity for independent thought in the handling of historical sources
• critically engage with the relevant main historiographical debates, and develop their own ideas and positions in relation to them

modules.moduleName: ART, DESIGN AND SOCIETY IN SEVENTEENTH-CENTURY EUROPE
modules.moduleContent: This module considers artistic developments in seventeenth-century Europe.  It explores how, in the wake of the Counter Reformation, painting was used as a form of religious propaganda.  More broadly, it investigates the ways in which the socio-political and cultural background of the period shaped the production of art.  From nepotistic papal patronage to the secular tastes of the Dutch bourgeoisie, it examines the context in which artists like Bernini, Caravaggio and Rembrandt worked, and locates their work in an historical as well as stylistic context.  No previous knowledge of the History of Art is required.
learningOutcomes: Identify key artistic developments in seventeenth-century Europe.
Demonstrate an ability to contextualise artworks from this period, locating them in a broader cultural, social, and economic context.
Apply this knowledge to the analysis of artworks in the collection of the National Gallery of Ireland.
Interrogate historical evidence to construct effective arguments.

modules.moduleContent: This module is the first part of a year-long examination of the European continent during the Second World War (but may be taken without the second part, HY315). Its focus is on Nazi plans for the future of Europe once a German military victory had been secured. As such it considers the often contradictory political, economic and racial aims of the Nazis and how they proposed to bring them to fruition. Special attention is paid to the origins and implementation of the Final Solution and its relationship with the course of the war.
learningOutcomes: Understand and discuss with confidence the course of the Second World War in Europe and beyond;
Acquire a deep knowledge of the nature of Nazi control over Western and Eastern Europe, and the relationship between those modes of control an Nazi ideology and plans for the future;
Understand the origins, course and consequences of the Nazis’ Final Solution as well the historiography of the period;
Be familiar with the challenges posed by primary sources produced in wartime.

modules.moduleName: THE DECLINE AND RISE OF THE IRISH COUNTRY HOUSE, 1845 TO PRESENT
modules.moduleContent:
This module begins by providing an in-depth examination of the reasons for the decline of Irish country houses from the late nineteenth century onwards. It looks at the impact that the Great Famine, the Land War of the 1880s, the Land Acts from 1870 to 1909, the economic fallout from post—war global depression, the Great War 1914-18, and the revolutionary period 1919-23 all had on landed families and their homes. 
The focus then turns to the struggle for survival that faced big house owners in independent Ireland. It considers questions of management, sustainability and public and political perceptions of these houses up to the present day. It also introduces comparative aspects with country houses in Britain and wider Europe.
learningOutcomes: have a knowledge of the reasons for the decline of Irish country houses from the late nineteenth century onwards.
identify primary sources available for the study of landed estates/country houses, and how to assess their strengths and weaknesses.
engage with historiographical debates and to develop an understanding of historical research and how to use primary sources.

demonstrate in their written work an understanding of the main trends and factors that shaped the history of the landed estate/country house in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, and present cogent, coherent arguments.

modules.moduleContent: This module focuses on one historiographic method in great detail, including the genesis of that particular method, its evolution over time and its deployment within specific historical circumstances.  The module requires each student to conduct a comparative analysis of key issues and discuss their findings in class, gradually building up knowledge of the issue over the course of the semester.  Finally, each participant is required to critique both the advantages and limitations of the overall historiographic method in an essay submitted at the end of the semester, providing examples from our course of study.
learningOutcomes: gain an appreciation of historical method
achieve familiarity with current historiography
understand the development of an historical field
formulate evidence-based critical historical analyses

modules.moduleContent: The module will address major historiographical debates in history.  It will examine various approaches and assessments advanced by historians in relation to major events and themes in history. It will also demonstrate to students how an interdisciplinary approach can be used to assess and evaluate these debates and controversies.  On completing the module, students should have a good foundation in historiography.
learningOutcomes: Reflect on, and write critically about historiography
Understand the sources historical writings are based on
Have a critical understanding of themes in social history
Have a critical understanding of themes in economic history
Have a critical understanding of themes in political history
Use digital databases to find and critique original source materials
Demonstrate these skills through answering essay type questions, analytical exercises and critical reviews of primary sources.

modules.moduleContent: This module explores how we access past experiences of conflict. We will examine how the collection and preservation of archives shape understandings of the impact of violence on individuals and societies. Archives are an accumulated form of record that can include any medium and we will consider how an expansive sense of what constitutes an archive can broaden and deepen our knowledge of the past.
learningOutcomes: understand the importance of the collection and interpretation of archives on our knowledge of the past
identify and analyse the breadth of archival sources available to researchers of conflict (eg. material, textual, audio, visual, geographical, representational)
critically engage with primary material and historiographical debates
demonstrate awareness of the complexity of conflict and its legacies as represented in the records of the past

modules.moduleContent: To provide an opportunity for students in the first year of research to devise a research plan for their thesis featuring structured approaches to researching and writing their study. This includes articulating key research questions, locating the thesis within existing historiography, identifying and adopting appropriate methodologies and conceptual framework(s), developing skills in identifying and accessing relevant primary sources and secondary literature, honing skills in coherent argumentation. Students also practise compiling applications for postgraduate scholarships.
learningOutcomes: recognize the possible ways of structuring their thesis and consider appropriate methodological options and discussion of those options
distinguish between aims and objectives and create overall specific structures for their work
develop a strategy for organizing the project
monitor the coherence and consistency of argumentation
reflect in a structured form on the organisation and interpretative strategies for their thesis

modules.moduleContent: To provide an opportunity for students in the first year of research to develop a research plan for their thesis. In particular this involves the development of techniques on research and presentation specific to historians including citation conventions and bibliographical conventions as well as the development of presentation skills and discussion of their own work in progress for peer appraisal.
learningOutcomes: recognize the main aims and objectives of their thesis
distinguish alternative approaches and be prepared to defend in a seminar their distinctive approach
develop the presentation skills for written work
monitor the coherence and consistency of their emerging arguments and subject this to peer review
reflect in a seminar paper on their on-going research

modules.moduleContent:
To develop students’ knowledge (during the first year of research) of current scholarship in the discipline and his/her presentation skills. 

Attendance at the History Department's Research Seminar and a short introductory presentation on each student’s research topic (See History Postgraduate Handbook for details.) Students must attend all specified obligatory meetings of the Seminar and another seven over the course of the academic year, and they must make an introductory presentation.
learningOutcomes: developed a familiarity with ongoing historiographical and methodological  advances in the discipline;
developed an understanding of the operating norms of a University Research Seminar;
practised active participation in research seminars through listening and questioning;

honed their presentation skills through an individual presentation to the University Research Seminar and through ongoing critical observation of the presentation styles of peers and invited speakers;
begun to develop awareness of academic research as a collective and interdisciplinary exercise.

modules.moduleContent:
To develop students’ understanding of and critical engagement with current trends, methodological challenges, and innovations in scholarship in the discipline (during the third year of research). 

Attendance at the History Department's Research Seminar (See History Postgraduate Handbook for details.) Students must attend all specified obligatory meetings of the Seminar and another seven over the course of the academic year.
learningOutcomes: deepened their independent critical engagement with current trends, historiographical and methodological challenges and innovations in scholarship in the discipline;
practised active participation in research seminars through engaging in increasingly reflective discussion with speakers and peers;
refined their professional presentation skills through ongoing critical observation of, and reflection on, the presentation styles of peers and invited speakers;
developed their independent engagement in research as a collective and interdisciplinary exercise;
developed basic skills in convening a University Research Seminar.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To provide an opportunity for M.Litt/Ph.D students at an advanced stage of their research to present an aspect of their work to the History Department's research seminar, or the seminar of another department within Maynooth University, as agreed with the doctoral supervisor.

Module Content: The student will deliver a substantial research paper (c. 45 minutes in length) to the History Department's research seminar, or another department within NUIM, as agreed with the doctoral supervisor. Credit for such a presentation may not be obtained more than once.
learningOutcomes: developed and demonstrated skills in the preparation of a substantial scholarly paper, based on their research findings, to their peers;
demonstrated their mastery of professional presentation skills in transmitting research findings to an audience of their peers;
demonstrated their independent critical engagement with current trends, historiographical and methodological challenges and innovations in scholarship in the discipline;
demonstrated their independent engagement in research as a collective and interdisciplinary exercise;
demonstrated a capacity to engage in reflective discussion with his/her audience.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To provide an opportunity for the student in the first year of research to acquire skills in a language relevant to their research.

Module Content: The student will attend and participate fully in an appropriate Language for Academic Purposes module or equivalent course, in NUIM or elsewhere, as agreed with the doctoral supervisor.

The module will provide an introduction to economics, finance and accountancy. Students will be taught the basic ideas of economics and will be introduced to the practice of financial management and accountancy.

Responsible Department:
History, through the Centre for Military History and Strategic Studies, in partnership with the teaching staff at the Officer Training Wing of the Infantry School at the Military College (DFTC, Curragh Camp).

Module Content:
Students will explore historical and theoretical approaches to the understanding of military battles and campaigns, and ideas relevant to military leadership. They will undertake group-work in support of campaign analysis presentations, supported by a field trip to a relevant campaign site. While researching these topics students will also identify sources which they utilise and then conduct a review of the source and present their findings in the form of a podcast.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate the ability to analyse battles, campaigns and military leaders.
Explain military outcomes and activity with reference to relevant concepts and history.
Analyse how historical tactical plans were executed.
Compare and contrast with how these might be achieved with modern tactics.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objectives: 

1. To develop the students' knowledge and comprehension of Politics and of Political Science while recognising the need for reflection, self-awareness and self-directed learning. 

2. To lay the foundations for future learning and development at higher levels. The module will focus on developing the students' understanding of political theory and institutions. It will focus, in particular, on relevant Irish and European institutions and will include an exploration of the theory and practice of international politics. Topics examined include: 1. An introduction to politics and political theory 2. The European Union 3. Britain and Ireland 4. US hegemony and the world order 5. The Islamic world 6. Africa 7. Balkan politics 8. Political leadership.

This is a foundation course in Irish Cultural Heritage Studies.  While introducing students to a general understanding of early cultural history, the emphasis is on the importance of academic knowledge of the past as a foundation for the presentation and dissemination of cultural heritage for the general public.  This is an assessment of the diverse aspects of Irish cultural history including place names, the literature of places and the creation and manipulation of history in relation to some of the great prehistoric and early medieval locations in Ireland. The care, presentation and future development of heritage sites such as the Hill of Tara, Newgrange, Kells, Glendalough and Clonmacnoise will be assessed in the context of modern scholarly interpretation and the increasing importance of cultural tourism.

Note 1: Students taking the Certificate in Irish Cultural Heritage in Semester 1, must take ID001 or ID014 as the core module.  
Note 2: This module will also be available in Semester 2, under the code, ID001X Introducing Irish Cultural Heritage.
learningOutcomes: Define the term ‘cultural heritage’.
Identify and access archaeological, historical and literary sources for the study of a number of important prehistoric and early medieval Irish sites.
Discuss the landscape, architecture and settlements encountered and the socio-economic and ecclesiastical systems that these represent.
Appraise critically on-site evidence and relevant secondary as well as at least some primary literature.
Consider the presentation and dissemination of cultural heritage in a cultural heritage institution, e.g. a museum or interpretative centre.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCING THE CULTURAL HERITAGE OF EARLY IRELAND
modules.moduleContent: This foundation course offers an interdisciplinary assessment of Ireland’s rich cultural heritage. This approach investigates the archaeology, history, architecture and the arts, the literature of places and the creation and manipulation of history in relation to some of the great prehistoric and early medieval locations in Ireland. This will include a consideration of the expansive remains of the Clonmacnoise early medieval monastic centre, and reference to the Neolithic complex of the Boyne Valley.
learningOutcomes: Define the term ‘cultural heritage’
Identify and access archaeological, historical and literary sources for the study of a number of important prehistoric and early medieval Irish sites
Discuss the landscape, architecture and settlements encountered and the socio-economic and ecclesiastical systems that these represent
Appraise critically on-site evidence and relevant secondary as well as at least some primary literature.
Consider the presentation and dissemination of cultural heritage in a cultural heritage institution, e.g. a museum or interpretative centre

modules.moduleContent:
The module reviews the concepts and history of globalisation with a focus on the socio-economic impact of contemporary globalisation forces on societies. 
A key feature of contemporary globalisation has been the growth of Transnational Companies and the fragmentation of production into global value chains or global production networks. 
In the module we review the impact of GVCs at a global and state level and attempts through voluntary guidelines and legislation to identify the roles and responsibilities of governments and TNCs to address issues of human rights, labour conditions and environmental pollution associated with these production networks.
learningOutcomes: understand the history and drivers of globalisation,
understand the socio-economic opportunities and challenges that it creates,
understand the impact it is having on identity and the concept of cosmopolitanism,
understand the structure and impact of global value chains at global, regional, and state levels,
understand the range of voluntary and legislative initiatives that have been designed to address the labour and human rights issues and abuses associated with GVC operations.

modules.moduleContent:
This module looks at local and transnational social movements and activism within the context of globalisation and international development. It explores such questions as: 
What does it mean to struggle to achieve global justice? 
What are the ideologies, assumptions and political goals of contemporary social movements today? 
How has neo-liberal globalisation affected global justice activism and social movements? 
How are movements responding? 
What are the identities of social movements?
Who are the key actors within social movements today and how are they different from each other? 
NGOs, grassroots organisations, networks, trade unions, churches, political parties. 
What are the diverse strategies and tools used by social movements and how effective are they? 
What are the possibilities for achieving and sustaining change toward greater global justice? 
The module will also examine a number of case studies of global social movements, including environmental rights movements, anti-austerity and debt justice movements, 
Occupy, tax justice movements, housing movements, gay rights and racial justice movements.
learningOutcomes: Have an overview of the history of global movements and development activism, in the context of neo-liberal globalisation.
Understand the key theories and debates related to global movements.
Distinguish between different activist strategies – including diverse forms of popular education and protest and critically appraise their effectiveness.
Explore the power relations of global movements and the life cycles of social movements.
Critically examine a number of case studies of global social movements.

modules.moduleContent:
Concern about global and local environmental change has long been the focus of International Development studies and is central to the rhetoric and objectives of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). 
Challenges of climate change, loss of biodiversity and natural resource degradation have become an imperative for Development practitioners and policy makers. 
There is widespread recognition that social and economic change can affect the environment. 
The flip side of this is that a degraded environment makes the poor more vulnerable and impedes wellbeing. 
Meanwhile at the local level there are numerous conflicts of interest over access to, and control of, natural resources. 
Many communities around the world are resisting what the see as inappropriate forms of development that negatively impact upon their natural environment and livelihoods.  
No analysis of environmental change can take place without making explicit the social, economic and political dimensions and drivers. 
This module aims to provide participants with an overview of global environmental changes, challenges and responses, and to facilitate participants understanding and analysis of the inter-relationship between environmental and development processes.
learningOutcomes: Comprehend terminology and key concepts of Environment and Sustainable Development.

Be expected to have a good insight into the nature, underlying causes, and consequences of key Global environmental concerns and the implications of these issues for international development and to be able to offer a critique of the role of various Multilateral, State and non State actors in working to address these issues.
Have a good understanding of the linkages between population growth, resource extraction and consumption and wealth generation, poverty, and environmental change.
Will have critically reflected upon different theoretical perspectives on ‘sustainable development’ and the relationships between the economy and the environment.
Will be informed on contemporary debates around the socio-economic and environmental impacts of how land is used and food is produced and distributed globally.

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to provide students with an understanding of the nature, role and relationship between states, civil society, transnational stakeholders and the private sector in development practice.
This module will give students: 
Insight into the function of different state institutions and understanding of their roles. 
An appreciation of the institutional capacities, and political and economic agendas of different types of states.  
The implications of state welfare and the consequences of integrated global social policies and welfare systems. 
An understanding of the influence of political ideology on humanitarian and development action. 
An awareness of the role and function of non-governmental organisations and other civil society organisations in development practice. 
Knowledge of the impact of globalisation on historical and contemporary development practices. 
The capacity to critically reflect on and analyse the concepts of the state, civil society organisations and transnational actors in development practice.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role played by the media in the development process.
Identify the main ICT4D technologies, their roles and relevance in different contexts.
Understand the key focii of lobbying and advocacy campaigning.
Have developed the skills to plan and coordinate a media lobbying and advocacy campaign and to develop a case study illustrating the knowledge gained.

modules.moduleName: A PLACE TO CALL HOME: PROPERTY, RIGHTS AND DEVELOPMENT
modules.moduleContent:
This module will examine the property rights of non-formal owners of land and housing, the majority of the population in many countries of the Global South.
In every continent land and housing has been commodified leaving unregistered property owners vulnerable to dispossession. 
Traditional owners are often pitted against the state in a struggle over resources and the meaning of democracy and development.  
This module will unpack the relationship of people to property: rural small holdings and urban informal settlements.  
The module will examine case studies from Latin America, Africa and Asia.
learningOutcomes: Understand the complexity of relationships to property, the philosophical and legal theories underpinning property.
