Gain an insight into international forces shaping the relationship between people, land and property.
Analyse the role of the state and democratic institutions in upholding the property rights of citizens.
Critically engage with the debates on causes and solutions to informal settlements in low income countries.

modules.moduleContent: The module allows the students to develop a guided practical research project on a development topic of their choice under the supervision of a department lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Identify a research project topic.
Complete a literature review around the research topic and its related research questions.
Identify and select respondents and appropriate research tools.
Complete a primary data collection process.
Analyse and write up the data and findings as a research report.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO PEACE PROCESSES AND CONFLICT INTERVENTION
modules.moduleContent:
This module attempts to set out the lessons of various peace processes and compares how the sequential and incremental processes of conflict engagement, conflict resolution and conflict transformation have worked themselves out. 
In particular, it explores the Northern Ireland ‘Troubles’ as a case study of the peacemaking role that third parties play both at official and second track levels and identifies a number of key skills that are needed in a facilitation/mediation process. 
The module also provides participants with the critical skills to effectively analyse a conflict and to identify and assess realistic responses. 
It provides personnel working in crisis situations with the necessary conceptual frameworks and analytical skills to respond and develop integrated approaches to conflict prevention and crisis management.
learningOutcomes: Discuss and critique the theoretical field of international conflict interventions.
Critically analyse the structure of violent conflict and intervention strategies.
Critically analyse and discuss various approaches to the study of peace-making and conflict transformation with application to civil and international conflicts.
Discuss the nature of leadership and unofficial diplomacy in peace negotiations.
Formulate a relevant peace process project on a relevant topic.
Examine the dynamics of peace operations and missions.
Explain the difference between traditional peace keeping, peace enforcement, enforcement action under the UN Charter and peace support operations.

modules.moduleContent:
This module explores the broad human security approach, and assists students to identify and examine widespread and cross cutting challenges to the survival, livelihood and dignity of people. 
Human security differs from conventional security studies, which focus on the security of the state. 
This module on human security approaches considers the individual within their society, and protection of people from both traditional (military) and nontraditional threats to their safety such as food insecurity, the impact of climate change, hybrid warfare and population disease.
learningOutcomes: Critique the concept of ‘Human Security’.
Evaluate the Human Security debate in relation to specific problems and policy issues ranging from the environmental change through food insecurity to migration and International crime.
Understand the extent and limits of the impact of Human Security perspectives on the policies of the UN, EU and key states.
Develop critical abilities to analyse primary and secondary resources focused on Human Security.

modules.moduleContent:
Cur síos ginearálta ar an modúl: 
Déanfaidh rannpháirtithe tionscadal a bheidh bunaithe ar anailís a dhéanfaidh siad ar a gcuid teagaisc agus ar an aiseolas a fhaigheann siad faoina gcleachtadh teagaisc ón meantóir a bheidh ag obair leo. 

Ábhar an mhodúil: Tionscadal leanúnach é seo a bheidh á dhéanamh sa dá sheimeastar le linn an chleachtaidh teagaisc. Beidh an chéad chuid le comhlíonadh i ndiaidh an chéad chuairt ar an seomra ranga agus beidh an dara cuid le comhlíonadh i dtreo dheireadh an chúrsa i ndiaidh an dara cuairt ar an seomra ranga. 

Déanfaidh na rannpháirtithe plean ceachta don spriocghrúpa, déanfaidh siad anailís ar an gceacht ag baint úsáide as teimpléad a chuirfear ar fáil dóibh agus comhlíonfaidh siad sraith tascanna machnamhacha bunaithe ar an anailís a dhéanfaidh siad ar a gcuid teagaisc. 

Gheobhaidh siad aiseolas ón meantóir a bheidh ag obair leo agus bunaithe ar an aiseolas sin agus ar thaifeadadh físe den seomra ranga, ullmhóidh siad plean forbartha dá gcuid teagaisc. Beidh machnamh scríofa le déanamh acu bunaithe ar a n-anailís féin, ar aiseolas ón meantóir, agus ar an bpíosa físe. Spreagfar na rannpháirtithe le machnamh ar leith a dhéanamh ar na difríochtaí idir an anailís thosaigh a dhéanann siad ar an rang agus an anailís a dhéanann siad i ndiaidh dóibh an taifeadadh a fheiceáil agus aiseolas a fháil.
learningOutcomes: Déanfaidh rannpháirtithe tionscadal a bheidh bunaithe ar anailís a dhéanfaidh siad ar a gcuid teagaisc agus ar an aiseolas a fhaigheann said faoina gcleachtadh teagaisc ón meantóir a bheidh ag obair leo.
modules.notes: Clibeadh an modúl seo mar Shonraí/Digiteach (Data/Digital) ag úsáid Intleachta Saorga (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Cur síos ginearálta ar an modúl: 
Beidh sraith tascanna le comhlíonadh ag rannpháirtithe agus beidh na tascanna sin ina mbunús le fillteán foghlama. An aidhm a bheidh leis na tascanna ná scileanna na rannpháirtithe a fhorbairt i ndearadh gníomhaíochtaí ranga agus pleanáil ranganna. 

Ábhar an mhodúil: Beidh fillteán foghlama le comhlíonadh ag rannpháirtithe sa dara seimeastar. Beidh gnéithe éagsúla i gceist leis:
 • Pleananna ranga agus tascanna bunaithe ar na pleananna.
 • Léirmheas / anailís ar ábhar teagaisc atá ar fáil cheana féin agus atá úsáidte ag rannpháirtithe.
 • Cruthú ábhar teagaisc nua a bheadh oiriúnach do riachtanais an spriocghrúpa teagaisc.
learningOutcomes: Beidh sraith tascanna le comhlíonadh ag rannpháirtithe agus beidh na tascanna sin ina mbunús le fillteán teagaisc. An aidhm a bheidh leis na tascanna ná scileanna na rannpháirtithe a fhorbairt i ndearadh gníomhaíochtaí ranga agus i bpleanáil ranganna.

modules.moduleContent: Díreofar sa mhodúl seo ar ghnéithe den teoiric a bhfuil feidhm phraiticiúil leo, mar shampla leas a bhaint as an tséimeantaic chun fadhbanna aistriúcháin a réiteach agus critic a dhéanamh ar théacs aistrithe. Múinfear an modúl seo i Seimeastar 2.
learningOutcomes: Léargas a thabhairt ar ghnéithe den teoiric a bhfuil feidhm phraiticiúil leo, leas a bhaint as an teoiric sin ina gcuid oibre, agus aistriúchán a mheas go criticiúil.

modules.moduleContent: Is ag tógáil ar ábhar LC505 a bheifear sa mhodúl seo, agus leanfar den chíoradh ar na dúshláin a bhaineann le cineálacha éagsúla téacs a aistriú, mar shampla téacsanna liteartha, téacsanna dlí agus téacsanna oifigiúla.
learningOutcomes: Déileáil le seánraí téacs faoi leith mar shampla téacsanna liteartha, téacsanna dlí agus téacsanna oifigiúla.

modules.moduleName: GAIRM AGUS GNÓ AN AISTRIÚCHÁIN SAN EORAIP AGUS IN ÉIRINN
modules.moduleContent: Dírítear sa mhodúl seo ar ghnéithe éagsúla de ghairm agus de ghnó an aistriúcháin in Éirinn agus san Eoraip. Dírítear ar chúrsaí eitice, ar an aistriúchán dlíthiúil, ar chúrsaí aistriúcháin in institiúidí an Aontais Eorpaigh, ar théarmaíocht an dlí agus na reachtaíochta, agus déantar cleachtadh ar cheisteanna ó scrúduithe earcaíochta EPSO agus ón Séala Creidiúnaithe d’Aistritheoirí.
learningOutcomes: Tuiscint a léiriú ar chleachtais ghnó agus ar chleachtais ghairmiúla in earnáil an aistriúcháin agus na heagarthóireachta, feidhmiú go gairmiúil san earnáil sin, agus déileáil leis na cineálacha téacsanna a bhíonn le n-aistriú i scrúduithe an tSéala agus i scrúduithe aistriúcháin EPSO.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will aid the professional development of students by developing the practical skills required to teach effectively in a third level educational institution. By the end of this module students will demonstrate an ability to plan, develop and deliver course material suitable for dissemination and delivery to small group sizes. 
This module will require students to deliver pre-prepared course material to small groups and provide one to one assistance in demonstrating key concepts and techniques in geography. Students will work closely with a designated senior demonstrator/lecturer during this experience.

This module will normally be taken in Semester 1
learningOutcomes: Evaluate methods and resources for effective small group teaching and learning in higher education.
Apply a variety of methods and resources to enhance own practice as teachers in higher education.
Explore ways of evaluating student learning, both formal and informal.
Reflect on feedback on teaching practice which is received from a wide variety of sources: mentor; students; peers; session material; literature reviews; and self experience.
In response to this reflection on feedback, discuss own practice and how it might improve to improve student’s learning.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will require a student to devise, develop and implement an independent research project, in consultation with their supervisor and with agreed learning outcomes. The student will choose their own research direction, discover their own learning resources, formulate their own research problems, decide their own course of action and reflect on the outcome of that process. On successful completion of this module, the student will have developed the capacity for undertaking autonomous research.
This module will enable the student to work independently on specialized areas or topics of relevance to their research and not provided for in existing modules.
This module will normally be taken in Semester 2

Advanced specialist modules can be taken from those on offer from the Department of Geography, NIRSA, other Departments at MU, or a module on offer in another partner institution. Students select module from a range of options. 
Module Objectives: To enhance students’ ability to explore and debate complex issues at an advanced level.

This module will normally be taken in Semester 1
learningOutcomes: Identify and understand key geographical processes
Evaluate critically a variety of methods and approaches in geography
Discuss and analyze research findings in a research report format
Discuss research methodologies and findings in oral and written forms

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective
This module will consist of seminars, presentations, workshops and/or master class sessions tailored for PhD students. The Summer/Winter School may be comprised of thematically specialized and/or inter-disciplinary sessions.

Module Content
To be defined by summer/winter school organisers.

Learning Outcomes
On successful completion of this module, students should be able to:
• Describe the field of inquiry represented in the summer/winter school
• Identify major theories and paradigms employed and define key concepts and ideas
• Apply theories and concepts and relate them to particular problems, issues and phenomena addressed in the student's own research
• Analyse aspects of the student's own field of inquiry and illustrate them in terms of theories and concepts presented in the summer/winter school
• Explain phenomena and formulate particular research problems in terms of general theories presented in summer/winter school
• Criticise topics and evaluate issues and debates in terms of paradigms and theories presented in the summer/winter school.
learningOutcomes: Describe the field of inquiry represented in the summer/winter school.
Identify major theories and paradigms employed and define key concepts and ideas.
Apply theories and concepts and relate them to particular problems, issues and phenomena addressed in the student's own research.
Analyse aspects of the student's own field of inquiry and illustrate them in terms of theories and concepts presented in the summer/winter school.

modules.moduleName: AMERICAN HISTORY FROM REVOLUTION TO CIVIL WAR
modules.moduleContent: This module gives students an introduction to American history in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, focusing mainly on the turbulent period between the American Revolution and the U.S. Civil War.
learningOutcomes: have a sound knowledge of American history from the late-colonial period to the U.S. Civil War
have an appreciation of a range of primary source documents for the study of American history
have an understanding of how to identify and use academic secondary literature in the study of history, and a recognition of the range of approaches currently employed by historians

modules.moduleName: IRELAND IN THE MODERN WORLD: THE NINETEENTH CENTURY
modules.moduleContent: A broad introduction to the major social, political, economic and cultural developments that impacted Irish life and society from the Act of Union in 1801 to the passing of the Wyndham Land Act in 1903. The module examines key issues such as the impact of the Act of Union; the rise of the Catholic Emancipation and Repeal movements under Daniel O’Connell; pre-Famine agrarian tensions; the cause and consequences of the Great Famine; the birth of the two political ideologies – nationalism and unionism – that defined so much of Irish life and politics in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries; the role of the land question that dominated rural society after the Great Famine; the rise of separatist nationalists; emigration; the role of the Big House; and British government responses to the call for Irish social and political reforms.
learningOutcomes: have a broad understanding of nineteenth-century Irish history, and an understanding of the major themes and issues that have shaped the two Irelands today.
engage with historiographical debates and to develop an understanding of historical research and use of primary sources.
demonstrate in their written work an understanding of the main trends, personalities and factors shaping the history of modern Ireland in the nineteenth century.
analyse and critically assess secondary sources and present cogent, coherent arguments.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides an introduction to the history of health and medicine.
learningOutcomes: have an awareness of the importance of health and welfare in the lives of ordinary people living in nineteenth century Ireland.
be familiar with the emergence of key medical institutions and medical personnel in nineteenth century Ireland.
comprehend the complex nature of suffering and illness in the lives of ordinary people and their families in the nineteenth century.

modules.moduleContent: An overview of American history from Reconstruction to the present.
learningOutcomes: identify the key developments that occurred in the United States from the mid-nineteenth century to the late twentieth century.
have a knowledge of the chronology of American history from Reconstruction to the late twentieth century and of how historical events and processes interacted.
use a varied range of primary source material.
through completing module assessment, have further developed writing and analytical skills.

modules.moduleName: THE HISTORY OF SUICIDE: A GLOBAL OUTLOOK
modules.moduleContent: An overview of suicide in global context examined from a cultural perspective, this course sweeps time from Greco-Roman antiquity to the present wave of suicide bombings in the Middle East.
learningOutcomes: hone public presentation skills
learn to work together in groups
engage in the critical analysis of secondary sources
participate in website construction

modules.moduleContent: This module looks at the history of Europe since the end of the Second World War, looking at the competing trends of integration and disintegration. It asks if there is such a thing as a common European history and identity, and if so, where are its limits?
learningOutcomes: have a solid understanding of the key themes of European history since 1945
reflect critically on the most important themes and developments of the period covered
identify key points in primary and secondary sources'
Understand and write with confidence about the key themes and developments of the period covered'

modules.moduleName: THE TROUBLES: CONFLICT IN NORTHERN IRELAND
modules.moduleContent: This module provides a social, economic and political exploration of Northern Ireland during the Troubles and offers a detailed examination of the outbreak and escalation of conflict.
learningOutcomes: • have a critical understanding of the economic, social and political history of Northern Ireland during this period.
• assess the causes of conflict and the process of moving towards peace.
• engage with the defining social and cultural elements of unionist and nationalist identities during the Troubles.
• analyse and interpret primary source material and historiography relating to the Troubles.
• reflect upon the difficulties of ‘writing history’ in a contested society.

modules.moduleName: GEORGIANS REVEALED: THE DOMESTIC WORLD OF THE LONG EIGHTEENTH CENTURY
modules.moduleContent:
The Georgian era (1714-1830) was a period of innovation.  This module explores the ways in which travel, trade with the East, and new technologies contributed to a consumer boom that transformed the Georgian home.  Using a wide range of primary sources from diaries and letters to account books and inventories, this module will consider the impact of this consumer revolution, exploring what was new, who led taste and the competitive culture of keeping up appearances.

Please note students who have taken the module HY278 cannot also take HY3022.
learningOutcomes: identify and interpret key themes that facilitate comprehension of Georgian homes and interiors in Britain and Ireland.
assess the factors that contributed to the success of leading entrepreneurs.
evaluate digital resources, including Eighteenth Century Collections Online (ECCO) and museum databases as rich repositories of primary sources.
interrogate different types of historical evidence, to construct effective arguments.

modules.moduleName: EASTERN EUROPE IN THE MODERN WORLD
modules.moduleContent: This module explores the history of central Europe from the early nineteenth century to the present day, focusing on the process of nation-building.
learningOutcomes: be able to place nation- and state-building processes in a broader international context
demonstrate an awareness and understanding of contemporary historiographical debates in relation to the emergence of East European states
have an understanding of the primary sources available for the study of this topic
while engaging with secondary and primary sources, present clear, coherent and cogent argument and analysis on the topic

modules.moduleName: IRELAND AND THE BALKANS IN THE  MODERN WORLD
modules.moduleContent: This module explores cultural and political encounters and connections between Ireland and the Irish and the lands that made up Yugoslavia.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate understanding and knowledge of main developments in the history of Yugoslavia and Yugoslav-Irish cultural and diplomatic relations
demonstrate and apply understanding of representations and images of Yugoslavia in Irish sources
demonstrate the capacity for independent thought in the handling of historical sources
critically engage with the relevant main historiographical debates, and develop their own ideas and positions in relation to them

modules.moduleContent: This programme is designed to offer an introduction to the field of Defence Studies, with particular emphasis on how current security and defence issues impact on Ireland. It will outline the development of trends in modern warfare, covering topics such as Maritime Security, Cyber Warfare, the role of Intelligence in national security, Terrorism and CounterTerrorism, among others. Participants will be introduced to current debates in the defence sector and there will also be a skills element to facilitate their professional work development. This course is intended to give the participant an introduction to Defence Studies. Course content will cover: Introduction to Defence Studies; Introduction to Strategic Studies Contemporary Security Issues; Neutrality and Ireland's "Triple Lock" system; The Political-Military interface and military culture; Trends in Modern Warfare; Land, Air and Naval Warfare; Peacekeeping, Ireland and the UN; Gender Issues in modern defence forces; The role of Intelligence; Cyber Warfare; Terrorism and Counter-Terrorism; Academic Skills (Research & Writing)

Through lectures and seminars, students will be introduced to an area of specialisation in which the lecturer has special expertise.  The module will be taught as a seminar, with an emphasis on student participation.
learningOutcomes: identify some key developments in cultural history in the United States
have a knowledge of how historical events and processes in the United States have interacted with cultural processes and forms including, for example, writing, music and visual culture
use a varied range of primary source material and secondary literature
through completing module assessment, have further developed writing and analytical skills.

modules.moduleContent: Ireland has a very significant urban tradition stretching back well over a millennium. The earliest stages of town development can be traced at a few of the larger early medieval monastic sites such as Armagh and Kildare. A new wave of urban growth was instigated by Scandinavian settlers - five of Ireland’s largest cities were founded primarily as trading ports at strategic locations by the Vikings in the ninth and tenth centuries. The arrival of the Anglo-Normans in the second half of the twelfth century initiated another crucial phase of urban generation, with more than fifty towns being founded. A further stage of town creation was the plantation era of the late sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries. Through archaeological, documentary and other sources, this module will trace the development of urban Ireland from the early medieval period through to the seventeenth century.
learningOutcomes: be familiar with the origins and main phases of town development in Ireland;
be aware of the range of sources available for the study of Ireland’s historic towns;
recognise various surviving medieval components in the modern streetscape;
have a good idea of what daily life was like in a medieval Irish town.

modules.moduleName: EVOLUTION OF THE IRISH URBAN LANDSCAPE
modules.moduleContent: This course will not concentrate on the outcome of landscape evolution but rather on the process and in particular the evidence available for understanding that process. As such it is as much a way of dealing with sources and the problems in interpretation as with outcomes.
learningOutcomes: appreciate the importance of urban development as a factor in the evolution of local societies
identify the methods that can be used to explore the evolution of the Irish urban landscape
trace sources in various repositories related to the study of the Irish urban landscape
summarise and reflect on their findings in an analytical context using methodologies developed in the Irish Historic Towns Atlas project

modules.moduleContent: This module explores various aspects of the history of medieval Ireland, including social, political, economic, cultural, religious and ecclesiastical, archaeological and architectural developments.
learningOutcomes:
• be familiar with many aspects of the history and archaeology of medieval Ireland; 
be aware of the broad range of source materials available for the study of medieval Ireland;
recognise features of medieval date in buildings and the modern landscape;
appreciate ways in which knowledge of medieval Ireland can enrich our understanding of modern Irish history and society.

modules.moduleContent: This module deals with approaches to writing and interpreting history, from a number of distinct but often complementary perspectives. The overall aim is to give students undertaking a taught postgraduate programme in history a broad understanding of the different ways in which historical evidence is created and transmitted, the skills employed in the interpretation of that evidence, and how it is presented to the public.

In this module students are exposed to readings topically related to a theme (e.g. colonialism, genocide, empire, etc.). They are asked to conduct weekly readings which form the basis of weekly presentations and in-class discussion. These readings, presentations and discussions in turn form the basis for written reviews.
learningOutcomes: - appreciate multiple perspectives on selected topics in European history.
- distinguish different historical research strategies and discuss their respective analytical values in application to specific problems in European history .
- process a large body of secondary source material, and appreciate, based on able selection, complementary approaches and methods.
- monitor the coherence and consistence of historical argumentation critically.
- formulate a critical position towards body of existing research, and document it for the peer group.
- consider the transferability of approaches and methods to other fields of historical research.

modules.moduleContent: The module will provide an introduction to, and a foundation in, the key concepts, theories, issues and debates pertinent to the discipline of strategic studies. Students will engage with a series of topics that are critical to an understanding of the contemporary security environment, in addition to addressing issues relating to contemporary Irish and European security policy. The module will bring together the theory and practice of security in a format designed to be accessible to those without any detailed prior knowledge of the subject.
learningOutcomes: identify the main issues of relevance to the study of contemporary strategy and security
analyse complex issues relating to security and strategy, with reference to relevant literature
contribute meaningfully to discussion and debate on relevant topics
demonstrate understanding of key discourse and debate in contemporary strategic studies in written format and through in-class participation

modules.moduleContent: To provide an opportunity for students in the first year of research to develop a research plan for their thesis. In particular this involves the development of techniques on research and presentation specific to historians including citation conventions and bibliographical conventions as well as the development of presentation skills and discussion of their own work in progress for peer appraisal.
learningOutcomes: recognize the main aims and objectives of their thesis
distinguish alternative approaches and be prepared to defend in a seminar their distinctive approach
develop the presentation skills for written work
monitor the coherence and consistency of their emerging arguments and subject this to peer review
reflect in a seminar paper on their on-going research

modules.moduleName: PH.D. SKILLS AND METHODS IN HISTORY
modules.moduleContent: This module deals with approaches to writing and interpreting history, from a number of distinct but often complementary perspectives. The overall aim is to give students undertaking a taught postgraduate programme in history a broad understanding of the different ways in which historical evidence is created and transmitted, the skills employed in the interpretation of that evidence, and how it is presented to the public.

Module Objective: To provide an opportunity for the student in the third year of research to acquire skills in a language relevant to their research.

Module Content: The student will attend and participate fully in an appropriate Language for Academic Purposes module or equivalent course, in MU or elsewhere, as agreed with the doctoral supervisor.

Module Objective: 

1. To develop the student’s knowledge and comprehension of Irish history while recognising the need for reflection, self-awareness and self-directed learning.
2. To lay the foundations for future learning and development at higher levels.

The module will explore the history of Ireland from the 17th century through to the present. In particular it will focus on the history of Ireland in the 20th century and will include a particular emphasis on the history of conflict in Ireland and on the history of the Defence Forces.

modules.moduleName: MILITARY DECISION MAKING AT BATTALION LEVEL
modules.moduleContent:
Responsible Department:
History, through the Centre for Military History and Strategic Studies, in partnership with the teaching staff at the Officer Training Wing of the Infantry School at the Military College (DFTC, Curragh Camp).

Module Content: 
This module introduces the different methodologies associated with military decision making and builds student knowledge and understanding of how these can be applied in a variety of contexts. Students will examine and apply relevant concepts, including Military Decision Making Process as currently applied by the Irish Defence Forces, and will be required to demonstrate advanced level analysis and critical thinking in the application of this process. Students will receive lectures on decision making theory and practice and will test their knowledge and understanding in a series of case study exercises, tutorials and classroom discussions.
learningOutcomes: Examine and critically analyse different theoretical approaches to military decision making.
Apply such processes to practical examples relating to contemporary operations involving the land component.
Understand the application of decision making processes in the planning of military operations at battalion level.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 

1. To develop the student’s knowledge and comprehension of Defence Studies (including military history and strategic studies) while recognising the need for reflection, self-awareness and self-directed learning.
2. To lay the foundations for future learning and development at higher levels.

The module will focus on developing the students’ understanding of military theory, including relevant theory from strategic studies and international relations, and explore relevant historical case studies with reference to such theory. Topics examined include:

1. Strategy and the principles of war
2. The international system
3. Sources and instruments of power
4. The development of modern warfare
5. Air warfare
6. Maritime strategy and naval warfare
7. European security architecture
8. Irish foreign and defence policy
9. Military sociology

modules.moduleName: PSO AND CMO: PEACE SUPPORT OPERATIONS AND CRISIS MANAGEMENT
modules.moduleContent:
Responsible Department:
History, through the Centre for Military History and Strategic Studies, in partnership with the teaching staff at the Officer Training Wing of the Infantry School at the Military College (DFTC, Curragh Camp).

Module Content: The module will examine in detail the theory and practice of recent and contemporary crisis management operations today with a particular (but not exclusive) focus on battalion and brigade level matters. It will also address concepts, theory, doctrine and military practice relating to insurgency, counter-insurgency and counter-terrorist operations. It will examine the use of military force in such operations, in theory and in practice, and will also address the role of non-governmental organisations and the challenges posed by civil-military cooperation. The module will include a focus on the legal and ethical challenges inherent in such operations.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an advanced understanding of the impact of military force on peace support operations.
Appraise the role of international/regional organizations on peace support and crisis management operations.
Develop and articulate plans for the employment of battalion and brigade assets in such operations.
Show understanding of the employment of CIMIC assets.

modules.moduleName: TACTICAL LEADERSHIP IN OPERATIONS OTHER THAN WAR (OOTW)
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
To develop Cadet’s knowledge, comprehension and practical application of Platoon leadership in operations other than war. The training environment and instructional focus will support analysis, synthesis and evaluation of operations in OOTW.

This module will focus on subjects that support learning and development in Leadership in Non-Conventional Operations and will include:

a. Insurgency and Counter-Insurgency Theory.
b. History, Evolution and Trends in Terrorism.
c. Security Threat Analysis.
d. Aid to Civil Power Operations.
e. Peace Support Operations.
Contact department for details.
Contact department for further details.

modules.moduleName: CULTURAL HERITAGE AND EARLY IRISH HEROIC TRADITION
modules.moduleContent:
The Heroic Cycle or Ulster Cycle is the most substantial body of literature belonging to the pre-Norman period.  Students will be introduced to the defining features, themes, motifs and contents of this group of tales through a close study of a number of key texts in English translation.  Arguably, the most important text of Medieval Ireland is the Cattle-Raid of Cooley, which features a raid on Ulster by an army led by Queen Medb for the Brown Bull of Cooley.  The defence of Ulster falls upon the supreme warrior-hero, Cú Chulainn.  Conceptions, births, feasts, battles, cattle-raids, otherworldly outings, courtships and deaths are among the other topics featuring in these narratives.  The physical context and landscape setting for these tales, where possible, will be considered.  This depiction of early Irish society will also be appraised in the context of archaeological evidence and in a broader European context through a consideration of sources such as the writing of classical authors and other material. 

Note: This module will also be available in Semester 2, under the code, ID005X Cultural Heritage and the Early Irish Heroic Tradition.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate familiarity with early Irish narrative literature.
Identify the core themes, motifs, characters etc of the Ulster/ Heroic Cycle.
Consider the landscape context of this material.
Combine documentary and archaeological evidence to provide a balanced appreciation of Emain Macha.
Demonstrate the ability to communicate ideas in presentation and written form.

modules.moduleContent: This Micro Credential is designed to facilitate participants to understand the fundamentals of digital transformation, and how integrated technology can help organisations to work more effectively. It will also introduce participants to the cultural, structural, technology dynamics, and business value outcomes of transformation within organisations. It provides participants with a critical understanding of the major benefits and challenges associated with the management and execution of digital transformation programmes. The course will provide participants with the knowledge and practical skills to develop and demonstrate, through a practicum, professionally recognised change management knowledge and skills.
learningOutcomes: Describe the business value of digital transformation in the context of organisation change.
Use standard business analysis tools and techniques to measure current digital capability maturity within organisations.
Critically analyse and interpret qualitative and quantitative research data to diagnose organisational digital capability gaps.
Apply Design Thinking methods to innovate a digital processes, solutions, and services.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module explores different aspects of global development, the challenges we face at a global level and how we can create a better, more just and sustainable world. 
Participants are introduced to, and encouraged to critique, different theories and practices which affect economic, social, political, cultural and environmental development at local and global levels, including the sustainable development goals (SDGs). 
We examine different causes of poverty, inequality, conflict and injustice around the world and the role of governments, businesses, development organisations and global activist movements in relation to them. 
Throughout, we place emphasis on how we can realise more inclusive and sustainable futures, e.g., through socially responsible business practices, climate justice, alternative development practices, decolonialism, gender equality and human rights approaches to development.
learningOutcomes: Understand and critique some theories and practices which affect economic, social, political, cultural and environmental development at local and global levels, including the sustainable development goals (SDGs).
Discuss and explain different causes of poverty, inequality, conflict and injustice around the world from a social, economic, political and historical perspective.

Identify some of the main roles of governments, businesses, development organisations and global activist movements in relation to key aspects of economic, social, political, cultural and environmental development at local and global levels.

Demonstrate an appreciation for alternative development approaches which help to realise more inclusive and sustainable futures, e.g., socially responsible business practices, climate justice, alternative development practices, decolonialism, gender equality and human rights approaches to development.

modules.moduleContent:
This course aims to introduce students to the principles and rationale of management for development results, with a particular focus on participatory and results based approaches. 
It will provide students with an understanding of the issues and challenges of project/programme planning and management in the context of aid, and development partnership and practice.
The course will also develop students' familiarity with results based management and the different stages of the project/programme cycle and associated elements.
learningOutcomes: Critique the principles and rationale of Management for Development Results.
Understand the key concepts, terms and vocabulary related to project planning and management.
Use skills and understanding to develop a simple results chain.
Describe and differentiate between the phases of a project/programme cycle.
Appreciate Results Based Management (RBM) in controlling and improving projects/programmes.
Understand the results based approach and the use of a results framework in designing development interventions.
Critique participatory processes in the context of planning for development.

modules.moduleContent: Students will explore why gender and identity are important in development practice and the challenges of pursuing gender transformation within the prevailing development paradigm. This module will introduce students to gender and identity concepts and terminologies and explain how they are shaped by socialisation and cultural context. It will also introduce students to a range of tools and methods that have been developed to assist with gender-sensitive project planning. Finally, the module is taught in a participatory manner and will allow students to reflect on and engage in their own understanding and experience on gender and identity.
learningOutcomes: Differentiate between key Gender and Identity concepts in relation to Development Practice.
Understand the importance of mainstreaming Gender and Identity in Development Practice.
Critically engage with a range of tools and methods to assist with Gender sensitive project planning.
To participate in contemporary debates around Gender and Identity policies in Development.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to social research methods, emphasising developing an understanding of qualitative research approaches and tools. 
The module reviews the main stages of social research planning; research topic identification, developing research questions, literature review construction, sampling and research tools, and research ethics.
learningOutcomes: Construct a research proposal.
Identify a research topic.
Define the research aim and objectives.
Define the research aim and objectives.
Construct research questions.
Design a research sampling strategy and associated research tools.
Construct a literature review.

modules.moduleName: AFRICAN LIONS: AFRICA, DEMOCRACY AND DEVELOPMENT
modules.moduleContent:
This module will discuss the diversity of political and development paths taken by post-colonial African states.   
Key to this module is challenging the accepted representations of Africa which miss out the diversity of the continent and it’s growing economic and political influence.  
Case studies from around the continent will demonstrate the variety of political and economic landscapes and equally the varied responses of the African state.
learningOutcomes: Gain an insight into the importance of colonial and post-colonial legacies on contemporary political and economic life in Africa.
Understand democratic processes and good governance and their application to a diversity of African countries.
Critically engage with the literature on development and globalisation in the African context with an emphasis on China in Africa.
Analyse the challenges facing the African Union (AU) and the response of the AU to conflict, development needs and globalisation affecting the continent.

modules.moduleName: GLOBAL DEVELOPMENT CHALLENGES – THE STATE OF THE WORLD
modules.moduleContent:
This capstone module aims to review contemporary global development mega-trends and related policy responses. 
The module will offer students a chance to hear from practitioners, including International Development graduates, on various new thinking and initiatives, in the past - social protection, business ethics and environmental responses. 
During the module, students will look at the SDGs' progress and use Ireland as a case study to examine how SDGs are implemented at a country level. 
The module will focus on policy – what the term means, where policies are made and who are policy actors, and how policy is linked to systems change. 
On the latter, the module will review progress on climate action and the greening of the economic system.
learningOutcomes: Understand current thinking on a range of mega-trends and policy responses.
Developed an insight into how policies are formed and their role in systems change.
Have reviewed the SDG progress and developed an understanding of the challenges of policy implementation at the national level in Ireland.
Explored the issue of systems change and how it related to climate and environmental decoupling.
Reviewed a sample of new initiatives and thinking to address global development challenges from practitioners.
Undertake and present a piece of primary research in a specialist area related to development studies;
Have a good understanding of different theoretical approaches to social research;
Be familiar with different information collection tools which can be applied in primary development research;
Have an understanding of ethical issues in relation to carrying out research in a development context;
Be familiar with different sampling strategies and will be able to analyse primary research data;
Have been prepared to carry out specialist research and have a specialised and deep understanding of one area of development studies.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: POLITICAL ECONOMY OF ENVIRONMENT AND DEVELOPMENT
learningOutcomes: Have a critical understanding of the nature, underlying causes, and consequences of key global environmental concerns, for example climate change, biodiversity loss and water scarcity and the implications of these issues for the livelihoods of the poor, and to be able to offer a critique of the role of various multilateral, state and non state actors in working to address these issues;
Have an in-depth understanding of the linkages between population growth, resource extraction and consumption and wealth generation, poverty, and environmental change;
Have critically reflected upon different theoretical perspectives on ‘sustainable development’ and the relationships between the economy and the environment;
Have critically reflected upon different theoretical perspectives on ‘sustainable development’ and the relationships between the economy and the environment;
Be Informed on contemporary debates around the socio-economic and environmental impacts of how land is used and food is produced and distributed globally.
Have a critical awareness of different theories and approaches to development project/programme planning and management.
Have a broad understanding of planning and management, management processes.
Have developed competence in participatory and results based processes.

Have their skills and ability in formulating and objectively appraising a comprehensive results based framework and related project/programme funding proposals which meet current donor funding criteria.
Have the confidence and skills to critically reflect on issues and challenges around project/programme planning and management in the context of North South aid and partnerships praxis.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: CRITICAL LEARNING AND PARTICIPATORY DEVELOPMENT PRACTICE
modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to support learners to engage critically with theories and practices of transformative education and to develop their understanding and skills in the area of participatory development practice. 
Different models, theories and examples of transformative education will be explored, especially in the areas of critical pedagogy, adult education and global citizenship education. 
Through this exploration, learners will be supported to critically reflect on the factors affecting development in different contexts, on different approaches to knowledge and power, 
and on their own experience of learning and participatory practice. 
As such, the module will support learners to develop the critical, analytical and facilitation skills to engage with communities in critical learning and participatory change processes. 
These include skills in critical reflection, social analysis, group facilitation and change leadership.
learningOutcomes: Understand key theories and principles of transformative education, especially with regard to critical pedagogy, adult education and global citizenship education.
Distinguish between different concepts and approaches to transformative education and their influence on different forms of transformative and participatory development practice.

Have developed, and be able to demonstrate, some key skills in transformative education and participatory development practice, e.g., critical reflection, social analysis, group facilitation and change leadership.

modules.moduleContent:
Beidh deis ag rannpháirtithe foghlaim faoi ghnéithe éagsúla de mhúineadh agus d’fhoghlaim na Gaeilge agus an méid a fhoghlaimíonn siad a chur i bhfeidhm ar a gcleachtas féin sa seomra ranga nó sa seomra ranga ar líne. 
Rachaidh rannpháirtithe i ngleic le hábhar an mhodúil seo trí Moodle agus glacfaidh siad páirt i gceardlanna agus i gcruinnithe ar líne chomh maith. Pléifear le topaicí éagsúla:

• An múinteoir teanga 
• An foghlaimeoir teanga
• An Fráma Tagartha Comónta Eorpach
• An siollabas teanga agus pleanáil siollabas
• An rang teanga agus pleanáil ranga
• Gníomhaíochtaí don seomra ranga
• Léamh agus scríobh sa seomra ranga
• An chaint agus foghraíocht
• An chluastuiscint agus téacsanna físe
• An ghramadach agus feasacht teanga

 Cuirfear an t-ábhar i láthair i slite éagsúla - ag brath ar an topaic atá i gceist d’fheadfadh rannpháirtithe a bheith ag comhlíonadh réamh-thascanna machnaimh (scríofa nó taifeadta), ag féachaint ar fhíseanna, ag léamh, nó ag comhlíonadh tascanna scríofa. An aidhm a bheidh leis na tascanna ná cabhrú le rannpháirtithe ábhar na dtopaicí a chur in oiriúint dá gcleachtas féin sa seomra ranga. Beidh deis chomh maith ag rannpháirtithe freastal ar cheardlanna / cruinnithe ar líne, rud a thabharfaidh deis dóibh an t-ábhar a phlé, tacaíocht a fháil agus a dtaithí a roinnt le rannpháirtithe eile. Cuirfidh stiúrthóir an chúrsa uaireanta oifige ar fáil ar líne chomh maith chun tacaíocht bhreise a chur ar fáil.
learningOutcomes: Beidh deis ag rannpháirtithe foghlaim faoi ghnéithe éagsúla de mhúineadh agus d’fhoghlaim na Gaeilge, tascanna a dhéanamh bunaithe ar na gnéithe sin agus an méid a fhoghlaimíonn siad a chur i bhfeidhm ar a gcleachtas féin sa seomra ranga.
modules.notes: Clibeadh an modúl seo mar Shonraí/Digiteach (Data/Digital) ag úsáid Intleachta Saorga (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Beidh 30 uair an chloig teagaisc le déanamh ag rannpháirtithe. Déanfaidh siad machnamh ar na ranganna a bheidh á múineadh acu ag baint úsáide as uirlisí pleanála agus féin-anailíse. Gheobhaidh siad aiseolas ó mheantóir a thabharfaidh 3 chuairt ar an seomra ranga ó chian, ag baint úsáide as an teicneolaíocht. Beidh an t-aiseolas sin mar bhunús le machnamh a dhéanfaidh siad chun forbairt a dhéanamh ar a gcleachtas sa seomra ranga.

Ábhar an mhodúil:
Déanfaidh rannpháirtithe 30 uair an chloig teagaisc i suíomh aosoideachais (ranganna do dhaoine fásta) agus cuirfidh siad cruthúnas ar fáil do na ranganna sin. Gheobhaidh siad 3 chuairt ar líne ó mheantóir a bheidh ag obair leo – is gá go mbeadh teacht acu ar an teicneolaíocht chuí chuige seo. Gheobhaidh siad aiseolas ón meantóir i ndiaidh an chéad agus an dara cuairt agus bronnfar grád orthu i ndiaidh an tríú cuairt. Cuirfidh siad plean forbartha le chéile i ndiaidh an chéad chuairt agus cuirfear an plean i bhfeidhm sna cuairteanna eile. Cuirfear an fhorbairt san áireamh agus an grád á bhronnadh. Beidh na meantóirí ag obair i gcomhar le stiúrthóir an chúrsa.

Ullmhóidh rannpháirtithe pleananna dá gcuid ranganna, taispeánfaidh said an t-ullmhúchán a bheidh déanta acu agus déanfaidh siad anailís ar éifeacht na ranganna. Beidh an t-ullmhúchán seo mar chuid den fhillteán teagaisc (LC007)).
learningOutcomes: Beidh 30 uair an chloig teagaisc le déanamh ag rannpháirtithe. Déanfaidh siad machnamh ar na ranganna a bheidh á múineadh acu ag baint úsáide as uirlisí pleanála agus féin-anailíse. Gheobhaidh siad aiseolas ó mheantóir a thabharfaidh 3 chuairt ar an seomra ranga ó chian. Beidh an t-aiseolas sin mar bhunús le machnamh a dhéanfaidh siad chun forbairt a dhéanamh ar a gcleachtas sa seomra ranga.
modules.notes: Clibeadh an modúl seo mar Shonraí/Digiteach (Data/Digital) ag úsáid Intleachta Saorga (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module aligns with Level B1 as specified in the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages, and will develop the student's necessary competence in the Irish language for entry into Primary Initial Teacher Education. Oral, aural, reading and written Irish-language competence will be assessed through the Teastas Eorpach na Gaeilge (TEG) Meánleibhéal 1 (B1) examination. Students will develop productive and receptive skills across a range of themes. Opportunities for student immersion in the Irish language will be provided at various junctures throughout the year.

The primary aim of LC101 is to provide a solid basis in the Irish language for beginners, through developing comprehension, speaking, reading and writing skills at a basic level. Classtime will be focused on building a good basic vocabulary, introducing grammatical concepts and focus on pronunciation. Irish language culture and heritage are an integral part of the course and various topics will be presented in lectures by School of Celtic Studies staff.  

This class will follow TEG Bonnleibhéal 1 (A1) syllabus closely. TEG (Teastas Eorpach na Gaeilge, www.teg.ie) is an examination system for adult learners of Irish that is linked to the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (Council of Europe, 2001). A1 level is intended for complete beginners. 

At the end of LC101, students should be able to communicate basic information about themselves through the medium of Irish. LC102, in Semester 2, builds on the outcomes achieved in LC101. 

Students who wish to take LC102 must achieve an overall mark of at least 50% in the LC101 examinations.
learningOutcomes: Use single words and simple sentences while taking part in a conversation based on topics and general information that relate to everyday life.
understand simple speech delivered in a slow, carefully articulated but natural manner.
understand texts that are short and simple insofar as they relate specifically to everyday life.
understand texts on a phrase-by phrase basis, finding words and recognisable phrases.
write simple words and very simple basic phrases.
understand some principal aspects of Irish culture, particularly those aspects relating to Irish and the Gaeltacht (Irish speaking areas).

modules.moduleContent: Díreofar, sa mhodúl seo, ar na cineálacha téacsanna is mó a aistrítear go Gaeilge – téacsanna leathoifigiúla, fógraí, comharthaí poiblí, téacsanna airgeadais agus ábhair phoiblíochta, cuir i gcás – agus ar na dúshláin a bhaineann lena n-aistriú.
learningOutcomes: Déileáil leis na cineálacha téacsanna is mó a aistrítear go Gaeilge agus na dúshláin a bhaineann leo.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will require a student to prepare and deliver a poster or oral presentation at a recognised national or international research conference, forum or colloquium. The selection of an appropriate conference, forum or colloquium will be determined by an oversight committee comprised of the Head of Department, Postgraduate Director and the course director of the Structured PhD Geography Programme.

This module will provide students with an opportunity to disseminate their research to the wider research community. Students will develop skills in assembling and presenting their research findings.

This module will normally be taken in Semester 2

This module will require a student to devise, develop and implement an independent research project, in consultation with their supervisor and with agreed learning outcomes. The student will choose their own research direction, discover their own learning resources, formulate their own research problems, decide their own course of action and reflect on the outcome of that process. On successful completion of this module, the student will have developed the capacity for undertaking autonomous research.
This module will enable the student to work independently on specialized areas or topics of relevance to their research and not provided for in existing modules.

This module will normally be taken in Semester 1
learningOutcomes: Describe a range of research methodologies, and justify their use of specific methodologies to investigate their research topic
Design an individual research project, and carry out appropriate primary and secondary research
Evaluate methods for achieving the stated research goal(s)
Recount and report on research findings related to the research

modules.moduleContent:
This module will require a student to undertake a recognized module at a recognized international third level institution.
This module will normally be taken in Semester 1
learningOutcomes: To be defined by international third level institution
To be defined by international third level institution

modules.moduleContent:
This module will require a student to undertake a recognized module at a recognized international third level institution. 
This module will normally be taken in Semester 2

Advanced specialist modules can be taken from those on offer from the Department of Geography, NIRSA, other Departments at MU, or a module on offer in another partner institution. Students select module from a range of options. 

Module Objectives: To enhance students’ ability to explore and debate complex issues at an advanced level.

This module will normally be taken in Semester 2
learningOutcomes: Identify and understand key geographical processes
Evaluate critically a variety of methods and approaches in geography
Discuss and analyze research findings in a research report format
Discuss research methodologies and findings in oral and written forms

modules.moduleContent:
Advanced specialist modules can be taken from those on offer from the Department of Geography, NIRSA, other Departments at NUIM, or a module on offer in another partner institution. Students select module from a range of options. 
Module Objectives: To enhance students’ ability to explore and debate complex issues at an advanced level.
learningOutcomes: Identify and understand key geographical processes
Evaluate critically a variety of methods and approaches in geography
Discuss and analyze research findings in a research report format
Discuss research methodologies and findings in oral and written forms

modules.moduleContent: Basic MATLAB programming; MATLAB toolboxes (such as Control Systems, Optimization, Systems Biology); fundamentals of programming in C;  basic numerical methods in C; fundamentals of Perl programming; introduction to mathematica.
learningOutcomes: Write and compile programs in MATLAB, Perl and C.
Use MATLAB, Perl and C in their own research.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective
The purpose of this module is to allow postgraduate research students to develop and apply the skills required for presenting their research.   This module covers the presentations of results in poster or oral format at a conference as agreed with the departmental supervisory committee.

Module Content
• Attend lectures/seminars/conferences in the area of research 
• Undertake an informed literature survey around the research that is intended for presentation 
• Draft hypothesis and framework for oral presentation 
• Write up the presentation to include abstract, introduction and results and discussion content
• Delivery of an oral presentation at an international conference.

On completion of the module the student will be able to:
• Discuss the current state-of-the-art in their research
• Interpret results in the context of the impact on the particular field
• Evaluate results for potential presentation
• Formulate a hypothesis for the presentation 
• Concisely present and critically discuss research highlights that can prove/disprove the hypotheses
• Draw conclusions on the work and articulate the significance of the research in the wider context
• Identify a conference.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the current state-of-the-art in their research.
Interpret results in the context of the impact on the particular field.
Evaluate results for potential presentation.
Formulate a hypothesis for the presentation.
Concisely present and critically discuss research highlights that can prove/disprove the hypotheses.
Draw conclusions on the work and articulate the significance of the research in the wider context.
Identify a conference.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to an extremely important moment in global history: the multiple revolutions of 1917-1919, revolutions that introduced new definitions of left, right, and centre into mass politics. The course shows how and why these revolutions occurred, and what were their immediate and long-term consequences on global history.

modules.moduleName: THE ANGLO-IRISH ARISTOCRACY IN WAR AND REVOLUTION, 1914-23
modules.moduleContent: This module examines the experience of the Anglo-Irish aristocracy from the beginning of the Great War in 1914 to the end of the Civil War in 1923. It looks particularly at the complex reasons for the destruction of around 300 of their Big Houses, burned in the revolutionary period 1920-23.
learningOutcomes: have a knowledge of the reasons for the decline and fall of the Anglo-Irish aristocracy, with particular reference to their experience in the period from the Great War to the end of the Civil War.
identify primary sources available for the study of this topic, and how to assess their strengths and weaknesses.
engage with historiographical debates and to develop an understanding of historical research through the use of primary sources.
demonstrate in their written work an understanding of the main trends and factors that shaped a unique dimension of the Irish revolutionary period, and present cogent, coherent arguments.

modules.moduleContent: This module will introduce a number of thematic fields within the discipline of history and explore intellectual and practical questions that arise within them. In particular, it will focus on the practice of history across the research process, by exploring how historians select and employ primary sources; define research questions; make arguments; and engage with wider historical debates.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate an understanding of the value of different types of primary sources to historians
demonstrate an ability to distinguish key concepts relating to the practice of history
demonstrate an ability to evaluate key points in texts provided by the lecturer and their relevance in terms of the material discussed in
demonstrate competence to present analytical responses to well-signposted questions in continuous assessment and an end-of-module exam

modules.moduleContent: This module will introduce students to a variety of the document types available to the historian, whether in print, manuscript, or other media including online. It will give students the opportunity to practice the historical skills of locating sources, contextualising primary documents, reconciling conflicting claims and evaluating the reliability of evidence.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate skills in collating and synthesising source material
demonstrate skills in critical analysis of a variety of source material
demonstrate capacity to write a coherent narrative
demonstrate capacity to present written work that conforms to the professional citation conventions specified in ‘Rules for contributors to Irish Historical Studies’

modules.moduleContent: This module will examine a specialised topic in American history.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate an understanding of themes and topics covered in the module
use a varied range of primary source material and secondary literature
through completing module assessment, have further developed analytical and writing skills

modules.moduleName: A HISTORY OF STRATEGY IN WARFARE, FROM CLASSICAL TIMES TO THE DIGITAL AGE
modules.moduleContent: The module will provide an overview of the history of strategy from its classical beginnings to the modern age. It will examine key theorists, their strategic concepts, and the context that they operated in. The course will discuss key strategic thinkers, including Thucydides, Sun Tzu, Machiavelli, Jomini, von Clausewitz, Mahan, Svechin, among many others. There will be discussion of modern theorists such as Edward Luttwak, Colin S. Grey and Lawrence Freedman. Course content will discuss the development of strategic thought from classical times to the nuclear age and beyond. There will be discussion of how military strategy has informed modern political, business, and administrative strategy. The concepts of applied strategy will also be introduced. Key concepts will be examined using a series of historical case studies.
learningOutcomes: identify key strategic thinkers and their theories.
understand how new technologies and the expansion of warfare have impacted on strategic thought.
display a knowledge of the key concepts of applied strategy.
understand how the strategic cultures of nations and institutions develops.
recognise historiographical debates and to analyse specific strategies and how they were applied in warfare.

modules.moduleContent: This module will provide an overview of eighteenth-century Ireland, addressing a range of issues such as confessional divisions, social change and political developments.
learningOutcomes: identify the key developments that occurred in Ireland during the eighteenth-century, and some of the more significant contemporary and historiographical debates.
have a knowledge of the chronology of eighteenth-century Ireland and of the ways in which historical events and processes interact to create sometimes unexpected outcomes.
use a varied range of primary source material and historical writing.
through completing essays, have further developed writing and analytical skills.

modules.moduleContent: The module provides in-depth exploration of key historical developments in one or more European regions
learningOutcomes: identify key points in primary and secondary sources
identify relevant sources independently and to relate them to the material covered in the module
have a critical approach to historical sources, mindful of historical context, audience, bias
grasp the different types of historical evidence that can be used to study the topics covered
write an analytical essay related to the topic

modules.moduleName: POVERTY AND WELFARE IN PRE-FAMINE IRELAND, 1800-1845
modules.moduleContent: Irish society, institutions, family life, disease and death in the pre-famine period.
learningOutcomes: have a broad understanding of pre-famine Ireland.
understand some of the ways cottiers, labourers and their families reacted to poverty and distress though their involvement in secret societies.
be aware of the role of the British state in pre-famine Ireland and their provision of medical and social welfare to the poor.
identify the difficulties faced by foundlings and cholera orphans in prefamine Ireland and how these two groups ultimately fared.

modules.moduleName: SPORT AND POLITICS IN MODERN IRELAND
modules.moduleContent: This module will examine the development of sport in modern Ireland across all sections of society. Understanding the political, social, cultural and economic context in which these organisations were formed, the module will examine the development of a wide array of sports including cycling, athletics, association football, Gaelic games, boxing, hunting and horse racing.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate knowledge and critical understanding of sport and society in modern Ireland
assess and understand the historiography of development of Irish sport
understand the relationship between sport and modern society

modules.moduleContent: Investigation through lectures and texts of church, politics, social organisation, art, architecture, literature and learning.
learningOutcomes: Be familiar with the types of sources available for a study of Irish history at this time;
be aware of major developments in Ireland in the period c.AD1014–1169;
understand the breadth and significance of connections between Ireland, Britain and the Continent;
understand the relevance of these developments and connections in the lead-up to the arrival of the Anglo-Normans.

modules.moduleName: EUROPE IN THE AGE OF REVOLUTION AND NATION, 1789-1878
modules.moduleContent: This module explores revolutions in 19th-century Europe from the French Revolution in 1789 to the ‘Great Eastern Crisis’ of the 1870s, also taking in the reasons for the fragmentation of the Ottoman political space, the outbreak of Serb and Greek ‘revolutions’,  the (failed) 1830 Polish revolution, the impact of the 1848 revolutions on East-Central Europe, and the wider, pan European significance of Italian and German unification movements.
learningOutcomes: • demonstrate understanding and knowledge of main developments in the history of nineteenth century Europe
• demonstrate and apply understanding of the causes and consequences of the revolutions studied and the emergence of moderns nations in nineteenth century Europe
• demonstrate the capacity for independent thought in the handling of historical sources
• critically engage with the relevant main historiographical debates, and develop their own ideas and positions in relation to them

modules.moduleContent: This module will provide students with the opportunity to explore the historical methodologies underpinning the relationship between history and story-telling.
learningOutcomes: have developed an appreciation of the ways in which interpretations of the Great Famine have changed over time
have developed an appreciation of the factors that shape the production of historical narratives
have developed an appreciation of the development of history writing in Ireland
think in particular what ‘history’ is, and what the differences between polemic, antiquarianism and academic history are (or are not
develop an understanding of the study of historiography

modules.moduleName: EMIGRATION IN NINETEENTH AND TWENTIETH CENTURY IRISH SOCIETY
modules.moduleContent: This module will provide an overview of nineteenth and twentieth century Irish emigration to North America and Australia.  The social, economic and cultural context of this emigration will be the starting point of the lectures. The theme of voluntary versus involuntary emigration will be introduced through an analysis of convict emigration, landlord assisted emigration, and famine emigration. The assisted emigration of children and juveniles to Canada and the Earl Grey Orphan assisted emigration to Australia will also be analysed. Finally, the over-representation of women in post famine Irish emigration will be documented.
learningOutcomes: have an awareness of the agency of destitute people in nineteenth century Ireland.
have a knowledge of an alternative narrative and chronology of nineteenth century Irish history that prioritizes destitute people and their families.
work with a range of primary source material ranging from prisoner petitions to parliamentary inquiries.

modules.moduleContent: This module, which follows on from HY353, analyses the Peace of Westphalia and its impact on the Holy Roman Empire during the era of mounting tension between Prussia and Austria until its final dissolution in 1806.  We then examine subsequent portrayals of the Empire from German, Austrian, Central European and European perspectives.  Students participate actively in a series of in-class discussions, role-plays, panel discussions on historical documents and oral reports, composing an essay for submission at the end of the semester.
learningOutcomes: understand the development of the Holy Roman  Empire from the Peace of Augsburg until its dissolution in 1806
learn to work together in groups
hone public presentation skills
formulate evidence-based critical historical analyses

modules.moduleContent: The module will explore the origins and conduct of the 'Great War' of 1914-18, with lectures on topics such as the campaign on the Western Front and events in the Eastern and Middle Eastern theatres.

This module is intended to help deepen the student’s engagement with historiography and historical theory using examples from different historical approaches, such as social, cultural, political, gender, economic and local history. Students will be encouraged to reflect on the connection between the narratives of history offered by historians, the evidence that underpins or may undermine those narratives, and how the larger contexts within these narratives are written, received, revised, and contested.
learningOutcomes: Understand the origins and development of social history as a field
Engage with debates on historiography and historical theory
Appreciate the different problems and questions that social historians have addressed and their varying methods in doing so
Discuss these complex ideas orally and in written work

modules.moduleContent: This module seeks to examine the existence of an American Way of War. It will evaluate strategic policy and the use of the military from the Revolutionary period through present affairs. It will cover a wide range of issues and students will analyse the planning, conduct of war on land, sea and in the air in a variety of wars and conflicts that the U.S. has fought in at home and around the globe as it moved from a colony to a world power.
learningOutcomes: Link an “American Way of War” with developments that happened in Western Europe.
Understand the structural development and issues regarding building a permanent American standing military.
Understand how wars throughout American history have shaped the development of training, organization, and procurement in the military.
Examine how the military has been historically used in American Grand Strategy.

modules.moduleContent:
The historiography of local history reflects a constantly evolving field of study that is influenced by a wide range of social, cultural and technological factors. While there is no one ‘correct’ way to approach the subject, the key is to remain open to new perspectives and to constantly reassess our assumptions and methods.
This module is concerned with the historiographical evolution of the study of local history in Ireland from the Middle Ages to the present.
The module will adopt a thematic approach to examine developments in the conduct of war from the French revolution (1789) to the end of the First World War (1918).
learningOutcomes: Identify the main issues and events relevant to understanding the evolution of warfare during this period.
Use relevant theory to understand the conduct of war.
Analyse historical battles and campaigns and explain the implications of such analysis.
Contribute to meaningful discussion and debate on relevant topics.
Demonstrate understanding of key discourse and debate on relating to the conduct of war during this period.

modules.moduleContent:
To develop students’ knowledge and deepen their understanding (during the second year of research) of current trends, methodological challenges, and innovations in scholarship in the discipline. 

Attendance at the History Department's Research Seminar (See History Postgraduate Handbook for details.) Students must attend all specified obligatory meetings of the Seminar and another seven over the course of the academic year.
learningOutcomes: developed their understanding of current trends, historiographical and methodological challenges and innovations in scholarship in the discipline ;
practised active participation in research seminars through listening, questioning, and engaging in discussion with speakers and peers;
further developed their repertoire of presentation skills through ongoing critical observation of, and reflection on, the presentation styles of peers and invited speakers;
developed an understanding of academic research as a collective and interdisciplinary exercise.

modules.moduleContent: To provide an opportunity for students in the first year of research to devise a research plan for their thesis featuring structured approaches to researching and writing their study. This includes articulating key research questions, locating the thesis within existing historiography, identifying and adopting appropriate methodologies and conceptual framework(s), developing skills in identifying and accessing relevant primary sources and secondary literature, honing skills in coherent argumentation. Students also practise compiling applications for postgraduate scholarships.
learningOutcomes: recognize the possible ways of structuring their thesis and consider appropriate methodological options and discussion of those option
distinguish between aims and objectives and create overall specific structures for their work
develop a strategy for organizing the project
monitor the coherence and consistency of argumentation
reflect in a structured form on the organisation and interpretative strategies for their thesis

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To develop the Ph.D. student's skills in refining and communicating their research to an undergraduate audience.

Module Content:  Research paper delivered at a Maynooth University summer school.  Copy to be submitted to the doctoral supervisor.
learningOutcomes: Students will, working in conjunction with their supervisor, learn how to prepare a class for an undergraduate audience.
Students will acquire the experience of employing visual and other aids to put their point across to an undergraduate audience.
Students will gain experience of teaching an undergraduate class.
Students will acquire the experience of fielding and answering questions from an unspecialised audience.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To develop Ph.D. students’ skills in refining and communicating their research to an audience of their peers outside the confines of the Department of History’s research seminar (or other regular seminars run within Maynooth University).

Research paper delivered to a postgraduate-level academic conference.
learningOutcomes: Working in conjunction with their respective supervisor, students will learn how to prepare a substantial paper based on their research findings for delivery to a group of their peers drawn from other disciplines and other Universities.
Students will learn to deploy visual and other aids in order to help transmit their research findings to an audience of their peers outside Maynooth University.
Students will acquire the experience of delivering such a paper.
Students will learn to field questions in a more challenging setting.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To allow the student in the second year of research to study abroad and acquire specific skills relevant to their research.

Module Content: The module will allow students to study abroad to obtain particular skills not available in MU.  Students may take one or more modules in the host institution amounting to 10 ECTS.

Module Objective: To provide an opportunity for the student in the second year of research to acquire skills in a language relevant to their research.

Module Content: The student will attend and participate fully in an appropriate Language for Academic Purposes module or equivalent course, in MU or elsewhere, as agreed with the doctoral supervisor.

Responsible Department:
History, through the Centre for Military History and Strategic Studies, in partnership with the teaching staff at the Officer Training Wing of the Infantry School at the Military College (DFTC, Curragh Camp).

Module Content:
This module will introduce the student to relevant concepts, theory and military doctrine relating to the Command, Leadership and Organisational studies. It will address the key organisational issues facing the Defence Forces today, with reference to relevant theories of learning, management and change management. It will address the role of the media in the context of current military operations and also explore the impact of legal, social, ethical and technological factors and the impact that these have on military planning.
learningOutcomes: Examine, evaluate and discuss concepts and theories relating to military leadership.
Demonstrate advanced knowledge and understanding of the impact of legal, social, ethical and technological factors on military operations.
Show awareness of the impact of contemporary media.

modules.moduleContent:
Responsible Department:
History, through the Centre for Military History and Strategic Studies, in partnership with the teaching staff at the Officer Training Wing of the Infantry School at the Military College (DFTC, Curragh Camp).

Module Content:
