The module will provide an introduction to, and a foundation in, the key concepts, theories, issues and debates pertinent to the discipline of defence studies. Students will engage with a series of topics that are critical to an understanding of the contemporary defence and security environment, in addition to addressing issues relating to contemporary Irish and European security policy.
learningOutcomes: Identify the main issues of relevance to the study of contemporary defence, strategy and security.
Analyse complex issues relating to defence and security, with reference to relevant literature.
Contribute meaningfully to discussion and debate on relevant topics.
Demonstrate understanding of key discourse and debate in contemporary defence studies in written format and through in-class participation.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
To develop a Cadet’s Develop Cadet’s knowledge, comprehension and application of command, communication, leadership and organisational skills required for junior officers to serve as leaders and instructors in the early phase of their career. The training environment and instructional focus will provide for analysis in, synthesis of, and evaluation of the various aspects of the Module.


This module will focus on subjects that support learning and development in command, leadership and human resource management skills required for junior officers and will include:
a. Defence Forces Leadership Doctrine.
b. Defence Forces Leadership Framework.
c. Defence Forces Values, Ethos, Culture and Climate.
d. Leadership at Tactical and Operational levels.
e. Contextual Factors in Leadership.
f. The Defence Organisation.
g. Military Law.
h. Human Rights Law.
i. International Humanitarian Law.

Module Objective: 
To develop a Cadet’s knowledge, comprehension and practical application of tactical leadership at Section Level in offence scenarios. The training environment and instructional focus will provide for analysis, synthesis and evaluation of the particular characteristics and skills of military commanders.

This module will focus on subjects that support learning and development in knowledge, comprehension and practical application of tactical leadership at Section Level in offence scenarios and will include:

a. The Integrated Planning Process.
b. Preparation of Operational Orders.
c. The Combat Estimate Process at Section Level.
d. Section Level Leadership and Procedures.

Module Objective: 
To develop a Cadet’s knowledge, comprehension and practical application of tactical leadership at Platoon Level in offence scenarios. The training environment and instructional focus will provide for analysis, synthesis and evaluation of the particular characteristics and skills of military commanders.

This module will focus on subjects that support learning and development in knowledge, comprehension and practical application of tactical leadership at Platoon Level in offence scenarios and will include:

a. Characteristics of Offensive Operations.
b. Platoon level Leadership and Operating Procedures.
c. Limited Visibility Operations.
d. Preparation of Operational Orders.
e. Threat Evaluation and Integration Process.

Module Objectives:
1. To develop the student's knowledge and comprehension of Irish history while recognising the need for reflection, self-awareness and self-directed learning. 

2. To lay the foundations for future learning and development at higher levels. The module will explore the history of Ireland from the 17th century through to the present. In particular it will focus on the history of Ireland in the 20th century and will include a particular emphasis on the history of conflict in Ireland and on the history of the Defence Forces.

Module Objectives:

1. To develop the students' knowledge and comprehension of Economics while recognising the need for reflection, self-awareness and self-directed learning. 

2. To lay the foundations for future learning and development at higher levels. The module will focus on the theory of microeconomics and macroeconomics. It will also address the practice of economics within the contemporary national, European and global context and will explore the impact of different economic policies. The course will also explore the relevance of economic theory and practice to the Defence Forces, including the idea of 'defence economics'.
Contact Department for further details.

The Boyne Valley is one of the most significant cultural landscapes in Ireland and its designation as a UNESCO World Heritage Site underlines its justifiable international status.  This module provides a multi-disciplinary examination of the rich history, literature and archaeology of the Brú na Bóinne Complex including Newgrange, Knowth and Dowth.  In addition to the three great Neolithic passage tombs and the late Neolithic and Chalcolithic (Copper Age) ceremonial complexes, this area was the focal point for the Uí Neill kingdom of North Brega and features in the tales, history and genealogies of this and other important early medieval families and tribes. The presence of heroes, like Cú Chulainn and Finn mac Cumaill, in stories set in the area will also be considered.  An exception feature of the Boyne Valley is how successive generations built on and borrowed from past histories to re-create the landscape.  The way in which this wealth of heritage is interpreted and presented to visitors as well as the strategies of conservation, and occasionally re-construction will also be explored.

Note: This module will also be available in Semester 2, under the code, ID003X The Cultural Heritage of Newgrange.
learningOutcomes: Describe and assess significant archaeological monuments in the Newgrange/Boyne Valley Complex.
Appreciate and understand the main features of the early medieval literature connected to the area.
Discuss the prehistoric monuments and landscape as perceived by early medieval writers.

modules.moduleName: CULTURAL HERITAGE AND THE IRISH LITERARY TRADITION
modules.moduleContent:
Ireland has an extensive and diverse literary tradition in the vernacular which far exceeds its medieval European counterparts.  The origins and development of writing, including Ogham script, and the influence of Christianity on our rich manuscript tradition will be explored.   Many of the early manuscripts, including the Stowe Missal, the Book of Kells, the Book of Leinster and Lebor na hUidre, are among our most important national treasures. The practical aspect of manuscript production, illumination and writing will be introduced followed by a thematic examination of some of their contents.  A selection of myths and legends, in English translation, will be studied with particular emphasis on the representation of the deaths of kings, heroes and some female figures. 
  

Note: This module will also be available in Semester 2, under the code, ID004X The Cultural Heritage of the Irish Literary Tradition.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a good understanding of the history, production and content of Irish manuscripts.
Have a good understanding of handling techniques, basic preservation, and conservation practices relating to historical collections.
Identify and consult historical materials.
Demonstrate familiarity with early Irish narrative literature and some of its core themes.
Critically appraise some of the primary texts and manuscripts, and acquire an understanding of the principal secondary sources.

modules.moduleName: THE CULTURAL HERITAGE OF MEDIEVAL SOCIETY
modules.moduleContent: This foundation module provides an assessment of early medieval Irish culture and society. While providing a broad introduction to Ireland’s early cultural history, emphasis is placed, through a series of case studies, on the social and cultural heritage of some of our most important ecclesiastical and secular sites. Visits to these sites are combined with insight into the early medieval texts and stories, and the archaeological and historical material, relating to these important complexes and their attendant landscapes.
learningOutcomes: Identify and access archaeological, historical and literary sources for the study of a number of important prehistoric and early medieval Irish sites
Consider the phased developments, including medieval interest in the so-call ‘royal’ site of Tara
Assess the influence of Christianity on early Irish society
Appraise critically on-site evidence and relevant secondary as well as at least some primary literature.

modules.moduleContent:
Fortnightly research seminars; presentations by fellow PhD students, staff and invited guest speakers.

IMPORTANT NOTE
MA in German or equivalent
learningOutcomes: develop the student's skills in communicating their research findings and engaging in critical discussions on the research of their peers and experienced researchers.

modules.moduleName: INTERNATIONAL DEVELOPMENT CHALLENGES - CLIMATE JUSTICE, TECHNOLOGY AND THE PLANET
modules.moduleContent:
This module will explore some of the key International Development challenges around climate justice, technology and development. 
It will review the related potential ethical and equity implications of these issues, and possible International Development policy and other responses to enable systematic change to improve both our planetary ecological and socio-economic welfare.
learningOutcomes: A clear understanding of the main related concepts around climate change, inequality and technology and their relationship with development.
Some insights into the main component drivers, trends and development impacts around climate change, technology and inequality, with reference to both the global and regional levels.
An appreciation of the processes of systemic change, the difficulties in tackling these ‘wicked’ problems, and the challenges in developing policy and political solutions.
Sufficient comprehension and confidence to critically engage with peers, and others in society regarding the areas covered and related policy debates.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The module aims to provide an overview of recent trends in migration and conflict, explore the drivers and development consequences of these phenomena, and the connections between migration, globalisation and the changing nature of conflict.
Current events worldwide, including conflicts in Ukraine, Syria, DRC, and South Sudan, highlight once more the devastating effect that conflict can have on the lives and prospects of millions of people globally and create pressures for internal displacement and migration. Human migration is a central story to us as a species and has existed since our migrations out of Africa. Some academics have described the current era as the Age of Migration due to the changes in migration trends caused by events such as prolonged conflicts; environmental factors; the growth of middle-income economies; population growth, and the facility of movement in an era of globalisation and connectivity. These changes have made migration a more visible and contested issue and attracted the media and political elites' interest. 
Nevertheless, much about the relationships between migration, conflict & security are misunderstood. 
This module will explore these relationships. Drawing from diverse disciplines, it will examine different perspectives on migration, conflict and conflict resolution processes. 
It will tie this literature to development and security issues, focusing on human security, voluntary and involuntary migration and its relationship to development.
learningOutcomes: Understand the various theoretical frameworks used to explain international migration and related issues.
Have an appreciation of the debates on the impact of migration trends on sending and receiving countries.
Have insight into the effects of conflict on societal development, internal displacement and migration.
Understand the basic concepts and principles of conflict management and transformation and their relationship to development activities.
Be cognizant of current policy debates on migration, and understand how migration and asylum/refugee policies can be linked to development policy.

modules.moduleContent: This module will develop participants’ understanding and skills in the areas of leadership and participatory development practice.  Through an exploration of theories, debates and practices related to the area, participants will explore the crucial role of leadership in realising change, especially in participatory development contexts. They will develop leadership and group facilitation skills and an understanding of approaches and processes which support community and group participation in critical development processes. Themes addressed will include group dynamics, models and styles of leadership, communication, power and decision-making in groups, the changing role of development practitioners, people-centred and transformative development. It is intended that through this module, students will have developed a constructively questioning attitude towards development work in general, and will be able to articulate this within their own perspectives on what appropriate development practice might look like.
learningOutcomes: Understand, critique and apply some of the key theories related to leadership, critical development and participatory development practice.
Appreciate the difference between participatory and critical development processes and practices and those of other, more hierarchical or mainstream ones.
Identify different principles involved in leadership which are relevant to supporting participatory development practice.

Demonstrate some leadership and participatory development practice skills relevant for critical development, e.g., critical analysis of development work, group facilitation, decision-making and communication.

modules.moduleContent:
There is the need to ensure food, nutrition and livelihood security in the context of a changing climate, globalisation,  growing world population and increased pressure on natural resources such as land and water. 
For this reason hunger, malnutrition and climate justice are challenges  at the heart of the post 2015 international development framework. 
This module will look at what are the root causes of food and nutrition insecurity,  how climate change is contributing to increased vulnerability of the poor, particularly in the Global South, and how 'adaptation to climate change' is essential for any efforts to promote sustainable food systems and food, nutrition and livelihood security. 
This module will also introduce the 'Sustainable Livelihoods approach' (SLA) to poverty reduction and how SL thinking can inform community based climate change adaptation strategies and food security planning.
learningOutcomes: Have critically examined some of the underlying causes and drivers of food insecurity and malnutrition.
Be able to distinguish between various approaches to, and debates on, food and nutrition security.
Have an understanding of the current and likely impacts of climate change on food security and how sustainable agriculture is central to ensure climate resilient and food secure livelihoods.
Be familiar with how  Sustainable Livelihoods approaches to poverty reduction are increasingly being integrated into climate change adaptation, food security and development planning.

modules.moduleContent:
Each student produces an a Masters dissertation (20,000 words)  in a specialised area relevant to International Development Studies.  
The student will be allocated a supervisor to give support and advice.
learningOutcomes: Conduct specialist research and have a specialised and deep understanding of one area of International development studies.

modules.moduleName: THEORY AND PRACTICE OF MEDIATION, NEGOTIATION AND DIALOGUE
modules.moduleContent:
The course aims to develop participants’ personal capacity to negotiate for themselves and use dialogue and mediation processes, in helping other parties prevent, manage and resolve conflict. 
The practical part of the course (simulation) provides an opportunity to apply and put into practise the theoretical knowledge and relate it to a relevant peacebuilding simulated scenario. 
This module also explores the application of mediation, negotiation and dialogue in conflict themes such as Crisis Management, Freedom of Movement, dealing with Human Rights and Gender Issues, and Multilateral working betweenn IGOs, NGOs and high-level representatives within a host country.
learningOutcomes: Explain and apply alternative dispute resolution techniques, using the international norms of mediation and negotiation in international peacebuilding.
Recognise personal responses to conflict situations and identify own style and attitudes in conflict situations, and the implications arising for effective conflict resolution.
Apply mediation, negotiation and dialogue skills, techniques appropriately with underpinning methodology, concepts and frameworks.
Understand the dynamics of managing inclusiveness, stakeholder relationships and generating constructive, creative and concrete sets of options towards achieving consensus based agreement.
Apply micro-skills of analysing, questioning, listening and reframing.
Apply distributive and integrative strategies and tactics, while processing information and emotions and identifying zones of possible agreement.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of the module is to develop a critical understanding of gender, identity and inclusion in the context of development processes and practices and everyday lived experience.  
The module will explore the theoretical debates on gender and identity and critique the gendered experience of development. 
The tools and policies to address diversity and inclusion will be examined, and their successes and failures unpacked, with reference to contemporary theory and to students own experience.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the various interpretations of gender, gender theory and concepts, women and masculinity studies and their application to the wider organisation of society and everyday lived experience.
Explain the theories and concepts of identity formation, inclusion and exclusion.
Critique the gendered effects of development policies and practices.
Examine the various approaches to inclusion of marginalised groups in development.

modules.moduleContent: This module is a continuation of LC101. Language skills acquired in LC101 will be further developed through a range of communicative activities and students will be introduced to further aspects of Irish language culture and tradition.
learningOutcomes: Use single words and simple sentences while taking part in a conversation based on topics and general information that relate to everyday life.
understand simple speech delivered in a slow, carefully articulated but natural manner.
understand texts that are short and simple insofar as they relate specifically to everyday life.
understand texts on a phrase-by phrase basis, finding words and recognisable phrases.
write simple words and very simple basic phrases.
understand some principal aspects of Irish culture, particularly those aspects relating to Irish and the Gaeltacht (Irish speaking areas).

modules.moduleContent: Díreofar sa mhodúl seo ar chuid de na fadhbanna a thagann chun cinn go minic san aistriúchán Béarla–Gaeilge, mar shampla, fadhbanna a bhaineann le béarlagair, comhaonaid, réim, agus difríochtaí comhréire idir an teanga fhoinseach agus an sprioctheanga.
learningOutcomes: Déileáil leis na fadhbanna a thagann chun cinn go minic san aistriúchán ó Bhéarla go Gaeilge.

modules.moduleContent: Is ag tógáil ar ábhar LC501 a bheifear sa mhodúl seo, agus leanfar den chíoradh ar phointí deacrachta sa Ghaeilge scríofa.  Múinfear an modúl seo i Seimeastar 2.
learningOutcomes: Pointí deacrachta sa Ghaeilge a láimhseáil go cumasach, muiníneach, téacsanna Gaeilge a chur in eagar agus aistriúcháin a athbhreithniú.

modules.moduleContent: Féachfar go sonrach, sa mhodúl seo, ar cheist na téarmaíochta, go háirithe na dúshláin a bhaineann le flúirse téarmaí a bheith ar aon choincheap amháin. Pléifear an gá atá le téarmaí nua a cheapadh agus na dúshláin a ghabhann leis, agus cuirfear ar a gcumas do na rannpháirtithe an nuathéarmaíocht a úsáid go comhleanúnach, cruinn.
learningOutcomes: An téarmaíocht agus an nuathéarmaíocht a úsáid go comhleanúnach, cruinn.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with a solid grounding in the fundamentals of the basic structure of the Irish Legal System, lawyering skills, and the ethical responsibilities of lawyers.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Analyse complex problems relating to the interpretation of statutes, precedent, and complex ethical dilemmas facing a lawyer in a theoretical factual matrix.

Communicate clearly and professionally with future potential clients and apply analytical and logical reasoning to any problems on which they are tasked to advise, and research such problems quickly and effectively.
Clearly articulate the structure of the Irish Legal System and the roles of the various participants in this system, together with their ethical responsibilities.

modules.moduleContent:
This module analyses the fundamental concepts and policies which underlie the Criminal Law, and the principles and procedures that shape it.  The definition of a crime and its various classifications will be examined, as well as the construction of criminal liability. There will be a strong focus on research, case analysis and problem-solving. Critical topics include: nature of crime; elements of crime; forms of criminal liability; non-fatal offences against the person; sexual offences; homicide; defences; and property offences.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the basic elements of a criminal offence and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to the existence or non-existence of those elements in a given factual matrix
Comprehend the principles relating to the enforcement of the criminal law, explain their operation on different circumstances and distinguish a criminal offence from a civil wrong
Examine the application of criminal law in a given factual matrix, prepare an argument as to the culpability of the parties and predict the outcome of a judicial determination of the problem
Analyse, debate and criticise the law in a particular area of criminal law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of criminal law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position
Develop the capacity to conduct independent legal research

modules.moduleContent:
This is an introduction to the French legal system, with a particular emphasis on the constitutional structures and values of France, the court structure and legal system, the concept and operation of civil codes, and on the law of obligations.

This module will be taught through the medium of English.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Broadly discuss the key features of the constitutional structure of the relevant state, and of its courts and legal system.
Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the key principles underpinning the law of obligations in the relevant jurisdiction.
Outline and evaluate the main rules relating to the formation, terms and validity of contracts, and set out the rules governing breach of contract and remedies therefor.
Outline and evaluate the main rules relating to non-contractual obligations and liability for civil wrongs.
Apply the relevant law to unseen hypothetical scenarios.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides an accessible introduction to the social sciences for incoming higher education students and provides a sound foundation for later modules such as Researching Criminology and Criminological Theory in Year 2. The goal of the course is to enable students to complement intellectual grounding in their chosen discipline of Criminology with a broad understanding of social scientific approaches, concepts and values.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of what the social sciences are, including some fundamental concepts and values.
Discuss the purpose and value of the social sciences.
Apply the concepts of structure and agency to different ‘real world’ examples.
Demonstrate an understanding of some foundational social science theories, as well as how theory can be applied to guide and build social scientific knowledge.
Demonstrate an understanding of the concept of social institutions and why they are important to social scientists.
Reflect on the practical and dispositional skills needed to ‘do’ social science.

modules.moduleName: AN INTRODUCTION TO CYBER/ CRIME & TECHNOLOGY
modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to have students think critically about the role of technology in all aspects of crime and the criminal justice system. It will introduce students to the latest innovations in the adoption and use of technology in the criminal justice system. And will ask students to consider whether technology poses new challenges and opportunities to crime and criminal justice. Topics will shift from examining new technology used to commit crimes to new technologies adopted for crime control strategies. There will be a focus on both the “hard” (e.g., devices, equipment, hardware, etc.) and “soft” (e.g., classification devices, computer software programs, information systems, etc.). This will include an examination of the growing reliance on technologies in the pursuit of justice and public safety, as well as the implications for groups who are subjected to them. Central topics covered include: theoretical understandings of technology and criminology, cybercrimes, the advent of new technologies and related police investigative (such as the use of body worn cameras, drones, and biometric data collection and analysis) and restraint technologies (from electronic monitoring to tasers), security and surveillance, and the use of technologies in prisons and community corrections.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: To understand the role of technology in the criminal justice system
To gain knowledge on the topical issues chosen for discussion in the course
To think laterally and critically about the use, and implications, of adopting diverse technologies in the realm of corrections, surveillance and security and its broader impacts on society
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module seeks to provide an introduction to Irish constitutional law. It examines: theoretical aspects of constitutional law, the doctrine of separation of powers, focusing on the roles and functions of the legislature, judiciary, and executive organs of the State, and the principles governing constitutional litigation, interpretation, and amendment. Any aspect of this module (including the weighting of the module mark’s components between continuous assessment and University scheduled written examination) may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the fundamentals of Irish constitutional law and an understanding of its history and development.

Examine the operation of the separation of powers in Ireland, analyse the justicability of disputes in view of this doctrine and discuss the interaction between the organs of State as governed by the Constitution.

Demonstrate a thorough understanding of the principles governing constitutional litigation and an ability to apply interpretive techniques so as to predict the outcome of a constitutional challenge to a particular provision of Irish law.
Analyse, debate, and criticise the law in a particular area of Irish constitutional law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of constitutional  law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.

modules.moduleContent:
This module examines the law governing the formation, operation, governance, and dissolution of various types of company, together with the company’s internal and external relations with directors, shareholders, and creditors. Critical topics include: characteristics of a company, separate legal personality, limited liability, formation/incorporation, internal structures, directors' duties, treatment of shares, dissolution/winding up, liquidation, receivership, and examinerships.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the law governing the operation, governance and dissolution of the company and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to the applicability of such laws in a given situation.
Comprehend the principal legal rules affecting the company and explain their operation in different circumstances, and upon different actors, such as directors, shareholders, and creditors.

Examine the applicability of the Companies Act 2014 (as amended) in a given factual matrix, apply relevant sections of the legislation and predict the outcome of a judicial determination of the problem.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of statutory principles and case law, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.
Analyse, debate and criticise particular area of company law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with a critical insight into the sentencing process in Ireland and other common law jurisdictions as it relates to adult offenders. It explores the theories and general principles of sentencing and judicial sentencing practices, including the treatment of diverse types of offender. This knowledge base will be built upon in the latter half of the module with an evaluation of non-custodial approaches to sentencing, and an examination of selected restorative, therapeutic and re-integrative policies and practices.
 

Topic Outline:
Theories of sentencing: underlying rationales of punishment; deterrence, retribution, incapacitation; rehabilitation. 

Sentencing law and practice: constitutional imperatives; general principles, statutory powers; consistency in sentencing and the role of guidelines; discretionary sentencing and judicial activism; approaches from other common law jurisdictions; the role of the victim. 

Sentencing and the offender: mentally disordered offender; sexual offender; repeat offender. 

Non-custodial alternatives: suspended sentence; probation orders; community service orders; electronic monitoring; Criminal Justice Act 2006. 

Key movements in criminal justice: restorative justice principles and practice; therapeutic jurisprudence; problem-solving courts; drugs courts; mental health courts.
learningOutcomes: Critically discuss the underlying rationales and general principles of sentencing;
Evaluate judicial sentence practice and compare and contrast practices in selected common law jurisdictions;
Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of sentencing approaches towards different types of offender;
Identify, assess and critically compare non-custodial sentencing regimes;
Engage critically with key movements in the field of criminal justice, such as restorative justice and therapeutic jurisprudence, and assess their impact upon the Irish sentencing regime;
Clearly present research, policy debates and their own arguments in an academic manner.

modules.moduleContent:
This module analyses the fundamental concepts and policies which underlie the Criminal Law, and the principles and procedures that shape it. The definition of a crime and its various classifications will be examined, as well as the construction of criminal liability. There will be a strong focus on research, case analysis and problem-solving. Critical topics include: nature of crime; elements of crime; forms of criminal liability; non-fatal offences against the person; sexual offences; homicide; defences; and property offences.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.

LW271 should not be taken by Criminology students who have previously successfully completed LW157.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the basic elements of a criminal offence and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to the existence or non-existence of those elements in a given factual matrix.
Comprehend the principles relating to the enforcement of the criminal law, explain their operation on different circumstances and distinguish a criminal offence from a civil wrong.
Examine the application of criminal law in a given factual matrix, prepare an argument as to the culpability of the parties and predict the outcome of a judicial determination of the problem.
Analyse, debate and criticise the law in a particular area of criminal law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of criminal law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.
Develop the capacity to conduct independent legal research.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces the student to the theory, nature and scope of administrative law and to the difference between administrative law and constitutional law. The student will study, and gain knowledge on, the principles of judicial review of a public law decision, including the distinction between judicial review and review on appeal and the amenability of a decision maker to judicial review. The student will also acquire knowledge about the grounds for judicial review (illegality, irrationality and procedural impropriety) and the remedies available by way of judicial review. The module will expose the student to the idea of control of the use of discretionary power in public administration and the use of licensing to control economic activity and to the workings tribunals. 

The module lecturer has the discretion to change any aspect of this module may change in any given academic year.

You must take LW255 Administrative Law in Ireland in order to get the exemption for the King’s Inns (Barrister course). You do not need this module for the purposes of the Law Society (Solicitor course).

Note: this module is offered in other programmes under code LW255 (Administrative Law). Students are not permitted to register for both modules.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the nature of administrative law and the sources of law in administrative law
Explain the role of the doctrine of rule of law in administrative law and discuss its constitutive elements.
Analyse the role of discretion in public administration.
Compare judicial review and review on appeal.
Describe the bases for making a public law decision amenable to judicial review.
Discuss and evaluate grounds for judicial review.

modules.moduleContent:
The module addresses the position of children in Irish law, with a particular emphasis on children in the context of family law. It addresses, in particular, current legal developments in the area of reproduction and assisted human reproduction (as well as surrogacy). The module examines the position of the child under the Constitution of Ireland 1937, the European Convention on Human Rights and the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child. It interrogates the law as it relates to the care and upbringing of children, with particular emphasis on the concepts of adoption, guardianship, custody and access, and the provision of financial support for children. The module also examines legal responses to children and other family members identified as being at risk, with a particular focus on the Child Care Act 1991, as amended, and the responsibilities of the Child and Family Agency, as well as child abduction, and domestic violence. 

 
The module seeks throughout to highlight the increasingly international dimensions of child law, with particular emphasis on cross-border recognition of court orders, and the free movement of families. It also emphasises the importance, where appropriate and feasible, of seeking to resolve disputes by conciliation, mediation and collaborative practices, though being mindful throughout of the imperative of ensuring that the best interests of the child are always secured.  

 

Module Content:  

The Evolution of Children’s Rights: UN Convention on the Rights of the Child; the Constitution and Children: the 31st Amendment (Article 42A); international law 

Reproduction and the Law: Parentage, AHR and Surrogacy 

Disputes about Children: Guardianship, Custody, Access, Maintenance  

Children at Risk: Care proceedings, Fostercare and Residential Care; Domestic Violence 

Adoption and Adoption Information  

Transnational Family issues and Conflicts of Law; Brussels II bis; Child Abduction;  

Children in the Justice System 

 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse the law’s evolving approach to the concept of parenthood and the responsibilities and rights that flow from parenthood, with particular emphasis on parenting by those other than the genetic parents and birth mother of a child and on legal responses to assisted human reproduction and surrogacy;
Outline and critique the concepts of adoption, guardianship, custody, access, and maintenance as they apply to the care and upbringing of children, and comment critically on their application;
Examine and critically analyse the provisions of the Child Care Act 1991 (as amended) and legal responses to children at risk and needing protection, and families experiencing domestic violence;
Consider child law in an international context;
Engage in independent research and analysis of relevant topics of child law and apply the law to unseen fact scenarios.

modules.moduleContent:
Welfare Law examines the relationship between law and the welfare state and, in particular, the impact of government policies and changes in the law on those living in poverty. It explores the concept of socio-economic rights and provides insight into the legal and ethical obligations of the state and the citizen. In particular, it will examine issues like race, gender, immigration status, and how these interact with welfare policies and poverty. 

 

Indicative topic list:  

Introduction to the Welfare State 

Welfare Fraud 

Employment Activation 

Criminal Justice and Poverty 

Coursework Clinic 

Gender and the Welfare State 

Poverty, Health and Disability 

Access to Justice and Welfare Appeals 

Immigration and the Welfare State 

Asylum and the Welfare State 

Race, Ethnicity and the Welfare State 

Housing Law 

Recap and Revision 

 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Critically discuss the economic, political, and legal foundations of the law governing social insurance/social protection/welfare payments and housing supports
Critically evaluate the concept of socio-economic rights and the impact of law and government policy on those living in poverty
Critically assess how race, gender, disability, and immigration status interact with welfare policies and poverty.
Critically analyse the concepts of welfare fraud and employment activation, and how the impact of the criminal justice system on those living in poverty.

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to introduce students to variety of contemporary issues relevant to the study of criminology at university, including the criminal justice responses to these phenomena. Lectures will focus on theories on class, race, ethnicity, colonialism and gender that inform contemporary research in criminology. Through these lenses, they will critically examine government responses to crime and criminal behaviour, the politicisation of crime, and the criminal justice system.
learningOutcomes: Understand theories on class, race, ethnicity, colonialism and gender that inform contemporary research in criminology;
Be familiar with a variety of contemporary issues discussed by criminologists;
Demonstrate the ability to interpret and critique empirical research findings relevant to these issues;
Critically examine relevant government policies.

modules.moduleContent:
This module examines the law governing the formation, operation, governance, and dissolution of various types of company, together with the company’s internal and external relations with directors, shareholders, and creditors. Critical topics include: characteristics of a company, separate legal personality, limited liability, formation/incorporation, internal structures, directors' duties, treatment of shares, dissolution/winding up, liquidation, receivership, and examinerships.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the law governing the operation, governance and dissolution of the company and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to the applicability of such laws in a given situation.
Comprehend the principal legal rules affecting the company and explain their operation in different circumstances, and upon different actors, such as directors, shareholders, and creditors.

Examine the applicability of the Companies Act 2014 (as amended) in a given factual matrix, apply relevant sections of the legislation and predict the outcome of a judicial determination of the problem.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of statutory principles and case law, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.
Analyse, debate and criticise particular area of company law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to acquaint students with the function of employment law and labour law in the context of both the individual and collective labour relationship. The module will explain the basic common law principles of employment law and labour law and teach and assess both Irish and EU legislative developments and their impact on the employment relationship.  Topics covered include the employment contract, termination of employment, equality in the workplace, health & safety at work and industrial relations law. 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Appreciate the function of employment law and labour law.
Demonstrate a thorough knowledge of the important legal principles that underline the employment relationship by reference to relevant case law and legislation.
Solve legal problems with reference to case law and legislation.
Appreciate the role of the European Union in employment law and labour law.
Assess the influence of competing critical perspectives on employment law.

modules.moduleContent:
Constitutions and constitutional law raise a range of difficult philosophical questions. These questions are both descriptive and normative in nature. What exactly is a constitution? Does every legal system necessarily have one? What is the source of constitutional legitimacy? What role should judges play in interpreting and enforcing constitutional law? Should questions of social justice be resolved by and through the constitution?

This module is theoretically-focussed, and will be of particular interest to those with an interest in legal and political philosophy.

Topics that will be studied include:

1. The Nature and Functions of Constitutions

2. ‘The People’ and Constituent Power

3. Political Representation

4. Deliberative Constitutionalism

5. The Rule of Law

6. Constitutional Interpretation

7. Constitutions and Socio-Economic Justice
learningOutcomes: Understand contemporary philosophical debates on topics related to constitutions and constitutionalism.
Improve ability to develop philosophical positions and arguments through group discussion.
Learn how to close-read and engage with philosophical texts.

modules.moduleContent: This module examines, and encourages you to discuss, the interconnections between law, policy, gender, and sexuality. We will start by focusing on key concepts in feminist and queer legal theory, such as the dominance of the heterosexual family; the gendered subject of law; and intersectional inequalities. We will then relate these theories to current issues: diverse family formations; sexual violence;  transgender rights; reproductive justice. Finally, we tackle the really big questions. Should we use the law to change the law? Are rights really any use? What is neo-liberalism and how does this relate to gender?
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of  the relationship between law gender and sexuality¿
Demonstrate an appreciation of the significance of feminist and queer theory for understanding the contemporary formation of legal and political norms
Critically analyse the relationship between right-based claims, claims for sexual citizenship, neoliberal approaches to rights and social inclusion within legal discussions of gender and sexuality¿

Critically identify the wide range of influences on legal discourse, policy, and law-making in relation to gender and sexuality, including concepts from political theory, the social sciences, contemporary culture and the humanities, and dominant ideas from the sciences¿

Demonstrate an appreciation of the intersection of concepts of gender and sexuality with concepts of race, religion, disability and class and the effects of those intersections on legal theory, practice, and activism

modules.moduleContent:
Please note:
We will admit students to this module only with the express permission of an academic staff member in the Department of Law who agrees to act as sponsor and supervisor. Entry onto this module is by invitation only (following a selection process).   

Overview: 
The purpose of this module is to build relevant data gathering and writing skills by completing research on a topic of legal relevance with practical applications.

The mode of entry to this module is as follows:
- One or more academic staff member(s) may put forward a research proposal for which students will be invited to apply.  In appropriate cases, an external body such as a charity or non-profit body may pose a research question, and ask an academic staff member to supervise a student in addressing that question.

In all cases, it is at the discretion of the academic staff member(s) to determine whether to supervise an applied research exercise, and whether to permit a particular applicant to take this module. The Department also reserves the right to decline to entertain any proposal.
Formal entry and registration on this module is by invitation only (students cannot automatically enrol and will need to be approved by an academic staff member in advance of registration.)

Module Content: 
This module offers students an opportunity to complete a focussed piece of legal or law-related research or other academic work in conjunction with or on behalf of a member of academic staff.  This may include, for instance, a research paper for a public body, non-governmental organisation, charity or non-profit, research support to an academic staff member, completion of a submission for a public consultation or similar exercise, or data collection and analysis under the direction of an academic staff member. In all cases, an academic staff member will supervise the work.

Orientation will include project management, time management, engagement with stakeholders and a refresher on legal research methods.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Conduct legal research under academic direction
Gather relevant data for research purposes
Write a research paper on a topic of legal relevance
Work with academics and other professionals in a cooperative and timely manner
Work to deadlines
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The module examines the challenges presented by twenty first century biotechnology for law and regulation. An examination of the possible socio-economic concerns about the application of biotechnology techniques and the use of their products in various sectors, medical, agricultural and industrial, and the consequential demands on the law will be a central focus of this module. The module focuses on Irish, UK, European and Australian Law, and other jurisdictions are examined where useful. Topics include consideration of the challenges of regulating science generally; a comparative study of national and state regulatory systems for research and development using controversial biotechnology techniques, including genetic modification and cloning; analysis of legal concerns regarding access to genetic resources; as well as an evaluation of approaches to the regulation of end products of biotechnology.
learningOutcomes: Coherent and advanced knowledge of the principles and concepts underpinning the application of traditional legal rules, and the development of new legal rules, to socio-economic issues raised by biotechnology;
Skills and knowledge to provide basic advice to scientists and those wanting to commercially exploit biotechnology and its outcomes on their rights and responsibilities in law;

An understanding of the development of legal rules and institutions tailored to biotechnology (e.g., Gene Technology Regulation; legislation governing human embryo research and cloning; regulatory approaches to genetics research and commercialisation);
An understanding of, and the ability to critically analyse and evaluate, the interests and forces that influence and shape the development and application of the law to biotechnology;

Skills to review, analyse, consolidate and synthesis the above knowledge to identify and provide solutions to complex problems arising out of the practice or development of, or activities pertaining to, biotechnology;
Further developed their writing skills;
Enhance their skills of statutory interpretation in the context of technical and complex legislation
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Students will be expected to arrive at a suitable dissertation topic after consultation with a faculty supervisor. Thereafter, s/he will be expected to complete a dissertation of up to 10,000 words, including footnotes. 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate competence and skills necessary to complete and defend an appropriate research proposal
Demonstrate nuanced exploration of the research question and completion of a thorough literature review
Analyse academic literature and draw appropriate conclusions based on the evidence
Demonstrate competence to produce a coherent, erudite dissertation supported by strong evidence and a thorough analysis

modules.moduleContent:
The International Mooting module is aimed at undergraduate students who wish to develop their advocacy skills, particularly in an international law setting. Students on the module will participate in the Jessup International Law Moot Court competition. The main focus of the module is on student-centred learning and the developing of practical legal skills. These skills will include case analysis, writing skills and practical legal research. The module will provide greater options for students within the law school and will help prepare students for employment, both in the legal profession and other sectors.

The Jessup International Law Moot Court Competition is the most prestigious mooting competition in the world. Annually, roughly 700 teams from all over the world compete in the national rounds, with the winning teams going on to represent their countries in the international rounds held in Washington DC. Each year teams (of five student members) are presented with a case involving a fictitious dispute between two states. This dispute is put before the United Nations' most important legal organ, the International Court of Justice. It is up to the student-teams to defend the two states to the best of their ability, both in writing and through oral pleadings. The active involvement of judges from the real International Court of Justice, the Iran-United States Claims Tribunal, lawyers and professors of law guarantee the high intellectual standards of the Competition and its prestige.

In August the fictitious case is released and each team has to prepare the written pleadings on behalf of the applicant and respondent states. Two written memorials (one for each side) are sent to the organiser of the competition in January. National oral rounds are usually held in Ireland during February and the winning team of those rounds takes part in the international competition in April in Washington DC. 
More information about the Jessup Moot is available here Jessup 2024 – International Law Students Association (ilsa.org)
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of key features of public international law alongside a coherent, systematic and detailed knowledge of a selection of areas dealt with in the assigned case;
Demonstrate the ability to engage with, react to and develop critical insight on the topics focused on in the assigned case;
Demonstrate the ability to conduct independent research on the topics covered in the assigned case;
Demonstrate the ability to articulate in discussion different perspectives on public international law;
Demonstrate the ability to accurately communicate research findings and arguments both orally and in writing;
Demonstrate the ability to work independently and in teams.

modules.moduleContent: Courts have become increasingly important actors in contemporary societies. Courts evolved from the ‘least dangerous branch’ into bodies resolving fundamental societal and political disputes. The course ‘Courts and Judges’ will provide students with an understanding of current debates concerning courts. The course builds on solid theoretical and conceptual background, coupled with rich case law and empirical case studies. The course will provide an understanding of courts as legal and political institutions and demonstrate different perspectives on courts. It will demonstrate the growing power of courts and explain why it happened. Students will be able to critically discuss the pros and cons of strong courts and judicial review. The core of the course will focus on key concepts such as judicial independence, accountability, public confidence and legitimacy, and transparency. Finally, the course will elaborate on the benefits of diversity on the bench, the varieties of systems of judicial self-governance and the increasing significance of international courts. Students will also reflect on the role of courts in current trends of democratic decay/the rule of law backsliding.
learningOutcomes: Gain a new, richer perspective on courts as legal and political institutions.
Have an overview of different judicial systems, their main characteristics, and similarities and differences.

Comprehend key concepts related to modern judiciary, such as the rule of law, judicial independence, judicial accountability, transparency and legitimacy. Be able to critically discuss those concepts and be aware of current debates surrounding them.
Become familiar with both national and international case law putting those key concepts in practice. Understand the complex relationship between courts, society, politics and media.
Identify and examine different models of judicial self-governance. Be able to discuss the role of courts in the current international environment of democratic decay/rule of law backsliding.
Comprehend the role of the international judiciary and its influence on international and domestic law and politics. Be able to apply conceptual knowledge to real-world cases.
Demonstrate the ability to pursue an argument, using primary sources and academic literature, as well as the ability to synthesise material from a broad variety of sources.

modules.moduleContent:
Arbitration law module aims to introduce postgraduate students to the historical origins, theoretical foundations and the development of arbitration law. For this purpose, the course provides a thorough study of the principles and the practice of arbitration law, with particular emphasis on international commercial arbitration, the most important dispute resolution mechanism for cross-border commercial disputes. This module focuses on the experience of arbitration law in Ireland and in other leading arbitration legislations, particularly in an international law setting. 

Module Content
The purpose of the course is to provide thorough knowledge of arbitration law, which is today the principal method of resolving disputes between individuals, companies, and countries in connection with international trade and investment. The module is focused on the legal analysis and discussion of issues within essential themes of international commercial arbitration but selected aspects of investment arbitration will also be explored. These topics will include arbitration agreement, difference between institutional and ad hoc arbitration, selection of arbitrators, arbitration procedure, arbitral award, arbitrability, question of applicable law, and recognition and enforcement of arbitral awards. Lecture material will be mostly based on contemporary legal developments including the current case law with an insight into past legal decisions, relevant legal instruments, as well as selected academic literature.

The main focus of the module will be on student-centred learning and the developing of practical legal skills. These skills will include case analysis, writing skills and practical legal research. The lectures will not solely focus on the interpretation of legal rules and caselaw but also give rise to class discussions on challenges faced by participants to arbitration proceedings. Throughout the semester, several leading arbitration law scholars and practitioners will share their expertise with the students in guest lectures. 

This module will help prepare students for employment in arbitration law as solicitors, barristers, scholars, or in-house counsels, especially in Ireland which is considered more and more as an ideal arbitral seat.
learningOutcomes: have acquired understanding of the essential features of principles, elements and rules of arbitration law;
have gained the skills to determine and resolve main issues arising in arbitration practice;
have developed a deep knowledge and understanding of the major arbitration rules and key policy concerns;
have developed skills of legal analysis and research of caselaw and legal doctrine;
have developed written and oral communication in the legal context;
have acquired the ability to study in groups;
have acquired the ability to conduct independent research on the topics covered in module.

modules.moduleContent:
Content and structure of a thesis; Formulating a research question; Preparation of a thesis outline; Appropriate application of research methods; Appointment of thesis supervisor.

**This module must be successfully completed before the award of the degree.
learningOutcomes: Identify a research topic.
Write an initial research proposal.
Work under supervision to bring the proposal to the point where research can begin.
Identify sources and locate repositories, archives, libraries, private collections to be accessed.
Understand the structure, methodological process involved in carrying out an extended piece of historical research.
Under supervision bring the research topic to a successful conclusion.
Make an original contribution to knowledge.

modules.moduleContent:
Students will be able to gain a thorough understanding of the foundations of tortious liability. They will be able to give an account of the development of tort law which places it in its historical context and distinguishes it from contract and criminal law. Students will learn the essential elements of the torts of negligence, occupier’s liability, product liability and nervous shock. They will also be able to advise on the appropriateness of different remedies depending on the case at hand. 
 
Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Comprehend the principles relating to development of the law of torts as well as explaining its purpose and objectives
Recognise and identify the key principles relating to the existence of a private law duty in tort, as well as the origins of such duties
Analyse, debate, criticise the law in a particular area of torts and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness
Analyse, debate, criticise the law in a particular area of torts and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of tort law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position
Develop the capacity to conduct independent legal research.

modules.moduleContent:
This module seeks to equip students with a sound understanding of fundamental rules of contract law that determine when and how a contract is formed, under what circumstances a contract is binding, and the rules governing the terms of a contract. 

Topics may include:

- Sources of Contract Law
- Formation: Offer, Acceptance, Certainty, Intention to Create Legal Relations, Consideration, Promissory Estoppel
- Enforceability: Privity, Electronic Contracts, Capacity
- Interpretation of a Contract
- Express & Implied Terms
- Incorporation of Terms
- Exclusion and Limitation Clauses
- Sale of Goods
- Consumer Protection

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: explain the sources of contemporary Irish Contract Law;
recognise, explain and apply the key elements required in forming a valid and enforceable contract;
differentiate between different types of contractual terms and explain how terms are incorporated into a contract and how courts determine the meaning of those terms;
assess the operation of exclusion and limitation clauses in contracts;
explain and apply the protections afforded to consumers entering into a contract for the sale of goods and supply of services;
analyse a factual scenario by applying the rules governing the formation, enforceability and terms of a contract to determine the likely legal outcome.

modules.moduleContent: This module begins by introducing students to some of the main models and theoretical frameworks used to understand the operation of the criminal justice system before proceeding to map out the criminal process and the role of the various actors and institutions within it. These actors and agencies are then examined in further detail by utilising the results of criminological research to illuminate aspects of criminal justice in action and to compare accounts with official objectives. The module concludes by introducing a number of contemporary issues and debates relating to the operation of the criminal justice system and the punishment of offenders.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the aims of the criminal justice system and utilise theoretical frameworks to explore key debates relating to the criminal process.

Demonstrate a broad knowledge and critical awareness of the operation of some of the principal institutions and actors within the criminal justice system, the role of discretion and means by which they can be made accountable.
Compare and contrast the Irish criminal justice system with those in other jurisdictions, particularly in relation to matters such as the role of key actors and institutions.
Critically analyse some of the contemporary issues affecting the Irish criminal justice system such as the appropriate role of the victim.
Demonstrate written skills of argument.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO LAW AND COMPARATIVE LEGAL SYSTEMS
modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with a solid introduction to law, legal systems, the rule of law, the sources and classifications of Irish and international law, and the different world legal systems (common law/civil law/Islamic law/mixed systems). The module considers how laws are made, interpreted, and applied, the role of the courts and judges, and the doctrine of precedent in common law jurisdictions like Ireland, contrasting this with the formulation, operation, and interpretation of civil codes in states with a civil law system. Emphasis will be
placed on fundamental aspects of the Irish legal system, yet in a comparative context that allows students to compare and contrast the Irish legal system with that found in civil law states such as France, Germany, and Spain.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Clearly articulate the structure of the Irish Legal System and the roles of the various participants in this system, together with their ethical responsibilities.
Evaluate key features of the Irish legal system, the sources of law, the classifications of law, the process of law-making in Ireland and the role of courts and judges.
Explain how laws are interpreted and the role of the doctrine of precedent in Irish law.
Compare and contrast civil and common law jurisdictions, with reference to the difference between common law and codified laws.
Analyse complex problems relating to the interpretation of statutes, precedent, and complex ethical dilemmas facing a lawyer in a theoretical factual matrix.

Communicate clearly and professionally with future potential clients and apply analytical and logical reasoning to any problems on which they are tasked to advise, and research such problems quickly and effectively.

modules.moduleContent:
This introductory module will teach students the basic skills required for the study of law. The course is applied in nature, and will cover topics such as reading cases and statutes, basic writing principles, professionalism and client communication skills, library and internet resources for researching law, methods for citing various legal sources, and exam-taking techniques, all of which are necessary to ensure success in the law program.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Read and synthesise the rules from cases.
Read and interpret a statute.
Communicate effectively, both orally and in writing.
Use library and internet resources proficiently to locate relevant legal sources when faced with a research project.
Cite various legal sources using the OSCOLA Ireland method.
Use information technologies proficiently, in particular, the use of email, word processing, spreadsheets, online databases, and the internet.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module is based on four classic criminological research monographs which have been highly influential within the discipline, both internationally and in Ireland, and which would be widely recognised by criminologists as seminal studies. The broad aim is to provide students with an in-depth appreciation and understanding of these studies and their wider context, but also the skills necessary to critically analyse primary texts.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an in-depth understanding of the socially constructed nature of crime.
Discuss and demonstrate understanding of what prisons and coercive confinement are like for inmates.
Discuss the nature of female offending and the limitations of the traditional criminological project in responding to it.
Demonstrate key practical skills, including close reading, reflection, critical thinking, and written skills of argument.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed for students of Criminology. Criminological research may be conducted on government responses to crime, public policies, politicisation of crime, the criminal justice system, and criminal behaviour. Criminological research is grounded in criminological theory, itself linked to many social scientific disciplines. It also seeks to influence social policy and inform our understanding of criminal justice practice. The core research methods discussed in the module are common across many disciplines, including Criminology, Politics and Sociology, for example.

The module introduces students to the main research methods used by criminologists. Lectures will give students an understanding of different research philosophies that underpin research design. We will discuss the merits of various research methods, including desk-based research, quantitative methods, and qualitative methods. Students will be introduced to concepts like reliability, validity, and causality.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.

* Please note, students should NOT take LW227 if they have previously taken PO224 as the content is the same in both modules *
learningOutcomes: Understand theories that inform research in criminology
Understand the key elements of research design
Be familiar with a variety of quantitative and qualitative research methods used by criminologists
Demonstrate the ability to interpret and critique empirical research findings

modules.moduleContent:
This module focuses on the defences to a criminal charge in Ireland, and encourages students to develop an understanding of the social, moral and political forces underlying the criminal law, and the procedure that shapes it. This module also examines other specific offences. There is a strong focus on research, case analysis and problem-solving. Critical topics include: aspects of criminal procedure, theories of criminal law, inchoate offences, public order offences, capacity defences and general defences.

 Please note that this module is subject to change.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the range of criminal law defences and specific offences covered, and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to their existence in a given set of facts.
Comprehend the principles relating to criminal law defences, and explain their operation on different circumstances.
Analyse, debate and criticise the law in a particular area of crime and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of criminal law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.
Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the foundations of criminal law.
Develop the capacity to conduct independent legal research.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with knowledge and understanding of the operational rules of contract law, and particularly, the principles that guide formation of a valid legal contract. Topics covered include themes in, and the nature of, contract Law, elements in the formation of contract including the offer, acceptance, consideration, third parties and intention to create legal relations. 

The module lecturer has the discretion to change any aspect of this module may change in any given academic year.
learningOutcomes: Identify and discuss the underlying principles of the liberal contract.
Outline and compare the elements that form a valid contract.
Examine and write on the elements required to form a valid contract.
Evaluate the use of case law in particular areas of contract formation and draw conclusions on applicability and consistency with precedent.
Define, discuss and justify public policy.
Evaluate the import and role of public policy in the failure of contract formation.
Complete assignments and do independent legal research.

modules.moduleName: MENTAL HEALTH AND THE CRIMINAL JUSTICE SYSTEM
modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with an introduction to the complex interface between mental health and criminal justice. Students who take this module will explore developments in this area from an historical perspective and delve into key contemporary debates around ‘care’ versus ‘control’ and ‘treatment’ versus ‘security’.
The module will explore such areas as personality disorder and the criminal justice system, neurodiversity and the criminal justice system, and therapeutic jurisprudence.
learningOutcomes: Understand the relationship between law and mental illness from an historical perspective.
Demonstrate an in-depth understanding of the key legal instruments in the area.
Display an understanding of key contemporary debates around the intersection between mental illness and the criminal justice system.
Students will be supported in their development of key academic skills - critically engaging with the module material, literature search/review, and strong written skills.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the international legal framework for the protection of human rights. It reviews and critiques the framework and explains how the international human rights system operates. It queries the nature and scope of the rights protected under the framework and assesses how the rights can be implemented domestically. It asks if human rights are universal, or if cultural relativism applies, and also focuses, to an extent, on the regional protection of human rights.

The module provides an overview of the international human rights law framework, including international treaties and enforcement mechanisms. It covers the human rights framework of the United Nations, as has been inspired by the Charter of the United Nations and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. In addition to providing an overview of the international human rights legal system, the module will focus on a number of specific rights, including the right to life, the prohibition on torture, cultural rights and the rights of minorities and indigenous peoples. The module assesses how international human rights standards can be implemented domestically, and queries how States deal with their human rights obligations. The ultimate aim of the module is to provide students with a strong grounding in the international human rights law framework as well as the main academic and policy debates in this dynamic field. It will help students to develop the tools and critical thinking necessary to understand and apply the normative and doctrinal principles of human rights law to actual human rights crises, and to critique the current human rights legal framework and practice with reference to policy debates and academic commentary.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.

Indicative topic list:

1. Introduction to International Human Rights Law

2. The UN Human Rights Law Framework

3. Civil and Political Rights

4. Social Economic and Cultural Rights

5. Group Rights, including the Rights of Minorities and Indigenous Peoples

6. Regional Human Rights Systems

7. The Protection of Human Rights during Armed Conflict

8. The Enforcement of the International Human Rights Law Framework
learningOutcomes: * Critically analyse the UN human rights law framework
* Assess the nature and scope of rights protected under international human rights law treaties, through a discussion of provisions and jurisprudence
* Evaluate the successes and failures of the UN system for the protection of human rights
* Appreciate how human rights are dealt with under various legal regional systems, particularly the Council of Europe system
* Appreciate how international human rights standards are implemented domestically
* Apply the international human rights law framework to theoretical scenarios, as well as real life human rights crises
* Demonstrate an appreciation of contemporary human rights issues and challenges

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to key issues regarding ethnicity within the study of crime, punishment and the criminal justice system. In doing so, it addresses topics including the overrepresentation of minority ethnic groups in criminal justice systems internationally; interaction with law enforcement; sentencing disparities; and the impact of crime legislation on minority ethnic communities, for example the 'War on Drugs'. Taking a comparative criminological perspective, students will explore the Irish context as it relates to that in other common-law jurisdictions, with particular reference to the Irish Travelling community and their parallels with the Maori community and indigenous Australians, Canadians and Americans. This module also examines minority ethnic victims of crime, looking at punitive attitudes toward ingroup and outgroup crime, and hate crimes. Additionally, the cultural and media representations of ethnicity and crime are discussed, and the module overviews the representations of minority ethnic group members as victims and offenders and the impact that these may have on punitive attitudes and outcomes. Topics will also be explored using the concept of intersectionality, which looks at how other factors such as gender and socioeconomic status are felt alongside ethnicity. This module uses Irish and international case studies to illustrate the implications of differential treatment based on ethnicity in the criminal justice system and will draw on statistics, policy documents and academic literature. Topic Outline: Across this module, the following content will be covered. Lecture presentations will be complemented by guest speakers, video material and in-class activities. Minority ethnic offenders: Sentencing disparities, higher rates of death penalty verdicts when the victim is from the majority ethnic community, poor relationships with law enforcement. Minority ethnic victims: Hate crimes, domestic violence, differences in punitive attitudes and outcomes for minority ethnic victims. Legislative impacts: Disproportionate impact of criminal justice policy on minority ethnic communities in Ireland and internationally. Ethnic stereotypes: The role of the media in perpetuating minority ethnic stereotypes and in shaping responses to crime. The Irish context: Examining the overrepresentation of the Irish Travelling community in the Irish prison and probation service.
