Reviewing research which has found that non-Irish nationals receive significantly longer custodial sentences than their Irish counterparts for certain offences.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module examines a broad range of crimes which fall under the conceptual umbrella of white-collar crime—such as, occupational, corporate, state-corporate, and governmental and state crime. White-collar crimes typically occur within an occupational context, are financially motivated, and rarely involve direct, interpersonal harm, yet, have the capacity to cause harm on a wide scale. Students will draw on criminological theory in examining white-collar crime and its causes. Case studies of white-collar crime will be used to evaluate and identify the limitations of national and international law, regulation, and the criminal justice system more broadly in responding to and preventing white-collar crime. 

Topic Outline
• introduction—defining WCC, WCC typologies, the criminalisation and social construction of WCC.
• theoretical perspectives on WCC.
• occupational and avocational crime.
• corporate crime.
• intersections between organised and WCC—enterprise and contrepreneurial crime.
• state-corporate and finance crime.
• state crime, governmental crime, and political WCC.
• techno and cyber WCC.
• policing and regulating WCC.
• prosecuting WCC.
• WCC prevention.
learningOutcomes: Identify examples of white-collar crime and its typologies.
Explain the extent of and harm caused by white-collar crime.
Draw on theory and case studies to explain the causes of white-collar crime.
Evaluate the effectiveness of national and/or international law, regulation, and criminal justice systems in responding to and preventing white-collar crime.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: FAMILY LAW: MARRIAGE AND ADULT RELATIONSHIPS
modules.moduleContent:
The module explores the law as it relates to marriage and relationship formation and breakdown, with particular reference to marriage formation, the law of nullity, and the law relating to separation agreements, judicial separation, and divorce. It also addresses laws that enhance the rights and obligations of unmarried couples, including civil partnership and the law addressing non-marital cohabitation. Legal responses to relationship breakdown, including judicial separation, divorce and civil partnership dissolution, will feature prominently, with a focus on the court-ordered remedies available following judicial separation, divorce and dissolution, and the ending of a cohabiting relationship, as well as the criteria for applying these remedies.

The module seeks throughout to highlight the increasingly international dimensions of family law, with an emphasis on cross-border recognition of relationships and court orders, and the free movement of families. It also emphasises the importance, where appropriate and feasible, of seeking to resolve disputes by conciliation, mediation, and collaborative practices. In particular, it examines the main characteristics of separation agreements and the potential use of pre-nuptial agreements.

Topic covered;  
1. Defining the Family: The Constitution, the European Convention on Human Rights; Issues of Family Diversity: Civil Partnership and Cohabitation
2. Going to Court: The Family Court system and the in camera rule
3. Alternative Dispute Resolution: Mediation, Conciliation, and Collaborative Family Law 
4. Marriage Formation and Capacity to Marry; Nullity of Marriage
5. Separation Agreements and Pre-nuptial Agreements 
6. Judicial Separation 
7. Divorce; Recognition of foreign divorces
8. Ancillary Orders (Remedies) following Judicial Separation and Divorce; Maintenance and Child Support
9. Family Property
10. Alternatives to Marriage: Cohabitation and Civil Partnership
11. Domestic Violence and Families at Risk
12. Transnational Family issues and Conflicts of Law; Brussels II bis; Cross-border recognition and enforcement

The module will address some matters relating to children and the law, but this topic is generally the subject of the Child Law module.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Outline and analyse the legal formalities that apply to the formation of marriages, together with the rules that govern the recognition of cohabitants and qualified cohabitants.
Critically examine the concepts of nullity of marriage, judicial separation, divorce and civil partnership dissolution, with particular emphasis on the grounds for each remedy.

Outline and critique the court-ordered remedies available to spouses, civil partners and qualified cohabitants following the breakdown of a marriage, civil partnership or cohabiting relationship, and the criteria applied by judges in awarding such remedies.

Outline and critically assess strategies to avoid or minimise court-based litigation including mediation, collaborative practice and agreement, in particular, separation agreements, pre-nuptial agreements and cohabitation agreements.
Demonstrate an informed understanding of the rules on the cross-border recognition of marriages, civil partnerships and divorces.
Engage in independent research and analysis of relevant topics of family law and apply the law to unseen fact scenarios.
modules.notes: Students consider and analyse quantitative data on patterns in family life and critique how to read such data.

modules.moduleContent:
The law of evidence is that system or body of rules which regulates the admission of proof in a court of law. This module examines these rules which govern the gathering of evidence at the pre-trial stage and its subsequent presentation in court. Critical topics include: basic concepts of evidence, functions of the judge and jury, admissibility, relevance, corroboration, rule against hearsay, character evidence, opinion evidence, and privilege. 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.

* Please note, students should NOT take LW324 if they have previously taken LW254, as the content is the same in both modules *
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the operation of the criminal trial and an ability to identify the key elements of the trial and appraise the fairness of a particular trial on an examination of given facts.
Comprehend the principles relating to the trial and explain their operation in different circumstances.
Comprehend the principles relating to the operation of the doctrine of privilege and give examples of the circumstances when these principles may apply.
Analyse, debate and criticise the law in a particular area of evidence and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of evidence principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position

modules.moduleContent:
Climate change is recognised as one of the greatest threats facing humanity. This module aims to critically evaluate the legal frameworks governing climate change mitigation and adaptation at the international, European and domestic levels. Whilst the focus of the module will on the legal dimensions of climate change, the complex and multifaceted nature of the climate crisis means that the pedagogical approach will be interdisciplinary. The module will typically engage with the international climate change regime, EU climate law and the national legislative response. The module will also consider recent developments in the field such as the shift towards justice and human-rights based approaches to climate change, climate change litigation, energy law and just transition, supply-side climate change solutions like fossil fuel non-proliferation, the regulation of geo-engineering, and climate change activism at the edge of the law.   
 
The precise content of the module is subject to change and may vary, including from year to year.
learningOutcomes: Understand, discuss and critique the legal frameworks governing climate change at an international, European and national level,
Understand, discuss and critique the roles of different actors in the field;
Identify, evaluate and critique different approaches to tackling climate change including a climate justice and rights-based approach;
Understand, discuss and critique a few specific contemporary climate law areas such as climate litigation;
Communicate knowledge, ideas and arguments effectively by a variety of means; and
Undertake further independent research in climate law and governance, including on an international level and comparatively.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides an introduction to key concepts of mediation and negotiation with a view to becoming familiar with those processes in practice and the differences between them. Students will develop personal skills for resolving disputes and gain experience in the practical use of these skills as efficient and cost-effective alternatives to litigation. One of the central aims of this course is to facilitate students in the discovery of their own style and approach to mediation and negotiation in a variety of legal situations.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.

Jurisprudence asks: What is the nature of law? This module examines the question from the point of view of philosophy, as well as from that of other disciplines such as anthropology and sociology. 
 
Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer. 
 
You must take LW354 Jurisprudence in order to get the exemption for the King’s Inns (Barrister course). You do not need this module for the purposes of the Law Society accreditation (Solicitor course)
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the different theories as to what exactly constitutes "law" and an ability to summarise and explain the approach to the question across different disciplines.
Engage in an active and sophisticated discussion as to the differing nature of laws and legal systems and comment on the different types of rule that have been recognised as "law".
Develop and present arguments that espouse a particular view as to the nature of law.
Design and express a "lawful" system for the regulation of a range of human conduct when given a factual matrix in which to work.
Analyse, debate and criticise traditional definitions as to what constitutes "law" and draw conclusions as to the adequacy of such definItions, and suggest alternatives.

modules.moduleContent:
Policing and the State examines key issues in contemporary policing from both and Irish and international perspective. 

Throughout the module, we pay careful attention to the role of policing in, and its relationship to, the State: particularly democratic states. Policing and the State critically examines how modern ‘democratic’ police forces seek to build and maintain legitimacy for their use of coercive force over diverse and pluralistic populations. 

The module also examines changes in the relationship between the political system and policing: particularly the decline in confidence in policing in many democratic states. 

The first half of the module focuses on a variety policing scandals––such as police corruption, abuse of power, institutional racism, and collusion with terrorist paramilitary groups––and the cycles of reform that typically follow such scandals.

The second half of the module examines emerging issues of police legitimacy, police militarization, the role of policing in national security, and the privatization of policing.

Critical issues of race, gender, sexuality and socio-economic class––and how they relate to policing––are examined throughout the module.

This module is a stand-alone course of study. 

Prior study of policing (such as the second year criminology module Policing LW269), criminology or criminal law is helpful, but not necessary. 

International students are particularly welcome.

This module welcomes any student with an interest in exploring the nature and extent of police power with our society from a critical and rigorous perspective.

Some of the topics examined in Policing and the State include:

1. Police collusion in terrorism
2. Policing of political protest
3. Institutional racism in policing
4. Institutional sexism in policing
5. The treatment of police whistleblowers
6. Police-media relations
7. Policing gender-based violence
8. Privatization of policing
9. Policing and national security/counter terrorism
10. Policing migration
11. Police militarization and police warrior culture
learningOutcomes: Critically examine the role of the policing in the modern democratic state, and its relationship with different components of State, and the general public.
Understand and interrogate the asserted philosophical justifications for police power, such as concepts like ‘policing by consent’.
Provide sophisticated critical accounts of key aspects of modern policing practice, such as the policing of the vulnerable, and the policing of protest;
Reflect on the key forces and crises that have shaped the governance and accountability infrastructures of policing in Ireland, and other jurisdictions;
Examine the various strategies employed by policing to manage legitimacy;
Demonstrate written and verbal skills of argument and understanding of research methodologies.

modules.moduleContent:
The law of evidence is that system or body of rules which regulates the admission of proof in a court of law. This module examines these rules which govern the gathering of evidence at the pre-trial stage and its subsequent presentation in court. Critical topics include: basic concepts of evidence, functions of the judge and jury, admissibility, relevance, corroboration, rule against hearsay, character evidence, opinion evidence, and privilege. 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.

* Please note, students should NOT take LW364 if they have previously taken LW294, as the content is the same in both modules *
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the operation of the criminal trial and an ability to identify the key elements of the trial and appraise the fairness of a particular trial on an examination of given facts
Comprehend the principles relating to the trial and explain their operation in different circumstances.
Comprehend the principles relating to the operation of the doctrine of privilege and give examples of the circumstances when these principles may apply.
Analyse, debate and criticise the law in a particular area of evidence and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of evidence principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.

modules.moduleContent:
Intellectual property is concerned with creations of the mind, including songs, books, inventions, and logos. Intellectual property rights grant the owner an exclusive right which allows them to prevent others from using or copying these creations. Intellectual property laws attempt to balance these exclusive rights with the benefits that these creations provide to society. 

This module will provide a detailed examination of the legal protection of intellectual property rights encompassing such matters as copyright, trade marks, and patents as well as the law governing the registration of such rights and the penalties for their infringement.


Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the requirements that must be met for recognition of a patent and a trade mark as well as the process by which they may be registered.

Demonstrate knowledge of the principles relating to the establishment of copyright by an author over material and be able to distinguish between uses that infringe such copyright and those that do not.
Examine the policy reasons for the limitation on such rights and debate the utility and effectiveness of such limitations.
Interpret a given hypothetical situation in light of IP law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal protection afforded to the IP right in issue.

modules.moduleContent:
This module seeks to introduce students to the field of comparative constitutional law. We will examine the core areas of comparative constitutional law—namely, constitution drafting and revision, constitutional adjudication and interpretation (both international and domestic), and rights. The conceptual exploration of these areas will be done through case-studies on specific constitutions and courts from the Global North as well as the Global South.
Learning Outcomes: This module is intended to provide students with an understanding of the basic topics, concepts, processes and questions dealt in comparative constitutional law. As well as an understanding of the practical implications that are related to the study of this field

This module aims to acquaint students with the function of employment law and labour law in the context of both the individual and collective labour relationship. The module will explain the basic common law principles of employment law and labour law and teach and assess both Irish and EU legislative developments and their impact on the employment relationship. Topics covered include the employment contract, termination of employment, equality in the workplace, health & safety at work and industrial relations law.¿ 
 
Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer. 

 
Module Content:  

Topics can include the following: 

Employment Dispute Resolution 

The Employment Relationship: Employment Status, Atypical Work 

The Employment Contract: Terms, Pay, Working Time 

Termination of Employment: Wrongful and Unfair Dismissal 

Equality Law: Gender Discrimination, Race, Age, Disability 

Harassment and Bullying at Work 

Collective Employment Law
learningOutcomes: Appreciate the function of employment law and labour law.
Demonstrate a thorough knowledge of the important legal principles that underline the employment relationship by reference to relevant case law and legislation.
Solve legal problems with reference to case law and legislation.
Appreciate the role of the European Union in employment law and labour law.
Assess the influence of competing critical perspectives on employment law.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the field of international criminal law. It provides a discussion and analysis of the history of international criminal responsibility and its development over time. In particular, it focuses on how the international community has reacted to atrocity crimes in terms of prosecution and sentencing. The module will, in particular focus on international prosecutions since the Nuremberg Trials in 1945, the development of ad hoc and hybrid tribunals and courts and the creation and practice of the International Criminal Court. The scope and nature of specific international crimes will be analysed in depth, i.e. genocide, crimes against humanity, war crimes and aggression. The module will also discuss modes of liability and sentencing in respect of international crimes. The legal frameworks of atrocity crimes will be analysed in depth, in addition to jurisprudence of international tribunals. Academic commentary on the operation and challenges of the legal framework will also be analysed. 
The ultimate aim of this module is to provide students will a grounding in the legal framework of atrocity crimes with reference to academic commentary and tribunal practice. 

Indicative topic list: 
1. Introduction to International Criminal Law 
2. The History of International Prosecution of Atrocity Crimes 
3. Genocide 
4. Crimes against Humanity 
5. War Crimes 
6. Aggression 
7. Sentencing 
8. Reparations
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse the history and development of international criminal law
Assess the nature and scope of atrocity crimes
Evaluate the successes and failures of the international criminal law project, with reference to jurisprudence and academic commentary
Appreciate how international crimes can be prosecuted domestically
Apply the international criminal law framework to theoretical scenarios, as well as real life situations
Demonstrate an appreciation of challenges to the international criminal law framework
Develop clear, rationale, and well supported legal arguments in respect of atrocity crimes, with reference to legal provisions, jurisprudence, and academic commentary

modules.moduleContent:
This module examines the legal context for global migration, with a particular focus on international refugee law, universal rights norms, and the impact of the European Convention on Human Rights. Students will use prism of migration to critically assess the evolution of universal human rights and the erosion of state sovereignty in the sphere of human rights protection. It analyses contemporary trends in global migratory movements and introduces students to key concepts of Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Law, at the domestic, European and international levels. It questions the extent of fundamental rights protection for non-citizens under universal human rights law and  the ECHR. It explores the special legal regime for refugee protection in international and EU law and scrutinises the legal definition of a 'refugee'. The module traces the development of the rights of migrant workers in international law and also introduces students to the debates surrounding legal responses to irregular migration.
Any aspect of this module may change in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Outline and analyse core debates on migration and the law, both orally and in writing;
Describe and critically assess key concepts in international and EU refugee law;
Describe and critically assess the legal framework for the protection of fundamental rights of migrants, encompassing domestic, European and international law;
Critically evaluate and discuss the impact of the ECHR in the field of migration;
Apply human rights law to immigration scenarios.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides students with an overview of modern legal history post-1800. Students will be introduced to key moments in modern legal history, with a particular emphasis on Ireland and the United Kingdom. It will consider, amongst other topics, the entanglement of Church and State, the manner in which mass movements engaged with and articulated legal arguments and changes in the law that occurred as a result, and the structure of the Irish State after independence. It will also, where appropriate, us a comparative European and international lens to consider how local legal histories resonate with, interact with, are influenced by and influence more global trends.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an awareness of contemporary debates about modern legal history.
Identify relevant sources, whether of law or of history, and analyse these sources.
Demonstrate the ability to construct an argument, using techniques drawn from the field of legal history.
Demonstrate research skills, including the use of databases, internet resources, or, where appropriate, archives.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Since ancient times, the question of the nature of law has occupied leading thinkers in the Western philosophical tradition. Like other core philosophical questions, however, there remains today much disagreement about the answer – and even about what a good answer would look like. Whereas the modules Jurisprudence 1 and Jurisprudence 2 focus on the contributions of the leading legal philosophers of the last century, Advanced Issues in Legal Philosophy builds on this foundation to introduce students to the state of the art by exploring current debates.   
 
The module will develop three themes of contemporary legal philosophy, namely, a) inquiry into the general relationship of law and morality by way of traditional philosophical analysis, b) empirical research into general legal concepts, and c) analysis of the foundation of specific legal domains.  The aim of the module is to provide a unified overview of the field of legal philosophy; accordingly, it features a pair of module leaders, Dr Conor Crummey and Dr Brian Flanagan, with complementary research backgrounds, who will lead class discussion in tandem. Much of the module will be research-driven, allowing students to engage with the work of the module leaders in a relatively small-group setting.  
 
The method of instruction will involve discussion of one or two prescribed two journal articles or book chapters in a seminar style format. A key aim of the module is to develop students’ skills in close-reading and analysing philosophical texts.

Indicative list of topics (subject to change in any year) 
 
A re-introduction to general jurisprudence  
Contemporary Positivism I: Gardner (5 ½ Myths) and Raz I  
Contemporary Positivism II: Raz II and Shapiro  
Contemporary Non-Positivism I: Dworkin (‘Hard Cases’) and Greenberg I (‘How Facts Make Law I’)  
Contemporary Non-Positivism II: Greenberg II (‘The Moral Impact Theory’) and Hershovitz (‘The End of Jurisprudence’)  
Experimental Jurisprudence I (Flanagan and Hannikainen 2022; Flanagan and Almeida) 
Experimental Jurisprudence II (Struchiner et al 2020; Flanagan et al 2023) 
Law and Courts Scholarship (Gillman 2001) 
Philosophy of Private Law (Gardner; Shiffrin) 
Philosophy of International Law (Koskenniemi) 
Philosophy of Public Law/Constitutional Theory (Raz; Dworkin)
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a critical understanding of the general relationship of law and morality by way of traditional philosophical analysis.
Demonstrate a critical understanding of empirical research into general legal concepts.
Critically analyse the philosophical foundations of specific areas of law.
Develop the ability to closely read, analyse and discuss philosophical texts.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will introduce students to the fundamental legal principles which govern the relationship between banks, their customers and third parties in the retail banking context. It will draw on both private commercial and public regulatory law including contract law, equity, property law and the statutory frameworks which govern the provision of financial services including bank accounts, payment services and various forms of credit.

(Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.)
learningOutcomes: Analyse the sources and scope of the duties and obligations which govern the legal relationship between a bank and its customer;
Evaluate the rights and obligations of these parties in relation to such matters as confidentiality and the giving of financial advice;
Identify the rights and obligations created by paper and electronic payment mechanisms, the statutory protections available to paying and collecting banks;
Outline the key principles relating to the law of securities and guarantees and analyse the impact of insolvency on the enforcement of security by banks;
Assess the potential liability of the bank and its customers to claims and the ability of third parties to interfere in the privity of the banking relationship.

modules.moduleContent:
In this course, students examine the rights that are considered fundamental to individual liberty. While the course focuses on the European Convention on Human Rights, students will also consider how other documents – including the EU Charter and the US and Irish Constitutions – protect against government interference with individual rights. Key topics examined include freedom of expression, freedom of assembly, privacy, and the right to vote.
 
Students are introduced to the course by considering the historical and theoretical foundations of civil liberties. As it is important to place civil liberties in the appropriate political context, nonlegal sources, such as newspaper articles and parliamentary debates, will be considered alongside traditional legal texts. Issues addressed include the relationship between the individual and the state, the reconciliation of competing rights and interests, and tensions between the European Court of Human Rights and domestic institutions. 
 
Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
 
Topics covered in this course include: 
1. The concept of civil liberties and the place of civil liberties in modern debate 
2. European Convention on Human Rights and the European Court of Human Rights 
3. The Right to Vote 
4. Freedom of Expression 
5. Freedom of Assembly and Association 
6. Right to Respect for Private Life
learningOutcomes: Critically discuss the foundations and development of civil liberties globally;
Critically assess the impact of civil liberties on the operation of state institutions;
Identify the key challenges to the protection of civil liberties within an international context
Demonstrate detailed knowledge and understanding of key civil liberties and the interaction between competing interests;
Analyse, debate, and critically evaluate relevant legal materials and draw supported conclusions;
Develop the capacity to conduct independent legal research.

modules.moduleName: LABOUR LAW AND THE DIGITAL ECONOMY
modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to bring students into close contact with the impact of technology in the workplace and to begin considering frameworks for responses as well as more detail innovations. It takes labour law as its point of departure: how does technology affect the workplace and its regulation in law? Underpinning the course are consideration of the following topics: non-standard employment, platform economy, gig work. 

 

Innovations in information technology mark the turn and ensuing years of the 21st century as a period of transition from an analogue to a predominantly digital means of interaction. Discussion of the impact of information technology on the workplace has been largely limited to ‘gig’ work or the platform economy. This course explores these developments as part of a continuum. The commercial focus in economic liberalism has been the ethos over this time dating back to the early half of the 20th and continuing through the early 21st centuries. Approaches such as Fordism and Taylorism focused on maximising collective efforts of a labour force working as constituent elements of an enterprise. Production efficiencies were soon joined by cost reduction as similarly important aims. Models developed that deconstructed the regulatory apparatus as well as the (now) standard form of employment in order to effect these goals. Stimulation of the market economy became the driving factor in employment regulation. The platform economy enters here. 

 

There has been an unproductive artificiality in the current understanding of information technology in employment law which focuses on procedural safeguards instead of deliberating upon the substantive rights underpinning the claims. The procedural approach alone has become insufficient. One conceptual problem has been the treatment of the workplace and employees’ private lives as distinct, separate entities where interconnectivity is narrowly defined: ‘The employer-employee relationship is contractual, with particular rights and obligations on either side, and is characterised by legal subordination. It is governed by its own legal rules, which differ considerably from those generally applicable to relations between individuals’. Contemporary innovations in information technology contest the more traditional boundaries relied upon in law. There has been an intermingling of employees’ private lives with the workplace. The course will consider how to balance of interests in the intersection between the workplace and information technology. 

 
Module Content:  

Indicative Topic List: 

Introduction to labour law and the digital economy 

Historical developments: Fordism, Taylorism, leading to the digital economy 

Platform work and status of employment: employee, independent contractor, worker, independent contractor 

Algorithms and work 

Rights and the Digital Economy, including privacy in the workplace 

Social media influencers and work
learningOutcomes:
Overarching outcomes

· Develop knowledge of business operations

· Understand the basic concepts of management

· Apply management tools to existing problems

· Propose and develop innovative solutions

Global Perspective

· Grasp the complexity of the labour law in an international environment

· Adapt practices according to the context

Effective Communication Skills

· Produce presentations and express oneself clearly in public

· Be able to listen to others

Critical Thinking

· Be aware of the human impact of one's decisions

· Evaluate the consequences of one’s activity on the environment

· Use the available management tools to resolve ethical conflicts

· Select and use relevant information

· Adapt one’s response according to the situation

· Demonstrate a degree of creativity in one's responses
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides an introduction to the novel insights that an economic analysis of law provides. It explores applications of such analyses to inter alia the law of tort, property, contracts and regulation Students will study the theoretical ideas and applications of economics to law, and learn the manner in which economic methodology and reasoning have been applied to legal problems. No previous study of economics is necessary. Nor is detailed mathematical knowledge.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Understand the theoretical ideas and applications of economic analyses to law.
Examine and critique the legal and economic constraints of using economic reasoning to resolve legal problems.
Critically discuss the economic arguments concerning the law of property, contracts, tort, crime and legal procedure
Deploy economic concepts such as efficiency and rationality in the analysis and evaluation of key legal concepts and doctrines.

Discuss the assumptions, weaknesses and limitations of economic concepts and modelling techniques and their application to law, and use this critical awareness in their evaluation of law, policy and practice.
Understand that law is a social enterprise and of the relevance of economic analysis to law as it is taught in other modules and, more generally, how it is discussed in everyday life.

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED PERSPECTIVES ON INTERNATIONAL TRADE LAW
modules.moduleContent:
This module examines the cardinal and cutting-edge topics in the international trade system from the perspective of international law and international relations. The discussion covers a wide range of topics on agriculture, energy,pharmaceutical, digital trade, and trade and environment issues. The purpose of the module is to empower the students to develop an enriched and extensive view of the legal aspects of the international trade.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse the law as it relates to international trade in food and agricultural products, pharmaceutical products, digital services, energy and the environment.
Demonstrate critical understanding of the relevant international and supranational treaties and legislative measures governing the law in this field.
Assess the practical, real-world implications of such laws in a geo-political context
Offer creative solutions to challenges in international trade using hypothetical litigation and arbitration strategies.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module seeks to explore the relationship between feminism and constitutional law from a comparative perspective. Topics to be covered include but are not limited to, women in constitutional judging, gender parity in legislative bodies, the relationship between democratic theory, feminist theory, and constitutional theory, as well as feminist constitutional change and adjudication (e.g, equality, civil and political rights, reproductive rights, etc). The exploration of the relationship between feminism and constitutional law will be done through case studies on specific constitutions and courts from the Global North and the Global South.
learningOutcomes: Students will have a clear understanding of the different and diverging trends in the relationship between feminism and constitutional law in different regions of the world.
As a discussion-based module, the assessment is designed for students to further develop and put into practice their research, writing, verbal communication, and critical thinking skills.

modules.moduleContent: In this module, students will be introduced to Irish language literature in translation.
learningOutcomes: Reference particular aspects of the Irish language literary tradition.
Appreciate the power relationship between the English and Irish languages as an overarching consideration in translation.
Demonstrate a familiarity with a number of relevant texts.
Outline and evaluate current arguments for and against translation from minority into major languages.
Demonstrate the ability to research in detail a theme or aspect of the module content, and to present a critical argument based on this research.

modules.moduleName: THE BIG HOUSES & LANDED ESTATES OF IRELAND
modules.moduleContent: This course considers how Irish landed estates first evolved and then declined from the sixteenth century to the winding up of the Irish Land Commission towards the end of the twentieth century. Students will be introduced to the vast array of sources available for the study of rural landscapes and the methodologies which can be employed in this field.
This module will provide a survey of the Irish revolutionary period, starting with the Home Rule Bill of 1912 and ending with the consolidation of the Irish Free State in 1924.
learningOutcomes: understand and reflect upon the development of independent Ireland from 1912-24
be familiar with the historiography of the Irish revolutionary period
be familiar with selected material from the Military Archives
engage critically with primary sources, in particular first person accounts and post-facto recollections
interpret digital resources for scholarly research.

modules.moduleContent:
The module will examine one or more aspects of social history in Ireland c. 1850-1950, from a range of topics such as poverty, childhood, education, gender, land ownership, medicine and science, emigration.

In the 2024-25 academic year the focus will be on childhood and growing up in Ireland.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a knowledge and understanding of a key topic (or topics) in Irish social history.
Identify and interrogate relevant primary source material.
Familiarise themselves with the relevant secondary literature and engage with historiographical debates.
Engage in informed discussion on the key themes covered.
Write an analytical essay related to the topic.

modules.moduleContent: In this module, students who have completed LS6, or who have studied Irish to a sufficient level before coming to university, will further develop their skills in the Irish language. They will proceed through grammatical study and graded reading towards proficiency in writing and speaking the language. The grammar, vocabulary and syntax of the language will be studied with a view to helping students realise a greater degree of competency. The content will consist of topics drawn from everyday life with a view to help the student build a practical competence in the language.
learningOutcomes: Recognize a wide range of Irish vocabulary.
Demonstrate proficiency in the spoken language.
Demonstrate an ability to read graded Irish passages.
Demonstrate the ability to compose material in Irish.

modules.moduleContent:
This module helps students to learn the essential skills of legal research and writing. It focuses on how to plan an approach to legal essays and problem questions, how to read and understand cases and legal literature, and how to write effectively about law. 
Students will hear the skills and techniques explained in lectures, and practice them in written work. Students will additionally be shown how to find legal sources and cite those sources.
learningOutcomes: Identify key legal issues in essay and problem question, and plan an effective response to these issues.
Read case law and literature effectively and efficiently.
Write clearly, precisely, and succinctly about legal issues.
Find legal sources efficiently and cite them according to the OSCOLA Ireland citation convention.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with a solid grounding in the fundamentals of the basic structure of the Irish Legal System, lawyering skills, and the ethical responsibilities of lawyers.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Analyse complex problems relating to the interpretation of statutes, precedent, and complex ethical dilemmas facing a lawyer in a theoretical factual matrix.

Communicate clearly and professionally with future potential clients and apply analytical and logical reasoning to any problems on which they are tasked to advise, and research such problems quickly and effectively.
Clearly articulate the structure of the Irish Legal System and the roles of the various participants in this system, together with their ethical responsibilities.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with knowledge and understanding of the operational rules of contract law. The module primarily is concerned with the principles that guide vitiation of a contract. The module builds on the knowledge acquired on contract formation and topics covered may include contractual illegality, restraint of trade, undue influence, duress, frustration, contracts of guarantee, misrepresentation, insurance contracts and contract remedies.

The module lecturer has the discretion to change any aspect of this module may change in any given academic year.
learningOutcomes: Outline and explain the different circumstances a contract fail due to vitiation.
Compare different vitiating factors.
Discuss the bases for the termination of contract.
Define, discuss and justify public policy.
Evaluate the import and role of public policy in the failure of contract.
Complete assignments and do independent legal research

modules.moduleContent:
This module will provide students with an introduction to the study of criminology at university. Students will become familiar with the importance of theory as a means of explaining crime and deviance, and the criminal justice responses to these phenomena. The module will introduce students to some of the primary offence categories, looking in particular at selected serious offences. The module will also overview the history of crime and deviance in Ireland. Students will learn practical skills throughout the module. In particular, students will be supported in learning how to develop skills in academic research, reading and writing.

Topic Outline:
• Criminology – What is criminology?; History and development of criminology as a discipline.
• Crime – What is crime?; How do we measure crime?; Statistics and crime trends in Ireland and internationally.
• Introduction to theory – What is theory, and why is it important?; Overview of the key theoretical traditions in criminology.
• Key offence categories – Homicide and violence; Sexual offences; Drug offences; Organised crime; Terrorism.
• History of crime and punishment in Ireland – Crime trends in Ireland since the Famine; the development of criminal justice agencies in Ireland.
• Key skills in criminology – How to study, research and write at university; Referencing; Reading; Locating Sources; Critical Thinking.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an in-depth understanding of criminology as an academic discipline.
Discuss and demonstrate understanding of the importance of theory in criminology; compare and contrast the different theoretical approaches used in the study of criminology.
Identify the key offence categories in the study of crime and recognise the dominant trends, arguments and issues in relation to each.
Explain the broad trends of crime, criminal justice and punishment in Ireland.
Demonstrate key practical skills, including desk-based research skills, critical thinking, written skills of argument.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module seeks to introduce students to Irish constitutional law. It will examine the core principles of Irish constitutional law, with an emphasis on the powers and functions of the organs and agencies that constitute State power.

Module Content:
Students will examine topics including: the doctrine of separation of powers focusing on the roles and functions of the legislature, judiciary, and executive organs of the State, constitutional interpretation, the principles governing constitutional litigation, international relations, as well as reform and amendment. The course will also examine the operation of the courts, the trial of offences in due course of law and examine how the constitution protects the right to personal liberty.

modules.moduleName: KEY COMPETENCES FOR NEW LAW STUDENTS
modules.moduleContent:
This module seeks to develop and consolidate skills that law students will need for a productive university experience and generally for their careers and life. It comprises a series of workshops addressing skillsets that students may too readily be assumed to possess already, but which may require some fine-tuning. Topics to be covered on this module include

- Refresher on writing skills: Reminder of the main rules of punctuation, grammar, syntax, and of common spelling mistakes in English. Tips on effective writing. Avoiding plagiarism – developing your capacity to write independently.
- Basic computer literacy: using Word and similar applications; formatting documents for assignments; presentation, footnoting; using Endnote, Zotero and similar applications; making charts.
- Information literacy: where to find reliable information for assignments and research; developing informed critique of statistics; practical advice on navigating legal academic databases; distinguishing reliable from unreliable sources; finding case law; finding (up-to-date) legislation; identifying useful secondary sources.
- Critical Thinking and Reflection: how to develop a reasoned argument; reason and logic; challenging received wisdom and developing independent and informed viewpoints.
- Organisation and time management. The art of prioritisation. Tackling procrastination.
- Diversity in society and the workplace. Bridging the divides. Accommodating differences. Tackling stereotypes and unconscious bias.
- Approaches to problem-solving, resolving conflict, and working in groups.
- Personal goals and aspirations: reflecting on what students want to get out of university and developing life goals.
- Career Development and CV-writing: developing your CV; drafting and layout; effective communication of skills. Building experience. Preparing for FE1s and King’ Inns. Options for careers in and outside of law. Opportunities at home and abroad. The importance of adaptability and flexibility. Best practice in completing application forms. Effective interview skills.
- Self-care and supports for students: an introduction to Student Services at MU. Stress management. Prioritising.
Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides an introduction to key concepts in the field of negotiation with a view to developing skills for engagement in collaborative negotiation.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.

This course is designed for students with no previous experience of studying law or business law and will compliment knowledge acquired by students in the study of other commercially focused areas. Students will attain skills for the acquisition and the evaluation of the relevant legal principles and their application to commercially orientated factual situations encountered. These skills will equip students with the capacity to resolve legal problems or identify efficient ways of resolving legal problems in a coherent and reasoned manner. Topics covered include employment law, business ethics, the court system of Ireland, intellectual property and the basics of contract and criminal law.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the fundamentals of business law and an ability to identify legal issues which may affect a business and account for the way in which law impacts on commercial life.

Examine the structure and function of the legal system and appreciate how commericial life is regulated, directly and indirectly, by that system, as well as giving examples of instances of the interaction of businesses with the legal system.
Comprehend the principles relating to the enforcement of bargains and company law and explain their operation in different circumstances.
Analyse, debate and criticise the law in a particular area of business/commercial life and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of commercial law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.

modules.moduleContent:
The focus of this course is on contract and tort. These areas constitute important pillars upon which private law is built. Effective practice in any legal discipline necessitates a thorough knowledge of these rules as well as how these two areas are intertwined. In addition to substantive knowledge, familiarity with and an ability to think critically about its theoretical underpinnings will be an important element to the course. 

The law of obligations is the collective term for the constituent sub-topics of contract, torts and restitution (unjust enrichment).  Obligations fall under the private law branch (as opposed to public law). Within private law there are proprietary and personal rights. This course focuses on the latter and guides students through the identification process of legal issues categorized under contract and torts.  The course approaches obligations from the perspective of a lawyer presented with a problem by a client. The legal questions posed centre on the protection of rights: against whom (if anyone) could a violation of rights be lodged; to what is the rights-holder entitled; from what events do rights arise?

Students who want to take this module must have completed LW151 and either LW152 or LW259.
learningOutcomes:
Effective Communication Skills
• Produce presentations and express oneself clearly in public
• Be able to listen to others

Critical Thinking
• Be aware of the human impact of one's decisions
• Evaluate the consequences of one’s activity on the environment
• Use the available management tools to resolve ethical conflicts
• Select and use relevant information
• Adapt one’s response according to the situation
• Demonstrate a degree of creativity in one's responses

modules.moduleContent:
Students who take this module will explore key theories related to crime and the criminal justice system from both an historical and contemporary perspective. Classical, biological, psychological and sociological explanations of criminal behaviour and criminal justice system responses to such behaviour will be explored.

On completion of this module students will have a comprehensive understanding of key ideological underpinnings related to criminal behaviour, and how such understandings influence state responses.
learningOutcomes: Display an in depth understanding of key criminological theories.
Understand the historical development of theoretical perspectives and their relevance to broader social change.
Critically evaluate the various explanations of crime with reference to criminological theories and the states responses.

modules.moduleContent:
The module introduces students to administrative law as a public law subject through discussion and analyses of administrative law theories and functions; the relationship between law and public administration; the examination and analysis of the principles of judicial oversight of administrative power and the examination and analysis of non-judicial tools of oversight of administrative power. 

The module lecturer has the discretion to change any aspect of this module may change in any given academic year. 
You must take Administrative Law in order to get the exemption for the King’s Inns (Barrister course). You do not need this module for the purposes of the Law Society (Solicitor course). 

 
Module Content:  

Theories and functions of administrative law  

Principles of judicial review (judicial oversight of administrative power). 

Principles of constitutional justice (natural and statutory justice). 

The use of discretionary power in public administration decision-making. 

The management of expectation (legitimate and non-legitimate) created by public bodies. 

Non-judicial oversight of administrative power (tribunals, the Ombudsman). 

Regulation of private activity through licensing.

Note: this module is offered in other programmes under code LW255 (Administrative Law in Ireland). Students are not permitted to register for both modules.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the function and theories of administrative law.
Outline and explain some key terms of administrative law.
Outline and discuss the key principles for identifying a decision subject/amenable to judicial review.
Enumerate and examine the grounds for challenging a public law decision.
Discuss and analyse relevant principles gleaned from judicial precedent.
Give a critique of the law in a particular area of administrative law and draw conclusions on its effectiveness and fairness.
Read a factual matrix, identify, summarise the administrative law issues involved and present an opinion.

modules.moduleContent:
This module primarily addresses the fundamental and personal rights guaranteed by the Constitution of Ireland. The scope of the module includes: the values underpinning the Constitution; the nature and theory of constitutional rights; the concept of unenumerated rights; and some discussion of select constitutional guarantees including the right to life, privacy and bodily integrity, equality, expression and personal liberty, family rights, education, property rights, religion, as well as the range of remedies available to litigants and the process of amending the Constitution.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the fundamentals of rights-based jurisprudence in the Irish Constitution and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to the existence of such rights and their enforcement.
Comprehend the principles relating to the recognition of constitutional rights, explain their interaction in different circumstances and distinguish the differing types of rights from one another.
Examine the enforceability of a constitutional right in a given factual matrix, discuss the remedies available to a litigant and predict the outcome of a judicial determination of the problem.
Analyse, debate and criticise the law in a particular area of constitutional law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of constitutional law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.
Develop the capacity to conduct independent legal research.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with a thorough grounding in international law. It introduces students to the legal rules of public international law that govern relationships between states, and examines the effects of globalisation on legal systems.
The module will trace the evolution of international legal rules and institutions, analysing core principles and doctrines such as sovereignty and recognition, studying international law’s origins and development over time, and detailing how international law is ‘made’ today. We will examine the ideological, political and economic factors that are at play in the process of law-making at the international level. We will look at who the subjects of public international law are, as well as the sources of law, in order to understand what rights and obligations exist under international law, for whom, and with what legal force. 
The ultimate aim of the module is to ground students in the theories and ideas that underpin the field of international law, and to help develop the tools and thinking necessary to understand and apply the principles and rules of law in practice, as well as to reflect critically on the possibilities and limits of international law in the face of war, environmental degradation, and socio-economic inequality.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate clear knowledge of the history, institutional structures, and conceptual underpinnings of  public international law;
display an in-depth understanding of the content and rules of public international law, and of the mechanics of their operation;
recognise when and how international law is relevant to contemporary issues; how it applies to global, regional, national and local situations;
critically evaluate the jurisprudence of relevant international courts and legal bodies, highlighting lacunae within the law and shortcomings in its implementation;
describe and reflect critically on key ideas and theories which dominate international legal  debates;
understand the ideological, geopolitical and material contestations at play in international law.

modules.moduleContent:
The module introduces students to the subject of policing and explores some of the main theories, concepts and debates in this field. It starts by introducing the key ideas behind policing, outlining the history of the police and investigating the breadth of the police’s role and the importance of police culture and discretion. We then discuss some of the main attempts to reform frontline policing in recent decades. Later lectures explore various themes and debates in contemporary policing, such as restorative policing, policing the drug trade, police pluralisation, police misconduct and accountability, and whether police officers should be routinely armed. Some lectures will be delivered by guest speakers who are either policing researchers or practitioners. Students will be expected to prepare for, attend and participate in three tutorials over the course of the module. 

The module does not focus entirely on Ireland and An Garda Síochána – instead, it draws on theoretical and empirical literature from a wide variety of countries, contexts and policing organisations in order to ensure that students develop a well-rounded understanding of policing around the globe.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse the police and policing activities in Ireland and elsewhere by applying relevant theories and concepts;
Understand the central importance of police discretion and police culture, and their implications for operational policing in practice;
Reflect on the values and practical implications of different policing philosophies and the heterogeneity of policing approaches;
Explain how misconduct, accountability and legitimacy concerns can affect policing;
Identify and reflect critically on some of the key issues that confront contemporary policing, as well as assess the implications for the police and policework;
Demonstrate written and verbal skills of argument and understanding of research methodologies.

modules.moduleContent:
This module broadly addresses the law as it relates to commerce. In particular, it concerns transactions for the sale and transfer of goods entered into by commercial and business entities. Building on Contract Law I and II, this module addresses the legal principles relating to the ownership, possession, transfer, and sale of personal property (particularly goods), the supply of services and the law of agency.

Students who want to take this module must have completed either LW152 or LW259.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse the law as it applies to commercial transactions involving goods and services, in particular the ownership, transfer, possession, and sale of goods. Identify and outline the law relating to domestic and international sale of goods, including the transfer of property, risk and title, and sources of contract terms.
Outline and critique the legal protections in the context of the sale of goods and the remedies of buyer and seller.
Analyse and evaluate the law relating to agency, the creation and termination of the agency relationship, the authority of the agent and relations between the principal, agent and third parties.
Recognise legal issues in a hypothetical fact scenario and apply legal principles and precedents to such a scenario, with a view to explaining a likely legal outcome or solution.

modules.moduleContent: Social harm – an act or omission which is clearly harmful but does not violate criminal law – is a focal concern of¿the field of critical criminology, and more recently, zemiology – a distinct but parallel field of study. In this module, students confront the ontological challenges of orthodox criminology and definitions of crime through the study of social harm. Students will engage critically with international and interdisciplinary subject matter using a wide variety of theoretical perspectives, including, Marxist, feminist, queer, decolonial, Indigenous, southern, rural, green, cultural, and abolitionist. Students learn how those in power shape the processes of criminalisation and victimisation through the conceptual lens of the crimes and harms of the powerful, and from within and outside the field of criminology. Crucially, this module addresses the fundamental question of why certain harms are legal and often escape criminalisation.
learningOutcomes: Explain the type and extent of harm caused by the powerful.
Explain, contrast, and integrate theory from the major theoretical perspectives.
Apply theory from the major theoretical perspectives to critically analyse contemporary case studies of harm.
Critically discuss the implications of the case studies being examined in responding to the crimes and/or harms of the powerful from within and outside of criminology and the criminal justice system.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces contemporary issues in the practice of legal interpretation, the rule of law, the relation of law and morals, the justification of punishment, and the nature of legal authority.¿ 
 
Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Identify key issues in the practice of statutory and constitutional interpretation.
Critique the leading justifications for the imposition of punishment.
Evaluate the proper relation of popular morality to legal normativity.
Assess leading theories of the nature of the rule of law.

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of the relationship between societal understandings of childhood and ideological underpinnings of the youth justice system. The module will commence with an introduction to this relationship with the objective of providing students with a firm foundation from which to understand the historical developments of youth justice systems. It will then move on to explore contemporary areas of youth justice from both a national and international perspective. 
The module will explore such areas as the historical development of youth justice, police and young people, migrant young people and the youth justice system, young people and victimisation, and cybercrime and young people.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a deep understanding of the historical development of youth justice and its impact upon contemporary systems.
Display a deep understandings of key contemporary issues in youth justice and their contextual relationship with historical developments.
Students will be supported in developing skills in identifying and reviewing literature, and critical analysis.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

learningOutcomes: Explain and critically assess the history, structure and operation of the WTO and its substantive and procedural rules;
Explain how WTO rules and institutions fit within their political, economic and historical context, with particular reference to the functions they play and the trade-offs they express;
Analyse specific cases (real or hypothetical) by applying relevant WTO rules, and advise on the implications of those rules and associated remedies in particular circumstances;
Explain the practical significance of WTO disciplines for a number of specific issues including environmental protection and economic development;
Use both clear and eloquent written language and clear and confident oral presentation to communicate analysis and advocacy points;
Use a variety of legal and non-legal secondary literature and other media to support analysis and advocacy points.

modules.moduleContent:
This module gives students the opportunity to develop their knowledge and understanding of the constitutional and substantive elements of EU law, building upon and further developing the study of EU law to which students were introduced in second year. Students should acquire an in-depth understanding of the Treaty on European Union and Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union; better understand the division of competences between the EU and its Member States; be able to identify and understand the legal principles underpinning the application, implementation and enforcement of the EU Treaties and of secondary law; and be able to evaluate critically the application of substantive EU law.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: * Demonstrate an in-depth understanding of core areas of EU law;
* Apply an advanced knowledge of EU law to real-world and hypothetical scenarios, demonstrating problem-solving skills;
* Locate and analyse primary and secondary source materials;

* Demonstrate the ability to pursue an argument, with proper care and attention to primary sources and to relevant academic literature, as well as the ability to synthesise material from a broad variety of sources;
* Demonstrate enhanced research skills, primarily library skills, but also use of databases and appropriate use of Internet resources.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The purpose of this course is to provide students with an in-depth understanding of the role which psychology can play in helping us to understand terrorist involvement. Within this course students will be assessing why people join, stay involved and ultimately leave terrorist groups. It will critically assess the psychological evidence for radicalisation and deradicalisation. This course will challenge students to consider whether or not the psychology of terrorist actors is any different from the psychology of non-terrorist actors. In doing so students will be introduced to concepts and theories from a variety of areas of psychology that may be applicable to understanding terrorist engagement.

Throughout this course there will be a respect for the heterogeneity of terrorist actors. The course will therefore question whether the psychology of group actors may be different to lone actors, similarly questioning the differences between the psychology of foreign fighters and home-grown terrorists. The course will also ask whether there is a difference in the psychology of terrorist actors based on the role that they occupy within a group or movement. A core element of this course will be a critical assessment of the role that mental health may or may not play in terrorist activity. Students will also find out about different risk assessment techniques that are used. This will be introduced to students within a segment of the course which will critically look at the role psychology can play in the development of effective counter-terrorism and countering violent extremism (CVE) strategies.

Module Content: The content of this module will constantly be reviewed. However, below are indicative lecture topics which could be covered:

1. Conceptual Debates in Terrorism
2. Terrorism Research
3. The Psychological Impact of Terrorism
4. The ‘Terrorist Mind’?
5. Psychology of Radicalisation
6. From Radicalisation to Recruitment
7. Being a Terrorist
8. Psychological Understanding of Suicide Terrorism
9. Gender and Terrorism 
10. Deradicalisation and/or Disengagement
11. Risk Assessment
12. Psychology of Risk Perception and Communication
learningOutcomes: Gain a critical understanding of the role which psychology plays in helping us to understand involvement in terrorism
Critically understand the psychological motivations for individuals to join terrorist groups
Critically understand the psychological motivations for individuals to leave terrorist groups
Critically understand the psychological motivations for individuals to remain involved in terrorist groups
Critically understand the role of psychological insight in countering terrorism
Gain a critical understanding of the role of mental health in terrorist involvement.

modules.moduleContent:
This module concerns the law of real property in Ireland, ownership of land and the rights and responsibilities that arise under it.  Students will be introduced to the concepts and the principles of property law, and the different estates or interests that can be held in land. In addition, the course will also include the ways in which interests in land are acquired, transferred and extinguished.  We will also look at the sources of land law, including the changes introduced by the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 (LCLRA 2009).

Note: this module is offered in other programmes under code LW352 (Land Law). Students are not permitted to register for both modules.
learningOutcomes: Understand the legal terminology employed in Irish real property law.
Identify and describe the different interests, which can be held in land and the features and rights of such interests.
Explain and comment upon the methods by which real property can be transferred in Ireland.
Comment upon the development of property law in Ireland and the manner in which it has been affected by extra-legal factors.

Critically analyse hypothetical legal problems, identify the legal issues arising and applicable principles, and present logical arguments as to the probable outcomes of the issues based on those principles.
Identify the sources of Irish Property law, in particular the seminal legislation.
Demonstrate an ability to conduct independent research and be able to gather and critique information from a variety of sources.

modules.moduleContent: The response to crime since the mid-1990s has been characterised in many developed countries by an emphasis on prison expansion. This module will seek to understand the complex role of the prison in contemporary society by exploring some of the policy dilemmas that confront prison systems everywhere. By examining contemporary penal policy debates including prisoners’ rights, students will develop a critical understanding of prison governance, law and policy in comparative perspective. Particular attention will be paid to prison conditions, penological regimes, international human rights standards and oversight mechanisms.
learningOutcomes: Interpret recent international trends in imprisonment
Understand the role that the contemporary prison plays in the criminal justice system
Demonstrate familiarity with the theoretical perspectives that have been used to understand the purpose of the prison
Compare prison systems in different jurisdictions
Evaluate a number of contemporary policy debates including prisoners’ rights; prison conditions; the imprisonment of women; life sentence prisoners; deaths in custody.

modules.moduleContent:
This module examines the law governing the formation, operation, governance, and dissolution of various types of company, together with the company’s internal and external relations with directors, shareholders, and creditors. Critical topics include: characteristics of a company, separate legal personality, limited liability, formation/incorporation, internal structures, directors' duties, treatment of shares, dissolution/winding up, liquidation, receivership, and examinerships. 
 
Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.

Students who have taken LW353 previously are not permitted to register for this module.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the law governing the operation, governance and dissolution of the company and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to the applicability of such laws in a given situation.
Comprehend the principal legal rules affecting the company and explain their operation in different circumstances, and upon different actors, such as directors, shareholders, and creditors.

Examine the applicability of the Companies Act 2014 (as amended) in a given factual matrix, apply relevant sections of the legislation and predict the outcome of a judicial determination of the problem.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of statutory principles and case law, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.
Analyse, debate and criticise particular area of company law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.

modules.moduleContent:
Introduction to Criminology will provide students with a comprehensive overview of key issues in criminology and criminal justice. It will allow for intellectual engagement with questions such as: 

* What is crime and why does it happen? 
* How do society and the state interpret and react to crime and deviance? 
* What is the purpose of punishment and how should it be used? 
* How might social status influence how we perceive and react to crime? 
* To what extent do existing approaches to criminal justice represent the most effective and appropriate mechanisms with which to respond to the harm caused by crime? 

The aim of this module is to provide students with an understanding of criminological thinking and research and, in particular, with knowledge about the operations and limitations of criminal justice processes and institutions. This module will require students to think philosophically, sociologically and empirically about the topics under study, promoting their intellectual development. The module does not focus entirely on Irish criminologists or the Irish criminal justice system – instead, it draws on theoretical and empirical literature from a wide variety of studies, countries and contexts in order to ensure that students develop a well-rounded understanding of criminology and criminal justice around the globe. 

Some lectures will also be delivered by guest speakers who are either criminological researchers or criminal justice practitioners. Students will be expected to prepare for, attend and participate actively in lectures over the course of the module.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse key concepts such as crime and punishment;
Critically discuss some of the key theories and philosophical approaches in the contemporary study of crime and criminal justice;
Demonstrate a broad knowledge and critical awareness of the operation of some of the principal institutions and actors within the criminal justice system.
Understand and reflect critically on the relative merits of how societies, governments and institutions frame and respond to criminal, deviant and harmful behaviour;
Write, think and speak in a reflective and critical manner which highlights intellectual engagement and understanding of how knowledge is constructed in criminology and criminal justice.

modules.moduleContent: This module examines the law governing the operation, governance, and dissolution of the private limited company, together with its internal and external relations with directors, shareholders, and creditors. The first part of the module will consider the nature and consequences of incorporation and the management of the company’s affairs by the directors. Then we will move on to focus on corporate governance issues including directors’ duties and ownership of companies. Finally, we will consider the winding up of companies.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the law governing the operation, governance and dissolution of the private limited company and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to the applicability of such laws in a given situation.
Comprehend the principal legal rules affecting the corporation and explain their operation in different circumstances, and upon different actors.
Examine the applicability of the Companies Acts in a given factual matrix, apply relevant sections of the legislation and predict the outcome of a judicial determination of the problem.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of statutory principles and case law, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.
Analyse, debate and criticise particular area of company law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will profile the diversity of sources and forms of regulation which underpin the provision of retail financial services in Ireland. It will highlight the role of the Central Bank of Ireland as the national regulatory and supervisory authority and will trace the increase of its competence and powers from its establishment to date.
