It will identify varying legal mechanisms through which the enforcement of financial services law and regulation is undertaken, including administrative sanctioning by the Central Bank, dispute resolution through the Financial Services & Pensions Ombudsman and private rights of action through the courts. It will analyse the legal dynamic between respective national and EU authorities by assessing the impact on the Central Bank of Ireland of the mandate given to the European Banking Authority (EBA) and the European Central Bank (ECB) in post financial crisis reforms.

(Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.)
learningOutcomes: Discuss the concept of regulation as it applies to financial services and identify the varying catalysts to reform of the legal framework in Ireland;

Analyse the changing role, competence, and powers of the Central Bank of Ireland from its establishment to date, and in particular, the contemporary enhancement of its standard setting and enforcement competence in response to events including the Irish banking sector crisis;

Identify the stimulus to the creation of an EU institutional framework for supervision of financial markets and services and assess the current roles of respective national and European authorities in financial services regulation;

Evaluate the legal form and content of the conduct of business requirements which are imposed in the provision of retail financial services to consumers and the public and private law mechanisms through which such provisions can be enforced.

modules.moduleContent:
This module examines the rules and institutions of the legal system of the People’s Republic of China (PRC) and explores fundamental concepts in the core areas of Chinese law. The content ranges from Chinese legal history, the Chinese legal system, the Chinese Constitution, the Civil Law in China, to Chinese approaches to international law. The aim of the module is to introduce the structures, institutions, and features of the Chinese legal system and improve understanding of Chinese law as compared with common-law systems. 
Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Identify and critique the key features of the Chinese legal system, with particular reference to the Chinese Constitution and civil law system;
Compare and contrast the Chinese legal system with common law systems;
Explain and evaluate Chinese approaches to international law;
Assess the policy rationales and philosophies underlying the development of Chinese Law;
Demonstrate an understanding of how Chinese laws operate in their broader social, cultural, and historical contexts.

Cé go mbeadh tionchar ag ábhar na dtráchtas ar na saintorthaí foghlama, bheifí ag súil go scríobhfadh formhór na mac léinn tráchtas a mbeadh gné phraiticiúil ag baint leis, seachas díriú ar an teoiric amháin. Chuirfí fáilte roimh thráchtais den chineál seo thíos go háirithe, ach ní bheifí teoranta dóibh:

aistriúchán a dhéanamh ar bhuntéacs Béarla, tráchtaireacht a scríobh ar na dúshláin a tháinig chun cinn, na féidearthachtaí agus na roghanna éagsúla a bhí ann, agus na cinntí a rinneadh le linn an aistriúcháin, agus moltaí a dhéanamh a chuideodh le haistritheoirí eile agus iad ag dul i ngleic le dúshláin den chineál céanna i dtéacs den chineál céanna;

comparáid a dhéanamh idir téacs foinseach agus spriocthéacs, aiste chritice a scríobh faoin aistriúchán ina mbeadh an bhéim ar láidreachtaí, laigí agus moltaí feabhais, agus moltaí a dhéanamh a chuideodh le haistritheoirí eile an dea-chleachtas atá le feiceáil san aistriúchán a chur i bhfeidhm iad féin, agus teacht slán ar na laigí atá le feiceáil ann;

eagarthóireacht a dhéanamh ar bhuntéacs Gaeilge, tráchtaireacht a scríobh ar na dúshláin a tháinig chun cinn, na féidearthachtaí agus na roghanna éagsúla a bhí ann, agus na cinntí a rinneadh le linn na heagarthóireachta, agus moltaí a dhéanamh a chuideodh le heagarthóirí eile agus iad ag dul i ngleic le dúshláin den chineál céanna i dtéacs den chineál céanna;

athchóiriú a dhéanamh ar sheantéacs Gaeilge chun é a chur in oiriúint do léitheoirí na linne seo, tráchtaireacht a scríobh ar na dúshláin a tháinig chun cinn, na féidearthachtaí agus na roghanna éagsúla a bhí ann, agus na cinntí a rinneadh le linn an athchóirithe, agus moltaí a dhéanamh a chuideodh le heagarthóirí eile agus iad ag dul i ngleic le dúshláin den chineál céanna.

modules.moduleName: THE CULTURAL HERITAGE OF EARLY IRISH SOCIETY
modules.moduleContent:
This is a foundation course in Irish Cultural Heritage Studies introducing students to a general understanding of early Irish society. Late prehistoric social organisation was controlled by a warrior aristocracy who ruled dispersed agricultural communities but with some evidence of emerging provincial political structures. Early medieval society was based around the family (fine, a complex multigenerational social and legal unit) and the tribe (about 150 examples) cradled in a number of important social systems including kinship, clientship, fosterage and formal concepts of honour. Students will be introduced to this period through multidisciplinary perspective including archaeology, history and literature that will include the role and status of women. Sources will include both historical documents (such as the Annals, Law Tracts, Genealogies, Hagiographies and a wealth of early literature) and archaeological data derived from modern excavation of many of residential and funerary sites.

Note: Students cannot take LS75 if they have already completed ID014.

This module seeks to introduce students to Irish constitutional law. It will examine the core principles of Irish constitutional law, with an emphasis on the powers and functions of the organs and agencies that constitute State power.

Module Content: 
Students will examine topics including: the doctrine of separation of powers focusing on the roles and functions of the legislature, judiciary, and executive organs of the State, constitutional interpretation, the principles governing constitutional litigation, international relations, as well as reform and amendment. The course will also examine the operation of the courts, the trial of offences in due course of law and examine how the constitution protects the right to personal liberty.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish between exercises of judicial, executive and legislative power,
Critically discuss the manner in which each organ of government supervises the other
Evaluate the constitutional framework for the State conducting international relations
Understand the principles governing constitutional litigation, as well as the justifications for them
Analyse the meaning of the constitutional guarantee that trials must be in due course of law
Understand the manner in which the constitution protects the right to personal liberty and evaluate the justifications for it

modules.moduleContent:
Students will develop a knowledge of the essential legal principles of torts including statutory negligence, vicarious liability, passing off, defamation and nuisance. They will also be able to advise on the appropriateness and desirability of different remedies depending on the case at hand. 
 
Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the fundamentals of torts such as nuisance and defamation, and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to the existence or non-existence of legal duties based on these torts.¿
Comprehend the principles relating to the statutory and strict liability torts, explain their operation on different circumstances and distinguish them from torts of negligence.¿
Recognise and identify the key elements which must be proved before any given action can successfully be maintained.¿
Analyse, debate and criticise the law in a particular area of torts and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.¿
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of tort law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.¿
Develop the capacity to conduct independent legal research.

modules.moduleContent:
This introductory module will teach students the basic skills required for the study of law. The course is applied in nature, and will cover topics such as reading cases and statutes, basic writing principles, professionalism and client communication skills, library and internet resources for researching law, methods for citing various legal sources, and exam-taking techniques, all of which are necessary to ensure success in the law program.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Read and synthesise the rules from cases.
Read and interpret a statute.
Communicate effectively, both orally and in writing.
Use library and internet resources proficiently to locate relevant legal sources when faced with a research project.
Cite various legal sources using the OSCOLA Ireland method.
Use information technologies proficiently, in particular, the use of email, word processing, spreadsheets, online databases, and the internet.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL SKILLS FOR NEW LAW STUDENTS
modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to some of the key professional skills that law students must develop if they wish to successfully pursue a professional career after graduation, especially if they are seeking a career in legal practice. Employers, and particularly law firms, demand graduates who are not only academically capable but who also have the skills that will allow them to work effectively within a professional environment. These professional skills often take time to develop, so this module will help students to start thinking early in their degrees about which skills they possess, which they do not, and what they can be doing to develop their skills.

*Please note, students should NOT take LW177 if they have previously taken LW154, as the content is the same in both modules *
learningOutcomes: Identify key professional skills and explain why they are important in the workplace.
Find accounts of professional skill development and critically assess how they apply to their own situation.
Reflect upon their own professional skills in order to identify how they could be developed.

modules.moduleContent:
This is an introduction to the German legal system, with a particular emphasis on the constitutional structures and values of Germany, its federal system, the court structure and legal system, the concept and operation of civil codes, and on the law of obligations.

This module will be taught through the medium of English.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Broadly discuss the key features of the constitutional structure of the relevant state, and of its courts and legal system, with particular reference to the concept of federalism.
Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the key principles underpinning the law of obligations in the relevant jurisdiction.
Outline and evaluate the main rules relating to the formation, terms and validity of contracts, and set out the rules governing breach of contract and remedies therefor.
Outline and evaluate the main rules relating to non-contractual obligations and liability for civil wrongs.
Apply the relevant law to unseen hypothetical scenarios.

modules.moduleContent:
This is an introduction to the Spanish legal system, with a particular emphasis on the constitutional structures and values of Spain, including its autonomous regions, the court structure and legal system, the concept and operation of civil codes, and on the law of obligations.

This module will be taught through the medium of English.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Broadly discuss the key features of the constitutional structure of the relevant state, and of its courts and legal system.
Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the key principles underpinning the law of obligations in the relevant jurisdiction.
Outline and evaluate the main rules relating to the formation, terms, and validity of contracts, and set out the rules governing breach of contract and remedies therefor.
Outline and evaluate the main rules relating to non-contractual obligations and liability for civil wrongs.
Apply the relevant law to unseen hypothetical scenarios.

modules.moduleName: BUNREACHT NA HÉIREANN AGUS AN GHAEILGE: CEARTA TEANGA AGUS BRÍ AN BHUNREACHTA
modules.moduleContent:
Students will be able to gain an understanding and critical appreciation of the Constitution of Ireland through the prism of the Irish language text of the document. The module will examine the status of the Irish and English languages as official languages, the constitutional position of Irish as first official language, the approaches of judges to the interpretation of the Constitution, and the role of the Irish language text in the interpretation of the Constitution. Emphasis will be placed on case law examining the right to use Irish in various official contexts.

This module will be taught through a mix of Irish and English.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.

modules.moduleName: NEW PERSPECTIVES ON LAW AND TECHNOLOGY
modules.moduleContent:
Digital technologies are revolutionizing the world we live in, thereby disrupting the legal profession, impacting how we communicate, do business and work in the public sphere. We are seeing the emergence of new ways of doing law, creating new types of legal jobs, with major investment in artificial intelligence replaces human beings in the workplace and beyond. By the end of the module, students will have acquired both intellectual understanding and practical skills to tackle the problems in the field of law and technology, and the differing approaches to their solutions.

This is a clinical law module. The module will provide an overview of the law of technology (TechLaw) and legal technology (LegalTech, LawTech & LegalOps) and will focus on innovation by addressing the legal principles and evolving regulation in technology and innovation, such as Blockchain, Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning, and Robotics, and will specifically focus on recent major changes in data privacy and GDPR, Intellectual Property, and surveillance technology. Students will develop their knowledge and skills in relation to client and stakeholder needs, including new challenges for legislators and regulators tackling global change. The course will also look at the legal issues of technology in the context of FinTech and provide insights into the role of risk and compliance in legal strategy. Students will be introduced to legal technology tools and will draw on their knowledge of technology and the legal tools to develop strategic plans, and how to offer a solution to a client.

The module will enhance career prospects in the legal profession, in regulatory bodies at the international and domestic level, third sector organisations, the industrial and service sector in Ireland and abroad. The module incorporates a Virtual Internship. 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse the law as it applies to specific areas of law & technology.
Basic understand of the role of technology in risk and compliance.
Understand the evolution and cutting edge of FinTech.
Outline and critique the legal dialogue with other disciplines.
Develop a strategic understanding of the use of legal technologies used in the practice of law and responses to legal disruption.
Have an understanding of how legal tools work and link this to client and stakeholder needs.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to give students an understanding of the law in action in the courts, by providing them with the opportunity to represent the parties to a hypothetical case, to prepare written arguments and to deliver those arguments by way of oral presentation to a Moot Court. By requiring active research, preparation of written memorials and presentation of oral submissions, the Moot Court programme develops students’ ability to construct cogent legal arguments and improves advocacy skills.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Understand the structure of legal argumentation and the use of legal precedents and other sources of authority.
Demonstrate competence in selecting from a range of complex materials to marshal arguments directed at attaining optimal outcomes.
Argue for their assigned clients.
Present written legal argument in a manner that is professional and persuasive.
Communicate effectively legal arguments both orally and in writing appropriately to the needs of a variety of audiences.
Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the principal methods of legal research.
Engage in academic debate in a professional manner and use a range of formats, such as oral presentation and word-processed documents, to present specialist material.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to the institutional frameworks that provides for human rights protections in Ireland, on an international, European and national level. Each human rights system will be explained, and students will learn how each system applies in Ireland. At an international level, the application of the UN human rights system in Ireland will be examined. At a regional level, the human rights sources and mechanisms within the European Union and the Council of Europe will be investigated and distinguished. At a national level, the constitutional and administrative framework will be explored. Importantly, the roles, functions and scope of each of these human rights systems will be differentiated from each other. The advantages and challenges of each system will be illustrated through a series of case studies on human rights issues that are particularly relevant in the Irish context, such as socio-economic rights, the right to life, Travellers’ rights, housing rights, and the right to a fair trial.
learningOutcomes: Identify and classify the institutional frameworks that protect the fundamental human rights of people in Ireland and differentiate these human rights systems from one another;
Critically assess the scope of each framework, its advantages and limitations;
Comprehend the principles of Irish fundamental human rights and distinguish the different categories of rights from one another;
Apply the principles of Irish human rights law to hypothetical legal problems and predict the outcome of a judicial determination of the problem;
Demonstrate and develop the capacity to conduct independent legal research.

modules.moduleContent:
This module broadly addresses the law as it relates to commerce. In particular, it concerns transactions for the sale and transfer of goods and the supply of credit entered into by commercial and business entities, including transactions involving consumers. Building on Contract Law I and II, this module addresses the legal principles relating to the ownership, possession, transfer, and sale of personal property (particularly goods), and the supply of services. The module places particular emphasis on consumer protection and the rules governing transactions between traders/service providers and consumers. The module also addresses the law relating to money and its variants (including negotiable instruments, bills of exchange, cheques, promissory notes), as well as the control of credit (particularly consumer credit and moneylending) and the rules relating to guarantees and sureties. It examines the rules concerning business partnerships and insurance contracts, and addresses and evaluates the concepts of bailment, hire purchase, leasing, and agency. The module emphasises throughout the legal implications of electronic transactions, e-commerce and distance sales. The module may also address aspects of the international sale of goods and the carriage of goods by sea.

Students who want to take this module must have completed either LW152 or LW259.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse the law as it applies to commercial transactions involving goods and services, particularly the ownership, transfer, possession, and sale of goods, and the supply of services.

Outline and critique the legal protection afforded to consumers in the context of the sale and leasing of goods, and the supply of services (with particular emphasis on EU law relating to unfair contract terms and distance sales).

Analyse and evaluate the law as it relates to money, bills of exchange, promissory notes, cheques and similar instruments, with particular emphasis on the control of credit, especially consumer credit, and the rules relating to guarantees, sureties, and indemnities.
Identify and evaluate the principal features of the law as it applies to business partnerships.
Assess the main principles of law underpinning the possession of goods, including bailment, leasing, hire purchase, and pawnbroking.
Outline and analyse the legal framework as it applies to insurance contracts.
Assess the law relating to the marketing of goods and services.
Demonstrate understanding of the need for a gendered lens of analysis in the discipline of criminology.
Explain the development of the inclusion of women in criminology, both historically and now, drawing on the emergence of feminist criminology.
Discuss the gendered patterns which emerge from a study of crime and punishment, particularly regarding victimisation and offending.
Critically examine the use of gendered punishment regimes.
Demonstrate familiarity with contemporary arguments on the role of masculinity in men’s offending behaviour.
Debate the role of the media in perpetuating gendered understandings of crime.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module focuses on certain aspects of criminal liability, particularly certain defences, accomplice liability, and inchoate liability. This module also examines other specific offences, such as offences against the state, and misuse of drugs offences
This modules builds on the foundational topics covered in LW157 Criminal Law. This module encourages students to develop an understanding of the doctrinal criminal law and the social and cultural contexts in which it operates.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the range of criminal law defences and specific offences covered, and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to their existence in a given set of facts.
Comprehend the principles relating to criminal law defences, and explain their operation on different circumstances.
Analyse, debate and criticise the law in a particular area of crime and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of criminal law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.
Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the foundations of criminal law.
Develop the capacity to conduct independent legal research.

modules.moduleContent:
This module examines the role of law within the medical context, considering issues at the interface of law, medicine, and ethics. It highlights ethical dilemmas which medical practitioners, lawyers, and policymakers face and explores how law can/should respond to such scenarios. It will examine contemporary challenges posed to medical law by, for instance, emerging technologies and societal changes. We will consider how the law should respond to these developments, the limitations of law in such respects, and divergence which can arise across national frameworks on such issues.

A range of topics will be explored, including, for example: the provision of healthcare in Ireland; regulation of medical practice in Ireland; the role of ethics in medical law; autonomy and consent in medical decision-making; medical confidentiality; mental health and mental capacity; health research; clinical negligence; organ donation and transplantation; genetics and the law; and contemporary challenges such as those related to assisted dying and reproductive medicine.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Identify and discuss the relevance of key legal and ethical principles to decision-making within the medical context;
Demonstrate knowledge of contemporary debates in medical law and ethics;
Assess the policy rationales underlying the development of laws in the areas discussed;
Identify and examine problems with current laws and suggest and evaluate reform proposals where relevant;
Demonstrate an understanding of the role of law in responding to emerging technologies or societal developments, and the challenges which arise;
Develop an understanding of how laws operate in their broader social and ethical contexts; and
Apply legal and ethical principles to medico-legal scenarios.

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED PERSPECTIVES ON LAW AND TECHNOLOGY
modules.moduleContent:
Digital technologies are revolutionizing the world we live in, thereby disrupting the legal profession, impacting how we communicate, do business and work in the public sphere. We are seeing the emergence of new ways of doing law, creating new types of legal jobs, with major investment in artificial intelligence replaces human beings in the workplace and beyond. By the end of the module, students will have acquired both intellectual understanding and practical skills to tackle the problems in the field of law and technology, and the differing approaches to their solutions. 
This is a clinical law module and builds on work in the previous module LW222. The module will focus on policy and regulation issues by addressing the legal principles and evolving regulation in technology and innovation. This module will give students insight into the policy level of law & technology issues. Students will draw on their understanding of the principles and policies to review the state of regulation, assess current and ongoing policy papers and proposals to understand the current state of the law, but also to look at where technology needs to develop and where new regulation is need. The focus will very much be on drafting issues in legislation through to issues in the outcomes of regulation and role of self-regulation, including use of regulatory sandboxes and legal incubators in legal innovation. This will include a deep dive into risk and compliance issues. Students will also be introduced to innovation design concepts and rudimentary principles of coding.

The class will undertake a project to create a White Paper on a chosen area of law & technology. Students will be introduced to legal technology tools and will draw on their knowledge of technology and the legal tools to develop strategic plans, and how to offer a solution to a client. Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
The module will enhance career prospects in the legal profession, in regulatory bodies at the international and domestic level, third sector organisations, the industrial and service sector in Ireland and abroad. The module incorporates a Virtual Internship. Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse the law and link it to policy work
Engage with the public and private sector in law & technology
Understand how the law and the profession innovate
Understand holistically RegTech and TechReg
Understand holistically technology, risk and compliance
Create a White Paper for public consultation
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to give students a thorough grounding in Irish property law, its origins and development. It includes an examination of the basic concepts of private ownership and the means by which interests in land can transfer. The will explore Irish property law in depth, as well as including an examination of specific areas of land law, will provide an introduction to landlord and tenant law and the law of succession.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.

Note: this module is offered in other programmes under code LW392 (Property Law). Students are not permitted to register for both modules.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of the basic concepts relating to private ownership and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to the existence or non-existence of property rights and the consequences of such a distinction.
Comprehend the principles relating to the enforcement of property rights, explain the conditions necessary for their existence and their operation in different circumstances.
Analyse, debate and criticise the law in a particular area of property law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Analyse a given factual matrix in light of property law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the key legal issues affecting traditional and new media. The module will begin by considering a number of fundamental themes that underpin the rest of the syllabus. The role of the media in democratic society (including conceptions of the “public interest”) and the rights jurisprudence will be examined.

The module covers topics such as defamation law, privacy law, freedom of expression, freedom of information law, copyright law, and the regulation of journalistic practices. 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Comprehend the role of the media in society and its value and to recognise and identify the key principles relating to freedom of expression jurisprudence.
Comprehend and explain the meaning of “the public interest” and the interpretations thereof.
Appreciate the balance that must be struck between “the public interest” and other competing interests such as reputation, privacy, religious belief etc.
Comprehend the key elements of the tort of defamation, the law of privacy, copyright law, and freedom of information law.
Examine the laws that relate to news gathering practices such as data protection, freedom of information etc.
Critically analyse the debates that surround the balance between private interests and media interests, such as reputation and privacy.
Understand and evaluate the relevant regulatory structures.

modules.moduleContent:
This module offers final year Criminology students an opportunity to complete, by invitation, a meaningful, focussed piece of research or other academic work in the fields of Criminology or Criminal Justice in conjunction with or on behalf of a member of academic staff. This may include, for instance, meaningful research support to an academic staff member, completion of a submission for a public consultation or similar exercise, work on behalf of or for submission to an NGO or advocacy group, or meaningful data collection and analysis under the direction of an academic staff member. In all cases, an academic staff member will supervise the work. 
The purpose of this module is to build relevant research skills, data gathering skills, and writing skills by completing research on a topic of relevance in the fields of Criminology or Criminal Justice. The work in question should involve meaningful research providing a substantive learning experience.
Entry to this module is strictly by invitation only following an application process. One or more academic staff member(s) may put forward a research proposal(s) for which Year 3 Criminology students with a 2.1 or higher result in year 2 Criminology will be invited to apply.
Formal entry to and registration on this module are by invitation only (students cannot automatically enrol and will need to be approved by an academic staff member in advance of registration.) Admission to the module is conditional on the approval and agreement of an academic staff member.

Module Content: 
This module offers final year Criminology students an opportunity to complete a meaningful, focussed piece of research or other academic work in the fields of Criminology or Criminal Justice. This will be done in conjunction with or on behalf of a member of academic staff. This may include, for instance, meaningful research support to an academic staff member, completion of a submission for a public consultation or similar exercise, work on behalf of or for submission to a public body, an NGO or advocacy group, or meaningful data collection and analysis under the direction of an academic staff member. In all cases, an academic staff member will supervise the work. The work in question should involve meaningful research providing a substantive learning experience.
Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module co-ordinator.

**LW370 is not available at online registration. Please contact the School of Law and Criminology for registration details**
learningOutcomes: Conduct legal research under academic direction.
Gather relevant data for research purposes.
Write a research paper or submission on a topic of relevance.
Work with academics and other professionals in a cooperative and timely manner.
Work to deadlines.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Victimology will provide students with a comprehensive overview of key issues in the study of victims of crime and victimisation. It will allow for intellectual engagement with questions such as:  
         -How does society understand victimisation and apply the label of ‘victim’? 
         -How is victimisation distributed in modern societies? 
         -To what extent does the criminal justice system satisfy the needs and interests of victims of crime? 
         -What rights and services are available to victims of crime? 
         -How else might we respond to the harms caused by victimisation? 
         -How might we apply victimological concepts to those affected by broader social harms? 

The aim of this module is to provide students with an understanding of victimological thinking and research and with knowledge about the status of victims in modern societies and criminal justice processes. This module will require students to think philosophically, sociologically and empirically about the topics under study, promoting their intellectual development. The module does not focus entirely on the Irish criminal justice system – rather, it draws on theoretical and empirical literature from a variety of countries and contexts in order to ensure that students leave with a well-rounded understanding of victimisation and victimology. 

Some lectures will be delivered by guest speakers. Students will be expected to prepare for, attend and participate actively in lectures over the course of the module.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse key concepts and issues in victimology
Critically discuss some of the key theories and philosophical approaches in the contemporary study of victims and victimisation
Demonstrate a broad knowledge and critical awareness of the ways in which society and the criminal justice system respond to victims of crime
Understand and reflect on the ways in which the label of ‘victim’ is defined and applied in modern societies
Write, think and speak in a reflective and critical manner which highlights intellectual engagement and understanding of how knowledge is constructed in victimology

modules.moduleContent: The purpose of this module is to understand why the United States of America came to have the largest known prison population in the world.  It introduces students to the subjects of mass incarceration and the carceral state.  The course begins by defining these concepts and describing the ways that the US has become an international outlier in terms of criminal justice practice.  The course will then explore theories and research from criminology, sociology, and political science that seek to explain how and why mass incarceration came to be.  Particular attention will be paid to the relationships between criminal justice, politics, and race relations in the US over the past 50 years.  The course will explore the ways that policing, sentencing, and punishment (both incarceration and community supervision) are all related to the carceral state.  The primary focus of this course will be on the US, but comparisons will be made to Ireland and other European nations throughout the semester.

Environmental protection is one of the most significant issues of our time. It faces considerable challenges, e.g. through biodiversity loss and climate change, as well as being affected by increased human populations, war and disease. Addressing these is a highly complex matter and engages with numerous disciplines.

This module seeks to introduce students to some of the issues and challenges, focussing on  legal structures, regimes and toolkits. It considers the underpinnings of environmental law and governance, as well as its multilevel nature and the importance of EU and international law within Ireland. It will then typically engage with two or three substantive regimes, e.g. nature conservation, water pollution, waste or climate change (this may be considered through examining issues such as agriculture, where these regimes overlap and interact). The module will also consider recent developments, e.g. the climate cases, the role of climate strikes, Brexit etc. 

The precise content of the module is subject to change and may vary, including from year to year.

learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of the Offences Against the State regime and the challenges taken thereto;
Present a critical analysis of the measures taken to protect national security in response to subversive activity;
Critically assess the use of special powers and special evidential provisions used in the context of subversive activity;

Discuss the conflict of interests in the context of the protection of national security and the protection of fundamental rights, including the right to a fair trial, the right to cross-examination, the right to privacy, the right to silence.

modules.moduleContent:
Tax is a fundamental part of our social and political systems. It is the way that our governments obtain the resources to advance whatever projects they may have. As such, it is what makes politics (and particularly democratic politics) possible. Without tax, there would be no need to argue about public education, or transport, or housing. Without tax, there would be no law and order, no courts, no policing. Tax is the foundation on which the modern state, the legal system and the political order is built. 

 

Tax is central to how our society determines who wins and who loses, economically and politically. The tax system plays an essential role in addressing economic inequalities – or leaving those inequalities unaddressed. Tax is thus closely tied up with important philosophical questions, about equality and social justice. We impose different taxes on different people, depending on their income, their wealth, their family status, their choices about work and leisure activities. Each of these tax differences reflects a value judgment, and a contestable political choice to impose burdens on some, and relieve burdens on others.  

 

The tax system is also a key tool through which, as a society, we express approval or disapproval of particular behaviours, and indeed seek to change those behaviours. This is a function of many other parts of the law also, perhaps most obviously the criminal law. However the criminal law is a blunt tool for addressing many behaviours that we might want to modify, so we turn to tax for a more nuanced regulatory response. Think about the high taxes imposed on cigarettes and fuel-inefficient vehicles, or the tax reliefs for pension contributions and nursing care. Through imposing or relieving taxes, we make particular options more or less attractive, and thereby (hopefully) lead some people to choose the options that we, as a society, prefer. 

 

Tax also plays a central role in managing the wider economy: ideally helping to ‘smooth’ demand through the cycle of economic boom and bust; in practice often exacerbating it as politicians compete to offer the biggest tax cuts to voters. We rely on tax to ensure our international competitiveness, offering low corporation tax rates and (perhaps?) sweetheart deals to international businesses to attract inward investment. In so doing, we face challenges from other countries, who perceive our tax policies as unfairly undermining their tax bases, ‘stealing’ their taxes and/or their businesses. 

 

Perhaps the most important reason to think about tax law, however, is that tax is everywhere. More than criminal law, or constitutional law, or even employment law, tax law has pervasive impacts on our daily lives. Very few of us have been arrested or prosecuted, but we have all paid taxes. The state has never restrained my physical liberty, but it picks my pocket on a daily basis. Tax is a – perhaps the – key relationship that we all have with the states under which we live. It is therefore important to think critically about the laws through which that relationship is expressed, about how they work, why they are the way they are, and how they might be different. That is what we are hoping to do in this module. 

 
Module Content: Topics covered in any given year may include: 

Interdisciplinary perspectives on tax law: efficiency, fairness, stability 

Institutional aspects of tax law: the making of fiscal policy and the interpretation of tax statutes 

Income tax and progressivity: taxation and inequality 

Taxation and the family: feminist perspectives on income tax 

Corporation Tax 

Taxing Capital and Taxing Wealth 

Value Added Tax 

Environmental Taxes: Using taxes to pursue social policy 

International Taxation: the challenge of international tax avoidance 

 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Explain and critically assess the history, structure and operation of Irish tax law and its substantive and procedural rules;
Explain how tax laws and institutions fit within their political, economic and historical context, with particular reference to the functions they play and the trade-offs they express;
Analyse specific cases (real or hypothetical) by applying relevant tax rules, and advise on the implications of those rules and associated remedies in particular circumstances;
Explain the practical significance of tax laws for a number of specific issues including inter alia environmental protection, gender equality, economic justice and international development;
Use both clear and eloquent written language and clear and confident oral presentation to communicate analysis and advocacy points;
Use a variety of legal and non-legal secondary literature and other media to support analysis and advocacy points.

modules.moduleContent:
Intellectual property is concerned with creations of the mind, including songs, books, inventions, and logos. Intellectual property rights grant the owner an exclusive right which allows them to prevent others from using or copying these creations. Intellectual property laws attempt to balance these exclusive rights with the benefits that these creations provide to society.

Continuously, advancements in technology have raised difficult questions around this balance in all fields of intellectual property. Whether the current systems can and should adapt because of these developments is a crucial question.

In this module, you will engage with and critically analyse key advanced debates in the field of patents, trade marks and copyright law. For example, in patent law, we will encounter debates on access to medicines. In trade mark law, we will investigate the question of NFTs and trade mark infringement in the Metaverse. In copyright law, we will consider the impact of developments in artificial intelligence. These debates, as well as other advanced issues in intellectual property law, will challenge you to reflect critically on the regulation of creation, and whether the law can adapt to keep pace.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate deep and systematic knowledge and understanding of the law relating to intellectual property rights
Undertake critical evaluation, comparing and analysing the principles, rules and theories relating to the protection of intellectual property rights
Engage with and critically analyse key advanced debates in the field of patents, trade marks and copyright law
Demonstrate effective written communication skills, work independently and effectively and manage time efficiently in preparing for scheduled learning activities and assessments
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed as a capstone module for students taking year 4 LLB. It aims to synthesise learning from throughout the LLB degree by challenging students to avoid and address complex, multi-faceted legal problems in a systematic manner, considering, in particular, approaches that avoid unnecessary litigation such as careful drafting of contracts and other legal documents, mediation, arbitration, and conciliation. It proceeds using case studies to examine how common legal problems can be best avoided and effectively addressed. These case studies seek to draw together and synthesise module content studied in previous years in a manner that acknowledges that legal problems rarely fit squarely into one sub-disciplinary area.  The module also introduces students to the rudiments of civil procedure, introducing students to the drafting of documents such as affidavits, statutory declarations, civil bills, entering an appearance or delivering a defence, and the plenary summons. 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Advise on the operation and appropriateness of litigation and alternatives to litigation and examine their legal basis
Advise effectively on techniques used to avoid legal problems
Offer effective, nuances solutions to complex legal problems
Draft a contract addressing a complex legal situation
Draft documents such as an affidavit or civil bill based on a complex legal situation.

modules.moduleContent:
This module gives students the opportunity to develop their knowledge and understanding of the current constitutional and substantive elements of EU law, building upon and further developing the study of EU law to which students were introduced in second year and in the advanced EU law module in third year. Students should acquire an appreciation for legal issues that are at the cutting-edge of the development of EU law. In doing so, students will acquire a deep understanding of selected aspects of EU primary and secondary law. They will enhance their critical reasoning skills. Students will further learn to work in a legal environment where legal doctrine is not yet settled or currently being unsettled. Some of the topics under discussion will therefore require students to work with primary sources and little academic commentary, but rather force them to think and reason on their own.

It lies in the very nature of a current issues course that the topics covered in any given academic year may vary. Equally, any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.

Indicative topic list:

1. The primacy of EU law and national constitutional courts

2. ‘The democratic deficit’ and Lisbon Treaty reforms

3. EU migration and asylum law

4. Mutual recognition in the Area of Freedom Security and Justice

5. EU citizenship law: latest developments

6. The rule of law crisis

7. Governance of the Eurozone

8. Brexit and the Law I

9. Brexit and the Law II

10. Brexit and the Law III

11. What is the future of EU integration?
learningOutcomes: * Demonstrate an awareness and knowledge of contemporary debates in EU law;
* Appreciate the complexity of some of the legal questions currently facing the European Union;
* Assess the policy rationales and political background underlying current developments in EU law;
* Identify and examine problems with EU law as it currently stands suggest and evaluate reform proposals where relevant;
* Apply an advanced knowledge of EU law to real-world and hypothetical scenarios, demonstrating problem-solving skills;
* Locate and analyse primary and secondary source materials, including very recent legal developments independently of existing academic commentary;

* Demonstrate the ability to pursue an argument, with proper care and attention to primary sources and to relevant academic literature, as well as the ability to synthesise material from a broad variety of sources;

modules.moduleContent:
This module offers students a broad engagement with questions of how race and racism interact with law.

The module begins with an overview of the concept of race, the processes of racialisation, and the societal, institutional and material manifestations of racism. It traces the origins of race-thinking and racism in legal systems from the structures of colonialism and empire to segregation and apartheid, through to 21st century articulations of race and racial discrimination. We look at how law has on the one hand been implicated in continuing forms of racial stratification and material inequality, while on the other hand it is mobilised (from above by the state; from below by social movements) to pursue aims ranging from formal equality and affirmative action to reparations and abolition.
The legal frameworks for this module are domestic, comparative and international. We study how inequality and racial justice claims have played out in legal systems in specific jurisdictions in other parts of the world. We then examine some of the particularities of how race is a factor in the Irish legal context – including in relation to constitutional status and citizenship laws; migration, refuge and direct provision; labour and human rights; criminal justice and policing; and Mincéirí/Traveller rights. We will reflect on the commonalities and differences between Ireland and other jurisdictions. We also look at international law: how it has been complicit historically with white supremacy, what it now says and does about race and racial discrimination, and what legal tools and sites are available to anti-racist movements.
The module adopts a broadly socio-legal approach: studying the law in its social context, using an interdisciplinary lens to analyse the relationship between legal phenomena and wider society, and listening to the realities of how people experience the law ‘in action’ in their everyday lives. 

Students will be asked to engage with a range of different materials through the module including social science literature, legal sources and case law, film, music and podcasts, and contributions from guest speakers.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a solid understanding of the concepts of race, racism and racialisation in socio-legal and political context;
Contextualise the history and evolution of the relationship between law and race;
Understand and explain some of the key ways that race and racism impact on and interact with the legal system in particular jurisdictions;
Recognise when and how race and racism are implicitly or explicitly relevant to contemporary legal issues in Ireland, and analyse how this compares to other national and regional contexts;
Critically evaluate legal sources and materials as they relate to race and racism
Highlight the successes and shortcomings to date of legal mechanisms aimed at combatting individual, societal and structural racism;
Identify the key instruments and institutions of international law that are relevant to race and racial discrimination;

modules.moduleContent: Transactional skills are becoming more and more important in the consultant’s day-to-day activity. The course, through an immersive experience, helps the students practicing those skills in a scenario of the negotiation of a commercial contract. Students will be divided in two groups, one for each party of the commercial contract. They will work with a colleague, but also interact with another team of students acting on the side of the counterpart. They will also have some interaction with US law students who will play the roles of the clients in the transaction.

This module explores the substantive rights, legal procedures and institutional mechanisms that seek to protect economic, social & cultural rights in both domestic legal systems and international human rights law. What are economic, social & cultural rights, and on what terms are they justified? Does every individual hold, for example, a right to a house, or a right to a free education, or a right to a constant supply of water? Are such things ‘legal’ rights, in a judicially enforceable sense, or social aspirations whose availability inevitably oscillates with the politics of the day and the whims of the market? What relevance do budget allocation and public finance decisions have as sites for the ‘progressive realisation’ of economic, social & cultural rights? What is the relationship between economic development and socio-economic rights? What does the pervasiveness of models of neo-liberalism and austerity mean for socio-economic rights? What are the mechanisms for claiming economic, social and cultural rights? To what extent, if any, can they be claimed collectively? 

The module will examine state obligations in this context under international human rights law. It will analyse the extent to which economic, social & cultural rights are protected in domestic legal systems, and will review relevant jurisprudence from comparative jurisdictions. The challenges for socio-economic rights advocates in redressing domestic and global inequalities remain substantial. This module aims to help students to understand and analyse the roots and scale of these challenges, and to develop the tools and thinking necessary to tackle them.
learningOutcomes: understand the content of economic, social & cultural rights, and the workings of the relevant legal frameworks at domestic, constitutional, regional and international level;
engage with legal, policy and economic arguments with respect to economic, social & cultural rights;
critically evaluate the economic, social & cultural rights regime under domestic and international law and jurisprudence, highlighting lacunae within the law and barriers to progress;
understand the ideological contestations in human rights discourse vis-à-vis economic, social & cultural rights.

modules.moduleContent:
Throughout history, the protection of privacy has faced an ongoing challenge to keep pace with developments in technology. Never has this challenge been more apparent than in the current digital age where technological innovation is constant and the accumulation of electronic information is massive. Accordingly, appreciation of the technological context is an essential aspect of this course.

The growth of the internet has led to a certain globalisation of privacy issues that poses both opportunities and threats for the protection of privacy. With the lack of jurisdictional boundaries online, internet companies can be compelled to comply with local privacy and data protection regulations worldwide. Differences in regional understandings of privacy remain however, and the study of different approaches to the protection of privacy can enable better understanding of privacy and its role in society.       

Several different fields of law are relevant to the issue of privacy and suitable topics have been selected to explore how privacy operates in these distinct contexts. Topics to be studied include: 
(1) The foundations and history of the right to privacy: US, UK, Irish, and European approaches 
(2) Data Protection and the global reach of privacy laws
(3) Developments in technology: a comparison of US and European approaches
(4) Government surveillance
(5) Privacy and the Press 
(6) Social networks and privacy
(7) Access to Information Law

Questions of policy are central to the course and students are encouraged to consider the political, economic, and technological issues that inevitably surround the regulation of privacy issues. Critical engagement with the debate taking place in both the academic literature and the general press will be encouraged through assigned readings.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Critically discuss the foundations and development of the right to privacy globally.
Identify the key challenges to the protection of privacy within the international political and technological context.
Demonstrate detailed knowledge and understanding of the various fields of law where privacy rights are relevant.
Analyse, debate and critically evaluate relevant legal materials and draw supported conclusions.
Develop the capacity to conduct independent legal research.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: INTERNATIONAL AND EUROPEAN DISABILITY LAW AND POLICY
modules.moduleContent:
The module introduces students to International and European disability law and policy, focusing on the significant changes resulted from the entry into force of the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD). The course equips the students with the necessary theoretical and conceptual background on disability, and focuses on the perspectives and challenges purported by the social model of disability as opposed to the medical model. It examines the development of international standards on disability and analyses the UNCRPD in light of the decisions of the UN Committee on the rights of people with disabilities. Attention will be paid to the national monitoring system envisaged by the UNCRPD and to its implementation in selected States and in the European Union. The module covers the Council of Europe’s action in the field of disability, and discusses relevant decisions of the European Court on Human Rights. It also analyses the EU disability law and policy, paying attention to the role of the Court of Justice in endorsing the rights of persons with disabilities. Since the UNCRPD is the first human rights treaty that the EU has concluded, the module will critically assess the interaction between the Convention and EU legislation, as well as the actual implementation of the UNCRPD at the EU level. 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer. 

Module Content:  

What is Disability? 

The models of disability  

The social model and its critique 

The development of International Standards on Disability 

The UNCRPD  

Rationale and Structure 

Art. 33 CRPD and the national monitoring system in a nutshell: challenges and perspectives 

The Optional Protocol 

Accessibility in the UNCRPD 

Equality and Non-Discrimination in the UNCRPD 

Legal Capacity in the UNCRPD 

The role of the Council of Europe in promoting the Rights of persons with disabilities 

The Council of Europe Disability Policies 

Disability in the European Social Charter  

The European Convention on Human Rights and the Rights of persons with Disabilities 

 The Rights of Persons with Disabilities in the EU legal framework 

EU disability policies 

Directive 2000/78 and related case law of the Court of Justice 

The European Accessibility Act
learningOutcomes: Describe and assess key theories on disability
Outline and discuss the rationale, the structure, the general obligations laid down in the UNCRPD
Critically assess the monitoring system envisaged by the UNCRPD
Evaluate the role of the Council of Europe in shaping a European disability policy
Outline and discuss EU disability law and critically assess the implementation of the UNCRPD in the EU

modules.moduleContent: The module provides an analysis of development and evolution of public international law over time. It focuses on the sources of public international law, as set out in the Statute of the International Court of Justice, and how international law is ‘made’. It provides an analysis of theories of international law and the relationship between international law and international relations. It analyses subjects of international law and provides a discussion of the remedies which are available when international law is breached. Specific branches of international law are dealt with in detail, including human rights, environmental law etc and students will be able to appreciate how international law applies to specific scenarios.
learningOutcomes: Describe and critically assess the main principles and theories of public international law;
Identify and analyse sources of public international law;
Evaluate the application of the principles of public international law to specific scenarios;
Appreciate the subjects of international law and their rights and obligations;
Assess the remedies available for violations of public international law;
Evaluate the relationship between public international law and international relations.

modules.moduleContent:
As far as accounting is generally considered as the language of business, understanding this language is of strategical importance for business players, especially at the international level. 

The course is a presentation of the two most used accounting standards systems: the United States Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (US GAAP) and the International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS). Continuous assessment and a written exam at the University.

Module objectives: Students will learn about the issues and legal solutions pertaining to cyber law, especially for dealing with legal opinions and pleadings. The main objective of this course is to teach students the specific legal terminology and systems of e-commerce regulation. Assessment will be by written exam at the University.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Module objectives: The aim of this course is to provide students with a general understanding of what “Legal Skills” are.

Module content: Legal Skills encompass the management of clients and lawyers’ relationships. Legal Skills include legal drafting, interviewing skills and office management. In addition, we will also be looking at the drafting of legal documents (i.e. letters, pleadings and contracts…).

This course will use practical examples and genuine documents as teaching aids. We will also consider drafting recommendations and drafting tips. At the end of this course, students will have a better understanding of all the various facets that are included in legal skills.

Penal policy across diverse jurisdictions provides an opportunity for comparison and the identification of differences and similarities in the development of policy and in the evolution and implementation of penal practices. The purpose of this module is to permit the exploration of penal policy in two distinct national contexts: Canada and Ireland. Both countries have been influenced by their colonial inheritances and their cultural connections to large and influential geographic neighbours. Yet both have evolved separate and distinct approaches to penal policy. Various influences and trends can be identified as having an impact such as risk, penal populism, mass incarceration and the legacy of the ‘rehabilitative ideal.’ Specific examples of penal policy and practices with respect to indigenous/ethnic minorities, women, young persons, domestic violence offenders and sex offenders will be considered. Comparisons will be drawn where appropriate with other countries in Europe, as well as the USA, the UK and also Australia and New Zealand.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate detailed knowledge of data sources as they relate to the study of penal policy in comparative perspective
Demonstrate an understanding of the comparative process in the critical analysis of penal policy in national and sub-national contexts
Demonstrate awareness of the connections between penal policy and penal practices

Identify and evaluate key debates on specific penal policy issues and practices with, for example, indigenous/ethnic minorities, female offenders, young offenders, sex offenders and domestic violence offenders

modules.moduleContent:
Restorative Justice will provide postgraduate students with a comprehensive and advanced overview of key issues in restorative justice theory, research and practice. It will involve a deep intellectual engagement with questions such as: 

* What is restorative justice? 
* To what extent does the concept differ from other justice ideals? 
* How can restorative justice be used in criminal justice in practice? 
* To what extent might restorative justice remedy the limitations of Western criminal justice systems? 
* What does empirical research indicate about the application of restorative justice? 
* What is the current status of restorative justice around the world? 
* To what extent could restorative justice support cultural change in criminal justice? 

The aim of this module is to provide students with an understanding of restorative justice thinking and research and with knowledge about its use, potential benefits for stakeholders and practical limitations in the criminal justice context. It will require students to think both philosophically and practically about the topics under study, promoting their intellectual development. The module draws on theoretical and empirical literature from a variety of fields and countries in order to ensure that students leave with a well-rounded understanding of restorative justice. It will also involve participation in restorative pedagogical methods so that students can experience restorative principles in practice. 

Some lectures will also be delivered by guest speakers. Students will be expected to prepare for, attend and participate actively in classes over the course of the module.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse key concepts and issues in restorative justice;
Critically discuss some of the key theories and philosophical approaches in the contemporary study and development of restorative justice;
Demonstrate a broad knowledge and critical awareness of the potential benefits and limitations of restorative justice within the criminal justice system;

Understand and reflect on the empirical study of restorative justice, focusing in particular on its impact on participants and its application with different crimes and at different points in the criminal justice process;
Write, think and speak in a reflective and critical manner which highlights intellectual engagement and understanding of how knowledge is constructed in restorative justice.

modules.moduleContent: The module focuses on enhancing LLM and MA students’ research and analytical skills and helps to prepare them for writing an LLM or MA dissertation. It also gives them the opportunity to engage with current legal and criminological issues through seminars given by guest speakers. In addition, the module helps to prepare students for life after the LLM or MA programme by providing sessions on further research options and careers in legal, criminological and cognate fields.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of legal and criminological research skills.
Complete a research proposal on the topic on which they will complete their LLM or MA dissertation.
Examine the options available to graduates of the LLM and MA programme.
Discuss current events in a variety of legal and criminological spheres.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will introduce students to the theoretical and practical concepts underpinning quantitative and qualitative research methods. In doing so, it will provide students with the skills required to prepare a research proposal;conduct a literature review; complete an application for ethical approval;select and/or develop data collection tools;conduct data analysis;and disseminate findings. This module utilises lecture-style presentation, video content, guest speakersand practicalcomputer-basedworkshops. 

Module Content:
Unit 1: An introduction to research.
Unit 2: Designing research.
Unit 3: Conducting a literature review.
Unit 4: Research ethics.
Unit 5: Quantitative analysis(SPSS data entry; SPSS data coding; t-tests; correlations).
Unit 6: Qualitative analysis.
Unit 7:Mixed methods research.
learningOutcomes: Following this module, students will be well placed to complete their research dissertations.Students will be able to design a research project; conduct a literature review, synthesising current research and policy documents; complete a research ethics application; conduct quantitative analysis using SPSS; carry out thematic analysison qualitative data.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: This module aims to provide postgraduate research students with an understanding of the research designs commonly employed in legal research.  

Module Content: This module consist of a series of one-day events (or one-week intensive module depending on the number of students enrolled) which introduce students to various methodological approaches to the study of law. It requires students to consider in-depth the issue of research methodology and the factors that influence the choice of research methods. It combines both a theoretical and a practical approach to learning, which enables students to develop the research skills that are essential for completing their PhD (or M.Litt.).
Topics include (but are not limited to): 
• Introduction to legal research methodologies 
• Jurisprudence and legal research 
• Research design 
• Socio-legal research methods 
• Comparative legal research 
Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Outline and critically analyse key legal research methodologies
Identify appropriate research methods for different kinds of legal research
Construct coherent legal research proposals
Employ a range of legal research methods

modules.moduleContent: This module will examine a range of social, cultural and political issues of importance in contemporary Ireland and the ways in which these are reflected on television viewed in Ireland. It will focus upon the ways in which these reflect underlying ideologies and values, as well as identifying signs of social and cultural change. The module will also explore the ways in which Irish social and political issues might be explored in a variety of media forms.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain a range of key social, cultural and political issues in contemporary Ireland and their relationship to lived experience in contemporary Ireland
Generate original and insightful discussions of Irish society and politics which would contribute to public discourse
Critically interrogate the role of media in Irish political culture
Reflect ethically upon the relationship between their lived experience and political, social, economic and media structures.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will teach students basic web-based technologies such as html, CSS and JavaScript in order to create an interactive story. Students will be introduced to the impact of the digital apparatus on the standard narrative form and the resulting implications of this on human communication and creative expression. Students will critically examine contemporary examples of interactive storytelling and will then use a web-based tool such as Twine https://twinery.org/ to tell an interactive non-linear story.

Please note: MD228A and MD233 cannot be taken together.
learningOutcomes: Conceptualise, design, and produce a web-based interactive story for a specific target audience.
Solve coding problems.
Reflect critically upon interactive storytelling processes and practices .
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: In this capstone module, students will conceptualise, plan, pitch, and create a digital media practice project based on an existing or new concept. They will be required to research, conceptualise, scope, plan and create an interactive project for a specific target audience. Students will work in teams, allowing individual students to develop their own areas of capacity or interest within digital media practice. By maintaining a log of production decisions, students will also reflect upon their practice.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate skills in project management of an interactive digital media project.
Analyse and critique existing contemporary media practice.
Conceptualise, design, plan and create a digital media project.
Work independently and collaboratively to a production deadline.
Reflect critically on project development processes and practices.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module will allow the students to conduct independent research and creative practice on a topic chosen and produced in consultation with an academic supervisor. The research topic and the format of the dissertation/project will be relevant to areas the student has pursued in taught components of the MA programme. Students will produce either a written or practice-based thesis. The written dissertation should be 12,000 to 16,000 words and the practice thesis should consist of the artefact or performance/installation with an accompanying 6,000-8,000 word dissertation serving as a critical and theorised analysis of the practice project. Students will also be exposed to leading research in the field through attendance at the departmental seminar series and will undertake workshops throughout the year to develop their research project.
learningOutcomes: Design and execute a substantial piece of original research;
Summarise, synthesise, and critique key theoretical frameworks of creative media analysis or practice;
Locate their work within the body of current research on the chosen area;
Express their argument effectively in an appropriate form.

modules.moduleContent: This module will train students in practical digital literacy skills such as coding, image, and audio editing as well as equipping them with a critical understanding of core web tools and techniques as well as the ethics of technology.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding and competency in digital literacy skills.
Critically appraise contemporary digital content, rights, access and privacy issues.
Work independently to solve problems in the creation of digital texts.
Demonstrate an understanding of digital project management.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module lays the theoretical foundation for a variety of methodological approaches to media and creative practice. Students will explore key research methods and conceptual frameworks used within the field of Media Studies, including emerging digital methods and practice-based approaches. The ethical, practical and conceptual considerations of research and dissemination will be investigated. Students will design an appropriate methodological, creative, and/or analytical approach to their PhD project.

This practice-based module aims to provide students with the creative methods and research techniques involved in the development of narrative screenplays for film, television, and the web. As part of the process, students will deepen their understanding of the mechanics of screen narrative. They will also learn the practical skills necessary to package and pitch their scripts. Class meetings are discussion based and include screenings, script analysis, and workshops. Students should expect to take active part in weekly discussions, to offer constructive critiques of each other's work, and to participate in script readings and other elements of the workshop throughout the term. In addition to the workshop aspects of the course, students will develop material through a series of writing exercises and assignments. Moreover, they will analyse and discuss different industry approaches and guiding principles for successful screenwriting.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This is a student-focused and student-led module about collaboration in an international business environment. International firms increasingly seek new ways of organizing work in an international context; global teams that operate across organisational boundaries, time zones and geographic locations, with members from multiple countries, are used more and more to achieve organizational objectives by multinational enterprises (MNEs), internationally active small and medium-sized enterprises, in strategic alliances, international joint ventures and virtual corporations. The objective of this module is to combine conceptual learning with experiential learning and learning-by-doing with reflective learning. Students will acquire and develop skills and competencies related to working in multicultural face-to-face and global virtual teams. This course will contribute to enhancing the student’s self-confidence (know-what) and professional conduct (know-how) in collaborating within diverse groups and organising work in multicultural global virtual teams.

In this module students will learn how to build, organise and lead a global virtual team to successfully achieve outcomes. They will be provided with numerous tools for doing so, decide on how best to use these tools and implement them in working through a real-life problem within a global virtual team. Students will prepare for and reflect on their experiences both individually and in collaborative learning groups.
learningOutcomes: Manage a collaborative team project and deal with different stakeholders;
