Use theoretical knowledge and apply appropriate research methods to a real-world business problem;
Develop effective strategies for working in a multicultural cross-border context;
Apply theoretical frameworks to real-life problems and reflect upon their own experiences;
Apply theoretical concepts and terminology when identifying and analysing various inputs, processes and outcomes related to working in multicultural teams (both co-located and distributed);
Present their analyses and recommendations in a professional, concise, and accurate manner.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: GLOBAL OPERATIONS AND SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed for graduates who are not familiar with foundation material in this subject area. A fundamental objective of the module is to develop participants’ general understanding of the material and to move beyond foundational material to:

a) lead to an advanced knowledge of operations, supply chain, and services management; and
b) understand the process and practice of applying this knowledge in specific contexts.
learningOutcomes: Describe key concepts in global operations and supply chain management, including the design and management of supply chains, key operations processes and inventory systems;
Appraise the business implications of global operations choices, including revenue and cost models, and how offshoring and outsourcing apply in practice;
Identify the range and impact of information systems and technology that apply to contemporary supply chains and operations systems;
Apply typical business skills to solving practical problems.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module considers the analysis, formulation, and implementation of strategy. Business level and corporate level strategy are evaluated in this module. Central to the module is an understanding of the competitive environment and its interaction with choices about how a firm grows and organises to succeed. A central purpose of strategy formulation and implementation is to improve the relative performance of a firm. This module uses concepts from economics and management to provide students with an understanding of the foundations upon which the processes of strategy formulation and implementation are based.
learningOutcomes: Critically appraise the strategy and business model of an organisation to establish the boundary conditions under which it is likely to succeed or fail using the principles of an economic perspective of strategy;
Be capable of offering an analytic diagnosis of these boundary conditions and insights into how to improve the likelihood of strategic success;
Be competent in defining, describing, discussing and applying a selection of strategic management principles to analysis of the strategy of an organisation;
Critically appraise a strategy case study business problem using the concepts and tools of strategic management;
Describe the strategy process elements of analysis, formulation, and implementation; and
Apply strategy tools / theories / frameworks to the analysis of an organisation and interpret the findings.

modules.moduleContent:
Decision makers in organisations often have a need for information that goes beyond their own and their teams’ expertise and experience. This may be the case in novel situations or when complex phenomena occur. For example, what causes problem X and how can we address it effectively? In such decision situations managers often rely on insights from business research to better comprehend phenomena or to test different ideas or suggestions. Using research to inform business decisions raises further questions: How to evaluate whether the available evidence is of a good quality and how to interpret it correctly? How to collect new evidence (that is, to carry out research) that will inform the business problem in question? 
The purpose of this module is to foster an understanding of how research feeds into the decision-making process in organisations and to prepare you to locate and evaluate existing knowledge (i.e., be thoughtful consumers of research) and to carry out independent research to address a business problem. 

We will discuss how to transform a decision problem into a question (or a set of questions) that can guide collection of evidence. Students will explore what types of evidence exist, where and how to locate them and how to critically evaluate and analyze them. Students will learn how to identify or formulate causal models of how the world ought to work (i.e., what is the underlying causal logic behind the business problem) with the goal of informing decision-making. Furthermore, students will learn how to test these models against various types of empirical evidence. Throughout this module, students will begin developing relevant practical skills in identifying, evaluating and interpreting evidence, such as undertaking a literature review, research design, data collection and analysis of data.

The module is experiential in design. Time in class will include some lectures and discussion, accompanied by exercises that lead to the development of essential research skills.
learningOutcomes: Explain how business research is linked to the needs of managerial decision makers;
Transform a decision-making problem into a research question that can guide collection of evidence;
Identify different types of evidence that are needed to inform a given decision-making problem and critically evaluate them;
Extract evidence from a review of the academic literature;
Assess the strengths and weaknesses of qualitative and quantitative research methods for gathering empirical evidence;
Select the most suitable research method for gathering empirical data to inform a research question; and
Carry out simple analyses of qualitative and quantitative data to inform a decision-making problem.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The Business Consulting Project has dual interconnected processes and objectives. For students, this is an opportunity to research and apply a theme in greater depth and apply the concepts and skills learned from both your research and your 65 credits of taught modules to a real organisation in practice. For organisations that provide projects, it is an opportunity for them to have a problem or question thoroughly, rigorously and practically investigated and to receive feedback on the findings and conclusions.

Students will identify a consulting question, review the literature to identify knowledge and best practices that can be applied to this question, develop a research methodology, analyse findings, and make recommendations. Typically, students work in pairs with a host organization although assessment is on an individual basis.

To meet these objectives students will write a Business Consulting Project report that explores the business practice and academic problem that is embedded in the research question. They will work with a faculty mentor to guide them on the problem solving journey. A key aspect of this learning process is working with the organisation that has provided the project. This will involve the student using their communication and business skills to negotiate with the client, guided by the School of Business. There are three key outputs of this Business Consulting Project: a written BCP report, a demonstrated client engagement process over the project, and a presentation and/or management report that provides the host organisation with a clear analysis and set of recommendations that can be implemented.

Typically, the host organisation is obtained by the School of Business for full-time students. Part-time students will normally work individually on projects associated with their own organisations. Part-time students can alternatively be assigned a project organisation by the School of Business.
learningOutcomes: Develop and defend an appropriate consulting proposal;
Write a literature review using a systematic process of collection, synthesis and appraisal;
Gather and analyse primary and secondary data relevant to a research question. Discuss findings in the context of the academic literature;
Generate a set of recommendations that are of business value to the client organisation using analytic and critical thinking skills;
Engage effectively with the client organisation over the life; and

Communicate the key processes, findings and recommendations of the consulting project, using methods appropriate to the host organisation (e.g. presentations, written reports, multi-media approaches).

modules.moduleContent:
** Commencing September 2025 **

Consumer behaviour is a dynamic area of marketing that draws on different fields including psychology, sociology, and economics, to understand how consumers think and behave. Consumer insights inform marketing strategies such as integrated marketing communications and branding strategies. Understanding consumers also enables managers to monitor the effectiveness of their strategies. This module draws on core theories of consumer attitudes and decision-making and explores their application in marketing practice.
learningOutcomes: An understanding of core theories and frameworks associated with consumer decision-making.
An understanding of consumer attitudes and behaviour.
An appreciation of the importance of consumer insights.
An understanding of the application of consumer behaviour theory to real-world marketing practice.

modules.moduleName: EMERGING TRENDS IN CONSUMPTION, CONSUMER TECHNOLOGIES & BRANDING
modules.moduleContent:
** Commencing September 2025 **

Marketing is a dynamic field, and this module provides students with an understanding of the latest thinking and practice. Having an appreciation of emerging trends helps us to understand how marketing activities can benefit business and society. The module focuses on contemporary issues, and therefore the topics will change from year to year, but will be informed by nascent trends in marketing literature, by faculty research, and by emerging trends in marketing practice. Illustrative examples of topics include:

1. The rise of technologies such as AI, and robotics, and their implications for customer relationship management, services brand building, and value co-creation.
2. Luxury goods and services marketing and consumption, in the context of organisations’ and consumers’ sustainability goals.
3. The role of the self-concept, self-identity and the virtual self in consumption.
4. The ‘dark side’ of marketing, including anti-brand communities and the diffusion of ‘fake news’ and disinformation, their implication for marketing strategies, and the role of storytelling, authenticity and trust in offsetting these challenges for brands and organisations.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a broad understanding of emerging theories as they relate to marketing strategy.
Analyse how emerging marketing trends affect organisations and consumers.
Critically evaluate key marketing theories in the context of emerging trends and explore how marketing trends may shape existing theories.
Demonstrate the ability to apply knowledge of emerging marketing trends in the development of marketing goals and strategies.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
** Commencing September 2025 **

In marketing, communications are key. Marketers have more opportunities to interact and engage with consumers than ever before, heightening the importance of how to design and implement and integrate effective marketing communications tools and techniques. Consumer-brand interactions occur across multiple channels in real-time, requiring responsive marketing communications for seamless consumer experiences. New platforms and technologies enhance the opportunities for communication, while adding to the complexity of the overall communications process. A key focus is on target audiences with insights delivered into behavioural, geo-locational, and psychographic targeting. This module gives students a comprehensive overview of the strategic practice of integrating marketing communications, from advertising to content marketing, all while placing the consumer at the centre of this process. The module will introduce students to concepts and frameworks for the development of creative marketing communications strategies.
learningOutcomes: Critically assess core concepts and theories of integrating marketing communications and apply these to the expanding media context;
Synthesise communications theory and practice to understand how messages are created and implemented in a strategic manner across platforms;
Understand and assess the impact and influence that digital channels, platforms, and technologies have on the development of digital marketing communications strategies;
Evaluate target audiences for marketing communications including behavioural, geo-locational and psychographic qualities;
Explain how to manage the integrated marketing communications mix; and
Demonstrate how to measure the effectiveness of marketing communications.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Dissertation

It is argued that the sole non-imitable resource that organisations have are their human resources. Using theory and case examples, this module guides students to appreciate how human resource management (HRM) empowers organisations to achieve sustainable competitive advantage through people. The module begins by considering the emergence of HRM and key principles of effective people management. The module then moves on to evaluate different forms of work, careers and the implications for HRM. The module also introduces a range of core HRM practices; employer branding, resourcing, job evaluation, people analytics, total reward and negotiations. The module provides important insights into the nature of contemporary work, careers and people management.
learningOutcomes: Explain the evolution of people management and the key learnings throughout each phase from Industrialisation up to and including debates concerning ‘The Future of Work’;
Identify what makes HRM a novel and distinct approach to people management that is central to delivering organisational competitive advantage;
Evaluate macro-micro connections to understand how national economic policy and the labour market connect with approaches to HRM in the workplace;
Discuss HRM practices including employer branding, resourcing, job evaluation, total reward and negotiations and the theories underpinning these practices;
Critically assess the relationship between HRM and different forms of work and concerns such as rising income inequality, job insecurity and work intensification.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The aim of module is to provide in-depth knowledge about the fundamentals of Operations and Supply Chain Management required for the creation of products and services on which we depend. This module equips students with the knowledge and skills necessary to manage operations and supply chains in the business world.  Students will learn key concepts, tools and techniques to design and manage operations and supply chains, from raw materials to finished products.
learningOutcomes: Explain and critique the strategic importance of operations and supply chain management in the global competitive business environment;
Demonstrate knowledge of current supply chains and operations management theories;
Analyse and discuss the interaction between operations and supply chain management and other functional areas of business;
Apply theories, tools and techniques to design and manage operations and supply chains.

modules.moduleContent: The aims of this module are to enable students to learn the importance of project management planning and execution, to understand the drivers of project performance and to apply structured methods in project delivery. As projects drive business change, students will learn to apply project management techniques and tools for effectively managing operations and supply chain projects, ensuring results that are delivered on time and within budget.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of project management in organisational and wide business context;
Explain the importance of project management planning and execution;
Apply structured methods and tools in project planning, delivery and execution;
Appreciate the myriad skills required of an effective project manager;
Develop and analyse project budgets, quality control standards and risk controls.

modules.moduleContent: Given the growing interest in digitalisation and the crucial role of technology in Supply Chains, this module familiars students with emerging technologies and tools such as supply chain software, Internet of Things (IoT), artificial intelligence (AI) and blockchain, and their role in improving supply chain visibility, efficiency, and decision-making.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the importance and impact of technological change in business operations;
Identify key digital technologies used in modern supply chains;
Understand the challenges and barriers of technology adoption in operations and supply chains;
Apply digital tools to manage and optimize supply chain processes such as procurement, production, logistics, and distribution;
Critically evaluate the role that quantitative techniques can play as a decision aiding tool, as well as their limitations.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: CROSSING BOUNDARIES: PREPARATION FOR THE YEAR ABROAD
modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to prepare students for the Year Abroad linguistically and interculturally, and to assist them in identifying their own learning strategies in academic, linguistic, cultural, pragmatic and personal terms. Students will engage with concepts such as 'culture', 'interculturality' and 'intercultural communicative competence', while also being introduced to a corpus of scholarly research on living and working abroad within a year abroad context. Additionally, students will be exposed to the lived experiences from final-year students who have recently returned in order to benefit from fellow students' lived experiences. By adopting a dual theoretical and applied approach, this module will provide students with relevant tools for dealing with practical matters upon arrival as well as assisting them to generate a critical awareness of the key learning experiences of the period abroad, thus encouraging them to develop sound intercultural communicative competence and to document the personal, academic and other benefits of their year abroad.

This module is strongly recommended for students who are interested in spending the third year of their studies in their respective target-language country.
learningOutcomes: Investigate and gather information on practical matters before and during the year abroad e.g. application procedures, information mining, registering residency, setting up bank accounts etc.
Identify academic, linguistic, cultural, professional and personal Year Abroad goals and develop strategies for reaching such goals
Summarise the scholarly work on the Year Abroad and explain how it applies to students' own learning
Define concepts such as 'culture', 'interculturality' and 'intercultural communicative competence' and clarify their relevance to the Year Abroad
Research, structure and develop a balanced argument in writing.
Comprehend the general principles of international criminal law and distinguish international crimes from one another.
Recognise and research the sources of international criminal law.
Analyse how international criminal law principles were applied in international jurisprudence.
Apply the principles of international criminal law to real and hypothetical scenarios.
Research and analyse literature on the topic of international criminal law and complete assignments in this field.
Critically analyse the general principles of international criminal law and how they are applied in practice.

modules.moduleContent: This module will provide students with a comprehensive understanding of the central issues and debates in the field of international trade law. The course will introduce the student to the legal system governing international trade, particularly through the international agreements, structures and functions of the World Trade Organization (WTO), the primary international organisation in the sphere of economic globalisation. In analysing the treaties, jurisprudence, legal principles and rules of international trade, the course will also necessarily interrogate the dominant theories that underpin the world trading system, and will critically examine the alleged spin-offs of development, poverty reduction, innovation and employment. We will examine the divergent perspectives held by participants in the international trading system, and the underlying ideological and geopolitical contestations at play.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate clear knowledge of the history, institutional structures, and conceptual underpinnings of  international trade law;
display an in-depth understanding of the normative content of international trade rules, and of the mechanics of their operation;
critically evaluate the jurisprudence of relevant international dispute resolution bodies, highlighting lacunae within the law and shortcomings in its implementation;
understand the ideological, geopolitical and economic contestations at play in international trade law discourse.

modules.moduleContent: This module aims to provide student with a good understanding of various legal mechanisms/tools that the contracting parties could use to secure the performance of obligations they are entitled to expect from the partner under the contract (rights on goods or immoveable property belonging to the partner, a third party undertaking himself to pay if the latter fails to do so…). Written exam (University premises or take home)

Module objectives: To develop student’s ability to draft and analyze international contracts, in a transnational and international perspective.

Module content: Fewer margins, indirect damage, judicial appeals... For small and medium-sized companies, the consequences of a business contract not properly clinched can be disastrous. In order to avoid such litigation, to manage the risks and not to go to court, a well drafted business contract is indispensable, in France and on an international level. This course puts the focus on the essential aspects of International Contract Law and proposes rules enabling to draft and negotiate contracts. Numerous contract terms and examples of contracts are analyzed. This teaching is thus very practical so that students may identify and assess the risks for business operations.

Many contracts are presented such as the sale contract, the retailer contract, the sales rep contract, consultancy, technical support and the know-how license.
learningOutcomes: Develop a student’s ability to draft and analyse contracts from a transnational and international perspective.

modules.moduleContent:
Globalism, transnationalism, and the social construction and performance of the border have furthered opportunities for the commission of the crimes and harms of the powerful, including organised criminals and criminal groups, corporations, governments, states, and other ‘elites’ in positions of power. This module provides students with a critical take on transnational and global crime. 
Students will examine a range of transnational and global crimes and harms, including, trafficking, tax evasion and avoidance, irregular/illegal migration, smuggling, modern slavery, cybercrime, ecocide, and crimes and harms of international institutions (e.g., The World Bank). Students will examine critically the nexus between these crimes and harms, and their definition and criminalisation under national and international law. In so doing, students will consider the implications of such framing for responding to and preventing transnational and global crime.
learningOutcomes: Explain the extent of and harm caused by transnational and/or global crime.
Draw on theory and/or case studies to explain the commission of transnational and/or global crime.
Evaluate the effectiveness of national, supranational, and/or international law and its enforcement in responding to transnational and/or global crime.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Funds law incorporates elements of corporate law, contract law and EU law.  It is a very dynamic area of legal study, as legislation and regulation is constantly evolving in order to respond to a rapidly developing area of financial services.  Speed of legislative and regulatory action is needed so that the European single market for investment funds can continue to be strengthened, while balancing investor protection concerns and potential financial stability risks.  The module addresses the European regulatory frameworks under the UCITS Directive and the Alternative Investment Fund Managers Directive (“AIFMD”), the various legal vehicles used to establish investment funds in Ireland and the structuring of investment funds.  European investment fund regulation and supervision and the role of the European Securities and Markets Authority (“ESMA”) is examined.  The legislative initiatives impacting investment funds under the European Commission’s Sustainable Finance Action Plan are examined and discussed.  These initiatives are aimed at directing capital towards sustainable investments in order to fulfil the objectives of the European Green Deal, recognising the role of capital markets in reaching the EU’s 2050 climate neutral target.

This course will be taught only if a sufficient number of students enrol. Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module coordinator.
learningOutcomes: Outline the key considerations for a fund manager establishing an investment fund in Ireland, including identifying the appropriate regulatory framework, fund vehicle, fund structure and corporate governance matters;
Examine and critique the regulatory justifications for and the evolution of the UCITS regulatory framework;
Examine and critique the regulatory justifications for and the possible future evolution of the AIFMD regulatory framework;
Outline and critique the European system of supervision of financial services and the role of ESMA and its powers;
Outline and critique the specific corporate governance issues that arise in an investment funds context and outline the sources of fund directors’ duties;
outline and critique the case law relating to the suspension of redemptions;
engage in independent research and analysis of relevant funds law topics and apply the law to various factual scenarios;

modules.moduleName: HISTORICAL GENDERED VIOLENCE AND THE STATE
modules.moduleContent:
Over the course of the past three decades countries across the world have been forced to reckon with revelations of historical violence against women and children in state institutions.  Taking an international and comparative approach, the module explores how States have responded to survivors’ demands for justice and recognition. Students are introduced to key ideas in feminist legal theory, criminology, post colonial theory  and transitional justice on the nature and harms of state institutional violence against women and children. This is followed by a selection of case studies examining political and legal responses including: State apologies; criminal sanctions for individual and corporate wrongdoing; redress scheme for survivors; and public inquiries and truth commissions. Students will investigate the role of law and legal institutions in vindicating, deferring or contesting survivors’ justice claims.  

This module surveys feminist legal theory, gender theory and key ideas in transitional justice criminology and post colonial studies. It also examines selected national and international case studies (from eg the  Commission to Inquire into Child Abuse in Ireland; Canada’s Truth and Reconciliation Commission on Residential Schools, the Independent Inquiry into Child Sexual Abuse in England and Wales).
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of  the relationship between law and historical State institutional violence¿against women and children

Demonstrate an appreciation of the significance of feminist legal theory, criminology and transitional justice for understanding the contemporary formation of legal and political norms around historical wrongdoing by the State.

Demonstrate an appreciation of how concepts of victimhood, gender, rights and sovereignty are imbricated in legal discourse, practice and activism around historical injustice against women and children.

modules.moduleContent: Studies in Lyon

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO MEDIA AND CULTURAL STUDIES II
modules.moduleContent: This module continues the introduction to foundational Media and Cultural Studies concepts and theories begun in Media and Cultural Studies I (MD 160). This module concentrates on the reception and circulation of media stories and the socio-cultural and political-economic contexts of media production.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a critical understanding of the production, reception, and circulation of media stories.
Recognise and apply key theoretical frameworks in the analysis of media industries and audience activities.
Develop a sustained written analysis that displays an understanding of key analytical and formal dimensions of academic writing.
Communicate effectively in academic contexts.

modules.moduleContent: Media & Cultural Theory 1 introduces students to key theoretical and critical approaches to media technologies, cultural industries, and the study of power in digital media environments. The module examines concepts developed in different media-historical periods and introduces key theories and theorists of the contemporary media environment.
learningOutcomes: Identify a range of theoretical and methodological frameworks and place them within disciplinary context.
Apply theoretical perspectives to interpret media and cultural phenomena.
Synthesise and critique theoretical frameworks.
Communicate effectively in written format.

modules.moduleContent: This module will explore the impact of algorithms on cultural and creative communication.  Students will learn about the different types of algorithms that exist currently, how they impact our daily use and focus in on creative and innovative case studies of algorithmic use in the cultural sector.
learningOutcomes: Contextualise the impact of algorithms in contemporary society.
Identify and recognise the use of algorithms in a variety of cultural forms.
Anticipate future algorithimical development and its potential impact on society and the cultural sector.
Critically analyse existing cultural algorithms and their uses.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This class provides an introduction to film as an aesthetic, economic, and cultural phenomenon. Mastering the vocabulary associated with narrative, editing, cinematography, mise-en-scene, and sound, which are the fundamental tools required for the close analysis of film texts, lays the groundwork for the interpretive analysis of film texts.  We will further consider key historical, economic and ideological contexts that inform the production and consumption of films and film genres. Topics that link to second semester modules in the Film/Screen studies elective stream will be incorporated.
learningOutcomes: Utilize the technical  terminology of film studies in writing and speaking
Analyse and interpret narrative, visual form, and sound design in film/screen texts
Situate cinema as an aesthetic and cultural form within historical and social context
Construct written and oral arguments related to film/screen studies subjects.

modules.moduleContent:
During this semester, students will continue to work independently on the research project identified in MD315. Under the direction of appointed supervisors, they will complete the study and develop and write a short dissertation based on this research.

Note: 2.1 minimum standard on either MD231 or MD232 to undertake this module.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a depth and breadth of understanding in their chosen area of media or cultural research
Conduct a process of data gathering and/or analysis
Synthesise findings and secondary sources into a cogent argument
Write effectively in a structured academic format
Work independently

modules.moduleContent: In this module students engage in filming and in post-production for a short film project. Hands on workshops in directing and editing will complement the key stages of short film making and editing. Students will operate camera, lights and sound to shoot their self-initiated short script. They will collaborate in post-production to develop a rough cut and have screenings for feedback from peers, tutors and industry professionals, which will be incorporated into the final online version of the short.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate skills in production and postproduction management in a self-initiated short film project
Evidence specialization in practice within cameras, lights sound and editing within short film production
Collaborate effectively as part of a multi-functional production team to meet a strict deadline
Identify phases of production, production roles and the creative skills of short film group production
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module examines key and emerging debates surrounding film and television authorship in relation to the detailed analysis of the work of individual directors, showrunners, and other crewmembers who may act as potential film authors. It explores notions of authorship articulated and developed in film, television, and other media studies. It also considers how these ideas, whether popular or scholarly, inform our understanding of the production, distribution, and exhibition of the media in question.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of the questions, theories, and controversies which have informed critical and theoretical debates on authorship
Recognise and evaluate concepts of authorship within industrial, historical, cultural, and commercial practices
Analyse narrative and formal strategies in relation to the work of specific film and television practitioners
Utilise a range of concepts in the detailed analysis of film and TV media
Demonstrate a detailed understanding of the development of the work of specific television and film practitioners.
modules.notes: No longer running

modules.moduleName: GLOBAL CINEMA: IRELAND AND THE WORLD
modules.moduleContent: This module examines debates and films classified under the rubric of Global Cinema. Since the 1920s, US cinema has dominated cinema screens around the world, while European cinemas have most often defined themselves in national terms and/or in relation to such art practices as neorealism, impressionism and expressionism. Cinema beyond these US- and Eurocentric models has often been ghettoized as the consumption category “world cinema.”  Moving beyond these categories, this module focuses on film and filmmaking practices that meaningfully address the global, examining how a medium consisting of moving images and recorded sound engages with experiences of international mobility, border crossing, migration and population flows.
learningOutcomes: Situate global cinema within relevant historical and aesthetic debates
Demonstrate an awareness of a range of international filmmaking practices
Analyze global cinema texts in relation to such phenomena as international mobility, border crossing, migration and population flows
Construct written and oral arguments related to global cinema topics

modules.moduleContent: This module explores the political-economic contexts and structures of contemporary media and cultural work in Ireland and internationally. Through a series of lectures with industry professionals, students will develop an understanding of work life in a number of sectors of the media and cultural industries. Students will engage with critical theorisations of media production and cultural work across a variety of sectors. A final dimension is critical appraisal of structural and cultural inequalities in media production and cultural work.
learningOutcomes: Identify and critique key dimensions of the organization of work in contemporary Irish cultural and media industries.
Communicate effectively in both professional and academic language.
Place Irish media and cultural industry workers in social and economic context.

modules.moduleContent: This module considers the social, industrial, and cultural processes that support collaborative filmmaking. In doing so, it looks at inclusive and democratic forms of cinema, while asking students to challenge what, how, and by whom films circulate. The first part explores key theories related to collaborative filmmaking. The second examines specific forms of filmmaking such as collectives, mobile film festivals, and user-generated content. Your assessment will draw inspiration from one of those forms to produce a short film. But this is not a practice module. The technical quality of the film will not be assessed and the module is open to those with zero production experience. Your most important learning will derive from your critical reflection on the collaborative filmmaking process.
learningOutcomes: Gain practical experience in collaborative methods of film production.
Develop teamwork, communication, and project management skills.
Evaluate the impact of structural inequalities on contemporary film circulation.
Demonstrate their theoretical understanding of collaborative filmmaking cultures through a critical reflection of their practice.
modules.notes: No longer running

modules.moduleContent: This compulsory module lays the theoretical foundation for a variety of methodological approaches to media and creative practice. Students will explore key research methods and conceptual frameworks used within the field of Media Studies, including emerging digital methods and practice-based approaches. The ethical, practical and conceptual considerations of research and dissemination will be investigated. Students will design an appropriate methodological, creative, and/or analytical approach to a media research topic in preparation for the dissertation or final project.
learningOutcomes: Select appropriate methods and/or critical approaches in the design of a robust media research project;
Critically appraise the validity, strengths and weaknesses of a variety of methodological and critical frameworks;
Contextualise methods and theoretical paradigms within the history of the discipline;
Communicate effectively in diverse written formats.

modules.moduleContent:
This module presents key theoretical concepts, approaches, and research questions in the fields of Media and Cultural Studies to enable students to develop a thorough understanding of the disciplines’ conceptual underpinnings as well as to inform research undertaken for MA dissertation and PhD thesis projects. 
 
Students will explore a range of media theories from humanities and social sciences, engaging with their historical and scholarly contexts. Exploring and critiquing central arguments and critical debates in the field, students develop a repertoire of theoretical frames for media studies inquiry.

This module will train students in practical digital literacy skills such as coding, image, and audio editing as well as equipping them with a critical understanding of core web tools and techniques as well as the ethics of technology.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding and competency in digital literacy skills.
Critically appraise contemporary digital content, rights, access and privacy issues.
Work independently to solve problems in the creation of digital texts.
Demonstrate an understanding of digital project management.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Working across cultural boundaries and contexts and within firms has become standard in multinational and local firms alike. People increasingly need to hone their intercultural communication and interaction skills to succeed in modern organisational settings. This unit takes an in-depth look at the ways in which culture manifests itself in daily interaction at the societal, organisational and individual levels.
The objective is to provide the student with the skills needed to recognise and overcome potential challenges posed by cultural differences in different settings. Students will acquire knowledge and skills to build their intercultural awareness and competencies in preparation for a career in the diverse workplace. The module examines the influence of culture in shaping societies and societal values, the role of culture and values in an organisational setting, and on interpersonal aspects, such as communication and interaction.
learningOutcomes: Competently discuss the concept of culture and its determinants at various levels of analysis;
Develop an understanding of the principles of effective cross-cultural communication and intercultural interaction and apply them in different settings;
Critically evaluate the role of culture and cultural values in international business operations and management practices;
Understand the cross-cultural negotiations process, identify and analyse the challenges to effective cross-cultural negotiations and strategies for success;
Develop their own intercultural awareness and cultural intelligence by building a toolbox of skills to apply when faced with challenges in different real-life situations.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed for graduates who may not be familiar with foundational material in this subject area. A fundamental objective of the programme is to develop participants general understanding of the material and to move beyond it to:

a) Explore advanced content and
b) Understand the process and practice of applying their knowledge to specific contexts.

Learners will gain an understanding of human behaviour at the level of the workplace. Key theories from social psychology, sociology, and organisational studies will be employed to develop understandings of the complexity of relationship on organisational and individual performance. Learners will study the role of individual personality traits, attitudes and motivations as well as the fundamentals, dynamics and challenges of teamwork. Leadership styles, organisational culture and the broader role of the organisation in society will also be examined.
learningOutcomes: Discuss and evaluate the relevance of psychological, sociological and organisational theories introduced in this module for specific contexts and organisations;
Discuss and evaluate the opportunities and challenges that organisations confront when trying to implement these theories;
Apply key elements of these theories to examining real-time situations and organisations;
Develop a research project that analyses real organisations using these theories; and
Synthesise results of analysis into theory-informed recommendations to real organisations.

modules.moduleContent: Companies must be able to turn internal and external data that is relevant to its business into insights that can help them innovate, account for risks and open new collaborations. This module will afford students the opportunities to work with business analysts from industry to tackle business analytics challenges provided by client companies. The business analysts will share their expertise from real-world analytics-driven digital transformation projects and introduce students to different approaches to successfully manage their projects.  The experience will enable students to develop their analytics skills and apply their knowledge of concepts, techniques, and research methods obtained throughout the programme to solve business analytics problems. Students will also be able to propose alternative analytics approaches to specific organisational problems. Furthermore, students will be able to critically interpret their findings and present their analysis and recommendations to client organisations.
learningOutcomes: Manage an analytics-driven innovation project and deal with different stakeholders;
Use theoretical knowledge and apply appropriate analytics methods to a real-world business or organisational problem;
Evaluate feedback from different stakeholders and develop an action plan upon it;
Develop insights from data for a specific business or organisational problem and recommendations to support data-driven decision making and innovation; and
Present analysis, findings, and recommendations in a professional, concise and accurate manner.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: There is a need to look beyond current IT Leadership and Governance capabilities to develop an enterprise view of IT governance, by creating a “business lens” through which to enable effective oversight of IT across the organisation. The opportunities presented by new technological and digital possibilities, for example with regard to customer data, also increase risk and exposure to an organisation. This module will address these issues. The module is designed for graduates who will not be familiar with foundational material in this subject area. A fundamental objective of this module to develop participant’s general understanding of the material and to move beyond to: a) explore advanced content and b) understand the process and practice of applying their knowledge in specific contexts.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the academic perspective on IT and digital technologies governance and management;
Explain the role of the CIO in the increasingly dynamic technology environment in organisations;
Design IT and digital technologies governance structures;
Examine the ways IT and digital technologies can deliver business value and improved organisational performance;
Discuss the challenges and opportunities of emerging digital technologies for IT governance; and
Discuss the ethical requirements for digital technologies.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The objective of this course is to familiarise students with disruptive and emerging digital technologies and develop an understanding of key approaches, issues and challenges to their implementation and management in organizations within different contexts. Topics will be drawn from strategically important emerging technologies and may include Cloud Computing, Digital Platforms, Business Analytics, Machine learning, Artificial Intelligence, Internet-Of-Things, Robotics, Blockchain, NFT's, Metaverse and/or other emerging topics. Approaches will be drawn from contemporary organizational practice such as Open Innovation, Real World Experimentation, Servitization, Platformization, Innovation & Business Ecosystems, Crowdsourcing and Sustainability etc. 

Module Content: The programme is designed for graduates who will not necessarily be familiar with foundational material in this subject area. A fundamental objective of the programme is to develop participant’s general understanding of the material and to move beyond this by: a) exploring advanced material and b) developing an understanding of approaches and factors to consider when implementing and managing new technology within different contexts.
learningOutcomes: Identify and evaluate disruptive and emerging technologies and their potential for supporting and enhancing organizational, as well as societal value;

Demonstrate knowledge of key principles related to these technologies that may include; Cloud Computing, Digital Platforms, Business Analytics, Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Internet of Things, Robotics, Blockchain, Metaverse, etc.;
Discuss key approaches, issues and challenges associated with implementing and managing these technologies in organizations; and
Explain the social and ethical implications of contemporary information systems in organizations.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to enable participants to have a deep understanding of the role of campaign strategy in a digital context and its management in building value for firms' stakeholders and a thorough appreciation of the close link between digital campaign strategy and the digital customer value journey. This is becoming more important for marketers as digital tactics become mainstreamed into the customer acquisition and retention journey.

The Digital Customer Value Journey strategically builds relationship with new prospects and converts them into loyal, repeat customers.  This Journey is the process every prospect goes through to become a new customer which is the aim of any digital marketing campaign. Campaign strategy involves developing a plan which encompasses key steps in the journey from awareness through to customers becoming promoters of your brand.  Participants will learn that the key to managing any digital campaign strategy is to successfully manage the stages that move prospects from one stage to the next of the digital customer value journey primarily using digital but also including more traditional marketing tactics.
learningOutcomes: Design, develop and execute a marketing campaign inclusive of digital content;
Carry out a strategic analysis of the market and key competitors for a digitally oriented campaign;
Articulate in a convincing manner the importance of digital campaign goals in determining campaign success;
Analyse the customer lead monetisation process in a digital context;
Critically assess, manage and monitor ongoing digital campaigns; and
Utilise and adapt marketing tactics to maximise return on investment for digital assets.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: In the digital domain, communications are key. Marketers have more opportunities to interact and engage with consumers than ever before, heightening the importance of how to design and implement effective marketing communications tools and techniques. Consumer-brand interactions occur across multiple channels in real-time, requiring responsive marketing communications for seamless consumer experiences.  New platforms and technologies enhance the opportunities for digital communication, while adding to the complexity of the overall communications process.  A key focus is on target audiences with insights delivered into behavioural, geo-locational, and psychographic targeting. This module gives students a comprehensive overview of the key principles and practices of digital marketing communications, from advertising to content marketing, all while placing the consumer at the centre of this process. The module will introduce students to concepts and frameworks for the development of creative digital marketing communications strategies in recognition of the fundamental shift towards digital media today. While exposing students to theory, the ethical aspects of communications are also stressed.
learningOutcomes: Critically assess core concepts and theories of the marketing communications mix and apply these to the digital context;
Synthesise communications theory and practice to understand how messages are created and implemented in a strategic manner on digital platforms;
Understand and assess the impact and influence that digital channels, platforms, and technologies have on the development of digital marketing communications strategies;
Evaluate target audiences for digital communications including behavioural, geo-locational and psychographic qualities;
Explain how to manage the digital marketing communications mix in an ethical manner; and
Demonstrate how to measure the effectiveness of marketing communications.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The role of marketers has significantly changed over the past years, and marketing managers can now gather information from a seemingly endless sea of customer data that stem from traditional and digital market research, eCRM systems, websites and/or social media. Further, marketers can utilise an increasing number of digital research methodologies to collect this fast-growing volume of customer data in structured and unstructured ways. 

Yet, data is not information, and information is not insight. Data is only of value when it is collected rigorously, analysed correctly, and more importantly, when it is translated into insights that help marketers to make better decisions. In this module, students will learn the scientific approach to marketing with hands-on use of technologies to collect, analyse, and act on customer information. This is the key part of learning how to take advantage of marketing data. While students will employ quantitative methods in the course, the goal is not to produce experts in statistics; rather, students will gain the competency to interact with and manage a marketing analytics process.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an ability to translate managerial problems into research problems and how to approach customer insights problems with the “strategic end” in mind;
Comprehend the benefits and limitations of various data sources and research methods and their capabilities to marketing problems;
Learn how to collect, clean and analyse customer data; and
Critically assess the importance of customer insights and analytics in developing marketing strategies and tactics.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
As evidence of the climate crisis grows daily, from rising temperatures to unprecedented extreme weather events, sustainability concerns are becoming a central business issue. Supply chains, energy dispatch and production are being impacted; customers are demanding change; employees are raising concerns; investors are calling for greater transparency; and an increasing amount of litigation and regulation are driving climate action rapidly up the corporate agenda.   

To date in Ireland, businesses have already begun to act on climate change with multinational corporations and large indigenous employers embedding sustainability across their businesses at different stages. SMEs are also responding as sustainability works its way from manufacturing to the supply chain. Their actions are driving access to new markets, innovation, greater resilience, and higher employee engagement.

With the scale of the challenge ahead, corporate climate action is now entering a new phase. Organisations are now tasked with taking increasingly ambitious and significant action including setting science-based targets, addressing emissions across scope 1, 2 and 3, aligning with the Sustainable Development Goals, while managing the complexities between climate and social issues and navigating through simultaneous crises.
  
The aim of this module is to equip future leaders with conceptual tools for addressing the complex and difficult issues surrounding the formulation and implementation of organizational strategies for success in the 21st century, in which sustainability will no longer be perceived as a “nice to have” and will become a fundamental business imperative.
learningOutcomes: Recognize and understand the systemic nature of sustainability and the challenges it poses for “business as usual” across different sectors such as manufacturing, mobility, and energy;
Conceptualize and analyze the forces establishing the environment, sustainability, and sustainable development as important issues for business;
Conceptualize and develop innovative business models and frameworks for innovation that both foster sustainability and confer competitive advantage, rather than viewing them as trade-offs;
Comprehend the linkages between sustainability and economic theory, green premium including common misconceptions, limits and paradigms, both entrenched and emergent; and

Explain how and why firms might collaborate with governments, competitors, energy actors and non-governmental organizations (NGOs) and other stakeholders in the process of managing and resolving sustainable development issues.

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to enhance students’ appreciation and application of Career Planning and Development (CPD) activities. The module guides students to direct and elevate their personal CPD through active, formative and reflective learning organised around four themes. These themes stem from competencies central to career development namely ‘I am Ambitious’, ‘I am a Citizen’, ‘I am Resilient’ and ‘I am Agile’. Students identify and undertake a range of CPD activities under each theme that target their personal CPD priorities. Specifically, the CPDs that students propose should be consistent with their own career development needs, and with the learning outcomes of the module and should realistically advance student competencies beyond their current level.  These activities are pursued outside of lecture hours and will be approved by the module lecturer who provides feedback on initial reflective learning logs. The module also covers HRM topics relating to career development including the ‘Future of Work’, organisational career structures, total reward and negotiations. Upon completion of the module students submit a reflective learning CPD portfolio which documents their competence progression and their learnings regarding their future career aspirations and plan.
learningOutcomes: Review the evolution and changing nature of work, careers and the breadth of employment models within the labour market from ‘full-time permanent’ to the ‘gig economy’;
Critically assess own competencies objectively and relative to career aspirations;
Select and activate, through the CPD activities, a personal career planning and development strategy;
Explain the purpose and benefits of engaging in planned and reflective career planning and development; and
Convincingly articulate and demonstrate career interests and professional competence.

modules.moduleContent: Business Data Analysts are able to analyse a business problem or need and translate it into an analytics solution. Such business needs may arise in any business function such as market basket analysis, fleet management, sales prediction, fraud detection, customer classification for targeted advertisement, workforce turnover prediction, financial credit scoring, etc. An analytics solution would include the preparation of relevant datasets, selecting among alternative analytics modelling techniques, and evaluating the results. Business analysts would also be able to critically interpret their findings, visualize them, and communicate them with decision makers. This module will equip students with the principle concepts big data management, and techniques and hands-on-experience in descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive analytics. Students will also learn how to deal with various types of data, being structured such as spreadsheets or unstructured such as text in social media feeds.
learningOutcomes: Translate a business problem into data-driven analytics solutions through the concepts and technologies of business analytics;
Understand the concepts of structured and unstructured data, data management and big data processing pipelines;
Demonstrate knowledge of the concepts and techniques of descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive analytics;
Communicate analytics results through effective data visualization;
Apply the analytics process to collect, prepare, analyse, and present results,on real-world datasets.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces participants to the principles and key theories of Sustainable Enterprise and examines how these are applied in practical contexts. It will begin by exploring the foundations of ethical theory as it applies to sustainability in business and will outline and will discuss the various ways in which they have been applied. It will then examine how various business and management functions have evolved and developed to meet the requirements of the drive for environmental sustainability. As businesses do not function outside the social, political and environmental spheres, it is essential to understand the international, regional, national and local governance contexts in which sustainable enterprize continues to develop. Finally, as strategy is a key concern of all business education programmes, students will be introduced to key mechanisms by which sustainable strategies and approaches are planned, developed and implemented within businesses.
learningOutcomes: Describe the key ethical frameworks which underpin sustainable enterprise.
Demonstrate how various business contexts and instruments have been used to ‘de-carbonize’ businesses.
Develop strategic approaches for developing sustainable enterprises, or changing corporate culture to one that is supportive of Climate Neutrality.
Reflect on how sustainable leadership and responsible management theories and practice influence change within an organisational context.

modules.moduleContent: This module will build on 'Introduction to Sustainable Enterprise' module and aims to (1) allow students commence the building of their personal sustainability practice as professionals / managers, (2) facilitate understanding how this practice relates to other keys of the business and organisations they currently work or will work for in the future, and (3) ensure that the businesses they work for (and society and the natural environment) will benefit from this practice. This module will introduce contemporary Climate Action Policy & Environmental Governance relating these to the challenges and opportunities they present to enterprises in Ireland. The reason for doing this is to ensure that graduates of the programme are familiar with the evolving aspects environmental governance and the requirements this places on business and will explain how these frameworks are developed in the practice of managers responsible for key business functions. Recent scholarly inquiry has demonstrated an interested in the emergence of the sustainability and CSR profession. The emergence of this new professional field and its implications for the sustainability agenda in Irish business will be addressed. The introductory module also discussed strategy and strategic leadership. This module allows participants demonstrate an understanding of how these concepts are 'put to work' in real business scenarios by exposing students to the challenges of responsible organisational development and change.

The module is designed to give participants knowledge of and experience with business analytic techniques. These techniques will be implemented in a business problem-solving environment. This module introduces best practice for conducting analytical projects, and a baseline capability is established in spreadsheet modelling and quantitative methods. The software used is Microsoft Excel and other relevant software.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate the fundamental concepts of decision making and problem solving;
Apply appropriate software tools to solve business problems, identify opportunities and recognise changing business environments;
Demonstrate an understanding of analytical skills using different types of data;
Make informed choices about techniques to be applied to solve specific business problems;
Exercise individual and team problem-solving, critical thinking and analysis skills to find efficient and effective solutions to business challenges.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The module is broadly divided into three main themes: 1) International Business Theory, 2) The Institutional Environment of International Business, and 3) The Complexities of International Business. This module focuses on key theories of international trade, foreign direct investment and internationalisation, entry into foreign markets and the decisions that international firms face amidst the challenges of a complex institutional environment. The applicability of theoretical concepts in different international environments is explored during the module with the aim of appreciating differences across the legal, political and cultural environments, ethical decision making, and working in challenging markets. Businesses operate in an increasingly globalised environment and most business graduates will seek careers that will involve working and managing in international environments; the skills acquired in this module will be relevant in real life situations.
learningOutcomes: Understand the impact of economic development and technology on industry, business practice, and consumers;
Appreciate the effects of institutional context on international business;
Understand alternative routes to internationalisation;
Understand the frameworks for analysing cultures and the implications of cultural differences on international business;
Critically assess management theories and practices related to ethics in different countries;
Apply key frameworks to identify and analyse global industries.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module provides a practical component where students apply their knowledge and skills to contemporary real-world supply chain problems. Students build on the skills obtained throughout the programme to develop solutions for a specific operations or supply chain problem. Students evaluate the benefits and drawbacks of their solutions and develop suggestions for their implementation. This module also prepares students for their summer pathway options.
learningOutcomes: Use theoretical knowledge and apply appropriate research methods to a real-world supply chain and operations problem;
Manage a supply chain project and deal with different stakeholders;
Evaluate feedback from different stakeholders and develop an action plan upon it;
Develop insights from data for a specific operations and supply chain problem, and provide recommendations to support decision-making;
Present analysis, findings, and recommendations in a professional, concise and accurate manner.

modules.moduleContent: This course is designed to facilitate participants to consider current concepts, policy, legislation, and good practice relating to whistleblowing law and practice in Ireland. Participants will be introduced to the concept of whistleblowing/protected disclosures from both a legal and practical perspective. The course focusses on the key legislative provisions and case law of the Protected Disclosures Act 2014 and the recently adopted Protected Disclosures (Amendment) Act 2022. The course will provide participants with the practical tools to develop and implement best practice procedures for dealing with whistleblowing/protected disclosures in the workplace.
learningOutcomes: Identify a whistleblowing/protected disclosure situation and apply their legislative and practical knowledge to that situation.
Assess the legal requirements that must be satisfied in order for a disclosure to attract statutory protections.
Critically evaluate existing and emerging whistleblowing law principles at national, EU, and international level.
Develop and implement best practice procedures for dealing with disclosures of wrongdoing in the workplace.

modules.moduleContent:
Students who take this module will develop an advanced and critical understanding of the theoretical models of youth justice, their application across various international youth justice systems, and the influence of model and system intersection. This module will facilitate students with developing their comparative skills by requiring students to compare and contrast a number of youth justice jurisdictions with the aim of identifying convergence and divergence in terms of ideology, policy and practice. 

Students will firstly explore the models of youth justice and develop a deep understanding of their various characteristics. Thereafter, students will spend a defined period of time examining youth justice systems across a number jurisdictions - e.g. Ireland, US, Germany, Netherlands – with the objective of identifying the model/s evident in each and the implications for policy and practice.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a deep understanding of the various models of youth justice.
Display strong comparative analysis ability and be able to compare and contrast international youth justice systems with reference to theoretical models of youth justice.
Students who undertake this module will be supported in developing their presentation skills.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to international and regional systems for the protection of human rights. What is the international human rights system for, and how does it work? Whose rights does it serve to protect? What rights? What are the ideological, political and economic barriers to the realisation of rights? Is there a hierarchy of rights? Can or should rights be constructed as ‘universal’? What allowance should be made for cultural differences across the globe? Should certain vulnerable groups be guaranteed special protections? 

The module covers the various pathways for the enforcement of human rights at national, regional and international levels, in order to understand how these various jurisdictions attempt to curb state and corporate power and to bolster rights protection. It examines the different categories of human rights that have been constructed – civil & political rights, socio-economic rights, cultural rights, religious rights; individual and communal rights – and analyses the international treaties and conventions and judicial interpretations that have contributed to the evolution of human rights jurisprudence. The ultimate aim of the module is to ground students in the academic and policy debates in the dynamic field of international human rights law and practice, and to help students to develop the tools and thinking necessary to understand and apply the normative and doctrinal principles of human rights law, as well as to reflect critically on the possibilities and limits of human rights discourse.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate clear knowledge of the history, institutional structures, and conceptual underpinnings of  international human rights law and discourse;
display an in-depth understanding of the normative content of international human rights, and of the mechanics of their operation;
recognise when and how international human rights are relevant to contemporary issues; how they apply to global, regional, national and local situations;
critically evaluate the jurisprudence of relevant international human rights bodies, highlighting lacunae within the law and shortcomings in its implementation;
describe and reflect critically on key theories which dominate international human rights literature;
understand the ideological, geopolitical and economic contestations at play in international human rights discourse.

modules.moduleContent:
In this module, students study the role of law and policy in International investment relationships. In particular, students identify and examine the relevant legal principles, underlying rationale, policy and practices applicable to international investment, the diverse contexts international investment occurs and the assumptions that underpin such legal principles, policy, practices and investment contexts. Students will assess the role of bilateral and multilateral investment treaties, the competing arguments on the efficacy of foreign investment, the definitions of foreign investment and foreign investor, the sources of the law and principles applicable to foreign investment, the impact on investment of tension between principles of public and public law, the controls available to States and the role and effect of applicable dispute settlement mechanisms.

The module lecturer has discretion to change any aspect of this module in any academic year.
learningOutcomes: Discuss and evaluate theoretical and practical arguments for foreign investment
Outline and examine the sources of the law on foreign investment as well as the structure and content of investment treaties
Identify and evaluate the standards of treatment applicable to investors and investments
Critically examine the roles and impact of competing actors in foreign investment
Grasp and evaluate the efficacy of Entry and Establishment Rights and State Control in Investment law
Discuss the concept of responsibility of states for internationally wrongful acts in relation to international investment law
Evaluate legal, economic, democratic, ethical and human rights related issues thrown up by the investor state dispute settlement mechanism

modules.moduleContent: This module provides students with an introduction to the rationales, methods and different theoretical approaches to studying crime and criminal justice comparatively. Following discussion of these general issues, the module aims to broaden and deepen students’ critical awareness of crime rates and criminal justice models/procedures by locating Irish arrangements in a comparative context. Convergences and divergences in criminal justice and penal policy are also analysed through discussion of globalisation and its impact on comparative inquiry. This foundational knowledge is further built upon in the final part of the module through a discussion of case studies on topics such as global policing and counter-terrorism.

Contemporary Issues in Global Policing (Global Policing) undertakes a broad and critical look at the shared challenges for policing in different parts of the world.

Police forces across the globe are faced with similar and often complex ‘crime problems’. These include the role of the police in: 

• ‘fighting’ the ‘war on drugs’
• addressing gender-based violence
• negotiating challenges to police authority in the form of vigilantism 

Global Policing explores how these crime problems have emerged in different contexts and states.

Police forces across the globe also face similar organisational challenges in addressing crime problems. These include:

• questions about the democratic legitimacy of certain police tactics
• doubts about the evidence base for certain politically popular police operations
• concerns about the human rights implications flowing from police crime control practices

Global Policing examines these organisational challenges, and the policing philosophies that have emerged in response to them.

While in many ways police culture is institutionally closed, ideas about how to ‘do policing’ have proliferated in international debates among police practitioners. Global Policing explores these philosophies and strategies of policing, and how they have spread and influenced policing in different states.

Some of the globalised ‘crime problems’ which attract police attention have encouraged unprecedented levels of cooperation between police authorities in different jurisdictions. This has the potential to undermining traditional conceptions of sovereignty and the nation state. Other ‘crime problems’, such as the policing of migration, serve instead to reinforce these same ideas. Global Policing critically examines transnational policing through the lens of national sovereignty.

Topics include (but are not limited to):
1. Policing Frameworks and Philosophies (e.g. policing ‘by consent’ and ‘through human rights’)
2. Transnational Policing (e.g. Interpol & Europol)
3. The Politics of Crime Control
4. Rationing of Policing
5. Policing Complex Harms (e.g. gender-based violence and organised crime)
6. Policing in New, Developing, Post-colonial, and Post-Conflict states (e.g. policing the ‘war on drugs’ and vigilante policing)

Approach to teaching and learning in Global Policing:

This is an advanced degree module, and students are expected to come to class well-prepared, having read assigned materials.

The teaching and learning approach in this module will be a discussion seminar format. There are assigned materials each week, around which the seminar discussion is centred.


This module is taught by Dr Cian Ó Concubhair.
learningOutcomes: Describe key transnational policing institutions, their functions, and their relationship to state policing.
Describe, discuss, and evaluate the theoretical literature on global policing phenomena, such as policing the war on drugs, gender-based violence, and policing and vigilantes.
Explain and critically evaluate modern policing strategies, their origins, and their proliferation across borders.
Describe key challenges for policing in developing states, and how these relate to policing in advanced industrial economies.

modules.moduleContent:
Firstly, the module introduces environmental governance and law, focussing largely on international elements. This encompasses the fundamental underpinnings, including objectives, principles and core frameworks. 

Secondly, it introduces some of the core links between environmental matters, trade and business. Within international law, these are exemplified by WTO law including the Agreement on Agriculture and TRIPS, as well as Free Trade Agreements and for instance, exportation of norms through considering level playing fields. 

Finally, the module will focus on the role of business and corporations - considering issues such as monocultures and seed banks; GMOs; CSR and self-regulation.

This content module is subject to change depending on key developments occurring internationally or within the EU.

This Micro Credential is designed to facilitate participants to understand current concepts, policy and good practice relating to mediation. Participants will be introduced to the concept of mediation and the practical application of core skills and process. It will also introduce participants to the practice of mediation across a diverse range of situations, such as family; commercial; workplace; community; and peace-building. The course will provide participants with the knowledge and practical skills to develop and demonstrate, through assessment, professionally recognised mediation knowledge and skills.

This Micro-Credential is designed to facilitate participants to consider current concepts, policy and good practice relating to restorative practices in Ireland.
