Participants will be introduced to the concept of restorative practices as they fit with restorative justice and their practical application. It will also introduce participants to designs for the use of restorative practices across a diverse range of situations, such as workplaces, education, communities, prisons, youth, survivors of abuse. The course will provide participants with the practical tools to develop and implement best restorative practices in a variety of settings.
learningOutcomes: Critically  analyse  restorative  philosophy,  principles  and  practices  as  well  as  key concepts and issues in restorative practices and restorative justice.

Critically  discuss  some  of  the  key  theories  and  philosophical  approaches  in  the contemporary study and development of restorative practices and restorative justice -including  shame,  anger,  and  vulnerability  and  the  role  of  revenge,  apology  and forgiveness.
Critically analyse conflict theory and the principles and practices of mediation within restorative practices and restorative justice.
Develop  practice  skills  including  circle  processes,  restorative  conferences,  victim-offender dialogues.

Understand and reflect on the empirical study of restorative practices and restorative justice, focusing in particular on its impact on people and its application in different situations and environments including as a response to trauma, grief and adversity to build atrauma-informed approach to restorative practices.

Write, think and speak in a reflective and critical manner which highlights intellectual engagement  and  understanding  of  how  knowledge  is  constructed  in  restorative practices and restorative justice.
Directly  engage  in  completing  a  project  whereby  the  concepts  and  principles  of restorative  practice  is  applied  within  communities  and  organisations  to  implement change.

modules.moduleContent:
The goal of this module is to develop an understanding of the legal implications of technological advancement. This module will provide a survey of information technology law covering aspects of data protection, computer crime, intellectual property law, and internet regulation.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an in-depth knowledge of Information Technology Law;
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of the relevant law, summarise the main legal issues involved, and construct an opinion as to the legal position;
Display the ability to independently research legal questions;
Analyse the effectiveness and fairness of specific aspects of IT Law, and consider potential courses for reform;
Place legal issues in the appropriate economic, political, and social context;
Identify current areas of debate that are of importance to Information Technology Law and be able to assess competing arguments and adopt an original perspective.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: AN INTRODUCTION TO THE SURVEILLANCE SOCIETY
modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the area of surveillance studies, an exciting and interdisciplinary field of study that engages with the ways in which surveillance shapes, permeates, and impacts everyday life. Some topics that shape the study of surveillance centre on the management of everyday life, as well as spaces of exception, such as: gender, personal data, privacy, race, security, and terrorism. Students will engage with foundational theories, key concepts, and empirical studies of surveillance.

Almost every aspect of contemporary life is permeated by multiple forms of surveillance. These might be connected to security practices: such as airport security screening, to CCTV cameras, to ID cards. However, surveillance is increasingly occurring outside of the realm of security: from all kinds of data collection and online monitoring, to consumer loyalty programs, to fitness tracking devices. Many individuals willingly participate in surveillance, sacrificing their data and privacy for the seductive aspects of a given program or system. However, the constant collection of personal information has a profound effect on our social experiences, and our private lives. However, these systems form an information infrastructure that underpins and structures contemporary societies. Most profoundly, perhaps, is that while surveillance touches everyone, its hand is heaviest in communities already disadvantaged by their poverty, race, religion, ethnicity, and immigration status. Technology and stealth allow government watchers to remain unobtrusive when they wish to be so, but their blunter tools—stop-and-frisk, suspicion-less search, recruitment of snitches, compulsory questioning on intimate subjects—are conspicuous in the lives of those least empowered to object. From the mundane to the extraordinary, to domestic policing and international security, this course will cover the breadth of surveillance topics while providing foundational theory building in this interdisciplinary area.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: understand what surveillance is - and what it is not;
understand the varied forms of surveillance in contemporary and historical societies;
understand the different ways of conceptualizing and analyzing surveillance from a range of philosophical and social theoretical viewpoints;
understand the social relations around surveillance;
understand current trends and debates in the study of surveillance.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The placement module allows students to work part-time with an organisation to gain concrete experience of the realities of international and national law and/or criminology, as well as to develop their professional skills and experience of the workplace. Organisations providing placements are usually national NGOs or public authorities, and may vary each year. Students are supervised in their work by an individual within their placement organisation, as well as by an academic staff member from the School of Law and Criminology. Places on this module are limited, and are allocated on the basis of application and interview.
learningOutcomes: Research and prepare a written presentation on various topics;
Assess the importance of advocacy in the work of NGOs;
Make successful oral presentations;
Participate in simulation exercises.

modules.moduleContent: This practice-based module aims to provide students with an introduction to the creative methods and research techniques involved in the development of narrative screenplays for film and other screen media. Students will deepen their understanding of the mechanics of screen narrative and learn the practical skills necessary to package and pitch their scripts.
learningOutcomes: Identify the stages necessary for planning and producing a completed screenplay
Demonstrate familiarity with the practise and use of screenwriting conventions, narrative structures, and other devices in screen media
Comprehend the screenplay's function as a blueprint for screen media production and its role in the screen industries
Practise the time management skills necessary to produce a major piece of creative writing
Analyse their own and others’ work in a professional critical manner and effectively communicate their observations.
modules.notes: Use of scriptwriting software like Final Draft or Celtex

modules.moduleContent: From the earliest actualités to YouTube, factual filmmaking has encompassed a heterogeneity of practices that are linked by their supposed special relationship with the reality they represent. The ways in which these practices are interpreted, however, have relied on audiences’ reception of them, and definition of the term documentary remains open. The module will examine key historical developments and theoretical articulations of documentary as a form encompassing the first films through wartime propaganda, ethnographic film, city-symphonies, cinéma vérité/direct cinema and both more recent feature documentaries and a wide array of non-fiction television genres. It will consider the work of such documentarists and theorists as Dziga Vertov, Robert Flaherty, John Grierson, Leni Riefenstahl, Joris Ivens, Jean Rouch, Fred Wiseman, Errol Morris, Claude Lanzmann, Werner Herzog, Michael Moore, Nick Broomfield and Alex Gibney.
learningOutcomes: Recognise and discuss the key genres, styles, histories and industries of factual film-making
Locate documentary films within their production contexts
Apply key concepts and methods of film studies in the analysis of non-fiction film
Synthesise ideas and communicate these effectively in written contexts

modules.moduleContent: This module explores the relationships between advertising, audiences and media producers in order to critically examine the political economies of media production and consumption.  Changing landscapes of audience behaviour, media technologies, regulatory frameworks and economic structures will be explored with respect to a variety of media.
learningOutcomes: Identify and critically discuss the structural and economic relationships between contemporary media producers, advertisers and audiences
Apply a range of theoretical frameworks to the industrial and textual practices of advertising
•Synthesise and contrast key issues in the political economies of media and advertising industries
Communicate effectively in written formats

modules.moduleContent:
This module explores various dimensions of the academic research process through a series of workshops and seminars. Classes will explore the practical implications of methods and analytical approaches as experienced in the work of Department researchers. Students will use this module to identify the topic and primary site of their independent research project, design a viable research project and identify relevant and related secondary sources.

Note: 2.1 minimum standard on either MD231 or MD232 to undertake this module.
learningOutcomes: Recognise and negotiate key issues relating to the process of research development and practice
Identify and design a media or cultural research project
Identify, contrast and synthesise key theories and approaches to their preferred research topic
Articulate effectively a coherent, viable and achievable research project and a clear synthesis of key ideas

modules.moduleContent: While traditionally the medium for communicating philosophical ideas has been texts, we now increasingly encounter philosophical ideas in film. In this module students engage with key philosophical concepts related to questions around reality, personal identity, and self-consciousness via film discussion and analysis which will take place each week in the form of lectures and screenings throughout the module. Touching on philosophical questions such as the nature and perception of reality, the mind-body problem or substance dualism, the module presents students with the language and tools to understand and evaluate how films, especially sci-fi, reflect on or propose philosophical positions and debates.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate critical thinking and analytical skills through in-depth film analysis.
Develop the ability to convey complex philosophical ideas in a clear, concise, and engaging manner.
Recognize how films provide the formal framework to engage with philosophical questions.
Demonstrate critical reasoning skills by examining and challenging philosophical issues within the context of film.
Demonstrate the ability to apply theoretical concepts to practical scenarios.
Showcase teamwork and communication skills by actively participating in the collaborative production of a podcast.
Showcase the ability to convey complex ideas effectively through verbal and written documentation.

modules.moduleContent: This module will examine the history of mass media in Ireland from mid-19thC print culture to the early years of television.  It will examine the creation of a mass media audience, the development and reception of new media technologies and cultures, and the political economy of media industries in an Irish context.
learningOutcomes: Recognise and appraise the historical development of mass media in Ireland;
Apply and evaluate critical methods and theoretical frameworks in to the development of media technologies and cultures in Ireland;
Synthesise ideas and communicate these effectively in written contexts;
Develop and pursue independent research activities.

modules.moduleContent: This advanced practice module enables students with existing digital practice skills to critically engage with the theme of creative interactive computing, developing interactive digital applications, installations or performances that are multidisciplinary in nature and underpinned by the principles of human-computer interaction (HCI). Students will experiment with cutting edge digital technologies and techniques within a sophisticated theoretical methodological context. They will be introduced to established software and hardware, digital interactive resources, and platforms but will also be encouraged to experiment with emerging technologies that extend or build upon their existing digital skills. Lectures will explore best practice of interactive, performative, and networked media along with theoretical frameworks of digital performance, mixed reality, locative media, and the mediated city. Case studies of interactive applications, installations, and performances as well as ethnographic studies conducted on them will be presented and discussed during lectures.
learningOutcomes: Produce a digital application, installation, or performance underpinned by an appropriate digital media theoretical framework;
Identify and experiment with established and emerging digital technologies in their creative practice;
Critically situate their practice within an appropriate theoretical framework and methodology;
Work independently to solve problems in the creation of projects.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module presents key theoretical concepts, approaches, and research questions in the fields of Media and Cultural Studies to enable students to develop a thorough understanding of the disciplines’ conceptual underpinnings as well as to inform research undertaken for MA dissertation and PhD thesis projects. 
 
Students will explore a range of media theories from humanities and social sciences, engaging with their historical and scholarly contexts. Exploring and critiquing central arguments and critical debates in the field, students develop a repertoire of theoretical frames for media studies inquiry.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate familiarity with the central arguments and historical and scholarly contexts of a range of media studies theories from humanities and social sciences.
Synthesise, appraise, and/or critique theoretical frameworks from humanities and social sciences.
Apply media studies theories appropriately and effectively to specific research questions, including those relevant to MA dissertations and PhD theses.
Engage in effective scholarly argument and debate, both orally and in written formats.

modules.moduleContent: This module will examine the history of mass media in Ireland from mid-19thC print culture to the early years of television. It will examine the creation of a mass media audience, the development and reception of new media technologies and cultures, and the political economy of media industries in an Irish context.

modules.moduleName: MANAGING THE MNE: THE HUMAN EXPERIENCE
modules.moduleContent: This module gives students a comprehensive understanding of the key issues encountered by multinational enterprises in the management of stakeholders, strategy and structure, knowledge, policies and practices and people in an international and/or global context. The focus is on international managers, the decisions they face in the multinational firm and the choices they face in overcoming various challenges. Students will analyse issues from the perspective of the international manager in order to prepare them for a career in an international environment. Classes will be structured on active discussion, application of theory and practice through in-class activities, experiential learning and analysis of real-life scenarios. This course begins by setting the context for the multinational firm. Various theoretical frameworks will be introduced, and students will analyse them by comparing different stakeholder perspectives and the implications of different decisions facing the international manager. Finally, students will engage the knowledge they have acquired throughout the module by experiencing, first-hand, the challenges faced by international managers in the multinational firm.
learningOutcomes: Examine the workings of a multinational enterprise from the perspectives of external stakeholders, headquarters and subsidiaries, international managers and employees;
Appreciate the various dualities facing multinational enterprises when entering different countries and contexts and the strategies that firms employ when managing these challenges;

Critically assess (and experience) the difficulties faced by international managers in planning, decision-making, negotiating, and balancing stakeholder demands in an international and/or global context, and the skills needed to meet these challenges;
Evaluate various theoretical perspectives on multinational firms; and
Apply key theories and concepts in practice.

modules.moduleContent:
This module develops participants awareness of strategic management tools and concepts. The module covers classic material from business policy and planning through to contemporary approaches. The module content is heavily underpinned by relevant academic literature and successful participants can expect to complete the course with a thorough knowledge of strategy and practice. 

Working in groups and individually, participants will develop their capabilities in strategic planning, analysis, formulation and implementation. Capabilities to communicate and participate effectively in a strategy making process will be developed through class interaction, group work, and written and verbal presentations.
learningOutcomes: Define and describe fundamental concepts and tools for strategic analysis, strategic planning, and implementation of strategy, providing relevant examples for each concept and tool;
Evaluate academic literature in the strategy field and be aware of its application in practice;
Analyse an organisational problem (both at the stage of strategy formulation and at the stage of strategy implementation), using strategy concepts and tools;
Compare and contrast possible solutions to an organisational problem, building on available evidence from reliable scientific and practice-oriented sources; and
Communicate strategic initiatives effectively to decision-makers at different levels of organisational hierarchy.

modules.moduleContent:
This module explores how to lead and how to follow efficiently and effectively in the modern diverse, multicultural and technologically-changing world. The content of this module covers key theoretical perspectives on leadership – from classic to contemporary ones - and discusses their strengths, weaknesses and practical implications. The module also explores the challenges contemporary leaders face due to changes in the society and economy that reshape relationships at work. The module also discusses how leaders can deal with these challenges.

The module explores core elements of the leadership process, such as leader’s characteristics and behaviours, influence processes, role of followers and relationships with them.
learningOutcomes: Critically appraise extant leadership concepts and identify the conditions under which each is likely to be effective;
Discuss the challenges contemporary organisations face and explain how these challenges impact leadership;

Apply these concepts to the analysis of real- life situations, identify potential problems related to leadership issues and develop informed solutions on how to improve leadership capacity in a particular context; and
Appraise their own leadership and followership preferences, styles and behaviours and discuss how to adjust one’s own leading or following behavior in order to fit better to the given context.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module offers students the opportunity to integrate their academic study with the action learning experience of work placement. Placement seeks to bring the concepts, tools and competencies developed on the MSc programme into the realities of work place implementation. Students develop an understanding of the professional and practical competencies and skills required by employers. During the placement period, students will both apply their knowledge from the programme to, and gain new insights into, the practicalities of work life. This learning is supported in this module by taught classes, in-group discussions and exercises which are designed to promote students’ self-reflection on the relationships between theories and competencies central to career development, career self-management, and their work placement. Students are encouraged to critically analyse their own career capital and their career management strategies & priorities.

**Students must be registered for the full time Master's programme. Entry to the Placement Project is a competitive process and students must also complete and pass an interview with the employer/have secured a suitable work placement position. The Placement Summer Pathway is limited to 50 students.
learningOutcomes: Apply intelligent career theory to their own experience in the work place;
Deconstruct, reflect upon and appraise their respective career capital;
Evaluate theoretical concepts related to their and others’ careers in action;
Discuss practical career experiences through different career theories and concepts; and
Appraise their continuing learning requirements in line with their future career-oriented goals.

modules.moduleContent:
The academic dissertation is one of the ‘summer pathways’ available to students undertaking an MSc degree within the School of Business. An academic dissertation provides students with the opportunity to develop and demonstrate highly specialised knowledge which is at the forefront of an area of work or study, as the basis for original thinking and research. An academic dissertation is distinguished from other forms of writing by its focus on analysing and understanding situations via a theoretical lens. An academic dissertation evaluates literature, makes comparisons, seeks explanations and offers conclusions all of which can be used to better understand the case in context, as well as the theory in general. Academic dissertations generally involve some practical primary data gathering by students, but can also centre on secondary sources.

The Academic Dissertation summer pathway is best suited to those considering an academic or research career. Acceptance onto this pathway will, therefore, be based on a research proposal to be developed by the student. Places are limited.

Students completing an academic dissertation will: identify a suitable research topic; set research objectives; locate, organise and evaluate the relevant authoritative literature; devise an appropriate research methodology; gather and analyse primary data; and offer relevant conclusions, recommendations and implications for the theory and future research.
learningOutcomes: Independently manage a sizable research project to meet project milestones, resolve academic and procedural complexities and manage communications with relevant stakeholders;
Write a literature review using a systematic process of collection, synthesis and appraisal;
Design an appropriate research methodology;
Gather and analyse primary and secondary data relevant to a research question;
Discuss findings in the context of the academic literature and the broader business context; and
Apply and evaluate theory in a business context.

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to enhance students’ appreciation and application of Career Planning and Development (CPD) activities. The module guides students to direct and elevate their personal CPD through active, formative and reflective learning organised around four themes. These themes stem from competencies central to career development namely ‘I am Ambitious’, ‘I am a Citizen’, ‘I am Resilient’ and ‘I am Agile’. Students identify and undertake a range of CPD activities under each theme that target their personal CPD priorities. Specifically, the CPDs that students propose should be consistent with their own career development needs, and with the learning outcomes of the module and should realistically advance student competencies beyond their current level. These activities are pursued outside of lecture hours and will be approved by the module lecturer who provides feedback on initial reflective learning logs. The module also covers HRM topics relating to career development including the ‘Future of Work’, organisational career structures, total reward and negotiations. Upon completion of the module students submit a reflective learning CPD portfolio which documents their competence progression and their learnings regarding their future career aspirations and plan.
learningOutcomes: Review the evolution and changing nature of work, careers and the breadth of employment models within the labour market from ‘full-time permanent’ to the ‘gig economy’;
Critically assess own competencies objectively and relative to career aspirations;
Select and activate, through the CPD activities, a personal career planning and development strategy;
Explain the purpose and benefits of engaging in planned and reflective career planning and development; and
Convincingly articulate and demonstrate career interests and professional competence.

modules.moduleContent:
** Commencing September 2025 **

Many organisations now recognise that brands are one of their most valuable assets. When marketers develop marketing strategies, more positive outcomes may arise when their product or service has a strong brand than if that same product or service did not have a strong brand. Building a strong brand is therefore very important for products and services, in business sectors including luxury goods and services, and fast-moving consumer goods, and even for building personal brands.

The differential marketing effects that can be directly attributed to a brand are known as customer-based brand equity (CBBE). This course explores the design and implementation of marketing strategies from the perspective of CBBE (Keller, 1993). CBBE asserts that the value of a brand resides in the mind of the consumer. The course examines marketing strategies that can be harnessed to develop and enhance CBBE, it presents methods for measuring consumer mindset, and it provides tools to build and sustain brands over time.
learningOutcomes: An understanding of customer-based brand equity and the importance of creating a strong brand.
An understanding of the role of brand elements in building customer-based brand equity.
An insight into the contribution of marketing campaigns to building customer-based brand equity.
A greater awareness of the relationship between integrated marketing communications and brand building.
An ability to recommend an appropriate research method to capture customer mindset about brands.
An insight into brand strategies, managing brand portfolios, and leveraging strong brands over time.

modules.moduleContent: Modern business analytics is not only concerned with answering the "what happened" business questions through reporting. Rather, it draws probabilities of "what might happen" allowing organizations to seize opportunities or take precautionary measures to avoid risks. Problems such as predicting customer churns, workforce turnover, and sales predictions are amongst examples of predictive analytics. This module will equip students with necessary concepts and techniques from artificial intelligence and machine learning to understand and apply predictive analytics with hands-on experience. Students will also develop critical skills to avoid pitfalls of predictive models, properly evaluate and validate their performance, and interpret their results.
learningOutcomes: Build and understanding of machine learning and artificial intelligence and their increasing role in modern business decision making;
Understand and apply predictive analytics techniques such as regression, classification, nearest neighbour models, and deep learning on real-world datasets;
Demonstrate knowledge of issues such as bias and over-fitting, as well as evaluation methods of predictive analytics approaches;
Critically choose suitable predictive analytics technique for given business problems.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: INTRODUCTION TO SUSTAINABLE ENTERPRISE

This module addresses the core functions and process of operations and supply chain management including the world class manufacturing, world class business, extended resource planning systems, just-in-time and lean business. This module aims to ensure students understand the role of the operations function within an organisation and the outward impact operations and supply chain management has on all aspects of the business.
learningOutcomes: Explain and critique the strategic importance of operations and supply chain management in the global competitive business environment;
Analyse and discuss the interaction between operations and supply chain management and other functional areas of business;
Critically evaluate the role that quantitative and qualitative techniques can play as a decision aiding tool, as well as their limitations;

Demonstrate and employ important workplace skills (e.g. co-operation, teamwork, meeting deadlines, problem solving, project management, presenting professional written and oral case study reports) in case study work;
Explain the role of technology in operations and supply chain management and its impact on the different elements of operations and supply chain management.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module focusses on how organisations deploy marketing resources to effectively communicate their product and service offering to consumers. Marketing today is not just online but integrated, to a large extent, with offline marketing. This module will emphasise the centrality and breadth of consumption in our lives. Students will be challenged to reflect on their own experiences as consumers.
learningOutcomes: Explain how marketing management is affected by the marketing environment;
Explain the role of marketing in creating, communicating and delivering customer value;
Explain and critique key models and approaches used in customer and industry analysis and in the management of the marketing mix;
Formulate and apply data-driven approaches to managing efficiency and effectiveness of marketing activities;
Illustrate how consumers make purchasing decisions;
Identify and explain perceptual processes that underly consumer behaviour;
Understand how consumers form attitudes and what role these play in consumer behaviour.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module explores how to lead and how to follow efficiently and effectively in the modern diverse, multicultural and technologically-changing world. The content of this module covers key theoretical perspectives on leadership – from classic to contemporary ones - and discusses their strengths, weaknesses and practical implications. The module also explores the challenges contemporary leaders face due to changes in the society and economy that reshape relationships at work. The module also discusses how leaders can deal with these challenges.

The module explores core elements of the leadership process, such as leader’s characteristics and behaviours, influence processes, role of followers and relationships with them.
learningOutcomes: Critically appraise extant leadership concepts and identify the conditions under which each is likely to be effective;
Discuss the challenges contemporary organisations face and explain how these challenges impact leadership;

Apply these concepts to the analysis of real- life situations, identify potential problems related to leadership issues and develop informed solutions on how to improve leadership capacity in a particular context;
Appraise their own leadership and followership preferences, styles and behaviours and discuss how to adjust one’s own leading or following behavior in order to fit better to the given context.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the theory of translation by exploring historical and contemporary debates that shape and influence translation practice in Europe and further afield. Each week is dedicated to a different approach, and will consider relevant case studies in different languages, geographies, and historical periods. 

In addition to building on theoretical understandings of translation, this module will also provide students with an opportunity to work on their research and presentation skills, as well as translation analysis and criticism.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a sound understanding of key concepts, norms, and issues in Translation Studies, as well as theories of translation
Apply theory in an informed manner in order to analyse translations and specific approaches to translation
Describe the socio-cultural, political, and historical evolution of translation theory and practice
Reflect on their own practice as translators
Complete research-based tasks relating to translation theory.

modules.moduleContent:
Students will be expected to arrive at a suitable dissertation topic after consultation with a faculty supervisor. Thereafter, s/he will be expected to complete a dissertation of up to 20,000 words, including footnotes. 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate competence and skills necessary to complete and defend an appropriate research proposal
Demonstrate nuanced exploration of the research question and completion of a thorough literature review
Analyse academic literature and draw appropriate conclusions based on the evidence
Demonstrate competence to produce a coherent, erudite dissertation supported by strong evidence and a thorough analysis

modules.moduleContent:
This course will enable students to acquire a thorough knowledge of the theory and the law of human rights protection in Europe through the workings of both the European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) and the EU’s Charter of Fundamental Rights as well as the relationship between both systems. The course includes an introduction to the ECHR and the European Court of Human Rights with a particular emphasis on the methods of interpretation used by the Court. Students will additionally study selected substantive areas of the law of the ECHR before embarking on a study of fundamental rights protection in the European Union. In that part of the course, students will engage with the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights and learn to appreciate the differences between the two fundamental rights regimes.

By the end of the course, students should have a thorough knowledge and understanding of the main institutions and instruments of human rights protection in Europe, with a specific emphasis on the European Convention of Human Rights and the Court’s case law, the system of fundamental rights protection in the European Union and the interaction between the two. Students should also have developed critical analytical skills as well as improved skills in the presentation of reasoned argument both orally and in written work through engaging with the case law of the European Court of Human Rights and the issues which emerge from the Court’s role in protecting human rights in Europe.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: * Identify, define and critically analyse the core doctrinal concepts of the European Court of Human Rights and substantive concepts of the ECHR.
* Demonstrate the advanced understanding of the themes and trends in the case law;
* Apply rules and standards of ECHR law to real life situations
* Demonstrate an appreciation of contemporary debates around the European Convention on Human Rights;
* Demonstrate an advanced understanding the differences in the two systems of human rights protection in Europe and critically analyse their interaction;
* Use both clear and eloquent written language and clear and confident oral presentation to communicate analysis of European human rights law.
* Use a variety of legal and non-legal secondary literature and other media to support analysis and advocacy points.

modules.moduleContent: An understanding of the Irish legal system and core legal topics within that system -such as contract law, employment law, company law and EU law -is increasingly relevant to those in non-law careers. Reflecting  this,  administrators,  policymakers  and  technical  personnel  in  a  variety  of  industries  are now expected to have a foundation of legal knowledge. Maynooth University’s Certificate  in  the Foundations of Irish Law is, therefore, designed to be of interest to those in, or aspiring to enter, a career  in  fields  such  as  government,  administration,  and  the  social  sciences  and  humanities  more generally. It will also be of interest to those considering a degree in law in the future. The  aim  of  this  course  is  to  provide  students  with  an  understanding  of  the  fundamentals  of  law  in Ireland. Students will study the foundations of the Irish legal system, a range of foundational legal topics and key legal concepts that are relevant to those topics. Students will also be equipped with an understanding  of  legal  research  and  writing.  During  this  course,  students  will  learn  from  subject specialists from Maynooth University’s Department of Law. Students will also have an opportunity to engage with industry speakers.

This module introduces students to the field of international humanitarian law, i.e. the law that applies during armed conflict situations. It provides a discussion and analysis of the history of this branch of law, from its first manifestations in early religions and ancient civilisations, through the adoption of the Geneva Conventions in 1949, and up to current attempts to regulate lethal autonomous weapons. It provides a critique of the different legal regimes of jus in bello and jus ad bellum, and analyses the general principles of international humanitarian law, i.e. distinction, proportionality, humanity and military necessity. It focuses on both the methods and means of warfare, as well as the conduct of hostilities and provides a discussion of relevant legal treaties on these issues, including the Geneva Conventions of 1949 and their Additional Protocols of 1977. The module also seeks to evaluate the application of these rules to case studies and scenarios and to critically analyse how international humanitarian law is enforced.

The ultimate aim of this module is to provide students will an appreciation of the international humanitarian law framework, the challenges in implementing this framework in conflict situations and the gaps in the current legal regime.
learningOutcomes: Discuss and evaluate the role and nature of IHL
Critically examine the historical background to the development of IHL
Critically inspect the provisions of the Geneva Conventions and Additional Protocols
Analyse and discuss the criteria for distinguishing between categories of conflict
Critically examine the rules governing the treatment of combatants and prisoners of war
Explain and apply the rules governing the conduct of hostilities

Explain and evaluate the protection of civilians under IHL. Discuss and evaluate the relevance of IHL to peace support operations. Explain the distinction between international humanitarian law and international human rights law

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides an introduction to key theories underlying the field of criminology against the backdrop of criminal justice policies and processes.  It investigates the nature and origins of the discipline of criminology, and provides an insight into the complex trends and theoretical paradigms which shape the study of crime. A broad overview of the operation of the criminal justice system is provided, and its functioning considered in the context of criminological theory.

This module is compulsory for all MA students who do not have a strong background in criminological theory (defined as a 2:2 grade or higher in an undergraduate criminology theory module). It is also optional for other MA students and for LLM Global Legal Studies students.

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Explain the historical development of the discipline of criminology and appraise basic criminological queries relating to the meaning and scope of crime and its study.
Describe and analyse critically the theories which have dominated the field of criminology historically, and compare and contrast competing ideologies.
Display an understanding of criminal justice policy and procedure.
Apply criminological theory to a study of the criminal justice system in order to demonstrate an appreciation of the cultural and political context within which that system operates.
Consider and recommend proposals for reform in the area of criminal justice policy in Ireland.

modules.moduleName: THE RIGHTS OF MINORITIES AND INDIGENOUS PEOPLES
modules.moduleContent:
The course introduces students to the legal framework concerning minorities and indigenous peoples. It presents an analysis of the protection of these groups from a legal, sociological and political perspective and explores the challenges to the protection of these rights under international, regional and national laws. In particular, it focuses on Article 27 of the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights which seeks to protect minority groups and the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples which is the latest legal initiative which seeks to protect the rights of indigenous peoples. It compares the protection of minority rights to the protection of indigenous peoples under the international legal framework. It analyses the development of the legal protection of these groups through the jurisprudence of UN and regional bodies, such as the Human Rights Committee, the Inter-American Commission and Inter-American Court on Human Rights and the African Court on Human and Peoples’ Rights. In addition it analyses the work of the UN Special Procedures in relation to these groups and assesses how these groups interacted with the universal periodic review mechanism of the Human Rights Council. It analyses the right to self-determination of these groups and assesses specific topics such as minority membership and the concept of free prior and informed consent in the context of the protection of indigenous peoples.

This course will be taught only if a sufficient number of students enrol. Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Undertake research the documentation and jurisprudence on minority rights;
Identify and explain the core legal framework pertaining to minority rights and the rights of indigenous peoples at an international, regional and domestic level;
Discuss and evaluate the scope and content of the rights of minorities and of minority groups;
Critique the human rights legal framework with regard to minorities and indigenous peoples.

modules.moduleContent:
Module objectives: To acquire the legal foundations of the trade relations at an international stage.
Module content: The internationalization of domestic economies has been in constant evolution for several decades. The pace of the development of exchanges between countries is now intense and leads to the drafting of legal rules to be enforced by different trade actors. This legislative corpus which is taking shape nowadays plays a major role. Without being exhaustive, the proposed courses aim at enabling students in Master 1 to discover the major principles of law which rule International Trade Law through an updated summary.
Several points will be presented: the originality of this law which lies mainly in the right choice of applicable rules but also in the existence of a special legislation as to litigation.
Besides, the institutional actors of International Trade will be studied, the WTO being at the forefront.

Finally, the legal rules as to international trade operations will be essentially taken into account thanks to the Vienna Convention considered as the reference in this domain.
learningOutcomes: Survey the law applicable to international trade.

modules.moduleContent: Education litigation is becoming increasingly common in Ireland. This practical module focuses on a wide range of potential areas of litigation, including but not limited to admissions, child protection, codes of behaviour, data protection, employment law, equality law, special educational needs and tort law. It will also critically examine the major judgments of the Irish courts pertaining to education and discuss the impact of the law on almost every aspect of the day-to-day running of schools. The module will be of particular interest to principals, deputy principals, members of boards of management and aspiring school leaders.
learningOutcomes: Identify and examine the main statutory provisions relating to schools and consider their implications for school practice;
Describe and explain the key principles of Education Law;
Analyse primary and secondary law, case law and policy publications relating to schools and evaluate their effect on practice;
Identify child protection issues;
Examine the legal issues relating to special educational needs;
Explain and discuss issues pertaining to admissions policies;
Critically evaluate the most pressing legal issues affecting schools;

modules.moduleContent: In an increasingly globalised society, the health of populations is no longer majorly determined by factors that remain within the control of national governments. Infectious diseases spread rapidly across borders as a result of global transit networks. Global industrialisation and urbanisation degrades human health through its impact upon the natural environment. Multinational corporations engage in coordinated transnational lobbying to prevent regulation of their health-harmful products, while global trade agreements increase the availability and affordability of these products. International institutions and their member states have a responsibility to collectively act to control these global threats to health, but agreeing upon the appropriate targets for action and the mechanisms to employ has so far proven extremely challenging. Law should certainly play a role in improving global health and promoting global health justice, however this must be part of a wider governance approach to global health issues. This modules gives students the opportunity to explore and debate the global dimensions of health and the extent to which law can usefully contribute to mitigating the factors that threaten it.
learningOutcomes: Describe the forces of globalization that impact upon world population health
Understand the normative foundations for global health law
Identify and critically analyse relevant sources of global health law and the institutions that produce it.
Demonstrate an appreciation of contemporary challenges in global health law.

modules.moduleContent:
Students will undertake the research and writing of a dissertation (20,000 words including footnotes) on a topic of their choice. Students will be assigned a supervisor who will guide them on the research and writing process. 

Any aspect of this module may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Complete an appropriate research proposal.
Identify a focussed research question and a clear line of argument in response to that question.
Critically analyse sources, including relevant academic literature, and draw evidence-based conclusions.
Write coherently and persuasively, using appropriate scholarly conventions.

modules.moduleContent:
Expected Course Content-Year 1:                          

Introduction au droit (6 credits)                     
Droit des personnes (6 credits)                        
Global Contemporary issues * (3 credits)               
Histoire du droit    (3 credits)                       
Histoire constitutionnelle française (6 credits)       
Legal Method 1*                 (3 credits)            
Droit constitutionnel       (6 credits)               
International and European Relations* (6 credits)      
Protection internationale des droits fondamentaux (6 credits)                       
Institutions administratives   (3 credits)            
Institutions juridictionnelles   (3 credits)           
Introduction to Common Law: Countries and Legal Systems *     (2 credits)         
Introduction to Common Law: Constitutional Law *  (2 credits) 
Legal method 2 *     (3 credits)                         
Introduction to the Irish Legal System *  (2 credits)  

Total credits for the year: 60

* Delivered in English
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an in-depth knowledge and understanding of the fundamental areas of legal study sufficient to immediately enter professional training, in Ireland and France.
Demonstrate a broad skill base in terms of presentation, reasoning and analytical skills which is attractive to employers.
Critically assess theories, concepts and methodological approaches relevant to all aspects of contemporary legal inquiry in Europe.
Critically evaluate law and policy in Ireland and France, from an informed international perspective.
Demonstrate research skills for applied use in the field of law, and in social research more generally.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO MEDIA AND CULTURAL STUDIES I
modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to foundational media and cultural studies concepts and theories through a focus on the centrality of narrative and storytelling to mediated communication and cultural texts. This module concentrates on an introduction to forms of textual analysis.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a critical understanding of the importance of narrative/storytelling to mediated communication across genres, media platforms, and exemplary texts.
Apply relevant theoretical perspectives and evidence through written analysis, and reflect on the quality and adequacy of this written work for the specified writing formats.
Relate theoretical concepts and analytical frameworks covered to their own media production practice.
Communicate effectively in academic contexts.

modules.moduleContent: This module engages students in exploring a variety of important questions regarding conceptualizations of gender in the analysis of media texts, audiences and industry practices. They will examine the history of change and continuity that defines various waves of gendered and gendering dimensions of media discourses and creative practice. Students will explore key ideas used to describe features of the relationship between gender and media in production, policy, representation, audience engagement and the place of gender in the discipline of media and cultural studies.
learningOutcomes: Critically discuss the structural dimensions of gender with regard to media production, policy, audiences, representations and within the discipline of media and cultural studies
Identify and critically appraise conceptualizations of gender in a variety of theoretical, production and research contexts
Contextualise the concept of gender within the history of the discipline
Communicate effectively in written formats

modules.moduleContent: Media & Cultural Theory 2 continues the exploration of media and cultural theory begun in semester 1. It introduces students to key theoretical and critical approaches to identity in media cultures, mediated community and collectivity and theories of media publics. The module examines concepts developed in different media-historical periods and introduces key theories and theorists of the contemporary media environment.
learningOutcomes: Identify a range of theoretical and methodological frameworks and place them within disciplinary context.
Apply theoretical perspectives to interpret media and cultural phenomena.
Synthesise and critique theoretical frameworks.
Communicate effectively in written format.

modules.moduleContent: This practice module will advance the student’s knowledge and understanding of a working TV studio and the roles required to produce studio programming. This module will address the different genres of production. Students will generate ideas, pitch, draft scripts, and source talent for these productions. Students will record “as-live” television programmes and cognitively learn by evaluating their role within the productions through critical reflection.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate competence in technical television production skills.
Conceptualise, plan, develop and run a TV production in a specific genre.
Demonstrate the ability to creatively collaborate and contribute to a team production in a professional manner.
Critically analyse contemporary television production formats and genres.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module will examine the emerging role of new digital platforms in the media sector and how it can be used by inhouse content creators to create and underpin marketing and brand storytelling across the digital shop window and beyond.  The module will be practice based and will develop organisational, creative skills and media production within a commercial environment with a focus on short form video.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the role of short form video content within online media branding and marketing campaigns.
Conceptualise, plan, pitch, and create a multimodal digital marketing campaign in a team.
Analyse and critique digital storytelling in media marketing.
Demonstrate an understanding of different media platforms for targeting different audiences.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module explores the context, structures and dynamics of the contemporary media and cultural industries in Ireland. Through a series of lectures with industry professionals, students will develop an understanding of the requirements of various industries both for new entrants and for those developing sustainable careers. Students will also be encouraged to develop a career focus through classes lead by the Career Development Office. A final dimension is critical appraisal of media and cultural industry dynamics in seminars focusing on studies of media production, cultural work, media economics and media industry policy.
learningOutcomes: Identify and critique key trends in the organization of labour, administration and technologies in contemporary Irish cultural and media industries
Place Irish media and cultural industry dynamics in social and economic context
Reflect upon and become active in developing career pathways in these sectors
Generate a compelling CV targeting these industries
Communicate effectively in both professional and academic language

modules.moduleContent: In this module students engage in the development and production phases for a major short film project. Students will present ideas for shorts in the form of pitch presentations and treatments, they will work through script development in the pre-production stage to organize locations, cast and crew. Hands on workshops in scriptwriting, camera operations, lighting and sound will complement the key stages of short film development covered in lectures.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate skills in scriptwriting and development for a self-initiated short film project
Evidence specialization in practice within script writing and film-making operations
Collaborate effectively in a functioning production team
Identify the two first phases of production, production roles and the creative skills of writing and developing short film group production

modules.moduleContent: This module will examine the origins, form and economic structures of podcasts.  It will explore the relationship between podcasting and traditional broadcast radio, as well as discussing it as an important example of convergence culture in which radio and digital culture combine.  Throughout the module, topics covered will include the development of podcasting genres, the political economy of commercial podcasting production as opposed to its incorporation into public service broadcasting, and the nature of audience relationships and interactions with podcasts.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of contemporary research on podcasting and radio structures and cultures
Apply theoretical frameworks to key areas of podcast production and reception
Demonstrate independent research skills
Demonstrate effective written communication skills

modules.moduleContent: This module explores the production of live and studio-based radio. Emphasis is placed on exploring the roles of the producer, presenter and researcher across various genres of production such as news, sports reporting, magazine and debate formats. All students will work on key skills such as researching, interviewing, scripting and editorial decision making. The practice elements of the module enable students to learn by doing in all areas of radio journalism and production. The emphasis on critical as well as creative approaches to radio ensures that students gain a capacity to reflect on their practice. Students will work in teams, and learn how to cooperate and negotiate in order to agree creative content as well as to meet strict deadlines This module also provides students with audio material for a show-reel for future use in the radio and podcasting sectors.
learningOutcomes: Manage the organization of live radio production.
Make editorial decisions.
Demonstrate skills in script-writing, on-air presentation, interviewing and research.
Reflect critically on the practices and process of live radio production.

modules.moduleContent:
This practice-based module aims to provide students with the creative methods and research techniques involved in the development of narrative screenplays for film, television, and the web. As part of the process, students will deepen their understanding of the mechanics of screen narrative. They will also learn the practical skills necessary to package and pitch their scripts. Class meetings are discussion based and include screenings, script analysis, and workshops. Students should expect to take active part in weekly discussions, to offer constructive critiques of each other’s work, and to participate in script readings and other elements of the workshop throughout the term.

In addition to the workshop aspects of the course, students will develop material through a series of writing exercises and assignments. Moreover, they will analyse and discuss different industry approaches and guiding principles for successful screenwriting.
learningOutcomes: Plan and produce a completed long script, or series of linked short scripts for screen media, including effective manipulation of image, text, and sound;
Appreciate and deploy a critical approach to screen narrative and the role of conventions, narrative structures, and other devices in the production of a successful screenplay;
Engage with scripts both creatively and professionally as components of screenwriting and industry practice;
Manage time effectively to produce a major piece of creative writing.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This advanced practice module enables students with existing digital practice skills to critically engage with the theme of creative interactive computing, developing interactive digital applications, installations or performances that are multidisciplinary in nature and underpinned by the principles of human-computer interaction (HCI). Students will experiment with cutting edge digital technologies and techniques within a sophisticated theoretical methodological context. They will be introduced to established software and hardware, digital interactive resources, and platforms but will also be encouraged to experiment with emerging technologies that extend or build upon their existing digital skills. Lectures will explore best practice of interactive, performative, and networked media along with theoretical frameworks of digital performance, mixed reality, locative media, and the mediated city. Case studies of interactive applications, installations, and performances as well as ethnographic studies conducted on them will be presented and discussed during lectures.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module explores the international business environment and the key challenges for managing across multiple markets. Students will examine differences in the institutional context of various international markets. They will acquire knowledge of different entry modes (exporting, FDI, licensing, etc.), appreciate the characteristics of successful global managers and contrast the strategy of international expansion between small and large companies.
learningOutcomes: Apply key frameworks for analysing the international business environment;
Critically assess the challenges of managing business relationships and negotiations across cultures and contexts;
Discuss and critique the alternative modes and strategies for international expansion;
Recognise the characteristics of successful SMEs, EMNEs and MNEs; and
Examine the applicability of key theories in solving real-world international business problems.

modules.moduleContent: The overall aim of this module is to enable participants to understand the key elements of marketing and in particular how they apply in the digital arena. In particular the module concentrates on the student as consumer and increasing awareness of the role of marketing in business. The nature of consumer marketing is becoming more and more digital with integrated campaigns becoming the norm. The module considers the implications of key marketing concepts such as the marketing mix, market segmentation, marketing planning, multiple market channels, and integrated marketing communications for the current market environment. While the key focus of the module is consumer marketing, content will also cover business to business applications and international marketing applications.
learningOutcomes: Explain how consumers / customers make purchasing decisions;
Evaluate socio-cultural and psychological influences on consumer behaviour;
Describe what market segmentation is and apply segmentation based on different characteristics;
Explain the important elements within the marketing mix (the basic “tools” of marketing); and
Adapt the traditional tools of marketing for a digital world.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: During the past decades established companies have been increasingly challenged by entrepreneurial companies through the commercialisation of new technologies as well as through the design of new, innovative business models. Against this background, this module enables students to form their critical judgements about the specific advantages and challenges entrepreneurial as well as incumbent companies face in their innovation activities. Moreover, students will elaborate on how entrepreneurial companies as well as incumbents can discover innovation opportunities, capture value based on their innovation activities, and become leaders of technological change. In doing so, students apply the concepts, tools, and frameworks discussed in this module to real-world innovation challenges and reflect on how companies can advance their innovation activities.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the threats and opportunities related to innovation for established as well as entrepreneurial companies;
Apply innovation management concepts/tools/frameworks to the analysis of companies’ innovation activities;
Critically assess entrepreneurial and established companies’ innovation activities using academic and non-academic sources of information;
Evaluate how companies can facilitate entrepreneurial thinking and acting in order to increase their innovativeness; and
Develop recommendations for the improvement of innovation activities and their implementation.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: More than ever, companies must pay attention to intensifying competition, the emergence of new competitors, increasing globalization, and technological developments. While these developments threaten existing business models they provide, at the same time, many opportunities for the development of new businesses and sustainable growth. In order to capitalize on these opportunities, companies need to develop appropriate strategies that deviate from established ways of thinking and problem solving. Against this background, strategy consultants will share their expertise from real-world consultancy projects and introduce students to different approaches to successfully manage consultancy projects. Students develop their consultancy skills and apply their knowledge of concepts, frameworks, and research methods obtained throughout the programme to develop alternative strategic recommendations for a specific business problem. Students evaluate the benefits and drawbacks of their recommendations and develop suggestions for their implementation. The business problem students need to address in their consultancy project may be specified by a client company.
learningOutcomes: Manage a consulting project and deal with different stakeholders;
Use theoretical knowledge and apply appropriate research methods to a real-world business problem;
Develop alternative strategic recommendations for a specific business problem and assess their advantages and disadvantages;
Develop recommendations for the implementation of the recommended strategies; and
Present their analyses and recommendations in a professional, concise, and accurate manner.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Without sales, there is no income and without that there is no future for an organisation. This module focuses on this relatively neglected part of the business and enables the student to develop a strong robust sales process bearing in mind that in small open economy like Ireland much of these sales will be internationally focussed. This module is focused on the business to business environment rather than business to consumer markets.

This module introduces students to the challenges of successful sales management with specific emphasis on customer relationship building and maintenance. In today's competitive global environment, the successful sales manager is a leader guided by strategic vision and a set of competencies that include planning and interpersonal skills. Selling is a fundamental part of not only business, but everyday life. Indeed, you are called upon to sell all the time – whether it is an idea, product, service, or point of view. Interestingly, personal selling is the single most expensive component of the Marketing Mix and has the most direct impact on purchase decisions made by the customer. This module will provide students with the necessary tools to implement an organizational structure for sales force management while emphasizing the fundamentally relational nature of the selling activity.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the sales process and its role in generating turnover;

Appreciate the key concepts within international business-to-business marketing including implications of international relationship marketing for efficient business management and improved performance;
Appraise of the differences between key international markets;
Evaluate the role of different prospecting tools within the sales process and how they apply in different markets;
Evaluate the role of key account management in international selling; and
Analyse the decision making process within organisational buying and deliver appropriate communication responses.

modules.moduleContent: Companies must pay attention to trends such as intensifying competition, increasing globalization, and technological developments. While these developments threaten existing business models they provide, at the same time, many opportunities for the development of new businesses and sustainable growth. In order to capitalize on these opportunities, companies need to develop appropriate marketing strategies that deviate from established ways of thinking and problem solving, and provide a source of advantage. Against this background, marketing consultants will share their expertise from real-world consultancy projects and introduce students to different approaches to successfully manage consultancy projects. Students develop their consultancy skills and apply their knowledge of concepts, frameworks, and research methods obtained throughout the programme to develop alternative strategic marketing recommendations for a specific business problem. Students evaluate the benefits and drawbacks of their recommendations and develop suggestions for their implementation. The business problem students need to address in their consultancy project may be specified by a client company.
learningOutcomes: Manage a consulting project and deal with different stakeholders;
Use theoretical knowledge and apply appropriate research methods to a real-world business problem;
Develop alternative strategic marketing recommendations for a specific marketing problem and assess their advantages and disadvantages;
Develop recommendations for the implementation of the recommended strategies; and
Present their analyses and recommendations in a professional, concise, and accurate manner.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Today we are witnessing the emergence of a Digital Marketing era, where continual technological advances integrate social media into both personal and public spaces. However, managing social media is not only about understanding these social media platforms, but rather about understanding how consumers are using various social media platforms, and how we can leverage these social media platforms to engage with consumers more effectively. Social media is also being increasingly adopted by businesses in terms of initiating, developing and maintaining relationships of what is traditionally a B2B process. 

This module aims to provide students with a panoramic view of social media and to facilitate both theoretical and practical learning. The main objective will therefore be to provide students with the necessary tools so that they can move away from their role of “consumer” of the content of these platforms to that of managers, who are capable of having a complete vision of both opportunities and targeted consumer profiles.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the different social media platforms, their operation and their online content;
Appreciate the different zones of social media and how to integrate them into a digital marketing strategy;
Understand the role of social media in the consumer journey and its link with marketing strategies; and
Understand the psychology of online consumers in different contexts.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The key focus of this module is to understand and implement search marketing. Search marketing is a wide area which includes elements of user experience (UX) and relives heavily on analytics. Organisations have a rudimentary appreciation of digital analytics however it is a largely underutilised resource. This module aims to address this skills gap by providing a deeper level of understanding of how analytics driven insights can fuel modern marketing campaigns, and play a large part in the success of search marketing – a key component of digital marketing Analytics play a significant role in customer segmentation, customer relationship management, marketing channel management and search engine optimisation, and many more core marketing concepts. A pivotal area where analytics driven insights plays a key role is in its support of strategic modification and enhancement of an organisations digital footprint, referred to as user experience (UX) research.  An appreciation of the impact of UX on the marketing function highlights a cogent example of application of organisational success through analytics driven insights. UX is also important in the visibility of the business to search techniques and as such is a key component of a search marketing strategy.
learningOutcomes: Understand and apply the techniques of search marketing;
Appreciate the role and function of digital marketing analytics within a strategic marketing decision-making context;
Explain how analytics driven insights shape search marketing;
Evaluate the application of analytics driven insights can enhance user experience (UX) and search success; and
Critically reflect upon challenges within the modern-day analytics landscape and the impact these have on organisational strategy.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The management of digital technology has become an increasingly important activity in organisations in recent years. Information and digital technologies have moved beyond the implementation of functional applications to playing a crucial role across all business activities and levels within the organisation. The ubiquitous nature of IT and digital technologies means that individual successful instances of these technologies are not enough to guarantee the achievement of organisational goals. Instead, organisations must integrate several rapidly changing technologies with overall strategic goals.  Managers need to understand how IT and digital technologies are governed within the organisation, how they can influence innovation, how organisations respond to disruptive technologies and the changing ways in which innovation occurs within and outside the organisation.

The focus of the module is on the importance of IT-enabled changes and digital transformation within the organisation and realizing competitive advantage from the IT and digital technologies portfolio. Many of the ideas are broadly applicable irrespective of context.
learningOutcomes: Examine and evaluate the range and impact of information systems and digital technologies that apply to business today;
Discuss the differences between IT/ digital technologies investments and traditional capital investments;
Demonstrate the role of IT and digital technologies in operational efficiency and effectiveness in service delivery;
Debate factors contributing to building the IT/ digital technologies capabilities of the organisation;
Evaluate how IT/ digital technologies can contribute to innovation within the organsiation;
Articulate a framework for benefits realisation from investments in IT/ digital technologies; and
Develop a critical understanding of the role of the CIO and digital transformation leaders.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module is tailored for non-business graduates seeking to acquire a solid understanding of accounting tools and approaches for assessing organisational performance. The student will learn key approaches to understanding and interpreting financial results and put them in the context of overall organisational performance. Learners will explore how financial strategies are shaped in the context of stakeholder engagement and understand the financial implications of strategic decision making.
learningOutcomes: Conduct financial analysis;
Assess organisational performance from multiple perspectives;
Appreciate the importance of corporate governance; and
Critically assess the financial implications of strategic decisions.

modules.moduleContent: During the past decades established companies have been increasingly challenged by entrepreneurial companies through the commercialisation of new technologies as well as through the design of new, innovative business models. Against this background, this module enables students to form their critical judgements about the specific advantages and challenges entrepreneurial as well as incumbent companies face in their innovation activities. Moreover, students will elaborate on how entrepreneurial companies as well as incumbents can discover innovation opportunities, capture value based on their innovation activities, and become leaders of technological change. In doing so, students apply the concepts, tools, and frameworks discussed in this module to real-world innovation challenges and reflect on how companies can advance their innovation activities.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the threats and opportunities related to innovation for established as well as entrepreneurial companies;
Apply innovation management concepts/tools/frameworks to the analysis of companies’ innovation activities;
Critically assess entrepreneurial and established companies’ innovation activities using academic and non-academic sources of information;
Evaluate how companies can facilitate entrepreneurial thinking and acting in order to increase their innovativeness;
Develop recommendations for the improvement of innovation activities and their implementation.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)
