modules.moduleContent: This module supports MBA Placement project, MI6399, by taught classes, in-group discussions and exercises which are designed to promote students’ self-reflection on the relationships between theories and competencies central to career development, career self-management, and their work placement.
learningOutcomes: Apply intelligent career theory to their own experience in the work place;
Deconstruct, reflect upon and appraise their respective career capital;
Evaluate theoretical concepts related to their and others’ careers in action;
Discuss practical career experiences through different career theories and concepts;
Appraise their continuing learning requirements in line with their future career-oriented goals.

modules.moduleContent:
This module offers students the opportunity to integrate their academic study with the action learning experience of work placement. Placement seeks to bring the concepts, tools and competencies developed on the MSc programme into the realities of workplace implementation. Students develop an understanding of the professional and practical competencies and skills required by employers. During the six month placement period, students will both apply their knowledge from the programme to, and gain new insights into, the practicalities of work life. This learning is supported in this module by taught classes, in-group discussions and exercises which are designed to promote students’ self-reflection on the relationships between theories and competencies central to career development, career self-management, and their work placement. Students are encouraged to critically analyse their own career capital and their career management strategies & priorities.

This module is taken in conjunction with Placement prepration module MI6398.
learningOutcomes: Apply intelligent career theory to their own experience in the work place;
Deconstruct, reflect upon and appraise their respective career capital;
Evaluate theoretical concepts related to their and others’ careers in action;
Discuss practical career experiences through different career theories and concepts;
Appraise their continuing learning requirements in line with their future career-oriented goals.

modules.moduleContent: This module covers the end-to-end supply chain, from sourcing and procurement through production and distribution, with a focus on international operations. It also provides a comprehensive overview of the principles, strategies, and practices involved in managing global supply chains. Students will explore the complexities and dynamics of global supply chains, including international transport, risk management, and the management of different global logistics providers.
learningOutcomes: Understand the strategic role of global logistics and supply chains in complex international markets;
Describe the keys issues relating to globalisation and international trade and their impact on logistics and supply chain;
Analyse and manage the logistics of global transportation, including the selection of appropriate modes of transport and logistics services providers;

Identify potential risks in global supply chains, such as geopolitical instability, economic fluctuations, and natural disasters, and develop robust risk mitigation strategies to enhance supply chain resilience and continuity;

Utilize Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) and other metrics to measure the performance of global supply chains, identify areas for improvement, and implement continuous improvement processes to enhance overall supply chain effectiveness;
Identify what sustainability involves in the context of logistics and supply chain management.

modules.moduleContent: The student should select and attend a national or international conference of significance to their doctoral research. Participation at the conference does not require presentation. The student is required to attend the conference fully and compile a short report evaluating critically the content and outcomes of the conference and relate it clearly to their doctoral studies.
learningOutcomes: Evaluate critically the content of academic papers presented at a conference
Demonstrate in writing the relevance of the conference content for their doctoral studies
Write a clear and concise report on the conference

modules.moduleName: PRACTICUM IN THIRD-LEVEL TEACHING II: CONTENT MODULE
modules.moduleContent:
This module provides PhD students with supervised practical training in designing, delivering and assessing research-led optional modules. Students who wish to enroll in ML 802 must consult with their main supervisor on the timing of this and must also confer with the lecturer whose course is to be shadowed to secure their permission for this. Please refer to the “SMLLC Shadowing Contract” for further information.

TIMETABLE
The student will attend all the lessons (usually 24 hours) of the module they are shadowing and meet regularly with the lecturer to discuss course design, content, delivery, and student evaluation. These regular meetings are to be arranged by the lecturer and the student at the beginning of the semester.
learningOutcomes: Plan a lesson, taking into account the learning outcomes of the module as a whole and of that particular lesson.
Compare and evaluate different assessment formats and formulate questions for different types of assessments.
Mark assignments under close supervision.
Develop a reflective practice on their teaching through reflecting on specific lessons and the module as a whole.
Explain and demonstrate techniques for classroom management and the development of an active learning classroom.

Design their own research-led module, including formulating appropriate learning outcomes and devising formative and summative assessment that will support students to achieve those learning outcomes.

modules.moduleContent:
As a pre-sessional programme, the MLEAP11 module presents students with the opportunity to improve their English language skills in reading, writing, listening and speaking to a level consistent with IELTS 6.0 for undergraduates (CEFR B2.2) and 6.5 for postgraduates (CEFR B2.2/borderline C1.1).  In addition, MLEAP11 prepares the students for the challenges of effective essay writing in higher education, presentation of their work and the taking of an IELTS-type exam in order to gain entry to their offered degree programmes at Maynooth University.*
The module also aims to provide students with key academic skills necessary to embark on their offered degree programmes at Maynooth, so enabling them to demonstrate appropriate graduate attributes, and to fully engage with their studies, including required reading, presentations, lectures and assessment. Basic research skills through English will also be taught alongside appropriate citation and referencing, enabling students to become more independent in their work and study, and allowing them to credit other authors’ work, where applicable.
*MU degree offers are made on the condition that undergraduate and postgraduate students will have attained an overall IELTS score band of 6.0 or 6.5, in order to be admitted to their offered degree programmes of study. Other individual IELTS section criteria may apply depending on the programme offered.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a level of academic English language usage that is consistent with overall IELTS 6.0, for undergraduates, (CEFR mid B2) and 6.5 for postgraduates, (CEFR high B2) through completion of an IELTS-style exam containing content, tasks, timings and assessment similar to IELTS exam conditions.
Use appropriate citation and referencing conventions utilising The Harvard Referencing System.
Interpret question and essay titles correctly.
Demonstrate effective essay-writing skills, including organisational structure with appropriate punctuation, sentence and paragraph construction, introduction and conclusion.
Apply critical reflection to students’ own work and studies in an effort to gain a better understanding of how the process of learning works most effectively, at both a personal and individual level.

modules.moduleContent:
The field of business ethics addresses (1) how individuals engage with the practicalities of organisational life in the late capitalist era (2) how organisations work to meet their social, cultural and environmental responsibilities, and (3) how organisations work to ensure that they treat employees and customers in an ethical way.  This module aims to introduce new business students to these concepts by building on the perspectives and theories of management theory, organisational behaviour, work psychology, and the various approaches which have emerged to inform the language of contemporary business ethics.  These approaches include: business & society; corporate social responsibility; corporate citizenship; sustainability, etc. 

As the module is introductory in nature it will introduce students to the fundamentals of business & society theory, ethical theories and moral psychology.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a broad understanding of the fundamentals of ethical theories as they relate to business;
Apply ethical critiques to practical business issues;
Understand appropriate contexts for evaluating the ethics of business and organizational practices; and
Appreciate the central teachings of, and the main differences between, the main frameworks understanding ethical and CSR initiatives.

modules.moduleContent: Information Systems together with Digital Technologies offer critical resources and capabilities for any type of business organisation today. Technological innovations often lead to transformations of businesses and entire industries, with emerging technologies like Generative AI, Virtual Reality and IoT enabled smart environments; affording businesses new ways of organising, working and a reimagining of the very products and services being offered. The objective of this course is to equip participants with knowledge of how to effectively manage and exploit information systems and information technology in modern organisations and businesses. This course is centred around the fundamental premise that a critical role of information technology is to provide organisations with strategic advantage by facilitating problem-solving, increasing productivity and quality, increasing speed, improving customer service, enhancing communication and collaboration, and enabling business process restructuring. Rather than focusing wholly on the technology itself, the emphasis is placed on foundational concepts needed to understand and contextualise the innovative uses of information technologies for achieving business functioning and competitiveness. In addition, the course will expose participants to debates on; current issues in Management Information Systems (MIS) for business and the usefulness and limitations of information systems in organisations.
learningOutcomes: Explain foundational concepts needed to understand and contextualise information systems, Information Technology and the role of Management Information Systems (MIS) in organisations;
Discuss how different types of information technologies support the operations of business organisations and enable their competitiveness in different industries;
Critically assess how new digital technologies are creating opportunities and threats for established firms and new start-ups; and
Explain the ethical and social issues associated with the use of contemporary Information Systems in organisations and the society.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module addresses the core functions and process of operations and supply chain management including the world class manufacturing, world class business, extended resource planning systems, just-in-time and lean business.  This module aims to ensure students understand the role of the operations function within an organisation and the outward impact operations and supply chain management has on all aspects of the business.
learningOutcomes: Explain and critique the strategic importance of operations and supply chain management in the global competitive business environment;
Analyse and discuss the interaction between operations and supply chain management and other functional areas of business;
Critically evaluate the role that quantitative and qualitative techniques can play as a decision aiding tool, as well as their limitations;

Demonstrate and employ important workplace skills (e.g. co-operation, teamwork, meeting deadlines, problem solving, project management, presenting professional written and oral case study reports) in case study work; and
Explain the role of technology in operations and supply chain management and its impact on the different elements of operations and supply chain management.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
In a constantly changing global business environment multiple internal and external stakeholders influence an organisation’s strategy and ultimate success or failure. Managing staff as well as managing relationships with customers and other external stakeholders is a core management role. However, interactions with staff and other stakeholders can sometimes lead to problems and disputes. It is essential, therefore, that Business students grasp the essentials of negotiation as the primary formal method for resolving significant problems. Managers use negotiation in a variety of settings: negotiating their own contracts of employment, negotiating business ventures, negotiating with colleagues, peers and superiors, and in the handling of employment relations.

It is also essential that Business students understand the wide range of other methods that are available for addressing disputes of an inter-personal, commercial and employee relations kind. These included Mediation, Facilitation, Arbitration and a range of approaches badged as Alternative Dispute Resolution or ADR.

This module offers a solid foundation in Negotiation, Mediation, Conciliation, Facilitation, Arbitration, and Alternative Dispute Resolution methods in theory, research and practice. The Module will focus on developing understanding of these approaches as an added interpersonal competence, but with emphasis on the workplace and commercial environments.

The module will assist students to identify their own styles and preferences in the handling of negotiation and different forms of dispute resolution.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of negotiation theory and practice in commercial and employment relations contexts;
Display knowledge of the differences between competitive, collaborative, and principled negotiation, and of the different conflict styles;
Demonstrate knowledge of skills in analysing, preparing for, and conducting negotiations;
Discuss processes, theories, literature, and research relevant to negotiation and dispute resolution in both commercial and workplace environments;
Develop insights into the role of communications and personal styles in negotiation and dispute resolution.

modules.moduleContent: To meet the challenges of the international business environment managers need a fundamental grasp of the process of building and maintaining corporate entrepreneurship. This module introduces the student to management’s role in promoting corporate entrepreneurship at all levels of multinational corporations. The purpose of the module is to enable the student to understand the influence of the organisational context set by management on driving corporate entrepreneurship. The focus is not on start- up companies but on transforming existing large and international organisations.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the key elements of corporate entrepreneurship and how it acts within the broad aspects of the large, multinational organisation;
Critically assess the managerial challenge of creating organisational environments supportive of corporate entrepreneurship, particularly as organisations grow and mature;
Appreciate the inter relationship between creativity, innovation, and corporate entrepreneurship from a managerial perspective; and
Demonstrate an understanding of the main conceptual frameworks for assessing the constraints and opportunities within the organisation’s operating and business environment.

modules.moduleContent: This module is a capstone module and involves student teams running a strategy simulation. This simulation gives you the opportunity to manage an organisation as it struggles to remain profitable. You will work as a team to compete with each other to analyse markets and develop strategic plans, make functional decisions including marketing, operations, sales, manufacturing and R&D. The simulation runs over multi-year horizons and presents challenges very similar to those faced by general managers in industry. The module requires the formulation and implementation of a strategy, using your knowledge from a wide range of business modules that you have taken during your business degree.
learningOutcomes: Describe the strategic planning process;
Conduct a strategic analysis of environment/market;
Interpret market/product analysis and propose actions;
Create a strategic plan;
Recognise time, resource, and knowledge constraints that affect strategic planning and implementation;
Develop a team plan of action;
Assess the impact of team dynamics on performance; and

modules.moduleName: RESEARCH QUESTIONS, LITERATURE REVIEWS AND THEORY BUILDING IN BUSINESS & MANAGEMENT RESEARCH
modules.moduleContent:
Finding good research questions, positioning them in existing literature, developing a theoretical framework and clarifying your contribution to the field are basic building blocks of doing a PhD. Irrespective of the topic on which your PhD project is focused, you will have to address these issues. At the same time, these are quite challenging tasks, and it is often around these areas where PhD examiners or journal reviewers often question our research work.
 
This module aims to provide students with insights into how to engage with these tasks and ultimately to develop their skills as future researchers, the skills that can be transferable between different research projects in the future.

Registration restricted to students on the structured doctoral and research masters programmes in the School of Business.
learningOutcomes: Identify a meaningful research problem that is grounded both in theory and in reality;
Prepare a literature review in order to position a research problem in the literature;
Develop a well-argued theoretical framework and identify potential contribution; and
Prepare constructive feedback to peers and interpret and apply the feedback received from peers.

modules.moduleContent:
Finding effective ways of socializing research outputs to the relevant academic audiences is an essential part of the research process. In addition, the effective socialization and communication of research is an enabling factor for raising interest and obtaining meaningful feedback. The module supports PhD students in developing their capacity of socializing their own work, in developing their skills of being a constructive participant in the socialization process of others’ work, and in reflecting about the interaction between the two.

In this module, students will reflect upon different approaches to socializing and communicating research to academic audiences. These reflections are based on students’ experiences made when attending a range of research seminars of the School of Business Research Seminar Series. Students will implement their reflections into a presentation of their own research (for example a chapter of their thesis or a peer-reviewed research paper) to an academic audience. Providing constructive and critical feedback when reviewing others’ work is an important skill of PhD students. Students will elaborate on this skill in interactive and non-interactive settings.

In order to register on MN807, students must be registered as a student on the structured doctoral programme in the School of Business and need to have completed MN805 - Research Questions, Literature Reviews and Theory Building in Business and Management Research and MN806 - Business Research Design and Methods.
learningOutcomes: Reflect upon different approaches to communicating research to academic audiences;
Present and communicate their research (for example a chapter of their thesis or a peer-reviewed research paper) and their contribution to knowledge to an academic audience;
Discuss the work of other researchers and communicate their feedback to these researchers; and
Respond to feedback obtained from academic audiences.

modules.moduleContent:
Teaching is an essential part of a faculty members’ professional activities and professional identities. To build a sustainable career in a competitive academic job market, PhD graduates may need to provide evidence that goes beyond teaching experience and that demonstrates their continuous engagement in developing their teaching skills as well as their knowledge about teaching and learning more broadly. 

Against this background, this module aims to provide an opportunity for PhD students from within the School of Business to develop their teaching skills in the specific context of business modules. In addition, the module provides an opportunity to develop an understanding of best practices in teaching business students in large and small class settings that can be applied in PhD students’ teaching support roles.

Students interested in taking this module are advised that they should – in consultation with their supervisors and other relevant faculty members – plan their portfolio of activities early in their PhD journey.
learningOutcomes: Identify appropriate teaching methods depending on class size and content.
Evaluate their own teaching practices.
Identify ways for improving their own teaching practices.
Implement changes in their own teaching practices.

modules.moduleContent: Thermal equilibrium and temperature.  Ideal gas law and kinetic theory of gases.  Work, heat and energy - first law of thermodynamics. Statistics and large systems - Entropy and second and third law of thermodynamics. Systems in contact with a heat reservoir. Equipartition. Specific heats of gases and solids.
learningOutcomes: define and distinguish the concepts of thermal equilibrium, temperature, heat, energy and entropy
calculate or estimate equilibrium and transport properties of ideal gases from a particle model
determine the heat exchanged and work performed in a variety of thermal processes
state the laws of thermodynamics and discuss some of their macroscopic implications, for example for heat engines
calculate thermal averages using the Boltzmann distribution
derive the equipartition theorem for the energy and apply it to solids and gases
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Simple harmonic motion - description with complex exponentials.  Superposition of periodic motions.  Forced and damped oscillations and resonance.  Coupled oscillators and normal modes.  Vibrations in continuous systems.  The wave equation.  Travelling and standing waves.  Sound and light as transverse and longitudinal waves.  Dispersion and group velocity.  Fourier transform and frequency spectrum. De Broglie waves.  Uncertainty principle as a bandwidth theorem. Schroedinger equation.  Quantum particle in a box.
learningOutcomes: describe oscillations using complex exponentials
solve problems involving forced and damped oscillations
calculate characteristic frequencies and normal modes of coupled oscillators
discuss the fundamental properties of waves
perform fourier transforms of simple functions and differential equations
describe oscillations and waves in terms of Fourier modes
solve simple problems in quantum mechanics

modules.moduleContent: Kinematics of fluid motion. Euler's equations, steady flow. Bernoulli's equation. Circulation. Kelvin's theorem. Velocity potential for two and three dimensional flows. Sources, sinks and doublets. The complex potential. Method of images. Vortex lines. Supersonic flow. Viscosity. Viscous flow. Navier Stokes equation. Reynolds number. Turbulence. Poiseuille flow. Couette flow. Kolmogorov Scaling.
learningOutcomes: enumerate the basic variables and forces involved in the description of stationary and moving fluids
derive the equation of conservation of mass and deduce basic consequences from it
use Bernoulli's principle to relate velocity and pressure in a fluid
employ the concepts of vorticity, viscosity and Reynolds number in the description of fluid motion
use the Euler and Navier-Stokes equations to analyse fluid flow and drag forces in simple geometries (flow through straight pipes, between flat plates etc.)

modules.moduleContent:
Introduction to forces and particles. Symmetries and conservation laws. Discrete symmetries (CPT). The four forces; classification of leptons, hadrons, mesons, baryons. Strangeness and isospin. The quark model: quark model of mesons and baryons; charm (J/psi-particle);  QCD; asymptotic freedom and confinement. Neutrinos and neutrino masses.  Electro-weak theory and Higgs bosons. P and CP violation (KL-KS oscillations, CKM matrix). Relativistic quantum mechanics: the Dirac equation. Current developments.

This module cannot be taken together with EP440.
learningOutcomes: Classify particle decays and interactions in terms of the electromagnetic, weak and strong interactions
Explain the relation between symmetries and conservation laws in quantum mechanics
Apply conservation laws to evaluate the feasibility of specific processes
Define cross sections and explain their relation to transition matrix elements in quantum mechanics
Calculate simple cross-sections in elementary particle collisions
Present and explain the quark model of hadrons
Describe the Dirac equation and derive the solutions of the Dirac equation for non-interacting fermions

modules.moduleName: CONDENSED MATTER THEORY: INTERACTIONS, MAGNETISM AND SUPERCONDUCTIVITY
modules.moduleContent: Description of many particle systems with interactions, using second quantization.  Treatment of weakly interacting electrons.  Response to external fields (conductivity).  Models for the transition between conducting and insulating systems, notably Hubbard models.  Introduction to superconductivity.  Theory of ferromagnets and antiferromagnets, including spin waves. Overview of modern experimental techniques.
learningOutcomes: Use creation and annihilation operators in the description of many particle systems.
Recognise and derive the salient features of models for weakly interacting electrons, tightly bound electrons, superconductors and the most common types of magnets.
Describe the basic phenomenology of superconductors and magnets and make quantitative predictions for concrete systems.
Calculate the linear response of condensed matter systems to external stimuli using linear response theory (e.g. the Kubo formula).
Connect theoretical concepts in condensed matter systems to experimental signatures that can be observed using a variety of modern experimental techniques.

modules.moduleContent: The student will work independently on a research or literature project in a chosen area of mathematical physics.  The results will be written up in a report and presented in an oral presentation at the end of the year.
learningOutcomes: Carry out independent research on a given topic in theoretical physics or related areas
Present their results in a coherent, structured report
Give an oral presentation of their research to an audience of their peers

modules.moduleContent: Noether's theorem, the Klein Gordon field and its quantisation; LSZ reduction formula; Path integrals in quantum mechanics and field theory; Perturbation theory, Feynman diagrams, S matrix
learningOutcomes: Derive conserved charges in classical field theory using Noether's theorem
Carry out canonical and path integral quantisation of scalar fields
Compute tree level S matrix elements
Describe the principles of renormalisation and renormalisability
Compute scattering cross sections and decay rates to one loop in scalar field theory

modules.moduleContent:
The Department of Theoretical Physics runs modules in association with the School of Mathematics,  Trinity College Dublin, the School of Theoretical Physics, Dublin Institute for Advanced Studies and the School of Mathematical Science, U.C.D.  This is a catch-all module and represents modules that can be chosen, in consultation with the Head of the Theoretical Physics Department, from the above universities.  Advanced level 6 modules normally on offer are: High Energy Physics, General Relativity, Statistical Mechanics, Quantum Gravity and others that may arise and run by agreement of the Heads of the above schools and departments.

Students must consult with their Course Coordinator before registering for this module.

Introduction to group theory, finite groups, Lie groups and Lie algebras and their representations, roots and weights, Young tableaux, discrete groups, the rotation group and SU(2), SU(3) and the quark model of particle physics, classification of Lie algebras and Dynkin diagrams.
learningOutcomes: Define groups and group representations, and prove fundamental theorems of finite group representations
Construct irreducible representations of finite groups
Define Lie groups and Lie algebras
Formulate adjoint representations of su(2) and su(3) Lie algebras
Describe tensor operators and the Wigner-Eckart theorem
Construct root systems of Lie algebras
Construct Lie algebra representations

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to group theory, finite groups, Lie groups and Lie algebras and their representations, roots and weights, Young tableaux, discrete groups, the rotation group and SU(2), SU(3) and the quark model of particle physics, classification of Lie algebras and Dynkin diagrams.
learningOutcomes: Define groups and group representations, and prove fundamental theorems of finite group representations
Construct irreducible representations of finite groups
Define Lie groups and Lie algebras
Formulate adjoint representations of su(2) and su(3) Lie algebras
Describe tensor operators and the Wigner-Eckart theorem
Construct root systems of Lie algebras
Construct Lie algebra representations

modules.moduleContent:
The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic, typically in preparation for the chosen research project.

Students must consult their Course Coordinator before registering for this module. Reading courses are quite varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.
learningOutcomes: Master a topic in theoretical physics by way of independent study

modules.moduleContent: 10 45-minute research seminars given by leading researchers in a variety of areas of Mathematical Physics.  Credit can be given for attendance at seminars at other universities.  The student must submit a summary of one of these seminars.  Preparation and delivery of a 30-minute departmental seminar on the student’s own research.

modules.moduleName: MAJOR ADVANCED SUMMER SCHOOL, TECHNICAL COURSE OR INTERNATIONAL PLACEMENT
modules.moduleContent: Content depends on the supervisor-approved course.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO PROGRAMMING II COMPUTATIONAL PHYSICS.
modules.moduleContent: This course introduces students to the fundamentals of programming and numerical methods for mathematical and scientific applications, including random numbers and Monte Carlo methods; linear algebra; partial differential equations.  The module mirrors and follows the timetable of MP468C Computational Physics 2.
learningOutcomes: solve partial differential equation (initial and boundary value problems) using a variety of methods
analyse the stability of differentiation schemes for initial-value problems in partial differential equations
outline the principles behind the most commonly used methods for solving systems of linear equations (Gauss-Jordan elimination, conjugate gradient), their advantages and limitations
describe and apply the basic principles fo Monte Carlo methods
apply the methods and principles learned in MP468C to solve a substantial problem in physics or applied mathematics
present their results in a well structured and presented and properly referenced written report.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Introduction to High Performance Computing: Computer architectures, parallel programming paradigms, introduction to the ICHEC systems. Introduction to shared-memory parallel processing and
OpenMP. Introduction to MPI.
learningOutcomes: Understand key HPC concepts and how they are applied in scientific research.
Devise parallel strategies to solve computational problems.
Develop basic parallel applications using OpenMP and/or MPI.
Leverage numerical libraries for better performing code.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quire varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 25 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quite varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 35 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
Develop advanced skills in Algebra.
 
Selected topics in Algebra.
learningOutcomes: State some classical theorems in advanced Algebra
Prove any of the theorems given in the course.
Deduce properties which follow from these theorems.
Prove other algebraic results using rigorous arguments

modules.moduleName: PRESENTATION AT A SUMMER SCHOOL/CONFERENCE IN MATHEMATICAL SCIENCE
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
To improve research-related presentational skills in Mathematics or Statistics.

Supervised preparation of material for presentation at approved summer school or conference.  The same summer school/conference cannot be the basis for credit in both this module and any other module, such as GSE1 or MT850.
learningOutcomes: Choose conferences/summer schools to attend that are appropriate to the student’s research interests.
Apply for such support as may be available for conference/summer school attendance.
Apply to give a talk in the appropriate section of a conference/summer school.
Prepare material to be presented at a conference/summer school.
Adjust the presentation plan in the light of the amount of time allocated to the presentation.
Present material in an appropriate way at a conference/summer school.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Eucharist in the early Christian communities.  Historical moments in the development of the Eucharistic celebration.  Missal of Paul VI.   Future perspectives.
learningOutcomes: On completion of the course the student should be able to give a detailed description of the historical development of the celebration of the Eucharist.
On completion of the course the student should be able to give a detailed description of the nature of the Eucharist.
On completion of the course the student should be able to understand the structure of the liturgy through exploring the Roman Missal.

On completion of the course the student should be able to articulate an understanding of the liturgy as currently celebrated, and show an awareness of  perspectives for the future of liturgical celebration.

modules.moduleContent: At the end of the module the student should have the ability to perform a number of important skills related to liturgical organ playing and have enhanced technique.
learningOutcomes: On completion of the module, the student should be able to present two solo organ pieces for performance.
On completion of the module, the student should be able to comfortably sight-read a basic four-part accompaniment.
On completion of the module, the student should be able to improvise an accompaniment to a given melodic setting.
On completion of the module, the student should be able to make significant use of the pedal division.

modules.moduleContent:
This module contains the following:
1) The study of music repertory from a wide historical range with focus on rhythm, melody, harmony, texture and formal design. 
2) An introduction to basic orchestration and arrangement techniques through the study of basic instrumental (and vocal) technique and timbres.
3) The development of score-reading, writing and notational skills.
learningOutcomes: apply basic methods of analysing music.
distinguish between different types of phrase structures and musical forms (both with and without scores).
understand, recall and apply basic instrumental characteristics and technique for standard orchestral instruments and voices.
understand and apply good notational practice in producing musical scores.

modules.moduleContent: Overview: origin of opera and its development; culture and politics in selected operas; music and dramatic language in operas across the centuries.
learningOutcomes: analyse the music and dramatic language in selected operas.
evaluate the music-text relationship in selected operas.
identify the main musical styles used in operas.
assess the links between opera, politics, and culture.

modules.moduleContent: This module will explore the area of electronic music composition, from the technical, aesthetical and historical point of view. Students will study the main musical developments since 1945, from the birth of Musique Concrète to today’s Computer Music. From a technical perspective, the module will teach the basic methods of sound generation and manipulation: oscillators, sampling, envelopes, looping, filters, delays and other effects processing. In complement to this, students will learn how to apply these elements to electronic music composition.
learningOutcomes: apply principles of electronic music composition to an original work.
list the major techniques of sound montage for electronic music.
employ editing techniques on a given source material.
manipulate soundfiles using a software sound editor.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: MUSIC IN THE BRITISH MARKETPLACE C. 1815-1914
modules.moduleContent: Music in the British marketplace underwent a considerable transformation between the Battle of Waterloo and the outbreak of World War I. This module examines the main events, developments, inheritances and legacies of this turbulent period. It investigates the institutions and audiences that underpinned concert life. The relationship between music and commerce is re-evaluated through an exploration of nationalism, promotion, reception and canonization. What did British audiences make of the music of J.S. Bach, Handel, Beethoven, Mendelssohn, Berlioz, Wagner and R. Strauss? How were British musicians and their music viewed at home and abroad? Case studies focus on areas including: the cult of virtuosity, the thirst for large-scale choral music, the evolution of the orchestra and the role of the press.
learningOutcomes: Identify the main events, developments and legacies of the period in this specific context
Evaluate the relationships between secular musical institutions, the establishment/state, central European traditions, musicians (performers, composers) and their audiences (critical reception)
Analyse the role of the press in the commodification of British composers, performers and musical institutions
Discuss the reception and canonization of the works of, for example, J.S. Bach, Handel, Beethoven, Mendelssohn, Berlioz, Wagner and R. Strauss in Britain

modules.moduleName: ENGLISH FOR ACADEMIC PURPOSES (LEVEL C1.1 OF THE CEFR)
modules.moduleContent: This English for Academic Purposes (EAP) module focuses on enabling students to acquire a sufficient command of the English language approximate to CEFR level C1.1 and involving all four skills of reading, writing, listening and speaking, to enable them to engage successfully to a high degree with their academic studies across a range of subject areas. At CEFR C1.1, students will be able to understand extended speech and lectures, follow complex lines of argument in extended, unstructured speech and lectures with minimal signalling; understand long, specialised texts, including technical instructions; present clear, detailed descriptions on complex subjects; write clear, highly-detailed texts including reports and essays while selecting the appropriate style required. All of the above will be accomplished using excellent and highly appropriate levels of grammatical accuracy and lexical range.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a level of Academic English language usage that is consistent with overall CEFR C1.1 through completion of assessment procedures and tasks that simulate authentic EAP language abilities and critical skills in Higher Education study;

Design and deliver an academic presentation relating to a relevant EAP topic using a technological, slide-based format incorporating text, graphics/diagrams with appropriate (C1.1) oral commentary in support;
Reade academic texts with an excellent level of understanding and critical awareness using the academic reading strategy SQ4R;
Make highly accurate academic notes from lectures and show ability to summarize content as required;
Produce written language in an appropriate academic style at both sentence and paragraph level.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The module provides a foundational understanding of business landscapes from macro, meso, and micro perspectives. Respectively, students are introduced to the general context of business at the macro level, the market and industry structures at the meso level, and the organisation ownership and various functions of a firm at the micro level. This module also extends the understanding of the business environment in local and global markets and the interplay between business and the environment.
learningOutcomes: Develop comprehension and analytical skills;
Explain the key considerations of business environment from macro level, using PESTEL framework;
Explain the key considerations of business environment from meso level, using market structure and Porter’s five forces model;
Explain the key considerations of business environment from micro level, explaining organisations ownership and various functions;
Understand the Ireland business environment and global market;and
Critically evaluate how the business environment interacts with business organisations, with a specific emphasis on the analysis of the recommended management decisions.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will introduce students to the academic discipline of consumer behaviour and foster a critical understanding of both historical and contemporary issues in the field of consumer behaviour, and basic concepts in the study of consumers. This module emphasises the centrality and breadth of consumption in our lives. Students will be challenged to reflect on their own experiences as consumers. 

The module thereby draws on different scientific disciplines (e.g., psychology, sociology, economics, and anthropology) to uncover the complexity of modern-day consumer behaviour. For example, students will learn how individual consumer characteristics, such as motives, personality, identity, and their cultural background, etc. shape consumption decisions.

Furthermore, students will develop an understanding of the practical relevance of the discussed concepts through a series of exercises and the continuous assessment component of the module.
learningOutcomes: Define and recognise the historical context of consumer behaviour and where consumer behaviour as a field is at the moment;
Illustrate how consumers make purchasing decisions;
Identify and explain perceptual processes that underly consumer behaviour;
Understand how consumers form attitudes and what role these play in consumer behaviour;
Explain how motivation, personality, and lifestyles shape consumer behaviour; and
Demonstrate how culture and social/reference groups influence behaviour.

modules.moduleContent:
The structure of this course follows the logic of management through several interconnected marketing themes:

1) Understanding customers
2) Understanding competition
3) Creating competitive advantage
4) Managing marketing communications
5) Managing distribution
6) Capturing value for the company
7) Managing marketing efficiency.
learningOutcomes: Explain how marketing management is affected by the marketing environment;
Explain the role of marketing in creating, communicating and delivering customer value;
Explain and critique key models and approaches used in customer and industry analysis and in the management of the marketing mix;
Formulate and apply data-driven approaches to managing efficiency and effectiveness of marketing activities; and
Demonstrate and employ important workplace skills (e.g. co-operation, teamwork, meeting deadlines, problem-solving, project management, and presenting professional reports) in coursework.

modules.moduleContent: This is a foundation module in International Business, designed to provide you with comprehensive knowledge about key aspects of international business. The module is broadly divided into three main themes: 1) International Business Theory, 2) The Institutional Environment of International Business, and 3) The Complexities of International Business. This module focuses on key theories of international trade, foreign direct investment and internationalisation, entry into foreign markets and the decisions that international firms face amidst the challenges of a complex institutional environment. The applicability of theoretical concepts in different international environments is explored during the module with the aim of appreciating differences across the legal, political and cultural environments, ethical decision making, and working in challenging markets. Businesses operate in an increasingly globalised environment and most business graduates will seek careers that will involve working and managing in international environments; the skills acquired in this module will be relevant in real life situations.
learningOutcomes: Understand the impact of economic development and technology on industry, business practice, and consumers;
Appreciate the effects of institutional context on international business;
Understand alternative routes to internationalisation;
Understand the frameworks for analysing cultures and the implications of cultural differences on international business;
Critically assess management theories and practices related to ethics in different countries; and
Apply key frameworks to identify and analyse global industries.

modules.moduleContent:
In this module students will be introduced to theories of entrepreneurship, will begin to understand the process of getting a business off-the-ground, and will develop their own business plan.  Students will gain insights into how entrepreneurship might be a career pathway for you, be that as an entrepreneur setting up your own business, an entrepreneur developing business ideas within an existing organisation, or supporting entrepreneurs in achieving their goals to create economics and social value for customers and society.

This is a hands-on module where the goal is to learn by doing from the outset.  You will find that you are asked to engage with content in a practical way from week 1 and your group project will commence in week 2.  Students may also be asked to spend a short part of their study week on-site with the live organisation for which they are developing their business plan.  The module is organised in three key phases. 
 
Phase I: The Business Plan.  
Here you will learn about the key sections of a business plan and what needs to be included within each section.  Modern entrepreneurship is not a solo experience. Entrepreneurs develop ideas individually and collectively, working in teams to provide solutions to customers and society.  Students will, therefore, work in teams to develop each section of the business plan as they learn the structure, tools and frameworks for each section in class.  So, as you are learning about, for example the marketing section of a business plan you will also be drafting the marketing section of a business plan.  

Phase II: The Innovative Entrepreneur
In the second phase of the module we delve into the entrepreneurial mind and think about how to generate new ideas, test them, develop them and protect them.  In phase 1 you will have developed a business plan responding to a pre-defined challenge or need. Here, however, you will learn to diagnose need/gaps to generate, promote, and develop your own innovative ideas. 
 
Phase III: Funding and Growing the Venture
In phase III you will put yourself into the mindset of an entrepreneur who now has a tested and validated idea around which you have developed an initial business plan.  This third phase will therefore focus on how you then fund and grow a business.  In the first instance, this centres on identifying and securing sources of capital.  It then progresses to strategizing for growth and accessing external resources to aid in that growth.  The module completes with a look at how to exit or end the business.
learningOutcomes: Identify and define the characteristics of entrepreneur and entrepreneurial activity;
Identify and describe the processes involved in the generation, testing, development and protection of new ideas;
Develop a business plan in a team setting to practically apply the concepts and tools of entrepreneurship to a business idea; and
Identify and describe the processes involved in funding and growing a business from a tested and validated idea.

modules.moduleContent:
This module addresses the role and importance of Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC) for organisations. IMC concerns the integration of all types of promotional tools and appropriate media to deliver synergistic communications campaigns. Such promotional tools include Advertising, Public Relations, Sales Promotions, and Direct Marketing, delivered on- and off-line.

It will provide students with tools for evaluating communications campaigns and gaining insight into the discourse of marketing communications. It will also help students to develop insights into how account planning, creative development and media planning work with the other communications areas in order to produce marketing communications campaigns that deliver on business and marketing objectives.
learningOutcomes: Define Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC) and the main terms involved, while also understanding what needs to be integrated when adopting IMC;
Know the basic steps in the IMC planning model;
Appreciate the importance of internal marketing in IMC;
Describe the basic communication model and how it applies to IMC;
Understand how message strategies are created and communicated through various promotional tools;
Develop a broad understanding of the media landscape; and
Appreciate how IMC can influence consumer decision making.
Evaluate the main tools used in digital marketing;
Identify appropriate online tools and platforms for achieving strategic digital marketing objectives;
Plan and implement a digital marketing campaign;
Discuss how companies leverage digital technologies to deliver added value to customers; and
Apply digital marketing tools to increase customer satisfaction and enhance customer relationship management.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Innovation is a powerful means of competitive differentiation, enabling firms to be successful in the marketplace. At the same time, innovation activities are conducted in a highly competitive environment. Against this background, innovation must be skillfully managed in order to be successful and to create innovation-based competitive advantages. This module provides students with a set of tools and concepts that will enable them to participate in, and manage, value creation through innovation. Students are introduced to different tools, concepts, and frameworks that help companies to develop strategies for their innovation activities, to benefit from different sources of innovation, and to capture value from their innovation activities. Students apply these frameworks and tools to real-world business cases and discuss the implications for companies’ innovation activities.
learningOutcomes: Critically assess real-world companies’ innovation activities, building on the knowledge of: types of innovation; sources of individual and organisational creativity; approaches to the exploitation of innovation through different appropriability strategies; and approaches to collaboration for innovation.
Apply different innovation management tools, concepts, and frameworks to real-world business cases; and
Develop recommendations for the improvement of innovation activities in real-world business cases.

modules.moduleContent:
The module explores the landscape in which organisations operate and function at the macro, meso and micro level. It does this with the themes of change management and organisational development in mind, looking at how change manifests itself, theories of change and organisational development, the advantages and challenges of change management processes and programmes, the leadership of change, and best practices in change management and implementation leading to superior organisational development. 

The context of change and organisational development is addressed. The processes and outcomes of change management and organisational development are covered, including the types of change and organisational development taking place, and how organisational managers deal with these changes and developments on a strategic and operational level.

Questions concerning the imperative of change management and organisational development, whose interests are being served, the impact on individuals within organisations, and the rationale for engaging in change management and organisational development are unpicked in this module.
learningOutcomes: Critically reflect and analyse real-life case studies on different change management and organisational development topics;
Understand the fundamentals of change management and organisational development theories;
Unpick the facilitators, advantages, and challenges of change management and organisational development processes and programmes;
Explore the importance of organisational culture and leadership in the change and OD (organisational development) process;
Explore best practices in change management and OD through case study work and exercises;
Understand the importance of individual change to organisational development;

modules.moduleContent: This module continues and deepens the understanding of human resource management that students gained in their second year on the programme. Strategic Human Resource Management (SHRM) stresses the importance of people (human resources) management for organisations in achieving and maintaining a sustainable competitive advantage in the market place. SHRM focuses on longer-term people concerns, such as total rewards, employee engagement, talent management, as well as meso concerns concerning structure, culture, strategic fit and performance. As such, the inclusion of human resource management policies, processes and practices as core within the organisation’s corporate strategy framework is emphasised. The module assesses what strategic HRM prescribes and delivers in reality, acknowledging the constraints under which organisations operate. The importance of perceived organisational justice in explaining employee reactions to HRM policies and practices is unpacked. Contemporary developments in the field are explored with regards to the implications these have for SHRM (such as increased prominence of the psychological contract and the growing use of flexible working practices, short-term contracts and telework, that have profoundly reshaped the employment relationship).
learningOutcomes: Define and critically assess the role of human resource management in contemporary employment relationships;
Critically assess the role of human resource management in contributing to an organisation’s sustainable competitive advantage/to an organisation’s corporate strategy;

Identify the key challenges and trends that are affecting the employment relationship (such as altered work-life balance dynamics, unbalanced flexibility, diversity, equity and inclusion in the workplace) and understand the implications for both the organisation and human resource management respectively in terms of achieving organisational goals;
Evaluate the significance of the psychological contract and perceptions of organisational justice in the contemporary employment relationship;
Critically reflect on the tensions between flexibility and security in the context of increasingly deregulated labour markets;

Understand and describe the key human resource management practices which affect the behaviour of individuals in organisations, and how their operational implementation impacts on organisational performance and the strategic needs of the business; and
Critically reflect and analyse real life case studies on different strategic HRM topics.

modules.moduleContent:
This module considers the analysis, formulation, and implementation of strategy. Strategy seeks to convert the purpose of an organisation into a plan that brings that purpose to life. Core to purpose is what the organisation is seeking to do for its customers and society. Strategy is about how to collectively improve the performance of the firm in achieving its purpose. Strategy is fundamentally a team-based process, where individuals with the expertise and experience to achieve the purpose of the organisation combine together to create and implement a coordinated plan to achieve purpose. Often, these individuals will have worked in separate parts of the organisation and may have limited prior experience of working together, with varying interest in collective strategy, as opposed to their function expertise. The interaction between people, functional expertise and levels within an organisation are a key element of the strategy experience.

Thus, both business level and corporate level strategy are considered in this module. Central to this module is an understanding of the competitive environment and its interaction with choices about how a firm grows and organises itself to succeed. A central purpose of strategy formulation and implementation is to improve the relative performance of a firm, rooted in the achievement of an organisation’s purpose. This module uses concepts from management and economics to provide students with an understanding of the foundations upon which the process of strategy formulation and implementation is based.

Your learning about strategy will be supported by a series of lectures, where core concepts and tools are introduced and tutorials, which support the development of your group and individual project. This approach mirrors the reality of Strategy, where one undertakes individual work, and ultimately coordinates work between groups of people from different backgrounds and expertise to recommend a strategy and methods for implementation.  Key to your learning journey is that you actively read the materials to understand the context of the lectures and actively engage in both the individual and group project process. As with strategy in reality, your outcome performance is a blend of your individual work, your ability to work with teams of difference, and the collective strategy and implementation plan that you develop.
learningOutcomes: Working in a strategy team and individually, critically appraise a strategy business problem of an organisation using the concepts and tools of strategic management and make recommendations for how firm performance can be improved;

Critically appraise the strategy and business model of an organisation to establish the boundary conditions under which it is likely to succeed or fail using the principles of an economic perspective of strategy;
Be capable of offering an analytic diagnosis of these boundary conditions and insights into how to improve the likelihood of strategic success;
Be competent in defining, describing, discussing and applying a selection of strategic management principles to analysis of the strategy of an organisation;
Be able to describe the strategy process elements of analysis, formulation, and implementation; and
Be able to apply strategy tools/theories/frameworks to the analysis of an organisation and interpret the findings.

modules.moduleContent:
This module deals with contemporary issues in marketing practice and thought. Topics will change from year to year in light of issues facing marketing as a discipline. Sample topics include the following but these are subject to change.

Evolution of Marketing as a discipline
A contemporary perspective on marketing thought and its genesis from economics and psychology. Defining marketing in terms of its orientation.

Issues in Services Marketing
The marketing of services. Unique issues facing services marketers in the current environment. Service Design and service dominant logic.

Issues in contemporary retailing
The move from traditional retailing to omni-retailing. Customer satisfaction, service quality and retail performance.

Relationship Marketing
Moving from transactional to relational marketing. B2C, B2B and B2G implications.

Luxury Consumption
Defining luxury. Conspicuous consumption and its antecedents. Luxury marketing in a recession.

Marketing and its Social Impact
Marketing an idea or a social cause. Implications for the marketing mix.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate the ability to analyse, diagnose, and evaluate a range of theories as they relate to the theoretical and conceptual framework of marketing;
Critically analyse marketing situations facing organisations;
Select appropriate marketing frameworks to analyse service organisations and sectors;
Apply extended marketing mix strategies appropriate to the objectives, resources, and environment of both profit and non-profit service organisations; and
Envisage the impact of current and future trends on the role and implementation of marketing.

modules.moduleContent:
The purpose of this module is to explain and demonstrate the relevance of concepts related to Corporate Social Responsibility, Sustainability and Responsible Management to business students. 

The module will adopt a learning perspective at the individual and organisational levels and will ask how managers and employees can implement and influence change that produces societal and environmental benefits at the same time as helping businesses meet their goals.

The module is based on the concept of Responsible Management & Organisational Learning which seeks to engage students, professionals and managers with the development of decent work and economic growth opportunities (SDG 8), sustainable industry and innovation (SDG 9) and the sustainable production of goods and services designed for responsible consumption (SDG 12). Key ethical frameworks and approaches will be explored.

The module involves a significant amount of group learning and project teamwork.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a broad and deep understanding of the fundamentals of ethical theories as they relate to business;
Apply ethical critiques to practical business issues;
Understand appropriate contexts for evaluating the ethics of business and organisational practices; and
Employ a graduate level of appreciation on how CSR, Sustainability and Responsible Management & Organisational Learning impact on business activities.

modules.moduleContent:
This "Sustainability, Circular Economy & ESG Investing" micro-credential is designed to meet the evolving needs of professionals navigating the increasingly complex domains of environmental sustainability and financial decision-making. The development of this micro-credential arises from the critical recognition that sustainable development and investment are not only moral imperatives but also key drivers of innovation and competitiveness in the modern economy. The urgency of addressing environmental challenges, coupled with the rising influence of ESG factors on investment decisions, underscores the need for a micro-credential that equips participants with the skills to implement sustainable practices and investment strategies. The circular economy offers a framework for minimizing waste and optimizing resource use, which is crucial for long-term sustainability. Meanwhile, ESG investing represents a shift in how value is assessed, emphasizing the importance of sustainability in financial performance.

This dynamic online MC offers an in-depth exploration into the intersections of sustainability, circular economy practices, and ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) investing, tailored for individuals seeking to make a meaningful impact in their professional fields through sustainable practices. Participants will gain a robust understanding of the critical theories and practical applications that underpin sustainability in business and investment decisions, focusing on innovative strategies that align with global sustainability goals.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse and apply sustainability and circular economy concepts within a business or investment context.
Evaluate investments using ESG criteria to inform sustainable investment decisions.
Develop and implement sustainable business strategies that contribute to a circular economy.
Communicate effectively with stakeholders about sustainability efforts and performance.

modules.moduleContent: Dimensions and units. Linear kinematics and dynamics of a particle and of a system of particles. Principles of momentum and energy. Particle dynamics in more than one dimension. Vector methods. Motion in a circle, motion under constant gravity. Projectile motion. Conservative and Non-conservative forces. Work and potential energy.  Motion under a central force. Introduction to special relativity: Postulates of special relativity, Lorentz transformations, spacetime, time dilation, length contraction, Doppler shift.
learningOutcomes: apply the concepts of velocity and acceleration to solving problems in kinetics
carry out basic vector algebra operations including dot and cross products
make appropriate use of vectors in the description of physical quantities
calculate the properties of particles interacting via conservative and non-conservative forces
formulate and solve problems involving conservative forces and energy conservation
describe the basic concepts of special relativity and apply them to solving problems involving time dilation and space contraction

modules.moduleContent: One and two dimensional dynamics of a system of particles, including collision and impulse. Centre of mass and moment of inertia.  Rotational motion: angular velocity, torque, angular momentum. Simple harmonic motion, wave phenomena. Quantum Physics: Photoelectric effect, wave-particle duality, Heisenberg uncertainty principle, Bohr atom.
learningOutcomes: use conservation of momentum to analyse simple collision problems
describe and calculate the properties of systems undergoing simple harmonic motion
define the centre of mass for a system of particles or an extended object, and use it to describe the motion of the system as a whole
apply the concepts of angular velocity, moment of inertia and torque to solve problems involving rotational motion
describe how the particle nature of light and the wave nature of matter manifest themselves

modules.moduleName: MATHEMATICAL METHODS (VECTOR CALCULUS AND FOURIER SERIES)
modules.moduleContent: Functions of several variables. Partial derivatives. Geometric interpretation, curves, surfaces.  Scalar and vector fields.  Vector calculus.  Grad. div and curl. Integrals: line, surface and volume. Gauss's and Stokes's theorems. Orthogonal expansions. Fourier Series.
learningOutcomes: calculate partial derivatives of functions of many variables
solve problems involving the gradient of scalar fields and the divergence and curl of vector fields
make appropriate use of scalar fields and vector functions in the description of physical quantities
evaluate line, surface and volume integrals
apply Stokes' and Gauss' theorems
calculate Fourier series for periodic functions and use them to evaluate infinite series

modules.moduleContent: Electrostatics, Gauss' dielectric flux theorem, electric potential, Laplace's equation, Poisson's equation, electric currents, magnetic fields, Ampere's theorem, Biot-Savart law, magnetic induction, Maxwell's equations
learningOutcomes: derive Gauss' dielectric flux theorem
calculate electric fields for various collections of electric charges
appreciate the origin of magnetic fields
use Ampere's theorem and the Biot-Savart law to calculate magnetic fields
describe the electromagnetic nature of light using Maxwell's equations

modules.moduleContent: Lagrange theory: Hamilton's Principle of least action, Euler-Lagrange equations, canonical momenta and conservation laws. Central forces. Two-body motion: bound states. Rigid Body Motion: Rotation transformations, Euler's theorem, inertia tensor, Euler's equation of motion, the heavy symmetrical top, small oscillations and stability. Hamiltonian theory: The canonical equations, Poisson brackets.
learningOutcomes: formulate the basic principles of the Lagrange-Hamilton formalism
use the Lagrange-Hamilton formalism to derive equations of motion of dynamical systems
explain the relation between symmetries and conservation laws
apply conservation laws to analyse the motion of dynamical systems
describe the mathematical properties of rotations and systems with rotational symmetry

modules.moduleContent: Wave mechanics in one dimension. Angular momentum. Translational and rotational symmetry in the two-body problem. Bound states of central potentials. Perturbation theory
learningOutcomes: Define general properties of angular momentum in quantum mechanics, and specifically the relevant observables, their commutation relations, and their eigenvalue equations
Outline the exact solution of the non-relativistic theory of the hydrogen atom using polynomial method
Describe the Pauli non-relativistic theory of electron spin
Perform addition of arbitrary angular momenta using general methods
Use stationary perturbation theory to calculate first and second order corrections to eigenvalues and eigenvectors
Describe the spectrum of the hydrogen atom, the relevant degeneracies and quantum numbers, and explain the relativistic origin of the fine and hyperfine structure

modules.moduleContent: Partition functions (simple sub-systems, ideal gas, simple solids, ferro-magnet, Ising model). Micro-canonical and grand canonical ensembles. Phase transitions and critical phenomena. Bose-Einstein and Fermi-Dirac statistics. Bose-Einstein condensation (superconductors and super-fluids). Information theoretic approach to entropy and partition function.
learningOutcomes: Recall the microscopic definitions and basic properties of entropy, temperature and chemical potential
Give a microscopic interpretation of the second and third laws of thermodynamics
Deduce and apply the canonical and grand canonical distributions for the states of systems in contact with heat and particle reservoirs
Calculate partition functions and thermal averages for non-interacting systems such as ideal gases and paramagnets
Derive the equipartition theorem for the energy and discuss its physical implications and its regime of validity
Deduce the Fermi-Dirac and Bose-Einstein distributions and apply them in combination with the density of states
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to classical and quantum information, quantum communication and cryptography, quantum teleportation, physical and conceptual models of computation and computational complexity classes, quantum algorithms, theory of open quantum systems, quantum error correction, fault-tolerant quantum computing, topological quantum computing, physical realization of quantum information processing.
learningOutcomes: Define quantum bits, their composition and elementary quantum operations including measurement
Use Schmidt decomposition to characterize entangled and separable two-qubit states and their application in teleportation and dense coding
Demonstrate exponential speedup of quantum computing with the example of the Deutsch-Jozsa quantum computing algorithm
Compare computational complexity classes of classical, probabilistic, quantum and non-deterministic computing models
Explain the concept of open quantum systems, their states using density matrix formalism and their operations using operator sum representation
Present basic single-qubit error processes in the Bloch representation
Describe quantum error correction on the example of the Shor nine qubit code in the standard and stabilizer formulations
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Homotopy, homology and cohomology in physical contexts; Solitons, including vortices, monopoles and other symmetry breaking defects, instantons; Momentum space topology topological insulators and generalizations; Topological field theory, axioms and computational framework/diagrammatics, examples including Chern Simons theory, topological stabiliser codes; Quantum Hall and related effects; Edge modes and bulk edge correspondences.
learningOutcomes: Classify symmetry breaking defects in various dimensions.
Calculate topological invariants appearing in physical models.
Be familiar with the framework of topological field theory and able to perform basic diagrammatic computations.
Describe the role of topology in the phase diagrams of physical systems, with relevant examples.

modules.moduleContent: Attendance at at least 10 research seminars given by leading researchers in a variety of areas of Mathematics and Theoretical Physics. The student must submit a summary of two of these seminars (one in Mathematics and one in Theoretical Physics).

The student will work independently on a research project in a chosen area of mathematical physics.  The results will be written up in a report and presented in an oral presentation at the end of the year.
learningOutcomes: Carry out independent research on a given topic in theoretical physics or a related area
Present their results in a structured report in accordance with standards of academic writing in theoretical physics
Give an oral presentation of their research to an audience of their peers

modules.moduleName: ORAL AND POSTER PRESENTATION OF RESEARCH WORK AT AN INTERNATIONAL CONFERENCE, MEETING OR WORKSHOP
modules.moduleContent: Students write and submit research abstracts/papers (with assistance from their supervisor) and then present their work at conferences in the form of an oral presentation.  (The same work cannot be submitted for this module and module GSE1).

Module Objective:
Develop advanced skills in Analysis.

Selected topics in Analysis.
learningOutcomes: State some of the classical theorems in analysis.
Prove any of the theorems given in the course.
Deduce properties which follow from these theorems.
Prove analytic results using rigorous arguments.

Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course. Reading courses are quite varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic. Students will submit a report of approximately 15 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.
learningOutcomes: To enable the student to master some mathematical topic.

modules.moduleName: TECHNICAL PRESENTATION SKILLS IN MATHEMATICAL SCIENCE
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
Learn essential technical skills for presenting Mathematics or Statistics research in a paper and in an academic talk.

Essential typesetting skills for Mathematics and Statistics in TeX and or LaTeX; Beamer or other slides package; PSTricks or other graphics package.

The same work cannot be submitted for credit in this module and GSE1.
learningOutcomes: Typeset documents containing both displayed and inline formulae involving common mathematical symbols.
Typeset material containing displayed and inline formulae designed for slide presentations.
Create simple diagrams designed for inclusion in Mathematics/Statistics documents.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
Learn to read mathematics or statistics textbooks, monographs, and research papers in German.

Self study of:
- basic textbooks for learning German;
- mathematics or statistics source material in German.

Read Mathematics or Statistics monographs and research papers in German.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
To expose students to new results and research in mathematics presented by national and international speakers.

The research topics vary from year to year. The students attend lectures by invited experts over a period of one year. The student must attend at least 70% of the lectures given.
learningOutcomes: Learn at least a little from all well presented mathematics seminars and colloquia.
Make useful summary notes on a talk during the course of that talk.
Write up a summary report on a talk that is close to the student’s research area.
Ask appropriate questions to help clarify some points of the talk.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
Learn programming skills useful for Mathematics and Statistics research. 

This course introduces students to the fundamentals of programming for mathematical and scientific applications.
learningOutcomes: Understand and use basic data structures.
Construct loops in a computer programme.
Write basic computer programmes.
Use a programming language to solve mathematical problems.
