modules.moduleContent: The Exsultet. Chanted readings (including the Sung Passion). The psalm settings of Ronan McDonagh. The Communion Antiphons of the Gregorian tradition.
learningOutcomes: On completion of the module, the student should be able to confidently engage with the Gregorian repertoire of Communion Antiphons.
On completion of the module, the student should be able to accurately interpret substantial through-composed psalm settings in English and Irish.
On completion of the module, the student should be able to apply chant formulae to liturgical prayers and readings.
On completion of the module, the student should be able to vocally and musically sustain the delivery of challenging liturgical elements.

modules.moduleContent: This module explores a selection of music from the 19th to the 21st century, including non-western musical traditions.
learningOutcomes: identify the music of the 19th to 21st century, and of different musical cultures.
describe selected musical instruments, forms, textures, genres, and performance contexts from around the world.
transcribe and/or analyse short pieces or extracts of music.
write a structured essay in an academic style about selected music cultures.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to fundamental skills relating to audio. Acoustics and psychoacoustic principles are considered alongside an introdution to audio recording. Key concerns of the module include the practical use of microphones and music programming software.
learningOutcomes: explain key concepts of acoustics and psychoacoustics
demonstrate the ability to effectively use various recording techniques
utilise audio software for synthesis and signal processing

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces learners to basic orchestration and arrangement techniques through the study of basic instrumental (and vocal) technique and timbres, doubling, blending, and genre arrangement strategies, culminating in a structured orchestration assignment.
learningOutcomes: Understand, recall and apply basic instrumental characteristics and technique for standard orchestral instruments and voices.
Recognise, understand and apply basic doubling and blending techniques in orchestration/arrangement.
Understand and apply good notational practice in producing musical scores.
Render a short piano sketch for ensemble.
Produce basic mock-ups of musical scores.

modules.moduleContent: An exploration of some of the critical issues raised by the role of music in film. The course will offer an introduction to the rapidly emerging field of film music studies, providing students with the critical tools to address a number of key questions: what functions does music perform in film? How does it relate to image and dialogue? Do we actually notice music in the cinema? Students will apply these questions to case studies representative of a range of historical and cultural practices, from so-called silent film to the contemporary pop soundtrack
learningOutcomes: utilise the technical terminology of film music studies
situate film music studies within the wider context of scholarship on music and on film
analyse and interpret the role of music in film
recognise and contextualise a range of film music practices

modules.moduleContent: Chamber music platform class
learningOutcomes: examine the wider musical theatre, Irish Traditional, classical, and pop/rock ensemble repertoire - as appropriate to their strand.
gain valuable teamwork skills
perform a short ensemble music recital.

modules.moduleContent: As a pre-sessional programme, the module presents students with the opportunity to improve their English language skills in reading, writing, listening and speaking to a level consistent with IELTS 5.5 (CEFR B2.1) to 6.0 (CEFR B2.2) in preparation for taking the official exam. It also aims to provide students with the specific lexis, terminology, academic knowledge and study skills necessary to embark on first-year studies in Economics at Maynooth University, enabling them to fully engage with their studies, including required reading, presentations, lectures and assessment. Basic research skills through English will also be taught alongside appropriate citation and referencing, enabling students to become more independent in their work and study, and allowing them to credit other authors’ work, where applicable.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a level of academic English language usage that is consistent with overall IELTS 5.5 (CEFR B2.1) to 6.0 (CEFR B2.2) through completion of an IELTS-style exam containing content, tasks, timings and assessment similar to IELTS exam conditions.
Use appropriate citation and referencing conventions utilising The Harvard Referencing System.

Demonstrate effective essay-writing skills, including appropriate language, structure and punctuation, across a range of essay types, including Comparison/Argument/Cause & Effect/Problem-solution and Examination.
Employ a range of key lexis appropriate to an economics register in their academic work, consistent with first-year studies.
Apply critical reflection to their academic work and studies in an effort to gain a better understanding of how the process of learning works most effectively at an individual level.
Prepare and give a presentation relating to an economics topic using a technological slide-based format incorporating appropriate text, graphics/diagrams and oral commentary.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: ENGLISH FOR ACADEMIC PURPOSES (LEVEL B2.2 OF THE CEFR)
modules.moduleContent: This English for Academic Purposes (EAP) module focuses on enabling students to acquire a sufficient command of the English language approximate to CEFR level B2.2 and involving all four skills of reading, writing, listening and speaking, to enable them to engage successfully with their academic studies across a range of subject areas. At CEFR B2.2, students will be able to understand extended speech and lectures; follow complex lines of argument; understand particular attitudes and viewpoints in extended written texts; present clear, detailed descriptions on a wide range of subjects; write clear, detailed texts such as essays or reports, on a variety of topics and subjects. All of the above will be accomplished using very good, appropriate levels of grammatical accuracy and lexical range.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a level of Academic English language usage that is consistent with overall CEFR B2.2 through completion of assessment procedures and tasks that simulate authentic EAP language abilities and critical skills in Higher Education study;

Design and deliver an academic presentation relating to a relevant EAP topic using a technological, slide-based format incorporating text, graphics/diagrams with appropriate (B2.2) oral commentary in support;
Read academic texts, including visual information texts, with increased understanding and awareness using academic reading strategy PQRR;
Apply improved levels of academic note-taking in relation to lectures and aural content;
Produce written language in appropriate academic style at individual word, clause and sentence levels, including visual information.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module will introduce students to the principles of managing innovation, focusing in particular on how innovation is managed in entrepreneurial and global settings, the management of innovation systems and the dynamics of technological innovation. Innovation is a key priority for companies operating in dynamic and fast changing business environments characterised by the emergence of new competitors and blurring boundaries between different actors. Against this background, this introductory module provides students with a clear understanding of the multifaceted nature of innovation and how innovation impacts businesses and society. Students are introduced to the origins and to the systemic nature of innovation as well as to practices, tools, and frameworks that help companies to manage innovation and to develop strategies to make use of the increased interconnectivity of different actors. 

The module is structured into for main themes: 
1. The Types, Dynamics and Systemic Nature of Innovation 
2. The Origins of Innovation and the Role of Entrepreneurship 
3. Managing Innovation 
4. Globalisation of Innovation Activities and Recent Developments
learningOutcomes: Explain the importance of various sources of innovation and the role of entrepreneurship for innovation activities;
Evaluate the consequences of innovation and technological change;
Discuss the importance and the key principles of innovation management;
Discuss the interconnectivity among different actors in innovation processes; and
Examine the application of innovation management tools/theories/frameworks in real-world business cases.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces the student to key topics in international business and marketing. The module is broken into two elements: Introduction to International Business and Marketing. 

International Business

The module provides introduction to international business. This course is structured into four main themes:

1) What is international business? 
2) Globalisation and international business.
3) International business, a world of constant change – regional trends and key challenges in international business. 
4) The role of government in international business.

Marketing

The module provides an introduction to the principles and concepts of marketing and serves as a foundation for more advanced subjects in the field of marketing and sales. This element of the course is structured into the following themes: 

1) Consumer Buyer Behaviour (including consumer analysis and purchasing behaviour);
2) Product and Brand Management; 
3) Pricing Decisions and Distribution Channels;
4) Marketing Communications and Promotion Methods.
learningOutcomes: Define and recognise key concepts in international business;
Understand key drivers of globalisation and international business;
Appraise the rise of emerging market multinationals (EMNEs);
Define and recognise core marketing concepts and basic marketing terminology (the “language” of marketing);
Illustrate how consumers/customers make purchasing decisions and explain the influences on consumer behaviour;
Describe what market segmentation is and understand how to segment a market;
Identify and explain the basic concepts and principles within the marketing mix (the basic “tools” of marketing); and

modules.moduleContent:
Organisations play an important role in modern society from social, economic, political, legal, and ethical perspectives. They have become a dominant means of creating wealth and employment, are crucial sources of innovation and allow addressing several contemporary social, political, and economic problems. Organisations are “living” systems, whose characteristics, forms and activities are subject to continuous evolution. In particular, the emergence of new economies, the decline of traditional multinational corporations and the emphasis on new technologies have posed new challenges to organisations, making it essential to be able to design, manage, analyse and understand organisations in contemporary times. Organisations make things happen by designing systems to formulate and implement their strategies. Organisational design is strongly connected to organisational success, as it is through the design of an organisation that things get done and performance is achieved. 

This module provides students with an understanding of the principles of organisational design, and explores how organisational design influences the effective functioning of organisations in attainment of their mission and objectives. Also, the module illustrates how managers and other organisation members can use different perspectives on organisational design as tools for structuring activities, for managing the relations with the external environment and for coping with rapid changes. In order to engage students in a creative effort to apply empirically supported theoretical principles to actual business examples, the module builds on examples, case studies and assignments about real-world companies in a variety of industries, businesses, and countries.
learningOutcomes: List and describe the main concepts proposed by each perspective in organisational theory and design;
Discuss the differences between the theoretical perspectives of designing organisations;
Discuss and compare the opportunities and challenges that organisations confront when trying to modify their characteristics and design;
Identify and explain where one approach to organisational design would be more applicable than others;
Apply key concepts of organisational design to analysis of real-time situations in organisations; and
Provide theoretically informed recommendations on how organisations should modify elements of their design and management to be successful.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces various elements that comprise the field of Project Management. It aims to build an understanding of where project management fits into the organisation and the interaction between project management and the other management functions within an organisation. The module integrates quantitative and qualitative tools for project management. The core purpose of the module is to develop students’ comprehensive understanding and provide them conceptual lenses and analytic tools in the project management field.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of project management in organisational and wide business context;
Know the key stages of progression all projects must go through;
Use basic project management tools and techniques;
Appreciate the myriad skills required of an effective project manager; and
Develop important team skills.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides a comprehensive analysis of our modern understanding of brands as it has developed over the last decades. This module takes a multidisciplinary approach drawing on material from a variety of areas of literature in order to present a deep understanding of what a brand is, what it can do, and how it can create and deliver value for both organizations and for consumers. Managerial implications of brands and implications of various perspectives on, and functions of, brands will be discussed.
learningOutcomes: Comprehend how brands are linked to their organizations and what implications this has for brand management;
Understand customer-based brand equity and how it can be created, managed, and utilized;
Discuss the role brands play in consumer self-expression and what implications this has for brand managers;
Understand why and how consumers can form relationships with brands;
Comprehend the foundations and consequences of brand communities; and
Identify and explain the role certain brands can play in our modern society, how this comes about, and what consequences this has.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO THE IRISH EQUINE INDUSTRY
modules.moduleContent:
This module was designed to enable students to understand the business environment within the Irish equine industry. It examines the range of equine firms operating within Ireland. It will offer insights into the interplay between the various equine business, industry stakeholders, and the business environment in which they operate in Ireland.

This module will give students a comprehensive knowledge of the size and economic importance of both the thoroughbred and sport horse sectors of the Irish equine industry relative to our main international competitors, including population/production figures, participant numbers, regional centres of excellence, numbers employed, and performance parameters. It examines the organisational structure and responsibilities and critically evaluates the objectives of the governing and relevant industry bodies. It appraises the input and response by the governing bodies and industry stakeholders to Government policies. It identifies and assesses the financial support structures in place within the industry and the role of public funding.

Students will learn about and can discuss Government policy and topical issues of concern within the industry with expert staff and, where appropriate, industry practitioners as part of a guest speaker series within the module. Specifically, the module will assess the status of the Irish equine industry within the international equine industry. It will explore the potential for future growth and development of the equine industry on the world stage.

This module aims to ensure students critically evaluate the following: the structure, size and economic importance of the Irish equine industry, the historical and current Government policy towards the sector, the financial support structures in place, the role of all industry stakeholders and the status of the Irish equine industry within the international equine industry.
learningOutcomes: Define, describe, and discuss the thoroughbred and sport horse sectors within the Irish equine industry relative to our main competitors (types of businesses, size of equine firms, those operating within Ireland and those operating internationally, numbers employed, numbers involved, equine population, economic importance, and performance parameters).
Critique the aims and objectives of the relevant governing and industry bodies within the Irish equine industry.
Appraise the financial structures supporting the Irish equine industry and evaluate possible future alternatives.
Demonstrate knowledge of the interplay between the various equine business, industry stakeholders, and the business environment in which they operate.
Apply concepts and best practice from the business management, marketing and financial management literature to equine firms and organisations.
Challenge accepted norms within the equine industry; Establish and defend their own views and opinions on topical issues within the Irish equine industry; and
Assess the importance of and potential for further development of the Irish equine industry within the international equine industry.

modules.moduleContent: This module will give a brief overview of the Irish and international leisure and tourism industries before examining the main equestrian disciplines/activities in Ireland which contribute to the sector here. It will assess the role of equestrian ‘gateway’ organisations and associated bodies such as Failte Ireland and Tourism Ireland. It will examine the popularity, funding and economic performance of the different disciplines/activities which make up the Irish equine leisure and tourism sector. It will review policies in place to broaden and enhance the appeal of equine activities and events to the public and tourists. As part of this review, iconic equine events, for example, the Dublin Horse Show will be examined and the role and appeal of such events within the wider leisure and tourism sector discussed.
learningOutcomes: Define, describe and discuss the main equestrian disciplines and activities which contribute to the sport, leisure, and tourism sectors in Ireland.
Evaluate the economic impact of major equine events and their appeal as leisure and tourism attractions.
Explain and critique the role of the relevant industry bodies within the leisure and tourism sector and how they interact with their counterparts in the equine industry.
Appraise the various policies which have been formulated to increase attendance (participants and spectators) at equine events.
Establish their own views and opinions on topical issues facing the equine industry within the broader sport, leisure, and tourism sectors.

modules.moduleContent: The aim of this course is to show that the effective use of modern Information Systems (IS) in Business depends on understanding the relationship between the systems, data and its context. The difference between success and failure depends on managing the elements of that context, not just the technological aspects. An ‘interaction model’ will be introduced that will drive the agenda of the course – exploring the nature and roles of stakeholders, outcomes, implementation and learning, and internal and external contexts.
learningOutcomes: Appreciate how, in recent years, technology has helped bring about radical changes across industries and throughout societies;
Explain the significance of information in organisations and why people depend on information that is accurate and timely;
Apply critical analysis to the effective use of modern IS in so far is they depend on understanding the relationship between the system and its context;
Assess how the difference between success and failure in IS can depend on managing the elements of that context as a whole, not just the technological aspects;
Explain the theoretical and empirical basis for the IS ‘interaction model’ which comprises stakeholders, outcomes, implementation and learning, and internal and external contexts; and
Understand why data is often considered a defensible source of competitive advantage and how advantages based on capabilities and data that others can acquire could be short-lived.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module takes a multi level perspective of the complexities of managing large multinational corporations (MNCs). The student’s understanding of the challenges facing MNC managers, including subsidiary CEOs and other middle managers in the new global economy and multiple institutional and organisational contexts, is developed and extended. Understanding when and how to adapt subsidiary operations to meet the demands of local contexts while delivering on organisational imperatives is critical if managers are to effectively manage across borders. The key dilemmas arising from this imperative, as MNC activities are increasingly fine sliced across the globe are considered.
learningOutcomes: Identify key internal and external challenges facing MNCs as they intensify their global activities and strategies to overcome them;
Apply the key frameworks to explain how MNCs attain their goals of efficiency, flexibility and learning;
Demonstrate an understanding of the relationship between strategy, structure, processes and policies in the MNE; and
Examine interunit relationships in the MNC and their role in deploying resources and building capabilities.

modules.moduleContent:
Having developed a literature review, where the student has identified a relevant and compelling set of research questions, and having demonstrated an understanding of an appropriate stream of research from MN805, the next question is how to design a research study that can translate these contributions into reality. This module will enable students to make a careful assessment of an appropriate research design, including specific methods for collecting data. Students will evaluate different research designs and will discuss the appropriateness of specific research designs and research methods by reference to the research questions, literature, and competing research design options.

Students will be introduced to selected research designs. Students will need to carefully consider the underlying assumptions upon which a research design is based, the mechanics of its implementation, and the wider context in which a research design is deployed in the field and the outputs may be interpreted by the research and practice community. The choice of a specific research design is often subject to intense scholarly debates. This module intends to assist students to develop appropriate responses to these debates and to develop convincing arguments for justifying the appropriateness of their research design.

Registration as a student on the structured doctoral programme in the School of Business and need to have completed or be currently be registered on MN805 Research Questions, Literature Reviews and Theory Building in Business and Management Research.
learningOutcomes: Identify appropriate research designs to address a research question and to make a meaningful contribution to the state of knowledge;
Delineate and discriminate between competing research designs;
Choose an appropriate research design for addressing their research question(s) among competing alternatives; and
Justify the appropriateness of the chosen research design.

modules.moduleContent: Schroedinger wave equation, Probabilistic interpretation of the wave function, Simple piecewise linear potentials, Tunnelling, Observables. Hilbert space formalism, Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle. Harmonic oscillator, Ladder operators, Planck's law of radiation. Entanglement, Bell’s inequalities.
learningOutcomes: explain the Planck radiation law and the significance of the photoelectric effect
pass between differential equation and operator formulations of quantum mechanics
work with quantum mechanical Hilbert space formalism
obtain the energy levels and eigenstates for the harmonic oscillator
calculate quantum tunneling amplitudes

modules.moduleContent:
Crystal Structure; Free Electron Theory of Metals; Energy Bands; Semiconductors; Diamagnetism and Paramagnetism;

MP464 cannot be taken together with EP402.
learningOutcomes: Identify crystal structures and associated reciprocal lattices, including Brillouin zone structure
Describe the criteria for Bragg reflection
Interpret physical properties of crystals in terms of binding energies and phonons
Calculate simple transport properties of conductors and semi-conductors
Reproduce the basic physics underlying band theory

modules.moduleContent: Review of Maxwell's equations in vacuo. Scalar and vector potentials. Multipole expansions in electrostatics. Multipole expansions in magnetostatics. Dielectrics. Diamagnetism and Paramagnetism. Maxwell's equations in the presence of matter.  Radiation from simple systems: multipole expansions and energy transport.  Relativistic formalism of electromagnetism. Gauge invariance.
learningOutcomes: Develop multipole expansions due to localised charge distributions in both static and dynamic situations
Apply Green function techniques to solving electrostatic and radiation problems
Demonstrate the ability to solve Maxwell's equations in dielectric and magnetic media
Assess energy transport properties of radiation from simple systems
Describe the relativistic formulation of electromagnetic theory

modules.moduleName: MATHEMATICAL METHODS (DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS AND COMPLEX ANALYSIS)
modules.moduleContent: Sturm-Liouville theory and expansion in orthogonal bases. Fourier and Laplace transforms. Partial differential equations: Heat equations, Wave equation, Laplace's equation. Spherical harmonics. Green's functions for boundary value problems. Complex analysis up to Cauchy's residue theorem.
learningOutcomes: Solve linear inhomogeneous ordinary differential equations, involving elementary and special functions, with initial conditions or boundary conditions
Recognise and use the basic theory of linear, ordinary differential equations (Sturm-Liouville theory, orthogonal function expansions)
Solve partial differential equations using separation of variables (with specific applications to the wave equation, the heat equation and Laplace's equation)
Recognise solutions of the three-dimensional Laplace operator in terms of spherical harmonic functions
Apply the principles of complex analysis to integrate complex functions using the calculus of residues to compute definite integrals
Manipulate integral transforms and evaluate Fourier transforms

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to General Relativity and the Equivalence Principle. Curved spaces and metrics. Geodesics in metric spaces. Vectors, tensors and coordinate transformations. The Christoffel connection and Riemann curvature. Einstein's equations and some of their solutions.
learningOutcomes: Describe the geometry of curved spaces and curved space-times in terms of a metric; derive the Levi-Civita connection associated with a given metric; obtain the Riemann curvature tensor, the Ricci tensor, the Ricci scalar and the Einstein tensor associated with such a metric
Obtain the geodesic equations in arbitrary space-times
Derive the Schwarzschild solution to the vacuum Einstein field equations under the assumption of time translational and spherical symmetry
Derive expressions for the gravitational redshift, the precession of the perihelion of planetary orbits, and the deflection of light rays in the Schwarzschild geometry
Show that there exist plane-wave solutions to the vacuum Einstein equations in the linear approximation, corresponding to gravational waves, and derive some of their physical properties

Obtain the energy-momentum tensor for a fluid,  discuss the consequences of spatial isotropy for a Universe with a cosmological constant filled with a fluid, and hence construct the Friedmann-Robertson-Walker metric
Obtain the Friedmann equations from the Einstein field equations, solve these equations for the scale factor and discuss the implications of these solutions

modules.moduleContent: The Dirac field and its quantisation; Feynman rules for Quantum Electrodynamics (QED); Elementary processes of QED; S matrix: Scattering and decay; Trace technology; Crossing symmetry; Radiative corrections: Infrared and ul traviolet divergencies, Loop computations, Optical theorem, Ward Takahashi identities; Renormalisation of electric charge.
learningOutcomes: Carry out quantisation of spin 1/2 fields
Exp lain the implications of gauge invariance for quantisation and renormalisation of gauge fields
Carry out one loop calculations in quantum electrodynamics
Describe the principles of the renormalisation group equations
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The Department of Theoretical Physics runs modules in association with the School of Mathematics, Trinity College Dublin, the School of Theoretical Physics, Dublin Institute for Advanced Studies and the School of Mathematical Science, U.C.D.  This is a catch-all module and represents modules that can be chosen, in consultation with the Head of the Theoretical Physics Department, from the above universities.  Advanced level 6 modules normally on offer are: High Energy Physics, General Relativity, Statistical Mechanics, Quantum Gravity and others that may arise and run by agreement of the Heads of the above schools and departments.

Students must consult with their Course Coordinator before registering for this module.
Noether's theorem, the Klein-Gordon field and its quantisation; LSZ reduction formula; Path integrals in quantum mechanics and field theory; Perturbation theory, Feynman diagrams, S-matrix
learningOutcomes: Derive conserved charges in classical field theory using Noether's theorem
Carry out canonical and path integral quantisation of scalar fields
Compute tree-level S matrix elements
Describe the principles of renormalisation and renormalisability
Compute scattering cross-sections and decay rates to one loop in scalar field theory

modules.moduleContent: The Dirac field and its quantisation; The Maxwell field and its quantisation; Feynman rules for Quantum Electrodynamics (QED); Elementary processes of QED; S-matrix: Scattering and decay; Trace technology; Crossing symmetry; Radiative corrections: Infrared and Ultraviolet divergencies, Loop computations, Optical theorem, Ward-Takahashi identities; Renormalisation of electric charge.
learningOutcomes: Carry out quantisation of spin-1/2 fields
Explain the implications of gauge invariance for quantisation and renormalisation of gauge fields
Carry out one-loop calculations in quantum electrodynamics
Describe the principles of the renormalisation group equations

modules.moduleName: MINOR ADVANCED SUMMER SCHOOL, TECHNICAL COURSE OR INTERNATIONAL PLACEMENT
modules.moduleContent: Content depends on the supervisor-approved course.

Module Objective: 
To introduce students to the mathematics underlying financial calculus.

Review of basic ideas from probability. Introduction to Brownian motion and geometric Brownian
motion. Stopping times and the reflection principle. Introduction to martingales.

Evaluation of risk: Expected Value Criterion; Mean Variance Criterion; Maxmin Criterion; Expected Utility Criterion. The asking price and the risk premium. Measurements of Risk Aversion. The Portfolio Selection Problem and Risk Aversion.
Binomial models of the stock market. The arbitrage theorem. Pricing options using the Black-Scholes formula.
learningOutcomes: Solve problems associated with Brownian Motion and martingales.
Solve problems associated to portfolio selection and risk aversion.
Use the methods of the course to price options.
Explain methods and interpret problem solutions.
State definitions and theorems from the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: to develop advanced skills in geometry with an emphasis on Riemannian geometry as well as topics in pseudo-Riemannian geometry relating to theoretical physics. This will ensure students receive a thorough grounding in topics like smooth manifold theory, tensor analysis and curvature.

Core Topics: smooth manifolds and corresponding tangent and vector bundles, tensors including differential forms and Riemannian/pseudo-Riemannian metrics, connections, geodesics and curvature (sectional, Ricci and scalar), exponential map, extrinsic curvature, second fundamental form and the Gauss curvature equation.

Optional Extra Topics (which may vary) drawn from: Jacobi fields, comparison geometry, curvature constraints and topological obstructions, Gauss-Bonnet Theorem.
learningOutcomes: State, derive and prove relevant definitions, formulas and theorems in classical and modern geometry.
Perform relevant curvature and geodesic calculations.
Deduce properties arising from theorems in the course.
Prove analytic results using rigorous mathematical arguments.

modules.moduleContent:
Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course. Reading courses are quite varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic. Students will submit a report of approximately 15 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.
learningOutcomes: To enable the student to master some mathematical topic.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  Learn essential skills for motivating people to enjoy Mathematics/Statistics; learn essential skills for helping people to solve their difficulties with the subject and to develop confidence in it.

Essential skills of motivating people to enjoy Mathematics/Statistics; essential skills of helping them with their difficulties in the subject, and helping them to develop confidence in it.  Communicating mathematics or Statistics to a general audience.
learningOutcomes: Recognise common difficulties that students have with Mathematics/Statistics.
Help the student deal with such common difficulties.
Help students discover how to overcome difficulties with specific problems in homework and class material without actually solving such problems for the students.
Communicate Mathematics or Statistics to a general audience.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
Learn to develop CVs and application letters appropriate to job search and grant applications.  Learn where to find job listings and available grants, and how to tailor application materials to the job or grant.

Write CV for self and for possible future self five years down the road, and write supporting application letters.  Search for suitable jobs and grants.
learningOutcomes: Write a good CV for future job applications.
Find advertised job listings and available grants.
Tailor application materials to particular jobs or grants.
Read cues in advertisements regarding what are the key requirements for the job or grant.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
Develop advanced skills in Algebraic Topology.
 
Relate topology to algebra, and then use algebraic techniques to solve topological problems.
learningOutcomes: State some of the classical theorems of Algebraic Topology
Prove any of the theorems given in the course.
Deduce properties which follow from these theorems.
Prove other topological results using rigorous arguments.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
To learn more about a specialized area of Mathematics or Statistics.

Depends on summer school/conference.  The same summer school/conference cannot be the basis for credit in both this module and MT860.
learningOutcomes: Choose conferences/summer schools to attend that are appropriate to the student’s research interests.
Apply for such support as may be available for conference/summer school attendance.
Pick appropriate talks to attend at the conference.
Write up notes on talks attended suitable for writing a brief report on such talks.
