modules.moduleContent: Christian understanding of time.  Sunday.  Liturgical seasons.  The Liturgy of the Hours.  Occasional liturgy
learningOutcomes: On completion of the course the student should be able to articulate an understanding of the celebration of liturgy through the year.
On completion of the course the student should be able to demonstrate a familiarity with the nature and structure of the Liturgy of the Hours
On completion of the course the student should be able to understand the nature of Sunday and the liturgical seasons.
On completion of the course the student should be able to understand the nature of daily and occasional liturgies.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will be an overview of some of the major musical forms, techniques and styles cultivated in the Baroque and Classical Eras to the beginnings of early Romanticism. Students will be able to write critically about music.

Topics covered: The development of vocal and instrumental music from the mid-seventeenth to early nineteenth centuries; the suite; the concerto; the invention of opera; the da capo aria; oratorio, the string quartet; the symphony; the classical concerto; art song.
learningOutcomes: recognise some of the principal forms, techniques, and styles of European art music from the period c. 1650 to 1830;
understand the development of early music in historical context;
prepare a rigorously researched essay for this final project and show evidence of being able to engage with music critically;
to relate music to other disciplines;
to begin to discuss music and emotion.

modules.moduleContent: This module aims to equip the student with a detailed knowledge of the advanced forms of consonance and dissonance, both diatonic and chromatic, in tonal and non-tonal harmony.
learningOutcomes: Recognize and describe in detail various forms of musical dissonance
Recognize, describe in detail various forms of consonance (added diatonic chords)
Recognize and describe in detail various forms of modulation, directly and indirectly, and identify tonalities and their relationships and tonicization
Write an analytical commentary on selected music from the 18th to the early 20th century with regard to the harmonic language.

modules.moduleContent: This module will introduce the studio to the students and provide the basic information needed to safely handle the studio equipment. In addition, foundation topics on recording will be covered, to provide the students with the basic skills necessary to use the recording studio. Topics include: studio layout, basic audio theory, mixers, microphones and recording. Students will be assessed by regular assignments and a final practical project. In the second semester, the module will cover more advanced aspects of studio practice, recording techniques and production. Topics will include location recording, multitrack and overdubbing, mixing routines, mastering, etc.
learningOutcomes: list the main elements and functions of a recording studio.
operate a standard mixing desk.
apply microphone placement techniques in a recording scenario.
use mixing and overdubbing techniques for audio production.

modules.moduleContent:
Weekly group performance class (''Platform'') for first-study instrumentalists/vocalists; group keyboard lessons, membership of an approved ensemble. 

Visiting International Students in Maynooth for one semester or one academic year should contact the Department of Music for information before registering.
learningOutcomes: perform a short recital at post-Grade 7 standard (only applicable to students taking the first-study instrument/voice strand).
perform technical studies, scales, and arpeggios as relevant to their instrument/voice of at least Grade 8 standard (only applicable to students taking the first-study instrument/voice strand).
demonstrate intermediate techniques in harmonisation, transposition, and improvisation at the keyboard (only applicable to students taking the group piano lessons strand).
Critique the performance of others

modules.moduleContent: This module is grounded in the humanistic study of music, where music history is interpreted in the broadest sense: including Western art music and hidden music histories. This course is especially suited to those interested in seeing music within a broad cultural context, embracing the literary, the social, the historical and the political. This course introduces students to critical and sustained engagement with multifarious musical works, studied in relation to the historical and social conditions that shaped their genesis and to the meanings ascribed to them by diverse audiences at the time of their creation and since then. Through the study of selected representative musical works from various cultures, this module explores the historical conditions under which music was produced and the purposes it served for composers, performers, patrons and the public. While students are encouraged to become conversant with related fields such as gender studies, history, literature and cultural studies, the study of music, musicians, listeners, and the mechanisms that create and disseminate music remain at the centre of this module. We aim for a balance between the study of historical and social factors that shape music (and are shaped by it in turn) and an examination of musical texts.
learningOutcomes: define the musicological canon, apply it to music which they have studied and outline its advantages and disadvantages.
identify different categorizations of periodization in music history and be able to discuss the pros/cons of each type of periodization
identify key elements in reception history in relation to two composers they have studied
discuss alternative histories and give examples of the recent recovery of forgotten composers.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to musical sources and texts and how they provide both an insight and a challenge to researchers and performers alike. It will begin by asking ‘what is a musical text?’ and will, through case studies, address key issues such as the problem of musical notation, contemporary and historical ideas of creativity and authorship, musical ‘borrowing’, quotation in music of the past and present, the varied mediatized disseminations of music and its sources today, musical sources in their wider context, and digital archives.
learningOutcomes: Discern different types of musical sources.
Question and engage critically with musical texts.
Understand the value and role in research of diverse bibliographical tools and directories.
Engage with and discuss critically the dissemination of musical sources in various forms, including their mediatization, remediation, and intermediality.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
As a focused 7-week pre-sessional programme, the MLEAP12 module presents undergraduate students with the opportunity to improve their academic English language skills in reading, writing, listening and speaking to a level consistent with IELTS 6.0 (CEFR B2.2).  In addition, MLEAP12 prepares the students with appropriate strategies for taking an IELTS-type exam in order to demonstrate attainment of the above IELTS score band, and so gain entry to their offered degree programmes at Maynooth.*
*MU degree offers for participants on MLEAP12 are made on the condition that the participating undergraduate students will have attained an overall IELTS score band of 6.0 (CEFR B2.2) in order to be admitted to their offered programmes of study.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a level of academic English language usage (listening, reading, writing and speaking) that is consistent with overall IELTS 6.0, for undergraduates, (CEFR mid B2) and 6.5 for postgraduates, (CEFR high B2) through completion of an IELTS-style exam containing content, tasks, timings and assessment similar to IELTS exam conditions.
Interpret IELTS question formats and writing task titles/requirements accurately.

modules.moduleName: ENGLISH FOR ACADEMIC PURPOSES (LEVEL C1.2 OF THE CEFR)
modules.moduleContent: This module focuses on enabling students (i) to select an appropriate formulation from a broad range of language to express themselves clearly, without having to restrict what they want to say; (ii) to express themselves clearly and with minimum sign of having to restrict what they want to say.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a level of Academic English usage that is consistent with overall CER C1.2 through completion of assessment procedures and tasks that simulate authentic EAP language abilities and critical skills in Higher Education study;

Design and deliver an academic presentation relating to a relevant EAP topic using a technological, slide-based format incorporating text, graphics/diagrams with appropriate (C1.2) oral commentary in support;
Read academic texts with an excellent level of understanding and critical awareness of stance, evaluation, perspective, objectivity, subjectivity, connections/relationships and sources;
Make highly accurate academic notes from lectures, including visual information, and show ability to summarize, organize and paraphrase content as required;
Produce written language in an appropriate academic style at both paragraph and essay level.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces the student to key topics in business models and marketing. The module is broken into two elements: Business Models and Marketing.

Business Models

The module introduces the student to the concept of business models. A business model is at its most basic a description of how a firm creates value for its customers. It is about how a firm designs itself to deliver a product or service a customer needs; how it gathers together and organises the resources and capabilities to create the product or service for the customer; and how it makes money from doing so. It is a simple way to think about and model the strategy of a firm and how to communicate this to its employees, customers, suppliers, investors and the wider stakeholder community. This element is structured into four main themes, which answer the questions below:

1) What is a business model of an organisation?
2) What are the main types of business models?
3) How do we assess potential profitability of a business model?
4) How do we design, implement and innovate business models to create value for the firm and its stakeholders?

Marketing

The module provides an introduction to the principles and concepts of marketing and serves as a foundation for more advanced subjects in the field of marketing and sales. This element of the course is structured into the following themes: 

1) Consumer Buyer Behaviour (including consumer analysis and purchasing behaviour);
2) Product and Brand Management; 
3) Pricing Decisions and Distribution Channels;
4) Marketing Communications and Promotion Methods.
learningOutcomes: Identify all basic components of a business model and their linkages and describe the main types of business models;
Assess the expected profitability of a business model and understand the process of business model innovation;
Define and recognise core marketing concepts and basic marketing terminology (the “language” of marketing);
Illustrate how consumers/customers make purchasing decisions and explain the influences on consumer behaviour;
Describe what market segmentation is and understand how to segment a market;
Identify and explain the basic concepts and principles within the marketing mix (the basic “tools” of marketing); and
Understand the different forms of integrated marketing communications used in reaching different customers at different times.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces the student to key topics in consumer research and marketing. The module is broken down into two elements: Consumer Research and Marketing.

In terms of Consumer Research this module introduces the student to the concept of the consumer and provides insights into contemporary issues that affect the consumer experience. This element is structured into three main themes:
1. The concept of a consumer
2. The consumer in a retail context
3. The socially responsible consumer

In terms of Marketing, this module provides an introduction into the principles and concepts of marketing and serves as a foundation for more advanced subjects in the field of marketing and sales. This element of the course is structured into the following themes:
1. Consumer Buyer Behaviour (including consumer analysis and purchasing behaviour)
2. Product and Brand Management
3. Pricing Decisions and Distribution Channels
4. Marketing Communications and Promotion Methods
learningOutcomes: Define the concept of a consumer and what trends are influencing our conceptualisation of the modern consumer including the use of marketing for social purposes;
Critically appraise how the servicescape influences consumer decision making;
Define and recognise core marketing concepts and basic marketing terminology (the “language” of marketing);
Illustrate how consumers/customers make purchasing decisions and explain the influences on consumer behaviour;
Describe what market segmentation is and understand how to segment a market;
Identify and explain the basic concepts and principles within the marketing mix (the basic “tools” of marketing); and
Understand the different forms of integrated marketing communications used in reaching different customers at different times.

modules.moduleContent: Every individual spends most of his/her life in organisations. Everyone’s work life involves working with, in, or for organisations, and, potentially, creating one’s own organisation. Everyone’s life is also full of managing – from the daily practice of organising yourself, to organising others to undertake individual and group activities to achieve organisational objectives. The aim of this module is for students to develop a better understanding and appreciation of the different human-related elements that influence organisational behaviour and management, leading to a better appreciation of why and how people and groups behave in organisations, and how management practices may influence and direct their behaviour towards achieving organisational goals. The module will consider what shapes the behaviour of people in organisations and how their behaviour can be influenced.
learningOutcomes: Define and describe/explain classic and contemporary organisational behaviour (OB) and management theories and concepts;
Apply classic and contemporary OB and management theories and concepts to different workplace contexts/to practical scenarios;
Critically assess if/how different OB and management theories and concepts may improve individual, group and organisational outcomes;
Identify contemporary topics, issues and challenges in work organisations; and
Compare and contrast different OB theories/concepts/tools and their application in different organisational settings.

modules.moduleContent:
In addition to academic achievements, a large number of employers are placing significant emphasis on 21st Century skills as part of their hiring practices. Some of the most important skills employers deem essential for graduates entering the workforce are teamwork, collaboration, and communications skills. In addition, critical thinking, problem solving, analytical thinking, creativity and innovation, emotional intelligence, and leadership skills have all been ranked highly by employers.

Accordingly, this module is designed to expose students to a variety of 21st century skills and to allow them to develop their skillset which will aid them throughout their university journey, but also in their business career. Using an experiential learning approach, this module blends theory and practical application to equip students with the necessary 21st Century skills and develop the knowledge needed to thrive in an ever-changing and dynamic labour market, whilst also contributing positively to society.
learningOutcomes: Define their learning style(s) and its implications for their development;
Apply newfound knowledge of 21st century skills to enhance personal and team outcomes;
Contribute to teamwork using effective strategies for communication and collaboration;
Have developed their interpersonal skills and the ability to work more effectively as a team member;
Develop lifelong learning skills and strategies in order to maximise learning within and outside of university;
Have enhanced their decision-making skills, and the ability to influence others through their communication and presentation of problems/scenarios or solutions/responses; and
Produce creative, action-oriented, practical solutions to modern business and societal problems.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces the student to entrepreneurship based from the perspective of small to medium-sized businesses (SMEs). SMEs play a central role in delivering solutions to business and social problems. The entrepreneurial work of SMEs is an engine of economic growth, innovation, job creation and social integration at a local, regional, national and international level. SMEs not only solve problems of value to their community, but in turn they face great challenges themselves. SMEs often have limited resources in terms of personnel, capital and market information, relative to larger organisations. This resource challenge can be both a source of innovation and efficiency, as well as a limiting factor. This impacts on how SMEs manage themselves, innovate, and on their ability to compete, grow or succeed in a competitive market. This module provides insight into these challenges as well as the opportunities that SMEs have in overcoming these barriers through their entrepreneurial activities. 

SMEs are often characterised as more nimble and innovative than large organisations, yet large organisations originated, typically, as an SME seeking to compete against large firms, or to create a new market. This module will explore how SMEs can succeed, remaining an SME, or transitioning into a larger successful regional or global player. We will also explore the supports that SMEs can seek, or obtain from external stakeholders such as government, trade bodies and or universities, and how these can impact on the operational and strategic development of the SME.
learningOutcomes: Define and describe the meaning of small to medium-sized businesses (SMEs);
Identify the opportunities and challenges facing SMEs within a competitive market;
Understand the role of entrepreneurship for SMEs;
Illustrate how SMEs respond to the market using entrepreneurial activities; and
Understand the different forms of support mechanisms for SMEs.

modules.moduleContent: It is argued that the sole non-imitable resource that organisations have are their human resources. Using theory and case examples, this module guides students to appreciate how human resource management (HRM) empowers organisations to achieve sustainable competitive advantage through people. The module begins by considering the emergence of HRM and key principles of effective people management. The module then moves on to evaluate different forms of work, careers and the implications for HRM. The module also introduces a range of core HRM practices; employer branding, resourcing, job evaluation, people analytics, total reward and negotiations. The module provides important insights into the nature of contemporary work, careers and people management.
learningOutcomes: Explain the evolution of people management and the key learnings throughout each phase from Industrialisation up to and including debates concerning ‘The Future of Work’;
Identify what makes HRM a novel and distinct approach to people management that is central to delivering organisational competitive advantage;
Evaluate macro-micro connections to understand how national economic policy and the labour market connect with approaches to HRM in the workplace;
Discuss HRM practices including employer branding, resourcing, job evaluation, total reward and negotiations and the theories underpinning these practices; and
Critically assess the relationship between HRM and different forms of work and concerns such as rising income inequality, job insecurity and work intensification.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module is concerned with the manager’s role in complex, multinational environments operating within a dynamic, uncertain global environment. By taking this module, students gain basic and general knowledge on international management. Students will learn to effectively utilize the main analytical techniques and conceptual frameworks provided by contemporary international management theory. Students are also supposed to develop understanding and skills of managing international organizations, preparing themselves for further studies in international management research or business practice in multinational firms.
learningOutcomes: Understand the key challenges of managing across multiple markets, including various political environments and institutions that pose potential risks on international management;
Appreciate the cultural context of global management;
Acknowledge the strategic role that ethics play in global management;
Gain an understanding to strategic alternatives for overseas operations of multinational firms;
Critically assess the managerial implications of alternative organizational architectures of multinational firms; and
Gain an overview on the leadership and communication skills for effective management in different international arenas.

modules.moduleContent: This module addresses the core functions and process of global supply chain management. This module aims to ensure students understand the role of supply chain management function within an organisation and the global impact supply chain management has on all aspects of the business.
learningOutcomes: Explain and critique the strategic importance of global supply chain management in the global competitive business environment;
Analyse and discuss the interaction between global supply chain management and other functional areas of a global business;

Demonstrate and employ important workplace skills (e.g. co-operation, teamwork, meeting deadlines, problem solving, project management, communicating the process and outcomes of analysis of SCM organisational problem) in the context of both organisation problem analysis and in class discussions; and
Explain the role of technology in global supply chain management and its impact on the different elements of the global supply chain.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The aim of this course is to enhance and develop the skills and knowledge of students regarding international marketing strategy and the marketing issues that international businesses face. An understanding of the worldwide market place is critical for marketers, taking into consideration the challenges and opportunities this marketplace presents. Businesses must take account of, and respond to, a range of issues such as culture, regulations, the political landscape, competitors, languages and etiquette when entering and maintaining a presence in new markets. From a marketing perspective, we need to understand how to put in place marketing strategy, objectives, actions and tactics to benefit from the international environment.
learningOutcomes: Develop a clear understanding of the nature and complexity of international marketing strategy;
Identify opportunities and challenges associated with the expansion into a new geographical market; and
Formulate and analyse local and global strategies for product development, distribution, pricing, and promotion.

modules.moduleContent:
In a digitally connected world, it is becoming increasingly important that social media and social networking skills are integrated into our business and marketing programmes. Social media has grown to be one of the primary communication channels on the internet. It has also become an essential component to many organisations in their digital marketing strategies.

Social media has revolutionised how we buy. We now react to social media messages and can often purchase directly from retailers within social media platforms. The social media landscape is continually changing from the (relative) demise of Twitter to the rise of Instagram and the fast-emerging phenomenon that is TikTok. This has led to a lot of questions are the individual level. Examples include the following. How does this new online consumer act and how are they different to traditional offline consumers? How do influencers actually influence? How do online communities work especially those that operate asynchronously?

This module also takes an organisational perspective and looks at how different social media tools can work together to create an integrated marketing communications strategy. As organisations are spending more and more on social media, the question should be asked: what is the return on their investment? In other words, is a Like, Comment or Share worth money?
learningOutcomes: Critically appraise the role of influencers in the consumer decision making process;
Evaluate the social media presence of an organisation and its return on investment;
Understand the psychology of the online consumer in different contexts;
Critically evaluate the various social media platforms and online content; and
Appreciate the value of online communities for organisations of all types.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The module is designed to give participants knowledge of and experience with business analytic techniques. These techniques will be implemented in a business problem-solving environment. This module introduces best practice for conducting analytical projects, and a baseline capability is established in spreadsheet modelling and quantitative methods. The software used is Microsoft Excel and IBM SPSS.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate the fundamental concepts of decision making and problem solving;
Apply appropriate software tools to solve business problems, identify opportunities and recognise changing business environments;
Demonstrate an understanding of analytical skills using different types of data;
Make informed choices about techniques to be applied to solve specific business problems; and
Exercise individual and team problem-solving, critical thinking and analysis skills to find efficient and effective solutions to business challenges.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module will examine the concepts and principles of event management and their application to the organisation of events in various sectors. Students will examine and discuss every aspect of event planning. This will include idea generation/purpose of event, target audience, marketing/branding of the event, advertising /promoting the event, public relations, customer service, sponsorship, health & safety requirements, risk assessment, budget & cost control, event operations, logistics and time lines and post event evaluation. The module will review the objectives and legal responsibilities of the event host and critically evaluate the role of all stakeholders and contractors from the event management perspective. The discussion and critical evaluation of all stages of event organisation and management is done in the context of current theory and best practice.

Students will learn about, and have the opportunity to discuss, current and/or topical issues relating to the planning and organisation of events with a number of practitioner experts who will join the class for a speaker series where appropriate.
learningOutcomes: Define, describe, discuss, and apply the concepts and principles of event management;

Critically appraise the importance of event planning, organisation, and management using the following headings: idea generation/purpose of event, target audience, marketing/branding of the event, advertising /promoting the event, public relations, customer service, sponsorship, budget and cost control, event operations, logistics and time lines and post event evaluation;
Discuss health and safety requirements and risk assessment for an event and the legal obligations of the event host, event stakeholders and contractors;
Critically review the importance of financial planning and control in event management; and
Complete a comprehensive and critical event evaluation for an event.

modules.moduleContent: The concept of Entrepreneurship has been largely associated with the context of business ventures, however, more recently it has been increasingly applied to the context of social problem solving. Social Entrepreneurship is perceived by academics and practitioners alike as making a real difference. Although the concept of social entrepreneurship may be new, the initiatives that employ entrepreneurial capacities to solve social problems are not. This module looks at the phenomenon of social entrepreneurship, its origins, manifestations and practical implications. Social entrepreneurship as a subject has evolved through two academic routes; the study of entrepreneurship and the study of social enterprise / non-profit sector. This is still a developing subject of study but is increasingly relevant in the current economic environment with reduced public-sector expenditure and increasing social demands. The use of entrepreneurship and business models to achieve social value appears to be an increasingly important phenomenon in Ireland and Europe.
learningOutcomes: Discuss and critique different theories of social entrepreneurship and social enterprise;
Describe the processes by which social entrepreneurs ideate and implement social enterprises;
Understand the pros and cons of different models of social entrepreneurship, and the scenarios in which each is more or less appropriate; and
Assess the social impact and financial potential of a social enterprise concept.

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed for final-year undergraduate business students to explore the transformative impact of AI in the business sector. The course covers fundamental AI concepts, machine learning, and data analytics, emphasizing their applications in decision-making, customer insights, and operational efficiency. Students will analyse case studies, engage in hands-on projects, and utilize AI tools to solve real-world business problems. The module also addresses ethical considerations, data privacy, and the future trends of AI in business, preparing students to leverage AI for strategic advantage in their careers.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate understanding of fundamental AI concepts and technologies relevant to business applications.
Analyze and evaluate the impact of AI on various business functions, such as marketing, finance, and operations.
Design and implement AI-driven solutions to improve business efficiency and decision-making processes.
Assess ethical considerations and data privacy issues related to AI in business.
Critically examine emerging trends in AI and predict their potential effects on the future of business.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: QUALITATIVE METHODS IN BUSINESS AND MANAGEMENT RESEARCH
modules.moduleContent:
Having developed a literature review and having selected an appropriate research design, the next step in the development of a PhD project is the selection and execution of appropriate research methods. In conjunction with MN812 - Quantitative Methods in Business and Management Research, this module will enable students to make an informed selection of appropriate research methods for their project.

In this module, students will gain an overview of the underlying principles of qualitative research and of different qualitative methods and their use. Students will gain insight into the relationship between theory and qualitative methods, how rigour is assessed in qualitative research and how qualitative research contributes to knowledge. The module will cover a variety of qualitative data collection and analysis methods, with examples of how these are used in practice.

The module will enable students, guided by their supervisors, to decide if and how qualitative methodology is appropriate to their research, and to select the most appropriate qualitative data collection and analysis methods for their project.

In order to register on MN811, students must be registered as a student on the structured doctoral programme in the School of Business and need to have completed or be currently registered on MN805 - Research Questions, Literature Reviews and Theory Building in Business and Management Research and MN806 - Business Research Design and Methods.
learningOutcomes: Identify appropriate qualitative data collection and analysis methods for their PhD research, in line with their research question and theoretical framework.
Discuss the relevant advantages and disadvantages of different qualitative methods in terms of their PhD research design.
Discuss how selected qualitative methodology is informed by theoretical frameworks and contributes to theory building.
Justify the appropriateness of the selected methods in terms of their research design (or justify why qualitative methods are not appropriate for their research).
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: QUANTITATIVE METHODS IN BUSINESS AND MANAGEMENT RESEARCH
modules.moduleContent:
Having developed a literature review and having selected an appropriate research design, the next step in the development of a PhD project is the selection and execution of appropriate research methods. In conjunction with MN811 - Qualitative Methods in Business and Management Research, this module will enable students to make an informed selection of appropriate research methods for their project.

This module introduces students to fundamentals of quantitative research methods and discusses different methods of collecting and analysing quantitative data. Students will develop an understanding of how to identify appropriate quantitative methodologies for answering their research questions. The module will cover a variety of quantitative methods, with examples of how these are used in research projects. Students will discuss the underlying assumptions and contextual appropriateness of different quantitative research methods as well as the interpretation of results.

In order to register on MN812, students must be registered as a student on the structured doctoral programme in the School of Business and need to have completed or be currently registered on MN805 - Research Questions, Literature Reviews and Theory Building in Business and Management Research and MN806 - Business Research Design and Methods.
learningOutcomes: Identify which methods of data collection are appropriate for their chosen research question and research design;
Identify which quantitative methods are appropriate for their chosen research question and research design;
Assess the quality of a variety of quantitative methodologies in published and unpublished studies; and
Interpret the findings of studies which are conducted using a variety of quantitative methods.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The ether, Michelson-Morley experiment, Lorentz transformations, Minkowski space, length contraction and time dilation, relativistic kinematics, four vectors, relativistic optics and electromagnetism, experimental tests of relativity, GPS, Einstein's 1905 papers.
learningOutcomes: explain the need for Einstein's two postulates
perform calculations using four-vectors in Minkowski space
calculate time dilation, length contraction effects and explain the twin paradox
formulate relativistic kinematics and carry out standard computations
appreciate the experimental validity of special and general relativity

modules.moduleContent: Each Lecture is followed by 2 hours laboratory work. Introduction to Unix. Introduction to Python. Error, accuracy and stability. Finite differences. Integration. Ordinary Differential Equations: Euler method, Runge-Kutta methods. Root finding. Boundary value problems: shooting method.
learningOutcomes: recognise and use basic programming structures (variables, expressions, functions, control statements)
analyse the truncation error of numerical differentiation and integration schemes
perfurm numerical integration of ordinary differential equations
apply these skills to solving physical problems
present project results in a coherent report
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Linear differential equations, ordinary differential equations, complete analysis of equations with constant coefficients, equations with variable coefficients, initial and boundary value problems, Green’s functions, solution by expansion in power series, treatment of regular singular points, Fourier series, Gibbs phenomenon for Fourier series, Fourier transform.
learningOutcomes: recognise the category to which an ordinary differential equation belongs
solve differential equations with constant and variable coefficients
construct Greens' functions
solve differential equations by expansion in power series
construct, and estimate convergence of, Fourier series

modules.moduleContent: Gravitational radiation. Galactic structure and evolution: active galactic nuclei; radio galaxies; quasars.  Cosmology: the Big Bang; microwave background radiation; nucleosynthesis;  cosmological constant; dark matter; inflation. Astro-particle physics: Cosmic rays; monopoles. Inter-galactic medium, reionisation, recombination, The First Stars, First Galaxies, Cosmic Dawn, First Black Holes, Lyman Alpha Forest, Lyman Break Galaxies
learningOutcomes: Interpret the evidence for cosmological expansion
Calculate the dynamics of the Universe at cosmological scales using different equations of state in the Friedmann equations
Give an overview of the Big Bang theory of cosmological evolution and nucleosynthesis
Describe the physics of the early Universe and the transition out of the  Dark Ages
Explain observational diagnostics of the high redshift universe including the Lyman-alpha forest and high redshift quasarsModelling the formation of structure in the Universe
Model the formation of structure in the Universe

modules.moduleContent: Tangent spaces; tensors; parallel transport and the geodesic equation; Riemann curvature; the metric tensor
learningOutcomes: Obtain a coordinate-induced basis for the tangent space and cotangent space at points of a differentiable manifold, construct a coordinate induced basis for arbitrary tensors and obtain the components of tensors in this basis
Determine whether a particular map is a tensor by either checking multi-linearity or by showing that the components transform according to the tensor transformation law

Construct manifestly chart-free definitions of the Lie derivative of a function and a vector, to compute these derivatives in a particular chart and hence compute the Lie derivative of an arbitrary tensor
Compute, explicitly, the covariant derivative of an arbitrary tensor
Define parallel transport, derive the geodesic equation and solve problems invloving parallel transport of tensors

Obtain an expression for the Riemann curvature tensor in an arbitrary basis for a manifold with vanishing torsion, provide a geometric interpretation of what this tensor measures, derive various symmetries and results involving the curvature tensor
Define the metric, the Levi-Civita connection and the metric curvature tensor and compute the components of each of these tensors given a particular line-element

modules.moduleContent:
Introduction to General Relativity and the Equivalence Principle. Curved spaces and metrics. Geodesics in metric spaces. Vectors, tensors and coordinate transformations. The Christoffel connection and Riemann curvature. Einstein's equations and some of its solutions.

This module mirrors and follows the timetable of MP474. It cannot be taken by students who have taken MP474 previously, and cannot be taken together with MP474.
learningOutcomes: Define the Einstein-Hilbert action and derive Einstein's equations from an action principle

Define the stress-energy-momentum tensor, obtain its components in an orthonormal tetrad, and obtain explicit expressions for the stress-energy-momentum tensor describing a perfect fluid matter distribution
Derive the canonical form of the Schwarzschild solution to the vacuum field equations under the sole assumption of spherical symmetry, and hence state Birkhoff's Theorem
Derive expressions for the gravitational redshift, perihelion advance of the planets, and light deflection in the Schwarzschild space-time and hence discuss solar system tests of General Relativity
Obtain the geodesic equations in arbitrary space-times and hence describe various trajectories such as radially in-falling particles or circular geodesics

Define spatial isotropy with respect to a universe filled with a congruence of time-like world-lines, discuss the consequences of global isotropy on the shear, vorticity and expansion of the congruence and hence construct the Friedmann-Robertson-Walker metric

Obtain the Friedmann and Raychaudhuri equations from the Einstein field equations, solve these equations for the scale factor and discuss the cosmogonical and eschatological consequences of the solutions

modules.moduleContent: The student will work independently on a research project in a chosen area of theoretical physics. The results will be written up in a report and presented in an oral presentation at the end of the year.
learningOutcomes: Carry out independent research on a given topic in theoretical physics or a related area
Present their results in a structured report in accordance with standards of academic writing in theoretical physics
Give an oral presentation of their research to an audience of their peers

modules.moduleContent: Teaching practice (1 semester) in Mathematical Physics through the delivery of lectures or tutorials or demonstrating in laboratories.  Training in giving tutorials, in laboratory demonstrating and the teaching of Mathematical Physics.  (The same small group teaching cannot be used for this module and CTL1.)

modules.moduleName: MAJOR ADVANCED TOPICS IN MATHEMATICAL PHYSICS
modules.moduleContent: Contact department for details.  This may be a one-off module run by the department, a module at an external institution, or a journal club where advanced graduate level topics are discussed.

This course introduces students to the fundamentals of programming and numerical methods for mathematical and scientific applications.  Topics include the Unix/Linux operating system, programming languages (Python, Fortran, C or C++ as appropriate); numerical differentiation and integration; numerical methods for ordinary differential equations.
This module mirrors and follows the timetable of MP354 Computational Physics 1.
learningOutcomes: recognise and use basic programming structures (variables, expressions, functions, control statements)
analyse the truncation error of numerical differentiation and integration schemes
perfurm numerical integration of ordinary differential equations
apply these skills to solving physical problems
present project results in a coherent report
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module will develop the student’s competency in Mathematics for progressing into Primary Initial Teacher Education. The student’s entry-level competency in the subject will be assessed at the start of the first semester and this will be used in supporting students to identify and meet their own learning targets. To partake successfully in the programme, the student will need to understand, and be able to use, key mathematical skills and mathematical language and symbols comfortably, in a broad variety of contexts. Students will be supported to think about what they are learning and applying it in the context of their prior knowledge and future possible uses.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate competence in the mathematical concepts, skills and terminology necessary for teaching across all ages in the primary school.
Understand, recall and apply precise definitions of fundamental mathematical concepts related to the primary mathematics curriculum.
Use a collaborative learning and problem solving approach to the learning of mathematics.
Compute, with mastery, basic calculations in the domains of N, Z, Q, and R.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:
Develop advanced skills in Algebra.

Selected topics in Algebra.
learningOutcomes: State some of the classical theorems in algebra.
Prove any of the theorems given in the course.
Deduce properties which follow from these theorems.
Prove analytic results using rigorous arguments.

modules.moduleContent:
Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quire varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 15 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: EPIDEMIOLOGY & THE MODELLING OF HUMAN DISEASE
modules.moduleContent: Topics covered include: History of epidemic modelling, components of the modelling process, study design including case control, cross sectional, cohort and experimental studies. Inferential statistics: hypothesis tests and confidence intervals for means and proportions. Analysing, solving and interpreting various models of diseases such as the Kermack-McKendrick Susceptible, Infectious and Recovered (SIR) models using differential equations, applications to childhood diseases. Consideration will be given to the implications of the models and their results for policy and planning in the health services.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: MSC IN MATHEMATICS - PROJECT AND DISSERTATION
modules.moduleContent:
Overview
The thesis should be written on a research topic in Mathematics.
learningOutcomes: Carry out independent research in Mathematics.

modules.moduleContent: Directed reading and research on a chosen topic.  The student will prepare a report of approximately 45 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic. The course includes laboratory instruction on mathematical report writing.  Students should consult their course coordinator before registering for a research project.

Module Objective:  
Learn essential skills for teaching Mathematics or Statistics in a third level environment. 

Essential skills of Mathematics and Statistics teaching: appropriate content for slides, handouts, and virtual learning environments, good blackboard and slide techniques in class, handling questions.
learningOutcomes: Prepare appropriate material for teaching third level mathematics and statistics.
Present the material in a clear and precise way.
Adjust the presentation in the light of questions and other feedback.
Make use of virtual learning environments and other modern teaching technologies.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: ORAL PRESENTATION SKILLS IN MATHEMATICAL SCIENCE
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
Learn essential skills for presenting Mathematics or Statistics research to an academic audience.

Essential skills for making presentations: appropriate content for slides and prepared material, good blackboard and slide techniques, handling questions.

The same work cannot be submitted for credit in this module and GSE1.
learningOutcomes: Prepare slides and other presentation materials that are suitable for presentation to an academic audience.
Orally present material to an academic audience in an appropriate manner.
Make good use of presentation technologies.
Respond to questions from an academic audience.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
Develop advanced skills in Real or Geometric Analysis.

Selected topics in Real Analysis or Geometric Analysis.
learningOutcomes: State some of the classical theorems in real analysis.
Prove any of the theorems given in the course.
Deduce properties which follow from these theorems.
Prove analytic results using rigorous arguments.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
Learn to read mathematics or statistics textbooks, monographs, and research papers in French.

Self study of:
- basic textbooks for learning French;
- mathematics or statistics source material in French.
learningOutcomes: Read Mathematics or Statistics textbooks in French.
Read Mathematics or Statistics monographs and research papers in French.
Understand standard mathematical French vocabulary.
Use dictionaries and other translation aids to help overcome translational difficulties.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
Develop advanced skills in Complex Analysis.
 
Selected topics in Complex Analysis.
learningOutcomes: State some classical theorems in advanced Complex Analysis.
Prove any of the theorems given in the course.
Deduce properties which follow from these theorems.
Prove analytic results using rigorous arguments.

modules.moduleContent: First Movement of Vivaldi’s Gloria (Choir and Instrumental Ensemble) Supplementary material provided by Course Tutor.
learningOutcomes: On completion of the module, the student should be able to utilise all the essential elements of choral conducting technique.
On completion of the module, the student should be able to deal with a range of choral textures, from homophonic to polyphonic.
On completion of the module, the student should be able to work confidently with ensembles combining choir and professional instrumentalists.
On completion of the module, the student should be able to engage with a range of choral styles from different musical periods.

modules.moduleContent: Mass texts in English, Irish and Latin will be provided and discussed with contemporary musical examples of each studied.
learningOutcomes: On completion of the module, the student should be able to meet the challenge of setting all seven congregational parts of the mass.
On completion of the module, the student should be able to engage with the standard liturgical sung texts in English, Irish and Latin.
On completion of the module, the student should be able to move with confidence within a particular harmonic context.
On completion of the module, the student should be able to exercise control over broader compositional considerations, such as structural clarity and thematic unity.

modules.moduleContent:
This module consists of two parts:

A.  Development of aural skills, including sight singing, rhythmic skills and music dictation. (66.6% of module total)

B.  The study and application of basic orchestration techniques, doubling, blending, and genre arrangement strategies, culminating in a structured composition assignment. Learners will also be introduced to the basic skills required to construct computer-aided realisations of their music. (33.4% of module total)
learningOutcomes: recognise, understand and apply basic doubling and blending techniques in orchestration/arrangement.
render a short piano sketch for ensemble.
produce basic mock-ups of musical scores.
demonstrate basic sight-singing, rhythmic and music dictation skills.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to fundamental skills relating to audio. Acoustics and psychoacoustics are considered alongside critical listening skills; a component of the module, in which audio software is explored, allows for practical applications of the theoretical components. Key concerns of the module include:

- study of the physiology of hearing, auditive perception, acoustics of spaces and musical instruments
- an improved ability to express ideas relating to audio
- critical listening skills
- practical use of audio software
learningOutcomes: Describe the physical nature of sound and the various related parameters and phenomena
Listen critically to audio, and describe attributes of audio in recorded formats
Apply understandings from acoustics and listening skills to use of music software
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module concentrates on elements such as dissonance; seventh chords; processes of modulation; chromatic harmony; transposing instruments; long and short score reading and analysis.
learningOutcomes:
Recognize and describe in detail dissonances and non-harmony notes, including:
Passing notes,Auxiliary notes, Appoggiaturas, Échappées, Suspensions

Recognize and describe in detail a range of chords common to eighteenth- and nineteenth-century Western music, including the following:
Secondary seventh chords,Neapolitan sixth chords, Augmented sixth chords,Diminished seventh chords
Write progressions of two or three chords in SATB format, demonstrating an understanding of good voice-leading practice

Provide analysis of passages of music using figured roman chord indications and figured bass indications
Realize a chord from a given figured bass indication
Identify keys, tonicization, and modulation to remote keys and the various processes through which these are achieved.
Engage fluently with music presented in orchestral score.
ngage with additional transposing instruments and write their parts at concert pitch

modules.moduleContent: The module aims to expose students to the wide variety of styles and ideas in Contemporary Western Art Music so that they can make informed judgements about their own creative expression through composition. Students will be introduced to composition through the hands-on application of some historical and contemporary techniques in short exercises, through discussions on the fundamentals of compositional material and through regular exposure to recordings of modern languages of music.
learningOutcomes: Produce original compositions at a level commensurate with academic year and within a given timeframe
Identify the key elements of a successful composition
Discuss in some detail issues concerning contemporary composition
Critique, in a meaningful way, compositions by others as well as by themselves
Reflect analytically on core works of this past century

modules.moduleContent: This module provides a general introduction to the various forms of Irish traditional music, both instrumental and vocal, through a historical survey of the tradition.
learningOutcomes: Describe and analyse the main instrumental and vocal forms in Irish traditional music.
Outline in broad terms the history of Irish traditional music.
Describe, analyse and differentiate the different performance contexts in traditional music.
Demonstrate an ability to listen analytically to Irish traditional music.

modules.moduleContent: Centred on Western art music (1500s–1800s), this module explores music and musicians in society, contextualizing the relationship between music, culture and commerce. Encompassing a range of cultural milieux it employs a variety of methodologies designed to open up understandings of the function and status of music, musicians and the music business. Through a series of case studies it explores the agency and influence of creators, recreators, institutions, critics and audiences. The module opens up awareness of European musical centres, genres, institutional structures, musical roles and music industry activities. The progress of works—from commission, to creation, to performance, and dissemination—is grounded in contexts of reception and commercial opportunity. Performance traditions (public and private; sacred and secular; professional and amateur) are discussed. The case studies are chosen to deepen knowledge of the changing roles, practices and reception of music and musicians across this time period and will vary according to staff expertise and current developments in research.
learningOutcomes: understand and navigate the scholarly discourse in relation to the cultural, social and commercial contexts surrounding music and musicians in this period.
discuss music and musicians in society, contextualizing the relationship between music, culture and commer
explore and assess the performance traditions surrounding specific genres and locations within this time period
critically examine the role of music and musicians in diverse contexts
evaluate and apply historical methods and written arguments
demonstrate skills in interpreting, balancing and interrogating sources
evaluate the relationships between secular musical institutions, the establishment/state, central European traditions, musicians (performers, composers) and their audiences (critical reception)

modules.moduleContent:
This module is concerned with the exploration of various sound-design structures and how such structures can contribute to the overall message conveyed. 
A variety of media forms, both experimental and established, are accessed. 
The module also considers the use of software programmes, the management of project materials, sound recording, and concepts for creating coherent structures.

Learning Outcomes:

On completion of the module, students will:
1. Apply existing knowledge of audio production software to create original sound design
2. Manipulate digital audio through software applications
3. Present and interpret audio material
4. Be able to evaluate work-in-progress.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The module aims to expose students to the wide variety of styles and ideas in Contemporary Western Art Music. It focuses on the creation of a small, directed portfolio of new works for varying forces, building on the techniques learnt in Creativity & Technique I & II (MU155/156) and Introduction to Composition (MU218). All compositions are accompanied by a preface that explains any non-standard performance practices or notations, and a programme note which illuminates the expressive and technical features of the work.

The module is divided into four blocks, each dealing with an area of compositional exploration embedded in learning from core works from the early modern and contemporary repertoire. Each of the blocks culminates with an assignment that specifically engages with the technical issues dealt with in lectures. There is also a ‘pitstop’ in the middle of each block where assignment drafts are presented in class and feedback offered before the final assignment is submitted, the peer discussion of work being vital to a creative and supportive learning environment for composers.

Visiting International Students in Maynooth for one semester or one academic year should contact the Department of Music for information before registering.
learningOutcomes: Produce original compositions at a level commensurate with academic year (Produce small scale works within a given timeframe; Produce a piece for larger ensemble)
Discuss contemporary compositional techniques
Discuss contemporary compositional aesthetics
Critique, in a meaningful way, compositions by others as well as by themselves
Reflect analytically on core works of this past century
Work with performers in creating new work

modules.moduleContent:
OBJECTIVES
* To develop research skills and critical faculties
* To develop writing and organisational abilities
* To develop the capacity for independent learning
* To allow the student to explore a musicological topic in a large project
* To guide the student towards a professional approach to music research
* To facilitate the student's engagement with a network of music researchers
learningOutcomes: report on current developments in musicology, as addressed in regular research seminars.
independently apply modern research methodology to a suitable musicological subject.
critique a vast range of scholarly views, approaches and models.
produce a dissertation of c.6,000 words on an agreed research topic.

modules.moduleContent:
Tuition and coaching leading to a 15/20 minute final public recital in late May/early June. The programme should include substantial items but also stylistic contrast and should demonstrate the student’s interpretative and technical skills. The choice of programme is approved by the module convenor. Students will take advanced individual tuition on their approved first study instrument or voice. Students are required to attend and participate in regular performance seminars. Students must register for one of the Approved Ensembles of the Music Department (attendance will be monitored and a system of penalties will be in place).

Visiting International Students in Maynooth for one semester or one academic year should contact the Department of Music for information before registering.
learningOutcomes: perform a 15-20 minute recital at post-grade 8 level.
critically listen to other performers' recordings.
produce a researched and contextualised essay on their choice of programme.
manage transferable presentation skills.
discuss Irish women composers from the nineteenth century to the present
describe how Irish women shaped musical infrastructures such as educational institutions and festivals
comment on the global impact of Irish women performers
evaluate the presence (or absence) of women in historical narratives of Irish music
analyse how contemporary Irish women musicians engage with feminist politics and movements such as #MeToo and the ‘Repeal’ campaign

modules.moduleContent: The module will examine the theory informing music therapy processes, and consider it under the headings of improvisation, analysis, psychology, therapies and knowledge of clients.
learningOutcomes: evaluate music therapy literature (for instance by preparing a critique of a music therapy article).
discuss the role of music in the music therapy process (for instance by identifying the use of musical elements in music therapy sessions).
show how improvisation informs music therapy practice (for instance by illustrating how their experience in improvisation tutorials demonstrates insight into what occurs in the music therapy space).
describe how music therapy is used as an intervention for people with different pathologies (for instance by outlining music therapy for people with autism, depression etc.).

modules.moduleContent: Working in small groups in a keyboard lab, students will develop competency at the keyboard and learn a range of keyboard skills compliant with Teaching Council requirements for Music at post-primary level. The module is not available to students who have taken group piano during year 1 and/or 2. The module is not available to students who have taken first-study piano or organ during year 1 and/or 2.
learningOutcomes: Sight reading
Demonstrate the scales and arpeggios of the most commonly used keys.
Harmonise a melody and be able to play it with chords.
Transpose a melody with chords up or down a tone.
Demonstrate accompanying skills in a range of styles.
