Discuss contemporary compositional techniques
Discuss contemporary compositional aesthetics
Critique, in a meaningful way, compositions by others as well as by themselves
Reflect analytically on core works of this past century
Work with performers in creating new work

modules.moduleContent:
Tuition and coaching leading to a 25-30 minute final public recital in late May/early June. The programme should include substantial items but also stylistic contrast and should demonstrate the student’s interpretative and technical skills. The choice of programme is approved by the module convenor. Students will take advanced individual tuition on their approved first study instrument or voice. Students are required to attend and participate in regular performance seminars. Students must register for one of the Approved Ensembles of the Music Department (attendance will be monitored and a system of penalties will be in place).

Visiting International Students in Maynooth for one semester or one academic year should contact the Department of Music for information before registering.
learningOutcomes: perform a 25-30 minute recital at post-grade 8 level.
critically listen to other performers' recordings.
produce a researched and contextualised essay on their choice of programme.
manage transferable presentation skills.

modules.moduleContent: This module is concerned with advanced concepts in the theory and practice of sound recording and mixing. The module presents approaches and techniques at the forefront of current music production, allowing scope for individual expression, while emphasizing current best practice. Topics covered include pre-mix, timing, tuning, balance, E.Q., Compression, Reverbs, Delays, Masking, Parallel Compression, Master Buss Processing, Mastering.
learningOutcomes: Realise a structured piece of work from source material.
Demonstrate familiarity with current best practise in mixing standards through working methods
Negotiate the cross-platform workflow of an audio project

modules.moduleContent: In this module, student performers and student composers are required to collaborate in the creation and subsequent performance of a newly composed work for a specific instrument/voice (or combination of instruments as designated by the module coordinator).
learningOutcomes: Explain to a composer the main feature of his/her instrument/voice;
Collaborate with a composer and provide performance input about the new composition at different stages of its composition;
Evaluate the aesthetic and the performance challenges of a newly composed work;
Perform a newly composed piece.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides the student with the tools to analyse twentieth- and twenty-first century scores in terms of structure and detail of content, using listening and score study to assess the scores. It also includes lectures of compositional genres relevant to on-going student compositions.

The supervised research and submission of a thesis (c15,000 words), providing the opportunity for detailed independent research and study of a topic to an
advanced level. Regular individual tutorials are held with an assigned supervisor until the middle of June. Students will give one presentation of their research lasting 10 minutes in duration as part of the Department of Music Research Colloquia Series (in Semester 2). A draft of the thesis should be submitted to the supervisor by mid-June (so that the student receives constructive feedback by late June). Supervision (in person, by email, etc.) ends in late June. The completed thesis must be submitted online(no hard copy) by 31 August. 

Module objectives:
- To develop a research project at master’s level
- To cultivate the student’s ability to problematize a research topic and to think critically
- To refine the student’s writing and organizational abilities
- To reinforce the student’s capacity to work independently
- To allow the student to present their research ideas in a supportive research
community
- To facilitate the student’s wider engagement with music research both nationally and
internationally

Learning outcomes: On successful completion of the module, students should be able to
(i) identify a suitable topic for research
(ii) present one paper on the subject of their research
(iii) compile an up-to-date bibliography and present examples using Sibelius
(iv) write a successful master’s dissertation with a suitable critical/analytical content for
masters’ level.
learningOutcomes: identify a suitable topic for research.
present a paper on the subject of their research.
compile an up-to-date bibliography and present musical examples using Sibelius.
write a successful masters dissertation with suitable critical/analytical content for masters' level.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Recording:

Performance: A public recital lasting 45-50 minutes. The programme to be developed and agreed upon in consultation with the supervisor. The programme should be coherently structured and demonstrate technical, interpretative and communicative skills that are at a professional level. Spoken introductions should be included in the programme time.

modules.moduleName: WRITING ABOUT MUSIC – CRITICISM, JOURNALISM, AND PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT
learningOutcomes: understand and distinguish different types of writing about music in a range of professional scenarios
understand and apply diverse writing styles for a range of professionally relevant audiences
critically engage with a range of musical artforms
become competent in creating and disseminating different types of writing work for employment

modules.moduleContent:
**Commencing September 2025**

This module aims to foster deep engagement with key areas of musicological research, specifically related to the research expertise of staff in the Department. To develop an understanding of different scholarly perspectives on any given issue, the module entails individual close reading of a substantial number of texts and group discussion of the same.

It will introduce the central debates in the discipline relevant to each topic (topic 1 in weeks 1–5; topic 2 in weeks 6–10) and will explore other salient points. How do intellectual positions on a
given issue shift or evolve over the years? How are discussions shaped by the voices of those who participate and how does gatekeeping exclude other voices? How do contemporary modes of
communication such as social media platforms mediate and steer musicological debate?

The module will explore two topics; the topics in question will vary from year to year. Sample topics will draw from subfields of musicology such as: music and gender, ethnomusicology; musicology and popular music; historical musicology; music and media technology.
learningOutcomes: Engage critically with historical and current discourse in two specific subfields of musicology/ ethnomusicology.
Relate areas of musicological debate to one another and assess their development over time in relation to academic enquiry more broadly.
Demonstrate an understanding of key issues and an ability to apply this understanding to new case studies.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: SCORING AND COMPOSITION FOR SCREEN MEDIA
modules.moduleContent:
**Commencing September 2025**

This module introduces students to the technical, aesthetic and narrative strategies employed in the creation of music and sound design for screen media. Guided by reflection on their experience and skillset as composers, and informed by critical examination of case studies from the history of screen media, students will choose an appropriate pathway within the module. Students will be introduced to virtual instruments, sound design and related software and learn how to use these tools and techniques in the context of composition for screen media.
learningOutcomes: critically evaluate the use of music and sound in screen media;
understand and apply basic orchestration strategies in the creation of music and sound for screen media;
compose in response to an (audio)visual stimulus;
navigate and manipulate DAW software and virtual instruments with skill for the purposes of composing for screen media.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
**Commencing September 2025**

This module offers an opportunity for placement with an external organisation that houses a nationally significant music collection. As part of the organisation’s project to classify, curate and facilitate awareness of its holdings, students will be invited to undertake research into chosen materials within the collection and to disseminate their findings digitally via an online article to be hosted on the partner organisation’s website.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate familiarity with the mission of an external partner organisation and the scope of its research collection;
critically evaluate key research materials, including primary sources;
apply appropriate musicological tools and methodologies to a defined research project;
collate and digitise research findings in the form of a web-based article.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Students present a 20-minute paper (followed by c.10 minutes of Q&A) at the Department's annual Postgraduate Research Symposium at the end of Semester 2. The focus of the paper should be the work completed in the academic year in which the symposium takes place. The precise format of the paper is to be agreed with the supervisor in accordance with the mode of research being undertaken. In preparation, students are also strongly encouraged to attend the regular research seminar series taking place throughout the academic year, featuring local and international guest speakers across a broad range of research areas.
learningOutcomes: Plan and prepare a presentation based on the advanced stages of the research project
Communicate the research effectively and succinctly with appropriate audio-visual aids
Deliver the presentation within the required time limits
Respond effectively to questions from the seminar audience

modules.moduleContent: The objective of the module is to facilitate the student to undertake a fixed, agreed programme of reading, where a student can critically engage with and reflect on a body of literature needed for their PhD thesis. Under the guidance of their supervisor (or assigned mentor), the student will devise a reading list, attend periodic discussion meetings, and complete a literature review / critical essay which will enhance their research and/or their writing skills.
learningOutcomes: Discover, synthesise and apply knowledge and/or theories from a body of literature.
Develop a knowledge and understanding of current thinking and research in a relevant field.
Write a literature review / critical review essay.
Apply concepts and ideas from this material independently to their own research topic.

modules.moduleContent: This module contains two primary components: 1) an introduction to the Python programming language and its application in spatial data analysis, and 2) an overview of the general principles of designing and executing a research project. Students will combine these components by completing a significant data analytics research project using Python. This will require the identification of analytical questions to be addressed; identification of appropriate sources of data; consideration of appropriate analytical techniques to apply; and reporting of the results of the analysis. Students will work in teams and assessment will be based primarily on a final report and team presentation. On successful completion of the module, students should be able to: 1)  employ basic and advanced spatial data science methods in Python and 2) understand foundational scientific research concepts such as developing a research question, conducting a literature review, and completing a final research report.
learningOutcomes: have completed a significant data analytics project.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module aims to introduce students to a range of applications that shape the current state-of the art in professional GI Science (GISc) and to show how various elements of GISc can be brought together to solve real-world problems. The module will be taught as a mix of lectures, practicals, workshops and employer visits. This module is intended to extend the theoretical aspects of GISc into practice by taking a problem-oriented approach, incorporating some group-work. The module will include introductions to the design and use of Web-GIS, storyboards and big data. In addition contemporary applications in a range of subject areas will emphasise how geospatial data knowledge shapes society today. A final strand will incorporate a visitor programme from industry professionals to prepare students for work placements and contemporary directions in the GI Science industry. There will also be a significant independent project component to encourage students to engage with contemporary applications in Web-GIS data and technologies to develop their own ideas and knowledge.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Léachtaí ar chruinnscríobh na teanga agus ar léamh na litríochta. Ranganna praiticiúla i labhairt agus i scríobh na Gaeilge, agus i léamh na litríochta.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a c(h)umas úsáid a bhaint as an aidiacht sa tuiseal ainmneach uatha agus sa tuiseal ainmneach iolra.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint éigin aige/aici ar cheartúsáid na haidiachta sa ghinideach uatha agus sa ghinideach iolra.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a c(h)umas bunuimhreacha, uimhreacha pearsanta agus orduimhreacha a úsáid i gceart.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a c(h)umas tabhairt faoin léitheoireacht neamhspleách.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a c(h)umas anailís a dhéanamh ar théacsanna próis.

modules.moduleContent: Staidéar a dhéanamh ar stair shóisialta na Gaeilge agus ar ghnéithe éagsúla de shochtheangeolaíocht na Gaeilge: an dátheangachas; an débhéascna; réim; pleanáil teanga, canúintí na Gaeilge; An Caighdeán Oifigiúil; athruithe teanga; An Ghaeilge sa chóras oideachais.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú, bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas léargas a thabhairt ar stair shóisialta na Gaeilge ó aimsir na Sean-Ghaeilge anuas go dtí an lá atá inniu ann.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú, bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas buntéarmaí na sochtheangeolaíochta a aithint agus a thuiscint.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú, bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas ionad reatha na Gaeilge i sochaí an lae inniu a thuiscint agus a phlé i dtéarmaí na sochtheangeolaíochta.

modules.moduleContent: Tabharfar deis do mhic léinn, a bhfuil caighdeán maith Gaeilge acu, móraiste a scríobh ar ábhar a bhfuil spéis acu ann. Faoi stiúir duine de léachtóirí buana na Roinne a dhéanfar an modúl seo, agus iarrfar ar mhic léinn aiste 4500-5000 a sholáthar faoi dheireadh an tseimeastair. I gcás saothar cruthaitheach, aontófar líon focal leis an stiúrthóir ach, mar shampla, beifear ag súil le 8 ndán agus nótaí cúlra le haghaidh saothar filíochta agus idir 2,500 agus 3,000 le haghaidh gearrscéil.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas taighde ginearálta a dhéanamh ar ábhar.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas anailís chuimsitheach a dhéanamh ar an ábhar atá roghnaithe acu.

I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas torthaí a chuid taighde a chur le chéile agus aiste chuimsitheach, 4500-5000 focal, a scríobh. I gcás saothar cruthaitheach, aontófar líon focal leis an stiúrthóir ach, mar shampla, beifear ag súil le 8 ndán agus nótaí cúlra le haghaidh saothar filíochta agus idir 2,500 agus 3,000 le haghaidh gearrscéil.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas córas tagartha a úsáid i gceart agus leabharliosta cuimsitheach a chur le chéile.
modules.notes: Ag brath ar an ábhar taighde a roghnaíonn an mac léinn

modules.moduleContent: Pléifidh an cúrsa seo stair, forbairt agus mórthéamaí na nuafhilíochta ó lár na haoise seo caite anuas go dtínár linn féin. Déanfar mioniniúchadh ar mhórdhánta le filí ar nós Nuala Ní Dhomhnaill, Bhiddy Jenkinson, Choilm Breathnaigh agus Chathail Uí Shearcaigh, agus pléifear réimse leathan filí comhaimseartha. Beifear ag súil leis go mbeidh mic léinn sásta a n-anailís féin ar dhán / dánta a chur chun cinn agus a chosaint.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh eolas acu ar stair, forbairt agus mórthéamaí na nuafhilíochta ó lár na haoise seo caite anuas go dtínár linn féin.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann anailís chriticiúil a dhéanamh ar dhánta éagsúla.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with the ability to solve real world problems using embedded electronic systems through an engineering design led process to build and develop 'works like' and 'looks like' prototypes through iteration. Through demonstration and project based learning the students will develop their engineering design processes to select electronic components and sensors to perform actions based on captured data. The students will then further develop their prototypes to improve and develop their project based on user feedback.

Upon completion of their final prototype, the students will demonstrate their project, discussing and defending their design decisions and explaining in detail the inner electronic workings of their project.
learningOutcomes: Use engineering design process to design embedded electronic systems.
Identify electronic components and sensors.
Select appropriate electronic components and sensors.
Simulate and design electronic circuits.
Develop C/C++ software for use in embedded electronic systems.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module teaches students advanced communication tools used in design and manufacture. Module PD152, covering Computer Aided Design fundamentals using SolidWorks, is a pre-requisite to this module. Topics covered include; advanced solid feature generation, design validation through CosmosXpress, advanced assembling, advanced drawing generation, surface modelling, photorealistic rendering.
learningOutcomes: Create and edit complex parts & product assemblies in SolidWorks
Communicate complex multi bodied products to third parties
Validate product performance through simulation
Prepare photorealistic images for marketing, posters, customer feedback, etc.
Apply fundamentals in image manipulation and product board creation through Photoshop

modules.moduleContent: This module teaches students ergonomics, aesthetics and usability and the importance of these in product design. Students learn how to apply these principles to practical projects and to appreciate the importance of user centred design in the design process. Topics covered include; fields of ergonomics-physical and cognitive, elements of aesthetics- colour and composition, anthropometric tables and percentiles, user experience and user centred design, tools and approaches for usability testing, improving ergonomics in everyday products.
learningOutcomes: Apply ergonomic & anthropometric principles in design
Appreciate emotional design in products for consumers
Appreciate the sensory and physical attributes to products
Create appropriate aesthetic, anthropometrically correct and usable products
Apply user centered design (UCD) methods to the design process

modules.moduleContent: The world economy is increasingly characterised as a service economy with the service sector becoming a vital contributing factor to world economic growth. For a majority of the world’s economies, the services sector is by far the biggest employer. However, companies struggle to develop and communicate service solutions that meet customer needs, create a competitive advantage and can be provided in a sustainable way. The service design and innovation module teaches students design thinking methods to develop useful, usable and desirable services from the customer’s point of view; and effective, efficient and competitive services from the service provider’s point of view. This is achieved through a combination of design and business orientated theory and hands-on learning through applied service design research, tools and methodologies. Continuous assessment is used to reflect industry practice and provide students with the confidence and experience to apply service innovation methods when out in industry (private and public sector).
learningOutcomes: Explain why service innovation has become increasingly important role of services in the public & private sector.
Analyse the critical role of the user & stakeholder in the design, development & implementation of services.
Apply the principles, methodologies & models of service design.
Develop new service solutions &/or adapt an existing service to meet user needs.

modules.moduleContent: The world economy is increasingly characterised as a service economy with the service sector becoming a vital contributing factor to world economic growth.  For a majority of the world’s economies, the services sector is by far the biggest employer.  However, companies struggle to develop and communicate service solutions that meet customer needs, create a competitive advantage and can be provided in a sustainable way. The service design and innovation module teaches students design thinking methods to develop useful, usable and desirable services from the customer’s point of view; and effective, efficient and competitive services from the service provider’s point of view.  This is achieved through a combination of design and business orientated theory and hands-on learning through applied service design research, tools and methodologies.  Continuous assessment is used to reflect industry practice and provide students with the confidence and experience to apply service innovation methods when out in industry.
learningOutcomes: Discuss service innovation principles & strategies; & why service innovation has become increasingly important in the public & private sector.
Apply service innovation methods to critically analyse service user experiences & define key user need(s).
Design & develop a new innovative service solutions to meet user needs.
Evaluate & validate the effectiveness of service solutions.
Identify & effectively communicate with key stakeholders for the streamlined implementation of the service solution.

modules.moduleName: MORAL PHILOSOPHY IN A GLOBALISED SOCIETY
modules.moduleContent: This module introduces a distinction between ethics and morals, which it explores in order to shed light on the moral problems encountered with globalization, in particular immigration, which brings the globalized world home to us. It defines ethics as ‘what we think it is appropriate to do’, and sets out to investigate the various elements of this definition by examining action-theory (what it is to do something), value-theory (what it is to consider something appropriate) and political- or community theory (who ‘we’ are). Authors studied include Thomas Aquinas, Hannah Arendt, Jürgen Habermas, and Edith Stein. The aim is to discuss how moral philosophy, defined as concerned with what is right or good, can help us address the problems associated with creating a common world for contemporary, pluralist societies.
learningOutcomes: explain and evaluate the definitions of ethics and moral philosophy proposed in the module;
read philosophical texts to shed light of the problems posed;
discuss the ethical and moral problems arising from globalization, in particular the problems associated with immigration;
grasp the meaning of ‘value’, ‘motivation’, ‘valuation’, ‘rationality’ and ‘justification’ in the light of the texts studied in the module;
identify and analyse the role of values in the formation and understanding of personalities and communities;
discuss whether there is such a thing as moral rationality and in what it would consist of;
define the relationship between nation and state and assess the distinction’s importance for the ethical problems studied;

modules.moduleName: UNIQUE ANIMALS? ANTHROPOLOGICAL CONCEPTS IN PHILOSOPHY
modules.moduleContent: This module will examine some major philosophical responses to the question ‘What does it mean to be human?’ with special focus on the 20th century and some important developments in the 21st first century. We will approach the topic under a number of different headings, using thinkers and paradigms as ‘case studies’: e.g.; 1) humans in the context of life and other animals (Max Scheler and Ernst Cassirer), 2) the impact of society, gender perceptions and racism on human self-understanding (Simone de Beauvoir, bell hooks, and Frantz Fanon), 3) humans and technology (with focus on the transhumanist movement and some implications of AI). Our focus will be on the questions as to whether there is anything unique about the human way of existing, our relation to other life forms and animals, the role of our ‘biological make-up’, and if it still makes sense to have a universalising approach in the face of diversity and on-going discrimination, and the potential for and challenges to our notions of human existence as a result of technological development.
learningOutcomes: Describe and evaluate the different concepts of what it means to be human discussed in the module;
Evaluate the different responses to the question as to whether there is a unique human way of existence, especially in the light of 21st century techno-scientific insights.
Critically discuss the transhumanist movement and the idea of post-biological evolution.
Critically discuss the impact of social expectations and different forms of discrimination on human self-understanding.
Assess the role of our ‘biological make-up’ for our self-identity, i.e., critically discuss the notion of biological essentialism.
Describe and explain how your own understanding of what it means for you to be human has been confirmed, challenged, or changed by the positions discussed in class.
Discuss the relevance of these ideas and their limits for the 21st century.
modules.notes:
This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module examines a range of historically significant and contemporary views on the nature, origins, extent and justificatory status of human knowledge. It begins with Plato’s discussion on knowledge and his famous formulation of the problem of knowledge in the Theaetetus. It then addresses the quest for certainty and scepticism and its motivation in the modern period on Descartes’s rationalist formulation of the problem of knowledge and the subsequent empiricists’ responses of Locke and Hume.  The module concludes with some contemporary discussion and analysis of knowledge as justified true belief and ‘the Gettier Problem’, Foundationalism and Coherentism.
learningOutcomes: Identify the meaning of basic terms used in epistemology, such as: knowledge, opinion, belief, doubt, conjecture, probability, certitude, certainty, apodictic certainty.
Describe the central notions of philosophical interest used in epistemology.

Distinguish the approach taken in the philosophical theory of knowledge (epistemology) from other approaches to knowledge taken in other disciplines (e.g. in sociology, psychology, anthropology, history).

Distinguish the context of discovery of an idea (e.g. its psychological, cultural or historical origins) from the context of justification, and discuss the inadequacy of the former to found the truth or validity of that idea (the ‘genetic fallacy’).
Explain contemporary foundationalist and coherentist accounts of knowledge and evaluate their limits and compatibility as justifications of knowledge.
Provide a philosophical account of the problem of knowledge and debate the relevance and importance of truth in discourse about knowledge.

modules.moduleContent: Digital Ethics is an emerging field of philosophy which is essentially concerned with the ever-increasing ‘digitalization’ of human life and the recognition that digital technologies not only present new and novel ethical problems but also reframe, restructure, and reformulate traditional ‘pre-digital’ ethical concerns. As such, a broad approach is required that is – by definition – interdisciplinary in nature. This module presupposes two fundamental premises: 1) despite the fact that ethical reflection struggles to keep pace with digitalization, it is critical – from a social, political, and philosophical perspective – that we undertake the task of digital ethics itself; 2) not only does digital technology require ethical attention, it also throws up philosophically interesting and significant issues in and of itself. Thus, the module aims to develop critical, creative, and independent thinking through engagement with both ‘foreground’ and ‘background’ concerns and by connecting the dots between a wide of disciplines and areas of research and ‘real-world’ contexts that are of interest to digital ethics.
learningOutcomes: Identify and analyse ethical issues related to digital technologies such as automated decision making, privacy, misinformation, and the spread of extremist political ideologies.
Give a brief account of the history of digital ethics as a field of research.
Evaluate the impact of digital technologies on society, including issues of social and economic inequality, surveillance, political extremism, gender bias, and ethnicity bias.
Develop strategies for promoting ethical and responsible use of digital technologies.
Apply ethical principles to real-world scenarios related to digital technology.
Identify and assess some of the broader philosophical issues that underpin digital technologies such as the Big Data paradigm, Dataism and Information Essentialism, and Digital Feudalism
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
In which world do you want to live? This question is as thought-provoking as it is urgent and of interest not only in general, but for philosophers, in particular. In our seminar, we are going to reconstruct issues of sustainability from a philosophical point of view. Some key concerns of today’s world, such as equality, social cohesion, and solidarity, can be rendered comprehensively as ecological concerns. The quest for global justice as economic equality from North to South is voiced and understood today as the concern of ‘climate justice’. Global warming, food security, but also ending new wars, also fall into this category. They all threaten the continued existence ‘our oikos’. Given these far-ranging ‘ecological concerns’ against which we need to sustain an oikos (a place of dwelling for ourselves and our children) we can agree on the need of more work in philosophy (and we could call this branch ‘philosophy of sustainability’).
We will begin by reading Callenbach’s 1975 classic of the ecological movement ‘Ecotopia’ and take first cues from Eisenstein’s 2018 ‘Climate – A new story’. We will then turn to sources from intercultural philosophy (i.e. ‘interbeing’ of zen-Buddhism in Ueda’s analysis of ‘nihilism’), and to critical classics from the Western philosophical canon (i.e. Kant, Husserl, Arendt and Nussbaum) which we will read in excerpts to deepen our understanding of questions of a permanence of meaning. These questions can be understood as questions of sustainability. We will retrace them systematically and genealogically to be able to evaluate (historical and contemporary) answers to the quest of sustainability, and to formulate our own answers. There are three areas which we will investigate: climate change and acting ‘into nature’; just living: sustainable structures of equality in society; in light of / enlightened self: agency and meaning.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate philosophy as a discipline, understand its tasks and their own engagement with it.
Work exegetically with canonical texts and have fun while doing it.
Approach complex issues systematically.
Form and justify their own opinion on current societal, political questions and events.
Present and discuss ideas constructively

modules.moduleName: GREEK METAPHYSICS FROM THALES TO ARISTOTLE
modules.moduleContent: The aim of this module is to discuss the metaphysics of the first philosophers of Western civilization, roughly between the 6th century BCE and the 4th century BCE. We shall proceed chronologically, from the Presocratic thinkers and their discussions of nature, being and guiding principles, to the Sophists and their relativistic perspective, and then to Plato and Aristotle, focusing on methodological issues as well as on some fundamental theories pertaining to metaphysics. The module pays particular attention to the significance of the concept of scientific knowledge in both Plato and Aristotle, noting the main differences between their notions of scientific knowledge, problems arising out of the Platonic dialogues and how Aristotle deals with them, and how the Aristotelian division of sciences is related to his notion of scientific knowledge and overall to his metaphysics.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish some basic historical periods in Greek philosophy (Presocratic, Classical, Hellenistic, Roman, Neoplatonic), while dealing with the questions why we need these divisions and what might be problematic in such divisions.
Identify some essential methodological problems we have today studying ancient Greek philosophy and relate those problems to some key philosophical issues.

Address pertinent questions in ancient Greek philosophy such as, for instance, what should we know in order to understand ancient Greek philosophy? What are the main difficulties modern readers of ancient Greek philosophers should be aware of? Have we ‘lost’ the Presocratics? Do we know how to deal with a Platonic dialogue?
Appreciate the metaphysical speculations on nature by the Presocratic thinkers, paying particular attention to the development and changes between different philosophers.
Evaluate the advantages and disadvantages of a materialistic approach to the study of nature.
Discuss the progress and change in ancient Greek philosophy before and after Socrates, while analysing the philosophical importance of the Sophists and the main issues in Plato’s early dialogues.

modules.moduleContent:
A performance-oriented study of plainchant based on the neumatic notations of  the St Gall and Laon manuscript traditions. Solo chanting will form a core element of the module, in addition to group work. Students will also be required to complete short written assignments on aspects of chant semiology and/or modality.

Delivered online for the first 4-5 weeks, delivered live thereafter. 

Note:
This module will not be available for online registration in September. Interested students should contact John.OKeeffe@mu.ie to arrange a time for audition. If successful, students will be able to amend their initial module selection to include MU348.

Visiting International Students in Maynooth for one semester or one academic year should contact the Department of Music for information before registering.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate proficiency in the interpretation of square and neumatic chant notations.
accurately transcribe chants from  key manuscript witnesses of western plainchant from 9th-12th centuries.
intelligently evaluate any Latin chant  with respect to the preparation required for solo or group performance.
confidently function as a solo performer in a liturgical or concert context.

modules.moduleContent: In this lab-based module, students will be introduced to the practice of sound synthesis using analogue and digital hardware.The programme will consist of a study of classic synthesis components such as oscillators, filters, envelopes, and amplifiers, as well as hardware sequencers and other controlling devices. The principles of voltage control and MIDI (as applied to synthesisers) will be explored, and the students will be working on practical creative projects for sound design, as well as electronic music composition, performance, and improvisation. The module will also include some elements of hardware hacking for musical applications and an introduction to sound synthesis electronics.
learningOutcomes: operate and program analogue and digital synthesisers with a high level of skill
create sound synthesis patches for specific musical and sound design applications
apply sound synthesis and processing methods in studio production
interpret and apply simple electronics schematics for sound and music applications

modules.moduleContent: Everyone, whether they are a musician or not, has favourite songs, songs with which we identify, which speak personally to us and can place it right back in time to when we first heard them or offer comfort in times of stress. What is it about this worldwide phenomenon, which is there in every culture, every form of music across the ages and across the globe? Why do people sing? What makes song such a unique human and musical phenomenon? What can we learn through song about human history and about ourselves? This course will change students' whole perception of song, even songs which are their own personal favourites.
learningOutcomes: Perceive and write about familiar and favourite songs in a new way through placing them in historical, sociological and biographical contexts and analyse the relationship between music and text, using technical terms to show more skillfully how meaning is reflected in music
Explore the Art of Song (Women’s Songs and Voices; Songs of Travel; Love Songs) across many different cultures from the 18th to 21st centuries
To show how our perception of song has remained constant and how it has changed by identifying recurring musical tropes, gestures and themes
To leave the course with a new understanding of the centrality of song within human society

modules.moduleContent: The module aims to expose students to the wide variety of styles and ideas in Contemporary Western Art Music so that they can make informed judgements about their own creative expression through composition. This is done through the examination of scores of contemporary music with some analysis relating to particular techniques employed. Topics covered include advanced harmonic techniques, microtonality, extended techniques and notation.
learningOutcomes: Produce original compositions at a level commensurate with academic year and within a given timeframe making use of designated techniques
Analyse compositions for use of contemporary compositional techniques

Discuss in some detail issues concerning contemporary compositional
techniques
Critique, in a meaningful way, compositions by others as well as by themselves
Reflect analytically on core works of this past century

modules.moduleContent: Students will devise and present a public recital lasting 15-20 minutes. The programme to be developed and agreed upon in consultation with the supervisor. The programme should be coherently structured and demonstrate technical, interpretative and communicative skills that are at a professional level. Spoken introductions should be included in the programme time.
learningOutcomes: design a coherent and professional recital programme
present a professional standard recital.
communicate effectively with an audience.

modules.moduleContent: With a focus on recent theory and practice, this module will introduce students to key issues raised by music’s encounter with screen media. Topics include music and sound design in cinema, music’s role in ‘prestige’ television/streaming and the audiovisual practices facilitated by online video platforms like YouTube.
learningOutcomes: understand the technical terminology of screen music studies
recognise and apply a range of methodologies in screen music studies
critically evaluate and contextualise screen music practices
identify key issues in the theory and practice of music for screen
analyse case studies of screen music in depth

modules.moduleContent:
**Commencing September 2025**

This module aims to foster deep engagement with key areas of musicological research, specifically related to the research expertise of staff in the Department. To develop an understanding of different scholarly perspectives on any given issue, the module entails individual close reading of a substantial number of texts and group discussion of the same.

It will introduce the central debates in the discipline relevant to each topic (topic 1 in Weeks 1 – 5; topic 2 in Weeks 6 – 10), and will explore other salient points. How do intellectual positions on a given issue shift or evolve over the years? How are discussions shaped by the voices of those who participate and how does gatekeeping exclude other voices? How do contemporary modes of communication such as social media platforms mediate and steer musicological debate?

The module will explore two topics; the topics in question will vary from year to year. Sample topics will draw from subfields of musicology such as: music and gender; ethnomusicology; musicology and popular music; historical musicology; music and media technology.
learningOutcomes: Engage critically with historical and current discourses in two specific subfields of musicology/ethnomusicology.
Relate areas of musicological debate to one another and assess their development over time in relation to academic enquiry more broadly.
Demonstrate an understanding of key issues and an ability to apply this understanding to new case studies.
