Students will work on an independent research project, as approved by the module co-ordinator, and under the supervision of an assigned supervisor. Contributions to knowledge may be demonstrated through multiple artifacts, in the form of music, media, performances and dissertations.
learningOutcomes: independently plan and execute a research project of significant scale resulting in a largescale research artefact (e.g. thesis, portfolio, performance);
articulate arguments and insights derived from scholarly research or creative work;
demonstrate a depth of understanding and proficiency in scholarly or practice-based research.

modules.moduleContent:
**Commencing September 2025**

This module will introduce a range of music technology equipment and related essential skills in the recording studio and beyond. Through both theory and practice, participants will learn about PA set-up, multitrack recording, overdubbing, live concert recording, microphones, podcast creation, voiceover capture, Foley and field recording as well as vital skills in operating a DAW (digital audio workstation) for recording, editing, transforming and mixing digital audio. Practical work will be informed by an investigation of equipment specifications and sound parameters commensurate with this level of study. Activities will be carried out individually and in groups and participants will have independent access to the Department’s studios and field recording equipment for individual work and practice outside of class times.
learningOutcomes: expertly set up and operate a range of music technology equipment for live sound reinforcement, studio recording and mobile recording;
record, edit and manipulate digital audio to a professional standard for a range of music, broadcast and sound-art goals;
plan and implement recording-related projects, coordinating with clients and problem solving independently during the project;
articulate and justify decisions made regarding hardware and so¿ware choices and set up in pursuance of a recording project based on knowledge of technical parameters.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
**Commencing September 2025**

This module focuses on performance systems characterised by high degrees of interaction between musician and computer. There is a software focus on the Supercollider software in the lectures, but participants can incorporate the use of other software they have experienced and learnt elsewhere in the projects they submit. The hardware component explores beyond the computer to explore haptics, existing interfaces with unorthodox human interaction, sensors, DIY electronics, Arduino, Raspberry Pi and other options and equipment.

Participants work towards the realisation of a portfolio of projects that explore the range of techniques – software and hardware – and possible outcomes explored throughout the module. Those outcomes could be compositions, performances, improvisations, installations or new pieces
of hardware or software.
learningOutcomes: create environments for the live-signal processing of a musical instrument;
create environments for haptic synthesis and gestural control of instruments;
design and implement hardware devices for interactive control of so¿ware;
solve problems relating to the use of object-oriented programming;
formulate ideas for the construction of virtual instruments.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
**Commencing in September 2025**

This module focuses on the performer in the recording studio from two perspectives: (1) through critical engagement with the history of recording and the recorded legacies of performers; and (2) by developing the performer’s professional skills and competencies when working in a recording studio environment. It introduces research and analytical methods relevant to the contextualisation and evaluation of the recorded archive. It aims to equip students with critical and practical skills that foster their creative development within and outside the studio environment, culminating in a self-directed recording project. Students taking this module must also take MU692 Digital Audio Skills.
learningOutcomes: Evaluate the legacies inherited by performers and assess the role of performers in interpreting music history;
Critically engage with the issues surrounding performance practices in the age of recording;
Work productively within the recording studio environment to create a demonstration recording of their own performance;
Demonstrate the ability to reflect on and evaluate their recording studio experience.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Students present a 20-minute paper (followed by c.10 minutes of Q&A) at the Department's annual Postgraduate Research Symposium at the end of Semester 2. The focus of the paper should be the work completed in the academic year in which the symposium takes place. The precise format of the paper is to be agreed with the supervisor in accordance with the mode of research being undertaken. In preparation, students are also strongly encouraged to attend the regular research seminar series taking place throughout the academic year, featuring local and international guest speakers across a broad range of research areas.
learningOutcomes: Plan and prepare a presentation based on the intermediate stages of the research project
Communicate the research effectively and succinctly with appropriate audio-visual aids
Deliver the presentation within the required time limits
Respond effectively to questions from the seminar audience

modules.moduleContent: This module will focus  on the study of the basic techniques of synthesis, including a detailed study of computer music languages (sound compilers) Topics include basic elements of synthesis: additive, subtractive, distortion, granular techniques and physical models.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module will study several different aspects of music systems programming. These will include low-level MIDI, audio programming, signal processing and component development (plugins, opcodes, classes, etc.). The will also include an introduction to Object-oriented programming and the C++ language.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: In this module, student performers and student composers are required to collaborate in the creation and subsequent performance of a newly composed work for a specific instrument/voice (or combination of instruments as designated by the module coordinator).
learningOutcomes: Explain to a composer the main feature of the performer's instrument/voice; explain to a performer the main features of the composer's style;
Collaborate with a composer/performer and provide/receive performance input about the new composition at different stages of its composition;
Evaluate the aesthetic and the performance challenges of a newly composed work;
Compose/perform a newly composed piece.

modules.moduleContent: The module will consist of individual tutorials leading to a presentation at a national or international conference, or to a musical performance on a national or international platform. An oral presentation or a musical performance at national or international level are the requirements for completion of this module. Only works which have not yet been presented at national/international level qualify for assessment for this module.
learningOutcomes: Prepare and submit either (a) an abstract for presentation at a national or international conference or (b) a sample for performance on a national or international platform
Deliver a presentation or performance based on the intermediate stages of the research project
Observe the specified time limits
Respond effectively to questions and/or feedback from delegates/audiences

modules.moduleContent: The module will consist of individual tutorials leading to a presentation at an international conference, or to a musical performance on an international platform. An oral presentation or a musical performance at international level are the requirements for completion of this module. Only works which have not yet been presented at international level qualify for assessment for this module.
learningOutcomes: Prepare and submit either (a) an abstract for presentation at an international conference or (b) a sample for performance on an international platform
Deliver a presentation or performance based on the advanced stages of the research project
Observe the specified time limits
Respond effectively to questions and/or feedback from delegates/audiences

modules.moduleContent: Léachtaí ar ghnéithe den teanga labhartha agus scríofa. Ranganna praiticiúla i labhairt agus i scríobh na teanga.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh do mhic léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ardchumas sa Ghaeilge labhartha agus scríofa acu.
I ndiaidh do mhic léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a gcumas na pointí gramadaí a chlúdaítear sna léachtaí a láimhseáil i gceart.
I ndiaidh do mhic léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a gcumas anailís a dhéanamh ar earráidí gramadaí.
I ndiaidh do mhic léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a gcumas sleachta a aistriú ó Bhéarla go Gaeilge.

modules.moduleContent: Pléifear gnéithe éagsúla de nua-litríocht na Gaeilge
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar ghnéithe de nualitríocht na Gaeilge.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar chuid de théamaí na nualitríochta Gaeilge.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann nualitríocht na Gaeilge a phlé.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann anailís liteartha a scríobh.

modules.moduleName: OIDHREACHT GHAELACH THUAISCEART LAIGHEAN AGUS CHÚIGE ULADH
modules.moduleContent: Pléifear gnéithe éagsúla d’oidhreacht Ghaelach Thuaisceart Laighean agus Chúige Uladh, i dtréimhse na Nua-Ghaeilge (1650-1900) go háirithe. Múinfear cúrsa tosaigh ar phaileagrafaíocht na lámhscríbhinní; agus déanfar roinnt téacsanna simplí a léamh agus a thras-scríobh as na lámhscríbhinní iad féin. Pléifear stair ghinearálta agus scóip thraidisiún na lámhscríbhinní Nua-Ghaeilge agus caithfear súil, ina dhiaidh sin, ar thraidisiún na Gaeilge i gColáiste Phádraig, Maigh Nuad.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar ghnéithe d’oidhreacht Thuaisceart Laighean agus Chúige Uladh.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann roinnt téacsanna simplí a léamh agus a thras-scríobh.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann stair agus ábhar na lámhscríbhinní Nua-Ghaeilge a phlé.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar shaothrú na lámhscríbhinní i gcontaetha ar leith i dTuaisceart Laighean agus i gCúige Uladh.

modules.moduleContent:
An Ainmeolaíocht: Déanfar staidéar ar logainmneacha agus ainmneacha pearsanta na Gaeilge ó thaobh staire, struchtúir, míreanna ainmníochta agus úsáide.
An Fhiannaíocht: Déanfar staidéar agus plé ar thraidisiún agus ar ábhar na Fiannaíochta.  Tabharfar cuntas ar phrós agus ar fhilíocht na Fiannaíochta trí roinnt téacsanna Fiannaíochta a léamh.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas traidisiún agus cúlra na Fiannaíochta a thuiscint.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas carachtair agus saothair mhóra na Fiannaíochta a aithint.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas anailís a dhéanamh ar roinnt téacsanna Fiannaíochta.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas eolas a thabhairt ar logainmneacha agus ar ainmneacha pearsanta na Gaeilge.

I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas logainmneacha agus ainmneacha pearsanta na Gaeilge a thuiscint ó thaobh staire, struchtúir, míreanna ainmníochta agus úsáide.

modules.moduleContent: Sa chúrsa seo, dírítear ar léamh, scríobh agus labhairt Ghaeilge na hAlban. Breathnaítear freisin ar stair na Gaeilge in Albain, agus ar na príomhdhifríochtaí idir Gaeilge na hÉireann agus Gaeilge na hAlban.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas roinnt téacsanna i nGaeilge na hAlban a léamh agus a thuiscint.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas bunchomhrá a bheith aige i nGaeilge na hAlban.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas abairtí bunúsacha a scríobh i nGaeilge na hAlban.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas na príomhdhifríochtaí idir Gaeilge na hÉireann agus Gaeilge na hAlban a aithint.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige ar stair na Gaeilge in Albain.

modules.moduleContent: Cuirfear trí mhórchanúint na Gaeilge i gcomparáid le chéile agus díreofar aird ar na príomhdhifríochtaí atá eatarthu. Díreofar go mion ar chúrsaí fuaimnithe, gramadaí agus foclóra. Déanfar plé chomh maith ar shaothrú stairiúil chanúineolaíocht na Gaeilge.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú, bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas trí mhórchanúint na Gaeilge a aithint óna chéile
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú, bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas trí mhórchanúint na Gaeilge a chur i gcomparáid le chéile
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú, bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas príomhthréithe mórchanúna amháin a aithint agus a thuiscint
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú, bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar chumas an mhic léinn tuiscint a léiriú ar shaothrú stairiúil chanúineolaíocht na Gaeilge

modules.moduleContent: Scrúdófar nádúr na drámaíochta ó thaobh na difríochta idir drámaíocht choinbhinsiúnach agus drámaíocht na háiféise agus idir an dráma i dtéacs agus an dráma á léiriú. Ní mór do mhic léinn a roghnaíonn an modúl seo a bheith rannpháirteach sa rang.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas na príomhdhifríochtaí idir an drámaíocht choinbhinsiúnach agus drámaíocht na háiféise a aithint agus a phlé.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas anailís chuimsitheach a dhéanamh ar dhráma.

I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige ar thábhacht an léirithe; tuiscint aige ar thábhacht na dtreoracha stáitse agus na n-eilimintí amhairc san áireamh.

I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint réasúnta leathan aige ar réimse na drámaíochta Gaeilge agus go mbeadh ar a chumas samplaí de dhrámaí agus de dhrámadóirí Gaeilge a thabhairt.

modules.moduleName: OIDHREACHT AN BHLASCAOID MHÓIR & FILÍOCHT NA SCOL
modules.moduleContent:
Tá dhá chuid i gceist leis an modúl seo: 
1) Oidhreacht an Bhlascaoid Mhóir: Díreofar sa chúrsa seo ar shaol mhuintir an Bhlascaoid Mhóir agus ar oidhreacht liteartha an oileáin.
2) Filíocht na Scol: Díreofar sa chúrsa seo ar fhilíocht shiollach na Nua-Ghaeilge Moiche.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint ar leith aige ar na deacrachtaí a thug orthu an t-oileán a thréigean.

I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige ar an saol ar an mBlascaod Mór san fhichiú haois, go háirithe, i bhfianaise phríomhshaothair litríochta an Oileáin.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige ar ‘leabharlann an Bhlascaoid’.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas tuiscint agus eolas ar chúlra sóisialta agus stairiúil fhilíocht na Nua-Ghaeilge Moiche a léiriú.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas anailís a dhéanamh ar ghnéithe de theanga na Nua-Ghaeilge Moiche.

I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas téacsanna ionadaíocha filíochta ó thréimhse na Nua-Ghaeilge Moiche a léamh, a thuiscint agus a aistriú go Béarla.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas anailís a dhéanamh ar théacsanna ar leith ó thaobh ábhair agus saintréithe liteartha de.

modules.moduleContent: Seimineáir ar mhodhanna, scileanna agus acmhainní taighde. Tabharfar deis do mhic léinn a gcuid tionscnamh taighde (tráchtais nó togra ealaíonta) a phlé agus a fhorbairt. Déanfar scrúdú ar shamplaí den dea-chleachtas taighde agus tabharfar aird ar leith ar luacháil agus úsáid foinsí. Tabharfar deis do mhic léinn na ceisteanna taighde agus na modhanna oibre a bheas á bhforbairt acu a phlé agus a chur i láthair.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh réimse scileanna riachtanacha acadúla sealbhaithe aige agus go mbeadh réiteach cuí déanta aige don mhiontráchtas.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas léamh, mionscagadh agus plé éifeachtach a dhéanamh ar ailt acadúla.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas achoimre a scríobh ar alt acadúil.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas léirbhreithniú litríochta a scríobh.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas cur i láthair éifeachtach a dhéanamh ar ábhar taighde.

modules.moduleContent: Féachfar le cruinneas teanga an mhic léinn a fhorbairt ag leibhéal iarchéime. Leagfar béim, dá réir sin, ar ghnéithe faoi leith d’fhuaimniú agus de scríobh na Gaeilge. Pléifear go mion sleachta éagsúla Gaeilge ó thaobh na brí agus na gramadaí. Díreofar ar roinnt áiseanna cuí leictreonacha/ téacsanna a chuideos leis an mac léinn dul i dtaithí ar bhlas agus ar dhul ceart na Gaeilge. Iarrfar ar an mac léinn cleachtaí cuí agus aistriúcháin ar ábhair éagsúla a réiteach – is orthu seo a bhunófar marc an mheasúnaithe leanúnaigh.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas cruinneas sásúil Gaeilge a léiriú agus é ag labhairt agus ag scríobh na teanga.

I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas leas cóir a bhaint as áiseanna cuí leictreonacha/ téacsanna a bhaineann le ceartfhuaimniú agus le cruinneas na Gaeilge.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas profáil cheart a dhéanamh ar shliocht Gaeilge.
modules.notes: Clibeadh an modúl seo mar Shonraí/Digiteach (Data/Digital) ag úsáid Intleachta Saorga (AI)

modules.moduleName: AN GHAEILGE SNA MEÁIN Ó 2RN GO DTÍ AN RÉ DHIGITEACH
modules.moduleContent: Pléifear dúshlán na teanga sa tréimhse ó na mórmheáin nua a tháinig chun cinn díreach tar éis ré na hAthbheochana: 2RN go Radio Éireann, Amharc Éireann, Mise Éire agus iarracht Ghael Linn cead an Rialtais a fháil an tseirbhís nua teilifíse a bhunú.  Lena chois sin, déanfar plé ar Telefís Éireann agus an geall a briseadh. Déanfar luacháil ar an ré órga sin ó Raidió na Gaeltachta go TG4 agus déanfar cíoradh ar an réabhlóid dhigiteach, baol nó deis?
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas cuntas a thabhairt ar dhála na meán Gaeilge ó aimsir 2RN i leith.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas anailís a dhéanamh ar na dúshláin a bhain le forbairt na meán Gaeilge.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas anailís a dhéanamh ar na dúshláin a bhaineann leis an réabhlóid dhigiteach san aois ina mairimid.

modules.moduleContent: Díreofar ar scileanna na scríbhneoireachta gairmiúla agus leagfar béim ar leith, dá réir sin, ar na riachtanais a bhaineann leis an iriseoireacht, an scríbhneoireacht scripte agus an scríbhneoireacht léannta. Chuige sin, díreofar ar thascanna praiticiúla scríbhneoireachta d'fhonn taithí a fháil ar an scríbhneoireacht ghairmiúil i gcomhthéacsanna éagsúla. Beidh aoichainteoirí againn a bhfuil saineolas acu i réimsí éagsúla scríbhneoireachta, ina measc sin, Liam Mac Cóil, údar agus úrscéalaí, agus Pádhraic Ó Ciardha, iarLeascheannasaí TG4.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas riachtanais na scríbhneoireachta gairmiúla a thuiscint.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas anailís a dhéanamh ar stíleanna na scríbhneoireachta gairmiúla.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas tascanna scríbhneoireachta a chur i gcrích i gcomhthéacsanna éagsúla gairmiúla.
modules.notes: Ag brath ar an ábhar taighde a roghnaíonn an mac léinn

modules.moduleName: LITRÍOCHT AGUS CRITIC CHOMHAIMSEARTHA NA GAEILGE
modules.moduleContent: Cad é mar is féidir téacs liteartha a mheas? Cén traidisiún critice atá againn sa Nua-Ghaeilge? Tabharfar faoi na ceisteanna sin a fhreagairt sa mhodúl seo agus plé á dhéanamh againn ar rogha aistí agus téacsanna ina bhfuil léargas le fáil ar bhunchlocha na critice sa Ghaeilge. Leagfar béim ar leith ar an phlé oscailte agus ar an smaointeoireacht tríd síos.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas taighde éifeachtach a dhéanamh ar litríocht agus ar chritic chomhaimseartha na Gaeilge.

I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas anailís éifeachtach a dhéanamh ar ghnéithe de litríocht chomhaimseartha na Gaeilge agus tuiscint a léiriú ar an dioscúrsa criticiúil a bhaineann léi.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh an mac léinn in ann aiste chritice a scríobh agus gnéithe de stíl na haiste liteartha a léiriú ina shaothar fein.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas córas tagartha a úsáid i gceart agus leabharliosta cuimsitheach a chur le chéile.

modules.moduleContent: Taighde ar ábhar an tráchtais.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann mórthráchtas a scríobh.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann leabharliosta cuimsitheach a chur le chéile.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann córas tagairtí a chur i bhfeidhm go héifeachtach.
modules.notes: Ag brath ar an ábhar taighde a roghnaíonn an mac léinn

modules.moduleContent:
This module describes the fundamental chemistry of typical materials and their associated properties. It is focused on how to use this knowledge and material properties for the selection of suitable materials for specific product design applications. This selection considers cost, properties, manufacturing and environmental concerns. 
The course also covers design for flexibility and strength to weight ratio, whilst also introduce 3D printing.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate they have gained the required knowledge of material properties (Mechanical, Electrical, Thermal, Failure, Aesthetic, Biodegradable).
Assess and critically consider the strength of materials (Improving Strength by Design, Area Moment of Inertia).
Identify a wide variety materials for different design applications (Ceramics, Metals & Alloys, Polymers, Composites).
Demonstrate effective selection of materials for different design applications (Selection for Density, Cost, Recyclability, Energy, Strength, Stiffness).
Demonstrate they have gained the required knowledge of material processing (Heat Treatment, Case Hardening, Cold Working).
Complete a range of product analysis methods (Case Studies, Interpreting Design Data, Processing Routes, Costs).

modules.moduleContent:
In this module students will be required to complete two simple design projects. Students are typically tasked with designing and making simple consumer products suitable for small batch production. They are guided through each design stage through an intensive practical studio and workshop-based course. The chosen products are usually items of gift or homeware and are designed to a specific theme. Students are required to produce among the deliverables, a professional quality presentation board outlining their idea. They are also required to produce a high-quality prototype as close to a final product as possible. The packaging requirements for the product are also addressed. Students will be encouraged to incorporate programs such as Solid works to develop and present their products. Students are encouraged to explore a broad range of materials and manufacturing processes to bring their ideas to life. Students will utilise and further develop visual thinking and presentation skills covered in PD15, 

The module aims to encourage familiarity and fluidity in the way students use sketching and sketching notebooks as a designer’s key medium of conceptual expression. It aims to establish a pattern of behaviour in the students that will be encouraged and monitored throughout the full duration of the BSc programme, and that will provide a solid foundation of behavioural practice for entry into professional life. 

The module will be split into two six-week projects. One of these will be a group project.
learningOutcomes: Identify and complete the key components of an introductory design project.
Develop explorative and development skillsets in a variety of media.
Develop advanced visual thinking and 2 D presentation techniques.
Develop practical project planning and production skillsets.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to the processes and frameworks of design thinking. It will will provide students with a range of skills and tools relevant to human-centred problem solving. Creative and critical thinking will be addressed and encouraged as and when they appear across the design thinking process. This module will also offer a way for students to make sense of the key design understandings that they should take from the first year of their studies on the BSc in Product Design.
learningOutcomes: Identify the main models and frameworks of design thinking.
Discuss key concepts and theories of creative and critical thinking.
Understand basic research techniques and considerations when applying design thinking to problem solving (including wicked problems).
Apply a variety of creative design techniques and approaches to the processes of idea generation and development.
Identify the key design principles required from year one of the BSc in Product Design.

modules.moduleContent: This module teaches an overview of key design movements from the turn of the 20th century to date. It draws of a range of different design examples from designed objects, buildings, cars and fashion, to explore the movements origins and cultural, social and economic influencing factors.  From this, students will learn that design is evolutionary, not revolutionary.  By understanding the history of previous design movements, students will be learn to predict future design, business and social trends for impactful product, service and business solutions. Topics include: The Industrial Revolution and its impact on society; The Arts & Crafts movement as a social movement; Stylistic and decorative movements of Art Nouveau, Art Deco, Modernism and Bauhaus, Post Modernism in design; The changing face of design in consumer society, Design in popular and youth culture, and Forecasting design for the future.
learningOutcomes: Understand the historical and cultural context of design history from the turn of the 20th Century to date.
Explain the significance and influence of design in society, and the main movements that shaped this.
Explore the changing face of consumer society and political, social and economical discourse through design.
Learn from the past to help forecast design trends for the future.
Describe machining, metal forming and joining and differentiate between the processes that fall into these categories.
Explain the casting and powder metallurgy processes and detail how they are used to manufacture metal parts.
Discuss some of the methods used to process polymers, specifically looking at moulding.
Give the advantages and disadvantages of the processes covered.
List some of the materials applicable to these processes and the typical products that can be manufactured using these techniques.
Outline the design for manufacture and assembly guidelines of these processes.

modules.moduleContent:
Visual communication is an essential means of branding and presenting ideas to an audience. A designer's portfolio is an essential calling card to employment.
As an essential part of the designer’s toolkit, effective visual communication aids the comprehension and sharing of research data, a new innovative solution or elements of a service or system. This module introduces the methodologies and tools of visual communication and leaves students with the essential skills to generate new graphics and manipulate images for both print and digital media. These skills are particularly relevant to the creation of a designer’s portfolio. The portfolio provides prospective employers with evidence of the designer’s repertoire and quality, and will be a key deliverable of this module. 
 
CONTENT 
• Introduction to visual communication 
• Principals of effective visual communication 
• Introduction to adobe suite software
• Elementary photography 
• Image editing
• Signs and infographics
• Typography 
• Product design boards
• Booklet layout and formatting 
• Portfolio design 
• Planning for print
learningOutcomes: Understand the importance and potential of effective visual communication.
Discern and discuss the principles of effective graphic design through the basic aesthetics of layout composition, colour, image and typography.
Have a working knowledge of the principals and tools of visual communication.
Interpret different types of information to design and originate graphic communication from sketch to digital and print ready media.
Understand the role and requirements of a professional design portfolio.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module focus on Universal Design in product design. Students will explore the challenges currently facing today’s society (wicked problems that are systemic in nature such as homelessness, healthcare, education). In response to these challenges students will learn how to create universally designed products and implement universal design principles at various stages of the design process. Covered in this module are: The 7 principles of Universal Design, The 29 guidelines of Universal Design, Universal Design case studies (good and bad examples), and the Universal Design checklist.
learningOutcomes: Understand and communicate Universal Design principles.
Research and define opportunities and develop skills in visual acuity.
Provide grounded insights with well executed research.
Develop critical thinking and communicate thinking effectively.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will develop the student’s ability to manage a design project from start to finish. It consists of a single, large design project and will include contributions and insights from industry partners. Students will learn to integrate and manage the different phases of a design project from early research to final design, including appropriate design sketching, CAD visualisation and model 3D prototyping. As the design industry moves at an ever-increasing pace this project captures the trends, topics, thinking, materials and technologies most relevant to the industry today.

CONTENT 

· Industry live brief

· Group work

· Ideation and testing

· Prototyping and modelling

· Using video to communicate

· Presentation of final design to industry
learningOutcomes: Interpret and address an industry design brief
Undertake appropriate research
Generate innovative and/or novel & realisable concepts
Communicate ideas clearly and in detail to a third party
Finalise design for realisation

modules.moduleContent: Well designed digital interfaces are intuitive and simple to use.  User-centred digital interfaces are intuitive to use while meeting a true user need.  This module provides an introduction to Human-Computer Interaction, targeted specifically towards user-centred research, design, and evaluation of digital product and services.  Students learn how to identify the needs and behaviors of users; and utilise these learnings to develop engaging and desirable UX solutions.  This is achieved with lectures, workshop discussions, and applied learning through a variety of practice assignments.
learningOutcomes: Apply the principles of Human-Computer Interaction.
Identify and apply appropriate methods to identify user requirements.
Identify and apply appropriate evaluation techniques and undertake a usability and user experience evaluation.
Capture and appropriately analyse findings.
Produce mid-level fidelity prototypes and prioritise design recommendations and goals.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with an introduction to the multiple influences that determine how a new business (either new firm or new product to market) might develop and grow internationally.
The influences are considered in three categories: Industry/Market, Political/Social, Intellectual Property Rights.
Industry/Market
• Competitive analysis (Forces of competition [Porter]; Strategic Group Analysis; Profit Pools [Bain]; Competitive Position Mapping [D’Aveni]); Strategy Canvas [Blue Ocean].
• Dynamic capabilities; Absorptive capacity
• R&D intensity; Technology trends and trajectories
• Product Costing

Political/Social
• Labour Laws
• Regional cost competitiveness
• Taxes & Tariffs; Free trade areas
• PESTEL analysis
• Government incentives, grants and other supports

Intellectual Property Rights
• Patents; Trademarks; Copyright; Registered Designs
• Standards; CE mark
• Managing Intellectual Property; Design practice
learningOutcomes: Compare and contrast the macro-competitiveness of various regions for doing business
Evaluate an industry’s internal competitive structure and intensity, and assess the relative strengths of various competitive influences
Manage business activities and projects so as to maximise the utility gained from its intellectual property
Calculate the cost-to-market of various product types
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Visualisation is a vital tool in facilitating concept ‘sense making’. Via live brief tasks, participants learn best-practice methodologies. 
In the early stages of innovation, visualisation and “data display” are core competencies of designers and researchers alike. These are valuable design thinking skill sets that allow meaning to develop and to progress further to strategies for satisfying consumer requirements. Visual thinking benefits the process of ‘sense making’ when dealing with complexity. Participants will learn visual thinking methods and integration models for broad application in organisations. You will experience and develop basic skills in print and 3D design, as well as storyboarding and rudimentary video. 
 
CONTENT 
• Introduction to visualisation of ideas. 
• Using photography to record process. 
• Storytelling – brand and service/product
• Basic graphic design concepts for type, layout and image files. 
• Group work on ‘live’ projects.
• Client presentations
learningOutcomes: Create and present visual documents that excite, entertain and inform by synthesising independent research and visual prototypes for impact.
Apply visual methods to build visual frameworks that transfer field data and distil research findings into visual and actionable design/marketing material e.g. mood boards, concept boards, personas.
Use story and storyboarding to inform and communicate new concepts by rudimentary video.
Demonstrate basic competency in graphic publishing software (Adobe InDesign) to create documents for print.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module studies the behaviours and experiences of users interacting with devices or systems. It includes the perceptual, cognitive, ergonomic and other scientific underpinnings of usability (‘usability science’).
Learners will learn the methods for evaluating, testing, designing, and developing better interfaces between users and systems, including computer-based systems.
The module also presents user centric design methods as a way of responding to user insights. Methods of prototyping will be introduced here and practised throughout this and other modules. This course consists of lectures and workshop discussions reinforced through a variety of practice assignments.
learningOutcomes: Understand and explain how human beings take in and process information
Carry out a usability evaluation by conducting tests and writing test reports
Explain why product and software developers should not depend on own intuitions for what is a usable design
Represent user insights and creative solution concepts
Create and evaluate solution prototypes
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: UNDERSTANDINGS OF HUMAN DIGNITY IN EUROPE
modules.moduleContent:
The idea of human dignity plays a key role in the human rights tradition as a fundamental, guiding principle. This use has shaped how it is understood today and was itself a product of political compromise between various traditions of thought. Studying the conceptualisation history of the idea allows us to see how different epochs have contributed understandings that still account for tensions regarding the content and meaning of human dignity present in current debates. We shall concentrate on Western European traditions and integrate Eastern European traditions to the extent interest and linguistic expertise among students allow us access texts in Eastern European Languages. Mette Lebech: European Sources of Human Dignity. An Anthology of Texts on Human Dignity (Oxford: Peter Lang, 2015) will be used as a textbook.

Students who complete this module should attain the following transferrable skills: 

1. Research skills: Accurate identification of relevant texts conforming to very specific criteria from a very large amount of material.
2. Translation and interpretation skills: culturally sensitive engagement with the text to present it in a context different from its origination.
3. Database and library literacy: Finding relevant literature independently and with a specific public in mind.
4. Communication skills: Presenting the chosen text to a potentially large and varied public represented by the participants in the module.
5. Comparison: clarifying the content of chosen texts with reference to other texts presented in the module.
6. Critical thinking: Situating constructively and with an awareness of potential bias each text in its own setting to gain a comprehensive understanding of the role of these texts in cultural development. 
7. Presentation skills: Orally presenting results with a view to contribute to the overall goal of the module including the expansion of cultural awareness of all participants.
8. Respectful discussion: asking questions of other students and responding to them in ways that are of value to all present in the learning environment.
9. Preparing, carrying out and analysing an interview: compiling relevant questions, pursuing an adaptive strategy to obtain material for writing up an essay.
learningOutcomes: Identify a variety of texts on human dignity from different European traditions.
Discuss a variety of texts on human dignity from European traditions, situating them in their historical and cultural contexts.
Critically discuss connections, similarities, and differences between various European texts situated as part of their respective historical and cultural traditions.
Critically discuss the framework for understanding the development of the idea of human dignity proposed in the module.

Critically discuss the definition of human dignity proposed in the module [human dignity is the fundamental value of human beings entitling them to respect] and discuss its compatibility with other definitions encountered.
modules.notes:
This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI).
Changed as currently students are not trained in digital and data as specified.

modules.moduleContent: It is primarily due to Franz Brentano’s re-introduction of the Scholastic concept of intentionality in Psychology From an Empirical Standpoint (1874) and his student Edmund Husserl’s elaboration of this concept in phenomenology in the early decades of the twentieth century that the medieval Scholastic terminology of ‘intentionality’ regained widespread currency in contemporary philosophical circles. Behind the terminological agreement that exists between Brentano and the Scholastics on intentionality, however, there are real and substantive conceptual differences. Identity in terms is not identity in concepts. This module examines ‘Brentano’s thesis’ of the intentionality of consciousness, paying attention to identifying and analysing what he accepted, rejected and modified in the original scholastic concept of intentionality in light of his growing awareness of the fledging new natural science of empirical psychology and his new science of ‘descriptive psychology’. The module concludes with some discussion and assessment of some of the main philosophical implications of Brentano’s tenet of the intentionality of consciousness on later analytic and continental philosophies of human consciousness in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries.
learningOutcomes: Identify different concepts of intentionality in Scholastic metaphysics, epistemology, theology and theory of the will that are of relevance to the evaluation of Brentano’s new concept of intentionality in his idea of a modern science of empirical psychology.

Distinguish and evaluate two descriptive-psychological theses of intentionality elaborated by Brentano, one concerning the intentional in-existence of an object in consciousness, the other the intentional relation of the act of consciousness towards (its) objects.

Compare concepts of psychology elaborated in the history of thought (e.g. Platonic, Aristotelian, Thomistic, Cartesian, Lockean-Humean, modern natural-scientific) and Brentano’s new science of descriptive empirical psychology.

Discuss the significance of the two functions of Brentano’s descriptive psychology (clarifying basic concepts in the natural science of empirical psychology and in the normative sciences of logic, ethics and aesthetics) in the development of his thought.

Locate and explain Brentano’s elaboration of intentionality in its philosophical- historical context of the debate on philosophia perennis, nineteenth-century science, modern British empiricism, logical psychologism, and post-Kantian philosophy.

Assess naturalistic conceptions of human consciousness elaborated in analytic and continental philosophies of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries with reference to ‘Brentano’s thesis’ of the intentionality of consciousness.

modules.moduleContent: The issue regarding the extent to which the law should take into consideration the moral convictions of its citizens has become a topic of much debate in our society today. Some argue that morality is each person’s private affair and not the law’s business. Others argue that it is the state’s business by means of the law to protect its citizens from harm, offence, exploitation, fraud, degradation and discrimination (etc.), and legislate against such wrongs. These convictions are not readily reconcilable. To shed light on this topic, this module examines three influential texts in the philosophy of law, morality and the state that underpin our contemporary debate on this issue: Thomas Aquinas, Summa Theologiae (I-II) (c.1270), ‘Q. 94: Natural [Moral] Law’, and ‘Q. 95: Human [State] Law’; John Stuart Mill, On Liberty (1859) and his ‘Harm-to-Others Principle’; Lord Patrick Devlin, The Enforcement of Morals (1959) and his critics (Hart, Dworkin). In conclusion, some discussion will be given to the view that ‘democracy and legislation will be in a state of some tension; for legislation, if it is to take due account of moral considerations, cannot be reduced to a counting of heads’ (O’Donnell, 1991).
learningOutcomes: Identity and discriminate different concepts of written (State) and unwritten (Natural) law, and address the complexities of that relationship

Evaluate arguments for and against the opposing theories on the proper relation of morality to law, namely: (1) that the law should legislate against immorality as such and (2) that the law should legislate only against that which is damaging to society.

Debate the significance of important philosophical texts underpinning contemporary discussion in relation to morality and the law and particular issues of moral concern regarding human and legal rights in society today.
Distinguish different questions of morality pertaining to the prudential framing of laws and the moral evaluation of the content of laws.

Examine the connection between the function of state law and the function of the criminal law and assess the moral reasons for the justification of the state’s infliction of punishment on those who infringe state law.

modules.moduleContent: The aim of this module is to develop students’ independent research methods in philosophy and to deepen their critical awareness and writing ability on a significant topic in philosophy. The module requires students to select: (a) a significant topic of research for a Minor MA thesis in philosophy; (b) think this topic through; and (c) present it (c. 12,000 words) adhering to normal academic-scholarly written conventions. Appointment of supervisors for each student’s thesis-topic proposal will follow upon a consideration and review of the theses’ proposals that have been submitted to the Department of Philosophy and as approved by the Head of the Department. Revision and refinement of theses’ proposals are necessary, and such will be conducted, in due course over the first semester, between appointed supervisor and student. It is the responsibility of both supervisor and supervisee to arrange and fix appointment times during both the First and Second Semesters, and to discuss the progress, or lack of progress made in the researching and in the writing of the thesis. The format of the dissertation must conform to the guidelines set out in the Departmental Handbook for Students and the University’s Submission Procedures.
learningOutcomes: Identify and choose a topic for masters research in philosophy
Think this topic through in a consistent manner, identifying primary and secondary sources

Express accurately and evaluate properly, in written format, the viewpoints, opinions and beliefs of others and of oneself relevant to the topic selected, in a reasonable, fair, consistent and critical fashion
Develop skills of independent learning and self-evaluation
Demonstrate control, through argumentation and line of argument, over the material researched
Present, in written format, a scholarly academic thesis in philosophy following academic standards of referencing (as laid out by the MHRA Style Book)
Write clearly for the reader, displaying critical written communication skills and argument style in philosophy
Understand the different meanings and relevance of political theory and its relationship to democracy and citizenship;
Have insight, understanding and appreciation of the main issues and concepts dealt with in the course and the main perspectives with which these are viewed;
Obtain a broad level of familiarity with some of the major political theorists espousing differing positions on these issues;
Achieve an in-depth familiarity with at least two theorists on one theme;
Appreciate the relevance of political theory to political practice and its impact on contemporary society;
Know how to engage with and utilize a broad range of primary and secondary sources relating to the subject matter, including journals and internet sources;
Develop the ability to write in an informed and critical way producing a research paper on course subject matter.

modules.moduleContent: This module reassesses notions of creativity in music, capturing the interactive and experimental nature at the base of musical processes such as composition, performance and production. Furthermore, it observes how collaborative working modes interrelate in the contemporary musical scenario, providing a fresh perspective on the distributed creativity at the core of music-making. The module aims to bring together final-year students from different pathways (music technology, performance, composition, and musicology) and to project an understanding of the potentially innovative approaches they can apply to their own specialist area.
learningOutcomes: Assess creativity in different musical processes and histories.
Identify creative approaches to musical writing, performance and production.
Reflect on the political role of creative performance in a live and mediated environment.
Stimulate creative collaboration among students hailing from different backgrounds.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
• An introduction to key issues and concepts about popular music.

• The module treats popular music in its widest sense, embracing a wide range of genres and forms.

• A consideration of ideas about the production, reception, and culture of popular music, as well as the ‘music itself’.

• The module aims to develop a musicological approach to popular music.
learningOutcomes: describe and evaluate the development and transformation of rock and popular music in the 20th and 21st centuries
evaluate key issues in popular music, such as identity, authenticity, and the role of technology
distinguish and critically evaluate different styles of popular and rock music
discuss and critique recent musicological scholarship on pop and rock music

modules.moduleContent:
This module gives students the opportunity to explore a topic of their choice, as long as this is aligned with the expertise of the Music Department and once a suitable member of academic staff  is available to supervise. In the first instance, students interested in this module should contact the coordinator, Dr Laura Watson. 

The project may be completed in Semester 1 or Semester 2. Accordingly, tutorials with the supervisor take place in Semester 1 or 2 only. Students must discuss initial plans with Dr Watson early in Semester 1. After this meeting, students may avail of up to 4 hours of tuition with the supervisor over Semester 1 or 2.

The form of this musicological project is decided between the student and supervisor. Examples of projects include: an essay c. 5,000 words; a podcast c.40 minutes; an edited score and short commentary. 

NB: the topic of the Individual Project must be substantively different to that addressed in MU640 Thesis or in the work submitted for assessment in any other module.

NB: students based outside the Music Department must have demonstrable musical knowledge (including score literacy) in order to register for the module.
learningOutcomes: to engage with the literature in the chosen topic;
to contextualise the project within recent research;
to write a literature review;
to develop research questions manageable within the constraints of the 10-credit module and assignments/s;
to write a research paper (or any other format decided in agreement with the lecturer: compositions and technology assessments are also considered).
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: An extended, individually supervised thesis (10,000) on a topic directly related to the final recital (submitted at the end of August). Presentation on the dissertation topic.

Students will complete a Sound and Music Computing project which will consist of a software and/or hardware system. Supervision will be provided on both semesters. Students will submit a thesis/report (10,000 to 14,000 words) together with system documentation, code, and any other relevant artefacts.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module facilitates a critical engagement with the music of Ireland in its broadest sense, and musicological and ethnomusicological research into music in Ireland. Through case studies which build on the interests of a range of staff members, students will explore issues and current trends relating to the music of Ireland, including topics such as identity and representation; colonialism and post-colonial thought; music and nationalism; authenticity; Irish art music in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries; new composition in Irish traditional music; and music and politics.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the broad history of music in Ireland
Critique current musicology and ethnomusicology on music in Ireland
Appraise and evaluate selected key works of music
Convey an understanding of key issues and be able to apply them to new case studies
Construct a coherent argument in an extended paper or presentation which develops different conceptual approaches introduced in the module

modules.moduleContent:
MU649 Music and Gender explores how musicology has been shaped by critical theories relating to gender (and sexuality). The module is delivered as five discussion seminars, each of which engages with particular theoretical frameworks. The module is broadly conceived in chronological terms. We start with the foundational texts of ‘New Musicology’ in the late 1980s and early 1990s—when feminist scholars began systematically challenging the marginalisation of women from the classical canon—and conclude by investigating twenty-first century feminist musicology in the USA, UK, and Ireland. Over the course of the module, we observe how the field of ‘music and gender’ has expanded over the decades: it is no longer necessarily synonymous with the study of ‘women and music’ nor is it limited to the study of classical composers or performers.  The module addresses how gender may be analysed as part of an intersectional framework, paying attention to how gender-based discrimination overlaps with other markers of identity such as race, class, and sexuality.


LEARNING OUTCOMES
Upon successful completion of the module you should be able to:
* Discuss theoretical concepts about gender and sexuality as they relate to music.
* Outline the broad history of how thought about gender and sexuality has evolved in
musicology from the late 1980s to the early 2020s. 
* Apply theoretical concepts relating to gender, sexuality, and intersectional identity
topics, to various repertoires in Western art and popular musics, especially of the nineteenth and
twentieth centuries.
* Critique the intellectual and political strategies employed by scholars working on music, gender, and sexuality.
learningOutcomes: apply gender theories to a range of music genres;
analyse how performers, especially singers, construct and subvert gendered identities;
discuss how gender and sexuality can be encoded in music, particularly when music interacts with other art forms, or is associated with certain modes of performance;
critique intersectional issues of gender and race in subgenres of popular music.

modules.moduleContent: Students undertaking one of the projects in MU670 are required to undertake research leading to a dissertation (c. 10,000 words) directly related to the project. Students will also be expected to present on their research at postgraduate research seminars.
learningOutcomes: identify a suitable topic for research.
present a paper on the subject of their research.
develop a research plan and identify suitable research methods appropriate to their topic (library/archive work; ethnography; analysis etc.).
write a successful masters dissertation with suitable critical/analytical content for masters' level.

modules.moduleContent: The student will undertake supervised independent research and complete a thesis (c20,000 words) on an advanced topic of their choice. Regular individual tutorials will be held with an assigned supervisor up to the end of June. Students will also give two presentations on their research at the postgraduate research seminars in the Music Department.
learningOutcomes: identify a suitable topic for research.
present a paper on the subject of their research.
develop a research plan and identify suitable research methods appropriate to their topic (library/archive work; ethnography; analysis etc.)
write a successful masters dissertation with suitable critical/analytical content for masters' level.

modules.moduleContent: Topics to be included: fieldwork and ethnography techniques (including note taking and interview technique); locating sources; library and database skills; evaluation of primary sources; bibliography; ethical considerations and responsibilities.
learningOutcomes: plan and carry out a short fieldwork project (which can include participant observation, note taking or journal writing, interviews, survey).
research a topic and compile a comprehensive bibliography, utilising a range of printed and electronic sources
locate and evaluate appropriate primary source material
discuss the ethical principles of ethnographic research involving direct work with humans, and apply for ethical approval for potential projects
