modules.moduleName: CRITICAL APPROACHES TO IRISH TRADITIONAL MUSIC
modules.moduleContent: In this module students will develop independent critical approaches to contemporary Irish traditional music through reading and critiquing recent scholarship and critical writing on Irish traditional music, and through an engagement with additional relevant material from the related areas of ethnomusicology and popular music. The module content will focus on a different range of traditional music texts, including but not limited to recordings, performances, film etc.
learningOutcomes: Critically engage with a range of traditional music texts
Critique and evaluate recent critical work in traditional music and related fields
Deliver a well-informed and critical presentation applying relevant concepts on a case study
Write fluently and critically on traditional music in a range of styles (academic essay, review, essay)

modules.moduleContent: Whilst the emergence of concepts of autonomous music wrought to create a distance from the visual dimensions of the act of listening and performing, sound, images, and spaces have always been intimately connected. By looking at  theories of sound and image, and  exploring a wide range of music materials – from painting to album covers, from opera to sound installation -  aim of this module is to explore the relationship between the two sensory realms in performance spaces and how they influence the dimension of spectatorship, the way we associate music with sites of performance, how the bodies of the performers act, and change the act of listening, and how we understand traditions.
learningOutcomes: identify key terms in theories of sound and image
critically evaluate different approaches to discourses about sound, images, and spaces
understand how the evolving connections between aural and visual imaginations affect spectatorship and the act of listening.
evaluate the role of the performers’ bodies in the sensory dimension between music and images
appraise how we understand musical traditions in the aural and visual realms
analyse case studies of connection between music and visual culture in depth
synthesise scholarly investigation and practical applications;
contribute towards new perspectives and narratives across media forms in response to specific research questions;
document their shared contribution to, and reflections on, collaborative projects;
Demonstrate effective collaboration strategies.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
**Commencing September 2025**

This module aims to equip students with the tools to develop a research project on a performance-related topic such as autoethnographic performance practice; critical editions; a study of historical performance practices; the relationship between performance and composition; performance agency; visual culture and performance; music installation and more. Students preparing a recital as part of their capstone project are required to use this module to develop a research project connected to their recital programme. Non-recital students with demonstrable performance experience are encouraged to develop a project appropriate to their expertise. (Module coordinators reserve the right to audition students for this module.)
learningOutcomes: assess the musicological literature on a topic related to their instrument/voice;
formulate research questions;
develop in depth-knowledge of a chosen topic;
conduct independent research;
produce a medium length research project;
present a paper on their research subject.

modules.moduleContent: Students present a 20-minute paper (followed by c.10 minutes of Q&A) at the Department's annual Postgraduate Research Symposium at the end of Semester 2. The focus of the paper should be the work completed in the academic year in which the symposium takes place. The precise format of the paper is to be agreed with the supervisor in accordance with the mode of research being undertaken. In preparation, students are also strongly encouraged to attend the regular research seminar series taking place throughout the academic year, featuring local and international guest speakers across a broad range of research areas.
learningOutcomes: Plan and prepare a presentation based on the initial stages of the research project
Communicate the research effectively and succinctly with appropriate audio-visual aids
Deliver the presentation within the required time limits
Respond effectively to questions from the seminar audience

modules.moduleContent: This module will focus on the nature of sound and sound perception. It will present the basic concepts involved in the phenomena of sound, such as, frequency, amplitude, spectra, etc., and their perceptual counterparts (pitch, loudness, timbre, etc.). It will also include topics  on  the physiology of  hearing, auditive perception, acoustics of spaces and musical instruments.
learningOutcomes: describe the physical nature of sound and the various related parameters and phenomena.
apply the physical properties of sound to the study of timbre and musical acoustics.
describe the human hearing mechanism.
discuss the multi-faceted nature of the perception of pitch, localisation and loudness.

modules.moduleContent: This module will explore the techniques of signal processing for musical applications: delay lines, filtering, spectral processing and transformation. Student will also study the applications of these techniques in computer music environments such as csound and PD.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: The module will consist of individual tutorials leading to a presentation at a national or international conference, or to a musical performance on a national or international platform. An oral presentation or a musical performance at national level - or a poster presentation at international level - are the requirements for completion of this module. Only works which have not yet been presented at national/international level qualify for assessment for this module.
learningOutcomes: Prepare and submit either (a) an abstract for presentation at a national or international conference or (b) a sample for performance on a national or international platform
Deliver a presentation or performance based on the initial stages of the research project
Observe the specified time limits
Respond effectively to questions and/or feedback from delegates/audiences

modules.moduleContent: The module will introduce the main complementary methodologies with geocomputation: geographical information systems; spatial statistics; exploratory spatial data analysis; and the science of spatial data handling. It covers fundamental concepts, techniques and ideas that shape Geographical Information Science and associated GIS Software. The module will be taught as a mix of lectures and intensive practicals. The lecture series will introduce key concepts and analytical approaches used within GI Science including; the foundations of GIS, spatial data models, data input and output, core spatial modelling and specialist analytical approaches and techniques. The practicals will be based around the following software applications programs: ArcGIS and MapInfo, with a particular emphasis on the former. The module will familiarise students with the software using a series of cumulative practical exercises based on a series of GIS applications from a range of subjects including geography, environmental modelling and planning. Students will also gain experience of manipulating and understanding a range of digital spatial data in the course of this module.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Léachtaí ar chruinnscríobh na teanga agus ar ghnéithe de litríocht na Gaeilge. Ranganna praiticiúla teagaisc i labhairt agus i scríobh na teanga, agus i léamh na litríochta.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar bhunstruchtúr abairtí Gaeilge.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar théarmaíocht na gramadaí.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar aimsirí agus ar mhodhanna an bhriathair.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar cheartúsáid an ainmfhocail, ó thaobh inscne, tuisil agus uimhreach de.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar cheartúsáid réamhfhocail shimplí na Gaeilge.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a c(h)umas anailís a dhéanamh ar dhánta agus ar shleachta próis.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a c(h)umas dul i ngleic leis an litríocht as a stuaim féin.

modules.moduleContent: Léachtaí ar ghnéithe den teanga labhartha agus scríofa. Ranganna praiticiúla i labhairt agus i scríobh na teanga.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh do mhic léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ardchumas sa Ghaeilge labhartha agus scríofa acu.
I ndiaidh do mhic léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a gcumas na pointí gramadaí a chlúdaítear sna léachtaí a láimhseáil i gceart.
I ndiaidh do mhic léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a gcumas anailís a dhéanamh ar earráidí gramadaí.
I ndiaidh do mhic léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a gcumas sleachta a aistriú ó Bhéarla go Gaeilge.

modules.moduleContent: Staidéar ar thionchar na Críostaíochta ar theanga agus ar phobal na hÉireann agus grinnléitheoireacht ar roinnt scéalta miotaseolaíocha agus Rúraíochta.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas sraitheanna litríochta na hÉireann a aithint.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas eolas ar sheansochaí na hÉireann a léiriú.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas tionchar na Críostaíochta ar litríocht na hÉireann a thuiscint.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas eolas ar shaothair mhóra na Sraithe Miotaseolaíche a thaispeáint.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas Cath Maige Tuired a phlé mar shaothar.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas eolas ginearálta ar scéalta móra agus ar charachtair mhóra na Sraithe Ultaí a léiriú.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas traidisiún agus ábhar an tsaothair cháiliúil, Táin Bó Cuailnge, a phlé.

modules.moduleName: AN NUA-GHAEILGE MHOCH AGUS AN NUALITRÍOCHT
modules.moduleContent:
(a) An Nua-Ghaeilge Mhoch: Tabharfar deis do mhic léinn staidéar a dhéanamh ar theanga agus ar ghramadach na Nua-Ghaeilge Moiche trí roinnt téacsanna sa Nua-Ghaeilge Mhoch a léamh go mion.
(b) An Nualitríocht: Ag tógáil ar na scileanna a foghlaimíodh in NG254, beidh mic léinn an mhodúil ag tabhairt faoi anailís oilte a dhéanamh ar thuilleadh mórthéacsanna de chuid na Nua-Ghaeilge.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh do mhic léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a gcumas tuiscint agus eolas ar chúlra sóisialta agus stairiúil litríocht na Nua-Ghaeilge Moiche a léiriú.
I ndiaidh do mhic léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a gcumas anailís a dhéanamh ar ghnéithe de ghramadach na Nua-Ghaeilge Moiche.
I ndiaidh do mhic léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint acu ar stair agus ar chanóin na Nua-Ghaeilge.
I ndiaidh do mhic léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a gcumas téacsanna nualitríochta a phlé agus anailís liteartha a scríobh orthu.

modules.moduleName: BÉALOIDEAS NA GAEILGE AGUS COIMISIÚN BÉALOIDEASA ÉIREANN
modules.moduleContent: Pléifear dála thraidisiún béaloidis na Gaeilge sa 20ú haois, na hiarrachtaí a rinneadh chun an traidisiún sin a chaomhnú agus an díospóireacht a spreag bunú Choimisiún Béaloideasa Éireann. Déanfar caibidil ar na tuiscintí éagsúla a bhí acu siúd a bhí ag saothrú i ngort an bhéaloidis Éireannaigh sa 20ú haois agus déanfar luacháil ar an traidisiún lena raibh siad ag plé. Beidh aird ar leith againn ar obair Choimisiún Béaloideasa Éireann (1935-71).
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh eolas maith aige/aici ar dhála thraidisiún béaloidis na Gaeilge sa 20ú haois, na hiarrachtaí a rinneadh chun an traidisiún sin a chaomhnú agus an díospóireacht a lean.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar an dearcadh a bhí ag tráchtairí éagsúla ar an rud is traidisiún ann.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé ar a c(h)umas tráchtaireacht chriticiúil a scríobh ar cheisteanna a bhaineann le stair an bhéaloidis.

modules.moduleContent: Beidh ar na mic léinn freastal ar chruinnithe, agus tascanna a dhéanamh bunaithe ar chomhdháil a eagrú.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann tascanna a bhaineann le comhdháil a eagrú a dhéanamh.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann páirt a ghlacadh i gcruinnithe eagarthóireachta.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces the concepts and applications behind ‘Design for Manufacturing’ (DFM) as well as recent developments in Automation.  To expand on the materials topics covered in year one by introducing advanced materials used in microfabrication processes as well as surface processing. To introduce ‘Process Control and Process Optimisation’ techniques when applied to a manufacturing environment.
Syllabus elements are:
Introduction to Advanced Materials and DFM; Design for Manufacture; Automation; Microfabrication and Materials; Advanced Surface Processing; Process Control and Optimisation; Project presentations and feedback.
learningOutcomes: Apply Design for Manufacture principles in designs processes.
Be familiar with advancements in automation and applications within a manufacturing environment.
Describe materials and processes used in the microfabrication of semi-conductors and other micro-mechanical devices.
Discuss some of the applications of micro-mechanical devices within the Automotive and Medical field.
Discuss some of the advanced materials now used in the surface treatment, preparation and finishing of specific products.
Use ‘Process Control and Process Optimisation’ methods and techniques, applied to a ‘high volume’ automation environment.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO PHILOSOPHY: CULTIVATING CRITICAL THINKING AND INCLUSIVE PERSPECTIVES
modules.moduleContent: This course is designed to introduce students to the fundamental concepts, arguments, and methods of philosophy, with a focus on fostering critical thinking and engaging with diverse perspectives. Through a variety of readings, podcasts, discussions, and assignments, students will explore major philosophical questions, including philosophy and the good life, what can we know, philosophy of religion, what it means to be human, philosophy of technology, philosophy in media and film, logic, and critical thinking. The course places a strong emphasis on developing the skills required for in-depth and open-minded philosophical inquiries, encouraging their application across various disciplines and practical contexts.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate the ability to think critically about philosophical arguments and concepts, and to evaluate them using logical and analytical reasoning.
Analyse critically the key ideas and debates within the major fields in philosophy and evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of different philosophical positions.
Engage with diverse perspectives and recognize the importance of inclusive language and perspectives in philosophy.
Critically examine philosophical arguments that exclude or marginalize certain groups of people.
Apply philosophical theories to real-world issues and their other subjects, and to
Demonstrate effective communication skills, both orally and in writing, in order to articulate their ideas clearly and persuasively using philosophical concepts and arguments.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI). Correct to only digital

modules.moduleContent: This module will examine the thought of some women philosophers from classical times to today, for example Diotima of Mantinea (c. 450 BC), Hildegard von Bingen (1098-1179), Heloise (1100?-1164), Anna Maria van Shurman (1607-1678), Elisabeth of Bohemia (1617-1680), Anne Conway (1631-1679), Damaris Cudworth Mashham (1658-1708), Émilie du Châtelet (1706-1749), Catharine Macaulay (1731-1791), Mary Wollstonecraft (1759-1797), Harriet Taylor Mill (1807-1858), Jane Addams (1860-1935), Hannah Arendt (1906-1975), Simone de Beauvoir (1908-1986), and Elisabeth Anscombe (1919-2001). Their philosophy will be examined in the light of that of their male contemporaries and the historical context, and an attempt will be made to assess their contribution as women and as philosophers to the development of philosophical thought. Reflection on women’s roles and the significance of these for cultural development will accompany the reading of the texts. Karen Warren (ed.): An Unconventional History of Western Philosophy: Conversations Between Men and Women Philosophers (Plymouth: Rowman and Littlefield Publishers, 2008) will be used as a textbook.
learningOutcomes: Account for the central positions of the philosophers studied and for the shape of their literary production.
Discuss the philosophical thought of each of the thinkers studied in relation to their male contemporaries and in relation to the historical context.
Reflect on and assess the contribution of women thinkers (and to those studied in particular) to the development of philosophical thought.
Discuss and assess the relevance of characterizing a particular philosopher as a woman philosopher.
Discuss and assess the reasons there might be for editing women thinkers out of the history of philosophy.

modules.moduleName: PHILOSOPHY OF RELIGION IN THE MODERN AGE
modules.moduleContent: This module examines Kant's critique of transcendental arguments in metaphysics in his Critique of Pure Reason (1781; 1787) as background to some important new lines of thinking which it provoked in philosophy of religion in the 19th-, 20th- and 21st- centuries, beginning with Hegel's concept of absolute spirit in his 1820s Lectures in the Philosophy of Religion; Kierkegaard's rejection of Hegel's account of divine immanence, in deference to a recognition of the reality of the absurd and a thinking about God as absolute other in the realm of individual religious faith beyond aesthetic and ethical spheres of human existence in Philosophical Crumbs (1884); Levinas’s critique of Heidegger's amoral-atheistic existential phenomenology of ‘Dasein’ and project of fundamental ontology in Being and Time (1927) and retrieval  of the rationality of transcendence in 'the face of the other' in ethical-religious experience in Totality and Infinity: An Essay on Exteriority (1961); Desmond's contemporary metaxological approach to the existence of God going beyond Hegel and Kant in a return to a philosophical faith in thinking about metaphysics and the createdness of all things out of nothing in God and the Between (2008).
learningOutcomes: distinguish and evaluate Kant’s logical, metaphysical, epistemological, and religious reasons against previous attempts in philosophy to prove the existence of God.

explain Hegel’s account of the dialectical unfolding of the concept of absolute spirit from religion in the form of nature, into religion in the form of art, into religion in the form of absolute manifest religion.

assess Kierkegaard’s concept of the absurd in relation to his philosophy of existence understood in the strong existentialist sense of concrete human individual existence, lived out in the three stages of human existence: the aesthetic, the ethical and the religious.

appraise central concepts in modern philosophy of religion, such as: alienation and reconciliation (Hegel); faith as the crucifixion of the understanding (Kierkegaard); the feeling of absolute dependence on the infinite (Schleiermacher); the trace of the infinite in the presence of the ‘other’ (Levinas); a metaxological approach to creation out of nothing by the Creator (Desmond).

recognise different perspectives in modern philosophy of religion through an examination of some of the main rejecters of Hegel’s account (e.g. by Kierkegaard, Schleiermacher, Levinas, Desmond) in the development of modern and contemporary philosophy of religion.

identify and articulate different concepts of theisms, atheisms, nihilisms, finitude and infinitude, and assess their relevance to the evaluation of a particular philosophy of religion after Kant in the modern age.

modules.moduleContent:
To provide an opportunity for a PhD student in the second or third year of research to collate, express and present an aspect of his or her work primarily to other researchers from the home department, or in another department within MU, as agreed with the doctoral supervisor. This activity will develop the student’s skills in refining and communicating their research to an audience of their peers, as well as assisting in peer-to-peer learning.
The student will prepare and deliver a substantial research paper (to last c. 30 minutes) or an equivalent presentation, at the Philosophical Seminar or some other agreed venue within MU. The student will also be expected to respond appropriately to questions from the audience, and will subsequently offer a report on the experience in a form agreed with the doctoral supervisor.
learningOutcomes: Develop proficiency in researching relevant literature within the field of philosophical inquiry related to a specific research topic.
Acquire skills in critically evaluating research methodologies, analysis, and findings within the context of a chosen research topic.
Demonstrate the ability to synthesize complex information and articulate key concepts effectively through an oral presentation.
Enhance proficiency in designing and delivering professional presentations tailored to diverse audiences within academia.
Gain experience in utilizing visual aids, such as slides or posters, to enhance the clarity and impact of research presentations.
Cultivate effective communication skills, including active listening, responding to questions, and engaging in constructive dialogue during Q&A sessions.
Develop strategies for organizing and structuring research presentations to maximize coherence, flow, and audience engagement.
To compare and contrast the different systems of politics and government to be found in Ireland and Europe;

To understand the role of key institutions in modern politics, how checks and balances between institutions emerge, and be able to conceptualize the functions of these institutions in different jurisdictions;

Be able to understand how institutions shape political interaction and evaluate how different political institutions foster different forms of democracy and levels of inclusion, equality and active citizenship;
To understand the different forms and contributions of civil society to Irish and European political systems;
To understand the relationship between institutions and society in a range of European countries;
To engage in research on Irish and European politics and government and produce a research paper dealing with one or more of the central themes of the course.

modules.moduleName: THE EUROPEAN UNION: HOW DOES IT WORK?
modules.moduleContent: How does the European Union work? This module examines the European Union and its institutions to interrogate how and why the EU works and why it matters. The course will explore the history of the EU and its key institutions, such as the Council of the EU, the European Council, European Commission, European Parliament and Court of Justice and explain how each of the EU institutions work, and how they contribute to politics and policy making. It will examine issues such as the rule of law and the relationship between the EU and its member states. It will also ask whether the EU might expand its membership to the Western Balkans and Ukraine and how the EU is changing as a result of the Ukraine War.
learningOutcomes: Be able to understand the role and powers of each of the main EU institutions, including the European Council, Council of the EU, the European Commission, the European Parliament and the Court of Justice of the EU.
Demonstrate an understanding of how the institutions combine to make policy within the EU.
Understand how to use EU documents, including the resources provided by the websites of the key EU institutions.
Understand the relationship between the EU and its member states.
Understand the nature and dynamics of EU Enlargement policy.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: CRITICAL STUDIES IN IRISH TRADITIONAL MUSIC
modules.moduleContent: This module focuses on recent musicological work on Irish traditional music, particularly concentrating on aspects of the contemporary tradition. Students will engage with a range of key traditional recordings and musicological texts, critically examining the music through topics such as style, gender, tourism, globalisation, and innovation.
learningOutcomes: describe and evaluate the development and transformation of Irish traditional music in the late 20th and 21st centuries.
analyse a range of Irish traditional music styles and contexts, considering issues such as repertoire, arrangement, performance practice, region, etc.
discuss and critique recent scholarship in contemporary Irish traditional music.
apply the concepts and ideas of recent scholarship to a self-selected case study.

modules.moduleContent: Students often think about composers as very distant figures who lived hundreds of years ago and whose lives - or even their music - has very little immediate relevance to their own. This course challenges that common belief by looking at the life and music of Schubert, and considering how the two relate. Schubert composed 600 of his 928 works by the age of 21, faced numerous personal challenges within his own short life, and yet went on to release his life's aim and world renowned, lasting fame for doing what he wanted to do most: to compose. This is a course which shows the importance of our friends in helping us to reach our goals. This is a course about having the courage to believe in ourselves, to be inspired by a historical figure from who we can still learn so much. It is a course about musical biography and the class will introduce students to a range of very memorable, thought-provoking works which will remain with students far beyond their college life.
learningOutcomes: Appraise how music is shaped by society and also shapes society and discuss that idea in relation to a variety of musical works

Be able to discuss works which are directly related to a composer’s biography and also problematize the vexed question in the biography as to how – or whether – pathways between life and art can be mapped

Understand how musical works raise broad existential questions that are compelling for example: what is the human condition? What is the nature of the world in which we find ourselves? And what does music tell us about our place in the world

Be able to discuss the life and works of Franz Schubert who made a mark on music history when he was the same age as the students: to understand the role of friendship in the evolution of his musical style and to be inspired by his transcendence of incredible life challenges to realize his life’s aim
Discuss issues of identity and their fluctuations in Schubert and a composer of their choice.

modules.moduleContent: MA students are required to undertake a thesis/portfolio research, starting at the beginning of the second semester. Students undertake research leading to a thesis or portfolio in one of the following three options: (a) Thesis/dissertation (12,000 to 16,000 words) on a chosen topic of study within Music Technology; (b) Project report (10,000 and 14,000 words) plus a beta-version of the software for a computer music software research/development project; (c) Portfolio of electroacoustic music composition or compositions amounting to between 15 and 20 minutes.
learningOutcomes: plan a piece of substantial original research.
develop research ideas over the medium term.
locate their work within the body of current research on the chosen area.
create a composition portfolio/dissertation/project report.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module's aim is to explore how performance itself grants access to music history and musicology. In particular, it will show and investigate how performers create and give voice to music history through their own approaches to playing and interpreting.
learningOutcomes: Evaluate the debate about Historically Informed Performance
Assess the role of performers in interpreting music history
Critically engage with the issues surrounding performance in the age of recording
Present an informed viewpoint on the relationship between analysis and performance.

modules.moduleName: IRISH TRADITIONAL MUSIC: THEORY, PRACTICE AND CREATIVITY
modules.moduleContent: This seminar series examines key theoretical and practical issues in Irish traditional music, and how they might inform each other. Drawing on new theoretical work on topics such as pitch/scale, tuning, rhythm and metre, the module encourages a fresh consideration of the fabric of traditional music.
learningOutcomes: critique and assess work on the theory and practice of Irish traditional music.
discuss how theoretical issues might inform practice.
apply theoretical concepts and models to a range of traditional music.
deliver a presentation on an original case study utilising course materials

modules.moduleContent:
**Commencing September 2025**

This module provides the student with the tools to analyse twentieth- and twenty-first century scores in terms of structure and detail of content, using listening and score study to assess the works featured. The scores studied provide the basis for two short composition assignments based on the compositional ideas discussed in seminars. There is a final composition assignment based on a score not studied in the seminars which includes a short analytical commentary. The score for this is chosen for this in consultation with the module coordinator.
learningOutcomes: appraise contrasting 20th and 21st century works;
critique characteristic features of 20th and 21st century musical language;
identify compositional practices in 20th and 21st century music;
delineate formal strategies in 20th and 21st century music.

modules.moduleContent: This module will focus on computer programming in general. It will first introduce the  different operating systems and programming environments used in the lab. The module will provide a thorough study of the C programming language and its potential uses in music application development.

modules.moduleContent: The module is designed to allow students the experience of editing a research-related publication in the area of Music. Types of publications can include for instance, a Music Department Research magazine, an outside post-graduate level research journal, as well as some more specific music editing projects.

modules.moduleName: CASE STUDIES IN DATA SCIENCE & ANALYTICS
modules.moduleContent: The techniques to be covered in this module are: methods for spatial prediction, network analysis, spatial interaction, spatial patterns, models for raster data, regionalisation algorithims, and simulation techniques.
learningOutcomes:
implement data analytics techniques on data from
a broad range of fields,

modules.moduleContent: Following five lectures on research methods and techniques, candidates will be required to submit a proposed dissertation title for approval by the Degree Programme Director using the Proposal Form which is made available by the Module Leader. This will be submitted by mid-April. Candidates will be required to demonstrate familiarity with GIS software and methods in completing the dissertation.

modules.moduleContent: Déanfar teoiric ghinearálta na foghraíochta a mhíniú agus breathnófar go grinn ansin ar chóras foghraíochta na Gaeilge. Déanfar mionanailís theicniúil ar fhoghair na Gaeilge, go háirithe ar chonsain na Gaeilge. Leagfar béim ar fhoghair na Gaeilge a mbíonn deacrachtaí ag cainteoirí Béarla leo agus déanfar cleachtaí a dhíreoidh ar na deacrachtaí seo.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a dhéanamh bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas teoiric ghinearálta na foghraíochta a thuiscint.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a dhéanamh bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas cur síos beacht a dhéanamh ar fhoghair na Gaeilge.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a dhéanamh bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas foghair na Gaeilge a aithint agus a fhuaimniú.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a dhéanamh bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas feabhas a chur ar fhuaimniú a chuid Gaeilge.

modules.moduleContent: Pléifear gnéithe éagsúla de thraidisiún amhránaíochta na Gaeilge sa mhodúl seo ón stair a bhaineann leis suas go dtí an ról atá aige sa lá atá inniu ann. Forbrófar tuiscint na mac léinn ar thraidisiún beo béil na hamhránaíochta chomh maith le forléargas a thabhairt ar chuid de na téamaí is tábhachtaí i dtraidisiún na n-amhrán. Beidh rogha ag an mac léinn gné phraiticiúil amhránaíochta a chur i gcrích mar chuid de mheasúnú an mhodúil seo le cead roimré ón léachtóir.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar ghnéithe den stair a bhaineann le traidisiún amhránaíochta na Gaeilge.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar chuid de théamaí agus seánraí na n-amhrán.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar thraidisiún beo béil na hamhránaíochta Gaeilge.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann anailís a dhéanamh ar thraidisiún amhránaíochta na Gaeilge.

modules.moduleContent: Léachtaí ar ghnéithe den teanga labhartha agus scríofa mar aon le cleachtaí rialta sna réimsí sin.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a dhéanamh bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas séimhiú agus urú a chur i bhfeidhm i gceart ar an ainmfhocal.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a dhéanamh bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas séimhiú agus urú a chur i bhfeidhm i gceart ar an aidiacht.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a dhéanamh bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas séimhiú agus urú a chur i bhfeidhm i gceart ar an mbriathar.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a dhéanamh bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas réamhfhocail a úsáid i gceart.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a dhéanamh bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas 'h' roimh ghuta, t(-) roimh ghuta agus roimh 's' a chur i bhfeidhm i gceart.

modules.moduleContent:
Léachtaí ar ghnéithe den teanga labhartha agus scríofa mar aon le cleachtaí rialta sna réimsí sin.
Ceardlanna teanga tasc-bhunaithe ina ndírítear ar scileanna éagsúla teanga agus cumarsáide a bhaineann leis an téarma ‘Cúrsaí reatha, fadhbanna sóisialta agus na meáin chumarsáide’.
Ranganna teagaisc ina ndéantar cleachtaí aistriúcháin agus eagarthóireachta a bhaineann le hábhar na léachtaí agus na gceardlann teanga. 

Tá an modúl seo ag teacht le leibhéal B2.2 – C1 ar an bhFráma Tagartha Comónta Eorpach (FTCE) ach ní hionann pas sa mhodúl agus a rá go bhfuil leibhéal B2.2 bainte amach. 
Léiríonn taighde na Roinne gur féidir na gráid a bhronntar i scrúduithe scríofa agus cainte an mhodúil teanga tríú bliana, NG311, a thagarmharcáil do leibhéil an FTCE mar seo a leanas: 60%+ = B2.2+; 50-59% = B2; < 50 % = < B2
learningOutcomes: Feasacht chultúrtha: Beidh taithí ag an mac léinn ar na foinsí nuachta agus ar na meáin a bhaineann leis an nGaeilge.

Smaointeoireacht chriticiúil: Beidh machnamh déanta ag an mac léinn ar chineál agus ar chaighdeán na hiriseoireachta a bhaineann le foinsí éagsúla nuachta.  Beidh sé/sí in ann scéalta nuachta a mheas go criticiúil.

Léamhthuiscint & Cluastuiscint: Beidh an mac léinn ábalta ailt agus míreanna raidió agus teilifíse a bhaineann le cúrsaí reatha agus fadhbanna sóisialta a thuiscint i nGaeilge, agus iad a mheas go criticiúil.

Labhairt: Beidh an mac léinn ábalta labhairt ina (h)aonar (m.sh. cur i láthair a dhéanamh nó podchraoladh a thaifeadadh) agus páirt a ghlacadh i gcomhráite faoi thopaicí a bhaineann le cúrsaí reatha agus fadhbanna sóisialta. Beidh sé/sí in ann plé a dhéanamh ar an gcúis atá le fadhb, ar an tionchar a bhíonn aici agus réitigh éagsúla a mholadh agus a mheá. Beidh sé/sí in ann tuairimí a thabhairt, a mhíniú agus a chosaint.

Scríobh: Beidh an mac léinn ábalta alt nuachta a scríobh, bunaithe ar shuirbhé, nó ar alt eile atá léite aige/aici, agus sleachta a bhaineann le cúrsaí reatha agus fadhbanna sóisialta a aistriú go cruinn.
Stór focal: Beidh an mac léinn ábalta an stór focal a bhaineann le cúrsaí reatha agus fadhbanna sóisialta (atá leagtha amach i lámhleabhar an chúrsa) a thuiscint agus a úsáid mar is cuí.

Gramadach: Beidh an mac léinn ábalta na struchtúir ghramadaí seo a leanas a láimhseáil: struchtúir a úsáidtear go minic i gcúrsaí nuachta (m.sh. an briathar saor, an aidiacht bhriathartha, an modh coinníollach, an clásal coibhneasta, frásaí ama, srl.) agus na pointí gramadaí a chlúdófar i léachtaí NG311 (an chopail, an t-ainm briathartha, dobhriathra, an t-ainm teibí).

modules.moduleContent: Is ag plé le próslitríocht chomhaimseartha na Gaeilge a bheifear sa mhodúl seo agus leis an ghearrscéalaíocht go háirithe. Cé go dtabharfar léargas ar staid na próslitríochta comhaimseartha trí chéile, beifear ag díriú ar cheisteanna agus ar théamaí ar leith, mar shampla, insint agus stíl, ficsean liteartha agus ficsean éadrom, cúrsaí inscne agus gnéaschlaonta, i measc nithe eile.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar ghnéithe de phróslitríocht chomhaimseartha na Gaeilge.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint aige/aici ar shaothar próis triúr scríbhneoirí comhaimseartha.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann téamaí de chuid phróslitríocht chomhaimseartha na Gaeilge a phlé.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann anailís liteartha a scríobh ar shaothar comhaimseartha próslitríochta.

modules.moduleContent:
Beidh deis ag mic léinn tuiscint a fháil ar na réimsí taighde is mó a shaothraítear sa Nua-Ghaeilge, ina measc sin, léann na litríochta agus na teangeolaíochta, an traidisiún béil agus traidisiún na hamhránaíochta, léann na lámhscríbhinní, an stair chultúrtha agus staidéar ar na meáin. I gcomhar le comhordaitheoir an mhodúil, beidh faill ag mic léinn imlíne thaighde dá gcuid féin a fhorbairt de réir a spéise féin.
Mar chuid den phlé ar réimsí taighde na Nua-Ghaeilge, tabharfar léargas do mhic léinn ar na háiseanna a bhíonn in úsáid ag taighdeoirí agus ar na ceisteanna taighde a phléitear. Leagfar béim fosta ar na scileanna criticiúla agus anailíse a bhíonn de dhíth chun fianaise chuí a bhailiú agus a mheas. Tabharfar aird ar leith ar na réimsí taighde is spéis leis na mic léinn ionas go mbeidh faill acu ábhar taighde dá gcuid féin a aimsiú.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint acu ar théamaí taighde sa Nua-Ghaeilge agus ar réimsí spéise éagsúla i léann na Gaeilge.

I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann cuntas a thabhairt ar na cineálacha éagsúla taighde a dhéantar sa Nua-Ghaeilge.
.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann creatlach a chruthú do thionscnamh taighde fochéime ar nós móraiste.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint acu ar mhodhanna taighde.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh tuiscint acu ar áiseanna taighde sa Nua-Ghaeilge.

modules.moduleContent: Breathnófar ar cheird an aistritheora. Cuirfear béim ar an aistriúchán ó Bhéarla go Gaeilge go háirithe ar an aistriúchán teicniúil. Scrúdófar modhanna an aistritheora Gaeilge agus na háiseanna agus na hacmhainní atá ar fáil dó/di.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas cur síos a dhéanamh ar na cáilíochtaí atá riachtanach do ghairm an aistritheora.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas modhanna an aistritheora Gaeilge a aithint.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas na háiseanna agus na hacmhainní atá ar fáil dó a úsáid i gceart.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas tabhairt faoi aistriúchán ceart Gaeilge-Béarla.

modules.moduleContent: Féachfar le scileanna teanga an mhic léinn a fhorbairt ag leibhéal iarchéime. Leagfar béim, dá réir sin, ar ghnéithe casta faoi leith de ghramadach na Gaeilge. Pléifear na háiseanna cuí leictreonacha a chuideos leis an mac léinn i réimse na teanga. Iarrfar ar an mac léinn cleachtaí cuí agus aistriúcháin ar ábhair éagsúla a réiteach — is orthu seo a bhunófar marc an mheasúnaithe leanúnaigh.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas pointí casta gramadaí a thuiscint agus a láimhseáil go sásúil ina chuid oibre féin.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas leas cóir a bhaint as áiseanna cuí leictreonacha na teanga.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas tabhairt faoi shliocht casta Béarla a aistriú go sásúil go Gaeilge.

modules.moduleContent: Taighde ealaíonta trí mheán na Gaeilge in aon ghné nó réimse d’ealaíona traidisiúnta nó comhaimseartha na Gaeilge agus na Gaeltachta, mar shampla an ceol, an amhránaíocht, an damhsa, an traidisiún béil, an amharclannaíocht, an scannánaíocht, an scríbhneoireacht chruthaitheach, nó aon ábhar cruthaitheach oiriúnach eile. Bíonn ionad lárnach ag an gcleachtas cruthaitheach sa taighde ealaíonta agus mar sin bíonn an ghné phraiticiúil go mór chun cinn. Déanfar an togra a dhearadh de réir riachtanaisí an taibhléirithe nó na n-ealaíon cruthaitheach i gcomhpháirt leis an bhfoireann stiúrthóireachta. De ghnáth bíonn cuid scríofa agus cuid phraiticiúil i gceist, ach tá an-solúbthacht ann maidir le dearadh an togra.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann togra sa taighde ealaíonta a dhearadh agus a chur i gcrích.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann foinsí cuimsitheacha a chnuasach.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann córas tagairtí a chur i bhfeidhm go héifeachtach.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú, bheifí ag súil go mbeadh sé/sí in ann ardscileanna cruthaitheacha ina réimse speisaltóireachta a léiriú.
modules.notes: Ag brath ar an ábhar taighde a roghnaíonn an mac léinn

learningOutcomes: have a thorough understanding of the nature of data and different types of data
be familiar with key philosophical debates concerning how to make sense of data
have a detailed knowledge of how big and open data are being used in context
be able to apply a critically reflexive analysis to datasets
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module provides students with sketching and sketch modelling skills to unlock their creativity and express their ideas to others. Through demonstration and project-based learning the students will develop their visual thinking, sketching, and modelling competence and familiarise themselves with the design process and with materials & equipment. Students will learn to work with different sketch mediums and levels of drawing resolution and will learn to make sketch prototypes for concept communication and development. Primary topics covered are design habits; sketching through different mediums; perspective sketching; line quality; adding colour and highlights; composition and annotation of sketch visuals; development sketching & sketching for final presentation; marker rendering: card modelling and foam modelling - including an introduction to making a foam model in the workshop.
learningOutcomes: Deliver effective sketches to develop design ideas & details
Deliver presentation quality sketches to communicate design ideas
Deliver effective sketch models to develop and communicate concepts & solutions
Demonstrate an understanding of effective sketching & sketch modelling as part of their creative process and design practice

modules.moduleContent: This module teaches students a range of modelling tools and techniques required within designerly practice. Physical modelling involves hands on prototyping in a workshop setting. Creating 3D forms from card and foam requires spatial awareness, hand eye coordination, understanding of simple tools and templates. Virtual modelling requires an understanding a Computer Aided Design (Solidworks), an ability to read and generate technical drawings, and fundamentals of orthographic projection. Modelmaking processes allow designers to explain and justify their design decisions throughout a project.
learningOutcomes: Correctly interpret technical drawings
Create and use technical drawings as a formal communication tool
Select appropriate standards and conventions to communicate through technical drawing
Integrate Solidworks into the design process
Generate 3D designs in Solidworks
Communicate designs to a third party through solidworks
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module will introduce the fundamentals of Physics and Mathematics that prepare you for understanding and calculating the forces on materials in the manufacturing and use of products, and also quantitative survey analysis. It aims to build mathematical confidence applied to calculating probability, statistics, as well as volumes, converting units and other calculations that Product Designers will come across in both physical and digital domains. Problem Based Learning activities will develop the competencies that are necessary to solve practical design problems.

Syllabus elements are:
Sequences and Series
Limits and continuity
Elementary calculus:
* Sum, product and chain rules for derivatives
* Critical points, maxima and minima
* Integrals as areas
Solving simple linear systems of equations
Probability
Statistics and quantitative survey analysis
Mechanics: velocity and acceleration, motion with constant acceleration
Heat and energy
learningOutcomes: Identify arithmetic and geometric sequences and evaluate their series
Differentiate commonly occurring functions
Perform simple integrals and express areas in terms of integrals
Determine the motion of objects undergoing constant acceleration
Design and analyse results from quantitative surveys
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module covers the complete design process from brief to product launch. Through project based learning the students will ascertain project management and design process competence. Primary topics covered are; Brief interpretation, research, conceptualisation, development & final presentation.
learningOutcomes: Interpret and develop a comprehensive design brief
Recommend and undertake appropriate research
Generate innovative and/or novel & realisable concepts
Communicate ideas clearly and in detail to a third party
Plan for product realisation
Work as part of a team
Successfully meet a design brief

modules.moduleContent: The objective of this module is to teach students Systematic Tools and Techniques for Product Design. This includes understanding methodologies for identifying customer needs and developing specifications for those needs. Techniques for Concept Generation, Selection and Testing are explored in detail. Engagement with Value Engineering techniques open the possibilities in alternative design solutions while Failure Mode and Effect Analysis provides a process to structure product design improvements. Financial Analysis are completed for various design scenarios.
learningOutcomes: Apply and learn different value engineering and analysis techniques including QFD (Quality Function Deployment), Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA), Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), TRIZ (theory of solving inventive problems), Risk Analysis Charts, Decision Trees, Product Design Specification (PDS), Total Quality Management (TQM), Product Life Cycle (PLC) and Product Design Process (PDP).
Recognise the need for establishing value to the customer and value within the Product Development Process to create and drive efficiency.
Perform a risk and cost analysis for a product using various methodologies of cost evaluation and product value analysis to solve design / value problems.
Apply tools and techniques for driving quality and cost improvement.
Describe quality requirements and understand regulatory and statutory obligations which must be complied with whilst also looking at other issues relating to hazard identification and risk analysis.
Be able to prepare Technical files and Design dossiers in accordance with relevant regulatory and quality requirements.

modules.moduleContent: The dissertation gives students an opportunity to carry out independent and original research on a topic of their choice.  The final topic is agreed with a dissertation supervisor who acts in a support role from initiation to completion.    Students then independently apply the module learnings from problem scoping to research question, designing and conducting first-hand research, solution implementation and validation. Projects can be independent or in collaboration with an organisation.  The project is captured in a written dissertation and presented to the class (and senior management representatives if working with an industry partner) where students can discuss and defend their work.
learningOutcomes: Strategically analyse an organisation’s/user’s needs.
Carry out rigorous and focused field research and develop innovation proposals that are rigorously defensible and appropriate to these needs.
Develop and implement appropriate and comprehensive validation testing and refinement processes for developed solutions.
Prepare clear implementation proposals and defend these in a presentation forum.

modules.moduleContent:
This interdisciplinary module is designed to navigate the complex landscape of implementing robotics and AI, focusing on collaborative, challenge-based learning. Students will use Design Thinking techniques to critically analyse and tackle the practical challenges involved in deploying robotic and embedded AI systems. This module emphasises a practical, team-based approach and a technical, problem-solving focus, with a strong emphasis on ethics, sustainability, and real-world impact.
Upon completing this module, students will be able to:
• Critically evaluate the complexities and challenges associated with implementing robotics and AI solutions in diverse environments and contexts.
• Apply design thinking to navigate and overcome obstacles in the deployment of technology-driven innovations.
• Consider and address the ethical, sustainability, and practical challenges in the implementation of robotics and embedded AI systems.
• Effectively communicate the intricacies, solutions, and outcomes of projects through multiple forms of expression, including written, verbal, and multimedia.
• Collaborate efficiently in interdisciplinary teams, utilising and integrating the varied expertise and viewpoints of team members to address the multifaceted challenges of AI and robotics implementation.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO ETHICS: EXPLORING VALUE, JUSTICE, AND OUR RELATIONSHIP TO THE NON-HUMAN WORLD
modules.moduleContent:
This module provides an introduction to ethics. Students will explore foundational ethical theories like consequentialism, deontology, virtue ethics, and care ethics and extend these theories to discuss their impact on arguments for inclusion and exclusion, our conceptions of the good life, justice, bioethical and environmental concerns, including the ethical dimensions of human actions on the non-human world and human relationships with non-human animals. Another central theme will be authenticity and personal ethics. Students are encouraged to question established ethical systems and to reflect on the role of individual autonomy in determining the value of life, what it means to live authentically, implications for interpersonal relationships, aging, and the dynamic landscapes shaped by technological advancements and social media.
Overall, this module aims to help students develop the critical thinking, analytical, and communication skills necessary for engaging in ethical inquiry and decision-making through a variety of readings, podcasts, discussions, and assignments. By the end of the module, students will have a deeper understanding of key ethical concepts, as well as a greater appreciation for the importance of inclusion and social justice considerations, as well as environmental concerns, in ethical decision-making.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a critical understanding of key ethical concepts and theories, including consequentialism, deontology, virtue ethics, and care ethics.
Apply ethical theories to real-world ethical dilemmas.

Evaluate ethical issues, especially related to inclusion, diversity, and social justice, including discrimination, inequality, and oppression, and develop strategies for addressing these issues from an ethical perspective.

Analyze ethical issues related to non-human animals and the environment, including climate change, pollution, and resource depletion, and evaluate different ethical frameworks for thinking about the relationship between humans and nature.
Critically reflect on their own ethical values and beliefs, and understand how these values and beliefs shape their ethical reasoning and decision-making.

Demonstrate communication and collaboration skills necessary for engaging in respectful and constructive dialogue with those who hold different ethical viewpoints, and understand the importance of ethical considerations in personal and professional contexts.

modules.moduleContent: This module begins with the thought of Augustine (d. 430) and concentrates on the most prominent representatives of the period up to c. 1350: Bonaventure, Aquinas, Henry of Ghent, Scotus and Ockham. We will also examine the golden age of Oxford thought in the 1330s: Richard FitzRalph, Adam Wodeham and Robert Holcot. Some mention will also be made of Irish thinkers of the Middle Ages.
learningOutcomes: Define ‘medieval’, ‘Middle Ages’ and ‘medieval philosophy’ in a way which serves to underline the importance of considering the Western intellectual tradition from Antiquity to the 14th century.
Identify key thinkers from the medieval period in Western thought and analyse their contribution to philosophy.
identify and assess key trends and key movements in medieval philosophy;
discuss the complex issue of the sources for medieval thought: Neoplatonism, Aristotelianism, medieval Islamic and Jewish thought.

modules.moduleContent: This module examines different methods and practices in Renaissance Philosophy. It begins with a discussion of the methods and practices found in the late medieval philosophical schools in the 14th century as the immediate background of Renaissance Philosophy and then addresses some theories, methods, and practices of prominent thinkers between the mid-fourteenth century and the beginning of the seventeenth century, mainly in Italy (Francesco Petrarca, Coluccio Salutati, Leonardo Bruni, Lorenzo Valla, Giovanni Dominici, Georgios Gemistos Plethon, Antoninus of Florence, Marsilio Ficino, Giovanni Pico della Mirandola, Giorgio Benigno Salviati, Bernardo Torni, Desiderius Erasmus, Niccolò Machiavelli, Francesco Patrizi.) It also deals with the significance of the relations between philosophy and theology, pagan antiquity and Christian teaching, man and God, various themes in moral psychology and political philosophy, as well as in the philosophy of language, science, and in metaphysics, and specific terms like Aristotelianism and Platonism during this period.
learningOutcomes: Identify what a philosophical canon is and its limitations in relation to the place of Renaissance Philosophy in the contemporary philosophical canon.

Distinguish between Renaissance Philosophy and medieval philosophy on the one hand, and early modern philosophy on the other, with particular reference to the contribution of Renaissance Philosophy to the development of the concepts of modernity, individuality, and secularization.

Elaborate on the issue of reception in the history of Western philosophy as a philosophical and scholarly problem and the methodological problems involve in the reception of classical philosophy in the Renaissance and the philosophical significance of the reception of the Fathers of the Church.

Address questions such as can we speak of the reception of the medieval Doctors in the early modern era? is the concept ‘scholasticism’ a historical one? do we have only one scholastic philosophy?, appreciateing the philosophical achievements of the late scholastic schools of thought: Thomists, Scotists, Augustinians, Mertonists.

Distinguish between the via antiqua and the via moderna, realists and nominalists, while dealing with the questions: what is the scholastic concept of language and style? is it only one concept? how logic is related to theology? what is the scholastic conception of science? what is the philosophical foundation for the critique of humanist thinkers by scholastic philosophers? is it really a war?

Appreciate the philosophical achievements of the humanist thinkers and their critique of the scholastic thinkers, focusing on the notions of sources, origin, truth, language, interpretation, translation, eloquence and philosophy.

Discuss the new image of Aristotle in the Renaissance leading to a better philosophical understanding of Aristotelian philosophy, the significance of the return to Plato, Platonism, and Neoplatonic thinkers, and how this legacy is used by humanist thinkers.

modules.moduleName: RATIONALISTS AND EMPIRICISTS: TOWARDS MODERN PHILOSOPHY
modules.moduleContent: This module aims to present, discuss, and criticize some of the main philosophical achievements of five canonical figures in early modern philosophy: René Descartes (1596–1650), Thomas Hobbes (1588–1679), Baruch Spinoza (1632–1677), George Berkeley (1685–1753), and David Hume (1711–1776), in the context of the new philosophical and scientific discourse of the 17th and 18th centuries. We shall examine their contributions to the formation of different aspects of modernity, on the one hand, and their critique of the ‘men of the schools’ (i.e., contemporary scholastic philosophers and their ‘traditional methods’), on the other hand.
learningOutcomes: point out and describe the distinctive philosophical achievements of Descartes, Hobbes and Spinoza, Berkeley and Hume;

identify the relations between these five thinkers, with particular reference to central concepts of common philosophical concern, such as substance, knowledge and nature, abstraction and general ideas;
discuss and evaluate some of the central tenets promoted by these five philosophers and their contributions to the emergence of modern philosophical thought;
discriminate between agreement with and deviance from conceptions of science in the transition from late medieval and Renaissance thought to the modern period;

compare various methods of ‘old’ and ‘new’ metaphysics that are deployed in the development of early modern thought and are of relevance to the articulation of the philosophies of Descartes, Hobbes and Spinoza, Berkeley and Hume.

modules.moduleContent: Democracy is often exclusively associated with elections, political parties and parliaments and associated bodies – that is the formal or institutional aspects of democracy. However, democracy can also be viewed from a substantive perspective, that is how the citizen experiences the democracy in which she or he lives. In this case democracy can be examined and evaluated from the perspective of the content and experience of citizenship. This course aims to examine democracy from this perspective – to interrogate the concepts of democracy and citizenship and the relationship between the two; to review contending conceptions of citizenship in a democratic context; and, to problematise these and how and if they can be realised in a globalised, multicultural and unequal world. The course also aims to be as participative, inter-active and student-led as possible, that is to allow students to help construct content and not just be passive recipients of that content. As a result many of the assignments have been designed to facilitate this as much as possible. In this way, not only does the course present different models of citizenship – but also seeks to bring these alive within the classroom context.

This module interrogates the nature of democracy in the 2020s and examines the different threats to democracy evident In the contemporary world. Focusing mainly on Europe and the United States, the module will examine the various crises of politics that have engulfed democratic states over the last two decades as well as the significant revival of authoritarianism. Beginning with the global financial crisis in 2008, the module will trace the rise of populist and radical politics and political parties and the impact this has had on the United States and European Union. It will use the empirical evidence gathered by international bodies such as Freedom House and Varieties of Democracy (V-Dem) to trace the jurisdictional decline in democracy and ask whether democracy can survive the extraordinary challenges posed by disinformation and the rise of Artificial Intelligence.
learningOutcomes: Understand the theoretical understandings of democracy and pluralism as well as authoritarianism and totalitarianism;
Have a good understanding of the threat landscape faced by democracies, especially in North America and Europe;

Know how to read and use the rich data sets on the state of democracy produced by leading bodies/projects such as Freedom House, Varieties of Democracy (V-Dem), Global Democracy Index, the Corruption Perceptions Index and Reporters without Borders;
Understand how misinformation, disinformation and Artificial Intelligence are impacting on democratic polities, including elections and institutions.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
To equip students with theoretical frameworks and practical case studies to explore the political processes,  social practices and lived experience of creating and governing social transformation, a triple transition of climate sustainability, equality, and digitalisation. 
 
In four sections of this module students will examine societal attitudes towards social transformation in our contemporary world. The module will first introduce key concepts including theories of change, power,  democracy, the sustainable state, and transformation. The module then turns to the process of creating demand for social transformation, a process that focuses on the importance of social power ‘from below’ including intersectional approaches to power and transformation. The module then explores challenges of decision making and implementation, and interrogates supply side politics and challenges of legitimacy for elite power ‘from above’. The module ends by re-examining theories of change and social transformation asking how we can imagine balancing strong sustainable states with strong democratic societies.
learningOutcomes: 1. Engage with debates on theories of change and transformation, power and society
2. Appreciate the role of the emerging sustainable state in decision making and implementation of policy relevant to triple transition
3. Evaluate the role of democracy  and whether or how it may transform to enhance inclusive forms of societal power
4. Understand and debate about potential multilevel governance scenarios and their impacts on democracy and society.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO PSYCHOLOGY I: RESEARCH FOUNDATIONS OF PSYCHOLOGY, BIOLOGICAL AND DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLOGY
modules.moduleContent:
Introduction of psychology as a scientific discipline. Introduction to fundamental principles of research in psychology. Basic neuroanatomy and neurophysiology, with special reference to psychological function. Introduction to branches of biological psychology, including psychopharmacology and psychophysiology. Genetic and environmental influences on psychological development. Major theories of cognitive and intellectual development. 

Module Objective: To introduce the research basis of contemporary psychology and introduce core topics and theories in the areas of biological and developmental psychology.
learningOutcomes: Describe the fundamental features of psychology as a scientific discipline;
Outline core features of research approaches in psychology;
Outline the main facets of how the brain, and its constituent components, underpin human behaviour;
Describe the core principles/areas of developmental change in the period from conception through childhood;
Discuss issues that comprise social, emotional or cognitive development throughout childhood;
Construct an appraisal, informed by the psychological literature, of a current topic in biological or developmental psychology.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To consider concepts of health and illness and to introduce key models and theories in health psychology. 
Module Content: Perspectives on health and illness. Models of health behaviour and approaches to health behaviour change. Adjustment to long-term illness.
learningOutcomes: Compare and contrast biomedical and biopsychosocial models of health;
Describe major determinants of health-related behaviour;
Discuss psychological aspects of health and illness;
Outline how psychological interventions can be deployed to improve health outcomes.

modules.moduleContent: Conceptualizations of mental health; the concept of 'abnormality' in psychology; key theoretical paradigms in understanding mental health; the social context of mental health and illness; the classification of psychological disorders including psychosis, mood disorders, anxiety disorders and personality disorders; current evidence regarding aetiology, prevention and treatment of common disorders; the lived experience of mental illness. Module Objectives:  To introduce students to the key conceptual approaches to understanding mental health and current evidence regarding the aetiology, diagnosis and treatment of psychological disorders.
learningOutcomes: Define and critique the concept of ‘abnormality’ in psychology;
Describe the characteristic features of the main types of psychological disorder;
Understand how psychological disorders are classified and assessed;
Critically appraise current evidence regarding the aetiology of mental illness;
Understand the broader and more complete conceptualization of mental health.

modules.moduleContent: Undertaking independent empirical research based on quantitative and/or qualitative methods; The quantitative and/or qualitative analysis of collected data; The presentation and critical analysis of those data in the context of a research report ; The presentation of those data and research findings in an oral presentation. Module Objective: To provide experience in conceptualising and conducting empirical research in psychology, and to establish the skills necessary to produce a report of that research presented according to current academic standards within psychology.
learningOutcomes: Organise the design, planning, and completion of a research study in a scientific and professional manner;
Rigorously apply professional ethical standards in designing, conducting and reporting psychological research;
Collect the primary data necessary to complete a research project;
Analyse data using the appropriate quantitative and/or qualitative methods;
Produce a written report of a research study that follows the conventions of the discipline.

modules.moduleName: CONTEMPORARY ISSUES AND DEBATES IN PSYCHOLOGY
modules.moduleContent: Psychology as a Science; Fundamental assumptions in psychology; Its definitions, subject matter and impacts; Psychology in the Tree of knowledge; Philosophy of science for psychology; Paradigms and approaches to psychological enquiry; Psychology & Politics; The distinction between science and pseudo-science, and its relevance for psychology; Contradictions, paradoxes, and conflicts in psychology; Critical approaches to psychology; Fundamental assumptions in psychology and its definitions, subject matter and impacts; The relationship between psychology and culture; What psychology can learn from other disciplines (social and natural sciences, and humanities); Philosophy of science for psychology; Paradigms and approaches to psychological enquiry; The relationship between psychology and culture; Insights from modern philosophy for psychology; What psychology can learn from other disciplines (social and natural sciences, and humanities); Public perceptions of and misconceptions about psychology.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish between science and pseudo-science in the context of psychology;
Demonstrate understanding of the role of evidence and reason in constructing arguments on topics relevant to psychology;
Discuss philosophical influences on contemporary psychology and its methodologies;
Critically assess contemporary controversies and debates in psychology;
Construct an appraisal, informed by the relevant literature, of a current topic involving a conceptual or methodological question in psychology.

modules.moduleContent: Contemporary classic studies in psychology. The replication crisis in psychology and what we have learned about what constitutes classical studies. Methodology and impact of seminal studies in psychology. Critical discussion of contemporary classic studies in psychology published since 2000.  Group presentation and discussion of critical analysis of student-selected papers.
learningOutcomes: Describe the history and theoretical bases of contemporary classic studies in psychology;
Critically evaluate the methodology and conclusions of influential papers in psychology;
Present and discuss results of key experiments in a number of domains of psychology.

modules.moduleContent:
This module examines the influence of the environment on human experiences, behaviour and well-being and explores the factors influencing environmental behaviour, and ways to encourage pro-environmental behaviour. The genesis of methodologies within the field historically and their prominence today is evaluated.

• History and the emergence of environmental psychology.
• Environmental influences on human behaviour and well-being
• Factors influencing environmental behaviour.
• Encouraging pro-environmental behaviour, citizen science and conservation action.
learningOutcomes: Understand the historical antecedents that led to the development of this field of research.
Critically evaluate the principal theories and concepts related to environmental psychology.
Evaluate strengths and weaknesses of the methodologies used of environmental psychology.
Apply key methodological approaches from environmental psychology in an ethical way.
Describe the roles of objectivity, rigour and systematic approaches to the conduct of experimental psychology;
Appreciate the links between experimental design and simple forms of inferential statistics;
Consider critically the link between psychological theory and experiment;
Critically evaluate published psychological research;
Reflect on the experience of the research participant across a range of psychological studies.

learningOutcomes: Describe the role of experimentation and observation in empirical psychology;
Appreciate the links between experimental design and qualitative statistical analysis methods;
Consider critically the link between psychological theory and experiment;
Produce comprehensive research reports on experiments in psychology;
Describe the roles of objectivity, rigour and systematic approaches to the conduct of experimental psychology;
Critically evaluate published psychological research;
Reflect on the experience of the research participant across a range of psychological studies.
