modules.moduleName: THEMES FROM THE PHILOSOPHY OF THOMAS AQUINAS
modules.moduleContent:
-          To present a historical and systematic account of Aquinas’s philosophical thought.

-          To show how the various themes in Aquinas’s metaphysics, epistemology, and ethics fit together to give him a view of reality as a whole.

-          To situate Aquinas’s philosophical thought within his theology.

-          To show the relevance of some of his philosophical teachings to central themes in Christian theology.

modules.moduleName: CELTIC CIVILIZATION 1 - POWER AND STATUS IN THE CELTIC WORLD
modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to the rich and fascinating world of Celtic culture and literature, illustrated by a wide-range of literary and historical sources. From stories of Cú Chulainn through to the reality of the social inequalities of the medieval world, this module will develop critical skills in reading and analysing historical sources while opening a window on pre-modern Ireland, Wales and other Celtic-speaking societies.
learningOutcomes: Identify the variety of archaeological, historical and linguistic sources for the study of the Celts.
Discuss the background to, and history of, the development of the concept of the 'Celts'.
Delineate the broad characteristics of Hallstatt and La Tene society and describe the principal archaeological and historical evidence.
Evaluate the processes that led to the adoption of Celtic culture and language in Ireland.
Appraise critically the relevant secondary and at least some of the primary literature.

modules.moduleContent: Taught through the medium of English, this module continues exploration of the Old Irish language of the so-called classical period of the 8th and 9th centuries. Aspects of Old Irish grammar and syntax are discussed in detail (esp. noun classes, remaining present stem classes of the verbs).
learningOutcomes: Understand moderately advanced aspects of Old Irish nominal and verbal morphology and syntax.
Analyse and identify nominal and verbal forms (present indicative).
Translate simple Old Irish sentences.

modules.moduleName: HISTORY 3 - THE ARCHAEOLOGY OF EARLY MEDIEVAL IRELAND
modules.moduleContent: Archaeological evidence for secular settlement and social systems, the development of the early Church and monastic sites, the emergence of late ‘Celtic’ artistic traditions and the wider European, including Anglo-Saxon, Carolingian and Viking, influences that contributed to this rich period of Irish society is introduced and examined in detail. The contribution of new archaeological discoveries are assessed in the wider context of interdisciplinary studies, including history and environmental research.
learningOutcomes: Identify and access the variety of archaeological and historical sources for the study of early medieval Ireland.
Detail chronologically, within a wider European context, the main developments in Ireland from the late Iron Age to the arrival of the Anglo-Normans.
Discuss the settlement evidence and the socio-economic systems that this represents.
Delineate the broad characteristics of Early Medieval art and describe the La Tène background.
Combine the archaeological and documentary evidence to provide a balanced understanding of the key features of this period.
Critically appraise the relevant secondary and at least some of the primary literature.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces the students to advanced aspects of Old Irish grammar, esp. the formation of non-present tenses and moods, and advanced aspects of syntax, esp. dependent clauses.

Students who want to take this module should have a good knowledge of the fundamentals of linguistics. Previous experience in the learning of languages is required. Students who want to take this module should contact the module coordinator first.
learningOutcomes: Analyse and identify non-present-stem verbal formations.
Understand the system of infixed and suffixed grammatical elements.
Demonstrate familiarity with the syntax of dependent clauses.
Translate simple Old Irish sentences.

modules.moduleName: SPACE, PLACE AND OTHERWORLDS IN MEDIEVAL IRISH LITERATURE
modules.moduleContent: This module will explore the large body of written sources surviving from medieval Ireland that centre on ideas about topography and travel. As inhabitants of an island at the edge of the known world, medieval Irish scholars were keenly interested in understanding the geographical relationship of their home to the wider ‘global’ sphere (understood as comprising Europe, Africa and Asia) and the place of their cultural inheritance within the broader frameworks of Biblical learning and Greco-Roman history. They produced a diverse topographical literature that includes origin-legends for population groups; poetry and prose constructing a history of placenames and landscapes; and entertaining narratives concerning the wanderings and adventures of heroes, kings and pilgrims. Alongside these works are tales that involve travel to a supernatural Otherworld or Christian visions of heaven and hell. Students will explore how the theme of real and imagined journeys, both physical and spiritual, could be used as a literary device to hold a critical mirror to human behaviour and to respond to various political, cultural, religious and economic changes in early medieval Ireland. They will also consider how ideas about voluntary and involuntary exile, migration and geography could – as they can still – shape a people and its culture. All texts will be read through English translation; no prior knowledge of the Irish language is required.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of key texts and the historical contexts that shaped the development and circulation of ideas in medieval Ireland.
Show familiarity with the relevant primary and secondary literature set for readings.
Understand a range of theoretical approaches for the study of medieval Irish literature.
Have experience in the close reading and critical analysis of literary, documentary and visual sources.
Write an essay on a chosen topic, making clear and persuasive arguments based on primary and secondary reading.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces the students to the basic concepts of palaeography: the development of scripts and writing styles, common abbreviations and notae, and codicology. The students will gain practical experience in reading medieval Irish manuscripts.

This course examines the central issue of how power is distributed between the state and society, how it is institutionalised and how it is exercised.  It examines competing definitions of power and then applies theories of power to contemporary Ireland. These include pluralist, Marxist, radical, corporatist,  totalitarian and feminist theories of power as well as  theories of citizenship, participative democracy and  social capital.  In exploring the interdependent relationship between the state and society it examines various arguments for the state and/or society to have a more minor or more central role in modern societies and economies.  A key theme is the relationship between power and wealth inequalities and who benefits from political decisions. We also examine how the state society relations have been impacted by globalization.
learningOutcomes: Detail and evaluate the major theories and key concepts used by social scientists to analyse the relationship between the society and state in the modern world.

Critically assess the contribution of social scientific analyses to an understanding of how the state has been characterised and the emergence of and now challenges to nation states as territorial and political units.

Evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by social scientists exploring issues such as: the implications of globalisation for nation states, the emergence of new social movements and identity formation processes that complicate the performance of the state''s key tasks of managing conflict and orchestrating cohesion.
Develop an independent and critical perspective on the social scientific analysis of the relationship between the Society and State in the modern world.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To explore fundamental issues involved in the assessment and measurement of individual differences, some of the main theories in the field, and key questions and debates in  both personality and intelligence/ability. 
Module Content: An introduction to psychometric testing including the principles of psychological test design, administration and interpretation. Some well-known theories of personality and intelligence/ability. Applications of individual differences theory and research. The measurement of human abilities and associated questions/debates. The role of genetic and environmental factors in personality and intelligence.
learningOutcomes: Understand and discuss some of the key aspects of psychometric testing including the design and administration of psychological tests and associated criteria;
Describe and critically evaluate some of the main theories of personality and intelligence/ability;
Explain some of the practical applications of individual differences research;
Critically assess the importance of the measurement of human abilities and associated questions in the field.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To consider different evolutionary and comparative viewpoints have contributed to psychology and the understanding of human behaviour. 
Module Content: Evolutionary theory and its applicability to the behavioural sciences. Proximate and Ultimate causes of behaviour. Comparative analysis of learning. Animal cognition. Evolution of the human brain. The evolution of social structures.
learningOutcomes: Appreciate the development of evolutionary theory and its applicability to the study of human behaviour;
Describe what is meant by proximate and ultimate causes of behaviour, and how these concepts can be used to understand human behaviour in an evolutionary context;
Describe the comparative study of cognition and behaviour;
Describe how evolutionary and comparative perspectives can be applied to the understanding of co-operative behaviour.

modules.moduleName: ETHICS AND HUMAN RIGHTS IN PSYCHOLOGICAL RESEARCH AND PRACTICE
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To develop strong understanding and appreciation of the central role of ethics and human rights in psychological research and practice. 
Module Content: Ethics, justice and rights. Professional codes of ethics for psychology. Core aspects of ethical conduct, including confidentiality, consent, dignity, competence and integrity. The role of ethics in psychological research. The role of ethics in psychological practice. Contributions of psychology to human rights
learningOutcomes: Describe the key theoretical and philosophical bases of ethics in psychology;
Demonstrate knowledge of the Codes of Professional Ethics of key professional bodies (PSI, BPS, APA);
Describe the application of core ethical principles to research in psychology;
Describe the application of core ethical principles to the professional practice of psychology;
Describe ways in which psychology can contribute to promoting social justice and human rights;
Demonstrate self-reflection of ethical issues as they pertain to undergraduate education in psychology.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To continue instruction in objective, systematic, empirical investigations in psychology and the presentation and evaluation of research data. 
Module Content: Undertaking supervised qualitative and quantitative experiments in psychology; Producing written research reports on data derived from each experiment; Appreciating the role of practical considerations (ethics, data management etc.) in the conduct of psychological experiments; Reflecting on the experience of the research participant through participation in research projects.
learningOutcomes: Describe the role of experimentation and observation in empirical psychology;
Appreciate the links between experimental design and qualitative statistical analysis methods;
Consider critically the link between psychological theory and experiment;
Produce comprehensive research reports on experiments in psychology;
Describe the roles of objectivity, rigour and systematic approaches to the conduct of experimental psychology;
Reflect on the experience of the research participant across a range of psychological studies.

modules.moduleContent: Module Objective: To help students identify and develop key skills for employment and practice in psychology or in other professions, including identification of personal career goals and paths towards achieving those goals. Module Content: Self appraisal and reflection; Transferable skills of psychology graduates; Career options for psychology graduates. Working as a team. Forming professional networks. Presentation skills;  Creating job applications and CVs
learningOutcomes: Appreciate the career options that are available to psychology graduates;
Identify personal goals and ambitions through self-assessment and reflection;
Articulate the range of transferable skills required for effective work in psychology and beyond;
Produce quality job applications and CVs, and display skills for effective interviewing.

modules.moduleContent: Experience in the conduct of psychological research as part of ongoing projects; Working as a team member in psychological research; Development of professional and inter-personal skills in the research setting; Self-reflection on personal development and maintaining a professional development portfolio. Module Objective:: To provide students with exposure to the conduct of psychological research as part of ongoing projects, in order to illustrate the link between research, knowledge and practice in psychology.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the role and conduct of research in developing new psychological knowledge;
Appreciate the connection between research in psychology and the ultimate application of that research;
Reflect on the roles of individual and team work in research in psychology;
Demonstrate self-reflection in domains of professional and inter-personal skills required for effective work in psychological research;
Produce both written and oral summations of the experience of research in psychology.

modules.moduleContent: The study of topics in psychology in partner institutions abroad; Exposure to differing cultures, and reflection on cultural influences in psychology and education; Self-reflection on the learning experience abroad. Module Objective: To provide students with the opportunity to study topics in psychology abroad.
learningOutcomes: Successful demonstrate learning to the required standard in the topics in psychology studied abroad.
Show evidence of reflection on the role of culture and context in the teaching of psychology.
Demonstrate self-reflection on the experience of living and studying in another country.

modules.moduleContent: 1. Criminal Behaviour in the population: Facts and figures; 2. Perpetrators and victims of crime; 3. Evolutionary theories of crime; 4. Biological factors and crime; 5. Developmental pathways to crime I; 6. Developmental pathways to crime II; 7. Criminal Thinking Styles; 8. Criminal Social Identity; 9. Psychopathy I; 10. Psychopathy II; 11. Psychopathy III; 12. Case Study of Richard Kuklinski; 13. Juvenile Offending; 14. Serial Killers; 15. Psychology of Homicidal Offending; 16. Sex Offenders; 17. Mental illness and crime I; 18. Mental illness and crime II; 19. Interventions to reduce crime I; 20. Interventions to reduce crime II.
learningOutcomes: Describe how biological, psychological, and social factors contribute to engagement in criminal behavior.
Evaluate alternative models of criminal behaviour.
Understand how frequent criminal behaviour is in society and those most likely to perpetrate crime.
Applying different theories of criminal behaviour to explain the onset, maintenance, and desistance of criminal activity.

modules.moduleContent: Core features of the biological bases of learning; Classical Conditioning; Operant Conditioning; The Behavioural Analysis of Language and Cognition; The Cognitive Approach to learning; Cognitive maps and spatial learning. Module Objective: To consider cognitive and behaviour analytic approaches to learning.
learningOutcomes: Describe the key features of the behavioural approach to psychology;
Outline the main technical terms and concepts of behaviour analysis;
Appraise the utility of a modern behavioural approach to language and cognition;
Understand the neural and molecular underpinnings of learning;
Compare and contrast spatial learning across various species.

modules.moduleContent: Social psychology and the built environment; Social relationships and prosocial behaviour; Theories of intergroup conflict; Group processes and decision making in legal and political contexts; Positive Psychology; Creating social contexts for a happy and healthy society.  Module Objective: To explore advanced conceptual issues in social psychology and the application of social psychology to real-world problems.
learningOutcomes: Provide examples of large-scale social problems that can be understood as psychological problems;
Review prominent theoretical and empirical accounts of how to effect large-scale behaviour change;
Describe the role of social interactions and culture on constitution of the self and a variety of psychological phenomena;
Recognise different aspects of intra and inter-group dynamics and their impact on efficacy, decision making, and conflict;
Review complex social phenomena, such as racism and misinformation, and how they have been studied in social psychology.

modules.moduleContent:
Introduction to the practical application of psychological research to everyday, real-world settings and the challenges of applied/engaged interdisciplinary work; • The module will focus, in particular, on: (1) promotion and prevention programmes/initiatives in the field of mental health and social well-being; (2) the design and implementation of innovation in the activities of researchers and practitioners in a variety of domains; and (3) evaluation research. Several projects - based on innovative interventions and initiatives undertaken by staff and doctoral students in the Department of Psychology’s Centre for Mental Health and Community Research, the Assisting Living and Learning Institute and Maynooth University Social Science institute - will provide the context for this module.  
Module Objective: To introduce and explore the application of psychological research methods, theoretical approaches and concepts to a wide range of ‘real world’ community-based topics, including: those related broadly to the mental health and well-being of children, young people and adults; health services research; major emergency management; security, law enforcement and counter-terrorism; and critical infrastructures and industrial organisation. The module emphasises how psychologists interact with numerous other professionals and client groups from various societal, health care and industrial domains.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the application of psychology research methods and approaches in community-based research;
Critically and constructively discuss the role of psychologists in working with interdisciplinary teams in applied contexts;
Describe the benefits and challenges of evaluation research in applied settings;
Apply theoretical and methodological tools and resources for understanding client needs and evaluating interventions.

modules.moduleContent: The aim of this module is to discuss and appraise various post-PhD career pathways inside and outside of psychology and cognate areas for psychology PhD graduates.   This includes discussion of academic, industrial, professional and other career routes available to PhD graduates in psychology; the examination of the barriers that may operate on such routes; and the development of a post-PhD career development plan.
learningOutcomes: Describe and appraise post-PhD pathways to an academic career in Psychology or cognate disciplines.
Describe and appraise post-PhD pathways into professional practice in Psychology.
Describe and appraise post-PhD pathways in careers and applications in industry, the broader public service and the voluntary sector.
Describe and evaluate barriers to career development in psychology in various pathways and sectors.
Develop a post-PhD career development plan.

modules.moduleName: ETHICS IN PSYCHOLOGY RESEARCH AND PRACTICE
modules.moduleContent:
Module objective: To develop knowledge and expertise in ethical issues and guidelines for responsible conduct relevant to the study and application of psychological science with special relevance to the field of research. 
Module content: Ethical and professional standards and obligations under codes of ethics; applicable laws and statutory requirements; informed consent; minimal intrusiveness; protecting confidentiality; ensuring dignity, health and safety; guidelines for responsible conduct; ethical issues arising from the use of the research methods in psychology; where appropriate, preparing an application to a relevant research ethics review body in collaboration with the research supervisor.
learningOutcomes: Apply professional guidelines and principles of ethical conduct;
Consider the ethical issues relevant to participant recruitment, induction and debriefing, research design, data collection and handling, and reporting findings;
Identify a formal procedure by which to examine the ethical issues of relevance to their work and engage in systematic consideration of the relevant factors;
Recognize legal obligations in the conduct of research;
Evaluate the ways in which one's own culture, beliefs and values influence their perceptions of and interactions with others, and use this awareness to improve their practice;
Demonstrate an appreciation of diversity and equality issues.

modules.moduleContent:
This module draws upon the knowledge and competencies gained from taught modules, and focuses on the application of small-scale interventions or micro-interventions. 

• Undertaking group research based on a range of methodologies.
• The quantitative and/or qualitative analysis of collected data.
• Application of digital (e.g. Virtual Reality), nature-based interventions, micro-interventions and nature-based solutions.
• Participatory approaches 
• SDG mapping of applied research and innovation activity.
learningOutcomes: Evaluate critically, based on current insights, the major challenges and problems in national and global societies related to climate change, sustainability, mental health and well-being.
Apply participatory approaches to understand the needs of citizens, client-users and others in the community.
Select and demonstrate and apply a range of advanced research techniques from psychology.
Rigorously apply professional ethical standards in applying research methods and reporting research.
Demonstrate a critical understanding of the benefits and challenges of evaluation research in applied settings.
Engage in reflective activities.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides an analysis of the current issues in research across the extant literature in in sustainability, well-being and mental health and environmental psychology.

• Emerging research paradigms in in sustainability, well-being and mental health and environmental psychology.
• Bibliometric trends in the extant research in in sustainability, well-being and mental health and environmental psychology.
• Critical analysis of the research gaps in the in sustainability, well-being and mental health and environmental psychology.
• Cognition and cognitive neuroscience perspectives.
• Policy frameworks and their development.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate contemporary research trends in sustainability, well-being and mental health and environmental psychology.
Reflect on the ethical challenges of research in sustainability, well-being and mental health and environmental psychology.
Demonstrate a critical understanding of the current research gaps in environmental psychology.
Rigorously assess the pathways to policy development in sustainability, well-being and mental health and environmental psychology.

modules.moduleContent:
• Attendance at departmental research seminars in psychology.
• Critical analysis of contemporary psychological research findings
• Ethical considerations for psychological research
• Viewing psychological research in its broader context
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate contemporary psychological research;
Reflect on the practical applications of ethical principles in psychological research;
Describe links between fundamental research and real-world applications in topics in psychology;
Appreciate the evolving nature of research and knowledge in psychology.

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to the practical application of psychological research to everyday ‘real-world’ settings and the challenges of applied/engaged interdisciplinary work.   The module will focus, in particular, on: (1) promotion and prevention programmes/initiatives in the field of mental health and well-being; (2) the design and implementation of innovation in the activities of researchers and practitioners in a variety of domains; and (3) evaluation research.  Several projects - based on innovative interventions and initiatives undertaken by staff and doctoral students in the Department of Psychology’s Centre for Mental Health and Community Research, the Assisting Living and Learning Institute and Maynooth University Social Sciences institute - will provide the context for this module.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the application of psychology research methods and approaches in community-based research;
Constructively discuss the role of psychologists in working with interdisciplinary teams in applied contexts;
Describe the benefits and challenges of evaluation research in applied settings;
Apply theoretical and methodological tools and resources for understanding the needs of service users, clients and others in the community.

modules.moduleContent: The module is designed to meet the needs of students who are, or who intend to become, members of professional associations relevant to the discipline of psychology or who are undertaking teaching training.  Students must complete two of the following experiential elements: participation in/organisation of CPD events and workshops accredited by the appropriate professional body or otherwise recognised for CPD purposes; participation in committees and other professional body structures; participation in activities that promote psychology as a discipline and the public understanding of psychology.
learningOutcomes: Describe advances and developments in their field and/or related fields or disciplines;
Demonstrate effective interpersonal communication skills;
Contribute to the advancement of the discipline.

modules.moduleContent:
• Introduction to Literature Reviews. Narrative Reviews, features and level of evidence; Rapid Reviews, when to use them, methodology and level of 
        evidence. How to Define the Research Question and Review Goals; How to Define Inclusion and Exclusion criteria in Rapid Reviews; pros and cons of rapid 
        reviews; biases in rapid reviews.
• Introduction to Systematic Reviews; differences between umbrella, scoping and regular systematic reviews; Step-by-Step guide to PRISMA methodology; How 
        to Define the Research Question and Review Goals; How to Define Inclusion and Exclusion criteria in Systematic Reviews; Database selection; Pros and 
        Cons of Open Grey Literature; Biases in Systematic Review Research and how to overcome them; Assessing Eligibility of Selected Studies
• Supporting Platforms to Develop Systematic Reviews: Rayyan and CADIMA; Writing your systematic review: how to write the introduction; Considerations 
        when writing the Methodology of your Systematic Review; How to present Results in a Systematic Review; How to write a Discussion in a Systematic Review.
•       The role of AI (Artificial Intelligence) tools in supporting literature reviews.
learningOutcomes: • Demonstrate an understanding of how to practically perform a step-by-step literature review, be it narrative, rapid or a systematic review.
• Be capable of developing a review protocol based on PRISMA guidelines.
• Learn the basics of how to use Rayyan and CADIMA supporting platforms to organize their reviews.
• Be capable of critically assessing the methodology of a systematic review with narrative synthesis.

modules.moduleContent: In this module, we will be considering the foundations of analytic philosophy. We will begin by looking at how analytic philosophy became a distinct tradition in philosophy in the twentieth century. This will involve a consideration of the key writings of thinkers like Frege, Russell, Wittgenstein, Quine etc. Having done that we will proceed to observe the impact of such foundational thinkers in diverse areas of philosophy such as metaphysics, epistemology, philosophy of mind, and ethics. We will end with a reflection on the status analytic philosophy today.
learningOutcomes: To be able to read technical papers in analytic philosophy
To be at ease with technical discussions in logic and metaphysics
To have an awareness of the divergence of philosophical traditions
To have an awareness of the weaknesses of a philosophical tradition despite its technical profundity.

modules.moduleContent:
This module begins with a deep exploration into the fundamentals of role-playing game (RPG) writing, and participants will learn how to analyse the structural elements of storytelling within the context of tabletop RPGs, drawing on traditional narrative theory. Participants will learn to recognize and respect diversity in RPG narratives and apply ethical considerations in the depiction of sensitive topics. They will explore ways to incorporate narratives into RPG design and understand how game mechanics and scenarios serve narrative goals. All of these skills will help participants master the construction of dynamic narratives that respond to player actions.

Participants will also focus on gaining technical and professional writing savvy, with instruction focused on crafting clear, concise, and accessible RPG documentation such as game rulebooks, campaign guides, and player aids. Collaboration will also be a point of emphasis in the micro-credential, and participants will learn how to work effectively within a team to achieve shared narrative and design goals and present their ideas to various stakeholders.

This module will consist of a series of intensive workshops and activities over the course of a week where participants work together with peers, instructors, and industry professionals to develop and finalise a portfolio demonstrating proficiency in writing for Tabletop Role-Playing Games.
learningOutcomes: Operate at the forefront of the RPG industry and gain mastery of emerging techniques and trends in tabletop RPG design: Incorporating innovative storytelling and narrative design techniques; Analyse the structural elements of storytelling within the context of tabletop RPGs; Apply traditional narrative theory to the interactive medium of tabletop RPGs.

Understand and Apply Technical Writing Skills: Demonstrate the capacity for independent content creation while producing RPG materials, such as rulebooks, campaign guides, and player aids, with clarity and conciseness; Understand the importance of style, voice, and tone in technical writing and how to adapt to various project needs and audiences; Learning to self-evaluate writing deliverables in response to unpredictable and often-shifting professional requirements

Collaborate and Communicate Effectively: Initiate and lead collaborative activities that foster creative brainstorming and idea generation among team members; Demonstrate the ability to work collaboratively in a team to achieve shared narrative and design goals; Present narrative and design ideas effectively to a team, stakeholders, and wider 
audiences; Use project management tools to organize and track narrative and design tasks.

Practice Ethical Storytelling: Recognize and respect cultural, racial, gender, and other diversity in RPG narratives; Apply ethical considerations in the depiction of sensitive or controversial topics; Consider the impact of narratives on the player and wider society.

Implement Narrative into RPG Design: Incorporate narrative elements into RPG design using various game development techniques; Understand how game mechanics, systems, and scenarios can serve narrative goals; Manage the pacing, tension, and resolution of RPG narratives to achieve desired player experiences.

Craft Interactive Narratives for RPGs: Design branching narratives and dialogue possibilities in a manner that serves gameplay and player agency; Construct compelling player-driven narratives that adapt to player choices and actions

Master World-building for RPGs: Construct immersive and consistent game worlds, encompassing factors such as history, geography, culture, and magic or technology systems; Understand and apply the concept of 'show, don't tell' within the context of world-building; Balance the needs for depth and detail with player accessibility and engagement; Design dynamic worlds that evolve and react to player actions through the use of specialized research workflows using industry and open-source
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: LITERATURE 8 - KING ARTHUR'S BRITAIN: MEDIEVAL WELSH LITERATURE
modules.moduleContent: This module offers the student a first introduction to select topics of medieval Welsh literature. In the first half of the semester, students will examine key manuscript sources and texts that provide insight into the beginnings of Arthurian tradition and will consider the ways in which the writing of history intertwined with that of narrative fiction to create a compelling origin legend for the British people. They will then read and analyse examples from the collection of medieval Welsh tales known as the Mabinogion with a view to understanding how the medium of narrative literature could be used for both entertainment and instruction, particularly as a means to comment on ideas about marriage, gender relations, friendship, loyalty and contemporary political developments. In the second half of the semester, students will further advance their knowledge of these themes by exploring the Welsh contribution to European chivalric literature centring around the court of King Arthur, as well as legends concerning the magician Merlin and the genre of heroic poetry. At key points in the module, they will be encouraged to consider the resonances of Irish literary sources in medieval Welsh tradition and the significance of modern cinematic reworkings of the Arthurian legend. All texts will be read through English translation; no prior knowledge of the Welsh language is required.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of key textual sources and the historical contexts that shaped the development and circulation of ideas in medieval Wales.
Show familiarity with the relevant primary and secondary literature set for readings.
Understand a range of theoretical approaches for the study of medieval Welsh literature.
Have experience in the close reading and critical analysis of literary, documentary and visual sources.
Write an essay on a chosen topic, making clear and persuasive arguments based on primary and secondary reading.

modules.moduleContent: Structured reading of key articles, chapters and monographs and texts from the field of Celtic Studies. Students must contact the module coordinator if they wish to take this module.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate analytic skills in Early Medieval Irish studies.
Demonstrate effective and efficient research, writing and study skills.

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to the grammar of Middle Welsh
learningOutcomes: By the end of this module, the students will have a fundamental understanding of Middle Welsh grammar (nominal and verbal morphology, the basics of syntax).

modules.moduleContent: Taught through the medium of English, this module refines the study of the Old Irish language from the so-called classical period of the 8th and 9th centuries. Advanced aspects of Old Irish grammar and syntax are introduced (the remaining moods and tenses, subordinate clauses).
learningOutcomes: Understand and apply moderately advanced aspects of Old Irish verbal morphology (relative and passive verbs, imperatives and past formations) and syntax.
Demonstrate familiarity with some aspects of the syntax of dependent clauses (esp. relative constructions).
Demonstrate familiarity with the syntax of infixed and affixed grammatical elements.
Translate simple Old Irish sentences.

modules.moduleContent: Students will have the opportunity to engage in independent research on an approved topic. Students will be expected to produce a dissertation of c. 8,000 words.
learningOutcomes: Identify a suitable research topic in the field of Early Irish and Celtic Studies.
Demonstrate familiarity with the major primary sources and secondary literature on that Topic.
Development of analytical and critical skills necessary for the undertaking of original research
Development of advanced written skills, and ability to use consistent referencing and formatting skills.

modules.moduleContent: This module focuses on the issues facing contemporary Ireland and seek to enable students apply  their basic theoretical knowledge to key political questions and policy problems the world of Irish politics and society experiences today. By using fundamental conceptual and analytical tools used in the discipline to consider such questions the module seeks to enable students to critically analyse the major political questions in Irish society and beyond. Particular attention is paid to how political and social discourse is framed and who frames and sets agendas in Ireland.
learningOutcomes: Students should be able to identify the core issues and debates at the heart of political and social discourse.
Students will be able to use a variety of different theoretical and practical approaches to analysing political questions.
Students will be familiar with and able to demonstrate applied knowledge in a number of issues dominating Irish politics and society.
Students will be able to examine media social and political discourse and identify how and when differing ideas inform political decision-making

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO PSYCHOLOGY II: SOCIAL PSYCHOLOGY, COGNITIVE PSYCHOLOGY AND INDIVIDUAL DIFFERENCES
modules.moduleContent:
Introduction to major theories of personality; Introduction to theories of intelligence/ability; Introduction to key topics in social psychology; Description of the basics of the cognitive approach; Fundamentals of core areas of cognitive psychology, such as perception, memory and problem solving. 

Module Objective: To introduce the core topics and theories in the areas of social, cognitive and individual difference psychology.
learningOutcomes: Outline different perspectives on personality;
Describe the construct of intelligence/ability and outline the history of intelligence testing;
Identify, and differentiate between, prominent theories in social psychology;
Describe seminal experiments and studies in social psychology;
Compare and contrast different theoretical perspectives in cognitive psychology;
Describe seminal studies in cognitive psychology;
Construct an appraisal, informed by the psychological literature, of a current topic in social psychology, cognitive psychology or individual differences.

modules.moduleContent: Approaches to studying cognition. Perception. Attention. Memory. Problem Solving. Concepts. Language. Decision making and Judgement. Cognition and Emotion
learningOutcomes: Explain the key cognitive processes involved in the acquisition, storage and processing of information;
Appraise how findings from experimental psychology, neuropsychology, neuroscience and cognitive science can contribute to theories and models of cognition;
Describe the main characteristics of the cognitive processes studied using research evidence;
Evaluate cognitive accounts across a range of domains (e.g., perception, attention, memory, problem solving, language, decision making).

modules.moduleName: STATISTICAL AND QUANTITATIVE APPROACHES FOR PSYCHOLOGY
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To introduce computer-based data analysis techniques for the exploration of key issues in designing, executing and analysing psychological data. 
Module Content: Familiarisation with SPSS as a data handling and analysis programme. Descriptive and simple inferential statistical analysis using SPSS. Graphical representation of data using SPSS. Introducing factorial ANOVAs and factorial design. Introducing multiple regression.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate competence in the use of SPSS for quantitative data manipulation, graphical presentation and inferential and descriptive statistical applications;
Describe the theoretical basis, applications and limitations of analysis of variance (ANOVA) techniques;
Identify the theoretical basis, applications and limitations of multiple regression;
Conduct thorough statistical analyses on psychological data sets to test stated hypotheses.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED STATISTICAL AND QUANTITATIVE METHODS FOR PSYCHOLOGY
modules.moduleContent: P-values: A critical evaluation; Issues surrounding statistical power; Standard and hierarchical multiple regression analysis; Binary and multinomial logistic regression analysis; Moderated multiple regression analysis; Mediated multiple regression analysis; Analysis of covariance; Introduction to Factor Analysis; Exploratory factor analysis.
learningOutcomes: Have an advanced appreciation of the usefulness and limitations of hypothesis testing;
Appreciate the vital role of statistical power in designing and executing reliable studies in psychology
Undertake various types of advanced regression analyses on psychological data;
Undertake various types of factorial ANOVA on psychological data;
Undertake factor analysis on psychological data.

modules.moduleContent: Attendance at departmental research seminars in psychology; Critical analysis of contemporary psychological research findings; Ethical considerations for psychological research; Viewing psychological research in its broader context. Module Objective: To consider cutting-edge research and practice across the disciplinary spectrum of psychology
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate contemporary psychological research;
Reflect on the practical applications of ethical principles in psychological research;
Describe links between fundamental research and real-world applications in topics in psychology;
Appreciate the evolving nature of research and knowledge in psychology.

modules.moduleContent: The aim of the module is to support postgraduate research students in: developing their thesis topic; setting out their research plan with respect to key stages and milestones; and beginning their research. The module also provides a structured opportunity for mentorship and development of supportive peer relationships. A range of learning experiences with peers and academic staff will address: planning a postgraduate research programme in psychology; designing your research; synthesizing the literature related to your thesis topic; justifying your research design and critically appraising alternatives; effective communication of research ideas to peers and an academic audience; where appropriate, preparing an application for ethical review in collaboration with the research supervisor.
learningOutcomes: Evaluate a thesis topic with reference to its conceptual and theoretical elements;
Synthesize the literature related to a thesis topic;
Justify a research design and critically appraise alternatives;
Communicate research ideas effectively;
Plan and initiate a student-led discussion on a research topic.

modules.moduleContent:
- Undertaking independent research based on quantitative and/or qualitative methods.
- The quantitative and/or qualitative analysis of collected data.
- The presentation and critical analysis of those data in the context of a research report
- The presentation of those data and research findings in an oral presentation
learningOutcomes: Organise the design, planning, and completion of a research study in a scientific and professional manner;
Rigorously apply professional ethical standards in designing, conducting and reporting psychological research;
Collect the primary or secondary data necessary to complete a research project;
Analyse data using the appropriate quantitative and/or qualitative methods;
Produce a written report of a research study that follows the conventions of the discipline;
Present and disseminate their findings of their study in different formats.

modules.moduleContent:
Ethics, justice and rights
Professional codes of ethics for psychology
Core aspects of ethical conduct, including confidentiality, consent, dignity, competence and integrity
The role of ethics in psychological research
The role of ethics in psychological practice
Contributions of psychology to human rights
learningOutcomes: Describe the key theoretical and philosophical bases of ethics in psychology;
Demonstrate knowledge of the Codes of Professional Ethics of key professional bodies (PSI, BPS, APA);
Describe the application of core ethical principles to research in psychology;
Describe the application of core ethical principles to the professional practice of psychology;
Describe ways in which psychology can contribute to promoting social justice and human rights;
Demonstrate self-reflection of ethical issues as they pertain to undergraduate education in psychology.
Distinguish between science and pseudo-science in the context of psychology;
Demonstrate understanding of the role of evidence and reason in constructing arguments on topics relevant to psychology;
Discuss philosophical influences on contemporary psychology and its methodologies;
Critically assess contemporary controversies and debates in psychology;
Construct an appraisal, informed by the relevant literature, of a current topic involving a conceptual or methodological question in psychology.

modules.moduleContent:
Theoretical and philosophical basis of qualitative enquiry in psychology.
The role of qualitative research methods in psychology.
Qualitative data elicitation methods.
Approaches to qualitative data analysis.
Using qualitative data analysis software to undertake qualitative analysis.

Note: PS652 and PS657 must be completed by MHW56 MSc Environmental Psychology students who have not completed the equivalent content in their primary degrees.
learningOutcomes: Outline the theoretical and philosophical bases of qualitative methods in psychological research.
Describe various qualitative approaches in psychology.
Undertake a piece of qualitative research in psychology.
Demonstrate competence in the use of appropriate software to undertake qualitative analysis.

modules.moduleName: INDEPENDENT RESEARCH PROJECT IN PSYCHOLOGICAL SCIENCE
modules.moduleContent:
Undertaking independent empirical research based on quantitative and/or qualitative methods.
The quantitative and/or qualitative analysis of collected data.

The verbal presentation of research.
learningOutcomes: Organise the design, planning, and completion of a research study in a scientific and professional manner;

modules.moduleContent: The Mentoring for Equality, Diversity and Inclusion module introduces mentors to the practical steps involved in building relationships. It introduces the basic concepts of equality, diversity and inclusion and walks mentors through the interactive and participatory processes that can be used to enhance positive Relationship building. Upon completion of the module the mentor will have a broader understanding of the skills associated with mentoring and communicating effectively. The course involves learners’ participation in training and reflection, as well as engaging in a mentoring relationship with a young person. The module is linked to the STEM Passport for Inclusion, and students will be facilitated to mentor a group of students in this programme. This will be arranged by the course providers and the mentors will be fully supported to achieve the learning outcomes.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the concepts of equality, diversity, and inclusion.
Critically evaluate mentoring practices and theory.
Build and support and effective mentoring relationship.
Provide objective guidance and feedback based on personal experience.
Evaluate inclusive and ethical mentoring practices.

modules.moduleContent:
In this module we will be considering some of the central themes of Aquinas's philosophical thought. We will begin with his metaphysics, and from there we will consider his thought on: the existence of God, the nature of creation, the constitution of the human person, knowledge, the good life, the role of philosophy in the life of faith.

Note this is a SPPU module - further information on the module can be obtained direct from SPPU.
learningOutcomes: To be aware of some central themes in the thought of Aquinas
To be able to read and interconnect various texts from the writings of Aquinas
To attain a familiarity with Aquinas's particular contributions to philosophy
To become familiar with Aquinas's philosophical vocabulary
To see how a systematic philosophy can play a role in the life of a saint

modules.moduleContent:
In this module we will be considering the history of modern philosophy from Descartes to Kant. We will be focusing on the particularly modern concern of the relation of mind and world and how modern philosophers thought about the meaningfulness of mental content. The course will be divided broadly into three sections with each section signifying a particular group of philosophers: (i) The Rationalists, (ii) The Empiricists, (iii) Kant and German Idealism.

Note this is an SPPU module, further details can be obtained from SPPU

This module is comprised of two components. The first foundational aspect of the module treats philosophical theology from the point of view of an analogical metaphysics of creation. In do, it elaborates the distinctive understanding of the Catholic understanding of the God/world relation grounded in the freedom of God’s love and the correlative understanding of being-as-gift. This view is presented in contradistinction to the two major metaphysical presentations of the God/world relation in the history of Western philosophy: pantheism and theopanism. In arguing against the latter, the Catholic view of nature/grace will be explicated.  The second, component of the module consist in an exploration of modern atheistic thinkers who challenge and deny the above view. Figures treated include: Comte, Feuerbach, Nietzsche (and Dostoevsky).

Indicative Syllabus:
• Introduction to the major metaphysical positions in Philosophical Theology. 
• Catholic analogical philosophy of God 
• Pantheism/theopanism
• Nature/Grace
• Modern atheistic movements and figures
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the fundamental traits of an analogical metaphysics of creation.
Be able to understand and recognise the conceptual differences between pantheism and theopanism in relation to an analogical understanding of the God/world relation.
Recognise the crucial connection between nature/grace in the Catholic metaphysical understanding of God/world relation.
Be able explain and identify major modern atheistic challenges to the analogical approach in the figures of Comte, Feuerbach, Nietzsche.
Ability to take a critical stance towards the texts read as well as the figures treated.

modules.moduleName: CELTIC CIVILIZATION 2 - GENDER AND SEXUALITY IN THE CELTIC WORLD
modules.moduleContent: This module takes an interdisciplinary approach to questions of gender and sexuality in the Celtic-speaking world from the earliest sources in the Classical period through to the thirteenth century. Literary and documentary sources – such as legal texts, saga literature, ethnographical writings and hagiography – will be examined in conjunction with linguistic, material and visual culture in order to investigate a range of issues, including the status of women in Celtic societies, laws relating to marriage, paternity and sexual offences, and depictions and subversions of masculinity and femininity in medieval Celtic saga narratives.
learningOutcomes: Have familiarity with a range of primary sources pertaining to gender and sexuality in the ancient and medieval Celtic-speaking world
Understand a range of theoretical approaches for the study of gender and sexuality
Have experience in the close reading and critical analysis of literary, documentary and visual sources
Development of critical, analytical and essay-writing skills

modules.moduleContent: This module will focus on the medieval Irish saga literature known as fíanaigecht, that is, literature concerned with Finn mac Cumaill and his fían (‘warband’). Significant tales from the so-called ‘Fenian Cycle’, ranging from ‘Finn and the Man in the Tree’ to Acallam na Senórach (‘The Colloquy of the Ancients’) will be studied in detail, and various literary, moral and political interpretations of the tales will be explored. The circumstances of composition of the literature, and its striking interest in aspects of geography and cultural memory, will be discussed in detail. The modern reception and reworking of ‘Finn Cycle’ literature will also be explored.

modules.moduleName: LITERATURE 4 - HUMOUR IN MEDIEVAL CELTIC LITERATURE
modules.moduleContent: There are a number of texts surviving from twelfth-century Ireland and Wales which are generally accepted as being ironic or parodic in intent, and are thus regarded as humorous compositions. This module will trace the earlier development of humour in medieval Celtic narrative, examining the use of subversive literary tropes – particularly irony – to undermine certain social or religious conventions. Students will explore a range of ironic and parodic narratives, and the relationship between humour and the more serious genre of satire will be considered. Students will explore the way that humour is used to subvert the so-called ‘heroic ethos’ and to express disapproval at cases of clerical immorality. This will lead to a consideration of some of the more extensive parodies and ironic narratives written in medieval Ireland and Wales, including Aislinge Meic Con Glinne and Culhwch ac Olwen.

The students are gradually familarised with all aspects of Old Irish grammar. In semester 1, first the phonology and orthography of Old Irish are introduced. After this, the inflectional classes of the nouns and adjectives, the present stem classes of the verbs, as well as central chapters of syntax, e.g. inflected prepositions and infixed pronouns, are the main focus of the attention.

Previous experience in the learning of languages is required before taking this module. Students who want to take this module  should contact the module coordinator first.
learningOutcomes: Understand the phonology of Old Irish and its orthographic representation.
Analyse and identify nominal (esp. vocalic and consonantal-stem nouns) and verbal forms (esp. forms derived from the present stem).
Translate simply Old Irish sentences.

modules.moduleName: HISTORY 1 - EARLY IRELAND, FROM ST PATRICK TO THE BATTLE OF CLONTARF
modules.moduleContent: This module offers an introduction to early Irish history, beginning with the life of St Patrick in the fifth century and ending with the death of King Brian Boru at the Battle of Clontarf in 1014. Key themes from the history of early medieval Ireland will be explored, including kingship, identity, social relations, and religion. This module provides a foundational overview of Ireland’s early history and will introduce you to some of the most important primary sources from that period. An era of violence, political struggles and social inequality, it was also a time of great cultural sophistication, when Ireland had a reputation as the ‘island of saints and scholars’. Both aspects of this fascinating period of history will be explored.

modules.moduleName: HISTORY 2 - LAW, SOCIETY AND LEARNED CLASSES IN EARLY IRELAND
modules.moduleContent: This module focuses on the large surviving body of early Irish law, often popularly known as ‘Brehon law’, which was unique in medieval Europe for the volume and sophistication of the texts produced. The range and types of legal texts which survive from medieval Ireland will be explored, and a series of early Irish law tracts will be read (in English translations). These laws cover the regulation of violence; marriage, divorce, paternity, and sexual offences; social status and hierarchies; church-state relations; and much more besides. We will analyse how the law functioned and operated, and examine the status of the legal specialists who administered justice. The relationship between legal theory and historical reality will be considered and we will explore how legal texts can be used as a source of evidence for the workings of medieval Irish society.

Taught through the medium of English, this module introduces the students to the remaining chapters of Old Irish grammar, esp. verbal formations of the preterite, subjunctive and future stems, as well as advanced topics of syntax.
learningOutcomes: Analyse and identify verbal formations (past tenses, subjunctive, future).
Demonstrate familiarity with advanced aspects of the syntax of Old Irish, esp. dependent clauses.
Translate simple Old Irish sentences.

modules.moduleName: HISTORY 5 - THE VIKING WORLD II: IRELAND
modules.moduleContent: Vikings occupy an important place in the modern cultural imagination, but who exactly were they and what impact did they have in Ireland? This module explores representations of the Vikings in medieval sources and popular culture, shedding light on their origins, their first raids on Ireland from the late eighth century and their later settlements on the island. This module will explore questions of ethnic identity, immigration, gender, urbanisation and the cultural legacy of Vikings in Ireland and the wider medieval world. The historical reality of the Vikings will be contrasted with their representation in films and TV shows in the modern era.

The students are introduced to the Irish manuscript tradition, especially those manuscripts that are relevant for medieval literature of Ireland. The students will learn how to read and transcribe medieval Irish manuscripts.

It is advisable to take SG382 with this module. 

Students who wish to take this module may wish to consult with the Head of Department first.

The students are gradually introduced to all aspects of Old Irish grammar. In semester 1, first the phonology and orthography of Old Irish are introduced. After this, the inflectional classes of the nouns and adjectives, the present stem classes of the verbs, as well as central chapters of syntax, e.g. inflected prepositions and infixed pronouns, are the main focus of the attention.
Students taking this module outside of Medieval Irish, please contact the programme coordinator, Prof. David Stifter (david.stifter@mu.ie), before registering. No previous knowledge of the Irish language is required, but a readiness to engage with basic linguistic concepts is necessary.
learningOutcomes: Understand the phonology of Old Irish and its orthographic representation.
Analyse and identify nominal (esp. vocalic and consonantal-stem nouns) and verbal forms (esp. forms derived from the present stem).
Translate simply Old Irish sentences.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed for students of Politics and Criminology.  Political research may be conducted on governments, public policies, political processes, civil society sector and political behaviour. Criminological research may be conducted on government responses to crime, public policies, politicisation of crime, the criminal justice system, and criminal behaviour.  Both types of research are grounded in either political theory or criminological theory; both seek to influence social policy and both inform our understanding of the practice of politics and criminal justice practice.

The module introduces students to the main research methods used by both political scientists and criminologists. Lectures will give students an understanding of different research philosophies that underpin research design. We discuss the merits of various research methods such as interviewing, survey analysis, polling, focus groups and observation. We will also be sensitive to issues of power and ethical considerations as we explore the use and abuse of research data to further political agendas of lobbyists, politicians and a range of non-governmental actors and organisations. 

The module is delivered through 16 core lectures, and 8 small group tutorials. In the Tutorial groups you will get hands-on experience of researching in your own primary discipline (Politics or Criminology).
learningOutcomes: Students will have a clear understanding of the basic methods used to conduct political/criminological research.
Students will be able to assess the merits of utilising different methodological strategies depending on the research question.
Students will have developed a critical perspective such that they can provide critical commentary and interpretation of political and criminological research.

modules.moduleContent: This module will provide an assessment of the key debates about the relation between gender and politics in the Western political tradition. It will explore how gender has shaped and been shaped by key political ideas (including the public-private distinction, the idea of contract, political representation, rights, justice, identity and equality), connections between conceptual debates institutional politics and, more specifically, gender inequality and political representation.
learningOutcomes: Understand the major theories and key concepts used by social scientists to analyse the relation between gender and politics.

Critically assess how gender has shaped and has been shaped by key political ideas (including the public-private distinction, the idea of contract, political representation, rights, justice, identity and equality).
Evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by social scientists exploring the participation and representation of women in politics.
Develop an independent and critical perspective on the social scientific analyses of gender inequality and political representation.

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to the scientific method and the research process; Measurement in Psychology; Sampling;  Experimental design in psychology; Ethical Considerations in psychological research; Searching the psychological literature; Conducting an experiment in psychology and presenting the results in a practical report. Module Objective: To introduce the process of scientific experimentation in psychology and the presentation of data from psychological experiments.
learningOutcomes: Outline the basis of psychology as an empirical, scientific discipline;
Differentiate between scientific and pseudo-scientific psychological research;
Define the main technical terms relating to research design and implementation;
Apply the concepts of experimental design to the analysis of psychological phenomena;
Undertake a search of the psychological literature on a given research topic;
Conduct a research practical exploring a specific topic in psychology;
Present the results from this practical appropriately, within the context of a research report.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO PSYCHOLOGICAL RESEARCH METHODS AND ANALYSIS
modules.moduleContent: Descriptive statistics; Elementary statistical inference; Qualitative research methods; Introduction to the use of SPSS in psychology. Module Objective: To introduce quantitative statistical approaches and the use of qualitative methods in psychological research.
learningOutcomes: Outline the use and interpretation of statistical tests as applied to psychological data;
Explain key technical terms and concepts relating to data analysis and interpretation;
Compute descriptive and inferential statistics;
Summarise data using appropriate tables and graphical methods;
Describe the use of qualitative research methods in psychology;
Demonstrate competence in the use of SPSS for basic statistical analysis.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To examine how the brain influence various aspects of behaviour, the mechanisms by which this may occur and the consequences of brain dysfunction and disease. 
Module Content: The biological basis of rudimentary and complex issues such as stress, sleep, learning and memory. Synaptic transmission and psychopharmacology. Functional neuroanatomy, including functions of the frontal, parietal, temporal and occipital cortical lobes. Neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease. Modern neuroscientific techniques.
learningOutcomes: Appraise the contribution of specific brain regions to particular behaviours and psychological phenomena;
Discuss in depth the processes of inter-neuronal communication;
Critically review the various types of memory and their underlying neural mechanisms;
Describe the symptoms and neural bases of various neurodegenerative diseases;
Evaluate the relative merits of neuroscientific techniques used in research.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To consider different perspectives and theoretical approaches to the psychology of development and its applications; and to explore personality, social, emotional, educational and cultural changes in the lifespan. 
Module Content: Conceptual, historical and contemporary influences in developmental psychology. Different approaches to understanding developments in socialisation, emotion and personality. The role of other factors, such as culture, in human development.
learningOutcomes: Discuss contemporary lifespan development and multi-level treatment;
Outline the history of developmental psychology;
Describe the main schools of thought in developmental psychology;
Describe applications that might follow from theory and concepts in Developmental Psychology;
Evaluate empirical evidence in developmental psychology.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To instruct students in objective, systematic, empirical investigations in psychology and the presentation and evaluation of research data. 
Module Content: Outlining the role of the experimental research process in psychology; Undertaking supervised experiments in the areas of cognitive psychology and biopsychology; Producing written research reports on data derived from each experiment; Appreciating the role of practical considerations (ethics, data management etc.) in the conduct of psychological experiments; Reflecting on the experience of the research participant through participation in research projects.
learningOutcomes: Describe the roles of objectivity, rigour and systematic approaches to the conduct of experimental psychology;
Appreciate the links between experimental design and simple forms of inferential statistics;
Consider critically the link between psychological theory and experiment;
Produce comprehensive research reports on experiments in psychology;
Critically evaluate published psychological research;
Reflect on the experience of the research participant across a range of psychological studies.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Module Objective: To introduce qualitative research methods used in psychology and to develop an understanding of how various qualitative approaches can be utilised in psychological research. Module Content: Theoretical and philosophical basis of qualitative enquiry in psychology. The role of qualitative research methods in psychology. Qualitative data elicitation methods. Approaches to qualitative data analysis. Using qualitative data analysis software to undertake qualitative analysis.
learningOutcomes: Outline the theoretical and philosophical bases of qualitative methods in psychological research;
Describe various qualitative approaches in psychology;
Undertake a piece of qualitative research in psychology;
Demonstrate competence in the use of appropriate software to undertake qualitative analysis.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Content will vary depending on the specific placement in which the student is involved. However it is expected that this will include the following core components:Experiencing real-world applications of psychology and psychological principles in a professional setting; Making the connection between the academic study of psychology and its applications; Developing a range of professional and inter-personal skills required for working in an organisation; Reflecting on personal and professional development throughout the placement experience.

Objective: To expose students to real-world applications of psychological principles in an external setting so that they can better appreciate the link between psychological theory and practice in a range of organisational and community contexts .
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the role of psychological principles in real-world applications.
Appreciate the connection between fundamental knowledge in psychology and its application in the community.
Reflect on the roles of individuals and teams in applying psychology.
Document and reflect on the professional and inter-personal skills developed throughout the placement.
Produce both written and oral evaluations of the placement experience.

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to the scientific method and the research process; Measurement in Psychology; Sampling; Experimental design in psychology; Ethical Considerations in psychological research; Searching the psychological literature. Module Objective: To introduce the process of scientific experimentation in psychology and the presentation of data from psychological experiments
learningOutcomes: Outline the basis of psychology as an empirical, scientific discipline.
Differentiate between scientific and pseudo-scientific psychological research.
Define the main technical terms relating to research design and implementation.
Understand the concepts of experimental design to the analysis of psychological phenomena.
Understand how to conduct a search of the psychological literature on a given research topic.

modules.moduleContent: Neuroanatomy of cortical and subcortical regions; Neuropsychology of the visual system; Neuropsychology of acquired brain injury; Neuropsychology of disorders of the temporal lobe; Neuropsychology of attentional disorders; Neuropsychology of aging and neurodegenerative disorders; Technological developments in the field of neuropsychological assessment and intervention: computerized tools and virtual reality applications. Module Objective: To explore aspects of neuropsychology in relation to specific areas of the brain, and damage and pathologies of these areas, with an emphasis on discussion and critical thinking in relation to classic studies in neuropsychology.
learningOutcomes: Describe the history and theoretical bases of neuropsychological research;
Demonstrate knowledge of functional human neuroanatomy;
Describe key methods used in neuropsychology;
Differentiate between acquired, congenital and pathological syndromes of brain injury and their neuropsychological features;
Appreciate the use of neuropsychology principles in clinical practice;
Describe key experiments that have shaped contemporary behavioural and cognitive neuroscience;
Critically evaluate the methodology and conclusions of seminal papers in neuropsychology.

modules.moduleContent: Topics covered include: the role of systems, structures, policies, and institutions in global health; core aspects of ethics in global health; the role of human rights, social inclusion, equality, and equity in global health; the importance of democratic health systems; inclusive and equitable policy processes; and the role of scaling to expand interventions.
learningOutcomes: Describe ways in which systems, structures, policies, and institutions influence global health, using concepts such as systems thinking.
Describe the application of core ethical principles to global health.
Demonstrate knowledge of the role of human rights, social inclusion, equality, and equity in global health
Describe the importance of democratic health systems and how patient and public involvement can be achieved.
Demonstrate an understanding of inclusion and equity across policy processes, including policy development, implementation, monitoring, and evaluation.
Describe factors that may influence scaling to expand interventions.

modules.moduleContent:
Ageing is not a uniform process nor is age a category determined by numbers. Instead it is a space for social and cultural space of cultural expectations and contestations. It has become apparent through research that it is important to emphasise the heterogeneity of “older people” and to deconstruct “age” as a in similar ways as gender, race or ethnicity and to discuss the breaking of legal, economic and social norms associated with aging. This includes scrutinizing the “burden discourse” and to engage with older adults as cultural and social producers (e.g. volunteering, informal care givers).

- Traditional approaches to ageing research and gerontology.
- Ethnographic and qualitative approaches to deepen our understanding of needs, obstacles and interpretations of older adults’ ways of living.
- The role of Smart and Healthy ageing environments.
- The genesis of a new research paradigm: The capability approach to healthy ageing.
- Participatory methods in Ageing Research.
- European guidelines and policies for age-friendly environments.
- Universal environmental design.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate contemporary research on the development and monitoring of age-friendly, multi-generational and inclusive environments.
Reflect on the methods used to capture the voices and experiences through development from people themselves in determining what it means to age well across the lifespan.
Evaluate the technological, organisational, clinical, educational and societal solutions to support health ageing across the lifespan.
Appreciate the evolution of new research paradigms in the field and the implications for policy makers.
Apply design thinking approaches and appreciate how this can be used across different populations.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides an opportunity to hear from international experts, stakeholder organisations and leaders in sustainability on the latest research and practice. It provides an opportunity for reflection by the students on their learning and how it may influence their own approach to addressing societal issues.

- Interdisciplinary perspectives in the global and European research and innovation context.
- SDGs and their indicators.
- International perspectives in research and innovation in environmental issues, sustainability, mental health and well-being 
- Ethical considerations for applied research.
- Development of research from inception of the idea to measurable impact.
learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate contemporary research in sustainability, well-being and mental health and environmental psychology;
Reflect on the practical applications of ethical principles in the applied context;
Demonstrate a critical understanding of the links between fundamental research and real-world applications in sustainability, well-being and mental health and environmental psychology;
Appreciate the evolution of new research paradigms in sustainability, well-being and mental health and environmental psychology;
Apply mapping of UN Sustainable Development goals to real-world research and innovation.

modules.moduleContent: Classic studies in psychology: are they really classic? -The replication crisis in psychology and what we have learned about what constitutes a “classic” study. -Methodology and impact of seminal studies in psychology; -Critical discussion of eight classic studies in psychology. -Group presentation and discussion of critical analysis of student-selected papers
learningOutcomes: Describe the history and theoretical bases of classic studies in contemporary psychology;
Critically evaluate the methodology and conclusions of seminal papers in psychology;
Describe the “replication crisis” in psychology and discuss how this has improved psychological science;
Present and discuss results of key studies in a number of domains of psychology.
Outline the use and interpretation of statistical tests as applied to psychological data.
Explain key technical terms and concepts relating to data analysis and interpretation.
Compute descriptive and inferential statistics.
Describe the theoretical basis, applications and limitations of analysis of variance (ANOVA) techniques.
Identify the theoretical basis, applications and limitations of multiple regression.
Conduct thorough statistical analyses on psychological data sets to test stated hypotheses.
