modules.moduleContent:
Advanced factorial designs
Corrections for multiple hypothesis testing
Statistical power and pre-study odds
Replication
P-hacking
Advanced graphical representations of data
learningOutcomes: Undertake various types of factorial ANOVA on psychological data;
Have an advanced appreciation of the usefulness and limitations of hypothesis testing;
Appreciate the vital role of statistical power in designing and executing reliable studies in psychology;
Be able to represent psychological data graphically in the most meaningful manner.

modules.moduleName: CRITICAL SKILLS FOR CAREERS IN PSYCHOLOGY
modules.moduleContent:
Career options for psychology graduates
Working as a team
Forming professional networks
Self-appraisal and reflection
Creating job applications and cvs.
learningOutcomes: Appreciate the career options that are available to psychology graduates;
Identify personal goals and ambitions through self-assessment and reflection;
Appreciate the communication and inter-personal skill-set required for effective work in psychology and beyond;
Produce quality job applications and cvs, and display skills for effective interviewing.

modules.moduleContent: A broad range of topics will be introduced via papers from relevant journals.
learningOutcomes: Discuss recent advances and developments in their field;
Critically read published research;
Evaluate the quality of information and validity of conclusions presented in psychological research;
Accurately summarize research literature.

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to the Latent Variable Modelling approach; Confirmatory Factor Analysis; Basic Structural Equation Modelling; Theory of Change: Mediation analysis in Structural Equation Modelling. The objective of this module is to introduce students to the theory latent variable modelling; how to conduct, interpret, and present Structural Equation Modelling analyses; and how Structural Equation Modelling can be used to determine mechanism of change.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the value of latent variable modelling for psychological research.
Be capable of conduction confirmatory factor analysis and Structural Equation Modelling analysis.
Be capable of interpreting and presenting findings from Structural Equation Modelling analysis.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Aims: 

To understand the main features of Gabriel Marcel’s thought.

To understand hope as the unifying theme in his philosophy.

To understand why Marcel still matters today.

Indicative Syllabus:

- Marcel’s philosophical quest and the place of music and theatre in his thought.
- The technological mentality and how human beings have become strangers to themselves.
- Two kinds of thought – primary and secondary reflection.
- The human being as an incarnate being.
- Presence, communion and the “thou”.
- The Absolute Thou.
- Hope as the force that drives the incarnate being in the direction of transcendence.

Note this is an SPPU module - further details can be obtained from SPPU
learningOutcomes: Understand the principal features Marcel’s thought.
Understand what he means by primary and secondary reflection
Understand his particular approach to existence and being
Understand his metaphysics of hope

modules.moduleContent: Taught through the medium of English, this module provides an introduction to the Old Irish language of the so-called Classical period of the 8th and 9th centuries ab initio.The studied topics are: the sound system and the spelling of Old Irish, the inflection of nouns, adjectives and pronouns, the present stem of verbs.
learningOutcomes: Analyse Old Irish texts nominal and verbal forms (present tense).
Understand the system of spelling and pronunciation of Old Irish.
Understand fundamental grammatical terms and concepts.
Translate simple Old Irish sentences.

modules.moduleContent: Reading a Middle Welsh text.
learningOutcomes: The students will be able to translate a simple Middle Welsh text

modules.moduleContent: Structured reading of articles, chapters, and monographs or texts from the field of Celtic Studies.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate effective and efficient research, writing and study skills.

modules.moduleContent: Using the celebrated narrative tradition of medieval Ireland only as a quarry for heroic, mythological or pagan motifs, or as an entertaining window on the past, is turning a blind eye on the contemporary intellectual, literary and historical fabric into which those tales were woven by their composers. In this module, the students will read selected pieces of the Irish saga tradition and subject them to a full textual analysis and interpretation.
learningOutcomes: Deeply engage with primary texts, their modern reception and current scholarship about them.
Development of analytical and critical skills necessary for the interpreting texts.
Development of writing and presentation skills

modules.moduleContent: Reading of select Middle Welsh texts, prose and/or poetry

modules.moduleName: GRAMMAR AND READINGS IN OLD IRISH
modules.moduleContent: This module will enhance proficiency in aspects of Old Irish grammar and syntax for students of the structured PhD programme. Fluency in the system of spelling and pronunciation will be expanded and exemplified by reading and systematic analyses of Old Irish texts.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish aspects of Old Irish grammar and syntax.
Detail and exemplify the system of spelling and pronunciation of Old Irish.
Systematically examine and analyse extant Old Irish texts.
Demonstrate effective and efficient research, academic writing and study skills.

modules.moduleName: RESEARCH SEMINAR IN MEDIEVAL IRISH AND CELTIC STUDIES
modules.moduleContent: This module will augment the critical research and analyse aptitude, academic writing and presentation skills for students of the structured PhD programme. learningOutcomes: Demonstrate the capacity to work through complex problems by breaking them into smaller parts; including analysing data, evaluating options and ideating solutions.
Interpret data sets to make informed, ethical decisions and be able to present these decisions in an evidence informed manner.
Demonstrate an understanding of design thinking as a methodology.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to introduce students to anatomy of the human musculoskeletal system and biomechanics of movements relevant to sport and exercise.

This module will begin by introducing students to key concepts of anatomy and biomechanics, including anatomical terminology, biomechanical principles of human movement, osteology, arthrology and myology. Students will then learn to apply the key concepts of anatomy to regions of the human body most relevant to sport and exercise, by identifying and classifying the major bones, joints, muscles and nerves of the lower limb, trunk and upper limb. Students will also learn to apply the key concepts of biomechanics, including kinematics and kinetics of lower limb, trunk and upper limb movements relevant to sport and exercise.
learningOutcomes: Use appropriate anatomical terminology
Understand the key principles of biomechanics
Identify and classify the major bones, joints, muscles and nerves of the lower limb, trunk and upper limb
Describe the biomechanics of lower limb, trunk and upper limb movements relevant to sport and exercise.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to introduce students to the major physiological systems of the human body, the concept of homeostasis, sources of energy and physiological responses to exercise relevant to health and performance in sport.

This module will begin by introducing students to the major physiological systems of the human body, including the cardiovascular, respiratory, gastrointestinal, urinary and endocrine systems. Students will learn about the concept of homeostasis and how these systems interact to maintain equilibrium. The sources of energy available in the human body will also be covered, along with metabolic and physiological responses to exercise relevant to health and performance in sport.
learningOutcomes: Describe the major physiological systems of the human body
Understand the concept of homeostasis and how physiological systems interact to achieve equilibrium
Describe the sources of energy in the human body
Explain the physiological and metabolic responses of the human body to exercise.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to introduce students to acute and chronic musculoskeletal injuries, which can occur as a result of participation in sport and exercise.

In this module, students will learn about the key principles of sports injury epidemiology, risk factors, mechanisms, prevention, rehabilitation and return to play testing. Students will apply these principles to acute and chronic sports injuries of the lower limb, trunk, and upper limb, building on their foundational knowledge of musculoskeletal anatomy and biomechanics. This module does not include diagnosis or medical aspects of treatment for sports injuries, which are beyond the scope of practice for sport scientists and exercise professionals.
learningOutcomes: Define acute and chronic sports injuries
Describe the key principles of sports injury prevention, rehabilitation and return to play testing
Understand the epidemiology, risk factors and mechanisms of common sports injuries of the lower limb, trunk and upper limb
Apply the key principles of prevention, rehabilitation and return to play testing to common sports injuries of the lower limb, trunk and upper limb

modules.moduleContent: This course introduces the ways in which contemporary Northern Irish society is organised, experienced and represented. The major social and political divisions within Northern Irish society are analysed.
learningOutcomes: Detail and critically assess the major theories and key concepts used by sociologists to explore Northern Irish society.
Appraise the research argument and evidence provided by sociologists on issues such as the major social and political divisions within Northern Irish society
Evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by sociologists exploring the ways in which contemporary Northern Irish society is organised, experienced and represented.
Develop an independent and critical perspective on the sociological analyses of Northern Irish society.

modules.moduleName: MEDIA, CULTURE AND THE CONSTRUCTION OF CRIME
modules.moduleContent: This module will explore the relationship between crime, criminal justice, media and culture. Specifically, we will analyse the ways in which the media construct and frame the problem of crime, and the consequences which flow from such framing.  In particular, the focus will be on identifying the key forces involved in shaping whether an issue gets defined as ‘a crime’ in the first place, how it gets defined or framed, and the factors influencing that process. Media spaces – including television, cinema, print media and social media – provide important discursive and visual interpretative grids, lenses and prompts through which people make sense of their everyday experiences but these resources are rarely put forward in a neutral or innocent way.   Discourses involves both the written/spoken word and visual representations which work together to produce an ‘interpretative package’ (Gamson & Modigliani 1989).  There will be a strong focus on media literacy so that students develop the capacity to evaluate media sources, to recognise bias, to compare levels of reported crime with actual recorded crime and to critically deconstruct prevalent constructions of crime.
learningOutcomes: Detail and evaluate the major theories and key concepts used by social scientists to analyse the construction and representation of crime across media platforms.
Critically assess the contribution of social scientific analyses to an understanding of how the media characterised and represents crime.
Evaluate different kinds of interpretative schemata for “reading” media messages about crime.
Demonstrate how media literacy enhances capacity to distinguish between the framing of crime in the media and the prevalence and nature of crime in society.
Develop an independent and critical perspective on the social scientific analysis of the relationship between crime, culture and the media.

modules.moduleContent: Contemporary trends in Irish family life, placing them in comparative historical perspective. Sociological explanations of those trends, the consequences for individuals, groups and society as a whole. Changing patterns of family formation, changing gender roles, reconciliation of family and work life, family violence.
learningOutcomes: Detail and evaluate the major theories and key concepts used by sociologists to analyse the family.
Critically assess the contribution of sociological analysis to an understanding of contemporary trends in Irish family life in comparative and historical perspective.

Evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by sociologists exploring issues such as: changing patterns of family formation, changing gender roles, reconciliation of family and work life, family violence.
Develop an independent and critical perspective on the sociological analyses of the family.

modules.moduleContent: In the ‘new world order’ traditional threats - such as nuclear annihilation - fade while new threats - such as ‘global warming’ - gain currency.  The focus on military threats emanating from other states is being contested – despite criticisms from the realists – by a far broader interpretation of security as any factor that conspired against human or individual security.  The new conceptualising of security entailed in the term human security– albeit contested by the realist school and the dominant states – is both based on, and leads to, a new understanding of world politics.  Human security, its focus on protecting people from threat, risk and negative impact of conflict in the context of globalisation processes, is the main subject of this course.   Students will be presented with some social, cultural, economic, and political aspects or facets of contemporary globalisation and in this context security issues will be critically examined from a human security perspective, in its broader and deeper sense.  In this way, students will be introduced to the re-conceptualisation of the globalisation debates and the re-visioning of what security means in the 21st Century. The areas of intersection between global human security and development, environment, health, war, migration, and gender policy feature specifically on this course.
learningOutcomes: Be familiar with definitions of security and human security, and a range of different academic approaches to the study of global security;
Have an understanding of how the concept of gender operates as both a constitutive and disruptive element within the mainstream security paradigm;
Have explored the sociological, political science and international development literature wherein contestations of the mainstream security paradigm and alternative approaches have been debated;
Have conceptualised a socio-political approach to securitisation;
Be able to identify securitization processes and elements of their practice as they emerge in substantive contexts;
Have developed a reflexive critical approach to the construction and practice of securitization.

modules.moduleContent: Is crime more or less prevalent now than before?  Why are some people more likely to engage in criminal activity than others? Why are some behaviours defined as crime and others are not?  These are some of the questions taken up by sociologists concerned with the relationship between crime and society.  In this module, students learn about the major theoretical and empirical debates and issues in the sociology of crime literature.  Special attention is given to placing the Irish case in comparative perspective.
learningOutcomes: Detail and evaluate the major theories and key concepts used by social scientists to analyse crime in society.
Critically assess the contribution of social scientific analyses to an understanding of the causes of criminal activity.
Evaluate different kinds of approaches to the interpretation of "crime" as it is legally and socially defined in society.
Understand how to analyse, evaluate and interpret information.
Develop and defend reasoned opinions in oral and written form.
Search, obtain and present information.
Participate in group discussion.

modules.moduleContent: This module will critically examine how gender is constructed at the level of society as well as how we engage in the re-creation of gender in our everyday lives. Gender will be explored as an institution and a system as well as how it influences individuals. We will consider sociological approaches to studying gender, including gender as an identity, a performance, and a structuring feature of discourses and institutions; how gender is expressed through configurations of femininity and masculinity, embedded in power relations, and co-constructed and in relation to its intersection with other social locations such as race, ethnicity class, sexuality, age, ability and citizenship. Topics will include gender beliefs and practices; socialization practices that reproduce gender identities and the relationship between gender, power, and social inequality.
learningOutcomes: Gain an understanding of the main theories and concepts used within the sociology of gender.
Develop an understanding of sociological evidence and argument used to research gender as a social force.
Evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by sociologists to understand gender as a sociological phenomenon.

modules.moduleContent: This module takes place over five days with three days devoted to exclusive lectures, workshops and field trips and two days when students join existing second year sociology modules in Structures of Inequality, and other electives of their choice. The overall theme addresses comparative inequalities in a European context and draws on national economic, political and social context setting, methodology and measurement and intersectionality.
learningOutcomes: Understand the economic, political and social context of Irish inequality.
Familiarity with key methodology and measurement debates concerning inequality.
Compare Irish and German inequality data.
Discuss key policy issues related to inequality in a European context.

modules.moduleContent: This module will require a student to undertake the collection and analysis of raw data in either a field or laboratory situation. Students will develop key skills in developing and preparing fieldwork, undertaking primary data collection and developing analytical techniques for assessing collected data. Students will be required to clear ethical approval (and where necessary Garda Vetting) with responsible authorities when appropriate, in addition to developing suitable research methods, for each site or experiment prior to undertaking field work or laboratory work.
learningOutcomes: Design an individual research project, and carry out appropriate primary and/or secondary research.
Collect and record data on specific topics in field or laboratory based situations.
Recount and report on research findings related to the research.

learningOutcomes: engage in group discussion and communicate in group
participate in team work exercises throughout the process

modules.moduleContent: Students will develop a formal research proposal in conjunction with their research supervisors. This proposal will include a) a description of the research project; b) an outline methodology statement; c) a working chapter outline, with targeted delivery dates; d) a select bibliography.
learningOutcomes: Search for grant application calls, write application, revise and submit.

modules.moduleContent: This course offers students a theoretical and practical guide to a broad range of innovative qualitative research methods. It locates these methods within an interpretivist tradition, which explores the meanings people attach to human behaviour and the subject’s perspective. The course explores human behaviour through methods such as participant observation, interviewing and focus groups, discourse analysis and visual methods. The course offers practical experience to students in these methods as well as on analysis and writing of qualitative research. Students should be able to understand the epistemological foundations behind qualitative research, assess which qualitative methods are the most appropriate to answer particular research questions, demonstrate a knowledge of various qualitative research methods, demonstrate a competence in MAXQDA and understand how qualitative data is generated, analysed and written up.
learningOutcomes: Identify and to work within Interpretive and phenomenological approaches
Execute formal, comparative, mixed and interdisciplinary methodologies
Utilise qualitative secondary analysis software incorporating Big Data approaches
Extract data from qualitative interviews and focus groups and from other textual and narrative sources
Work with two different coding programmes and use the software to do analysis
Assess and utilise a variety of  qualitative research strategies including ethnography and Participatory Action Research

modules.moduleContent: The student will prepare a major publication in their research field. Forms of publication may include a paper in a local history, national or international research journal.
learningOutcomes: engage in process of preparing for publication - time and project management
participate in team work exercises peer reviewing drafts for publication
explore and understand the thematic topic for publication
Prepare a major publication to publishing criteria and standards.

modules.moduleContent:
Consistent with good research design this module is organised in the frame of the research data cycle and takes a mixed approach to quantitative and qualitative research methods. Quantitative analysis is one of a wide menu of approaches to social science research. This module introduces you to the fundamentals of quantitative data analysis, with a particular emphasis on visualisation, interpretation, and critical appraisal. Working with real-world data, and with few assumptions of prior knowledge, it will equip students with a range of techniques to perform independent analyses, present your results intuitively, and ultimately design your own theoretically informed studies. The course will involve working with cross-sectional, longitudinal, and a mix of micro and macro-data, enabling study patterns of social change over space and time, whilst gently exposing students to the practice of multivariate data analysis.

This course also offers students a theoretical and practical guide to a broad range of innovative qualitative research methods. It locates these methods within an interpretivist tradition, which explores the meanings people attach to human behaviour and the subject’s perspective. The course explores human behaviour through methods such as participant observation, interviewing and focus groups, discourse analysis and visual methods. The course offers practical experience to students in these methods as well as on analysis and writing of qualitative research. 

Students should be able to understand the epistemological foundations behind qualitative research, assess which qualitative methods are the most appropriate to answer particular research questions, demonstrate a knowledge of various qualitative research methods, demonstrate a competence in MAXQDA and understand how qualitative data is generated, analysed, and written up.
learningOutcomes:
The student will be able to conceptualise and understand different approaches to measurement of quantitative data
- The student will know and understand different options for data visualisation and presentation
- The student will be able to consumption and critique quantitative research
- The student will be familiar with logics of statistical modelling
- The student will be able to operationalise regression-based approaches
Students will be able identify and to work within Interpretive and phenomenological approaches
Students will be able to execute formal, comparative, mixed and interdisciplinary methodologies
Students will be aware and be able to utilise qualitative secondary analysis software incorporating Big Data approaches
Students will be skilled in extracting data form qualitative interviewing and focus groups and from other textual and narrative sources
Students will have practical experience of two different coding programmes and have used the software to do analysis
Students will explore various ethnographic, participant observation research methods including PAR and collective memory work
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: CRITICAL SKILLS FOR SOCIAL SCIENCE 1: INQUIRY, DISCOVERY AND DISCOURSE
modules.moduleContent:
Modules SP153 and SP154 are jointly designed to enable students to become knowledgeable and skilled in relation to:

• Critical inquiry, discovery, reading, writing and discussion in social science.
• Theorising, conceptualization, analysis and communication in social science.
• Effective use of key IT/software packages.

SP153 focuses on promoting and developing a sense of inquiry and discovery and the basic skills to engage in social science discourse. 

Key social science themes (e.g. poverty, power, equality, distribution, recognition, and policy itself) will form the substantive ground. 

There will be a series of class exercises to explore learning styles, support the development of individual learning plans, and develop a range of critical skills relating to social science scholarship, including digital skills.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module aims to enable students to develop an advanced knowledge of social policy. Content includes concepts and theories of welfare state origins, development and dynamics; theories of social justice, social need and relationship between social and economic policy, and public policy processes.

Policy analysis is the systematic evaluation of alternative solutions (policies) that are put in place or proposed to tackle social problems or achieve social goals. 
This module is designed to address two skill sets. 

First, to enable students develop the understandings and skills for policy analysis. This module critically examines how policy problems are represented and how problem streams, policy streams and political streams interact. Also, it presents a step-by-step guide to: identifying specific policies, examining the values involved, identifying stakeholders and decision-makers, assessing the resources needed to implement a policy, and measuring its effects on real life.
 
Second, this module aims to enable students develop relevant research skills for policy analysis such as defining and designing a policy research project; reviewing literature; framing policy research questions; writing a policy analysis proposal; exploring different prototypes for policy analysis projects.

This module aims to enable students develop an analysis of social policy from transnational and comparative perspectives. This module introduces a range of approaches to comparing social policies and developments in the welfare state across countries; it explores the relevance of "welfare regimes", approaches to comparing different branches of social policy; it examines the relevance of international bodies and social policy, and, in some detail, the Social Dimension of the European Union.

modules.moduleName: MENTAL HEALTH IN IRELAND: POLICY, PRACTICE AND CULTURAL CONSIDERATIONS
modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to provide a comprehensive and critical analysis of mental health policy in Ireland. The module addresses the evolution of mental health policies, assessing the implications for contemporary polices and strategies. In conjunction with this, there will be international comparisons drawn to other countries approaches to mental health. Key stakeholders from the field will contribute to the teaching and learning experience for students. Focusing on aspects of contemporary mental health policy, some notable topics that will be explored include the following: 

• The contentious nature of ‘mental health’ and 'mental illness'
• The stigma and social exclusion of people labelled as ‘mentally ill’
• The issues faced by both users and providers of mental health services.
• The challenges involved in delivering a recovery-oriented agenda from policy to practice

modules.moduleName: SOCIAL POLICY AND WELFARE STATES IN TIMES OF CHANGE
modules.moduleContent: This module explores change and continuity in welfare states, particularly since the 1970s. Themes such as crisis, austerity and welfare state reform, European integration, globalisation, demographic trends, environmental challenges and wider social change as they impinge on welfare states. The Irish case is explored in the wider context of change and continuity. The module also covers issues that have come to the fore in recent times such as household indebtedness, housing issues and policy responses.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate a clear grasp of alternative accounts of change and continuity in welfare states, particularly since the 1970s.
critically engage with scholarly debates on crisis, retrenchment and welfare state reform in a comparative perspective
Show an understanding of the influence of Europeanisation and globalisation on welfare states
demonstrate a critical understanding of arguments related to demographic and labour market developments as they relate to welfare states
outline wider social changes including social movements and ideological shifts in public poolicy as they impinge on welfare states

modules.moduleContent: This module includes critical review of contemporary Irish child policies and strategies. These national responses are placed in the context of our international obligations to children. The mechanisms by which these obligations are honoured, and their implementation monitored, is reviewed. The module follows these international obligations, through European and national policies to local level implementation, mapping the realisation of rights for Irish children. The module will variously draw on a range of aspects of contemporary child policies in Ireland such as child poverty, child protection, alternative care and the health and wellbeing needs and treatment of children.
learningOutcomes: Provide a comprehensive and critical analysis of child policies and policy infrastructure in Ireland.
Identify the influences of international policies on Irish child policy.
Outline the implications of contemporary practices and strategies.
modules.notes: This is a 5 credit optional module.

modules.moduleName: SOCIAL SCIENCE, THE SOCIAL PROFESSIONS AND THE FOUNDATIONS OF SOCIAL RESEARCH
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To provide students with detailed knowledge of the foundations of social science research and the context of the development of the social professions

Module Content: 
Development of the social sciences 
History, context, development and current issues in the social professions
Philosophy of social research

Module Objective: 
To provide students with the knowledge and competence to effectively analyse issues in organisational governance and take leadership to promote change 

Module Content: 
Issues in governance (State and civil society, corporatism; democratic network governance; deliberative and participative modes of governance)
Organisational culture and dynamics
Policy and politics in organisational governance 
Change theory
Leading Change

modules.moduleName: SPANISH FOR PROFESSIONAL AND BUSINESS PURPOSES
modules.moduleContent: This is an optional intermediate module in Spanish based on increasing students' familiarity with the use of Spanish in a professional environment. Professional Associations/Accreditation information Achieve linguistic competence commensurate with CEFRL level B 1. A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with EFR level B 1.
learningOutcomes: Read and extract information from business-related texts, charts and graphs, and summarise that information in oral and written form
Describe working environments, relationships with colleagues, employee and company profiles, business graphics, and business performance using appropriate vocabulary and structures

Communicate effectively with a variety of interlocutors, e.g. colleagues, customers, and executives, and demonstrate appropriate language skills for negotiation and mediation with these interlocutors in written and oral form
Produce some business-related texts, such as formal and informal emails and written reports
Justify a business idea or product in both written and oral form
Work effectively as part of a team to deliver a presentation in a coherent and meaningful manner
Demonstrate intercultural awareness and sociolinguistic competencies, as they relate to the business world

modules.moduleName: ARTISTS, MANIACS, AND MISFITS: MENTAL HEALTH AND SOCIAL ORDER IN LATIN AMERICA
modules.moduleContent: How have cinema and TV contributed to reshaping notions of mental health over history? How do cultural images of the mentally ill inform our social structures of medicine, education, and crime? This course will invite students to reflect on the role of culture in the administration of life and the discipline of behaviours and emotions. Through Latin American films and TV shows from the 20th and 21st centuries, we will study how fictional portrayals of mental health have popularized images of the “normal” and the “different” as a means of either reinforcing or challenging social order. Depictions of therapy, hospitals, schools, and prisons will allow us to interpret how the concept of mental health has varied in subsequent historical contexts and in response to pressing social debates. Looking at key events in Latin American history, such as political revolutions in the 1960s and feminist movements in the 2010s, we will examine how cultural narratives of mental health have contributed to either marginalizing or empowering “inconvenient” figures such as the rebel, the creative, the homosexual, and the neurodivergent.
learningOutcomes: Describe and interrogate the changing concept of mental health in subsequent historical periods.
Identify the links between cultural views of mental health and key socio-political events in Latin American history.
Formulate close-reading analyses of depictions of therapy, hospitals, schools, and prisons in cinema and TV.
Undertake independent research and engage with theories of mental health to present academic interpretations of Latin American cinema.
Develop teamwork and creative skills through group screenwriting projects.

modules.moduleName: SPANISH LITERATURE IN CONTEXT: TEACHING AND LEARNING
modules.moduleContent:
This module focuses particularly on studying Contemporary Spanish Literature in context. Its main emphasis is on the impact that cultural processes have in the creation of Spanish identities. It aims to build upon and develop key concepts raised within the level-two module “Introduction to Hispanic Literature” but can also be taken independently.  Its objectives are to apply fundamental teaching and learning principles in order to practice course design and classroom teaching.

**This 5-ECTS credit module may be used as part of the 15-ECTS credits in the study of literature (including drama, poetry, fiction, film studies etc.) required for accreditation by the Teaching Council of Ireland. All applications submitted for Teaching Council accreditation are, however, subject to the Teaching Council’s internal evaluation processes.

UPDATE September 2024: Change of semester: will be offered in semester 2
learningOutcomes: LO1: Critically evaluate the ways in which Spanish cultural texts portray, reflect and influence the formation of Spanish cultural identities.
LO2: Develop student’s ability to apply theoretical concepts to literary texts in Spanish.
LO3: Communicate effectively in Spanish orally and in writing for academic purposes.
LO4: Demonstrate both a detailed and overall understanding of oral and written documents in Spanish.
LO5: Apply and practice basic principles of teaching and learning to raise awareness of what an effective and efficient teaching requires.

modules.moduleContent: A course in translation focusing on basic theory and practical work.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate familiarity with basic methodological approaches to the study of translation.
Identify and explain different types of lexical non-equivalence.
Identify and apply relevant problem-solving techniques.
Analyse the register used in a source text.
Explain grammatical and cultural transpositions.
Apply relevant linguistic concepts and translation methodologies to specific texts.
Organize and structure information in a coherent and meaningful manner.

modules.moduleName: METHODOLOGIES FOR TEACHING SPANISH AS A FOREIGN LANGUAGE
modules.moduleContent: This module begins with a brief overview of some of the basic linguistic, cognitive and sociocultural concepts informing Second Language Acquisition (SLA) Theory, and a review of the dominant methodologies of teaching/learning foreign languages. The module concentrates on the didactics of language teaching. Students will learn how to create activities and to assess different components of the Spanish language (e.g. grammar, pronunciation, vocabulary) along with the components of effective teaching tools, such as lesson planning, student needs analysis, materials developments and evaluation tools.

An advanced course in Spanish which includes grammar, reading, composition, speaking and listening work.
This module enables students to achieve a linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level B2.2. A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level B2.2.
learningOutcomes: Compose essays on a range of subjects; construct communicative and creative texts based on work done in class; summarise the meaning of written and audio texts, write and deliver presentations.

Construct sentences and passages based on linguistic expressions encountered in class; complete exercises using various aspects of language; employ vocabulary suited to a particular topic, register or area of language use; use relevant linguistic concepts.

Identify linguistic elements of a given structure; identify how grammatical rules apply in a given structure; apply grammatical rules to written and spoken expression; determine how linguistic rules aid communication; detect patterns in linguistic usage; recognise grammatical rules in use.

Organise information in a coherent and meaningful manner; present information in a coherent and meaningful manner; apply relevant linguistic concepts to phrases, sentences and longer texts; demonstrate the linguistic meaning and usage of expressions; write creatively and analytically at an advanced level of linguistic complexity; communicate at an advanced level of linguistic complexity.

Produce a range of texts based on work covered in class; summarise the content of texts; analyse various aspects of texts; prepare a variety of compositions; deliver presentations on a particular topic; discuss texts, events and ideas in a competent and convincing manner.

Rearrange elements of phrases in a syntactically and grammatically correct manner; complete sentences accurately using the required linguistic skill; recognise and use expressions encountered in class; demonstrate the linguistic meaning and usage of expressions; manipulate phrases, sentences and passages in the application of grammatical rules.

Demonstrate advanced linguistic competence; communicate ideas, opinions and feelings of advanced complexity; transmit relevant information in a meaningful and coherent manner; appraise data, events and opinions; show intercultural awareness.

modules.moduleContent: Independent research on topic chosen in consultation with minor thesis supervisor.
learningOutcomes: 1. describe an academic framework for the analysis of specific texts, films or concepts
2. apply relevant theoretical concepts to the analysis of specific texts, films or concepts
3. draw conclusions about the material studied
4. organize and structure information in a coherent and meaningful manner
5. summarize material effectively, synthesize arguments and make clear points in a lucid and grammatical manner
6. demonstrate appropriate use of relevant subject-specific electronic resources and other library and archival resources

modules.moduleName: METHODOLOGIES FOR TEACHING SPANISH AS A FOREIGN LANGUAGE
modules.moduleContent: This module begins with a brief overview of some of the basic linguistic, cognitive and sociocultural concepts informing Second Language Acquisition (SLA) Theory, and a review of the dominant methodologies of teaching/learning foreign languages.  The module concentrates on the didactics of language teaching.  Students will learn how to create activities and to assess different components of the Spanish language (e.g. grammar, pronunciation, vocabulary) along with the components of effective teaching tools, such as lesson planning, student needs analysis, materials developments and evaluation tools.
learningOutcomes: Synthesize the main ideas on dominant methodologies of teaching/learning foreign languages.
Create didactic strategies to teach different components of the Spanish language.
Develop appropriate planning and materials for the L2 classroom.
Actively reflect upon their development as teaching professionals.

modules.moduleContent: In the first half of the module, the students receive an introduction to the grammar of Middle Welsh. In the latter part of the semester, a Middle Welsh text is read in the original.
learningOutcomes: By the end of this module, the students will have a fundamental understanding of Middle Welsh grammar (nominal and verbal morphology, the basics of syntax).
The students will be able to translate a simple Middle Welsh text.

modules.moduleContent: Reading an Old Irish text
learningOutcomes: Read, translate and critically interpret an Old Irish text in independent work.

modules.moduleName: UNIVERSAL CRITICAL SKILLS (INTRODUCTION TO UNIVERSITY LEARNING AND BEYOND) - A
modules.moduleContent:
The development of the critical skills will be based on exercises designed around weekly readings of appropriate journal articles or similar, these to be decided.
• Finding information (from printed materials and texts, academic databases, digital/electronic sources).
• Recording information through use of a range of media (e.g., visual, audio).
• Presenting and using different representations (e.g., text, diagrams, tables, charts, images, posters).
• Planning and writing academic essays.
• Academic writing skills, styles and conventions.
• Understanding plagiarism.
• Self-assessment and reflection.
• Critical thinking (forming an argument, examining evidence, judgement and reasoning, drawing conclusions).
• Creative thinking (solving problems, generating ideas, discussing solutions and possible outcomes, approaching tasks from different perspectives/ viewpoints).
• Organisation and time management (making plans, allocating tasks/ roles, designing schedules).
• Memory and approaches to learning.
• Working in groups.
• Preparing for assessments.
• Making oral presentations and public speaking.
• Awareness of supports and initiatives for students at Maynooth University.
learningOutcomes: Develop academic research skills including the ability to find, analyse, interpret, and evaluate information and sources.
Develop confidence in public speaking through an awareness of the affordances of technology and rhetorical communication strategies.
Develop the ability to engage in professional writing through an understanding of genre conventions and the role of texts in professional workspaces.
Appreciate the value of a university education and acknowledge their responsibility for their own education taking advantage of the supports, resources, and opportunities available.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: UNIVERSAL CRITICAL SKILLS (INTRODUCTION TO UNIVERSITY LEARNING AND BEYOND) - B
modules.moduleContent:
This course focuses on digital literacies. Students will:

• Be able to critically evaluate online information sources for credibility, accuracy, and relevance, demonstrating proficiency in navigating digital information landscapes.
• Examine how digital technologies influence knowledge production, dissemination, and access, critically evaluating the impact of digital inequalities on society and exploring potential strategies for addressing these disparities.
• Demonstrate proficiency in creating digital content across various platforms and mediums. They will apply principles of effective digital storytelling, media design, and content creation tools to communicate ideas effectively in the digital realm.
• Develop a foundational understanding of artificial intelligence, including its capabilities, limitations, ethical implications, and societal impact, enabling them to critically evaluate AI technologies and their implications in various contexts.
learningOutcomes: Strengthen quantitative reasoning and numeracy skills including a basic understanding of descriptive statistics.
Learn the principles of data visualisation; gain confidence in interpreting data visualisations; develop skills to create reliable visual narratives with data.

Appreciate the advantages and disadvantages of working collaboratively; understand the characteristics of effective teamwork and group communication; approach teamwork with empathy for individual members of and appreciate how diversity strengthens teamwork.
Learn how to use electronic tools to professionally organise and manage team projects.
Transfer academic writing knowledge and practice developed within the module to rhetorical situations beyond the classroom and academic audiences.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module is the first in a series of modules, which will provide students with foundational knowledge and skills relevant to coaching, exercise and sport science. The aim of this module is to provide students with a framework around which they can begin to develop their understanding and skills of fitness, exercise and coaching, within a safe, ethical and inclusive environment for all people.

In this module, students will be introduced to a framework for long term development in physical activity and sport, which begins by developing physical literacy to provide the basic skills to be active for life and the foundation for those who choose to pursue training for performance. Students will learn the key principles of fitness and develop foundational skills in exercise prescription, delivery, progression and regression. This module will also introduce students to philosophy and science of coaching, including leadership and ethical considerations, which ensures a safe and inclusive environment for people of all genders, ages, abilities, cultural and demographic backgrounds.
learningOutcomes: Understand the philosophy of long term development in physical activity and sport
Describe key principles of fitness, exercise prescription, delivery, progression and regression
Discuss leadership and ethical considerations in sport and exercise to ensure an inclusive environment for all people
Describe the different philosophies and scientific principles of coaching.

modules.moduleContent: This module will introduce students with a broad foundation in the area of food, and the relationship between diet and health. It will provide an introduction to the key aspects of the composition and manufacture of the major food and beverage types, discuss food safety, and some of the health related issues around food choice and consumption. The module will introduce briefly the key food types, the balanced diets, food labelling, sustainability in the food sector, the concept of functional foods, and key policy issues in the area. The module will also introduce some of the developing trends in the area of food and nutrition.
learningOutcomes: Understand healthy eating guidelines and role of nutrients in health and disease.
Read and interpret a food label.
Demonstrate an awareness of the contribution of these foods to a healthy diet.
Identify key food safety issues, including the pathogens and spoilage microorganisms in foods and the conditions under which they will grow.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to introduce students to the major physiological systems of the human body, the concept of homeostasis, sources of energy, physiological responses to exercise and interaction with food and nutrition.

This module will begin by introducing students to the major physiological systems of the human body, including the cardiovascular, respiratory, gastrointestinal, urinary and endocrine systems. Students will learn about the concept of homeostasis and how these systems interact to maintain equilibrium. The sources of energy available in the human body will also be covered, along with metabolic and physiological responses to exercise. This module will also cover how the different food components (proteins, vitamins, carbohydrates, fat) impact on human physiology, how they are processed and used in the body. It will also briefly describe the effect of deficiencies in the diet of these components.
learningOutcomes: Describe the major physiological systems of the human body
Understand the concept of homeostasis and how physiological systems interact to achieve equilibrium
Describe the sources of energy in the human body
Explain the physiological and metabolic responses of the human body to exercise.
Explain the role of different food components in the body, and the impact of a deficiency in these components.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is the second in a series of modules, which provide students with foundational knowledge and skills relevant to coaching, exercise, and sport science. The aim of this module is to further develop a framework of understanding and skills relevant to  fitness, exercise and coaching by developing students’ ability to conduct fitness assessments, prescribe training programmes, and coach an individual based upon their health, fitness and sports performance goals.

Building on their foundational knowledge of fitness testing, students will develop their ability to test key components of fitness and athletic performance relevant to different sports and activities. Students will learn how to interpret and communicate fitness testing results to an individual and use this information to set relevant goals and provide an individualised exercise program. Students will also learn about the key principles of strength and conditioning in order to be able to prescribe and deliver a range of exercises through an individualised training program, which can be progressed over time to safely and effectively achieve health, fitness and sports performance goals.
learningOutcomes: Describe and test the key components of athletic performance required in different sports and activities
Interpret test results to design and individualise an exercise program to meet specific fitness goals
Develop a library of different exercises that can be progressed and regressed to achieve individualised health, fitness and sports performance goals
Understand and apply the key principles of strength and conditioning to prescribe and progress exercises to achieve individualised health, fitness and sports performance goals.

modules.moduleContent: An introduction to the way sociologists examine the social world by looking at the way they do research work. The type of research questions sociologists have addressed and how they did their research work. It discusses some major pieces of sociological research, the work of some specific sociologists and the research methods employed by them.
learningOutcomes: Detail the principles and methods of social research
Analyse the questions and approaches addressed by sociologists in specific pieces of social research
Assess the merit and limits of various social research methodologies
Critically evaluate the contribution of social research to understanding society, social behaviour and social problems
Explain and apply key concepts in research design

modules.moduleName: STRUCTURES OF INEQUALITY: CLASS, RACE & GENDER
modules.moduleContent: Social and economic divisions in terms of class, race/ethnicity and gender as well as some of the central issues of identity, culture and politics surrounding them. Theoretical approaches to these inequalities will be reviewed, both separately and in the context of their interrelationships with one another. How historical, cultural and institutional factors come to shape individual experiences.
learningOutcomes: Explain the major theories and key concepts used by sociologists  to analyse  the social divisions of class race/ethnicity and gender as structural phenomena and sources of identity
Critically assess the role of historical, cultural and institutional factors in shaping the patterning of social divisions in society and the experience of these aspects of identity
Evaluate  the empirical trends and patterns identified by sociologists exploring structures of inequality including their interconnections
Develop an independent and critical perspective on the sociological analyses of social and economic divisions and inequalities
Apply major theories and concepts to the analysis of social inequalities

modules.moduleContent: The evolution of the mass media (both old and new) and issues of ownership and control, production process and media content, and media audiences. The current restructuring of the media and culture industries in Ireland, and across the European Union. The outlook for public service broadcasting The potential of the mass media to act as an instrument of cultural integration.
learningOutcomes: Explain and apply the major theories and concepts in the sociological analysis of the mass media.
Detail and analyse the empirical trends and patterns in the relationship between media and society.
Assess the role of both the state and the marketplace in shaping the modern media and culture industries.
Develop an independent perspective on issues within the sociological analysis of mass media.
Develop their own perspective on the potential of the mass media to act as an instrument of cultural integration.

modules.moduleName: RESEARCH DESIGN AND SURVEY, FIELDWORK & DOCUMENTARY METHODS
modules.moduleContent: This course develops your skills in research methods moving from understanding the major concepts and research techniques employed in research design to formulating a research project as part of a team, and understanding what methods might best be used in context of different research questions. This course explores how to carry out three primary research methods: online, visual and archival texts and documents which are often used within historical, comparative and ethnographic research methodologies; the techniques of survey research methods and the applied assessment and analysis of quantitative data: fieldwork techniques in qualitative research, key research methods in sociology, the principles and practice of fieldwork through hands-on learning as students design and carry out a piece of fieldwork research together with a group of other students.
learningOutcomes: Detail the major concepts and research techniques employed in research design
Formulate a research project as part of a team, and understand what methods might best be used in context of different research questions
Understand the field of survey research methods and appraise the merits and limits, including the ethical and political implications of the survey research method approach
Critically evaluate the contribution of survey research to the discipline of sociology and the study of society, social behaviour and social problems
Discuss and apply basic principles in documentary research on physical, digital and visual texts and detail key methodological concerns in  documentary data
Assess the strengths and limitations of documentary research methods of data collection and understand and use documentary data within ethnographic, comparative and historical research methodologies
Detail the theoretical traditions and areas of practice within fieldwork and assess the contributions of fieldwork to sociological analysis
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: In this course we move beyond common sense and ‘natural’ explanations of sexuality to show how it is a social construct of competing scientific, religious and legal discourses. We focus specifically on the state’s regulatory role in the field of sexuality and how it has contributed to the creation of sexual insiders and outsiders through the extension of sexual citizenship (e.g. marriage, civil partnerships, hate crime laws etc).  We examine resistances to this regulatory code governing sexuality and how it has contributed to the construction of sexual identities and social movements. The course also focuses on contemporary debates around the political economy of sexuality exploring controversies around the criminalisation/decriminalisation of sex work and the sexualisation of children.
learningOutcomes: Understand how sexuality is shaped and constructed in social, legal, political and religious contexts.
Identify the state's regulatory role in sexuality and how it changes over time.
Critically compare key thinkers within the field of sexuality and how they theorize the shifting discourses that govern sexuality.
Assess how gendered conceptions of law impact upon the discourse and practice of sexuality in everyday life.

modules.moduleContent: This module will consist of seminars, presentations, workshops and/or master class sessions tailored for PhD students. The Summer/Winter School may be comprised of thematically specialized and/or inter-disciplinary sessions.
learningOutcomes: engage in group discussion and communicate in group
participate in team work exercises throughout the process
explore and understand the thematic topic under discussion
be able to critically analyse  the thematic topic relevant to the sessions .

modules.moduleContent: Seminar discussions on the craft of ethnographic writing, especially focused on descriptively integrating your primary data into an academic argument.
learningOutcomes: Work on writing a chapter of the thesis

modules.moduleContent: Seminar discussions on the craft of ethnographic writing, especially focused on descriptively integrating your primary data into an academic argument.
learningOutcomes: Review and draft a full thesis for submission

modules.moduleContent: This module will require a student to undertake a recognized module at a recognized international third level institution.
learningOutcomes: engage in group discussion and communicate in international setting
participate in team work exercises and understand cultural contexts
explore and understand the thematic topic under discussion
be able to critically analyse  the thematic topic relevant to the session
learning outcomes defined by international third level institution.

modules.moduleContent: Students will organise, or assist in the organisation of, an event relevant to the field. Students who wish to make such arrangements will be facilitated, subject to availability.
learningOutcomes: Able to analyze the ‘field’ of research on which their thesis is based; assessment is based on written essay summarizing the readings conducted.

modules.moduleContent: This module aims to develop and apply concepts learned in SP151 in the Irish case. It includes the evolution of the welfare state in Ireland, key periods or phases of development; how the Irish welfare state has developed to the present level; how social policy today reflects developments, past debates and decisions, and the main pillars of the welfare state (health, housing, employment, income maintenance, personal social services and education). An additional component of this module is an overview of key concepts and themes in equality.

modules.moduleName: CRITICAL SKILLS FOR SOCIAL SCIENCE 2: IDEAS, ANALYSIS AND COMMUNICATION
modules.moduleContent:
Modules SP153 and SP154 are jointly designed to enable students to become knowledgeable and skilled in relation to:

• Critical inquiry, discovery, reading, writing and discussion in social science.
• Theorising, conceptualization, analysis and communication in social science.
• Effective use of key IT/software packages.

SP154 focuses on the development of skills in theorising, conceptualizing and analysing and in linking academic scholarship with policy and action.

It will use practical examples and topical issues to explore key themes and ideas in social science, social research and social policy; and will enable students to develop their skills of analysis and communication.  As in SP153, there will be a digital skills component.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: THE SOCIAL PROFESSIONS (SOCIAL WORK, COMMUNITY AND YOUTH WORK)
modules.moduleContent:
Introduction to the theory, values, traditions and practice of the social professions and the associated areas of theory and policy in which they function, specifically: social work theory, values, skills; community and youth work theory, values, skills. perspectives on the commonalities and differences between the social professions; case studies of the social professions in action; debates on professionalisation; supervised professional practice and its role and application in different professions; the wider policy context of the social professions; comparative perspectives.

** Social Work Staff have advised that MSoc Social Work applicants are expected to have taken this module **

This module focuses on the links between socio-economic inequality (redistribution) and group/identity-based inequalities (recognition). Conventionally the responses of social policy to these dimensions of inequality are compartmentalised. The module looks at the challenges and opportunities for developing a more integrated approach, for example in relation to ethnicity and poverty.
learningOutcomes: show a critical understanding of different approaches to concepts of inequality
demonstrate an understanding of the links and overlaps between different forms of inequality
show a critical appreciation of the challenges and opportunities for integrating social policy responses to inequality

modules.moduleContent:
This module explores concepts and theories related to ageing, including the development of concepts of social inclusion and social citizenship in old age. It explores the context of social and demographic change, particularly related to ageing, pensions policy, health and long-term care. It covers alternative theories on the significance of ageing societies, the efficiency, distributional and sustainability implications of alternative policy choices. It looks at the issues of ageing and social policy in comparative and European contexts.

This is a 2.5 Credit module (1 hour per week for 12 weeks).
learningOutcomes: appraise the social and demographic context and main lines of policy development relating to ageing and older people
explore the implications of alternative policies for old age in relation to efficiency, distribution and sustainability
criticallly evaluate the main features of pensions policy, health care and long term care in Ireland
critically analyse Irish policy on ageing in comparative and European contexts

modules.moduleName: EQUALITY AND INCLUSION – RIGHTS, RECOGNITION AND REDISTRIBUTION
modules.moduleContent: Theories of rights, recognition and redistribution; theoretical contributions focused on linking these agendas; critique of policy initiatives such as equality proofing, rights proving, poverty proofing; mainstreaming and specific targeted policy responses; opportunities for linkages challenges in linkages, policy implications of linkages; recognition and re-distribution; case study examples in areas of poverty and social exclusion, gender, age, disability, race, sexuality, class, integration and cohesion.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an advanced critical understanding of issues of human rights, identity based minorities and socio-economic minorities.
Demonstrate an advanced critical understanding of historic and contemporary separate social policy responses to issues of human rights, identity based minorities and socio-economic minorities.
Demonstrate an advanced critical understanding of current debates and initiatives to link issues of human rights, identity based minorities and socio-economic minorities.
Show an advanced critical appreciation of the challenges and opportunities for joined up social policy responses to rights, recognition and redistribution.
Develop integrated social policy proposals, bringing together issues of rights, recognition and redistribution for special groups and agendas.
modules.notes: This is a 5 credit core module

modules.moduleContent:
The module content will include philosophy and concepts of social research; research design; framing research proposals; undertaking literature reviews; research planning; quantitative research methods; qualitative research methods; using research for social policy purposes; action, participatory and empowering social research; becoming a reflective researcher. 

Content will be delivered on a parallel track to student’s application of learning on a research dissertation, supported by individual supervision.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an advanced critical understanding of a range of social research methods including quantitative and qualitative methods.
Use a range of social research techniques for policy purposes.
Critically evaluate published social research.
Demonstrate a clear understanding of the appropriateness of a range of applied forms of research, including action, participatory and empowering research.
Draft a sound, workable and feasible policy research proposal.
Successfully complete a research ethics application.
Complete a social policy focused dissertation including literature review, methodology, findings and conclusions chapters.
modules.notes: This is a 30 credit core module.

modules.moduleName: PARTICIPATORY AND FEMINIST ANALYSIS AND RESEARCH
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
To provide students with the theoretical insights and practical knowledge to utilise participatory and feminist analysis and methodologies in research contexts

Module Content: 
Philosophical underpinnings and analysis 
Principles of participatory approaches
Feminist analysis and action
Methodological issues: potential and challenges

Module Objective: 
To provide students with the necessary insight and analysis to engage with current debates in social research and utilise them effectively in their individual research process

Module Content: 
Epistemological/Ontological debates
Ethics in social research 
Insider and outsider research 
Politics  and processes of practitioner research
Researcher reflexivity 
Politics of research production
Themes and issues in the research process

Module Objective:
To provide students with the knowledge and competence required to design a rigorous and original Doctoral level research project 

Module Content: 
Formulating the research question 
Research design issues and considerations
Methodology and Methods (Quantitative and Qualitative)
Project Planning and Implementation
Analysing and utilising research findings

modules.moduleName: SOCIAL POLICY AND THE SOCIAL PROFESSIONS - CRITICAL PERSPECTIVES
modules.moduleContent: Critical social policy focusing on equality and social inclusion, sociology of the professions, debates about professionalisation and de-professionalisation.

A continuation of the beginners' course in Spanish (SPA151) for students with little or no preparation in the Spanish language including oral expression and group work in class; written expression exercises; grammatical exercises; laboratory work; conversation classes. Additional work with self-access material is also recommended.
The module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages at level A2.1 where the emphasis is on achieving a pre-intermediate level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing). A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A2.1.
learningOutcomes: LO1: Construct brief essays on a variety of subjects; translate sentences from Spanish to English; give examples of how phrases or sentences illustrate grammar rules.
LO2: Construct sentences based on grammatical rules; complete exercises using various aspects of grammar; employ vocabulary suited to a particular topic or area of language.

LO3: Identify how rules on issues such as agreement apply in a given phrase or sentence; determine how rules can aid in the conjugation of verbs; detect patterns in the use of tenses, prepositions, or other areas of language.

LO4: Compose a variety of texts based on work done in classes; Rearrange elements of sentences in a syntactically and grammatically correct manner; complete sentences using the required language skill correctly, summarise the meaning of texts.
LO5: Complete continuous assessment and class assignments correctly; answer exam questions with relevant and precise information based on course work throughout the semester.

modules.moduleContent:
Continuing Spanish for students with previous knowledge of the language. Additional work with self-access material is recommended.
Supplemented by language lab and conversation classes. The course includes oral expression practice, group work in class, written expression exercises, grammatical exercises.

This module enables students to achieve a linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level B1.1. A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level B1.1.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate familiarity with the basic grammatical rules of the Spanish language.
Apply relevant linguistic concepts to written and oral work.
Organize and structure information in a coherent and meaningful manner in the Spanish language.
Identify and use Spanish vocabulary appropriate to this level.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at students who are interested in learning Catalan language and culture alongside the core subjects of their degree programmes. It will appeal to all types of students: those who are already studying Spanish or French and would like to learn a new romance language; and those without prior experience of learning romance languages and who will find in Catalan an exciting entry to the Mediterranean linguistic and cultural world. Practical reading, writing, speaking and listening skills will be taught through in-person classes with an experienced tutor who is native speaker. The teaching and learning will be student-centred and based on a communicative approach. Classroom-based learning will be complemented by weekly exercises and independent work.

Please note: This module is designed for students who have no previous knowledge of the Catalan language.

This module is made available to students of Maynooth University with the financial support of the Institut Ramon Llull.
learningOutcomes: Understand commonly used sentences and expressions that relate to basic needs.
Exchange basic information about family and topics related to daily life.
Describe, in a simple way, experiences and aspects of their cultural background and environment in relation to their immediate needs.
Use simple linguistic structures and vocabulary to fulfil basic linguistic uses.
Demonstrate some basic knowledge of some Catalan writers, musicians, and/or politicians, and of Catalan geography.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at students who are interested in continuing to learn Portuguese alongside the core subjects of their degree programmes. It will appeal to students who have a genuine interest in deepening their understanding of the Portuguese language and culture, as well as those who are motivated to further develop their Portuguese language skills and (inter)cultural competence to enhance their employment prospects in Ireland and further afield. There is some emphasis on the linguistic and cultural diversity of the Portuguese-speaking world. Practical reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills will be taught through in-person classes with an expert in Portuguese as a Foreign/Second Language who is a native speaker. Classroom-based learning will be complemented by independent work to be completed weekly.

This module has been made available to students of Maynooth University with the financial support of the Camões Institute.
learningOutcomes: Explain and summarise key information from relatively straightforward written, audio and video texts related to situations from everyday life that contain words and expressions dealt with in class;

Exchange relatively straightforward information in spoken or written form about family, occupation, plans, holidays, daily and leisure activities, and/or other familiar matters relating to everyday life;

Produce short written texts using familiar structures and expressions and communicate in relatively straightforward terms on topics of relevance to everyday life and using vocabulary dealt with in class.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides students with a comprehensive understanding of the workings of language with special emphasis on Spanish. Students will become familiarised with basic notions of Spanish grammar from a practical point of view. To this end, the module will address topics such as the variety of sounds of the Spanish language, the way words and sentences are structured in this language, and the linguistic diversity of the Spanish speaking world. A whole array of practical activities will be proposed to better understand the elements of the Spanish language. Although the contents of this module will follow an applied approach, the emphasis is not so much on language practice but on structural language analysis.
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms some key linguistic characteristics of the Spanish language.
Interpret and explain in English key grammatical terms introduced in the module, using examples where appropriate.
Interpret and explain in English key grammatical terms introduced in the module, using examples where appropriate.
Write responses to exam questions, making convincing arguments in a clear and structured manner and giving examples where appropriate.

modules.moduleName: MULTILINGUALISM & BILINGUALISM IN THE SPANISH SPEAKING WORLD
modules.moduleContent: This module offers an overview of the Spanish-speaking world with a focus on the political and social issues related to the dynamics of bilingual and multilingual societies. The course begins by presenting some key concepts in the fields of bilingualism. Later, it moves into analysing the social aspects of multilingual and bilingual societies such as the relationship between language, power, education, and identity. Students will then explore the main sociolinguistic characteristics of multi/bilingual communities in Hispanoamérica, Spain, and the United States.
learningOutcomes: Synthesize the main ideas and concepts on current understanding of multi and bilingual communities;
Develop a critical view on the situation of the Spanish language in the World;
Actively reflect upon the complexity of bilingualism as a sociopolitical and cultural phenomenon;
Research, develop and articulate in writing balanced and well-structured arguments.

modules.moduleName: SING IT LIKE SHAKIRA! LATINX STARS ON THE GLOBAL STAGE
modules.moduleContent: How has Latinx identity become a cultural brand and multi-millionaire industry? How can we relate the dynamics of the global media to the political ambitions and social dilemmas of Latin America? This course will introduce students to some of the most relevant events in Latin American history by focusing on the lives and works of its most impactful celebrities. Our list will include music and movie stars such as Shakira and Jennifer López, athletes like Diego Maradona and Lionel Messi, but also political figures-turned-pop icons such as Evita and Che Guevara. In this way, we will regard Latin American cultural identity as inherently global, marked by the legacy of colonialism as much as by its contemporary circulation in the international media. We will examine the role of music, cinema, and entertainment as paradigmatic means of expressing and debating issues of race, gender, sexuality, class, and (neo)colonialism.
learningOutcomes: Identify key cultural debates and social conflicts in Latin American history.
Examine and interrogate the figure of the Latinx celebrity in the global media.
Formulate close-reading analyses of films, music videos, lyrics, and TV shows.
Undertake independent research and present interpretations of Latin American culture through academic writing.
Demonstrate teamwork skills through creative group projects.

modules.moduleContent: This module is intended to provide students with a more detailed knowledge of Portuguese vocabulary and grammatical structures. It focuses on the structures that allow for clear expression of thoughts and opinions and on the structures necessary for the narration of events, descriptions and predictions. The use of newspaper articles and simple literary texts in Portuguese aims to present students with authentic and varied texts.
learningOutcomes: Express opinions on abstract/cultural matters in a limited way;
Offer advice within a known area;
Understand instructions or public announcements;
Understand the general meaning of non-routine information within a familiar area;
Write letters and make notes on familiar or predictable matters;
Write elaborate texts related to personal information;
Demonstrate linguistic competence commensurate with CEFRL level A 2.1 (on achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module).

modules.moduleName: HISPANIC LITERATURE AND CULTURE IN THE 21ST CENTURY
modules.moduleContent: This module will provide students with the opportunity to explore selected aspects of Spanish-language literary and cultural production from recent decades. During this time, literature and culture have undergone change in response to globalisation, technological advance, and the social, political and ecological challenges that have shaped 21st-century life so far. The module will focus on specific literary and audiovisual texts, in addition to digital and/or web-based media.
