Students taking the module will be supplied with the critical frameworks and concepts that will enable them to engage in close readings of these texts, and to thereby uncover how they relate to 21st-century life and what they can tell us about the present historical moment.
learningOutcomes: Identify, interpret, and analyse a wide range of Spanish-language literary texts and cultural productions relating to a range of contemporary concerns.
Situate Spanish-language literary and cultural texts within their wider social, economic, political and cultural contexts.
Identify and critically assess texts according to a range of relevant theoretical frameworks.
Develop media literate and critically aware strategies for online research and online engagement in the target language.
Build effective, efficient and well-organised project-oriented groups, founded on principles of collaboration, mutual advancement, and intercultural communication.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: LABOUR, CAPITAL AND NATURAL RESOURCES IN LATIN AMERICAN CINEMA
modules.moduleContent: How does the exploitation of human and natural resources that marked Latin America’s colonization still shape the social and environmental challenges of our contemporaneity? How have local and grassroots forms of resistance responded to the effects of global capital and extractivist economies? This course studies the social, political, and environmental impact that different variants of economic production have had in Latin America since the colonial period until today. We will use cinema as a means of exploring national and regional economies from a renewed angle that conflates questions of (neo)colonialism, gender, race, and class. Each week will be dedicated to examining a fictional film or documentary focused on a specific country and a specific natural or human resource, ranging from rubber in Brazil and sugar plantations in Cuba to financial speculation in Argentina and migrant labour in Mexico. In this way, cinema will invite us to reflect on the potential of art to debate the most pressing issues of our times, such as the displacement of indigenous communities, drug trafficking, sexual exploitation, and the climate crisis.
learningOutcomes: Identify the decisive social conflicts and environmental effects that have marked Latin America since the colonial period until today.
Describe and criticize the specificity of regional economies with regard to the exploitation of key natural and human resources.
Evaluate and interpret the position and status of Latin America in the global economic and geopolitical map.
Formulate close-reading analyses of depictions of work and natural resources in Latin America cinema.
Undertake independent research and write academic essays focused on the interconnection between cultural and economic production.
Demonstrate interpersonal and presentation skills through group work and audio-visual projects.

modules.moduleContent: This course examines contemporary Chicana/o theatre, novels, cinema and visual art with reference to key issues in Chicana/o culture, such as political and metaphorical borders, code switching and caló, gender and class divisions, and subaltern and transnational identities.
learningOutcomes: Identify key issues in Chicana/o literature and cinema, with a particular focus on examining developments in Chicana/o culture from the era of Brown Power in the 1960s and 1970s to the present.

LO2
Describe different types of Chicana/o filmmaking and writing, from the 1960s and 1970s to the present.

LO3
Distinguish between different tendencies in Chicana/o writing and filmmaking, such as the non-fiction novel, poetry, theatre, the novel and film adaptations from literary texts and documentary films.

LO5
Critique and evaluate texts and films in lectures and tutorials with reference to theories of Chicana/o identity and analyse the significance of issues such as code switching, gender and generational conflicts, and the interaction between Mexico and the Chicana/o community.

LO6 
Write an essay or essays, making convincing arguments based on reading of primary and secondary material and explaining key issues in Chicana/o studies.

modules.moduleContent: The students produce a thesis of c. 15.000 words in indipendent work on a research topic that they have developed in consultation with the supervisor.
learningOutcomes: Engage proficiently in reading and writing on topics related to Early Medieval Ireland.
Critically appraise primary and secondary literature relating to Early Medieval Ireland.
Demonstrate effective and efficient research, writing and study skills.

modules.moduleContent: A considerable part of the extant literature of medieval Ireland is dedicated to learned matter, that is, to subjects that could be termed academic or scientific in modern understanding. These subjects include grammar and the philosophy of language, biblical exegesis, geography, astronomy, history, law and legal commentary, and poetic composition. In this module, select examples of texts treating these topics will be read, critically analysed and evaluated in their literary and historical context.
learningOutcomes: Familiarity with major primary sources and secondary literature on that topic.
Development of analytical and critical skills necessary for the interpreting texts.
Development of writing and presentation skills.

modules.moduleContent:
The students are introduced to the Irish manuscript tradition, especially those manuscripts that are relevant for medieval literature of Ireland. The students will learn how to read and transcribe medieval Irish manuscripts.

A basic knowledge of medieval Irish palaeography is necessary in order to take this module.
learningOutcomes: Identify the major writing styles in Early Ireland.
Read and transcribe from Irish manuscripts.
Demonstrate familiarity with the main notae and abbreviations used in the medieval manuscript tradition.
Demonstrate familiarity with the major Irish manuscripts.

modules.moduleContent: Reading an Old Irish text
learningOutcomes: Read, translate and critically interpret an Old Irish text in independent work.

modules.moduleContent: For students of the structured PhD programme this module will expand their expertise in palaeography and codicology. This is exemplified through hands-on experience of reading, translating, interpreting and understanding selected Medieval Irish Manuscripts and texts.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate efficient reading and interpretation of palaeographic characters and symbols.
Detail and exemplify effective systems of textual editing.
Systematically examine and interpret Medieval Irish Manuscripts as cultural artifacts.
Confidently engage in self-directed research and textual editing of selected Medieval Irish Manuscript material content.

modules.moduleName: UNIVERSAL CRITICAL SKILLS (INTRODUCTION TO UNIVERSITY LEARNING AND BEYOND) - A
modules.moduleContent:
The development of the critical skills will be based on exercises designed around weekly readings of appropriate journal articles or similar, these to be decided.
• Finding information (from printed materials and texts, academic databases, digital/electronic sources).
• Recording information through use of a range of media (e.g., visual, audio).
• Presenting and using different representations (e.g., text, diagrams, tables, charts, images, posters).
• Planning and writing academic essays.
• Academic writing skills, styles and conventions.
• Understanding plagiarism.
• Self-assessment and reflection.
• Critical thinking (forming an argument, examining evidence, judgement and reasoning, drawing conclusions).
• Creative thinking (solving problems, generating ideas, discussing solutions and possible outcomes, approaching tasks from different perspectives/ viewpoints).
• Organisation and time management (making plans, allocating tasks/ roles, designing schedules).
• Memory and approaches to learning.
• Working in groups.
• Preparing for assessments.
• Making oral presentations and public speaking.
• Awareness of supports and initiatives for students at Maynooth University.
learningOutcomes: Develop academic research skills including the ability to find, analyse, interpret, and evaluate information and sources.
Develop confidence in public speaking through an awareness of the affordances of technology and rhetorical communication strategies.
Develop the ability to engage in professional writing through an understanding of genre conventions and the role of texts in professional workspaces.
Appreciate the value of a university education and acknowledge their responsibility for their own education taking advantage of the supports, resources, and opportunities available.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: UNIVERSAL CRITICAL SKILLS (INTRODUCTION TO UNIVERSITY LEARNING AND BEYOND) - B
modules.moduleContent:
Upon successful completion of the second semester of Critical Skills, we expect that students will:
• Strengthen quantitative reasoning and numeracy skills including a basic understanding of descriptive statistics.
• Learn the principles of data visualisation; gain confidence in interpreting data visualisations; develop skills to create reliable visual narratives with data.
• Appreciate the advantages and disadvantages of working collaboratively; understand the characteristics of effective teamwork and group communication; approach teamwork with empathy for individual members of and appreciate how diversity strengthens teamwork.
• Learn how to use electronic tools to professionally organise and manage team projects.
• Transfer academic writing knowledge and practice developed within the module to rhetorical situations beyond the classroom and academic audiences.
learningOutcomes: Be able to critically evaluate online information sources for credibility, accuracy, and relevance, demonstrating proficiency in navigating digital information landscapes.

Examine how digital technologies influence knowledge production, dissemination, and access, critically evaluating the impact of digital inequalities on society and exploring potential strategies for addressing these disparities.

Demonstrate proficiency in creating digital content across various platforms and mediums. They will apply principles of effective digital storytelling, media design, and content creation tools to communicate ideas effectively in the digital realm.

Develop a foundational understanding of artificial intelligence, including its capabilities, limitations, ethical implications, and societal impact, enabling them to critically evaluate AI technologies and their implications in various contexts.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: UNIVERSAL CRITICAL SKILLS (INTRODUCTION TO UNIVERSITY LEARNING AND BEYOND) - B
modules.moduleContent:
Upon successful completion of the second semester of Critical Skills, we expect that students will:
• Strengthen quantitative reasoning and numeracy skills including a basic understanding of descriptive statistics.
• Learn the principles of data visualisation; gain confidence in interpreting data visualisations; develop skills to create reliable visual narratives with data.
• Appreciate the advantages and disadvantages of working collaboratively; understand the characteristics of effective teamwork and group communication; approach teamwork with empathy for individual members of and appreciate how diversity strengthens teamwork.
• Learn how to use electronic tools to professionally organise and manage team projects.
• Transfer academic writing knowledge and practice developed within the module to rhetorical situations beyond the classroom and academic audiences.
learningOutcomes: Be able to critically evaluate online information sources for credibility, accuracy, and relevance, demonstrating proficiency in navigating digital information landscapes.

Examine how digital technologies influence knowledge production, dissemination, and access, critically evaluating the impact of digital inequalities on society and exploring potential strategies for addressing these disparities.

Demonstrate proficiency in creating digital content across various platforms and mediums. They will apply principles of effective digital storytelling, media design, and content creation tools to communicate ideas effectively in the digital realm.

Develop a foundational understanding of artificial intelligence, including its capabilities, limitations, ethical implications, and societal impact, enabling them to critically evaluate AI technologies and their implications in various contexts.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to build upon students’ understanding of the interlinked functions of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems and the mechanisms underpinning their physiological functions. Specifically, students will be provided with an understanding of the responses each of these physiological systems have to different exercise intensities. Students will also be introduced to the major diseases and conditions that can impact the optimal function of these systems, including hypertension, COPD, and acute and chronic heart failure. Students will also be provided with an understanding of the behavioural and lifestyle factors that can influence these systems’ structures, including diet, ageing, exercise, and smoking.

NOTE: This is not a medical module.  Clinical diagnoses are outside the scope of this module.
learningOutcomes: Describe the mechanisms underpinning the integrated responses of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems to different exercise intensities
Understand the interlinked physiological principles underpinning cardiovascular and respiratory homeostasis

Describe the impact of various lifestyle and behavioral factors impacting the function of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems, including exercise, ageing, hypertension, COPD, and acute and chronic heart failure
Describe optimal cardiovascular and respiratory mechanics, including the respiratory exchange cycle and the cardiac cycle.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to how sociologists explore and explain the key features of society. The module explores how contemporary Irish society is formed through global interdependencies and connections, including topics such as environment and climate change, migration, global culture, new technologies and economic globalisation. How are the boundaries and self-identity of Ireland changing? How is Ireland similar to and different from other societies?  
The module also introduces students to how sociologists use research methods to investigate and explain key features and puzzles in the social world. The module analyses real world sociological questions using examples of evidence such as observation of social situations, analysis of cultural objects, surveys of the public and interviews and focus groups. Students are given the tools to begin the journey to become researchers of the social world.
learningOutcomes: Understand and evaluate core theories and concepts used by sociologists in understanding global changes and how they affect national societies.
Understand how sociologists gather, analyse and interpret data and evidence and information.
Understand how sociologists form questions, provide sociological explanations and assess different arguments based on evidence.
Search, obtain and present information, and develop independent critical analysis and defend reasoned opinions in oral and written form
Critically assess the role of sociological analysis in representation of contemporary Irish society.
Evaluate different kinds of interpretative schemata for “reading” Irish society.

modules.moduleContent: The theories in the sociology of children and childhood with empirical examples of fieldwork with children. Sociology of Childhood is concerned mainly with how societies come to construct children and childhood. The course examines the history of childhood, kids'' culture, children in families and schools, children as workers, the ''traffic'' in children, toys, children''s myths and stories.
learningOutcomes: Understand the major theories and key concepts in the sociology of children and childhood

Critically assess how children shape and are shaped by social institutions such as schools, families, the state and global capitalism, with specific reference to social factors including race/ethnicity, gender and class
Evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by sociologists exploring how societies come to construct children and childhood

Develop an independent and critical perspective on the sociological analyses of issues such as:  the history of childhood, kids' culture, children in families and schools, children as workers, the ''traffic'' in children, toys, children's myths and stories

modules.moduleName: THE POLITICAL ECONOMY OF GLOBAL CAPITALISM
modules.moduleContent: This course examines the changing nature of global capitalism over the last half century. We will consider how capital seized the crises of the 1970s as an opportunity to reconfigure itself, introducing a new international division of labour and installing finance as the principal generator of profit. We will also address how this new order of things almost imploded in the Great Financial Crash and how the world economy has responded to the challenges of the pandemic era. While the course addresses the economic changes that have occurred in the last couple of generations, it further seeks to examine how these transformations have been reflected and refracted through political ideologies and popular culture. This course attempts, then, to provide a critical account of the complex interactions between politics, economics and culture that define and deform the world in which we now live. Although global in its focus, the course will centre largely on Ireland, which illustrates better than most other countries the complex changes that capital has undergone since the convulsions of the 1970s.
learningOutcomes: Appreciate the transformations in global capitalism over the last half century.
Engage with the political ideologies associated with these transformations.
Trace the economic connections between Ireland and the wider world.
Examine these international trends in a critical and imaginative way.

modules.moduleContent:
To introduce sociological perspectives on education. 
The social meaning of education, institutions and organisation of education, education and inequality of opportunity, the politics of education and schooling, theoretical perspectives on the sociology of education.
learningOutcomes: Detail and evaluate the major theories and key concepts used by sociologists to analyse education as a social institution, social process and lived experience.
Critically assess the contribution of sociological analyses to an understanding of issues including: the social meaning of education, institutions and organisation of education.
Evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by sociologists exploring issues such as: education and inequality of opportunity and the politics of education and schooling.
Develop an independent and critical perspective on the sociological analysis of education.

modules.moduleContent: Workshop based presentations delivered by national and international experts will illustrate issues and methodological challenges associated with undertaking research. Presentations will place emphasis on detailing specific methods, issues encountered and outlining decisions made to overcome these challenges, rather than just on research outcomes.
learningOutcomes: Detail the foundations and development of a set of key concepts within sociology
Appraise the relevance of key sociological concepts to the analysis of contemporary society
Assess the contributions of leading social theorists and sociological concepts to the discipline of sociology
Explain key sociological concepts

modules.moduleContent: This module will require a student to undertake the collection and analysis of raw data in either a field or laboratory situation. Students will develop key skills in developing and preparing fieldwork, undertaking primary data collection and developing analytical techniques for assessing collected data. Students will be required to clear ethical approval (and where necessary Garda Vetting) with responsible authorities when appropriate, in addition to developing suitable research methods, for each site or experiment prior to undertaking field work or laboratory work.
learningOutcomes: Design an individual research project, and carry out appropriate primary and/or secondary research.
Collect and record data on specific topics in field or laboratory based situations.
Recount and report on research findings related to the research.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module will provide students with an opportunity to disseminate their research to the wider research community. Students will develop skills in assembling and presenting their research findings. This module will require a student to prepare and deliver a poster or oral presentation(s) at recognised national or international research conferences, fora or colloquia. The selection of appropriate conferences, fora or colloquia will be determined by an oversight committee comprised of the Head of Department, Postgraduate Coordinator and the PhD supervisor in the department of Sociology.
learningOutcomes: Introduce and discuss research findings.
Discuss research methodologies and findings in oral form.
Communicate their research to an audience of specialists.

modules.moduleContent: Quantitative analysis is one of a wide menu of approaches to social science research. This module introduces you to the fundamentals of quantitative data analysis, with a particular emphasis on visualisation, interpretation, and critical appraisal. Working with real-world data, and with few assumptions of prior knowledge, it will equip students with a range of techniques to perform  independent analyses, present your results intuitively, and ultimately design your own theoretically informed studies. The course will involve working with cross-sectional, longitudinal, and a mix of micro and macro-data, enabling study patterns of social change over space and time, whilst gently exposing students to the practice of multivariate data analysis.
learningOutcomes: Conceptualise and understand different approaches to measurement of quantitative data
Know and understand different options for data visualisation and presentation
Generate, analyze and critique quantitative research
Work with logics of statistical modelling
Operationalise regression-based approaches
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module explores contemporary social change through key transitions in modern world history. The module explores theories of social transformation through an examination of major transitions, including for example: the development of capitalism on a world scale; the intertwining of state and citizenship; changing gender relations in the public and private spheres; transnational racial orders from colonialism to globalisation; and environmental trends and crises. The module also undertakes a comparative analysis of different pathways through these transitions, examining similarities and differences across primarily ‘advanced capitalist’ countries and their networks. The implications of these transformations and pathways for key issues such as inequality, environmental crises and the degradation of citizenship are considered throughout the module.
learningOutcomes: Understanding, application and critique of major theories of social change
Understanding of the causes, processes and contestations of key world-historical social changes
Understanding of how these changes are realised in different ways in different times and places
Ability to critique and develop comparative-historical analysis and methodologies
Broad comparative knowledge of key social changes in contemporary society

modules.moduleContent: contact department for details
learningOutcomes:
Show how they should be given credit for prior learning and experience.

Assessment: Student must fill out the learning and experience ‘form’ with documentation attached (transcripts, portfolio work or essay) to be considered by the departmental postgraduate committee and the Head of Department. If approved, the Head of the Sociology Department will make a recommendation to the Graduate Studies subcommittee for oversight.

modules.moduleContent: Students will carry out a significant programme of reading in a particular topic area, under the guidance of an academic supervisor. They will discuss the reading with the supervisor and/or prepare written analyses as directed by the supervisor.
learningOutcomes: Able to analyze the ‘field’ of research on which their thesis is based; assessment is based on written essay summarizing the readings conducted.

modules.moduleContent: The overall aims of this module are to provide students with an introduction to the subject of Social Policy, key concepts, key themes, an overview of public administration in Ireland as it relates to social policy, and a broad foundation for the ongoing study of social policy.

modules.moduleName: CASE STUDIES IN SOCIAL POLICY FOR CRIMINAL JUSTICE
modules.moduleContent: This module aims to introduce and analyse selected social policy case studies, on a range of key social justice issues. These may include but are not limited to domestic, sexual and gender-based violence, human trafficking, hate crime and drugs. This module aims to provide an analysis of key policy areas in relation to  criminal justice and their impacts, together with the challenges facing these policy cases and factors likely to influence future developments. This module is contextualised with contemporary perspectives on crime and policy making.

modules.moduleName: CIVIL SOCIETY & POLICY CHANGE FOR SOCIAL JUSTICE
modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to civil society organisations (NGOs & charities) in Ireland working for social justice and their role in policy influencing and advocacy, citizen participation and policy change. Students will undertake a practice based voluntary placement with a civil society organisation, or undertake an in-depth case study into a civil society organisation.

Introduction to housing theory and policy and the Right to Housing; Comparative housing regimes; Development of Irish housing policy and the Irish housing system; Neoliberalism, marketisation, austerity and financialisation; Current housing crisis origins and impacts on inequality; Homelessness in Ireland Case study: Family Hubs: Homelessness policy measures and impacts on gender and children; Social housing regeneration, participatory planning and sustainable communities; Recent Policy Responses to Housing crisis; Civil society and social movement housing responses; Alternative equality and rights-based policies and housing models

Social policy making models and practices; policy making and ideology and values; policy cycles; policy elites and policy communities; top down and bottom up policy making; policy consultation and policy participation; policy impact and evaluation; policy accountability and scrutiny.
learningOutcomes: Critically appraise the range of social policy
Competently use a range of social policy analysis skills
Competently frame social policy proposals
modules.notes: This is a 10 credit core module.

modules.moduleName: CRITICAL THEORY, STUDIES AND PERSPECTIVES ON SOCIAL POLICY
modules.moduleContent: Theoretical perspectives on social policy in contemporary contexts including social democratic, Marxist, feminist, neo-liberal approaches; welfare states in post-industrial societies; welfare state regimes and their critique; poverty and social exclusion-conceptions and measurement; the “cultural” turn, social capital; welfare and power; an examination of the social-scientific nature of social policy.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an advanced understanding of a range of alternative critical perspectives in social policy analysis.
Critically evaluate scholarly publications on social policy theory.
Identify the policy and practice implications of alternative theoretical perspectives.
modules.notes: This is a 10 credit core module.

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL PRACTICE: PERSPECTIVES ON EDUCATION, DEVELOPMENT, GENDER, DIVERSITY AND CONFLICT & MEDIATION STUDIES
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
To provide students with specialist knowledge of aspects of professional practice pertinent to their research interests

Module Content: 
Debates in Development
Teaching and learning theory and practice
Perspectives on gender in society
Diversity and Equality theory and issues 
Conflict & Mediation theory

modules.moduleName: REALISING RIGHTS: SOCIAL POLICY DEBATES & ISSUES
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: (It is sufficient to give one brief objective)
To enable students to critcally analyse social policy debates and issues in the context of promoting action to realise rights.
Module Content: 
(Module content should be similar to the current course description calendar entry)
Human rights, identity based/recognition issues and socio-economic equality (re-distribution)
Theories of rights, recognition and re-distribution
Critique of policy proofing initiatives; mainstreaming and specific targeted policy responses
Barriers to realising rights
Taking action to realise rights

Module Objective: 
To provide students with critical theoretical insights into and analysis of current debates in professional practice 

Module Content: 
Historical and contemporary developments in professional practice 
Key policy contexts
Praxis and politics
Contest and contention in practice theory

Identifying learning objectives and possible organisational contexts, journal keeping; approaching organisations, neogiating entry, role and work plan for an internship 6-8 weeks full -time or equivalent

A beginning course in Spanish for students with little or no preparation in the Spanish language including oral expression and group work in class; written expression exercises; grammatical exercises. Additional work with self-access material is also required.

Lectures are supported by language laboratory work and conversation classes.

This module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR) at level A1, where the emphasis is on achieving a fundamental level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing). A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A1.
learningOutcomes: Recognise and construct sentences based on the rules of Spanish grammar.
Develop basic vocabulary to apply it to the discussion of everyday situations.
Explain and summarise the content of written texts and answer questions based on this content using appropriate vocabulary.
Develop basic spoken Spanish and listening skills.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the ways in which language and culture are interwoven, with a particular focus on Spanish language and Spanish and Latin American culture. It trains students in the skills for exploring language as a system and looks at how linguistic issues are represented in particular examples from film, literature, music, and visual culture. 
Students will learn how to identify and talk about linguistic and cultural phenomena and explore how they play out in everyday life. The module will equip students with the vocabulary and terminology to talk about language, as well as helping them to understand how language is used in different social and cultural contexts. Students will have an opportunity to explore this through close reading of different types of aural, oral, written, and digital texts. 
This module will consider how language and culture both reflect and shape the way we view the world. It will consist of three units, each of which will focus on a particular linguistic and cultural issue: 
Unit 1: Language Variation and Change 
Unit 2: Language Politics 
Unit 3: Language and Migration 
This module will offer students who want to study a language to degree-level a thorough grounding in the skills necessary for success in their studies. It will also appeal to students with an interest in and passion for languages and how they work in different social contexts.

NOTE: Since this module focuses on Spanish-language texts, including aural, oral, written, and digital texts, and parts of the module may be delivered through the medium of Spanish, some prior knowledge of Spanish is required. (Recommended: Higher-level Spanish at Leaving Certificate or equivalent)
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms the structural similarities and difference between Spanish and English;
Describe in some detail how the Spanish language is used in different parts of the Spanish-speaking world, using examples where appropriate;
Interpret and analyse how language is used in cultural texts and performances through close readings and contextualisation;
Design and create an infographic that represents relevant data in a visually stimulating way;
Research, prepare and deliver an oral presentation;
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and well-structured argument.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Continuing Spanish for students with previous knowledge of the language. Additional work with self-access material is recommended.
Language classes are supplemented by language lab and conversation classes. The course includes oral expression practice, group working class, written expression exercises, grammatical exercises and use of technology for language learning.

This module enables students to achieve a linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A2.2. A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level A2.2.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate familiarity with the basic grammatical rules of the Spanish language.
Apply relevant linguistic concepts to written and oral work.
Organize and structure information in a coherent and meaningful manner according to the rules of the Spanish language.
Identify and use Spanish vocabulary appropriate to this level.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: SPORT, LEISURE & POPULAR CULTURE IN THE SPANISH-SPEAKING WORLD
modules.moduleContent: This module will provide students with the opportunity to explore the cultural, social, and political significance of sport, leisure and popular culture in the Spanish-speaking world. The module will focus on case studies, including events such as controversies surrounding the 1978 World Cup in Argentina and Spain’s victory in the 2023 Women’s World Cup; celebrity personas such as Lionel Messi; and unique sporting/leisure cultural phenomena such as lucha libre wrestling in Mexico. The module will take an approach that is rooted in cultural studies, viewing sport and leisure themselves as cultural practices and as objects of representation through literature, film, internet and other media. Students will be provided with critical tools and frameworks that will enable them to engage in close readings of sport and leisure, and to thereby uncover how they relate to everyday life, history, ideology, and national identity.
learningOutcomes: Situate sports and leisure practices in Spain and Latin America within their wider social, economic, political and cultural contexts.
Identify, interpret, and analyse a wide range Spanish-language media artifacts and cultural productions concerning sports and leisure.
Identify and critically assess strategies related to the media presentation of sports events and personalities across various platforms and media.
Critically analyse the ideological functions of sports events (e.g. ‘sportswashing’).
Develop media literate, critically aware, and ethically sound strategies for engagement with social media platforms’ coverage of sports events and personalities in the target language.
Build effective, efficient and well-organised project-oriented groups, founded on principles of collaboration, mutual advancement, and intercultural communication.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at students who are interested in learning Portuguese – a major world language with over 250 million speakers all over the world – alongside the core subjects of their degree programmes. It will appeal to all types of students – those who have a genuine interest in learning about the Portuguese language and culture for their own sake, as well as those who are motivated to develop Portuguese language skills and (inter)cultural competence to enhance their employment prospects in Ireland and further afield. There is some emphasis on the linguistic and cultural diversity of the Portuguese-speaking world. Practical reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills will be taught through in-person classes with an expert in Portuguese as a Foreign/Second Language who is a native speaker. Classroom-based learning will be complemented by independent work to be completed weekly.

Please note: This module is designed for students who have no previous knowledge of the Portuguese language.

This module is made available to students of Maynooth University with the financial support of the Camões Institute.
learningOutcomes: Answer simple questions and offer some personal details, e.g. name, nationality, address, age, hobbies, in written and spoken form, and ask the same questions of another person;
Extract key information from simple written, audio and video texts related to situations from everyday life that contain words and expressions dealt with in class;
Write simple messages and communicate in a very simple way about situations related to everyday life and using vocabulary dealt with in class.
Demonstrate some basic geographical and cultural knowledge of the countries where Portuguese is spoken.

modules.moduleName: VISUAL WORLDS OF SPAIN AND LATIN AMERICA
modules.moduleContent:
An introduction to Spanish and Latin American visual culture and cinema.  This course examines the evolution of visual culture in Spain and Latin America from 15th century religious and political art to surrealism, and contemporary Spanish and Latin American cinema.

**This 5-ECTS credit module may be used as part of the 15-ECTS credits in the study of literature (including drama, poetry, fiction, film studies etc.) required for accreditation by the Teaching Council of Ireland. All applications submitted for Teaching Council accreditation are, however, subject to the Teaching Council’s internal evaluation processes.
learningOutcomes: LO1: Identify key issues in the evolution of visual culture in Spain and Latin America.
LO2: Describe different types of Spanish and Latin American political film making, from Surrealism to the road movie.
LO3: Explain how paratextual issues have been reflected in the development of Spanish and Latin American film practice and theory.
LO4: Critique and evaluate films with reference to theories of Spanish and Latin American cinema.

modules.moduleName: THE SHORT STORY IN LATIN AMERICA
modules.moduleContent: This module will consider short narrative forms as they have evolved in Latin America throughout the twentieth century and up to the present day. It will consider how genres such as the Gothic and the Fantastic have historically inflected Latin America’s rich storytelling tradition, and will also consider how more recent short fictions have aimed to engage with contemporary political and social issues facing the region.
learningOutcomes: LO1: Engage in close-reading of short narrative texts.
LO2: Situate texts in the context of major literary traditions in Latin America.
LO3: Interpret texts through the lens of social and political issues.
LO4: Develop arguments in respect of texts and their literary, historical, and cultural contexts.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at students who are interested in deepening their knowledge and understanding of Catalan language and culture. It will appeal to students who are already studying Spanish or French and would like to learn a new romance language, but also to students without prior experience of romance languages and who will find in Catalan an exciting entry to the Mediterranean linguistic and cultural world. Practical reading, writing, speaking and listening skills will be taught through in-person classes with an experienced tutor who is native speaker. The teaching and learning will be student-centred and based on a communicative approach. Classroom-based learning will be complemented by weekly exercises and independent work.

Please note: This module is designed for students who have no previous knowledge of the Catalan language.

This module is made available to students of Maynooth University with the financial support of the Institut Ramon Llull.
learningOutcomes: Explain and summarise key information from relatively straightforward oral and written texts related to situations from everyday life.

Produce short oral and written texts using familiar structures and expressions to communicate information about family, hobbies, holidays, daily activities and other matters relating to everyday life.
Describe, in a simple way, experiences, and aspects of their cultural background and of the close environment.
Display a general knowledge of Catalan Geography, History and Arts.

modules.moduleContent:
This is an optional second-year module that explores the ways in which literature transforms into other expressions of popular culture. Its main emphasis is on the way that some of the twentieth century literature texts such as children's books, poems and novels are re-written into television series, poems and songs.

**This 5-ECTS credit module may be used as part of the 15-ECTS credits in the study of literature (including drama, poetry, fiction, film studies etc.) required for accreditation by the Teaching Council of Ireland. All applications submitted for Teaching Council accreditation are, however, subject to the Teaching Council’s internal evaluation processes.
learningOutcomes: LO1: Familiarise with key literary and popular culture concepts.
LO2: Explore society through different means of culture.
LO3: Raise awareness about the different functions of literature.
LO4: Improve knowledge of the background issues, processes and themes relevant to an understanding of contemporary Spain through literature and popular culture.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at students who are interested in learning Spanish alongside the core subjects of their degree programmes. It will appeal to all types of students – those who have a genuine interest in learning about the Spanish language and culture for their own sake as well as those who are motivated to develop Spanish language skills and (inter)cultural competence to enhance their employment prospects in Ireland and further afield. Practical reading, writing, speaking and listening skills will be taught through in-person classes with experienced tutors who are native speakers. Classroom-based learning will be complemented by asynchronous online material and exercises to be completed weekly.

Please note: Prospective students should note that this language elective is intended for beginners. Students who took Spanish for the Leaving Certificate examination (or equivalent) cannot be accommodated in this class but should consider signing up for a Beginners’ language elective in French, German, or Chinese instead.
learningOutcomes: Answer simple questions and offer some personal details, e.g. name, nationality, address, age, hobbies, in written and spoken form, and ask the same questions of another person;
Extract key information from simple written, audio and video texts related to situations from everyday life that contain words and expressions dealt with in class;
Write simple messages and communicate in a very simple way about situations related to everyday life and using vocabulary dealt with in class.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: HISTORIES, POLITICS, AND SOCIETIES OF CONTEMPORARY SPAIN
modules.moduleContent: The concept of ‘Spain’ is ever-changing and triggers a wide range of responses, both positive and negative, from different quarters. Introducing students to different perspectives on Spain, this module begins with the so-called ‘Disaster’ of 1898 and continues through to the 1993 General Elections. Running along this timeline, students will explore key texts and events to engage with different histories and politics, including but not limited to: colonialism, memories of conflict, women’s and LGBTQ+ rights, the bourgeoisie and working class, as well as underground radical politics. We will also look at vibrant cultural outputs, particularly football, songs, art, and films, and then contextualise them as part of a response to wider socio-historical, economic, and political upheavals. Students will also engage with the evolving perspectives on Spain from different ‘peripheries’, including the Catalan Countries, Euskal Herria, Galicia, and the Maghreb (namely the Western Sahara and Morocco).
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a clear understanding of contemporary Spain, alongside its complexities, contradictions, and challenges
Understand and identify the different historical factors that drive cultural, social and political change within Spain
Understand social, political, and cultural developments in Spain, and locate these within wider historical narratives in Europe
Demonstrate good research skills and be able to provide clear summaries of key texts
Situate and present arguments about the development of contemporary Spain, with reference to its history, politics, and culture
Analyse and engage with contemporary issues in Spain and identify the historical roots of these matters.

modules.moduleContent:
A continuing intermediate course in Spanish for students who have successfully completed a full year of Spanish. It includes grammar, reading, writing, speaking and listening work.
The module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages at level B1 where the emphasis is on achieving a higher intermediate level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing). A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level B1.2.
learningOutcomes: Construct compositions on a variety of subjects; summarise the meaning of texts; give examples of how phrases or sentences illustrate grammar rules; analyse short texts.
Construct sentences and short texts based on grammatical rules; complete exercises using various aspects of grammar; employ vocabulary suited to a particular topic or area of language use.

Identify how grammatical rules apply in a given structure; determine how rules aid in the conjugation of verbs and other grammatical constructions; detect patterns in the use of tenses, moods, voices, pronouns and other areas of language; recognize grammatical rules in use.

Organise information in a coherent and meaningful manner; apply relevant linguistic concepts to phrases, sentences and short texts; write expressively at an intermediate level of linguistic complexity.

Rearrange elements of phrases in a syntactically and grammatically correct manner; complete sentences accurately using the required linguistic skill; recognise expressions encountered in class; demonstrate the linguistic meaning and usage of expressions; manipulate phrases in the application of grammatical rules.
Demonstrate intermediate linguistic competence; communicate ideas, opinions and feelings of medium complexity; show intercultural awareness.
Complete continuous assessment and class assignments correctly; answer exam questions with relevant and precise information based on course work completed throughout the semester.

modules.moduleContent: An intermediate course in Catalan language and culture.
learningOutcomes: Understand the most usual sentences and expressions in relation to the communicative basic needs
Express him or herself in basic communication situations, as well as express his or her opinion in relation to a wide range of topics
Describe, in a simple way, experiences, and aspects of his/ her cultural background and of the close environment, in relation to his/her immediate needs.
Understand and use linguistic structures and idiomatic expressions typical among Catalan native speakers.
Describe experiences of the past as well as future plans using a wide range of tenses and grammatical structures
Read and understand basic texts in Catalan on a wide variety of topics, from newspaper articles to basic Catalan narrative
Show a general knowledge of Catalan myths and oral traditions from all times

modules.moduleContent:
A continuing advanced course in Spanish that includes grammar, reading, composition, speaking and listening work.
This module enables students to achieve a linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level C1.1. A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level C1.1.
learningOutcomes: Compose essays on a range of subjects; construct communicative and creative texts based on work done in class; summarise the meaning of written and audio texts, write and deliver presentations.

Construct sentences and passages based on linguistic expressions encountered in class; complete exercises using various aspects of language; employ vocabulary suited to a particular topic, register or area of language use; use relevant linguistic concepts.

Identify linguistic elements of a given structure; identify how grammatical rules apply in a given structure; apply grammatical rules to written and spoken expression; determine how linguistic rules aid communication; detect patterns in linguistic usage; recognise grammatical rules in use.

Organise information in a coherent and meaningful manner; present information in a coherent and meaningful manner; apply relevant linguistic concepts to phrases, sentences and longer texts; demonstrate the linguistic meaning and usage of expressions; write creatively and analytically at an advanced level of linguistic complexity; communicate at an advanced level of linguistic complexity.

Produce a range of texts based on work covered in class; summarise the content of texts; analyse various aspects of texts; prepare a variety of compositions; deliver presentations on a particular topic; discuss texts, events and ideas in a competent and convincing manner.

Rearrange elements of phrases in a syntactically and grammatically correct manner; complete sentences accurately using the required linguistic skill; recognise and use expressions encountered in class; demonstrate the linguistic meaning and usage of expressions; manipulate phrases, sentences and passages in the application of grammatical rules.

Demonstrate advanced linguistic competence; communicate ideas, opinions and feelings of advanced complexity; transmit relevant information in a meaningful and coherent manner; appraise data, events and opinions; show intercultural awareness.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces the students to advanced aspects of Old Irish grammar, esp. the formation of non-present tenses and moods, and advanced aspects of syntax, esp. dependent clauses.
Students taking this module outside of Medieval Irish, please contact the programme coordinator, Prof. David Stifter (david.stifter@mu.ie), before registering. No previous knowledge of the Irish language is required, but a readiness to engage with basic linguistic concepts is necessary.
learningOutcomes: Analyse and identify non-present-stem verbal formations.
Understand the system of infixed and suffixed grammatical elements.
Demonstrate familiarity with the syntax of dependent clauses.
Translate simple Old Irish sentences.

modules.moduleName: DEVELOPING RESEARCH SKILLS IN MEDIEVAL IRISH STUDIES 1
modules.moduleContent: In a series of seminars, the students will develop their familiarity with the main research tools of medieval Irish studies, such as the use of handbooks, lexicons, dictionaries and electronic databases.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate familiarity with the main research tools used in medieval Irish studies.
Apply the research tools in independent research.
Develop advanced written and presentation skills.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces the students to the basic concepts of palaeography: the development of scripts and writing styles, common abbreviations and notae, and codicology. The students will gain practical experience in reading medieval Irish manuscripts.
learningOutcomes: Identify the major writing styles in Early Ireland.
Demonstrate familiarity with the main notae and abbreviations used in the medieval manuscript tradition.
Read and transcribe from Irish manuscripts.

modules.moduleName: GRAMMAR AND READINGS IN MIDDLE IRISH
modules.moduleContent: This module will enhance proficiency in aspects of Middle Irish grammar and syntax for students of the structured PhD programme. Fluency in the system of spelling and pronunciation will be expanded and exemplified by reading and systematic analyses of Middle Irish Texts
learningOutcomes: Distinguish aspects of Middle Irish grammar and syntax.
Detail and exemplify the system of spelling and pronunciation of Middle Irish.
Systematically examine and analyse extant Middle  Irish texts.
Demonstrate effective and efficient research, academic writing and study skills.

modules.moduleName: UNIVERSAL CRITICAL SKILLS (INTRODUCTION TO UNIVERSITY LEARNING AND BEYOND) - A
modules.moduleContent:
The development of the critical skills will be based on exercises designed around weekly readings of appropriate journal articles or similar, these to be decided.
• Finding information (from printed materials and texts, academic databases, digital/electronic sources).
• Recording information through use of a range of media (e.g., visual, audio).
• Presenting and using different representations (e.g., text, diagrams, tables, charts, images, posters).
• Planning and writing academic essays.
• Academic writing skills, styles and conventions.
• Understanding plagiarism.
• Self-assessment and reflection.
• Critical thinking (forming an argument, examining evidence, judgement and reasoning, drawing conclusions).
• Creative thinking (solving problems, generating ideas, discussing solutions and possible outcomes, approaching tasks from different perspectives/ viewpoints).
• Organisation and time management (making plans, allocating tasks/ roles, designing schedules).
• Memory and approaches to learning.
• Working in groups.
• Preparing for assessments.
• Making oral presentations and public speaking.
• Awareness of supports and initiatives for students at Maynooth University.
learningOutcomes: Develop academic research skills including the ability to find, analyse, interpret, and evaluate information and sources.
Develop confidence in public speaking through an awareness of the affordances of technology and rhetorical communication strategies.
Develop the ability to engage in professional writing through an understanding of genre conventions and the role of texts in professional workspaces.
Appreciate the value of a university education and acknowledge their responsibility for their own education taking advantage of the supports, resources, and opportunities available.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: UNIVERSAL CRITICAL SKILLS (INTRODUCTION TO UNIVERSITY LEARNING AND BEYOND) - B
modules.moduleContent:
Upon successful completion of the second semester of Critical Skills, we expect that students will:
• Strengthen quantitative reasoning and numeracy skills including a basic understanding of descriptive statistics.
• Learn the principles of data visualisation; gain confidence in interpreting data visualisations; develop skills to create reliable visual narratives with data.
• Appreciate the advantages and disadvantages of working collaboratively; understand the characteristics of effective teamwork and group communication; approach teamwork with empathy for individual members of and appreciate how diversity strengthens teamwork.
• Learn how to use electronic tools to professionally organise and manage team projects.
• Transfer academic writing knowledge and practice developed within the module to rhetorical situations beyond the classroom and academic audiences.
learningOutcomes: Be able to critically evaluate online information sources for credibility, accuracy, and relevance, demonstrating proficiency in navigating digital information landscapes.

Examine how digital technologies influence knowledge production, dissemination, and access, critically evaluating the impact of digital inequalities on society and exploring potential strategies for addressing these disparities.

Demonstrate proficiency in creating digital content across various platforms and mediums. They will apply principles of effective digital storytelling, media design, and content creation tools to communicate ideas effectively in the digital realm.

Develop a foundational understanding of artificial intelligence, including its capabilities, limitations, ethical implications, and societal impact, enabling them to critically evaluate AI technologies and their implications in various contexts.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)
