modules.moduleName: CRITICAL SKILLS: UNDERSTANDING SCIENCE WRITING AND COMMUNICATION
modules.moduleContent: The goal of this module is to help students gain a deeper understanding of how scientific writing functions inside and outside of expert communities. By enrolling in this elective, students will gain the opportunity to work closely with landmark scientific texts and practice translating specialized knowledge for broader audiences.
learningOutcomes: Develop an awareness of standard conventions in scholarly scientific writing
Identify techniques of manipulation used in misrepresenting scientific findings
Translate scholarly findings for a broader audience

modules.moduleName: CRITICAL SKILLS: INTERSECTIONS OF SCIENCE WRITING AND MEDIA
modules.moduleContent:
Module two will build upon the skills developed in SK251 and ask that students begin considering how digital and new media influences the landscape of scientific communication. 
• Understand principles of visual rhetoric and semiotics
learningOutcomes: Compose multimodal/digital texts that convey accurate representations of scientific findings
Develop a set of communicative skills crucial for employability in science and science-adjacent careers
Understand principles of visual rhetoric and semiotics
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of the module is to provide students with an understanding of measuring the different physiological systems of the human body and how they can be tested through exercise.

In this module, students will build on their foundational knowledge of the major physiological systems and their response to exercise by learning how to measure key physiological variables, including blood pressure, heart rate, oxygen consumption and blood lactate at different exercise intensities. Students will learn about the key scientific principles underpinning the measurement technologies used to test these variables, as well as how to interpret results and use this information to prescribe evidence-based exercise training programmes for improved health and sports performance.
learningOutcomes: Understand how to measure the major physiological systems of the human body
Conduct physiological measurements during exercise tests at varying intensities
Interpret physiological measurements and communicate relevant data to an individual, athlete or coach.

modules.moduleContent:
This introductory module explores the distinctive contribution of the sociological perspective and equips students with the necessary analytical skills to think sociologically about the world around them. Students are encouraged to develop their own sociological imagination through linking their lived experience to the social and historical processes unfolding around them.

The module explores how we can see ourselves as shaped by the social world, To do this, the module explores how social structures shape our experiences, discussing concepts such as social interaction, culture, institutions, race, class and gender.

The module also explores how Irish society and our place in it has changed over time. It provides students with concepts to help them understand how everyday lives have been changing as Irish society has gone through major social transformations.
learningOutcomes: Detail and evaluate theories and concepts used by social scientists to develop an understanding of the social forces that shape lived experience.
Critically assess the contribution of sociological analysis to an understanding of the key concepts such as class, gender, race and ethnicity which frame individual and collective life chances.
Evaluate different kinds of interpretative schemata employed by sociologists to understand the dynamic impact of social structures such as family, education and work in society.
Understand how to analyse, evaluate and interpret information.
Develop and defend reasoned opinions in oral and written form.
Search, obtain and present information.
Participate in group discussion.

modules.moduleContent:
To introduce social science perspectives on social movements and revolutions.
May include: The variety of forms of social movements and revolutions; theories and explanations of movements and revolutions; the consequences of social movements and revolutions.
learningOutcomes: Detail and evaluate some major theories and key concepts in the social science study of social movements and revolutions.

Evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by social scientists studying social movements and revolutions in areas such as their development processes; who takes part and in what way; interactions with the state and other actors; or issues of culture and identity.

Critically assess the social science evidence and research on topics such as the explanation of social movements and revolutions; the variety of forms involved; their social, political and cultural significance; or the outcomes of movements and revolutions.
Develop an independent and critical perspective on the social science analysis of social movements and revolutions.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to a sociological analysis of the development of the contemporary cityscape. It explores the changing landscapes of cities, with a particular focus on central landscapes, and the implications of these changes. The module is divided into three sections. Section one examines the changing urban landscape from industrialisation through to de-industrialisation and uses a range of case studies to examine the spatial and social implications of these processes. This section also examines the Utopian Modernists and how their responses to the conditions of industrialisation influenced our understanding of the city. Urban change is embedded in changing configurations of capitalism, and the global restructuring of capitalism, which will also be analysed. Section two examines the contemporary cityscape and explores this in relation to the post-industrial cityscape, the creative city, cities and sustainability and the divided city. Case studies from a range of cities will be used as examples. Section three examines processes occurring within the contemporary cityscape and critically examines topics such as gentrification, public space, the right to the city and greening the city. 
Key theorists, approaches to understanding the cityscape, case studies and a range of visual material are used to develop critical understanding and to provide visual examples.
learningOutcomes: Detail and critically examine the processes associated with urban change and the spatial and social implications of these processes
Apply global processes and changing configurations of capitalism to understand changing cityscapes
Critically examine the spatial and social organisation of the contemporary cityscape
Evaluate the debates, theories and literature reviewed in the module to develop an independent analysis of different aspects of the contemporary cityscape
Have a critical understanding of the myriad of landscapes encapsulated in the city and the implications of changing landscapes on people’s lived experiences

modules.moduleName: SCREENING SOCIAL CHANGE: CAPITALISM, CINEMA AND TELEVISION
modules.moduleContent: In this course, we consider how the complex processes of social change are articulated through two of the principal cultural forms available to us: cinema and television. The historical frame of the programme will stretch half a century, from the political and economic turbulence of the 1970s through to the political and economic turbulence of today. In class we will consider how the social and cultural evolutions of recent decades have been reflected and refracted, critiqued and affirmed though a range of movies and tv series. Taught in a way that is designed to be interactive and participative, the course is intended to provide a critical document of how capitalism has evolved over the last couple of generations.
learningOutcomes: Appreciate the transformations in global capitalism over the last half century.
Engage with theories of cultural change.
Critically examine movies and television series.
Present short synopses of movies and TV series to their peers.

modules.moduleName: SOCIOLOGY OF LAW AND SOCIAL CONTROL
modules.moduleContent: This module is designed as a sociological investigation of law both as a mechanism of social regulation and as a field of knowledge. It explores classical and contemporary theoretical contributions to the sociology of law and social control including a focus on crime and punishment. We will critically examine core issues of social control, social justice, the language of the law and social change with reference to class, gender, race and ethnicity.
learningOutcomes: Explain and apply the main theories and concepts in the sociological analysis of law and social control.
Detail and analyse the empirical trends and patterns in the relationship between law and social control.
Assess the role of organisational, political and transnational factors in shaping law and social control
Develop an independent perspective on issues with the sociological analysis of law and social control.
Develop their own perspective on the social and political factors shaping law and social control.

modules.moduleContent: The course will examine methods and techniques (e.g. quantitative and/or qualitative) for undertaking research in order to acquire a familiarity with research methodologies for use in their research area.
learningOutcomes: Practice the design of effective data analysis techniques
Analyse data using appropriate methods and techniques
Display data using graphical methods
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: This module instructs students in sets of professional skills such as writing a conference paper, how to make a conference presentation, writing reports, time management and technologies for work management.
learningOutcomes: This module instructs students in sets of professional skills such as writing a conference paper, how to make a conference presentation, writing reports, time management and technologies for work management.
At the end of this module, students will be able to demonstrate their newly gained professional skills in a written assessment.

modules.moduleContent: This module instructs students in sets of professional skills such as writing a conference paper, how to make a conference presentation, writing reports, time management and technologies for work management.
learningOutcomes: This module instructs students in sets of professional skills such as writing a conference paper, how to make a conference presentation, writing reports, time management and technologies for work management.
At the end of this module, students will be able to demonstrate their newly gained professional skills in a written assessment.

modules.moduleContent: Advanced specialist modules can be taken from those on offer from the Department of Geography, NIRSA, other Departments at MU, or a module on offer in another partner institution. Students select module from a range of options.
learningOutcomes: Explain and critically evaluate the major theories and key concepts used by sociologists to analyse a field of sociological study.
Critically analyse and assess the contribution of contemporary sociological research to an understanding of a field of sociological study.
Explain and critically evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by sociologists relating to a field of sociological study.
Develop an independent and critical perspective on the sociological analyses of a field of sociological study.

modules.moduleContent: Students will develop a formal research proposal in conjunction with their research supervisors. This proposal will include a) a description of the research project; b) an outline methodology statement; c) a working chapter outline, with targeted delivery dates; d) a select bibliography
learningOutcomes: Search for grant application calls, write application, revise and submit

modules.moduleContent: This module aims to provide a comprehensive and critical analysis of Irish child and family social policies. The module addresses the evolution of child policies, assessing the implications for contemporary policies and strategies. These national responses are placed in the context of international obligations to children. Focusing on aspects of contemporary child and family policies in Ireland, the module will consider such issues as child poverty, child protection, child trafficking, alternative care, education and welfare and the needs and treatment of children from outside the jurisdiction. The broader context of family life for children is highlighted via such scenarios as living in lone-parent households and/or domestic violence situations. The module will draw on an extensive evidence-base of research and literature to augment policy and discourse analysis.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a knowledge of the evolution of Irish child and family policies.
Demonstrate an awareness of the existing Irish and international policy environment and infrastructure.
Demonstrate an understanding of the nature and implications of old and new risks and challenges for contemporary policy
Demonstrate an understanding of contemporary influences on child and family policy
Demonstrate an appreciation of the evidence-base supporting and influencing the child policy discourse.

modules.moduleContent: In third year, students undertake a policy analysis research project, or minor dissertation of approximately 7,500 words, on a policy issue. Each student is assigned a supervisor and the work is undertaken during both semesters. Students submit a research proposal during the first semester and submit the completed dissertation during the second semester, In addition to individual supervision, up to 20 sessions for the whole class are organised on various aspects of policy analysis research project management, methodology, and report writing.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a proficiency in independent research on a relevant social policy topic.
Develop a clear, researchable and relevant research question
Review the relevant literature on the chosen topic in a thorough, clear and succinct manner.
Implement a small scale research plan with clear methodology, in a given time frame.
Present and analyse the research findings clearly and relate them back to the literature.
Develop a set of conclusions and, where applicable, frame recommendations.

modules.moduleContent:
This module addresses the variety of ways in which welfare state is "gendered", including the dimensions of work, income and care. It applies these theoretical insights to the evolution of social policy in Ireland in particular, as well as more widely, through the lens of gendered welfare state regimes

This is a 2.5 Credit module (1 hour per week for 12 weeks).
learningOutcomes: outline the ways in which welfare state is "gendered"
Demonstrate an understanding of including the dimensions of work, income and care.
apply theoretical insights to the the evolution of social policy in Ireland and more generally

modules.moduleContent: This module explores philosophical perspectives on a number of ethical issues in contemporary social policy, including moral dilemmas and choices, and the boundaries between the public and private spheres, society and nature.
learningOutcomes: critically explore different philosphical perspectives on ethical issues in contemporary social policy
critically review the arguments relating to social policy and the beginning of life
critically review the arguments relating to social policy and the end of life
Explore the arguments relating to the boundaries between public and privare spheres on the ethical issues addressed in the module

modules.moduleContent: This elective module provides a critical insight into conceptual, policy and social justice dimensions of the current debates about environmental sustainability. The module may include a study visit to an environmental organisation.
learningOutcomes: demonstrate a critical understanding of the links between key concepts of economic and social development, environmental sustainability and social justice
discuss the national and international policy context of environmental sustainability in general and climate change in particular
explore the implications for society and social & climate justice of key environmental issues such as climate change, food sovereignty, degraded ecosystems, flooding, drought and the carbon economy
demonstrate insight into the relationship between environmental sustainability and social justice with reference to relevant case studies nationally and internationally

modules.moduleName: POLITICAL ECONOMY OF THE WELFARE STATE
modules.moduleContent: This module aims to provide a political economy perspective on the welfare state, tailored to social policy students. It will review and build upon students' understanding of key concepts such as efficiency and equity, GDP, taxation, social expenditure, social insurance, welfare, redistribution, inequality, poverty etc., and develop a critical approach to them, locating these concepts in their political context. It will critically explore various options for government intervention in the economy as they apply to the key areas of social policy, such as social welfare, health, housing, and education.

Deviance, crime and society; criminal justice system structures and policy; crime and control; comparative criminal justice policy, practices and regimes; youth justice; communities and crime; violence against women; hate crimes and targeted violence.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an advanced critical understanding of the criminal justice system
Demonstrate a critical understanding of criminal justice policy and strategy
Demonstrate a critical understanding of a range of key equality issues in relation to criminal justice
modules.notes: This is an optional 5 credit module.

learningOutcomes: Provide a critical understanding of Irish housing and homelessness policy and comparative housing regimes
Provide an understanding of key concepts and theories in housing and homelessness
Explain in particular the impact of neoliberalism and financialisation on housing systems and policy
Understand human rights based frameworks for analysing housing policy
Develop rights-based housing policy
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: HEALTH AND MENTAL HEALTH POLICY: PERSPECTIVES AND DEBATES
modules.moduleContent:
Module Content:
• Health Policy
o Conceptualising health & patterns and determinants of health and disease 
o Development of health care systems 
o Personal & public responsibility for health 
o Development of the Irish health care system in comparative perspectives
o Health care funding systems
o Inequalities in health care – causes & consequence 
o Key indicators of health, e.g., life expectancy rates and the quality of life
o Challenges to health care systems
o Complexities of inter-action between health care systems and other support systems, particularly personal care systems
o Role of carers & support for carers
o Professional power 
o Implementation & evaluation of health policy 
 
• Mental health policy
• Ireland in the global context
• Historical background to mental health policy and service provision – key themes and developments
• From institutionalisation to community-based care – drivers and obstacles
• The discovery of recovery
• Patients, users and survivors – the rise of social movements in the field of mental health policy
learningOutcomes: Critically describe the Irish health and mental health systems and associated policies
Outline the evolution and development of health and mental health policy and provision in Ireland
Outline key concepts and theories underpinning policy provision in both health and mental health fields
Locate Irish health and mental health policies within the wider comparative and international context
Discuss key concepts within the mental health field including associated social movements such as disability movement, deinstitutionalisation, discovery of recovery, from patient to user to survivor
Critically analyse issues of inequality, responsibility, power and implementation within the health sector
modules.notes: This is a 10 credit optional module.

modules.moduleContent: Perspectives on the role and function of education in society; barriers and equity of access to education at each level of preschool, primary, secondary, further and higher education; policy developments to address inequalities at each level, current debates and challenges; Focus on inequalities in access to, participation in, and attainment in education in terms of gender, disability, LGBT, race/ethnicity and socioeconomic status; Irish policy context and contextualization in wider Education policy debates at OECD and other international levels; The future of University education and creating an equitable education system, examining meritocracy, instrumentalism alongside the importance of critical education in engaged citizenship.
learningOutcomes: Discuss Perspectives on the role and function of education in society;
Outline barriers and equity of access to education at each level of preschool, primary, secondary, further and higher education;
Understand inequalities in access to, participation in, and attainment in education in terms of gender, disability, LGBT, race/ethnicity and socioeconomic status;
Understand policy developments which address inequalities, current debates and challenges.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the ways in which language and culture are interwoven, with a particular focus on Spanish language and Spanish and Latin American culture. It trains students in the skills for exploring language as a system and looks at how linguistic issues are represented in particular examples from film, literature, music, and visual culture. 
Students will learn how to identify and talk about linguistic and cultural phenomena and explore how they play out in everyday life. The module will equip students with the vocabulary and terminology to talk about language, as well as helping them to understand how language is used in different social and cultural contexts. Students will have an opportunity to explore this through close reading of different types of aural, oral, written, and digital texts. 
This module will consider how language and culture both reflect and shape the way we view the world. It will consist of three units, each of which will focus on a particular linguistic and cultural issue: 
Unit 1: Visual Culture, Arts and the Media 
Unit 2: Intertextuality – Borrowing and Stealing
Unit 3: Gender & Diversity in Language and Culture
This module will offer students who want to study a language to degree-level a thorough grounding in the skills necessary for success in their studies. It will also appeal to students with an interest in and passion for languages and how they work in different social contexts.

NOTE: Since this module focuses on Spanish-language texts, including aural, oral, written, and digital texts, and parts of the module may be delivered through the medium of Spanish, some prior knowledge of Spanish is required. (Recommended: Higher-level Spanish at Leaving Certificate or equivalent)
learningOutcomes: Interpret and analyse how language is used in cultural texts and performances through close readings and contextualisation;
Interpret and critically analyse literary and visual texts related to the Spanish-speaking world;
Research, prepare and deliver a group presentation using visual aids, where appropriate;
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and well-structured argument.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at students who are interested in continuing their study of Spanish alongside the core subjects of their degree programmes. It will appeal to students who have a genuine interest in deepening their understanding of Spanish language and culture as well as those who are motivated to further develop their Spanish language skills and (inter)cultural competence to enhance their employment prospects in Ireland and further afield. Practical reading, writing, speaking and listening skills will be taught through in-person classes with experienced tutors who are native speakers. Classroom-based learning will be complemented by asynchronous online material and exercises to be completed weekly.

Please note: Prospective students should note that this language elective is intended for beginners. Students who took Spanish for the Leaving Certificate examination (or equivalent) cannot be accommodated in this class but should consider signing up for a Beginners’ language elective in French, German, or Chinese instead.
learningOutcomes: Explain and summarise key information from relatively straightforward written, audio and video texts related to situations from everyday life that contain words and expressions dealt with in class;

Exchange relatively straightforward information in spoken or written form about family, occupation, plans, holidays, daily and leisure activities, and/or other familiar matters relating to everyday life;

Produce short written texts using familiar structures and expressions and communicate in relatively straightforward terms on topics of relevance to everyday life and using vocabulary dealt with in class.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
An intermediate course in Spanish for students who have successfully completed a full year of Spanish. It includes grammar, reading, writing, speaking and listening work.

The module is aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR) for Languages at level B1, where the emphasis is on achieving an intermediate level of communication in all four skills (listening, speaking, reading and writing). 

A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with CEFR level B1.1.
learningOutcomes: Construct compositions on a variety of subjects; summarise the meaning of texts, give examples of how phrases or sentences illustrate grammar rules; analyse short texts.
Construct sentences and short texts based on grammatical rules; complete exercises using various aspects of grammar; employ vocabulary suited toa particular topic or area of language use.

Identify how grammatical rules apply in a given structure; determine how rules aid in the conjugation of verbs and other grammatical constructions; detect patterns in the use of tenses, moods, voices, pronouns and other areas of language; recognize grammatical rules in use.

Organise information in a coherent and meaningful manner; apply relevant linguistic concepts to phrases, sentences and short texts; write expressively at an intermediate level of linguistic complexity.

Rearrange elements of phrases in a syntactically and grammatically correct manner; complete sentences accurately using the required linguistics skill; recognise expressions encountered in class; demonstrate the linguistic meaning and usage of expressions; manipulate phrases in the application of grammatical rules.
Demonstrate intermediate linguistic competence; communicate ideas, opinions and feelings of medium complexity; show intercultural awareness.
Complete continuous assessment and class assignments correctly; answer exam questions with relevant and precise information based on course work completed throughout the semester.

modules.moduleContent:
This intensive module introduces students to the theory and practice of translation from Spanish to English. Paying close attention to different registers and text types, students will be given a firm grounding in translating texts from across the Spanish-speaking world. As well as equipping students with the basic skills to handle written translation tasks, this module also provides students with an introduction to sight translation.

Each week, students will engage in a practical translation class and a theory-based class. These classes work in dialogue with one another to further enhance students’ translation skills and to improve their awareness and understanding of the translation industry. Students will be expected to complete weekly translations as part of this module.
learningOutcomes: Translate a range of texts from Spanish into English
Identify and navigate specific issues when translating
Understand and follow a translation brief
Navigate and deploy a variety of language tools
Compile a translator’s glossary
Be familiar with and deploy basic translation strategies
Demonstrate basic skills in Sight Translation.

modules.moduleContent: The texts used in this module include newspaper articles and excerpts from literary works in Portuguese.
learningOutcomes: Express opinions on abstract/cultural matters in a limited way;
Offer advice within a known area;
Understand instructions or public announcements;
Understand the general meaning of non-routine information within a familiar area;
Write letters and make notes on familiar or predictable matters;
Write elaborate texts related to personal information.
Demonstrate linguistic competence commensurate with CEFRL level A 2.1 (on achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module)

modules.moduleContent: This course will encompass Spanish to English translation. A variety of texts will be used: literary, historical, commercial, technical. While the focus will be on the written word, some interpreting may be included in the course. In addition to practical exercises, consideration will be given to theoretical, linguistic and cultural questions posed in translating and interpreting.
tbc

The course is case-study based and involves lots of practical work using R. Exploratory data analysis: summaries and graphics. Mosaic plots. Fisher’s exact test. Calculating probabilities, quantiles. Assessing Normality, Normal probability plots. Transformations. Simulation of CLT. Analysing data using one and two sample t-tests and confidence intervals. Correlation, simple linear regression inference and anova.
learningOutcomes: Calculate probabilities in R.
Analyse one and two samples using standard parametric methods.
Display data using graphical methods.
Fit, interpret and predict from simple linear regression models.

modules.moduleName: R FOR STATISTICS AND DATA SCIENCE
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
To introduce students to data manipulation, visualisation and programming in R.

Basics of R. Data manipulation. Control structures. Data wrangling. Data import. Writing R programs. Reproducible analysis. ggplot and dplyr. Report writing with Rmarkdown.
learningOutcomes: Understand and use R data structures.
Use R to produce statistical graphics.
Programme with R.
Write reports in R markdown

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:
To introduce students to Probability Theory.

Axioms of probability, Conditional probability and independence, Discrete random variables: bernouilli, binomial, poisson, geometric, hypergeometric. Expectation; mean and variance. Continuous random variables: exponential, gamma, normal. Transformations. Joint distributions, marginal distributions and conditional distributions. Sums, order statistics. Multinomial. Bivariate normal. Expectation of functions, linear combinations. Covariance and correlation. Moment generating function. Limit theorems: inequalities, law of large numbers, central limit theorem, approximation. Sampling distributions: F and chi-squared.
learningOutcomes: Compute probabilities for a variety of discrete, continuous and joint random variables.
Determine marginal and conditional distributions from the joint distribution.
Understand the theory underlying a range of distributions.
State, prove and apply the Central Limit Theorem.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent: Design and implementation of data visualisations for exploratory data analysis, and assessment of structure and dependencies. Principles of graphical perception. Visual encoding of quantitative information. Use of colour. Interactive techniques. Spatial data. Making maps. Software includes the R packages ggplot, Shiny and leaflet.
learningOutcomes: Understand how to encode quantitative information.
Design suitable data visualisations.
Understand and use interactive techniques.
Implement these techniques in software.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:
To develop the students knowledge of Statistical learning techniques.

Introduction to statistical learning. Classification: logistic regression, LDA and QDA. Unsupervised learning: PCA, clustering methods. Resampling methods: cross-validation and the bootstrap. Linear Model selection and regularisation. Modelling with regression splines and smoothing splines. Tree-based methods. Support vector machines. Implementation in R.
learningOutcomes: Construct and apply statistical learning models including classification models, generalised additive models, tree-based models and support vector machines.
Apply and interpret principal component analysis and clustering algorithms.

learningOutcomes: Construct and apply statistical learning models including classification models, generalised additive models, tree-based models and support vector machines.
Apply and interpret principal component analysis and clustering algorithms.

Probability: Sample spaces, events, counting methods, conditional probability, independence, Bayes theorem. Discrete random variables, mean and variance. Binomial and Poisson. Continuous random variables. The Normal model. Bivariate discrete random variables. Covariance and correlation. Sampling distribution of the mean. Confidence intervals and hypothesis testing: one and two samples, paired samples. Confidence intervals and hypothesis testing for one proportion. Computation using statistical software.

For Registered Research students. Permission is required from the Head of the Mathematics & Statistics Department.

modules.moduleContent: The purpose of this module is to update Social Workers regarding the theories that inform professional social work practice. Students are expected to critically engage with areas such as: conceptions of social justice, equality and rights; theoretical approaches and perspectives in social work; and social work as a ‘social profession’. A component of this module addresses the application of theory into professional practice.

The purpose of this module is to enable students to acquire the knowledge and skills essential for fieldwork practice and support them to successfully complete a block fieldwork placement. This requires a demonstration of their professional competence to practice and includes assessment by a social work practice teacher.  

The module consists of a series of preparatory seminars and workshops followed by a 500-hour fieldwork placement (with professional supervision in situ as well as monitoring visits from university staff), followed in turn by further seminars/workshops. Full attendance is required at the preparation for placement components of this module and the student must demonstrate suitability to proceed to placement before they will be permitted to commence their practice placement. 
Outline Content:
 - Reflective learning and reflective practice. Students will be introduced to a range of learning tools including: process recording, journals, critical incident templates. 
 - Professional supervision. Students will be introduced to a range of models of supervision (including peer supervision) and explore the role of supervision in supporting professional, educational and personal development. 
 - Use of self: Students will receive instruction in small groups regarding their use of self in practice contexts. 
 - Learning objectives and plans. Students will be supported in the formulation of learning objectives and in the process of relating these to CORU/SWRB Standards of Proficiency while also situating them within their own evolving sense of professional identity and development. 
 - Knowledge and skills necessary for placement.

This module comprises of three components, social policy, law and sociology for social workers. It focuses in detail on selected areas of social work law and of social policy, paying attention to the ways in which the professional practitioner’s knowledge of law and policy supports all aspects and stages of the social work process. 

The first component of this module builds students’ knowledge of social policy as it is developed and implemented in Ireland and Europe. It includes an overview of the policy-making and legislative processes and the relevant institutional and administrative frameworks, while also enabling students to adopt a critically informed perspective on social and public policy. The module focuses specifically on the key policies which underpin the sectors in which social workers are employed.  
 - Social policy for social work: theories and concepts of social policy development and implementation and how they relate to social work practice. 

The second component provides students with an in-depth knowledge of law relevant to the practice of social work, with particular reference to child and family law. It also considers the ways in which social work practice is influenced or determined by (and in turn interacts with) the policy and legal contexts. Content of this component includes the following: 
 - The constitutional, institutional and administrative frameworks for the law and for policy in Ireland, and the relevant international dimensions (EU, CoE, UN). 
 - Law for social work practice: the legal basis for social work; social workers and legal proceedings. 
 - Law in relation to children and family breakdown; child protection; children in care; adoption; family law; domestic violence. 

The third component of the module aims to equiip students with sociological insights on key areas of society relevant to social work practice.
learningOutcomes: Describe and discuss the policy-making and legislative processes and their administrative and institutional contexts.
Demonstrate an understanding of how social policy creates the context for social work practice and impacts on the lives of individuals, families and communities.
Engage in social policy analysis of the specific areas addressed and discuss the relationship between such analysis and the social work process.
Demonstrate a familiarity with, and understanding of, those aspects of the law that are most relevant for social work practice.
Describe the statutory role of social work in child protection and welfare, mental health and disability contexts.
Demonstrate a capacity to apply legal ideas and principles to the social work process.

modules.moduleContent:
This module is designed to ensure that students develop the skills of disciplined enquiry and investigation that are increasingly important for social work practitioners. On completion of this module, students will comprehend how to gather, analyse and interpret data in a rigorous and systematic way, and communicate their analysis to others using diverse methods and media. Students will gain an understanding of research methods and of the relationship between research questions, designs, methods and findings. The module is also intended to enable students to develop as ‘researcher-practitioners’ and to value this as a central part of their professional identity as social workers. 

Outline Content 
 - Philosophy, theory and method in social research. 
 - Research problems and research designs. 
 - Stages in social research: framing research question(s), literature review, ethical considerations, data collection/analysis, report writing. 
 - Major quantitative and qualitative methods (surveys, interviews, focus groups, systematic reviews). 
 - Documentary sources and official statistics. 
 - Participatory research; action research; narrative and creative methods. 
 - Interface of research, policy development and practice in social work; evidence-based policy and practice; acknowledging and upholding the distinctiveness of research in social work and the social professions. 
 - Using new technologies for research, information and communication purposes.
learningOutcomes: Identify and critically discuss the key philosophical and methodological concepts in social science, and the relationship between theory and method in social research.
Appreciate and articulate the contested nature of social research in the context of the social professions.
Make informed judgements regarding the appropriateness and adequacy of given research questions or problems and the suitability of different methods for different problems.
Critically assess the methods and findings of a range of social research outputs.
Access and interpret relevant official statistics and documentary sources.
Apply a range of quantitative and qualitative methods, individually and in combination, in social work contexts, making appropriate use of information technologies.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
In Year 2 the Social Work Methods and Issues module continues its focus on social work skills and group work practice. It also introduces a focus on social work advocacy and human rights practice.  
Content includes the following components: 

 - Community work (part 2) 
 - Probation services and the criminal justice system (advanced) 
 - Social work in mental health and disability services
 - Group work (part 2) 
 - Social work methods & skills (advanced) 
 - Specialist Workshops, for example: applying legal knowledge to practice, e.g. : preparation of court reports
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of, and capacity to apply, theory, concepts and skills relating to a number of specific counselling perspectives and approaches.
Demonstrate understanding and competence in leading and facilitating groups of different types and for different purposes in social work and community contexts.
Apply advocacy and empowerment methods and approaches in social work settings.
Practice in a professional, proactive and critically aware manner in a number of specific alternative settings and/or with different clinical populations.

modules.moduleName: GOOD AND EVIL: RESPONDING TO THE REALITY
modules.moduleContent:
Module Aims
To consider questions raised by the human experience of good and evil, and how it may impact understandings of God, humanity, ethics, and social issues of crime, punishment and reconciliation.

Module Structure
This module is interdisciplinary, delivered by members of the Departments of Law, Philosophy and Theology. It is comprised of three units per semester. Each unit, delivered over six classes, focuses on a different aspect of Good and Evil. The sixth class will be an integrative seminar where students are encouraged to identify themes, questions, and interactions between the disciplines. 

Indicative Syllabus
Understanding the Experience of Good and Evil from the perspective of: classical philosophy; the question of human dignity; moral atheism; the search for meaning as we live together; Christian ideas of forgiveness and reconciliation; the punishment of crime; changing social and legal norms.
learningOutcomes: Recognise key questions relevant to the theme of good and evil
Identify how the theme can be explored in philosophy, literature, religion, socio-cultural realities and legal debates
Recognise how assumptions about Good and Evil are implicit in, and come to bear on, a plethora of cultural suppositions, existential experiences, religious beliefs and legal policies
Characterise how visions of the human person affect understandings of Good and Evil
Discuss social and legal efforts to direct behaviour and punish evil.

modules.moduleName: HISPANIC LITERATURE AND CULTURE IN THE 21ST CENTURY
modules.moduleContent: This module will provide students with the opportunity to explore selected aspects of Spanish-language literary and cultural production from recent decades. During this time, literature and culture have undergone change in response to globalisation, technological advance, and the social, political and ecological challenges that have shaped 21st-century life so far. The module will focus on specific literary and audiovisual texts, in addition to digital and/or web-based media. Students taking the module will be supplied with the critical frameworks and concepts that will enable them to engage in close readings of these texts, and to thereby uncover how they relate to 21st-century life and what they can tell us about the present historical moment.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

To introduce students to Statistical Inference.

Point estimation: method of moments and maximum likelihood. Consistency, bias and mean squared error. Information and the Cramer-Rao lower bound. Asymptotic distribution of MLEs. Confidence intervals. Testing hypotheses: power, Neymann Pearson, Likelihood ratio tests, large sample approximations. Bayesian estimation: prior and posterior distributions, loss functions, Bayes rule.
learningOutcomes: Use the method of moments and maximum likelihood to determine parameter estimators.
Find the bias and mean square error of estimators.
Derive approximate confidence intervals of maximum likelihood estimators based on their asymptotic distribution.
State and prove the Rao-Blackwell Theorem.
Find best unbiased estimators.
Derive a likelihood ratio test for testing statistical hypotheses.
Derive confidence intervals

modules.moduleContent: This module will provide students with a comprehensive overview of social work principles, values and ethics. Students will explore the application of ethical theory in practice contexts. This will include a critical evaluation of the CORU/SWRB Code of Ethics and Conduct and CORU/SWRB Standards of Proficiency. Teaching will incorporate the use of case studies and practice issues. Principles and processes relating to fitness to practice will also be introduced and discussed.

This module teaches social work skills and methods. On completion of this module, students will understand social work as a process that involves a number of stages, typically described as assessment, planning, intervention, reflection and evaluation. This module will appraise the strengths and weaknesses of various methods and will explore how each reflects the values and principles of social work.  

Given their centrality to social work practice and the range of approaches that can be adopted, counselling and engagement skills and group work skills feature in this module. Teaching and learning methods will facilitate the integration of theory and practice. This module will include skills development in small group classes.

modules.moduleName: THEORIES & PRINCIPLES OF SOCIAL WORK & SOCIAL JUSTICE 1
learningOutcomes: Describe and discuss how social work theory informs social work practice, with specific reference to individual theories
Demonstrate a clear understanding of the principles and values of social work, their relation to ethical frameworks and precepts and their application in practice
Articulate concepts of social justice, human rights and equality and how they relate to social work practice
Integrate insights and findings from across the social sciences in discussing and explaining human development and the life course
Describe how social work is governed and regulated in Ireland and how it relates to and interacts with cognate professions in the health, youth work, community work and social care fields.
modules.notes: Students complete digital posters as part of the assessment

modules.moduleContent:
The purpose of this module and its Year 2 counterpart is to enable students to acquire the knowledge and skills essential for fieldwork practice and support them to successfully complete two contrasting block fieldwork placements. This requires a demonstration of their professional competence to practice and includes assessment by a social work practice teacher. Continuation into Year 2 of the programme is dependent on the student passing the Year 1 placement. In both years of the programme the module consists of a series of preparatory seminars and workshops followed by a 500-hour fieldwork placement (with professional supervision in situ as well as monitoring visits from university staff), followed in turn by further seminars/workshops. Full attendance is required at the preparation for placement components of this module and the student must demonstrate suitability to proceed to placement before they will be permitted to commence their practice placement. 
Outline Content:
 - Reflective learning and reflective practice. Students will be introduced to a range of learning tools including: process recording, journals, critical incident templates. 
 - Professional supervision. Students will be introduced to a range of models of supervision (including peer supervision) and explore the role of supervision in supporting professional, educational and personal development. 
 - Use of self: Students will receive instruction in small groups regarding their use of self in practice contexts. 
 - Learning objectives and plans. Students will be supported in the formulation of learning objectives and in the process of relating these to CORU/SWRB Standards of Proficiency while also situating them within their own evolving sense of professional identity and development. 
 - Knowledge and skills necessary for placement, (introduction). Professional boundaries; the role of networking; record keeping (part 1); team working; partnership and inter-agency work; presentation skills.
learningOutcomes: Learning objectives and plans. Students will be supported in the formulation of learning objectives and in the process of relating these to CORU/SWRB Standards of Proficiency while also situating them within their own evolving sense of professional identity and development.

Knowledge and skills necessary for placement. Professional boundaries; meeting skills; the role of networking; advocacy skills; conflict management; record keeping; team working; partnership and inter-agency work; presentation skills.
Demonstrate an ability to practice methods and strategies of self-care for social workers.

modules.moduleContent:
This module comprises of components related to policy, professional practice and advanced legal issues in social work. 
The policy component will enable students to further develop their knowledge and understanding of social policy and its relevance to their professional practice, specifically critical and radical social work.
A component of this module introduces the study of organisations and management, which themselves have policy-related and legal dimensions. 
In addition, lectures will focus on interdisciplinary practice, professionalism and advanced legal issues.
learningOutcomes: Engage in policy analysis of the specific areas of social policy addressed in this module and discuss the relationship between such analysis and the social work process.
Identify and avail of opportunities to influence and contribute to the policy-making process.
Outline the major theoretical perspectives and the key concepts and ideas in the study of organisation and management, and critically assess their usefulness and relevance in social work.
Demonstrate an awareness of the key principles and procedures involved in the management and administration of social work organisations and agencies.
Engage in informed discussion and debate about the economic dimensions of social work and social policy.
Describe and discuss legislative provisions relating to social work practice in specific areas covered by the module.
Apply principles of quality assurance and best practice approaches.

modules.moduleContent: This module supports students as they conduct an independent research dissertation (or other appropriate research output) focusing on a social work theme or a policy issue with direct relevance for social work practice.
learningOutcomes: Conduct an independent research project making appropriate and informed use of qualitative and/or quantitative methods and taking account of the distinctive nature, challenges and opportunities of the social work context.
Describe and discuss evaluation theory and principles and apply a range of evaluation methods in social work settings.

modules.moduleName: GOOD AND EVIL: REFLECTING ON THE EXPERIENCE
modules.moduleContent:
Module Aims
To consider questions raised by the human experience of good and evil, and how it may impact understandings of God, humanity, ethics, and social issues of crime, punishment and reconciliation.

Module Structure
This module is interdisciplinary, delivered by members of the Departments of Law, Philosophy and Theology. It is comprised of three units per semester. Each unit, delivered over six classes, focuses on a different aspect of Good and Evil. The sixth class will be an integrative seminar where students are encouraged to identify themes, questions, and interactions between the disciplines. 

Indicative Syllabus
Understanding the Experience of Good and Evil from the perspective of: classical philosophy; the question of human dignity; moral atheism; the search for meaning as we live together; Christian ideas of forgiveness and reconciliation; the punishment of crime; changing social and legal norms.
learningOutcomes: Recognise key questions relevant to the theme of good and evil
Identify how the theme can be explored in philosophy, literature, religion, socio-cultural realities and legal debates
Recognise how assumptions about Good and Evil are implicit in, and come to bear on, a plethora of cultural suppositions, existential experiences, religious beliefs and legal policies
Characterise how visions of the human person affect understandings of Good and Evil
Discuss social and legal efforts to direct behaviour and punish evil.

modules.moduleName: INTRODUCTION TO TEACHING, LEARNING AND ASSESSMENT IN HIGHER EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent:
This is a mandatory first module of the PCHETL/PDHETL programme. It introduces participants to core topics related to teaching, learning and assessment in higher education.

The aim of this module is to introduce participants to the context, theory, principles and recognised good practice strategies in respect to specific topics across teaching, learning and assessment in Higher Education.

Indicative topics include:
Higher Education context; developing a personal teaching philosophy; learning theories; reflective practice writing; active learning approaches; student engagement; module design concepts; digital learning approaches; assessment and feedback; inclusive teaching and learning; and peer observation.
learningOutcomes: Articulate their own values, philosophy and goals in relation to Teaching & Learning and their current role in Higher Education
Engage with core theory, policy and readings that influence current practice in teaching, learning and assessment in higher education and articulate how they could apply them in practice
Write critically and reflectively on their own practice and continuous professional development.
Plan and facilitate teaching sessions that are grounded in good practice informed by pedagogical literature, and consider this module's core themes
Critically review their own and peers' teaching practice through an observation of teaching process as a professional development activity
Plan an assessment and feedback strategy at module level, which is reflective of good practice and the module's core themes such as EDI
Incorporate principles and approaches to using digital technologies to enhance and support student learning

modules.moduleName: SOCIAL POLICY FOR SOCIAL WORK PRACTICE
modules.moduleContent: The module builds students’ knowledge of social policy and how it is developed and implemented in Ireland and Europe. It includes an overview of the policy-making and legislative processes and the relevant institutional and administrative frameworks, while also enabling students to adopt a critically informed perspective on social and public policy. This module explores theories and concepts of social policy development and implementation and how they relate to social work practice.

modules.moduleName: THEORIES & PRINCIPLES OF SOCIAL WORK & SOCIAL JUSTICE 2
modules.moduleContent:
This module builds on its counterpart in Year 1 by exploring further aspects of, and approaches to, social work theory, with a particular emphasis on those that (a) relate to contemporary developments in social theory more generally and (b) directly inform anti-oppressive practice. The commitment of social work to social justice and human rights is developed with a focus on the philosophy of rights and the multifaceted nature of power. As in Year 1, emphasis will be placed on the integrative understanding of social science theories and concepts, including psychological and sociological perspectives on human agency and social structure. 
- Contemporary critical social issues and their implications for social work practice. 
- The philosophy of rights; natural, social and discursive approaches. 
- Power and/in social work – multidisciplinary perspectives. 
- Equality, diversity and social work (specific issues and identities; intersectionality).
learningOutcomes: Describe and discuss how social work theory informs social work practice, with specific reference to individual theories.
Relate key developments in contemporary society and contemporary social theory to social work theory and practice.
Give a critical account of the operation of power in diverse social work contexts.
Apply rights and equality frameworks to the situation of specific groups experiencing disadvantage and oppression, and to the experience of intersectionality.
Locate and analyse their own personal experience in the context of the module’s content.
modules.notes: . There are specific lectures on teaching 'Digital Social Work'. Students complete digital posters as part of the assessment

modules.moduleName: CURRICULUM DESIGN, ASSESSMENT AND FEEDBACK IN HIGHER EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent:
This module aims to support participants in developing the necessary knowledge, skills, and self-confidence, to take the lead in designing (or redesigning) innovative and responsive programmes and modules.

The module provides participants with opportunities to explore current theory, research, and trends, in curriculum design, assessment, and feedback, and to gain practical experience of collaboratively designing a programme with peers from the initial proposal to a detailed curriculum document.”
learningOutcomes: Analyse the issues to be considered when designing a programme taking into account, learner needs; disciplinary, institutional, sectoral, and international drivers, relevant policy, and key requirements and standards.
Review the literature and example programme design approaches and processes to identify best practice in curriculum design and development.
Make a convincing case (academic and business) for the development of a new programme.
Apply curriculum design principles and best practice to design a coherent and learner- centred higher education programme.
Write programme and module learning outcomes that adhere to recognised conventions, are aligned, and appropriate to learners’ level and stage

Develop an authentic learning, teaching, assessment and feedback strategy that will promote learner engagement and deep learning, enable learners to meet the intended programme learning outcomes, and support and evidence their learning achievements
Analyse and reflect on their learning, its significance, and application in their own teaching and learning practice.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to give participants an understanding of the contemporary landscape with respect to digital technologies and higher education teaching and learning. The module will give participants practical opportunities to design, implement, and reflect on the use of digital technologies-for learning, assessment and feedback in their own teaching contexts. Through engaging with the module curriculum, participants will develop their competencies in line with the European Framework for The Digital Competence for Educators (DigCompEdu).

Through engaging with the module curriculum, participants will develop their competencies in line with the European Framework for The Digital Competence for Educators (DigCompEdu) [https://ec.europa.eu/jrc/en/digcompedu]. They will explore models, theory and principles of good practice in the design and use of digital technologies in higher education contexts. They will also reflect on this learning in relation to their own practice, discipline and teaching and learning contexts.
learningOutcomes: Describe the political, social, theoretical and pedagogical factors that influence the use of digital technologies in higher education.
With reference to DigCompEdu, evaluate the opportunities and challenges that digital technologies might generate for teaching, learning and assessment within their own context.

Create fully online or blended digital learning resources, activities and learning experiences for their own context that adhere to principles of good pedagogical practice and that accord with best practice guidelines in accessible and inclusive design.
Map their past, current and intended professional development activities to the DigCompEdu Framework.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleName: EQUALITY AND DIVERSITY IN HIGHER EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent:
Diversity is at “… the core of vibrant communities and strong economies …  our institutions (should be) beacons of inclusion welcoming all, with a focus on groupings that are under represented at present.” (Statement of Strategy 2021-2023, Higher Education, Research, Innovation and Science 2021 p.13).  This module explores what being a “beacon of inclusion” might mean for lecturers’ in their day-to-day work.  It aims to facilitate participants to reflect on their own teaching and learning practice and contexts, and to identify their strengths and areas for development in creating inclusive learning environments for their students. 

Module content will consider strategies to embed equality and inclusion in higher education contexts and in particular within their own discipline and practice.  Participants are asked to select a module they currently deliver as an exemplar or lens to focus on while completing the module learning activities.  The selected module serves as a “test case” to sense check their learning and its application to their context and practice.
learningOutcomes: Analyse the legal and policy context for EDI with specific reference to their legal obligation to promote equality and inclusion in their teaching and assessment practices.
Recognise the diversity that exists within their own teaching cohorts and the potential barriers to learning that might exist.
Demonstrate a self-aware, reflexive, informed and critical approach to understanding and meeting the needs of diverse students.
Redesign elements of their teaching and assessment practice based on their engagement with the Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principles.
Commit to continuing to embed UDL principles in their classroom-based and online-based teaching practice."
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
This module is aimed at Masters-level students who may require a higher level of written Business Spanish, to level C1 CEFR, for the completion of essays, reports and other written work related to their studies, work placements and assessment.

Please note that entry onto this module is restricted to students whose first language is not Spanish.
learningOutcomes: Communicate correctly, clearly and efficiently in Spanish in a variety of different types of written texts and oral discourses based on their communicative purpose, using the necessary strategies to adapt the communication to the situation and the addressee.

Participate in oral, written and multimodal interactions in the Spanish language and produces texts of different types, demonstrating appropriate use of structures and vocabulary for the situation and context.
Recognize and use specialized registers and languages within the professional field (company, business, or organisation) in Spanish.
Produce a variety of textual genres needed for different business situations and contexts, so that communication circulates within and between companies.
Manage the internal and external communication of the company: reports, electronic mail, memoranda, catalogues and communications with clients, investors, and other relevant stakeholders.

Identify linguistic mediation needs in professional contexts, as well as situations and issues that can generate cultural misunderstanding, while recognising and counteracting one's own cultural expectations regarding the behaviour of the participants in the intercultural encounter.

modules.moduleContent: tbc
tbc

Bayesian Analysis for simple models: Bayes' rule, inferring a binomial probability, prior distributions, Markov Chain Monte Carlo, Gibbs sampler, assessing model convergence, Bayesian analysis with JAGS, simple regression models, hierarchical/multilevel models, generalized linear models, model checking.
learningOutcomes: Understand the concepts of prior and posterior distributions.
Compare Bayesian and classical models.
Implement Bayesian models and apply them to problems in data science.

Overview
--------
Bayesian Analysis for simple models: Bayes' rule, inferring a binomial probability, prior distributions, Markov Chain Monte Carlo, Gibbs sampler, 
assessing model convergence, Bayesian analysis with JAGS, simple regression models, hierarchical/multilevel models, generalized linear models, model checking.

ST645 is equivalent to ST405 (i.e. lectures and tutorials as for ST405).
learningOutcomes: Understand the concepts of prior and posterior distributions.
Compare Bayesian and classical models.
Implement Bayesian models and apply them to problems in data science.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: 
Equip students to carry out data science projects in R.

Basics of R: Data structures, control structures, functions. Exploratory data analysis. Reports with Rmarkdown. Data wrangling with dplyr. Visualisation: ggplot, shiny. Big data techniques, Sparklyr.
learningOutcomes: Understand and use R data structures.
Use R to produce data visualisations.
Program with R.
Use R for large data problems.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Overview
--------
Checking distributional assumptions. Consequences of non-normality for t-and F-tests. Ranks and order statistics. Sign test and Wilcoxon signed-rank test for single samples. The Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney test for two-samples. The Kruskal-Wallis test for k-samples. Association and correlation. Bootstrap methods. Density estimation. Non-parametric smoothing.

Lectures and tutorials as for ST204.

For Registered Research students. Permission is required from the Head of the Mathematics & Statistics Department.
learningOutcomes: Understand a wide range of non-parametric tests and identify when they are appropriate to use.
Implement non-parametric methods using software such as R.
Fit non-parametric regression curves using various algorithms.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Overview
--------
The stationarity of time series models. The forecaster's toolbox. Judgmental forecasts. Regression. Time series decomposition. Exponential smoothing. ARIMA models. Advanced forecasting methods. Using software such as R for forecasting.

Lectures and tutorials as for ST306.
learningOutcomes: Fit, predict and understand time series models.
Implement time series decompositions, exponential smoothing and ARIMA models.
modules.notes: This module was tagged as Data/Digital using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

modules.moduleContent:
Overview of linear models and generalised linear models. For a range of LMMs and GLMMs: a single random effect, multiple random effects, nested random effects, split plot design, repeated measures, random coefficient models. Model estimation and model selection procedures. Model diagnostics. Predictions. 
Lectures and tutorials as for ST401.
learningOutcomes: Understand the concepts of random effects, and repeated measures models.
Implement and interpret models and use them to understand statistical data.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To introduce students to Statistical Inference. Point estimation: method of moments and maximum likelihood. Consistency, bias and mean squared error. Information and the Cramer-Rao lower bound. Asymptotic distribution of MLEs. Confidence intervals. Testing hypotheses: power, Neymann Pearson, Likelihood ratio tests, large sample approximations. Bayesian estimation: prior and posterior distributions, loss functions, Bayes rule.

Lectures and tutorials as for ST402.
learningOutcomes: Use the method of moments and maximum likelihood to determine parameter estimators.
Find the bias and mean square error of estimators.
Derive approximate confidence intervals of maximum likelihood estimators based on their asymptotic distribution.
State and prove the Rao-Blackwell Theorem.
Find best unbiased estimators.
Derive a likelihood ratio test for testing statistical hypotheses.
Derive confidence intervals.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides students with an in-depth knowledge of law relevant to the practice of social work, with particular reference to child and family law. It also considers the ways in which social work practice is influenced or determined by (and in turn interacts with) legal contexts. Content of this component includes the following: 
 - The constitutional, institutional and administrative frameworks for the law in Ireland, and the relevant international dimensions (EU, CoE, UN). 
 - Law for social work practice: the legal basis for social work; social workers and legal proceedings. 
 - Law in relation to children and family breakdown; child protection; children in care; adoption; family law; domestic violence. 
 - Applying legal knowledge to practice, e.g. : preparation of court reports

This module will explore current practice across a range of social work settings and relevant issues, including the following: 
 - Social work and addictions 
 - Introduction to health-related social work 
 - Introduction to community work 
 - Introduction to probation services and the criminal justice system 
 - Introduction to social work with mental health & disabilities 
 - Introduction to group work 
 - Child wellbeing

This module introduces social work skills and methods and how these relate to different areas of practice. The purpose of this module and its Year 2 counterpart is to lay a foundation for students regarding key social work methods and how these are adopted in social work practice in various settings and with different populations. Students are introduced to the diversity of context in social work practice. 

On completion of this module, students will understand social work as a process that involves a number of stages, typically described as assessment, planning, intervention, reflection and evaluation. This module will appraise the strengths and weaknesses of various methods and will explore how each reflects the values and principles of social work.  

Given their centrality to social work practice and the range of approaches that can be adopted, counselling and engagement skills and group work skills feature in both years of the programme. Teaching and learning methods will facilitate the integration of theory and practice. This module will include skills development in small group classes. This module will introduce students to the practice of social work in diverse settings. 

Outline Content: 
 - Social work and addictions 
 - Introduction to health-related social work 
 - Introduction to community work 
 - Introduction to probation services and the criminal justice system 
 - Introduction to social work with mental health & disabilities 
 - Introduction to group work 
 - Child wellbeing 
 - Introduction to social work methods & skills
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of, and ability to apply, theory, concepts and skills relating to a number of specific counselling perspectives and approaches.
Demonstrate an understanding of, and ability to apply, theory, concepts and skills relating to group work and group development in a social work context.
Demonstrate an ability to build and sustain interpersonal and professional relationships in group and social work settings.
Apply the social work process and principles in the professional settings of child welfare, child protection and family support.
Practice in a professional, proactive and critically aware manner in a number of specific alternative settings and/or with different clinical populations.

modules.moduleContent:
This module takes the same form as Professional Development and Fieldwork Practice 1 and similarly includes a 500-hour supervised fieldwork placement, which must be in a substantially different setting from the Year 1 placement. International fieldwork placements will be facilitated and encouraged provided that the requirements relating to professional approval and standards of proficiency are adhered to. 

Outline Content:
 
 - Professionalism in practice. Students will examine situations and scenarios that present issues relating to professional discretion, judgement and decision-making, accountability, and explore how discourses of professionalism vary from one context to another.  

 - Knowledge and skills necessary for placement, (advanced). Professional boundaries; meeting skills; advocacy skills; conflict management; record keeping (part 2); team working; partnership and inter-agency work. 

 - In addition: 
     - Interview and job-seeking skills. Creative and experiential workshops providing students with the simulated experience of applying for social work jobs and attending interview (students ‘interview’ each other along with staff). 
     - Continuing Professional Development (CPD). Introduction to CPD as the means by which social workers maintain and improve their knowledge, skills and competence and develop their professional qualities throughout their careers.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an integrated development of knowledge, skills and personal qualities that confirms their suitability for a professional career in social work and meets the SWRB Standards of Proficiency.
Present themselves in interviews and other formal situations in a way that allows them to make optimal use of their academic and professional learning and development.
Demonstrate a commitment to their own ongoing personal and personal development, to social work as a profession and to the interests and wellbeing of those they work with.

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL PRACTICE, LEADERSHIP & SCHOLARSHIP IN HE
modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to provide participants with the knowledge, skills, and structures, to consider their strengths, weaknesses, aspirations, and needs, in respect of their professional practice, educational leadership, and scholarship of teaching and learning (SoTL) abilities and potential.  Participants will have opportunities to explore their own practice and build their skills in these fields, for example, they will share examples that demonstrate a scholarly approach in their teaching practice, and will explore how to design, lead, and write-up a SoTL initiative.  Throughout the module participants will be challenged to critically reflect on their knowledge, skills, abilities, and future aims with respect to their SoTL and educational leadership activities.

In support of this aim, the module is designed to give participants the space to meet their individual learning goals and to plan future learning opportunities. Each workshop typically comprises an overview of the topic, time for independent study of an assigned reading or completion of a learning activity aimed at enabling participants to apply the concept to their practice or desired future role(s), and a community of practice group session where participants will have opportunities to learn from each other, share insights, learning, and resources.
learningOutcomes: Self-evaluate their scholarly teaching, scholarship of teaching and learning (SoTL) and leadership abilities and capabilities.
Critically analyse the literature and examples of SoTL initiatives and identify a toolkit of resources to help with developing well-designed and robust SoTL initiatives.
Explore opportunities within their professional practice to effect innovation and change that promote quality teaching and/or enhance the student experience.
Plan the design, conducting, and writing-up a SoTL project that directly relates to student learning or the enhancement of the student experience.
Recognise educational leadership opportunities that are compatible with their values and aspirations.
Create an individual development plan detailing how they will actively grow their scholarly teaching, SoTL and educational leadership capabilities.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to engage participants in the theory and practice of recognised microteaching skills applied to higher education teaching. Through individual, peer and group activities, participants will have the opportunity to practice a variety of core classroom teaching skills used to teach, facilitate, and engage students in learning. Participants will observe and reflect on their own development of these skills during the module. 

Core topics include: reflecting on values and teaching philosophy; principles of good teaching practice; creating a positive learning culture; exploration of specific core teaching skills such as questioning, clarifying, verbal/non-verbal cues and areas for development; employing positive classroom management approaches and scenarios.
learningOutcomes: Develop and enact teaching and learning approaches and activities that allow a positive learning culture to exist in their own teaching contexts.
Demonstrate core teaching skills and competencies, under observation, that enhance and support students’ learning journey.
Self-audit their practice of core classroom skills and competencies.
Perform the role of a Critical Friend for a fellow teacher, in line with best academic practice.
Formulate personal principles of good teaching that they strive to implement in their professional practice.

modules.moduleContent:
This is a 5 ECTS capstone module that is taken as a final module of the programme.
