learningOutcomes: discuss the nature of physical knowledge, measurement of physical quantities and the use of units in describing the laws of nature;
describe the physics of motion and forces (kinematics & dynamics) using an algebraic approach and vectorial description of motion;
discuss the basic properties of matter and thermal physics;
solve simple numerical problems relevant to fundamental mechanics and thermodynamics;
carry out relevant laboratory experiments to demonstrate and test physical principles and report the results.

modules.moduleContent: Indicative Syllabus â€¢ Introduction, motivation and scope of course. â€¢ Number systems â€¢ Complex numbers â€¢ Complex conjugate â€¢ Exponential and polar form â€¢ Inequalities â€¢ Sequences and series â€¢ Arithmetic and geometric series â€¢ Finite and infinite sums â€¢ Convergence â€¢ Limits and continuity â€¢ Maclaurin and Taylor series â€¢ Differential calculus â€¢ Tangents to curves â€¢ L'Hopital's rule â€¢ Sum, product, quotient and chain rules for derivatives â€¢ Critical points, maxima, minima and points of inflexion â€¢ Integral calculus â€¢ Integrals as areas and limits of sums â€¢ Definite and indefinite integrals, integration techniques â€¢ Applications of Integration â€¢ Calculation of lengths, areas and volumes â€¢ Mean and RMS values â€¢ Introduction to numerical integration â€¢ Differential equations â€¢ First order differential equations â€¢ Introduction to second order differential equations
learningOutcomes: add, subtract and multiply complex numbers and Convert complex numbers between Cartesian and polar forms;
differentiate all commonly occurring functions including polynomials, exponential functions, trigonometric functions, hyperbolic functions and combinations thereof;
distinguish between the two main meanings of a derivative, namely the derivative as a tangent and the derivative as a rate of change;
integrate certain standard functions, constructed from polynomials, exponential functions, trigonometric functions, hyperbolic functions and combinations thereof;
distinguish between the two main meanings of integration, namely the integral as the inverse of the derivative and the integral as the area under a curve;
apply Taylor series to numerically approximate functions and Apply Simpson's rule to numerically evaluate integrals;
solve simple first and second order ordinary differential equations;
apply and select the appropriate mathematical techniques to solve a variety of associated engineering problems.

modules.moduleContent:
• The Basics of C (Revision)
• C++ and Object Orientation
• Input and Output Syntax
• Encapsulation and Information Hiding
• Objects, Private and Public parts
• Objects and Classes
• Abstraction, Attributes and Methods
• Constructors and Destructors
• Scope and Object Lifetimes
• Static Member Functions
• Inheritance and Hierarchies
• Aggregations and Associations
• Polymorphism
• Input Error Handling and Exception Handling
• File Input and Output Syntax
• UML, Class Diagrams, Design techniques
learningOutcomes: Outline and apply the basic principles of object oriented programming; classes, object, encapsulation, inheritance etc.
Write code for simple C++ programs.
Design and implement software using different object oriented programming design methodologies and techniques.
Applying knowledge to design a program addressing an unseen problem

modules.moduleContent: This module is designed to introduce students to the principles of accounting enabling them to support decision making and meet business challenges.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of the accountant and the financial environment in which they operate;
Develop an understanding of the double entry bookkeeping system and its role in the preparation of financial statements of a business;
Develop the ability to interpret the various elements of a set of financial statements to inform decisionmaking;
Evaluate the performance of a business using financial statement analysis;
Demonstrate an ability to advise on working capital management techniques including the preparation of cash budgets.; and
Assess alternative sources of finance to meet organisational finance requirements.

modules.moduleContent: Accounting work placement

This module examines important analytical techniques such as:

(i) Chromatography: Material covered includes the theoretical principles of chromatographic separations, application of liquid chromatography, with emphasis on HPLC and on the specific chromatographic techniques used in downstream processing for biopharmaceutical manufacturing and protein purification, i.e. IEX, SEC, HIC, and affinity purification, the selection and optimisation of separation conditions, and detection methods.

(ii) NMR spectroscopy of organic molecules. Includes an introduction to the technique, and the concepts of how signal position, relative integration and coupling patterns in proton NMR spectroscopy can be interpreted to identify the carbon-hydrogen framework of an organic compound, and an introduction to both 13C NMR spectroscopy and 2-D spectroscopy.

(iii) Electrochemistry: Material covered includes conductivity, molar conductivity, limiting molar conductivity, ionic strength and the application of conductivity measurements in analytical chemistry. Next, the concept of an electrochemical cell and the cell potential are introduced.
learningOutcomes: Have a good understanding of the theoretical principles behind chromatographic separations.
Discuss and evaluate the most important factors that affect HPLC separations.
Have a good knowledge of the steps involved in method development.
Describe the basic operation of an NMR spectrometer.
Account for and be able to predict the resonance position and the coupling pattern of the signals in a 1H NMR spectrum of a simple organic molecule.
Predict and be able to account for the 13C NMR spectrum of a simple organic molecule. Analyse a 2D spectrum of a simple molecule.
Predict the behaviour of ions in solution.
Outline the operation of electrochemical cells.

modules.moduleContent: Biopharmaceutical chemistry and structure determination methods for biomolecules. This module examines two important areas of pharmaceutical and biomedical chemistry: (i) Biopharmaceutical chemistry. This part of the module focuses on the concepts behind the design and development of biopharmaceuticals and biologics and their roles in modern pharmaceutical chemistry and biotechnology. The course material reviews the different sources of biopharmaceuticals, and mostly focuses on the biomanufacturing process. The biochemical and molecular mechanisms of action of specific classes of biopharmaceutical drugs, such as antibodies, and the role of glycosylation of biologics is also reviewed. (ii) Structural determination methods for biomolecules: Material covered includes: Structure of biomolecules for example, biological macromolecules- proteins, nucleic acids, polysaccharides and fats and small biomolecules for example small peptides, amino acids, monosaccharides and nucleotides; chemical analysis of proteins and peptides, ESI, MALDI and Tandem Mass spectrometry, NMR spectroscopy and hardware development, FT-NMR, 2-D NMR, X-ray crystallography. Emphasis will be placed on relating each technique to determining protein structure.
learningOutcomes: Discuss a number of factors that are important to the techniques commonly used to obtain structural information on biomolecules.
Be able to interpret the mass spectrum of a biomolecule.
Be able to interpret or predict the NMR spectrum of small biomolecules.
Demonstrate an understanding of what biopharmaceuticals and biologics are and their role in modern pharmaceutical chemistry.
Discuss the different sources of biopharmaceuticals and the role of genomics and proteomics in biopharmaceutical chemistry.
Discuss the mode of action and the uses of specific biopharmaceutical drugs.

modules.moduleContent:
Alkaloid chemistry and advanced organic synthesis.

This module covers two areas of organic chemistry:
(i) Alkaloid chemistry. Material covered includes: introduction to alkaloid chemistry; elucidation of their structures using modern methods; classical and modern methods of their synthesis.

(ii) Organic synthesis I: The material covered in this section includes the use of enolate equivalents, epoxide ring formation and regioselective epoxide ring opening, as well as the use of catalysis in synthesis.

  Organic synthesis II:  The retrosynthetic or disconnection approach to the synthetic design of organic molecules.  (i)  overview of retrosynthetic analysis including associated nomenclature and graphic symbols, (ii)  evaluation of good and bad disconnections, (iii)  retrosynthetic analysis of selected molecules drawn from different structural classes.
learningOutcomes: Explain various alkaloid structures and their biosynthesis.
Employ 2D NMR techniques in the elucidation of alkaloid structures.
Use appropriate symbols and terminology to describe a retrosynthetic approach to the synthesis of simple organic molecules.
Exercise judgement between good and bad disconnections.
Suggest conditions for reaction schemes devised by the retrosynthetic analysis.

modules.moduleName: QUALITY ASSURANCE, FORMULATION AND PROCESS DEVELOPMENT
modules.moduleContent: This module examines three important areas of pharmaceutical chemistry: (i) Formulation of drug products. The course introduces the principles and purpose of formulating active pharmaceutical ingredients. The factors that must be considered in developing an effective formulation are considered and the role of drug delivery as part of the drug development process is discussed. Formulation of both small molecule and biopharmaceutical drugs is examined. (ii) Pharmaceutical Industry Overview: This course covers topics relevant to the structure of the Pharmaceutical Industry including drug research & development, fundamentals of Good Manufacturing Practice (including Case Studies), Quality Assurance, Quality Control and Validation. It includes calculations of chemical reaction hazards and the importance of plant & process safety will be considered. (iii) Thermodynamics of Scale-up. Material covered includes a description of the important considerations in the scale up of any chemical reaction; differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and the application of DSC in the analysis of pharmaceutical compounds; reaction calorimetry and the application of reaction calorimetry in the scale-up of chemical reactions.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the basic principles that are considered in designing an appropriate dosage form for a drug.
Describe the most common types of dosage forms for different routes of delivery.
Describe the different challenges encountered when formulating small molecule drugs compared to biopharmaceutical products.
Demonstrate a detailed knowledge of the structure of a Pharmaceutical Plant, and the importance of GMP within this area.
Describe the steps used in a typical drug development process from discovery to market.
Discuss the responsibilities of the FDA and describe how to identify and evaluate hazards in the Pharmaceutical Industry.
Identify the important steps in the scaleup of a chemical reaction.
Demonstrate an understanding of differential scanning calorimetry and its applications in pharmaceutical chemistry.

modules.moduleContent:
This experiential and reflective learning module is designed to offer students the opportunity to put theory into practice, as well as to learn new skills and acquire new knowledge. This module is designed to assist students in translating their first- and second-year conceptual learning into practical actions during their work placement. Through this, our students develop an understanding of the skills and competencies required by the world of industry. By translating the conceptual insights of the undergraduate modules into a set of actions, and applying them to real problems at work, students will learn and reflect on how to bridge theoretical knowledge and practical competencies. 

This module supports the student’s career development, by facilitating students in optimising their first steps into the professional labour market. In doing this, students will enhance their employability through analysing and reflecting how they can best utilize and apply their conceptual and theoretical learnings and experience in a contemporary professional organisation. 

This module’s supporting tutorials will the provide students with the background and critical skills required for reflecting on their placement experience and assist in preparing their placement report. To pass the module, students must have completed their work placement and met the requirements of the placement tutorials.
learningOutcomes: Undertake an analysis of their career experiences and development in their placement organisation. Using appropriate concepts from the tutorials, students will identify their career dispositions following their placement experience;

Undertake a critical analysis of their placement organisation's structure and of group and teamwork, proposing ideas for improving team dynamics and performance. The students should demonstrate an improvement in the skills of managing leadership/follower dynamics, working with colleagues and under supervision, leading and initiating activities. Students should be able to identify learnings that can be transferred to their degree group projects upon return to their studies;

Undertake a critical analysis of their sources of motivation at work and the relationship with job satisfaction. Suggest how they would leverage employee motivations to improve job satisfaction if they were the top managers of the placement organisation;
Undertake a critical analysis of aspects of the HRM system (as explored in the tutorials) in their placement organisation; and

Drawing from the competencies of year one and two of their degree, further develop their business acumen within the industrial setting of their placement employer, applying theory in practice and reflecting practice in theory.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this course is to provide participants with a broad overview of key theoretical frameworks in relation to substance misuse and dependence and to reflect on basic helping and intervention skills. 

This course focuses on introducing participants to current debates on addiction. On completion, participants will have become familiar with contemporary addiction theory as well as prevention and treatment strategies. They will have explored associated issues such as public policy, factual information on substance misuse, gender issues and community drug prevention issues to name but a few. The course will provide participants with adequate knowledge and foundation skills to respond effectively and positively to addiction-related issues as they arise at work or in the community.
learningOutcomes: Understand some of the professional issues and debates which arise within drug and alcohol services.
Understand the main theories and principal treatment regimes aimed at tackling substance misuse and dependence.
Identify and evaluate the direction of drug and alcohol policy development over the past thirty years in the Irish context.
Compare and contrast the merits and demerits of approaches to substance misuse e.g. the harm reduction model vs the abstinence model.
Engage in critical debate through inclass discussion on topics and themes covered throughout the course, with a particular emphasis on learner selfreflection.
Use academic and stylistic conventions appropriately and consistently in accordance with the rules governing academic assignments.
Locate and recognize substance misuse within it’s biological, psychosocial, cultural and political contexts.

modules.moduleContent: Nanochemistry, polymer chemistry and colloid chemistry. This module examines three important areas of materials science: (i) Nanochemistry: This course introduces the new topic of Nanochemistry and the application of Chemistry to Nanotechnology. The course starts with AFM and STM (nanoprobe techniques). The synthesis of nanoparticles is then introduced and some of the more recent applications of nanoparticles are described. Finally, carbon nanotubes are described together with some of their recent applications. (ii) Organic Polymer Chemistry: This course starts with the history and classes of synthetic organic polymers. Polymerization mechanisms (radical, cationic, anionic) and initiator types, chain transfer, head-to-tail addition, polymer chain branching and polymer stereochemistry are discussed. Thermoplastic and thermosetting polymers. Biodegradable polymers: synthesis and structural properties. The physical properties of polymer: molecular weight and glass transition temperature. (iii) Colloid Chemistry: This course introduces the basic principles of colloid science, including the nature of colloid solutions, the forces involved in colloidal stability, and the link between colloids and surfaces. Topics covered are: surface tension and wetting, self-assembly, emulsions, charged colloids and granular materials. Industrial and biological applications of colloid science are also discussed.
learningOutcomes: Construct synthetic protocols for common organic polymers.
Calculate the polymer molecular weight and the polydispersity index of common polymers.
Interpret and predict the physical properties of polymers.
Describe the fundamental principles on which the AFM and STM techniques are based.
Describe simple methodologies that are used in the synthesis of nanomaterials.
Describe what a colloidal solution is and discuss the factors affecting colloidal stability.
Discuss the applications of colloid science in industry and in biology.

modules.moduleName: COMMUNITY WORK IN A CHANGING IRELAND
modules.moduleContent: Community work in a changing Ireland

modules.moduleName: LEADERSHIP AND MANAGEMENT IN COMMUNITY BASED PROJECTS
modules.moduleContent:
Please note this Certificate programme does not run every academic year.  Contact appliedsocialstudies@mu.ie with any queries.

The NUI Certificate in Leadership and Management in Community Based Projects is aimed at managers and co-coordinators of community based organisations or staff who in the future may work at this level. The content of the programme covers all the main areas of work undertaken by managers of community based projects. It is designed to align with the ethos of social inclusion and equality work in the community and youth sector, and is framed in an overall approach consistent with the values of youth work and community work. The certificate is framed around the following themes: Context of Community Based Initiatives , The Role of Manager, Governance responsibilities and obligations, Financial Management, Managing Difference and Diversity and  Strategic Planning and Evaluation.
learningOutcomes: Appreciation of the complexities and challenges involved in leadership and management roles;
Skills and theoretical frameworks of management and leadership;
Critical awareness of the legal, professional and policy context of management in community based organisations.

modules.moduleContent:
This is a group project module. The application area of project is dependent on the program, having either an (a) Software Engineering or (b) Multimedia, Mobile and Web focus.  It is intended that the project will develop a range of skills from analysis, to critical thinking, collaboration, and academic writing. It initially focuses on problem-solving through an analysis of the agreed project requirements, leading to system design, followed by group programming and testing. This will enhance their skills of logic, computational problem solving, and implementation. This is realised through regular supervised laboratory work that must be carried out together. The emphasis throughout the labs is on producing programs in an organised fashion informed by a software engineering where communication among the team members will be essential to running of the project. Additionally, through group meetings, team members will evaluate the evolving project, critically assess its progress, and make decisions. This interactivity will be measured as part of the module evaluation. At stages during the project there will be assessed formal presentations to the class cohort. At the close of the project the group will be required to submit their code archive and a written report that must include testing, evaluation results, and a reflective analysis on the data generated and the project outcomes.
Project organisation fundamentals, coding skills, analysis & use of data, group interaction and effective communications training, presentation skills, academic writing
learningOutcomes: exhibit that they have grasped the fundamentals of applying an 'Agile' approach to a software project;
show that they know how to apply their coding knowledge to be able to produce working software;
demonstrate an understanding of how basic System Testing can be carried out;
show that they engaged in group interaction for the purpose of effective problemsolving;
skillfully create and manage group presentations that illustrate their work progression;
communicate the trajectory and outcomes of the project clearly through the medium of academic writing.

modules.moduleContent: Software requirement elicitation; consensus estimation; design, testing and implementation as a team; project management techniques and meeting skills; combining new knowledge and knowledge from previous modules to the design, development and deployment of a mobile/web application using SCRUM; using DevOps in a group context.
learningOutcomes: Work effectively as a member of a software development team in an Agile environment.
Deliver technical presentations during the software development cycle.
Apply learned skills and experience more effectively in future project work.
Plan and manage team activities & communicate effectively with other team members.
Use collaborative tools (e.g. software repositories) in a group context.
Be able to design and deploy a product (including SSL certificate installation, configuring/using virtualised servers, serverless hosting, use hosted NOSQL DBs).
Be able to use modern DevOps approaches.

modules.moduleContent: A minimum six months work placement which extends beyond the end of the summer (into the next academic year)
learningOutcomes: Compile a suitable CV and write a suitable application for a work position.
Evaluate knowledge gained during a work placement.
Evaluate how the placement reflected the practice of Computer Science and its relationship with the degree programme.
Document work placement experience in a diary or log.
Complete and return a work placement report documenting the student’s main contributions to the place of work.

modules.moduleContent: A minimum of 6 months work experience
learningOutcomes: Take direction on applying work placements from the work placement officers, meeting all deadlines set by them and by any potential employers
Find a suitable work placement by participating in suitable application and interview processes
Participate in and contribute to a place of work for at least six months (unless curtailed due to circumstances outside the student’s control) in a manner that is agreeable to your employer
Work effectively with others within the social context of a software development team.

modules.moduleContent: Machine learning principles; The probabilistic perspective on machine learning; Supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning; Biological neurons and their relation to linear classifiers; Supervised learning techniques: linear regression, the perceptron learning rule, backprogation, recurrent networks, deep learning, support vector machines; Unsupervised techniques: k-means clustering, EM of Gaussian mixtures, hidden Markov models; Reinforcement learning: Policy-value iteration, Q-learning, TD-learning, deep reinforcement learning systems.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish the different categories of Machine Learning techniques and identify situations in which they might be used.
Describe the implementation of simple algorithms for machine learning.
Discuss the adaptation of a standard technique to a given specific problem.
Describe the implementation and evaluate some standard machine learning algorithms in a general purpose programming language such as Java.
Obtain a basic understanding of machine learning approaches and neural networks.
Discuss the relationships between training set, test set, generalisation, cross validation.
Describe how various machine learning techniques work and their strengths and limitations.
Select and use appropriate machine learning techniques to solve real problems.

modules.moduleContent: This course provides an introduction to mobile development with Javacript libraries or frameworks which are used for building user interfaces for mobile applications. The course will focus on handling the view layer for platform independent web and mobile applications. Students will be introduced to the capabilities and limitations of modern mobile platforms and the challenges which are posed by this development environment. The module will carefully consider the technology and business trends impacting modern mobile application development. In particular, the module will deal with providing an effective user interface for mobile devices. Solutions to managing application data within the mobile application will be discussed using Object Oriented Programming (OOP) approaches. Through appropriate coursework and a team project students will gain experience in the design and development of mobile application using a suitable Javascript library or framework. An overview of the enterprise scale requirements (performance, scalability, modifiability, availability, and security) of mobile applications will also be provided.
learningOutcomes: Describe the capabilities and limitations of mobile platforms that affect application development and deployment.
Describe the challenges that mobile development has in providing an effective user interface.
Describe the technology and business trends impacting modern mobile application development.
Design and develop introductory level mobile applications using a mobile application development framework.
Design solutions to manage application data in mobile applications using object oriented programming (OOP) approaches.
Describe and explain the enterprise scale requirements (performance, scalability, modifiability, availability, and security) of mobile applications.

modules.moduleContent: Defining intelligence, Turing's imitation game; problem-solving, state-spaces, generate and test, game playing; graph-searching, two-player games, optimisation strategies; biologically inspired computation. Parsing, semantics and knowledge representation; information retrieval, document ranking
learningOutcomes: Explain the underlying principles, and evaluate the advantages and limitations, of the AI approach to problem solving.
Describe and implement a range of algorithms and discuss the limitations associated with each.
Demonstrate the application of artificial intelligence techniques to game playing.
Define the concepts of different systems and explain how they differ from classical search techniques.
Apply the machine learning approaches and evolutionary computation.
Distinguish between the various recommendation approaches and understand how related research has addressed key problems in this area .

modules.moduleContent:
Introduction to Distributed Systems and Common Applications in E-Commerce, Big Data, Modelling and Analytics, Ubiquitous Computing, Pervasive Systems, Massive Multiplayer Games, High Performance and High Availability Applications, Cloud Computing, Content Delivery Networks and Edge Computing.
Architectural Paradigms: Client/Server, SOA, Peer to Peer, Broker Architecture, Event Driven and Shared Memory models.

One primary theme explores the evolution of Internet based systems for Client/Server communication and data exchange formats from the TCP/IP protocol, socket interface, early RPC, ORB, RMI, text based HTTP dependent web services using XML technologies, REST style with JSON, high performance synchronous, asynchronous and streaming binary RPC using HTTP/2, gRPC and protocol buffers, multicast group communication, reliability and ordering algorithms and analysis of resource and latency costs.
The other primary theme explores systems for expressing and managing application concurrency, multithreading theory, thread management and synchronisation for Posix threads, Java threads, concept of threadpools and executors. The OpenMP API for expressing parallelism on multiprocessor shared memory architectures. MPI for large scale parallel distributed computing demonstrating the power of high performance collective communication primitives for moving data between processing elements when implementing Map/Reduce style programming patterns. All of these systems are chosen as they can be installed and explored on a standalone desktop environment.
Finally, a general introduction to transaction systems and reliability of Client/Server interactions is presented explaining how to implement atomicity, isolation and durability propeties of transactions in concurrent systems and understanding latency and resource costs of these implementations.
Students will carry out coursework from a selection of programming assignments given out on specific topics.
learningOutcomes: Gain an understanding of the benefits & complexities surrounding parallel and distributed systems design in an Internet context. They will have an overview of the principle architectural approaches to design and be able to assess, and appreciate through historical experience, the characteristics of accessibility & useability of a system or application in the context of its understandability & simplicity of its technologies, its use of open protocols and platform and language neutrality feature.

Assess the likely performance and scalability of a design, identify bottlenecks and points of failure and be capable of suggesting design or technological improvements to communication and/or processing structure, by applying an indepth understanding of the evolution of communication protocols, coupled with the design and motivations for current technological capabilities in conjunction with knowledge of client side and server side concurrency mechanisms and techniques.
Gain a basic understanding of how to use well known APIs for expressing parallel algorithms to run on multiprocessor shared memory or networked architectures.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides an introduction to Robotics. The main focus is on building basic mathematical methods for describing and controlling the motion of robotic systems.
Integrated product design; Sensors; Use of software observers. Vision Systems: Image processing techniques; Perspective Transformations; Robotics; Basic Configurations; Statistical methods, Robot Navigation and Locomotions; Statistical robotics used for mapping and location estimation.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the history of industrial robots, robots in popular culture and the anatomy and features of a typical robot.
Evaluate mobility of a simple machine using number synthesis, joints and pairs.
Describe the motion of articulated robots.
Identify suitable actuators and controllers for a specific task.
Understand control strategies.
Describe the approaches and techniques to describe mobile robot motion, localisation, mapping and path planning.
Have an appreciation of the software used to control and model robotic systems.

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED CONCEPTS AND ISSUES IN COMPUTER SCIENCE
modules.moduleContent: TBA
DIGIAL CREATIVITY IN YOUTH SETTINGS
modules.notes:
Learning Outcomes
By the end of the certificate, participants will be able to:

1. Explain and apply key concepts, models, and frameworks of digital creativity in youth work, including the TechSpace approach and non-formal, experiential, inquiry-based learning.

2. Design and facilitate engaging digital media, STEM, and maker activities that support young people to become creative, critical, and collaborative digital creators.

3. Use a range of digital tools, platforms, and production techniques (e.g., digital media, audio, STEM technologies) confidently and creatively in youth settings.

4. Create inclusive, youth-centred learning environments that foster active participation, experimentation, and youth-led digital creativity.

5. Critically reflect on facilitation practice, drawing on observation, feedback, communities of practice, and youth work principles to improve digital and STEM delivery.

6. Develop and implement an organisational plan for ICT integration, demonstrating an understanding of whole-organisation approaches to digital capacity-building.

7. Design, deliver, and evaluate an applied digital creativity project with young people, demonstrating the ability to link theory, practice, and youth development outcomes.

8. Demonstrate understanding of national and sectoral policies and strategies relevant to digital creativity, youth work, and 21st-century skills, and apply these to inform practice.

modules.moduleContent:
Sociological perspectives on ''youth'' and ''community''; Key debates in community/youth studies; Young people in contemporary society; Changing and emerging communities. Sociology of education; Social stratification and social class; Crime and deviance; Media and popular cultgure; Contemporary social change. Introduction to basic concepts and principles: - welfare, equality, redistribution, citizenship, and welfare state, selectivity & universality, citizenship and social policy; Origins of social policy and the welfare state - historical and sociological approaches; Key policy issues today: social exclusion, ageing, migration; disability; Application to Ireland. Psychology: its nature and concerns, major theoretical approaches; Perspectives on development; Social Psychology, interpersonal communication; prosocial behaviour; prejudice and oppression.

Please note that this module is available to visiting international students only. Students must be studying youth work or community work/development in their home university. Interested students must contact the Department of Applied Social Studies before registering.

Roles, tasks and methods in community work; Contemporary issues in community work; Contemporary policy, developments and issues in community work; Issues and problems in community work practice. Youth work and community work issues and structures in Northern Ireland, Britain and in selected other countries in the EU and elsewhere; Cultural differences; the experience of Travellers and other ethnic minorities; inter-culturalism and intercultural learning.

modules.moduleName: CURRICULUM, METHODOLOGIES AND ASSESSMENT 1: PHYSICAL EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent:
This module will introduce students to the principles, aims and methodologies of national curricula. Key teaching skills and pedagogic tools in Physical Education will be explored through a practical workshop approach. Students will also engage with the principles and processes of planning, assessing and evaluating subject-specific content across the Games and Dance strands at junior class level.

Students will be introduced to a number of fundamental areas as part of this module. These include:
 - The central role of play and active learning across the curriculum
- The rationale for observation
Organisation and attributes of a Froebelian learning environment
Motivation as key to management of learning
- The use of concrete materials as part of guided discovery learning
The rationale for the use of particular approaches to the teaching and learning process.

Note this modules is intended for short term visiting Erasmus and international students.
learningOutcomes: Apply a range of curricular methodologies outlined in national curricula through Physical Education.
Identify opportunities to connect theories of learning with the learning process and practice, and transfer methodologies across a range of curricular areas.
Articulate the principles and aims of national curricula in planning, observation, recording and assessment.
Apply subjectspecific skills, and recognise the transferability of key teaching methodologies across curricular areas.
Plan, organise and manage a variety of learning environments through applying Froebelian, Play and childcentred principles.

modules.moduleContent:
In this module, the teaching skills in PE in primary school settings are developed through practical workshop approaches 
Through reflection on, and evaluation of, their own teaching and learning experiences, students will be encouraged to think critically about the characteristics, organisation and management of an inclusive, Froebelian, enquiry-based environment. 

Students will be introduced to Integrated planning, recording and evaluation for a child-centred curriculum. 

This module will explore assessment principles and practices; collaborative and problem-based learning.
learningOutcomes: Plan, implement and critically evaluate the applied use of key curricular methodologies outlined in national curricula through the lens of PE
Identify and plan for the realisation of opportunities to connect and transfer methodologies in an integrated manner
Apply principles and methods of observation, recording and assessment to inform future planning of teaching and learning
Implement teaching plans and processes based on observation and assessment to guide opportunities for differentiated teaching

modules.moduleName: PROFESSIONAL AND PERSONAL SKILLS FOR TEACHING AND LEARNING 2
modules.moduleContent:
This module develops students’ personal and professional skills required to function and flourish at third level and on School Placement. With reference to The Code of Professional Conduct (Irish Teaching Council, 2016), students will be introduced to the four ethical values of integrity, care, respect, and truth, and consider their relationship to the educational philosophies and practices of Friedrich Froebel. 

Students will engage critically with literature pertaining to relevant concepts and theories with the express aim of developing sound theoretical and conceptual frameworks. Strong links are identified between theory and practice by means of engagement with pertinent national and international literature.
learningOutcomes: Consult and analyse a wide range of literature/resources which will encourage analysis of competencies and skills relating to personal and professional development.
Engage critically with the ethical values for teaching as presented by the Irish Teaching Council (2016).
Draw explicit links between theory and practice in reflecting upon identities of student and studentteacher.
Develop competency in the area of critical reflection for the purposes of developing professional practice.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to a range of additional needs that students may present with in the classroom, many of which are complex needs.
learningOutcomes: Students will be familiar with a range of interventions that will support the learning of students with additional needs.
Students will develop new understandings of the child with additional needs and become proficient in meeting their diverse needs.

modules.moduleName: CURRICULUM, METHODOLOGIES AND ASSESSMENT 3: SOCIAL PERSONAL AND HEALTH EDUCATION
learningOutcomes: SPHE and the global education movement.
Relationships and Sexuality Education
Teaching sensitive topics – LGBTQI+, drug misuse.
Emotions and emotional regulation – strategies
Wellbeing – the ‘science’ of happiness, building resilience
Citizenship – global goals, fast fashion, lesson ideas.
Diversity in education – how SPHE can help

modules.moduleContent: Electronic Engineering Fundamentals will introduce students to the language and methodologies that engineers use to solve problems. The module will also teach students the ability to analyse any DC circuit and simple electronic circuits containing diodes and transistors. Indicative Syllabus *Introduction and Motivation; Electronics in Society Scientific Notation and Exponents; *Engineering Notation; Electricity; Electric Potential and Current â€“ Water Models; *Voltage and Current; Resistance and Conductance; Resistivity *Voltage Division; Current Division; Series and Parallel Circuits; *Fundamental Relations: Ohmâ€™s Law, Energy, Power; *Ideal Circuit Elements; Kirchhoffâ€™s Laws; *Circuit Theorems: Source Transformations; Theveninâ€™s Theorem; Nortonâ€™s Theorem; Principle of *Superposition *Multi-loop Circuits; Nodal Analysis; Mesh Analysis; *Basic Electronics: Ideal Diodes; PWL diode model; Transistors *Simple Circuits; Diodes and FETs F
learningOutcomes: explain the role of electronics in modern products and society;
detail the relationship of current, voltage, power for resistors, capacitors and inductors, diodes and transistors;
undertake simple circuit analysis, up to an including Kirchoff’s current law, multiloop nodal and mesh analysis;
design and construct simple electronic circuits;
demonstrate electrical safety in the context of the home and work environments;
explain the role and responsibilities of engineers to their customers, their colleagues, their employers and to society.

modules.moduleContent: The Aim of this module is to introduce students to the world of digital design and to equip the students with the necessary skills to tackle real world problems in the design of complex digital systems. See full module descriptor at https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors Indicative Syllabus: * Binary numbers â€“ the currency of computers * Binary representations â€“ sign and magnitude, twoâ€™s complement, BCD * Binary arithmetic â€“ addition, subtraction, multiplication * Boolean functions and Boolean minimisation * Minimisation using Karnaugh maps (up to and including 5-variables) * Implementation using NAND and NOR functions * Sequential Logic â€“ SR, D, JK, T and Master-slave flipflops * Counters * Registers * Programmable Logic Devices * Programmable Array Logic (PAL) * Programmable Logic Arrays (PLA) * Read Only Memory (ROM)
learningOutcomes: conduct basic arithmetic with binary numbers;
perform Boolean algebra;
minimise logic using Karnaugh Maps;
implement a logic circuit using only NAND / NOR gates;
describe the operation of basic flipflops;
design a synchronous counter;
distinguish between different programmable logic devices.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to theories of numeracy acquisition,mathematical and scientific learning, content knowledge, methodologies, skills and resources appropriate to 3rd – 6th class. 

The Primary School Curriculum (2023) – Mathematics and Science –3rd to 6th class will be explored. 
 
The module addresses the creation of an enquiry-based and independent learning environment involving the utilisation of the indoor and outdoor environments. Linkage and integration across the curriculum, particularly project work will be explored.¿ 
 
The module will study the scientific and mathematical knowledge and vocabulary necessary for teaching Mathematics and Science in Primary School. Methods of children recording their work, documentation and assessment of children’s work including self and peer evaluation will be also be addressed.

Note: this module is only available to visiting short term international students.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a working knowledge of the Primary School Curriculum (1999)  Mathematics and Science – Knowledge, skills, values and attitudes – for 1st to 4th class.
Demonstrate content and pedagogical knowledge and vocabulary of mathematics and science necessary for teaching in Primary School.
Examine theories of mathematical and scientific learning relating same to practice.
Utilise a range of methodologies specific to mathematical and scientific enquiry in 3rd  6th classes (including use of ICT).
Review, select and use appropriate resources and materials for teaching and learning Mathematics and Science for 3rd  6th classes.
Link and integrate mathematics and science across the curriculum with an emphasis on project work.

modules.moduleContent: The aims of this module are (1) To introduce students to the principles of materials, atomic structures, and semi-conductor material physics. (2) To study the application of these principles in the construction of electronic devices. Indicative Syllabus: *Bohr and Quantum mechanical pictures of the hydrogen atom. *Bonds between atoms; Ionic Bonds; Covalent Bonds; Metallic bonds. *Crystals and crystalline solids; close-packed structures; lattice parameters; X-ray diffraction. *Semiconductor physics - band theory; n-type and p-type semiconductors; *majority & minority carriers; effective mass; carrier concentrations; *Hall effect; Drude & Bloch Theories of electrical conduction; *Fermi-Dirac distribution and Fermi level; optical properties of semiconductors. *Transistor equations (FET and BJT), comparison of performance, manufacturing technologies and examples of same.
learningOutcomes: explain the basics of electronic materials and their characteristics including the basic crystallography structures and electron configuration;
describe the function of certain semiconductor devices in terms of the underlying physics and calculations of same;
calculate the performance of semiconductor device given materials and doping conditions;
outline the function of a PN junction;
explain the size and complexity of a modern semiconductor device;
describe the function of a device in appropriate technical form.

modules.moduleContent: Electronics in Society Scientific Notation and Exponents; Engineering Notation; Electricity; Electric Potential and Current â€“ Water Models; Voltage and Current; Resistance and Conductance; Resistivity Voltage Division; Current Division; Series and Parallel Circuits; Fundamental Relations: Ohmâ€™s Law, Energy, Power; Ideal Circuit Elements; Kirchhoffâ€™s Laws; Circuit Theorems: Source Transformations; Theveninâ€™s Theorem; Principle of Superposition Multiloop Circuits; Nodal Analysis; Mesh Analysis; Basic Electromagnetics: Charge, Vectors and Coulombs Law and Applications Electricity Generation and Distribution; Electrical Safety; Household Supply; Plugs and Fuses Impact of Electronic Products on the environment Communications: basic principles Concept of a "bit" of information Receivers and transmitters (architectural) Communication networks (wired and wireless) Principles of wireless communications (spectrum, frequency, interference) Privacy, health and environmental considerations for communications Engineering Ethics For further information please use the following address: http://www.nuim.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students
learningOutcomes: explain the role of electronics in modern products and society;
detail the relationship of current, voltage and power;
undertake simple circuit analysis, up to and including Kirchoff’s current law, multiloop nodal and mesh analysis;
design and construct simple electronic circuits;
describe household electricity systems;
demonstrate knowledge of electrical safety;
explain the operation of communication networks (specifically wireless communications).

modules.moduleContent: Critical disability studies is an examination of the systematic discrimination that disabled people face in society. It examines areas like access to employment, transport, housing, cultural depictions of disabled people, in literature, film, TV, the news and the rights of disabled people as enshrined in both national and international law. It takes the focus off the condition (e.g. blindness, cerebral palsy, deafness, down syndrome, etc) and instead aims to look at social attitudes and barriers that prevent disabled people from fully integrating into society. The module will examine the history of disability rights in the US and Europe, and look at the remaining legal, social and attitudinal barriers that are standing in the way of disability equality.

This course will introduce learners to theories and holistic approaches to adult guidance counselling and the importance of networking and referral. It will increase learners understanding of the socio-economic and psychological impact of unemployment and social exclusion on the individual and society. It will provide an opportunity for learners to develop increased self-awareness and reflection to enhance their personal and professional development. It will develop learners applied career guidance and relationship building skills within the context of adult guidance provision. It will equip learners with an understanding of how to support individuals at transitional points in their lives e.g. Redundancy, Changing Career, Retirement, Unemployment throughout their life-long journey of career. 

Workshop Themes include;
Adult Guidance Theoretical Models, Career Development and the Guidance Process, Applied Careers Guidance and Relationship Building Skills, Support Systems and Referral, Contract and Boundary Setting, Ethics and Confidentiality, Change, Transition and Loss. 
The Integration of ICT, Careers Information Tools and Resources within Guidance Practice. Learners will gain an Understanding of Resistance and Conflict and become more aware of developing meaningful and decent work Life balance.

*80% attendance is a requirement for this course*

When applying for this course, please outline any previous education, qualifications, and/or training.
COMMUNITY EDUCATION & EQUALITY STUDIES

Topics appropriate to research interests of departmental staff members.

Students join one of the research groups in Chemistry and complete a research project under the guidance of the group supervisor. Students submit a written report and make an oral presentation describing their research findings. 
The research project can take two forms: (i) experimental based project with a small literature review or (ii) a literature review based project.  The nature of the research project is assigned at the discretion of the project supervisor.
learningOutcomes: Complete a literature search and a literature review on the topic of the research project.
Assess, recognize and understand the issues of safety regulation in relation to the use of chemicals in laboratory experiments.
Analyse and interpret experimental results and draw reasonable conclusions.
Prepare a report on the research findings.
Prepare and give a presentation on the research findings.

modules.moduleContent: Work Placement

The chemistry of benzene. Extended reaction chemistry of the carbonyl group. Pericyclic reactions and Frontier Orbital Theory. This module examines three core areas of organic chemistry: (i) The Chemistry of benzene. The material covered in this section includes an introduction to the structure, nomenclature and reactivity of benzene and its monosubstituted derivatives. (ii) Application of carbonyl group chemistry in organic synthesis The material covered in this section includes (i) a-hydrogen acidity, (ii) acid and base promoted keto-enol tautomerism, (iii) a-enolate chemistry, including alkylation (iv) named reactions including Michael reaction, Aldol addition, Aldol condensation, Claisen condensation (v) ring forming reactions including Dieckman condensation and Robinson annulations (iii) Pericyclic reactions. The material covered in this section includes analyses of electrocyclic reactions, cycloaddition reactions and sigmatropic rearrangements by both a frontier orbital approach and by application of Woodward-Hoffmann rules.
learningOutcomes: Describe the structure of benzene.
Describe electrophilic and nucleophilic aromatic substitution reactions using curly arrows.
Draw mechanisms for ketoenol tautomerism.
Write equations and draw mechanism for selected reactions of enolates such as alkylation, Aldol reaction, Claisen ester condensation, conjugate additions and Robinson ring annulation.
Understand the frontier orbital approach to pericyclic reactions.
Conduct syntheses of selected organic compounds and prepare associated laboratory reports.
Interpret spectroscopic data to determine keto enol equilibrium and to identify the structures of organic compounds.

modules.moduleContent: This module examines three important areas of pharmaceutical chemistry: (i) The Physicochemical Basis of Pharmaceuticals: The application of physical chemistry to pharmaceutical science. Key concepts such as phase transitions in formulations of pharmaceuticals and the physical chemistry of disperse systems are covered. Particle sizing in the pharmaceutical industry is also discussed. (ii) Drug Metabolism. The course introduces the following concepts: phases and purpose of drug metabolism; sites of drug metabolism; mechanisms of biotransformation; liver metabolism and the cytochrome P450 system; metabolic enzymes induction and inhibition; biologically reactive metabolites. (iii) Pharmacokinetics. The course covers the general principles of pharmacokinetics, drug formulations and routes of administration, the factors which affect the efficacy of compounds (particularly those associated with absorption, distribution, metabolism and excretion as core processes), transport across biological membranes, plasma concentration-time curves, and compartment modelling.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the purpose of the different phases of drug metabolism.
Describe the main types of reactions involved in each phase of drug metabolism.
Propose the chemical structure of possible metabolites of a given drug.

Explain pharmacokinetics and describe the role it plays in drug action. Evaluate the significance of the processes of absorption, distribution, metabolism and excretion of drugs, and the factors that affect their respective rates.
Explain transport across biological membranes, compartment modelling and associated kinetic parameters.
Interpret phase diagrams of one and two component systems and discuss the thermodynamics of important pharmaceutical phase transitions.
Describe the use of disperse systems used in pharmaceutical formulations.
Understand the primary techniques used for particle sizing in the pharmaceutical industry.

modules.moduleContent: Programming fundamentals: variables, types, expressions and assignment; simple I/O; Conditional and iterative control structures (if statements and while loops); Strings and string processing; Use of class APIs for creating objects and calling methods; Understanding data abstraction and encapsulation; Problem solving: understanding and developing algorithms; Implementing algorithms as simple programs. Computer architecture (underlying concepts, the Von Neumann architecture, processors, storage, communications); Basic components of modern operating systems; Basic digital communications in the context of modern, internet-accesible computing devices.
learningOutcomes: understand and evaluate simple algorithms;
create simple algorithms;
write simple programs;
debug runtime errors;
identify the fundamental types of modern computing devices and peripherals;
explain the underlying logical concepts that provide the basis for modern computation;
describe basic computer architecture on a theoretical and practical basis & identify the workings of modern operating systems and differentiate between them;
explain the fundamentals of computer communications and identify security issues relating to it.

modules.moduleContent: Topics from the breadth of computer science, for example Artificial Intelligence, Computer Vision, Signal processing, machine learning, cryptography, robotics, graphics, information theory, graph theory, visualisation. Tools for the creation of computer and software systems (visual programming systems, web and mobile applications); computational thinking; computational problem solving.
learningOutcomes: use visual programming tools to create computer systems;
understand the breadth of topics in computer science;
Describe in detail a number of specific topics in computer science;
understand what is meant by computational thinking and apply it to solve simple problems
solve problems by designing algorithms, composing and testing programs.

modules.moduleContent:
Students will develop an understanding of current policy in Ireland in the field of adult and community education. Students will become familiar with the various formal /structured programmes and initiatives currently provided or planned by the statutory adult / second chance education sector, students will become familiar with current policy re: assessment and accreditation, with the role of the various agencies involved in this area, and with existing and proposed frameworks for progression and transfer, students will critique these policies, programmes and initiatives in the light of current adult and community education theory.


Designed to familiarize students with current trends and practice in the statutory adult and community education sector in Ireland. It will also enable students to place these in the context of national policy, and to critically reflect on both policy and practice within the framework of current theoretical approaches in the field.
learningOutcomes: Explain current trends and practice in the statutory adult and community education sector in Ireland in the context of national policy.
Explain the various formal /structured programmes and initiatives currently provided or planned by the statutory adult / second chance education sector,
Critique policy implementation re: assessment and accreditation,  the role of the various agencies involved in this area, and  existing and proposed frameworks for progression and transfer
Critically reflect on both policy and practice within the framework of current theoretical approaches in the field.

modules.moduleContent: This module will focus on multimedia technologies for the generation, analysis, and production of multimedia content. The module will first introduce multimedia and its convergence within the media of television, the internet and mobile communications. The module will provide a thorough study of software processes for the generation of multimedia content. Topics will include: introduction to multimedia and multimedia systems and standards; multimedia representation and processing; multimedia storage and compression; software, user interfaces and authoring tools.
learningOutcomes: Understand the basic theory of multimedia technology and multimedia applications.

modules.moduleContent:
This module provides an introduction to Robotics. The main focus is on building basic mathematical methods for describing and controlling the motion of robotic systems.

Integrated product design; Sensors; Use of software observers. Vision Systems: Image processing techniques; Perspective Transformations; Robotics; Basic Configurations; Statistical methods, Robot Navigation and Locomotions; Statistical robotics used for mapping and location estimation.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the history of industrial robots, robots in popular culture and the anatomy and features of a typical robot.
Evaluate mobility of a simple machine using number synthesis, joints and pairs.
Describe the motion of articulated robots.
Identify suitable actuators and controllers for a specific task.
Understand control strategies.
Describe the approaches and techniques to describe mobile robot motion, localisation, mapping and path planning.
Have an appreciation of the software used to control and model robotic systems.

modules.moduleContent: Architecture of a Small Microprocessor based Computer, Assembly language programming, Interrupts and IO, Machine Cycle, Representation of data, Memory Buses.
learningOutcomes: Describe the architecture of a small microprocessor including the function of microprocessor registers and addressing modes;
Describe semiconductors and device construction, PN junction and FET;
Describe a simple calculator (operator/operand) and memory;
Implement x86 code to do both binary and floating point calculation and estimate code run time and to modify the function of interrupt routines;
Describe memory including static and dynamic memory cells, explain cache strategies and estimate effective access time for a cache;
Compare RISC and CISC design philosophies and parallel/modern architectures.

modules.moduleContent: Software requirement elicitation; consensus estimation; design, testing and implementation as a team; project management techniques and meeting skills; combining new knowledge and knowledge from previous modules to the design, development and deployment of a mobile/web application using SCRUM; using DevOps in a group context.
learningOutcomes: Work effectively as a member of a software development team in an Agile environment.
Deliver technical presentations during the software development cycle.
Apply learned skills and experience more effectively in future project work.
Plan and manage team activities & cCommunicate effectively with other team members.
Use collaborative tools (e.g. software repositories) in a group context.
Be able to design and deploy a product (including SSL certificate installation, configuring/using virtualised servers, serverless hosting, use hosted NOSQL DBs).
Be able to use modern DevOps approaches.

modules.moduleContent: A minimum six months work placement that extends beyond the summer (into the next academic year)
learningOutcomes: Take direction on applying work placements from the work placement officers, meeting all deadlines set by them and by any potential employers
Find a suitable work placement by participating in suitable application and interview processes
Participate in and contribute to a place of work for at least six months (unless curtailed due to circumstances outside the student’s control) in a manner that is agreeable to your employer
Work effectively with others within the social context of a software development team

modules.moduleContent: Topics from: Error explosion; floating point arithmetic; systems of linear and non-linear equations interpolation; numerical differentiation; ordinary differential equations; boundary value problems; Partial differential equations; non-linear equations; non-standard computations. Emphasis: On applications not theory; on adapting mathematical algorithms; on error control; on programming solutions, Matlab, Java, Perl.
learningOutcomes:
The purpose of this course is to introduce students to the basic techniques of Numerical Computation used to solve real world problems from a variety of disciplines. 
Good software engineering practice tells us not to reinvent the wheel if we can avoid it so the course will concentrate on utilizing powerful numerical software packages.

The emphasis will be on the role of the computer scientist in the implementation of these techniques. Students will be introduced to a variety of numerical algorithms and study their strengths and weaknesses, highlighted by interesting and relevant examples. The course will not include rigorous theoretical analysis but powerful mathematical techniques will be discussed and implemented.

modules.moduleContent:
In this course students will be introduced to the field of computer vision. The structure of the course will be to first provide an overview and motivation for each of the stages in a typical computer vision system. Each of these stages will then be addressed in turn, where a selection of problems, techniques and algorithms from each stage will be presented.

Although the content of the course may vary from year to year, the course will have a structure based on the following topics:
1. Introduction (human visual perception, basic principals of industrial machine vision systems).
2. Image formation (lenses, sensors, and signals, the geometry of imaging, camera modelling and calibration).
3. Binary image processing (image representations, segmentation, image moments, mathematical morphology, region representations).
4. Image filtering (convolution, frequency domain representations, noise removal filters).
5. Gradient based edge detection.
6. Selected topics in Image analysis (template matching, image based pattern recognition, shape detection, interest point detection & matching).
learningOutcomes: Design and implement computer vision systems to detect, localise and recognise objects within images.
Mathematically model the image formation process, and calibrate a given camera using this model.
Describe the different types of image sensors and identify a suitable camera setup based on the requirements of a computer vision system.
Identify suitable morphological operations for a specific binary image processing task.
Implement algorithms to segment, label, and compute the position and orientation of a set of objects in an image.
Implement the 2D convolution operation and describe the mathematical properties of the operation.
Describe, compare, and implement the main gradient based edge detection operations.
Understand, implement and apply a selection of image analysis techniques.

modules.moduleContent: Time domain speech processing; frequency domain speech analysis; time frequency representations; implementations of digital audio effects. Algorithms for the analysis, synthesis, and modification of instrumental sounds.
learningOutcomes: Illustrate the mathematical theory underlying each signal processing approach for speech and audio.
Design, implement and critically evaluate signals processing algorithms for speech and audio.
Integrate individual algorithmic elements to create an audio processing system to extract particular features from speech or audio.
Describe key digital filter structures used for the processing of audio signals.

modules.moduleContent: This module depends entirely on the individual project, which may be proposed by either the student or a member of staff.
learningOutcomes: Conduct a reasonably thorough investigation into a topic of interest, importance or relevance to computer science or its applications.
Where appropriate, accomplish an acceptable implementation (or other useful model) to fulfil the main goals of the project.
Work largely independently, within agreed project requirements, with minimal supervision.
Deliver technical presentations during the project development cycle.
Complete a detailed project report covering all central aspects of the project.
Work within all project deadlines, interim report deadlines and consultation requirements giving regular and timely feedback to your project supervisor(s).
Plan and manage all your own project milestones.

modules.moduleContent: This module is about the design of programming languages. In particular it is concerned with the syntax, the semantics and usability of a programming language. The basics of lambda calculus, both untypes and simply typed, will be studied, with an eye towards its use in formalizing the semantics of programming languages. Working knowledge of three languages Scheme, Haskell, and Prolog) will be acquired. Semantics will be studied using both formal machinery and an interpreter-based approach.
learningOutcomes: Read and write simple programs in Scheme, Haskell, and Prolog.
Understand lambda calculus reductions, renaming, variable capture, and confluence.
Understand the erasure theorem, the termination properties of both typed and untyped lambda calculus, and typing rules.
Describe the syntax and semantics of Scheme, Haskell, Prolog, and lambda calculus.

modules.moduleContent: Youth work concepts and principles. Youth work history, policy development and structures. Youth work theory and practice

modules.moduleName: YOUTH AND COMMUNITY IMAGES AND ARTS
modules.moduleContent:
Youth and community arts concepts, principles and practices. Arts and creativity in youth and community work. Introduction to specific art forms; arts-based project.
Brainstorming 'youth' and 'community'; 'Youth' and 'community' in film, fiction, on TV and other media; Observing 'youth' and 'community' in our own lives, our own work

Module objectives: To provide a practical introduction to the main issues and methods in social research 
Module content: The nature of social research; Formulating research problems: 'asking the right question'; Matching questions and Methods: workshops/project; Making sense of findings

Facilitation skills; professional values, ethics, boundaries, reflective practice; meetings skills; report writing/presentations; curricula vitae; interviewing and being interviewed.
Power and leadership in groups; Approaches to working with groups; Conflict, diversity and equality in groups

Management theories, concepts and principles; Organisational culture, development; Management roles, responsibilities, skills; Planning and evaluation; Legal and financial issues in management. Perspectives on gender, class, racism & ethnicity, age, disability, sexuality and related issues; policy implications and developments relating to inequality. Theory and method in social research; Research designs; qualitative and quantitative research methods; Participative research; action research; Research as reflexive practice; values and ethics in research. 

Please note that this module is available to visiting international students only. Students must be studying youth work or community work/development in their home university. Interested students must contact the Department of Applied Social Studies before registering.

The Certificate in Directing for Theatre is a practical course taught by Irish professional directors. It focuses on methods, tips and techniques, application of theory and practice. This course is aimed at those who wish to explore the field to discover if students wish to pursue a career in directing. In this sense Directing for Theatre serves as a skills-based training course for new directors; those in the amateur, youth theatre and community theatre sectors who wish to formalise their experience into specific training, acquire accreditation and gain additional skills by doing so; those who are emerging directors in fringe and professional theatre.

The course aims to introduce students to fundamental skills of directing for theatre and to prepare students for further study in directing for theatre

By the end of this course students will be familiar with the following:
Working With Actors 
Introduction to Stanislavski/Introduction to Viewpoints
Actioning and working with text
Devising new material for performance
Introduction to Physical Composition and Movement Theory
Site-Specific and Site-Responsive Theatre
Introduction to Directing in Applied Theatre contexts
Introduction to Directing Theatre-of-the-Oppressed
Physical Comedy
Auditioning and Rehearsal
learningOutcomes: Understand the social and historical development of theatre performance.
Recognise the different approaches of directing which are used to elucidate meaning from literary texts.
Direct a performance.
Record the experience of directing in a director's log.
Devise a performance in class with class mates.
Write an essay reflecting on the course and on significant learning.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces student teachers to reflective practice and journaling and prepares them for entry to their professional role by fostering agency and reflexivity on their own professional learning. It promotes critical reflection on practice and foundation modules and seeks to identify how both inform and shape each other. They will be required to think critically about their ideas of the ‘good teacher’ and locate the emergence of these ideas in their own educational history.
The dialogical approaches and professional conversations between student teachers and HEI lecturers employed foster a creative mind set, individually, and in collaboration with colleagues, through reflection on the attitudes, beliefs and core values that guide their professional practice. 
Using a variety of structured creative and reflective exercises, student teachers learn how to create and maintain a safe, inclusive Froebelian learning environment using strategies that promote congruent, unconditional regard and relational esteem approaches to foster positive behaviour. This component has a particular focus on how to establish good relationships based on mutual respect, trust and meaningful interactions, while also demonstrating how student teachers can communicate effectively with all school stakeholders and the wider community. 
Through a variety of interactive, learner-led Froebelian lessons, this module ensures that teaching itself is understood and practised as a form of self-critical learning by student teachers, with ample opportunities afforded for creativity in artistic individual and collaborative reflection, and engagement in and with literature. Student teachers are encouraged to connect School Placement and lived experiences to social and policy contexts, the rights of children, school culture and dominant discourses in education. Ongoing, structured journaling activities and engagement with outdoor spaces on campus connect to Taisce e-portfolio-based learning and opportunities for further reflection.

Note this module is intended for short term visiting Erasmus and international students.
learningOutcomes: Develop a structure to support reflective practice approaches and reflexivity on their own professional practice to enable articulation of key learning points with creativity
Recognise and reflect on their emerging professional identity as a Froebelian teacher
Recount significant learning experiences, linked to personal and professional identities and their influence on emerging practice
Use reflective practice to journal on supportive and dismissive feedback received and assess the impact it had on their identity, motivation and achievement
Identify characteristics of a safe Froebelian enquirybased indoor and outdoor learning environment
Reflect on the nature and purposes of education and the social and policy contexts and their impact on the identity of emerging teachers
Engage in Taisce eportfoliobased learning

modules.moduleName: CURRICULUM, METHODOLOGIES AND ASSESSMENT 2: VISUAL ART
modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to the principles, aims and key methodologies of the Irish Primary School Curriculum for Infants to 6th Class inclusive.¿ 
 
The key teaching skills in Visual Arts for those classes will be explored. 
 
Students will also engage with Visual Arts curricular planning for middle standards in primary school. Through reflection on their own teaching and learning experiences, students will be invited to think critically about the characteristics, organisation and management of an inclusive Froebelian enquiry based environment. 
 
They will be introduced to Integrated planning, recording and evaluation for a child-centred curriculum.¿ 
 
They will explore assessment principles and practices; collaborative and problem-based learning, differentiated learning and ICT as a pedagogical tool.
learningOutcomes: Plan, implement and critically evaluate the applied use of key curricular methodologies outlined in the Primary School Curriculum (1999) through the lens of Visual Arts for middle and senior primary classes
Identify and plan for the realisation of opportunities to connect and transfer methodologies in an integrated manner
Apply principles and methods of observation, recording and assessment to inform future planning of teaching content
Further develop the critical use of ICT in teaching and learning

modules.moduleName: PLACEMENT LEARNING 2:EDUCATION FOR THE MIDDLE YEARS
modules.moduleContent: This module will prepare the students for the primary school classroom with a focus on classroom organisation, behaviour management and lesson planning for the Middle Years.
learningOutcomes: Identify key aspects relating to the role of the teacher.

Familiarise themselves with preparation and planning practices with a specific emphasis on project methods, integration of subjects and group teaching practices for classes from infants to fourth class.
Utilise a range of methodologies suitable for use in infants and middle years classrooms.
Review, select and use appropriate resources and materials for teaching infants to fourth class.

modules.moduleContent: The weekly module consolidates the application of the content and pedagogical skills of drama in the primary school. Drama as a learning medium is explored with a focus on drama for integration and inclusion. Short-term and long-term planning strategies for drama are developed. Assessment and differentiation strategies are modelled, planned and analysed through practical workshops and course reading.
learningOutcomes: Plan, implement and critically evaluate the applied use of drama methodologies outlined in the Primary School Curriculum for middle and senior primary classes.
Identify and plan for the realisation of opportunities to connect and transfer methodologies in a creative and integrated manner.
Apply principles and methods of observation, recording and assessment to inform future planning of arts education content

modules.moduleName: CURRICULUM, METHODOLOGIES AND ASSESSMENT 3: SOCIAL AND SCIENTIFIC EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: This module will demonstrate integrated planning, recording and evaluation for a child-centred curriculum. It will also examine and apply a broad range of assessment principles and practices. Collaborative and problem-based learning and differentiated teaching strategies are a feature of this module.
learningOutcomes: Utilise assessment and differentiation strategies, appropriate to the developmental stages of the individual primary school child
Consider the value of problemposing approaches to promote a Froebelian inquirybased learning environment
Demonstrate the centrality of language as a fundamental component of synthesised learning
Critically analyse and reflect the use of ICT to achieve quality of learning across the digital learning framework outcomes

modules.moduleContent:
This module consolidates and extends the understanding of the content and pedagogical skills of PE in the primary school setting. 

They will examine and apply a broad range of assessment principles and practices. Collaborative and problem-based learning and differentiated and inclusive teaching strategies will be examined through practical workshops.
learningOutcomes: Reflect on, analyse, justify and utilise curricular methodologies in PE at whole school level.
Plan shortterm and longterm units of work in PE in an integrated manner.
Utilise assessment and differentiation strategies, appropriate to the developmental stages of the individual primary school child.
Consider the value of problemposing approaches to promote a Froebelian inquirybased learning environment.

modules.moduleContent: PROFESSIONAL LEADERSHIP

This weekly module extends students’ knowledge and skill in the area of drama pedagogy. It introduces and develops a range of drama methodologies as part of a thematic planning approach. The module content focuses on active exploration of the Irish Primary Curriculum for the middle primary classes (children age 7-11 approximately).
learningOutcomes: Plan, implement and critically evaluate the applied use of key curricular methodologies in drama outlined in national curricula.
