Identify and plan for the realisation of opportunities to connect and transfer methodologies in an integrated manner.
Apply principles and methods of observation, recording and assessment to inform future planning of teaching and learning in drama.
Implement teaching plans and processes based on observation and assessment to guide opportunities for inclusive teaching in drama.

modules.moduleName: PLACEMENT LEARNING 1 (EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION  4 TO 7 YEARS)
modules.moduleContent:
With a clear focus on early childhood education, behaviour management and lesson planning, this module is designed to prepare and equip the students school placement.

Note: this module is for short term visiting international students
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate a working knowledge of the structure, purpose and appropriate application of a. Aistear – the Early Childhood Curriculum Framework b. Síolta  the National Quality Framework for Early Childhood Education
Relate theories of early childhood development and learning to their practice.
Identify a range of methodologies suitable for use in an early childhood classroom.
Review, source, select and use appropriate resources and materials for teaching and learning in an early childhood classroom.

modules.moduleContent: The aim of this module is to introduce C language programming in an embedded system environment (its key application area) assuming basic programming skills in another language. Note that it is C language programming rather than embedded systems which is the focus of this module. Indicative Syllabus: * Introduction to C language and embedded system development environment * Review of programming basics incl. conditional and loop blocks * Literals versus constants; bitwise operations and bit masks; Scope and extent: global, static, local * Basic functions in C using primitive non-pointer types * Arrays * More advanced data types: typedef, enum, structures, unions, and bit fields * Pointers and the typical mapping of code and data to memory * Advanced pointers: functions with composite types; pass by value, out, and in-out parameters; *functions returning non-primitive types; arrays and pointer arithmetic * Strings: literals, character arrays, and string arrays * Other concepts: using multiple .c/.h files; recursion (and limits in embedded environment)
learningOutcomes: design, write and debug small C programs (in an embedded system environment) using good coding and naming conventions;
decompose a problem into subproblems and implement the solution using the appropriate code features;
use and manipulate C pointers and arrays;
understand and explain the operation of a C programme;
explain key C programming concepts.

modules.moduleContent: The aim of this module is to introduce students to the physics of vibrations, waves and light, and electromagnetism, and to tutor them in the solution of elementary problems in these areas. *Vibrations and Waves: (6 lectures) *Elasticity and simple harmonic motion; Pendulums; Damped Harmonic Oscillators; *The nature of waves; The mathematical description of a wave; Transverse and longitudinal waves *Linear superposition *Standing waves and Sound *Light: (6 Lectures) *The electromagnetic spectrum; Polarization *Reflection, refraction and dispersion; Lenses; Coherence and diffraction *Electromagnetism: (12 lectures) *Origins of Electricity, Coulombâ€™s Law, Electric Fields, and Electric Field Lines; *Electric potential and Electric Potential Energy, Voltage, Capacitors and Capacitance *Magnetic Fields and Forces, Basic Circuits.
learningOutcomes: describe the fundamental principles of electricity and magnetism;
discuss the techniques required to analyze simple electric circuits;
analyze the behaviour of sound and light in various physical situations using the theory of vibrations and waves;
describe the operation of a variety of optical instruments using geometrical optics;
solve simple numerical problems relevant to fundamental electricity and magnetism, optics and waves;
carry out relevant laboratory experiments to demonstrate and test physical principles and report the results.

modules.moduleContent: Indicative Syllabus â€¢ Introduction, motivation and scope â€¢ The Laplace Transform â€¢ Basic Laplace Transform â€¢ Inverse Laplace Transform â€¢ Solving first-order ODEs using the Laplace Transform. â€¢ Vector algebra â€¢ Vectors in coordinate independent form and in 2-Space, 3-Space and n-Space. â€¢ Addition, multiplication of vectors by a scalar. â€¢ Basic properties of vectors, subtraction and scalar (dot) and vector (cross) product of vectors. â€¢ Rules of Vector algebra. â€¢ Unit vectors i,j,k. â€¢ Inner (Dot) product in n-space and Vector (Cross) product in 3space. â€¢ Orthogonality of vectors. â€¢ Applications of inner and vector products. â€¢ Vector equation of a line in 3-space, Equation of plane in 3-space. â€¢ Triple scalar product, Triple vector product. â€¢ Time derivative of a vector function. â€¢ Vector equation of a curve in parametric form in 2 and 3 space. â€¢ Arc length, unit tangent, unit principal normal vectors. Curvature. â€¢ Matrix algebra â€¢ Overview of Matrix algebra and its usefulness in solving systems of linear equations. â€¢ Matrix addition, subtraction and multiplication. â€¢ Matrix properties â€“ transpose, symmetric, rank â€¢ Solving linear systems of equations using Gaussian and Gauss-Jordan elimination. â€¢ Special matrices such as the identity matrix, the zero matrix, and orthogonal matrices. â€¢ Solving linear systems using the inverse of a matrix. Properties of the inverse matrix. â€¢ Calculating the inverse matrix using row operations. â€¢ Definition of the determinant and discussion of its properties. â€¢ Calculating the inverse matrix using the method of cofactors. â€¢ Solving linear systems using the inverse matrix and Cramerâ€™s rule. â€¢ Eigenvalues and eigenvectors. â€¢ Diagonalization of matrices. Cayley-Hamilton theorem. â€¢ Linear Independence, basis and dimension, with connection to row operations and determinant.
learningOutcomes: carry out basic vector operations and algebraically reduce multiple vector products to their simplest form;
obtain the unit tangent vector, unit principal normal vector and curvature of curves in 2 and 3space and Perform basic arithmetical and special operations with matrices;
write a system of linear equations in matrix form and to use row elimination methods to solve the system;
determine the nature of the solutions to a linear system by examination of the properties of the coefficient matrix;
calculate the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix and determine matrix properties such as linear independence, rank, nullity, basis and dimension;
determine the Laplace transforms of elementary functions;
solve simple firstorder ODEs using the Laplace transform;
apply and select the appropriate mathematical techniques to solve a variety of associated engineering problems.

modules.moduleContent: The main aim of this module is introduce the basic problem solving techniques, approaches to planning and organizing computer programmes, and the common computer programming language elements used to express the task to be performed when implementing a computer programme. Indicative Syllabus: *How to launch python and write python scripts *Programming fundamentals: variables, types, expressions and assignment *Creating and printing text to output window *Creating variable names of various types and assigning values *Reading in user data *Converting between types *Simple mathematical operations using integers and floats; programming with complex numbers *Basic comparisons using different types *Boolean operations *Loops, â€œifâ€ and â€œelseâ€ statements *Formatting programs: indentation and comments *Creating and using lists Making use of list indices, lengths, changing list items and extending lists Using list methods, including creating and testing lists and removing from lists Using list iterators; Using lists inside loops; Making use of list â€œslicesâ€ Reading from and writing to files Writing and using functions Using â€œtuplesâ€ Understanding â€œmodulesâ€ (libraries) *Finding and making use of system modules and external modules *Python â€œdictionariesâ€. *Gentle introduction to classes and inheritance
learningOutcomes: Design, write and explain simple computer programs.
Communicate algorithms effectively using comments and appropriate naming of identifiers.
Use literal values, constants, and variables.
Make use of Boolean expression appropriately.
Use selection and iteration appropriately.
Define and use functions appropriately.
Make use of libraries (Python “modules”).
Use basic file input/output functions.

modules.moduleContent:
• Error control codes
• Combinational logic with MSI and LSI
• Sequential logic design - Flip-flops.
• Programmable logic
• Finite state machines (Mealy and Moore machines); Analysis. Design of minimal equivalent machines through state reduction. Analysis of invalid states.
• Memory (ROM and RAM) and storage devices. SRAM and DRAM timing.
• Analogue to Digital and Digital to Analogue converters. Nyquist rate converters. Non-ideal behaviour.
• Multiplexers. Combinational logic design using Multiplexers as Universal Logic Modules
• Introduction to FPGA technology - RTL level, comparison with Embedded processors/CPUs
learningOutcomes: Perform calculations using different number systems.
Distinguish between different error control codes.
Design combinational logic circuits using multiplexers as universal logic modules.
Analyse and design Finite State Machines.
Outline the key features of memory and storage devices (RAM, ROM etc.).
Explain the basic operation of Analogue to Digital and Digital to Analogue converters.
Outline the difference between CPUs and FPGAs.
Explain the function of both CPUs and FPG

modules.moduleContent:
• Introduction to signals
• Sources of signals (electrical, physical, biological,etc)
• Signal transfer mechanisms (or channels) (eg current, voltage, numbers, air-pressure)
• Simple mathematical Models of a Signal
• Signal Representations (time and frequency domain)
• Signal Sensing Systems (from sensor to digital processor)
• Noise, distortion, interferers, sources and impacts.
• Analog and Digital Conversion (including quantisation, sampling, resolution,  Nyquist Theorm)
• Introduction to signal processing in the analog and digital domains
• Simple analog and digital filters
learningOutcomes: Explain what a signal is and why signals are important in areas such as communications, control, signalprocessing and electronics.
Provide examples of signals generated from different sources such as chemical, electrical, audio, biological and mechanical
Analyse a signal in the time and frequency domains.
Mathematically model an analogue timedomain signal.
Construct a means for capturing a signal from a sensor and deliver it to a digital system.
Quantify the effects of sampling and quantization.
Quantify the impact of noise, unwanted signalpickup, and distortion in the time and frequency domains
Design simple filters to minimize the impact of noise in the analog and digital domains.

modules.moduleContent:
• RLC resonance, bandwidth and Q-factor
• Passive filter design – Low Pass, High Pass, Band stop and band pass filters
• RLC filter effects stage, Butterworth filters
• Bode plots
• Realisation of various filter specifications
• Stability of filter circuits
• Oscillators
learningOutcomes: Explain conceptually what resonance and bandwidth mean in the context of RLC networks.
List and draw the frequency specifications of the four basic types of filters.
Draw a bode plot of a network function.
Design and build a passive filter based on a Butterworth response.
Use RLC circuits to modulate bandwidth and resonance as needed.
Use Matlab to analysis signals with the appropriate filter hardware.
Design, build and analysis a filter circuit in a lab environment.

modules.moduleContent:
Probability Theory
• Introduction to Probability Theory and Forms of Data Presentation - Contingency Tables 
• Review of Basic Set Theory 
• Definition of Events in terms of sets
• Complementary and Mutually Exclusive Events.
• Interpretation of Probability in terms of relative frequencies.
• Axioms of Probability Theory
• Addition Formula for probability 
• Conditional Probability
• Bayes Theorem and Law of Total Probability
• Concept of Independent Events
• Network Problems and Determination of Reliability of Networks
• Counting Techniques and Application to Sample Spaces with large numbers of sample points
• Discrete Random Variables and the Probability mass function
• Special Discrete Probability Distributions – Binomial, Poisson Distribution
• Application of Poisson distribution to engineering problems - Queuing Theory
• Expected Value and Variance, Chebychev's Inequality
• Continuous Random Variables (Probability Density Function and Cumulative Distribution Function)
• Properties of a probability density, expected value and variance.
• The Gaussian (Normal) distribution and its properties and importance in application.
• The exponential distribution and its relation to the Poisson process and queuing theory
• The Gamma distribution and the Weibull Distribution and their application to modelling times to failure

Statistics
• Introduction to Statistics – Inference and Estimation of Parameters.
• The central limit theorem.
• Large/Small Sample confidence interval estimates for the population mean and the T-distribution.
• Large Sample confidence interval estimates for a population proportion.
• Introduction to hypothesis testing and the idea behind the process.
• Hypothesis testing on a population mean (large and small samples).
• Hypothesis testing on a population proportion (large sample).
• Categorical Data: Chi-squared goodness of fit test. Chi-squared independence test.
• Simple Linear Regression. Correlation/Causation. Prediction Intervals. Hypothesis testing.
• Discussion of relation of studied material to simple engineering experiment design.
learningOutcomes: Apply appropriate probability models to solve problems requiring a probabilistic approach.
Quantify the reliability of series/parallel networks using independence and probability theory.
Derive interval and point estimates of unknown population parameters.
Apply statistical inference techniques to test various hypotheses about population parameters and to make appropriate decisions accordingly
To use chisquared procedures to ascertain independence and goodness of fit of probability models.
To fit leastsquares regression lines to observational data and use these to make prediction, construct interval estimates and test hypothesis on the dependent variable.
Appreciate the factors involved in simple experiment design.
Apply and select the appropriate mathematical techniques to solve a variety of associated engineering problems.

modules.moduleContent:
• Review of small signal current models for FET and BJT transistor amplifiers
• Basic transistor Circuits:  amplifiers, active loads, level shifters
• Differential amplifier using active loads and current sources.
• High speed analog circuit design
• Introduction to power electronics
• Thyristors and SCRs, power transistors
• PWM, PDM, and other pulse power control modulation schemes
• Resonant DC-DC Converters: Buck Converter, Boost Converter, Flyback Converter, Buck-Boost Converter
• EMI, noise, heat management
learningOutcomes: Analyze and design basic switchmode DCDC converters
Analyze and predict the performance of PWM based converters
Demonstrate competency in electrical safety issues for power electronics
Design a high speed circuit such as may be used for an FM radio receiver
Design a range of circuits for handling and manipulating analog signals (level shifters, amplifiers, load matching)
Design a BJT and a CMOS amplifier circuit.
Work as part of a team.

modules.moduleContent:
This module ensures students had a working experience of electronic, computer and telecommunications engineering as practised in a representative,
modern industrial or commercial environment.
learningOutcomes: Work productively in a technical role in an organisation.
Appreciate the importance of the multidisciplinary relationships and interfaces.
Perform as a team member, with typical industrial deadline and reporting relationships, to solve practical engineering problems in real life circumstances.
Apply learnt theory and principles within a practical project
Appreciate the issues surrounding plan adherence and performance in a practical situation.
Outline the ethical framework within which the organisation operates.
Apply general ethical principles to identified operational circumstances encountered in the IWE placement.
Prepare a professional Curriculum vitae and attend Interviews for a professional post.

modules.moduleContent:
To give the student a thorough introduction to radio-frequency engineering, the areas and systems in which RF is prominent, and the special types of circuit used in RF engineering.

Indicative Syllabus
Part 1 – Basic Concepts
• Basic Concepts; Radio Waves; Noise; Modulation; Historical Context
• Frequency Selective Circuits and Matching
• Transmission Lines and Scattering matrices
• Antennas and Propagation

Part 2 – RF Components 
• Active Devices and Amplifiers
• Low Noise Amplifiers
• Mixers, Modulators and Demodulators
• Power Amplifiers

Part 3 – RF Systems
• Radio spectrum allocation and uses
• Radio Transceiver architectures (heterodyne/homodyne)
• Digital Communications
learningOutcomes: Chart the development of radio around the turn of the 20th Century.
Outline the different modes of propagation of radio waves in the atmosphere.
Develop and apply LC matching algorithms to RF systems.
Design a variety of oscillators for use at RF frequencies.
Derive transfer functions for first and secondorder PLLs and describe where they are used.

modules.moduleContent:
• Robot perception using non-visual sensors including sensor modelling and signal processing
• General introduction to robot modelling, sensing, planning, and control
• Practical implications and effects of various actuators, electro-mechanical drives, and sensors
• Motion control algorithms (e.g. positional control, torque control, force control, impedance control, and gravity compensation)
• Motion and trajectory planning
• Case studies (e.g. robot manipulator arm, humanoid robot locomotion)
• Machine learning techniques for motion control
• High level software control of robot motion
learningOutcomes: Recognise, differentiate, and describe the general properties of different sensors.
Apply appropriate signal processing and probabilistic modelling techniques to sensor inputs.
Select the appropriate control approach based on robot hardware and desired operational features.
Model and develop solutions to certain robot control problems.
Implement the solution to motion control problems using appropriate software frameworks.

modules.moduleContent:
To introduce a range of numerical methods for solving common engineering problems. To instil an appreciation of the limitations and pitfalls of simulation.

• Introduction and motivation
• Data representation
• Numerical solution of systems of linear algebraic equations
• Numerical solution of systems of non-linear equations
• Interpolation 
• Curve fitting
• Review of modelling
• Numerical solution to differential equations
 - initial value ordinary differential equations
 - boundary value ordinary differential equations
 - partial differential equations
• Modelling of discrete event systems
learningOutcomes: Choose an appropriate numerical method to solve a particular problem.
Quantify the strengths and weakness of a range of algorithms.
Program several numeric algorithms.
Reduce the errors which can occur when performing numeric calculations.

modules.moduleName: ADVANCED ENGLISH FOR THE EMI CLASSROOM
modules.moduleContent:
This two-week intensive programme is a course of learning through the medium of English, for specific academic purposes, for academic faculty working and teaching in Higher Education. Degree programmes utilising EMI/EME are becoming increasingly popular within EU Higher Education institutions. However, academic staff, while experts in their own fields, may experience linguistic challenges in delivering their content through the medium of English, in both verbal and written forms. 

The focus in ESAP2 is firmly on the English language, rather than teaching and learning pedagogy, and participants will experience authentic Academic English and learn how it is used by academic teaching staff within Higher Education studies for a variety of purposes. These include lectures, feedback, task instruction, assessment design, formal emails, presentation delivery, and academic writing style, including the language of research, which will form a major part of the module. Each day’s instruction will begin with a discussion on one of the key topics (above) from the module, which will allow participants to share their own experience, knowledge and ideas in these important areas of academic writing in Higher Education. Discussion will be followed by the input (instruction) stage, using a variety of modes, e.g. lecture, PowerPoint, MP4s and PDFs. Participants will then actively apply their learning in a variety of individual and group tasks. Finally, each class session will conclude with a summary of content covered, highlighting the most useful Academic English lexis and metalanguage. A Q&A session will also be included. 

Due to the nature of the module, participants should be aware that a significant amount of time will be devoted daily to in-class writing tasks, on which feedback will be received.

The programme level is set at CEFR C1.1.
learningOutcomes: Identify the features of appropriate academic style in English and practice these in written texts and oral presentations.
Analyse and reflect on their own academic style in written and spoken English to identify and improve areas of weakness.
Plan and deliver effective lessons and lectures in their subject areas, using EMI/EME.
Evaluate the appropriateness of their students’ written work in Academic English.
Demonstrate an improved level of verbal academic style of English in presentations and talks, which will also enable more confident and effective interactions with colleagues.

Participate in group discussions on theories, topics and themes related to academic writing style and how it is used in Higher Education, sharing their experiences and insights with other members of the group.

modules.moduleContent:
This 2-week intensive programme is a course of learning through the medium of English for professional services staff working in Higher Education. The emphasis of this course is on Academic English language usage within the context of internationalisation.

The module will highlight the importance of possessing a high level of English language proficiency for professional services staff, especially when supporting their university’s internationalisation strategy. Participants will discuss their shared experiences of using English in a professional services setting and how it has contributed to their professional development to date.

The module uses immersive methods of language instruction, contextual usage and focussed discussion to help raise awareness of specific language areas in need of improvement. Practical application of learning will contribute to improvement in these areas, in both spoken and written modes of communication. All lexis utilised for instruction on this module is academic in style, helping ensure that professional services staff possess the necessary language range and flexibility to communicate effectively and confidently with both academic staff and students, as required.

The design and delivery of presentations will also be covered, allowing module participants to improve their level of engagement and communicative competency in this important area.

The programme level is set at CEFR B2.2.
learningOutcomes: Identify areas and aspects of their specific administrative areas and daytoday work in which they currently use English or would like to be able to use English with greater competence and confidence.
Analyse and reflect on their own style in written and spoken communications in English to identify and improve areas of weakness.
Read, analyse, and summarise written and aural texts related to internationalisation in Higher Education.
Participate in group discussions on topics and themes related to internationalisation in Higher Education, sharing their experiences and insights with other members of the group.
Plan and deliver effective and engaging presentations related to their specific administrative areas, using appropriate vocabulary and register.
Plan and produce effective written texts related to their specific administrative areas, using appropriate vocabulary and register.

modules.moduleContent:
The study of quantitative finance and related subjects in partner institutions abroad; Self-reflection on the learning experience abroad. 

Module Objective: To provide students with the opportunity to study topics in quantitative finance abroad.
learningOutcomes: Successful demonstrate learning to the required standard in the topics in quantitative finance studied abroad.
Show evidence of reflection on the role of culture and context in finance.

modules.moduleContent:
Research project on a chosen topic under the direction of a supervisor from the French department.  Completion of 3500 - 5000 word essay in French with footnotes and bibliography.

If the long essay is on a literary topic, this module is recognised as a literature module by the Teaching Council of Ireland.  It represents 5 credits of the minimum of 15 credits in literature required by the Teaching Council.
learningOutcomes: Carry out individual research on a topic approved by the Coordinator and the Supervisor.
Demonstrate awareness of the core skills required in research.
Analyse key issues at stake in the chosen research topic, incorporating both primary and secondary sources into the body of the essay.
Produce a wellstructured, wellresearched piece of written work in French (3,5005,000 words), displaying evidence of personal insight.
Compile and present a bibliography.

modules.moduleContent:
The successors to GC296a & 296b, these modules continue with the introduction to the ancient Greek language. The GC297 pair of modules cover the essentials necessary for higher-level study of Greek literature and will see students move on to start work on adapted readings and exercises from Greek originals; moving towards the consideration of the epics of Homer, the dramas of Aristophanes, or the philosophical works of Plato as they were first delivered. Building on the work done in Semester 1, now students delve deeper into the grammar, vocabulary, and syntax of the language, even as they consider thematic material on many aspects of Greek culture and history.

NOTE: These modules cannot be taken unless a passing grade across both GC296 modules was achieved in Semester 1. A minimum number of enrolments is required for these modules to run each semester.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain morphology and syntax necessary for reading Greek.
Recognize a wide range of Greek vocabulary.
Demonstrate the ability to compose sentences in Greek.
Demonstrate the skills of analysis and synthesis inherent in the art of reading Greek.
Demonstrate the ability to read adapted Greek passages, seen and unseen.
Develop broadly transferable skills of analysis and synthesis.

modules.moduleContent:
These modules - GC2999a & GC299b - continue the systematic introduction to the grammar and vocabulary of the Latin language that is offered in GC298. In Semester 2, the primary focus remains on thorough language acquisition (the grammar, syntax, and vocabulary of Latin), but to this end extracts of many Roman authors are read with little adaptation, deepening students’ awareness of cultural contexts and the richness of the Latin literary heritage. By the end of the year, students will be reading unadapted selections of classic authors such as Caesar, Catullus, Cicero, and Virgil.

NOTE: These modules cannot be taken unless a passing grade across both GGC298 modules was achieved in Semester 1. A minimum number of enrolments is required for these modules to run each semester.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain morphology and syntax necessary for reading Latin.
Recognize a wide range of Latin vocabulary.
Demonstrate the ability to compose sentences in Latin.
Demonstrate the skills of analysis and synthesis inherent in the art of reading Latin.
Demonstrate the ability to read adapted Latin passages, seen and unseen.
Develop broadly transferable skills of analysis and synthesis.

modules.moduleContent:
These modules - GC2999a & GC299b - continue the systematic introduction to the grammar and vocabulary of the Latin language that is offered in GC298. In Semester 2, the primary focus remains on thorough language acquisition (the grammar, syntax, and vocabulary of Latin), but to this end extracts of many Roman authors are read with little adaptation, deepening students’ awareness of cultural contexts and the richness of the Latin literary heritage. By the end of the year, students will be reading unadapted selections of classic authors such as Caesar, Catullus, Cicero, and Virgil.

NOTE: These modules cannot be taken unless a passing grade across both GGC298 modules was achieved in Semester 1. A minimum number of enrolments is required for these modules to run each semester.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain morphology and syntax necessary for reading Latin.
Recognize a wide range of Latin vocabulary.
Demonstrate the ability to compose sentences in Latin.
Demonstrate the skills of analysis and synthesis inherent in the art of reading Latin.
Demonstrate the ability to read adapted Latin passages, seen and unseen.
Develop broadly transferable skills of analysis and synthesis.

modules.moduleContent:
‘Denn zu Zeiten der Not bedarf man seiner Verwandten’ (J.W. von Goethe): The family in German literature 1880-1920
The course looks at transformations in the nature and function of the family as presented in key German literary texts during a period of radical upheaval whether viewed in social, political, economic or other terms. The course will deal with some texts in full and others on the basis of selected extracts:

Course Texts:
A. Holz/J. Schlaf, Die Familie Selicke (1890) (Extracts)
G. Hauptmann, Vor Sonnenaufgang (1889) (Extracts)
A. Schnitzler,Reigen (1900) (Full text)
F. Kafka, Die Verwandlung (1912) (Full text)

**This 5-ECTS credit module may be used as part of the engagement with texts required for accreditation by the Teaching Council of Ireland. All applications submitted for Teaching Council accreditation are, however, subject to the Teaching Council’s internal evaluation processes.

IMPORTANT NOTE: 
Since this module involves reading of the German language, a good knowledge of German is required.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain key aspects of historical, societal and cultural development in the Germanspeaking countries during the period in question.
Summarise in broad terms developments in the literature of this era, and analyse closely designated key texts.
Interpret literary works through close reading and contextualisation.
Evaluate critically secondary literature.
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and wellstructured argument.

modules.moduleName: TEACHING GERMAN AS A FOREIGN LANGUAGE
modules.moduleContent: This module begins with a brief overview of some of the basic linguistic, cognitive and sociocultural concepts informing Second Language Acquisition (SLA) Theory, before introducing students to the dominant methodologies of teaching/learning foreign languages, e.g. Grammar-Translation Method, Direct Method, Audio-visual Method, Audiolingual Method, Communicative Approach, Intercultural Approach and Alternative Methodologies including the post-methods era. In analysing the various methodologies, the salient issues of lesson planning, learner typologies, language learning strategies, learner autonomy and target-language usage will be discussed.
learningOutcomes: Explain in broad terms basic linguistics, cognitive and sociocultural theories and principles of Second Language Acquisition, with particular reference to their relevance for the teaching and learning of foreign languages.
Summarise in general terms and compare the various methodologies used to teach foreign languages, referencing each methodology's salient issues.
Listen and contribute to inclass discussions in clear and correct German.
Critically evaluate appropriate research literature.
Write responses to exam questions, making convincing arguments in a clear and structured manner and giving examples where appropriate.

modules.moduleContent: This module allows for work placement to be accepted within year abroad structures towards the award of a BA International. Students will be required to prepare application materials for work placements on offer and apply to the employers under the advisement of the International Coordinator. If students are successful, they would then take up the role which is deemed beneficial personally, professionally and academically to students, on a basis of 10 – 12 months. Additionally, students may wish to source their own paid internship in a German-speaking country. If this internship meets the requirements outlined by the Department and International Coordinator, it will be accepted as a viable placement.
learningOutcomes: Undertake a critical analysis of how their placement organisation works, of its structure and of group and team work within the organisation
Demonstrate in their essay (written in German) how an improvement in the skills of working with colleagues and under supervision, leading and initiating activities have been achieved
Identify what knowledge and further competences (academic, linguistic, cultural, personal, professional) he/she has developed on work placement
Discuss how the academic, linguistic, cultural, personal, professional knowledge and competences developed during the placement can be transferred to their studies

modules.moduleContent:
The primary aim of LC101X is to provide a solid basis in the Irish language for beginners, through developing comprehension, speaking, reading and writing skills at a basic level. Classtime will be focused on building a good basic vocabulary, introducing grammatical concepts and focus on pronunciation. Irish language culture and heritage are an integral part of the course and various topics will be presented in lectures by School of Celtic Studies staff.  

This class will follow TEG Bonnleibhéal 1 (A1) syllabus closely. TEG (Teastas Eorpach na Gaeilge, www.teg.ie) is an examination system for adult learners of Irish that is linked to the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (Council of Europe, 2001). A1 level is intended for complete beginners. 

At the end of LC101, students should be able to communicate basic information about themselves through the medium of Irish.
learningOutcomes: Use single words and simple sentences while taking part in a conversation based on topics and general information that relate to everyday life.
understand simple speech delivered in a slow, carefully articulated but natural manner.
understand texts that are short and simple insofar as they relate specifically to everyday life.
understand texts on a phraseby phrase basis, finding words and recognisable phrases.
write simple words and very simple basic phrases.
understand some principal aspects of Irish culture, particularly those aspects relating to Irish and the Gaeltacht (Irish speaking areas).

modules.moduleContent: This module builds on EU Law&Policy I – Constitutional and Institutional Law. Its focus is on the substantive law and policies of the EU. The module will commence by analysing the development of the EU internal market and its ‘four freedoms’, and will then concentrate on how the CJEU has developed the law on the free movement of goods, persons and services, as well as how it has developed the concept of EU citizenship. The module will then look briefly at two important fields of EU substantive law, competition law and state aid law, the rules of which help to protect the integrity of the single market.
learningOutcomes: Outline and comment upon the development of the EU from a common market to a single market.
Explain and critically discuss the development of EU law with regard to the free movement of goods, services and workers, and with regard to EU citizenship.
Explain the aims of key fields of EU social and economic policy, and critically discuss the legislative relationship between the EU and the Member States in these areas.
Provide an overview and basic appreciation of EU competition law and EU state aid law.

modules.moduleContent: Students will learn to think creatively and critically about film and television production while they also develop key production skills. Students work in groups to generate creative ideas for documentary formats. They will execute ideas in audiovisual production and postproduction. They will learn how to operate DV cameras, lighting, and sound equipment and how to edit. They will learn to express ideas verbally and in written form, to reflect critically on their practice and to respond to live verbal analysis and feedback on their practice.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of audiovisual production processes in a the context of film and tv production.
Generate and execute creative group and individual projects that conform to broadcast standards.
Manage the conceptual, technical, and interpersonal complexity of media production practice.
Communicate effectively in verbal, written, and audiovisual modes.
modules.notes: Contributor and subject research via data gathering; editing via Creative Cloud Premiere Pro

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To introduce students to linear systems, matrices and vectors.

Linear systems; row reduction; matrices and matrix algebra; determinants and inverses (of 2x2 matrices); vectors; dot and cross products; lines and planes.
learningOutcomes: Solve systems of linear equations using the method of row reduction.
Use matrix algebra.
Obtain the determinant and inverse of a 2x2 matrix and understand their meaning.
Find products of vectors and be able to use these in simple geometrical problems.
Write down the equations for lines and planes in space.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Objectives: To introduce students to Integral Calculus.

Anti-derivatives, area, the definite integral, indefinite integrals, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the substitution rule, areas between curves, inverse functions, inverse trigonometric functions. Techniques of Integration. Tables of integrals, integration by parts, trigonometric integrals, Substitution, integration of rational functions, trapezoidal rule, Simpson's Rule. Applications of Integration: Volumes and surfaces of revolution, length of a curve.
learningOutcomes: Integrate the basic functions and combinations of these functions using standard techniques.
Use integrals to find areas and volumes.
Compute an integral numerically to a given degree of accuracy.
State the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To introduce the student to multivariable differential calculus.

Calculus of several variables, especially in two and three dimensions. Functions of two or more variables. Graphs. Limits. Continuity. Partial derivatives. Gradient. Tangents and normals. Max-min. Lagrange multipliers. Introduction to Sequences. Computation using mathematical software.
learningOutcomes: Draw the level curves for a function of two variables.
Obtain the partial derivatives and gradient vector of a function.
Obtain tangents and normals to surfaces.
Solve maxmin problems in several variables.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module objective: An introduction to multivariable integral calculus and its uses.

Double and triple integrals. Surface integrals. Green’s theorem in the plane. Conservative vector fields. Complex Numbers: Argand diagram, modulus, conjugate, polar form, Euler’s formula. Introduction to Series and Convergence Tests. Computation using mathematical computing software.
learningOutcomes: Evaluate single, double, triple and surface integrals.
Use these integrals in applications.
State and apply Green's theorem in the plane.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module details:

Sets and functions; cardinality; equivalence and order relations; properties of real numbers; sequences; limits and continuity of functions.
learningOutcomes: Prove simple set theoretic results.
State the properties of the real numbers.
Deduce simple properties from any theorems or definitions given in the course.
Prove that a sequence has a given limit using rigorous analytic methods.
Prove that a function is continuous and understand what this means.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
The aim of this module is to further to students knowledge of Finite Mathematics.

Number theory: Induction. Division algorithm, g.c.d., Euclidean algorithm, linear Diophantine equations, primes, Euclid''s lemma, Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, Modular arithmetic, congruences, solving linear congruences, divisibility tests, Chinese remainder theorem. Complex numbers: multiplication and De Moivre''s theorem. Set theory: product, relations, equivalence relations. Combinatorics and Probability: Applications of probability. Terminology: experiment, sample space, event. Assigning probabilities: equally likely outcomes, long run frequency. Axioms of probability. Consequences of axioms. Examples. Equiprobable outcomes: Random sampling without replacement. Equiprobable outcomes: Random sampling with replacement. The birthday problem. Conditional probability, independence. Law of total probability. Bayes theorem and examples. Discrete random variables. Probability mass function, probability histogram. Expected values, mean, variance and standard deviation. Bernoulli trials, Binomial distribution. Mean and variance of the binomial distribution. The Poisson distribution.
learningOutcomes: Use Euclid's Algorithm to solve problems in elementary number theory.
Perform arithmetic in the complex field.
Solve problems in combinatorics and probability.
Understand the concept of a random variable.
Calculate expected values and probabilities for some standard distributions.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Overview:
Problem solving – analysis of mathematical behavior. Proof –Mathematical notation. Basic notions from formal logic. Nature and techniques of proof. Counterexamples.

Euclid's postulates.  Fundamental theorems in Euclidean Geometry.  Vectors; dot and cross products. Basics of matrix algebra and its applications to geometry; determinants, inverse matrices, linear algebra and row reduction.

Other topics may include:
Lines and planes; conic sections. Positive definite quadratic forms in two variables.
learningOutcomes: Use various different types of proof methods.
Use a wide variety of methods to begin solving unseen problems.
Understand the importance of proof.
Produce axiomatic arguments to prove some results from Euclidean geometry.
Carry out calculations involving dot and cross products.
Carry out calculations involving matrices.
Solve linear systems of equations.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems. State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent: To analyse a range of both continuous and discrete time systems. To introduce the concept of state-space. To introduce frequency-domain system analysis. To further the use of Matlab and Simulink in laboratories See full module descriptor at http://www.nuim.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors
learningOutcomes: Develop mathematical models for a range of dynamical systems.
Change between different mathematical model representations (differential equation, statespace and transfer function).
Linearise a nonlinear system about an operating point.
Calculate responses of simple dynamical systems.
Analyse simple systems using Bode plots.
Use Matlab and Simulink to simulate and analyse a range of systems.

modules.moduleContent:
• Overview of communications
o Use of communication systems in the real world (mobile phone, satellite television, etc)
o The nature of data, analogue/digital, bandwidth requirements – real-time versus non-real-time communications (e.g. phone vs email)
o Communication signals – electrical, optical, quantum, electromagnetic (radio)
• Communication concepts
o Basic communication concepts 
¿ Transmitter, receiver, channel, additive noise
¿ Bit rate, power, the dB and dBm – amplification/attenuation/SNR/Noise figures
¿ Point-to-point vs broadcast systems
¿ Channels properties and utility –  co-axial cable, twisted pair, optical fibre, air interface
o Signals and noise
¿ Communications by means of signals – definition of a signal
¿ Signal characterisation in the time and frequency domain
¿ Analogue to digital conversion of signals
¿ Noise definition – noise margin, thermal noise, white noise, Friis equation
o Analogue and digital modulation (and demodulation)
¿ The need for modulation, introduction to basic analogue modulation types (AM/FM)
¿ Introduction to basic baseband and band-pass digital modulation
¿ Study of different types of AM time domain and frequency domain
¿ AM transmitter and receiver architectures
o Communication limits and channel multiplexing
¿ Power, noise and channel capacity
¿ Spectrum as a limited resource – spectrum allocation issues
¿ Channel multiplexing – TDM, FDM, spatial multiplexing (GSM use case example)
o Communication networks and ethical issues
¿ Communication network topologies – pros/cons, packet concept, congestion concept
¿ Ethical issues related to telecommunications – carbon footprint, security, health
learningOutcomes: Describe the major components of a communication system.
Apply the dB system to describe power gain/loss in an arbitrary system.
Calculate basic parameters of signals in the time and frequency domain.
Describe the differences between analogue modulation types and between analogue and digital modulation
Design a basic AM communication system.
Identify ethical issues linked to current trends in telecommunications.
Calculate basic communication link budgets considering signal

modules.moduleContent:
• Differential equations
• Review of Basic Differentiation techniques and an introduction to modelling.
• Second-order differential equations
• Linear constant coefficient equations. Solution using method of variation of parameters and method of undetermined coefficients.
• Application to RLC series circuit and spring-mass systems.
• Higher order linear differential equations.
• Laplace Transform
• Introduction to the Laplace transform. Idea of a linear operator. Rationale for solution method for differential equations.
• Inverse Laplace Transform and its relation to the solution to a differential equation.
• Laplace transforms of the elementary functions.
• Shifting theorems: s-shifting and t-shifting.
• Applying the Laplace transform to solving first and second-order differential equations with elementary forcing functions.
• Introduction to the unit step function and its use in modelling switching functions.
• Introduction to the Dirac delta function and its use in modelling impulsive functions.
• Applying the Laplace transform to solving first and second-order differential equations with non-trivial forcing functions (switching, impulsive, periodic).
• Laplace transform of periodic functions (square waves, triangular waves) and solving differential equations with forcing functions of this type.
• Laplace transform of integrals.
• Convolution theorem. Solving integral equations using the convolution theorem and their relation to time-delay systems.
• Fourier Series
• Introduction to Fourier series and its importance in applications.
• Derivation of the Fourier coefficients.
• Calculation of Fourier series for several types of periodic function.
• Development and motivation of the Fourier transform.
• Calculation of the Fourier Transform for various and special functions 
• Relation of the Fourier transform to signal analysis.
• Z-Transform
• Introduction to simple difference equations. Idea that solution is a sequence. 
• Motivational examples of how difference equations arise in practice.
• Solution techniques for simple linear first and second order difference equations.
• Introduction to discrete transforms and specifically the Z-transform.
• Calculation of the Z-transform of certain special sequences. (Unit Step sequence, Unit Impulse sequence)
• Using the Z-transform to solve difference equations and the analogy between this method and the use of the Laplace Transform.
learningOutcomes: Find solutions to linear secondorder ODEs and apply solution techniques to simple physical models.
Use the Laplace Transform to solve simple ODEs with elementary forcing function terms.
Formulate first and secondorder differential equations for physical problems involving switching/impulsive/periodic forcing functions.
Solve integral equations using Convolution theorem and the Laplace transform.
Obtain Fourier series representations (trigonometric & exponential) of general periodic functions.
Obtain Fourier transforms of arbitrary and special functions.
Solve simple linear difference equations using elementary techniques and using the Ztransform approach.

Use a range of problemsolving skills that can be used to investigate systems modelled by both differential, and difference equations. Apply and select the appropriate mathematical techniques to solve a variety of associated engineering problems.

modules.moduleContent:
• Introduction to embedded systems
• Basic microcontroller features and development environment
• Interfacing and Input/Output 
• Communication
• Real time applications
• Memory and addressing
• Processor technologies and system architecture
• Requirements, specification and design choices
• Reliability and fault tolerance
• Health, safety and ethics
learningOutcomes: Design simple embedded systems.
Implement and test embedded applications.
Implement and test realtime embedded applications.

modules.moduleContent: This course aims to provide an in-depth knowledge of RLC circuits and filter design. Aims: to provide an in-depth knowledge of filters. See full module descriptor at https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors.
learningOutcomes: Explain conceptually what resonance and bandwidth mean in the context of RLC networks.
Design and build a passive filter based on a Butterworth response.
Draw a Bode Plot of a network function.
Use the RLC circuits to modulate bandwidth and resonance as needed.
Use Matlab to analyse signals with the appropriate filter hardware.
Design, build and analysis a filter circuit in a lab environment.

modules.moduleContent:
• Introduction to Control and Review of Key Results from System Dynamics
• Properties of Feedback Systems – Sensitivity and Disturbance Rejection
• Stability of Feedback Systems 
o Routh criterion
o Nyquist, Bode criteria
• Transient Response Specifications and Root Locus
• PID Control
o Design concepts, tuning rules, advantages / disadvantages
• State-space control design
o Pole placement and Ackermann’s formula
o Use of nonzero reference input
o Optimal control
o State estimation
learningOutcomes: Recognise the utility of  feedback control
Relate the position of a system’s closedloop poles to its step response
Design output feedback controllers using PID and leadlag design
Design statefeedback controllers using pole placement and optimal control

modules.moduleContent:
• To give a detailed overview of the theory of DSP.
• To give a detailed overview of the application of DSP to selected areas in electronic, computer and telecommunications engineering.


• Introduction:
  Summary of EE301 Signal & Systems course, Real-time processing of signals,
  Motivation examples: Speech compression, Image compression
• Discrete Transform Techniques:
  FFT, Fast convolution using the FFT, Correlation, Fast correlation, DCT, Walsh transform, Hadamard transform, Spectral analysis, Short-Time Fourier Transform (Spectrogram)
• Finite Impulse Response Digital Filter Design and Implementation:
  Review of FIR design issues, The optimal FIR design method, FIR realization, Transversal structure, Linear phase structure, Analysis of finite word-length effects, Coefficient quantization  effects, Roundoff and Overflow errors, FIR implementation on a DSP microprocessor
• Infinite Impulse Response Digital Filter Design and Implementation:
Review of IIR design issues, Pole-zero placement method, Realization structures for IIR filters, Analysis of finite word-length effects, IIR implementation on a DSP microprocessor
• Multirate Digital Signal Processing: Sample rate conversion, Efficient techniques, Polyphase filter sample rate conversion
• Adaptive Digital Filters: The Wiener filter, The LMS adaptive algorithm, Adaptive speech compression
• Two- & Three-Dimensional DSP: DCT-based still image compression, Sub-band coding, Wavelet transform, 2-D filtering, Edge detection
• DSP Hardware: Fixed-point DSPs, Floating-point DSP, Interface hardware
learningOutcomes: Compute the fast convolution and fast correlation of two discretetime sequences.
Compute the ShortTime Fourier Transform (STFT) of a sampled signal.
Design and implement a finite impulse response (FIR) digital filter using the optimum FIR design method.
Quantify finite wordlength effects and coefficient quantization effects in FIR digital filter implementation.
Design and implement an adaptive FIR digital filter using the Least Mean Square (LMS) algorithm.
Explain the principles of DSP realtime implementation.
Work as part of a team to solve a substantial engineering problem.

modules.moduleContent:
To introduce the students to the design and analysis of computer feedback control systems with an emphasis on the design of digital compensators and implementation issues.

Discrete-data theory
• Introduction, key features of digital control, some of the problems to be addressed.
• The theory of sampling, non-ideal and ideal sampling
• Frequency spectrum of a sampled signal, Nyquist sampling rate, aliasing
• Data reconstruction - from sampled signal to continuous-time signal, Zero-Order Hold
• The z-transform, relationship between s-plane and z-plane, 
• Properties of the z-transform - the pulse transfer function & the Jury test
• The Inverse z-transform - 4 methods - (i) Power series, (ii) Partial fractions, (iii) Residues or Inversion integral, (iv) Difference equations 
• Discrete State Space standard forms – control and observer canonical and diagonal. The similarity transform and state transformations 

Design of control algorithms
• Overview of design techniques
• Direct design in the z-domain
• Discrete PID
• Pole placement & Ackerman’s formula

Implementation considerations
• Realisation of algorithms using direct, cascade and parallel forms
• Selection of the sampling time, T
• Hardware and software considerations, interfacing, hardware options.
learningOutcomes: Develop a variety of discrete time models (e.g. Z transfer function, difference equation, state space) for a range of dynamic systems and be able to simulate them.
Design a digital controller using digital design methodologies.
Compare the performance of digital control systems and know what affect the parameters of the controller, such as data sample interval, pole positions, have on the closed loop system response.
Implement digital PID and digital pole placement control algorithms.

modules.moduleContent:
To give a detailed overview of the current developments in wireless digital communications.

• Introduction – The digital communications model, wireless vs. wireline.
• The wireless channel – Random channels, coherence time, coherence bandwidth, the scattering function, capacity, free space transmission.
• Frequency and time orthogonal signals.
• Mobile communications concepts – TDD, FDD
• Modulation types – PSK, QAM, OFDM, GMSK, PAM
• Spread spectrum – Orthogonal codes, single user detection, multi-user detection, RAKE receiver.
• Diversity reception -  Time, frequency, spatial
• Multiple access – TDMA, FDMA, CDMA
• Wireless LAN, cellular and satellite technologies.
learningOutcomes: Determine and motivate appropriate power/bandwidth requirements and modulation scheme for data transmission over an arbitrary timeinvariant noisy channel.
Describe different channel access methods used in wireless communications (time, frequency, code).
Recognise different multipath channel types (flat vs nonflat / slow vs fast fading) and display an understanding of the issues associated with data transmission over these channels.
Identify and use orthogonal signals for transmission, and interpret these signals in signal space (constellation diagrams).
Describe the transmission of data using various modulation schemes, including multitone modulation and spread spectrum communications.
Analyse the performance of different diversity reception methods.

modules.moduleContent: To give Robotics and Intelligent Devices students an experience of a large-scale research and development project with associated professional skills.
learningOutcomes: Learn independently and extract, through literature search or experiment, information pertinent to unfamiliar problems.
Combine technical expertise from multiple technical areas as needed to analyse, design, and implement solutions or techniques for a project as appropriate.
Demonstrate understanding and application of theoretical knowledge in solving problems.
Conduct experiments, perform tests, and analyse results to evaluate solutions or techniques.
Discuss any ethical issues, societal impacts, environmental impacts, and health and safety issues associated with their project.
Manage their project effectively, working to deadlines and communicating regularly with project stakeholders.
Communicate their work effectively in various forms.

modules.moduleContent: Study abroad 30 ECTS

modules.moduleName: FROM TEENAGE KICKS TO TEENAGE ANGST: REPRESENTATIONS OF ADOLESCENCE IN CONTEMPORARY FRENCH AND FRANCOPHONE CINEMA
modules.moduleContent: A survey of French film makers, films and techniques.
learningOutcomes: Explain the ways in which films make meaning, though textual analysis of techniques such as editing, mise en scène etc., and use the découpage format as a means of analyzing film form;
Understand how cultural texts such as films emerge through a combination of social, political and economic processes;
Recognise the relation between film narratives and wider social and ideological 'stories';
Appreciate the close relation between text and context through knowledge of French history, society and politics;

Discuss comfortably some key examples of French cinema, including its most important modern directors, and give an informed account of its chief concerns, including gender, ethnicity and history, as well as its main formal aspects and genres.

modules.moduleContent: This module will focus on the self and will enable an excavation of our own biographies as activists to understand these in the context of our current practice. It will also explore ethical dimensions of activism as well as self-care. It will be integrated across all other modules.
learningOutcomes: Comprehend the concept of reflective practice.
Reflexively and critically analyse their own positioning, within the social movement spaces they occupy.
Reflect critically on how they themselves learn.
Incorporate reflective learning in a learning journal and a portfolio of practice.

modules.moduleContent:
GC296A & GC296B are modules designed for students new to ancient Greek, who want to explore the language of Homer, Sophocles, and Plato in their original form. 'Introduction to Ancient Greek' offers a rigorous 'starter' on the Greek language and its profound impact on literature, philosophy, and history. Beginning with the alphabet and proceeding through the basics of Greek grammar, syntax, and vocabulary, students will be guided through a series of progressive readings that provide a thorough grounding in the language, while also introducing many aspects of ancient Greek culture.

NOTE: A minimum number of enrolments is required for these modules to run each semester.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain basic Greek grammar and syntax.
Recognise a broad range of Greek vocabulary.
Demonstrate the ability to read and translate simplified passages of ancient Greek.
Demonstrate the ability to compose short phrases of Greek.
Develop a deeper understanding of general language structures.
Develop broadly applicable skills of language analysis.

modules.moduleContent:
The successors to GC296a & 296b, these modules continue with the introduction to the ancient Greek language. The GC297 pair of modules cover the essentials necessary for higher-level study of Greek literature and will see students move on to start work on adapted readings and exercises from Greek originals; moving towards the consideration of the epics of Homer, the dramas of Aristophanes, or the philosophical works of Plato as they were first delivered. Building on the work done in Semester 1, now students delve deeper into the grammar, vocabulary, and syntax of the language, even as they consider thematic material on many aspects of Greek culture and history.

NOTE: These modules cannot be taken unless a passing grade across both GC296 modules was achieved in Semester 1. A minimum number of enrolments is required for these modules to run each semester.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain morphology and syntax necessary for reading Greek.
Recognize a wide range of Greek vocabulary.
Demonstrate the ability to compose sentences in Greek.
Demonstrate the skills of analysis and synthesis inherent in the art of reading Greek.
Demonstrate the ability to read adapted Greek passages, seen and unseen.
Develop broadly transferable skills of analysis and synthesis.

modules.moduleContent:
This module - along with GC298a - serves as a gateway to the language and cultural heritage of ancient Rome. 'Introduction to Latin' explores the grammar, syntax, and vocabulary of Latin for students new to the language. The focus will be on language acquisition, but some attention is also given to Roman cultural and literary contexts, as students work to unravel the complexities of Latin literature and its ensuring influence on later civilizations. Taken together with the two GC299 modules (in Semester 2), these modules will enable students to proceed to the guided reading of original texts - seminal works by authors such as Cicero, Virgil, and Ovid - towards the end of the year.

NOTE: A minimum number of enrolments is required for these modules to run each semester.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain basic Latin grammar and syntax.
Recognise a broad range of Latin vocabulary.
Demonstrate the ability to read simplified passages of Latin.
Demonstrate the ability to compose short phrases of Latin.
Develop a deeper understanding of general language structures, including English grammar.
Develop broadly transferable skills of analysis and synthesis.

modules.moduleContent: Dutch is a West Germanic, Low German language spoken by around 24 million people worldwide. It is grammatically similar in many ways to standard German, but spoken Dutch can be very different in speech. This course offers an introduction to Dutch for those who have no prior knowledge of the language. It will focus on basic grammar, pronunciation, oral expression, and elementary aural and written comprehension. The course will also offer some useful background information on the culture and society of the Low Countries.
learningOutcomes: Recognise a range of straightforward grammatical structures and employ them to produce meaningful and correct Dutch sentences.
Construct simple sentences based on grammatical rules and using vocabulary dealt with in class.
Ask and answer simple questions in a manner that enables basic conversation on an everyday topic.
Converse in Dutch with some level of accuracy about basic and family information, university studies, student life etc.
Produce short written texts on a variety of topics related to everyday life.

modules.moduleContent:
This course provides an introduction to the study of crime fiction on the basis of two unusual examples, "Der Richter und sein Henker" (1952) by Friedrich Dürrenmatt and "Tann¿d" (2006) by Andrea Maria Schenkel. It will offer an introduction to the genre and encourage discussion of some historical, political, social and philosophical issues raised in the texts. 
Former Beginners should note that English translations of both course texts are available. 
**This 5-ECTS credit module may be used as part of the engagement with texts required for accreditation by the Teaching Council of Ireland. All applications submitted for Teaching Council accreditation are, however, subject to the Teaching Council’s internal evaluation processes.

IMPORTANT NOTE
The texts are available in German and English and so the course is available to all. However, the expectation is that students who have completed GN104/GN182 (or equivalent course elsewhere) will use the German texts; students who completed GN102/GN152 (or equivalent) may support their learning through using the English versions of the text at home.
learningOutcomes: Interpret selected literary works of differing kinds through close reading and contextualisation.
Evaluate critically secondary literature.
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and wellstructured argument.

modules.moduleContent:
Twentieth and Twenty-first Century Germany: History, Memory & Politics

Drawing on key moments and events in twentieth and twenty-first century German history (i.e. the Holocaust, the experience of war, terrorism, German-German division and unification etc.) and how these have been critically discussed and represented in memory, visual and museum cultures, this module offers students an in-depth understanding of the complexities of contemporary German history, societies and politics. Throughout the module, we will explore popular debates, controversies and scandals, as well as issues around memory and memorialisation. 

Course reader will be provided. 


**This 5-ECTS credit module may be used as part of the engagement with texts required for accreditation by the Teaching Council of Ireland. All applications submitted for Teaching Council accreditation are, however, subject to the Teaching Council’s internal evaluation processes.

IMPORTANT NOTE
Since this module involves aspects of the German language, which will also be tested in the Continuous Assessment and course essay, an intermediate knowledge of German is a prerequisite.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain key aspects of historical, societal and cultural development in Germany during the period in question.
Demonstrate a broad understanding of the main issues regarding German unification and its impact on German identity and memory discourses.
Summarise in broad terms the complexities of German memory culture and the role of historical witnesses, the archive and cultural memory.
Interpret texts through close reading, discussion and contextualisation.
Evaluate critically secondary literature.
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and wellstructured arguments.

modules.moduleContent: Dutch is a West Germanic, Low German language spoken by around 24 million people worldwide. It is grammatically similar in many ways to standard German, but spoken Dutch can be very different in speech. This course follows on from the module GN213A 'Dutch for Beginners' and is only suitable for students who already have some knowledge of the language. It will focus on basic grammar, pronunciation, oral expression, and elementary aural and written comprehension. The course will also offer some useful background information on the culture and society of the Low Countries.
learningOutcomes: Recognise a range of tenses and structures and employ them to produce meaningful and correct Dutch sentences.
Interpret and apply new vocabulary introduced in class.
Explain and interpret the meaning of more complex audio texts that are based on familiar themes regularly encountered in everyday life.
Produce short narratives and descriptive texts about personal experiences.

modules.moduleContent: This module will focus on translating German-language literary texts, including (eg) excerpts from novels, dramas and short prose, into English. Students will be taught to be sensitive to elements of style, language, and tone in their translations.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish the linguistic and stylistic peculiarities of a variety of Germanlanguage texts.
Interpret German texts and reproduce their content in English, using sophisticated vocabulary and reflecting a high level of grammatical and syntactical correctness.
Modify the English translation where fitting to ensure that it reads coherently and reflects appropriately the style of the original German text.

modules.moduleContent: Year Abroad.
In this module our discussions will focus on, and problematise ‘feminism’ as a concept and its interpretation and application across the globe.
learningOutcomes: Discuss different interpretations of feminism.
Engage with literature pertinent to social movement theory.
Articulate an understanding of reproductive health, rights, and justice.
Understand and critique the spectrum of gendered violence including state violence.
Apply an intersectional lens to their practice as an activist that incorporates ableism, racism, sexism, homophobia, trans phobia, borders, and other oppressive forces.

modules.moduleContent:
Waste: (of a material, substance, or by-product) eliminated or discarded as no longer useful or required after the completion of a process.

From plastics to food to carbon emissions, the global economy generates vast quantities and diverse forms of waste. Much of this accumulates in the ocean, land, atmosphere, as well as in human and non-human bodies. Microplastics circulate in the depths of the oceans and the cells of microorganisms; residues of radioactive material released in the 1950s lace the surface of the planet; smog produced from rapidly expanding cities slowly kills millions of people every day; vast shipping containers that can’t compete with larger, faster ships, end up on the shores of Bangladesh to be taken apart by shipbreakers. This is the Anthropocene, a purportedly new geological epoch demarcated by the planetary effects of human activity.

While waste may be everywhere it does not affect everyone equally. As the long history of environmental (in)justice attests, the risks and burdens associated with toxic pollution and waste disposal are disproportionately experienced by racial minorities, workers, the poor, indigenous, and the Global South.  Drawing on the critical traditions of discard studies, political ecology, and science and technology studies, this module will provide students with a critical understanding of waste, its uneven geographies, and its intimate connections with broader political and economic systems. The course will examine: the economics of waste and the waste economy; the role of scientific expertise, including citizen science, in the classification and management of waste and pollution; and the ethical and political challenges that contemporary society faces in a context of unprecedented quantities and qualities of waste.
learningOutcomes: Critically discuss how waste is part of broader political, economic, social and environmental systems.
Apply key geographical perspectives to the challenges posed by waste and pollution at local, national and global scales.
Demonstrate the importance of Geography, as an inherently interdisciplinary discipline, in understanding and responding to the challenges of waste and pollution.
Communicate geographical knowledge effectively and fluently by written, oral and graphical means.

modules.moduleContent:
This module allows a student to apply and enhance their geographic knowledge and skills in an external working environment (outside Maynooth University). The academic supervisor will visit the workplace whilst the placement is in progress.
The placement should be identified by the student and discussed with, and approved by, the module coordinator before the work placement can commence. 

Registration notes: 

1. 
Students who would like to register for GY399A need to contact the module coordinator to discuss this, and get approval for this, a few months in advance of the start of Semester 1. Students who are approved to register for this module will not initially be able to register for when online registrations commence in early September, but their GY399A registration details will be sorted out by the Department during the first few weeks of Semester 1.
2.
Students cannot take GY399A and GY399B; only one of these modules may be taken.
learningOutcomes: communicate with external public and/or private sector organisations
identify appropriate geographical solutions for realworld projects
project manage at least one geographical application
evaluate and communicate the results of the placement

modules.moduleContent: This programme is designed to offer an introduction to the field of Defence Studies, with particular emphasis on how current security and defence issues impact on Ireland. It will outline the development of trends in modern warfare, covering topics such as Maritime Security, Cyber Warfare, the role of Intelligence in national security, Terrorism and CounterTerrorism, among others. Participants will be introduced to current debates in the defence sector and there will also be a skills element to facilitate their professional work development. This course is intended to give the participant an introduction to Defence Studies. Course content will cover: Introduction to Defence Studies; Introduction to Strategic Studies; Contemporary Security Issues; Neutrality and Ireland's "Triple Lock" system; The Political-Military interface and military culture; Trends in Modern Warfare; Land, Air and Naval Warfare; Peacekeeping, Ireland and the UN; Gender Issues in modern defence forces; The role of Intelligence; Cyber Warfare; Terrorism and Counter-Terrorism; Academic Skills (Research & Writing)

modules.moduleName: GLOBAL DEVELOPMENT ISSUES AND RESPONSES SUMMER SCHOOL
modules.moduleContent:
This module on 'Global Development Issues and Responses' focuses on our world's significant challenges and how we can respond to them. It aims to enhance students' understanding of international development priorities and policies and their knowledge of Ireland's and the EU's relationship with the broader world. The module will examine the causes and consequences of climate change, Globalisation and migration and the roles of government, business, civil society, and individuals in addressing these global challenges.

Broad questions addressed:

1. What are the primary development challenges facing our world today?

2. How does Globalisation affect Poverty and Inequality?

3. What are the effects of Climate Change on development worldwide, and how are communities responding to it?

4. How does migration affect poverty and the human rights of people and communities?

5. Who can make a difference in reducing global poverty and inequality?

6. Field Trip
learningOutcomes: An understanding of global development, how it is measured and the links with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
An insight into Globalisation's social and economic dimensions, including Ireland's and the EU's relationship with the wider world and the development impact on Low and MiddleIncome Countries.
An appreciation of economic and environmental sustainability challenges, population dynamics and sustainable development practices.
An understanding of the causes and consequences of migration on development.
An insight into the role of civil society and media in advocating to address global poverty and inequality and skills helpful in active global citizenship.

modules.moduleContent: This module will aim to provide training and experience in field-based Geographic research.  Field-based research and teaching allows the application of knowledge and skills to real world landscape features and issues.  The module will involve data collection and analysis, the preparation and presentation of group work and reporting on experiences during fieldwork and comparative analysis between field and published case studies.
learningOutcomes: Identify the geographical processes at work in the visited (Irish or overseas) locations.
Practice the design of effective field data recording techniques.
Collect and record data on specific topics in field based situations.
Analyse the geographical processes at work in Ireland using collected field data, observations and literature review.
Recount and report on research findings about specific topics related to field based projects.

modules.moduleName: FIELD METHODS FOR GEOGRAPHY AND ECOLOGY
modules.moduleContent: Physical Geography and Ecology are inherently field based sciences. The concepts and ideas connecting the two subjects will be explored in this field trip. The module offers a behind the-scenes look at the natural and human processes that drive habitat patterns and condition in the landscape through time. You will learn skills in how to conduct research in the field. The module combines hands-on experience in observing, collecting data and understanding landscape processes. We will guide you through some of the basic techniques in preparation for your own research project in fourth year, and for work beyond university. The module will run as a week-long field course in conjunction with BI320.
learningOutcomes: Develop an understanding of Physical Geographical processes.
Develop skills in data collection, analysis and presentation with real data.
Develop skills in independent enquiry, critical thinking, decisionmaking, teamworking.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this module is to provide students with skills relating to research design, academic report writing and oral presentation, as well as research ethics, critical assessment and academic argument, while working within the context of small group setting with a dedicated staff tutor. They will then apply these skills in the carrying out of an individual geographical research project, and the writing up of their research findings in a final research report.
