Each GY310B group (consisting of no more than 16 students) will work to a broad work schedule set by their tutor/supervisor, involving the completion of a mid-module individual (or group) presentation and a c.5,000 word individual research report (to be submitted at the end of these semester), as well as other small tutorial exercises that will be set by the tutor (most to be done within class sessions, with a small number to be done outside of these).  

Students MUST sign up for a tutorial group/option during Semester 1 but will have a range of options (each of which taking place at different times) to choose from; details on tutorial group/project options (and how to sign up for these) will made be available on the GY310B Moodle page and by the module coordinator ahead of the commencement of the sign up process. Students must stay with the option/group that they have signed up for at the end of the sign-up process (they may change options/groups before the sign-up process concludes, but their options will be limited, given that a number of options/groups will be full by that stage).
learningOutcomes: Evaluate, and conduct geographical research techniques to investigate their individual research topic.
Design an individual research project, and carry out appropriate primary research, as well as original analysis of secondary data, to achieve this.
Introduce, discuss and analyse research findings in a research report format.
Communicate research methodologies, approaches and findings effectively and fluently by written, oral and graphical means.
Work actively, effectively, and ethically on your own to achieve your research objectives.
¿Apply specialised geographical knowledge to social, political, economic and/or environmental challenges (depending on which research option you are focusing on).
Gather, analyse and interpret new and existing geographical data across a range of scales.

modules.moduleName: THE THREE PS OF TEACHER AGENCY: THE PERSONAL, THE PROFESSIONAL AND THE POLITICAL
modules.moduleContent:
…education is politics! After that, when a teacher discovers that he or she is a politician, too, the teacher has to ask, what kind of politics am I doing in the classroom? That is, in favour of whom am I being a teacher? (p. 46)

Drawing on Paulo Freire and his belief that education is political and never neutral, this module aims to provide spaces for educators to engage in dialogue that has the capacity to not only critique, but disrupt dominant and pervasive hegemonic ‘deficit’ ideologies that underpin the language, discourse and policy of ‘educational disadvantage’. The module will encourage educators to think of children/youth sociologically, and explore social influences on children's/youth educational well-being and the intersection of social class, gender, ethnicity, disability, inequality and care in shaping student identities and learning outcomes for different groups of children/students. This policy and sociological analysis aims to foster participant criticality around the role educators can play in social justice terms. The module will be experiential, and the teaching methods will be underpinned by democratic and facilitative approaches.  Participants will be given the opportunity to appraise the factors and processes that shape their personal, professional and political identity, and explore their social justice implications in a supportive, dialogic environment.


This module is jointly offered by the following Maynooth University departments: Department of Education, Froebel Department of Primary and Early Childhood Education, Department of Adult and Community Education and the Maynooth University Access Programme

modules.moduleName: EU LAW I – CONSTITUTIONAL AND INSTITUTIONAL LAW
modules.moduleContent: The objective of this module is to provide students with knowledge of the constitutional and institutional foundations of the European Union (EU) legal order, and with thorough understanding of EU institutions and procedures. The course also aims to equip students with the tools necessary to discuss the impact of EU law on the national legal orders of the Member States. The module addresses a range of different topics, and focuses on the role of the EU institutions and the interaction among the political institutions in the law making process, the sources of EU law, the role and jurisdiction of the Court of Justice and the judicial remedies available to institutions, Member States and private parties, including the Charter of Fundamental Rights.
learningOutcomes: Outline and comment upon the constitutional development of the EU with reference to the principal founding and amending Treaties.
Identify the main institutions of the EU, outline their structure, functions and powers and critically analyse the relationship between them.
Outline and evaluate the primary and secondary sources of EU law and the legislative processes by which such law is created.
Describe the nature of EU law and analyse its position in the domestic legal order, with particular emphasis on the doctrines of supremacy and direct effect.
Understand how EU law is enforced by way of private (state liability) and public enforcement (Commission enforcement).
Be familiar with the key procedures before the Court of Justice of the EU, their advantages and drawbacks.
Have a basic understanding of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU and what distinguishes it from the European Convention on Human Rights.

modules.moduleContent: The objective of this module is to provide students with knowledge of the constitutional and institutional foundations of the European Union (EU) legal order, and with thorough understanding of EU institutions and procedures. The course also aims to equip students with the tools necessary to discuss the impact of EU law on the national legal orders of the Member States. The module addresses a range of different topics, and focuses on the role of the EU institutions and the interaction among the political institutions in the law making process, the sources of EU law, the role and jurisdiction of the Court of Justice and the judicial remedies available to institutions, Member States and private parties, including the Charter of Fundamental Rights.
learningOutcomes: Outline and comment upon the constitutional development of the EU with reference to the principal founding and amending Treaties.
Identify the main institutions of the EU, outline their structure, functions and powers and critically analyse the relationship between them.
Outline and evaluate the primary and secondary sources of EU law and the legislative processes by which such law is created.
Describe the nature of EU law and analyse its position in the domestic legal order, with particular emphasis on the doctrines of supremacy and direct effect.
Understand how EU law is enforced by way of private (state liability) and public enforcement (Commission enforcement).
Be familiar with the key procedures before the Court of Justice of the EU, their advantages and drawbacks.
Have a basic understanding of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU and what distinguishes it from the European Convention on Human Rights.

modules.moduleContent:
This module generally examines equity as a legal system, and more specifically, trust formalities and administration, trustees’ fiduciary duties, the scope of liability faced by trustees and by third-parties who have contact with trustees and/or trust property. It examines, in detail, express trusts, resulting trusts, constructive trusts, void and voidable trusts, secret trusts, purpose trusts, and charitable trusts. 
 
Any aspect of this module, may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an indepth knowledge of the fundamentals of trust law, the operation of trusts, the different types of trust, and the legal rules governing them.
Comprehend the principles relating to trust administration, the duties of trustees as well as the scope of trustee (and third party) liability.
Interpret a given factual matrix considering trust law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.
Analyse, debate, and criticise particular areas of trust law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of equitable principles relating to trusts, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the position in equity.
Analyse, debate, and criticise the law in a particular area of trusts law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.

modules.moduleContent: WORK PLACEMENT, FULL ACADEMIC YEAR

This module will develop the student’s knowledge and understanding of live television production. It will explore the roles, skills and techniques required to produce team-based live programming. Students will become more familiar with various forms of production equipment used in live television and will further hone their technical skills, while working together as a team, to creatively complete a number of production briefs.
learningOutcomes: Identify phases of production, production roles and the creative skills of live television production
Demonstrate skills in camera work, sound, lighting, graphics, presentation and interview techniques
Understand the significance of the necessary skills required to produce a variety of programme formats and genres
Critically appraise live production of media content
modules.notes: Digital tools for editing. Data gathering for contributors, topics.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to key principles and practices of screen design. Students will learn how to not only create appropriately formatted visual content but also how to use such content effectively as part of multimodal digital communication. Students will learn visual, textual, and technical methods and tools in order to produce and critique content for the contemporary online environment.

Note: MD171B and MD171C are a disallowed combination and cannot be taken together as the module content is the same.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the principles of design in a screenbased environment.
Demonstrate a basic understanding of the appropriate computer programs and tools used for visual communication in static forms.
Plan and Design Visual Design projects.
Identify the appropriate tools and concepts for Screen design practice.

modules.moduleContent:
This programme is an introduction to Leadership and Management Studies. It is designed to contribute to the professional military education of officers (Captains/Majors) serving in the Armed Forces of Malta (AFM). The aim of the course is to broaden the officers’ knowledge and understanding of key concepts, theories, issues and debates within the field of leadership and organisational studies. It will introduce them to core topics within these fields and will help students develop their analytical and critical thinking skills in order to better understand the contemporary challenges organisations face and how these challenges impact leadership.

Content:
The course will introduce theoretical and applied concepts related to leadership and management studies. It will cover the following:
1. Introduction to leadership and management (organisational behaviour) concepts
2. The nature of leadership
3. Leadership, Management and Followership
4. Early theory: Traits and behaviours
5. Contingency theories
6. Dimensions of trust, ethics in the workplace
7. Contemporary theory: transformational leadership
8. Substitutes and neutralisers (of leadership)
9. Fundamentals of teams (and being a team leader)
10. Knowing and managing self 
11. Leader development, Stewardship
12. Feedback/ Coaching for development
13. Plenary session 1: Contemporary European Security
14. Plenary session 2: Emerging challenges – climate leadership, environment
learningOutcomes: Identify key issues and debates in the fields of Leadership and Management Studies
Discuss contemporary challenges organisations face and explain how these challenges impact leadership
Develop informed solutions on how to improve leadership capacity in a particular context
Critically appraise their own leadership and followership preferences, styles and behaviours
Deliver briefings and reports on current leadership issues in a concise and professional manner
Present the findings of research, based on primary and secondary source, in a concise and professional manner

modules.moduleContent: Monte Carlo Methods and simulation: random number generators, integration, Metropolis algorithm. Minimisation of multi-dimensional functions: direction set methods, simulated annealing. Linear algebra: Gauss-Jordan elimination, conjugate gradient. Partial Differential Equations. Static solutions/boundary value problems: direct matrix methods, relaxation methods, spectral methods. Time evolution problems: explicit and implicit schemes, stability analysis. Each section will include a range of problems from physics and applied mathematics.
learningOutcomes: solve partial differential equation (initial and boundary value problems) using a variety of methods
analyse the stability of differentiation schemes for initialvalue problems in partial differential equations
outline the principles behind the most commonly used methods for solving systems of linear equations (GaussJordan elimination, conjugate gradient), their advantages and limitations
describe and apply the basic principles fo Monte Carlo methods

modules.moduleContent:
This experiential and reflective learning module is designed to offer students the opportunity to put theory into practice, as well as to learn new skills and acquire new knowledge. This module is designed to assist students in translating their first and second year conceptual learning into practical actions during their work placement. Through this, our students develop an understanding of the skills and competencies required by the world of industry. By translating the conceptual insights of the undergraduate modules into a set of actions, and applying them to real problems at work, students will learn and reflect on how to bridge theoretical knowledge and practical competencies. 

This module supports the student’s career development, by facilitating students in optimising their first steps into the professional labour market. In doing this, students will enhance their employability through analysing and reflecting how they can best utilize and apply their conceptual and theoretical learnings and experience in a contemporary professional organisation. 

This module’s supporting tutorials will the provide students with the background and critical skills required for reflecting on their placement experience, and assist in preparing their placement report. To pass the module, students must have completed their work placement and met the requirements of the placement tutorials.
learningOutcomes: Undertake an analysis of their career experiences and development in their placement organisation.  Using appropriate concepts from the tutorials students will identify their career dispositions following their placement experience;

Undertake a critical analysis of their placement organisation's structure and of group and team work, proposing ideas for improving team dynamics and performance. The students should demonstrate an improvement in the skills of managing leadership/follower dynamics, working with colleagues and under supervision, leading and initiating activities. Students should be able to identify learnings that can be transferred to their degree group projects upon return to their studies;

Undertake a critical analysis of their sources of motivation at work and the relationship with job satisfaction. Suggest how they would leverage employee motivations to improve job satisfaction if they were the top managers of the placement organisation;
Undertake a critical analysis of aspects of the HRM system (as explored in the tutorials) in their placement organisation; and

Drawing from the competencies of year one and two of their degree, further develop their business acumen within the industrial setting of their placement employer, applying theory in practice and reflecting practice in theory.

modules.moduleContent:
This "Sustainability, Circular Economy & ESG Investing" micro-credential is designed to meet the evolving needs of professionals navigating the increasingly complex domains of environmental sustainability and financial decision-making. The development of this micro-credential arises from the critical recognition that sustainable development and investment are not only moral imperatives but also key drivers of innovation and competitiveness in the modern economy. The urgency of addressing environmental challenges, coupled with the rising influence of ESG factors on investment decisions, underscores the need for a micro-credential that equips participants with the skills to implement sustainable practices and investment strategies. The circular economy offers a framework for minimizing waste and optimizing resource use, which is crucial for long-term sustainability. Meanwhile, ESG investing represents a shift in how value is assessed, emphasizing the importance of sustainability in financial performance.

This dynamic online MC offers an in-depth exploration into the intersections of sustainability, circular economy practices, and ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) investing, tailored for individuals seeking to make a meaningful impact in their professional fields through sustainable practices. Participants will gain a robust understanding of the critical theories and practical applications that underpin sustainability in business and investment decisions, focusing on innovative strategies that align with global sustainability goals.
learningOutcomes: Critically analyse and apply sustainability and circular economy concepts within a business or investment context.
Evaluate investments using ESG criteria to inform sustainable investment decisions.
Develop and implement sustainable business strategies that contribute to a circular economy.
Communicate effectively with stakeholders about sustainability efforts and performance.

modules.moduleContent:
Objectives: To introduce students to Differential Calculus in One Real Variable.

Overview: Why study calculus?
Sets and Functions. Review of Exponentials, Logarithms and Trigonometry. Inequalities, Limits: Idea of a limit, definition of a limit, continuity. Derivatives: Definition of a derivative, tangents, product rule, chain rule, quotient rule, higher derivatives, implicit differentiation, Newton's method for roots, exponential and logarithmic functions. Applications of derivatives: Maxima and minima, monotonic functions and the first derivative test. Concavity and points of inflection. Symmetry and curve sketching. Applied max-min problems and related rates.
learningOutcomes: Describe and manipulate functions.
Evaluate limits of basic functions..
Obtain derivatives of basic functions and combinations of such functions.
Solve maxmin problems.
Use Newton's method
Sketch curves of the basic functions and combinations of such functions.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleName: LINEAR ALGEBRA FOR THE HEALTH SCIENCES
modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To introduce students to linear equations, determinants, vectors, and matrices.

Matrix algebra, inverses, determinants of 2x2 and 3x3 matrices. Systems of linear equations, and solution by Gaussian elimination and matrix inversion. Vector arithmetic in two and three dimensions. Linear transformations via 2x2 matrices.
learningOutcomes: Solve a system of linear equations using Gaussian Elimination.
Perform operations with matrices.
Compute the determinant of 2 x 2 and 3 x 3 matrices. Find the inverse of a 2 x 2 matrix.
Draw and understand 2 and 3dimensional diagrams.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems. State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module objective: An introduction to multivariable integral calculus and its uses.

Double and triple integrals.  Surface integrals. Green’s theorem in the plane.  Conservative vector fields.  Complex Numbers: Argand diagram, modulus, conjugate, polar form, Euler’s formula.  Introduction to Series and Convergence Tests. Computation using mathematical software.
learningOutcomes: Evaluate double, triple and surface integrals.
Use these integrals in applications.
State and apply Green's theorem in the plane.
Decide if a series converges or not.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To introduce the student to multivariable differential calculus.

Calculus of several variables, especially in two and three dimensions.  Functions of two or more variables.  Graphs.  Limits.  Continuity.  Partial derivatives.  Gradient.  Tangents and normals.  Max-min.  Lagrange multipliers. Introduction to Sequences. Computation using mathematical software.
learningOutcomes: Draw the level curves for a function of two variables.
Obtain the partial derivatives and gradient vector of a function.
Obtain tangents and normals to surfaces.
Solve maxmin problems in several variables.
Discuss the convergence of sequences and series.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To introduce the student to rigorous multivariate calculus and its uses.

Functions from  R^n to R.  Examples. Quadrics. Visualisation. Limits and continuity. Partial Derivatives. Gradients, directional derivatives, mean-value theorem, chain rules, Tangent lines and planes, level surfaces, max-min, Hessian, 2nd Derivative Test. Double and triple integrals.
learningOutcomes: Draw level curves for functions of two variables.
Describe the standard quadric surfaces.
Find the partial derivatives and gradient vector of a function.
Obtain tangents and normals to surfaces.
Solve maxmin problems in several variables.
Obtain solutions to double and triple integrals. Use double, triple and surface integrals in applications.
Determine whether a function of several variables is differentiable at a given point.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems. State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To introduce the student to abstract algebra, with a firm emphasis on linear algebra over R and C.

The rationals, reals and complex numbers as examples of fields, the field with 2 elements and field of rational functions;  Rn and Cn as vector spaces, vector spaces of functions; the axioms of a vector space; subspace, direct sums, linear maps; kernel, image and quotient spaces; span, linear independence, basis and dimension; linear maps and matrices, change of basis, rank and nullity; normed vector spaces, orthonormal bases, orthogonal projection, Gram-Schmidt process; eigenvalues and eigenvectors, diagonalisation; dual spaces, tensor products, endomorphism algebras; other topics may include exterior algebra and determinants.
learningOutcomes: Define a field and a vector space.
Prove simple consequences of the axioms.
Understand subspaces, span and linear independence.
Compute a basis and the dimension of a vector space.
Represent and manipulate linear maps using matrices.
Compute the rank and nullity of a linear map and a matrix.
Understand quotient spaces and the first isomorphism theorem.

Find eigenvalues and eigenvectors of linear maps.
Define inner products spaces.
Perform the GramSchmidt process and diagonalization.
Use methods from MT251P and the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.
Understand the role of axioms in algebra.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To introduce students to Ordinary Differential Equations and Modelling.

First Order Equations: separable, homogeneous, linear, Bernoulli.  Second Order Equations: constant coefficient, Euler type, linear equations. Methods of solution including reduction of order, variation of parameters, undetermined coefficients. Miscellaneous Equations of special type. 

Modelling of applied problems such as the spread of infections, radioactive decay, population growth, Newton's Law of Cooling.
learningOutcomes: Solve the standard types of first order equations.
Solve second order equations using methods covered in the course.
Use any other method of solution covered in the course.
Use ODES to model and solve applied problems.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent: Complex numbers, regions in the complex plane. Complex functions, the derivative of a complex function, holomorphic functions, harmonic functions. The Cauchy-Riemann equations, Cauchy's Theorem, Cauchy's Integral Formula. Taylor series and examples, Laurent series. The residue theorem.
learningOutcomes: Differentiate and integrate functions of a complex variable.
Define the exponential, logarithmic and trigonometric functions.
Test for the analyticity of a given function.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.
Prove simple results in complex analysis using rigorous arguments.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To introduce students to Coding Theory.

Selected Topics from the following:
Introduction of  information transmission and error detection.  Binary encoding of messages.  Hamming distance.  Error-correcting codes over finite alphabets.  Sphere-packing bound, perfect codes.  Gibert-Varshamov bound.  The coding theory problem.  Error probabilities.  Discussion of Shannon’s theorem.
Linear codes.  Equivalence of codes.  Generator matrix, row operations modulo p and the    standard form, encoding.  Parity check matrix.  Hamming codes.  Syndrome decoding.  Dual code.
Cyclic codes and polynomials modulo xn-1.  Generator polynomial.  Linear recursions and cyclic codes.  Weight enumerator, MacWilliams identities.  Reed-Muller codes.  Golay codes.  Reed-Solomon Codes and Compact Disc player.  Convolutional codes.
learningOutcomes: Find the Hamming distance of a code.
Obtain the generator matrix and parity check matrix for linear error correcting codes.
Correct transmission errors for linear error correcting codes.
Determine whether an error correcting code with predetermined properties exists.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
The different modes of assessment employed (reports, presentation and interviews) evaluate learning 
outcomes 5 – 7. Learning outcomes 1 – 4 are primarily evaluated in the final report and interview. 
The number and scheduling of the assessment procedures are designed to indirectly evaluate learning outcome 7. 

The aims of this module are:
• To promote project based learning in the field of embedded and intelligent devices.
• To instill the creative spirit in students. 
• To develop oral and written communication skills. 
• To develop students experience of working in a group. 
• To engender an awareness of ethical issues in engineering.
learningOutcomes: Apply problembased learning to solve unforeseen problems in the areas of embedded and intelligent devices.
Apply structured design to a range of problems in the domain of signals and control.
Apply theoretical knowledge in solving problems encountered.
Discuss any ethical issues, environmental impacts and health and safety issues associated with their project.
Prepare and deliver an oral presentation.
Defend their work through interview.
Demonstrate appropriate project management techniques (including time management and project planning).

modules.moduleContent:
• Introduction:
 Signals, Systems, Linear Systems, Time-Invariant Systems, Continuous-time signals and systems,  Discrete-time signals and systems, Fourier Series representation of signals and systems.
• Continuous-time signals and systems:
  The impulse function, properties of the impulse function, Fourier transform of the impulse function, impulse response and frequency response of continuous-time systems, convolution, the  sifting integral, the Laplace transform, pole-zero models.
• Discrete-time signals and systems:
Sampling, uniform and non-uniform quantization, Linear difference equations, frequency  response of discrete-time systems, discrete-time convolution and unit sample response, sampled bandpass signals. Intro to digital filters.
• The Z-transform: Convergence of the ZT, properties of the ZT, Applications of the ZT.
• The Fourier transform (FT):
 The discrete-time FT (DTFT), properties of the DTFT, Parseval's Theorem, Sampled signal spectrum, Repeat spectra, Alias, Periodogram.
• Random Signals:
 Types of noise, Frequency-response estimation using noise. Random variables, Probability Density Function (PDF), Random processes. Spectral estimation. The periodogram. Bartlett's method.
learningOutcomes: Represent and analyse signals in the time and frequency domains.
Represent and analyse linear, timeinvariant systems in the time and frequency domains
Perform continuoustime and discretetime convolution of signals
Compute the continuoustime and discretetime Fourier transform of signals and systems.
Explain the fundamentals of sampling theory, alias and repeat spectra.

Compute the Ztransform of discretetime signals and systems.

Compute the discrete Fourier transform of discretetime signals and systems.

Determine the frequencyresponse of a system using a white noise input.

Compute the periodgram of a signal.

Analyse the frequency content of a signal using Bartlett's method.

Work as part of a team and learn through collaboration.

modules.moduleContent:
• Signals, power, and energy in the time and frequency domains (review)
• Noise, channel properties, and motivation for modulation and coding
• Analog baseband modulation
• Exponential analog modulation: Phase and Frequency Modulation (PM and FM)
• Digital baseband modulation techniques (including PAM, PWM, PCM) and line codes
• Digital bandpass Modulation techniques (including ASK, FSK, PSK, and quadrature techniques)
• Channel capacity and multiple access techniques
learningOutcomes: Derive the basic equations for a range of modulation schemes.
Describe the timedomain and frequency domain characteristics of a range of modulation schemes.
Sketch and explain the operation of one or more basic circuits for performing modulation/demodulation.
Quantify and explain the effect of noise on communication systems.
Calculate the channel capacity of a dispersive channel using the waterfilling theorem.
Describe different channel access methods used in wireless communications (TDMA, FDMA).
Investigate and explain a communication system not described in class.

modules.moduleContent: In this class students are introduced to writing, both poetry and prose. The class focuses on the practicalities of writing, editing and giving and taking criticism of work. Students will produce writing for discussion and criticism and work on developing these extracts in response. Students will also look critically at the work of established writers.

The aim of this module is to introduce students to the physics of vibrations, waves and light, and electromagnetism, and to tutor them in the solution of elementary problems in these areas. 

_Vibrations and Waves: (6 lectures) 

* Elasticity and simple harmonic motion; Pendulums; Damped Harmonic Oscillators;
* The nature of waves; The mathematical description of a wave; Transverse and longitudinal waves
* Linear superposition
* Standing waves and Sound 

_Light: (6 Lectures) 

* The electromagnetic spectrum; Polarization
* Reflection, refraction and dispersion; Lenses; Coherence and diffraction

_Electromagnetism: (12 lectures)

* Origins of Electricity, Coulomb’s Law, Electric Fields, and Electric Field Lines;
* Electric potential and Electric Potential Energy, Voltage, Capacitors and Capacitance
* Magnetic Fields and Forces, Basic Circuits. 

A more detailed module descriptor is located on: https://www.maynoothuniversity.ie/electronic-engineering/current-students/module-descriptors

Note: this module previously carried the EE110 code.
learningOutcomes: Discuss the nature of physical knowledge, measurement of physical quantities and the use of units in describing the laws of nature.
Describe the physics of motion and forces (kinematics & dynamics) using an algebraic approach and vectorial description of motion.
Discuss the basic properties of matter and thermal physics.
Solve simple numerical problems relevant to fundamental mechanics and thermodynamics.
Carry out relevant laboratory experiments to demonstrate and test physical principles and report the results.

modules.moduleContent: Finance work placement (30 ECTS)

Please note this Certificate programme does not run every academic year. Contact appliedsocialstudies@mu.ie with any queries.

The NUI Certificate European Youth Mobility Project Management aims to develop youth practitioners’ knowledge, skills and competence in high-quality, mobility project management in the context of European programmes.
learningOutcomes: Knowledge of the national and European historical and policy contexts of European mobility projects.
Understanding of the principles and practices that underpin European mobility projects.
Competence in managing, delivering and implementing a European mobility project.

modules.moduleContent: Each student is assigned a project topic and a departmental adviser will be available to give advice on a regular basis at an agreed time. The central aim of the project is to assess the student's ability to formulate and test a theory or model using the methods of quantitative finance, statistics, and/or computer science. The projects should be no more than 20 pages in length and must be word-processed and suitably bound.
learningOutcomes: Produce an extended piece of quantitative financial analysis.

modules.moduleContent:
This module - along with GC298b - serves as a gateway to the language and cultural heritage of ancient Rome. 'Introduction to Latin' explores the grammar, syntax, and vocabulary of Latin for students new to the language. The focus will be on language acquisition, but some attention is also given to Roman cultural and literary contexts, as students work to unravel the complexities of Latin literature and its ensuring influence on later civilizations. Taken together with the two GC299 modules (in Semester 2), these modules will enable students to proceed to the guided reading of original texts - seminal works by authors such as Cicero, Virgil, and Ovid - towards the end of the year.

NOTE: A minimum number of enrolments is required for these modules to run each semester.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain basic Latin grammar and syntax.
Recognise a broad range of Latin vocabulary.
Demonstrate the ability to read simplified passages of Latin.
Demonstrate the ability to compose short phrases of Latin.
Develop a deeper understanding of general language structures, including English grammar.
Develop broadly transferable skills of analysis and synthesis.

modules.moduleContent:
This experiential and reflective learning module is designed to offer students the opportunity to put theory into practice, as well as to learn new skills and acquire new knowledge. This module is designed to assist students in translating their first and second year conceptual learning into practical actions during their work placement. Through this, our students develop an understanding of the skills and competencies required by the world of industry. By translating the conceptual insights of the undergraduate modules into a set of actions, and applying them to real problems at work, students will learn and reflect on how to bridge theoretical knowledge and practical competencies.

This module supports the student’s career development, by facilitating students in optimising their first steps into the professional labour market. In doing this, students will enhance their employability through analysing and reflecting how they can best utilize and apply their conceptual and theoretical learnings and experience in a contemporary professional organisation.

This module’s supporting tutorials will the provide students with the background and critical skills required for reflecting on their placement experience, and assist in preparing their placement report. To pass the module, students must have completed their work placement and met the requirements of the placement tutorials.
learningOutcomes: Undertake an analysis of their career experiences and development in their placement organisation. Using appropriate concepts from the tutorials students will identify their career dispositions following their placement experience;

Undertake a critical analysis of their placement organisation's structure and of group and team work, proposing ideas for improving team dynamics and performance. The students should demonstrate an improvement in the skills of managing leadership/follower dynamics, working with colleagues and under supervision, leading and initiating activities. Students should be able to identify learnings that can be transferred to their degree group projects upon return to their studies;

Undertake a critical analysis of their sources of motivation at work and the relationship with job satisfaction. Suggest how they would leverage employee motivations to improve job satisfaction if they were the top managers of the placement organisation;
Undertake a critical analysis of aspects of the HRM system (as explored in the tutorials) in their placement organisation; and

Drawing from the competencies of year one and two of their degree, further develop their business acumen within the industrial setting of their placement employer, applying theory in practice and reflecting practice in theory.

modules.moduleContent:
This programme is designed to offer an introduction to the field of Defence and Strategic Studies. It is designed to contribute to the military education of officers (Captains/Majors) serving in the Armed Forces of Malta (AFM). 

The aim of the course is to broaden the officers’ knowledge and understanding of key concepts, theories, issues and debates within the field of defence studies and strategic studies. It will introduce them to core topics within these fields and will help students to develop their analytical and critical thinking skills. It will examine and introduce concepts and ideas that are important within contemporary debates about defence and security, set within an appropriate ethical and humanitarian framework, and will equip students with the knowledge and skills needed to understand the complex security challenges facing Europe today and into the future.

Class Size: 7-10 students

Content:
The course will provide an introduction to defence studies and strategic studies. It will cover the following:
1. Introduction to defence and strategic studies
2. Strategy, ethics and the conduct of war
3. Trends in modern war and security
4. Land warfare
5. Air power
6. Maritime power
7. Joint operations
8. Unconventional warfare / terrorism
9. Cyber security and cyber operations
10. Peacekeeping and UN operations
11. Contemporary European Security
12. Malta, security and the AFM
learningOutcomes: Identify key issues and debates in the fields of Defence Studies and Strategic Studies
Critically appraise concepts relating to the conduct of joint operations today
Demonstrate understanding of the importance of ethical and moral considerations when planning military operations
Analyse the role of the AFM within the context of current European security challenges
Deliver briefings and reports on current security issues in a concise and professional manner
Present the findings of research, based on primary and secondary source, in a concise and professional manner

modules.moduleContent:
This module sets out to explore the importance of the teacher- student relationship and offers a set of critical practices to help teachers effectively develop an empathetic mind-set and create culturally responsive mentoring relationships with students.

This module will build cultural competencies and prepare teachers for effective, empowering relationships with students through the development of self-awareness, empathy, listening, problem solving, and cross-cultural communication skills to engage diverse learners across all groups and communities.  Themes to be explored include gender, ability/disability, class, race, and ethnicity.  The module will define the roles, responsibilities and boundaries within the teacher-student mentoring relationship. 



Module co-ordinators: Dr Katriona O'Sullivan and Dr Gareth Burns

This module is jointly offered by the following Maynooth University departments: Department of Education, Froebel Department of Primary and Early Childhood Education, Department of Adult and Community Education and the Maynooth University Access Programme

This module will teach students basic web-based technologies such as html, CSS and JavaScript in order to create an interactive story. Students will be introduced to the impact of the digital apparatus on the standard narrative form and the resulting implications of this on human communication and creative expression. Students will critically examine contemporary examples of interactive storytelling and will then use a web-based tool such as Twine¿https://twinery.org/¿to tell an interactive non-linear story.

Please note: MD228A and MD233 cannot be taken together.
learningOutcomes: Conceptualise, design, and produce a webbased interactive story for a specific target audience.
Solve coding problems.
Reflect critically upon interactive storytelling processes and practices.

modules.moduleContent:
This module continues with concepts developed in its first semester component. In this second semester component, this module provides an introduction to the operation of equitable remedies, for instance, injunctions, specific performance, estoppel, tracing, rectification, and rescission. 
 
Any aspect of this module, may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an indepth knowledge of equitable remedies, including the various types of injunction, specific performance, estoppel, tracing, rectification, and rescission.
Interpret a given factual matrix considering equitable principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.
Analyse, debate, and criticise particular areas of equity and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Display an indepth knowledge of the law of equitable remedies and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to the appropriateness of utilising such a remedy.
Comprehend the principles relating to the equitable remedies, explain their origin, operation in different circumstances, and distinguish an equitable from a legal remedy.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of equitable principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the position in equity.
Analyse, debate, and criticise the law in a particular area of equity and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.

modules.moduleContent: MEDIEVAL IRISH STUDIES

Every individual spends most of his/her life in organisations. Everyone’s work life involves working with, in, or for organisations, and, potentially, creating one’s own organisation. Everyone’s life is also full of managing – from the daily practice of organising yourself, to organising others to undertake individual and group activities to achieve organisational objectives. The aim of this module is for students to develop a better understanding and appreciation of the different human-related elements that influence organisational behaviour and management, leading to a better appreciation of why and how people and groups behave in organisations, and how management practices may influence and direct their behaviour towards achieving organisational goals. The module will consider what shapes the behaviour of people in organisations and how their behaviour can be influenced.
learningOutcomes: Define and describe/explain classic and contemporary organisational behaviour (OB) and management theories and concepts;
Apply classic and contemporary OB and management theories and concepts to different workplace contexts/to practical scenarios;
Critically assess if/how different OB and management theories and concepts may improve individual, group and organisational outcomes;
Identify contemporary topics, issues and challenges in work organisations; and
Compare and contrast different OB theories/concepts/tools and their application in different organisational settings.

modules.moduleContent:
Objectives: To introduce students to Differential Calculus in One Real Variable.

Overview: Why study calculus? 

Sets and Functions. Review of Exponentials, Logarithms and Trigonometry. Inequalities, Limits: Idea of a limit, definition of a limit, continuity. Derivatives: Definition of a derivative, tangents, product rule, chain rule, quotient rule, higher derivatives, implicit differentiation, Newton's method for roots, exponential and logarithmic functions. Applications of derivatives: Maxima and minima, monotonic functions and the first derivative test. Concavity and points of inflection. Symmetry and curve sketching. Applied max-min problems and related rates.
learningOutcomes: Describe and manipulate functions
Evaluate limits of basic functions.
Obtain derivatives of basic functions and combinations of such functions.
Solve maxmin problems.
Use Newton's method
Sketch curves of the basic functions and combinations of such functions.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To introduce students to linear equations, determinants, vectors, complex numbers and matrices.

Overview of coordinate geometry in 1, 2 and 3 dimensions.  Describing lines and planes by means of linear equations.  Systems of linear equations and augmented matrices.  Row echelon form matrices and back substitution.  Elementary row operations and Gaussian elimination.  Bound and free variables.  Matrices and rules of matrix arithmetic.  Noncommutativity of matrix product.  Determinants of 2 x 2 and 3 x 3 matrices.  Determinant of a product.  Effect of elementary row operations on determinants.  Determinants as areas and volumes.  Matrix inverses, in particular 2 x 2 inverses.  Overview of vectors in 1, 2 and 3 dimensions.  Position and displacement vectors.  Triangle and parallelogram laws.  Vector arithmetic, norm, distance, dot product and angle, orthogonal projection.  The triangle and Cauchy-Schwartz inequalities.  The cross product and triple product in 3-dimensions.  The normal vector of a plane and the vector equation of a line.  Complex numbers and the plane.  Other topics, such as the eigenvalues of 2 x 2 matrices, roots of unity, geometric problems, quadratic forms, linear maps via 2 x 2 matrices.
learningOutcomes: Solve a system of linear equations using Gaussian Elimination.
Perform operations with matrices.
Compute the determinant of 2 x 2 and 3 x 3 matrices. Find the inverse of a 2 x 2 matrix.
Draw and understand 2 and 3dimensional diagrams.
Perform vector operations, dot products, distance, norms, angles and projections.
Write down the equation of lines and planes in 2 and 3 dimensions. Coordinatize lines and planes in space.
Handle the algebra and geometry of complex numbers.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems. State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To introduce Operations Research as a collection of descriptive and analytical techniques, centered around optimization, which can be used to facilitate decision making in business and industry. This course focuses on a few selected mathematical ideas which can be applied to a diverse range of ‘real world’ problems. 
Linear Programming: constrained optimization, convexity, the Simplex Algorithm.
Game Theory: zero-sum two-person games, strictly determined games, mixed strategies, the minimax theorem.
Graphs and networks: maximal flow problems, the Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm, the max flow-min cut theorem; shortest path problems, Dijkstra's Algorithm; project scheduling, critical paths.
learningOutcomes: Use the algorithms covered in the course.
Solve a variety of optimization problems.
Demonstrate an understanding of Game Theory.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
• Brief revision - dynamic and static systems, categorizing systems, transfer functions, block algebra
• Mathematical modelling of systems - Continuous-time and discrete-time modelling (Differential and difference equation models; transfer function models)
• State-space representation including relationship between state-space and transfer function forms
• Linearisation
• Solution to linear differential and difference state-space equations (using state transformation)
• Stability 
• Frequency domain analysis 
- Nyquist diagram
- Bode plots
learningOutcomes: Develop mathematical models for a range of dynamical systems.
Change between different mathematical model representations (differential equation, statespace and transfer function).
Linearise a nonlinear system about an operating point.
Calculate responses of simple dynamical systems.
Determine the stability of dynamical systems.
Analyse simple systems using Bode plots.
Use Simulink to simulate simple dynamical systems.

modules.moduleContent:
• Resistors, Inductors and Capacitors – basic concepts & revision
• Revision of basis resistor-based circuit analysis (mesh, nodal, Thevenin’s Theorem, etc.) 
• Transient Analysis of RL and RC Circuits
• Laplace Transforms for Analyzing Transient in Circuits with Switches
• Sinusoids – Periodic Waveforms and Complex Number Representation
- Average, Mean Square and RMS
- Leading and Lagging Phase Angles
- Complex Numbers – Cartesian and Polar Form
• Phasors and Phasor Analysis of AC Circuits
• Power in AC Circuits
- Real, Reactive, Complex and Apparent Power
- Power Factor
• Resonant Circuits 
- Resonant Frequency, Bandwidth and Q factor
learningOutcomes: Analyse transient behavior in DC circuits containing resistors, inductors and capacitors.
Use Laplace and Inverse Laplace transforms to determine transients in circuits with switches.
Express a sinusoid in phasor form.
Analyse simple oneloop AC circuits
Determine the power in a simple circuit.
Build simple two and threeloop circuits on a breadboard.
Connect up power supplies, signal generators and oscilloscopes.
Simulate simple circuits using Electronics Workbench.

modules.moduleContent:
• Vector Calculus
• Review of vector basics, applications of vectors in physical examples. 
• Develop scalar and vector triple product formulae. 
• Functions of several variables. Geometric interpretation. Level Curves. 
• Partial derivatives. Geometric interpretation of the partial derivative.
• Chain rules for function of several variables. 
• Scalar and vector functions. Physical examples.
• Introduce the "Del" vector differential operator. 
• The gradient of a scalar field. r-dependent vector and scalar fields. Physical examples.
• Directional Derivative. Examples involving steepest ascent/descent. Directions of zero change. 
• Divergence of a vector field. 
• The Laplacian operator. Poisson's and Laplace's equation.
• The Curl of a vector field. Conservative vector fields. 
• Discussion of Maxwell's equations. 
• Derivative of vector functions. Unit tangent, normal, and binormal vectors. 
• Curvature, radius of curvature and torsion. 
• The Frenet-Serret formulae. 
• Velocity and acceleration vectors vectors. 
• Line integrals.
• Path independence. Scalar potential of a conservative vector function. 
• Complex Analysis
• Basic concepts of complex numbers. Functions of a complex variable. 
• The Cauchy-Riemann equations. Analytic functions. 
• Harmonic functions. Connection between harmonic functions and analytic functions. 
• Solving equations in complex variables.
• Integration of a complex function and line integrals in the complex plane. 
• Cauchy's Integral theorem. 
• The Cauchy Integral Formula and contour integrals. 
• The Cauchy Integral Formula for derivatives.
• Laurent Series and the Residue theorem.
learningOutcomes: Make appropriate use of vector and scalar functions in the description of physical quantities.
Calculate extreme points and directional derivatives for functions of several variables.
Calculate line integrals and determine if a vector function is conservative and if so to obtain the corresponding scalar potential.
Demonstrate an ability to perform vectorcalculus calculations with scalar and vector fields using the deloperator.
Calculate and interpret the geometric properties of space curves in two and three dimensions.
Ascertain the properties of functions of a complex variable and to solve equations involving complex variables.
Calculate line and contour integrals of functions in the complex plane
Apply and select the appropriate mathematical techniques to solve a variety of associated engineering problems.

modules.moduleContent:
The syllabus will be dynamic and adapt to changes in the field, but example topics to be discussed include:
• Robotics and intelligent machines in media (movies, newspapers, books)
• Impact on employment 
• Impact on the medical field.
• The issue of person-in-the-middle criteria for safety-critical issues (including war, policing)
• Impact on privacy in an ever-watching society (eg Alexa, Cortana)
• Legal and societal implications
• Technology and personal relationships and child development
• Fear and Loathing for the Machine
learningOutcomes: Define the scope of an intelligent and connected machine
Identify the key impacts of such machines on society – for example privacy, employment, health, safety
Recommend means for minimizing some of these social impacts
Write a succinct argument on the ethical and social issues arising from technology developments

modules.moduleContent:
The different modes of assessment employed (reports, presentation and interviews) evaluate learning outcomes 7 and 8. Learning outcomes 1 – 6 are primarily evaluated in the main report and interview.
The number and scheduling of the assessment procedures are designed to indirectly evaluate learning outcome 9. Direct assessment of learning outcome 9 also occurs in the project management report through the requirement for a project completion plan.
The aims of this modules are:
• To promote project based learning in the field of analogue electronics.
• To instill the creative spirit in students.
• To develop oral and written communication skills.
• To develop students experience of working in a group.
• To engender an awareness of ethical issues in engineering.
• To develop a basic business proposal.
learningOutcomes: Apply problembased learning to solve unforeseen problems in the area of analogue electronics.
Apply structured design to a range of problems.
Apply theoretical knowledge in solving problems encountered.
Apply a structured process to business proposal research, including market research, user research and competitor analysis.
Prepare a set of manufacturing documentation (costed BOMs, Assembly and Test specifications)
Discuss any ethical issues, environmental impacts and health and safety issues associated with their project.
Write a product concept report (including technical and business feasibility issues) and prepare and deliver an oral presentation.
Defend their work through interview. Demonstrate appropriate project management techniques (including time management and project planning).

modules.moduleContent:
*Software processes and agile software development 

*Software requirements and specifications  

*Software design 

*Software implementation 

*Software verification and validation  

*Using APIs 

*Software tools and environments
learningOutcomes: Select an appropriate software process for a project and outline its execution.
Analyse requirements and software for defects.
Interpret a UML software model.
Design software solutions to constrained problems.

modules.moduleContent:
Students will be trained on the preparation of a curriculum vitae (CV) and also job interview preparation. 
Students are given an overview on the required continuous assessment work to be completed during the work placement, as part of the credits awarded for the Work Experience module.
learningOutcomes: Compile a suitable CV and write a suitable application for a work position.
Evaluate knowledge gained during the work placement.
Evaluate how the placement related to your degree programme.
Document work placement experience in a diary or log.
Complete and return a work placement report documenting the student’s main contributions to the place of work.

modules.moduleContent:
• To introduce the field of unconstrained optimisation.
• To outline basic line search methods for 1-variable functions
• To outline basic direct and gradient-based search methods for multi-variable functions.

• Fundamental principles of optimization
• Line searches for functions with one variable 
- Grid, Fibonacci, Golden Section & Quadratic
• Direct search methods for functions of more than one variable 
- Univariate, DSC algorithm, Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalisation & Simplex method
• Gradient methods for functions of more than one variable 
- Steepest Descent, Conjugate Gradient Algorithm – Fletcher &  Reeves
• Hessian-based Gradient methods for functions of more than one variable 
- Newton’s method, Quasi-Newton’s method, DFP & BFGS algorithms
• The Sum of Squares Problem 
- Gauss-Newton and Levenberg-Marquart algorithms
• An introduction to constrained optimisation
learningOutcomes: Carry out basic line searches for functions with one variable.
Apply direct search methods to functions of more than one variable.
Apply gradientbased methods to functions of more than one variable.
Apply hessianbased methods to functions of more than one variable.
Solve the ‘sum of squares’ problems using the GaussNewton method.

modules.moduleContent: In this class, students will discuss a number of exemplary works from the early decades of the twentieth century, a period of remarkable creativity in Irish writing. Students will explore the complex relationship between literature, culture and politics in this formative period of Irish history.

modules.moduleName: EQUALITY STUDIES IN TRAINING AND DEVELOPMENT
modules.moduleContent:
The NUI Certificate in Equality Studies focuses on delivering a high quality relevant qualification using a unique blend of face to face workshops and interactive online learning with full tutor support. This course will equip students with the knowledge and understanding needed to deal effectively and sensitively with the different equality and diversity issues at all levels of Irish society, the community and the workplace.

This course is relevant to all who espouse the values of social inclusion and equality. It has particular resonance to private sector companies and businesses and staff of community and voluntary organisations who seek to create workplaces where diversity is prized as an asset for generating new ideas and new ways of working.
The course will facilitate the development of individuals, team leaders and managers in having a real appreciation of the importance of equality, diversity and their impact on the organisation contributing to a supportive learning and development culture. It will equip individuals with the essential competencies necessary to meet the challenges of today's rapidly changing world.

Module 1: Reflecting on Equality and Diversity: Reflective Practice; History, concepts and ideologies; Sources of prejudice and discrimination; Conducting practitioner research
Module 2: The Shape of Irish Society: Demography; Irish State and legal system; Outline of Equality legislation.
Module 3: Equality and Diversity in the Workplace: Equality through the employment cycle - Focus on models and practice in relation to gender, work life balance, age, ethnicity and disability
Module 4: Delivering Equality: Development of equality and diversity policies; Managing equality and diversity issues - Recruitment and selection, Training and development; Facilitation and mentoring.
learningOutcomes: Become a reflective practitioner in the context of their own work. Understand the meaning of the terms equality and diversity
Discuss the processes that underlie prejudice and discrimination in society
Understand the skills and processes involved in conducting any writing practitioner research and understand the population structures and demography of Ireland

Describe the Irish state and government; describe major aspects of employment law in Ireland focusing specifically on equality legislation; be familiar with theoretical models of equalitypreference theory, labyrinth theory
Understand the issues relating to work life balance.  Be able to define disability and understand the main models of disability; be familiar with mental health issues

Understand the issues relating to immigration and social change; be familiar with research into the travelling community; understand the difference between assimilation, multiculturalism and interculturalism

Describe diversity in terms of the four layers model.  Understand equality opportunity policies and practices throughout the employment cycle.  Be able to discuss the factors which impact on diversity management and be familiar with the main concepts of coaching and mentoring within a diversity context.

modules.moduleContent:
The aim of this course is to introduce the principles of computer networking using a top down approach, that is, by beginning at the application layer and working down toward the physical layer.

• Computer Networks & the Internet, packet switching, delay, loss, throughput.
• Application Layer: Web, eMail, SMTP, DNS, P2P, CDNs, Socket Programming
• Transport Layer: Multiplexing, UDP, TCP, Congestion Control
• Network Layer: Virtual circuits, IP and Routing Algorithms, Routers, ICMP
• Link Layer: Error detection/correction, ALOHA, CSMA, Sliding window protocols, Multiple Access, Addressing, Switching
• Wireless Networks and Security, Wifi 802.11, mobility management, mobile IP 
• Network security
learningOutcomes: Describe the functional layering of computer network architectures.
Explain the concept of packetswitching, and identify and analyze the different types of packet delay in packetswitched networks.
Describe major internet applications such as the Web and email and the service model that the application layer requires.
Describe the essential principles of a transport layer protocol (reliable data transfer, flow control, congestion control).
Use IP addressing and apply routing algorithms to find shortest paths for networklayer packet delivery.
Describe and compare data link layer services and multiple access techniques.
Describe network security issues and some of the methods that address them.
Use networking tools such as Wireshark to observe and analyze behaviors of networking protocols.

modules.moduleContent:
This experiential and reflective learning module is designed to offer students the opportunity to put theory into practice, as well as to learn new skills and acquire new knowledge. This module is designed to assist students in translating their first and second year conceptual learning into practical actions during their work placement. Through this, our students develop an understanding of the skills and competencies required by the world of industry. By translating the conceptual insights of the undergraduate modules into a set of actions, and applying them to real problems at work, students will learn and reflect on how to bridge theoretical knowledge and practical competencies.

This module supports the student’s career development, by facilitating students in optimising their first steps into the professional labour market. In doing this, students will enhance their employability through analysing and reflecting how they can best utilize and apply their conceptual and theoretical learnings and experience in a contemporary professional organisation.

This module’s supporting tutorials will the provide students with the background and critical skills required for reflecting on their placement experience, and assist in preparing their placement report. To pass the module, students must have completed their work placement and met the requirements of the placement tutorials.
learningOutcomes: Undertake an analysis of their career experiences and development in their placement organisation. Using appropriate concepts from the tutorials students will identify their career dispositions following their placement experience;

Undertake a critical analysis of their placement organisation's structure and of group and team work, proposing ideas for improving team dynamics and performance. The students should demonstrate an improvement in the skills of managing leadership/follower dynamics, working with colleagues and under supervision, leading and initiating activities. Students should be able to identify learnings that can be transferred to their degree group projects upon return to their studies;

Undertake a critical analysis of their sources of motivation at work and the relationship with job satisfaction. Suggest how they would leverage employee motivations to improve job satisfaction if they were the top managers of the placement organisation;
Undertake a critical analysis of aspects of the HRM system (as explored in the tutorials) in their placement organisation; and

Drawing from the competencies of year one and two of their degree, further develop their business acumen within the industrial setting of their placement employer, applying theory in practice and reflecting practice in theory.

modules.moduleContent:
To give engineering students an experience of a large-scale design problem, with all the associated administrative, communication and managerial aspects.

Each year the Department Lecturers provide a pool of suitable projects. Final Year Project takes place in the second semester. The list of projects, together with a one page description of each project, is made available to students prior to the start of the second semester. Students are encouraged to approach staff for further information on individual projects. 

Students must submit their preference list (with projects ranked in order of preference) within one week from the date that the list of projects is released. Projects are then allocated according to preference, taking into account staff workload and student position in class as determined by their 3rd year class average (excluding IWE).

Students can also propose projects of their own (e.g. connected with their Industrial Work Experience) subject to the approval of the Department Final Year Project committee.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate appropriate management techniques in the execution of their project (including time management and project planning).
Combine technical expertise from multiple technical areas to design and specify solutions for their projects.
Apply theoretical knowledge in solving problems they encounter.
Develop and improve personal skills to undertake new challenges posed by their project.
Demonstrate competent technical communication skills (including meeting, presentation and reporting).
Discuss any ethical issues, environmental impacts and health and safety issues associated with their project.

modules.moduleContent:
GC296A&B are modules designed for students new to ancient Greek, who want to explore the language of Homer, Sophocles, and Plato in their original form. 'Introduction to Ancient Greek' offers a rigorous 'starter' on the Greek language and its profound impact on literature, philosophy, and history. Beginning with the alphabet and proceeding through the basics of Greek grammar, syntax, and vocabulary, students will be guided through a series of progressive readings that provide a thorough grounding in the language, while also introducing many aspects of ancient Greek culture.

NOTE: A minimum number of enrolments is required for these modules to run each semester.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain basic Greek grammar and syntax.
Recognise a broad range of Greek vocabulary.
Demonstrate the ability to read and translate simplified passages of ancient Greek.
Demonstrate the ability to compose short phrases of Greek.
Develop a deeper understanding of general language structures.
Develop broadly applicable skills of language analysis.

modules.moduleContent: Drawing from key theoretical contributors and the experience and insights of participants, this module will explore key thinking in gender theories that include discussions on intersectionalism, gender essentialism, imperialism and queer theory.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain key aspects of the production and performance of gender in cultural and social contexts.
Analyse the differences and similarities between geographies and generations of gender theorists.
Identify the key debates in gender theory and within feminism.
Engage critically with primary and secondary texts of gender theory.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will focus on recent German-language films. It will provide an introduction to reading film, taking into account its narrative, ideological, and contextual aspects. We will consider how these films reflect and feed into debates about contemporary culture and society in the German-speaking countries.


**This 5-ECTS credit module may be used as part of engagement with texts required for accreditation by the Teaching Council of Ireland. All applications submitted for Teaching Council accreditation are, however, subject to the Teaching Council’s internal evaluation processes.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain key aspects of historical, societal and cultural development in the Germanspeaking countries in the 20th and 21st century.
Analyse and interpret films through close reading and contextualisation.
Express in writing core content and ideas, including relevant terminology.
Read some secondary literature and evaluate its usefulness for understanding the prescribed films.
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and wellstructured argument.

modules.moduleContent:
This module will focus on contemporary film of the German-speaking countries. Following a brief introduction to reading film, particular emphasis will be placed on how contemporary film both reflects and feeds into debates about contemporary culture and society in the German-speaking countries. 

**This 5-ECTS credit module may be used as part of the engagement with texts required for accreditation by the Teaching Council of Ireland. All applications submitted for Teaching Council accreditation are, however, subject to the Teaching Council’s internal evaluation processes.

IMPORTANT NOTE
Since substantial parts of this module will be delivered in German and parts of the Continuous Assessment will also test German language skills, an advanced knowledge of German is required.
learningOutcomes: Identify and explain key aspects of historical, societal and cultural development in the Germanspeaking countries in the 20th and 21st century.
Summarise in broad terms developments in film from the 1990s to the presentday, and analyse closely a selection of films from this period.
Interpret films through close reading and contextualisation.
Interpret and explain in English and/or in German key terms introduced in the module, using examples where appropriate.
Express in writing core content and ideas, including relevant terminology, in English and/or German.
Evaluate critically secondary literature.
Research, develop and articulate in writing a balanced and wellstructured argument.

modules.moduleContent:
Twenty-first Century German-language Narratives of Migration and Belonging  

This module brings together film and literature to explore different aspects of migration, from the German experience of expulsion at the end of World War II, to the fate of “guest workers” and asylum seekers in unified Germany, and the contemporary refugee crisis.  

Course texts: 
Birgit Whye Madgermanes (2016)
Olga Grjasnowa, Gott ist nicht schüchtern (2017) 
Die Flucht (dir. Kai Wessel, 2007)
Wir sind jung, wir sind stark (dir. Burhan Qurbani, 2015) 

**This 5-ECTS credit module may be used as part of the engagement with texts required for accreditation by the Teaching Council of Ireland. All applications submitted for Teaching Council accreditation are, however, subject to the Teaching Council’s internal evaluation processes.

IMPORTANT NOTE
Since substantial parts of this module will be delivered in German and parts of the Continuous Assessment will also test German language skills, an advanced knowledge of German is required.
learningOutcomes: Summarise in broad terms the effects of migration on individual and national identity and belonging, using German case studies;
Analyse cultural representations of migration through close reading and contextualisation;
Plan, research, and deliver an oral presentation (online or in person), using appropriate visual aids where necessary;
Evaluate critically secondary literature.

modules.moduleContent: Year Abroad.

Drawing from key theoretical contributors, the experience and insights of participants, and examples from practice, this module explores the relationship between education and social movements with an emphasis on the concept of praxis.
learningOutcomes: Understand the concept of critical pedagogy and consciousnessraising education.
Understand the principles of groupwork in adult education contexts.
Appreciate the role of engaged critical research.

Explore a history of education within feminism, trade unionism, community education and 
other spaces of resistance.

modules.moduleContent:
This module allows a student to apply and enhance their geographic knowledge and skills in an external working environment (outside Maynooth University). The academic supervisor will visit the workplace whilst the placement is in progress. The placement should be identified by the student and discussed with, and approved by, the module coordinator before the work placement can commence. 

Registration notes
1.
Students who would like to register for GY399B need to contact the module coordinator to discuss this, and get approval for this, a few months in advance of the start of Semester 2. Students who are approved to register for this module will not initially be able to register when online registrations commence in early September, but their GY399B registration details will be sorted out by the Department during the first few weeks of Semester 2.

2.
Students cannot take GY399B and GY399A; only one of these modules may be taken.
learningOutcomes: communicate with external public and/or private sector organisations
identify appropriate geographical solutions for realworld projects
project manage at least one geographical application
evaluate and communicate the results of the placement

modules.moduleName: VIVE LA RÉPUBLIQUE: FRENCH POLITICS AND SOCIETY IN THE 20TH & 21ST CENTURY
modules.moduleContent: Starting from the French concept of the Republic, this module will explore French political culture and society over the 20th Century up to the present day.
learningOutcomes: Recognise key aspects of social and political organisation in France, and their influence on politics and society;
Understand key elements of French political landscape (e.g. political groupings);
Understand and analyse contemporary trends and issues in France.

modules.moduleContent: This module builds on EU Law&Policy I – Constitutional and Institutional Law. Its focus is on the substantive law and policies of the EU. The module will commence by analysing the development of the EU internal market and its ‘four freedoms’, and will then concentrate on how the CJEU has developed the law on the free movement of goods, persons and services, as well as how it has developed the concept of EU citizenship. The module will then look briefly at two important fields of EU substantive law, competition law and state aid law, the rules of which help to protect the integrity of the single market.
learningOutcomes: Outline and comment upon the development of the EU from a common market to a single market.
Explain and critically discuss the development of EU law with regard to the free movement of goods, services and workers, and with regard to EU citizenship.
Explain the aims of key fields of EU social and economic policy, and critically discuss the legislative relationship between the EU and the Member States in these areas.
Provide an overview and basic appreciation of EU competition law and EU state aid law.

modules.moduleContent:
This module generally examines equity as a legal system, and more specifically, trust formalities and administration, trustees’ fiduciary duties, the scope of liability faced by trustees and by third-parties who have contact with trustees and/or trust property. It examines, in detail, express trusts, resulting trusts, constructive trusts, void and voidable trusts, secret trusts, purpose trusts, and charitable trusts.
 
Any aspect of this module, may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an indepth knowledge of the fundamentals of trust law, the operation of trusts, the different types of trust, and the legal rules governing them.
Comprehend the principles relating to trust administration, the duties of trustees as well as the scope of trustee (and third party) liability.
Interpret a given factual matrix considering trust law principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.
Analyse, debate, and criticise particular areas of trust law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of equitable principles relating to trusts, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the position in equity.
Analyse, debate, and criticise the law in a particular area of trusts law and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.

modules.moduleContent:
This module continues with concepts developed in its first semester component. In this second semester component, this module provides an introduction to the operation of equitable remedies, for instance, injunctions, specific performance, estoppel, tracing, rectification, and rescission.¿ 
 
Any aspect of this module, may be changed in any given academic year, subject to the discretion of the module lecturer.
learningOutcomes: Display an indepth knowledge of equitable remedies, including the various types of injunction, specific performance, estoppel, tracing, rectification, and rescission.
