Interpret a given factual matrix considering equitable principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the legal position.
Analyse, debate, and criticise particular areas of equity and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.
Display an indepth knowledge of the law of equitable remedies and an ability to construct or dissect a legal argument as to the appropriateness of utilising such a remedy.
Comprehend the principles relating to the equitable remedies, explain their origin, operation in different circumstances, and distinguish an equitable from a legal remedy.
Interpret a given factual matrix in light of equitable principles, summarise the main legal issues involved and construct an opinion as to the position in equity.
Analyse, debate, and criticise the law in a particular area of equity and draw conclusions as to its effectiveness and fairness.

modules.moduleContent:
This module introduces students to key principles and practices of screen design. Students will learn how to not only create appropriately formatted visual content but also how to use such content effectively as part of multimodal digital communication. Students will learn visual, textual, and technical methods and tools in order to produce and critique content for the contemporary online environment.

Note: MD171B and MD171C are a disallowed combination and cannot be taken together as the module content is the same.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the principles of design in a screenbased environment.
Demonstrate a basic understanding of the appropriate computer programs and tools used for visual communication in static forms.
Plan and Design Visual projects.
Identify the appropriate tools and concepts for Screen design practice.

modules.moduleContent: The course examines the “local dimension of Ireland’s past”. The course aims to provide participants interested in local history with an opportunity to obtain formal training in the subject. This course sets out to provide students with a methodological approach to the research and writing of local history. This certificate level course is offered by the Department of History, Maynooth University.
learningOutcomes: Understand the concept and meaning of local history and recognise the central themes and challenges that historians face
Be aware of the distinction between primary and secondary sources and be conscious of the importance of context in interpretation of source materials
Identify repositories of interest for the completion of a local history research project
Compile a short bibliography of relevant secondary sources in order to conduct a literature review of the current academic discussion of local history
Design a research proposal that meets the approval of the course tutor containing a clear statement of the aims of a local history study
Locate relevant source materials for the completion of a research project
Evaluate different types of sources and determine their value for a research project
Use academic and stylistic conventions appropriately and consistently to complete the research project that meets the criteria of the discipline of history.

modules.moduleContent: This module will explore the economic, technological and social forces driving media convergence through digital practice. Students will critically examine and create examples of cyborgian hybridity in order to, understand the impact of digital technological convergence on the self, the other, society and its products.
learningOutcomes: Identify and discuss theoretical and practical issues related to the convergence of media technologies
Apply theoretical concepts to explain emerging media technologies and practices
Relate the creative possibilities of media forms to theoretical concerns
Communicate research in technically coded creative forms

modules.moduleContent: Work Placement.

modules.moduleName: DIFFERENTIAL CALCULUS FOR THE HEALTH SCIENCES
modules.moduleContent:
Objectives: To introduce students to Differential Calculus in One Real Variable.

Introduction to Functions. Brief review of exponentials, logarithms, trigonometry, and inequalities. Limits: Idea of a limit, continuity. Derivatives: Definition of a derivative, rates of change, tangent lines, product rule, chain rule, quotient rule, higher derivatives, derivatives of basic functions including trigonometric, exponential and logarithmic functions. Applications of derivatives: first and second derivatives and the shape of a graph, optimisation, curve sketching. Newton's method for roots.
learningOutcomes: Describe and manipulate functions.
Evaluate limits of basic functions.
Obtain derivatives of basic functions and combinations of such functions.
Solve maxmin problems.
Use Newton's method.
Sketch curves of the basic functions and combinations of such functions.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.

modules.moduleContent: Project work in computational physics, including a report.
learningOutcomes: Apply the methods and principles learned in MP468C to solve a substantial problem in physics or applied mathematics
Present their results in a well structured and presented and properly referenced written report.

modules.moduleContent: Functions with brief explanation of domain and range;  introduction to the trig functions and the exponential and log functions; derivatives of functions and what they mean in terms of rates of change and slopes of curves (no first principles). A heavy emphasis on curve sketching and interpreting information from curves.
learningOutcomes: Describe and manipulate functions.
Obtain derivatives of basic functions and combinations of such functions.
Understand the connection between derivatives and slopes of curves.
Sketch curves of the basic functions and combinations of such functions.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.

modules.moduleContent:
Objectives: To introduce students to Integral Calculus. 

Anti-derivatives, area, the definite integral, indefinite integrals, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the substitution rule, areas between curves, inverse functions, inverse trigonometric functions. Techniques of Integration. Tables of integrals, integration by parts, trigonometric integrals, Substitution, integration of rational functions, trapezoidal rule, Simpson's Rule. Applications of Integration: Volumes and surfaces of revolution, length of a curve.
learningOutcomes: Integrate the basic functions and combinations of these functions using standard techniques.
Use integrals to find areas and volumes.
Compute an integral numerically to a given degree of accuracy.
State the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To introduce students to coordinate geometry in n-dimensions, vector spaces, linear transformations, eigenvalues and applications of linear algebra.

Definition of n x n determinant via permutations, determinants via expansion along a row or along a column, determinants via Gaussian elimination; inverses of n x n matrix via row reduction, mention adjoint formula and Cramer's rule.

Coordinate geometry and vectors in n-dimensions; norm, distance, dot product; triangle and parallelogram laws; angle, triangle and Cauchy-Schwartz inequalities; cross and triple product, volume of a parallelopiped.

Vector spaces with a focus on real vector spaces; linear span, linear independence, basis and dimension; linear maps and matrices; matrix multiplication as composition of linear maps; rank and nullity; rotations, reflections and orthogonal projections in 3 dimensions; Eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix, diagonalization.

Applications, for example computer graphics, ranking in search engines, linear regression, Markov processes, Quantum systems. Computation using mathematical computing software.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of determinants in linear algebra.
Compute the determinant of a matrix.
Invert a matrix and solve matrix equations using inverses.
Find a basis and the dimension of a vector space.
State and prove results about vector spaces and subspaces.
Find a matrix of a linear map.
Use matrix multiplication to compose linear transformations.
Compute the rank and nullity of a matrix. Obtain the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of an n x n matrix. Have some appreciation for abstract algebra. State relevant definitions and theorems.

modules.moduleContent: Calculus of several variables, especially in two and three dimensions. Functions of two or more variables. Graphs. Limits. Continuity. Partial derivatives. Gradient. Tangents and normals. Max-min. Lagrange multipliers. Introduction to Sequences. Computation using mathematical software.
learningOutcomes: Draw the level curves for a function of two variables.
Obtain the partial derivatives and gradient vector of a function.
Obtain tangents and normals to surfaces
Solve maxmin problems in several variables
Discuss the convergence of sequences and series
Use the methods from the course to solve problems
State definitions and theorems covered in the course

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To complete the student’s grasp of the techniques and skills of one-variable calculus.  It proceeds largely in the same non-rigorous spirit as the Leaving Certificate course, with the emphasis on problem-solving techniques.

Some time will be spent as needed on prerequisites, including Differentiation, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the Mean Value theorem, Riemann Sums, the notion of continuity and continuous functions. Certain limits will also be emphasised, in particular those involving trigonometric functions. Some applications of integration: volume by parallel cross-sections, volume by the shell method. Elementary transcendental functions. Logs, exponentials, trigonometric functions and their inverses. Techniques of Integration: Parts, partial fractions, powers and products of sines and cosines. Numerical integration. Improper integrals.
learningOutcomes: State and use some of the classical theorems of calculus in one variable.
Integrate the basic functions and combinations of such functions using standard techniques.
Use integrals to find areas and volumes.
Evaluate integrals using numerical techniques.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quite varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 10 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.

Module Objective:
To introduce students to coordinate geometry in n-dimensions, vector spaces, linear transformations, eigenvalues and applications of linear algebra.

Definition of n x n determinant via permutations, determinants via expansion along a row or along a column, determinants via Gaussian elimination; inverses of n x n matrix via row reduction, mention adjoint formula and Cramer's rule.

Coordinate geometry and vectors in n-dimensions; norm, distance, dot product; triangle and parallelogram laws; angle, triangle and Cauchy-Schwartz inequalities; cross and triple product, volume of a parallelopiped.

Vector spaces with a focus on real vector spaces; linear span, linear independence, basis and dimension; linear maps and matrices; matrix multiplication as composition of linear maps; rank and nullity; rotations, reflections and orthogonal projections in 3 dimensions; Eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix, diagonalization.

Applications, for example computer graphics, ranking in search engines, linear regression, Markov processes, Quantum systems. Computation using mathematical software.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of determinants in linear algebra.
Compute the determinant of a matrix.
Invert a matrix and solve matrix equations using inverses.
Find a basis and the dimension of a vector space.
State and prove results about vector spaces and subspaces.
Find a matrix of a linear map.
Use matrix multiplication to compose linear transformations.

Compute the rank and nullity of a matrix.
Obtain the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of an n x n matrix.
Have some appreciation for abstract algebra.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module objective: To introduce the student to elementary Number Theory.

Induction. The division algorithm and the Euclidean algorithm in Z. The fundamental theorem of arithmetic. The integers modulo n. Fermat's Little Theorem, Euler phi function, Euler's generalisation of Fermat's Little Theorem. Wilson's Theorem. Solving Linear Diophantine equations. Solving systems of linear congruences using the Chinese Remainder Theorem.
Legendre symbols, quadratic residues, quadratic Law of Reciprocity. Primitive roots. Successive squaring mod n for fast multiplication.
learningOutcomes: Solve systems of linear congruences.
Prove and apply the classical theorems of elementary number theory.
Define the Legendre symbol and determine whether a number is a quadratic residue.
Find primitive roots.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State accurately any definitions given in the course.
State, prove and apply any theorems given in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To give a gentle introduction to groups.

Definitions and examples: the integers, the integers mod n, permutation groups, finite groups of symmetries (for example dihedral groups). Mappings: group homomorphisms and isomorphisms, kernel and image definitions, examples. Subgroups: examples, orders of elements and groups, Lagrange's Theorem. Conjugacy, normal subgroups, quotient groups.  Groups of matrices: examples (to include GL(n), O(n), and SO(n)), geometry of corresponding linear transformations, Euclidean isometries.
learningOutcomes: State the axioms defining a group and any definitions or theorems given in the course.
Decide whether given examples satisfy these definitions.
Prove simple results using definitions and theorems given in the course.
Perform calculations in a group theoretic setting.
Give examples which illustrate concepts from the course.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent: This module builds on previous knowledge and gives an in depth understanding of Rings and Fields, covering the following topics. Field Extensions. Fields as factor rings. Algebraic elements, minimal polynomials, existence of roots of polynomials. Existence and uniqueness of splitting fields. Finite Fields. Fields of rational functions.  Theory of a single linear transformation.  Jordan normal form. Cayley-Hamilton theorem.
learningOutcomes: Determine the splitting field of a polynomial.
Do calculations inside a finite field.
State and prove the classical “impossibility proofs”.
Compute the Jordan Normal form of a linear transformation.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.
Prove simple algebraic results using rigorous arguments.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To give an introduction to Euclidean & Non-Euclidean Geometry.

Euclid's postulates. Euclidean, Spherical, and Hyperbolic Geometry, with special emphasis on two dimensions.  Models of the hyperbolic plane and geodesics.
In addition, selected topics from Differential or Algebraic Geometry may also be presented.
learningOutcomes: State and prove a variety of results in Euclidean geometry.
Convert certain problems in Euclidean geometry to algebraic form and solve them.
Give examples of results that are true in all geometries.
Give examples of statements that hold in Euclidean geometry, but not in NonEuclidean geometry, and viceversa.
Demonstrate an understanding of NonEuclidean geometry.
State and prove a variety of results in NonEuclidean geometry.
Apply and manipulate geometric transformations.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.  State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To introduce students to Graph Theory.

Isomorphism, cycles, circuits, Euler circuits and Hamilton cycles, planar graphs including Kuratowski's theorem, colourings including the 5 colour theorem, trees.
learningOutcomes: Define the standard objects of graph theory.
Understand and apply some of the classical theorems of graph theory.
Solve problems on any other topic covered in the course (these topics may change from year to year).
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Overview: to introduce students to point-set topology.

Topological spaces. Continuity of functions between topological spaces. Limit points, closure, interior and boundary of subsets. The subspace, product and quotient topologies. Separation axioms. Compactness. Connectedness and path-connectedness. An informal introduction to homotopy.
learningOutcomes: Define the topological concepts introduced in the module.
Prove that various spaces have topological properties defined in the module.
Deduce simple results using these definitions.
Deduce simple topological results from definitions.
State, prove and apply and theorems presented in the module.

modules.moduleContent: A rigorous introduction to Group Theory. Group Axioms and examples of groups. Direct products. Cyclic groups. The symmetric groups. Subgroups and Lagrange's Theorem. Conjugacy and the class equation. Normal subgroups, quotient groups. Homomorphisms, isomorphisms, the First Isomorphism theorem, the correspondence theorem. Cayley's Theorem.  The classification of finitely generated Abelian groups.
learningOutcomes: State the axioms defining a group and any definitions or theorems given in the course.
Decide whether given examples satisfy these definitions.
Prove results using definitions and theorems given in the course.
Perform calculations in a group theoretic setting.
Give examples which illustrate concepts from the course.
State, prove and apply some of the classical theorems of elementary Group Theory.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To introduce students to PDE’s and Modelling.

First order constant coefficient PDEs, Cauchy data. More general first order linear PDEs, characteristic curves. Classification of second order PDEs as elliptic, parabolic, or hyperbolic. The one-dimensional wave equation, d'Alembert's solution. Fourier series. The one-dimensional heat equation, series solutions. Time permitting: brief treatment of the two-dimensional Laplace equation.
learningOutcomes: Find Fourier series and understand their use.
Solve first order linear and quasilinear PDEs
Classify second order PDEs.
Use series methods to solve the classical PDEs.
Use methods from the course to solve problems.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To introduce the students to programming.

This course introduces students to the fundamentals of programming for mathematical applications. The topics covered are: basic data types, variables and constants, arithmetic operators. Decision making and looping. Functions and recursion. Arrays and pointers. Searching and sorting. Applications will be chosen from areas such as matrix calculations, operations research and statistics.
learningOutcomes: Construct loops in a computer programme
Use arrays and pointers efficiently.
Create simple programmes to sort and search.
Write a simple computer programme to solve mathematical problems.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:

To demonstrate how Mathematics can be used in Biology.

Single species models, continuous and discrete models, exponential, logistic and difference equation models. Steady states and stability, introduction to qualitative methods. Models for interacting species, Lotka-Volterra equations, qualitative behaviour of solutions of linear systems, predator-prey systems, harvesting. Structured population models, epidemic models. Deterministic modelling (ordinary differential equations and difference equations).
learningOutcomes: Model a variety of biological phenomena.
Use these models to predict future behaviour.
Decide whether the model is appropriate for the given system.
Solve various ordinary differential equations.
Apply basic qualitative techniques to continuoustime and discretetime models arising in Biology.
Use methods from the course to solve problems.

modules.moduleContent:
Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quire varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 15 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.
This module covers several fundamental topics in Real Analysis: Series, Differentiation in a single variable, Uniform convergence, Power series, Taylor series, Fourier Series.
learningOutcomes: Prove and apply the standard convergence tests for series.
Understand some of the properties and consequences of differentiability and continuity.
Obtain Taylor and Fourier series and understand their significance.
Prove simple analytic statements using rigorous arguments.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To give a rigorous introduction to Rings and Fields.

Modular Arithmetic, Euclidean algorithm and Euclid's Lemma.
Concrete Rings: Polynomial rings and Matrix rings. The abstract definition of a ring. Elementary concepts: subrings, ideals, quotient rings. Divisibility in rings. Prime and irreducible elements.
Concrete Fields: the rationals, reals and complex numbers. The integers mod p, p a prime. The abstract definition of a field.  
Euclidean Domains, Principal Ideal Domains, Unique Factorization Domains.  Gaussian Integers and Applications. Gauss’ Lemma, Irreducibility of Polynomials.
learningOutcomes: State the axioms defining a ring and a field.
Deduce simple properties from these axioms.
Provide examples of rings and fields, including noncommutative rings.
Determine using the axioms whether a given object is a Principal Ideal Domain or a Unique Factorization Domain.
State, prove and apply any definitions and theorems covered in the course.
Determine whether a given polynomial is irreducible.
Determine ring theoretic properties of concrete commutative rings.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.

modules.moduleContent: This module is primarily directed towards the realisation of an original electroacoustic music composition, where technical accomplishment, and an understanding of current best practice are emphasized. Technical concerns are cultivated via the generation of a series of studies. Participants present work in the public arena, and develop a composition of appropriate scale and ambition.

This module is based on the development of a final project in one of the three areas: 
o music composition; the completion of a portfolio of compositions
o audio production; the completion of an original album 
o music application development. 
The student will choose one of these areas in consultation with the module convenor and will be assigned a supervisor who will guide him/her through the development of the project via one-to-one consultation. In semester 1, lectures provide an introduction to the module. In semester 2, students attend a combination of seminars and individual tutorials. A number of seminars are provided by audio professionals, composers and technologists, in order to enhance career development, and to provide additional insight into working with sound technology as a career; seminars are also intended as a platform for students to report on work-in-progress.
learningOutcomes: plan a significant piece of independent work.
develop ideas towards a project over the medium term.
create a composition/production/music application.
formulate work in response to professional practice.

modules.moduleContent: This component aims to equip the student further with the rudiments of theory and harmony, and the foundations of musical language. Builds upon the content explored in semester 1, to develop the student’s music literacy.
learningOutcomes: Identify and construct cadences for four voices (SATB)
Analyse extracts of tonal music and identify and interpret key melodic and harmonic features
Analyse extracts of tonal music using figured roman numerals
Demonstrate an understanding of fundamental functional harmony (including triads of different qualities, the dominant seventh chord, and basic harmonic progressions)

modules.moduleContent: Introduction to anthropology for designers, methods for design ethnography, schools of ethnography within design, ways of interpreting ethnographic information. The course is about the dialogues between design and anthropology which arise within the practice of ethnography. Syllabus includes: Anthropological research methods of ethnography and participant observation and its intersection with design practice; Ways of thinking about technology and innovation, material culture and the senses in exploring everyday lived worlds; The pragmatic role of anthropological methods in envisaging as-yet non-existent 'future' artefacts, in product design.

In this module, students will showcase their preparedness for professional practice by completing an original design project. The project will encompass all aspects of design, technical skills, administration, communication, and management knowledge acquired over the past three years, progressing from a design brief to product launch. Students will participate in comprehensive project-based learning, involving concept development, market analysis and testing, and the creation of manufacture-ready drawings and prototypes. A studio-based approach is encouraged to cultivate a collaborative environment similar to professional design practices. The project will conclude with a detailed design report and a showcase at the graduate show.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate appropriate management skills, both project and personal, in the execution of the project including time management and project planning in order to complete the project by a given deadline.

Demonstrate the practical skills and design decisionmaking required of a practicing industrial designer through the generation and documentation of concept drawings, CAD engineering and rendering, digital media, prototyping, presentation and exhibition of work.

Undertake and present market and precedent product research in order to position your design in a realworld business context by engaging industry partners and investors in assessing the commercial viability of the project.
Generate innovative and/or novel solutions to complex problems utilising design thinking skills and by selecting an appropriate design process and methods for the problem identified.
Identify and critically evaluate the need for additional design research.
Communicate clearly to a third party product function, features, scale and proportion through the production of a finished prototype.

modules.moduleContent: “Active citizenship” as an element of the politics course is a practical as well as a theoretical issue. This module offers students a key experiential learning opportunity to learn and practice the skills of active citizenship in a real-life environment, as well as to develop research skills ethnography, participant observation, qualitative field work as well as case study methods. In this module, students reflect on their theoretical tool kit,  learn new research skills and engage in an experiential learning group exercise which enables them to reflect through regular workshops and maintenance of a learning journal.
learningOutcomes: Detail and evaluate the skills of active citizenship through practical learning in a political context.
Critically assess the contribution of social scientific analyses to an understanding of the actual practice of active citizenship and participation.
Demonstrate use of research skills including ethnography, participant observation, qualitative field work as well as case study methods and report writing.
Develop an independent and critical perspective on the practice of politics and active citizenship as a function of participation in a real life political context.

modules.moduleContent:
This elective module is for students of all disciplines. There are no prerequisites for this module, and we invite the participation of students who wish to explore their creativity and entrepreneurial spirit by engaging in human-centred approaches to problem solving.

The role of future entrepreneurs is rapidly changing. Design, innovation, and entrepreneurship are now key drivers in creating a more socially and environmentally sustainable world. This module builds upon human-centred innovation and design futures practices to provide students with the core tools and techniques to engage in ethical creative entrepreneurship. Through a mixture of lectures, critical thinking activities and practical workshops, students will understand the role of the creative entrepreneur within the Irish economy, be aware of emerging mega-trends within the world of work and understand their impact on society, and develop a business idea (including business pitch and business plan) for a socially responsible product or service.

This module aligns with the submission criteria for national and international entrepreneurship competitions and teaches students the core principles necessary to apply to these competitions, giving students key opportunities to network or win financial support for their ideas.
learningOutcomes: Prepare an entrepreneur competition entry with business plan.
Articulate a business idea in a 2minute video.
To identify business opportunities in the market and apply entrepreneurial solutions.
Work collaboratively to solve creative and business problems.

modules.moduleName: SKILLS FOR LEARNING IN HIGHER EDUCATION
modules.moduleContent: Skills for learning in higher education module is interested in equipping learners with the skills and confidence for successful learning as an adult in higher education. The module will put into context learning from other Return to Learning modules so learners can make informed decisions about their abilities and interests in following a degree programme at higher education level. Exploiting and honing creativity and motivation to increase satisfaction with study and to decrease anxiety using reflective learning will be central to the module.
learningOutcomes: Reflect on their educational journey to date
Manage timetables effectively, research and write to the university's required standards
Understand the requirements expected to succeed in higher education
Demonstrate the skills needed to complete the university's academic assignments in various formats
Set goals and meet deadlines in accordance the demands of the course
Demonstrate library skills for effective research

modules.moduleContent:
In this module, Sociology will be used to encourage students to think critically about how they as individuals, fit into the ‘bigger picture’ of society.  By developing what sociologists call ‘the sociological imagination’ students will begin to recognise that their personal biographies (life-stories) are the result of historical and social forces which can either enhance or limit their life chances. 

In this module students will learn that many of the problems that people are faced with have social roots and are shared by many others. These roots, are often embedded in the structure and culture of society itself and as such, they cannot be solved by the individual alone. However, on the other hand, by learning not to take the world at face value and by critically analysing why and how society is as it is, students may begin to acquire the tools that are necessary to transform both themselves and the world in which they live.
learningOutcomes: Be familiar with the idea of the sociological imagination
Consider current political and economic questions from a sociological perspective
Think critically about social issues
Write a social science essay using appropriate referencing and bibliographic conventions

modules.moduleName: KILLING FOR THE NATION: THE POLITICS OF ETHNIC CONFLICT
modules.moduleContent: This course introduces students to the causes, nature and consequences of ethnic conflict in Europe and the wider world. It aims to familiarize students with the theoretical debates surrounding ethnicity and nationalism in the contemporary world and moves from the general to the particular by introducing a series of case studies which emphasise the importance played by ethnicity in divided societies. Students will thus get the opportunity to compare and test the applicability of different theories of ethnic conflict against ‘real world’ cases where division has produced inter-ethnic contestation, confrontation and violence. Key case studies to be considered may include: Bosnia , Kosovo , Rwanda , Sri Lanka , and the Basque Country.
learningOutcomes: Detail and evaluate the major theories and key concepts used to analyse conflicts around ethnicity and nationalism in the contemporary world
Critically assess social science research and argument on issues pertaining to conflicts around ethnicity and nationalism in the contemporary world

Evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by researchers and compare and test the applicability of different theories of ethnic conflict against ‘real world’ cases where division has produced interethnic contestation, confrontation and violence
Develop an independent and critical perspective on social scientific analyses of ethnic conflict

modules.moduleContent: This module reviews, analyses and evaluates theoretical perspectives and core concepts that have been central to the development of Sociology. The module considers how these perspectives continue to be of significant relevance to analysing contemporary societies and students are given the opportunity to apply and critique the theories. Typically, these include the theories of Marx, Durkheim and Weber and work in those traditions; theories of race and racialisation and of gendering and patriarchal social relations; and a range of other perspectives that have shaped Sociology, including interactionist sociologies and the theories of Bourdieu.
learningOutcomes: Detail the origins and development of the major theoretical traditions within Sociology
Assess the contributions of social theorists to the discipline of Sociology
Explain the concepts advanced by social theorists
Appraise the relevance of social theory to social theory and the analysis of contemporary society

modules.moduleContent: This module reviews, analyses and evaluates the range of contemporary theoretical perspectives in Sociology. It builds on perspectives such those of Critical Theory, Foucault, Feminist theory, theories of race and racialisation and others to assess a range of contemporary theories. Students are provided with the concepts and critical skills to assess, apply, critique and develop theories of the social world.
learningOutcomes: Understand the origins and development of major contemporary theoretical traditions within Sociology
Assess the contributions of social theorists to the discipline of Sociology
Explain and apply the concepts advanced by social theorists

modules.moduleContent: Numerical and graphical data summaries. Probability: Sample spaces, events, conditional probability, independence, Bayes theorem. Discrete random variables, mean and variance. Binomial and Poisson. Continuous random variables. The Normal model. Sampling distribution of the mean. Confidence intervals and hypothesis testing: one and two samples, paired samples. Confidence intervals and hypothesis testing for proportions. Correlation and regression. Inference for slope. Computation using statistical software.
learningOutcomes: Describe various types of data using numerical or graphical techniques
Calculate probabilities and conditional probabilities and for unions and intersections of events
Work with discrete random variables, including Binomial and Poisson
Calculate probabilities based on the Normal model
Test statistical hypotheses
Calculate and interpret confidence intervals
Calculate and explain correlation
Calculate, interpret and perform inference using the regression line

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:
The objective is to develop statistical literacy for students in the Health Sciences.
Students will learn how to apply methods of statistical data analysis and apply critical thinking to the results, with case studies of health data applications.

Objectives and pitfalls of statistical studies: data collection, surveys, and study designs.
Exploratory data analysis for one and two variables: numerical and graphical summaries.
Probability: definitions, conditional probability, Bayes theorem.
Random variables: means and variances.
Distributions: Binomial and Normal models.
Introduction to statistical inference: confidence intervals and hypothesis tests for numerical samples.
Introduction to simple linear regression modelling: correlation, interpretation, and prediction.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish between continuous and categorical variable types, and calculate and interpret suitable summaries.
Identify and interpret suitable visualisations for different types of data.
Understand the strengths and limitations of different strategies researchers use to collect data and plan experiments.
Use the rules of probability to calculate probabilities and conditional probabilities.
Calculate means and variances and use the Binomial and Normal distributions.
Test statistical hypotheses and calculate and interpret confidence intervals for one, two, and paired numerical samples.
Relate two variables by calculating and interpreting correlations.
Apply and interpret simple linear regression and calculate predictions.

modules.moduleContent:
This course will give participants a practical grounding in the writing of fiction and creative non-fiction for publication. The course allows students to work with professional writers who will act in an advisory capacity. The practical emphasis will help students to progress towards producing work of publishable standard and marketing it.

The development of critiquing and editing skills are essential for creative writing. Student interaction and engagement with each other’s work guided by experienced writers and lecturers is aimed to provide a supportive environment for the development of these skills.The course is open both to students who are beginning their creative writing journey and students with prior experience of creative writing in some capacity.

• Understand the techniques of writing creative non-fiction and have cultivated a disciplined approach to writing. 
• Critically assess your own and others’ work. 
• Research a topic to form the basis of a short piece of writing 
• Differentiate between the structural demands of writing for non-fiction, short story and the novel. 
• Write a short story or stories demonstrating competence in at least one of the following areas: descriptive prose, plot, characterisation, dialogue 
• Prepare a synopsis and submission letter for a piece of work for the purpose of publication 
• Write a novel excerpt of approximately 4,000 words showing understanding of premise, time and place and character styles. 
• display a basic understanding of the economics and structure of the publishing industry.
learningOutcomes: Understand the techniques of writing creating nonfiction and have cultivated a disciplined approach to writing
Critically assess their own and others’ work
Research a topic to form the basis of a short piece of writing
Differentiate between the structural demands of writing for nonfiction, short story and the novel
Write a short story or stories demonstrating competence in at least one of the following areas: descriptive prose, plot, characterisation, dialogue
Prepare a synopsis and submission letter for a piece of work for the purpose of publication
Write a long fiction/non fiction excerpt of approximately 4,000 words showing understanding of premise, time and place, character as well as plot and structure of novel length works
Display a basic understanding of the economics and structure of the publishing industry.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  

The objective of this course is to introduce students to the concepts of experimental and computational mathematics. The investigation of these ideas will be facilitated by means of simple computer programming.

Minimal introduction to computer programming. Applications to various problems in experimental mathematics, and in either computational mathematics or symbolic algebra.
learningOutcomes: Understand some of the basics of computer programming.
Apply programming methods to investigate mathematics experimentally.
Apply programming methods to implement some basic problems of a computational or symbolic nature.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective: To introduce students to Cryptography.

Basic concepts: enciphering, deciphering, plaintext, ciphertext, primes and primality testing.
Simple cryptosystems: substitution cipher, Vigenere cipher, Caesar cipher, Hill cipher.
Public key cryptograpy: RSA and possible attacks on RSA. Discrete Logarithm as a one-way function. Diffie-Hellman key exchange.
Primality testing, Carmichael numbers. Factoring, Fermat’s method, other methods. 

Some of the following topics may also be included:
Stream ciphers, one-time pad. Randomness and pseudo-randomness of a bit sequence. Shift registers and linear recursions. Attacks on shift register sequences for one-time pad, Berlekamp-Massey algorithm, one-way functions. 
Coin toss over a telephone.  Digital signatures.  Authentication.  The Data Encryption Standard (DES), Advanced Encryption Standard (AES). Perfect Secrecy. Unicity Distance. Certificate authorities and trust.
learningOutcomes: Encode and decode using simple cryptosystems.
Encode and decode public key cryptosystems.
Understand the strengths and weaknesses of a cryptosystem.
Test for primality.
Apply algorithms to factorise numbers and calculate discrete logarithms.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To give a rigorous introduction to metric space topology.

Metric spaces: definition and examples. Convergence and continuity in metric spaces, uniform continuity, pointwise and uniform convergence.
Open and closed sets, basic properties. Continuity in terms of open sets.
Limit points, closure, interior, boundary. Completeness. Compactness.
learningOutcomes: State the axioms which define a metric space.
Deduce simple properties from these axioms.
Define the terms convergence, continuity, uniform continuity, uniform convergence, open and closed sets, limit point, closure, interior, boundary, completeness, compactness.
Use rigorous arguments to prove that these properties hold in a variety of spaces.
State and prove theorems concerning the relationship between these concepts.
Deduce simple results using these properties.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent: Overview: smooth functions and vector fields, curves in 3-space, Frenet-Serret formulas, surfaces, implicit and inverse function theorems, the shape operator, Gaussian and mean curvatures, minimal surfaces, geodesics, discussion of Gauss' Theorem Egregium
learningOutcomes: Define a surface.
Understand the shape operator.
Define the different types of curvatures and understand their properties and uses.
Define minimal surfaces and geodesics.
Deduce simple properties from any definitions or theorems given in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module objective:
To introduce students to fractals and dynamical systems.
 
Examples of fractals. Iterated Function Systems (IFS). Minkowski and Hausdorff dimension. Conjugacy. The dynamics of linear and quadratic polynomials. Symbolic dynamics. Chaos. Sharkovsky's theorem. The Schwarzian derivative and bifurcation theory, if time allows.
learningOutcomes: Show how to construct a given fractal using IFS.
Compute the dimension of a fractal.
Find and classify the fixed/periodic points, etc., of a given dynamical system.
Describe the dynamics of a given dynamical system.

modules.moduleContent:
Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quire varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 35 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.

This module introduces to Phenomenology as a method and to Continental Philosophy as a set of traditions originating with the phenomenological movement. Texts by Husserl, Reinach, Stein, Heidegger, De Beauvoir, Conrad-Martius, and Levinas will be studied. Emphasis will be placed on Phenomenology as a philosophical and academic practice with potential importance for all or most subjects. Tracing the history of the various traditions issuing from the criticism of the practice of phenomenology will enable us to reflect on the various varieties or definitions of phenomenology and their relationship with each other. Timothy Mooney and Dermot Moran (eds.): The Phenomenology Reader (London and New York: Routledge, 2002) will be used as textbook.
learningOutcomes: Interpret and compare the texts read.
Account for the themes touched upon: intentionality, object and content, essences, empathy and intersubjectivity, the body, the gendered perspective, being and the other.
Account for and discuss a number of different versions of Phenomenology, e.g., ‘eidetic’, ‘early’, ‘realist’, ‘transcendental’, ‘late’, ‘hermeneutic’, ‘genetic’ and ‘dialogic’ phenomenology.
Discuss the development and nature of Phenomenology.
Apply the phenomenological method; conduct basic constitutional and eidetic analyses.
Discuss the relevance of Phenomenology as a method, science, and style of thought to philosophy and assess the potential for the application of Phenomenology in other academic disciplines.
Critically discuss the claim that Phenomenology is First Philosophy.

modules.moduleContent: The Special Topic reading and discussion groups provide a forum within which research projects are conceived and executed under the guidance of the group director. Sign up for workshop times will be done through the SO303A module Moodle site, on a first-come, first-served basis. Details on group meeting times are available on the SO303A Moodle site at the beginning of term.  A number of compulsory shared lectures provide an overview of the research process.
learningOutcomes: Understand the theory and concepts associated with social research methodologies
Critically assess the merits and limits of various social research methodologies
Appraise research literature in a specified field of study, adjudicate its relevance and contribution to a chosen topic and apply it to a study of that topic

Demonstrate mastery of research and analytical skills and the capacity to develop an independent and original contribution to social research through the formulation and execution of a specific research project

modules.moduleContent: The analysis of the changing relationship between technology and culture, including perspectives on the cultural shaping of technology, how new technologies are shaping popular culture, the uses of technology in everyday life and the relationship between technological, human and social characteristics and identities.
learningOutcomes: Detail the major theories and key concepts used by sociologists to analyse the relationship between technology and culture.
Appraise sociological evidence and argument exploring the changing relationship between technology and culture.

Evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by sociologists exploring issues such as: the cultural shaping of technology, how new technologies are shaping popular culture, the uses of technology in everyday life and the relationship between technological, human and social characteristics and identities.
Develop an independent and critical perspective on the sociological analyses of the relationship between technology and culture.

modules.moduleName: SPANISH FOR PROFESSIONAL AND BUSINESS PURPOSES
modules.moduleContent:
This is an optional intermediate module in Spanish based on increasing students' familiarity with the use of Spanish in a professional environment. Professional Associations/Accreditation information Achieve linguistic competence commensurate with CEFRL level B 1. A student achieving an overall mark of 60% or higher in this module will have demonstrated linguistic competence commensurate with EFR level B 1.

Note 1: Students cannot take SPA247X if they have already completed SPA247 in the first semester.
learningOutcomes: Read and extract information from businessrelated texts, charts and graphs, and summarise that information in oral and written form.
Describe working environments, relationships with colleagues, employee and company profiles, business graphics, and business performance using appropriate vocabulary and structures.

Communicate effectively with a variety of interlocutors, e.g. colleagues, customers, and executives, and demonstrate appropriate language skills for negotiation and mediation with these interlocutors in written and oral form.
Produce some businessrelated texts, such as formal and informal emails and written reports.
Justify a business idea or product in both written and oral form.
Work effectively as part of a team to deliver a presentation in a coherent and meaningful manner.
Demonstrate intercultural awareness and sociolinguistic competencies, as they relate to the business world.
Apply appropriate vocabulary and grammar rules to complete quizzes and oral and written assignments, using relevant ITC, when necessary.

modules.moduleContent:
The NUI Certificate in Training and Continuing Education has been developed in order to:
- Equip individuals with the essential competencies necessary to meet the training challenges in today's changing world. 
- Provide formal qualifications for those involved in training or who would like to move into this area. 
- Encourage those in training roles to update themselves in new developments in training methodology and delivery systems. 

This course is constantly revised and updated to reflect current trends in training theory and practice.There are no formal academic entry requirements. However, participants must be 21 years of age or over. It is desirable, though not essential, that you currently have a training role in some capacity.
learningOutcomes:
Apply systematic training methods and good learning and training principles to the design and delivery of training programmes.
To describe the roles and responsibilities of a trainer, differentiate between a trainer and a facilitator.
Be familiar with the primary models of training which will assist the student in designing and delivering training.
Understand job analysis and job design.
Understand the concept of training needs and analysis.

In a given training situation, choose appropriate training styles and methods.
Describe the main theories of learning and the adult learning cycle.
Describe the main theories of motivation and the relationship between learning and motivation.
Write training objectives.
Design a training syllabus and training plan.

Use communication and feedback methods in a way that maximizes learning.
Decide on structure, content and sequence of the training.
Write a session plan.
Plan and deliver a presentation using visual aids.
Plan and deliver a demonstration.
Understand the important of verbal and non verbal communication.
Manage difficult participants and encourage participation.

Identify training and learning needs in individuals and groups.
Design effective assessment for training.
Develop an evaluation strategy for your training programme.
Give and receive effective feedback.
Be familiar with elearning its main benefits and drawbacks.
Design and implement training talks and demonstrations.

modules.moduleContent:
Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quite varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 10 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.

Module Objective:  
To introduce students to the History of Mathematics.

The Origins of mathematics and Numerical Systems.  Ancient Egypt and Babylonia.  The birth of modern mathematics in Greece, through the classical period and the Alexandrian period of Ancient Greece.  Arabic mathematics and connections with Indian Mathematics.  The renewal of the mathematical spirit in Europe and the Renaissance. Calculus and Number Theory, the marriage of Algebra and Geometry in the 17th Century. 18th century mathematics in Europe, dealing with infinite processes and the rigourisation of Calculus in the 19th century.
learningOutcomes: Describe and analyse major mathematical theories.
Understand and appreciate the fundamental role that the history of mathematics has played in civilisations.
Explain the origins and development of much of the mathematics they have encountered prior to this course.
Write on recurring themes that arise throughout the history of mathematics, e.g. how we deal with infinite processes.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To give a basic introduction to Real Analysis.

Basically the chapters 2-4  of the text – R. G. Bartle and D. R. Sherbert. Introduction to Real Analysis. Wiley. Functions, partial order, orders. Set theory, countability. Properties of the real numbers. Sequences. Limits and Continuity.
learningOutcomes: Prove simple results from the course.
State the properties of the real numbers.
Use rigorous arguments to prove that a sequence converges to a limit.
Understand the concept of continuity and be able to test for the continuity of a function of a single real variable.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems.
State definitions and theorems covered in the course and explain the proofs of these theorems.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To introduce students to Galois Theory.

The impossibility proofs: squaring the circle, duplicating the cube, trisecting an angle.
Automorphisms of fields, Frobenius map. Finite, algebraic, separable and normal extensions. The fundamental theorem of Galois theory.  Applications, including solvability by radicals.

This module may be taught as a reading course.
learningOutcomes:
Prove any of the other theorems given in the course. 
Deduce properties which follow from these theorems.
Compute Galois groups of polynomials and field extensions.
State, prove and apply the Fundamental Theorem of Galois Theory.
Analyse the solubility of polynomials by radicals.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To instruct the students in practical numerical techniques, together with an introduction to the methods used to analyse the effectiveness of numerical methods.

Preliminaries: computer arithmetic, Taylor series, and iteration. Solution of nonlinear equations in one variable. Direct methods for solving linear systems. Interpolation and extrapolation. Curve fitting. Numerical differentiation and optimization. Numerical integration. Iterative methods for linear and nonlinear systems. Solutions of differential equations.
learningOutcomes: Use numerical techniques to solve different mathematical problems.
Use iterative methods to solve linear and nonlinear systems.
Solve differential equations using numerical techniques.
Write a computer program to implement existing algorithms.

modules.moduleContent:
Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quite varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 25 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.

Tabharfar deis do mhic léinn, a bhfuil caighdeán maith Gaeilge acu, móraiste a scríobh ar ábhar a bhfuil spéis acu ann. Faoi stiúir duine de léachtóirí buana na Roinne a dhéanfar an modúl seo, agus iarrfar ar mhic léinn aiste 4500-5000 a sholáthar faoi dheireadh an tseimeastair. I gcás saothar cruthaitheach, aontófar líon focal leis an stiúrthóir ach, mar shampla, beifear ag súil le 8 ndán agus nótaí cúlra le haghaidh saothar filíochta agus idir 2,500 agus 3,000 le haghaidh gearrscéil.
learningOutcomes: I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas taighde ginearálta a dhéanamh ar ábhar.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas anailís chuimsitheach a dhéanamh ar an ábhar atá roghnaithe acu.

I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas torthaí a chuid taighde a chur le chéile agus aiste chuimsitheach, 45005000 focal, a scríobh. I gcás saothar cruthaitheach, aontófar líon focal leis an stiúrthóir ach, mar shampla, beifear ag súil le 8 ndán agus nótaí cúlra le haghaidh saothar filíochta agus idir 2,500 agus 3,000 le haghaidh gearrscéil.
I ndiaidh don mhac léinn an modúl seo a chríochnú bheifí ag súil go mbeadh ar a chumas córas tagartha a úsáid i gceart agus leabharliosta cuimsitheach a chur le chéile.

modules.moduleContent: SEMESTER ABROAD

In this module product design students must compile a proposal for an original design project resulting in their own brief and statement of design intent. The proposal will be broken into 3 key areas, 1. Business Case 2. Feasibility Report 3. User Investigation, identifying and validating project relevance supported by primary and secondary research. This module will demand skills in design, user research, business and marketing which students have gained from the previous 3 years of the degree.  Through project based learning the students will ascertain academic rigour, project management and planning competence by taking an initial idea, validating its relevance, and preparing a comprehensive plan and design proposal.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate appropriate management skills, both project and personal, in the execution of the project including time management and project planning in order to complete the business plan by a given deadline.
Develop a comprehensive design brief from which novel, innovative and realisable viable concepts can be generated.
Identify and validate a new product opportunity.
Identify, critically evaluate and select appropriate design research methodologies.
Undertake appropriate research and analyse this research and communicate the results effectively into the project.

Demonstrate competent communication skills and communicate detailed ideas clearly in activities including the project report, meetings, oral presentations, presentation boards, prototype and to a third party at the graduate show.

modules.moduleContent: This module teaches students sustainable and environmental design approaches. Students will learn how to evaluate a products environmental impact, identify the key sustainability drivers and design for sustainable competitive advantage. Topics covered are; products and their environmental impacts, sustainable design innovation strategies for practice, simplified life cycle analysis, material impacts, design for end of life, social and environmental legislative considerations.
learningOutcomes: Apply qualitative and quantitative methods to evaluate products environmental impact.
Identify and critically evaluate the different material options and their impacts.
Identify and critically evaluate the social and legislative drivers.
Explain the role of the different established sustainable models in the design and idea creation process; The Natural Step, Triple Bottom Line & Cradle to Cradle (Waste=Food).
Design for competitive advantage through sustainable design.

modules.moduleContent: This module provides students with professional skills and experience in a commercial/industry based organisation.
learningOutcomes: Work productively in a technical role in an organisation and understand the importance of the multidisciplinary relationships and interfaces.
Perform as a team member with typical industrial deadline and reporting relationships to solve practical design problems in real life circumstances.
Apply learnt theory and principles within a practical project and understand issues surrounding plan adherence and performance in a practical situation.
Understand the ethical framework within which the organisation operates and apply general ethical principles to identified operational circumstances encountered in the IWE placement.
Prepare a professional CV and undergo an interview for a professional post.

modules.moduleContent: This module will engage with some of the philosophical issues that come to the fore with the emergence and proliferation of late-modern technology - or what can be called 'the digital age'. Perhaps the most prescient of these - the philosophy of artificial intelligence (AI) - will be our point of departure. We will assess the philosophical concerns of AI and the associated paradigm of ‘human enhancement’ from an ethical perspective, an evolutionary perspective, and from the perspective of a philosophy of mind. This will be followed by an introduction to the philosophy of technology which will lay the groundwork for an investigation into the human-technology relation from both a ‘digital’ and a ‘pre-digital’ perspective. Thus, our concerns will extend across a number of different areas from the technological and scientific to the political and the cultural. Finally, we will take a detailed look at some of the technologies and paradigms that characterise the digital age – primarily transhumanism and posthumanism – and thus set ourselves up to ask the question: are we dealing with science fact or science fiction?
learningOutcomes: Identify and respond to major and uptodate issues and trends in contemporary philosophy relating to the ‘digital age’.
Explain the emergence of digital issues in recent philosophy, and characterize the central notions involved.
Contextualise issues of the digital age within the discipline of philosophy.
Describe and evaluate philosophical approaches to machine learning, AI, human enhancement, postbiological evolution and critically assess contributions in the literature.

Demonstrate an ability to apply philosophical approaches to digital technology and the idea associated with the emerging ‘postbiological’ paradigm through written communication and argumentation skills.

modules.moduleContent:
This module explores sound by manipulating its generation (synthesis) and transformation (processing) using the computer. It will explore the design of virtual instruments for composition and sound design using flexible music software tools. Students will learn to code and produce new synthetic textures and manipulate sounds from real and virtual
sources, gaining practical skills for music creation and production.
learningOutcomes: list and discuss the properties of major computer music languages.
program a computer to synthesise sound.
apply a music programming language to a composition task.
apply sound synthesis techniques to musicmaking activities.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to the main theoretical approaches to and contemporary issues in the study of International Relations (IR). Focusing on the inter-relationships between states, international organizations and non-state actors, the module begins by outlining the key theoretical frameworks which inform IR debates. These include Realism, Liberal Internationalism, Marxism and Social Constructivism. Building on these conceptual foundations the module then introduces the key debates within the world of contemporary international politics, including Francis Fukuyama’s The End of History, Samuel Huntington’s Clash of Civilisations, the relationship between Islam and the West and the US “war on terror”, and the nature and significance of global economic structures. The mixture of theoretical and empirical analysis will allow students to develop ideas about the international political system, what it is made up of, how states interact with each other and with wider structural forces. Particular attention will be paid to the intellectual history of the discipline and to contemporary IR theory.
learningOutcomes: Detail and evaluate the major theories and key concepts used by social scientists to analyse contemporary issues in the study of International Relations (IR) including Realism, Liberal Internationalism, Marxism and Social Constructivism.
Critically assess the contribution of social scientific analyses to an understanding of the interrelationships between states, international organizations and nonstate actors.

Evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by social scientists exploring issues such as: debates within the world of contemporary international politics, including Francis Fukuyama’s The End of History, Samuel Huntington’s Clash of Civilisations, the relationship between Islam and the West and the US “war on terror”, and the nature and significance of global economic structures.

Develop an independent and critical perspective on the social scientific analysis of the international political system, what it is made up of, how states interact with each other and with wider structural forces.

modules.moduleContent: This module guides students in the digital age as to how to conduct research, write and communicate to an academic standard using the tools at their disposal.  It will introduce students to the university's student communication platform Moodle and to Office 365.  Students will learn how to present in front of a live audience, reference and write reports.  They will also learn how to save data safely and securely. Students will practice various applications in a practical environment and submit work based on the content of these sessions.
learningOutcomes: Be able to use University logon/PCs, email and Moodle
Research, write, reference and submit assignments online
Design slideshows and present academic work
Gather, collate and correctly represent data using graphs/tables.

modules.moduleName: UNIVERSAL CRITICAL SKILLS (INTRODUCTION TO UNIVERSITY LEARNING AND BEYOND) - A
modules.moduleContent:
The development of the critical skills will be based on exercises designed around weekly readings of appropriate journal articles or similar, these to be decided.
• Finding information (from printed materials and texts, academic databases, digital/electronic sources).
• Recording information through use of a range of media (e.g., visual, audio).
• Presenting and using different representations (e.g., text, diagrams, tables, charts, images, posters).
• Planning and writing academic essays.
• Academic writing skills, styles and conventions.
• Understanding plagiarism.
• Self-assessment and reflection.
• Critical thinking (forming an argument, examining evidence, judgement and reasoning, drawing conclusions).
• Creative thinking (solving problems, generating ideas, discussing solutions and possible outcomes, approaching tasks from different perspectives/ viewpoints).
• Organisation and time management (making plans, allocating tasks/ roles, designing schedules).
• Memory and approaches to learning.
• Working in groups.
• Preparing for assessments.
• Making oral presentations and public speaking.
• Awareness of supports and initiatives for students at Maynooth University.
learningOutcomes: Develop academic research skills including the ability to find, analyse, interpret, and evaluate information and sources.
Develop confidence in public speaking through an awareness of the affordances of technology and rhetorical communication strategies.
Develop the ability to engage in professional writing through an understanding of genre conventions and the role of texts in professional workspaces.
Appreciate the value of a university education and acknowledge their responsibility for their own education taking advantage of the supports, resources, and opportunities available.

modules.moduleContent: This module introduces students to the study of environment, and society by sociologists. It introduces you to some classic theories of society, environment, and nature, and considers how each have been viewed at different points in human history, and through different theoretical traditions. We also consider some classical political economy views on the relationship between society and environment, and how processes fundamental to social development such as urbanisation and industrialisation have modified our environments. The course will also introduce you to some key concepts in modern environmental sociology, such as commons and common-pool resource systems, metabolism and metabolic rift, ecological resilience, and complexity theory.
learningOutcomes: Detail and evaluate theories and concepts used to understand human societies, their environments, and their interactions, and to trace their historical development.
Understand key periods of historical change in humanenvironment relations, from the late medieval, to agricultural and industrial revolutions, to the present day.
Critically assess the views of different social science disciplines on humanenvironment relations, including sociology, history, economics, anthropology, and geography.
Understand how social scientists gather and interpret evidence in different ways to understand humanenvironment interactions.
Develop and defend reasoned opinions in oral and written form.

modules.moduleContent: The sociological study of international similarities and differences in the social construction of race and ethnicity, and the theories that seek to explain these comparative racialisation processes.
learningOutcomes: Detail the major theories and key concepts used by sociologists in the study of race and ethnicity in comparative perspective.
Appraise the sociological evidence and argument examining international similarities and differences in the social construction of race and ethnicity.
Evaluate the empirical trends and patterns identified by sociologists exploring such issues as comparative racialisation processes
Develop an independent and critical perspective on the sociological analyses of race and ethnicity in comparative perspective.

modules.moduleContent: Summer School University of Duisburg; students must have prior permission from the Department of Sociology to register for this module.
learningOutcomes: Summer school module in University of Duisburg taken during the summer of second year.

modules.moduleContent:
The NUI Certificate in Training and Continuing Education has been developed in order to:
• Equip individuals with the essential competencies necessary to meet the training challenges in today's changing world.
• Provide formal qualifications for those involved in training or who would like to move into this area.
• Encourage those in training roles to update themselves in new developments in training methodology and delivery systems.

This course is constantly revised and updated to reflect current trends in training theory and practice.There are no formal academic entry requirements. However, participants must be 21 years of age or over. It is desirable, though not essential, that you currently have a training role in some capacity.
learningOutcomes:
Apply systematic training methods and good learning and training principles to the design and delivery of training programmes.
To describe the roles and responsibilities of a trainer, differentiate between a trainer and a facilitator.
Be familiar with the primary models of training which will assist the student in designing and delivering training.
Understand job analysis and job design.
Understand the concept of training needs and analysis.

In a given training situation, choose appropriate training styles and methods.
Describe the main theories of learning and the adult learning cycle.
Describe the main theories of motivation and the relationship between learning and motivation.
Write training objectives.
Design a training syllabus and training plan.

Use communication and feedback methods in a way that maximizes learning.
Decide on structure, content and sequence of the training.
Write a session plan.
Plan and deliver a presentation using visual aids.
Plan and deliver a demonstration.
Understand the important of verbal and non verbal communication.
Manage difficult participants and encourage participation.

Identify training and learning needs in individuals and groups.
Design effective assessment for training.
Develop an evaluation strategy for your training programme.
Give and receive effective feedback.
Be familiar with elearning its main benefits and drawbacks.
Design and implement training talks and demonstrations.
