modules.moduleContent:
Please note this Certificate programme does not run every academic year. Contact appliedsocialstudies@mu.ie with any queries.

The NUI Certificate in Youth Studies aims to develop critical awareness of the nature of youth and youth issues in contemporary society. It is designed to provide participants with an overview of the position of young people in contemporary society and the services provided for them through youth work services.
learningOutcomes: Awareness and knowledge of adolescent development and the needs of young people in relation to youth service provision.
An appreciation of the complexities, challenges and potential involved in the youth worker role.
Skills in communication, planning, research etc.
Explore their personal interest, attitudes and values in youth work and how that influences approaches to work with young people.
Analytical thinking, academic study skills and the ability to undertake research and write reflectively, analytically and coherently.

modules.moduleContent:
Please note this Certificate programme does not run every academic year.  Contact appliedsocialstudies@mu.ie with any queries.

The NUI Certificate in Youth Arts aims to developed skilled practitioners who can integrate youth arts with their developmental work with young people. It is designed to further enhance youth arts practice in youth work, increasing access to high-quality youth-centred arts practice that empowers young people and supports their development.
learningOutcomes: Understanding of the cultural and sociological background to, and the theories and principles underpinning, youth arts;
Awareness of the relationship between youth work and youth arts;
Ability to assess a variety of methods of implementing experiential artsbased learning in youth work settings;
Develop skills for working with young people through a range of selected art forms;
Competence in planning, delivering and evaluating a youth arts project.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To give a rigorous introduction to Complex Analysis.

Basic theory: differentiation and integration of a function of a complex variable.  The exponential and trigonometric functions.  Cauchy''s integral formula.  Cauchy's Theorem.  Taylor's Theorem and Taylor Series. Singularities and Laurent series.
learningOutcomes: Differentiate and integrate functions of a complex variable.
Define the exponential, logarithmic and trigonometric functions.
State and prove Cauchy's integral formula and theorem.
Evaluate contour integrals.
Define and identify the types of singularities.
Test for the analyticity of a given function.
Prove simple analytic statements using rigorous arguments.
Use the methods from the course to solve problems. State definitions and theorems covered in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quite varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 15 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.

Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quite varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 15 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.

Module Objective:  
To continue the student's education in Complex Analysis.

The Residue theorem and applications. Schwarz' Lemma. Arzela-Ascoli and Montel theorems. Riemann mapping theorem. Other topics as time allows to be chosen from Elliptic Functions. The Gamma Function. The Riemann zeta function and the prime number theorem.
learningOutcomes: State, prove and apply the Residue theorem.
State, prove and apply the some of the classical theorems of Complex Analysis.
Solve problems on other topics covered in the course (these may change from year to year).
Apply the Riemann Mapping Theorem to classify various domains.

modules.moduleContent: Module Objective: To further develop the student's understanding of group theory.  Group actions, orbit stabilizer theorem. Sylow’s theorem, p-groups. Semi-direct products. Soluble groups. Simplicity of A_n for n>4. One additional topic, which might be selected from: Nilpotent groups; Linear groups; Free groups, generators and relations; Jordan-Holder theorem.
learningOutcomes: State and prove Sylow's Theorem.
Compute various central series of a finite group.
State and prove the Orbit Stabilizer Theorem.
Understand concrete finite simple groups.
Understand the basics of free presentations.
State, prove & apply any theorems covered in the course.
Deduce simple properties which follow from any definitions or theorems given in the course.

modules.moduleContent:
Module Objective:  
To introduce students to classical analytic Number Theory.

Topics to include some of the following: The arithmetic functions and identities. Algebraic and transcendental numbers.  Continued fractions (including solving congruence equations by continued fractions, periodic continued fractions, Brounkner's Algorithm and Pell's equation).  Approximation of irrationals by rationals (Liouville's theorem and construction of transcendental number).  Quadratic residues, Euler's criterion and the Quadratic Reciprocity Law (with proof).  Distribution of primes. Chebyshev's Theorem.
learningOutcomes: Define the standard arithmetic functions and their properties.
Define and find continued fractions.
Prove and understand some of the theorems of classical number theory covered in the course.
State, prove and apply the Quadratic Law of Reciprocity.
Understand what is known regarding the distribution of primes.
Use methods from the course to solve problems.

modules.moduleContent:
Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quire varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 15 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.

Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quire varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 15 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.
This module will equip the student with the rudiments of theory and harmony and the foundations of musical language in order to develop the student’s music literacy.
learningOutcomes:
Recognise salient elements of musical language such as keys, 

scales, timesignatures, intervals, harmonic progressions, etc.

Read and notate music in four clefs and transpose music from one 

clef or pitch to another

modules.moduleContent:
This module consists of the following: 

1) An Introduction to the study of music at university. Students investigate practical research skills (including library and reference skills), explore writing and listening techniques and learn about the disciplines and practices associated with the study of music. 

2) A focused study of four or five works from different genres and periods. 

3) Participation in an approved departmental ensemble, and either solo performance (First Study) or group piano tuition.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate familiarity with the various disciplines of music
Understand issues of style and structure in music
Recognize and discuss a range of music genres and traditions

modules.moduleContent: Participation in an approved departmental ensemble, and either solo performance (First Study) or group piano tuition, building on semester 1.
learningOutcomes: understand issues of style and structure in music
demonstrate familiarity with performance practice in music
work alongside others in ensemble musicmaking

modules.moduleContent:
Students must consult their Mathematics & Statistics Department Course Coordinator before registering for a reading course.  Reading courses are quite varied and flexible, and details must be agreed in advance of registration with a supervisor.

The course involves directed reading and assigned exercises on a chosen topic.   Students will submit a report of approximately 25 pages and/or give a presentation on the chosen topic.

This elective module is for students of all disciplines. There are no prerequisites. Almost every day, students and graduates of all disciplines encounter situations where practical skills of empathy, creativity, collaboration and innovation are needed to resolve a difficult situation. Design is a discipline that integrates these skills to help solve problems and improve outcomes. Human-centred innovation has come of age. 
All organisation, public and private, now acknowledge that innovation is necessary for progress, Further, a better understanding of human perspectives in context is essential to guiding that innovation to be successful. This module introduces students to techniques, tools, behaviours, principles, and some underlying theory, of design that enable great innovation. 
Students learn practically through exercises and assignments, working in teams to experience the power of a systematic approach to innovation and collaboration. Students learn how to structure creativity to guarantee useful outcomes. They experience the power of empathising with target users and learn the easy-to-practise methods for acquiring a deeper empathy. Students practise these skills in class and through carrying out team assignments on interesting topics and contexts of their choice. Students also learn to appreciate the diversity of skills and contributions of teammates from various unconnected disciplines.
learningOutcomes: Recognise and appreciate different learning and communication styles.
Apply foundation principles of researching markets and human behaviour to identify market insights.
Apply entrepreneurial solutions to creative problems leading to new ventures.
Work collaboratively in selfdirect groups tasks to achieve successful outcomes.

modules.moduleContent: The aim of this module is to present a historical and a systematic account of the philosophy of science. Historically, we shall be moving from antiquity (Aristotelian worldview) to the early modern period (Newtonian worldview), to Contemporary world-view in physics (Einstein, quantum theory etc.) and biology (evolution), and systematically we shall discuss modern theories of scientific method and practice, where notions such as progress, experiment, theory, observation, discoveries, continuities, breaks, paradigms, and revolutions play a key role. The conceptual shift from a scientific approach with is base on essentialism and teleology, and to a scientific account of causes and effects to natural phenomena by means of universal and mathematical laws, will be outlined historically by recourse to the ideas of thinkers from Aristotle to Galileo. Problems with causality (and induction) will be raised in terms of Hume’s reflections on inferences. Reponses to these problems will be considered in terms of John Stuart Mill, and Karl Popper. As such our systematic discussion will include some classical theories in the philosophy of science by Karl Popper, Thomas Kuhn, Imre Lakatos, and others.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish ‘philosophy of science’ from ‘science’ in maters of methods and practices, while dealing with the questions: what is the role of the philosopher of science? what should be the relations between philosophy of science and hardcore science?

Elaborate on the process of transition of worldviews in ancient, medieval, and early modern scientific contexts. The question here is: what can an awareness of the process of transition in scientific worldviews indicate to us?

Appreciate the Aristotelian science in terms of teleology, essentialism, causes and causality, elements, movements, while looking at the questions, in what sense Aristotelian science can be regarded as ‘modern’ and in what sense it is not ‘modern’.
Discuss the transition to the Newtonian worldview, its achievements and limitations.

Analyse the scientific revolution of the 17th century by using Popper’s theory of conjectures and refutations addressing question such as what is a scientific progress, what is the connection between scientific activity and education, what is the role of history in science, what is the connection between history of science and philosophy of science and how Popper criticized the analytic philosophy of science.
Discuss the theory of evolution and its developments, from 1859 to the present day in relation to the science and religion debate which emerged in response to the theory of evolution.

Discuss Husserl's phenomenological critique of scientism and the mathematization of nature and appreciate the analytic approach to the philosophy of science, and philosophy's role as second order description.
Appreciate Hume’s contribution to the philosophy of science (the problem of induction) and John Stuart Mill and Karl Popper’s proposed solution to this ‘scandal of science’.

modules.moduleContent: Students will be expected to engage in supervised independent research on an approved topic in philosophy and to produce a written dissertation of ca. 5000 words on that topic. The format of the dissertation must conform to the guidelines set out in the Departmental Handbook for Students.
learningOutcomes: identify and choose a topic of research in philosophy;
think this topic through in a consistent manner, identifying relevant primary and secondary sources;
demonstrate mastery of the material researched;
offer a personal assessment of a significant topic of debate in philosophy;
develop skills of independent learning and of selfevaluation;
develop skills of productive communication with a supervisor;
display critical written communication skills and an appropriate style of philosophical writing;
present a scholarly academic essay following academic standards of referencing (as laid out by the MHRA Style Book).

modules.moduleContent:
This Certificate level course is designed to provide participants with the opportunity to develop an understanding of psychology and to explore its relevance to the individual, family, community and society. The course aims to introduce participants to the main psychological theories and examine their contribution to the development of the discipline. This course is aimed at people who would like a comprehensive introduction to the subject discipline of Psychology at university level.

1)  Overview of what psychology consists of, its history and study skills.
2) The Biological Basis of Behaviour; Most behaviour is rooted in our biology; this module considers the role of the brain in sleep, sensation and perception.
3) Learning and Feeling: This module focuses on how we learn, remember and experience emotions.
4) Personality: This module reviews the most important theories of personality, and how personality is assessed under each of these theories.
5) The Development of the Individual: This module considers the early development of the child from conception through to thought, language and moral development.
6) Introduction to Social Psychology: Key areas in social psychology such as social perception, attitudes and prejudice will be explored in this module.
7) Introduction to Abnormal Psychology: This module introduces students to the diagnosis, classification and treatment models in abnormal behaviour.
8) Introduction to Social Research Methodology: An introduction to the main techniques involved in social research.
learningOutcomes: Understand the main psychological theories and schools and their contribution to the development of the discipline
Understand the biological basis of behaviour
Apply critical psychological theory to the processes of learning, memory, feeling and personality development
Understand and apply psychobiological, psychodynamic, behavioral/cognitive and phenomenological approaches to lifespan development
Illustrate self directed learning by completing a research proposal with relevance to psychology and presenting this proposal to the class
Conducting a literature review for the research proposal and submitting a bibliography with the proposal
Use academic and stylistic conventions appropriately and consistently

Submit a learning journal which reflects the impact of the course on the student’s understanding of psychology and how this has influenced their knowledge of themselves, ideas, perceptions and attitudes.

modules.moduleContent: The Special Topic reading and discussion groups provide a forum within which research projects are conceived and executed under the guidance of the group director. Sign up for workshop times will be done through the PO303A module Moodle site, on a first-come, first-served basis. Details on group meeting times are available on the PO303A Moodle site at the beginning of term.  A number of compulsory shared lectures provide an overview of the research process.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the theory and concepts associated with social research methodologies
Assess the merits and limitations of various social research methodologies
Appraise research literature in a specified field of study and adjudicate its relevance to a chosen topic
Demonstrate mastery of research and analytical skills and the capacity to develop an independent and original contribution in the formulation and execution of a specified research project.

modules.moduleContent: This module will introduce students to the major themes of modern Irish history. This will include topics such as religion, politics, identity, rural and urban development. Students will be introduced to the research methods that historians use in interpreting documentary evidence and historical sources in various formats in order to gain a fuller understanding of the past.
learningOutcomes: Understand the concept and meaning of local history and recognise the central themes and challenges that historians face
Be aware of the distinction between primary and secondary sources
Be familiar with academic and stylistic conventions as these relate to the discipline of history
Write an essay on a historical topic using credible source material.
